07/06/24, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 378

“The way humans used fire made them unique. Almost all animals depend on their own bodies for power: the strength of their muscles, the size of their teeth, or the sharpness of their claws. But thanks to fire, humans gained control of a limitless source of power that had nothing to do with their bodies. A single weak human with a fire stick could burn down an entire forest in a matter of hours, destroying thousands of trees and killing thousands of animals.”
Yuval Harari      unstoppable us.

The important material required for a house construction is cement. It was under government control. So there was a scarcity in the open market leading to a higher price. The market price was very high compared to the control price - almost double.  I was looking for ways of getting cement at the control price.
There are two approaches. One was to use  the chief minister's sanction. I didn't want to use it. Another was to get it from the factory . But there was a pollution case going on against the cement factory. So I can't ask the owner . It becomes a favour. 
The second approach was to show foreign exchange remittances. I had about 1000 dollars from my visit in 1978 to the US - reimbursement for my travel expenses. So I  decided to encash the dollars. 
My cousin Jayanthi and her husband , Subramanian,  were in bangalore at that time. He was working in UCB and got a transfer to Bangalore.  He helped me, and I opened an account in UCB  . So I converted the dollars to rupees and got cement at control rates. It would meet only less than half my construction needs. I had to buy a lot from the open market at a high cost. There was a pilferage by the truck driver . Also the watchman,  appointed by the mason,  sold a few bags without our knowledge but he did it by conniving with the mason. They shared the loot. I lost the money. 
We ordered for the kitchen granite slab - a single unit integrated with a washbasin at one end and a grinding unit next to it along with a long platform for cooking purposes. 
At that time, the red oxide floor was out of fashion. Marble was yet to come in. Mosaic tiles were available. Mostly gray tiles were used by most people . I used white mosaic tiles. It was expensive , but gave a great appearance.  We bought tiles from the Parijatha tiles factory in yeshwantpur.  Laying these tiles, grinding , polishing were skilled jobs and took a month almost.  
We knew Mr Mahanthesh , who was working at Central Power Research Institute. Mr KVK Premkumar, my ground floor neighbour and a great friend ,  introduced him to me. I entrusted the electrical and painting work to him. Mahantesh was with me for more than 25 years doing all my electrical and painting work. He was a great help for us. 
After we got loan approval, we started with a Bhoomi Puja in July 1982. It took more than a year to complete. 
More later.

“If a man walk in the woods for love of them half of each day, he is in danger of being regarded as a loafer; but if he spends his whole day as a speculator, shearing off those woods and making earth bald before her time, he is esteemed an industrious and enterprising citizen. As if a town had no interest in its forests but to cut them down!”
Thoreau

Let me look at a comment by Prof Vasudev Parvati. It gives me happiness when I see people reading the quotes in my post. I received a few comments from some readers. This comment is on reading - quote by Thoreau in post 377.

“It's a really beautiful quote on reading, sir! I don't think many of us employ that much of intensity in reading a book!”

My response
Thanks.
The habit of normal reading and casual reading is reducing. I have found that voracious readers are leaders in society. Also , Readers become thinkers. 
Life is not worth living if one doesn't read at least a good non fiction every year. 
I had a very unhappy incident / shock that happened to me. I was asked to talk to the faculty individually in an engineering college . I spent a week talking to them individually. 
One question I asked to every one was “ what general books did you read in the last year? “ The reply was shocking. None have read a book in a year or a few years. That was shocking. I feel that  reading habits have disappeared from many faculty.  But, fortunately  it exists in many people still. Many do intense reading. So both opposites exist. 
Reading a book is only the first step. The important question is the remembering power. That is where Thoreau makes important observations. Some have a capacity to remember pertinent points from a book they read decades ago. It is due to the concentration in reading and the impact of certain statements. 
Let me give my experience here. 
I had interviewed hundreds for faculty positions in many engineering colleges. When I asked them about ,  say,  concurrency or the third normal form , many used to reply that they had forgotten  . Some couldn't talk about logical independence in database systems.  That is the sad state of learning and remembering. How will they teach, if selected?
So we should heed to Thoreau and read with more attention.  
In Indian households of the yester years , reading was a sacred activity. Ladies read Ramayana or Mahabharata everyday for about an hour. That was our tradition.
We had a lot of manuscripts in palm leafs in my house in the 1940s and 50s .
I started reading during my school days itself. I was reading one fiction book per week. When I went to college, I diversified my reading to include English classics , English humour , biography and history books and Shakespeare. This is in addition to text books and engineering books borrowed from the college library.This continued till I joined IISc. At IISc, my interests increased to technology books, journals , research articles and seminal and classic books. I started reading philosophical books . I read Newton, Bertrand Russell, Whitehead , Plato and Upanishads and many more authors.  Past two decades, I have been focusing on mostly non fiction books and concentrated on technology, AI, cognition, psychology, Indian philosophy , Upanishads and biography. The habit of reading for me started at a young age and so it was easy to continue that practice.

But many will have to be super smart to avoid disasters and slavery.

AI is not intelligent. It is nowhere near humans. So AI,  in the beginning , will lead to a lot of teething troubles and will not help us. There is a big learning curve. Who will learn rightly, who will not learn rightly , who will misuse - we don't know. It heralds uncertainty and crises. It won't help in improving our life, building a harmonious society and increasing jobs. 
We need to evolve strong, controlled , human driven methodologies to curb independence of AI. We need a good , focussed learning strategy and systems very different from current education system.current education system is not capable of handling AI. It is a copy paste system. 
AI warrants humans to go for higher conscious levels. Humans need to query, think, analyse, contemplate , be alert  put in controls, curb AI independence. So they need a higher cognition and conscious level.

“Thinking is no more than a tiny aspect of the totality of consciousness, the totality of who you are.”
“You then no longer derive your identity, your sense of who you are, from the incessant stream of thinking that in the old consciousness you take to be yourself. What a liberation to realize that the “voice in my head” is not who I am.”

There is another comment by Dr Sanjay Chitnis on my earlier quotes. It is a deep one. 

“Dear Sir, in yesterday's post 352, you gave a quote that mentioned stoic philosophy and today you started quoting the Stithaprajna and the most important part of our Sanatana Dharma. How do you compare the two - stoic living and living as sthithaprajna?”

My response. 
This needs a detailed reply. 
First of all, we need to know the differences between western philosophy and Indian philosophy. 
The western philosophy initially focused on human behaviours . Socrates and Plato insisted on goodness , integrity, ethics, 
and compassion for people. The focus afterwards was more on humans. By middle ages , this aspect shifted to psychology. Still Kant , Santayana, Russell,  Whitehead , Wittgenstein  concentrated on humans . Some looked at nature. 
The western philosophy was and is looking at knowns. So they ignored the unknowns. Astronomy entered the domain of scientists like Galileo only in the middle ages. 
The quest of all philosophies is to find out the truth. The western philosophy started to depend on rational approaches like logic and mathematics. Some used linguistic approaches. But it was restricted to “ understanding known observed facts and phenomena and trying to explain their behaviours”. It ignored unknown and unobservable phenomena. It felt religion is a mystery and steered clear of it. 

Indian philosophy is the opposite. It looked for unknowns at the higher level of vedanta. That purifies and elevates the mind and provides the clarity between real and temporary. It discusses Illusions leading to mistakes. So it goes beyond normal sensory perceptions and uses consciousness at many levels to understand and explain facts. It wants correctness and clarity. It uses language, logic , analysis, and mathematics  but it goes beyond them to look for unknowns. It also gives a primacy for mind over matter. 
Indian system is multidimensional. 
It  didn't ignore the human system  . Yoga provides eight way approaches - ashtanga yoga. It considers character as important for living. So it defines fos and don'ts for people and yoga practitioners. These are the yamas and niyamas. These are quite detailed. Then it considers a unifying energy called  prana and suggests ways to improve this which improves blood circulation, increases oxygen intake and regulates breathing. It also calms the mind and reduces the pulse rate . 
Next, yoga gives exercises for keeping the body in shape. Meditation , concentration and contemplation improves the mind  .
Also we have vaiseshika and sankhya  dealing with the material world including nature. It is an  integrating system. 

It is a brief explanation of the six darshanas. 
Let us discuss  stoic philosophy in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks

10/06/24, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 381
What is Stoicism?
 Let me quote the three philosophers involved in developing this philosophy. 

“ In life our first job is this, to divide and distinguish things into two categories: externals I cannot control, but the choices I make with regard to them I do control. Where will I find good and bad? In me, in my choices.” 
~ Epictetus
The Stoic principle, however, exhorted the achieving of happiness despite the ruinous situations in which one might find oneself. Nowhere did the teaching guarantee that you would not fall upon misfortune. 
 Instead, it encouraged its subjects to face hard times from a reasonable and practical perspective. Instead of cursing fate or the things that affect and cause distress in our lives, Stoicism teaches control of the emotions by looking at the cause of distress objectively. 
Stoicism is the practice of directing your mind and actions. You were born into a world where you learn to respond or react to what happens in the environment around you.
First time the concept of mind was accepted. But this was discarded till 19th century. 

   Stoicism appeared in 3BCE—an ancient school of philosophy that advocated a kind of mental discipline and detachment from external forces. Those who abided by these principles were known as Stoics. They included Zeno of Citium (Stoicism’s founder), Epictetus, Seneca, Marcus Aurelius, and many more.
According to Zeno’s philosophy,  happiness is a virtue. It was held in the same high regard as wisdom, justice, faith, temperance and charity. 
 The philosophy asserts that you cannot control the occurrences, events or situations coming at you from external sources. 
 You cannot rely on or put your faith in them. You can only rely on yourself and your response to them. The philosophy effectively determines that you have control over what you think and can do, and therefore, you can choose how you respond to these events.
Having developed the philosophy of control of behavior and of steadfastness in the face of misfortune, Zeno began teaching at the famous Stoa Poikile in the ancient Angora located in Athens. Hence  the name stoicism.

Seneca stated so well in the first century of the common era, in his essay “On the Shortness of Life”:
“Were all the geniuses of history to focus on this single theme, they could never fully express their bafflement at the darkness of the human mind. No person would give up even an inch of their estate, and the slightest dispute with a neighbor can mean hell to pay; yet we easily let others encroach on our lives—worse, we often pave the way for those who will take it over. No person hands out their money to passersby, but to how many do each of us hand out our lives! We’re tight-fisted with property and money, yet think too little of wasting time, the one thing about which we should all be the toughest misers. “
A very illuminating statement to understand and follow. Why do you rent your mind to others when you guard your physical possessions?
More in the next.

Regarding  your next question on leaning on to or away from AI, 
My response is:
Do you have a choice?
We don't have a choice. We have to accept new technologies or be left behind. 
The question is how do I  adapt to AI. 
It is a lot  tougher than adapting to steam engines. 
But in those days technologies like steam engines or electricity didn't affect common people immediately. It took years for electricity to reach homes and centuries for us to use electric appliances. 
That wasn't the case with computers and definitely AI is in a hurry to attack most of us within a decade. 
So getting adjusted to AI is important. 
As Alistair Maclean wrote the novel “ Fear is the key” in 1960 , we have to understand the fear and respond correctly and positively. There are many supporters of AI and many doomsday people against AI. 
Let us be realistic. AI is there. We will see many new generations of AI coming in the next few years. 
So be prepared.
Accept AI. Adjust to to it. Take advance measures to protect order. Remember the Taittiriya upanishad
Rtm vadishyami.
Rtm is global order. Respect nature. 
AI will shortly make our jobs redundant .
AI is better than people with average intelligence. 


Your last question of making AI to manage humans is dangerous but will not happen now , but  may be much later. It will take a directional change to move in the direction of managing our mind. Neurologists are working on it, not AI people. That is the stage when we should be cautious . Unlike cloning research, control of mind management research is not easy ,as it will be carried out by many people.  but the control and directioning need to be done.

Harari predicts that there will be two types of religion which will emerge due to man’s quest for happiness, divinity, and peace: Techno-humanism and Data Religion. The former speaks of man still hanging on to his authority over his decisions while the latter is about man relying on data in making decisions for him.
The future is seen with humans relying heavily on data and disregarding their inner voice in making decisions. Man is foreseen to be constantly connected to the internet and constantly feeding it data for the use of the society. Although there is no certainty to this prediction, the current events show that this future is very likely to happen unless man changes his actions and steers the future in another direction.
See the dangers.

11/06/24, 4:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 382

Whereas stoics considered  the mind to be important in living , stithaprajna goes beyond giving a compelete description and prescription. It talks about buddhi part of mind. It wants one to have stronger control of ego , senses and emotions. It looks for a no desire state.  It is explained in Gita  .
Sthitaprajña (स्थितप्रज्ञ). is defined as a person firm in judgment or wisdom, free from any hallucination, calm  and contented. It starts with minimising wants , controlling emotions, following yamas and niyamas and keeping the mind calm and clear of biases. It leads to single focussed concentration towards self awareness to be obtained in stages.  It moves towards renunciation and self awareness through focused meditation. 
According to the Gita, a sthitaprajna:
1.Is free from worldly attachments and aversions
2.Does not need to seek the truth because he sees truth
3.Is free from motives and ego
4.Has attained full knowledge of the Ultimate Reality
5.Does good deeds with no expectations of reward
6.Is aware of the oneness of Reality

There are many characteristics and many hard steps needed to become a stithaprajna. 
Some are listed below 
1.The person who is not disturbed by the fluctuations of sukha and dukha is eligible to reclaim their own indestructible and immortal nature.
2.That which is asat - false will not endure, while that which is sat - truth can never cease to exist. Both these states of being are known by the seers as that which is tattva ‘always true’.
3.Arjuna, these dehas - body  which must someday end and then be burned are inhabited by an undying, indestructible, immeasurable being.
4.For those attached to enjoying material pleasures, their immortal potential is distorted and stolen away. As a result, they do not have the discernment to see their atma or taste the greater pleasures of the highest states of transcendental samadhi.
5.Because you have free will, you can control your actions, but you are never in control of the fruit or ultimate outcome of what you do.
Therefore, never let attachment to the fruits be the ultimate reason for your actions. Conversely, do not simply retreat into a state of detached inaction.
6.Instead, perform all your actions in a state of yogic awareness, without attachment to the result, undisturbed by pursing siddhis ‘perfections’ or avoiding asiddhi ‘imperfection’ in whatever you do. It is said that such balanced consciousness is called karma yoga.
7.Listen, Arjuna, buddhi yoga is greatly superior to mere material actions. Take refuge in loving surrender One who is permanently linked to the buddhi faculty goes beyond the relativity of sukha and dukha as the determiner of what they do.-to the wise discernment of the buddhi faculty.
8.When your buddhi remains steady and unwavering in meditation upon  your atma ‘true self’, you will no longer be distracted with the details of vedic rituals. Then, absorbed in samadhi, you will directly perceive your true nature and achieve the perfection of buddhi yoga.
A person whose manas is not disturbed in times of dukha ‘pain’; who is not controlled by the need for sukha ‘pleasure’; who has overcome all attachment, fear, and anger; and whose focus upon their atma is always steady is called a muni.
See the cause effect sequences explained in Gita. 
While contemplating the objects of the senses, attachment to them is born. From such attachment, intense desires arise.From unfulfilled desires, the seeds of anger appear.
From unrestrained anger, delusion arises. From this delusion, memory is lost. When memory is lost, discernment is lost. When discernment is lost, this leads to harmful or destructive actions.
When the atma controls the indriyas ‘material senses’ and when one no longer experiences compulsive attachment or aversion to the objects of the senses, then the atma attains a tranquil, divine state known as prasada.
When the buddhi is not connected with the atma, and when the manas is not steady, there is no shanti. Without shanti, how can there be lasting sukha?
When the manas runs after the objects of the wandering indriyas ‘senses’, they carry away one’s prajna, exactly as a ship on the water is driven by a strong wind.
So it is a final state. But it has many prescriptions. It is holistic . 
That is what a highly conscious state implies. 
Greetings 
Dks
12/06/24, 4:08 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 383
‘Who, again, is more heartless than he, who, though possessed of affluence, eateth himself and weareth excellent robes himself without distributing his wealth  to his dependents? ‘
Vidura niti

Let me return to my activities during the year 1982. 
TCS , as discussed before,  had  set up the research centre at Pune. Mr Kohli wanted me to visit the centre . They wanted me to join that centre. Prof EC Subba Rao from IIT Kanpur , a materials engineering expert , joined as director of the research centre. The centre was named as Tata RDD centre.  I took my family to Pune for a three day trip. 
Prof Rao put in a lot of effort to attract me to the centre. Knowing my interest in rural technologies, he arranged a visit to some villages where Tata trust was working. It was a one day affair.  
He also arranged a visit to Mr Salunkye who runs pani panchayat in many villages. It was very impressive.  The idea was water distribution is more important than land distribution. So he wanted villages to join in this scheme voluntarily. They should accept the following. 
1.No Sugarcane cultivation. 
2.A water distributor is appointed . Everyone get equal water rights, not proportional to their land holding  He regulates water supply to the lands and houses.
3. Those who didn't have land also  get their water rights. They can sell it to anyone. 
This is the first experiment on using common resources equitably . 
I liked Dr Subba Rao's understanding of what was happening in Pune within a short time of his arrival there. It was really great. 
My wife visited a speech centre. Nothing was going on in the Tata RDD  centre wrt computer science research. Rao was planning to get a few people to work on biotechnology. Funding was open ended. 
We spent three days there . There was nothing to see or no one to discuss with in the centre except my discussions with Dr Rao. 
We discussed the research scenario in computer science in India and why it wasn't picking speed. That conversation was quite interesting. We identified many resource problems like availability of computers , hardware, software systems - even Unix was not available in india then - , databases - we didn't have a relational database in india then -. I didn't get any assurance from Dr Rao that TCS will provide these resources. He just commiserated  with me on the sad state in India. 
I didn't see any glimmer of hope there  . So  I wasn't keen on joining the centre. We went to Bombay from Pune by Deccan queen.  I met Nirmal jain. 
Actually I went to the 11th floor of the Air India building by elevator. My family stayed downstairs. My daughter was not comfortable with the elevator.  I told him that I am not interested in joining the Tata RDD centre. When I and Nirmal  came down by the elevator, Nirmal met my family. Later he visited us at our bangalore residence. 
 Later , it was proved  that  my decision was correct. The centre grew very slowly concentrating only on materials research . Even biotechnology didn't pick up. It took more than a decade for the centre to get into software tools research. I always believed that a viable minimum group of researchers - a critical mass - were required to come up with meaningful activity. I suffered for more than a decade being a lone CS researcher at IISc. The other but  most important aspect was that I will miss interactions with young and fresh students. I had a belief that association with young minds is essential to keep our mind sharp and searching. I recalled the fact that Sir CV Raman was not in favor of setting up CSIR labs by Nehru and Bhatnagar. 
I felt a university with a strong research culture and teaching with a great ambience for work is the well suited place for  me. So it had to be only IISc  for me. That was keeping me happy in my life. Plus my interests in  multiple areas of research and many other activities made me reject the offer.
 I had done this , rejecting offers, many times during my career. If I had taken up every offer that came my way , how would I have developed myself and would I have been happy? Now,  I think providence helped me a lot in rejecting those offers. Good sense prevailed. 
More in the next.

But on social media, the platform presents itself as the owner of the channel, but not the content.
So the licensing is an idea proposed to regulate what they can channel, to whom they can channel, and how they can channel. 
Do you have any ideas on how this tool can be used not only to regulate, but also to create a body of experts who can work continuously to improve the licensing in such a way that it remains meaningful as the world changes with technology?”

I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately, to front only the essential facts of life, and see if I could not learn what it had to teach, and not, when I came to die, discover that I had not lived. I did not wish to live what was not life, living is so dear; nor did 
I wish to practice resignation, unless it was quite  necessary. 
Walden,      Thoreau 

After returning from Pune, I told Mr Anagol, secretary to CM and science and technology,  about pani panchayat. He liked the idea and suggested that we should explore that in Karnataka. I talked to Mr Bhattacharya,  secretary , rural development and panchayat Raj. But we couldn't take it forward. 
We were looking at increasing the tree cover in the state in a sustainable, environment friendly way. There were many experiments going on in the country. Bharatiya  Agro Industries Foundation had been working on many livelihood experiments.  So while at Pune, I arranged for a visit to BAIF.
I also visited Uruli kanchan village near Pune to look at the activities of BAIF. They were propagating the planting of a babul tree christened as subabul. They had done a good job of propagating this tree. They created an economic model for a family and demonstrated it. 
At that time, Karnataka was also popularizing this species .  Our forest and agriculture departments were planting millions of saplings . It was found successful in Bijapur. We visited Bijapur and saw the saplings growing well. The agriculture department was responsible for this work. So we decided to conduct a two day seminar inviting all district deputy commissioners and CEOs of the Zilla panchayats. So I went again for the seminar - two visits to Bijapur. . The development commissioner inaugurated it. I met Mr Veerakyataiah who was the deputy secretary of the rural development department in that seminar. 
I went to Bellary on the way to Bijapur for the seminar   to look at the salinity problem in the downstream fields of the TB dam. The reason was seepage of water from one of the main canals into the fields. I met the DC- equivalent to district collector in many states-  of Bellary district , Mr Gokulram , a very nice person. The dam was managed by the central government and the state didn't have control over it. But the situation was bad. About 50000 ha of land was lost to cultivation.  I learnt one more aspect of negative design. Don't run a canal at a higher level to the adjoining fields or line them well. 
I didn't neglect my family activities. I attended my daughter's sports day . She got a cup . I attended my son's school day function. He was dressed like Bharati and he was in a boat along with his friend Nikhil. Nikhils father Kasturi and mother Uma  became our friends . We were in contact with them and are in touch with his mother and sisters . Unfortunately Nikhil died in a motorcycle accident in Mysore.  He was a very sweet smiling boy  . 
Another task was to get primary school admission for my son. So as my daughter was in KV IISc,  I applied for his admission. Some people objected to his admission as I was in the state government.  But I told the school that I was on lien from IISc and will return to IISc service shortly. So my son got admission in KV IISc.  Both children traveled to the  school by iisc bus together.

“There is one only defect in forgiving persons, and not another; that defect is that people take a forgiving person to be weak.
That defect, however, should not be taken into consideration, for forgiveness is a great power. Forgiveness is a virtue of the weak, and an ornament of the strong. “
Vidura niti

Let me discuss my role on the world environment day held on June 5,1982. 
We decided to conduct it for a week across the state , not just in Bangalore. We chose six cities. We chose an agency which has a mobile exhibit of environmental posters and some devices. The agency agreed to travel through the six centres. 
I visited Shimoga, Hubli, Belgaum , and Sirsi , not in that order. I went with my family. In Sirsi, I was taken to a falls - Mathighatta falls. It had a potential for a micro hydel plant. We stayed there for three days. I addressed a public meeting also in kannada. All my speeches were in Kannada now. My children went to Byrumbe and saw a different rural setting. They saw the arecanut plants, pepper and cardamom plants. My son liked the papad - jackfruit  papad - and he called it Byrumbe happala . He liked it a lot. We used to specially look for it in Bangalore or other cities  and buy it . 
I also had a very tight schedule on June 5 th at Bangalore. I talked in several functions from morning to evening including in  the University of agricultural sciences. I made a remark on vidhana soudha  that the canteen there was using half a ton of firewood every day for cooking . We needed to reduce this. It came as a front page bold box item in the newspapers the next day stating that Soudha logs a half ton everyday. Prof CNR Rao also talked in a meeting. His talk didn't appear in the paper. He commented on this to me the next day. 
There were a number of pollution cases pending both with the government and courts. The pollution control board was not effective. While the secretary was clear in his statements, the chairman was evasive , giving excuses instead of action. He was good at inventing excuses. 
The finance minister ,Mr Veerappa Moily called a meeting. He was also the tourism minister. He was worried about the impact of pollution on our monuments. Two important tourism spots were Belur and Shravanabelagola . Belur has the famous chennakeshava temple and shravanabelagola has the lord gomateshwara statue.  
There was a plywood factory close to the Chennakeshava temple. They burnt the waste plywoods in their boiler. The chemical fumes affected the small soapstone figurines in the temple.
The minister wanted us to stop that factory producing chemical fumes. According to the board , the factory doesn't want to change the boiler to oil firing. They said they had no legal mechanism to take action. The case was going on for several years. The board got strong technical opinion from Prof AR Vasudevamurthy , our IPC professor. He said these fumes coming out of the factory will destroy the figurines over time.  The board didn't take action for five years. The minister asked me to visit and submit a report. 
Similarly , a lot of quarrying work was going on in the hills on which the huge lord gomateshwara statue was located. The minister wanted that to be stopped. 
Let me discuss the next steps in the next post.

14/06/24, 5:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Mr Bala has more comments:
I am not sure how a person's personality can be changed through regulations as the traits of that personality may have to be altered to change a person from a bad person to a good person. It can be viewed as moving away from a certain set of undesired values towards another set of desired values. 
How each person constructs a certain  personality can be nurtured through multiple stimulation points comprising of a sense of belonging to family or religion or national or community values. 
Nurturing transitioning into regulation, or should, needs to be determined in terms of both time and the level of regulation. 
The level of regulation should be dependent on the level of nurturing that has or has not taken place before a person transitions into adulthood. 
So it comes to each individual family getting some help to nurture their offsprings into adulthood. When this help is lacking or not available, regulation might be the way to go to decide the time and the level.

This is not a solution by any means but comments that are intended to help a move towards a solution.

I visited the two places - Shravanabelagola and Belur both in Hassan district - along with the chairman of the board, superintending archaeologist, and the DC of Hassan district .  We first went to shravanabelagola as it was on the way. We saw the extensive quarrying going on in both hills - vindhyagiri and chandragiri- and felt bad. But I was surprised at the reaction of the archeology department. They looked unconcerned.
 We visited the temple at Belur next. It is a temple with a remarkable architecture , sculpture and arts. It was constructed by the Hoysala  King vishnuvardhana. He was converted to Vaishnavism by Sri Ramanuja. But his wife Shantala remained a jain. She showed a lot of interest in the temple construction. The temple at halebid was also constructed in his time. The temple at Somnathpur was also constructed at this time - 12th or 13 th century. Jakkannacharya was the architect for both temples.   There are several stone panels with soapstone carvings of many aspects of life . It is a famous world tourist attraction with no tourist infrastructure. We saw the effect of pollution on the flag pole - dhwaja stambha.  It had become black. I wasn't  happy even  with the puja being done in the  temple. Temple lamps and camphor are producing vapors. They can also affect the sculptures and arts. 
On my return from Hassan   ,  I submitted a report to the chief secretary on the sad state of affairs.  Mr R A Naik had become the chief secretary by then. So he called a meeting , and the revenue secretary was also present  . He said that action can be taken even under the public nuisance act  . I suggested that there is a rule in the museums act on actions to be taken against those defacing the monuments.  so I said we can take actions on that basis  . It was decided to issue directions to DC and SP of Hassan to stop both activities. I prepared the directions - it is the first time to format a direction and I did it within an hour and I quoted the rule in the museums act and in pollution control act  , and took it to the CS- and it was issued under the CS signature  immediately within an hour of the meeting . I skipped my lunch that day. 
The SP immediately seized the trucks by the quarrying person in Shravanabelagola   . He also sealed the plywood factory . So we saw action,  where the board was sleeping for more than five years. 
It was a satisfying moment for me.

There were several water pollution problems with most of the rivers in Karnataka.  As an additional secretary of environment, I had to address this aspect also. I got a complaint from Nanjangudu city  about the contamination of drinking water. I went on an inspection trip along with the board chairman. I found that most industries in that area were discharging their waste water without treatment. Most industries resist setting up  treatment plants and operating them .  Also  there was another design error in water intake for city water supply   The drinking water to the city is from the Kabini River. The inlet for the drinking water was placed after the wastewater was  discharged by the industries.  So  ,  only polluted water was supplied to houses  . It was not potable. We had a public meeting. Industries were not prepared to treat their waste water. They wanted the government to put up treatment plants. I told them that there is a law which restricts the BOD , COD and other elements in their wastewater discharges . They were not even removing the suspended particles by a simple filter. 
I also told them that BOD means energy. Put up a simple biogas plant and use the gas to fire their boilers. Biogas technology is pretty simple enough to implement and run. I told them that they are violating the law. The law also wanted them to get consent from the pollution control board and most had not obtained consent. The chairman of the board was present and he kept quiet. 
I said that we don't want to take them to the court for action. So it was in their interest to treat the water. Otherwise the municipality may taken action on them with people's agitation growing day by day with increased awareness of the reasons for pollution. 
But the industrialists felt that they can't afford a treatment plant. 
There were many such water pollution problems in the state. There was a rayon factory in Harihar city on the banks of Tnga Bhadra River. They used wood to produce the Fibre and discarded the lignin and chemicals into the river without treatment. It affected the health of people living downstream of the river  and people didn't get proper potable water. This case was in the courts for years. Solution was not possible. Because the only solution was to close down the factory. As cotton was available in plenty and a mix of cotton with terene/ nylon became popular, the demand for rayon went down. The factory closed down after several years due to a lack of demand for rayon fibre. That shows their regard for people and environment  .

Vaiseshika 

One important thing in every one's life is to answer the questions:
How do I live my life? 
What are the qualities one should acquire and what qualities one should discard? 
How do I  acquire good qualities?
Let me discuss my life.  I got guidance from my father, grandfather  , uncles initially,  then from my teachers , friends and colleagues. There was a lot of learning through observations of human behaviors. I did a lot of introspection to learn from many mistakes I made during my interactions and due to my actions. But I also learnt a lot from observing many leaders . I had good interactions with many academic , industry, bank and government leaders. I found that attitudes and behaviors of many leaders are different and success does not have a cocktail of a few fixed behaviors.  It is multidimensional and depends on flexibilty, adaptation and a quick understanding of the existing situation inside and outside as well as  adjustments to the existing reality .  I summarize some of my learnings.
I met a large number of heads of institutions- industry CEOs, IIT directors , university vice chancellors, chairmen and principals of colleges , heads of government  , secretaries, ministers, chairmen / MDs of banks,trustees of NGOs , spiritual leaders etc. There was a continuity of looking at successors in many , to name a few-  industries like Tatas, TCS, Wipro, kirloskar electric  ,banks like  Indian Bank, corporation Bank, PNB  , syndicate bank, Vijaya bank, universities like Bangalore, Mysore, Karnataka,  directors of IITs  and NITs  , principals of many colleges like SSIT, SIT, RVCE, MSRIT, BMSCE,  SDMCE, TCE, MCE, BIET, UBDTCE, NMAMIT, and many secretaries of government departments. This continous contacts and observations helped in my understanding different leadership styles and their impacts on the organization.  

It was my serious hobby to study them, observe their decision making capacities , their approach to problems, their success rates, their support structures  etc. I have participated in hundreds of decisions by many such heads. It was a rewarding experience and definitely a learning experience. I learnt many pitfalls , many strict no nos, many good approaches. I can write a book. I have consolidated some observations and will share them here.
 Let me categorize many heads of organizations as:
Those who didn't do anything worthwhile, but allowed the organization to run.
Those who followed the rules strictly without discretion . A clerk can do this job. You don't need a senior officer or manager for this job. Most colleges, government offices follow this model. When I became the dean , I told my deputy registrar that he should follow rules and I will break the rules with enough reason. When necessary , I will go to the senate to approve  a different decision.  
Those who don't delegate work . I know a principal of an engineering college who will spend several minutes sanctioning a casual one day leave to an employee . He spent his time only on such trivial matters.  I was sitting with him and   saw this  happening  . I know a chairman of KEB who will handle power failure problems in the houses of people, not necessarily his friends. He was still doing operational jobs of an assistant engineer, not strategic ones. 
There are some who delegate everything . I know a HoD who did post office work. As soon as he got a paper or request or letter, he will pass it on to a faculty , maybe randomly. If the person to whom he forwarded a paper says that it is not in his domain, the HoD will ask him a suggestion as to whom it should go and forwarded accordingly.

Good comments and questions.  Let me go step by step to address the problem. 
First:
Conscious living is not good living or ideal living . It is the first step in living. 
We need many more steps. One of the important steps is to develop proper  social skills. 
Behaviours are normally built at childhood by the family in the  house, teachers and classmates in the  school and people in the society. Normally it stays with us. 
But it is very different now. There are powerful influencers. Lots of people listen to these influencers.  Influencers used to be religious and pious people in the early days. But not so. There are powerful social and political influencers  today , hundreds of them flooding YouTube with venom  . They don't follow any rules or laws, don't care about impacts. So we need to  put the fear of god on the influencers. So regulation is definitely needed. Fake news is a bigger and hidden menace. It can destroy people .  Only regulation can resolve this. 
 Punish them for missteps or fakes or misuses. 
So we need a stricter law. Apologies won't do. They are meaningless  . Here we need regulation. 
Also it is not an either or situation. We cannot ignore nurture and education. We seem to be ignoring this aspect of education  both at the family level and school level.

“These two persons never shine because of their incompatible acts, viz., a householder without exertion, and a beggar busied in schemes. “
“These two, O king, live (as it were) in a region higher than heaven itself, viz., a man of power endued with forgiveness, and poor man that is charitable.”
Vidura niti

Let me continue my observations further. 
There are those who don't take decisions at all  . They delay decisions hoping problems disappear. I know one HoD at IISc who used to put papers inside his desk without taking action. 
Those who took decisions based on a trusted person's advice. This is a coterie based leadership. 
The above six do not reflect initiative, but don't destroy the organization. 

The most important component I saw in most leaders is the commitment to work. While most were committed to their work, some were committed to social causes also. There were many who wanted a better Bangalore.  I saw the pursuit of Mr AS Lakshman  , chairman, Senapathy whitlay company  for the growth of industries in bangalore. I also saw the phenomenal interest that Mr SG Ramachandra , senior VP, kirloskar electric Ltd,  took in the matters of Bangalore and energy development . He also ran the Gokhale institute branch at Malleswaram. Mr Agarwal ,MD of Bhoruka group showed interest in bangalore's water and power problems. 
Another component that I saw in some was the great interpersonal communication skills exhibited by them . Some like Ramadorai , CEO of TCS are very soft spoken and don't get angry. Some are pleasant and tough . Mr KC Reddy who became secretary to the PWD department in the government of Karnataka and also held many senior positions was a pleasant person . Another quality I saw in him was his active participation in helping his subordinates, in encouraging them and in promoting them. Mr. Karumuthu kannan, a textile magnate and chairman of TCE , was another pleasant person interested in the progress of his college and also in the general growth of the state. 
Those who had strong beliefs and likes and dislikes and took the organization in their desired path. This curbs the progress of the organization and creates discords. 
This may lead to chaos in the organization and a lot of unhappiness. 
There are some who are power and status hungry. They are self centred. Many managements of private engineering colleges that I knew personally , were ignorant of academics and education and concentrated mostly on the construction and day to day firefighting. They retained control on everything and didn't give freedom to the principal. That was the negative side. 
There are some who strongly believed that discipline and total obedience  are the only aspects needed to run an organization. Many institutions were run on this premise. They produced slaves.

Vaiseshika 

There are some who are flexible and open minded ,but weak and take decisions democratically by listening to all. It may weaken the organization. Courage is essential in leadership. Compassion is needed upto a limit. Decide whether your priority is your organization or your people. If the organization survives, then only the people in it will survive. 
There are many great learners who also put in a lot of effort  . Most leaders from family business and first time entrepreneurs fall in this category. I had seen a person like Mr K Easwaran, director of Easun Engineering company and MD of Hackbridge Hewittic and Easun , who didn't know any engineering and had no engineering background. He built his business from nothing. He was a first time entrepreneur. But his grasp was amazing. He had great language skills. I had a lot of encounters with him. He pinpointed the fallacy in an argument and detected problems in advance ,and  didn't wait for them to happen. He visited the factory everyday. He knew the market, the economics and customers. But most importantly  ,  his interpersonal skills were great and wonderful. He wasn't biased. He got good talents from across india. I met many non-Tamils in the factory for the first time including a Britisher.  He assessed people, built long term relationships and maintained cordial relationships with customers, competitors, governments and suppliers. He personally used to take all heads and senior officers  of electricity boards and government heads through the factory explaining every aspect of production and quality control  . He understood marketing better. I know some MDs who considered marketing inferior to production. That is a biased mindset. This is a lesson for startups to learn and follow.

There are some deep thinkers, who analyzed the problems, discussed the problems with others and made decisions. Some had multiple interests.  Mr Jayakumar Anagol is a good example . Mr V Balasubramanian,  who became the secretary to the CM - Mr. Ramakrishna Hegde who became the CM after Mr. Gundu Rao-  after Mr Anagol was also an inventor. 
There are disciplined and focused thinkers and implementers and innovators  with a broad view of the world  like Sir M V . He was also a multidimensional thinker and visionary. His self  discipline is famous.
The biggest mistake the governments commit today is the belief that managing an organization is purely based on administration and regulation. So they think the generalist IAS will manage anything. In today's world or even yesterday's world, this is not true. Organizations depend on people. Most ignore that. Secondly , organizations depend on transformations. Most government organizations run with a mind of  vegetables- instead of a thinking mind. While operational aspects are important, transformations lead to long term survival. Thirdly, motivation and innovation are to be encouraged. These are missing today. Why do we find most government departments and public sectors used dated policies and dated approaches? That is because of the straight jacket approach of the bosses and total faith in rules . That is why private systems thrive . Government systems stagnate. Occasional sparks keep the government systems moving forward. That is mostly accidental or political. I am not even talking about corruption and servile behaviors.

21/06/24, 5:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 524
The problems raised by Bala Ajjampur are less serious, but important , compared to other life skills that our education system ignores. 
That  second aspect is interpersonal skills. I quote the following from David Brooks’  book “How to know a person”.
“Being open-hearted is a prerequisite for being a full, kind, and wise human being. But it is not enough. People need social skills. We talk about the importance of “relationships,” “community,” “friendship,” “social connection,” but these words are too abstract. The real act of, say, building a friendship or creating a community involves performing a series of small, concrete social actions well: disagreeing without poisoning the relationship; revealing vulnerability at the appropriate pace; being a good listener; knowing how to end a conversation gracefully; knowing how to ask for and offer forgiveness; knowing how to let someone down without breaking their heart; knowing how to sit with someone who is suffering; knowing how to host a gathering where everyone feels embraced; knowing how to see things from another’s point of view.
These are some of the most important skills a human being can possess, and yet we don’t teach them in school. Some days it seems like we have intentionally built a society that gives people little guidance on how to perform the most important activities of life. As a result, a lot of us are lonely and lack deep friendships. It’s not because we don’t want these things. Above almost any other need, human beings long to have another person look into their face with loving respect and acceptance. It’s that we lack practical knowledge about how to give each other the kind of rich attention we desire. I’m not sure Western societies were ever great at teaching these skills, but over the past several decades, in particular, there’s been a loss of moral knowledge. Our schools and other institutions have focused more and more on preparing people for their careers, but not on the skills of being considerate toward the person next to you. The humanities, which teach us what goes on in the minds of other people, have become marginalized. And a life spent on social media is not exactly helping people learn these skills. On social media you can have the illusion of social contact without having to perform the gestures that actually build trust, care, and affection. On social media, stimulation replaces intimacy. There is judgment everywhere and understanding nowhere.”
So the purpose is social skills plus job skills  . Job skills alone are useless if social skills are missing. 
But does education fulfill this purpose. The answer is no. The earlier Indian gurukula system was holistic. It taught them manners, behaviours,  habits, attitudes , habits etc. It gave directions to lead a humane life  . But it is not so now  .
We don’t have a solution  now . Maybe an individual AI assistant seems to be one answer. One to one teaching   attention to each student, education tailored to each students capabilities and needs are possible through specially tailored AI tools. Special individual AI assistant will be able to mentor and guide a person to master social skills. It may also be a fake reducer. 
Greetings 
Dks
22/06/24, 4:33 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 393
“autobiography is much too grand a word. It suggests a purposeful study of one’s whole life. It implies names, dates and places in tidy chronological order. What I want is to plunge my hand into a lucky dip and come up with a handful of assorted memories.”
Agatha Christie 

Let me continue my learning about many leaders. 
One aspect worried me a lot. Where are the ideas coming into an organization?  A good leader should have ideas to improve the systems. Leaders should come with ideas and goals and keep modifying them. Or leaders should get ideas from the society or experts or even common man  . Let me give examples: Kohli stumbled on the idea of IT services and it kept TCS going for a long time. It became a model followed by many IT services companies like Infosys,  Wipro,  HCL tech, etc.  Ramadorai scouted for ideas. He was always on the lookout . He saw opportunities for outsourcing,  total services and transformation  . He moved to communications, security , data centres , total banking and many other sectors .  Chandra made digital as his priority focus. Later both  realized that cloud computing is coming and started ION . ION became a major Indian cloud agency. Chandra realized the great potential of a total technology based exam management system with high confidence and integrity. He saw a huge business there. It happened. Then, Chandra saw AI on the horizon and got researchers developing IGNEO ,an AI system which is running for more than 10 years now. Now all IT companies are jumping into genAI. They get ideas of new applications for AI tools. Their ideas came from customers and technology developers.  They merged both sets of ideas. 
I Saw an idea going through stages of implementation. The idea started with a discussion I had with Mr MBN Rao, CMD, Indian bank. I was an IT advisor to Indian bank at that time. The question was “ there are more than 70 to 80 percent of the population who don't bank at all. So how do we bring unbanked people into the banking system?” I said we need to simplify the procedures of opening and managing accounts  . I said we need to take this in a mission mode and routine processes won't help. Just like cards management was outsourced we need to do this in that mode. I also said we need a device to help the rural population. After coming back to Bangalore, I talked to Mahesh Jain, chairman, Integra micro systems. He liked my idea and went about working on that. He built a system on a laptop with a couple of devices hooked up - cameras , printers etc. It was demonstrated to both MBN Rao and Sambamurty,  ED at that time  . MBN Rao wasn't convinced. But Sambamurthy liked the idea . I told Mahesh that we need to build an integral device to onboard customers and do their transactions. So our search to build a device began. I came up with a functional requirements. Then we searched the market for devices. I even went to exhibitions in Singapore for two successive years . We didn't find any device. The devices I saw weren't satisfactory. So Integra decided to build a device. After a few false starts, we got a device , named it as imfast . By this time Sambamurthy moved to corporation Bank as CMD  . He asked Ramachandran to start looking at branch less banking  . He was happy with imfast demo and entrusted this work to Integra.  That heralded the financial inclusion era in banking. It has grown by leaps and bounds now . Now there is a new organizational structure of servers, settlements, business associates and mobile banking. We are able to do direct transfer of benefits to chores of people , eliminating middleman and corruption. So ideas will lead to disruptive innovation.
It was a great satisfying experience for us

“There are two levels to belief. There’s the surface level, which our coaches, teachers, therapists, and parents love to preach. “Believe in yourself,” they all say, as if the thought alone can keep us afloat when the odds are against us in the battle of our lives. But once exhaustion sets in, doubt and insecurity tend to penetrate and dissipate that flimsy brand of belief.
Then there’s the belief born in resilience. It comes from working your way through layers of pain, fatigue, and reason, and ignoring the ever-present temptation to quit until you strike a source of fuel you didn’t even know existed. One that eliminates all doubt,makes you certain of your strength and the fact that eventually, you will prevail, so long as you keep moving forward. That is the level of belief that can defy the expectations of scientists and change everything. It’s not an emotion to be shared or an intellectual concept, and nobody else can give it to you. It must bubble up from within.”
David Goggins , Never Finished.



Let me continue my discussions about leaders.

Another  aspect worries me here. Many big companies like Birlas, Kirloskars , TVS , Mahindra , Mafatlal, Surya and several more jumped into IT services. Most had an idea that IT is a great business . I had discussions with many companies who showed interest in starting an IT business. But they had no concept of a totally different business thinking and models required for IT. So many failed in the eighties. Some came back with niche areas and established success. Mindset change and transformative approaches are essential .
 A cable manufacturing company wanted to go for microwave ovens manufacturing in the late eighties  . But they decided to skip it. I told them that time is not right.  
Unfortunately, most hardware companies like  NELCo, Uptron, DCM, ORG, Pertech , Zenith, etc stopped hardware manufacture. Companies like Wipro  , HCL  , Patni computers closed down hardware operations and moved into software. Many trading companies like Computronix, Hinditron,  closed down operations. Today, we are dominated by foreign companies for hardware supply and Chinese companies for mobile phones and Taiwanese companies for chips. Indian manufacturing still struggles.
Most training institutions also didn't survive. These are due to a focus on a single product and rigidity ,- no resilience. 
One key aspect that is underlying with most  good leaders is alertness and seeing through the apparent reality. Apparent reality is created by misinformation,  fake information and wrong analysis by his associates. It is a great asset to see through this nor only inside the organization , but also outside.  This is a critical requirement for bankers .
 I knew one chairman of a private engineering college who appointed his village people in lower skill jobs in the college and got inputs from them about the activities of the principal and faculty. The principal was a decent person and a friend of mine. Such practices are not acceptable. 
Today's leaders need to get a reasonable level of utilization of  technology in society. Success depends on the transformations needed at regular intervals. Nothing is static or stagnant. Most organizations  are dynamic and subject to internal and external changes. This means that we need both knowledge inside the well and also outside, both birds eye view and worm's views. 
There are some who had phenomenal clarity and knew what to do next to take the organization forward. N Chandra of TCS and now chair of Tata Sons is a great example. I admired his clarity of IT scenarios in the world. Another person with great clarity was Mr KC Chakraborty,  CMD, PNB, and Deputy governor, RBI. He was bold in taking decisions. He was the first to say that core banking should cover the entire bank , not just some branches. He asked the IT department in Indian bank and later PNB to go for core banking for all branches. We had difficulty with Infosys  to take up core banking for all branches of PNB. Only a simple threat worked. Whereas , TCS provided a vision for scaling up of the total  operations automation   for SBI. TCS surprisingly , took up as their first core banking implementation the largest bank with more than 9000 branches and succeeded in it. They concentrated on performance. They had domain knowledge and had a good collaboration . Staff of both - TCS and SBI were committed to successful Implementation.

The key to the solution remains with a major attitudinal change.
Treat a human being as a human being. 
Human beings need recognition as much as they need food and water. No crueler punishment can be devised than to not see someone, to render them unimportant or invisible. “The worst sin towards our fellow creatures is not to hate them,” George Bernard Shaw wrote, “but to be indifferent to them: that’s the essence of inhumanity.” To do that is to say: You don’t matter. You don’t exist.
Our school textbooks describe a household as one having a family and a servant. Servants are treated badly. 
When and how will we change and respect humans and nature? The how part is to deal with the purpose of education  . 

We are in the state as described by Antonio Gramaci. In 1930, while imprisoned by Mussolini’s fascist regime in Italy, the philosopher Antonio Gramsci wrote this in his prison notebooks: “The crisis consists precisely in the fact that the old is dying and the new cannot be born; in this interregnum a great variety of morbid symptoms appear.”
This will prolong with AI. 
We are moving from a centralized system to a decentralized system  . Today's power centre lies in YouTube not TV.

I recall the scare of BCG vaccination that happened in the seventies. Both books look at deception, very useful ones no doubt. But won't give a solution to the problem of improving the life structure and systems. 
  Is there a third side? Look at Hindu philosophy for an answer.

Agatha Christie

“
Law schools, business schools, and medical schools, music conservatories and athletic
programs, all strive to harness the latent power of the human brain to deliver ever
higher levels of achievement, to provide an edge in a world that is increasingly
competitive. Through the sheer force of human ingenuity, we have devised systems to free our brains of clutter, to help us keep track of details that we can’t trust ourselves
to remember. All of these and other innovations are designed either to improve the brain we have, or to off-load some of its functions to external sources.”
Daniel Levitin  organized mind.

I also had personal experience and exposure to some chief ministers of Karnataka like Nijalingappa, Gundu Rao, Ramakrishna Hegde,  Veerappa Moily,  JH Patel, SM Krishna . I had interactions with Ramakrishna Hegde.  He had a lot of ideas on governance. His major contribution was on Panchayat Raj.  He became CM after Gundu Rao.  The state was in some kind of chaos. The first thing he did was to order the arrest of all charge sheeted rowdies. The city became calm within a few days. He was accessible to all. Even after becoming CM , he used to come into IISc for morning walks. I used to meet him and also saw that many outsiders coming to meet him with their problems. I met Mt Abdul Nazeer Saab, his minister for rural development, also known as neer Saab because he was very active in getting rural water supply to most villages. He was encouraged by Ramakrishna Hegde.  I met him with Prof Narasimhan of TIFR. 
Another interesting reform was in governance. He had an excellent secretary, Mr V Balasubramanian.  So he would prepare a one page note on each file . Ramakrishna Hegde would go through and apply his mind before arriving at a decision. That was his intellectual capability. He was a workaholic  . There was one occasion where I was involved with him. He wanted his office to be equipped with computers. It was 1984 or 85. I was advising the office - Mr Gokulram was a deputy secretary in the CMs office then - on the selection of computers. I asked Gokulram what functions the CM wanted the computers to do. I was told that he wanted to use it for transfers of officers - a major and high pressure activity of the secretariat.  So I selected a vendor - HCL and the server and PCs. That showed his reform mind. He did a lot in a short period . 
The other CM who is also a thinker and intellectual is Mr SM Krishna. I had met him several times when he was minister, then deputy CM and then CM. He was keen on developing the state. He devised a new major project called Mahithisindhu , the objective was to provide computer education and computer based education to 1000 government high schools, most in rural areas . It was a very ambitious program  . I was advisor for this project and will discuss it later in detail. 
I met Mr Veerappa Moily as a finance minister in 1982 and later as CM. He used to say he became CM because of an act of me and others. He was another intellectual. 
To sum up my views on leaders, I found 
1.Many just survived in an organization. Most managements and principals fall in this category.
2. Many stabilized an organization giving it a direction and goal. 
3.Many built mechanisms for growth and survival. 
4.Many took new transformative paths. 
5. Many took it to a higher level. 
6. Many created excellent organizations. 

That completes my discussions on leaders.

Hinduism defines many responsibilities for a ruler - raja dharma.
It is explained in kautilya's arthashastra and also in Mahabharata. It makes it the duty of the king to protect his subjects from all evil doers. That means good laws, detection of crimes, punishments, and giving fair justice. Manusmriti gives details of behaviors and punishments. 
The vedas say rtm vadishyami. They worship order, harmony and stability. 
It is understood that the world is filled with three types of people - 
Devas with high moral and noble characters and conduct.
Ausuras with evil mind and evil actions.
And
Common people with normal and innocent life. 
The world is filled with these three kinds of people. 
EF Schumacher says that the world is filled with both good and evil people. 
This offsets evil and balances the world into an acceptable living place. 
Only when evil dominates   the other people are in trouble. Our puranas say that at that time , a powerful divine avatar happens to remove/ destroy evil. We have seen many such instances in the past. 
So in the case of fake / disinformation also, we will find a solution. Already many alerts are happening in your mobile phone - blocking spams, alerting of frauds, protecting passwords, alerting you of clones , etc. Lots of researchers are working on fake detection algorithms. There is a lot of successes in this. There is a lot of work on creating alerts for dark and deceptive patterns. 
As I said AI will provide a great solution to this problem. 
Let me quote about our resilience.
David Giggins has written a book 
“Never finished”. I am quoting resilience from his book.
“There are two levels to belief. There’s the surface level, which our coaches, teachers, therapists, and parents love to preach. “Believe in yourself,” they all say, as if the thought alone can keep us afloat when the odds are against us in the battle of our lives. But once exhaustion sets in, doubt and insecurity tend to penetrate and dissipate that flimsy brand of belief.
Then there’s the belief born in resilience. It comes from working your way through layers of pain, fatigue, and reason, and ignoring the ever-present temptation to quit until you strike a source of fuel you didn’t even know existed. One that eliminates all doubt,makes you certain of your strength and the fact that eventually, you will prevail, so long as you keep moving forward. That is the level of belief that can defy the expectations of scientists and change everything. It’s not an emotion to be shared or an intellectual concept, and nobody else can give it to you. It must bubble up from within.”
David Goggins , Never Finished.
Another important factor is humans work towards sharpening their intellect and mind. They collaborate and adapt . 
David Levitin in his book organised mind says
“We humans have a long history of pursuing neural enhancement—ways to
improve the brains that evolution gave us. We train them to become
more dependable and efficient allies in helping us to achieve our goals.”
So we are on the track.

Let me return to my job in the Karnataka government. 
Let me look at some controversies at that time when I was with the state government.  
1.We saw that the Bedthi project was shelved. That was the first problem I started with. 
2.There was a proposal for constructing a power plant in the silent valley forests in kerala.  Environmentalists opposed it . Most engineers supported the construction of a dam and power plant in the silent valley. Only a few opposed it. 
Fortunately, the ministry of environment took note of the silent valley problem and appointed a committee to look at the  different aspects and come up with recommendations. 
. The verdict was that it is a well preserved ecosystem and should be preserved and permission for the power plant should not be given . So the project was stopped. I was one of the supporters for scrapping this project. 


3.Similarly we handled another controversy in Karnataka. A company called jungle lodges wanted to set up a tourist resort in the kabini river area. Many felt that their project falls in the elephant corridor . The forest department also raised its objection. Even Mr Prabhakara , secretary in the commerce and industries ministry  raised his concerns  . It was discussed in many newspapers and magazines. But the  state government wanted to go ahead with this project. Mr Ullas Karanth , an environmentalist , who later became well known with his projects on tiger habitats , opposed the project of jungle lodges. His father and the famous jnanpith awardee, Mr Shivarama karanth  also opposed this project. He, RK Narayan and some others wrote to the prime minister, Mrs Indira Gandhi. She asked the state to take proper action. The state immediately got into action mode and canceled the permission given to jungle lodges. The company now runs  a unit on the banks of the Kabini river in a building with some tents outside. It won't disturb the movement of elephants. 

4. A major crisis started in Karnataka on giving primacy to kannada in education. A committee appointed by the previous government with Prof V K Gokak came up with a report to make kannada as a compulsory medium of instruction in Karnataka and also insisted that all students should go through kannada as a compulsory  subject in the schools  . The people wanted the government to implement the Gokak report . The agitation snowballed into a big one across the state and led to violence.  Many government cars were burnt. Even the car of the law secretary was burnt. My car narrowly escaped. We showed them the number plate and said we got the number plates written in kannada. Mobocracy ruled the state. Even Dr Rajkumar was mobbed and had to get into vidhana soudha for protection. 
Later, I went with Mr Shivarama karanth to a college in Siddhapur , near Sirsi. He said love for kannada should be shown in developing books on science in kannada. We can't be frogs in the well. 
This agitation was a major cause for dethroning the Congress government in Karnataka. 
I left the government in November 1982 and returned to IISc. Then elections were held. Ramakrishna Hegde became the chief minister. He brought the state under control in no time.

“I shall turn to the question of why it matters that we notice that there is such a category as meaningfulness, distinct from the categories of happiness and morality that we are more used to invoking in thinking about what to do and how to live. As I shall argue, awareness that meaning is a third sort of value a life can possess should affect our understanding of the first two sorts: that is, adopting models of human motivation and reason that are attentive to meaningfulness should affect the way we think about happiness and morality—and about self-interest as well.”

Susan Wolf

Prof Vasudev Parvati has the following comments. 
“Sir,  your post shows that people who were perceived as ruthless and cunning politicians by the lay public were actually great thinkers and workaholics! Certainly an eye opener!
Sir, please don't stop here, please do pen your thoughts on educational leaders, including maybe, some of the Directors and Vice Chancellors of prestigious institutions!”

My response 
This is a difficult question. I knew hundreds of principals, directors and vice chancellors.  Most were academically serious. That is a positive aspect. 
My problem is I was brought up by IISc.  So I had very high expectations. 
In IISc,  research is the primary focus. I didn't see it in all engineering colleges and even IITs during their first decade of operation. 
But, relevant to engineering programs in the country , we changed our curriculum every year. Since we had many faculty with degrees from abroad, we were familiar with the curriculum and activities in most famous western universities. Also , we got more than 50 plus academic visitors from the western university every year. So we were aware of the international scenario. So in mid sixties itself , I looked at MIT syllabus to prepare my course curriculum for computer techniques. Similarly in 1967, I took an electrical machines course with a lot of mathematical content and I introduced a power systems course with computer algorithms. I introduced many courses during 1967 to 1970 like optimization techniques,  computer simulation, computational methods, computing systems etc. Did  any institution in India give such a freedom to their junior most faculty? Did any institution built new courses despite some faculty going abroad for a graduate program? 
All engineering institutions in India were at a stagnant curriculum and didn't think of graduate programs or research. 
We ,at IISc, galloped with many new programs. We were leaders in most graduate programs. We had several new departments opened . 
So I look at institutions in India with this IISc mindset. So I am very critical in  my discussions. I needed to get adjusted . I did that. But I didn't come down a lot. So my discussions will be based on this background. But I will follow a neutral path.
Most faculty in colleges and even universities  had no freedom for curriculum development or even teaching. Or sometimes,  they don't want to use the freedom . 
Initially in the sixties and seventies, we saw most aided colleges doing well. They got good students. Teachers were sincere and knowledgeable.  They were selected for their capabilities and not recommendations. Managements had no role in running the college  . Their job ended with admitting students from their quota. Most won't pay for equipments and maintenance. So the principals were responsible not only for academics , but also for finance and accounts . Accounts are audited. DIrector of technical education had a strong  control  on administration,  faculty selection, principal selection and finances. University maintained academic standards and conducted exams.  So while the principals were constrained in their job, they focused on running the college. They didn't bother about development. They didn't plan for the future. They ran the traditional programs. Concentration and focus was on the undergraduate programs. That was the state in Karnataka. 
More in the next.

27/06/24, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 527

Let me now turn to your question on the accomplishment of the purpose of education. 
The purpose is never served when you finish school or university education. They seem to be ignored by formal education. Formal Education doesn't provide even discriminatory skills. So look outside this system.
First you don't get all these adjustment skills in schools and colleges. 
Second you learn social skills partially by actual online and real time - I mean by actual interactions with various kinds of people. You first learn it at home, with your peers, friends, then in the market, in the school, in various public places . I know a friend of mine. He was brought up in a reclusive and protected manner in his house. He didn't know how to go to market and buy things. When he joined IISc for ME, it was his first exposure to the outside world , though a protected one still. He wanted me to with him to buy essentials like bath soap , tooth paste, paper etc. He was literally hanging onto me for a year. By the second year, he gained confidence and conducted his social affairs independently. Still he didn't know anyone outside his class and his teachers. Even the teaching was strange to him. I have seen  many instances of such secluded and protected upbringing of children and their effects on the growth of their children . The first effect is fear of everything. The second is diffidence and loss of confidence. This happens despite their attending school and college education. So it is important for the parents to know their responsibility to bring up their children with all round social and interpersonal skills. They should own this responsibility and execute it fully. Allow for social interactions first. Don't scold a child not to talk to anyone. 
Once in a job, another level of learning happens - professionalism, etiquette,  ethics, sincerity  , punctual approaches etc. 
Once you become a leader, lots of learnings happen.
Third, children learn observation and discrimination - what to talk? How to react ,? What not to talk,? How to behave,? How to listen? etc. It is a crucial life long education. It doesn't stop some time. So purpose is a goal post. 
Lesson is to learn and adapt throughout your life. 
Have goals in life and achieve them. 
Being recluse or isolated is not a disaster. Try to avoid it. Social mingling is good. 
But keep away from evil. 
To conclude, education is a continuous process throughout one's life. 
Greetings 
Dks
