Rabindranath Tagore, Gitanjali

Let me continue my response to the request by Vasudev. 
I would categorize the principals according to the following levels. 
1.run a normal steady institution. No reforms , no innovations . - Most principals fall in this category. 
2. Manage the faculty and students  . Inculcate discipline , good teaching practices. - Many will fall in this category also. Some will miss it. 
3. Improve labs, attendance , performance of students , and increase ranking. Many will fall in this category also. 
4. Build a stable , academic and value based institution. I definitely remember a few names. Prof KA Krishnamurthy , MCE, Hassan . Prof MN Chennabasappa, SIT Tumkur , Prof Nityanandan and Prof Abhaykumar, TCE, Madurai, are some good examples. Both Prof Prahlada Rao of MSRIT and Prof Holla of RVCE brought their institutions to a stable state. 
5. Build an institution . There are many in this category. Prof Dhananjaya, JCE, Mysore, Prof Reddy ,SSIT, Tumkur , Prof Ramegowda, BIT  , and many others are in this category. 
6. Transform the institution. Prof Ashok Shettar comes to my mind. 
7. Build a research culture. Most failed in this . We needed to force this through TEQIP.  
8. Create new areas. Most accepted it after it evolved in other places. 
9. Transform the faculty. Most tried , but success is marginal. Most faculty stayed in their comfort zone. This, according to me, is a critical job of a principal. 
10. Take the institution to great heights. 
I know many principals who managed  their colleges well. But I can't list all of them here as I may miss many names. There are definitely many positives about most colleges principals like:
They did a good job with academics, with teaching schedules  , discipline, proper conduct of university exams, getting approvals from the university, DTE, AICTE etc, running the governing board, handling management and motivating students and faculty. They kept a good ranking and ran a good program. Autonomous colleges built a modern curriculum, took care in conducting exams, and evaluations . There were a lot of apprehensions about autonomy. Most senior academics and vice chancellors were not in favor of autonomy. Now colleges have fine a wonderful job for more than 20 years independently in autonomous mode. That is commendable. I feel happy about it. I played  fighting and guiding roles to bring autonomy into our colleges in Karnataka against opposition by vice chancellors. 
My general observations are :
1. Colleges that  had a principal with tenure ,  capability and responsibility had done well.  Tenure meant at least  three or more years of tenure as principal. 
2.Colleges where managements had a strong presence and control didn't do well.
3. Colleges with roll in roll out principals didn't do well.
4.Colleges with poor management died . 
5. Colleges with reforms oriented principals have grown well. 
6. Colleges with visionary management and principals went to very great heights  . 
We have enough examples of these . I don't want to name individuals or colleges. 
I don't have any great or even good opinion about vice chancellors.  They just existed. The only one who tried to improve an institution was NR Shetty  as VC of  Bangalore University.  
Hope this answers one query. Let me look at some directors next.

29/06/24, 4:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 400
“The source from which the world and the mind rise and into which they set is termed Reality, which does not rise nor does it subside.
—Ramana Maharishi

Let me continue with Vasudev’ s request.  
I had met many directors of IITs  . I knew about 8 directors of IISc. I knew several directors of IIM Bangalore.  I also saw directors of NITs at the transition point , when RECs were made NITs. 
I saw the evolution of IITs.  Initially IITs didn't know their strength. Even faculty selection was standard. Teaching was the primary job. The greatest strength of IITs is their students' capabilities. They got the top most students in the country. The second strength was their national character which permitted students to understand the different cultures of India and to appreciate the unity and diversity. It broadened their vision. Even today, IIT s strength is their BTech program. Research focus came pretty late. They are still catching up . 
To show the administrative and teaching focus of IITs,  let me start with my first visit to  IIT  Madras in 1964. It just started operations. My impression wasn't  great. But my exposure was minimal. The director was busy with the construction of buildings, definitely an urgent activity then.
 Let me discuss an encounter of mine in 1967. I was in Delhi and went to IIT looking for a faculty position. The culture I experienced was totally  different from IISc. I met the head of the EE department.  He is a famous person and became a director of an IIT later. During my interview  , as assistant professor came in and both the head and he discussed the number of transistors available and the number missing. I thought it a trivial matter not worthy of discussion at that level. That was my first shock. I was asked to meet the director and he was a famous person  , and was a principal of a famous Delhi College, an absolute zero on research.  He exhibited his college culture. It was June and pretty hot in Delhi.  So I had gone in a casual dress of a pair of trousers , a half sleeved slack shirt and chappals. He asked why I didn't wear a formal dress with shoes and tie. I said that it was IISc practice. I also told him that Delhi was very hot and so I thought this was an appropriate dress. I also told him that I didn't plan to get interviewed. Since I had a free day  I decided to explore the possibility of a job at IIT.  He was particular about tie and shoes and disapproved of me. Later I heard that he used to check on his faculty and saw to it that they go to their departments in the morning regularly in formal attire. See this focus. 
My visits  to IIT Bombay in 1969 and IIT  madras in 1969, 1970 and later on also confirmed my view that they were focusing on teaching. What I was very upset about was the fact that IIT Madras had a good number of faculty trained at IISc.  Fortunately IIT  Madras got a great director in 1969 in Prof A Ramachandran , again from IISc.  He changed the direction of IIT  madras and did his best to bring in a research culture  . That was transformational leadership. It worked.Later , he became secretary for the department of science and technology , government of India.  That was the mindset of the first set of directors. 
The initial vision was only building the institution. So they concentrated on the construction work and management like a college principal. Academics was left to the heads of departments. But due to the collaboration with Germany, UK ,and USSR, they got modern lab facilities. That was a plus point.
But IITs quickly moved into a goal of academic excellence.  IITs practiced best practices, provided freedom to faculty and students and used their autonomy effectively, thereby becoming top institutions in the country. Directors were responsible for this. The value systems and governance were and are good. 
But the great thing I like about IITs  is their sincere and hard effort to transform themselves into a research institution. It was slow and took a lot of time. IIT  Kharagpur had good research culture from the beginning. I am aware of several faculty of IIT Kharagpur in ECE - prof Das- and EE  - professors krishnamurthy, Keshavamurthy, and Menon - publishing papers in the sixties .  By the late sixties , most IITs got into graduate programs and research. 
IIT Kanpur  had a good start. It got many faculty who were researchers. Prof Kelkar , another IIScian, was a good director. He decentralized administration and focused on building lab facilities and research. They moved into modern areas of research . 
Let me discuss about IISc directors next.

Let me return to the IISc directors. 
I had stronger and participative interactions with IISc directors . I had discussed my involvement with many directors before. The first one was Prof. Dhawan. I was fortunate that my first interaction as a student and young faculty was with him. He was an attentive director first. He listened to people and changed his decisions sometimes. He was a reformist director . His biggest contributions were in the domain of faculty development. He introduced the five year review and promotion scheme for faculty. It is a big reform. He identified and brought a good number of senior faculty from various US and Indian institutions.  Some names are Prof  ECG Sudharsan, Prof CNR Rao, Prof Modi, Prof GN Ramachandran, Prof G Narasimhan, Prof  V Krishnan, Prof KP Sinha, Prof LS Srinath, Prof Dutta, Prof Soundaranayagam , Prof Joshi . He was followed by Prof S Ramaseshan.  Ramaseshan created new centres. He planned for the supercomputer centre. He reformed the library and computer centre. 
Then came CNR Rao. He was fast , intuitive and correct. He also focused on improving the faculty culture. He focused on research. 
These three created a strong impression in me.

My response. 
I am unhappy at the manner at which IIM s are chasing money oriented actions  and focusing only on big businesses. I don't know why values are not given the prominent position. 
I was a visiting faculty at IIM Bangalore from 1985 to 1989. I taught computer systems there. I appreciated NS Ramaswamy,  the first director of IIM Bangalore.  He brought in many new areas like power  , transport, coal ,public sector etc. He recruited a number of research oriented faculty in economics, OR , finance. Many faculty were great.  I knew them personally and professionally.  But this differentiation between MBA faculty and non MBA faculty resulted in most leaving the institute. 
IIM s ignored research , ignored governance and even reality in India.  They concentrated on Harvard case studies. That total dependence was not acceptable. They moved towards business. But they didn't understand that businesses needed technology for survival. So they ignored technology and its adaptation to businesses. They didn't know about the disruptive innovation propounded by Prof Clayton Christensen,  Harvard business school. They didn't see the transformations happening. 
Initially IIM Ahmedabad had a mini HP computer and a few good math faculty taking care of it. But they also left after some years. 
I feel the neglect of technology on business and innovations due to technology on business is a grave error. Fintech was evolving . They didn't bother about it. I am worried how IIM s and even IIT s will respond to AI. 
But I should say there are many positives. Students are eager to learn. They want the final outcomes and don't worry about methodologies. They don't want to understand the internals ,but definitely want to know the externals. So they become successful managers. 
As regards your second point, internships are tried out by IIM s , IIT s and engineering colleges. Some institutions even provide foreign internships. It didn't provide the intended output unfortunately.

“The world seems more complex to Asians than to  Westerners, and understanding events always requires con-
sideration of a host of factors that operate in relation to one another in no simple, deterministic way. Formal logic  plays little role in problem solving. In fact, the person who  is too concerned with logic may be considered immature.
Human cognition is not everywhere 
the same. Without putting it in so many words, the humanities and social science scholars were making extremely 
important claims about the nature of thought. First, that  members of different cultures differ in their "metaphysics,"  or fundamental beliefs about the nature of the world. Second, that the characteristic thought processes of different  groups differ greatly. Third, that the thought processes are of a piece with beliefs about the nature of the world: Peo-
ple use the cognitive tools that seem to make sense—given the sense they make of the world.”

Richard Nisbett, in “ Geography of thought  “

D R Krishnan congratulated me for doing 400 posts without a break. It was a tough task to write more than a page everyday. The most difficult task was to decide on the contents for each day. Then remember the happenings. It wasn't easy. I am happy I passed your test. 
Thanks , Krishnan. 
Let me return back to my life at Bangalore from nov 1982 onwards. 
As I mentioned before, I decided suddenly on nov2, 1982 that I didn't like the government job. So I decided to return back to IISc full time. So I informed Mr Anagol and got relieved that day itself according to my wishes. Mr Anagol was very kind and understood my situation, predicament and temperament. My driver, Vijayakumar   ,  wept in the evening.  I forgot his name and remembered it only after writing this post. This retrieval process is amazing. It is random ,but the mind comes out with a spark suddenly. I was breaking my head about the name of the driver and it appeared after sometime when i wasn't thinking about it. My son was attached to him. Whenever we traveled, he used to be with my car driver . 
I rejoined the department . Prof Ramaseshan was happy. Many colleagues and friends were surprised that I returned. Prof R Kumar remarked that I left a good position with perks to write a few papers .
Prof SV Rangaswamy became the chair of the department.  Situation was good. The conflicting situations changed to a pleasant situation. I was busy with my teaching and research and lectures at PSTI and visits to TCS. I was also involved with KSCST. 
In the meantime , I got my first installment of the sanctioned loan from HDFC and started my house construction. I had ordered all the wooden fittings with Major Somasekhar, MSERA . I got bricks and cement. So we went ahead with foundation and curing. 
I shouldn't say I started my house construction. It was taken over by Mr Thimme Gowda . He took enormous interest. He fixed the mason, fixed the rates. He ordered granite slab and glass. We got sand from Yeshwantpur and ordered white mosaic tiles also from Yeshwantpur- Parijatha tiles. We visited the site everyday. Mr Thimme gowda had left the institute and joined the government as electrical inspector again on deputation from KEB.  
Since he lived closeby , we could visit the site everyday.  His help was immense. He took it as if he was constructing his own house. Major Somasekhar, who lived next to my site became a friend and was also helpful in several aspects of construction as he was a civil engineer.  
Work was progressing slowly and steadily. After about a year, we needed to cast the roof and got a gang to do the job of casting the concrete slab. Steel for the slab was prepared by the barbender.  I needed to stay in the site for a whole day to get the casting completed. It went off without a hitch.

The last stage of house construction was chaotic. We needed to do carpentry work to fix all wooden units like cupboards, show case, doors and windows. I gave the electrical work to Mahantesh from CPRI , who helped me for decades. He did the complete electrification. Then came the mosaic laying, grinding and polishing people. They worked for about ten days. Then the plastering started. Then came painting and final plastering work. 
Now I needed to get both water and electricity connections from BWSSB and KEB. That was a big corruption hurdle. I may not want to pay , but the mason insisted on paying the people. But I had enough influence. The KEB guy said it will take a month. I knew Mr B G Rudrappa very well for several years. He was the chief engineer at that time. So I talked to him. He directed the AEE to give the connection immediately.  So within a day the assistant engineer materialized to give connection from the KEB pole to my incoming meter cum MCB board. He had brought an energy meter also. He was upset . I was present . He told me that it would take a few months to get a direct connection without an energy meter. Energy meters were in short supply. He said that I was lucky. The supply was needed for mosaic polishing work. So I avoided a delay of about six months in construction. 
The house was ready by the year end of 1983.
Similarly I was doing work on bangalore water supply . Dr Srinivas was working with me . He had been visiting all service stations to collect data for me. So he arranged a water connection for me. So that was done quickly without any fuss. 
Sankara subramanian continued his association with me. He was manufacturing the CPRI stove and selling it. He gave me the grills. We put it up on the terrace parapet wall.

“PHILOSOPHICAL MODELS of human psychology—or, more specifically, of human motivation—tend to fall into one of two categories. Perhaps the oldest and most popular model conceives of human beings as egoists, moved and guided exclusively by what they take to be in their own self-interest. However, there have long been defenders of a dualistic model of motivation as well, according to which people are capable of being moved not only by self-interest, but also by something “higher.” Kant, for example, famously thought that in addition to being subject to inclinations, people are capable of being moved and directed by reason alone.”
Susan Wolf: Meaning of life.

Aparna Murthy commented on the Nisbett quote in post 403 that we are complex:
“I agree with your opening sentence. We as Indians especially are judgemental about things and further we don't want to change thinking that we are always right. Further talking about the past when it's not warranted is our way . Great job done by you Anna covering even miniscule details. Hats off to your memory 👏😃”

My short response
I don't like to typecast people . I think Hinduism is built on plurality . It accepts the fact that people have different behaviours, different ambience and different beliefs and hence provides multiple solutions to different kinds of our population. That is why it allows us to choose a path of dharma - jnana marga or pursuit of knowledge and reality, bhakti marga or devotion, karma marga or duty oriented living, and either  raja marga or ruling class 
Or yoga . Yoga is applicable to all. 
But Hinduism  has many foundational principles like sadachara, rtm - order ,harmony, giving and sharing, learning and teaching , other dos and don'ts - yamas and niyamas  etc which are applicable to all. But today there is no enforcement mechanism. 
That is the reason for many Siddhantas , many schools , many interpretations and many practices . So practices deviated widely from basic principles according to times. There are many divergent practices like atheism,  tantric systems  , vedic rituals, agamas and temple rituals, bhakti movements, guru cult , veerashaivism, shaktism, vaishnavism,  dvaita, smartha , kali worship , local gods worship etc. These also divided people into groups leading to quarrels and disagreements. 
In addition  cultural diversity , geographic diversity and political divides have added to the complexity of the people . 
I agree with you that we are complex and carry a burden. We have several attitudes of guilt, superiority, slavery, excuses etc. That is evident  today. 
Also there are no detailed and data oriented impartial studies on the behaviours of indians. The one who did great work was Prof MN Srinivas. But he looked at one aspect , did a lot of surveys and detailed analysis and excellent work. But I see a lot of highly biased subjective studies which are political statements of beliefs of individuals, not objective,  not scientific , not based on large samples. We are  divided by religion, caste, profession. Mostly, we are driven by ego - ahamkara- or slavery. But despite this complexity, most people worry about day to day survival for food and shelter. They are simpletons.  They follow the reverse of Thiruvalluvar's kural : they need to eat first before listening to philosophy or talks or opinions. 
The judgmental part comes from ego. Why do we criticize everyone and find faults with people? Why don't we accept people as they are? I feel our education system both formal school system and non formal - family and society have failed to inculcate a good value system in children. 
 To conclude , we don't have a unified population. We have diversity and unity. That is difficult to comprehend. The west refuses to accept it. West doesn't understand us. 
Another factor I want to add is the difference in views between west and India. West believes in discrete elements. Even an animal body consists of many discrete parts. Then they look at connections. They go bottom up - from parts to the whole. Indians go  top down - from whole to parts.  They believe that the universe is highly interconnected . Consciousness pervades everywhere.  They divide the humans into five sheaths.  Parts come later. That is the major difference between Indian and western philosophy. Indians accept unknowns. Swami Vivekananda even talked of unknowables. The West deals with only the known entities. It ignores unknowns.
Thanks.
Greetings

“Thirty years ago, travel agents made our airline and rail reservations, salesclerks helped us find what we were looking for in stores, and professional typists or secretaries helped busy people with their correspondence. Now we do most of those things ourselves. The information age has off-loaded a great deal of the work previously done by people we could call information specialists onto all of the rest of us. We are doing the jobs of ten different people while still trying to keep up with our lives, our children and parents, our friends, our careers, our hobbies, and our favorite TV shows. It’s no wonder that sometimes one memory gets confounded with another, leading us to show up in the right place but on the wrong day, or to forget something as simple as where we last put our glasses or the remote.”
Daniel Levitin    “organized mind” 

Let me return to my life in 1983. I was busy with my  house construction . Every morning I would leave home in the 11 th main  Malleswaram by 8.15 am and cross the railway line and walk to Subramanya Nagar to Mr Thimme Gowda’s house. We would go to the west of Chord road site and look at the construction, find out what needed to be done . Sometimes Major Somasekhar would join us and inspect the construction. His views were useful and helpful. Then I will go to the institute and Thimme Gowda to his office. This was a daily ritual for us. 
Then came another activity. IISc has a swimming pool. So we decided to teach both my children swimming. The pool opened  by 7am. So we - I and my children- left by 630 am and walked to the swimming pool. Some days we are lucky to find an auto . After swimming, we returned back home . I would quickly eat my breakfast and start my house construction ritual by walking to Thimme Gowda’s house. From our site , I will go to IISc. So it was a strenuous time . 
Let me discuss my activities at IISc.  
Since Mr Gowda was not there, I got another person to take care of the matters of power . But  I had some problems with power in the institute again . Two problems happened. 
One - institute's power demand was growing fast. We reached the maximum with our actual load exceeding our contract demand . Prof Ramaseshan was confident of the institute getting a super computer. So work on the construction of a building had started on that front. That meant  our maximum load would almost double according to the estimates. So there were frequent power shutdowns also. 
Two - KEB supply became erratic. The KEB station located in Gymkhana got overloaded due to its increased supply to not only the institute , but also to the porcelain factory, BWSSB jewel filters and neighbouring areas. It was planned as a KEB supply station only for the institute, but it spread its supply network wide. So disruptions were frequent. 
Hence , I started looking for alternate plans. I told Ramaseshan , our then director about it. He didn't bother about it. He felt strongly that we can't depend on KEB and we should go for  alternate DG units. Power situation stayed that way  till he retired from IISc.

“We also do a remarkable thing that no other creatures seem able to do: whenever our usual ways to think fail, we can start to think about our thoughts themselves—and if this "reflective thinking" shows where we went wrong, that can help us to invent new and more powerful ways to think. However, we still know very little about how our brains manage to do such things. How does imagination work? What are the causes of consciousness? What are emotions, feelings, and thoughts? How do we manage to think at all? “
Marvin Minsky      “ Emotions machine”

KSCST normally had a fixed mindset of looking at mostly rural problems and renewable energy as an off shoot of ASTRA.  It decided  on a major  new project called Bangalore Urban studies - a new learning and understanding of the magnitude of the problems. That was a huge transformation in KSCST as well as Prof Reddy. He was drifting from rural focus and this is a good evidence of his and KSCST's turning point to accept the fact that there are many problems of urgency in a big city like Bangalore and they deserved our attention.  He moved from ASTRA to management. I recall S G Ramachandra saying that urban poverty is worse than rural poverty. GVK Rao talked about the grave fuel crisis in India. 
 This project was expected  to study the various resources and their sources and utilization.  It was a new experiment. It was hoped to provide many new understandings and learnings and lead to better designs of urban systems. The study involved many people from various institutions. Many areas like energy, water, transport  , vegetables, education, etc were covered. One major mistake with this project was the fact that it looked at these as discrete components. It didn't provide an integral view and mechanisms for governance and planning. I will discuss this more at the end of discussions on water.  I took up water supply and distribution in Bangalore city. This study lasted for more than three years. It was a great opportunity for us to know more about Bangalore and its history. I got an assistant to help me with my studies. Initially ,Dr Srinivas assisted me for more than a year. Later two of my students - Ashok Kattimani and Ramakrishna- joined me. They along with TV Ramachandra did a project with me during their BE studies at BMS college of engineering.  Later Ramachandra joined CES and continued there. He worked with me on regional energy planning.  
Dr Srinivas visited all water service units and collected statistics on water consumption by different types of consumers. He copied data from their registers. The engineers were very helpful. Later I found that the head quarters of BWSSB didn't have this kind of data. I passed on most of the collected information to the chairman. There were no pipe diagrams. 
The consumption studies revealed highly  unequal geographic distribution of water. There were also temporal  variations in the  consumption pattern. Initial analysis  showed that losses were high - of the order of 25 to 30 percent -  and infrastructure was not uniform and water pressure and storage varied considerably.

“if this ability to truly see others is important in making the marriage decision or in hiring and retaining workers, it is also important if you are a teacher leading students, a doctor examining patients, a host anticipating the needs of a guest, a friend spending time
with a friend, a parent raising a child, a spouse watching the one you love crawl into bed at the end of the day. Life goes a lot better if you can see things from other people’s points of view, as well as your own. “Artificial intelligence is going to do many things for us in the decades ahead, and replace humans at many tasks, but one thing it will never be able to do is to create person-to-person connections. If you want to thrive in the age of AI, you better become exceptionally good at connecting with others.”
David Brooks 

Let me look at some basic flaws in our utilities ,which exist  even now. 
1.While major works like reservoirs , pump houses, generating stations are planned well, distribution networks are done in an ad hoc manner. This is true of water, power, roads, bus transport and even Fibre optic lines. 
2. While the transmission systems are provided with sufficient funds, the city networks for distribution gets a low priority and low allocation of costs. Normally ,for optimal design, cost ratios between transmission and distribution should be equal. But in India   it was 70 for transmission and 30 for distribution. 
3. Operation and maintenance are also handled in an ad hoc manner. 
4. While automated technologies were and are available , our utilities don't use them. It is the case even now even in bangalore. You will see an automated power substation in many offices in Bangalore, but  the BESCOM substations are in the old manual state without any automation.
5. Distribution as applied to utilities is not taught in our engineering curriculum. 

Similarly  , While BWSSB did a great job of planning, designing and implementing the scheme  of bringing water from cauvery to the city in several stages over several decades, and building a good pumping station and several reservoirs , it didn't do similar planning ,design and maintenance activities for the internal distribution. This was the same problem we encountered in the electricity boards. While they took a lot of effort to plan the generation and transmission systems  , they ignored city distribution. I had discussed how I redid Delhi electricity distribution.  It was bad, and skewed and created difficulties for people . Same is the case with water distribution. 
I developed a model for an urban water distribution , and several solution algorithms. Kshirasagar , my student, worked with me on urban water supply and distribution system planning and operations. That was in 1973. But I didn't see BWSSB doing this activity of even studying the water distribution patterns - pressures and flows. 

The design of the city's distribution network didn't feature intermediate pumping points particularly in elevated places  . Water was flowing based on gravity flow ,pumped from the ground level reservoirs.  So people living at a higher ground level suffered. I was one of the sufferers . My house in 13th cross, 11th main was at the highest point in 11th main, going down on both sides. So we got much less quantity of water compared to others. The valves were operated manually. Sometimes , the ground floor in my building will get water, but we in the first floor won't get it. The valve operating mechanic was clever. He won't open it fully. So we were at his mercy. 
Let us look at the water supply to the city next.

“How many times have you heard someone say

Let me look at our water sources. Bangalore till the beginning of 20th century depended on rain water stored in lakes in and around the city. Even in the 18th century , people suffered from water shortage. There was a circular issued by the British government asking people not to use the water for gardening. Also there was a record of draining a lake once because of contamination with sewage.   
Initially it was lakes in the city like Dharmambudhi and Kempambudhi that supplied water to the city. Then major lakes in the neighborhood like Hesaraghatta reservoir were used to supply water to the city. 
Both the two big lakes inside the city were converted - one into kanteerava stadium and another into the majestic area main  bus stands for both city services and state services . There were about 200 lakes in the city and outskirts. Mathikere and Sankey were huge lakes near us. All these were systematically destroyed mostly by the state government and land sharks.  Even the Forest  department occupied a part of Sankey tank. This was a simple process. First block the inlet drainage lines  and raja kaluve. The water inflow to lakes came down. Then sewerage was allowed into the lakes. Public will object and agitate. So close all inlets. Lake chokes to death. Water hyacinth was another menace.
We need to remember that Bangalore was not a single city. It consisted of a city municipality governed under the Mysore government of the Wodeyars. It consisted of Chamrajpet , and Basavana gudi. The second component of the city was the cantonment area under the British army's control. The third was the civilian areas in the east like Ulsoor which were under the control of the collector of Madras district  . IISc was also part of that section  . In addition , there were many villages which are now part of the city. The city gobbled hundreds of villages to expand itself. So designing and operating a water supply unit was complicated. Also You needed approval from three different entities for any additional work and the costs needed to be shared between the Mysore government and the British government.  It needed negotiations and arms twisting tactics by the British  who had the upper hand. 
Bangalore city got its piped water supply in 1896 for the first time. The first pumping station was built in a village called Soldevanahalli. This station supplied water even during the 1980s to the neighboring areas. When we first got water consumption data, we were not aware of this SD Halli pumping and water service station. Deep search got us  this information. This was untreated water. This pump house was located on the Arkavathi  River  Arkavathi river starts at Nandi hills flows on the northern part of Bangalore and joins cauvery. It is a rain fed river. Then came the water pumping from the Hesaraghatta reservoir. In both cases , the water head was not high. So pumps needed lower power. 
Let us continue in the next post the story of water supply to the city.

Dks
09/07/24, 4:30 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 410
“Why are Westerners more likely to apply 
formal logic when reasoning about everyday 
events, and why does their insistence on logic  sometimes cause them to make errors? Why are  Easterners so willing to entertain apparently contradictory propositions and how can this some-
times be helpful in getting at the truth?” 
Nesbitt   “ Geography of thought”

In the early part of the 20th century , Bangalore saw a big growth in population. Bangalore was growing faster. So water consumption was growing. The lakes couldn't meet the demands despite augmenting hessaraghatta storage capacity. The reservoir is called Chamarajendra reservoir.  
So there were discussions to get water from other sources. Sir MV didn't expect Bangalore to grow. He felt Bangalore was not suitable for industries. Still , he chaired a committee to decide on a source of water for bangalore  Arkavathi river  was nearby and so it was decided to store the water and bring it to Bangalore. A dam was built in Thippegundanahalli to store Arkavathi water . So it was constructed in 1933.  Water was brought to Malleswaram and treated at jewel filters. These exist even now and are in use. That  dam breached in 1936 . The engineers wanted to drain the water from the reservoir and repair the breach. Sir MV didn't like wasting the water.  He opposed draining the reservoir . He repaired the breach by putting a big pressure chamber near the breach point. The reservoir became fine and is still in good shape . This was the first major source. It could meet our water requirements till the late fifties. It was a very wise decision and the water from Arkavathi is still coming to Malleswaram , but the quantum is reducing. 

The south and west of bangalore grew faster than east and north . Both Rajajinagar in the west and jayanagar in the south became huge and very large extensions and suffered from severe water shortages. So in 1958, the government looked at many options. A momentous decision was taken that river cauvery is the right source to supply water to Bangalore.  This saved the city. 
Initially the Thippengundanahalli reservoir was augmented . It was proposed to get 140 mld of water from cauvery - equal to Arkavathi supply. 
More in next.

10/07/24, 4:24 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post  411
“Are people good intuitive statisticians? We already knew that people are good intuitive grammarians: at age four a child effortlessly conforms to the rules of grammar as she speaks, although she has no idea that such rules exist. Do people have a similar intuitive feel for the basic principles of statistics? Amos reported that the answer was a qualified yes. We had a lively debate in the seminar and ultimately concluded that a qualified no was a better answer.”
Amos Tversky 

The major problem was that Bangalore  is more than 400 meters above the level of cauvery river. So a massive quantity of water has to be pushed up to a height of more than 1000 and this required a lot of electrical  energy .   It was decided to pump water over 600 meters to Thorekadanahalli. This was done in stages.  It was a totally new project  . There was a need for electric energy of about 100 MW then. 
 Fortunately Karnataka constructed the massive 1000 MW capacity Sharavathi valley hydroelectric project. So it became  a big energy surplus state then and didn't know how to sell this surplus energy. So it invited Indalco to set up an aluminum plant at Belgaum. It subsidized energy at 5 paise per unit to industries then whereas it was about 30 paise per unit for domestic users. Karnataka had a separate generation company distinct from KEB . KEB was handling only transmission and distribution.  This division was historic starting from the shivasamudram project . 
I had visited the power generation company called hydro electric power corporation in the late sixties - it later became KPC - to discuss the sharavathi project. I remember my visit - the plaster from the roof fell down and there was a commotion. Later , I attended a meeting in the late sixties or early seventies in vidhana soudha convened by GVK Rao, then chief secretary. The purpose was to come up with a selling price for energy to be supplied to Tamilnadu. Karnataka wanted 4.5 paise per unit whereas Tamilnadu was prepared to pay 4 paise per unit only   . Karnataka said ,”we are forced to give you reactive power free.” Discussion was inconclusive. It was a desperate situation. 
Since energy was available , it was easily supplied to the huge pumping station at TK Halli. The first stage ,after delays  ,  brought cauvery water to the south end circle in the early 1970s. It brought a big relief to houses in the southern parts of the city like Jayanagar. The interconnection of pipe network between the Arkavathi supply and cauvery supply happened later. 
From that day, cauvery became the life-giver to Bangalore,  and started supplying more and more water. The government implemented several stages of this cauvery scheme over many decades  drawing very large quantities of water from cauvery to Bangalore. 
Still there has been a gap between supply and demand, demand always higher than supply. In one sense , it is very good. It made people conscious of their needs and availability and thereby reduced their consumption. Our average daily water consumption was less than the WHO standards. This was only a forced conservation of water. Water was wasted in doing many tasks. It is a separate topic .
More in the next.

11/07/24, 4:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 412
“our own intuitions were deficient. In spite of years of teaching and using statistics, we had not developed an intuitive sense of the reliability of statistical results observed in small samples. Our subjective judgments were biased: we were far too willing to believe research findings based on inadequate evidence and prone to collect too few observations in our own research. 
Amos Tversky and Daniel kahneman in
“Thinking fast and slow”

I recall the delays in implementing the various construction activities particularly in the second stage of cauvery water supply project.  
This happened when I was in the karnataka government. After delays of a few  years, a target was set to complete the works by 1983. We found that most ground level reservoirs were not ready and even pipelines were only completed partially. We recommended that Mr Gulam Mohammad be transferred to BWSSB to execute the works faster. He was very efficient in getting work done faster. Delays are due to financial issues with contractors and man power management . Inspections weren't planned at regular intervals. Firefighting was the accepted norm. But happily, he worked in a mission mode and completed more than 80% of the work and brought more water from Cauvery to Bangalore.  

 Today we are drawing 1450 MLD of water from cauvery . The city draws an estimated 750 MLD from groundwater today.  Still it is 30% less than the demand. We live in scarcity. But pilferage is estimated at 30 % and wastage may be another 10 to 15%
I will discuss consumption later.
Our policy and approach to the water supply problem considered only the supply side - keep on increasing supplies in order to match the demand. It didn't look at the demand minimization.  

The entire water planning was totally based on increasing the supply to meet the increasing demands. Unfortunately, we always try to increase supplies without looking at utilization.  
When we say people use water, only a small part is consumed and the remainder is let  off as waste water - both sullage  and sewage.  At that time - in the eighties, there was no effort to look at a proper treatment of waste water as an important source.  A very modern bio digester based treatment plant was put up in K and C valley , designed by Prof SC Pillai from our biochemistry department. But in 1970,   when we visited the plant it wasn't working properly. The partially treated water was let off for use in irrigation. The Vrishabhavathi Valley let off partially treated water for irrigation. So I felt we should use this water for industrial purposes. I did a case study in the Whitefield area. We found the treating and recycling used water is a cheaper and sustainable option. 
We also did a study on water wastage in washing cars . Even the dairy was using 8 litres of water per litre of milk production mainly for washing purposes. So there are many ways of reducing water consumption.  


We also did a cost analysis . We found that about 50  percent of revenue went for power charges. So this gave a strong financial motive for water conservation and recycling. We will save electrical energy also. 

The domestic water supply was subsidized. Any increase in tariff led to objections and agitations and newspaper condemnations. Design didn't take into account the variations in land levels in many places. Public fountains- a term used by BWSSB to refer to public taps -  were surveyed and we found that they were not working , water was not available in most of the fountains. and the environment near public fountains was unhealthy. 
More in the next.

12/07/24, 4:48 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 413
“The pace of innovation at the energy-climate nexus is accelerating; however, educational priorities have not effectively evolved to prepare students for the rapidly changing energy-climate landscape. Change is occurring with advances in distributed energy resources including innovations in renewable energy and new approaches to managing electricity demand, which are linked to growing awareness of the climate change risks of fossil fuel-reliant energy systems .” (Brown 2015; Princen  2015).

The quotes in the  past two posts deal with intuition. Mr Srinivasan PNB promptly commented on this and also talked to me. His comments are
“Good morning sir.  It's a good question to start with statistics.  That's why perhaps we are more anecdotal and not  analytical in our approach. Namaste 🙏”


Let us elaborate the context particularly with Nisbett's comments on the differences between east and west.
First ,let us ask -what is intuition?
Intuition is the feeling or understanding that makes you believe or know that something is true without being able to explain why. 
It is instant understanding without reasoning.  
According to Steve Jobs,  Indians are highly intuitive. Many have proved that humans are not rational. 
But western philosophy is based on logic, reason and has no place for intuition. The only place where West accepts intuition is the process of language learning. Since a child speaks in a language without formal introduction to Grammar and structures  , they consider it intuitive. 
But I believe anything obtained through training is not intuitive. Statistics is not natural ,it is an acquired skill. So we can't expect to have intuitive knowledge of statistics. Particularly projections are subject to manipulation. 
I believe there is a native , inherited intuition and a derived intuition. 
How do we get the derived intuition?
We mostly follow others. You see a successful businessman. You feel you can be successful and so follow him and get into business. He may succeed and you may fail. The best example is startups chasing unicorn status. Most startups fail. Their intuitive understanding is wrong and so their business fails.
 Children see bus drivers and want to become a driver, see a doctor and want to become a doctor  . Then at school , they see a good teacher, most try to emulate the teacher and play like a teacher. This becomes a habit. We have aspirations and find people successful in a job or task, we want to follow them. Is this blind belief and actions based on that good?
Children may  grow out of childhood fantasies ,but adults don't.  We act mostly based on our poor / partial understanding of others. When you see a successful businessman  , you don't see hundreds of failed businesses. So intuition may lead us astray in a wrong path. 
But intuition gives us alternate paths   and if your have developed correct intuition, you will get many paths. Then use reason to select a right , acceptable path. 
Intuition is known as part of hot cognition .
The hot system is the cognitive function that is automatic, spontaneous, fast, effortless, and mostly unconscious, and it is the primary driver of emotions. In the hot cognitive process there is no sense of voluntary control.
We are born with innate skills that we share with other animals. We are prepared to perceive the world around us. Our fear of snakes and spiders and our ability to differentiate between objects, orient our attention, and avoid losses are innate skills that help us to naturally stay safe.
Built upon innate skills is the hot function of learned association between ideas. Learned associations help glue the fabric of ideas and concepts into a common language. For example, we all agree that the capital of Japan is Tokyo. Thus we associate the specific name with the place and know that when we talk about Tokyo, we are all speaking of the same location. Society, culture, and religion depend on learned associations to function. Without them it would be difficult to communicate and understand each other properly. Hot cognition may be derived  intuition. 
Intuition is like a sword . Use it wisely. Many become visionaries due to the correct interpretation of their intuitive knowledge . 
Greetings
Dks

Let me return to 1983 to look at my other activities. 
My house construction was mostly completed by mid 1983 except for floor polishing , plastering and electrical and water fittings plus getting approvals . We decided to conduct the grihapravesam ceremony. I didn't print an invitation , but typed it along with a map of the location. I   invited most of my colleagues in the department and a few others. I organized puja by our regular vadhyar. We got a cow to go into the house. We got a new caterer to provide lunch to about 100 people. It wasn't easy. But the caterer managed the lunch well. My parents were present. Most of the visitors liked the house. My cousin DR Sundaram who had shifted to Bangalore  also attended the lunch. So it went off well.  My father left after lunch. I didn't know at that time  that was to be his last visit to my house and Bangalore. He passed away the next year. 
Lots of things happened during the period 1983-1984  - both good and bad. 
My children wanted a TV. So at last  we bought an Eiko color TV. It was with us for about a decade. 
It was also an important period. Nirmal Jain got married in bangalore.  Mukul Jain , Nirmal's relative, came to Bangalore around that time. We became very good friends. We are still in touch with each other. I also met Nirmal's brother in law and Mukul's father in law at that time. It was a life long association with those families. I cherish my friendship with them . 
I became the chair of the Bangalore chapter of the computer society of India. 
As I said earlier the housing situation in bangalore was getting worse. After the protest by faculty, institute started two sets of quarters D with two bedrooms and E with three bedrooms. It was ready for occupation. So by late 1984, I became eligible for an allotment of a quarter in the campus. I moved into IISc in December 1984.

14/07/24, 4:41 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 414
An explanatory note from me:
I wanted to conclude my water story and wrote post 414. I should have  posted 414 yesterday , but posted 415 instead. Why?
This morning as is my usual practice, I was copying post 414 already prepared by me , but suddenly the whole post vanished , not copied. I couldn't locate it. So I posted 415 . I rewrote 414 again . I am posting it now. 

"The question, 'Can machines think?' I believe to be too meaningless to deserve discussion. Nevertheless I believe that at the end of the century the use of words and general, educated opinion will have altered so much that one will be able to speak of machines thinking without expecting to be contradicted." Alan Turing

We saw bangalore getting its needs for water mostly from two rivers - Arkavathi nearby which is a rain fed river its catchment being Nandi hills  and the big Cauvery about 100 kms away from the city. Over the years , the city depended mostly on Cauvery only. 
We saw that the city generates  about 70% of supplied water as waste water. The waste water is treated in two valleys then in 1982 . But the water was not recycled back into the city , but was used for irrigation in downstream villages. So we suggested that this should be treated properly and reused. People use only less than 10% of their supplied water for potable purposes like drinking and cooking and use the remaining 70% for non potable purposes like washing vessels, floor , car and clothes and for bathing and  toilet usage. So we felt the treated water can be used for non potable uses. It can become a major source , reducing the dependence on Cauvery river. 
There is a third source. Traditionally the city used rain water for its use. Rain water was stored in hundreds of lakes across and outside the city . Some of  these lakes were also networked. Rain water collection from the city area and from its large outer periphery extending to about 50kms was done by a great network of drainage canals . There were small canals in the streets , followed by bigger canals and further draining into large / huge canals called raja kaluve which drained the water into the lakes . It was an excellent system executed by the Maharaja of Mysore , Sri Nalavadi Krishnaraja wodeyar in the beginning of the 20th century. 
Unfortunately, this system has been destroyed. Most canals were blocked such that Rain water didn't go to the lake as planned. That made the lakes dry. So the lake was encroached and buildings came up . Even raja kaluve had also been encroached. This was due to poor maintenance. 
Bangalore gets rainfall that can meet about 25% of its water requirements. So we suggested to go for distributed Rain water harvesting. This will reduce the demand on cauvery and also reduce electrical power and energy requirements. This will also reduce the flooding in our roads during rains  . We wanted an innovative ,distributed rain water harvesting scheme to be designed and implemented. But unfortunately it was not taken up by BWSSB. 
On the usage side, water conservation measures had to be identified and implemented. Many pipes were leaking. They had to be replaced. These simple measures like leakage detection and correction can reduce consumption by easily 10%. 
I also proposed a computerised data collection, alerts and management architecture. The twenty-first century saw a new area - cyber physical systems emerging. But I proposed it in 1982 itself. 
I wrote a monograph on our work on Bangalore's water supply and distribution system. It became a part of the four volumes books on Bangalore urban ecosystems studies brought out by KSCST. 
This concludes my discussions on water supply project undertaken by me for about four years.
Greetings

“Most of the time we live in an interior world of dreams, desires, and obsessive thoughts. But in this period of exceptional creativity, we are impelled by the need to get something done that has a practical effect. We force ourselves to step outside our inner chamber of habitual thoughts and connect to the world, to other people, to reality. Instead of flitting here and there in a state of perpetual distraction, our minds focus and penetrate to the core of something real. At these moments, it is as if our minds—turned outward—are now flooded with light from the world around us, and suddenly exposed to new details and ideas, we become more inspired and creative.”
Robert Greene in   “Mastery”

I missed a few activities in the 1983 period. My daughter reminded me about it. 
Madhav Gadgil prepared a proposal for setting up the centre of ecological sciences . I was another  signatory to the proposal. It was sent to the ministry of environment , government of India. The ministry agreed to fund it and support it . So in 1983 , the centre was set up and I associated myself with the centre. The first job we did was recruiting people. An extension centre was set up in Sirsi and many projects were focussed on the Uttara kannada district. 
I visited Sirsi several times. I also initiated a project on energy surveys similar to our ASTRA survey. 
As already stated , the Gokak agitation was the talk of the state. During one of my visits, Mr Shivarama karanth also came to Sirsi . He was invited to address the students in a college in Siddapur. Madhav requested me to accompany him. I did that. As expected the students asked about the agitation to implement  the Gokak report . He said that we don't have books in Kannada for various topics of mathematics and  science . If we decide to shift to kannada medium, we will go backward and become frogs in the well. After his talk, we were taken to the library . The librarian wanted to show the book collections , but Mr Karanth said ,” I don't want to see a dead library. I want to see a living library. Show me your issues register.” Nothing was shown  . We had lunch in Mr Ganesh Hegde’s house.  He was Ramakrishna Hegde's cousin. Prof SV Bhat , IISc physics department,  was his son in law. Then we returned to Sirsi. 
 I was active with most of the CES activities in addition to our departmental academic programs and I was also handling the hybrid computer lab , which continued to be a central facility .  
I became active with NSS . Mr Bhide was in charge of Karnataka and he invited me to several small cities. I visited and lectured in Bijapur, Dharwad, Gulbarga , Belgaum , KD Gudi  , Adichunchanagiri,  and many other places for NSS camps organised by the universities in karnataka like Bangalore,  Mysore, Karnatak, and Gulbarga and PUC board . It became hectic. 
At that time, there were many environment and forests related activities going on across India . We discussed the Silent Valley project. Similarly in the Himalayas two leaders started a chipko movement. It encouraged women to hug trees when the development project leaders came to cut the trees  . The names of two important people are Sundarlal Bahuguna and Chandi Prasad Bhat . Sundarlal came to Bangalore. Doordarshan wanted him to be interviewed by Prof Madhav Gadgil. Gadgil suggested my name. So they approached me. I agreed to the interview.  It was held in Cubbon Park  to have a green ambience appropriate for the topic of discussion. Mr Bahuguna answered the questions nicely . This was my first TV appearance.  Later I appeared in many panel discussions.  I also produced two documentaries - one on ecology and another on water.

“The confirmation bias is the mother of all misconceptions. It is the tendency to interpret new information so that it becomes compatible with our existing theories, beliefs, and convictions. In other words, we filter out any new information that contradicts our existing views (“disconfirming evidence”). This is a dangerous practice. “Facts do not cease to exist because they are ignored,” said writer Aldous Huxley. However, we do exactly that, as super-investor Warren Buffett knows: “What the human being is best at doing is interpreting all new information so that their prior conclusions remain intact.”

Rolf Dobelli   “The art of thinking clearly.”

Let me continue with our movement to IISc quarters. We were allotted E20. It was upstairs in a block of 4 flats - 2 in the ground floor and two in the first floor. Prof GPadmanabhan was our neighbour. These quarters were far inside the campus after the academic area. We needed to go to Malleswaram to shop . That was difficult  . There was an amenities block with a few shops. We got vegetables there. 
But life was heavenly. Children had lots of advantages. We can leave them alone in the house because of the secure environment.  There are many facilities in the campus  - play areas, lots of open space, swimming pool, movies in gymkhana , books borrowed from our faculty club or library, etc. They didn't depend on transport to go to school  . They walked  to the school. So they were independent. They had friends to visit and play. They loved the house and the ambience. They enjoyed iisc stay. It played an important role in mounding them with  good habits like reading general books, good communications , and interpersonal skills . They became independent and became responsible, but had no religious or spiritual exposures. We visited several places. 
 
We settled down quickly. We knew most of our neighbors as they were all IISc professors/ faculty.  Our life became tension free. 
I was busy with the departmental work. We started two ME programs  -one , computer science and engineering and another systems science and automation, a joint  multidisciplinary program with the EE department.  I attended a meeting to change the name of our department . We got approval from all for the new  name computer science and automation. We were no longer known as the school of automation. From then on we became CSA . We retained automation in the name. 

I chaired another committee to introduce a BE computer science program. We successfully started the BE program. 
I got active with both the computer society of India and the institution of engineers. We became the state with the largest number of student chapters. We were entrusted with the responsibility of organising an international conference titled “ CAD , CAM, CAE for industrial progress “ under the auspices of IFIP, an international federation . I became the organising chair and Prof  Rajaraman became the program chair. It was a great opportunity. It needed extensive preparation. We formed two committees. The conference was scheduled in June 1985. 
We employed two girls to take care of the secretarial work  . We fixed the newly opened Taj Residency as the venue as it had a large hall with seating capacity of 500 people.

Let me continue with my explanation about our preparations for the conference.
We decided on Taj Residency as the venue, had discussions with Taj people and finalized the rates. Next we decided on the call for papers and it was printed and sent to all institutions.

He said he can't stay in Madras and wanted to shift to Bangalore.

We conducted our work on the conference scheduled to be held  in June 1984. It went off well. That was a relief. There were minor hiccups in getting the registration kits. So that was a great experience of organising a major international conference. We got more than 20 sponsorships from industries and IITs. So we had a surplus budget. 
We got the accounts audited and returned the surplus money to CSI headquarters.

“Motivation 1 for living 
Initially. the assumption about human behavior was a simple one that  We were trying to survive.
Motivation 2 for living 
to seek reward and avoid punishment more broadly.  The engine of commerce has been fueled equally by carrots and sticks.
Motivation 2 has endured for a very long time. Indeed, it is so deeply embedded in our lives that most of us scarcely recognize that it exists.
Motivation 2.0 still wasn’t exactly ennobling. It suggested that, in the end, human beings aren’t much different from horses—that the way to get us moving in the right direction is by dangling a crunchier carrot or wielding a sharper stick.
Motivation 2 has become far less compatible with, and at times downright antagonistic to: how we organize what we do; how we think about what we do; and how we do what we do.”
Extracted from Daniel Pink's book  “ Drive”

Let me discuss about  a few friends acquired by me during this period.

He was also a strict follower of the principles of ahimsa expounded by jainism. He used to eat dinner before sunset. He was very particular about it. I arranged a cook for them. 19/07/24, 4:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 420
How you do it - for money or satisfaction?
Open source is a great movement . It is not for  money people developed open sources . Many open source like Unix, Linux, Firefox, Wikipedia, cloud stack,   etc were great successes. 
“MIT management professor Karim Lakhani and Boston Consulting Group consultant Bob Wolf surveyed 684 open-source developers, mostly in North America and Europe, about why they participated in these projects. Lakhani and Wolf uncovered a range of motives, but they found “that enjoyment-based intrinsic motivation, namely how creative a person feels when working on the project, is the strongest and most pervasive driver.” A large majority of programmers, the researchers discovered, reported that they frequently reached the state of optimal challenge called “flow.””
Daniel Pink.        Drive


I have said earlier that this period of 1984 onwards is a period of diffusion of my knowledge and modern computer and network  technology. This meant a lot of travels .
 I did it through  hundreds of lectures in more than 300 colleges, schools and universities. Then I went a step further. I started running courses in many colleges and universities which gave a startup boost. Then I took the next step of architecting computer labs and also helping in the purchase of computers and integrating with networks.
Then I took the next step of helping the institutions in developing a modern curriculum. 
Then I helped in recruiting faculty members. 
Later I did computer architecting , computer selection, networking  and software selection for many research labs and government departments including KSRTC, Bangalore diary, land army, mysore paper mills, and many others. It is a long list. We will discuss it in detail. 
 Let me continue discussions of  my association with Prof KAK, MCE, Hassan . He wanted to apply for a network program supported by UGC. So I gave a letter saying we are willing to network with them, and  support them academically . He got the project and  funds. 
Next, he wanted to start a MTech program on computers and communications. It was the first PG program to be run by an engineering college in a modern and relevant area.   He sent a proposal to UGC for approval and he put my name as a supporting and advising expert. I heard from UGC that they liked the proposal, they were happy with my association  and sanctioned it. 
I taught two courses for this MTech program. This was the starting of my involvement with teaching computer courses like data structures,  operating systems and databases in many institutions and universities over the next two decades. I continued this till 2000. Most colleges and some universities started computer science and engineering programs , but they didn't have enough knowledgeable faculty. Most employed electrical or electronics faculty to teach CS courses. It was a very bad situation . I felt that a push is needed for providing a standard form for CS in our colleges. So despite enormous inconveniences and physical stress , I supported this outreach activity in several colleges and universities in karnataka , Tamil Nadu and Andhra. It was a way of my helping in the generation and growth of CS expertise in the country beyond those coming from IISc and IITs. Today I feel happy about it .
I taught at MCE Hassan for a few years. I had to travel by ordinary bus for four hours . It wasn't easy. Their budget is minimal and couldn't put me in a good hotel. 
More in the next post. 
Greetings 
Dks
20/07/24, 4:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 421
“In real life our behavior is far more complex than the textbook allows and often confounds the idea that we’re purely rational. We don’t save enough for retirement even though it’s to our clear economic advantage to do so. We hang on to bad investments longer than we should, because we feel far sharper pain from losing money than we do from gaining the exact same amount.” 
Daniel Pink       Drive

During our international conference , I met Prof Santana Krishnan from TCE Madurai, my Alma mater. He requested me to visit the college and I agreed. So in 1985 , I visited TCE after more than 20 years of gap. I didn't visit before, surprising. I gave lectures for four days. Prof Nityanandan was the principal. He became a good friend. After that my visit to TCE became regular and my association continues till now  . I lectured at most of their conferences, seminars , short term courses and also  lectures to faculty and students. I have been with their academic council for decades. I also played a significant role in faculty selections . I had great interactions with many principals like Prof MV Ramamurthy, Prof R Srinivasan, Prof Abhaykumar and Prof Raja and provided advice on academics, curriculum and  administration. I am very happy that I could return a bit of my gratitude to my college. 

Another principal with whom I had a good association was Prof Viswanath of Gogte institute of Technology , Belgaum.  I visited his college for a few years and delivered lectures to students. 
I started teaching in the newly set up NMAMIT at Nitte  . That is where I met Prof NR Shetty ,Principal,  KREC. That association also continued for a very long time. I assisted him in Bangalore university when he was the vice chancellor there . 
Prof Radhakrishnan was the head of the computer science department . He didn't know much about computers. He invited me to teach two courses for their BE computer science students. I also met Mr Sridhar Hegde, principal of the college at that time.  I agreed to their request  and taught both computer organisation and data structures courses followed by a course on operating systems in the next year. 
Nitte is a small city. I went by a night bus to karkala.  Col.  BSGK Murthy received me at the bus stand and put me up in their hostel. I was upset. I went to the college and took the lectures. At lunch time - food was supplied in their  canteen- , I told him and later the principal that I didn't like the stay arrangements made by them . So they came with me in the evening and put me in a hotel close to the bus stand. 
During one of my visits, we visited the Gomateshwara  statue on a hillock  . I felt chest pain , still continued with my lectures.  
After returning from Karkala, I got a stress test done by Dr Ramesh, a cardiologist. He said there is a problem. We decided to consult a cardiologist in bombay. We left our children alone at our quarters in iisc , left by air to Bombay.  We stayed in  the Bombay Suburban guest house and met Dr Nair . He did an angio next day. It showed  one block, but in the right ventricular artery. So he ruled out surgery, put me on medication. That was in 1989, I think.

21/07/24, 4:15 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 422

“if people do things for lunk-headed, backward-looking reasons, why wouldn’t we also do things for significance-seeking, self-actualizing reasons? If we’re predictably irrational—and we clearly are—why couldn’t we also be predictably transcendent?”

For some people work remains routine, unchallenging, and directed by others. But for a surprisingly large number of people, jobs have become more complex, more interesting, and more self-directed. And that type of work presents a direct challenge to the assumptions of Motivation 2.0.
The consulting firm McKinsey & Co. estimates that in the United States, only 30 percent of job growth now comes from algorithmic work, while 70 percent comes from heuristic work. A key reason: Routine work can be outsourced or automated; artistic, empathic, nonroutine work generally cannot.
“Intrinsic motivation is conducive to creativity; controlling extrinsic motivation is detrimental to creativity.”

Daniel Pink.  Drive

Let me explain my problem with chest pain and my actions on that problem. 
When I felt the chest pain in Nitte that afternoon, I could have ignored that. I went to IISc health center on my return from Karkala .  Our doctor did an ECG and said I am fine. Three doctors said that I was overstressed and I should rest after lunch. This not once but several times whenever  I got chest pain. But I was concerned about the occurrence at karkala and felt I should do something about it. Only Dr Subba Rao, our CMO supported me. Even though IISc will not reimburse my expenses - it wasn't small and it ran into more than 30000 rupees expenses then, still  I decided to go through checkups. So first I did the stress test in Bangalore . Then we went to Bombay for an angiogram to be done. It saved me. 
So here it is my perseverance and both algorithmic and heuristic thinking that worked for me. I didn't believe in the opinions of our doctors except Dr Subba Rao.  
My next assignment was teaching a course at IIM Bangalore.  IIM ,even in 1985,  wasn't concerned about the technological developments and their impacts on businesses. It had a dated and conventional PG diploma program in management.  Some students met me and wanted me to teach a course on computer systems. I agreed. The institute approved my course. So I started teaching at IIM . It was difficult to travel to IIM Bangalore from IISc . I needed to take two buses . The bus to Bannerghatta was a rural bus. It was tough. Still I managed to teach for about 5 years regularly in IIM Bangalore.  Then as I found travel difficult, I told them that I won't be able to teach the course. I met several faculty there and had good interactions. 
I visited MIT Manipal several times for lectures.
There are two major instances I want to recollect. 
One was the fact that I did a full course on UNIX. That was the first time Unix was taught in the country and the first time I taught Unix. Prof Prithviraj organized these lectures . He put me in a small hotel then. The course went off well. 
Later  , MIT Manipal got a new principal during the late eighties. Prof APrabhakar became the principal. I knew him from his days in Osmania university.  I knew his brother Prof   A Ramakrishnan, his brother in law Prof KS Narendra and his nephew A Srinivasan . He started a PG MTech program in computer science. He invited me to give courses on data structures and operating systems.  I visited Manipal three times to conduct the courses. My iisc colleague Rajaram was the head then. He was agitated even on small issues like chalk pieces and duster. 
These teaching activities revealed the almost primitive state of the engineering colleges . That is not only on  infrastructure , lab facilities and curriculum , but also in the mindsets of the principals and faculty.

“Tom said to himself that it was not such a hollow world, after all. He had discovered a great law of human action, without knowing it -- namely, that in order to make a man or a boy covet a thing, it is only necessary to make the thing difficult to attain. If he had been a great and wise philosopher, like the writer of this book, he would now have comprehended that Work consists of whatever a body is OBLIGED to do, and that Play consists of whatever a body is not obliged to do. And this would help him to understand why constructing artificial flowers or performing on a tread-mill is work, while rolling ten-pins or climbing Mont Blanc is only amusement. There are wealthy gentlemen in England who drive four-horse passenger coaches twenty or thirty miles on a daily line, in the summer, because the privilege costs them considerable money; but if they were offered wages for the service, that would turn it into work and then they would resign.”
Mark Twain.     Stories of Tom sawyer 

Let me look at some more institutions where I ran a full course. 
DRDO came up with a scheme of getting young BSc passed graduates and giving them a specialized two year MSc program in computer science . One of the  universities chosen was Madurai Kamaraj university and another was Mysore university. I was given a special visiting professor status at MK university. I taught several courses for two to three years. Prof Chandrasekaran from the Physics department was the coordinator . He was a serious researcher . I visited the university several times to teach data structures and operating systems. I helped in the selection of a few lecturers and an assistant professor who became the head of the department later. I also helped a rural based student to write a paper. I took my family on one visit. We stayed in the guest house. Food was good. We took all the students for dinner in the city.  There was a near tragedy for our return. We asked a faculty member to book our return tickets by the karnataka bus. The karnataka super deluxe bus was better than the Tamilnadu bus. It also had better maintenance and breakdowns were less than the Tamilnadu buses. So i prefered karnataka buses for travel.  The faculty member booked our seats  ,  but didn't come and deliver the tickets. Prof Chandrasekaran was upset. We took a car to his house . He said he bought the reservations but when he called the guest house, they told him that I had left. So he didn't come to the university. It was gladly resolved. We left by the night bus. 
All that was a great experience. My son enjoyed the stay. 
I became a UGC Visiting fellow and spent 15 days at SV University.  I taught a full course of operating systems there. I split the  two weeks into two visits of six days each. During my second visit two incidents happened. 
One was that I started vomiting due to some food poisoning. I had to eat in a hotel in the Tirupati Town and that was the cause. Anyway I was better the next day. The second was that the marriage of Mr KVK Vijayakumar was held in Tirupati at that time. I attended the marriage on both days. There was a lot of police presence as his eldest brother was a DIG. 
I also taught operating systems in SK university , Anantapur for MCA students. Similarly I taught two courses for the management students of TAPMI in Manipal. Anil Revankar who was an officer of KSCST, was a faculty member at TAPMI.  I took my wife and son for one visit. We went by a KSRTC night bus. It turned turtle after crossing Sakleshpur . We were lucky that the area was a flat land. We could get out of the bus safely. We were not hurt. We took another bus and reached Manipal. 
I taught a database course for MCA students in  Bangalore university when NR Shetty was the vice chancellor.  I also taught in  Mangalore university, Mysore university and Karnataka university . 
I taught distributed operating systems for the first time at REC Trichy. I also taught at KREC for their MTech SACA program.  
BDT college of engineering at Davangere , the only government college ,started BE computer science program.  Prof Bhandari, principal requested me to start the program by teaching the first course. Later he became DTE .
I had conducted a course on decision support systems for the PG students at Asian Institute of Technology,  Bangkok.  I visited Bangkok for three months in the summer of 1987 to teach this course.  It was a new experience. 
My experiences were mixed. Let us see in the next post.

Om Bhadram Karnnebhih Shrnnuyaama Devaah |
Bhadram Pashyema-Akssabhir-Yajatraah |
Sthirair-Anggais-Tussttuvaamsas-Tanuubhih |
Vyashema Deva-Hitam Yad-Aayuh |

Meaning:
1: O Devas, May we Hear with our Ears what is Auspicious,
2: O (Devas who are) Worthy of Worship, May we See with our Eyes what is Auspicious,
3: With (Sense) Organs Steady and Body Praying (due to Hearing and Seeing the Auspicious) ...
4: ... May we Attain (i.e. Spend) the Lifespan allotted by the Devas (thus finding fulfillment in our lives).
Upanishads

Let me summarise my experiences. 
The infrastructure in most institutions was poor. There were not enough computers. Class rooms were not clean. Sometimes, it was an effort to find a vacant classroom or open one. 
The curriculum was not modern or good. It was mostly framed by electronics or electrical faculty. It had many general courses and many electrical and electronics courses. The saddest part was a low emphasis for programming and solving large problems. It pained me a lot. I had argued with many about this state. 
While many students were just above average, there were quite a few who were very bright.  I remember one student at Nitte, one at BIET,  and a few at Madurai and several in many colleges. 
Some students  from MTech programs taught by me joined as faculty in many colleges thereby strengthening the CS department.  
Many learnt rigour and quality from me. 
Many were motivated. 
Many got jobs. 
So I felt happy with my contributions. I felt I should do my best to improve this situation. I recalled the effort put by some CS faculty in famous universities like UCB teaching in many universities to improve the quality of CS education. 
In addition , I have delivered a series of lectures in many colleges and universities and IDRBT, Hyderabad.  I gave two courses for Future software company in Madras.  
In addition I have delivered lectures in many industries like TCS, CMC, DOS, etc.
I have also given several short term courses and tutorials on OS, databases, distributed transactions processing, federated databases, computer systems, etc at IISc, CSI , IEEE,  IE and Indian computer conference . I will discuss my proficience courses later. 
All these were done in addition to lectures and keynote addresses , paper presentations in hundreds of institutions and conferences and my regular academic, research and project activities in IISc.  
All these activities continued for decades. But I increased my activities in the period 1985 to 2004 and even beyond. 
I realised that I had the capacity to do more. I felt I should do more for the growth of computer culture in the country. I never thought about my health seriously but never ignored the precautions needed for good health.
