A question arises 
Why did I do all these extra , non remunerative activities ?
I visited more than 300 institutions , schools, universities and conferences in India and abroad? Why?
No visit gave me money or fame or award. Still I did it. Why?
I travelled by bus to most small towns and there was no direct train to madurai from Bangalore. Still I went to Trichy by train and took a bus or took a night bus. Many travels were not easy. Still I did it. Why?
I got diabetes in 1978 itself. Many would have stopped most of these travels and lectures. I have seen that both my father and my uncles didn't walk and got tired easily. They couldn't work long. But fortunately, I got my long walking habits from childhood  . That came from my elder uncle , Narayana Sarma , . As a child of six, I used to walk with him for miles with enthusiasm and wasn't feeling tired. That habit helped me even after I had diabetes.  
To add to that , I got a heart problem by 1987. Definitely , most wouldn't dare to travel regularly by bus. Most will sit and commiserate and worry about their health. I increased my activities. I didn't allow my health problems to come in the way of my travels and talks. I didn't ignore my health . I took a lot of precautions. Why did I do all these? 
The answers are
1.I liked to lecture. I wanted to give my knowledge to others. That was an innate feeling that came from my family background. 
2.I felt that education was not in a good shape and let me do my best to help in improving computer science education , which was in a bad shape. 
3.I felt happy talking to young and different types of students. 
4.I was curious to know about our computer culture, computer absorption and spread. That is why I got involved with TCS and many others. I was also curious about our education system. These visits gave me an opportunity to know and help. Today, if we are in a leadership position in IT global services , it is due to a synergic efforts of engineering colleges and agile  IT companies. I played a small part in focusing the awareness towards applications . 
5. I never went after money. That is  due to my family value system. Our family for generations  was frugal, ethical  and satisfied . They valued scholarship. I inculcated that spirit in me. I also never bothered about awards. 
Greetings 
Dks
25/07/24, 4:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 426
“Studies have shown that children at the age of 4 years  may ask anywhere from one hundred to three hundred questions a day (interestingly, some research shows the four-year-old girl asks even more questions than a boy of that age. She is the ultimate questioning machine.)
Questioning at these early ages may seem like child’s play, but it’s a complex, high-order level of thinking. It requires enough awareness to know that one does not know—and the ingenuity to begin to do something to remedy that. As the Harvard University–based child psychologist Paul Harris points out, young children discover early on that the information they seek can be easily extracted from other human beings, merely by using that certain combination of words and vocal inflection that forms a question.”
Warren Berger.  The book of beautiful questions

Let's continue the discussions about my intentions and work culture .
Did I stop the activities because of my health conditions  ?
The answer is no. Instead I enlarged  my activities into improving computer absorption in industries, governments, institutions, banks and NGOs. My activities were increasing year by year and reached their peak post retirement. 
Did I want to do something that I didn't do?
My regrets : I wanted to write text books. But I was reluctant. Because I see the fast pace of technological advances  and was worried about books becoming dated and obsolete.  I wrote almost about 80% of a book on operating systems and most of another book on energy resources ,utilization and conservation. Then I lost interest as I wasn't happy. Both books were not copies from other books. Both were based on my experiences, my understanding, my perceptions and teaching . I wasn't sure who would read them.

I asked the question why I did all these activities in the last post. Let  me continue it from another angle. 
Let me look at my life paths and reason some actions. Let me go back and look at a couple of major things that happened and my reactions. 
1.I have always found alternate paths under adversarial circumstances . I used to always prepare and follow two or more paths . 
When I found in 1970 that power systems research wasn't appreciated in IISc and in the world and most researchers moved to control systems, I decided to switch to computer science research. Later , I switched my areas from operating systems to databases to transactions processing to data mining. 

But when I found that  rural energy research was not appreciated, I plunged into it for more than two decades both in ASTRA and CES.  I had a PhD student - Dr KM Chetty-  working on optimisation of rural energy centres. I even did a district level energy plan for Uttara kannada district. TV Ramachandra worked on this model and prepared a total plan. 

Later I worked on sustainable living models . I developed a total energy model for hydro projects.  I developed a model for development planning based on carrying capacity for Dakshina kannada district. 

2.Why these  contradictory approaches - one switch due to a popular area like computers and another to an unpopular area like rural energy? 
It is not algorithmic thinking but heuristic and deep thinking that worked in both cases and made me take a good decision both times. It is due to my flexible approach and open mind. 
There was an internal urge in me telling me - “do something different . Don't do the same thing again and again like what Pattinathar said . It is that internal urge which pushed me in my actions , not any other motivations. No one told me what I should do , which direction I should move,  what is good for me etc. 
Similarly  I also didn't force my children to follow my ideas. If I say something , they may resist it. I gave them general thoughts. But they followed what they wanted and I am happy about them now - they defined their paths and are happy with their activities. Their curiosities were kindled and are helping them. They have sharp minds to understand many views of a problem and decide. 
I wanted them to walk many paths and look for many options and alternatives. 
That doesn't mean that I am not interested in their approach to life. I did discuss generally about their path , introduced them to good habits and scholarly approaches. So I encouraged both to have a good scholarship . They observed our frugal living. Through many travels with me, they saw the country and the behaviours of people. A simple nudge worked though sometimes  after a violent argument,   particularly with my daughter. 
More in the next.

Dks

“The wisdom of the highest scriptures, the doctrines of all sacred texts, the epics of the gods and wise sagas of life, the science of mantras, yantras, and so on, powerful incantations, the wisdom passed to each generation, the sacred teachings of those who worship the Absolute as Divine Father or Mother, and other revered works – all these doctrines and creeds only bring the downfall of those whose minds are restless and deluded.” Siva to Sakti. 
Guru Gita

Let me continue my self introspection and analysis.
3.That urge for looking for new aspects and learning them is evident from my concentration on teaching several courses at IISc and more than 20 institutions. I never shied away from things and areas that are unknown to me. I plunged into new learnings and new areas without fear and it continues even now.  I do it even now- the urge to learn new aspects  to understand nature, knowledge  ,   people, society, world. I was technological then and philosophical now.
4. In addition to my involvement in computer education in engineering institutions  , I looked at another area . I assisted in computer purchases in hundreds of institutions, government offices, banks . I also participated in many activities of NGOs.
5.  One of my major social and educational activities was the MahithiSindhu program of Mr SM Krishna, CM of karnataka to computerize 1000 schools at one go. It was a highly challenging job and most senior  officers stayed away from it. But I executed it successfully with cooperation and understanding from Mr Vijayabhaskar, commissioner of education then and Mr Viswanath, Minister of education. It was the first time we undertook a massive transformation in school education and put all our efforts into it. We were happy with the outcome. I had many learnings from that and will discuss it later. 
I realized that balance in life implies multiple paths - academic, social etc. I wasn't involved in direct politics ,but kept in touch with many politicians. 
Let me look at some important happenings  in my personal and family life. I want to discuss three happenings here. 
One was the death of my father immediately after I moved into IISc quarters. My father visited my malleswaram house several times and he felt that it was a good house from the Spiritual point of view. He was unfortunate not to visit my IISc quarters. Many will say that the quarters was unlucky for me. I should have stayed in malleswaram.  I didn't believe in it. I continued in IISc quarters. So here my rationalist approach worked. 
So adapt your thinking and actions. 
The next was my health - diabetes.  I felt I should do my best to handle this and should not curtail my other activities. My survival needed only the IISc job , not the other activities and associated travels. I didn't reduce any of my activities or travels but increased them despite certain difficulties. That is my positive mindset - let me take the bull by its horns as they say, don't run away, don't commiserate and worry. Adjust and adapt. 
The third was  the importance of following a regular discipline in  life. I adapted a regular regimen of activities on mindfulness, walking, yoga, food control, regular intake of medicine, regular consultations, regular testing- this I continued without fail for about 50 years . That has made me live actively this long. That is a miracle according to me . 

So the lessons from these are :
One should Lead a balanced, positive, flexible, adaptable and multidimensional life. Living  is not a binary activity either this or that . Living is this, that and that - awareness, mindfulness, positive approach, regularity, wisdom to choose, heuristic and algorithmic (not or) thinking and actions, contemplation before and after a decision, improving consciousness .
Strong, inflexible opinions need to be used only on some occasions, but  not always. Is it possible to have both a flexible and inflexible mind or as people advocate an either or binary thinking? Everyone should contemplate this seriously. It is not easy. 
 I followed both rationalism and common sense but not radicalism . I followed science and spirituality. I felt the need or urge for humanism not mechanical living . 
Humanism is not understood or taught in schools and even by parents. They follow the dictum “ what I don't know, my children need not know”.

The purpose of my writing these aspects can be stated as:
Others can learn from these and build their children and students. 
That concludes my discussions on my moving around these 40 plus years. 
Greetings
Dks

Mr. N Ramanathan has provided detailed comments. 
“When you are passionate about a thing, everything else including family, takes a back seat. So little wonder that you had pushed yourself too hard in the bargain.
Again at a tender age, we used to walk for long distances to attend school or buy small household items. In my mother’s village, there used to be no water in the canal during summer months and every morning we had to walk for a couple of miles to take bath in the river. Travel was in a crowded bus or train. One was physically stressed out on several counts. As a beneficial fall out, we were mentally tuned to accept travel hardship even after much improved life style in later years. My experience was similar to yours, when I had to frequently travel between Madurai and Bengaluru in the 80s on official work. But you have done same for a public cause.
Money is very necessary for survival but never a motivator for achieving excellence. It’s more so in the case of academicians and those involved in R & D. But awards and recognitions are clear motivators and these should have come in good measure in your case. Of course you are not the loser.”

“Dear Anna,
Namaskarams.
Your analysis of how you have led your life is highly inspirational. You have set high standards for yourself-

In early 2000s, rising environmental concerns had begun to stir the first rumblings of a backlash against all the nonessential “stuff” proliferated by designers, much of it disposable but not recyclable. As the economy contracted, those overdesigned and overpriced goods came to be seen as not just inaccessible but inappropriate. The world seemed to be crying out for a different kind of design: no less creative but more responsible and resourceful, with a greater emphasis on solving problems, embracing constraints, and doing more with less.”
Warren Berger “  CAD monkeys, —”

Let me return to 1984 and get going on my future activities. My research and teaching continued with more focus on databases. 
As I said earlier , I continued as chair of CSI . I organised many conferences in this period. 
CSI wanted to focus on scientific and engineering applications. We ran the first national science and engineering applications conference. We started a new conference series on data called COMAD. I was part of this group for a few years. Some people wanted to create confusion. So they started another annual conference called CISMOD. My friends in CSI wanted me to talk to them . I didn't consider CISMOD as an enemy. I participated in some CISMOD conferences and delivered keynote speeches. I also attended a few COMAD conferences. 
Well ,two  turning points happened at this juncture. One was on AI. We had discussions on AI and sanskrit based on a paper by R Briggs in an AI journal. It excited many of us. Prof Mahabala wanted to have an international conference. So I agreed to chair the conference. There were many novelties in this conference. We decided to conduct it in the Sankar mutt premises. Swami Paramananda Bharati was keen on this conference. We decided to invite both sanskrit scholars and computer scientists. 
The title was “Knowledge Representation in Samskritam . “. It was sponsored by CSI. We invited Briggs and a few more came from abroad. We got several Sanskrit  scholars from North and South.  Many Professors from IIT participated. N Seshagiri gave a talk on Sphota theory. He gave a new interpretation to the sphota theory. Prof Satchidananda murthy , vice chairman,  UGC inaugurated the conference. 
We went through a lot of preparations for conducting the conference and had several meetings in Sankar mutt  . We got a lot of national publicity from TV and All India Radio. It was a great event. We met Shringeri Jagadguru Abhinava vidya teertha Bharati swami after the conference. 
I also gave several talks in the institution of engineers. I became the chair if the computer engineering board also. 
The second turning point happened after a discussion with Dr PJ Naik , additional secretary planning. Even though I was no more in the government in a formal position, I was invited and consulted by my successor , Prof Prahlada Rao. I assisted him in selecting scientific officers. I continued my associations with the government in several departments.  My discussions with PJ Naik was about the Karnataka government computer centre and how to make it better. We decided to setup a high power committee to handle computer purchases under the chairmanship of the secretary to chief minister. Later it was shifted to the additional chief secretary. I was to become a key member of this committee. It was a long journey. I will discuss in the next post.

“Our belief system is our personal truth that governs our mind. Our judgments are all based on our belief system, even if they tend to challenge our innermost nature. There’s a part of our mind that constantly evaluates everything and everyone. There’s also another part that receives all the judgments and carries the guilt, blame, and shame.
Your belief system makes you fear anything that goes against it. Whatever it is that makes you question your beliefs will certainly bring you a sense of insecurity. This is the main reason why it’s necessary to have the courage to argue regarding our beliefs.”
Don't Miguel Ruiz

I need to describe the technological milieu we were living in the eighties. A technological explosion was happening quietly and most people including computer and management professionals in India  weren't aware of its impacts and didn't understand its significance. They lived in the previous era of costly mainframe computing. 
What happened then?
The arrival of IBM PC. 
It brought a vast turbulence in our understanding. It transformed many sectors.  It reduced the cost of a computer by several orders of magnitude - from crores of rupees to a lakh. A country which had a handful of mainframes and another handful of minicomputers suddenly found itself in a situation where thousands of computers can reach people at an affordable cost. 

I was upset that most institutions in India started computer science programs without any inhouse computing facility. Now all institutions and all industries can afford a centralised computing facility.  
Let me look at the micro processor history briefly. Computers were initially recognized by the hardware technologies and software was a standby item. The first generation computers used vacuum tubes and were difficult to operate. You spend time removing blown tubes and heat generated was very high. 
 The second generation in the late fifties used transistors. They had a very simple programming language. The two computers I used in 1965 at NAL and HAL were second generation computers. Then came the great invention - integrated circuits. It was possible to put together a circuit consisting of several transistors in a small silicon sheet. It reduced space requirements, costs  and also power requirements. It was more reliable. 
Then came the breakthrough- very large integration - putting thousands of transistors in a small sheet , now known as a chip. It looked like a potato chip in size. While Fairchild started the VLSI chips, Intel produced the first 4 bits  processor in 1971. But it was used in calculators , not useful in a computer. Zilog came with an 8 bits processor which found a lot of uses in telecom and micro controller industries. 
Intel went into designing and producing new processors starting with a popular 8 bits 8085 and then moving on to 16 bits procesdors 8086, 80186, 80286, 80386 etc. I built a desktop with a 80286 processor and used it for more than 15 years. Then came 32 bits processors packing lakhs of transistors. 
A big competitor to Intel was Motorola. They also came with 6800 processors followed by 16 bits 68000, 68010, 68020 etc. These processors had 32 bits operational capabilities and hence were superior to the contemporary Intel processors. 
Initially the PCs had an elementary operating system like CP/M. Later Bill Gates and Microsoft built a primitive and error prone and user unfriendly operating system known as MSDOS. It became popular . So we had a usable system . We'll one can use it to run simple programs and type documents. It looked like a front-end terminal and not a great computer . 
Let us see more of this in the next. 
Greetings
Dks

I am initiating a new discussion relating to spirituality and humanism vs commercialism in educating our children . I was and am concerned with many aspects of education throughout my life - what are we , parents and teachers supposed to do ? 
I have some questions about our schooling and upbringing of children .
Maybe I have some glimpses, but I feel uncomfortable with the current system. I don't think we can change it. But we need to post and discuss these questions. I am posing a few questions here . Looking for answers . I am happy if you as social entities and not as experts ask questions, think ,analyse and find answers. 
My clarifications.
1.I am sure there are a lot of positives in our education system  . It made us to think rationally, to direct our focus to problem solving, made us to understand the scientific concepts, made us strong in mathematics  and made us to  understand one view of the society .
2. It produced a lot of successful people - engineers, doctors, scientists , managers and politicians. 
3. Due to education, our standard of living improved. 
4. Education allowed for constant improvements in innovation , introduction of new technologies and management of the many complex systems in the country. 
5. It made the country to move forward to a good percentage of the people. 
But there are some concerns  that I am posing below. 
 1.Schooling today - a necessary torture to children ,parents and society?
2.Does it provide education, provide a moral basis for a society or should it. ?
3. Is this a positive forward movement?
4. Focus on stem - is it correct?
4.Should you force the children to adapt to your way of living?
5.Should you push your beliefs onto children ?
6. Are we making children into straight jacketed one dimensional morons suitable for factory work?
7.Do the children understand humans and interrelationships with nature ?
8. Do the children understand humanism?
9. Are the theories of human psychologists correct ?
10  Why are we ignoring common sense and depending on rationalism and experts?
11. Is common sense dying or destroyed? 
12. Why do algorithmic approaches dominate society today? 
13. Is there a subtle intoxication of people by the system and misguided but opinionated and motivated  intellectuals.?
14. is there a role for moralism or none?
15. who is responsible for educating people?
I am not asking how do we change the system. Because, I doubt whether we have understood the concepts,  motives, and workings of education system. Our gurukula had two components - one purely spiritual , another living skills and a third overlying aspect - understanding humans. 
I want to create some awareness to focus on common sense and realities 
Welcome comments and discussions.

“Simply sit down and write down five reasons why you failed. Now, you know what you have to work on; it might hurt doing this but it’s about as positive an action as you can get.  Taking this on is going to make your life much easier than ever before – so do it every time you fail. Failure is a journey, not the destination! 
If you don’t try to change this then you can prepare for a life of self-deprecating and a lack of self-belief. By reviewing failure, solutions become viable. 
 By looking and reviewing your success and failure it will become much easier to stay on the right track. It’ll also greatly increase the chances of finding a long-term solution.”
Michael Chapman   ‘ positive thinking ‘


 I was fortunate to have a front seat and watch and participate in the fast paced developments in the areas of computers, internet and web, parallel  processing  , software engineering,  data bases , graphics, networking  , information technologies, knowledge engineering and AI.  The developments were happening in parallel in many areas and hundreds of researchers , developers were working in many areas. Many new industries developed , quite a good number out of good universities.
The arrival of several concepts and technologies like VLSI,PCs, the bus concept for internal connectivity as well as external connectivity, the flexibility, a new look on OS, touch screen,  etc led to one revolution. Simultaneously software became an engineering activity with increased complexity. Data was looked at differently . AI went in another parallel path towards , natural language understanding, pattern recognition, speech processing, identifications, expert systems etc  . So a highly packed set of anticipation and excitement rose in many of us. These slowly crept into India with a time lag. But definitely from mid eighties India experienced these developments . India focused more on software development for applications. 
I saw the entire computer concepts already developed over the years being re enacted for PCs. It was a very interesting and exciting series of developments to me as  a researcher. 
The first biggest international achievement and transformation was to  take computers from isolated and expensive glass houses  to people. Computers till then were viewed like nuclear reactors - costly,isolated, very special entities not approachable and not understood by even many engineers , scientists and mathematicians leave alone the common man. 
While we found it difficult to get into computer manufacturing, the arrival of VLSI, processors, motherboard , memory chips , buses made it easier to build PCs locally. So India in the eighties moved to PC manufacturing as a major business. We were in the computer trading business with companies like Hinditron ,and computronics  . But now it changed.  Many companies came up. The notable ones were DCM, ORG, mostly in the calculator era in the seventies , but not very much in the PC era . These were followed by Zenith, HCL, Wipro, Pertech, PCL, etc . Zenith was popular with its super brain machines. I bought many of them. Later I bought hundreds from the other companies. HCL was quite popular. 
Stand alone PCs were not useful except for word processing and file management. 
So we came up with a solution for servers. All companies not only produced PCs , but started developing and manufacturing servers  . They needed a multiuser OS. How did it happen? Let us see in the next post.

“Most of us have adopted a strategy to get along called satisficing, a term coined by the Nobel Prize winner Herbert Simon, one of the founders of the fields of organization theory and information processing. Simon wanted a word to describe not getting the very best option but one that was good enough. For things that don’t matter critically, we make a choice that satisfies us and is deemed sufficient. 
Satisficing is one of the foundations of
productive human behavior; it prevails when we don’t waste time on decisions that
don’t matter, or more accurately, when we don’t waste time trying to find
improvements that are not going to make a significant difference in our happiness or
satisfaction.
All of us engage in satisficing every time we clean our homes. If we got down on
the floor with a toothbrush every day to clean the grout, if we scrubbed the windows
and walls every single day, the house would be spotless. But few of us go to this much
trouble even on a weekly basis (and when we do, we’re likely to be labeled
obsessive-compulsive). For most of us, we clean our houses until they are clean
enough, reaching a kind of equilibrium between effort and benefit. It is this cost-
benefits analysis that is at the heart of satisficing (Simon was also a respected
economist).”
Levitin      “ organized mind “

During the second half of the 1980s , computers were sold by a few companies and prices were chaotic . Even my department bought an Uptron machine and paid 5 lakhs rupees , there was a complaint on that purchase that I wasn't consulted. Within a year the price crashed to 2 lakhs rupees. Several others including KGCC bought computers at inflated prices . Super brain- an eight bits computer - was sold at about 7 lakhs. Many complained about this situation. Markets were opened up , demands were high and supplies were a few. So suppliers got a lot of price advantage. 
Mr PJ Naik met me at this time in 1984 85 and we discussed this situation . We anticipated the purchases by government units would go up  . So we decided to set up a technical advisory panel for the government of Karnataka. I played a crucial part. The panel was chaired by the additional chief secretary. At that time, there was only one ACS , a very powerful post in the government.  Now there are many ACS level officers. 
The panel decided to set up a subcommittee to workout the details . I became the chair and KGCC director became the member secretary. The government removed the corrupt director and appointed an IPS officer to that post. 
I held that position for about 20 years. It was a long and very fruitful and happening association with the government of Karnataka for me. This opened a new door for me and gave me a lot of opportunities to help in the computer absorption in the state and country in government departments, engineering colleges , universities and organizations. I also got involved in application software development in many organizations. All these activities continued for more than three decades. I feel happy with my activities these three plus decades till now. 
Before I discuss my role in the government and its impacts, let me look back at the developments in the computer market in India in the eighties and nineties in the next post.

“In the old days, the captain was king. His commands were not to be doubted. If a copilot suspected an oversight, he wouldn’t have dared to address it out of respect for—or fear of—his captain. Since this behavior was discovered, nearly every airline has instituted crew resource management (CRM), which coaches pilots and their crews to discuss any reservations they have openly and quickly. In other words: They carefully deprogram the authority bias. CRM has contributed more to flight safety in the past twenty years than have any technical advances.” 
Rolf Dobelli     the art of thinking clearly.

Let me get back to my discussions on the evolving Indian computer manufacturing scene from  the mid eighties onwards. 
Once the IBM PC was available, as I said earlier, many companies in India started manufacturing them.  The Intel and Motorola processors became bigger in size, capacity , speed and capabilities by following the path led by mainframes in the sixties and seventies and by introducing separate arithmetic processors, by pipelining techniques, by bringing in segmentation, paging and virtual memory etc. Both bus and ports became common attachments . Many devices can now be attached to a PC. Drivers were written and burnt into a ROM . So it was easy to use printers , terminals , and other external devices. Slowly hard disks of higher capacities , smaller physical sizes and compatible with PCs became available in the market. Insertable  floppy disks appeared . Floppy drives were part of a standard PC.  Plug and play was understood . 
The differences in the design approaches  between mainframes and PCs were
Mainframes concentrated on building a complex working system with better performances. They used discrete devices. PCs used packaged VLSI components . So the design was simpler. 
Mainframes had a complex design and circuitry. These were taken over by chip manufacturers. So integrators focused on user requirements. Management was easy and developments got distributed amongst many small companies  . The startup culture began. Monolith and big companies like IBMlost this game . 
Mainframes used many devices like magnetic tapes , disks, card readers etc . PCs were simpler. Devices were attached to the processor  through buses and ports. Input of programs and data was made simpler in PCs.  
Mainframe OS were complex multi user , multifunctional systems. Fortunately PCs started with a simple primitive OS and over a period of time , made it complex. 
The main advantages of a PC are its low costs, low power requirements, and its operational flexibility - like temperature tolerance, dust and humidity tolerance, space requirements etc. 
A lot of associated developments like motherboard, power supply , buses, ports, ethernet etc were happening very fast with many researchers and innovators working on them in an open ended manner. Standards and interoperability were evolving. So you can use third party units easily in a PC.  
PCs can be tailored for a stand alone application and were built for a single user usage. But their capabilities were limited. 
Intel came with an integrated platform called a motherboard. So in addition to the standard manufacturing companies which supplied branded PCs , many local assemblers came into the supply scene. They can easily assemble a PC and sell it at a lower price. 
But their access to space parts  was poor. They sometimes will sell and disappear leading to the term fly by night operations. 
Let us look at the evolution of servers in the next post.

“Happiness is not how many things you do, but how well you do them.

More is not better. Happiness is not experiencing something else; it’s continually experiencing what you already have in new and different ways. Unfortunately as we’re taught that passion should drive our every thought move and decision, we’re basically impaled with the fear that we’re unhappy because we’re not doing “enough.””
Brianna Wiest  

The later versions of microprocessors  had multi user capabilities built in the hardware . Interrupt processing was faster. They had a larger word length of 32 bits and large memory. PCs came with an attached hard disk - hence the name DOS- disk operating system. Ethernet allowed the connectivity of many PCs. So networking was possible. All these developments led to a design of multi user,  multi programming server. Fortunately Unix was available and can be ported on these higher end PCs like Intel 486/ pentium  processors or Motorola 68020/30 processors. Unix was designed as the first open ended OS suitable for minis.
So if a higher end microprocessor 
can have a Unix operating system instead of a MS DOS,  it can act like a server. It can connect many low end PCs for use by several users with higher capabilities. It was ideal for many beginner students to learn programming. It was still inexpensive. So a market existed.
But the question was who will port Unix in the higher end PCs in our companies like HCL  ,Wipro,  PSI etc,? All these companies had excellent hardware designers like Raman in HCL, Sridhar Mitta in Wipro and Ravi and Vinay in PSI. But they weren't interested in software. So the porting of unix on their PC s was done by a group of iisc alumni engineers - Mahesh Jain, Gopi and Ravi - who later started a very successful company called Integra micro systems in bangalore. The company is still operational and it is doing a lot of innovative developments. It is the front runner in fintech. I am still associated with this company. So with the successful incorporation of Unix,  servers became popular in the country.  
So now that PC s and servers were available at affordable prices, hundreds and thousands or organizations and institutions installed them. It reached schools only by mid nineties and homes later. 
So I had a lot of work on hand during these two to three decades in helping many organizations and institutions to choose and purchase computers. Later I assisted them to establish LANs and WANs. 
These servers allowed for people to develop utilities and application software. So most organizations moved away from COBOL to C language. This led to large scale unemployment of COBOL programmers. But banks totally ignorant of these developments moved to COBOL  training and writing cobol programs on mini computers like Digital's VAX or datageneral machines. 
Many large organizations used cobol including many software companies. They weren't aware of these developments and their impact. The IT companies woke up to these realities  only by the mid to late nineties.  
So we saw a lot of parallel developments in software and hardware. Networking also improved.

“As children, routine gives us a feeling of safety. As adults, it gives us a feeling of purpose. Interestingly enough, those two feelings are more similar than you’d think (at least, their origin is the same). It’s the same thing as the fear of the unknown: As children, we don’t know which way is left, let alone why we’re alive or whether or not a particular activity we’ve never done before is going to be scary or harmful. When we’re adults engaging with routine-ness, we can comfort ourselves with the simple idea of “I know how to do this, I’ve done it before.”
 You feel content because routine consistently reaffirms a decision you already made.
If said decision is that you want to write a book—and you commit to doing three pages each night for however long it takes to complete it—you affirm not only your choice to begin, but your ability to do it. It’s honestly the healthiest way to feel validated.”
Brianna Wiest 

Till mid eighties,  most software companies resisted databases due to their perceptions on performance reductions by database compared to processing with files. files. The main reason was the then existing two commercial systems ,both based on hierarchical and network models, were inefficient. Kindly recall our performance issues with dec10 for our accounting system. My students converted the accounting system into a file based application. This reduced both connect time and cpu time by orders of magnitude. So the fear of loss of performance  by  the software companies  like TCS was realistic.
Though EF Codd developed the total concept and details of relational database in 1970 itself in a classic paper and system R was built by IBM  in the  mid seventies,  commercial relational systems were slow in coming to the market . Actually, I got my student to build a basic relational system and build an application for agricultural data on top of the base unit in 1979. We found it easy to build a relational database system and found it doing transactions faster.
 Again it was Unix that gave the lead with unibase- a relational database system  , which was not enterprise scalable. By the late eighties , Oracle came up with a good enterprise level relational database system and that changed the scene. After the arrival of oracle database systems , all software developments were based on a database and not on file systems. Even the earlier developed applications were ported to databases. But it was not a rosy path. Indian airlines faced problems when they automated passenger check in. Modifications were needed. 
Let me start listing the streams in innovation and disruptive developments in the time period of 1984 to 1994.  
 There were multiple parallel streams of developer activities going on in multiple directions by many organizations . 
One stream continued with processor development - Intel is the main one here. From mid nineties  , Asic  based chip development became a major business with hundreds to thousands of applications. The Asic chip foundries overtook processor foundries. India did a half hearted effort in this by setting up the semiconductor industry in chandigarh.
It was called semiconductor complex and was set up in Chandigarh in 1984 . Prof Ramanathan from TIFR put in alot of effort to start research and manufacturing and this plant is a part of his effort. The semiconductor research in india started in a small way in the late seventies in a few institutions likeTIFR,  IISc, IIT Kharagpur etc but didn't build up . It literally died . 
The government sent a team to England to learn about the semiconductor industry, particularly the processor foundries and applications , and in specific their applications in schools. The team also supported the setting up of the SCL in India.  That plus free land offer  from the Punjab government helped in setting up the factory in Chandigarh, a big mistake . Because the city had no electronics culture , whereas Bangalore had an electronics culture for several decades. Ramanathan was unhappy with the location.  While the world  was producing 16 bits processors and going to 32 bits, India got a discarded 8bits processor technology and set up SCL. Unfortunately , there were no downstream activities and hence no demand for their product. There was a fire in 1989 and that was the end of the Indian dream of manufacturing processors. 
Indian computer industry's path was decided by a few bureaucrats in the ministry of Electronics mostly and by NIC - under Dr Seshagiri in the Ministry of Planning. Both these guided correctly the development of applications for government departments , but miserably failed in all other directions. They were unaware of the developments in microprocessors and accessories and servers and their impacts on many sectors. While the world is reaping  the benefits of developments by thousands of young innovators who were setting up and  collaborating with a large number of private industries,  we were governed by the myopic licensing policies of the government, a large distrust about the private sector and a fixed set of beliefs and attitudes by NIC. Prof Nag was removed from the secretary's position because he didn't get enough license funds. He was a liberal man. The change in the government from Indira Gandhi to Rajiv Gandhi made the power go to bureaucrats  from scientists and engineers in many ministries. That was another delaying process in the development of computer based activities in our country. 
Rajiv Gandhi even took a printer from India while on a visit to the US. When it wasn't working, a technician from NIC flew into the US to operationalise the printer.
Greetings 
Dks

“You can see there, Mrita Sanjivani (capable of restoring the dead to life), Vishalyakarani (capable of extracting weapons and healing all wounds inflicted by weapons), Suvarnakarani (restoring the body to its original complexion) and Sandhani, the great herb (capable of joining severed limbs or fractured bone.”
Jambhavan to Hanuman about four herbs to bring everyone back to life. 
Ramayana - yuddha kanda sarga 74 

The second stream of developmental activities in computers  was building or as we say ,manufacturing the computers based on the processor chips from Intel and Motorola and also to some extent Zilog . PSI in bangalore popularized microcontroller usage in india and built systems using zilog processors. Manufacturing  effort was picked up faster in India and many started in this business. 
As I said , these companies moved into the server business also. This combination helped a lot. It moved the country from Basic language programming to C programming. The use of the Unix operating system helped in promoting the use of C. So engineering colleges moved to C and discarded Fortran over a period of about ten years. But there were diehard Microsoft supporters who resisted Unix.  But they were in a minority. 
These efforts by many manufacturing units and a big group of services industries helped us to spread the computer culture across a large number of institutions, organizations,  industries and government departments.  The significant impact was in the field of computer education in most engineering colleges and the popularity of C. Training institutions also helped in the production of skilled programmers.  
We ,in our department, didn't use Fortran. My students used an assembly language initially and Pascal followed by C. We moved to pascal language by 1976 and moved to C by 1982. The transition was smooth. C survived and survives even now  despite other languages like Java and python emerging over the years. 
These developments moved India into the manufacturing domain from the trading domain. It made the computer based industry a major sector in India. 
It led to computer based job creations . The number of new jobs needed went on increasing every year ,  reaching very high levels of growth in the first two decades of the 21st century.

06/08/24, 5:28 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 438
“In a new job situation, we are ignorant of the power relationships between people, the psychology of our boss, the rules and procedures that are considered critical for success.
Although we might enter these situations with excitement about what we can learn or do with our new skills, we quickly realize how much hard work there is ahead of us. The great danger is that we give in to feelings of boredom, impatience, fear, and confusion. We stop observing and learning. The process comes to a halt.
If, on the other hand, we manage these emotions and allow time to take its course, something remarkable begins to take shape. As we continue to observe and follow the lead of others, we gain clarity, learning the rules and seeing how things work and fit together. If we keep practicing, we gain fluency; basic skills are mastered, allowing us to take on newer and more exciting challenges. We begin to see connections that were invisible to us before. We slowly gain confidence in our ability to solve problems or overcome weaknesses through sheer persistence.”
Greene      Mastery


The third stream was using microprocessors in various sectors. This happened very slowly over many years . The availability of a large number of affordable PCs opened a new era of generic application software production. It started slowly with IT literate people developing medium level complex  programs with thousands of lines of code. Initially programmers focused on writing a program to solve a well defined problem like say load flow program for power systems. So the focus of a programmer was only on the need to meet the functional requirements completely and correctly. If you meet all these requirements, your program becomes dependable and it can be run several   times. Such programs are called production programs . Programming moved from the experimental to production phase.  It needed to be modified whenever user requirements change. The best example of this was a payroll program for a company or organization.  Pay of a person is based on company rules and they may change. So the program has to be changed periodically and tested effectively. That was known as maintenance work. Most software companies worked in this area. They needed to know the computers ,programming and functions. 
When  we moved into the PC era, these forms of program development developed individually for  each organization became expensive and needed many skilled persons. Retention was not easy. Initially each organization wanted an exclusive program built for it. Later, due to innovations, many generic programming concepts evolved. System and processor environments were standard and generic. 
Performance became an important criterion. Now the user should be able to run the program himself without the physical presence of the developer. Programs should meet the different kinds of requirements of different users. So parametric programming emerged. Programming became an engineering activity, called or known as software engineering.  User requirements became important. Now maintenance became a centralised activity to be done by the developer. 
This created another startup structure  called software products development companies. This led to outsourcing software services also. There are organizations who don't want any software engineer or infra . They outsourced payroll preparation to a reliable, trust worthy third party company. So many small companies came up in cities like Bangalore.  Slowly small companies with a few people developed applications and sold them. Initially the focus was on office work like typing, filing, sorting and grouping, searching, petty cash maintenance, lists creation etc. Then it moved to payroll , purchase and store ,payment tracking , exam results processing etc. While some software targeted PCs , many focused on servers. 

 So there were great demands of computerised applications - automation of admin and finance functions-  in many countries. This led to globalization of software production , maintenance and usage. This also meant a large demand for programmers.  That created a  major , global employment producing industry . That is the start of today's big IT sector. 
In conclusion, we can see that these developments have  led to the following categorization of organizations. 
Those who don't computerize their activities.
Those who built their applications in house totally. 
Those who had an in-house computer centre and necessary  programming and operating staff but outsourced software development and maintenance.
Those who had in-house computer facilities and bought generic application software packages for admin and finance. 
Those who outsourced computerised functions of admin and finance to a third party but performed in an in-house computer facility
Total outsourcing to a third party
Those where Computers were  used for design and process control.
Those where Computers were used for production. 
Greetings
Dks
07/08/24, 4:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 439
“Even in the privacy of our own minds—when we’re mentally grappling with problems or trying to work through difficult decisions—we’re apt to worry, stew, or gripe about something (or avoid thinking about it altogether). What we should be doing is asking ourselves questions that can help break down the problem or get to the crux of it. But we may be unsure of how to formulate those questions. And we may fear not having the answers to them.
Warren Berger   


These three streams of computer based developments  impacted our life in several ways - personally, professionally , socially , and also employment related and production related. Awareness of computers was low in the beginning of the eighties. A lot of ignorance also existed amongst those who want to use computers, even amongst academics and computer based professionals as most were familiar with and attached to the concept of mainframes, computer centres , Fortran and cobol programming and were unaware of PCs , servers , and third party generic applications. The computer professionals didn't know which way to go - mainframe and computer centre route or servers and PCs route. The mainframe route was mature and the server route was evolving. Even if they chose the server route , they don't know what to buy and how to configure hardware . I had seen hundreds of doubts lingering with most computer professionals. Wrong choices were common. Initially most servers had unix as the operating systems. Later after almost a decade after servers appeared in the market, Microsoft came up with Windows NT for multi user operations to compete with Unix.  But it wasn't good from security point of view. Unix is secure even now . It has proven its security credentials to a large extent. So the confusion over selections persisted for several decades and exists even now to some extent. 
Most people who showed interest in computers thought that it is for computer specialists to build applications. They had no interest or role in it. They kept away from the development of applications. They didn't learn about computers. Computer engineers built a superiority complex and outlook and refused to understand the domain for which they built applications or the usage / user requirements. Metaphorically we look at the situation like -  they built a set of shoes and suggested to users to cut their feet to fit the shoes, not the other way about - build a fit and usable pair of shoes. This led to failures and mistrust. This failure is a classic constant feature of computerization. 
Failures started with mainframe applications , increased with generic server level applications and continued with the  later developed  ERP software . This happened till the end of 20 th century. 
With higher levels of responsibility and financial risks, developers and innovators changed their attitude and agreed that user is the reason for the software , not just the functions. They abandoned their older mindsets and perceptions about software development and hence we got better and highly usable products . So these failures have reduced considerably now. The situation is much better. 
But let us not relax. We still have many problems for professionals to handle with the onset of cloud, IOT, GPUs, VR, AR, smart mobiles, and AI. The problems of integration due to the use of many technologies, architecting, customization , and operationalization gave us a lot of  opportunities for a new breed of architects , consultants , builders , operational engineers, maintenance and troubleshooting experts. So life is going to be exciting with lots of jobs and new ways of living .
 So open up and see these vistas of the future , be prepared, accept them , build new systems and adapt to the future.
Indians are good at learning and adapting and using  many new technologies. So I hope Indians will lead the world in adapting technologies in new sectors like agriculture, MSME, health, education, societal problems, governance etc. 
Greetings
Dks

“If fear is the first enemy of questioning, running a close second is knowledge. The more you know, the less you feel the need to ask. But the problem here is twofold. First, we can easily fall into the “trap of expertise,” wherein knowledgeable people begin to rely too much on what they already know and fail to keep expanding upon and updating that knowledge. This is particularly perilous in times of rapid change. And there’s another problem with depending too much on our existing knowledge: To put it bluntly, we don’t know as much as we think we do.”
Warren Berger. 

I came to the conclusion ,at the beginning of the server and application software evolution itself ,that many organizations need professional help and advice in moving into this new computerization era. I was sure that my knowledge of computers extensively both on hardware and software and my close knowledge of the market and its movements could be put to use to help many organizations. Many academics didn't have similar exposure to IT engineering. It was ignored by them. Some made mistakes in the decisions. 
 That decided my journey in the next three decades. This was in addition to my teaching and research - my primary duty and others are extra activities. That was a second commitment of mine to increase the computer culture. My first  commitment was to help engineering colleges by teaching CS courses and lectures. Both ran in parallel. 

Let me look at our activities with the Technical advisory panel of the Government of Karnataka.  I was a leading  member for more than 20 years and also chair of the subcommittee during this period. So a lot of actions took place during this period. My description of the three streams happened during this period. In addition we saw networking also. 
Let us look at different aspects and changes that took place during this time. 


I interacted with several  directors of KGCC . I remember the names of most of them .
Sharad Saxena IPS was one of the earliest. My association with him lasted for decades even after he left KGCC.  After he became the MD of Keonics, he made me a director. Then we had a few more directors followed by Mr Shantappa , MN Reddy IPS, and Mr Vijayakumar, IAS. We also had a very active director in Mr MN Reddy after Mr Shantappa  . He had a short tenure, but a very effective period when we could streamline the activities. My association with him lasted for many years even after he left KGCC. He helped me on several occasions. He was a great friend. 
My last director of KGCC  was Ramana Reddy. We worked together very well and did many things including setting up a LAN for vidhana soudha.
After him KGCC was merged with the newly created egovernance department with Rajiv Chawla as its secretary. 
Let me look at  the problems we faced with respect to computerization in the government departments.  
Approval for computer purchases based on their applications and needs. This may be purchase of a few PCs to a large system say for the commercial taxes department. 
Suggestion of architecture , configurations, specifications, application software , etc
purchase of  of PCs and servers and application software packages by government departments. 
Decide on the companies Who  manufacture standard  and good quality of PCs , printers, servers, scanners , word processors, etc
Decide on the prices for theses items - produce rate contracts and approve them 
Selection of computers for CMs office during Ramakrishna Hegde’s regime as CM.
Approvals for special requirements like the systems for CET cell when it started. 
Handholding with departments
Handling supply, installation and maintenance problems. 
Introduction of new technology developments like LAN, WAN, Oracle,  etc. 
Suggest and approve software developments like admissions, exam processing , 
Coordination with NIC karnataka Centre, 
Coordination with software developed by departments.  
Training
Restructuring KGCC
Support to major programs of the government like Mahithisindhu undertaken during Mr Krishnas's CM period - 1000 schools computerization 
 Rejection of postfacto  approval for orders to classic computers. 

I encountered many situations and helped in many reforms like rate contract, standardisation, propagation of kannada software, vendor selection, price discovery, two part evaluation - technical and commercial, configuration approaches, etc.

“In terms of biases, some of them are hardwired in us; others may be based on our own limited experiences. But in either case, if we are predisposed to think something, we may be less open to considering questions that challenge that view.
we also must contend with hubris—which can lead us to believe our biases are correct or are not biases at all. (“Everyone else is biased!”) The relationship between humility and questioning is interesting—if you lack the former, you’ll probably do less of the latter. You may be more inclined to think and say things like: “If I don’t know it already, it can’t be that important,” or “I just go with my gut instincts—they’re usually right,” or “I don’t have to sit through intelligence briefings because I’m a really smart guy.”
Warren Berger  

Let me go back to 1985 and talk about my activities. I continued with CSI and we conducted a science and engineering conference, the inaugural national conference sponsored by CSI. Prof. Sashisekaran's work on cancer research was one of the highlights of this conference. 
After we conducted the International conference on knowledge representation in Samskritam in Sankara Mutt , Chamrajpet , we decided to conduct a workshop on Vedic mathematics with Prof Bhanumurthy as the main speaker with his book as a focus. We got Mr Veerappa Moily , Minister for Education to inaugurate the workshop and Dr Vidya Sagar, Director , CAIR, to deliver the keynote. It was a successful activity. 
During my chair of CSI, Prof Ramaseshan invited Prof Nag, director ,IIT Bombay and Gen A Balasubramanian for a review of our work. So we decided to felicitate them on behalf of CSI.  It was scheduled on oct 31th , 1984. In the morning, Mr Venugopalachar , joint secretary of CSI, came and met me. He said that Mrs Gandhi was assassinated. So we canceled the meeting. I informed both the members of the review committee. They were shocked and made arrangements for return. The country went into a chaotic situation for days. It was a very bad time. Many felt upset and sad. 
I became the chair of the computer engineering division of the institution of engineers.  It meant a lot of travels - one per month. Add this to my travels for CSI meetings and then add to that my travels for lectures .  I also traveled to Sirsi for CES work and I traveled for NSS camps across karnataka. It was crazy.  I took my family to Simla for one meeting of the computer board. 

At the institute's end, I was busy with my research on transactions processing, concurrency control and energy systems. I was busy with energy survey work at CES . I chaired CSA’s research interview committee and looked at selection and not rejection. We selected 34 research candidates in a year - a big record for the department.

“We are moving from an economy and a society built on the logical, linear, computerlike capabilities of the Information Age to an economy and a society built on the inventive, empathic, big-picture capabilities of what’s rising in its place, the Conceptual Age.” Daniel Pink.    “ A whole new mind”

I discussed in my previous posts about   the developments of PCs and servers and their impact in detail. Let us see how I used them in my lab. 
I used these developments to build my infrastructure in the department. 
In 1982, I wanted to set up a PCs lab in the department.  I met with strong opposition. Most said it won't be useful. I didn't find a single supporter. The divisional chair , Prof G Krishna and department chair opposed it in addition to most professors. 
I devised an alternate route. Fortunately I was still in charge of the hybrid computer facility. So I talked to the purchase officer and deputy finance controller and got their approval to get the PCs as assembled ones. They agreed that it won't be treated as equipment. I can't buy equipment with my hybrid computer budget. So I could get three PCs with this approach. My excellent rapport with admin and finance helped me in getting the PCs despite opposition in the department.  
Then , I decided to write off the pdp 11 and the obsolete analog computer . I went through an additional process of getting a committee of faculty appointed and got a recommendation from the committee.  Then I wrote to our  admin. They appointed Dr M P Srinivasan from ISU . He is a good friend of mine. It didn't matter. He came, looked at the report, saw the computer and agreed for the write off. He gave a letter to that effect. So the institute permitted me to write off the facility and buy another system. 
So I looked at the market and decided on a HCL Horizon 3 computer with Motorola 68030 with a provision to upgrade it to M68040 within a year. It had Unix as the operating system. Since the cost was low, I had extra budget available and decided to buy three more PCs from HCL along with the Horizon machine and connect all my PCs to the system. I got the purchase approved by a department committee though not required as per IISc rules. So I now had a full-fledged client server system , one of the first in the country. I got good  support from my lab assistant Ganga Raju. 
We put the new computer system for use by our BE  and ME students. It was very useful. 

Two events happened after this. 
One an anonymous letter was sent to the chair stating that the write off of the hybrid computer was not done properly.  It was written by an internal person or faculty in the department.  Most of us know that handwriting. I explained the procedures and the matter was closed. That person wrote a few more in subsequent years about others activities. The department ignored his letters. 
The second event was to do with HCL and Horizon. Based on my purchase, about six repeat orders went from other departments. The director of technical education was visiting me. He was buying computers. I told him about the Horizon machine. Immediately, he took my landline phone , called his concerned officer and told him to place orders for six Horizon machines. HCLs finances became better with this spate of orders. They marched ahead of Wipro and PSI.  So Mr Anil Bhisht of HCL was happy. 
This system worked for about a decade. 
Before all these, I got a PC with Intel 286  assembled for my office use. We had an email system working in the institute with ernet becoming operational. So I used the PC for typing and sending mails .

“As our lives move faster and have become more complicated little time is allotted for inquiry, contemplation, or critical thinking. We’re under pressure to make quick decisions and render snap judgments, and to do, do, do—without necessarily asking why we’re doing what we’re doing or whether we should be doing it at all.
The paradox is that in the rush to do more with our limited time, we may end up using that time less efficiently—because rushed decisions and actions can send us down the wrong path.”
Warren Berger 

Mr Mathew Chacko , CEO of two companies PrecisionIT and Legend systems, both located in Chennai , has commented on my activities With TAP . 
“Fabulous, Sir. Lots of learning. Feel I should have done more .
At an appropriate time, please talk about work, family life balance, and how you managed it.”
My response 
Thanks. I am happy that some of you  find my posts useful. 
As regards work life balance, I plead guilty. For me ,it is more work and less family life. It does not mean that I ignored my responsibilities. I was there in all important events in my family like :
Handling sickness of my children, selection and admissions to schools , colleges and universities for my children, being the president of PTA, getting books and other requirements for their education,  visiting them regularly when they were studying in Bombay , Mysore and Surathkal, as well as in US, support to their visa trips to chennai, creating awareness in them, getting toys and  books for them, shifting houses several times - more than 9 times before retirement and five times after retirement,  constructing my house, buying flats later on, managing my finances and providing financial support to the family, conducting marriages of my children, handling most of the major logistics for the house etc. 
I helped several people in getting jobs or school or college admissions. I advised a large number of my friends and acquaintances. So my friend circle is big and expanding and many helped me also. I have counseled a large number of kids and students. 
My regrets:
I didn't spend a lot of time talking to my children. I didn't spend time on routine activities like grocery purchases, but took care of major purchases like furniture, refrigerator, washing machine, TV etc. My biggest regret is that I couldn't visit my relatives, attend functions like marriages, house warming, 60th birthdays etc. I lost touch with most . I couldn't give good directions to  nephews and nieces. 

My overall take on balance is :
But if I didn't do all the professional activities, I am sure I would have had only this amount of family activities. I wouldn't have done anything more than this. I am a lazy person. Remember that  I wasn't a healthy person . I have many health problems like diabetes and angina. Without all these academic and professional activities, I would be terribly bored and unhappy. These gave me immense satisfaction. 
I am happy and very satisfied with the way I lived my life. As I keep saying , the following helped me. 
1.Excellent time management.  I said I am a lazy person, but I put a lot of mindfulness and effort to complete tasks. I needed to think, attend presentations  and write a lot and managed it. Even during travels whether long distance or within the city or while waiting at places, i had the habit of writing. It was a great practice- thinking most of the time and writing. Add my very good reading habits , it made my life great. I didn't ignore my walking. 
I was regular with my visits to our health centre and with medicines. Though I am not a ritualistic person, I had faith in goodness ,in friends and the world in general - positive optimism. 
2.Regularity in my life. I didn't postpone many activities. I was very regular with my food, walking, sleeping ,  reading and writing. Not impulsive. I had good and regular habits and loved frugality and conservation. 
3. I wasn't afraid of new developments. I spent time and effort to acquire new skills. My open mind helped me in forecasting and absorbing new technologies. 
4.I maintained good energy levels and a sharp mind but not calm .

Let me continue from where I left in the previous post on work family balance. Actually , this balance depends on certain basic factors and I want to discuss that. 
Qn.1 is living a mundane , mechanical process of a few well set routine activities of earning , eating , sleeping ? 
I decided  at an early age of 8 years itself that 
I don't want that routine, monotonous ,boring , mechanical life. My life should be meaningful and should have some purposes.
2. Why did I feel that life should have a meaning and purpose at that very young age? 
It is the ambience in which I grew up and the mentoring I got from my uncle. I tagged on to him, observed   his behavior and actions very closely. I assisted him wherever possible and whatever i can do. He is not given to gossiping. He didn't exhibit any  petty behaviour or jealousy . He helped a lot of people. He used to draft a lot of legal documents for many . He conducted Sri Rama Navami every year with meticulous planning, good accounting, good documentation, transparency, and integrity. These made a deep impression on me and I felt that I should follow his lead and ideals. 
This made me to understand that life is not just eating and sleeping . There is a lot more to life. Sadly most people don't know this simple fact. They struggle through their life to make enough money to eat and reside and sleep. 
I was convinced after listening to Upanishads that life has a greater purpose than mere survival.
3. How did I want to live?
First, I want certain qualities and principles in my life. 
I wanted scholarship , because I saw many  scholars in the family and outside. I wasn't happy with school education. It was really elementary. So I realised that real education comes by observation of the society and the country. So the components needed for Scholarship are
1.Good observation and analysis
2.Open and flexible mind 
3.Reading 
4.Listening to scholars and others and look for true statements
5.Questioning and looking for 360 °views.

I had some principles in life  - mostly simple and flexible ones. Let me list them
Help others and society as far as possible. provide knowledge to others. 
Trust friends. Don't judge your friends and family.
Lead a simple frugal life.
Money is not everything. Do a good financial Plan. Restrict expenditures. Conserve resources. 
Be honest, truthful and Ethical. 
Don't harm others. 
Have goals in life . 
Constant improvement is needed
Give freedom to the family to make decisions. Don't force the children on the selection of their higher education or spouse. Discuss and leave the final decisions to them. 
Bringing up children is a very important task. It is not an easy job, it needs a delicate balance between strictness and lenience. Realize that  they don't accept your views. Give them independence but not total freedom when they are young. Teach good habits . 

Those are my views of actions needed - a good balance between family, profession, public life and social service. 
Greetings

“The nature of Tao is to flow and let life unfold in a harmonious manner. Those who can tap into this flow will be led toward a state of conscious elevation; those who cannot will generally be led down a path of trivial concerns. “

There is a new comment from my cousin Mrs Rajalakshmi Lokanathan:
“There is a woman behind every man . You should thank your wife for hercooperation.”

My response
I wanted to give a short response ,but I decided to give a meaningful response. 
Thanks for your very keen interest in my posts from the beginning with a remarkable consistency of interest like my other cousins, Mrs Subha Ravi, Mrs Meena Muralidharan and Mr Murali venkatasubramanian.  
I agree with your comment . But I got support from many women and men. The first was my grandmother who brought me up till I was 11 years old and continued supporting me afterwards also. I admire her courage in staying alone , in handling her day to day financial activities in traveling throughout the country upto Badrinath and cooking her food everywhere and her nourishing me during a long typhoid illness of about two months when I was in 10th standard . I learnt a lot from her - courage and freedom from fear, independence , regular habits . During that period , my total world was around my uncle - S Narayana Sarma.  I learnt a lot from him - scholarship, spirituality, simplicity, frugal living, honesty, accounting and ledgers, transparency , helping tendency and involvement in community affairs. That literally provided foundation for me and my life was built on it. 
When I was taken by my uncle to Tirunelveli to study from 6th to 10th - a period of five years, I saw a different ambience and environment. Learning became the most dominant activity. I learnt about many vegetables like beans, cabbage ,potatoes etc. 
It should be surprising to many when I say that my aunt was a great reader. Most women of her age only read Ramayana or some slokas. But she was different. My uncle will bring three novels every week for her. It happened during the entire period of my stay. Her social awareness was high. So I also started reading , a habit of regularly reading two to three books every week . It continued throughout my life, even now. That is a great habit I picked from her. She was also involved with community work particularly taking care of the temple. She was very generous , helped many ladies in the neighborhood. She also introduced me to cinema . 
When we moved to Palayamkottai, a town close to Tirunelveli for my PUC, Ramanathan uncle , brother of my Tirunelveli aunt , helped us in finding a house and lending me a lot of English novels. 
When I moved to my younger uncle's house in Madurai to do my BE, my aunt helped me even though she wasn't well . She had a lot of interest in society, nation, governance, politics etc . We used to discuss topics related to various happenings of the day regularly. It continued much later also . She was very quick to grasp many happenings and highly intelligent. 
During my second year,  I had the support of my TVS uncle , L Narayanan and aunt a lot. Both were very helpful and generous. 

So I  acknowledge the support I received from my wife, daughter, son, parents and sister. “Man is by nature a social animal; an individual who is unsocial naturally
and not accidentally is either beneath our notice or more than human.
Society is something in nature that precedes the individual. Anyone who either cannot lead the common life or is so self-sufficient as not to need to, and therefore does not partake of society, is either a beast or a god.”
Aristotle

Let me get back to my activities from 1985. 
As I had been saying,  I was moving into another- new -  path of advisory and solutions consulting activities with my involvement with TCS. At that time ,the scene was simple. But by 1984, the computer scene changed it's face. It became an integral set of activities - 
Incorporate databases into applications;
Networking both LAN and WAN;
Move to client server architecture;
Use more PCs as front end devices;
Better presentation and user interfaces ; and 
Trust in Unix and third party generic applications. 
So it became IT, no longer computer. 
It became a complex and chaotic world of great expectations, failures, and disappointments. A new science had to evolve and it  didn't happen at that time or even later, but practices started evolving . It needed a mindset overhaul - not a simple change but seeing the old giving way to a brand  new set of things-  from mainframe to IT. So I saw a big helping role for me  here. 

Let me look at my IT advisory activities which played a big role in my life for the next four decades helping governments, organizations, research institutes, schools, colleges, cooperatives, industries and banks. It was a memorable period starting from 1984 and went on till now. 
My association with the Technical advisory panel of the Government of Karnataka brought me in contact with many government organizations and many senior officers. This brought about a different kind of activity from me - computer and Software advice and support for purchases. My knowledge of rate contracts helped me in knowing the market - technical capabilities, spare parts  and prices. 
The list of organizations I helped is a long one. I was associated with some of them for several years. 
This kind of advice and support started with government departments first , followed by KSCST and many government organizations. This was followed by advises and support to  many central government organizations , and many colleges . Later in the end of the twentieth century and in the first two decades of the twenty-first century, I extended the advice to banks in a big way. 

My advice consisted of 
Policy formulations,  Architecture and identification of IT systems and components like servers, os. Networking, routers and switches, end devices, security devices and application software, databases, support infrastructure like ups, power supply, DG sets ,  etc , decision on specifications , vendor discovery, performance tests, preparation of  detailed RFPs - request for proposals-, discussions with vendors and clarifications,  vendor rating, technical and commercial evaluations, installation, standard operating procedures,  and acceptance testing. It also included selection of technical skilled persons. It took a lot of my time and the remuneration was marginal. Mindset changes were the most difficult tasks. Fortunately most CEOs listened to me and accepted my advice. That and my advice to all organizations gave me immense satisfaction.

My  many advising and consulting activities  to organizations can be categorized  as:

One of my goals was and is - whatever system was setup by me should stay for 10 years with upgrades and work well in a smooth ,trouble free manner with good maintenance and service support. Price is not my main concern. I am not the L1 person.
