“You believe that creating your best life is a matter of deciding what you want and then going after it, but in reality, you are psychologically incapable1 of being able to predict what will make you happy. 

Your brain can only perceive what it’s known, so when you choose what you want for the future, you’re actually just recreating a solution or an ideal of the past. When things don’t work out the way you want them to, you think you’ve failed only because you didn’t re-create something you perceived as desirable. In reality, you likely created something better, but foreign, and your brain misinterpreted it as “bad” because of that. (Moral of the story: Living in the moment isn’t a lofty ideal reserved for the Zen and enlightened; it’s the only way to live a life that isn’t infiltrated with illusions. It’s the only thing your brain can actually comprehend.)’”
 Brianna  Wiest 

Let me get back to my chronological way of describing my life. I am talking about the period 1985-86.  
Let me talk about my next international trip. 
IFIP is an international federation of computer societies of several countries. It operated through many technical committees and many working groups. It was responsible for the development of COBOL language along with its  specifications and also for the evolution of commercial  database systems based on the network model. It was a powerful technical body. All its members were reputed academics and industrialists with a good depth of knowledge in a subdiscipline. 
CSI used to liaise with IFIP from India.  Gen A Balasubramanian, founding secretary of CSI and an active participant in computing ,  was handling IFIP matters in india. He was also the one who asked me to conduct a major conference on computers in power systems during 1976- 77 . I had mentioned this before. He was instrumental in bringing the IFIP conference on “ CAD, CAM, CAE for industrial progress “ which we conducted in Bangalore in 1984. I had talked about this major activity before. 
CSI made me  an Indian representative in IFIP TC7. It handled matters relating to systems and controls. I was scheduled to go to Tokyo for a conference and a meeting of TC7. 

I went to Madras for my Japanese visa . 
I met Gen AB. He introduced me to his travel agent. He was a very knowledgeable person. He recommended to me  that I travel by Thai airways. I said OK. He said I can extend my travel and go to Los Angeles on the same fare as for  Tokyo. So I agreed to book a ticket to LA. I didn't know where I would stay in LA and what I would do.
 He also gave me two  halts, one at Bangkok and another at Hong Kong  on the onward journey  and one at Singapore on the return trip with an overnight stay at Bangkok . The itinerary was a big one.  I needed to go to kolkata first and take a Thai airways  flight to Bangkok .  Then I had to go to Hong Kong.  From there I had to go to Tokyo . After a week's stay in Tokyo, I had to go to LA via Texas and Seattle.  Without any prior thinking I accepted the proposed itinerary.  I needed visas.
 I got my Japan visa after some hot discussions. I don't have an invitation from the Japanese organizers of the conference except a fax on the  acceptance of my paper, but i had a letter from IFIP for the meeting . They wanted a letter from the organizers. I said it might take a minimum of  fifteen days to get the letter. After some time,  they agreed to give me a tourist visa for a week. Trouble at the beginning itself. 
So I went to Chennai again  for my US visa. My mother and sister were still living in Ellaiamman colony at that time. It was about six months after my father's demise. 
So it was easy for me to go to the US consulate located near Gemini flyover. Luckily , I met a student of mine there and found out that he  was working in LA. His name is Eri Srivatsan, and  did his PhD with Prof Padayatti of the biochemistry department.  He did my course on Mathematics for biochemists ,and  got 100 out of 100. He remembered me and  told me that I can stay with him in LA. He said he would receive me at the LA airport on my arrival . It was a great relief. I was wondering how to spend 15 days in LA. 
I got my US visa.  This would be my second trip to the US but the last time I went to Boston and Washington and this time  to the west coast.

“we have seen a broad shift from a culture of humility to the culture of what you might call the Big Me, from a culture that encouraged people to think humbly of themselves to a culture that encouraged people to see themselves as the center of the universe.
in 1950, the Gallup Organization asked high school seniors if they considered themselves to be a very important person. At that point, 12 percent said yes. The same question was asked in 2005, and this time it wasn’t 12 percent who considered themselves very important, it was 80 percent.”
David Brooks.   “ Road to character”. 

I left the hotel by about 10 am , took a train and a bus to Narita airport. The airport is a good and clean one with less walking needed between gates. Systems worked. 
The travel from Tokyo to LA went across the Pacific ocean. It was a long travel of about 12 hours non stop. Seat was comfortable. Food was OK. I had a lot of tofu , rice ,fruits , bread, butter, coffee- not filter or cappuccino -,  lots of juice, no liquor . Asian vegetarian wasn't popular then. We crossed the International dateline. We reached Seattle . After a short one hour  halts  at Seattle, and San Jose , we reached LA. The flight continued further  to Texas. But I disembarked  in LA. Fortunately, Srivatsan was waiting at the gate itself.  There was no security gate or security check at that time even though indian airports introduced security checks by 1974 itself . Actually, the travel for a short distance of three quarters of a mile from Ravikumar‘s house to his department took us 40 minutes. It was because of a football match starting   at that time. I spent two days with them. 
I had a great experience. The university had a new Mac desktop . It was exciting. It had graphic user interface, touch screen, simple user friendly commands miles away from MSDOS and Unix that I was using at IISc.  I found it easy to use the machine. Within 15 minutes , I was checking my email. It was an awakening for me about the future path of graphics user interface systems. We have it today in our mobiles. See how old is this display technology. Touch screen was first identified by Steve Jobs and introduced in his computers and later in mobiles. While touch screen concept is old- mid eighties- , display technologies are improving from LCD to OLED to AMOLED leading to better accuracies / greater  clarity and lower power consumptions.  Now we also have POLED - plastic Light emiting diodes. 
My next lecture was at UCLA. I was told that it would take 40 minutes by bus to go to UCLA from USC. I decided to walk. It took me 40 minutes of brisk walk. 
Prof AV Balakrishnan gave me an office space to work . The talk went off well. He took me for dinner at an Indian restaurant.  Dinner wasn't good. He didn't like it. Srivatsan picked me after dinner. It was a very useful day. I read many papers of AVB and some books .

“humility leads to wisdom. Montaigne once wrote, “We can be knowledgeable with other men’s knowledge, but we can’t be wise with other men’s wisdom.” That’s because wisdom isn’t a body of information. It’s the moral quality of knowing what you don’t know and figuring out a way to handle your ignorance, uncertainty, and limitation.”
David Brooks    road to character 

I should mention one particular habit of people that changed over the years and that is smoking. During my visit to Manchester in 1984, I found most people smoking. Most common rooms and pubs were filled with smoke. When I used to  came out of a common room, people asked me whether I was smoking. Even during my visit to Boston by air France , I found fifty percent of people smoking. It was very difficult for me. I used to get headache frequently. But this visit changed the scene. There was a separate non smoke area. But very few smoked. All common areas including supermarkets were clear places with none smoking everywhere. It is surprising that about 80% had quit smoking or smoked a few cigarettes in private. The awareness was perceptible.  The second change I saw compared to my first visit was that some vegetarian food was available in restaurants. Most were beginning to understand some concepts of vegetarianism.  I didn't survive on apples, bananas , juices and bread. The breakfast spread improved considerably without that unbearable bacon frying smell that I suffered in Manchester. 
Well, a good positive progress. 
My next engagement was to visit Red lands with Chakrapani. He picked me from Srivatsan ‘s house and drove to his place - about two hours of drive. I met his wife, but sons  weren't present there. Chakrapani took me to his department,  introduced me to colleagues, but couldn't arrange a lecture. After two days with him, he drove me back to LA. That day was Krishna Janmashtami.  So he stayed till the  Puja was over. 

I went with Srivatsan for purchases. I bought two good backpacks for my children to take their books to school. Both were good ones and serviced them till they completed schooling. At that time, no backpacks were available in India. Those available were flimsy. So it was a good purchase. I bought a leather bag which was used till 2010. I also bought the famous California almonds and chocolates. 
 So it was time to go to Berkeley for a lecture. I flew to San Francisco and took a coach to Berkeley.  I stayed in the guest house. After the talk at the environmental and resources department,  I walked around and saw Prof CV Ramamurthy,  whom I had met in bangalore earlier. That is the work culture there. 
I met the developers of Unix Berkeley version - Ben Thompson and Bill Joy - and Ingress data base. I met Michael Stonebreaker ,who developed Ingress relational database, the first RDB in 1973. It was a memorable experience for me. Dr Srini dropped me at the SFO airport. Srivatsan picked me from an airport close to his house. So ended my trip to Berkeley.

“A good theory doesn’t change its mind: it doesn’t apply only to some companies or people, and not to others. It is a general statement of what causes what, and why. Competition in the computer chip market and the proliferation of global terrorism could not seem like more different problems to tackle. But they are fundamentally the same problem, just in different contexts. Good theory can help us categorize, explain, and, most important, predict.
People often think that the best way to predict the future is by collecting as much data as possible before making a decision. But this is like driving a car looking only at the rearview mirror—because data is only available about the past.
 Indeed, while experiences and information can be good teachers, there are many times in life where we simply cannot afford to learn on the job. You don’t want to have to go through multiple marriages to learn how to be a good spouse. Or wait until your last child has grown to master parenthood. This is why theory can be so valuable: it can explain what will happen, even before you experience it.”
Theory of disruptive innovation 
Clayton Christensen,    innovator's dilemma 



Now it was time to start the return leg . I took another flight and reached Seattle early. “For nearly all of history, people’s lives have been governed primarily by ignorance. This was nowhere more clear than with the illnesses that befell us. We knew little about what caused them or what could be done to remedy them. But sometime over the last several decades—and it is only over the last several decades—science has filled in enough knowledge to make ineptitude as much our struggle as ignorance.”
“Studies have found that at least 30 percent of patients with stroke receive incomplete or inappropriate care from their doctors, as do 45 percent of patients with asthma and 60 percent of patients with pneumonia. Getting the steps right is proving brutally hard, even if you know them.”
Atul Gawande.      Checklists 

There are some comments.
N Ramanathan has commented on smoking:
“I too had silently suffered in the midst of smokers in meetings endlessly in the early part of my career. Passive smokers’ plight was miserable then. In flights, we used to opt for non-smoking zone that could help just reduce but not eliminate the problem.
It’s a happy relief that since 2001 and onwards there is good reductio0n in per capita consumption of cigarettes in India too. Govt restrictions played a catalyst role. Air-conditioning of offices, railways and public places helped expedite the process, as smokers have to get out of their seat each time. This acted as a good disincentive. There is thus perceptible change in public area smoking.
Tobacco was also consumed in the form of snuff and along with betel leaf. The former inhaled through nose was no nuisance to others, except giving a brown coat to the bottom of users’ nose. With no Govt intervention, this habit has since died a natural death. 
The other form of chewing and spitting was spoiling the look of streets and walls besides being a health hazard. This practice was too common in the villages, though limited with office goers, because of the trouble in moving out of the seat for spitting at regular intervals. This too has disappeared in the South, though still largely prevalent in States like Bengal and Orissa.
In all, a welcome relief for the gullibles like us.”
H S Nagaraja writes about Chakrapani that he helped him in his computing work at IISc computer centre.

23/08/24, 7:47 am - Sanjay Chitnis: I am excited that Center of Excellence for Geopolitics and International studies, REVA University is hosting a Panel Discussion on
*“India: Seeking More Strategic Autonomy in Critical Technologies”*
with 3 very eminent personalities:
*Dr. Sridhar Vembu*, Padma Shri, Co-founder & CEO, ZOHO Corp, member of UGC and National Security Advisory Board
*Sri Sharad Sharma*, Co-founder, iSPIRT India, member, UN advisory board on AI; member of National Startup Advisory Council; member, SEBI's Financial and Regulatory Technology Committee, 
*Sri Rajiv Malhotra*, Eminent Indologist and Author of books including Artificial Intelligence and the Future of Power

Moderated by: Dr. T.S. Mohan, Founder, Pragyan Datalabs

Hon. Chancellor Dr. P. Shyama Raju will preside over the function.

Date: Saturday 24th August 2024, Time: 2:00PM- 4:00 PM (including special event)
Venue: Chanakya Auditorium, REVA University 

*ALL ARE WELCOME*

Abstract:
For the past several years, post Covid pandemic, geopolitical powers across the world have been scrambling to more effectively pursue efforts in seeking strategic autonomy in several pathbreaking critical technologies that have been predicted to define who will matter in influencing world affairs. The top critical technologies that one can count on making a huge difference for humanity at large include Artificial General Intelligence & Robotics, Material Sciences & Energy Management including Small Nuclear Reactors and Green Hydrolysers, Drug discovery & Life Sciences, Quantum Computing & Cryptography, Reusable Rockets, Semiconductors. Similarly, the top dimensions on which these critical technologies are going to impact us include sustainability; individual, community and cohesive societal empowerment; securing reliable supply chains apart from reskilling and effectively channelizing workforce across the board for effective economy. In this hour plus panel, we critically look back at India's past efforts in pursuing strategic autonomy as compared to its peers and posit avenues that India has missed out and should pursue in order to gain its edge in the pursuit of strategic autonomy in critical technologies.

“To the Indian it is clear that the Self as the originating ground of the psyche is not different from God, and that, so far as a man is in the Self, he is not only contained in God but actually is God. Shri Ramana is quite explicit on this point. The goal of Eastern religious practice is the same as that of Western Mysticism: the shifting of the centre of gravity from the ego to the Self, from man to God. This means that the ego disappears in the Self, and man in God. It is evident that Shri Ramana has either really been more or less absorbed by the Self, or at least has struggled earnestly all his life to extinguish his ego in it. If we conceive the Self as the essence of psychic wholeness, i.e., as the totality of conscious and unconscious, we do so because it does in fact represent something like a goal of spiritual development. This formula shows the dissolution of the ego in the atman to be the unequivocal goal of religion and ethics, as exemplified in the thought of Shri Ramana. “
Carl Jung


Let me return to the year 1986,  to bangalore and talk about my life after the trip. The trip created an awareness of some other countries, several institutions , varieties of governance, commitment by faculty members , social life etc. It broadened my world view. I saw the similarities in family  life in several countries of Asia.  The clockwork precision of various occurrences during my trip with no missteps or mishaps surprised me. I am reminded of Paulo Coelho's belief that the world helps you achieve your dreams. 

As I said earlier, I initiated a lot of teaching activities in many other institutions outside IISc. I started teaching at IIM Bangalore at the initiative of my wife's cousin , Narayanan Kapali. It wasn't easy. I had to travel by bus and auto. I went to the majestic bus stand and had to take a rural  bus going to Bannerghatta. The bus was infrequent . After somedays , I found an easier way of travel. I went from majestic to Jayanagar 7th block east by a minibus, a very convenient way of travel. Then I either took a bus to IIM or walked. I met several faculty members at IIM.  Prof Philip had taken over as director, but the employees were protesting on most days. I met Prof Kaujalgi who helped me to navigate through IIM.  I met professors Jagadish, Ram Mohan Rao, Chandrasekar . I saw one major difference in teaching IISc students and IIM students. IISc students mostly won't ask questions. They were focussed and were confident of learning by themselves. So they never asked me questions, but I used to be quizzed by IIM  students and I liked it. But I didn't see any motivation in the students there.
 I was surprised that neither  the students nor the faculty  saw or perceive the disruptive innovation coming. They didn't realise the future impacts of IT on business. Even the faculty had no interest  on  the impacts of technology on business .  I didn't have opportunity to discuss technology vs business ideas of mine with my exposure to TCS, Karnataka government, my shift to client server approach in my lab with HCLs Horizon computers. Actually I wrote a newspaper article titled “ computers and society”  in 1985 on the occasion of our international conference “ CAD, CAM, CAE for industrial progress.” It was published by times of India and it pictured the immediate changes that will happen in our society with computers. 

At that time , many training institutes were established at every street corner in bangalore and most were cheating the gullible. So I wrote another article called computer alchemists in times of India in 1986. It said that these institutions claim to transform an unemployed person to a level of prosperity. One institution even told its students to keep their passport ready hinting that they may get a job abroad. 
More in the next.

25/08/24, 5:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 457
Although this may seem a paradox, all exact science is dominated by the idea of approximation. When a man tells you that he knows the exact truth about anything, you are safe in inferring that he is an inexact man. Every careful measurement in science is always given with the probable error ... every observer admits that he is likely wrong, and knows about how much wrong he is likely to be.

Bertrand Russell
Everything we know is only some kind of approximation, because we know that we do not know all the laws yet. Therefore, things must be learned only to be unlearned again or, more likely, to be corrected.

Richard P. Feynman


Let me make my views clear on training. 
Though I was not happy with the mushrooming of training centres, I appreciated the need and utility of good centres. I was involved with Datamatics in 1970 itself. I saw the impact of NIIT. When a company floated by one of my students needed a unix aware person, I selected a student with BCom qualification trained at NIIT. I didn't find many engineers  employed by the public sector units or many engineering graduates suitable. This was a revelation for me. 
Let me shift to computer  engineering education during the eighties.
I saw that  some American universities started computer courses and specialisation in the mid sixties itself. Initially it was housed in EE department and later it became a separate department.  I saw some institutions running separate computer science programs in UK and many faculty involved in CS research, but mostly hardware.
 But the situation in india was distressing. 
Till 1986, no engineering institution outside IIT s and IISc,  were even teaching a course on computers,  let alone build a full-fledged program. To my knowledge , first IIT Bombay had a separate centre but under EE department even in 1966. It had some faculty , but focus was hardware. IIT kanpur had a strong computer science activity from the beginning.  IIT  madras started its separate CS department and recruited many faculty in early 70s. They had a big bang approach , started off very well.
 So it was the few good training institutions that filled the skills gap first in programming, then on hardware, then on databases , graphics etc. They were agile and capitalized on the market. They understood the growing need of a futuristic skill set and moved into that gap. Our educational institutions were static, didn't understand both the current and future markets even till the beginning of the twentifirst century. Did they learn a lesson? The answer is no. 
During the mid eighties, a few of our engineering colleges started a CS undergraduate program without any faculty. Most were handled by EE staff. The situation was bad and continued in that state  for more than a decade. 
I wanted to help. So my involvement with many colleges in teaching courses and recruiting faculty began. It became a major saga. 
Greetings
Dks
25/08/24, 6:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: For nearly all of history, people’s lives have been governed primarily by ignorance. This was nowhere more clear than with the illnesses that befell us. We knew little about what caused them or what could be done to remedy them. But sometime over the last several decades—and it is only over the last several decades—science has filled in enough knowledge to make ineptitude as much our struggle as ignorance.”
“Studies have found that at least 30 percent of patients with stroke receive incomplete or inappropriate care from their doctors, as do 45 percent of patients with asthma and 60 percent of patients with pneumonia. Getting the steps right is proving brutally hard, even if you know them.”
Atul Gawande.      Checklists 
“A good theory doesn’t change its mind: it doesn’t apply only to some companies or people, and not to others. It is a general statement of what causes what, and why. Competition in the computer chip market and the proliferation of global terrorism could not seem like more different problems to tackle. But they are fundamentally the same problem, just in different contexts. Good theory can help us categorize, explain, and, most important, predict.
People often think that the best way to predict the future is by collecting as much data as possible before making a decision. But this is like driving a car looking only at the rearview mirror—because data is only available about the past.
 Indeed, while experiences and information can be good teachers, there are many times in life where we simply cannot afford to learn on the job. You don’t want to have to go through multiple marriages to learn how to be a good spouse. Or wait until your last child has grown to master parenthood. This is why theory can be so valuable: it can explain what will happen, even before you experience it.”
Theory of disruptive innovation 
Clayton Christensen,    innovator's dilemma
Two views of problem solving

26/08/24, 5:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 458
“Plato believed that reality is divided into two parts: the ideal and the phenomena. The ideal is the perfect reality of existence. The phenomena are the physical world that we experience; it is a flawed echo of the perfect, ideal model that exists outside of space and time.
Plato calls the perfect ideal the Forms.
Plato demonstrates this concept of the Forms in an illustration called The Allegory of the Cave. In Plato's allegory, there is a dark cave in which people have been chained to a wall since birth. Behind the heads of these prisoners, there is a fire burning. Objects are passed in front of this fire and it projects silhouetted images onto the wall in front of the prisoners. The prisoners perceive these shadow objects as reality because they have never known anything else.

Sometimes, a prisoner is freed. They look around the cave and are frightened and confused by their surroundings. They then climb out of the cave in an arduous and painful quest. When they reach the mouth of the cave, they are forced to consider the sun, the world above, and the cave from which they came. This freed prisoner would then be compelled to return to the cave and attempt to free his fellow man.”
Plato.       Republic

My  dear   nephew, 

“ -Your  posts  on  Tokyo  and  US  are   simply  amazing,  awe-inspiring  and  adventurous,   How   many   Cities,   new  faces,  lodges,  so-called food,  loss  of  regular   habits  and  sleep,  high  level   lectures  delivered  before   elite   audience   in    reputed   universities   and   institutions   of   higher   learning,  time   spent   on  preparations   of  lectures,   a   possible    threatening  of mugging   - Thank   God,  Pass-port  was  saved, -      all  the   above  are  beyond   my   imagination.

  '  DRISHTA   SITA  '   to  Sri  Rama  and   Sri   Lakshmana,  and   made  them   happy,   you  too  ¹gave   two  good  backpacks  ( not  available   in   India  )  to   Anu  and  Murali  and  made  them   happy,
Let  your Tokyo    USA  visits  be  ever  green   in  our  memory, 
Ramakrishnan,   Uncle   24   -   8  -   24”

27/08/24, 3:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 459
“Know-how and sophistication have increased remarkably across almost all our realms of endeavor, and as a result so has our struggle to deliver on them. You see it in the frequent mistakes authorities make when hurricanes or tornadoes or other disasters hit. You see it in the 36 percent increase between 2004 and 2007 in lawsuits against attorneys for legal mistakes—the most common being simple administrative errors, like missed calendar dates and clerical screw ups, as well as errors in applying the law. You see it in flawed software design, in foreign intelligence failures, in our tottering banks—in fact, in almost any endeavor requiring mastery of complexity and of large amounts of knowledge.”
Clayton Christensen.       Checklists

Prof Vasudev Parvati has a comment on training institutions vs colleges. 
“Sir the current buzzwords in the training arena are AI based softwares and Data analytics tools. Educational institutions still do not expose their students to things like Power BI or Tableau which are met in present days by online educators like Unacademy.How do you view this trend?”

My response 
Thanks. It is an important point to ponder by teachers. So you have raised this aspect. 
You are correct. I have been watching this phenomenon for more than four decades . It hasn't changed. 
It is an accepted fact that academic institutions are not flexible, are not focussed on technologies and skills, concentrate on basics and take years to change. So we needed an alternate mechanism to train people on latest skills. This led to the mushrooming of training institutions. Even government of India's skills training is dated. So we see many of these institutions play a role. 
As I said earlier, they played a good role in producing higher skills people. For instance It was these institutions which trained lakhs of COBOL programmers who could write large application programs in our industries, banks and IT industries and not university or college students. Our institutions failed there. Similarly a total view of Java was taught by training centres producing industry usable Java people. Academic institutions concentrated basic Java but not J2EE or J2EE or servers or applets etc. I interviewed 800 BEs and MCAs  for corporation bank and none could explain these concepts. 
Even we are not able to scale up properly on our courses. That is why we saw the rise of coursera , edX, etc. Even a great teaching came from khan academy.  Their java tutorial was a good one. I suggested about 20 plus years back that we should adapt that course and not teach it in the class. Instead the teacher should guide the students to write a large program for a big application. I am against teaching programming , but we should focus on solving large problems. while many big institutions are partially moving in that direction, most run class room lectures explaining instruction formats. This isvlike teaching swimming in a class room. I am disappointed that we can't change this mindset. 
 YouTube  has thousands of contents. None of our institutionsuse these contents to a great extent. We need to change, adapt and provide flexibilty beyond curriculum and standard teaching. 
I feel we need to wake up. There was a discussion in the sixties and seventies on the differences between university BEs and IE, IETE graduates. Now we need to ask what are the goals for survival of engineering education with genAI replacing teachers and giving a holistic learning platform for people. More danger is coming . So wake up. 
Greetings 
Dks
28/08/24, 5:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 460
“Human beings evolved to live in small bands with people more or less like themselves. But today, many of us live in wonderfully pluralistic societies. In America, Europe, India, and many other places, we’re trying to build mass multicultural democracies, societies that contain people from diverse races and ethnicities, with different ideologies and backgrounds. To survive, pluralistic societies require citizens who can look across difference and show the kind of understanding that is a prerequisite of trust—who can say, at the very least, “I’m beginning to see you. Certainly, I will never fully experience the world as you experience it, but I’m beginning, a bit, to see the world through your eyes.”
David Brooks 

Mrs Rukmani Anantharaman comments on my long multi country trip.
“Dear Anna,
Namaskarams.
The narration of your international trips is truly amazing, I wonder how you managed all such travel and lectures so smoothly, especially at a time when there were no  mobile phones or net connectivity.
We can see that you have always been several steps ahead in anticipating the trends in the field of  computer education.Your remarkable  contributions to   various educational and social organizations are beyond one's imagination.  And I agree with you ,it makes me believe in  the theme in 'The Alchemist'.
Your reference to  your lectures at Berkeley makes me want to share this with you- my grandson has got admission in UC Berkeley this year (Bio Engineering course).
Happy Janmashtami.”
My response:
Thanks. 
Looking ahead has become a pastime with me from childhood. I wanted to improve my learning, my execution of tasks, my looking at the application of new  technologies and most importantly our societal transformations and education.  That was my mission in addition to teaching , mentoring, helping many  and social support. 
As I said in the beginning , nature is interconnected , multidimensional and complex.  One dimensional linearization through specialised focus ,I felt early in my life itself , not the only approach. I showed interest in multiple aspects of life even when I was ten. These include religious aspects like recitation of upanishads,Rudram  , chamakam,  spiritual aspects, construction, planning , organization, behaviour of people, living conditions, purchases, accounting etc. 

Even during my school days, I was fascinated with electricity,  engineering - got involved with construction work, carpentry, lime mortar production, masonry etc.- , and saw the changes In technologies without any understanding or use of technologies. My close association with my uncle and his mentorship  helped me  in sharpening my interest and knowledge. 
I learnt one lesson - life is not a stagnant eat and sleep activity. It is a  highly changing system of many complex and new activities. 
Let me explain changes in bus transport during the first ten years of my life. We traveled by bullock carts. But living on the national highway, we had bus service to go to Tirunelveli or Nagercoil.  Buses were small with no full body and were  initially  running on wood gas. I remember playing with the wheel to pump more air . Then within a few years , I saw diesel run buses. Then I saw buses changed from open entry and exit type to buses with closed doors. Buses became big and looked great. This made an impression on me as a child about the importance of technology in our life. 
Similarly, I saw the society transforming itself at good pace - migrations of people to far off cities for jobs, changes in work culture, onset of college education,  use of electricity, change in dresses and food habits etc, arrival of radio and telephone , poor families becoming rich , rich families becoming poor, etc. I will write about social transformation later. 
So from childhood , observing changes in our society and technology and predicting trends got into me as a hobby and later became a part of me. I didn't and don't have any special skills in this. I didn't anticipate most of the developments . I was just a small step ahead, that is all. 
Greetings 
Dks
29/08/24, 4:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 461
Our belief system is our personal truth that governs our mind. Our judgments are all based on our belief system, even if they tend to challenge our innermost nature. There’s a part of our mind that constantly evaluates everything and everyone. There’s also another part that receives all the judgments and carries the guilt, blame, and shame.
Your belief system makes you fear anything that goes against it. Whatever it is that makes you question your beliefs will certainly bring you a sense of insecurity. This is the main reason why it’s necessary to have the courage to argue regarding our beliefs.
Don Miguel Ruiz 

I was a small tool in 1967 to upgrade the power supply to IISc from KEB to 11kv and thereby reducing interruptions and  meeting our power demands for the next 15 to 20 years. 

Now in 1985, IISc was in a similar situation .  As the chair of the power committee at IISc,  I was concerned about the growth of our power demands. First the substation setup to meet IISc power needs at Gymkhana grounds started supplying power to BWSSB and BHEL . So it was overloaded and tripped regularly. 

Secondly, IISc was planning a new super computer and it needed a lot of power. Our existing system will not be able to meet our demands. 
So I felt that we needed a new look , not an augmentation. I talked to Prof Ramaseshan.  He wasn't receptive. 
But in 1984, Prof CNR Rao took over as director. So I talked to him about the need for a new design for power at IISc.  He understood my statements and agreed immediately. 
So I went through a series of steps to solve this problem. It was one of my good exercise in solving a major practical problem. I solved a few major  problems before like BCCL power distribution, Delhi electric supply , sysgel for TCS etc. 
So how did I go about it?
My first step was to decide that we need to change our power supply to meet our needs . That is recognition and identification of a problem. That was done. 
The next step was to find out whether it was  possible to make small changes to our power supply?
 I felt that the existing substation of KEB at Gymkhana area won't be useful as KEB was not upgrading it. So we need to look for a new supply feeder. 
So the third step was to look for another reliable power supply feeder from KEB. 
There are two options here: 1.go for 11kv itself and increase the capacity. 
2. Upgrade to the next higher voltage supply. 
I started on a search for a new feeder from KEB to IISc which will take care of iisc power requirements for the next 20 years at least. When I look at it now in 2024,  I am happy my solution has stood for more than 40 years and is meeting the IISc demands even now. 
We found that KEB was constructing a 220kv / 66 Kv substation nearby in the new BEL road about 4 kms from our institute. So I felt 66kv was a very good option for iisc and it means that interruptions will be much less compared to our existing 11kv feeder. I informed Prof CNR about my decision to go for 66Kv because of cost implications - more than a crore. He told me to go ahead. 
So I decided on   66kv supply to IIScfrom KEB. I met with  problems from within and without. It received two objections. 
 Prof Chandrasekariah of HV department met me and said that we should consider the option of three overhead 11kv lines instead of the 66kv cable that I was thinking of between the new BEL road substation of KEB upto IISc end point near Ramiah hospital. It is a fact that the 66kv cable’s cost was quite substantial. The estimate was about 60 lakhs.
Here is the dilemma. If I go with 11kv cost will be less ,but it had problems. 
Firstly, IISc load was growing at a rate of 10% every year. So within 10 years we need to look for a new  option for supply.  So if we look at the cost for a longer period , the 11kv option loses its cost advantage. 
Secondly , with 11kv incoming in overhead lines, the interruptions will be high. 
So I rejected his suggestion after discussing in the power committee.  
The second objection was a bigger and unexpected bomb. The chairman of KEB , Mr BG Rudrappa, a good friend of mine who helped me in getting power connection to my house , met Prof CNR Rao. He told him that   IISc    didn't need 66kv. 11kv was  good enough for IISc.  This needed a rebuttal. We met Mr Govindappa , the Technical director of KEB and took him to Prof Rao to convince him of the need for 66kv. I gave only one one fact to support my decision - “Do you want iisc to be one in thousands for it's power supply or one in ten. “ . The answer was in favor if 66kv. 
So this step is crossed. 
More in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks

30/08/24, 4:08 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 462
“Each of you has a call on your lives. Every one of you carries an instinct for excellence within your spirits. No one in this room needs to stay frozen in average and succumb to the mass mediocratization of behavior evident in society along with the collective de-professionalization of business so apparent in industry. Limitation is nothing more than a mentality that too many good people practice daily until they believe it’s reality. It breaks my heart to see so many potentially powerful human beings stuck in a story about why they can’t be extraordinary, professionally and personally. You need to remember that your excuses are seducers, your fears are liars and your doubts are thieves.”
Robin Sharma.   Five am club

We decided to move from 11kv and  go for 66kv as the next higher voltage input for IISc. The year was 1985. It was not an easy step. 
The next step was to decide on our main 66kv/11kv sub station. It was reasonably easy. We located a spot close to the new BEL road opposite to the  CEDT building. We decided 5 MVA capacity was enough . We introduced three rings of distribution throughout the entire campus. 
The next step was to decide on connecting KEB main station to our substation,  in the institute , the distance being about 3kms - partly about 1.6km on BEL road and the remaining inside the campus. The decision was the feeder coming along BEL road would be cable and the feeder inside will be overhead line. This decision reduced the total cost. 
Then started the detailed designs and cost estimations. We engaged Esquire engineers as consultants. They did a complete design of the substation and internal distribution network . Since the 11kv ring main inside the campus is overloaded, we added more 11kv/400v stations . Along with the supercomputer sub station , we decided to go for three rings to improve protection and operations. We decided to go for 11kv cables for the internal campus network instead of overhead lines as it would reduce interruptions. 
The consultants recommended taping a 11kv joint for insulation. I was puzzled . I had seen taping a joint in 1962 at Meenakshi mills during our internship. I asked myself ,” was there no improvement in jointing methods”. I did a literature check and found two approaches - one in situ mold preparation using synthetic insulation powders like ptfe. Another was precast porcelain  bushings.  
We decided to go for Raychem jointing for 11 kv cables used in the campus network .  The total cost estimate was about 1.5 crore rupees, a bit high compared to the 11kv input voltage based design. This excluded the cost of the 66kv cable.  It was worth it ,as it stood the test of time. 
I discussed the proposals with Prof Rao. He agreed to the proposal and costs. So,  we floated a tender. We had another problem coming up here on the eligibility conditions in the tender. The condition was that the bidding company should have installed a 66Kv substation. Many local contractors had not worked on 66kv at that time  and were upset that they were excluded. Some were big ones, some had been working in the institute for some time. I was not prepared to experiment with inexperienced companies.66kv systems needed special skills particularly while joining two cables. So  I felt it was risky. So despite the estate officer's request, I rejected the request. Sympathy won't work here. 
Bombay Suburban was the lowest tender. The company was a famous one, operating half of Bombay electricity and had a few top officers who were our students. So evaluation was easy. It was approved by the power committee,  and the director. Order was issued. They were given the complete 66kv substation electrical panel work and a few new 11kv substations. The cable work was tendered separately. 
There were not many companies making 66kv cables. Siemens, Birla and Gloster were known names. When we floated the tender a new company from Kochi premier cables submitted their quote. It brought in a new problem. Let us discuss it  in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks

“Each of you has a call on your lives. Every one of you carries an instinct for excellence within your spirits. You need to remember that your excuses are seducers, your fears are liars and your doubts are thieves.”
Robin Sharma.   Five am club

We decided to move from 11kv and  go for 66kv as the next higher voltage input for IISc. The year was 1985. It was not an easy step. 
The next step was to decide on our main 66kv/11kv sub station. It was reasonably easy. We located a spot close to the new BEL road opposite to the  CEDT building. We decided 5 MVA capacity was enough . We introduced three rings of distribution throughout the entire campus. 
The next step was to decide on connecting KEB main station to our substation,  in the institute , the distance being about 3kms - partly about 1.6km on BEL road and the remaining inside the campus. The decision was the feeder coming along BEL road would be cable and the feeder inside will be overhead line. This decision reduced the total cost. 
Then started the detailed designs and cost estimations. We engaged Esquire engineers as consultants. They did a complete design of the substation and internal distribution network . Since the 11kv ring main inside the campus is overloaded, we added more 11kv/400v stations . Along with the supercomputer sub station , we decided to go for three rings to improve protection and operations. We decided to go for 11kv cables for the internal campus network instead of overhead lines as it would reduce interruptions. 
The consultants recommended taping a 11kv joint for insulation. I was puzzled . I had seen taping a joint in 1962 at Meenakshi mills during our internship. I asked myself ,” was there no improvement in jointing methods”. I did a literature check and found two approaches - one in situ mold preparation using synthetic insulation powders like ptfe. Another was precast porcelain  bushings.  
We decided to go for Raychem jointing for 11 kv cables used in the campus network .  The total cost estimate was about 1.5 crore rupees, a bit high compared to the 11kv input voltage based design. This excluded the cost of the 66kv cable.  It was worth it ,as it stood the test of time. 
I discussed the proposals with Prof Rao. He agreed to the proposal and costs. So,  we floated a tender. We had another problem coming up here on the eligibility conditions in the tender. The condition was that the bidding company should have installed a 66Kv substation. Many local contractors had not worked on 66kv at that time  and were upset that they were excluded. Some were big ones, some had been working in the institute for some time. I was not prepared to experiment with inexperienced companies.66kv systems needed special skills particularly while joining two cables. So  I felt it was risky. So despite the estate officer's request, I rejected the request. Sympathy won't work here. 
Bombay Suburban was the lowest tender. The company was a famous one, operating half of Bombay electricity and had a few top officers who were our students. So evaluation was easy. It was approved by the power committee,  and the director. Order was issued. They were given the complete 66kv substation electrical panel work and a few new 11kv substations. The cable work was tendered separately. 
There were not many companies making 66kv cables. Siemens, Birla and Gloster were known names. When we floated the tender a new company from Kochi premier cables submitted their quote. It brought in a new problem. Let us discuss it  in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks
31/08/24, 4:36 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 463
“I recall these ideas in connection with my scheme of a Research Institute of Science for India, of which you have doubtless heard or read. It seems to me that no better use can be made of the ascetic spirit than the establishment of monasteries or residential halls for men dominated by this spirit, where they should live with ordinary decency, and devote their lives to the cultivation of sciences—natural and humanistic. I am of [the] opinion that if such a crusade in favour of an asceticism of this kind were undertaken by a competent leader, it would greatly help asceticism, science, and the good name of our common country; and I know not who would make a more fitting general of such a campaign than Vivekananda. Do you think you would care to apply yourself to the mission of galvanising into life our ancient traditions in this respect?”
JN Tata's  letter to Swami Vivekananda regarding the setting up of IISc.

I am wondering why I kept the cable component outside the scope of the main tender? The main reasons were that it is very expensive, so it will be better to buy it from manufacturers ,and  we can ensure quality and delivery time . This was found to be a good decision. 
We got responses  from three manufacturers, one of them not well known. But the problem was the difference in prices. The known companies quoted a little more than a crore of rupees for our requirement , the new company quoted less than 50 lakhs rupees. So the question was “ can we reject this quote from the not well known company?” We decided to investigate further to arrive at a decision.  
We decided to see the production facility. We went to Kochi to see the actual production. The extrusion machine was a new continuous extrusion machine . The insulation material was imported. So we were satisfied and gave the order. We saved about fifty lakhs rupees . They agreed to produce the entire length of 1.2 km in one run without break. So we didn't need a joint . We inspected the production . The company had to transport and lay the cable. 
One joint was needed at the institute entry point where we shift from the cable to an overhead line. We got Westinghouse engineers flown in to do the precast joint at the junction point between cable and overhead line. 
The entire work took one year. I inspected every equipment like transformers, circuit breakers, panels, 11kv cables etc. The transformer was manufactured by Cromptons in Mumbai,  11kv cables at Rewa, MP, panels at bangalore,  circuit breakers at Nasik etc. I visited all these cities and inspected the acceptance tests. 
By 1986, we commissioned the new substations and gave an operational contract to Bombay Suburban company. It was a job well done for me. The entire system is still working even now. 
There was one problem - an interturn fault occurred in the transformer after 15 months of service. So for a week we went to the old 11kv system. Fortunately it was still in usable state , not dismantled yet. So we could switch to it. After about a month , we got the transformer repaired. We decided to go in for a second transformer of 5 MVA so that if one fails, the other will be available.  
So now we have ideas on problem solving. It is not one dimensional like theory of Clayton or checklists of Atul Gawande or approximations. 
It has several aspects.
Discovery - find out the reality.
Identify the problem with its subcomponents.
Select goals and parameters like adequacy, maintenance, performance, cost, growth , longevity, reliability etc 
Decomposition - divide into steps
Estimation
Architecture
Design
Proper selections
Iteration
Consultation with users, experts, 
Optimization
So that completes my involvement in building  a new and stable power system for IISc. 
Greetings 
Dks

01/09/24, 6:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: We are not aware if any project at once so opportune and so far-reaching in its beneficial effects was ever mooted in India, as that of the postgraduate research University of Mr Tata. The scheme grasps the vital point of weakness in our national well-being with a clearness of vision and tightness of grip, the masterliness of which is only equaled by the munificence of the gift which it is ushered to the public.
Mr Tata’s scheme paves the path for placing into the hands of Indians this knowledge of nature . . .
By some the scheme is regarded as chimerical, because of the immense amount of money required for it, to wit about Rs 74 lakh. The best reply to this fear is: If one man—and he not the richest in the land—could find 30 lakh, could not the whole country find the rest? It is ridiculous to think otherwise, when the interest sought to be served is of the paramount importance.
Prabhuddha bharata
Vivekananda response to Tata.

02/09/24, 4:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 465

उत्तिष्ठत जाग्रत प्राप्य वरान्निबोधत।

क्षुरस्य धारा निशिता दुरत्यया दुर्गं पथस्तत्कवयो वदन्ति ॥

“Arise, awake, find out the great ones and learn of them; for sharp as a razor's edge, hard to traverse, difficult of going is that path, say the sages.”

Katopanishad

The year 1986-1987 had a few events in my life. But most importantly , I started on my journey on diffusion of computer science knowledge across many colleges. I will describe a few episodes here. The period  also revealed my heart problem. 

I visited TCE for the first time after leaving it in Jan 1964. The visit was arranged by profs. Srinivasan and Santhanakrishnan. I gave five or six lectures on operating systems. I met Prof Nityanandan , principal. Later I became a member of the academic council . This membership continues till date with a break of about five years in the middle. I had to travel by bus to Madurai at that time . There was no direct train from Bangalore to Madurai. The college guest house was yet to come up. So I stayed in college house hotel.  I was also associated with TCE in most activities like faculty selection, lectures , training programs, conference talks, etc continuously over the next 38 plus years . I am very happy with my participation and contribution to my alma mater. I see a great improvement in the institution. Research , publications and sponsored projects, association with industries took primacy in the views of the management, principals and faculty. Prof Nityanandan started the research activity in the college. 
This involvement in the academic council started another path - curriculum development for autonomous colleges. I helped many colleges to modernise and balance their curriculum. I love my involvement with TCE. 


We shifted to another house E07 , a ground floor one with a garage.  
My next big involvement was with Malnad college of engineering,  Hassan. Both Prof Dharmananda Rao and K A Krishnamurthy got me into their institution. I had been visiting MCE from 1976 onwards for evaluation of KSCST student projects. But I got involved with a UGC network program first. Then we came up with a unique Masters program on computers and communications which got the appreciation of UGC . So I had to teach two courses for two to three years for their ME students. I had to travel by bus and stayed in a small lodge there. The ME program went off well. I was also involved by Prof Dharmananda Rao in faculty selections for several years. My association lasted for more than two decades. 
Then I was invited by Prof Prithviraj of MIT Manipal for a three day training program on Unix to his students. MIT didn't have any faculty in computer science at that time.  Later when Prof A Prabhakar became the principal, he started ME program and requested me to teach OS to the ME students. I visited several times to Manipal. Prof S Rajaram, my IISc colleague of sixties and seventies had joined there as a professor. 

I taught at NMAMIT , Nitte, a course on data structures. I stayed in karkala. I traveled by bus to karkala. It was Col BSGK Murthy met me in the bus stand and put me in their faculty stay area. I didn't like it. So prof Sridhar Hegde, the principal shifted me to a hotel. One day after lunch, I climbed the hill to see the tall Gomateshwara . On return, I felt a chest pain, but ignored it and gave lectures for two hours. The pain persisted. I didn't see any doctor. Afterwards , I realised that God saved me that day. I decided to see Dr Ramesh, a good cardiologist in bangalore.  He did a stress test and told me that it showed a problem with my heart. So I decided to go to Bombay to see Dr Nair . Dr Nair wanted a cardiogram done the next day . My wife and I stayed at the BSES guest house . Mr Hegde  of BSES arranged it and I did an inspection also. The angio was done the next day at the National hospital. The cardiologist said that there is a block in the right ventricular artery , but  I don't need  a surgery. He prescribed medicines which I continue till date. That included a beta blocker, aspirin and a bp medicine. These are in addition to other medicines I was taking. My children stayed alone in the house for nearly two days. My daughter managed it well. IISc didn't reimburse the heavy expenses incurred by me. 
If I look back at this incident, I am puzzled by my behaviour . I wasn't affected by this incident of chest pain, angiogram and medications. I continued my normal life, infact I increased my workload. Nobody in my family was bothered about my health condition. It affected me for a few months and afterwards , I forgot about it . I got routine medicines and took them regularly. It was a part of my life and my daily routine, no worries. I didn't worry about my health condition till 1999. That was strange. I am normally a worryingperson. But here i didn't worry. Why? I don'tknow.
 I find many worried about problems of BP or diabetes at a late age and hence my surprise at my behavior.  I don't know what made me to continue with my activities and not worry about my health. I behaved like a normal person. But I didn't ignore it. I reduced oil from my food, avoided ghee and had more veggies. I also tried for a few years amrithaballi stem kashayam.  
More in the next. 
Greetings 
Dks

03/09/24, 5:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 466
“This is a strange and amazing fact: we know every nook and cranny of the human body, we have catalogued every animal on the planet, we have described and baptized every blade of grass, but we have left psychological techniques to their empiricism for centuries, as if they were of lesser importance than those of the healer, the breeder or the farmer.”

Jean Piaget, “La pédagogie moderne” (1949).

Prof Bhandari requested me to give a series of lectures to their newly started  BE computer science students in BDT college of engineering at Davangere.  I accepted and lectured there. They had only one faculty member , Mr Subba Reddy , associated with this program. He joined us at CSA, IISc for his ME program. 

During this period, I conducted two programs on Vedic mathematics and Indian systems in the Sankar mutt, Chamrajpet and had a series of discussions with Sri Paramananda Bharathi swamiji. Srinath sastry assisted me. 
During the workshop, I met a great and sincere academic , Prof MN Channabasappa, head of Mathematics department at KREC, Surathkal. He invited me to KREC for a board of studies meeting. That was a very purposeful meeting. That was my first meeting and visit to KREC. Again that association with KREC and later NITK continued for more than two and half decades. My association with Prof MNC continues even now. 


I was selected to go to AIT Bangkok to teach a course on decision support systems during the summer of 1987. It was a great experience. I had exposure to the working practices of another institute. I had students from different nations. it was mandatory to put the ppt for each lecture along with notes in the library beforehand. I liked it. There were three Indians in the faculty - Prof Sadananda and Prof Malhotra. Prof Nanda from Roorkee   left in the middle and went back to Roorkee.I was invited by Prof Sadananda several times for dinner. Prof Malhotra , Prof Setty invited me for dinner. There were three of us from India teaching st AIT. Prof K Subramanya was from IIT Kanpur and another person from IIM Ahmedabad - I forgot his name. 

We stayed in the guest house. Vegetarian food wasn't easy. There was a food court . We got rice and dhal or some vegetables. Coffee was available in the faculty coffee room like that in the university of Manchester. I went to the department by 730 am and stayed till 4 pm. The library was very good . We could borrow a lot of fiction and even Tamil magazines. The department facilities were good. 
A few aspects of Thailand impressed me.
1.Disciplined work culture, no supervisors needed.
2.All electronic components needed to assemble a radio, tape recorder to a computer were available at affordable prices . They were sold even in normal street shops unlike Singapore.
3.All technical manuals for assembly of electronic devices , all computer manuals including MSDOS,  office etc were available in Thai language. It led to the progress of the country. 
4.Textile industries were modern with lots of production units for blended textiles.  Costs were low. 
5. Buddhist piety was common amongst most people. King was respected as God. 
6. King used the title Rama and used Sanskrit word processor.  
7. Economic Disparity was high. A few were rich. Most were poor. 

We were paid per diem charges by the embassy. So every month we went there to collect our per diem payments. 
I did lots of purchases as textiles and tailoring were inexpensive and fast. 
I met Prof Venkatasubramanian, who was the DPI in Tamilnadu and helped in the introduction of mid day meals scheme in schools. I met his son much later in 2000 when he was a GM at Indian bank. He came to see our computer centre at AIT. I told him that IIT madras has a good computer centre and there are many others like IISc,  TIFR having excellent computing facilities and he could have got much better ideas from them. AIT computer centre wasn't a good one. 
The experience was good. I had a good idea of Thailand,  its people , its Ramakien , emerald Bhudda , Ayuttaya and Bhuddhist and cultural  aspects . I brought Michael Jackson's music cassettes and a few more for my son . I also bought a tape recorder.
Greetings 
Dks
04/09/24, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 467
“You think that to change your beliefs, you have to adopt a new line of thinking, rather than seek experiences that make that thinking self-evident.
A belief is what you know to be true because experience has made it evident to you. If you want to change your life, change your beliefs. If you want to change your beliefs, go out and have experiences that make them real to you. Not the opposite way around.”
Brianna Wiest 

Dr Sanjay  Chitnis has a query:
“Dear Sir, You have been contributing to the engineering education field in general and CS education in particular, by going out of the way, even though it took a toll on your health. Fortunately, you took corrective actions on time. Now, based on your experience, what is your advice to take care of one's health? It is a very basic question with a straight forward answer, but most of us don't seem to be able to implement it.”

My response 
Well it is an important aspect to be learnt , understood and put into practice by everyone. But it is complex , not easy. 
Let me start with a disclaimer. I am not an expert , instead I am a novice. I am a big bundle of emotions. I am very sensitive, get upset very fast, get angry very fast.I am also not a follower of strict discipline. I am not a strong believer in faith. So I am not a role model. All I can say is that I was alert , and proactive. I write my problems so others learn from them . That is all my motive with my posts.
Let me address your question - how does one take care of one's health?
Actually, Hindu sages understood it very early. Our upanishads describe our body system well. It says it is a well connected system . Mind is part of the body. They emphasized consciousness and conscious living as essential. They defined liberation as the goal. Vedas talk about societal order, understanding and respect for nature , sharing and harmonious living. Vedas emphasize values like truth, non violence, Peace, sharing wealth, dharma , karma, yoga  , belief in ultimate reality etc. They emphasize on knowledge and wisdom. 
Vedas say humans are of different kind and suggested self learning, understanding and realization as the starting point for life. 
The importance of health is understood by most middle class educated people. I saw a newspaper story which says that it is not fiction books that are sold Most,  but self help self improvement books. There are thousands. I am not talking about productivity improvement, success in life, career advancement etc, but about better health and longer life. So awareness is there, but approaches are not there. 
First there is no one size fits all. Each person should study about himself and his 96 tattvas. Then create a cocktail of habits and actions. He or she should define principles for his or her life, goals,  purpose and happiness quotient etc. 
The answer is in each person's mind and in everyone's hand. It is intrinsic . 
The problems from the beginning of civilization till today have been:
We run our life in general like an automaton. 
We always react. We ignore our health. Only after a health issue  like high BP or diabetes occurs.
We look  for easy palliative solutions . 
We don’t plan for our health. 
Many don't understand their body and ignore signals. They are not aware of their body. 
Most lead a routine life. 
Most compare their life with others. 
Materialism dominates today. Aspirations are high. Wants and greed are common. Selfishness is rampant. 
We expect support from the government. 
We don't have a purpose defined life. We don’t ask what we want from our life? Is there a meaning in life?
Most importantly ,the strong  relationships  between disciplined life , habits, life styles, food, emotions, exercises etc is totally ignored by most. Health problems mean medicines and doctors. 
More in the next. 
Greetings
Dks
04/09/24, 5:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 467

We always react. We ignore our health. Only after a health issue  like high BP or diabetes occurs.
We look  for easy palliative solutions . 
We don’t plan for our health. 
Many don't understand their body and ignore signals. They are not aware of their body. 
Most lead a routine life. 
Most compare their life with others. 
Materialism dominates today. Aspirations are high. Wants and greed are common. Selfishness is rampant. 
We expect support from the government. 
We don't have a purpose defined life. We don’t ask what we want from our life? Is there a meaning in life?
Most importantly ,the strong  relationships  between disciplined life , habits, life styles, food, emotions, exercises etc is totally ignored by most. Health problems mean medicines and doctors. 
More in the next. 
Greetings
Dks
05/09/24, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 468
“Over time, the animals that possessed even a rudimentary capacity to learn had a better chance of surviving than those with fixed behaviors—and they were more likely to pass their genome (now including genetically driven learning algorithms) on to the next generation. In this manner, natural selection favored the emergence of learning.”
Dehaene.    How do we learn.

Let me get back to proper living. 
Some common practices  we do in our life  are :
Non nutritious food intake. Erratic eating habits. 
Irregular habits of living like sleeping.
Tense behaviour in jobs
Worrying on small things and on the future. 
Fear of life itself , happenings in life,  other people 
Addictions - good or bad, excess is bad. Kural says even Nectar becomes a  poison if it exceeds the limit.
The greatest obsession today seems to be a mantra for success and wealth. Our wants have gone up and keep increasing .  We moved away from piety, humility, and harmony to a large extent. But there are some who practice these virtues. 
We disregarded nature in the belief that we are superior and can control nature. Living with nature is returning for some people. 

There are many doubts with many people who genuinely want to lead a healthy life. 
Some are listed below.
How and why do I live?
Can I control my emotions, beliefs, habits ?
Can I improve my health?
Are religion, prayer and puja important?
Should I build routine practices like proper time management, proper daily activities at scheduled time?
Will yoga calm my mind, remove my worries?
Can I be disconnected and different in my life from my neighbours, community and friends ?
Do I have the willpower to avoid undesirable food and drinks?
How do I control my anger , is it possible?
Can I lead an ideal life as defined in the vedas and upanishads in the context of present day survival, job and competitive requirements. What are the adjustments I should undertake?
What kind of balances - jobs, personal health regime, family responsibilities , society responsibilities, religious functions , our needs etc. It is a big jugglery  in life - should I practice in my life? This is a difficult question faced by most of us . We say that I am busy with my job and there is no time for exercises and family. I know people who don't have time to read . Time management is not easy. Or I am a foodie, my tastes are important   and I love to eat undesirable food like sweets, white rice, red meat, white bread or processed food or salty food etc. 
More importantly , I don't believe in many good practices- many didn't believe that tobacco was bad and many believe alcohol is good or coffee is necessary or I will get headaches. Many good practices are conjecture. So what should I follow?
Is there a set of proven dos and don'ts to be followed by a common person?
Is taking medicine for everything wrong? Do i have a role in deciding what medicines are good for me and what can be avoided by me?
When should I visit a doctor ? 
Is irregularity occasionally a crime? 
What is conscious living ? Is it desirable? Is it effective?  It is observed that many practices like yoga , meditation etc can not provide instant reliefs. So there is a lot of skepticism. 

So go through and find answers acceptable to you for your doubts. 
More in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks

05/09/24, 11:16 am - Sanjay Chitnis: <Media omitted>
06/09/24, 5:56 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 469
We are not simply Homo sapiens, but Homo docens—the species that teaches itself. Most of what we know about the world was not given to us by our genes: we had to learn it from our environment or from those around us. No other animal has managed to change its ecological niche so radically, moving from the African savanna to deserts, mountains, islands, polar ice caps, cave dwellings, cities, and even outer space, all within a few thousand years. Learning has fueled it all. From making fire and designing stone tools to agriculture, exploration, and atomic fission, the story of humanity is one of constant self-reinvention. At the root of all these accomplishments lies one secret: the extraordinary ability of our brain to formulate hypotheses and select those that fit with our environment.
Dehaene.    How do we learn.

So I feel that life is all about judgments, balancing between many essential activities  and a well reasoned  decision making. 
Life is a complex, uncertain  and highly balanced sequence of small and major actions . How do you decide on the importance of health - does it play a major role in your scheme? 
So the answer is a periodic learning, contemplation, review and self assessment. 

Happiness comes from giving , from a strong commitment to family , from a purpose in life. 
Let me come up with ideas for better health and life.  
Life is proportional to good health, contentment, purpose in life, sharing, cooperation, humility, helping attitude .
Health is directly proportional to  understanding body, understanding your needs, regular and good habits, nutritious food, exercises, yoga, meditation, good and caring friends, good balance, viveka, calmness, proper and timely actions, well thought out decisions, 
Health is indirectly proportional to bad habits like smoking and drinking, over eating, lazy living, poor time managements, greed and high ambitions, worry and anger, very high and tense moments, poor decision making, aviveka, 
Increasing the desirable aspects increases health. Decreasing the undesirable also increases health. 
We may take wrong decisions.  Find corrections. Build flexible approaches.
Take ownership of your life. You are responsible for your life. Others will help you. Your living affects your family and friends.      
Beliefs play a big role in our life. Our life is governed by hundreds of accumulated beliefs built by teachings by elders, observations and experiences. All beliefs are not provable. We make mistakes due to erroneous beliefs. Periodic assessment of beliefs is necessary for a good life. Fixed beliefs and mindsets are not very good. But beliefs are necessary. Don't change beliefs like changing shirts. 
We are not rational animals. We are not ideal people. But we need to define our life dynamics through principles and goals. These give directions. Children should be trained to have principles. Some principles like Speak the truth - Satyam vada - are absolute. Some like belief in god or surrender to god or visiting temples are left to the individuals to choose. Hinduism gives us many choices to live. 
Be aware. Be mindful. Try to listen to not only people but also the society and happenings everywhere.   Be conscious in taking major decisions. Use data carefully. Don't be a slave to data . Trust is needed in life.       
How do you change your practices to get good health? There is no one universal solution. incremental practicing works for many. Periodically take up one practice at a time. There is another approach - change everything at once. Sustainability is a key here. It is not easy to maintain all practices all the time.      

Actually in the early years, Hinduism developed many practices for harmonious and Ethical living. To create better understanding amongst all people, puranas were developed. People recited Ramayana for becoming an ideal person. Societal living and problems were explained through panchatantra and hitopadesha stories. A whole framework was built in a story form. Mahabharata gives a detailed description of different types of people - good and bad. But we ignored all these.
Greetings
Dks

07/09/24, 4:08 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 470
“It takes a lot of courage to be a research scientist. It really does. I mean, you invest an enormous amount of yourself, your life, your time, and nothing may come of it. You could spend five years working on a problem and it could be wrong before you are done. Or someone might make a discovery just as you are finishing that could make it all wrong. That’s a very real possibility.”
Vera Rudin , Astronomer. 
Let me continue with our discussions.  
I said life is complex. Let us look at who is responsible for making life complex. Each person individually and the society collectively are responsible for this. 
Until  the middle of the 20th century, life was simple. People were happy, didn't have big ambitions and aspirations, were more spiritual and religious, Community lived together closely. Then money became our master. Technology became the second master. We continue to be slaves of technology, data , and materialism. Consumption was important, frugality was discarded. All our greed, ambitions, and opportunities made life tense and complicated. We became tense and sick. 
We believe that work is the main purpose of life and success means reaching a high position and great wealth or name. Happiness is secondary. Balance is upset. So we have made life a complex system. We can untangle it. Many are trying to reverse this trend. 
Let me look at different types of people. 
I categorise living into the following groups.
Those who live for the day. Have no big purpose. Don't worry about the future, Don't have ambitions. They have more time for family and society. Most village people belong to this group. 
Those who are at the next level. They have ambitions. They want to get degrees, educate the children , look for career advancements etc. They are not the EMI people. They are careful with money, save for the future, live within their means. They know the limits of their capabilities. So they have small wants and ambitions.  But they are a confused set of people with lots of mistaken beliefs and ignorant ideas. But they merge with society well. They look after their family. A majority of the lower middle class fall in this group. 
There are some who are work conscious. They want to grow fast , reach higher levels ,and ignore the family to some extent. They ignore society and even their health. They take partial remedies.  They may aspire for positions beyond their capabilities. They may spend beyond their financial limits. This is a varied group- leading to several sub categories. Some are OK, some are over worked, some adopt illegal methods to make more money, some have marital discord, many have health problems, most are tense and unhappy. Academic and professional successes don't matter in life. 
Those who maintain a balance between work and family , society, friends , and who were compassionate , considerate , helpful . This is a rare group , but the number is not small. 
There can be many more categories if you look at people’s behaviours, mental makeup, thinking, expectations  and actions. 
So everyone should have a serious look at the way people live, how they live, are they happy , are they healthy , etc and try to build the thousands of bricks that make their life. Health and happiness deserve a very serious consideration from one's childhood onwards. These should stay with us throughout our life . They should guide us. 
Actually, this is not an easy topic. It can lead to hundreds of opinions . Thanks to Sanjay for starting these questions.
Greetings
Dks

“No matter where you are on the pathway of your life, please don’t let the pain of an imperfect past hinder the glory of your fabulous future. You are so much more powerful than you may currently understand. Splendid victories—and outright blessings—are coming your way. And you’re exactly where you need to be to receive the growth necessary for you to lead the unusually productive, extremely prodigious and exceptionally influential life that you’ve earned through your harshest trials. Nothing is wrong at this moment, even if it feels like everything’s falling apart.”
Robin Sharma.   5 am club

Mr Gurumurthy has a comment. 
“Sir, you have managed very well the toughest part of life - a balance between the asks of the brain and the heart (please see the pictorial below). This is not possible for every one. I think, it has something to do with one's innate nature.”

My remarks
Thanks.  I believe strongly that my training and great upbringing during my childhood is the reason for my contentment  and knowledge giving life. 
When I was born , the ethos and the belief systems  in the society was very different from what it is today.
There was a  tremendous level of idealism and enthusiasm to usher in Rama rajya. It got into you and those beliefs became part of me. To say it in brief, - be happy, do something for the society and for all, let us work together for the progress of a new India.  That was the motto. Secondly, most items were not available.  There was a scarcity and people lived a very simple, frugal life without ambitions. Most lived in poverty. Rich were a very very small number. It helped me to create a big set of principles - high degree of scholarship, learning and curiosity, understanding, some degree of courage , flexible mind that is no fixed mindset, review and contemplation, frugality, reduced wants,no expectations, commitment, doing a job well, self dependence, low importance to money, reduce tensions, spiritual not religious outlook, etc- on which I based my life structure.

So my simple answers to your comment are
1.mind functions are mostly conscious ones.
2. Heart functions are mostly impulsive ones. 
3. Both can be irrational and wrong. 
4. Both depend on emotions and ego. 
5.We need to balance between the two . Move towards more conscious actions leaving routine daily actions to subconscious levels. Avoid impulsive actions.
