09/09/24, 4:52 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 472
“humanity discovered that it could increase this remarkable ability even further with the help of an institution: the classroom. Pedagogy is an exclusive privilege of our species: no other animal actively teaches its offspring by setting aside specific time to monitor their progress, difficulties, and errors. The invention of the school, an institution which systematizes the informal education present in all human societies, has vastly increased our brain potential. We have discovered that we can take advantage of the exuberant plasticity of the child brain to instill in it a maximum amount of information and talent. Over centuries, our school system has continued to improve in efficiency, starting earlier and earlier in childhood and now lasting for fifteen years or more.”

Dehaene    how to learn 

 Let me get back to my activities from  1987 onwards.
I will call the period of 87 to 96 , the era of AICTE.  Government of india made AICTE as a mandatory institution for technical education. All courses run by engineering colleges, and Pharmacy institutions had to be inspected and approved by an AICTE committee. It was mandatory. Prof Khanna became the chair of AICTE. 
It set up a new regional centre in bangalore. Mr Manoharan was deputed from the DTE office to become the regional officer. He ran the office with only temporary staff.
Initially, AICTE set up task forces for every state. Karnataka regional office was responsible for two states - karnataka and kerala. I was made a member of both states' task forces. We evolved an inspection mechanism. 
So started my travels to approve existing engineering colleges. I visited most engineering colleges in karnataka and kerala. It was another round of hectic activities.  I visited many places in both states . Later I visited colleges in Tamilnadu and Andhra pradesh also.  Normally, we visited two institutions in a day and later it became three due to heavy workload and time deadline  considerations. 
I was involved with these inspections  for more than a decade. That was my service to engineering education in the South. 
AICTE  approvals happened in two stages - the first stage for about five years concentrated  on approving the existing programs run by the colleges. We included one representative from DTE of the state and one from the university in addition to experts in the inspection team. 
After inspection, we prepared a report. We gave a list of deficiencies to be corrected by each college.  The deficiencies were mainly in the areas of faculty numbers, faculty qualifications, library facilities, computer facilities , lab equipment , classroom requirements etc.
 We found most private institutions had a severe shortage of faculty. They could not meet the students to faculty ratio of 15:1. The shortage was very  bad in the computer science area. Also, they didn't have many senior faculty like professors and assistant professors. But most private colleges in karnataka  like BIET, Davangere, SIT, Tumkur, Gogte , Belgaum, KLECE, Belgaum, MIT Manipal, SDMCET, Dharwad, MSRIT, RVCE , Bangalore had excellent facilities . The managements in most of these institutions , except a few, had enlightened persons in the committee. So the focus on quality was reasonable. Their faculty strength in traditional disciplines like civil, mechanical and electrical was reasonable. But most faculty were inexperienced. Faculty turnover was high.The institutions struggled to get post graduate qualified faculty particularly in ECE and CSE. 
 The overall situation was good with the seven aided colleges in karnataka . They had the full quota of faculty positions filled up, including senior positions. Most had a good experience and many got their ME or MTech degree through the QIP scheme of the central government.  
But it wasn't good with UVCE and the government run BDT college of engineering.  

The approval letter issued by AICTE mentioned these deficiencies and wanted these to be corrected.
These situations continued for decades and even became worse. So the quality suffered. 
More in the next. 
Greetings 
Dks
09/09/24, 6:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 472

Normally, we visited two institutions in a day and later it became three due to heavy workload and time deadline  considerations. 
I was involved with these inspections  for more than a decade. That was my service to engineering education in the South. 
AICTE  approvals happened in two stages - the first stage for about five years concentrated  on approving the existing programs run by the colleges. We included one representative from DTE of the state and one from the university in addition to experts in the inspection team. 
After inspection, we prepared a report. We gave a list of deficiencies to be corrected by each college.  The deficiencies were mainly in the areas of faculty numbers, faculty qualifications, library facilities, computer facilities , lab equipment , classroom requirements etc.
 We found most private institutions had a severe shortage of faculty. They could not meet the students to faculty ratio of 15:1. The shortage was very  bad in the computer science area. Also, they didn't have many senior faculty like professors and assistant professors. But most private colleges in karnataka  like BIET, Davangere, SIT, Tumkur, Gogte , Belgaum, KLECE, Belgaum, MIT Manipal, SDMCET, Dharwad, MSRIT, RVCE , Bangalore had excellent facilities . The managements in most of these institutions , except a few, had enlightened persons in the committee. So the focus on quality was reasonable. Their faculty strength in traditional disciplines like civil, mechanical and electrical was reasonable. But most faculty were inexperienced. Faculty turnover was high.The institutions struggled to get post graduate qualified faculty particularly in ECE and CSE. 
 The overall situation was good with the seven aided colleges in karnataka . They had the full quota of faculty positions filled up, including senior positions. Most had a good experience and many got their ME or MTech degree through the QIP scheme of the central government.  
But it wasn't good with UVCE and the government run BDT college of engineering.  

The approval letter issued by AICTE mentioned these deficiencies and wanted these to be corrected.
These situations continued for decades and even became worse. So the quality suffered. 
More in the next.

Dks
10/09/24, 5:50 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 473
“All young children share abstract intuitions in the domains of language, arithmetic, logic, and probability, thus providing a foundation on which higher education must be grounded. And all learners benefit from focused attention, active engagement, error feedback, and a cycle of daily rehearsal and nightly consolidation—I call these factors the “four pillars” of learning, because, as we shall see, they lie at the foundation of the universal human learning algorithm present in all our brains.”
Dehaene.     How to learn 

The second phase of AICTE approvals  started after about five years and  looked at the proposals for the starting of new colleges. This led to a flood of applications for starting private colleges and the starting of many new colleges. 
AICTE came up with a set of requirements or guidelines. I was involved in both phases,  though I didn't agree with the emphasis of AICTE on physical infrastructure , and facilities needed to start a college . The guidelines ignored the capabilities of the managing committee, its interest in education, its concern for quality etc. I strongly believe that a college should not be started because someone had money. We need to look at the educational ethos and motivation of promoters. But AICTE ignored that.
 Many managements who proposed new colleges were single owner concerns with the owner having money but with no idea of education or running a college. Some were civil contractors and some were excise contractors without any educational ethos, culture, ambience or motivation. Most private college principals were just dummy fronts.  This was one important component leading to the commercialisation of education and higher tuition fees. Capitation fees went up in different forms. AICTE had no say on fee fixation. That was in the domain of the state government.  AICTE didn't provide norms for admission. That was left to the state. 
This proliferation led to many major developments in the country. 
Let us look at them briefly. 
It opened up tremendous opportunities to many rural and semi urban and middle class students to study engineering particularly IT. It definitely provided employment opportunities to many. 
The colleges started in semi urban areas helped marginally in the development of that area. Infrastructure improved. 
These institutions quickly scaled up the production of lakhs of engineering graduates every year within a short period of 7-8  years from the starting time. This provided manpower to the emerging IT services sector. 
Most importantly,  private colleges became agile to move into the new emerging areas of computer science. They became the main source of providing manpower to our growing IT services industries. They definitely played a role in the success of Indian IT services leadership. This revolution went unnoticed by most faculty and even educationists and many eminent academics even at IISc ,IIT s and IIMs. It was almost a silent, autonomous , undirected revolution aided by IT companies. I didn't see a similar revolution when mobile communications became prominent in the country with 4G and now 5G. 
While private colleges were without dogmas, government colleges in Kerala were mired in many dogmas. The faculty there believed that private colleges are poisonous and should not be allowed in the state. This belief existed in other states , but was not strong enough.  When we went to approve the starting of private colleges in Kerala,  there was a strong opposition from teaching faculty and politicians. Even many vice chancellors were against the setting up of private colleges in the Southern states of kerala, Tamilnadu and even karnataka.  I was surprised at the rigid and outdated beliefs and myopic outlook of these  eminent educationists including some who advised the state government.  
Many private colleges excelled in education leading most state government colleges. State government colleges were lagging behind these private  institutions in Infrastructure,  equipments, agility , campus placements etc. Faculty in government colleges lacked motivation. They were doing a job , not academics. Their thinking was backward looking - looking at engineering as it was 50 years back. But the faculty of  government colleges felt they were superior to the faculty of private colleges. So an unequal  caste system was built by these segregation. It was unfortunate.
greetings
Dks

As you gain more and more access to your true nature, you will also spontaneously receive creative thoughts, because the field of pure potentiality is also the field of infinite creativity and pure knowledge. Franz Kafka, the Austrian philosopher and poet, once said, You need not leave your room. Remain sitting at your table and listen. You need not even listen, simply wait. You need not even wait, just learn to become quiet, and still, and solitary. The world will freely offer itself to you to be unmasked. It has no choice; it will roll in ecstasy at your feet.


The affluence of the universe , the lavish display and abundance of the universe is an expression of the creative mind of nature. The more tuned in you are to the mind of nature, the more you have access to its infinite, unbounded creativity. But first, you have to go beyond the turbulence of your internal dialogue to connect with that abundant, affluent, infinite, creative  mind. And then you create the possibility of dynamic activity while at the same time carrying the stillness of the eternal, unbounded, creative mind.
Deepak Chopra 




Many colleagues and friends questioned me “ why are you getting involved in this inspection and approval processes? It affects you.”
I also thought about it . So I feel this a good time to give my reasons for involvement in AICTE during the initial period. 
While my regular attention is towards my teaching, courses development, and research, I had a general focus - education from school level onwards. I know I can't do anything significant about the education status in the country. But I did whatever is possible like the training program for high school teachers conducted at IISc for about 20 years, the extension lectures program, computer science education diffusion in many colleges, and curriculum development in many universities. 
But I always kept my eyes open on the education scene from high school days. I was happy during the fifties when the country started thousands of schools, hundreds of colleges and many professional colleges providing opportunities to young persons with both school and university education. It opened up the country ,many were motivated to go for college education. If you look at the 1950 scene, only a few privileged in big cities got access to a college education. Most could at the best, get only school education upto 10th standard.  This starting of a large number of higher education institutions brought university education at the door steps of many. It was a great development.  
But then educational developments slowed down. From 1960 to 90 ,only a few engineering colleges were started in some states. Karnataka took two important decisions. It introduced the concept of aided colleges. We had 8 colleges that moved into the aided category. The government in sixties itself permitted private trusts to start both engineering and medical colleges. 
True , many of these colleges had a reasonable standard. Still there were two problems. 
First was the quality aspect. It was at a middle level, not improving in most colleges. There was no serious effort by the universities or colleges or faculty to find new avenues to improve the quality. If I say that IISc does this, they were diffident and say they can’t do it. The faculty invariably blamed the students . They said that their students are useless. I used to tell them that most colleges get 15% or more of distinction students. I said the college didn't do much for them. They got credit for the college. I also told them that most students can do 10% more than their syllabus. Teachers don't want to do extra work . Some even complained about KSCST projects saying it makes them do more. It was strange. Very senior faculty say they don't want a change in the syllabus. They are happy with the existing syllabus. They don't want to read another text book. This was my constant worry. Today we have NIRF ranking and many institutions want to get into this . It is a good sign. 
The second problem concerned  the evolution of new areas like computer science and communications. We will discuss it in the next post.

“no institution has the wide acceptance to create values and direction for the majority of people. Markets in free societies are rapidly replacing governments and religious institutions as regulators of the highest authority, and markets perform without values; they do not converse in a human tongue. The arts can break new ground here, bringing human consciousness to bear on these flows of product and capital, energizing our interpersonal connections, and opening new doors for invention and practice. 
 Revolutionary shifts in the operational structures of our world seem to call for new definitions of who we are and what we are here for.”
Rosalind and Benjamin Zander.  “The art of possibility.”
The second problem is to move towards new disciplines by education institutions. Electricity was a developing area in the 19th century. Electrical engineering became an established discipline in the beginning of the 20th century. IISc started in 1909 with electrical technology as a new discipline, the first in India. In the first half of the 20th century, no new engineering discipline evolved.  But the second half saw major technological changes. Electronics started evolving with vacuum tubes quickly followed by transistors in the 1940s and 50s. Communications also became a new discipline .  While IISc started the ECE department in 1948, other engineering institutions slowly introduced ECE only by mid 70s, about 25 years of time lag. Similar situation existed with computer science . In 1985, no engineering college taught CS as a branch. The existing university system didn't help in introducing new departments.  
It was here that  AICTE came with a great help. It permitted the starting of new departments and programs. This resulted in most existing and new engineering colleges rushing to start CS programs without any faculty. While the US and UK started CS programs in 1965 , we started only by 1988 or later. 
AICTE facilitated a quick development of CS education in the country. It also scaled up the admissions by doubling it. It permitted IT as a separate discipline. That helped. Graduates were available when the demand for IT skilled engineers shot up. By  1998-99, the country produced about 3 lakh engineers. It was a great achievement. Private colleges played a big role in this transformation.  Their role deserves appreciation. Government institutions won't be able to  meet this increasing demand. Government didn't spend any money on building engineering colleges , private capital helped. 
These were the reasons I played a good role in AICTE inspections and saw CS culture grow in the south. 

Nagaraja has a question:
“Good morning sir. What is "NIRF" ranking? Please expand NIRF.
Thanks, with regards HSN”
I was talking about quality in education. The government of India took steps to improve the quality of engineering education first by making approval by AICTE mandatory. This was followed by the setting up of an accreditation body , NBA- National Board of  Accreditation similar to ABET. It made NBA accreditation mandatory. NBA used a rigorous method for evaluation. I was involved with the NBA evaluation  process for over a decade. The NBA was set up in 1994. 
Accreditation is a process of quality assurance and improvement, whereby a program is critically appraised to verify that the program continues to meet and / or exceeds the norms and standards prescribed by the regulatory authority from time to time.

NBA does not accredit institutions, instead, it accredits the programs offered by technical / professional institutions which have graduated at least two batches. 
Later many agencies came up with ranking of institutions and universities. Indian institutions got low scores in the ranking conducted by international agencies. So IITs refused to participate in that ranking. So the government in 2015 decided to rank institutions. It set up the NIRF- national institutional ranking framework-. It is going on every year ranking institutions in the country. 
IISc got first rank in university category all these years. Since we didn't have a BE program, we didn't get the first rank in engineering. It went to IIT Madras.

“Standard social and business practices are built on certain assumptions—shared understandings that have evolved from older beliefs and conditions. And while circumstances may have changed since the start of these practices, their continued use tends to reconfirm the old beliefs. For this reason our daily practices feel right and true to us, regardless of whether they have evolved to keep up with the pace of change. In just such a way a business culture arises and perpetuates itself, perhaps long after its usefulness has passed.

Let me continue with why I participated in AICTE inspections.  Another set of reasons that I joined the inspection teams is the fact that  my awareness on people and engineering education shot up because of these visits and meetings with managements.. 


These visits opened my eyes. I understood the state of technical education in many colleges. The dearth of faculty was striking. Most colleges didn't have enough qualified and interested faculty. Also managements didn't want to spend money of facilities. They don't mind spending money on buildings but not on equipments, salaries and consumables. Many faculty jumped jobs , moving from one college to another. Many principals were retired faculty from other colleges. That was OK with me because they were sincere , knowledgeable and honest. But they didn't have freedom to work and spend. I believe that principals can build institutions into a high quality and  reputed ones. Some of  the names of transformative principals  that come to my mind are Prof Prahlad Rao at MSRIT, Prof Holla at RV college of engineering,  Prof MN Channabasappa at SIT Tumkur, Prof KA Krishnamurthy at MCE and SSIT, Prof Viswanath at Gogte Institute of technology, Prof Ashok Shetter at BVB CE, and Prof Vrushabendrappa at BIET. 

The second aspect is the types of people I was meeting. Previously, I used to meet faculty only whenever I visited an engineering college. But now I meet Many management people. Most were tense. Some were antagonistic. Some were showing their power  both political and muscle. It was a front seat view of different kinds of humanity. Most felt we were their enemies. Only a very few appreciated our work. The names of people who appreciated the committee are Mr Rangarajan, Veltech engineering college, Fr Alex , Rajgiri institutions, Mr Vinay Hegde NMAMIT,  Nitte and a few more whose names I had forgotten. 
Many pleasant and unpleasant events happened during inspections including one headon car accident and a court case. AICTE officers run away when problems occur.

“The learning itself may produce bad or good habits. There is a competitive plasticity in the brain to accommodate learnings of the individual. This also teaches us that not only is learning embedded in the brain, but that the brain itself is learning to learn! Amazing, isn’t it? Thus, it is our habit and desire to learn, to be curious, that influences brain plasticity.”
Gopalakrishnan Tatas.  Innovators 

I spent my life in the field of teaching , research, approvals, accreditation , curriculum development . Hence it is very appropriate to talk about the education scene in india. We discussed the Indian computer and IT scene a few times. 
I was thinking of writing about this for quite some time.
 Dr Gopalakrishna , director , Integra micro systems andcvice president FAER, sent me a detailed email asking my response to education, growth of technologies, use of tools in education, their impacts and their effects. It came at a very appropriate time. 

So  let me give my understandings on the  education scene in india for the past 75 years  and how it helped in the transformation of the country. I will also discuss the  impact of tools on education based on my experiences in teaching and lecturing in IISc, IIT, IIM and several hundred  universities, colleges , schools and industries for over 50 plus years . 
I have been seeing the effect of tools even from my school days . I also wondered about our indian  teachers and students ignorance of tools and their utility. Education, its purpose, teaching and use of teaching aids and tools were intuitively understood by people , not explicitly understood with clarity. This led to a lot of misunderstandings, wrong expectations.
We have to keep in mind the fact that India has been lagging in adapting technologies by decades in general. Further our education system is rigid with monetary constraints.  Research on education and teaching is minimal in Indian universities. School education is buffeted by fixed mind sets, ideologies and politics. We destroyed the long traditional gurukula system . The gurukula system existed in all villages, it permiated all layers from abstract knowledge, scriptures, to arts to artisan education. It handled a large number of disciplines and allowed for deep learning.  Macaulay destroyed it in one stroke. So we moved from a holistic spiritual and utilitarian system of education to a clerical training system.Let us keep these in mind and we should not feel that I make negative statements. 
Let me start with education first before going to teaching. 
My first question on education is
What are the purposes of education as I understood from my exposure and experience?
Education is supposed to have several purposes.
1. Improve visual, oral and language cognition.
2. Build capabilities for concentration, questioning , contemplation and focussing.  practice them.
3.Build beliefs, behaviours, etiquette, values , concerns for society, order, governance ,  nature, environment,  etc.
4.Build mindsets.
5.Provide for creating capabilities for  holistic approaches to life's many problems -  includes personal , family , job and society aspects of life- , impacts, multiple views,  analysis, problem solving , applications and innovation.
6. Provide holistic knowledge and skills .
7. Understand various aspects of life and living with interconnections to dynamic changes in nature, society, technologies etc.

Most teachers and parents and educationists  think that education is supposed to do only knowledge transfer. Is that premise correct? Does that thinking   need corrections ?
While talking about education, we refer  to formal education and ignore other aspects. We expect children to learn all the above listed life saving aspects only by intuitive approaches. Is it a good strategy?
Who will provide the missing purposes?

“Prof. Anjan Bose, who is a regents professor of power engineering at the School of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science at Washington State University, confirmed that age is no impediment to new learning, though motivation and determination can become impediments.
This view contrasts with the Silicon Valley obsession with young people, epitomized by Mark Zuckerberg’s comment at Stanford University in 2007: ‘Young people are just smarter.’ Author Pagan Kennedy writes, ‘There is evidence to suggest that late blooming is no anomaly,’ quoting a 2016 study that shows that ‘inventors peak in their late 40s and tend to be productive in the latter half of their careers’. Nobel winners apparently make their discoveries at an average age of fifty.”
Gopalakrishnan.      Innovators 

There are a lot of comments. I am happy and will post them later. Thanks for your interest. 
Education is a vast area of research , practices and reforms. 
I want to clarify my intentions to write these posts. It is not to find fault with our education system , our schooling , our teachers etc. Actually, I want to share my thinking about what is learning, how to learn, what is the teachers role , how tools helped in education and some possibilities of reforms and guidelines.  as well as clear some doubts. 
 Now to my second question :
     2.Is there a good , but not mandatory , sequence of learning?
The answer seems to be yes. 
First we understand how humans develop. 
A working body with a partially filled brain is the child. Throughout the life the mind develops, filled with a lot of learnings through perceptions, intuitive cognition, experiences , education , careers etc. A child starts developing habits , values , understanding of life and beliefs. It fills it's mind with thousands of beliefs. 
There is prelearning process. 
Most educationists agree that our mind is designed to learn. That is why you see many get a good learning despite the poor educational system. The 50% school dropouts that is happening  gives us another side of the  story. The reasons for dropouts are
Economic conditions
No relevance of school education 
No interest in learning
Let us look at the No interest condition. 
Many can't concentrate. They can’t sit for hours listening to teachers. They can’t calm and focus their mind. 
So such people need a first step of aided learning, not self learning. 
This comes through yoga and meditation. So that should be learnt at the young age preferably. 
Let me talk of my experience which is true for many. I didn't learn yoga and meditation formally in a school. But from the age of three, I was involved in prayers , sitting still, listening to slokas carefully etc  Later I saw and heard about meditation. These prayers and sitting still and focusing the mind on god helped me to get a good and prolonged  concentration and focussing. It created an interest in learning. 
Similarly, values, and conduct need to be learnt in the young growing age. 
Something a child starts learning is using it's senses leading to perception. Aural, touch and sight perceptions start developing. But interpretations of these perceptions are taught by parents and family. This and practises at home and the neighbourhood build our beliefs. We live by beliefs. 
So most of these first stage of learning are Non formal, happens at home and society. Here ambience plays a role. 
Sankaracharya felt that one basic learning is viveka - discriminatory learning. The question is how do we learn viveka. Olden days learning through puranas, ithihasas, panchatantra and hitopadesa made children to think and ponder and build viveka. 
Children by age start questioning everything. They pickup language skills at home. 
Viveka leads to unlearning of bad habits, bad manners, bad beliefs and misunderstood theories. This aspect of unlearning is mostly intuitive today. 
We accumulate beliefs on what is good, what is bad, what is right or wrong, what is true or false, what to do and what not to do, how to speak , etc. Some beliefs need corrections. Even some science learnings need correction. So unlearning and unlearning are as important as learning. They help one to develop viveka. Story telling by grandmas is minimal now. Youtube won't be able to replace that as it doesn't have viveka. Other  aspects like analysis, knowledge etc  are learnt later. We will look at that in the next post.. 
Greetings
Dks
16/09/24, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 479
“Lectures at Nalanda were given in a hundred different halls each day, Xuang Sang , Chinese traveller to India wrote, ‘and the students studied diligently without wasting a single moment’. He described the lecture halls, the stupa relic mound, five temples and 300 apartments and dormitories which housed the 10,000 monks and international scholars who gathered there.3 Between them they studied the texts of the different schools of Buddhism, as well as the sacred Vedas, logic, Sanskrit grammar, philosophy, medicine, metaphysics, divination, mathematics, astronomy, literature and magic. “
Dalrymple.  The golden road 
My third question in my understanding of education:
The focus here starts with the second stage of learning - mostly at the school going age.
What are the mechanisms  of education?
Sources and learning methods are multidimensional,  not unidirectional. One may get correct knowledge or erroneous knowledge,  good for values or bad or indifferent.  
1. Parental , neighbours and societal guidance and hand holding in the beginning to build values, habits, etiquette. This continues from childhood.
2. Development of good habits of reading, writing, questioning, etc - partly self learning, partly observations, partly instruction by elders and older children. Questioning is the first trait a child picks up. By age four, it keeps asking why and what on everything. But afterwards, we kill it. If this habit is encouraged, we will have a different kind of students , creative and innovative. 
3. Discussions with friends in the school or outside . Learn good communications capability and observations of behaviours , and manners. This is important even for studying and understanding school subjects. Many gain from peer learning. Group learning is another method. I have seen the effectiveness of peer learning not only from my school experience,  but from that of my students. One name is very striking in my memory. It is vadiraj Bhatt,  a student of NMAMIT,  Nitte whom I taught two subjects. Most students were mediocre in the class. I saw him explaining and teaching other students. Most will crowd around him. Later we experimented this with a formal student educator for Fluid mechanics at SIT Tumkur. The results were great. It motivated the other students.
4. Reading books, a very important habit missing even amongst our teachers. I talked to about 160 faculty in an engineering college individually about teaching and research. One question I asked each one is what books you read? The answer was striking. No one read any book. 
5. Learning from society through newspapers, general books, youtube, internet etc. . All these provide general knowledge. But it can be dangerous. It makes one  biased and builds hatred. One can become a slave . Here viveka is a necessary requirement for all users. My grandson used to watch TV and youtube from a young age of five. Some asked me “ is it good,? I found his curiosity and viveka were and are great. He wasn't influenced by them. He got relevant information, analysed them carefully and saved them. It was remarkable. I told many , even seniors, to listen to youtube talks. 
6.Learning from the ambience of the school , college, office.
7. Using libraries, labs 
8. Learning from mentors
9. Learning from class room teaching. This is primarily school education. Regimental learning helps in learning basic language skills and arithmetic initially. Later we get more knowledge in schools and colleges. But it is partial , text bookish, maybe incorrect and not fully understood. Here supplementary forms of learning from reading many books and intelligent boys and girls will help .
10. Keen observations and experimentation. 
11. Learning through self exercises like problem solving, assignments, experiments, literature survey and critique, project work 
12. Learning to use tools and use tools extensively
13. Time management , mind management, meditation, maintaining health etc.
13. Self learning. This is emphasized in upanishads as swadhyaya.
All the above constitute learning and education , not class room teaching alone. 

Will these learnings help a student to understand life, to know one's capabilities, weaknesses, attitudes and aptitudes?
 Answer is a big and loud no. These help ,may be, in  getting jobs and knowing the state of the people. There are gaps in the education processes  of a person.
Spiritual and moral learnings are ignored everywhere. Materialistic learning is dominant . Is this a good thing for a country where Rig veda talks of giving, sharing, ahimsa, peace, truth and order?. 
It is here that the attitude of teachers matters a lot. Many have negative attitudes. They tell students that they are useless. Exams ,I consider, are unjust capital punishments.
 Remember that in this stage the teacher is the main pivot. Still the significance of others is equally important. Ambience is a good accelerator for learning. 
Greetings
Dks

“Despite—or perhaps because of—our hoard of data, we are continuing to spew greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, pollute rivers and oceans, cut down forests, destroy entire habitats, drive countless species to extinction, and jeopardize the ecological foundations of our own species. We are also producing ever more powerful weapons of mass destruction, from thermonuclear bombs to doomsday viruses. Our leaders don’t lack information about these dangers, yet instead of collaborating to find solutions, they are edging closer to a global war.”
Yuval Harari    Nexus 


Let me pose the fourth  question :
What is the impact of formal education in india. ?
Provides a good foundation for STEM education. 
Allows an opportunity to all to get school education and college education. 
There is a concern to improve the quality of education.
Midday meals is a good scheme. 
 Focus is on examinations. 
Teaches mostly old concepts 
Builds a biased, divisive,  fixed ,non questioning mindset.
Mostly rote learning. But  rote learning is useful. But what about understanding? . Cognition is not good. 
Treats all students  at the same level. This affects both the bright and mediocre and leads to large failures. 
Builds wrong and rigid beliefs and mindset. Still tradition helps. Family bonding is reasonable. The culture helps many students. 
Provides partial knowledge . Most find English and mathematics difficult. Social studies and history are not correctly built. 
Visual communications in most cases and oral communications in some cases are poor. 
Many teachers are  with limited knowledge .
Intuition is recognised. This is a big credit. 
Purposes of learning and teaching not clear. 
Use of tools is minimal. 
Tools are ignored , neglected. 
Practicals and practices were ignored. Focus is on theory. 
Community, nature concerns are ignored, economy has the primacy. 
Technologies taught  are not the latest. 
Here is where reforms like NEP come in . But most reforms are good in theory and bad in implementation. 
That is a general averaged picture. Let me look at the shining stars and unsung heroes.
There are a good number of high quality institutions like IISc,  IITs,  IISERs, ISEC, and now NITs and many private institutions who strive for excellence.  Kendriya vidyalayas , navodaya play a good role in providing good education. Government of india has played a significant role here. 
Many private schools hundreds in number experiment with education. They had produced great artists and linguists. 
I have seen many schools experimenting a combination of the gurukula concept and modern school system to provide a dual education - values and traditions at one end and traditions at the other end. 
Similarly, there are thousands of unsung teachers who and knowledgeable and great teachers and they obtained  credit to their schools and colleges. I know several. We have ignored them. Even in the midst of the current situation of politics, commercialisation and low commitments, I  see many teachers in private and government institutions  working sincerely , learning every day, taking care of their students, improving their teaching methods, imparting a correct version of knowledge and building labs. They are idealistic, not greedy and very helpful to the cause of education. We ignore them. 
Otherwise, how do you explain the many scientists and engineers occupying senior positions in the developed countries. It is due to these ideal teachers.  Indians are the only persons from a developed country who built these nations. Family traditions , ambience , motivation from teachers and formal education produced these scholars and business man. 
But unfortunately we didn't produce Nobel Prize winners but for Tagore and CV Raman, and we didn't produce great innovators . After Sir MV we didn't have civil engineers of that caliber.  Why ? 
We are looked down by others and our country men and hence we  loose confidence.
 We need to be motivated by the positives and to improve ourselves by looking at the negatives.
Viveka means be positive, be aware of pitfalls, choose  the positives , avoid the negatives, listen to your inner voice  , analyse and  do things in a fair and just manner completely and correctly and be wise. 
Let me look at the role of a teacher next  - is it only class room lecturing?

“There exist transformational—practices that may “feel” illogical or counterintuitive to our normal understanding of how things operate. Their purpose is to initiate a new approach to current conditions, based on uncommon assumptions about the nature of the world. The history of transformational phenomena—the Internet, for example, or paradigm shifts in science, or the spread of a new religion—suggests that transformation happens less by arguing cogently for something new than by generating active, ongoing practices that shift a culture’s experience of the basis for reality.”
Zander   Art of possibilities

“In accordance with the three modes of material nature, there are three kinds of knowledge, action, and performers of action. Listen as I describe them.
That knowledge by which one undivided spiritual nature is seen in all existences, undivided in the divided, is knowledge in the mode of goodness.
That knowledge by which a different type of living entity is seen to be dwelling in different bodies is knowledge in the mode of passion.
And that knowledge by which one is attached to one kind of work as the all in all, without knowledge of the truth, and which is very meager, is said to be in the mode of darkness.
Chapter 18 - 19 to 22 verses
Bhagavad Gita. 

We can infer from the  comments posted in post 481 that education is a big subject, it affects everyone, everyone has an experience and exposure to education , everyone knows a lot about education and all views are not identical. That is the reason why I titled this section of posts as my understanding about education.  It may be even totally  opposite of others views and understandings. Let us realise that. I respect all opinions. 
Let  us look at the teacher's role. 
To understand the role of a teacher , we need to know the focus of teaching and cognition  and so we need  to ask a number of  queries:
   1.What is learning?
Gaining knowledge through senses, observations,Interpretations, experiences, discussions, understanding and absorption and later using those facts we learnt on many occasions in our life. Next one learns analysis methods, experimentation rules, formalism,  and problem solving approaches . As we saw earlier, we learn in many ways - from perceptions and observations, parents, family members , friends, society,  offices , experiences ,internal contemplation, internal assimilation,  and through formal learning in schools, colleges etc. We absorb all facts and methods and later apply them.
 Bloom categorises learning processes into 
the six aspects of learning, Remembering, Understanding, Applying, Analyzing, Evaluating, and Creating. 
      2.How does a child start learning? Is it born with some data and processing capabilities? 
A child is born with some  tools containing  some knowledge about survival and some actions and a learning and absorbing mechanism . Then it starts observations - by sensing, by crying, by hearing and later by seeing. 
It starts   building good data acquisition, interpretation  and processing capabilities.  Then it starts aping others. It is the process of learning actions. It accepts instructions from parents and follows them. That is followed by questioning to know each and every object in the house and outside. It builds a great  level of curiosity. So the initial five years of childhood is an intense period of learning. It develops cognitive - understanding- ability . It  starts picking up a language simultaneously. It apes and emulates others.  So contrary to our belief, the first five years are a period of intense learning , building many characteristics in a child like attention, curiosity, questioning, understanding, concentration, discipline, obedience,  habits , and behaviours. 
       3.Do these vary with each child? 
It seems that  many aspects are learnt  autonomously by all children  , but many other aspects  are learnt with inputs and stimulation from parents and others. So in some aspects , most children are the same, but in some others , they are different. The initial seeds may be different and that also matters. 
        4. Can all children pick up capabilities ? Looks like yes. 
         5.  Then why do there exist unequal levels of knowledge ? 
It is due to the differences in  ambience, habits, beliefs, family circumstances and many other factors. 
       6.What helps in learning ? 
Parental help is the initial acceleration factor. This is followed by others. But the most important aspect of learning is the attention and response from parents and others in the house including siblings. If their responses are not encouraging, the child will take longer to learn. 
    How does a person learn?
We have seen this before. 
      7. How does a person build the requisite  capabilities to learn?
This is the pre learning we talked about - concentration, questioning, contemplation, sharp observations , interpretations etc. 
     8. Are people unequal in their capabilities to learn?
This seems to be true. But they can learn and improve their capabilities. Learning period varies .

“It is because we don't know who we are, because we are unaware that the Kingdom of Heaven is within us, that we behave in the generally silly, the often insane, the sometimes criminal ways that are so characteristically human. We are saved, we are liberated and enlightened, by perceiving the hitherto unperceived good that is already within us, by returning to our eternal ground and remaining where, without knowing it, we have always been.”

Aldous Huxley

Let us continue with the queries. 
What is knowledge?
Knowledge is a collection of facts, data, procedures, concepts, principles ,actions, processes, solution methods etc.  obtained from many sources like reading books, talking to people, listening to talks and lectures, reciting, classroom lectures , from job, from experiences etc. Facts may be true or false .
Bloom’s Taxonomy is situated in the four types of knowledge:1. Factual Knowledge of terminology and details, 2. Conceptual Knowledge of relationships among pieces of concepts or theories,3. Procedural Knowledge of processes and methods of theories and problems, and 4. Metacognitive Knowledge of learning strategies and processes.
Engineering needs additional types of knowledge : 1.visual knowledge 2. Imaginative knowledge  3. Design knowledge 4. Analysis and detailing 5. Experiential 6. Professional 7. Societal and users  knowledge 8. Innovations and applications knowledge
Computer science looks for algorithmic knowledge , meta knowledge about thinking and intelligence in addition. 

Who creates the additional knowledge? 
So many people - teachers, researchers, professionals, and even normal people - create knowledge ,but  a good part is fake. 

Is knowledge full of facts or fiction or biases, or opinions or false?
Yes. It is tough today with social networks. So our discrimination should help us  in resolving this. 
How do you use and apply knowledge ? How do you use discrimination - between good and bad , between correct and incorrect, between yes and no, between logical and illogical ?
This is not easy to answer. There are many ways like learning, talking to others, reading, analysing , using tools, searching the web , internal meditation , etc. A lot of practice is needed here. 
What are the methods used to analyze a given set of facts?  How do we get a specific set of facts for an analysis? Does an analysis depend only on the given or chosen set of facts or will depend on other facts also? 
 We need to  ponder about the need for the following traits: 
Move out of the frog in the well approach - 90% do that.
Have multiple views like birds eye views, worms views. 
Assume that  multiple solutions are possible. 
Look for external impacts. 

Should we focus on only known formal methods of analysis like mathematical, logical or scientific axioms based or extend it to practical, anecdotal, experiential, intuitive, heuristic, or follow the vedantic principles as enshrined in uttara mimamsa? 
Today we are in the unfortunate situation of the dominance of formalism. Engineering was descriptive in the fifties ,but has become mathematical now. Computer science was descriptive in the beginning , and merges algorithms with design and concepts. Programming is partly methodologies, partly algorithms. This needs a discussion to reduce biases and with AI, there is an urgent need for alternate strategies. 
Is wisdom dependent on knowledge or perceptions or discrimination called viveka  or actions or analysis, or  thinking or behaviours or a combination of all these? 
Wisdom is a cocktail of one's characters and values, fairness and maturity, judging capabilities, awareness and just decisions. 
Wisdom is a long process sharpened by correct , known and hidden knowledge, experience,  maturity , kindness,  and spirituality. A wise person  starts with an integrated picture of all these components , then arrives at a mature decision and based on various scenarios of the decisions and an analysis, he or she provides advice to others . Many times , lots of internal processes like intuition play a great role in wisdom. A wise person has good control of his emotions, mind , body and habits . That is the first requirement. A wise person is known by his actions and acceptance by people and is governed by fairness.
Can we ignore human emotions , beliefs and behaviours in performing formal analysis?
What is understanding and how does one understand ?
Bloom's  verbs for understanding
Classify, Confirm, Contrast, Convert, Decipher, Defend, Designate, Differentiate, Equate, Estimate, Examine, Express, Extend, Extrapolate, Generalize, Give Examples, Group, Infer, Interpret, Order, Paraphrase, Predict, Rephrase, Rewrite, Sort, Specify, Substitute, Tell, Translate.

21/09/24, 4:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 484

“From the fact that I know that I exist, and that at the same time I judge that obviously nothing else belongs to my nature or essence except that I am a thinking thing, I rightly conclude that my essence consists entirely in my being a thinking thing. And although perhaps . . . I have a body that is very closely joined to me, nevertheless, because on the one hand I have a clear and distinct idea of myself, insofar as I am merely a thinking thing and not an extended thing, and because on the other hand I have a distinct idea of a body, insofar as it is merely an extended thing and not a thinking thing, it is certain that I am really distinct from my body, and can exist without it. “          Descartes 

Should a teacher know the answers to the above questions before he becomes a teacher? 
It is desirable to understand these dimensions to become a good teacher. 
I have been mulling over these questions for a major part of my life.  I should say that there are many answers to these questions, not always one correct answer and many are contradictory. Also these are not questions only for philosophers and cognition science researchers. The solutions are many , a solution will work for some and may not work for another. Some answers affect us teachers. Some worry us. Some make us change ourselves and our teaching and improve our teaching. 
Let me start from the teacher's end - what is  her role?
The main focus of a teacher is to improve the cognition of a student in a discipline and also in a holistic manner. So cognition is the pivot on which a teacher should base his or her work. So let us look at cognition next. 
What is cognition?
Cognition is associated with the mind . It deals with knowledge, it deals with the processes of acquiring knowledge in several ways. It deals with verifying the correctness of facts .It deals with understanding the meaning of knowledge through perceptions, imaginations, interpretations, thinking, discussing , analysing and grouping and categorizing the  acquired knowledge through certain characteristics of facts and with the help of logic . It deals with creating a framework of connecting  facts and building a huge network. It deals with many methods of processing knowledge. It produces new facts , this is crucial. 
It refers to deep understanding in all aspects. It refers to the continuous development of mind. It sharpens the mind , Improves the actions of the mind in taking decisions and creates a better awareness of universe and life. 


Cognition is a term for the mental processes that take place in the brain, including thinking, attention, language, learning, memory and perception. These processes are not discrete abilities – they are a raft of different, interacting skills which together allow us to function as healthy and intelligent adults.
Examples of cognition include paying attention to something in the environment, learning something new, making decisions, processing language, sensing and perceiving environmental stimuli, solving problems, and using memory. 
Cognitive processes help us in improving our capability to process our known  knowledge , acquire new knowledge through several ways of learning and even try to look for unknowns. While nature didn't provide borders to cognition, we built barriers and disciplines hoping to bring in order and simplicity. 
Cognition, knowledge, communications , human mind  and learning are related. 
Cognitive science is the interdisciplinary study of mind and intelligence, embracing philosophy, psychology, artificial intelligence, neuroscience, linguistics, and anthropology. Its intellectual origins are in the mid-1950s when researchers in several fields began to develop theories of the mind based on complex representations and computational procedures.
Students face many problems in their college life. Solving such problems requires doing things with mental representations, such as reasoning that you still need five more courses to graduate, or deciding never to take another course from Professor Tedium. Cognitive science proposes that people have mental procedures that operate on mental representations to produce thought and action. Different kinds of mental representations such as rules and concepts foster different kinds of mental procedures.

More in the next.

22/09/24, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 485
“The sciences have developed in an order the reverse of what might have been expected. What was most remote from ourselves was first brought under the domain of law, and then, gradually, what was nearer: first the heavens, next the earth, then animal and vegetable life, then the human body, and last of all (as yet very imperfectly) the human mind. “
  Bertrand Russell, 1935

Let me expand my understanding of Cognition from a teacher's perspective. I describe below the many stages of cognition and also how teachers may help students to reach that stage.  It  includes the following stages:
1.Understand the principles , categories, characteristics , objects and definitions . Explain and interpret an observation . Many concepts and definitions need much better explanation. Most teachers  don't explain the second law of thermodynamics in detail. Let me give a simple example for a clear and distinctive definition. Whatapp uses clear and delete to remove contents. What is the difference? These two aspects  - concepts and definitions - are key to understanding. Knowledge construction starts with objects, classes,relationships and principles governing those objects. Intuitive understanding is not enough. 
Explanations can be simple or complicated or abstract, can be  mathematical or even heuristic. It should kindle the curiosity of the learner to go deeper.  
I was a party to one useful practice followed by AIT Bangkok when I taught there. Copies of all reference books  and all lecture ppt copies should be sent to the library in advance so the students get an idea of the lecture, prepare them for the lecture and not put up a blank face and mind. 
2.Understand the significance, surrounding theory . Use additional facts, give many examples. Create interest and awareness. Use tools, visualisation, experimentation, analogies, computer simulation . Visualisation was experienced by me during my school days. Our teacher did a simple experiment to show how air is needed for combustion, how carbon dioxide is produced after combustion.  Many more demos were done during my  school days particularly with my science studies. Many demos are staying with me vividly even now.  These were very effective in understanding. Similarly , my mechanical engineering teacher took several hours before a class drawing in detail a diagram of the device , boiler or engine to be discussed in the class. Prof AR Vasudevamurthy taught us materials science and he used the epidiascope to show us chemical structures .  This was before over head projectors came into use. 
Communication is important here for correct transfer of knowledge from teacher to student. For instance , we need to differentiate between  an isolator ,a load break switch and a circuit breaker in electrical engineering, between a hub, a switch and a router in networks and between a job, task, process and thread in operating systems. Language comes in here to play an important role. Follow Panini - who says the purpose of communications is to transfer the thoughts of the teacher to the mind of students without  error or loss of data and with clarity and without ambiguities. A language should follow this principle. Remember English is an ambiguous language with few cases based grammar.  So explain such sentences.    This becomes the first level of guidance for a teacher. Two aspects are important to follow. One, a  Teacher speaks slowly, precisely and clearly so the students receive the information correctly  and absorb it in their mind. He or she uses simple sentences. Secondly, the  teacher gives correct , complete and holistic knowledge to the students. This is the first basic requirement of a teacher and a learning process.
3.Understand and prove the validity of the fact. This is tough today. When I learnt social studies in high school, the teacher's views didn't coincide with ours and also the book's views. His bias was visible.
 Fake information is rampant now. I find many textbooks have errors. Some faculty were writing books on programming languages and they were lots of errors in them. 
4. Understand how to do analysis based on the fact or principle. This can be mathematical or statistical or logical or experiential or approximated or modelled or simulated . It can be simple or complex. One uses many techniques and algorithms here. This is the analysis stage. 
Next is the application stage to be seen in the next post.
Greetings
Dks
23/09/24, 4:31 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 486
“We think of trust as precious, and yet it’s the basis for almost everything we do as civilized people. Trust is the reason we’re willing to exchange our hard-earned paychecks for goods and services, pledge our lives to another person in marriage, cast a ballot for someone who will represent our interests. We rely on laws and contracts as safety nets, but even they are ultimately built on trust in the institutions that enforce them. We don’t know that justice will be served if something goes wrong, but we have enough faith in the system that we’re willing to make high-stakes deals with relative strangers.
 Trust is also one of the most essential forms of capital a leader has. Building trust, however, often requires thinking about leadership from a new perspective.”
Frei and Morriss.   HBR - 10 best on trust

    5.Understand additional about the principle or object under discussions like - 1. where it can be applied,2. how it affects other elements, 3.how it affects nature, and natural resources and environment,4. is it beneficial for humans, animals, insects and plants,      5.does it help the poor, 6. how does it impact with economy, 7.how to use it, 8.what are the sub principles that can be derived from this ,9. how is it related to other principles and objects, 10. what are the overall effects,          11.what are  the future directions, 12.what are the unsolved problems etc. Also look at the linkage of this principle to other principles. So in short, prepare a checklist for every principle. 
That will help a student to get 360°views.  It will clarify quite a number of  doubts and create a better cognition. This will motivate students, if told in a story form or told as a sequence of anecdotes and events that happened. 
My science teacher in the school took us to a factory nearby to show how air bale of cotton is compressed by a hydraulic Brahma press. It showed us how energy comes from water. During my 7th standard, I visited a hydro power plant at Papanasam. These two created interest in me in engineering, electrical engineering and energy. That showed me one direction - to go for engineering education, the first in my family. 
My physics professor Mr Ramaswamy at TCE Madurai used to discuss many of the 12  aspects mentioned above. He talked about transistors when we had only vacuum tubes in the lab and our radios were built with vacuum tubes.  His discussions in the class  in 1960 and 1961  made me look at electronics and computers with great interest. That motivated me and started my journey into computers. There were no computers in India then. Similarly , I was interested in economics and planning in 1960 and wrote my first article on planning in the college magazine. That was due to listening to a  talk by Schumacher followed by very intense discussions with many including Mr  V Sankaran, MLA of Madurai then.  I still cherish the daily lunch time discussions on many topics like the future of engineering, our trends in industrialization etc with Prof Krishna Kutty Menon. This habit started from the first year of my engineering. I learnt a lot in these discussions. I got a lot of interest in many areas and started reading more books. If I look back, those discussions were my foundations of scholarship and learning. 
Let me give an example of deep learning. Let us  say that  we  are talking about a tree. You should list out the benefits. Ask the students to calculate the amount of oxygen it will generate for say about 50 years. Compare it with oxygen production costs in a factory. Similarly ,calculate carbon credits, water storage , soil enrichment and cost them. A cost of a tree will be more than 3 million rupees. Students will understand this better. Build a concept of a forest on top of this and include biodiversity also. You also make the students feel that it is disastrous to treat natural resources of negligible cost or free. Economists do that. See the impact , you would have strongly imprinted the overall aspects of a tree and forest.  You may next look at the number of trees cut per year purely for firewood used in cooking. I used to say that everyone consumed a tree per year. It hits you hard. 
The methodology for holistic understanding  is to pose a series of questions. The questions should ignite their curiosity. You will understand their thinking processes and thereby your students better. 
 I used to ask a few questions in each lecture. It was useful. I knew whether students understood the topic, whether they could think and answer a deeper question and how to plan and  continue with the next lecture. 


Next is the problem solving stage. Students need to pick up tools and techniques required for problem solving and apply them. 

       6. The next step in cognition is to apply the acquired knowledge  to solve problems . There are hundreds of problems occurring in our life and a student should be able to see whether and where a particular  principle can be applied and what is an appropriate principle - by searching. A teacher  should accept non formal methods also. Appreciate if your students come with another approach. I used to ask a question on concurrency handling by the operating system of a computer and discuss some ways of doing it. If a student came up with an alternate solution , I gave him bonus marks - about 10% in the exam. That is encouragement. Let me also discuss a case of discouragement. 
A BE student living in the campus came to me one day with a problem in programming. He said that the teacher gave one solution. He tried another approach. He asked whether his approach is correct. I went through his algorithm and said it is correct. After some months , he came and told me that  he got zero marks as his answer was scored wrong by his teacher. I have come across  several such incidents including a few involving my children.  Most teachers don't accept the basic axiom that there are several possible solution methods for a problem. For them, the textbook is the Bible, nothing exists beyond that textbook. Teachers need an open mind. 
This understanding is crucial for engineers , scientists and even managers.

24/09/24, 3:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 487
How do leaders build trust? By focusing on its core drivers: authenticity, logic, and empathy. People tend to trust you when they think they’re interacting with the real you, when they have faith in your judgment and competence, and when they believe you care about them.
Frei and Moriss      HBR 

Let us continue further on teacher's role in providing knowledge and improving cognition.
     7.Look deep and find inner meaning in the  observations made by you everyday  - there are many  hidden facts out there in nature, in people, in consumption, in the society, in behaviours , in governance, etc . As they say, “ God is there everywhere in a small twig or in a pillar “, I feel that knowledge is there everywhere , we need to observe it and feel it and derive new knowledge. 
For example we know that  Prof. CV Raman observed that sea color was blue or black, a simple observation. But he spent days and nights on this , conducted simple experiments and came up with the fact that light is not a ray of a single frequency , but it is a lot  pipes   consisting of radiations of several frequencies and color is represented by frequencies. When light rays are bent  in a prism,  you see some dominant colors. He overturned Newton's corpuscular theory and expanded Huygen’s Wave theory.  We can even go further. Upanishads insist on learning about not only the knowns , but also the unknowns. They also look at unknowables according to Swami Vivekananda. 
These pipes of  rays provide not light alone, they carry  energy over millions of kilometers. In addition, they carry conscious knowledge. We use radio waves for communications. We see sound being carried in the atmosphere over a short distance. We receive signals from deep space billions of kilometers away. See their great potential . It helps to understand hard consciousness.
   8. The next step is to try to find answers to many such observations and  questions. Many random events like cyclones can be analysed and explanations found. Many are working on landslides for many years. These approaches will lead to research.  
   9. The next step is innovation. Today's focus for a teacher is less on knowledge transfer and more on clarifications,  discovery, inventions and innovations. 
    10. The next step is detailing , how to design, how to build, how to connect, how to integrate, how to simplify and how to improve the performance. This is engineering.  
   11. Then the next step is to show the principle is wrong , it is not universal, it does not work under some conditions . Even text book statements can be shown wrong. This critique is an essential part of a teacher. If you see the earlier Indian Acharyas, they will take a concept and argue whether it is true or false. We are familiar with advaita and dvaita discussions, but there are thousands of topics discussed. There were many groups of scholars debating throughout their life. 
Let us see the greatest example of a clear minded teacher in two people. One is Yajnavalkya. He didn't accept his Guru teaching Yajur veda. So he created a new form of yajur veda - Sukla Yajur veda, a remarkable achievement. In Brihadaranya upanishad, he followed  the debating methods to explain concepts. He debated with 1000 scholars including a woman to collect a gift of 1000 cows from king Janaka. He also debated with his wife Maitreyi in that upanishad. These are shining examples of critiquing . Sankaracharya also  debated with Mandana mishra for a week. He had even debated with a kapalika , considered to be a lower caste. He debated with Bharati, a woman. He debated on various topics -  from brahman, to the vedanta to the philosophical aspects of truth, to Jnana, Anjana, Maya  and viveka.  to bhakti to ritualism - fight with nambudiris in order to perform the last rites of his mother-, to sanyasam, to sadachara- good conduct -, and even normal life. This shows that such debates were common amongst scholars. There were hundreds of places in India like Pataliputra, Taxila, Benares , Darbhanga , etc where such debates had taken place for thousands of years. Even Mahabharata has many debates including the famous Bhagavad Gita and yaksha samvada. Our education system totally ignores this mode of learning and cognition.
A teacher is as good as the level of cognition he or she has and how effectively he or she transmits that  understanding and thought processes to the students and how he or she  improves the cognition  over years through analysing, thinking, questioning, looking for multiple interpretations etc. 
Good Cognition means having  holistic, correct and deep 360° knowledge . 
You see that there are many levels in cognition, many methods to improve cognition, and many tools. AI tries to understand these aspects and to build machine cognition. Machine learning is the first step. 
Actually cognition was first recognised as a subject of research in the fifties by the famous  AI quartet -Allen Newell, HerbertSimon, Marvin Minsky and McCarthy.  
Cognition was understood by Indian vedantists long ago . It is discussed in upanishads and the Darshana  shastras like Samkhya, Vaiseshika , yoga , nyaya and vedanta.

25/09/24, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 488
“to learn is to progressively form, in silicon and neural circuits alike, an internal model of the outside world. When I walk around a new town, I form a mental map of its layout—a miniature model of its streets and passageways. Likewise, a child who is learning to ride a bike is shaping, in her neural circuits, an unconscious simulation of how
the actions on the pedals and handlebars affect the bike’s stability. Similarly, a computer algorithm learning to recognize faces is acquiring template models of the various possible shapes of eyes, noses, mouths, and their combinations.”
Dehaene.    How to learn?

From our earlier discussions , one thing is clear that teaching is not just a classroom lecturing , without bothering to know whether students' understanding improved or not. 
So cognition needs to be fully understood by a good teacher. 
There are several ways and methods  to improve knowledge transfer and  cognition levels of students to be followed by teachers.  These were developed and practiced by many teachers.  Some methods  are given below. 
Effective communications - clarity, slow speaking, simple Sentences,  repetition, recap. 
Explanation with the help of pictures , demos, experiments, visits to sites, and actual doing by students. 
Check the absorption rates and levels of students and provide extra inputs. This is done by problem solving and applications , designs and experiments. Quizzes also help in this. Ask the students to summarise in a note of one or two pages what they learnt in a week. It is very effective. 
I followed a set of standard practices in teaching like 1.planning the full course, 2. Prepare syllabus and contents and references 3. Read, understand and  Prepare lecture notes 4.plan the assessment types like large programs, problems assignment, term paper , tests etc. 5. Modify teaching based on students' capacities and question them in every lecture. My delivery of talks is good and clear. My writing is clear and visible.   6. Explain definitions carefully. 7. Don't hurry. 8. Set timeliness for subparts of submission 9.prepare a balanced question paper with easy and difficult parts . 10. Grade generously. Don't fail students. I have a very good record here - a few S grades and only 2 F grades in my entire career. 

I normally gave a large programming assignment in the beginning of my course and that too at a time when computing resources were scarce. I made my students write a full assembler program, a small concurrent operating system and a full database application covering ER diagram,  data modeling, storage, implementation but not using any standard DBMS package, and testing with a few queries. All these were good training for my students. I kept some milestones and time limits for stages of work. I announced this in the beginning of the semester itself. This will appear in the final exam as a question. This motivated many.  When i discussed this aspect of large programming exercises in many engineering colleges and NITs , their CS faculty didn't want to follow this practice. We produced small problem solvers,  not engineers.     
 Similarly I introduced time management and process planning in my courses. In my software engineering course , students go through analysis, design , coding , testing stages for their assignment.   I visited many colleges for AICTE inspection and later TEQIP audit/ mentoring or for lectures and used to visit the computer labs. When I saw students programming on the machine directly, used to scold them and said “ please do the analysis and coding on paper, do a dry run and then move to the machine. The situation was that the students got limited machine access of a few hours every week. 
Tutorials and labs are important,  but we just trivialized them. Go for meaningful use of these two components of a course. 
I use the term paper route for the students to learn many aspects - literature search, planning and time management,  structuring a report, report writing, deep learning on a specific topic, building a focus on a topic.  This is a standard practice in all my courses. Some have written research articles. It sharpened the students.
Use visualisation tools. We started with ohp and moved to ppts and later to animations. Use computational tools. Simulation is very useful to understand the behavior and working of a real system. Use python exercises to solve large problems. 

Create  interest in students  through analogies, jokes, examples, experiences, anecdotes and real life situations. It is very effective. 
Create self learning through assignments 
Use the internet and search engines effectively to access information and critique them . 
Conduct discussion sessions. 
Encourage students to listen to online lectures, youtube videos and khan academy lessons . Ask them to discuss it in the class room. 
A Teacher should understand the many categories of imparting education that exist. 
Greetings
Dks

“IN studying the Perennial Philosophy we can begin either at the bottom, with practice and morality; or at the top, with a consideration of  metaphysical truths; or, finally, in the middle, at the focal point where mind and matter, action and thought have their meeting place in human psychology.
The lower gate is that preferred by strictly practical teachers —men who, like Gautama Buddha, have no use for speculation and whose primary concern is to put out in men's hearts the hideous fires of greed, resentment and infatuation. Through the upper gate go those whose vocation it is to think and speculate—the born philosophers and theologians. The middle gate gives entrance to the exponents of what has been called * spiritual religion*—the devout contemplatives of India.”
Aldous Huxley 

There are many categories of people who are  knowledge givers. These are 
Teachers
Researchers
Book writers 
Mentors,
 coaches
Trainers
Guides
Influencers
Guru
Upa adhyapak , adhyapak 
Acharya. 
The differences amongst these categories are 
:    1. the level and width of knowledge acquired by them , - a guru has even futuristic knowledge . He is more of a generalist . He may impart knowledge to many. Most gurus talk about the human systems, the society, one's life complexities, how to live, religious and cultural significances, spirituality, mind and emotions control, happiness etc. He knows humanism , is a selfless and very wise person, not greedy , not interested in worldly wealth, but interested in his disciples. He is not a time dependent person . 
An Acharya has a very large, indepth knowledge , has interests in building a good society by pointing out and if possible removing the evils plaguing the society. He leads a selfless , pious and simple life. 

Whereas a teacher handles many students. He or she is not interested in the personal problems of a student. He or she is highly constrained by time, a defined curriculum, and piecemeal teaching. He or she has knowledge in a small domain. His or her general knowledge and wisdom does not come into teaching. Their personal experiences are hidden and separated from professional experiences. There may not be lots of contact with individual students.  Teaching is a job or profession, a living.  
   2.the areas of knowledge,- a researcher has deep knowledge in a focussed area. A trainer has a lot of both indepth and undefined knowledge in a specific domain. It is highly utilitarian. He can train a student in specific skills, in areas like artisanship, sculpting, arts, games, etc. They are useful in a society. 
    3. the depth and understanding of knowledge,  - As said earlier, it varies from person to person. Many need to learn continuously. 
      4. the ways of acquiring that knowledge, 
This is where we see differences. A guru learns through self realization. A teacher goes through formal education. A researcher learns from peers . A trainer and coach learn from keen observations, and discriminations and through varied experiences.
    5. the level of expertise, the manner of delivery and reaching out to the students, the ways of communication and handholding etc.
     6. A coach is a lot more involved in knowing the capabilities of a person and using it effectively. He should know the defects and failures of a person and apply corrections. He is more closely involved with the people he is coaching. He is dynamically involved with his work. Success is his only goal. It is a tough and intensive job. Successful coaches move from baseball coaching to business coaching. 
     7. What about mentors? Mentors are supposed to be knowledgeable in a general sense, know the world, have good habits, have great interest in the upbringing of their mentees, do not have any monetary interest. The relationship with the mentee is due to a personal bond. They play a great role in the life of their mentees. A mentor may take care of one or two youngster at a time. They know the characteristics , behaviours and habits of their mentees.  They provide guidance and leave a mentee when he or she is neither interested ,nor listening and acting on the advices ,  or think that they  have grown bigger than the mentor. Mentorship is very effective and very useful at a young stage. A mentor can't mold an adult easily. It may lead to conflicts. Mentor mentee rapport is essential. Mentee should realise the importance and knowledge levels and empathy of a mentor. 
Influencers are a new category playing a big role in today's society. They are mostly biased, do not have good ideals and intentions and can destroy children and society. They dominate in youtube, are greedy and destroy the goodness in the society. They even peddle fake information. Even religious influencers have changed and are greedy and highly biased. Most influencers have a very low level of knowledge. It is a big business. We need to guard our children from them as they are becoming a big menace to the society. 

I consider most education dept persons as administrators and don't include here.
