Dks
27/09/24, 5:41 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 490
“If you were asked to name the human brain’s greatest achievement, you might think first of the impressive artifacts it has produced—the Great Pyramid of Giza, the International Space Station, or perhaps the Golden Gate Bridge. These are great achievements indeed, and our brains deserve their very own ticker-tape parade for producing them. But they are not the greatest. A sophisticated machine could design and build any one of these things because designing and building require knowledge, logic, and patience, of which sophisticated machines have plenty. In fact, there’s really only one achievement so remarkable that even the most sophisticated machine cannot pretend to have accomplished it, and that achievement is conscious experience. Seeing the Great Pyramid or remembering the Golden Gate or imagining the Space Station are far more remarkable acts than is building any one of them. What’s more, one of these remarkable acts is even more remarkable than the others.”
Daniel Gilbert stumbling on happiness 

Let me pose the questions of Dr Gopi on tools. 
“We have seen a lot of technologies and related tools in the last 2-3 decades including the Internet, Email, Mobile phones, tools like PowerPoint, Course material for distance learning, availability of online courses etc. We are going to see a lot more AI-influenced tools/products in the next 2-3 years. As a Professor involved in teaching and Research at a premier Institute, did you see a multiplier effect in terms of the quality (of outgoing students) and the quantity ( like the number of research students a Prof can handle) these tools/technologies could produce? Going further, how do you think AI and related tools (LLMs etc) can help teachers in general and educators involved in Tech education in particular?

A related question - Has the adoption of these tools and technologies resulted in any significant improvement in primary and high-school level education standards?”

My response
Teachers have been using  tools from the beginning - sand, blackboard , demos are early tools. 
But the past 30 years have introduced many new tools like PCs , internet, search engines like Google . This has seen a great change in the education scene.


Tools like powerpoint presentations ,when used correctly, have definitely improved the students capacity in receiving the  information contents correctly. Diagrams also had better visibility. We used overhead projectors and epidiascope from the sixties onwards. 
There was an adjustment problem in the pace of teaching. The pace  became faster and students' absorption level got  reduced.
But the 90s improved our reach to students. It is no more restricted to the class lecturing hours only. You can reach anytime. The magic was email. I saw research students in US complaining about their free times being encroached upon  by their guides. Demands also increased. But emails established a fantastic communications line. We could reach other faculty in many countries. I reached  many friends . 
But the greatest aid was Google search engines. It provided fast access to knowledge . The open access concept started evolving.  True the search engine provided a lot of junk and fake information,  but it provided an awareness of what is happening everywhere in your field. Slowly faculty started sharing their lecture plans and even ppts on the web. This started the open access revolution. This helped research students to know many things - who and which Institutions are working in his or her area, what are they working on , references of their work like papers and reports. They could establish contacts. Networking was easy. 
Most students benefited from the digital tools. Their cognition improved. 
Wikipedia was also helpful to know some facts and references. 
India got into tools not in a big way and also with decades long time lags. Students didn't use simulation in most places. Matlab became dominant only in the 21st century. Software tools were not available in most institutions. Even DBMS packages were not available in most institutions.  Most institutions  purchased a compiler and a few pirated software. Even word processing started late. 
But two  factors are clear .
Tools usage abroad was and is very good in most North American and European universities and schools. But tools are rejected by most Indian educational institutions. In the nineties, I advised and  assisted the directorate of technical education , Karnataka to buy software for civil, mechanical and electrical applications and even software for drawing , all these for polytechnic and ITIs and funded by the World Bank. Unfortunately, the usage was minimal. Even IISc didn't buy many tools. Even TCS moved into software engineering, operations and process management only in the late eighties. There were very few software companies producing application tools ,but the quality was poor. 
But the impact of tools on education was significant. I will discuss this later. 
More in the next post. 
Greetings
Dks

In the water-cut gullies the earth dusted down in dry little streams. Gophers and ant lions started small avalanches. And as the sharp sun struck day after day, the leaves of the young corn became less stiff and erect; they bent in a curve at first, and then, as the central ribs of strength grew weak, each leaf tilted downward. Then it was June, and the sun shone more fiercely. The brown lines on the corn leaves widened and moved in on the central ribs. The weeds frayed and edged back toward their roots. The air was thin and the sky more pale; and every day the earth paled..

Put it truly away, out of sight and out of reach, not just flipped over for a few minutes at a time. You’ll be amazed at the change in the quality of your interactions and your ability to build trust.”

Mr Joshi of Indian bank promptly responds to my posts . He has a comment on the teacher's use of tools.
“Are not teachers responsible for non use of modern technology tools , in teaching. ? Teachers seems to have forgotten to unlearn old and learn to adopt new technologies and methods”
I agree with the above comments to some extent.  The two comments, though valid ti a large extent , are too sweeping and universal. While there are many teachers in an institution who have fixed minds and are ignorant of tools, there are also some in the same institution who are aware of tools and not only use them, but also encourage students to use them. Most institutions fortunately provided some facilities for students to learn. So students were smart and learnt to use the tools.  The greatest impact of this is seen in the IT services industry using millions of these students. The dominance of IT industry and its survival is because of these institutions and students. It proves my point. There are several lakhs  of teachers, engineers, professionals, bankers , administrators in india who don't use tools , who have a fixed mind . Is it possible to correct all or some ? 
Thanks for your great comments.
Let me get back to our topic of use of tools in teaching. 
To understand the usage of tools in India , I want to divide the period 1960 to 2024 into three eras. A comparison of three eras will be helpful to know the impacts of digital tools. 
Let me look at era1 - the period 1960 to 1980
Let us call it the pre digital era. What are the educational scenarios then?
Let me start with the best- IISc.  We had great class rooms with huge glass boards, a big luxury ,I enjoyed at IISc.  It was a pleasant experience to teach in PE 302 and 303. 
We had many good labs in all engineering departments and modern equipment and facilities. So lab work particularly for researchers was more than adequate. The central workshop with more machines than a machine tool industry along with departmental workshops provided great fabrication facilities.  Most facilities were very modern , latest ones. Teaching competed with the best universities in the west. Our syllabus revised every year was and is modern , current and relevant to a large extent. 
We also had an excellent library.
Most teachers had limited knowledge in their subject. They couldn't access most books. They accessed research papers only after two to three years.  Normally a teacher used only one textbook and no reference.  Many books by Indian  authors started coming at that time. But we never used them at IISc.  So teaching was not easy . 
But I was lucky.  Institute had a great library with lots of books and journals dating to 1910 onwards. Many original books by Newton, Russel, Maxwell etc were available. Similarly , there was a book on computers prepared by  the staff of Burroughs corporation, a then famous computer manufacturing company. Then by mid seventies,  many books based on IBM came up. So I could teach my course on computer systems using these books. But I taught several courses like OS, data structures, compilers , simulation , computer applications in power systems,  computational methods etc without books. But I tried to refer to many books and papers for each course. 
When I taught my database course for the first time ,I used CJ Date’s book. It was useless - more than 50% dealt with the outdated hierarchical database system and very little on relational model. 
Still IISc had a lot of books. 
Let me give one example - Prof Dharma Rao left for Concordia university. He requested Prof Ramakrishna Iyengar to send a complete list of books in electrical engineering held in our department library. That shows we are better off than many universities in books and journals. Still quite short of a good teacher. 
We had an ammonia printing unit which produced many copies of diagrams and drawing, an epidiascope and a cyclostyling machine. They were very useful. Then we got a slide projector. 
Though we didn't have a computing facility - we had enough analog computers- , we could use computers elsewhere. I used the computer at NAL , at HAL and at TIFR Bombay.  I could do extensive computations using these machines, learn the nuances and differences in these machines and develop my courses based on them. Our students used these computers. I used to write programs for many faculty. 
That was all the tools we had - not bad, most institutions didn't have even 5% of books thst we had and had no journals.
Our library was open to all. A lot of people used our library.
Let me look at the second period 1980 to 2000 - digital preparation era. The situation on tools improved.

Greetings 
Dks
29/09/24, 5:09 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 492
“Employees in high-trust organizations are more productive, have more energy at work, collaborate better with their colleagues, and stay with their employers longer than people working at low-trust companies. They also suffer less chronic stress and are happier with their lives, and these factors fuel stronger performance.”
Paul Zak 
Let me look at the second era 1980 to 2000. This is the digital growth era, hardware like microprocessors, PCs,  servers , etc followed by software both system software and applications and followed by networks. Internationally it saw explosive growth but we were slow in the first ten years and picked up pace in the next ten years. 
Internationally, students used PCs effectively - most programming work was done on a computer . They started solving large problems. They used to spend several hours every day on a computer. Initially, the students in the west used hundreds of PCs installed in their university. They did serious simulation work and worked throughout the night hours to complete assignments. Assignments became tougher. The total work culture changed. The students became good programmers and problem solvers with great analytical skills and led the industry in designing and building not only complex systems software but also hundreds of applications. The programming culture moved from universities to schools. This was a great period for the west. Floppy disks became the common media for storage and found many uses and stored many applications software. It was a great convenience. 
But the situation in india was different. We concentrated on teaching hardware. Programming was not considered important. Even in hardware we were not near the western students. They used to design a VLSI chip and get it fabricated in a foundry as part of a course assignment. We taught about 8085 for decades. Colleges bought 8085 processors and had a lab for microprocessors. 
But the 90s saw the beginning of an explosion. C became usable along with unix. Most colleges bought a unix based  server with many PCs connected to it. Computer science became a favorite discipline in most colleges. So the students picked up programming in C and became available for the IT industry like TCS  that was fast becoming a leader in the International level. So absorption happened fast. 
But what tools did a teacher use ? It was mostly the overhead projector  and transparency sheets and pens. They were expensive. So the ohp was sparingly used. It didn't reach most colleges. Most had one or two ohps in a college, not enough for teaching. But we all used that mode for displaying our lectures. I might have prepared thousands of pages of these sheets filled with my lectures, my talks and paper presentations. We also used slide projectors. I made hundreds of slides on some topics . I used to carry a slide projector to schools to give a talk on computers. These two were the major tools. While students were trained to use PCs,  faculty usage was minimal. 
But PCs found use in sending and receiving emails. So communications became great. My children used my built 286 PC to send emails and receive reply from abroad. I don't remember to have send emails to many faculty in india. Ernet was a great infrastructure to help us in our communications through emails. Most colleges and universities didn't have and didn't use email facilities till the onset of the 21st century.  Smart boards made appearance towards the end of 20th century. 
But the impacts were still marginal. Chalk and talk was  still dominant.
Software was yet to make any impact in teaching. No institution had a good database system, even though we were teaching database courses for about 20 years. Open software was not used, even though the concept was there and open office made an appearance. Both word and powerpoint made their appearance near 1995. But even conferences didn't have facilities for ppt presentations. But by 2000,  it became the standard. 
But there was a silver cloud visible.
Internet was slowly reaching the country. But only urban institutions had a feasibility of internet connection. Even we at IISc struggled with poor bandwidth . BSNL was slowly waking up. But the web revolution started. Search engines appeared. But some ingenious attempts were tried out. One of our major projects was the Mahithi sindhu program. I was the prime architect involved with this program and subsequent IT programs for schools for the next 15 years.It started in 2000. Let me talk about it in the next post. 
Greetings 
Dks
30/09/24, 4:22 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 493
“The Tipping Point is the biography of an idea, and the idea is very simple. It is that the best way to understand the emergence of fashion trends, the ebb and flow of crime waves, or, for that matter, the transformation of unknown books into bestsellers, or the rise of teenage smoking, or the phenomena of word of mouth, or any number of the other mysterious changes that mark everyday life is to think of them as epidemics. Ideas and products and messages and behaviors spread just like viruses do.”
Malcolm  Gladwell.     Tipping point

Let us look at the period 2000 to 2020. 
This is the growth period. Actually it started by the mid nineties itself .  PCs were available in most institutions including schools. Powerpoint presentations were accepted by most teachers. It took knowledge transfer to a next level. People started to use PCs a lot - to learn programming, to type a paper , to do calculations and to prepare an illustrative presentation deck. I saw some company accountants doing most of their calculations and preparing balance sheets using spreadsheets. Engineers also did their  calculations  on  that. Architects used PCs for drawing views of their building. In a conference, i heard a person say ,” we can see a building without laying even a single brick.” Many educational contents came as a software package. The keys here were
1.user interface was considered essential . The arrogance of the knowall systems analysts were thrownout. Systems analysts lost their importance and jobs.
2. Mainframe died to a large extent. Servers dominated everywhere. 
3. Software became simple and a product for sales. Good examples were accounting software, kannada wordprocessor, and many educational software. 
It was a  good time to develop software. Many training institutions used software for teaching. Engineering colleges started using Autocad, Matlab , and many tutors . Labs were built on software. 
But what changed the scenario was the availability of search engines and web services. Most  Information was available on the web. Many faculty in the west put their ppts on the web ,so our teachers accessed them . 
The Web provided great advantages for education.  Some faculty used the web , internet and many used search engines. It had a great impact on teaching. 
Students were more aware of the developments happening everywhere in the world and so they put their teachers on the mat. This made some teachers use Google, quora  etc. 
Then came some pure teaching sites like khan academy and they taught many students. Online teaching started picking up with coursera and edX in the west and Nptel in india. While Nptel started with faculties of IITs,  it now has contributions from many faculty members - more than 4000 courses are available . This led to a quantum leap in students learning hard topics. 
Youtube also helped students to attend lectures by experts and even demos of experiments. The understanding level went up. A simple evidence was reflected in the outcome of exams. More than 50% of students failed in their first year engineering examinations.  This came down considerably now. The tipping point is the use of tools. 
By 2000 , CDROM replaced floppy disks as mobile data carriers. Small companies  were burning lessons in these devices and they sold them to schools. Many companies sprung up across the country to develop specific contents and sell them to schools. This concept of schools and colleges building up such CDs became  popular during the 2000 to 2010. During this period hard disk capacity went up . So files were conveniently stored in the hard disk. PCs became very powerful. So even teachers started using PCs. Laptops appeared on the scene. Some teachers took a laptop and projected it on the wall in the classroom. So teaching has improved. Some classrooms were fitted with projectors. 
All these tools have improved our learning and teaching activities. 
But broadband was still a distance away. Dialup systems were the only one available. Colleges started setting up a campus wide network. Most put up an optic fibre backbone and connected most labs and took a reasonable but still with only a low bandwidth - 64 or 128 kb connection . This situation improved over the years, particularly after optical fibre became available in most parts of the country, and private players jumped into the fray breaking the monopoly of BSNL. 
It is this good bandwidth networks becoming available in most cities and many switches and routers being available in the country, changed the scene. Access to the internet was much better at higher speeds. People got broadband connection in their houses. Web usage increased. 
This is also the period when offices in most organizations were automated. Typewriters became obsolete. This is also the period when most industries moved to ERP , CRM,MRP and other software to improve their efficiency and quality.  It is also the period when banks moved to a centralised database and automated transactions through core banking software and networking . Data centres came up in good numbers across the country. Governments moved to computerised file management. NIC played a big role in governmental work. All these above mentioned developments started from 1995 onwards and picked pace after 2000. 
We saw the onset of online examinations, online remote meetings and lectures .
This period saw lots of technological changes. Software became mature. Data centres anywhere in the world was accepted. Services got enlarged with cloud. SOA became a good tool to integrate many software packages. Many services like SAAS, PAS , etc became available. 
I felt that teachers were ahead in this era of digital transformation compared to banks and other organizations.  
Let us look at Mahithisindhu program next.
Greetings
Dks
01/10/24, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 494
In the late 1960s, a Harvard psychology professor took LSD, resigned his appointment (with some encouragement from the administration), went to India, met a guru, and returned to write a popular book called Be Here Now.
Now, why would anyone go all the way to India and spend his time, money, and brain cells just to learn how not to think about the future? Because, as anyone who has ever tried to learn meditation knows, not thinking about the future is much more challenging than being a psychology professor. Not to think about the future requires that we convince our frontal lobe not to do what it was designed to do, and like a heart that is told not to beat, it naturally resists this suggestion. Unlike N.N., most of us do not struggle to think about the future because mental simulations of the future arrive in our consciousness regularly and unbidden, occupying every corner of our mental lives.
Daniel Gilbert.  Stumbling on happiness 

While most engineering colleges established computer science programs and hence provided good facilities for learning programming and using the web, this was  followed by improving computer facilities in the regular colleges also. Let me talk about my involvement in this. 
By the early nineties , many colleges wanted to set up computer centres.  Computer science became a major subject in PUC itself.  I was involved with computers in two colleges. One was MES college , another was MLA college. MLA college started their computer courses very early by mid or late eighties itself. Sudha murthy was teaching there after returning to bangalore. I advised MES college to set up a computer lab, buy computers and even furniture. 
Later I was a member of a committee set up by the state government for sanctioning BCA courses. Because of our approvals,  most arts and sciences colleges started the BCA program. These really helped in reaching most districts . These covered college education. But what about at school level?
Many schools, mostly private schools in big cities and some in District headquarters wanted to start  computer classes. This gave birth to a new industry - computer facilitators. Almost every big city had  many such facilitators who would conduct classes and practicals in many schools at a fee per student. This happened in the mid eighties itself. I had helped the Gujarati high school in bangalore in selecting computers and fixing  a facilitator. It ran successfully. Some schools had an in-house teacher for computers and a good computer lab. I am familiar with national public school at Rajajinagar one of the early starters in computer education for students. It employed a teacher and had a very good computer lab. 
Another school I was involved with was the Purna prajna school at palace orchards . I was involved with this school for more than a decade. I selected the computer faculty for many years and helped in upgrading the computers. The computer culture was quite deep. Computers were used for learning many subjects. Many content developers came up in the country. 
But these are mostly private schools. There were no computers in government schools. 
So when SM Krishna became the chief minister of Karnataka in 1999, he announced the mahithisindhu program. The idea was to introduce computers in 1000 government high schools across karnataka and introduce computer based teaching of English,  sciences , mathematics and social sciences. It was the first major massive program initiated by any government.  I was involved in this fully. Mr Vijaya Bhaskar was the commissioner of education then. We planned a holistic approach. 
First we identified the bottlenecks.
No room available in schools for a computer lab.
Power supply is erratic. 
No telephone or dialup connections were available in most schools. 
We first decided to get the schools to build a lab and also get power connection. 
Then we decided the components of the program as
A computer centre with upto20 PCs and a server with LAN connections 
Office software and an interpreter or compilers
Prepare  computer text books for classes 8 to 10 based on a syllabus framed by us. 
Prepare a full schedule of lab work. 
Preparation of contents for the mathematics,  English,  sciences and social studies courses from 8th to 10th stds.
Running the centre professionally by a computer science graduate - either BE or MCA. He or she should teach the courses.
Most teaching should be  hands-on . 
Training for regular  teachers. 
Let me discuss the next step in the next post.
Greetings
Dks
02/10/24, 4:26 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 495
“I felt a new kind of calmness—one of a profundity never experienced before. I had just found that “I”, that scanning device—that point—that essence—that place beyond. A place where “I” existed independent of social and physical identity. That which was I was beyond Life and Death. And something else—that “I” Knew—it really Knew. It was wise, rather than just knowledgeable. It was a voice inside that spoke truth. I recognized it, was one with it, and felt as if my entire life of looking to the outside world for reassurance—David Reisman’s other-directed being, was over. Now I need only look within to that place where I Knew.”
Robert Alpert  / Ram Dass     Be here now 

Once we decided on the components required for a successful computer based learning , our next step was on “ how do we implement this massive scheme?”
There were three options. 
In-house activity - this can't be undertaken by the KGCC .
Partial outsourcing - computers supplied by companies and installed in schools and operationalised. Running the computer centre and teaching is left to the schools. This mode failed. 
Divide the total scope into three parts. Infrastructure development - to be done by the school . Computer supply , installation, text book preparation, internet connection, separate computer teacher appointment , running the classes , exams, teachers training - all these to be outsourced to a capable agency. The third was production of contents for subjects - given as a separate contract.
We followed  the third strategy. We prepared a detailed rfp- request for proposals along with cost structure-  to implement the scheme. We made the eligibility strict and floated the tender to prospective vendors. We conducted a clarification meeting for vendors before they prepared and submitted their response.   The pre bid meeting of intended bidders was chaotic. All education department seniors were missing. I had to handle it alone. Only Mr Satyamurthy from DSERT was with me. His support throughout the execution of this project was great. He just listened to my instructions  and executed  them sincerely. He stayed with me during the entire five years of execution and even beyond. Many small companies wanted to bid for the project. It was a great opportunity for them. Even some ministers floated companies to participate in the tender. Lots of  Fortunately, the education minister , Mr Viswanath was very helpful. He told me to handle it. 
We gave the order to three major companies. I set up an inspection team consisting of faculty members from about 15 engineering colleges to visit all schools every quarter and submit a report. I will see these reports and clear payments . This worked well. 
We also got the contents developed in CDs and distributed to schools. That wasn't an easy task. The developer just copied text book into a CD , no visualisation. I had to fight with them. To give an example, the prepositions - in, on, below, above,  top, bottom were not properly illustrated. I told them “ show a table. Take books and show how these propositions work. “ The developers were useless. The promoter of the company complained to the chief minister, SM Krishna ,” we can satisfy anyone in the world but not Prof DKS” i wasn't present. Both the two secretaries - IT and school education- told me this incident later. They said  that the CM just smiled. I said that was a credit for me. It took me several months of work to get the CDs approved. My lab assistant Gangaraju helped me in setting up facilities for the demo of these CDs.
The impacts were striking. 
Pass percentage in SSLC increased from 50% to 56% in the first year, then to 62% in the second year and to 70% in the third year. Attendance improved to 100%. Enrollment went up. 
That shows the impact of using CDs providing detailed explanations to many topics in the syllabus. 
Seeing our schools computerisation project, both Tamilnadu and kerala followed it with different models. By 2010 ,a large number - several thousands - used computers for teaching. More programs were launched by the government to cover most high schools. 

But we need to look at a new tool , a very controversial one - gen AI. Let us look at it next. 
Greetings
Dks
03/10/24, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 496
“When AI is used in isolation, the impact of major failures could be large but contained. However, as AI is being used more and more in connected systems such as in the stock market for automated trading, unexpected catastrophic failures could lead to the widespread failure of entire systems. We don’t need to go very far back in history to recall how credit default swaps caused the financial crisis of 2008 and the valuation of Russia’s ruble led to the 1998 crash of Long Term Capital Management (LTCM) – a $126 billion hedge fund – that subsequently required a bailout from the US Fed. Will the use of more AI in financial markets lead to similar catastrophic failures in the future?”
Susanne chishti.     AI book

Mr PV Joshi , Indian bank  has this comment on my last post:

My response
While I have done many major projects like the power systems for Delhi, BCCL, IISc, and sysgel for TCS before the mahithisindhu program and many afterwards , and a large number of minor projects like computerization in the state government of karnataka for more than 20 years, world bank schemes, computerization in many organizations and colleges- about 50 in number-, networking for many institutions , this project gave me immense satisfaction. 
It was unique in many ways. 
It reached the entire state, touched the poor rural children.
It was successful in achieving many goals. Internet happened only in cities, not in villages. That was upsetting to us. 
It was a very challenging job. I had problems with politicians, vendors, school managements, DSERT director and government officials . It was tough to handle all these. I learnt many tricks - vendor capability assessment ,  corrections based on learning like installing DG sets as power interruptions prolonged beyond 6 hours and batteries died, crowd handling, importance of participation by school managements HMs, teachers ,etc. I realised that technology needs  less than 5% of our attention.  But I got great support from the commissioners of education starting with Mr Vijayabhaskar, secretaries of the education department, senior officers of DSERT particularly Mr Satyamurthy, Mr Vivek Kulkarni,secretary IT, Mr Ramana Reddy ,Director, KGCCanminister Mr Viswanath, minister for education, Mr Jairam Ramesh and the CM, Mr SM Krishna. Their approvals and confidence in me took the project to success. There was no interference from officers. We kept out many officers from this work. I learnt a great lesson here - less number of people, more work, less nuisance,  less corruption and success of the project. I worked with only one person Mr Satyamurthy,  a very dedicated person and very devoted one, i haven't seen many like him. 
There was no corruption . I was surprised and worked with clean officers. This should open the eyes of most of us who criticise the government for corruption. There are many islands of honest, dedicated, sincere, poor officers in the government. I have experienced the support of many such sincere and pious officers.
This project was unique. It wasn't simply supplying computers to schools which was done before. It was the first massive project encompassing 1000 high schools in the state initially. Secondly it was holistic.
Most importantly I got a lot of freedom to work on this project in addition to cooperation. I could architect it completely and holistically. Vijayabhaskar’s support and advice was useful. My independence was unbelievable.  It was very enjoyable to work on this project. I worked for more than 6 years on this project. I don't run away from challenges. 
Also I could plan periodic  inspections. It was another cog in the wheel for the success of this project. This is another unique feature. I could network with about. 15 engineering colleges, not an easy task to develop a collaborative work. I worked with more than 200 faculty members . They followed my instructions, were excited to be part of this experiment. My reach with colleges helped me in this. Many faculty members were surprised at the planning and acquisition of latest computers for these rural schools. They said they don't have such machines in their colleges. 
I had many interventions by activists. There were open source enthusiasts who objected to our buying and using microsoft office. I was able to convince the committees that MS office is a good choice. I told the protesters that i organised a conference on IPR and open source quite early. I am a supporter of open office. But they should ask all government offices to move to open office. Don't start bottomup. It won't help. I hoped many of the students trained under this program will get jobs because of their office knowledge.  A second objection came from kannada ganaka parishad, inaugurated by me. I decided early that we will retain most English terms for technical aspects in the book. We don't have differentiated kannada equivalents for many terms like computer/ calculator/ counter, memory storage etc. Memory was translated as smarana meaning remember. Storage was translated as ugrana - store room in the kitchen. While the text books were in kannada, technical terms like cpu etc were retained so that the students are on par with others in this respect. 
 So I was happy to have architected and implemented this project. It gave me immense satisfaction. My senior colleague from our EE department,  Prof MAL Thathachar expressed surprises in an open talk and admired my capability to work with politicians. 
I will look at your second comment tomorrow.

the futures that our brains insist on simulating are not all wine, kisses, and tasty bivalves. They are often mundane, irksome, stupid, unpleasant, or downright frightening, and people who seek treatment for their inability to stop thinking about the future are usually worrying about it rather than reveling in it. Just as a loose tooth seems to beg for wiggling, we all seem perversely compelled to imagine disasters and tragedies from time to time. On the way to the airport we imagine a future scenario in which the plane takes off without us and we miss the important meeting with the client. On the way to the dinner party we imagine a future scenario in which everyone hands the hostess a bottle of wine while we greet her empty-handed and embarrassed. 
Daniel Gilbert.   Stumbling on  happiness 

Mr Joshi's  second comment of my being a taskmaster is also an important one. It reveals a characteristic of me that has  put me into trouble many times. Actually it worries me. But once I am committed to a task , I want to get it completed successfully. I won't say that I found success in all projects I was involved with. But I found success in a project where I was given control and my views were accepted and  there was at least  one sincere person in charge.    I felt that if I don't put myself into it, a project can become  a rudderless ship. So I was a bit strong in my opinions. But I had an open mind but with a goal in focus.  I never felt comfortable but stuck to my guns unless convinced by others. There are not many like you who understood my intentions and accepted my behavior. I don't like smart alecs and illiterates in influential positions. I have clashed with many.  My popularity has been low because of that strong quality . But  I needed it to succeed in a task . I am happy to have worked with many - several hundreds - like you in colleges, government, industries and banks. I am very happy and satisfied with my interactions with all these people in very senior positions. Most became lifelong friends. 
My interactions with you were memorable.  I cherish them.
Actually , I got  great support and respect from many EDs and GMs of SBI,  Canara bank,  Indian bank, Corporation bank, PNB, Syndicate bank , Vijaya bank and Equitas. I did a technology audit for SBI. Later I advised Canara bank on its first branch computerization project  for its parliament street branch. I  am in touch with many officers who worked with me in the banks, but lost touch with many. I can't forget the experiences I got while working for more than 20 years with banks. The core banking transformation was not easy and the problems were different for each bank. I actually went on a discovery trip here. 
My work with banks was my fourth dimension - the other three being academics, government and industries. I learnt a lot during my association with banks.

Mr Aravind also has another comment.  
“Good data points which can be easily related to the process followed”
My response 
Thanks. 
I started with a flexible process in mind and also had an idea on the various  steps to be done. My process was not completely decided in the beginning. But a framework was ready. 
I tried to look for an appropriate process for each transformation. Recall the process followed by me for KSEEB in which you were involved. I followed a different route and it succeeded. But when I followed another route for the same activity in a university - no SRS was prepared like in the school board case, it failed. I got great support from the vice chancellor, registrar, controller of exams and finance officer , and got a person appointed for support at the university end. But it failed because it didn't have one person in charge of the project from the university end . It was an orphaned baby being pulled in many directions . Also the vendor cheated us. 
All organizations have different culture . So your process should be context sensitive to each organization's culture, strengths , weaknesses and needs. It is a social and cultural problem solving, not logical or systemic or even procedural. I had difficulties to align the important staff to the objectives of transformation.  There were officers in banks even at very senior level who were comfortable with their then existing state and didn't want changes. So they opposed core banking. The next alignment is between internal people of the organization and vendor. This is very tough. If one is rigid - I saw one big IT services company telling banks “ take it or leave it. This is what we can give “. At the other extreme , i saw TCS bending to every request of the customer. This non alignment leads to misunderstanding, unsatisfactory solutions , and mistrust. 

I consider  my involvements with many projects - in IISc, government of Karnataka,  Mahithi sindhu,  KSEEB, KSCST,  APMC, Gadag, TCS, many  banks, Central silk board, Jindal Steels, Jayadeva institute of Cardiology, Vidhana soudha/ PWD, Mysore paper, KSRTC ,  land army corporation,  DST, government of India for NRDMS, Department of Space for COWWA, Integra micro systems  for fintech,  TEQIP a world bank scheme,JNCASR, ISEC,  DG shipping,  IDRBT, karle constructions, many other organizations,  many universities and colleges - very useful, very satisfying . These associations  gave me another focus and purpose in life. All these spanned for more than 40 years. I came in contact with hundreds of senior officers, saw different work culture, different beliefs, different kinds of experiences - a vast canvas of the country. I worked for more than 20 years post retirement from IISc.  I don't think many got this opportunity. I cherish the friendship of many. Most importantly, I was taken aback by the respect and trust bestowed by all these people  in me- that is remarkable. They sought clarifications,  but not questioned my actions. Overall I felt that most understood me , my commitment, my deep work culture and my expertise.  It was a great meaningful time in a major part of my life. I thank everyone for this happiness.

“our instincts—our natural tendencies to think or react in certain ways when faced 
with a decision—aren’t as trustworthy as we might believe. We’re subject to inherent biases, false confidence, irrational risk aversion, and any number of decision-making pitfalls. Mr Joshi , Indian bank is concerned about genAI.
“Sir , I was reading news today , came across , a headline '  The integration of generative AI is no longer an option for businesses but a necessity. Gen AI can help companies accelerate high-quality content production, gain new insights, scale personalized customer outreach, and enhance overall communication quality. The introduction of gen AI isn’t just a technological upgrade. It’s a fundamental shift in how employees communicate and how businesses operate. It’s the new language of business. '    Is our present education system in general capable of handling new challenges .  Highly rated institutions  , should create a system to percolate to all district level universities . Mr Nagaraja comments:
“Good morning sir. Your posts are really very interesting & makes us to recall the developments that took place in the last several decades. What about Meta AI  which is a software product developed in 2014 by Facebook in their FAIR lab, which is available as a contact in WhatsApp? May I request you to know your views regarding it's useful in improving quality of education system?
Thanks with regards. HSN”
My response 
These concerns are genuine. I have read more than 10 books including one by the famous Yuval Harari titled “ Nexus” . Many have raised ethical considerations. Many have raised their voices to the disasterous consequences due to the misdeeds of AI. The possibility of such misdeeds were well known. There is a book titled “ Life3.0. Being human in the age of AI” by Prof. Max Tegmark written in 2017 or earlier. It vividly describes transformations that will happen to us in the years to come. In the book “singularities are nearer”  by Kurzweil published in 2024 , the author lists many problems that AI will pose , but says these can be solved. Sam Altman, the inventor of open AI, has also alerted the authorities about many issues due to AI. So there is a lot of alertness amongst even AI researchers. 
Let me put my thoughts here.
Alister McLean wrote in the 1970s a fiction called fear is the key. I was a great fan. I realised we are destroyed by fear at that point in time. Then I read a beautiful one page article in Readers Digest in the eighties that was titled as “when I wanted to borrow a screw driver to repair my car when it stopped in the road”. The summary was that the person saw a house nearby and walked towards it. He started to worry if he didn't get support. He created arguments in his mind and developed anger. When he reached the house, he started shouting at the owner. I see these reactions of fear , worry and anger today against AI. Quite a lot of these reactions are genuine and need to be acted upon to prevent disasters. 
We know that experience based decisions can go wrong. We all have experiences of wrong and unexpected decisions. Past can guide us to some extent only. The earlier machine learning used experiential data to predict on a new situation. Its dependability was good on similar happenings. When a new unexpected and unknown event occurs, ML answer may be wrong. 
The current genAI uses language models also along with neural networks and Bayesian learning. It is good for some activities and can provide good answers. Examples are writing a fiction or poetry, talking, answering questions etc. It is improving and may lead to the level of good and intelligent  assistants and multilingual experts. 
Today we are using AI in a standalone mode. So control is easy. You can discard the results. But when AI is integrated with a system or process and used for online operations like using AI to control a nuclear power plant , it becomes  dangerous. So we need a strong regulatory authority to control such kinds of operations. Is that kind of strong regulation  possible in a soft  state like india?I doubt it.
Let us see  more of AI, particularly our preparedness in the next post.
Greetings
Dks
07/10/24, 4:39 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 450
there is a Tipping Point for the introduction of any new technology. Sharp introduced the first low priced fax machine in 1984, and sold about 80,000 of those machines in the United States in that first year. For the next three years, businesses slowly and steadily bought more and more faxes, until, in 1987, enough people had faxes that it made sense for everyone to get a fax. Nineteen eighty seven was the fax machine Tipping Point. A million machines were sold that year, and by 1989 two million new machines had gone into operation. Cellular phones have followed the same trajectory. Through the 1990s, they got smaller and cheaper, and service got better until 1998, when the technology hit a Tipping Point and suddenly everyone had a cell phone.
 Malcolm  Gladwell                  Tipping point.  
     
Let me continue on AI , its impacts and our preparedness.  
The effects of AI was understood by the founders of the new discipline called AI in 1956 in a workshop organized In Dartmouth by McCarthy.  It had also Minsky, Shannon , Rochester and others. McCarthy coined the name AI. They cautioned on the need for extensive research particularly about security. When I started teaching in 1967, I wasn't familiar with the word AI. But I was looking at the thinking aspects and gave my first talk on “ are computers thinking machines?” In 1967. So the intelligence capacity was perceived then itself. 
I think that  AI is not dealing with intelligence,  but aping humans in understanding natural languages, logical approaches,data analysis, questioning, and some levels of conscious decision making. Aitareya Upanishad talks of 16 forms of intelligence. When will our research on AI reach any of those levels, I don't know. But one thing is clear. Today's AI  is only an augmentation to our intelligence, a big tool. We need to build a super AI to control AI and keep that under  human guidance. We need to introduce AI into online systems slowly after testing thoroughly . We need to add trust into AI programs. We need to move into a new area urgently - that of impact analysis of AI results. That is urgent. 
Regarding the impacts of AI, initially it will replace most white collar jobs . There will be a great deal of unemployment. We need to find a way of feeding millions of unemployed people and their families. It will create a highly unequal society. AI developers will rule over AI users and AI illiterates. If it falls in evil hands , it will lead to massive destruction.
As regards the question of readiness of our educational systems, my conclusion is our institutions  are not aware of even AIs  impacts and implications. All big IT companies like TCS, Infosys, Wipro etc were preparing to use AI for the past 20 years. TCS developed Igneo long back - in  2005 itself. So they are using AI and are getting to use genAI.  Our engineering colleges have started an AI undergraduate program. But they didn't realise that AI is not a computer science area. We need to start applications courses in banking, fintech, commerce, health and medicine, agriculture, security, utilities etc in agriculture universities. Medical colleges, commerce institutions, arts colleges, language institutions, Sanskrit colleges  and even schools.
Today's  Hindustan times has this report.
“Thousands of IndiGo passengers were made to wait for hours for baggage drop-ins and check-ins after the airline experienced a temporary "system outage" leading to long queues at various airports across the country. Departures of domestic flights were also delayed in various cities causing massive chaos at the airports.”
We are yet to get adjusted to digital technology and communications. So the persistent question of Luddites is “ How will we handle both digital and AI, poor and rich, literate and illiterate , urban and rural divides?” We need to find out reasons for ese technology failures. We will be the only country going through the four waves of developments in parallel - agriculture,  industries, information and digital and AI. 

So in summary, we have to understand the role and impact of AI, prepare plans in advance to minimise the bad impacts, build a new army and security systems , build hundreds of applications. AI needs a large volume of data . We can't use public cooked up data. So we should activate data generation activities in many institutions. ISEC is creating a good data bank. We need to build language models based on Paninian grammar and Tholkappiam / Nannool. Lots need to be done to safeguard our people and security.
Greetings
Dks

Mr Joshi has one more query.
“AI is new demon or Angel ? And next question, after AI what next technology upgradation?”
My response:
AI is not a demon . It definitely helps us, it will improve our agricultural production, our diagnosis and treatment, surgery, our water, power, gas utilities, transport. It will help in reducing carbon emissions, cleaning water. reducing consumption of natural resources, preserving forests and building new forests, securing houses , etc. It can monitor and control nuclear power plants, alert us on disasters , provide solutions by analysing landslides in Himalayas and western ghats better.  It will help in reducing plastics or producing biodegradable plastics. It will help in monitoring urban garbage problem. It can bring in better governance , reduce corruption and even initiate political reforms and change a  democracy.
AI can increase cognition by students, enhance learning and knowledge. It will bring the entire world's knowledge to a student's computer and help him or her to solve new problems. The need for a teacher will be minimal. A student will know more than a teacher. 
 But it can destroy buildings, provide weapons for AI super powers with capability to destroy the world, destroy stored food grains , put many millions in survival problems, it will discriminate against people with its builtin biases and can destroy a country's economy. It can initiate war.
 Actually , current AI is a culmination of development of many technologies like big data, terahertz GPUs, IOT, 5G, analytics, terahertz frequency usages, language models, human psychology , etc and their integrated applications to solve our problems.  If data weren't available and very high speed and GPUs weren't available, then AI today will fail. 
Analysts expect total capital spending by Microsoft to represent 28% of Microsoft’s revenue this fiscal year and 27% in the next fiscal year, and revenue from AI not known . So AI is not surging. It is moving slowly. 
Lots of R and D activities  are waiting for us to move forward and control AI and direct it towards goodness. Future looks bleak for many and good for many. 
People like Sridhar Vembu will create alternate systems. Some people will create a computerless society in some small villages going back on these developments , and living in a natural and spiritual world. Won't that be good? 
Our vedas say that we have both daivatva and asura properties in all of us. They are not two independent separate categories of beings . So the question is - what will dominate in a society ? - daivatva or asura guna. Optimists will say daivatva - goodness- will prevail over evil. But transition is going to be very painful.
AI itself will evolve in the next 25 years to improve its consciousness. But I expect massive developments in applying AI in almost all areas , much bigger than the digital era, but with greater intensity and faster pace. More jobs there development and deployment both will go hand in hand in parallel. Hope we use the opportunity wisely. 
Dr. Sanjay Chitnis has already downloaded information about nannool. Both paninian grammar and Tholkappiam are more semantics embedded in the grammar, not syntax alone like English, not ambiguous like English. 
Bio nano tech will lead to further progress - natural bio protein based systems not manufactured but grown organically like food today. That is my mirror to the future - back to organic living. Is it a dream?
Thanks for all comments on AI, a very interesting and concerned topic 
Greetings
Dks
09/10/24, 4:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 502
“Americans gladly pay millions—perhaps even billions—of dollars every year to psychics, investment advisors, spiritual leaders, weather forecasters, and other assorted hucksters who claim they can predict the future. Those of us who subsidize these fortune-telling industries do not want to know what is likely to happen just for the joy of anticipating it. We want to know what is likely to happen so that we can do something about it. If interest rates are going to skyrocket next month, then we want to shift our money out of bonds right now. If it is going to rain this afternoon, then we want to grab an umbrella this morning. Knowledge is power, and the most important reason why our brains insist on simulating the future even when we’d rather be here now, enjoying a goldfish moment, is that our brains want to control the experiences we are about to have.”
 Gilbert                  Stumbling happiness

If people knew how hard I worked to get my 
mastery, it wouldn’t seem so wonderful at all.
—Michelangelo
We do not need magic to transform our world. We carry all the power we need inside ourselves already.

Prof P Raja has sent some comments.

“Certainly sir, your spectrum of in depth wisdom with multiple fields and your conviction have yielded results in many fronts. 
How do you get the instinct in respect of context and culture of our people? what is your understanding of others? it needs lot of experience and expertise to understand the nuances I hope. Your comments sir”

My response
Your question looks at a human being building many characteristics as he or she is growing. 
A child starts as a simple entity without any hatred, any opinions, any beliefs, any value judgments . Even, its observations were limited and started developing in the initial years. All children are born with one important trait - learning characteristics, the levels vary. But as a child starts growing,  it picks up many more characteristics. Several factors decide on the qualities of a person as he or she grows.

Many characteristics of a person are born. Examples are learning, observation, understanding, shyness, some emotions , attention, concentration 
Many inborn characteristics are modified by the family. Examples are understanding, cognition,  introvertedness , attention , observation, calmness etc 
Many characteristics are molded by the family. There are many examples here and we will see them in the course of this post. The most important ones are peace of mind, smiling, love and affection, language, attention to details, contemplation, piety, work culture, humility, frugality, or the opposites of these. This phase is crucial in the development of a child - to be good or bad, to be satisfied or dissatisfied ,  to be happy or sad, to be highly attentive or moderately attentive or not interested, to learn eagerly or be disinterested etc. This needs to be understood by parents . Lots of learning happens through observations and copying. Family atmosphere plays an important role in a child's behaviours. This period also builds several beliefs in a child. 
By the age of 8 to 10, a child acquires a number of habits, emotions, behaviours,  beliefs and characteristics. Let us call this overall picture as a humanistic characteristics. 
Later more characteristics are developed or changed by society , relatives, neighbours,  friends , school etc. 
Some characteristics of a person are developed through observation, learning and cognition. 
While most remain true to their childhood humanisms, some have an open mind and change their specific habits or behaviours.   

In my case, I was lucky to have lived in two large and major households and in several places - a village and towns . So I had guidance from many people in both  grandfathers’ houses. Another biggest advantage was mentoring by my elder uncle. I was always with him for about 12 years. He taught me values, good habits and behaviours . I learnt commitment , work culture , planning and meticulous execution from him. His integrity was unquestionable. He was respected by most people. I learnt empathy, responsibility, independence, self help and studiousness from him. He was close to being a sthithapjana - equanimity - contented, calm, and firm in judgment and wisdom . The village ambience was very useful to build a calm , serene and pious behavior in me . I maintained this composure in my life to a large extent. Piety goes beyond prayers and belief in Gods.  It extended to respect for elders in the family and outside, to listen to them calmly and don't speak against them. It also meant helping all. 
Most importantly, it built a strong value system in me- truth is important, don't hurt others, no violence,  don't use harsh words , be clean always etc. It built a very important characteristic in me. I was a shy person. There was a lot of poverty everywhere with scarcity of food grains. So I learnt empathy in the beginning itself. I feel sad even now , the sadness was inside.  It was a pre-independence period and also just after independence. Country was coming out of a big trauma and there was idealism everywhere. That rubbed on me.  
So my character was built during my childhood days. I was committed to a task and completed it with responsibility. My empathy made me treat everyone equally and listen to them. 
I am very  tough on the outside, impatient and angry. I relied on doing everything by myself as much as possible. But I got a lot of help from many friends throughout my life. 
Hottest give some answers to Raja’s  questions.

“Employees in high-trust organizations are more productive, have more energy at work, collaborate better with their colleagues, and stay with their employers longer than people working at low-trust companies. They also suffer less chronic stress and are happier with their lives, and these factors fuel stronger performance.
 Leaders understand the stakes—at least in principle. In its 2016 global CEO survey, PwC reported that 55% of CEOs think that a lack of trust is a threat to their organization’s growth.”
HBR on trust 

Mr Aravind has sent a detailed explanation on Hinton:
“Geoff Hinton (along with John Hopfield) won the 2024 Nobel prize in Physics. While Hopfield is a professor (retired) in a Physics department, Hinton is a Computer Science prof. at U. Toronto. He also worked for Google part time, until he retired so that he could freely criticize LLMs and other future AI advances as being high risk.
The Nobel prize in Physics was given for their work on "artificial neural networks for machine learning."  These inventions  would perhaps able us to better understand some of the wonders of human brain that no longer will remain wonder for the next generation.
Natural and productive physical labor were replaced by nonproductive gym exercises where you spend additional external energy (powering the treadmills etc.) to burn your internal energy producing nothing external.
In  the not-so-distant future, human beings will cease to be considered a superior intelligent species and may be some AI entity will be controlling us! 
Either that or some crazy person/crazy group will be controlling these AI tools to control all the rest of us. That crazy person could be a legitimate corporate head , legitimate head of a nation, a dictator or a terrorist leader. Elon Musk , Donald Trump, Putin , Hamas are all the indications of our potential future who will have the key to AI powered society. In my opinion, latter has the higher probability. 
Or perhaps , the human society will elevate itself to a higher plane of consciousness and inoculate themselves against the above said danger, so says the optimist in me.

Any views Sir?
 My response 
As regards the first part of understanding the brain, we need to go from physical aspect of brain as envisaged by the westerners to our upanishadic views of mind - Aitareya upanishad talks of 16 forms of intelligence and varaha upanishad talks of 92 tattvas-forms - . As I have written before, we should focus on consciousness. There are a number of questions there unanswered like
What is consciousness? How does one understand it? Is consciousness generated  only in our brain? Can brain studies answer this? Or do we need to know about the mind?
How is consciousness related to intelligence? What is intelligence? 
What do we know about the mind - vignanamaya kosha - the fourth sheath? 
Do we have answers in ancient Indian vedantic thoughts?
Should we not focus on mind and consciousness instead of brain?
Is looking for brain activity an answer for intelligence or something else?
Will it be able to read signals emanating in our body, their codes and meanings?
What are the many levels of it?
How does one develop it?
Why did it take so  long for humans to increase their level of consciousness?
What are the methods or ways of increasing consciousness?
AI sidestepped all these questions. 


My basic objection to AI research is its perception of intelligence. ML is not intelligence nor even learning. It analyzes experience data along with statistics and language models . It is mechanical. Neural networks are non model based blind forms of prediction based on a large volume of data. 
Second part of your comment is very valid and very dangerous. It will lead to undesirable dictatorship, silent wars, destruction of properties and people etc. Instability andxfear will rule the world if not controlled. 
We need to build a super controller to control AI operations, need stricter regulations, and control on research. 
But first control data generation and control and access. We are losing privacy. It is leading to frauds, insecurity, threats , and miserable living. 
We need to address this from various angles. One dimensional, linear , binary thinking has to give way to multidimensional,  multi solutions thinking. 
Let me list the different stages of AI in the next post.

“Share information broadly
 Only 40% of employees report that they are well informed about their company’s goals, strategies, and tactics. This uncertainty about the company’s direction leads to chronic stress, which inhibits the release of oxytocin and undermines teamwork. Openness is the antidote. Organizations that share their “flight plans” with employees reduce uncertainty about where they are headed and why. Ongoing communication is key: A 2015 study of 2.5 million manager-led teams in 195 countries found that workforce engagement improved when supervisors had some form of daily communication with direct reports.”
HBR on trust 

Let me get back to the year 1987. Mostly I had a reasonably settled but pretty busy academic life. I was also traveling frequently as usual. I became the coordinator of proficience,  running the evening courses for industry persons. I also taught in the proficience courses on operating systems and databases regularly.  I had stopped going to PSTI for lectures. But my visits to engineering colleges increased. I also continued with AICTE inspections. I was teaching at IIM Bangalore.  
During this period , I was requested to teach a course on distributed operating systems at REC , Trichy.  Mr Murthy was in charge of the computer centre. There was no computer science faculty. So I visited Trichy  a couple of times to teach this course. I also attended their board of studies meetings for a few years. I found the situation in REC very disheartening.  There was a stay on recruitment and so there were no regular faculty members to teach the CS programs. The principal  had no interest in academics. He was the most unimpressive person I had met. But the students were top class. Murthy tried his best to run the program. In a few years, he built an iconic computer centre called Octagon.  Then he left for his PhD program at IIT Delhi.  I lost touch with him afterwards . I visited REC several times for several years afterwards till 2010 . 
DRDO ,Ministry of Defense decided to strengthen their talent pool . So they ran a sponsored MSc program in computer science. I started teaching many aspects of computer science. Prof Chandrasekaran of the physics department was in charge of this program. The selected students were placed in many DRDO labs after successfully completing the course. I had very good interactions with Prof Chandrasekaran and the students. I took my family once to Madurai. My son liked the guest house. I liked the program. But it didn't achieve its objectives. Most were not placed properly. So they lost interest and left the job. The program was discontinued. But I still remember one important thing that happened. One student from a rural area with poor knowledge of English did some good work for her project . It was a publishable material. We helped her to write a paper and it was published in a good journal. 
I attended the academic council meeting of Thiagarajar college during this period. As I said earlier I had been involved with several activities at TCE - board of studies meetings, academic council meetings, lectures , faculty selections etc-. 
During this period 1988 89, Prof Nityanandan, principal, TCE decided to run a conference on optimisation. I was a member of the organizing committee and an invited speaker. There was no direct train to Madurai then. I used to go  by bus directly or to  take the night train to Trichy and then   to Madurai by bus. Dr Vidyasagar was the chief guest for the valedictory function of the conference. He talked about logic. Since the college arranged a car to drop him at Trichy , I returned with him in the car to Trichy and then to bangalore by train.  
That was also  the period when my uncle Sankaran, my father's younger brother retired from service and he undertook a trip to Bangalore and Udupi and Mookambika temples. When he returned to Bangalore from the trip to dakshina kannada , he wasn't well. He went back to Kolkata. He passed away after a few months. He had jaundice, not detected and treated. Since he didn't have any children, I had to go to kolkata to perform his 13 day ceremonies.

The learning theory specifies a clear division of labor between nature and nurture: the genes first set up vast spaces of a priori hypotheses—and the environment then selects the hypotheses which best match the external world. The set of hypotheses is
genetically specified; their selection is experience-dependent.
Dehaene.          How We learn?
I met Mr R Vasu and Kumar from NPC institute of productivity at Madras. I started visiting Madras for lectures on energy and computers to their trainees. I visited their institute at Kilpauk for a few years. Vasu decided to conduct workshops in Delhi and kolkata.  I visited and lectured in both workshops. I saw some engineers using spreadsheets to calculate energy efficiencies- impressive. 
During 1988 , we conducted a workshop on Vedic mathematics. I organised the workshop at Sankar Mutt, Chamrajpet.  It was inaugurated by Mr Veerappa Moily , Minister for education and Dr Vidyasagar , from CAIR gave the keynote. The lectures were delivered by Prof Bhanumurthy based on his book. I got support from Prof R Narasimha, then director of NAL. As a part of the workshop, we had a brief half a day paper presentations by researchers. Prof MN Channabasappa from KREC was one of the paper presenters. I met him for the first time. We became good friends. I visited KREC and later also SIT Tumkur where he became principal after retirement. 

I visited KREC surathkal during this period of 1989 or 1990 to attend their board of studies meeting. I was invited by Prof MN Channabasappa,  head of the mathematics department . They were running a masters program in systems analysis and computer applications. 
I had attended the board of studies meetings in several universities and autonomous colleges and RECs for several years. My intention was to help them to improve their curriculum as well as to educate the faculty  to  learn the reasons, objectives and processes of curriculum development. I had interest in curriculum development from the beginning. Even when I was a student, I used to discuss a few questions bygging me like
What will be the future directions of engineering?
What should we learn to get equipped for the changes?
Is our curriculum modern and relevant?
What will be the directions in which India should move for reducing poverty?
What job should I choose- academic or industry?
I got some confusing answers. 
But I realised a few aspects then itself but not clearly.
1.our curriculum is dated, old and not in the future direction .
2.many new areas are emerging like computers and electronics. Engineering is complex.
3. There was no cross learnings between disciplines. Electrical engineering was more advanced in modeling and analysis but other disciplines were behind. But they didn't learn from electrical engineering. Mechanical was more focussed on design ,others were not. Civil concentrated on engineering practices and diversity like foundations, transportation, hydraulics etc. Each discipline is a fortress not to be encroached by others.
4. Nehru's  approach to the Indian economy was focussed towards socialism and heavy industries like Russia. We felt it would fail. 
5. We should not depend on what the college teaches us , what the university tells us about what to learn through their outdated  curriculum . We should learn a lot more by reading books and articles. 

So I cultivated a deep interest in self education by  reading many books and articles in the libraries .
 Once I joined iisc ,i  focussed on curriculum modernisation.  I understood the various processes of education and curriculum. I understood that the role of curriculum is central to education particularly for engineers ,  though deep and analytical  contents, delivery, experiments , library studies are important. If the curriculum is old, then there is no opportunity to become a good practicing engineer.
That is why I played a major role in converting iisc education system into a credit system based, and  electives based program. That was a great learning experience for me. For more than 10 years , I assisted iisc faculty in understanding the grading system and awarding grades correctly. So whenever I visited colleges - that is hundreds of times- and interacted with many young and senior faculty members, I used to ask them “ why don't you modernize your curriculum “.Most evaded responsibility and said that it was the university's job.
When I attended the board of studies meeting , it normally  operated on two principles.
1.copy from  an existing program in some other university.
2.choose those topics , which  the faculty is comfortable to teach. 
This copying without any understanding is evident in the current undergraduate AI program. It is without focus, without thinking , and not even looking at   today's AI. Most curricula miss balancing and applications. They focus on theory. So I was dissatisfied with most boards of studies. 
And I found this meeting at KREC different. 
Let me discuss in my next post.

“A shoe factory sends two marketing scouts to a region of Africa to study the prospects for expanding business. One sends back a telegram saying,
 
SITUATION HOPELESS STOP NO ONE WEARS SHOES
 
The other writes back triumphantly,
 GLORIOUS BUSINESS OPPORTUNITY STOP THEY HAVE NO SHOES”

The meeting organised by Prof MNC was different from the usual BOS meetings. Normally BOS meetings were held for about 3 hours and approve courses proposed by the university or college without substantive discussions on the curriculum or the focus of a program- who are the target audience, what do they become, where will they go etc ? 
Prof MNC wanted a three day meeting , my first surprise. I wasn't sure what he wanted to discuss in the meeting. So I went to Mangalore by bus . I took a local bus to KREC. I stayed in the guest house. Though I had visited and lectured at KREC before, this was my first stay there. I was shocked. There were no arrangements for food. I had to go to Surathkal for everything, but the bus service was good. KREC was on the highway and you got a bus every five minutes to Mangalore or Udupi .  I wasn't happy with the arrangements. 
When I went for the meeting,  I wasn't in a great mood, I was reluctant initially. But when I saw the academic commitment of Prof MNC  , my mood changed and I got involved in the meeting in full force as was my usual practice. Within a few minutes, MNC handed over the meeting to me. There were no other external experts. I was the only outsider. 
I started the discussion for the MTech Systems Analysis and computer applications program with the questions I posed above. I asked the question, “ what should be the unique value propositions of the program?” 
At that time, the concept of MCA was not known. Also most CS programs in universities were not focusing on applications. So I suggested that the program should focus on database applications. India had moved into data bases only by 1985. So it was an opportune time. Secondly , Oracle was getting traction and popularity. So we designed the curriculum accordingly. There were no protests, despite the fact that there were none to teach these courses in KREC. So my proposal was accepted. We spent the whole day deciding on the courses, contents and books. 
Next day, we took up for discussions the MTech program on Computer science and engineering run by MIT Manipal. Actually when this program started , I taught two courses like I did in MCE Hassan. Prof A Prabhakar was the principal. Prof S Rajaram,  my earlier  colleague in the EE department, IISc was the professor. So Rajaram came for the meeting. He had no idea about the program and no answers to my questions - what should be the focus of the program , what should we do to make it different from others- a value proposition? I suggested that while basic CS foundation courses can be taught in the first semester, we should focus on problem solving in the second semester. I taught at course on systems analysis and design and structured systems design. We used data flow methods. So I told him to start with that course. I suggested a good book for that purpose and told them to solve a large problem based on data flow approach. I was puzzled that most CS faculty were not aware of these techniques. Even my friend at TCS, Dr PSB Murty was  told by me about dataflow diagram, and he later told me that he talked to many faculty  and they had no idea of this approach.  It wasn't popular in India. I also suggested Yourdon's book on structured design as a second course. Again, the faculty were unfamiliar with these topics, but after a lot of persuasion from me and MNC, they agreed to my proposal. 
I also looked at one more program on an undergraduate course on computers run by the commerce teachers in a local Mangalore college. 
Prof MNC dropped me in the Hampanaghatta bus stand to return to bangalore. 
Let me talk about the two  followups  of this meeting.

“Teachers act as instructors who design and deliver instruction. They often act as trainers who provide exercises and drills to help improve their students’ skills. They also coach their students with supportive encouragement and appropriate comments. Finally, they act as tutors who often provide individual help. Teachers often use various educational approaches that will help their students learn in a variety of ways.”
Michael Blocher. Digital tools for knowledge construction

I was requested by KREC to give a series of lectures on databases to faculty and students of SACA program. Padmanabha from Shimoga was a student. He helped me a lot. Dr Mohan Hegde, Dr Desikachar,  prof Raghupati Bhat and most of the faculty of Mathematics department became my friends. I met Prof NR Shetty, principal during my visits. I helped them to select a new faculty - Hosmane was selected as a professor in SACA. I became a regular visitor to KREC from that time onwards. I met Prof  Balaveera Reddy from mechanical engineering . We went for an AICTE inspection to a college in kerala. I stayed in the KREC guest house regularly . The caretaker became  a good friend of mine. KREC built a few good  guest rooms and I used to stay there. 
My student from IIT Bombay,  Mr Murthy joined KREC in the computer science department. He was the first computer science trained faculty. 
The second followup deals with my course on systems analysis and design . I had a friend , Mr Satish Tanga from my early days with TCS . He left TCS and started a consultancy company in bangalore. I met him several times . He bought a design tool and wanted to conduct training to the institutions where he sold the tool. I became the main speaker in his program. I found Rajaram from MIT as a participant in one course. So I was happy He took my advice seriously and decided to provide a software engineering focus to their MTech program.  My association with Tanga lasted for about  two decades till 2000. 
As I said earlier, i was busy doing many inspections for AICTE  in many colleges  kerala , karnataka and Tamilnadu. 
During the early nineties , both Profs NR Shetty and Reddy moved to bangalore- Prof Shetty as Vice chancellor of Bangalore university and Prof Reddy as DTE. My involvements with both University and DTE increased.
