“The only way to be truly satisfied is to do what you believe is great work. And the only way to do great work is to love what you do. If you haven’t found it yet, keep looking. Don’t settle. As with all matters of the heart, you’ll know when you find it.”
—Steve Jobs

Let me recall the fact that we completed the 66 kv substation work and inaugurated it in 1987. Let us look at two incidents that happened afterwards. 
The first one was a major disaster. 
After the inauguration of our 66kv substation in 1987,  I didn't want to ask our maintenance team to take care of our 66kv substation.  My experience with our maintenance staff after the first reform in IISc power supply completed in 1968-69 with  the installation of the 11kv ring main wasn't  good. They were not competent to run this system.  They knew only some minor electrical  work in buildings.  So we decided to handover maintenance to BSES, the contractors of our 66 kv project . It was a major decision , but it has worked till now effectively. BSES  agreed and appointed Mr Gopal Rao as engineer in charge. Both Mr Hegde , manager and Mr Balakrishnan , senior engineer  who executed the project for us, both from the local office of BSES, were very helpful. 
Our new substation was running smoothly. Then a tragedy struck after some months of operations. 
They decided to clean a 11 kv  pole near the amenities block and close to our B block staff quarters as a routine maintenance work . As is the standard  practice, they switched off power to the line and started cleaning. Two workers were cleaning the bushings by climbing the pole. Unfortunately, some operator in the substation was not aware of this important maintenance work. So he switched on the 11 kv power supply  on that line by mistake and a disaster struck . Both electricians working on the pole were electrocuted. It became a major problem. By the time I went there  , Mr Nagaraj, our maintenance supervisor, was making a big public issue of it. Someone sent a photo to the newspapers which was published the next day prominently.  I met Prof CNR and apprised him about this. It brought bad publicity to IISc and the project  engineering department.
We called Both Hegde and Balakrishnan and   told them to compensate the families . I personally supervised the compensation. Most of the compensation was put as an FD in a bank and only a small amount was given as cash to the wife. 
The second incident was the failure of the 66kv/11kv transformer. 
Unfortunately, within 18 months , it failed . It was under warranty.  So Cromptons had a legal obligation to repair it . But they refused . I told them to go ahead with repairing  work so that we get the transformer at the earliest back in service. The transformer was shipped to Mumbai. Fortunately ,the old 11kv supply from KEB , near our gymkhana ,  was still in place. So we could switch back and IISc had a power supply , though the load had to be limited. 
I visited Cromptons and met the vice-president to resolve the payment terms. Fortunately,  while we went for inspection of the transformer during its manufacture and before installation at IISc in 1986,  I wasn't happy with the HV test. So, I had asked Thimme Gowda to put a rider in the test certificate that the full HV test wasn't done. So I showed him the test certificate. He was convinced and waived the cost of repair including materials. After about six weeks, the repaired transformer was reinstalled and put back to service. We restored the  66kv supply. 
So we are back to a reliable supply. 
Greetings
Dks.

“There is bias in the nature of technology that tilts it in certain directions and not others. All things being equal, the physics and mathematics that rule the dynamics of technology tend to favor certain behaviors. These tendencies exist primarily in the aggregate forces that shape the general contours of technological forms and do not govern specifics or particular instances. For example, the form of an internet—a network of networks spanning the globe—was inevitable, but the specific kind of internet we chose to have was not. The internet could have been commercial rather than nonprofit, or a national system instead of international, or it could have been secret instead of public.”
Kevin Kelly.    What technology wants.

Then three  incidents pertaining to the power system of IISc  happened .
Firstly, Mr Thimme Gowda went back to KEB. 
Another engineer , Mr. Prithviraj was deputed to IISc by KEB. He concentrated on only household consumer problems and was familiar with only that level of power supply. He didn't get involved with 66kv. Immediately after joining , he organised a meeting with representatives from all departments with a view to know their problems . I thought it was a good idea and I  was happy with him. He wanted to garland me in the meeting.  I refused saying that it wasn't our custom and practice. 
But soon I found his knowledge level was low and he created many problems. He couldn't get along with our estate officer, Mr Munivenkataswamy. He had a big fight with him. So he was sent back after a short stint at IISc.  
Mr Gururaj ,another KEB engineer was deputed to IISc in his place. He was working in IIM  Bangalore on deputation  and I had met him there when I used to go there to teach the computer course . He became another great friend of mine and continues to be my great friend even now.  He helped me in many circumstances. 
Secondly, I decided to have a second transformer So that continuity of supply can be ensured. I had to convince Prof CNR about it. In the end he agreed to it. So we floated a tender and bought a second transformer. The substation has been working satisfactorily till now. I am happy to have provided a long term good solution to IISc's power needs. 


Thirdly, after a year of operations, BSES decided that they won't continue the operations. So we needed to look for another agency. Mr Kumar,  of Kumar electricals limited was doing a lot of electrical contracts work at IISc  for some years. So we entrusted this work to him. They continued with the operation of the IISc power system for many decades. Later he did the re-engineering of vidhana soudha substation under my supervision and also Jayadeva institute of cardiology work. I had a long association with him. 

Our estate officer was also changed after a few years. Mr Chikkappa joined as  the estate officer on Mr KC Reddy's recommendations.  We became good friends. Our friendship continues till now.

18/10/24, 5:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 511
“Years ago, psychologists discovered that there are two routes to achievement: conformity and originality. Conformity means following the crowd down conventional paths and maintaining the status quo. Originality is taking the road less traveled, championing a set of novel ideas that go against the grain but ultimately make things better.
 Of course, nothing is completely original, in the sense that all of our ideas are influenced by what we learn from the world around us. We are constantly borrowing thoughts, whether intentionally or inadvertently. We’re all vulnerable to “kleptomnesia”—accidentally remembering the ideas of others as our own. By my definition, originality involves introducing and advancing an idea that’s relatively unusual within a particular domain, and that has the potential to improve it.”
Adam Grant.      Originals

While I was continuing with AICTE visits to engineering colleges for approvals- for renewals and starting new ones-, I got more new activities added to my workload. 
The Institution of Engineers had a procedure for memberships - it  approved the BE degree awarded by colleges for recognition of the college and award of memberships to their students. This was done through  a visit by a committee to the colleges and inspect their lab facilities. I got involved in this. I visited several colleges in Mumbai , Pune , Nanded, Nagpur, Shiggaon, Badnera, and many other cities for this purpose. 
  I was also the chair of the computer engineering board of the institution.  This meant I had two sets of travels - one for the computer engineering board meetings and another for the inspections of colleges for approval by IE. In addition,  I attended the annual convention of the computer engineering board. I also gave keynote addresses and talks at many of the conferences of the institution and engineers day programs. I attended and gave a talk on “agricultural residues and their uses “ at the agricultural engineering convention held at Jabalpur. 
I participated in the discussions conducted by Doordarshan on “the role of engineers”  every year on sept 15th , the engineers day celebrated by the institution of engineers.   After some time,  this program was to be done in kannada and still I participated in it and spoke in kannada. Mrs Rukmani,  the director,  talked to me and I along with the Doordarshan staff made a short 10 minute program on the environment. I quoted chamakam there. 

In addition, I conducted two major conferences  for the Institution of Engineers - one on “the future directions of  communications “ in association with Bangalore Doordarshan, another on microprocessor based design with NDRF. I wanted to get Prof U R Ananthamurthy, author of the novel Samskara , who was the vice chancellor of Mahatma Gandhi university,  Kottayam at that time ,in the early nineties. He agreed to come at  first   , But later,  he decided against it when I said that we didn’t have a budget for his trip to bangalore.  We got Prof U R Rao to inaugurate the conference. His speech was disappointing. He didn't give futuristic directions to communications but talked about the past and even defended the S band and C band that was used by the department of space against the use of K, Ka, Ku bands for communications. He wasn't aware of the higher gigahertz frequencies.  Also we wanted to look at the opening up of communications to the private sector and the use of computers in communications. Most speakers had no idea about the future of radio, TV and print media. Mobile was still not on the Indian horizon at that time. But ISRO was putting into orbit communications satellites at regular intervals , thereby providing a number of channels for broadcasting .

“If you "understand  something in only one way, then you scarcely understand it at all—because when you get stuck, you'll have nowhere to go. But if you represent something in several ways, then when you get frustrated enough, you can switch among different points of view, until you find one that works for you! Accordingly, when we design machines to mimic our minds—that is, to create Artificial Intelligences—we'll need to make sure that those machines, too, are equipped with sufficient diversity: If a program works in only one way, then it gets stuck when that method fails. But a program that has several ways to proceed could then switch to some other approach, or search for a suitable substitute. “
Marvin Minsky.      Emotion machine 


Let me look at another major activity that was  initiated by the department of electronics, government of India, on computer training. 
At that time there were several thousands of computer training institutes in the country. There were no standards. Most were not even employing competent trainers, didn't have enough PCs for training and their training programs were dated. 
When Datamatics started it's training programs in bangalore, they had a strong concern for quality. They produced a lot of teaching materials and got experienced people to teach. That is why I was part of their Fortran, Autocoder and Cobol training programs from 1971 to 1978. It was a satisfying experience for me to train hundreds of engineers in our industries on Fortran and other languages. Also there were severe scarcity of Fortran programmers at that time. I was the only person with good programming experience even at IISc and helped many not only in IISc,  but also in many organizations like NAL, ISEC,  CMTI , CPRI, electricity boards , NGEF etc. So I felt that training institutions were needed to play an important role to train thousands of programmers needed by the country in the coming decades. So I supported them. 
But by the mid eighties,  the situation became bad. To give an example of the degradation of training qualities , let me give an example. A neighbor of mine with minimal knowledge of programming and no experience ran a training institute in his house. He was the sole trainer. He had one or two PCs. He gave talks but didn't allow students to use computers and write programs. Small programs were written in notebooks by the trainees, that is all the practical usage. 
To top it all , many such institutions advertised that the students joining them  should keep their passport ready. It implied that most got jobs abroad after a few months training. Even reputed institutions did this kind of advertising. So in 1986 I wrote in Times of India an article entitled “ computer alchemists” revealing the sad state of computer training institutions. 
So the department of electronics set up a centre - DOEACC - Department of electronics accreditation of computer courses- . It accredited training institutions across India and also conducted examinations for different levels of computer competencies  . It also awarded certificates. So I inspected many training institutes in Bangalore for this accreditation for a few years. The initiative lost its zeal in a few years after it's initiator - Brig. Sundaram retired.

“We can’t stop artificial intelligences and robots from improving, creating new businesses, and taking our current jobs. It may be against our initial impulse, but we should embrace the perpetual remixing of these technologies. Only by working with these technologies, rather than trying to thwart them, can we gain the best of what they have to offer. I don’t mean to keep our hands off. We need to manage these emerging inventions to prevent actual (versus
hypothetical) harms, both by legal and technological means. We need to civilize and tame new inventions in their particulars. But we can do that only with deep engagement, firsthand experience, and a vigilant acceptance.”
Kevin Kelly.      The inevitable.
Another big controversy erupted in the nineties.  The government was experimenting and building microprocessor based standalone voting machines at ECIL, Hyderabad.   During this period in the early nineties,  the government was proposing the use of electronic voting machines in our general elections. It is still a whipping boy even now. Though ECIL developed a prototype by the late seventies and it was used in an election in the early eighties, Supreme Court ruled  its use in elections as illegal. Later the laws were amended and it was PV Narasimha Rao's government that decided to use EVMs in elections. This led to a massive protest . Mr VP Singh who was a Prime minister earlier  made a public attack with a demo on the manipulations possible with an EVM. But he didn't use an EVM for his public demo , but a PC with a program developed and accessible openly .  He got a programmer to show the manipulations. 
I was approached to defend the EVM. I agreed and visited BEL. I met Mr Rangarajan, - known as Sujatha , a Tamil writer- who was in charge of production of the EVMs . I had met him in three or four meetings / panel discussions before and knew him. 
To digress a bit, one discussion meeting I attended with him was in the local chapter of IETE along with Prof Sonde. The topic was the future of PCs.  I talked about the popularity of PCs due to its word processing capability.  Many misunderstood my intention and said it should be used for scientific work only. Rangarajan came to my rescue saying that he uses a PC extensively for typing his fiction and that he found it very convenient. See the mindset of these ,most senior communications engineers, that a computer is a precious tool and must be used only for important scientific work and not for frivolous typing. I told them also that we had been popularising kannada word processing software. If you look at today , you find that PCs are mostly used for typing , messaging and video talking by most people, very little scientific work takes place in a PC. Of course today's PCs are capable of doing a lot of scientific work and are used by scientists extensively. 
Back to the EVMs,  I gave a press conference rebutting the claims of VP Singh. I said “ this EVM is not a PC. It is a standalone machine. It can’t be programmed. The operational program is burnt in a ROM. So it can’t be changed. It is not connected to a network so remote manipulation is not possible. It is better than the manual system as it avoids booth capturing “. I am not familiar with the changes done and the configuration of today's machines . But my views are correct as proven all these years by clean elections and new procedures adopted to improve the election mechanism. EVMs are still going through a difficult path, never accepted by all.

“In our new era, processes trump products.
This shift toward processes also means ceaseless change is the fate for everything we make. We are moving away from the world of fixed nouns and toward a world of fluid verbs. In the next 30 years we will continue to take solid things—an automobile, a shoe—and turn them into intangible verbs. Products will become services and processes. Embedded with high doses of technology, an automobile becomes a transportation service, a continuously updated sequence of materials rapidly adapting to customer usage, feedback, competition,innovation, and wear. Whether it is a driverless car or one you drive, this transportation service is packed with flexibility, customization, upgrades, connections, and new benefits.”
Kevin Kelly.         Inevitable

“What a person needs to understand is the observation made by sociologists and economists that there is a direct link between the emergence of an information-centric economy and the expansion of the service sector of the same economy at the expense of the agricultural and industrial sectors. In other words, as a percentage of the total economy of an advanced industrial society, providing services increased regardless of whether or not the economy as a whole grew in size. Again, depending on whose numbers you consult, in the U.S., for instance, the service sector is sometimes recorded as high as 75 to 80 percent of the Gross National Product, with lower percentages for Western Europe and East Asia.”
21st century business.     James Cortada


I thought that the nineties would reduce my involvement and reduce my workload.  But the opposite happened. My involvement increased. 
The computer scene changed a lot. PCs were available at lower costs. Many manufacturers supplied PCs. Slowly , many started assembling PCs after liberalization by the NarasimhaRao government.  So we had a dispute between branded and unbranded PCs. 
The demand for PCs increased in the departments of the Karnataka government .  Some departments like commercial taxes went for major computerization projects. That increased the demands for servers, PCs,  printers etc. So  the Karnataka government technical advisory panel decided to introduce rate contracts for PCs, printers and other accessories. The sub committee was entrusted with this work. We needed to talk to vendors and arrive at acceptable prices for PCs,  memory, disks, printers etc. It became a major activity. We needed to revise the prices at regular intervals as and when PC prices were reduced. So my workload increased. We standardize the configurations . We set up a standard procedure for the fixation process . We introduced two separate  parts - technical and commercial- tendering for the first time. Our prices were the lowest in the country. 
There was an instance when DST, government of India,  wanted to buy PCs and a server. They got a quote of around rs.8 lakhs for a server. Our rate contract price for the same server was around rs.3 lakhs. So they told me to procure it through KSCST. 
Our process of rate contracts became a standard for purchase by many government associated groups. 
KSCST decided to get my approvals for purchases of computers and accessories. Ramesh from KSCST worked with me. All KSCST purchases were done after my approval. I had some problems with some people. 
KPC, Karnataka power corporation,  requested my help to buy computers, set up their computer centre and even recruit officers for the computer centre. Mr Suryanarayanan , company secretary , R Balasubramanian and Nellaiappan became my good friends . I had many interactions with KPC. 
Karnataka land army corporation requested me to assist them regarding their computer purchases. Mr Lucose Vallatharai ,  IAS was the MD then. He even got me felicitated by Mr JH Patel, then Chief Minister of Karnataka. 
I had an interesting encounter with the Bangalore dairy. They had outsourced their accounting and payroll work to a small company on a monthly retainer. The company gave a new proposal - Dairy should buy a new computer, and pay double the amount for the company to do its monthly work. I laughed at the proposal and vetoed it. 
Later KSRTC requested  my help on many matters regarding their computer activities. I met several MDs and EDs of KSRTC and interacted with them for more than ten years. I remember Mr Srikumar IPS, and Mr Bhaskar Rao IPS . 
Let me discuss some more activities in the next posts.

“In addition to technology’s ability to satisfy (and create) desires, and to occasionally save labor, it did something else. It brought new opportunities. Right before my eyes I saw online networks connect people with ideas, options, and other people they could not possibly have met otherwise. Online networks unleashed passions, compounded creativity, amplified generosity. At the very cultural moment when pundits declared that writing was dead, millions began writing online more than they ever had written before. Exactly when the experts declared people would only bowl alone, millions began to gather together in large numbers. Online they collaborated, cooperated, shared, and created in myriad unexpected ways.”
Kevin Kelly.       What technology wants

The nineties saw a major improvement in IT. Computers started transforming into IT. The introduction of computer networking based on ethernet and Arpanet and the introduction of internet , world wide web and html  brought in a new changed era. The first effect was email. IISc  started operating email services using ernet, a government designed network connecting all IITs, IISc,  and later government departments with an external gateway. Later dialup was introduced by BSNL. So email slowly picked up in india. We used email to connect with faculty in western universities and even students. I couldn't connect with people in India for more than a decade. I needed to use regular postal services only to reach people in India. 
Only in the early 21st century,  broadband came in. Then connectivity became easy for most metro people. While the internet came slowly in India, LAN picked up traction in many organizations. I took responsibility for creating local area networks in Vidhana soudha , KREC, CPRI, BMSCE, SJIC, and many engineering colleges. 
Fortunately , as a futuristic action ,most colleges started information Engineering courses. We also started the BCA program in a big way in karnataka and I approved the BCA run by many colleges after inspection . 
By the late nineties , many industries and institutions had both local networks , campus networks and broadband. Wide area network also picked up pace by then . Most banks moved into both LAN and WAN and operated them well. 
So by 2000, we had a good IT architecture developed  in many  industries , banks , offices of governments due to NIC, engineering colleges, research institutions and all IT companies . We got a reasonable depth of IT culture by 2000. While we were good at IT use and IT services, we were weak in IT components , devices and software development.  That state continues even now.

“For good or ill, forming crowds comes so naturally to our species that it is even seen as a fundamental political right. It is codified in the First Amendment to the U.S. Constitution, which notes that “the right of the people peaceably to assemble, and to petition the Government for a redress of grievances” shall not be infringed by law. The right to assemble is similarly encoded in constitutions of countries all over the world, from Bangladesh to Canada to Hungary to India.2 As with the capacity for empathy, the inclination to assemble into groups and deliberately choose friends and associates is part of our species’ universal heritage.”  

Nikolas Christakis       blueprint 

The 21st century started with people centric technology applications. Computers and communications were developing hand in hand. Communications embraced  digital. 
While common people didn't take to emails  easily ,they picked up both text and video messaging fast. A very large population today uses WhatsApp,  skype,  Google,  easily. Many use social networks. Instagram has become popular. Many use twitter . We are spoilt by choices. 
While in the late  20th century , our computer activities  concentrated mostly on the use of computers and services by industries and organizations , the 21st century saw the focus shift to computer use by   people. With mobile services and smartphones, democratization of technology was achieved . It has reached most people - billions - literate or not, wealthy or poor , educated or not, professional or laborer, -  through these many avenues - social networks, messaging, video talks, youtube,  net banking, ecommerce  etc- of fast communications. These changes have provided a great source for knowledge and awareness dissemination. All of us   know what is happening globally, how things work, what to buy and where and how, what to watch and when and where to watch, how to be healthy, what to eat,etc . So today knowledge and learning is democratized, reaching all people  at low costs. 
Today's world is running on data . Data is expected to be  at our command ,but it is also enslaving us, and changing our habits. One should be aware of the implications.  
AI - artificial intelligence is also reaching our doorsteps now. The latest - genAI- will get us contents from across the world, put it together in an acceptable form for our understanding. Many jobs will become redundant, many will become easier . Alexa and Siri talk to us and execute simple commands. AI will not only do many  jobs automatically with robots and drones , but also will make us more informed, more knowledgeable,  more understood and more intelligent people, if we are careful . Else we end up as its slave. We can’t ignore AI. We need to become AI literate. 
I feel sad that we are not taking advantage of the learning facilities available on tap. Even literate, educated and professionals don't take full advantage of the various knowledge programs. Serious computer science lectures by reputed and renowned faculty from the world are available free now. We need to put our children through this alternate education and learning route. I have seen the impact of these media  on the intellect of my grandson, Neel.  He acquired enormous knowledge through these routes , not just through schools. It opened my eyes definitely to the vast unused resources out there.

“ in 1802, Johann Beckmann, an economics professor at Gottingen University in Germany, gave this ascending force its name. Beckmann argued that the rapid spread and increasing importance of the useful arts demanded that we teach them in a “systemic order.” He addressed the techne of architecture, the techne of chemistry, metalwork, masonry, and manufacturing, and for the first time he claimed these spheres of knowledge were interconnected. He synthesized them into a unified curriculum and wrote a textbook titled Guide to Technology (or Technologie in German), resurrecting that forgotten Greek word. He hoped his outline would become the first course in the subject. It did that and more. It also gave a name to what we do.”
Kevin Kelly 
I was involved with the computerization activities in many organizations. Let me look at some issues in computer usages in some organizations in India. 
Indian railways came up with a passenger reservations system and were looking for a similar system for goods. So CRIS approached me to help them. I gave my advice on their hardware purchases. 
The sizing of servers was a guess work at that time and even now. Most organizations had no proper past data . They can’t estimate their workload. The  goods booking problem is new and so they couldn't estimate the workload.  They first considered the data general mini computer sold by Patni computers and got no clarity. So they met me- Mr Thoopal, Mr Ramakrishnan , and Mr Rao. I said that the datageneral mini is not as good as digital VAX mini from design point of view. I also agreed with them that Patni didn't look very professional. I got the same impression when I visited Patni in 1982 at the invitation of my student , Lakshminarasimha  and they all - Narayana murthy, Nandan , Kris, and others - took me for lunch. I had discussions with the CRIS group and recommended the VAX mini of digital. Digital had a manufacturing base in Bangalore and Mr Som  Mittal was in charge. They agreed with my suggestion. Then they requested me to give a series of lectures to their staff on computers and I gave about six lectures. During my stay there, I got an invitation from the CSIO lab of CSIR to give a talk in their conference. I went there with T Viswanathan who was in INSDOC at that time. I stayed in Lodhi hotel in Delhi which had a woodlands restaurant.  I saw Mr Gundu Rao, former CM of Karnataka there. 
 I encountered this problem of hardware sizing on many occasions in many organizations  .  Let me look at some examples.

“ Human nature contains much that is admirable, including the capacity for love, friendship, cooperation,and learning, all of which allows us to form good societies and fosters understanding among people everywhere. Mr Joshi, retired GM, Indian bank,  comments on sizing . 
“Sir , you advised  us on sizing of our Banks hardware , since banking transaction increase has never got been predicted . Very difficult to imangine organic and fresh new transitions. Also in data available. 
Your advise was very very helpful and Bank has been benefited tremendously.”
My response 
Thanks. We did a stress test to check the throughput. In such cases, I always look for the queue buildup to check the dynamics. 
I insisted on a stress test for PNB also. I have conducted several such tests for sizing for many organizations. 
Let me return to sizing history. 
APMC Gadag was a well managed one and Mr D R Patil was the chair of the APMC. Mr D R Patil was a very enterprising and innovative textiles entrepreneur. Mr Jayakumar Anagol talked about the annual report brought out by the all india organization of textile manufacturers of which he was the chair. He wanted to computerize some operations of this APMC in the mid eighties. It was a pioneering effort. 
Mr Vasan was running a Consulting company in Malleswaram . Amarnath Bhat, KSCST  systems analyst, also joined him. I inaugurated their office. Vasan was providing IT support to APMC .

When we bought computers for APMC , Gadag  , there weren't many servers. Even the server concept itself  was evolving. Companies either had a main frame which was very expensive - close to a crore of rupees and needed large infrastructure and human talent to run it. Or the choice of many companies was a mini. As I said earlier, both VAX and data general were popular then in india. Indian manufacturing came up with TDC 12, 16, 32 by ECIL later. They were PDP11 based minis. But they were not popular. So stand alone higher end PCs were sold by Zenith, HCL, ORG and NELCO. Many organizations bought these computers. 
 At that time - mid eighties- , Eiko came up with its PCs at a very low cost . The market prices of the computers of Zenith, HCL were very high - four to five times that of the Eiko computers. It was a great strategy by Eiko. But their machine was smaller than the Zenith or HCL machines. ORG called its computer a mini. 
. Mr D R Patil  had a good clarity on a pain point of the farmers and so defined a limited purpose for computerisation - to reduce the turnaround time for farmers visiting the APMC to sell their produce. The time taken was about two days for a farmer  to bring his produce, weigh it , get it graded, participate in an auction, get the money from the buyer and return to his village. DR Patil thought that he needed to reduce this two day wait time to less than a day and introduced this automation. It was partial automation. But as the cost of the computers were low , we used the same computers for data entry , totalling of available produce from all farmers ,  vendor quotes, selection , accounting and settlements. The principle was to divide the jobs into many small ones and do each in an Eiko computer. Since we selected a lower cost computer with lower capacity, sizing was to be done carefully. We divided the tasks and used several  computers.  I inaugurated their APMC  computer centre .  It worked well for some time. But the system got overloaded quickly .

According to Kelly, people are just bit players; they only have a walk-on role in the digital revolution. It is an illusion to think that people shape technology; in reality, it is the other way round. Kelly agrees that technology needs people before it can move on to the next stage of its development, but this does not mean that people are the key factor. We are just the extras, the minor actors in the drama that will allow technology to become what it must. It is an unusual philosophy,
Kevin Kelly 

Let me continue with another involvement of mine on sizing.
  Before I do that, I should talk about my involvement in a fourth dimension of activities. Most know my educational , academic and research activities. Later , many saw my involvement in computerization , IT and allied industries based activities. By 1980, a third dimension was my work on ecology and environment which started with Bedthi hydro project , moved on to my government job to my study of Uttara kannada , Tumkur villages energy planning activities and continued with carrying capacity studies for Dakshina Kannada district. I also had involvement with social activities. 
But I was involved for about four decades in another sector that not many are aware of- finance and banking. 

I gained my first glimpse of accounting and ledgers and cheques , drafts and mail transfer when I was in primary school - in the third , fourth standard days. My uncle was continuing his father's  business of money lending as a means of supplementary income. Since I used to sit with my uncle and assist him during all his activities,  I became familiar with p note, interest rates, registration of loan deeds etc during that period itself . 
Secondly, he organised and conducted two major annual functions- Sri Rama Navami  and Sankara Jayanthi during summer time in April and May months. These were supported financially by donations from hundreds of people living all over the country and also in Singapore.  He was very meticulous with accounting of receipts and expenses and produced a complete balanced sheet - a very detailed one - at the end of each function, printed it and sent by post to all. So he built a ledger for receipts city wise and group wise as well as for expenses with a well defined groupings of expenses. I helped him in maintaining the accounts and creation of the ledger. So I knew some aspects of accounting internals ,budgeting and finance management. I learnt the importance of integrity and transparency from him. 

My knowledge of banking procedures like credit, debit, loans, drafts, savings and deposit accounts, ledger , tallying , etc started from my school days, quite early. I started having an account and managing it when I was in PUC. I got money through MT. I continued my banking from those days with a good external view. 
But my internal and detailed views started in 1972 when I started working on online transactions, Monte carlo simulation , performance measurements etc. I was teaching computer simulation then and did the bank's transaction processing simulations with  a set of cooked up data. I got my first  experience of  a computerised multi branch connected banking exposure at Westminster Bank in Manchester in 1974. I opened an account in one branch and operated that account from another branch.

28/10/24, 4:11 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 521
“When George W. Bush gained in the polls, Republicans rated him as more desirable, but so did Democrats, who were already preparing justifications for the anticipated status quo. The same happened when Al Gore’s likelihood of success increased: Both Republicans and Democrats judged him more favorably. Regardless of political ideologies, when a candidate seemed destined to win, people liked him more. When his odds dropped, they liked him less.
 Justifying the default system serves a soothing function. It’s an emotional painkiller: If the world is supposed to be this way, we don’t need to be dissatisfied with it. But acquiescence also robs us of the moral outrage to stand against injustice and the creative will to consider alternative ways that the world could work.”
 Adam Grant                        Originals 

Let me look at my  initial involvement  in the banking  field. 
I had been involved for a very long time , several decades long till now,   with the use of computers in banking , fintech and branchless banking, a term coined by Mr BSamba Murthy, ex CMD, corporation bank and ex director,  IDRBT.  Actually the first major activity that I saw was in 1981 looking at the complications of interbank reconciliations being carried out by TCS for many banks. It opened my eyes to several aspects.
The complexity of a real life problem . It involved many aspects - sorting, searching, data cleaning , resolution , handling large volume data , batch processing. 
The need for reducing cpu usage through several optimisation processes like creating a combined key - it was a forerunner for the IFSC code used by banks today- , prioritizing transactions , etc. 
The complexity of software - in those early days of computing, it was a very large program. A big team was working on it. Its maintenance was a big problem.
Importance of documentation. Team members were changing frequently. A good document enables both the induction of new members into the team and in making the requisite changes in the software.
Project management . This was a tough task - cost estimation, getting the right mix of people, training them, rotating people, delivering on time. I saw a lot of software engineering concepts evolving in this project by TCS.
 TCS realized by then that banking is an important sector for them and focussed their attention to banking . They recruited many with domain expertise in banking in addition to both computer science and CA persons. 

My second involvement was creating awareness of technology to bankers. I gave several talks to bank staff. Banks by the early 1980s decided to look at computer technology and its introduction to handle big tasks in the bank. Some had seen the TCS work on interbank reconciliations.  So at the first instance , the banks outsourced some tasks to TCS and others. Later they decided to train some officers in COBOL programming.  It was a great and wise move. We ,at CSI , also conducted two or three courses  on COBOL programming exclusively for the officers of banks. Mr Ganapathi subramanian used to handle a majority of lectures. The course was held in the classroom in my department. 
Ganapathi was very good. He passed away after a few years due to cancer. I lost a wonderful person. 
The third involvement was wading into a controversy. RBI set up a committee under Prof Rangarajan for computerization in banks. There were two sequential reports of the  committee. 
I found several aspects there deserving attention. 
One was a serious one. The committee used CMC to specify a computer suitable for a branch. CMC wrote a specification for a computer suitable  for banks, a good job. But it over specified the specs. At that time , India was producing small mini computers by a few companies like Wipro, HCL and Sunray. CMC was selling the Sunray computers  . It was based on a US company model - Charlie river- . It used a different internal bus - VME bus. The specifications done by the Rangarajan committee included VME bus  as a requisite component of a computer. This upset all others. The pure Indian manufacturers used multibus. They can’t supply their minis to banks. So they were agitated. I also felt that this specification was not correct. There was no need to include VMEbus in the specs  and exclude Multibus.  I also felt that the sizing was arbitrary. 
I felt that branch automation is wrong. The first job should be a whole bank looking at creating a data base . 
Fortunately,  during the implementation stage, banks were allowed to select their systems. 
Let us look at my involvement with Canara bank for branch automation next.

Beckmann - “There is no communication between machines without extruded copper nerves of electricity. There is no electricity without mining veins of coal or uranium, or damming rivers, or even mining precious metals to make solar panels. There is no metabolism of factories without the circulation of vehicles. No hammers without saws to cut the handles; no handles without hammers to pound the saw blades. This global-scale, circular, interconnected network of systems, subsystems, machines, pipes, roads, wires, conveyor belts, automobiles, servers and routers, codes, calculators, sensors, archives, activators, collective memory, and power generators—this whole grand contraption of interrelated and interdependent pieces forms a single system.”
Kevin Kelly.       What technology wants.

Aravind TCS has his recollections of early days in TCS.
“Nice recollection of the evolution of banking computerization. Vividly explained, Sir. Thanks. 
I only recall as a trainee, we were asked to work on Inter Branch Reconciliation, which as you rightly said was complex. Data cleaning by merely looking at blank fields, getting banks to look into their records, rectify the data with the log file was certainly time consuming. Then sorting was the next issue. Sorting was taking a lot of mainframe CPU time and we had to work out ways to optimize time for it, since CPU time was being billed. Huge volumes of data in several tapes was another challenge.  Production support of IBR was a great eye opener for young people like me during those days, to learn about so many things about computers, which became formalized as years went past.”

Actually, we shunned formalism for a long time. Even documentation was not streamlined. I was shocked by the way  many in  TCS were correcting COBOL codes almost every day. Agility was existing even then. But these corrections were not fully recorded. It was a struggle. But the insistence on reduction of compilation helped.
Database was not used, only tape based files. Computer time was at a premium then due to stupid government policies of not allowing imports of computers.

30/10/24, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 523
Let me look at the branch automation issue. 
The banks started branch automation based on the  Rangarajan committee report during the late eighties. 
Canarabank requested me to advise them on their purchase. They had already selected a branch in Delhi- parliament street- for automation  and given a contract to Infosys to develop the software required for the automation of branch activities.
 I was against branch automation . I had seen better automation in 1973 itself in the banks in England . I saw fully  networked banking operations  in 1987 in Bangkok. And it was in the late eighties and early nineties that our Indian banks were initiating branch automation activities in a small way, not bank automation. 
But the grandiose name given to this branch automation by the Rangarajan committee was TBA- is it total bank automation , or total branch automation. The committee, I felt,  was ignorant of tech use in banks in other countries.
I felt that this partial automation will not be beneficial , total bank automation was needed with a centralised database - that is the key . I had another objection that the banks were seeing a simpler function of customer transactions , though it was very important .Computerization was a low priority for banks then.
I didn't want to express my views to the managers of Canara bank , but I agreed to assist them. Normally, I play a major lead role in the  projects with which I was involved. That was my way of putting effort and seeing that the project is executed properly. The  bank group had some young enthusiastic and aggressive officers. Only the two seniors ,the DGM and CM played a mature role. But expert knowledge was  lacking. Most technology decisions by banks were mostly  intuitive and so my expertise needed to be applied with caution. So , I played a subdued role in this project.  The bank’s group of officers  and Infosys development team  were both new to the task. Both were learning. The bank had a good, enthusiastic group of about five officers and they participated with vigour in all meetings. Infosys also had a good team led by Nandan at that time. 
The software was under development.
We were to decide on a suitable hardware to run the Infosys  software and provide faster services without interruption. But they were working in the dark without any data or discovery. I had at least a lot of exposure to sizing computers for IISc,  IIT Bombay and many other places and was familiar with the methodologies used for sizing an appropriate computer using simulation. 
Most people even now have no idea of the sizing methodology. Hardware sizing depends on
1.operating systems and applications.  software features
2.workload say tps 
3.software Capability on throughputs.
4.concurrency levels
5. 24by7 availability
6. Data management capabilities
7.Latencies
8.communications delays 
9 Business continuity
10.types of machines available.
Normally sizing is done through simulations of increasing workloads.
Regarding the state  of mini computers in India, the country  was not producing many computers at mini range. The minis were small minis. One of the known minis was produced by ORG. So the bank's young officers strongly supported the ORG machine. They had no basis for that decision. I was reluctant. I told them that you are doing an experiment, in a small unknown territory and you want to make it a success. ORG did not have proven reliable operations. Recovery of data by these systems was very poor leading to several hours , thereby resulting in a loss of customer service or the necessity of  resorting to manual operations. I told them about the example of Indian airlines computerization and how it created chaos in airports. So I suggested a low end Vax machine.  While the senior team was convinced about my arguments , the youngsters felt that it was a waste of money. Finally after a lot of hesitation , Nandan also suggested Vax . The bank decided on a Vax machine. 
All the youngsters moved up and got good careers , one joining Infosys,  two others joining other banks on a higher grade . They did well in their jobs.

“Leadership in the digital transformation world is purposeful in its approach to creating a long term sustainable change. Change efforts will be designed in a top-down approach in some cases. And they will be constantly refined from the bottom-up.
The communication approach of digital executives is more like a campaign rather than a carefully refined command and control approach. Change activities are more likely to be a refine and reinforce approach.
The primary communication focus of the executives is one of why change is important rather than what is changing.”
Michael Wilson. Digital business transformation

Another instance where I got involved with the sizing/ selection of servers   happened in the mid nineties around 1994 or 95. 
The admissions for BE program in karnataka was done by a computer based ranking program . It was done by the karnataka government computer centre for a very long time - two or more decades. The ranking was done by the software and  seat allocation by DTE. While a lot of care was taken to conduct the data preparation and software run , the manual allocation was not effective. So the computer program was modified to rank and allocate colleges to individual candidates as per their choice list. This definitely was a great improvement. But it didn't work well.  The entire allotment process went through many iterations and took months. That was due to the students change requests and multiple options like JEE, Pilani, etc. 
So the government took a very far reaching and pioneering decision to do allotments with the direct involvement of the candidates and a quick computerized allotment and admission. Mr Venkatesan, officer in charge of NIC in karnataka took the entire responsibility of conducting the allotment activities. Admissions were done online. He was very committed to this job and did a remarkable job. 
The government created a CET cell to conduct the exams and do the  admissions also.  DTE had no role in this. Dr Reddy was appointed as officer in charge of the cell. The entire operations were meticulously planned . 
The technical advisory panel at that time was chaired by Mr Ghoshal. I was the chair of its technical subcommittee.  We discussed in detail the computer requirements. The TAP had very detailed discussions going upto 8pm on one day. The main issue was the servers. Venkatesan wanted to use the just announced Pentium machines. I said it wasn't proven and I wasn't sure about its reliability.  There was no scientific assessment of sizing. Concurrency was low dealing with about 5 to 10 at a time.  Finally the committee approved pentium  computers for the project. 
Venkatesan put all his resources including NIC computers to conduct the face to computer operations. He completed the task very successfully in the first year itself. It was a highly dedicated effort by his team. The entire process was over in a month itself and got the appreciation of all.

01/11/24, 4:36 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 525
“This single treatise on the science of politics has been prepared mostly by bringing together the teaching of as many treatises on the science of politics as have been composed by ancient teachers for the acquisition and protection of the earth.”
Chanakya.       Arthasastra 

I was unhappy about adhocism adopted by many developers of software. 
India had and has  hundreds of companies developing software, most of them with a few programmers, but a few large ones . 
Software developers in india belong to the following categories
1.generic applications software developers . Examples are core banking development by TCS, Infosys , Oracle Financials etc. ERP developer like Ramco systems Marshall, loan module developers, local language processors etc. 
2.Proprietary software developers - this is a very large number of large and small units. They Custom built software for a client, and maintained it. 
3. Government software system developers , for Income tax, MCA, passport , etc. 
4. In House developers. Adhaar is a good example. 
5. System software developers like operating systems, compilers , databases etc. 
6. Small applications developers - accounting , payroll , admissions , etc . Thousands of small companies developed thousands of programs for many sectors and small and medium industries.  
There are many who maintain software and many who run software as a service. So it is a major industry in India. 
But the problem was that  the focus of these developers - they built software only to meet the functional requirements. But unfortunately, most didn't follow a good, well defined process. 
Further the bigger problem was that they thought that both normal offline software and online software follow the same principles. They didn't design for performance , growth, scaling etc. Actually, in the mid seventies, I taught a new course on real time systems and spent time explaining performance Issues. I followed Stimler’s book which had detailed calculations on performance. Queuing theory was introduced in operating systems by Prof Coffman of Penn state university.  
A software today deals with thousands or lakhs of concurrent processes , so design should be very different. But many including students and teachers use a mindset of building a batch processing ,functions only , software. They emphasize on the correctness of the results, but not on performance. 
This led to an interesting development. Moore’s law made processors gallop in speed and capacity and programs were executed faster. So developers never bothered about performance. There was a joke in the eighties and nineties - “what Grove gives, Gates takes it away. “ Grove is chief of Intel. Gates is Microsoft boss. So software design for performance is essential particularly for online systems. 
I got involved in many software performance problems. One of the major performance studies was done by TCS.  TCS demonstrated  to State Bank of India that their Bancs software will definitely handle the workload of SBI. Bala was in charge of this project. This software  is running successfully even now in SBI.
Let us see more software sizing in the next post.

“Before one creates knowledge, one ought to thoroughly study the existing knowledge available.
The world of knowledge is continuously evolving. It always grows. The existing knowledge is expanded with the help of every generation. Each generation adds its experiences and wisdom and contributes to the field of knowledge.”
Chanakya 
Mr Joshi, GM rtd. Indian  bank , recalls our Indian bank experience on performance based on tps requirement. 
“Sir , for Indian Bank with your guidance we insisted to bench mark the software performance on HP machines purchased by Bank. TCS was reluctant but ultimately they did at their cost. 
You were very helpful 
Regards”
My response
Yes. While I had no doubt with the performance of the  bancs software, I had doubts about the capability of the HP server to handle the transaction load. More specifically, I wanted to check the maximum number of concurrent processes the HP server can handle. I knew IBM servers had a good concurrency design. I also knew that Sun processors used by PNB handled large concurrency.  TCS got an expert from Australia to conduct the tests. We inspected the results. In all such stress tests, we check both the queue length and concurrency management. OS needs to be specially  designed to handle lakhs of concurrent processes. 
Let me look at another example. 
PNB had core banking in about half of  its total branches.When PNB wanted to bring all its branches into core banking, they had discussions with Infosys.Infosys wasn't sure about the capacity of Finacle and its handling capability. After a strong stance by the CMD to Nandan, he agreed.  So we insisted on a stress test. It was done in England. Then we moved in to get core banking to all branches. It was a revealing situation for me. 
While Mr Chakraborty, CMD of PNB was clear that core banking means that all branches should be networked, transactions and customer data of all branches should be in a central server . He felt that the partial implementation of core banking in only some branches was not correct. But to my surprise ,I found  that most senior bankers in many banks didn't have that clarity. They were worried about customer reactions , rightly so. 
Even when it was decided to conduct the stress test , the concerned officers were worried about the details of the software, and not on the performance. 
When the RBI mandated that one can withdraw cash from any bank's ATM, the main switch couldn't handle the workload. Most transactions were rejected . Mr Sambamurthy, director of IDRBT,  called me. We discussed with the vendors and they initiated the   necessary corrective measures. 
Similarly IBMs MQ5 queue handler failed when NEFT load of usage increased. The queue size was not adequate to meet the increased demands and the queues  were overflowing. So IBM upgraded to MQ6 to solve the overflow problem. 

When a system becomes a connected one, we need to do sizing of the network also - routing capacity, bandwidth, latency etc. 
While the design process for a wide area network connecting many cities in the country is easy , LAN design needs to be done carefully looking at many parameters. I spent more than six months to design a LAN network for vidhana soudha and MS building. 
Network design is another major activity , I did for many institutions including KREC, CPRI, SJIC, BMSCE, JNNCE and many others. 
In all these , my biggest problem was educating the vendors. We conducted a full day workshop in BMS college of engineering for many engineering college faculty on designing the network. Mr Prabhu and Chowdhury of PAM networks helped me in running the workshop.

Vinod vyasulu , my friend for close to five decades , comments on vendors' behaviours. 

“It's interesting that you say your biggest problem was educating vendors. To a very large extent, in computers, it was driven by vendors who claimed they  represented Technology and they knew what the client wanted. In small organisations, there was pressure from accountants, funders, customers to get work done on a computer. With no knowledge of software or hardware these organisations fell prey to vendors whose only goal was to make a sale.”
My response 
You are right. There are many problems with vendors.
Their knowledge level is low. They know a little bit about their product. They don’t know the capabilities, impacts, and implications. So I had to tell them and teach them. 
They are misguided. They will sell the moon , but deliver a rag. This is the most difficult problem. So as you say , most companies fall flat for the sweet words of the vendors. I needed to get to reality and that wasn't easy. That had been a tough task. You had to do data mining to get the truth.
Most vendors were not producers of a product, but sellers of a software or computers or network components. Most don't supply the original data sheet.
Most vendor representatives were neither knowledgeable about the product, nor were empowered to take spot decisions. There are some exceptions.
 My encounters with vendors for more than  six  decades is a mini mahabharata. I will discuss it in the next post.

One of my major activities has been digitalisation of power utilities,  industries, governments, transport corporation, police, steel , central silk board, banks, schools, institutions, coop sector, hospitals, and even IT sector. It went on for more than 40 years of my life, not a pass time activity. My involvement spanned over 100 organizations. I had a deep commitment to this activity. It was a passion. I didn't get any income from this activity. I did this with full involvement .One of my friends, Mr Chikkappa,  our estate officer, used to say that I could have made millions in this activity, but didn't.  
This gave me immense satisfaction in life. I saw the success before my eyes - when i saw school kids in villages used computers with excitement, when i saw the production planning working in Jindal Steels, when i saw the LAN , power and elevators  working in Jayadeva hospital, vidhana soudha , power flowing without interruptions in JNCASR, IISc and a massive software development executed in the department of space or a small computer helping farmers in Gadag APMC,  core banking working in many banks and many more. That thrill was greater than money. This activity grew into a big and complex One. I consider this a very significant contribution.

“The bias toward high pressure/high temperature for industrial processes steers places of manufacturing away from humans and toward large-scale, centralized factories, regardless of culture, background, or politics. The bias toward cheap ubiquitous copies in digital technologies is independent of nationality, economic momentum, or human desire, and it steers the technology toward social ubiquity; the bias is baked into the nature of digital bits. In both of these examples, we can get the most from the technologies when we “listen” to the direction the technologies lean, and bend our expectations, regulations, and products to these fundamental tendencies within that technology.”
Kevin Kelly.      Inevitable

When I started on transformation advice and involvement in the sixties, I decided to have a set of rules on vendors to be followed by me. Let me enumerate some:
1.Treat all vendors equally. 
2.Look at branded products by stable companies.
3. The company should haexpthe necessary expertise to supply or execute the project. 
4.Dont accept whatever is told by vendors. Filter them
5.Be Open in discussions with vendors. Don’t accept a vendors comment on another . Don’t encourage it.
6. Rectify errors wherever needed. 
7.Be technical in discussions. Avoid gossip. 

Let me continue my discussions about my mini mahabharata with vendors. 
Normally vendors were well behaved. During my long discussions and negotiations with vendors, most were happy. None went for legal proceedings. 
Later, when DSERT issued a tender for purchase of computers and I wasn't in the picture, a vendor got a stay order. Then they requested me to help them. I told then to cancel the tender and return bank guarantees. Then we reinvited tender with some changes . I attended the prebid  meeting . There was a total change in the vendors behaviours.  Mr Kumar Naik , commissioner of education was surprised at the peaceful manner in which I conducted the meeting by patiently answering their questions and explaining the situation and finding alternates to their problems. 
As i said earlier, the most difficult and unruly prebid meeting , i managed was the prebid meeting for mahithisindhu program. No senior officer of education was present and i  had to handle the rough and tough. But I patiently listened to them, addressed their concerns and told them about the complexity of the project and explained to them that we needed a lot of capacity, capability to handle the many aspects of the program across the state and so it can’t be given to small companies. In the end, they left quietly. When we did the final allocation to three companies - NIIT, Aptech and Educomp- , all were happy. They worked and collaborated with each other. 

 Kindly recall an incident that happened at Bangalore dairy. They had a vendor who was maintaining  their accounting and payroll work on a monthly retainer. Then they sent a proposal to dairy asking them to buy a new computer at a high cost and also increase payment of monthly charges by almost twice. I told dairy not to accede to the request. 
In another case, Ullas Karanth  wanted a PC. A local dealer gave him a machine. When Ullas discussed with me , i said it won't work. He checked and verified with his counterpart in US. We confronted his seller. That guy was bluffing. So I took Ullas to wipro and got him a suitable machine. 
There are many such instances. We went through an era of systems analyst dominance and vendor dominance.

06/11/24, 4:46 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 530
Learning 
Vidya dadati vinayam, vinaya dadati paatrataam, paatrत्वात् धनम् आप्नोति धनाद् धर्मं ततः सुखम्
"Knowledge makes one humble, humility begets worthiness, worthiness creates wealth and enrichment, enrichment leads to right conduct, and right conduct brings contentment" 
Hitopadesa

Sanjay Chitnis has this query:
“Dear Sir, Good morning! I found this interesting quote from Prof Cameron Tonkinwise: “So-called-AI is a threat to human expertise, not because it replaces human expertise, but because it undermines how we learn to become experts.”
The learning theory says you learn more when you put more effort. Will AI undermine this?”

I have a detailed response for this query. It is not simple. 
Actually , i wanted to look at learning after I discussed about  teaching . But I wanted a gap - not too many of  heavy philosophy consecutively. 
Thanks Sanjay for this opportunity for picking my mind.  
Our religion , our upanishads , our darsanas deal with this aspect quite extensively. They give indepth views of many aspects of learning. 
Sankaracharya talks about both learning and teaching. Viveka chudamani is devoted to steps required for building a person through various processes of learning. 
Most concentrate on two paths - self learning and learning from a guru. They suggested several methodologies to learn like observing, analysing, listening, cognising, clarifying , interpreting etc. 

Let me start with a query  -What do we mean by Learning?
Learning involves finding answers to primarily  three questions - what , how and why.
We are born with some techniques to learn and surrounded by  a lot of unknowns - the whole world and everything is unknown. We start with unraveling the mysteries. A child  learns how to - initially.  How to eat, walk, speak etc. Many of the answers to these ” how “ are intrinsic. A child learns to move around by trying. 
Then comes the” what “ question?
A child learns by seeing , hearing and sensing. Then it learns perceptions. It tries to compare and correlate with things seen already. It learns relationships, things in the house and later outside.  Here it is partly intrinsic and partly guided. It starts building its knowledge base slowly. 
But serious learning starts with speaking and acquiring words and their interpretations. It develops language skills. 
 AI is built with explicit techniques but a child has multiple ways of learning -observe,  identify, classify, compare, get inputs, interpret, contemplate,. These methods multiply with time. We build a whole repertoire of methods to learn throughout our living time.  we have a lot of imaginative methods, lots of implicit ways of doing and knowing. Experimenting is one such. Initially intuition plays a big role in learning - the purpose of this learning is to get a clear picture of the environment. 
We are given a curiosity to to understand an unknown. Ask what why how
We have many methods to satisfy our curiosity.
We have a connected brain that connects many unconnected occurrences. Can AI do it.
Learning gives a solution or two to our ignorance of the  unknowns.

07/11/24, 4:22 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 531
“What separates Masters from others is often something surprisingly simple. Whenever we learn a skill, we frequently reach a point of frustration—what we are learning seems beyond our capabilities. Giving in to these feelings, we unconsciously quit on ourselves before we actually give up.The difference is not simply a matter of determination, but more of trust and faith. Many of those who succeed in life have had the experience in their youth of having mastered some skill—a sport or game, a musical instrument, a foreign language, and so on. Buried in their minds is the sensation of overcoming their frustrations and entering the cycle of accelerated returns. In moments of doubt in the present, the memory of the past experience rises to the surface. Filled with trust in the process, they trudge on well past the point at which others slow down or mentally quit.”
Greene.       Mastery

A child follows learning with unlearning . It gets an idea , later it revises, and may retrace from that idea. The way a child learns is that it uses perceptions and develops identification methods first, followed by grouping and classification. Then it learns the truth and falsity slowly. But it doesn't follow logic or mathematics or sciences. It identifies shapes in a different way. I saw children at age three identifying makes or models of cars by just looking at their shape ,at a time they can’t read. There is a different conceptual mechanism based probably on visuals  working there. 
Then it starts questioning everything it encouters. That is its period of learning and unlearning.  This builds a child's beliefs system. 
Later when a child goes to school, our formalised education system destroys all these intuitive learning methods and indoctrinates children with our formal structures . We dump heuristics, intuition , imagination,  intrinsic activities, and  observations into a dust bin. Our God is the formal STEM system. It destroys curiosity and discussions based learning. It doesn’t have provisions for internal learning, experimentation. Analysis is also  restricted. 
Problem solving taught in our system is rigid. It accepts only numeric data and expects  a single correct  solution mostly. It simplifies a problem. It is not realistic. 
I have a lingering doubt hanging within  me for many years - 
Do we ,in the name of formalism and scientific approach, 
1.ignore the innate learning strengths of a person?
2.move away from natural humanistic way of problem understanding, and solving through simplified and formal methods?
3.create an artificiality - rural kids have no idea of many aspects urban kids use.
4.ignore human sciences almost totally and concentrate on science and mathematics ?
5. Ignore learning and focus on examinations oriented system, thereby creating nervousness, tension and declaring 50% of students as failures, and about 80% to drop out of schools?
6. Ignore many arts like painting, music , sculpture etc almost completely?
7.accept the fact that teachers believe in fixed biases and beliefs and can't be changed?
8. Accept the reality that learning and education are determined by the market - say jobs , glamor like IITs?
9. Why do we ignore our centuries old  gurukula system's  good points ? Why do we hate It?

We went through many reforms. We did introduce the credit system , continuous evaluation and electives. It did provide a marginal relief, but didn't take us forward. 
NEP didn't address the fundamentals but went for a practical approach. It had a positive direction , but couldn't go far. We only discuss two language vs three language policies, a peripheral thinking. 
My next question
Why should education have to be uniform, rigid and old, 
as well as not localised, not environment , human and society sensitive, and not relevant to many ?. 
Answers to these questions are not easy and simple. They need to evolve starting with a big mindset change in people, educators and politicians. We need to start with a goal of humanism and harmony , not just jobs. 
The current open social networks based system should raise it's voice on the need for a flexible, balanced, relevant , modern , realistic, multi dimensional and partly localised educational system . 
All these questions were bugging me for several decades of teaching, but I did my job to the satisfaction and expectations of the society but not to the satisfaction of my inner self. 
In a sense,  I was satisfied that I did something useful to society , organizations and people. I had the urge to do more to make it more meaningful. That was my reason to use the whatsapp approach to discuss education and later  to write and whatsapp more than 500 posts on Hinduism for the past five years.

08/11/24, 4:48 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 532
Technological life in the future will be a series of endless upgrades. And the rate of graduations is accelerating. Features shift, defaults disappear, menus morph. I’ll open up a software package I don’t use every day expecting certain choices, and whole menus will have disappeared.
No matter how long you have been using a tool, endless upgrades make you into a newbie—the new user often seen as clueless. In this era of “becoming,” everyone becomes a newbie. Worse, we will be newbies forever. That should keep us humble.
That bears repeating. All of us—every one of us—will be endless newbies in the future simply trying to keep up. Here’s why: First, most of the important technologies that will dominate life 30 years from now have not yet been invented, so naturally you’ll be a newbie to them. Second, because the new technology requires endless upgrades, you will remain in the newbie state. Third, because the cycle of obsolescence is accelerating (the average lifespan of a phone app is a mere 30 days!), you won’t have time to master anything before it is displaced, so you will remain in the newbie mode forever. Endless Newbie is the new default for everyone, no matter your age or experience.
Kevin Kelly.         Inevitable 

Let me discuss what  Sankaracharya says about the ten  qualities for a student.
He introduced the practice of Sādhana catuṣṭya, the four preliminary qualifications as a means to attain knowledge. They are Vairāgya (detachment), Viveka (discrimination), Śamādiṣaṭsampatti (six treasures such as control of mind and the like) and Mumukṣutva (yearning for liberation from the bondage of ignorance). They are discussed in his work Aparokşānubhūti (AP).
The satsampatti are 
1.Śama: It means control and one-pointedness (concentration) of mind so that it becomes calm and steady.
2.Dama: It means control of sense organs.
3.Titikṣā: It means endurance of all types of suffering.
4.Uparati: It means renunciation of all karmas or turning away from all sense objects. Avoid distractions. 
5.Śraddhā: It is faith in the teachings of the Vedas and the teacher.
6.Samādhāna: One pointedness or concentration of mind on the Brahman is called as Samādhāna. It can be viewed as focussing and deep studies.
Sankaracharya defines the process of learning as
 Śravaṇa, Manana, and Nididhyāsana. Śravaṇa means to listen and study based on concepts and  principles. 
. Manana means reflecting on the teachings of the right teacher. 
Nididhyāsana means a continuous flow of similar mental states, united with self; like that of the continuous stream of oil. Most loose interest after some time. Can we go on for hours studying  a topic?
The depth Sankaracharya talks about is different from what Calport says about deep learning. Calport says that our concept of teaching a topic for One hour at a time is not correct. Concentrate and study one topic for a few days  , instead of intermittent learning. I learnt programming in a few days in the sixties. I wrote a large  program within a week. If I had attended a semester-long course, I might have lost interest and might not be in the computer field at all. So I saw the benefit of intense and deep learning. 
I saw the effect of concentration , depth and intensity of learning in my grandson at an early age of three or four. His intensity and concentration were and are phenomenal.  It is desirable to build these qualities in our children. 
Western educationists have the opposite ideas. They assume that children lose interest quickly and hence the psychologists  don't ask the question “ Are there methods for a child to practice so as  to increase the concentration for hours continuously?”
Our early gurukula system was based on the premise that children can be taught concentration, contemplation and intensity . The gurus start with that aspect in the beginning itself  by making students to recite vedas continuously. The practice of reciting vedas was normally done for several hours in a day. Music is learnt by practicing for several hours every day. This practice is called sadhana in carnatic music and riaz in Hindustani.  The great musician, Hariharan,  says that riaz , the practice of singing for hours is a daily  routine for him like eating , bathing , sleeping etc. We need to build this as a habit  in our children . A lot of learning is internal , churning ideas and thoughts in mind. 
I daily do a practice - churning ideas in my mind, particularly in the night on a topic that is bugging me at that instant. I get into an imaginative world without rationalism, logic or analytic approaches. You do get answers sometimes after many days of imaginations. 
Recall the panchatantra stories. What did that achieve - converted a few useless - the perception of the father- persons into worldly wise people. Does it not ring a bell on learning methods? 
Remember if we don’t learn and practice this intensity, Gen AI, in its present state, will beat us. So that mental attitude and the consequent development of intensity is crucial for us to survive. That is a warning signal.
