09/12/24, 5:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 563

Before I  continue with my experiences with the department of space on the development of an ERP system, I want to focus on an important component of understanding by me. By 1990 plus, IT started evolving with computers, software and networking becoming interconnected to provide an IT system. The terms IT and enterpriseIT started evolving in the nineties . 
I started helping several organizations to build IT systems. This made me look at some fundamental changes required in our thinking. True , I had and have a very  strong foundation of theory and a very good understanding of technology , its manufactures and uses ,. I had been watching the growth of computer manufacture in the country and also of network components and of software development.  I had discussions with several of them and also purchased from many of them. So my knowledge of vendors was also very good. Indeed I introduced many products like zenith's superbrain, HCLs Horizon mini computer etc. But I felt that that knowledge and awareness were not enough to successfully implement a solution like we saw in the cases of mahithi sindhu,  CET cell, and Jindal Vijayanagar steels. I needed to know that application of technologies in Indian organizations needed an Indian look and understanding. I realized that I am not embarking on a technology process, but a cultural transformation activity. So technology took a back seat for me and both transformation and culture took the prominent front seat. Let me explain. 
When you bring in a new technology into an organization , it has to meet  the following requirements.
It should be acceptable to the people. you need to  understand the  organization's culture. Who is against the project? Who is against computers? Is my first question. You may be surprised to know - in most cases I encountered many opponents. Nobody wanted a change. They are well entrenched in their positions and consider any change as a threat to their job. 
If you study the middle management deeper , you get a pattern:
Some opposing the project
Some indifferent
Some supporting 
Some cat on the wall -  will change their position depending on top management's views. 
2. The second is how to improve the culture?
Assess the impact of the organization's culture.  Understand organization's resistance. Ignore the indifferent people. Mobilise the supporting people. Create enthusiasm in them . Motivate them. Make them the project champions. Explain the project, and convert more into the  supporter group. The overall  effect should be positive. The whole thing will fail if top management is not keen on the project. I got enormous support from top management in all projects that I participated in. We needed their support. This step is a difficult but most important step for the success of the project. We need to remove the fear of the unknown. This was a serious problem in the 1990s . The situation was much better from the computer awareness perspective in the 21st century. But aversion to change still is dominant. So this has to be resolved slowly . Keep them in a partial loop of information transfer thereby reducing the unknown effect.
3. The third requirement is the willingness of a core set of middle to senior management to change and preparedness to put efforts into implementing the transformation. So it depends on the implementation team. 
4. The fourth requirement is related to the capability, commitment and passion of the implementation team. I depended a lot on this team . Let me give some names :
Mr. Uday Singh,Mr. Vijayaraghavan ,  Mr. Manohar in Jindal steels
Dr R K Dutta at central silk board
Mr P V  Joshi,Mr A Srinivasan,  Mr Ramaswamy,  in Indian bank
Mr Siddu, Mr Ajay Sharma, Mr R M Divan, Mr Subramanya Kumar in  PNB
Mr B R Bhat, Mr Kumar in  corporation bank
Mr Saxsena, Mr Atul Kumar in syndicate bank 
Mrs Nirmala in Vijaya bank 
Mr Vasudevan, Mr Mahalingam in Equitas bank 
and many more. 
I repeat that all top managements - CMDs,EDs,Secretaries  -  of all these organizations supported me and listened to me and speeded up the work. 
More in the next post.

10/12/24, 5:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 564

Our normal belief is that technology changes lifestyle. That was definitely true when electricity came into the houses. It extended our evening times by about 4 hours and increased cultural activities like cinema, music , shopping etc and led to a change in our lifestyle. 
But the 21st century born millennials have changed that perception. They are restless, get bored with the same food, same type of job, same living styles after a few years and look for changes. Now change is driven by people also in addition to technologies. So understanding this new dimensions of change is important. 
Let us next talk about something important for transformation that is culture. Success depends upon understanding culture and tailoring a solution to suit the culture of not only an organization but also people belonging to stakeholder groups, customers and the general public.
We don't acquire knowledge but culture. 
What does one imbibe and build - not just knowledge but a set of cultures?
Culture is a property of a group or community - practices, beliefs, principles , behaviours, activities etc. followed in an ecosystem .
We are familiar with social culture and 
Religious culture. 
But we now have many more: Art culture, Work culture , Professional cultures,  Digital culture,  Office culture, Business culture, Transformation culture , Startup culture etc.
So I tried to understand the startup culture and use those concepts in these transformations. We needed to innovate at several points in time.
But learning startup culture - thinking, learning constantly, approaches, focus , passion and discovery - is very useful and effective in one's  professional life even if you don't want to go and build a startup.
It builds leadership capabilities in a person like thinking, discrimination, communications, listening, Cool behaviours , team work, participative work culture, self confidence , time management ,understanding multiple alternates for a problem . Most importantly it says “ throw out the rule book and most theory learnt in the degree program”.
I believe startup culture is the starting point for problem discovery, its importance, effects and impacts . . This leads to a good feel on planning , design and  integration of multiple components to build a system End to end, a very good exercise for every engineering student.
Remember startups didn't appear in the 21st century.  We had many startups from the sixties onwards. Most NGOs were startups. Look at how the restaurant scene in bangalore has changed. Darshini  was a great move towards simplification of workflow. Home delivery existed in the seventies,  the dabba system is an old one. It taught us decentralisation and collaboration .  
So I used many techniques borrowed from small industries. It was a good and novel experience and learning.

11/12/24, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 565

As I got involved in many transformation projects , i was learning a lot of new concepts and new realizations. Let me look at some of them. 
My first question to myself was - is the top management serious about the project?
 I would say that most top managements were interested in the project and some were pretty serious, some were less involved, some were more involved. Let me give examples . In the case of setting up CET cell, the government was serious about reforming the admission system . The minister, secretary, and TAP members were serious about the project . They were all involved and some to a serious level. They are keen that this should succeed. They are clear of the hurdles of the opposition from DTE and hence set up a new organization called CET cell.                               In the second case, the government was equally serious and interested and involved with the big mahithisindhu program. The CM and EM reviewed the program once in a while. The secretary education was involved in vendor selections and budget allocations and approval processes. The commissioner of public instruction was totally committed to the program and spent a considerable amount of time on the formulation of project principles, plans and approaches in the initial stages. He streamlined the approval paths and processes. While we didn't set up a new organization for this program , we delinked the administration from DSERT and got approvals directly from the commissioner. We removed operations from the education department and moved into a totally outsourced mode with proper review and approval processes. So these commitments , involvements led towards the success at the first stage. 
The third one was the Jindal steels. It was clear from the beginning that management was keen on the digital process and supported it. There were no hurdles or oppositions. 
Let us look at the case of ERP for the department of space next. Here more than interest, it is the strong will of the top management that prevailed. DoS is a big department with many organizations . So they had no way of ensuring uniformity in administration in different units. So they asked the question - is there a central computerised answer and heard about ERP. But existing packages didn't meet their requirements and their restrictions on technology use like the oracle database system  was  not available to them. So they discussed and felt that they should develop an inventory house system for administration and finance. It met with strong resistance from some senior IT persons in some units. They said it can’t be done. But some seniors in a few units like SHAR, were prepared to build it. It gave a big opportunity for IT department to showcase and motivate their people. Three units came forward to build it. DoS headquarters supported it. So both Mr Prabhakaran, additional secretary and Dr Madhavan Nair , director ,VSSC decided to go ahead. I was roped in. That was a strong commitment. They were involved in some reviews. 
Let me look at the fourth example - banks . This is a mixed bag. CMDs and EDs had short tenures.  So their commitments varied from deep interest to total non interest. Secondly, banks had a lot of GMs , with no ownership or responsibility to IT, and they were involved in decision making. Convincing some was easy, but it was difficult with others. Fortunately IT had committed officers and so we managed the activities successfully, but not to my professional expectations and satisfaction .

12/12/24, 4:15 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 566

Normally, we think that technology is in the front seat, but I found that the  culture of the organization and people are  taking a front seat.  In some organizations whose names  I don't want to mention, jealousy and fear had destroyed the project. Particularly, ERP is not welcomed by employees. They felt it was too much work, and were afraid that they wouldn't be able to learn new ways of doing the job and may even lose the job.Even IISc had hiccups for decades  in computerising administration and accounts and selecting a good ERP. It struggled for decades due to the egos , overconfidence and wrong beliefs of the deciding committee. The committee was not deciding ,but vacillating in arriving at a decision for decades and only recently accepted a professional approach and implemented SAP .   So navigation is to be done carefully. 
Corruption is another factor that  I didn't want to mention. Whenever we had a vendors meeting to discuss technology or whenever we selected a vendor, there was curiosity in many people. Even the class 4 staff and drivers try to get information from me. While I admired their interest, I felt that they were exceeding the limit. Similarly , many in the office will ask me to find out our decisions . It was a tough decision . You can’t say no, but you need to be diplomatic about your replies. I normally give vague answers in these cases. 

My next biggest problem was vendor management.  I had interactions with thousands of vendors for  about four decades. This needed many skills.  Vendor management  has many subcomponents like vendor selections, team synergies, implementation management etc.  It is a field with lots of mines strewn around. Any one may explode.  Before I discuss the process for vendor selections, let me look at some normally encountered situations. 
Assess the vendor . Is he saying the truth? Mostly not. So the question is how does one distill the truth from the vendors’ proposals and discussions? We need to devise other mechanisms. My knowledge here came in handy . I used Google search and literature search for knowing the current trends. Today we have much better evaluation and rating of vendors. Also many vendors slowly learnt that trust is more important for long term stability and gains and decided to leave the short term gimmicks.  So many vendors became  frank and open .
Assess the cooperation of the vendor?
This also is decided based on past history. So new vendors with very little experience were not normally encouraged unless supported by some established and reputed companies.
Assess the capabilities of the vendor? It is my most difficult task. I had to go with past experiences and some reputations. I had difficulties with the capabilities of implementation teams in many organizations, because we don't know them apriori.  Remember implementations come later after vendor selections. It is one's luck to get a set of competent team with a good leader and necessary skills. Most vendor teams learnt at the expense of the organization . Many team leaders didn't have domain skills in banking. Sometimes no team member had domain knowledge or knowledge at a low level. Though  the vendor company has a great reputation , the team's capabilities are doubtful. I had fights with teams from TCS,Wipro , Infosys, HCL , Lasersoft and many others. This is not easy - a tough human relations job. I feel unhappy about these interactions with such vendor teams even now. I didn't see much professionalism in their approach or actions .
more in the next.

13/12/24, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 567
My next problem:
How can we predict vendor synergy in advance? We can't. We go by vendor reputation and past experiences. 
How do we decide whether the relationships and synergy between vendor and the IT team will be balanced and smooth? 
This is a great topic for research . Most think the relationships between the organization and the IT company were good. It depends on the perceptions of top management and the two leaders - IT department head and project lead from the vendor company. Their  expectations were different , understandings were different and even levels were different with the organizational IT head being a very senior IT and domain expert whereas the vendor's project lead will be a moderate person with unknown domain skills. These lead to friction and fights . This may have effects on the success of the project. In some cases , the organizations are powerful and try to browbeat the vendor. In some cases, the vendor shows his technical superiority and browbeat the organization.  This relationship is never a smooth one. There will definitely be many points of conflict and the maturity needed on both sides to resolve these is quite high. Proper documentation and understanding were difficult sometimes. One shows one's power , not realizations at that time. As I said , I had encountered these fractions hundreds of times in the course of the implementation.  
The only case where i found a good vendor organization relationship was in the core banking implementation for  Indian bank by TCS. It was a good example of relationships. Mr Jayaramakrishnan , senior management person from TCS ironed out minor differences. That worked very well. 
Similarly, there was a good rapport between PNB housing finance GM Mr Nitant Desai and 3i Infotech MD Mr Manivannan and it smoothened the frictions. One reason this succeeded was the decision taking powers at a senior level compared to a junior person sent as project lead. Project leads worry only about optimising their job and not about satisfying the customer. Project leads are normally technical people with minimal interpersonal skills and empowerment for decision making. 
I think this concept of a relationship manager is a good idea and leads towards a greater chance of success. It reduces work area tension and increases the harmony between both teams . It gives a realistic assessment of progress of the project. I am surprised why this concept is not a part of every  project activity. 
But a careful selection of a relationship manager by an IT or services company is important. The role should be well defined, the person should be familiar with his organization - I found that some relationship managers  of a bank were ignorant of rules- and should have decision making powers. The manager should be mature . 
My encounters with vendors were the hardest and highly unpleasant ones. I didn't like it ,because 
I met vendors for different purposes - prebid, sales presentations, technical issues in operations , implementations , performance issues , price negotiations etc. 
The vendors behaviours were very different. Some were good and nice, some morose, some particularly from multinational companies were indifferent. 
Many vendors were good , but many were incompetent.
 I found it strange that a particular vendor - let me name it because that was a unique case, Lasersoft- had a phenomenal superiority complex and arrogance that he felt that  he was doing us  a favour , not a service. It was difficult to talk to him. I was puzzled as to why  the bank tolerated his behaviour. Fortunately,  I didn't find this kind of attitude in others.
That concludes my discussion on some aspects for the success of a project.

Suleyman.      Coming wave

Let us move back to the DoS Project on building an inhouse ERP called COWAA.  
As I said earlier, it was a bold decision by DoS. It was opposed by many IT heads,  but the management had no options. 
Fortunately , the entire activity was well planned. They decided on the areas and functions to be computerised. Details about rules and practices were collected and codified. Then a project lead , Dr Srinivasa Rao and his deputy were appointed. Similarly, leads for each area were also identified. Each group built its teams from different centres. Most team members were motivated and excited. We got a separate V and V team from VSSC. Videoconferencing systems were established for communications . 
We decided to meet once a month physically at SHAR, Sriharikota.  So I visited SHAR once every month. It became a regular activity for me. This went on for three plus years. 
We went through the steps systematically and ironed out issues by anticipating them and listing them. 
The project ran smoothly. Mr Prabhakaran,  Additional Secretary, DoS had a review meeting and I was there with him. I also met him a few times to apprise the progress of work. Two officers in charge of IT at headquarters - Mr Ramani and Mr Dominic were also  part of my review team . All these helped in the smooth running of the project. The groups employed NIIT trainees on databases - Sybase trained persons- to assist them. But I insisted that they should stick to pure SQL and avoid special features of Sybase so that the transfer to Oracle would be smooth. 
It went off smoothly. Most work was done . Fortunately there were no transfers of people , so we had no manpower or team issues. 
The project showed good progress, but went  almost the full way. When more than 90% was complete , the teams lost interest , work slowed down. So they weren't keen on installing the software, training users and running the software in a systematic way. They were happy to develop the software but not enthusiastic to run it. It was run and was found useful in resolving issues of uniformity. 
So that was a long journey of big software development involving many teams from many centres, a great experiment and lots of learning.

“Awareness is not a school quiz that can be passed by reading and memorizing some facts. It’s a journey that is fueled by our intent to be open, honest, and free. It begins when we realize that our comprehension is in fact limited, it continues when we follow that realization with a desire to break free of artificial constraints,and it unfolds when we move forward toward knowing our vast potential. From those precious moments on, a new course can be charted as we discover worlds that had been previously hidden or obscured.”
Deitch.       Elevate

This DoS Project revealed some important components for successful implementation of a project. 
IT governance- while IT governance wasn't understood by most IT people, this project employed many  good governance principles employed by DOS, a culture built by Prof Dhawan . So there were some starting points like well articulated goal and objectives,  organised teams, detailed plans , good preparedness, top management commitment and involvement,  project leadership,  organizational culture and cohesion , reviews, feedbacks, participation, openness and transparency, good communications, motivation, team training, coordinated teamwork,good  business and IT synergy ,etc. That gave us a great start without hurdles. We added additional governance on progress reviews, coding standards, time and effort estimation, time management, target setting, separate V and V team , collective understanding , decision making, good and quick conflict resolutions, adhering to standards  etc. 
The total process of software development was well defined. This meant we were in CMMI level 3. 
We didn't start with a waterfall model. I never liked its rigidity. While we had all rules and existing procedures collected and codified, we didn't prepare an SRS. We went through a detailed design, allowing for additional rules and functions by modularisation. 
Other success factors were- deep familiarity and understanding of the tasks being handled , normally called domain knowledge; good leaderships; adequacy of capital and other resources ; operational synergies etc. 
We didn't worry about security very much as it was only for use by staff , not others. The Internet and web are yet to become popular. 
Documentation was good. V and V team wanted documents. 
 It is a major project with external constraints , but very well executed. 
Let me turn my attention in another direction -  my involvement and work for TEQIP.  
TEQIP is a World Bank program designed to improve the quality of engineering programs in our engineering colleges, both state and central institutions as well as a few private colleges. The World Bank had been supporting a number of programs to improve the quality of engineering education at different levels in the country for more than 25 years and I was involved in them either in buying equipment or in improving skills or developing curriculum and writing textbooks or conducting short term programs.  But TEQIP was different. It learnt from past problems and looked for a holistic , focussed and integrated approach to improving quality.
More in the next.

“They seemed to operate in a parallel universe, one defined by devotion to a shared set of principles, a worldview, which gave them citizenship in a tiny intellectual village. Call it Singletonville, a very select group of men and women who understood, among other things, that:
• Capital allocation is a CEO’s most important job.
• What counts in the long run is the increase in per share value, not overall growth or size.
• Cash flow, not reported earnings, is what determines longterm value.
• Decentralized organizations release entrepreneurial energy and keep both costs and “rancor” down.
• Independent thinking is essential to long-term success, and interactions with outside advisers (Wall Street, the press, etc.) can be distracting and time-consuming.
• Sometimes the best investment opportunity is your own stock.

Outsiders.            William  Thorndyke

TEQIP was started in 2002. It was well thought out, comprehensive and focused. About 180 institutions were selected based on proposals submitted by institutions . While the institutions chosen were mostly those funded by either the central government or the state government, a few private engineering colleges were included in the list. While in  karnataka,  all aided institutions and two government/ university colleges were chosen . In addition, about six  private colleges were also selected. Karnataka didn't have any objections to private institutions becoming a part of the TEQIP program whereas most states objected to the participation of  private colleges with Tamilnadu being a vociferous objector. Tamilnadu didn't even allow aided institutions to apply for TEQIP. 
 There was a focus on funding and activities. The main focus was research. So funding was focused on encouraging research work . Institutions were given large amounts of funds for purchase of equipment for starting research in new areas. We decided to equip all institutions with a good computer centre and hundreds of PCs plus some sets of applications software packages. That will strengthen computer based projects and research and employability in IT will increase.  We funded institutions for modernising their labs and class rooms . Some funding was given for constructions but restricted to less than 10% of the total allocation. 
There were some conditions to be met by  both governments and institutions like a good governance mechanism, a governing board , and the state governments to facilitate the grant of autonomy to these institutions. We wanted the faculty to register for PhD and to guide others for PhD. Financial assistance was given for PhD work and for publications in research journals. 
Institutions were encouraged to run several short term courses covering new areas in depth so that faculty knowledge increases and they can teach new courses. While there was an exchange program for faculties , it wasn't popular.
There was also a focus on social and community aspects. Many institutions did some interesting work. For example, SDM,Dharwad, conducted a welding course to jail inmates so that the trainees will have a job once they are out of jail.

17/12/24, 5:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 571
Before we start looking at the TEQIP program, it is appropriate to look at the engineering education scene in India in detail. This will give us an exposure on the education scene.
Our engineering education was marginal till the fifties just like our university education. With the five year plans focusing on industries and construction of dams , the government understood the importance of Swami Vivekananda and JN Tata’s concerns of the availability of an adequate number of trained engineers to design, build, maintain and operate engineering constructions, industries , and power supply. The demand for engineers was high and increasing every year at a steady rate.
So, the second five year plan focused on setting up several engineering colleges to provide a supply of engineers to state PWDs ,central PWD, CPWC, state  electricity boards and public and private sector industries. The central government devised an examination system through UPSC to select engineers to run central engineering services, railways ,and telecommunications.  The entire system was robust for that time and the curriculum was relevant. 
Engineering curriculum in the fifties and sixties focused on a good dose of general engineering principles and a reasonable amount of specialised disciplines. There was an emphasis on engineering design, and practices. Sciences and humanities had a less role.  This worked well till 1980. 
Fortunately, the central took a very impactful step in the sixties - to set up IITs with foreign collaboration. This gave a benchmark to our engineering colleges on what should be a standard education. IIT s played a big role in producing highly focused, intelligent and active set of graduates who became our ambassadors in many parts of the world. IIT s became beacons for our engineering education standards ,though somewhat misleading from reality. 
The sixties saw the emergence of engineering research in IISc and many CSIR labs set up by the government.  Defence also set up some major DRDO labs like aeronautical development establishment,  electronics and radar development establishment,  gas turbine research, defence electronics research lab etc. These two groups created a base for applications research in india. Manufacturing was also strengthened through the establishment of a large number of public sector units . So we created a reasonably balanced engineering ecosystem.  
By the late sixties and early  seventies ,we saw the first set of major changes - the modernization of electrical engineering from machines focus to systems focus , the arrival of control systems as a major part of electrical engineering , the introduction of analysis and optimisation necessitating the larger focus to mathematics , and a shift to theory. So, the emphasis on operations and practices were reduced. Mathematics became a major component of engineering. Electrical engineering diversified into power systems and control systems in addition to machines. Let us continue this further.
More in the next post.
Greetings
Dks
18/12/24, 4:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 572

The period of mid sixties saw the arrival of a major, highly impacting and  new area or discipline - electronics. Most institutions started teaching a few electronics circuits courses for electrical engineering students from the mid sixties onwards.
But by the seventies,  electronics found a lot of applications in industries and the telecom sector. We also saw changes happening quickly - from vacuum tubes to transistors and then to ICs. 
IISc realised these needs much early , started electrical engineering in 1909, the first institution to start a strong electrical program in the country and almost contemporary in the world. IISc also is the first to set up a communications engineering department in 1948, almost contemporary to major universities in US and UK and a major department of Aeronautics. IISc maintained its contemporary approach to engineering through its strong PG and research programs in cutting edge areas. So it became a torch bearer of engineering in India- both on the utilities and industry side and on the academic teachers generation side.  Many  students graduated from our  PG courses  and found a teaching job in several engineering colleges across India. Quite a good number also went abroad for PhD programs. Many joined electrical utilities, civil aviation,  HAL, BEL , and many other industries . It was a good dispersal of trained talents across many sectors. This happens even now . IITs story is a bit mixed. Many undergraduates moved to IIMs and joined the management stream . But most graduate students helped the many sectors in the country - education and industries. 
Some engineering colleges started a new discipline called electrical communications engineering in the seventies. It gave a new direction to our thinking and actions.
The eighties saw the next explosion in engineering with the arrival of computers, PCs, programming, software, algorithms and data processing . This opened up almost infinite opportunities for work in these many areas. It also integrated many disciplines through applications and functional integration and automation. 
In the late  eighties, we saw the arrival of IT- information technology  which provided a large opportunity for employment. Both CS and IT became major departments from an employment  perspective. 
While the government allowed the setting up of private colleges in the sixties itself, only karnataka took advantage and allowed setting up of many colleges. Till the eighties, quality in a traditional sense was maintained - motivated students eager to learn, motivated teachers eager to learn and teach, reasonable infrastructure etc. Examinations were understood to be the only indicator  of quality. There was only one metric then - the number of students who passed the exams.
There was no research focus, no industry focus, no modernization focus , no publication focus, only a good traditional teaching focus. This continued to a large extent till the arrival of the 21st century. 
While the eighties and nineties saw a quantitative improvement, quality lagged behind. Universities didn't pay attention to the engineering discipline. So many states started setting up separate technical universities in order to focus on engineering. This converted these new universities into big examination centres. It took decades for us to catch up on current trends in many areas. But we took the opportunities provided by the IT sector to increase the students intake by first allowing the setting up of a large number of private engineering colleges. That gave a big lead to our country to take a global top position in IT services.
Greetings
Dks

“Wolf and Bruhn had to convince the medical establishment to think about health and heart attacks in an entirely new way: they had to get them to realize that they wouldn't be able to understand why someone was healthy if all they did was think about an individual's personal choices or actions in isolation. They had to look beyond the individual. They had to understand the culture he or she was a part of, and who their friends and families were, and what town their families came from. They had to appreciate the idea that the values of the world we inhabit and the people we surround ourselves with have a profound effect on who we are.”
Outliers    Gladwell

Thanks for the above comments. Glad that many of you  read my posts.

Let me continue with my discussions on engineering education in India. 
Many reforms were initiated by the government of India like setting up regional engineering colleges and upgrading them to NITs, introducing an attractive quality improvement program leading to ME and PhD degrees in some great institutions for teachers of engineering colleges, teacher training programs etc. . ISTE was conducting a number of faculty knowledge improvement programs in several institutions. CSIR and UGC gave scholarships for PG and PhD programs . IISc gave scholarships to all admitted ME and PhD students , part of the expenditure met from internal grants.
AICTE was set up to ensure uniform measures for quality and to speed up establishment of new colleges and new disciplines.  It helped in creating a set of norms for quality- teacher Students ratio, qualifications, library books and journals, computer facilities, classrooms, class sizes, access to the institution, campus area and facilities etc. AICTE ensured that these norms were followed by all institutions.  The biggest advantages were the permissions given to start new programs and new institutions and increased intakes. This helped in a quick scaling up of new areas like computer science, IT etc. The ministry of human resources also gave funds to a large number of institutions for modernising equipments, and starting and strengthening thrust areas. It helped many institutions. 
So, to sum up,  we found the following gaps in 2000.
1.low to nil research activity in most institutions with focus on teaching.
2.poor lab facilities and computer facilities
3.low level of research journals access 
4.rigid and dated curriculum
5.low level of academic and governance autonomy
6. Low level of networking between institutions - no resource sharing.
7.faculty improvements were not taken up on a big scale. 
Let us return to TEQIP in the next post.

20/12/24, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 574
“The demand for new skills is spreading rapidly across all sectors. Not long ago, the need for new skills was concentrated in the tech sector. However, the spread of the demand for new skills is transforming industries and sectors, rendering some skills virtually ubiquitous. In 2011, for instance, there were 294,000 job posts seeking people with data analysis skills in only 17 occupations; 10 years later, 1.2 million such jobs were posted in 81 occupations. Many of the skills are spreading to existing jobs, where workers have to learn and apply them, but the spread is also happening in new occupations created by these evolving skills.”
Skills powered organization.   Ravin Jesuthasan ,Tanuj kapilashrami

We saw the gaps in our engineering education.  So we have doubts on whether we can improve our education. The confirmation is provided by the following positive aspects of our education. 
1.Our education system is very resilient.  It accepts changes. It adapts to the changes when forced. Our government keeps pushing changes and forcing them to adapt for their survival. The latest example of government is the NEP. It doesn't get implemented fully. There is confusion and opposition. Still,  there is a lot of  hope to make changes. 
2.We inadvertently moved into new disciplines fast enough to provide a large human power to power the IT sector. Both large scale privatization and adaption of CS and IT disciplines pushed us and our country forward. Contrast this with the sciences and mathematics education scene in India.  We are nowhere near a critical level in bio and nano sciences. Same is true with medical education . The PG programs in medicine  are restricted to a few candidates. 
3. There are a good number of institutions running post graduate programs in engineering.  
 TEQIP addressed some of the gaps. 
Let me look at one area of focus: to modernize the curriculum. Our existing curriculum is dated ,not modern, rigid with a fixed set of courses . It didn't give opportunities for students to choose their basket of courses of interest to them. Labs were routine with old equipment with no possibility of experimentation and innovation. So the curriculum drew our first attention.  We wanted all institutions to move from their existing rigid ,no electives curriculum to a credit based curriculum with many electives and a continuous evaluation by internal faculty.  I played a big role in developing a  complete structure for a credit based curriculum with complete details on various aspects like subareas, core , electives, credit values, in semester evaluation,  grades and grading, final exam evaluation, project work etc, in an exclusive workshop for senior faculty of all participating institutions in karnataka. This needed autonomy away from the rigidity of universities.  It was a great success. All institutions set up a curriculum committee and built a new credit based curriculum. They agreed after a lot of discussions on internal evaluation. Trust on faculty was established by this process. These were remarkable achievements. These were major reforms. 
Let me look at our current scene in the 21st century later. 

Good governance mechanisms for TEQIP were set up at both national level and at state level institutions. I again gave a lead talk on governance to functionaries of all participating institutions on governance aspects. The national level institution was called NPIU - national program implementation unit. Dr R N Mathur was the officer in charge of NPIU. He did a remarkable job of ensuring that the goals are met. He devised many review methods to achieve the goals. Persuasion, formal reviews , slight threats were used to get support from state governments to achieve the objectives of the program. We became good friends. He is running an NGO called EQUATE.
Similarly, the  states were asked to set up a separate SPIU with  autonomous powers in decision making and away from the  administrative red tape of the DTE office. These new organizational structures speeded up funds transfers, purchases approvals , travel approvals, honorarium approvals etc. It created a new structure for governance. Rules and regulations were established for purchases, funds transfers, accounting and audit procedures. A well oiled review mechanism was set up. It worked well and TEQIP achieved a very high degree of success. Almost all institutions got several benefits from this program.

21/12/24, 3:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 575

Mr Nagaraja has a query:
“Gaps found in 2000 is really disappointing and disturbing. What is the present status regarding these gaps? Can you please throw some light on this sir.? Thanks.
With regards.”
My response
The gaps remain. 
We wake up at some intervals and take some steps and go to sleep. My biggest worry is the curriculum thinking as it is happening now.  Our curriculum thinking by faculty is in the  20th century state, not the  21st century approaches. This needs to change. 
Recently I was in SDM Dharwad and Prof Vasudev Parvati and Biradar raised questions on the curriculum relating to AI program and its impact on IT and CS. I then spent more than an hour discussing the aspects of curriculum thinking with their faculty. There were a number of queries from the faculty. So let me explain in detail the various aspects for the benefit of many faculty in this group. 

The 21st century saw many trends and it reversed many principles of the 20th century. We shifted from job focus to skills and tools focus and from single skills requirements to multiskills requirements. Lots of jobs were in AI agents integration with the IT applications of businesses. Most Indian companies are shifting to AI. Secondly , 80% of jobs are in data analytics. These two dominate the job scene. Then there are many jobs on architecture and software design, cloud management and integration. Our curriculum should be in that direction.
The Major differences between 20th and 21st century are:
The 20th century built a monolithic , rigid organization and moved towards a big enterprise. The 21st century built   a startup culture, a distributed, flexible and integrated organization. 
The 20th century focused on a rigid highly centralised governance with powers concentrated at the highest level. It was a vertical organization. The 21st century moved towards a flexible  horizontal organization with many teams empowered with responsibilities and decision making powers.
The 20th century saw a slow pace of technology developments ,but the 21st century saw a rapid pace of developments of many technologies leading to a convergence approach in applications. Technology use became complex.Technology absorption was fast.
The 20th century viewed engineering from an industry perspective and ignored the customers. The 21st century is people centric with many technologies like mobile, social media, data commerce, payments, crowdsourcing, cloud , AI reaching people at affordable or low costs. This is a big change. Developments were driven by people. 
The 20th century saw a slow rate of technology Application. The 21st century saw a global reach of  business, more scope for applications , and a faster growth of technology uses. Multiple  Technologies like XPUs, processors, IOT, sensors, analytics etc  got embedded into systems .
The 20th century believed in a structured approach for developing a system. Detailed  Planning, complete specification of requirements, risk aversion were all part of the century and our curriculum focuses on these aspects. It wanted to mass produce good quality products at lower costs. But the 21st century understood the failure of businesses due to disruptive innovations and  moved towards a new set of approaches - a flexible and adjustable strategy planning, a shared vision, flexible management without rigid rules, incremental planning, pilots and experimentation, continuous innovation, understanding customer experiences, risk management and not aversion, etc.
While the 20th century ignored the customers, the 21st century went for a customer first approach and kept customers as a part of their processes. Processes became flexible. The concept of professionalism got changed. Changes were effected faster.
The 20th century focused on functions and the 21st century focuses  on data. Decisions are based on data. 
Automation was mostly at the process control level in the 20th century. But digital and AI dominate and pervade in all sectors in the 21st century.  Automation has reached every aspect of a process ,plant or service with robotics,  drones and AI agents playing a big role in almost all aspects. Human AI joint collaborations and actions are the most dominant areas of research and development 
We understood that the term skills meant some simple skills and our tools were more mechanical in the 20th century.  But now , the skills were many and tools were mostly computer based. There are lakhs of tools. So assembly in manufacturing In the 20th century was based on putting together mechanical parts or  electrical parts. But now it is integration of hundreds of software and AI tools in a system.
More in the next.

22/12/24, 4:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 576

We saw the changes that happened in the 21st century by discarding many 20th century practices. Next, let us see what is a view of the world in the 21st century.
The world is going the VUCA way.
1 Volatile- changing with the arrival of many  new technologies . We can't live on the “ business as usual”  philosophy. We need to adapt to the changes in our personal life and professional work. So adaptation is essential for survival. So futuristic vision with predictions using AI is essential. We can't live in the past. 
This changes our views of engineering.  We need continuous adaptation. so systems should use agile , flexible processes, not rigid and not centralised. We want incremental and continuous innovation to keep pace with volatility.  Connectivity to the extended enterprise including stakeholders, other businesses, customers, and feedback have  become part of engineering. Organizations need a startup mindset,  not a rigid set of rules, roles and processes. Job requirements are changing . Skills and tools competency , architecting and designing capabilities are essential for new jobs. AI application skills are important for the survival of employees and organizations  Many traditional jobs will vanish. Multi Skilling is a requirement in today's world. New skilling during our lifetime many times is another requirement. 
2.Uncertain - more of randomness, no more deterministic Systems. Statistics play a big role here, not mathematics. It is not just modeling . We need to transform our businesses and industries. Engineering should integrate multiple technologies with agile manufacturing ,services and user demands dynamically.  Techniques like digital twins, AI twins will  come to the front for problem solving. The entire approach to problem modeling and solutions will see frequent changes and additions.
3.Complex and dynamic - many technologies - IOT, data analytics, cloud, AI, 5G and mobiles, crowd sourcing etc-  are put together to create a complex system. Systems scale up fast , reach the entire world and change - metamorphize- dynamically. Systems will be integrated with hundreds of processors, communications, instrumentation and connections to millions of people and devices. So New thinking, new approaches and new methods are needed. We move from centralised and rigid control to distributed , federated and coordinated  control. The word ‘glocal ‘ has a new meaning for engineering. Architecting an engineering system becomes a major and critical activity. It should be amenable for changes.Integration becomes dominant. Designs and architectures needed to be strengthened. Our engineering should shift from problem solving in the small to problem solving in the large- real life problems. Look at systems as an integration of interdisciplinary subsystems , networked and interconnected. Go beyond disciplines.
4.Ambiguous - so we need futuristic vision. We need to look for multiple solutions, not a single solution.
So what does the curriculum in the 21st century should look like? It is a multilayered, multi skills focused one. 
Let us get one view of a 21st century curriculum in the next post.

We need to understand the following about today's ecosystem based on my previous posts.
Jobs are changing towards multiple skills. Roles are not important.  Integration is important.
Transformation and changes are other important factors to be considered in developing an engineering curriculum.
Complexity is a part of our engineering life. Build a curriculum based on complexity. Architect a solution . Data is dominant .
AI is changing a lot of our ways of working and our actions. AI is becoming an integral part of many sectors relevant to us. Focus is on large systems with applications. AI affects us.
India - particularly the industries , and many business practices , agriculture, health and education sectors - is living mostly on 20th century principles and is slowly moving into the 21st century concepts. The emergence of startups and focus on AI are good evidences for our catching up the 21st century concepts. Our digital public infrastructure is a mature one with cloud, aadhaar, UPI, wallets,ecommerce, 5G mobile usage, etc. Our engineering curriculum should reflect this.
While basic engineering principles remain valid, there are changes in the way we conceive, architect , design , build and operate an engineering system. We are talking of lakhs of smart microgrids of electricity, automated and driverless cars, precision agriculture , reachable health care, mechanical industries in industry 5.0 framework, resources conservation , recycling and reuse etc. Our curriculum should reflect this. 
So it is not enough just to tweak the curriculum . We need to transform our curriculum.
An important and essential first step in curriculum development is to change the mindset of the faculty.  There is a well established culture and strong set of beliefs with our teaching fraternity.  Let me look at these beliefs and changes needed on some of our beliefs.
Whenever we look at a problem, we ask three questions- why? What ? and how?.
So far we looked at why - why we need to reimagine our engineering curriculum. Several changes between the 20th and 21st centuries have made curricular redesign mandatory. I am writing these posts to reach at least some academics in our group who can reimagine their curriculum, particularly those in autonomous institutions. That is my motivation. 
We need to look at the question -  what is the curriculum?
I will start with a description of engineering in the 21st century. 
Engineering today is a complex multi dimensional ecosystem. It interconnects and impacts many other systems like businesses, environment ,  nature , economy, and people.

24/12/24, 4:53 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 578
Let us sum up the characteristics of today's engineering .
Engineering in the fifties and sixties concentrated on a device or machine or building /roads etc. Design and practices were prominent in our curriculum.  Jobs were mostly in the machines or buildings or substation areas . Systems approach was unknown. 
By seventies, electrical engineering had a combination of devices and power systems. Systems became dominant in electrical engineering,  but mechanical and civil concentrated on design , analysis and practices.  Some interdisciplinary components like controls, instrumentation and electronics were introduced in electrical and mechanical engineering. Civil looked at the analysis of large structures. 
By eighties, it was the introduction of computers and programming in all disciplines. Large and realistic problem solving became a part of some curriculum.
Robotics was a  topic of importance for mechanical engineering  and it led to an interdisciplinary area called mechatronics. 
Now we will see the impact of data,IOT and AI in our curriculum. We can't ignore these anymore. It will be unfortunate to stay with the current curriculum.
Characteristics of engineering today:
Engineering deals with a complex network of systems , and technologies.  It impacts many other entities like people, economy and nature. So it needs an integrated systems view.
Engineering is more human focused and applications and data focused. Engineering concepts find applications in many domains and sectors like health, hotels, agriculture, finance, urban systems , education . Engineering is now an inclusive discipline. 
Engineering uses new problem modeling and solving techniques like agentic AI.
Engineering is connected to many entities  like people , both operating and consuming  people, logistics, banks, suppliers, purchases, etc. 
Engineering systems are composed of many systems like processes,sensors,  controls,  communications,  computers etc. 
Engineering has many requirements like reliability,  user experiences, cost reductions, higher quality , reuse ,personalisation, fault tolerance,   safety , security, business continuity etc.
Engineering has many stages like analysis, designs, technologies selections, integration, testing, operations , repair, replacements and upgrades etc. 
Engineering needs architecting,  optimization, impact analysis and transformation.


What Is the Status of Engineering in India today ?
It is a highly diverse situation.
Some units are following 21st century principles like digital, horizontal organization, rapid technologies adaptation,  AI oriented , continuous innovation . Tata steel is a good example. Most industries have embarked on digitalization. 

But many also  stay with the  20th century principles of vertical organization, rigid rules based procedures and processes, highly planned and centralised entities. 
Digital usage varies from minimal to maximal levels. Most banks are internetworked, have automated transactions, have centralised databases, use chatbots, mobile apps and the web . Most customer faced commercial and business activities have been digitalised. Ecommerce use is strong and reaches almost the entire country. It handles most commodities and is growing rapidly. 
Unfortunately,  some organizations are primitive like our water, power, gas , and transport utilities. Technology hasn't reached them to a meaningful  level. 
Some like construction,  health and education use technologies moderately.  The use of technologies  is mostly for management purposes. But some processes like medical equipment use modern concepts.  
We need to focus our attention on utilities, urban systems, water resources, cyber security, agriculture and go for new approaches to design .
Governments have built good digital infrastructures. 
IT services are strong in India.  
Startups are booming. 
AI is absorbed by industries and businesses. Its use is pervasive. 
Many types of data analytics are used by many industries .
So, in conclusion, it is a mixed bag. We live with many mixed sets of  principles . The best example is the 1970 saying “ Boeing planes and bullock carts are both used in India”. We live in harmony with old and new . So any education should have a lot of options to choose from

25/12/24, 4:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 579
Let us look at a major  issue to be understood and resolved before going in to discuss curriculum .

 Our engineering faculty beliefs enshrined in us for more than 70 years are
Disciplines like civil, mechanical, electrical , electronics,computers etc are very important. Faculty are highly possessive about their discipline. Their thinking is totally internalized to their discipline. Some look at interdisciplinary ideas, but still the view is from their internal discipline point of view.  Almost everyone identifies himself or herself with their departments - like i am a mechanical engineer or civil engineer etc. This restricts us to a highly or totally internalised views of their domain knowledge and expertise. This internalised view ignores the external world to a large extent or it provides a partial understanding of the external world. It excludes many new developments . It doesn't give us  a holistic view. We need to change this by creating a holistic engineering view first - we should define engineering first, its components, its entities and functions , its linkages to our environment,  society, governance , manufacturing, services, people- . Such an engineering exploration is the first course in engineering to be taught. One of the essential learnings of 21st century is:

A completely internalized view practiced by us in the 20th century makes us a frog in the well. We need to create a complete externalities based  view. This is based on two premises: 
Everything is changing in the world. Nothing is constant forever. Only the time constants  are different.  Today, changes are happening fast. A generation of people see several changes in their lifetime like we saw electricity's impact on us first , followed by electronics changing our communications and entertainment, and then followed by computers and mobiles enhancing our capabilities and now the new change, impact of AI on us. So we need to understand and accept this.
The second premise is that there is a strong Connectivity in many entities and domains in the world. Physics taught us to look at matter - an entity focus. But it moved into interconnections like energy, waves, cosmos thereby making us understand the importance of relationships and connectedness. Engineering embedded mathematics into its modeling capabilities first and then embedded chips into products and will embed AI agents into products. Today's world is a highly connected one in multiple ways - through energy, consumption, communications, real time interactions, data/ information/ command transfers, functional interconnections. So our focus should shift from entities to relationships. 
We need to accept these two principles and change our one directional internalised views. We need to accept the fact that engineering has no borders. We created disciplines like civil , mechanical etc for our convenience. We need a different set of views on engineering.  
Engineering needs multiple skills. 
What are those skills?
People skills 
Design skills
Problem solving skills
Architecting and integration skills
Transformation skills.
We need to create a new startup mindset - select a real life problem, study it, analyse it, select technologies for its solution, get feedbacks , implement solutions, patent it - all these running through three years of the engineering course , providing an end to end views .  Focus should be on problem generation, innovation, problem solving ,large systems thinking etc. 
We will be seeing a very major transformation in the coming days. Let us see that in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks
26/12/24, 4:20 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 580

Let me explain the big transformation that has started now. 
Engineering initially mechanized a function like rotation or lighting. But a task was done by a person like a mechanic using a lathe to produce a threaded cylinder. 
Later functions were handled by software. That is the world we are living in today. 
Tasks needed work flow and dataflow. There were separate mechanisms like APIs or SOA to handle this. It is not a highly scalable and flexible approach. We need a better method of going up a layer beyond the functional layer. That is the job layer. A job may have many tasks to be done to complete the work. So agents will allow us to concentrate and automate from the task layer upwards. It is a big change in problem solving methods.
 Today , We are seeing a tsunami . Move away functions, move away software, here is a group of  AI agents doing your jobs.
Agent is not a new concept. It existed in client server architecture itself. But with AI, it is becoming a powerful super tools organizer. Functions and tools will be underneath the agents, built by AI  and covered by the agents. Agents will talk to other agents to perform a job. Agents will perform  reasoning activities and will take decisions . Agents will consult with humans for specific situations. 
 Another ten years, the world will be populated with millions of agents . It will replace your apps. Are we prepared for these  millions of AI agents? We should start building agents .
Our engineering should move to a new agent concept and era. so get ready for that. 
Like the initial concept of operating systems design by many layered approach, we need to think of complex problem handling also as a multi-layered activity. These layers can be at one level or many levels. A complex problem may have many sub problems - like process system, user system, business system , IT system, data system,  AI system,  etc with many interconnections . Each subproblem may have many sub problems again with many levels of interconnections. We can decompose each such subsystems into many units , each unit doing many jobs, each job having many tasks and each task having many functions.  We can also visualise several activities as strategy, planning, design ,constructions,  Integration and operations. So we end up with a multi networked, multilayered, multi level system. Agents will handle each layer. That can be a simple look of future engineering systems. These engineering systems can be a big manufacturing unit, a construction unit, a public utility like water, power, gas, transport etc. , a services unit like logistics, health, banks ,IT services etc, or a commerce unit or an urban ecosystem management , or a governance unit etc.
Let us construct a multilayered curriculum in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks
27/12/24, 5:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 581


Now, let me provide the layers for a balanced 21st century curriculum according to my thinking. 
Engineering principles relevant to the disciplines . This is well developed, but needs pruning and tuning.
Society and People focus-  there is a big shift of engineering from industry focus to people focus with people empowered by SMAC . Humans are intertwined with engineering systems like power ,water, gas utilities, transportation, smart city, smart and precision agriculture, and digital health systems. primacy is on cyber physical systems . 
          People use highly interactive and easy       to use devices and generate a lot of data on social media and ecommerce.  human way of solving problems and  processes for actions and decisions 
Problem discovery takes place at the people and society end  and not so much at the industry end . So start with a social projects and surveys program.
Data focus- big data and analytics are the main oils for industries and businesses. About 80% of jobs are estimated in this area. 
        4.   Design focus- designs and architecture of systems, technologies, data, applications and integration play a crucial role in building and  operating engineering systems. Design is considered from many critical success factors like reliability, real time responses, correctness, self repairing systems, adaptive systems, 
5. Problem solving layer - we need to look at many types of problem solving techniques. Use digital twins, heuristics , multi objective Optimization etc. 
6. Relevant engineering systems layer - security, safety, reliability, user experience based dashboards and apps, engineering subsystems , IOT based instrumentation, etc
7. Technology, integration layer - architecture,  convergence, integration principles, software architecture, 
8. Applications layer - applications in many sectors like health, education, governance, small and medium enterprises, fintech, agriculture, city management - water, drainage, power, transport, safety, security , data generation etc. Use AI techniques .
9.  Practices layer - how do we operate, fault detection and rectification, data collection, continuity of operations and services etc. Use data and social media .
10.  Support disciplines layer -materials,  mathematics, optimization, software development, mobile apps, statistics, ML, computers, IoT,  blockchain, process control , etc. 
11. AI and applications
We need to structure courses based on the above principles and layers. We need to reduce the overwhelming attention to theory , components and machines.

Use these layers to build the courses and curriculum.

Greetings 
Dks

“American society proclaims a commitment to individual freedom and autonomy and sees choice as a hallmark of liberty – the more choice, the more freedom. But too much choice isn’t so liberating. In fact, it can be paralyzing. Take charge of your decision-making by evaluating your recent choices. How much effort did you expend – in time, research or emotional stress – on each step of the process? How much did that benefit your results? Identify categories of decisions for which you could use simple rules of thumb to minimize the cognitive and psychological burden. You may find you don’t need to make some choices at all.
Schwartz.     Paradox of choice.

Your comments, Dr Mathur is interesting and thought provoking. It takes me back to look at my life and the interactions  with academics, CEOs , industrialists, bankers,administrators and politicians. Some are part of this group.
 It was a great journey started with a lot of struggles and disappointments In the beginning - definitely not for a weak minded person- . But it moved on from the eighties to a very exciting , interesting and useful journey till now. The experiences were great. The learnings were many. There were many challenges and a lot of opportunities and excitements.

Let me start with my struggles In the sixties and seventies.
Only a few loonies in India believed that Computer science will be a major developing area in the world. Most felt that it wouldn't survive. So I was a loner at iisc for about two decades and had a lot of trouble convincing other faculty about my work. 
It was  a surprise that most scientists and engineers didn't accept IT as a major influencer in the system. Even Prof Dhawan realised it only by 1970 . I had a long struggle to sell computers to most academics. While some are indifferent- most scientists were- , some were scornful and considered it inferior and some felt it a good tool for numerical problem solving. There were some who refused to believe in numerical methods and felt analytical solutions are the only answer. I was in an environment of strong control theorists and analytical Mathematicians. I was happy that Dr Devanathan appreciated my work and helped me. We built a strong mathematical and computational foundation for my thesis. But he died just after i submitted my thesis. He didn't see my thesis. I don't know what way I would have chosen , had he been alive . Till 1970, I was the only one working on major computer problems and till 1975, I was the only one teaching CS subjects. While seniors refused to believe that CS will be a major area, junior academics had a vague and mild curiosity in this area. That is the status not only in IISc  but also in most parts of India. Even though we started the automation masters program in 1973 itself, the program allowed only two CS courses . Slowly I started teaching courses on data structures, systems programming, operating systems  and computing systems. The school looked at a separate ME program in computer science only past the 1980s period . We started BE in CSE only in the nineties after a lot of fights. My stay at Manchester and IIT Bombay were exceptions. I had great interactions with many faculty members in several other disciplines. They all accepted CS as a separate discipline. Incidentally , I played a major role in introducing independent BTech and MTech in computer science and engineering in IIT Bombay  in 1978. 

Till 1980, there were only three  groups with whom I could talk about computer science. 
One was definitely TIFR , but my interactions were restricted here to a few people. But there was a good group there. The second was NAL, where I started my computer journey. NAL under Dr Sankar was working on numerical algorithms. I could talk to Mr Janardhan and Mr Panchapakesan there in addition to Dr Sankar. The third was HAL. Here I could talk to people on programming and hardware. Mr Desikan, Mr Ramakrishnan and Mr Venkatasubramanian were the hardware guys and Mr Venkateswara Rao,  Mr Kantaraj were programming persons. I used to talk to all these people. 
I will continue in the next one.

Let me continue my response to Dr Mathur's query. 
Past 1985, my professional life transformed into a large stream of activities in addition to my commitments to IISc , academics and research. I improved computer science teaching in many universities and colleges. I got involved in computerization, networking , and IT related activities in many organizations.  That continues till now. 

As regards political support, i got a lot of support from senior administrators, CMDs more than politicians. The best support I got was from Mr SM Krishna . I knew him as a minister, deputy CM, CM and external affairs minister. He was a thorough professional, never interfered in my advice and activities. 
I also got a lot of support from both Mr Ramakrishna Hegde and Mr Veerappa Moily, both chief ministers of karnataka. Ramakrishna Hegde was a reforms oriented person, encouraged new ideas and alternate thinking. I met Mr Abdul Nazeer Sab, a great minister who installed hand pumps in most villages in a mission mode. His work was impressive.  
Mr J H Patel ,who was also a  CM ,was a wise and witty person. He gave me a lot of respect . While giving me a shawl in a public recognition function organized by karnataka land army , he said with wit that the shawl is heavy and won't be of use to me.
I should remember three  education ministers - Mr Veerappa Moily,  Mr Viswanath and Mr Govinda Gowda . They  gave me respect and freedom and supported my activities. Mr Veerappa Moily used to discuss many things with me. His perceptions were pretty good.
Mr Viswanath helped us to run the mahithi sindhu program without political interference at any point. 
When I was involved with the starting of KSCST,  I interacted with Mr M Y Ghorpade, the finance minister then. Later , when I was KSCST secretary, I worked with Mrs Nafeeza Fazal, science and technology minister . She wasn't aware of S and T or even administration.  She listened to me carefully. Actually I started my discussions with her during the first meeting with a hurt statement that I will resign as secretary if she wants. She understood that I didn't like her attitude and talk. She changed immediately. She learnt very fast the many facets of S and T administration and was very helpful to us.
Our only interference occurred during Mr Bangarappa's regime. 
When I was in the state government,  I met many ministers and interacted with them.
I also met several MPs , MLAs  and MLCs and had smooth and meaningful interactions with them. 
I had met some union ministers ,but my interactions were with officers and Secretaries of various departments and agencies and not with any minister. 
That is my story of political interactions. My interactions brought me in contact with thousands of officers in many departments, organizations, institutions, colleges, universities, banks and industries at many levels and many kinds - serious researchers, sales managers, development engineers, faculty members, government officers, bankers, utilities officers, etc. I got  immense benefits from my association with all these thousands of people and also thousands of students. Almost all had a belief and trust in me and my advice and help. I cherish those memories. 
Even at this age I am able to interact with young faculty and students . That was due to my associations, friendships and trust.

I thank all those who read my posts and make value addition through their comments. 

Let me return to my involvement with the world bank's  TEQIP program during the period 2000 to 2015. 
We saw one reform under TEQIP- curricular aspects. 
The second aspect is the review process and almost 360-degree feedbacks. It helped us to understand the difficulties in the system and steer the college or institution towards better academics.  
My involvement was at a  deep level. At the national level, I  was involved in deciding on performance factors . At karnataka level, my involvements were more detailed and in depth. I will discuss this later.
I felt that I can influence changes in academics, equipment purchases , research and social concerns. 
I also felt that most institutions were keen to improve their academic standing. They went on to inculcate research culture and publications culture seriously. 
I visited several institutions several times in many parts of India.  My biggest opportunity was meeting thousands of people - some several times - , and discussing with them on various aspects. It was very revealing. It opened my mind to one most important aspect of our academic system. It is what I want to call as diffidence amongst the faculty and a big divide, a false pride  and ignorance  in academic thinking and a great degree of vacuuosness  amongst the faculty and management . The institutions ignored and neglected the staff particularly the lab staff. Managements used office staff mostly for spying. All these worried me and worries me even now. I feel that we don't find academic freedom in most institutions. Instead ,there was an academic fear. I can give a lot of examples why faculty keep their mouth shut as they are not sure of the  responses and reactions from the managements. There were some institutions which were exceptions. Most NITs had a good academic ambience and freedom, but also academic diffidence. 
Dr Mathur built  a good review mechanism. I became a performance auditor and mentor for India  and karnataka. I was an auditor for several karnataka colleges like SIT, SSIT, SDMCET, and Many NITs . I was also a mentor for three karnataka colleges and a few NITs. 
This meant that I had to visit about 12 institutions twice a year. 
We visited , had discussions with faculty and students . Our discussions were revealing. 
It opened my mind to the realities that was there in the country. 
Let me discuss some encounters and their effects in the next post.

“Life can be wonderful as well as terrible, and we shall increasingly have the power to make life good.  Look at the ways of making life wonderful and follow them . Let me discuss some incidents that happened during our discussions. 
In one engineering college , students said that faculty do not know how to speak English. The faculty of the same college said that students lack English communications capabilities. I discussed this lack of language skills with the management and introduced courses and training program to both groups. The improvements were visible in two years. So after two years, when I met the staff, one member asked me, “ you have focused on English training for faculty and students. We also want to acquire language skills”. I felt that it was a genuine request and recommended to the management to conduct training programs on language skills to all employees. 
Similarly the employees said that the management does not permit them to attend evening degree programs while they encourage faculty to pursue ME and PhD degrees. When I raised it to the management several times , they were not agreeable to the suggestion of permitting the staff to go for an evening BE program. I found most staff were having better articulation skills than the faculty. Generally I found that staff were more loyal and more realistic and more satisfied with their work than the faculty.
In general , in most institutions I visited , I saw some amount of dissatisfaction among faculty  for some reasons. One reason surprised me and showed our class consciousness clearly. In one of the NITs, during my interactions with faculty, many said that there is a discrimination between a regular professor and a CAS professor. CAS means Career advancement scheme. They said that even the wives of professors taunted the wives of CAS professors. This shows the deep entrenchment of class - equivalent to caste- amoungst academics in NITs. This meant that such programs aimed to motivate faculty had demotivated them. They are not able to look at this in a dispassionate manner and appreciate the effort of the government in introducing the scheme. 
In another institution, students said that they were happy that their teachers were using ppts. But the faculty copied ppt from some foreign university. They didn't read the ppt before projecting. It had details of a class test in a particular room in a particular Building. The faculty didn't mask this information.  It showed that no effort was put by the faculty to prepare the ppts.
I came across many such incidents.

02/01/25, 4:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 587
“The power to become habituated to his surroundings is a marked characteristic of mankind.”
—John Maynard Keynes

During my TEQIP interactions in another institution ,an NIT,  I asked the faculty ,” why have  the curriculum , contents and books  remained constant for 50 years?”
I got a shocking answer from a senior professor that the program has been running for 50 years and that is proof enough that it has withstood the test of time and so the program is good. 
He didn't look at changes happening during these years. It showed the deep divide in the thinking by faculty in  traditional disciplines as compared to the faculty in  the changing disciplines like electrical, electronics, computers etc. 
It made me think ,” what should be the primary qualities of a good academic apart from in-depth discipline  knowledge and teaching ?”
Our selection process for a faculty member looks at the educational and experiential credentials and the interview mostly explores the knowledge level of a person. I usually ask the question - what will you do in the coming five years?. The answers were disappointing . Most say primitive things and say they will do more research. They didn't have an outward look at what is happening in the world and didn't have any curiosity. The primary qualities that I considered essential in an academic  are - 
1.look outside not just inside.  Outside views are essential for survival of all. That is the mistake millions of people in their life, business and professions do and that is the main reason businesses die. 
2. know the past and present,analyse and pick the important aspects to remember.
3. look at the happenings in the world not just in your area but in a general perspectives 
Understand the broad realities of engineering, society,  nature , people . Professional life does not begin and end with knowledge in a discipline. Knowledge in multiple areas and multiple views is essential. Know the proverb - master of one and jack of all trades.
4.Realise that the world is changing faster. You can’t gain a path by looking at seniors in the profession.
5. Ask questions about the future directions 
And 
6. decide on your work to tune in with the changes and reality . Adjust and adapt to the transformations. 
That is the bare minimum set of  qualities expected from a good academic. That will remove the stagnation in our education system. But I found that most faculty don't like changes and don't want to change. They stay in the past. It is worrisome that even now many live in the 20th century , but students are in the 21st century. We need a mindset change here. 
Appreciate comments.
Greetings

Mr Uday Singh comments on my posts:
