Thanks. Your words are highly encouraging. I am surprised that I have been using WhatsApp to post information on various aspects like engineering,  education, hinduism, my life paths etc for about eight years and most of you read my posts and some comment on it . It is remarkable.

Mr Giri comments on academics. 
“Good morning sir, I totally agree with you. I have been in IAB for few institutions and what I have seen is faculty not willing to learn new things and teach students in a much more interactive way. I strongly recommend "Make it to learn it"  approach to teaching. Again, these are my view points.”
My response
I can't take a brush and sweep all academics. The incident I wrote about deals with one individual . Most academics I know are very good, committed and keen to learn. 
I should discuss two changes managed by faculty in the 1980s and 1990s.
A very large number of faculty picked up microprocessors, learnt most details and ran microprocessors courses in all colleges. Several wrote books also. Many became proficient in microprocessor applications and most students did project work using microprocessors.  
The second is a bigger transformation I saw amongst the faculty. The late eighties and early nineties saw most colleges starting computer science programs without any CS trained faculty to run the program. Again I saw thousands of faculty members in other disciplines rising to the occasion and they took the challenges head on, learnt many CS subjects and taught them. It was a first hand experience for me. I have trained many such faculty on CS subjects. Could we have waited to get trained teachers before starting the CS and later the IS programs ? My answer is no. The reason why India became the leader of IT services in the world is due to lakhs of graduates we produced just at that time. If we waited, we would have missed these opportunities. I played a major role in giving AICTE approvals for CS and IS programs in most colleges. My hunch worked well for the country. 
There are many academics who are part of this group and show keen interest in learning. I also mentioned in my comments the deep divide between the circuit disciplines and traditional ones. Even there, mechanical engineering is changing with controls and robotics as part of the curriculum. I am still amazed at a team of 14 students building a go-kart at PESCE, Mandya and several students working on electrical mobility. Both Prof Ravindra and  Prof  Dinesh Prabhu, who were responsible for these projects are members of this group. 
I am glad you gave me the opportunity to clarify my posts and my opinions.
Thanks.

04/01/25, 5:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 589
“In a set of articles written in the early 1980s, Leontief made one of the most infamous claims in modern economic thought. What technological progress had done to horses, he said, it would eventually do to human beings as well: drive us out of work. What cars and tractors were to them, he thought, computers and robots would be to us.”
“Today, the world is gripped again by Leontief’s fear. In the United States, 30 percent of workers now believe their jobs are likely to be replaced by robots and computers in their lifetime. “

Mr Joshi has commented on my interactions:
“Thanks Sir , your interactions with variety of people are very interesting. Reading such writing makes me feel , how everyone behaves relevantly or irrelevantely. Human nature. 
Happy new Year 
Regards”
My response
I want to bring out the following factors for success in life and profession.
Most engineers , both professionals and academics, think that engineering is to deal with technologies and their applications. They feel that engineering has nothing to do with people and interactions with many persons. Their interactions are mostly one-sided.i had seen most civil and electrical engineers treating contractors without respect. I wanted to show that people relations is an essential part of engineering and cannot be ignored. It should find a place in our curriculum. 
The 21st century is seeing more people involvement  in technologies and business through their feedbacks, social media posts etc. Mobile technology is dominating businesses today . People influence the success or failure of a business. Feedbacks play a very important role. That change is not understood by most. They are living in the past. 
Secondly , I want to discuss scale. Most businesses , banks , utilities, have expanded and become big. 
What do we mean by scale? 
Is it business as usual but with more branches and more Customers? 
Hindustan Lever scaled up with a large number of distributors and delivery points and massive and attractive advertisements. So it succeeded with a good strategy and a good reach to customers. 
But let me look at another example - banks.
Post nationalisation,  banks added a lot of branches . But they continued with the business as usual approach ,that is to ignore customers. I was IT advisor to Corporation bank and had an account with a branch in Malleswaram.  Whenever I went there, the staff were found gossiping and ignoring customers. A retired ED of the Corporation bank had to call the GM IT to resolve a problem as the branch ignored him. I had similar problems with other banks’ branches also. Fortunately ,net banking and mobile banking help us now. 
Private Banks are no better. My  relationship manager doesn't know anything and hence wasn't useful. 
Greetings
Dks
05/01/25, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 590

You may be wondering ,” why did I talk about people oriented engineering and scaling in the last post?”
There are reasons - 
The urgency to know  about life, living, and purposes and avoidance principles - no slavery
To be aware of current situations and consequently to change our life.
I am worried that we will miss the 21st century bus. Already we missed two decades of 21st century thinking. We stayed happily with  the 20th century ideas. This is not just for academics -  Don't think that only academics should carry the burden and say what they want, but I don't care , I will stay where I am.” This will not work now. All are affected. All of us should be prepared for the changes AI wil usher in and adapt to the situation. 
The next tsunami of AI agents is on us now. It will create major turbulence and disasters in our life. We need to urgently move from the complacent life to anticipate these turbulences , be prepared for them and make changes in our life. 
That was my concern. Also cosmetic changes ,like the way banks scaled up by ignoring people , will not be enough. 
Maybe , we should look at the vedic period organization of society, life and villages and see whether we can look back at a spiritual approach to life, - frugal and minimal and natural living, increased consciousness, harmony, Sharing, peace, ahimsa, love all , avoid hatred and jealousy, avoid competition, comparison, consumerism, etc. 
That is what sanatana dharma is, not what the intellectuals or pseudo intellectuals proclaim. Unfortunately, we discarded vedic living and moved first to the Puranic way of living and   later to the British way of living. We are aping the west happily calling the frugal livers as fools and misers. Consumption dominates our life today. Buy more cars, you can’t drive them in bangalore roads, you don’t have garages to keep the car ,keep it on the road and worry about theft. Think whether such a living , such a rat race is worth? How do we reverse and move away from the race? 
Is living for a job and being a slave to the company, as propounded by Narayana Murthy,  a correct thing to do? 
Are we aware of burnouts.
Last do we understand life and purposes/ meaning of life, balances in life?
Any thoughts? Please comment

I posed a number of questions in yesterday's post .Let me continue my discussions to look at some aspects. I hope these open our minds and make us question ourselves , to contemplate and get answers. We may get many answers , appropriate to our life. 
One aspect is clear: The  vedic kind of living slows down the  impacts of technology . When you slow down your living and work with others closely as a society and depend on local resources mostly  , you learn from the mistakes of the fast living urban , modern jetset and choose wisely the technologies you need. You don’t become a slave of quick commerce, meganopolis , slums. consumerism, wealth accumulation, aspirations, data, and AI. 
Gandhi talked about the sanatana  way of living and practiced it in Sabarmati ashram.  Well, in 1960 , that was what Schumakar reinvented as intermediate technology and some Indians called it appropriate technology. But we didn't listen. Nehru was going at full throttle on the soviet way of living leading to disasters. 
But today may be a turning point for us. We need to relearn - how to live a purposeful life?
 So a wise and careful living as well as a totally new paradigm need to be evolved by us to suit us and not copy the western models. Stop following the west and  start leading them through a different lifestyle. 
Life and living in an ever-changing world is very serious and needs our attention.  Politicians , intellectuals will not be of any use or guidance. Don't look at them. Look inward. 
Hence I thought a cautious post is desirable. I feel we need a changed mindset, a changed way of living - definitely not business as usual.
We need clarity on life and living. Life is multidimensional. We have personal life, family life, professional life, societal life and life with friends etc. So We talk about balancing amongst these multiple lives. That is what many people are talking about. Most talk of family work balance. But balancing is a complex act and most of us lead a one sided life missing the balance. 
First, let us ask: who are we?
Understand this:We are prisoners of our beliefs, habits, our limited knowledge, our myopic perceptions,our egos, our biases, our limitations and constraints, our dependencies, our deficiencies, our ignorance, our laziness, our limited skill sets etc. With all these, technology adds another dimension of slavery. We are bound by all these and live in a limited, ignorant,  small and error prone world. We need to unshackle the bonds and enlarge our vision . 
So start with becoming aware of yourself, mindful of activities, removal of biases, ego, fear, emotions, constraints, capable of alternative and genuine thinking or imagining , and learn to become insightful . All these are conscious and continuous activities. 
But there are two other important components in our life. One is the spiritual or religious part. It is an important part for many Indians.  It gives us peace of mind and satisfaction. The other aspect is the learning or Jnana part. It is considered important. But most ignore this. 
Now we need another aspect in life - adjustments and adaptations to transformations. We were doing it in a small and planned way till now - like changing jobs, houses, cities etc. But we need more changes in our lifestyles, our thinking, emotions, beliefs , behaviours and attitudes . 
In addition , life may be viewed as a series of decisions and actions. 
Lastly ,we need a purpose or a goal. It can be related to oneself, or family or society , or our professions or to our religion or country or nature . We may have multiple goals also. One common goal that some follow is to help others or advice and educate friends and associates. 
So a good understanding and 360 °view of life in a modified form is essential and we need to evolve a different paradigm of living.

Let me continue my discussions on life and living. Rigidity is the main influencer in one's life practices. There is a Tamil proverb:
“If someone doesn't bend by usually years of years of age, will he or she bend while reaching 50 years of age. “
What do i mean by rigidity ?
As we grow up, we pick up many habits, biases, behaviours and beliefs.  Let me call them as baggage that we carry with us all through our life. Rigidity means we don't discard the unwanted or irrational biases and beliefs. That means we don't learn in our life , we don't have an open mind , we don't have viveka- a knowledge of right and wrong, dos and don'ts, good and bad.  
The first ten years of life as a child is crucial in one's mental development.  Good habits , behaviours are important.  But we spend a lot of time only on the  physical development of a child. 
Do we address the mental development? Do we allow a free flow of curiosity  and questioning. A child asks thousands of questions at the age of four. A lot of doubts linger in its mind and it searches for answers. Do we handle these questions effectively and honestly? That is the time to start a child on the path of open and critical mind . Do we do it? That is the period when a child develops an analytical mind , an unbiased thinking mind, a mind without any mental baggage, and that is the time for a child to develop insights. We seem to fail at this stage. Some children who are genetically tuned for critical inquiry start thinking and finding answers. Today's danger is google and its biases. Tomorrow's danger is AI and its biases. We need to address these. Past  Experiences won't help us. We need to create new experiences just like a four year old child. 


Let me return to my journey with TEQIP.  
Other discussions I had in most institutions where I visited as TEQIP auditor or mentor centred around teaching practices. There is a consensus to the use of ppts in teaching. But there were problems :
Most didn't know how to prepare a ppt. I got complaints from students that some ppts had too much information that they couldn't even see it. The utility was gone. I suggested a rule that no ppt should have more than six or seven lines.  Also the use of black color on a white background was recommended. This crowding a ppt with a lot of information came from the practice of ohp transparencies. The transparencies were expensive and we minimized their usage by putting more information in a sheet. I had prepared thousands of ohp sheets for my courses. I used ohp for more than 15 to 20 years. But our teachers never used ohp or ppt. The preparation time for a lecture was minimal for many. But they struggled and improved their teaching. Many were good teachers, and learned new concepts quickly. 
TEQIP focused on research and the achievement here was great. About 4000 faculty registered for PhD by the end of the first phase. This was done through financial incentives . 
TEQIP gave me a lot of opportunities to meet with hundreds of faculty members, thousands of students and staff  in many institutions across the country.   
I was fully involved with TEQIP phase 1 and 2 for ten years.

Let me continue with my decade long association with TEQIP.  
The states of engineering education in karnataka at the beginning of TEQIP were :
1.It was controlled by the university VTU mostly but also Bangalore university and Kuvempu controlled one college each.
2.The curriculum was old and rigid.
3.No college had autonomy. 
4.No research was going on in any college including KREC. Some senior professors in KREC,  who were good and sincere and dispassionate, even argued with me that research is unnecessary.  It surprised me. 
5. True,  most colleges were in a better position in the country . All colleges had started computer science and information technology programs.  A good number of faculty were good and sincere and tried hard to do their best to improve the quality of education and built a good lab . Quite a good number of  faculty were really great.  They want to improve the standards and quality. Most improvements depended on them.  
6. The aided Colleges maintained a good quality and led the engineering education in the state and country.  Private colleges weren't in that league at that time ,but some were serious about improving their quality. That is the reason to include  some  private colleges in TEQIP.  
 7. Some  colleges had started MTech programs. 
So our first major  job was to work for getting autonomy for all TEQIP institutions. Universities didn't want to lose control over these colleges. So a bitter fight was on the cards.
We had an excellent state program implementation unit. Mr Chandrasekhar headed the unit and was very proactive and helpful.  The DTE was also supportive of this program. Education secretary was also very supportive of our suggestions and actions. Even the minister for education supported our efforts.  So in effect the program was a great success in karnataka. 
So we planned well , moved forward towards autonomy . Since the colleges had no exposure, we conducted a one day workshop on autonomy -types of autonomy like academic ,administrative and financial,  functions entrusted to a college, structures and governance mechanisms, curriculum development,  evaluation of students in courses , examination system etc. This program was organized by our new foundation -FAER- with support from SPIU. 
I developed  a complete blue book on curriculum - stressing on flexibility , balancing, credits ,total number of credits,  credit requirements, gpa,  pass criteria,  student's workload,  etc. SPIU conducted two workshops in Orange county. I discussed curriculum,  governance and related structures like academic council etc. It was a big and detailed task and effort from me. 
We also had several raps with colleges to review, advise and assess dedicated progress. 
We had a lot of difficulty in making these colleges autonomous. The major obstacle came from the University.  Mr Kausik Mukerjee, secretary education,  resolved this by piloting a special bill in the assembly. We appraised even the minister on the need for autonomy. The university wanted to retain its hold on the colleges and issued a mandatory guidelines to the colleges. 
Today , the colleges have gained a mature understanding of autonomy and have implemented autonomy responsibly .  It was a great experience for all people and stakeholders. I was part of several academic councils and we broke many myths and beliefs. 
 TEQIP second stage converted SPIU into a facilitation unit - SPFU. I was involved in most activities under SPFU. 
I found that  the TEQIP program was very useful in improving the quality of education. 
I am happy about my  participation in this program.

“It seems to me that without understanding the way our minds work, one cannot understand and resolve the very complex problems of living. This understanding cannot come through book knowledge. The mind is, in itself, quite a complex problem. In the very process of understanding one’s own mind, the crisis which each one of us faces in life can perhaps be understood and gone beyond.”

Let me continue with TEQIP. 
I got support from many for my activities in TEQIP. Officers of SPIU and later SPFU were very helpful. All principals of colleges and directors of NIT listened to me when I summed up the discussions in my meetings and my suggestions as well as my report. This briefing was a good system. 
Some didn't accept the  comments by the faculty fully  . This was normal. There was only one violent disagreement in NIT Hamirpur. The director told me that his faculty didn't understand my discussions and gave incorrect answers.  He wanted me to redo the exercise of discussions with his faculty. He wanted to talk to his faculty. I saw the fear prevailing amongst the faculty during my meeting with them. I thought that another meeting would be a doctored one and so I rejected the suggestion of the director.  He was upset. He talked to Dr Mathur and requested for another audit . Dr Mathur rejected it. That was my only discard.  Everyone  cooperated with me, appreciated my suggestions. 
Many suggestions were implemented. 
Most students had complaints on two aspects- one is poor teaching and insufficient attention to teaching by teachers.  This was common not only in the colleges but also in NITs. 
The second complaint by students was about the curriculum - it is old , not relevant , rigid etc. Some students complained that teachers didn't know Java and its applications. One student - in 2001- commented that Python was not taught. Many students were aware of the latest technologies but not their teachers.  
The students  appreciated the new equipment and computers bought under TEQIP. They were happy with the modernisation of class rooms.  
As regards the faculty, I didn't see much appreciation from them on the efforts made for improving research and publications as well as the conducting of hundreds of learning programs for them. There was no seriousness with most. But younger faculty appreciated these efforts and opportunities provided to them under TEQIP.  They took advantage of these schemes.
They had no interest in social activities. They thought it to be a hobby. 
I talked about English language improvement before. 
Let me look at a few suggestions. 
I talked about building responsibility in the younger faculty and motivating them. This was implemented in some NITs and a few colleges. 
Even NITs had difficulty in implementing  electives in their curriculum.  In one NIT, I was told most students opted for a few management courses on offer. This created a heavy workload to some faculty and heartburn to others.  I suggested that they should increase electives in each department. Running one or two electives in each department is not correct or enough.  
Most institutions lived for a long time on mandatory courses to be attended by all students without choices.  Faculty were happy with this arrangement. They didn't want to move to a choice based curriculum. This difficulty was experienced by most thousands. 
My second suggestion to the institutions was to allow students to register for other departments courses as electives. This was considered strange . Departments felt their importance will be reduced. 
While all institutions accepted to build a choice based curriculum , their mindset didn't change. They were thinking in terms of a rigid curriculum. Most allowed only two or three electives in their curriculum.  
It took a lot of effort. Let me discuss some changes brought by me in the institutions. 
I felt design concepts  and thinking is important for increasing creativity and team work. So design courses were introduced in some colleges. 
Some institutions introduced more than one project work. 
I was worried about the lab work becoming  routine without experimentation. My efforts borne fruit in BVB with Prof Shetter listening to my anguish. 
I felt programming courses were not effective. Students didn't write large programs.  In an head-on discussion with faculty, I found that faculty were reluctant to implement this suggestion. Even though I explained my practices in the seventies to make my students to design a full assembler as part of my course on systems programming and design a concurrent operating system at a time when computing power available was low. I also told them that the students appreciated this. Still most institutions live on programming in the small.  I didn't succeed in this . 
I will continue this in the next post.

Let me ask and answer a question. How did my interest in curriculum start and evolve?
When I was in school , I developed many  habits like  reading books, listening to elders, contemplating for long hours on many topics like engineering, economics, country's freedom struggles etc. I didn't know anything  about freedom or curriculum  But before I finished my schooling, I realised the following:
World is changing a lot. 
Electricity will be a game changer.  Industries are coming up. 
Our economy is struggling. 
Education systems are improving. 
Caste is becoming a very dominant force despite most decrying the caste system as bad. There is a strong anti brahmin sentiment growing in Tamilnadu. 
Idealism was dying  and greed was paving the way for life. 
People were complaining that values are dying. 
But I had no idea how my life will evolve and what I will be. 
I took changes as they appeared,  never thinking about them. 
I had imbibed  some principles and values like frugal  living, truthfulness, honesty and integrity in my dealings, respect for elders, responsible behaviors,  contemplation, analyse before acting or deciding, due diligence,  ownership and Keenness and curiosity  in learning about what is happening and what is new. I learnt many languages - English ,  Hindi and Sanskrit  in addition to Tamil. I knew a little bit of Telugu . 
I don't have principles or opinions on other aspects. I had an open and flexible mind. 
These guided me in my life. 
Actually , I wasn't sure what I should do after the completion of schooling . I was keen on studying further. I was strongly motivated to pursue higher studies.  I had an inclination towards mathematics and engineering.  
It was my uncle who admitted me in PUC in Xaviers college. He was also instrumental in directing my sister's education at Palayamkottai and later in  Madurai.
My desire to go for engineering crystallized at that time. 
When I joined the engineering college , I was told that our engineering course will be a new experiment. It will be a five year program.  The curriculum will be different. We had a very strong mathematical foundation covering several areas of mathematics. Also we had a good two years of general engineering courses. Also more general engineering courses continued till the fourth year. Electronics courses were introduced for the first time. All these made me realise the importance of curriculum in education. 
Later, my encounters with the engineering curriculum really started at  IISc in the year 1969. As I discussed earlier ,I played a significant role in moving the IISc program to a choice based credit system - we called it a unit system. That was a major reform in our curriculum . That wasn't an easy task. It met with many hurdles. We built structures , guidelines and advisories to construct a successful system that is working till now. I was part of every structure and was with the system for a decade. 
Then I chaired committees to build both a graduate program and an undergraduates program in computer science. That was not an easy task. Later I chaired a committee to convert the UG program into a four year integrated ME program. 
That strengthened my involvement in curriculum development.  
More in the next.

“If religion is not about God, then what on earth is it about (for heaven’s sake)? It is about us. It is about manipulating our brains so that we might think, feel, and act in ways that are good for us, both individually and collectively. Religious traditions work like the bow of a violin, playing upon the strings of human nature to produce harmonious relations between individuals and their social and physical environments. Religions have always been about this business of adaptation, and they will always remain so. This is not to say, however, that any particular religious tradition will remain adaptive. Religions sometimes outlive their adaptive utility and occasionally become positive threats to human survival.”
Religion is not about God. Loyal Rue

My interactions with universities , colleges, IITs and NITs were four dimensional.  
The first interaction with colleges in karnataka started in the late seventies with KSCSTs  students projects program. It brought me in contact  with a large number of principals,  and faculty. This relationship continued for several decades and was very useful for all of us. 

The second dimension was giving lectures and even running full courses in the area of computer science. This happened in many states and enlarged my horizon and understanding.  
 The third was curriculum development. We will see more of it .
The fourth was my participation in faculty selections in many colleges, REC/NITs, IITs and universities in many states. Most particularly were happy with my presence in their selection committees.
An awareness of the curriculum for engineering started in the eighties.  As most universities had mostly arts and science faculty and no inhouse engineering faculty , they needed experts to help them in their engineering curriculum development. I was invited by many universities in Karnataka , Tamil Nadu and Andhra states to their board of studies. I also participated in the curriculum process in both REC Trichy and later TCE Madurai. My involvement with TCE started with their board of studies for several years and with their series council. I continue with TCE even now. 
I had a tough time with most of these meetings. As most faculty who participated in these meetings had no knowledge of computer science and as there weren't many CS faculty, they used to fill the curriculum with many electrical and electronics courses. I literally fought in all these meetings. It used to be pretty bad. But I achieved some success in most places. I had to keep in mind their constraints.  So the curriculum was a compromise. I had similar problems in the faculty selections also. It was much worse. In one institution,  they picked a civil engineering ME as a lecturer in the CS department because he knew FORTRAN.  I protested and wasn't invited to the selection committee afterwards.  In an REC,  where I was a regular member of the selection committee,  I was not invited when they wanted to select an instrumentation person as a faculty member in CS. 
So I suffered on both counts.  
Slowly , the system improved. Still , many favoritisms and biases  persisted.  
You may ask me,  why then I got involved with many institutions and universities in their board of studies and in their faculty selections. 
There are two reasons. 
I get requests from many vice chancellors and principals , many are known to me. The requests are genuine and they all wanted to improve the quality of their programs. Many heads of computer science departments also wanted my support to run the board of studies. 
Secondly,  I wanted to help these institutions to grow. They respected my views and  accepted about 80% of my suggestions. I saw definite improvements in these institutions over the years. In addition, I also gave series of lectures in many institutions.  So i saw a positive growth of computer culture in those institutions. 
That gave me immense satisfaction and that was the reason for my continued  participation in these activities 
One of my meaningful and effective contributions happened in KREC in the 1990s. I could shape the curriculum of three programs in three days of work to my satisfaction. All participants accepted my suggestions enthusiastically. None came up with excuses. They were prepared to learn new concepts. It was a great experience for me.  
Another major change that happened was in REC Trichy. It was a very tough task. One member, HoD of computer science in Annamalai  university,  was a power engineer with no knowledge of computer science and he wanted to include many electrical courses in the CS curriculum. I was against it. It led to unpleasant circumstances. Finally I could build a balanced and modern curriculum. 
Similarly ,my stay with the academic council of TCE, Madurai for many decades ,has been encouraging and fruitful.  I cherish my association with my alma mater.  
We reconstructed the CS curriculum in Narsee Monjee institute of management.  Prof R Natarajan arranged this meeting. We developed a methodology of consultations with students and faculty , followed by the preparation of a detailed curricular program to be finalised after discussions with faculty. It was a three day program. It went on very well . It was a meaningful and participatory exercise.  I liked this visit .

13/01/25, 4:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 598
 Mr Aravind comments on religion:
“Very well said Sir, about religion. Religion is regarded by the common man as true, by the wise as false and rulers as useful. Religion is part of the human make-up. It's also part of our cultural and intellectual history. Religion was our first attempt at literature, the texts, our first attempt at cosmology, making sense of where we are in the universe, our first attempt at health care, believing in faith healing, our first attempt at philosophy.  Just as a candle cannot burn without fire, a person cannot live without a spiritual life. One cannot believe in God, unless he believes in himself.”
“For Einstein, "science without religion is lame, religion without science is blind." He told William Hermanns in an interview that "God is a mystery. But a comprehensible mystery. I have nothing but awe when I observe the laws of nature. There are not laws without a lawgiver, but how does this lawgiver look?”
My response
Wonderful. This deserves views from us. We ask the questions- what is religion? Who is god?
There is no universal answer.
When you want to get universal perceptions or views , do we realise that even science doesn’t have universal statements. Most statements were constrained with assumptions like linearity ,non interference etc, simplifications , approximations and conditions. Proof is relative. Even absolutism is not easy to define. So to expect universally acceptable answers for religion or god is not correct. 
Do we have a universally acceptable view of anything in our life? The answer is no. 
Reality itself means different things to different people. Also our view on reality is dynamic , keeps changing. Even our views on engineering have been changing drastically over the years. We have no difficulty in accepting the fact that everything is changing.  
When you accept that we live in a VUCA world , why don't we apply that kind of thinking to religion and God?
There are no single or easy answers to these questions. There are multiple answers , not easily refutable and most are valid and acceptable to groups . 
Each one of us needs to build some level of clarity on religion and God in consonance with the ecosystem and culture one is familiar with and understands  inwardly .
While many say that religion deals with mysteries or unknowns , it is also clear that religion itself is not properly understood by many. Multiple definitions and understandings exist. My quotes in the previous posts taken from the book “ Elevate” and J Krishnamurthy are related to this. 
So let us accept multi dimensional views on religion and God.  Upanishads try to define Brahman by different methods. They accept the fact that these need to be perceived by individuals through internal realizations and can't be explained in writing. Your views are similar.
Thanks
Greetings
Dks
13/01/25, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Today is Arudra Darshan . It honours the cosmic dance of Lord Shiva. This dance represents five activities: Creation, Protection, Destruction, Embodiment, and Release. It symbolises the continuous cycle of creation and destruction. The festival celebrates this ecstatic dance, which is believed to take place in every particle.
Today is also Thiruvathirai. It  is the nakshatra or "star" of Shiva as per the Tamil calendar. It is believed that on this day, the Goddess Parvathi finally met Shiva after her long penance and Shiva took her as a saha-dharma chaarini (equal partner).
Let us pray to Lord shiva as Nataraja and Parvathi. Let us understand the cosmic cycle.

14/01/25, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 599
There is a query from Mr BS Murty on spiritual infrastructure. I will take it after the query on religion.
Dr Sanjay Chitnis adds to our discussion on religion.  
“Very insightful comments on religion Sir. Regarding dharma, we say it is much deeper than religion. Yad dharayati Sah dharmah.. Is it the cosmic design that ties the world together? Realising and following that will make society ethical and progressive. Please give your views on this Sir.”
My response:
I said religion has many views. Let us expand that idea.

There are several components to a religion. 
Religion can be viewed as principles, scriptures,  practices, etc. 
Let me list the core aspects of a religion as perceived by us, Hindus. It is based on my understanding of the Scriptures and not on what is happening in the world today. 
1.Religion looks for unknowns . Cosmic world is a great unknown . While the western religion ignored the cosmic space, Indian philosophy looked at a vast cosmos. It identified various constituents of the cosmos like planets, stars etc. It plotted the movement of the sun and some stars. So the major focus of a Religion is search , search for unknowns, search for causes, search for existence , search for life, search for understanding etc. It is a search for ever and about eternity and everything.
2. Religion looks at infinity. Indians  looked at the cosmos as infinity. Scientists found that space is expanding. Infinity is a key concept of our philosophy. 
3.Religion looks at eternal as another aspect .  It refers to two different domains. One is a code of conduct for a society called sanatana dharma.  There are several aspects discussed here. Dharma becomes a major operational, not just philosophical , constituent of a religion. It is called eternal. It is what we expect everyone to follow.  It is the core of an operational religion. The other component of “eternal” deals with  cosmic consciousness. Even stars have birth and death. They are not eternal.  Only cosmic consciousness is eternal not the constituents 
4. As a corollary of eternal, it also postulates that everything is changing and not eternal. what is seen and perceived varies with each person and so that picture   is not real , it is a myth or Maya. There are millions of views of the world and the happenings in the world. 
5. Religion looks at wholeness - purnam.  
6. Religion is not concerned with relative aspects.  It focuses on the absolute. 
7.Religion looks at interlinkages,  relationships. Science focuses on finiteness,  matter and entities. Religion focuses on infinity, eternal, and interconnectedness. This is a great concept of our Religion. We looked at the cosmos and built links to our life. Hinduism is the only religion which discusses the relationships between stars, planets, zodiac signs, and people. Astrology studies the dynamics of the linking. Even our both calendars employ the positions of stars ,moon, and planets along with day and month. We understood that weather , seasons depend on the positions of stars and planets . We realised that emotions depend on the moon's waxing and waning.  We understood the fact that the sun determines our actions like planting , harvesting etc. We used solar energy to a maximum extent. 
8. Religion looks at evolution. Rig veda talks about life spans of each entity. 
9. Religion is primarily concerned with perceiving and understanding unknowns in a correct manner.
10. Religions like philosophies focus on truth , various forms of truth and also ascertaining ways of proving a statement as true or false. There are four types of negatives: something is different, something didn't exist, something existed before but not now, something may exist in future. A lot of discourses of a Religion deal with the aspects of ascertaining the truth.  
11. Religion looks at our life , purpose of our life, principles of life, societal living,  sustainability etc.  It deals with interrelationships with nature , harmony.  It wants an order - rhythm in Sanskrit- to be established in a society. So it creates an entity with unlimited powers and eternal and calls it God.
12. Religion emphasizes jnana- of different kinds. It talks about acquiring jnana from different routes and gives a strong emphasis on self introspection, contemplation and self learning.  
13. Religion defines good practices. Hinduism gives us options of selecting a path. It talks about nishkama karma - Bhagavad Gita. It provides detailed instructions for a correct living.  It defines a purpose in life- increase  your consciousness, search for the eternal. Some steps have been suggested to help in the search. 
14. Hinduism also looks at the human system and proposes ways of living longer and happier through many approaches like yoga, meditation, prayer, Homa and yaga, temples . It emphasizes holistic and combined and harmonious and shared living. 
15. Acharyas explain the various components and built easier systems and practices to follow.  Bhakti and rituals became the easiest aspects to follow. 
Comments please.
Greetings 
Dks
14/01/25, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Today is makara sankranti or pongal. It is based on the movements of sun. Sankranti refers to the transmigration of the sun from one zodiac to another in Indian astronomy. In Saurmana varsha (Hindu Solar year), there are twelve Sankrantis which correspond with twelve months of a year.
Makara Sankranti means sun transiting to north , also known as Uttarayana. This festival is celebrated across India and also neighbouring countries. Sun is worshipped on this day. It is also known as the harvest festival.  Let us pray for the prosperity of all. 
Greetings.

23/02/25, 5:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 639

Definitely , the period 1984 to 2000 was a good period for the country. There were many developmental initiatives that started in this period of liberalized economy. Quality of life improved. 
By the late 1990s the world saw the shift from industry to people . Two major developments in technology helped the people. 
One was the Internet,  another was mobile phones. 
Actually the 20th century ended with a comedy and a tragedy. 
The comedy was the Y2K problem. Doomsdayers predicted the entire computer systems in the world will collapse in 2000 due to confusion in the representation of the  year inside with only two digits, ignoring 19. This will make programs misbehave. Companies in the west panicked. It was a bonanza time for Indian IT companies like TCS , Infosys. They got huge projects to walk through computer codes, mostly written in Cobol , and rectify the year representations. This gave jobs to lakhs of people in India.  TCS developed an automation to pick out specific  codes representing or using a year and modify it. It was faster . Anyway, Indian companies didn't bother. I had discussions with governments , and they weren't worried. 
So when 2000 arrived, no disaster was in sight. Everything was fine. Maybe Indian IT companies may take credit. But it was much ado for nothing. 
But the major development was the Internet, the world wide web and the internet was the magical door of Alladin  that opened the entire world to a user. Its power wasn't understood by developers. 
So it started with the dotcom bust, not a boost . There was no indication that the Internet will be influencing our life, our global reach, our global communications, our decisions, our social and professional activities, our business , and most importantly our knowledge about various aspects of the world - world at our fingertips. It started slow, picked up pace and covered every aspect of the world’s actions. 
We at iisc started using email by early nineties.  But the first commercial mail was introduced by Sameer Bhatia and that was a success. Many startups which came up with .com suffix to offer services died down quickly. The reason was simple: the Internet was easy to use and embed in applications. There was no great technology to be developed to use the internet. Most people browsed the web easily. 
The Internet brought in the concept of standalone applications that are usable by people, the only requirement was a PC and a digital connection- dial up line first , DSL later . 
Initially, it was used for sending mails followed by skype,  a video calling application. Many older people used it for email and calls. It was convenient to talk to people anywhere, a big change in our lifestyle and a lot of happiness. 
Most software applications were modified to connect to users through the Internet. So online connectivity was easily established with people across the world. 
Google’s search engine was a great invention used by millions today. It made a PC an indispensable tool to access Information.  So Internet became very powerful. 
Then came the ecommerce revolution ,experienced by most of us. It brought materials, food to our doorstep. 
Internet development was based on standard protocols- http and tcp/ip. 
That ease of building websites, hosting them and putting and receiving  Information and accessing millions of websites are easy tasks. You don’t need an IT expert. So technology moved into people’s hands. 
More in the next post

05/03/25, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 643

Let me start from where we left off. We are looking at society technology interactions and the role of the government and people in our country's development. 
Let me sum up the situation till 1995.
I made a few  statements on the progress of our country. 
1.we initially went through improvements in agriculture after independence. We reached self sufficiency in food grains production by 1980 through the green revolution. Food grains were available at subsidised prices . Governments stored millions of tons of food grains in many locations across the country.  This was due to the efforts of researchers at ICAR. Government played an important role in this. 
2. Governments played a big role in improving our education infrastructure. We had many engineering colleges running in the country. But our technical education gained a big growth only in the nineties with a large number of colleges set up by private people . That helped in bridging the gap in technology with the west, and also allowed us to move into new disciplines like computers, software and IT.
3.  We followed wrong models and policies . This led to a big stagnation of industrial development in the country till 1990. But after 1985, our technology development moved faster. Still, technology restrictions by the US remained for us for a long time. IISc could not buy supercomputers. Dept.  of space, defence , DRDO could not import the latest computers. Even the Oracle database was not available for them. It took a decade more  to move towards normalcy in the relations with the US. That meant that our real technological progress started in the 21st century.  
4. The government constructed a number of power projects, but it didn't address many aspects in these activities - Firstly, the affected people were displaced with meagre  financial compensation and sometimes people are displaced twice.
 Secondly the ecology of forests was drastically affected. Madav Gadgil talked of desertification in once dense forest areas. Government didn't address catchment area development in their strategy. This also increased the silting rate in our reservoirs. 
Thirdly, most electrical energy is transmitted to larger cities far away from the power plants. Areas close to a power plant never got power supply. Most villages in two hydro  power rich districts of karnataka - Shimoga and uttara kannada districts- didn't have electricity till the nineties.  That led to inequalities in distribution.
5. In addition to the wrong Anti computer policies of our government,  the labor union also opposed the introduction of computers in any organization. Our banks , insurance, railways and governments suffered because of this opposition. The passenger reservation system of railways was successful and veey popular and hence was not opposed, but the freight scheduling system was scuttled by George Fernandez in the late eighties. I was involved with that project . It didn't see any progress for decades. 
Similarly the electronic voting machines , a great innovation , was opposed in the late eighties or early nineties  by Mr VP Singh , ex PM. I had to defend it to the press.  Both ECIL and BEL built these machines using robotic processes. Rangarajan - writer Sujatha- was in charge of this project in BEL. I supported the Introduction of this machine. The attacks continue even now due to total misunderstanding of the technology. I had heated arguments about the correctness of the machine with an electronics engineer from NAL.
6. The nineties saw a good pace of development. This was mainly due to a benign governance and people's efforts. People played a major role. Industrial progress was satisfactory. 
7. The major change we saw in the nineties is the emergence of IT services. We took leadership in this IT area. That kept our economy in good shape and created many jobs. The entire ecosystem was fueled by the private sector . The government only looked at IT development in the government sector with the help of NIC , but most activities in the government were conducted by the private sector. CMC was the only government company. ECIL’s efforts on building computers were abandoned by the nineties.  
8. Migration of a large number of Indians to the middle east for the general services like house construction, house work ,  technicians , engineers etc led to a comfortable foreign exchange position. 
9.. By 2000, India was not considered by many as a poor country with sadhus, snakes , beggars,and rope tricks. India's technology and services competencies  were recognised by the western developed countries . 
10. Communism as a soviet or Chinese model died in the seventies. The communists became middle class parties . Similarly, by the nineties ,socialism as a concept died. Is it due to vote buying in elections. All parties had only rich candidates and corruption flourished.
11. It shows that people loved freedom,  didn't bother about governments but changed them based on emotions and personal benefits . 
More in the next.
Greetings 
Dks
06/03/25, 4:08 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 644
We saw several aspects. Let me look at some negatives particularly of great concern to me - education and technology.
What are the areas with lacunae?
Education system - still Macauley based producing clerks. Innovation is ignored. Querying is forbidden. Rote learning is preferred. The focus was on science and mathematics, even in those areas,  analytic thinking approaches were not accepted. The system was highly colonial. 
It produced a large middle class of people with a colonial mindset. That is a big tragedy. We see that attitude percolating in our thinking and actions even now. It is a big danger. 
Education is not holistic, does not develop character, ethics, values in students. It does not encourage thinking and curious persons. This is a big disaster in today's AI world. Despite this system , many people did well due to societal ambience and genetics strengthened by thousands of years of gurukulam learning. 
Culture was thrown out. Mogul history was brought in with focus. Regional histories were ignored. Human sciences were neglected. This is a serious problem. It destroyed our heritage, made us inferior with a guilt feeling in our culture and bonded us to a western set of beliefs and practices. We almost discarded the extensive and well developed Indian philosophical system with vedanta and six darshanas and tried to follow western philosophical ideas. India is the only country which banished religious and spiritual thoughts from our education system. We don't tell people about the extensive knowledge repository in our upanishads, sastras , our grammar system and a very large collection of Indian literature in many Indian Languages. Even the philosophy is not understood by most . 
People ,though not taught cultural aspects  by the education system , learnt it from parents, neighbours, community , festivals ,temples , and tours to many culturally rich places. That tradition survives. Interest in Ramayana and mahabharata persists. Oral learning was more effective than formal education. I admire the resilience of common people, the greatest example being  the just concluded Kumbha mela.

Again we inherited an outdated university system from the British.  It was unfortunately an affiliation type within hundreds of colleges in a university with no independence and almost no say in the curriculum or education system or evaluations. This crippled our higher education. This system is still there . Fortunately there are some autonomous institutions designing their curriculum and conducting their courses and evaluation. Most university curriculum is old and irrelevant. This further crippled our education. Fortunately Country has many leaders in IISc, IITs, TIFR, BITS, and some good private universities. Most universities didn't have a good computer infrastructure and didn't initiate CS research. They were far behind engineering colleges in computer infrastructure and teaching. 
Story is the same now - most universities are  blissfully ignoring AI applications and research. It is a big tragedy. We need an action plan with a big stick to enforce it. 
Employment focus was on routine functions. Jobs didn't give opportunities or challenges to show one's  intellectual potential. I feel  that most retire intellectually at the age of thirty. 

Hope that gives a good picture of our society . It shows that development moved separately from education and had limited influence from education or government.  Our rich traditions of trading over centuries helped in our marching forward. 
More in the next. 
Greetings 
Dks

Later he started an NGO called EQUATE. I participated in his programs in many cities like Jaipur, Silcher,Surat, Delhi, Raipur, Karad etc. 
He has a query:

You conducted a large number of training programs in different institutions under the banner of EQUATE INDIA
that included many engineering collages NITs etc.
I remember though the progams were for selected faculty but all faculty were freed from their classes to attend your deliberations and all felt that your visit to their campus was just like a saint visiting some temple. I remember when faculty thanked me for bringing you to their institutions.

Unfortunately the period 2002 to 2020 were the busiest and craziest days in my life. I was very busy at IISc as a dean and chair of several committees, I was closely associated with NRDMS project of both KSCST and DST and was involved with the other world Bank on the state of environment for karnataka as a coordinator and a key topics of energy writer - this involved hundreds of meetings and travels including a small accident of my falling down in a town in karnataka , I think Bellary. 
Later from 2002 to 2020, I was busy with IT transformations in several banks starting with Indian bank and then to PNB, corporation bank, syndicate bank , Vijaya bank ,IDRBT etc. I was also with TCS in the research board.  It was tough . Still I kept my commitments to you and lectured in most of the programs . I liked those experiences . Also both you and Dr Bhatnagar were involved and committed in the program - kaaya - body-, Vaacha- voice- , Manasa- mind - ,  Buddhi - intellect- , Hriday - heart or love- ,  all in the task. That impressed me, who has participated in hundreds of faculty development programs for more than 40 years , conducted for teachers and industry persons , and government officers.
The second thing I liked was the topics given to me. 
I enjoyed preparing for my talks. I loved my talk on research methodology a lot. It started with definitions , processes, selection of topics , publications and anecdotes about my experiences at IISc.  This topic was discussed in many programs and liked by the participants.  I also sharpened my presentations. You also participated and made critical comments. 
I always loved to talk about pedagogy and teaching for more than forty years in several engineering and science colleges and so I was happy to give a series of  lectures on pedagogy . I still vividly remember the talk at Jaipur- I think it was our  first  visit . I was talking about definitions and discriminations with examples of the second law of thermodynamics, program vs  process vs thread, hub.vs switch vs router , etc.

I saw in most programs your keenness to improve pedagogy. You introduced many methodologies in those programs. 
It was a great time and great experience. Thank you and Dr Rajani for those excellent days.

09/03/25, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 647
Another comment:
HS Nagaraja
“It is really interesting & illuminating feedback from the readers of your posts. Pranams to all.
🙏🙏🙏🙏🙏”
Thanks.

There is a comment from B Sambamurthy :
“I was listening to one of the academic. He says that we focus more on Publishing Papers and less on getting patents. He suggests a paper to patent ratio of One. As you know patents are important to protect technology for our country's development and progress.
Any thoughts please?”
My thoughts. 
We need to understand the realities before setting targets. 
Publications culture is more than two  centuries old . It was an accepted norm for academics. It provided open access to people in libraries  without any commercial intentions. Many swear by it. 
Publications were the only approach to convey research outputs. Later conferences came up to provide opportunities for publications. 
These offer great interactions and cross fertilization of ideas. I have attended hundreds of conferences , given keynote talks and also research presentations. I saw a lot of benefits from them. Academic collaboration takes place through this route. 
There are hundreds of journals to publish. So there is a variety. 
Publications generate peer group interactions and meetings. This aids in generating new areas and strengthening existing areas. Most technology developments came through this route. Conferences play a big role in networking. 
Most advances came through research Publications and not patents. 
There is a well tested method for refereeing papers and maintaining standards. 
The process for publications takes lesser time leading to a compounded growth in research. 
But patents are at the other end. They are the outputs of a research and development activity. 
Patents process is not clear to many researchers.
Patent culture should evolve. It is happening slowly. 
There is one patenting authority compared to hundreds of journals and hundreds of conferences . Opportunities are high. 
Multiplier effects of patents are way low compared to publications. Publications quickly multiply. 
Most technical developments in many disciplines came from publications Not patents. This is true with all developments in computers. Patents lead to jugaad,a small innovation and  not a breakthrough. Breakthroughs will be rejected by the patent office. There may be exceptions but not the rule . It is rare that patents lead to multiplier effects and great innovation. Based on innovation one publishes many papers and apply for patents . These papers are available to most institutions and are indexed . 
We need to create an environment for patents similar to what is existing for publications. Then only they will become popular and acceptable.
The reviewers available for publications are in thousands in every area but not in the case of patents. The Patent process is more of a repository . 
So I feel it is unjust to equate both. Many academics will consider it a sacrilege. 
True , Patents have a commercial role and is doing  it well. But patents won't aid furthering of research by academics. Each has a separate role . Let us not replace one with another or reduce the role of research publications to promote patenting. 
Normally it is our practice at IISc  to measure an academic with a multidimensional approach - publications, research projects, consultancies, interdisciplinary work, teaching . All people are not measured on one yardstick. One may be a great paper producer. Another may handle large projects with development outcomes like large software, instruments, renewable energy devices etc, some do good theoretical research, some applied research. So the measurement indices and processes accept diversity. 

Hope my views are clear. Patents have a role but can't be compared to publications. The one-  one ratio is a disaster for research. 
Greetings
Dks
10/03/25, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 648

Before I  move to discuss the 21st century and discuss the  people first transformation that happened , there is a very good suggestion  from Mr Mahesh Jain . Let us look at it ,as it is important. 
Mahesh Jain:
“It will be interesting that one could design a sample education system until the 12th standard. The most important learning in human education is 1 to 12. We can create a graduate course and then post a graduation course. There are many takers as AI is out to democratize tutoring, at least. History has given a chance to India to re-establish its own education …”

My response 
I am glad you raised this. I know you are keen on improving the quality of education , not just teaching learning processes alone. 
This is an important approach for everyone of us  to think about. 
But our school education system is a politically sensitive, almost a mafia based system. Untangling is not easy. We shouldn't go for changing the system, but motivate about 100 schools to follow a different path and show that they can produce a better system that can be followed by others. But there is also a danger there - they may end up as elitist institutions and not motivating institutions. This will create a new caste like IITs.  But IITs did play a good role in modernising our engineering education. 

Before I start discussing this, i feel that we should know the problems and difficulties.  So let me start with a brief ,but not comprehensive  status of our school education . This should really focus us on feasibilities. Let me say upfront that at least some should look at a holistic map of multiple views and multiple approaches.
Let me start with a big positive. NEP , new education policy addresses some concerns raised by us on a holistic , enquiry based, practice oriented balanced education system.It wanted to reduce the curriculum.  It has many good points. But its implementation is not going in a good direction. It is embroiled in controversies. Some discuss its focus on three languages only. The biggest problems are: politicalisation
 Rigid views, policies and implementations
Poor understanding of NEPs focus. 
Looking for “ one size fits all” solution. 
Are we a single unitary and completely homogeneous society ?
Are all students have similar interests, similar learning capacities ?
Is our curriculum relevant?
Does teaching attract students to learn?
The answers are no. 
We don't look for locally tailored locally relevant and locally meaningful solutions.
 But most governments  missed the main point and found easy politically acceptable paths. 
NEP wanted to shift from Macauley's focus on colonialism and shift focus to Indian cultural roots, not just historic routes. That was misquoted by many. Hindi zealots and antihindi Dravida got into this tussle. 
Anyway,  unfortunately while the policy wanted to reduce regulation and inspections , implementation does the reverse. The only way it works is to dismantle the entire inspection machinery of the states and centre and go for voluntary accreditation processes , not by one agency but by many. This current inspection system builds a strong rigidity , a sense of fear and a strong belief in the essentiality of the state run education systems . These departments of education had a lot of staff , but focuses on trivialities like teacher transfers which breeds corruption and political power. Do we need these huge directorates of public instruction? Only the SERTs play a reasonable educative role. A lot of intellectual talent is wasted there in the DPIs. Bringing in IAS officers didn't solve the problem. States should abolish these separate directorates as most jobs are done by the secretariat departments and ministers. 
Most educationists focus on the delivery methods and not the foundation . Even their approach is constrained by formal and known thinking. Unorthodox approaches are not accepted by the experts. Pedagogy research needs to be modern and broader instead of firefighting. Unfortunately most educationists are doing firefighting operations. Firefighting is required, but we fail  to look beyond. 
There are hundreds of schools which experiment with education. Many private institutions have experimented with different models of education. We have several international schools which emphasize on personality based education and focus on English, Debates, sports , outdoor activities etc. Valley schools - three of them and I visited two- tried different approaches  . But the regulatory system is strong and forces such experiments to fail. 
I have seen some schools which provide hindu cultural and religious learning in the morning and the regular board based education in the afternoon. This works well , but we need dedicated, motivated and interested students and highly dedicated teachers and management. I see more such schools coming up in the south. 
The major hurdle to a holistic system is the very strong - and assumed to be proven - urban middle class belief that their child should get a strong STEM education so that they can join IITs or go abroad. That is the minimum  yardstick of a middle class parent .  We need to work with this belief. That is important.  You can’t ignore the current belief in the concept of “ What is success in life?” We can't swim against this tide.  Or we should focus on rural children. 
There are many avenues for learning. Internet, social networks and open access has brought enormous amounts of highly enriched and understandable  information within the reach of all. This is a great boon for all. This can revolutionise learning. This has proved the Rig veda dictum “ acquire knowledge from any source wherever it may be and from anyone .” We need to remove the notion that we learn only from the schools. That dictum is dead now. Learn from the whole world. Most information is at your mobile tips. But caution is important.  Many traps exist. 
We need to encourage their use by children instead of saying - don't use internet, don't watch youtube  etc. We should educate and guide our children to watch properly , discuss with them on the topics which they are watching and keep on advising caution.
 My grandson learnt a lot through these publicly available and good as well as in depth information to learn about animals, insects and birds and later on Roman history. He has written books based on this model of learning. He even learnt languages. 
Can we ignore the experiences of our earlier gurukula system which taught knowledge to some, warfare to some others, artisan skills like foundry , steel works ,masonry , carpentry etc to some and medicine to some others through a long apprentice scheme? Can we learn from those experiences lasting thousands of years or learn from Macauley?

Let me look at the problems of school education in the next post.
Greetings

11/03/25, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 649
We need to understand children, societal pressures and directions, purpose of education, formal and informal education, schooling, purpose of life before defining what one should learn. Again I emphasize there is no one size fits all solution. 
What are the realities wrt education now  ?
About children -
All don't have the same capabilities and interest in learning and absorption. This is accepted by the educationists. This means - 1. A graded time adjusted learning is needed.  2. Don't thrust your knowledge into children. Allow them to read, observe , discuss, experience and learn. Teaching should be minimum. 
           3. Let a child learn by itself. That is.     crucial . Let a child observe, draw conclusions , discuss and find errors and change conclusions.
All do not get interested in all subjects taught. Some like mathematics, some find it difficult. Some like music, some like psychology, some like nature etc. True, it is possible to inculcate interests in children but that needs original thinking and individual efforts. This means “ don't have a uniform curriculum . Don't thrust your ideas on children. Give them freedom. Have lots of choices . Learn from our gurukulam approach of diverse learning . A carpenter taught carpentry. An ayurveda doctor taught ayurveda. A yoga specialist taught yoga. A musician in the south taught carnatic music. A koothuppattarai taught Street plays. “  Rigidity should yield to flexibility in learning.
 Children learn faster through actions , games and stories. Ramayana, mahabharata,  Panchatantra, hitopadesha are good examples of learning. Today videos are also effective tools for learning. Khan academy is successful in teaching school level mathematics . Lots of video contents are available on the web. Schools should use these resources instead of chalk and talk. 
Children are normally curious, bubbling with questions. Schools don't look at this aspect of learning through Q and A. Use AI agents to address children's queries in a big way. We kill curiosity. That is a danger. 
Rote learning without understanding is Common. We need to look at cognitive ways of learning. 
Unfortunately there is a caste system in our syllabus. Arts , crafts and sports are the lowest caste subjects. Mathematics occupies the highest caste. This is a strong Directing force on children . It was necessary in the mid 20th century. We see its effects now - high level of stress, reluctance to go to school, poor health , poor behaviours and habits, competitive racing, work for today mentality as against a futuristic outlook , building ambitions in children , etc. We are producing slaves to jobs ,not thinking and reasoning persons. Most of us are in a hurry and a rat race  without knowing why we are running like this. It is not easy to get out of this system. We have more hackathons today, more TV competitions . We want to build superstars and end up with stressed and unhappy people.  

Let us look at what one should learn in the next post.

12/03/25, 4:45 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 650
First thing we need to understand is that there are no simple ready-made solutions which can be directly used by schools everywhere.
Localisation is important. One of my friends told me ,"Give me a few suggestions. I will go and implement it in a nearby school. “ There are no generic solutions.  But solutions are tailorable locally and we need to produce videos. 
Secondly, there are some who complain about our education system without coming up with solutions. 
Thirdly, there is a tendency to compare our system with other countries like US, UK etc. This is a highly myopic view. We can't compare . We need solutions for each district and each group of students, not an omnibus - one size fits all - solution or a cut and paste US or UK solution. 
Let me look at some low hanging fruits - some solutions easy to develop and implement.  Let me look at a few Problems in schooling that can be corrected. 
School teaching is one directional. Teachers talk about what is in the book . Most don't explain any concept.  It was routine and monotonous. It doesn't interest students in learning and it motivates a few. Students bunk classes because of this monotony. This can be corrected through use of supplementary resources available on the web. We need to build resources , modify some and publish them. 

Let me discuss an approach i followed in the mahithisindhu program. I found text books are condensed versions of concepts and had minimum exercises and practices.  So we decided to do the following. 
Identify difficult topics in mathematics, sciences , English and social sciences in 8th, 9 th and 10th class syllabus. This was done by selecting a few good teachers in each subject and requesting them based on their experiences to identify difficult topics. Then we decided to develop content for these problems with video and Hypertext.  
We gave the content development activity to a company.  They had no clue as to how to build content. I had to put them through exercises , told them to use the video media more effectively. After several sittings we got the CDs out and distributed them to 1000 high schools. 
The outcome was remarkable - the pass percentage in SSLC jumped from 50% to 75% in five years . 
We need NGOs to take  up content development in a big way and put it in youtube. It will be monetarily attractive. Also list reference resources for each topic that are available. The Khan academy example is known to us. 
Secondly we need a feedback oriented way of identifying good teachers - not the bureaucratic way followed now. We should introduce students' feedback. 
These are simple methods and can be handled. CSR funds can be availed for this purpose. 
School time table is packed. Every teacher wants to finish their portions. 

NEP talks about reducing the total content. States should implement this. Reduce the total content by 30%. 
Create many electives. Make history optional. This is doable. States need to address this aspect seriously. When you know that more than 50% of the government schools had no motivation and are slow learners, should you not address the problem of reducing the content. I feel the contents should be reduced by 50%. 
Teachers should worry about reaching important concepts to students rather than completing the portion. 
Just like the additional resources we talked about for students, we need to address the same issue for teachers. 
NGOs who develop contents should also develop teachers reference book containing reference materials, teaching methods and explanatory materials . 
Explanations are not clear for all. It is a big problem even in higher education systems. We need to explain the terms with examples and analogies extensively. Most books don't explain energy , work done, power, and effort . It is a simple job but cryptic statements are found in books. Look at the problem with an example like a car moving by a kilometer. 
What is the work done - distance travelled and total weight . 
What is the energy consumed- calculate based on fuel expended and calorific value. 
We need to build realistic understandable and localised examples. There are many such definitions which are not clear to many. There are many views to explain them. You can’t explain energy and working without talking about input , output and efficiency. I found even physics books for plus 2 or degree had not explained this properly. So again it is a task for NGOs to identify such Basic terms , take a few examples understandable by both teachers and students in rural areas and explain using videos and calculations.

13/03/25, 4:01 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 651
I am writing these posts on education- what do we teach? as the objective. I am looking for a constructive solution ,not a criticism of the existing system. We identified problems in the existing system in order to find an alternate method to solve the problem. 
I had no intention of criticizing school teachers. They are the victims. Some suggest - change them. It is not a solution. Solution is systemic . Teachers are powerless to change the system. so we are looking at building a support structure for teachers- provide them resources, contents and explanations. This will help both teachers and students.
Many of us see teachers in a big city and create a view of a teacher. We need to see a teacher in a village - what are the resources at her disposal? None. What is the freedom available to a teacher? No freedom. 
Hence I want companies to develop supplementary materials to improve delivery and understanding.

Let me look for a concrete basis for building a learning structure. That was the request of Mahesh also. 
What aspects should our education be founded on ? 

I think education has multiple levels. The first level deals with the primary purpose of living . Life and living are close to everyone. So all aspects dealing with life and living should form the core of the primary education system. 
So we need to look at 
Life and living
Personality development 
Society aspects
Our environment 
Reading ,writing and arithmetic , speaking, languages
Health and hygiene , 
Requisites for learning.
as core areas. These should be focused in the five years. 
The contents should be based on  answers to  the following questions:
First aspect 
Who am I?
Who are we?
What is a family, society, and why do we need it?
Why are the qualities like order , law , respect, help etc required ?
What should be good habits, values, behaviours, that one should follow ?
How do I live my life?
What should be the principles to be followed in life ? Why?
How do we live?
What do I need to live? 
Where do I get my resources - water, air, food, clothes , houses ? How are they acquired?
Are we wasting resources - conservation?

Second aspect :
What is nature?
What are water sources, trees, plants, Forests, soil ,and their  importance ?
What is the relationship of nature to us?
What should I do to preserve , conserve and enrich nature?

Third aspect 
Study of human body, functions, health, healthy living , Good practices, 
Yoga , meditation , concentration , physical exercises , cleanliness .
Language and literature - reading and writing habits. 

Fourth
Essentials for survival 
Basic Mathematics - arithmetic  , shapes , geometry
Science - energy, heat , plants, reactions etc
Technology - transport, electricity for light , 

Fifth
Society 
Governance
Culture
Geography , geology 

We can have electives - agriculture,  smithy, pottery, drawing , book reading at 4th standard level ,simple  accounting, 

These form the basis for contents development. The next level will deal with skills development.
