14/03/25, 4:03 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 652
I decided that any solution should consider our existing state of schools and teachers. I can't ignore them. Secondly, it should not rock the boat, don't suggest big changes. Thirdly, look for alternate support systems. 
The contents proposed by me looks similar to what is going on now. Some explanations are needed.
The differences are
Focus on localised content. Talk about the houses one lives, the street where a child lives and plays, the family , the trees, roads, water sources, local transports , etc. Discuss how does a family live and survived, role of materials in life , money in life. 
The next step is a starting level of discussions on the purpose of life. It is complicated. But start with the need for love, harmony, help , peace, respect , non violence , tolerance . Build values through stories of great people. Start with some stories from mahabharata,  hitopadesa - stories of friendship exists in many places. These two components- lives of leaders like Gandhi, Vinobha, etc , Scientists like CV Raman, JC Bose, Sarabhai, Bhabha, etc , engineers like M  MVisvesvaraya,  S Dhawan, etc, industrialists like JN Tata, TVS , etc, public service persons like Baba Amte, etc , great kings like Karikala chola,Rajaraja chola, nalawadi krishnaraja wodeyar etc and great administrators like Diwan Seshadri Iyer, .
Then move to community living, stability, order, community services etc.
There should be greater emphasis about nature - local plants, trees, water sources, water storage , rain, animals, insects, soil, agriculture, .
There should be discussions on resources used - water, metals, pots, dresses, food items, vegetables,  lighting, fuels, house construction materials . We should discuss how resources are used - this is missing . The sources of resources needs to be told.
The emphasis should be on conservation - nature can meet our needs not greeds. 
The most important part is human sciences -our body, mind, functions, our emotions, beliefs,  habits, etc. We may discuss our problem solving skills . 
Then comes communications - languages , reading, writing, discussing , etc.
The focus should be on what an child can relate to and experience in its home and neighbourhood.  No point in talking about planes. But simple technologies like irrigation, roads, house construction, furniture making, bricks making, cooking, lighting with electricity are useful.  
The other focus was on health - cleanliness, nutrition, yoga 
There is an important focus on meditation, yoga, concentration, contemplation and creativity. I will expand it later.

Let me give an example of Gandhi's philosophy ,deserving serious consideration to humanise education.
Core Principles:
Truth (Satya): Gandhi considered truth as the ultimate goal and the foundation of all actions. He believed that truth is God and vice-versa. 
Non-violence (Ahimsa): He saw non-violence as the means to achieve truth and a path to self-realization. Ahimsa meant not only refraining from physical harm but also from any form of violence, including hatred, anger, and injustice. 
Satyagraha: This is a method of conflict resolution and social change based on truth and non-violent resistance, emphasizing the power of love and moral persuasion over coercion. 
Educational Philosophy:
Gandhi believed in "an all-round drawing out of the best in child and man - body, mind and spirit". 
He emphasized practical education that equips individuals with skills and knowledge relevant to their lives and communities. 
Social and Political Philosophy: a very needed component in an education system.
Self-Sufficiency: Gandhi advocated for self-reliance and simplicity in living, promoting rural development and local industries. 
Equality and Justice: He fought for the rights of the marginalized and oppressed, advocating for a society based on equality and social justice. 
Sarva Dharma Samabhava: Gandhi believed in the equality of all religions and promoted religious harmony and tolerance. 
Seven Social Sins:
Gandhi identified seven societal ills that he believed were detrimental to human well-being, including:
Wealth without work
Pleasure without conscience
Knowledge without character
Commerce without morality
Science without humaneness
Religion without sacrifice
Politics without principles 
Influence of the Bhagavad Gita:
Gandhi drew inspiration from the Bhagavad Gita, viewing it as a guide to action and a source of moral values.

15/03/25, 5:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 653

Before we delve  deeper into education, i want to look at a few fundamental aspects:
What is knowledge?
What is culture, its role ?
Why religion?
What is thinking ?

There are some cobwebs within most Indians which need to be cleared ,there are  many biases to be understood ,addressed and rectified, there are guilt feelings which again should be discussed without caste feelings. 
Only then we should be able to come up with a good plan. We can't sweep all our beliefs, biases, our culture and roots under a carpet and keep a colonial mindset. That will produce an artificial education as it is happening now. 
An education system has to be open and balanced and should discuss all aspects of all sections of our society with some focus on the local society. 
Let me start with What is knowledge?
Children or even adults learn better if they know 
Why are they reading a topic? 
What is its relevance? 
Can they relate to the topic ? 
Then they learn and retain that topic better. 
I want to relate an incident from our mahithisindhu program. 
Children were excited.
1.they can see a computer and use it.
2.they read the book given to them with a lot of interest.
3. They stayed on after the class. Attendance was 100%
4.the teacher was good, knowledgeable. He or she explained well. 
So providing a complete environment excites learners.

What we are learning is not knowledge.  

Knowledge should answer the following questions with a lot of clarity and depth.
Why a concept? 
What is a concept? What are its characteristics? What are its relationships with other concepts ?  What are its uses? What are its applications? What are its advantages ? What are its disadvantages? What are needed to implement?
How do use use it, build applications, put it to use, 
How it will use resources and impact on people , environment, economy, 
Does it have a role on our economy or development or equality?
Can I derive additional concepts from this? 
What alternatives exist?
What improvements are possible?
What innovations are possible? What are needed for improvements and innovations? 

So it is clear that knowledge is not a collection of information.  But that is what is happening in our education system. So just developing content is not enough to improve our education . We need to build an architecture, a blueprint , a detailed design on every topic. 

Let me take an example - water. 
Start with why?
Properties of water- solubility, solid ,liquid and gas states.
We need water for survival. 
Can we live without water? Body has a large quantity of water. So We need water for survival. 
Our health depends on consuming adequate quantities of  Water. 
Where do you use water? Cooking, cleaning, bathing , washing, cultivation, industries  etc. 
Agriculture consumes water. What are the best practices? How do we reduce water used by industries? 
Ask students where do they use water in their houses, how much do they consume , where do they get water, who brings water home, is water in a stream,  pond or well available throughout a year? How do you reduce consumption, how do you purify water? 
We did a detailed survey in six villages in Tumkur district. They were revealing. A child is reserved to bring water everyday. 
So we  see  a demand for water. As population increases, our wants increase , water demand goes up. But the total water quantity is limited. So we should recycle water and use it after treatment . 
How do we purify water? Three pots. 
How do I get water?  Sources 
From well, lake, river. Which lake or river is nearer to you, what is the distance? 
How do I get water perennially? Rains and water cycle.
What happens to water after its use in cleaning? It becomes dirty. It spoils water sources. That is pollution. 
Ill effects of pollution - cholera, other water borne diseases, 
Pollution control . 
Treatment is needed. 
What is drought - water scarcity. 
Why draught? What are its effects? Can I predict it? Can I avoid it?
How do I store Water?
How do we get water  in the house? Taps. 
Water supply system - get water from a river, store Water , pump it to reach homes through pipes. We need a separate department to manage this. 
We need to take water to a higher level- say from cauvery river to bangalore city over a height of 500 metres. This needs energy. 
It costs money. 
Water use - wastage, conservation. 
Types of rivers - perennial , rain water drainage . Floods - reasons , how to avoid floods? 
Water coming down hills has energy that can be converted to electricity with a hydro power plant. 
Link between water and forests, water and monsoons, survival of rivers.
Each item needs more explanations, experiments , examples , best practices etc. We saw links between water, climate, rains, agriculture, industries, supply  engineering , treatments, energy, etc. 
So we need to create structures on most topics. 
We need to build a lot of resources that can be used by teachers for deeper understanding of various concepts. 
So knowledge implies questioning, going deeper into a topic, looking at it from many angles. Teach each topic through a series of questions ,not just a description as seen in books. Video based explanations are good. But students' responses are important. 
How do we achieve this huge task? Then only education will be meaningful.

16/03/25, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 654
Before I define knowledge , let me look at the question of how do I acquire knowledge?
One acquires knowledge through sensory perception- observations first . Then one tries to know several things first, shapes next, and actions later. One may start inferencing even as a child. That is a primary state . It is true throughout your life. 
Our daily house maintenance person  walks in saying “ sambar smells nice” on many days. She senses our cooking from outside. This knowledge is basic one used by many generations. One slowly enlarges one's world from the house to the street to the space, to the  city to the country etc. 
There are two types of sensory organs - karmendriya and jnanendriya. 
According to Sāṃkhya ontology, the five active senses (mouth /speaking, hands/grasping, feet/walking, excretory organs/excreting, and sexual organs/procreating) evolved from prakṛti are the karmedriyas.
Gyanendriyas are eye, ear, nose, tongue, skin (sense organs). 

The second level of acquiring knowledge is by listening to others and asking questions. This is the crucial bigger step in understanding the world and life. 
Here one in the older days learnt to acquire methods to get knowledge which is missing now. 
Let me look at the Indian  Concepts for acquiring knowledge in indian sastras:
Jñāna (knowledge): A general term encompassing all types of cognition, both valid and invalid.
Pramā (true knowledge): Accurate and valid knowledge, representing a true reflection of reality. 
Aparama (false knowledge): Knowledge that is not true or valid, a misrepresentation of reality. 
Pramāṇa (means of knowledge): The valid methods or sources through which we can acquire true knowledge. knowledge-generating processes, called pramāṇa, “knowledge sources.” The principal candidates are perception, inference, and testimony.
Pratyakṣa (perception): Direct sensory experience, considered a primary source of knowledge. 
Anumāna (inference): Reasoning or drawing conclusions based on evidence or observation. 
Upamāna (comparison): Knowledge gained through similarity or analogy. 
Sabda (testimony): Knowledge acquired through reliable sources, such as scriptures or experts. Arthapatti (postulation): Knowledge derived from circumstantial evidence. Anupalabdi (non-perception): Knowledge gained from the absence of evidence. 
Indian darsanas - Schools of Thought and their Views:
Nyaya:
This school is known for its rigorous logic and epistemology, emphasizing the importance of valid means of knowledge (pramāṇas). 
Vaisheshika:
Focuses on the atomic theory of reality and the nature of knowledge, often in conjunction with Nyaya. 
Samkhya:
A dualistic school that posits the existence of two fundamental realities: Prakriti (matter) and Purusha (consciousness), with knowledge as a means of understanding these realities. 
Yoga:
A school that emphasizes the importance of self-realization and spiritual liberation through practices like meditation, with knowledge as a tool for self-discovery. 
Purva Mimamsa:
Focuses on the study of the Vedas and the rituals prescribed therein, with knowledge of the scriptures as a key to understanding the divine. Vedanta:
A school that explores the nature of Brahman (ultimate reality) and the relationship between the individual self (Atman) and Brahman, with knowledge as a means of achieving liberation (moksha). 
We will continue further.
Greetings
Dks
17/03/25, 4:00 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 655
There is a query from Mr  Gurumurthy: 
“Sir, under the Schools of Thought and their Views above, the last that is listed is - Vedanta. By Vedanta, you must be referring to the Upanishads. I understand that while Upanishads such as  Brihadaranyaka, Chandogya etc..relate to Advaitic thoughts, some like Katha, Mundaka etc..relate well to Dvaitic thoughts and some like Taittriya, Aitareya etc..relate well to Vishstadvaita. Among the Upanishads available, there may be some relating to every one of the Schools of Thoughts of those days. So, Vedanta, as a generic term,  relates to all the known schools and not exclusively to Advaitic school alone. Am I right in saying this, Sir?”
My response
You are right. Let me explain. The term Vedanta is used in two contexts.
One vedanta means the essence of vedas which you have quoted. This normally refers to upanishads. 
The second reference is in darsanas . Here it refers to uttara mimamsa. 
Darsanas normally list all forms of indian philosophy . They are classified into two groups - orthodox  or Vedic which refers to the six darsanas quoted by me. The second group is called shamanic-  non Vedic dealing with Buddhist,  Jaina and lokayata philosophies. 
Jain philosophy says
Anekantavada: Jainism's core principle of non-absolutism, which acknowledges the relativity of truth and the multiplicity of perspectives. Ahimsa: The principle of non-violence, which is central to Jain ethics and practice. 
Buddhism talks about middle path and Eightfold Path: The practical path to liberation, which includes right understanding, right intention, right speech, right action, right livelihood, right effort, right mindfulness, and right concentration.
Darsanas support either advaitic or dualistic concepts . They looked at Life from different view points ,and generated different knowledge forms to show that
1.knowledge is multidimensional. 
2. There are many views and many approaches to look at a concept. 
Kautilya classifies all disciplines into four categories: scripture (the three Vedas, trayi), agriculture and commerce (varta), politics and public administration (danda-niti), and Ānvīkṣikī, the investigative reflective science. The distinction between Atma-vidya and Ānvīkṣikī is that while the former embodied certain dogmatic assertions about the nature of the soul, the latter contained reasons supporting those assertions. Thus Ānvīkṣikī dealt with two subjects, namely, atma, soul, and hetu, theory of reasons. The Samkhya, Yoga, and Lokayata, in so far as they treated of reasons affirming or denying the existence of soul, were included by Kautilya in the Ānvīkṣikī.Of the two subjects studied in the ambit of Ānvīkṣikī, the study of soul later developed and matured into a separate independent study described by the term Darsanas (meaning philosophy), and the theory of reasons was developed into an independent branch of study referred to as Nyaya or logic. This bifurcation of Ānvīkṣikī into philosophy and logic must have had its beginning in around 550 BCE with the exposition of the logical side of Ānvīkṣikī by Medhatithi Gautama.
Greetings.   Dks

My response 
Sorry. Nothing wrong in your views. 
It becomes my job to explain my post . Let me relook at what I discussed on knowledge in the past few posts.
We started with the premise that we are surrounded by millions of pieces of information called facts. These can be static, dynamic, occur once in a while or randomly , may deal with any area or sector, may be trivialities, simple or complex etc. Many of  these facts have uses and impacts on us. 
The next question was how do we get these facts. We saw that we use our senses to perceive these facts. But two questions arise - is the perceived data real or illusion ? Recall the classical analogy quoted in our upanishads - seeing a rope and perceiving it as a snake. So we need one validation process for this - error correction methods. The second question is:  How well and deeply can I know and understand the fact, remember kmowing or cognition leads to knowledge - that is how do I link facts and convert it to a valid set of knowledge units. We saw an example of water.
 This is where philosophical methods come into play to Verify and validate facts.  We use logic, mathematics, experiments, experiences and other methods for this validation. Science follows a set of well defined experimental techniques . But philosophy uses many tools.
Then we focused on Indian philosophy.   1.  The main discussion was on philosophy. We use different names for our philosophy like darsanas, vedanta, anviksiki etc. 
2.  The main focus of philosophy particularly Indian philosophy is to validate facts , observations, perceptions into knowledge by validation. The focus was on truth in all forms. Truth was essential according to rig veda.  
3. Validation is done through several methods. We discussed them in post 654. 
4. We needed to know what aspects or areas  a philosophy should address. Our philosophy looks at the following -   generation of facts about.            life , living ,                              yamas and niyamas, dos and don'ts , ethics, good qualities.  Universe and creation    ,         people , their characteristics ,    society, organization, governance order , peace,         culture                 nature and environment , energy water, fire, air, earth resources, panchabhutas,                    daivatva ,      asurathva.            Cyclical process of living , samsara.                    Four purusharthas                       four ashramas                  spirituality.    Consciousness, moksha,   liberation          knowledge       acquisition methods  religious aspects    bhakti                   materialistic world    , family, survival, necessities , etc.        Numbers , arithmetic, geometry,       astronomy etc
Mysticism Is an accepted part of our philosophy. It deals with not only the observable facts but also abstract facts and unknown aspects. That is a big difference with western philosophy. We don't constrain our methods to known facts only as science does. So in effect science produces restrictive knowledge.
5. So we cover a vast canvas of issues in our philosophy , most are related to our universe, our human systems, life and living, nature etc as listed above. Definitely our philosophy dwells a lot on religious matters. But more religious matters appear in smritis  and puranas and in agamas.  
The above shows that our philosophy covers a very vast area including many abstractions like infinity , completeness, absolute etc. Our philosophy accepted the mind as a specific intangible entity in human systems and created many levels  , contrary to the belief of many that Indian philosophy addresses only religious aspects. To show that it is a fallacious statement, I looked at the six darsanas  , as well as the lokayata, charuvaka ,  Buddhist and Jaina philosophies  in brief.

19/03/25, 3:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 657
Let me start with Hinduism principles first. Broadly speaking , let me explain some features of Hinduism as I understood them. 
Hinduism advocates dharmic living. It is an integrated dharma , much more than a religion. It integrates life purposes, paths to lead our purpose, living in harmony , order , ethical values, culture , respect for nature etc. 
It accepts the fact that people are not uniform and “one size fits all”  is not accepted. It moves away from a fixed set of principles to a flexible set of paths. It defines a goal for a person - moksha or liberation. It talks about four paths - jnana, karma, bhakti, yoga.  Similarly It gives us three paths to achieve our goal - called purushartas ,these are dharma, artha, kama, leading to moksha. Dharma is the pivot around which our activities should move around. Artha is not discarded or discredited . The early exposition of these aspects were to be seen in Kautilya's  arthasastra.  
It talks about rebirth , a cyclical approach to life. It talks about rotation of manus, Brahma etc. 
Hinduism as we see today was refined over millenia. We started with Vedic age , moved to the Puranic age where bhakti dominated , though vedas were maintained by a core set of people- gurus. Bhakti manifested in many forms. 
But the core were  the following major  principles.   Truth - Satyam vada. Truth in speech, thought and actions was and is emphasized. Avoid hypocrisy is what it says though  not explicitly.                               Om Shanti- maintain peace everywhere.                              Rthm vada. For successful living as a community ,an organization is important, it should be participative . Maintain order and harmony. Don't fight among yourself.        Ahimsa paramodharmaha - shun violence .                              Respect nature, natural forces, and act responsibly.                       Lokas samastha sukhino bhavantu.              Respect , guru, parents, atithis.  Shraddhaya deyam - share , give ,. Mostly importantly acquire knowledge - mathematics, geometry, astronomy , logic, grammar, music are important for all.     Also spiritual knowledge and self awareness and self realization are the ultimate goals.                                      There were many more  yamas and niyamas prescribed. 
Yoga, meditation, samskaras, culture were accepted as part of life - making life meaningful. 
We had one governing book - Rig veda  followed by  three more vedas. All principles are codified In them. 
Rig veda gives us an idea of Brahman . It says - “Ekam sat viprah bahudha vadanti”  .   This statement means while the underlying truth or reality is singular, different people, or "wise ones," perceive and describe it in various ways, using different names, forms, or concepts.   Think of different religions or spiritual paths, each with its own deities, rituals, and beliefs. "Ekam sat vipra bahudha vadanti" suggests that these different paths, while seemingly diverse, all ultimately point towards the same fundamental truth. 
What are the aspects contained in Rig veda.? Rig veda talks about evolution of life, ways of living like sharing, harmony, order, social habits, respect for all, proper speaking - vak-, dos and don'ts , various aspects of like wealth, agriculture , medicine etc, various stages of life and duties , essential constituents for life like water and rains and rivers, air and winds,sunlight, fire, etc., importance of knowledge,  regular learning, and acquisition of knowledge,  respect for nature , as well as showing gratitude to all divinity through prayers . It is a short summary of a few things in rig veda.
It is by no means a thorough discussion of rig veda and Hinduism. It is written to give a broad view hisanatana dharma.   Let us continue  further.
Greetings.  Dks
20/03/25, 4:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 658
We are looking at vedanta. The names are not always given in the beginning. The name vedanta came later. Even the name Hindu , Hinduism were given much later , in the nineteenth century. Hinduism was known as sanatana dharma.  It is not a religion but a complete methodology to life and living . Religion is one part of it. Let me next look at the evolution. Vedas were abstracted in the upanishads. That is called vedanta.   It involved three stages. 
First the operational stage - called brahmanas . These gave detailed descriptions for Vedic  practices . Four important emphases were revealed. 
Mind is the main actor - sutradhar one can say- for a person. Keep the body healthy through satvik living - satvik food, good values, harmony and close societal living. 
Deep concentration, peace of mind need to be built and practised .  This gave birth to yoga . 
Brahmanas focused on what a grihastha should do everyday. They built good practices. These were strengthened over the years. These were expanded into smritis. Since smritis contained detailed operating and living instructions to Brahmachari, education, snataka- graduate-, grihasta,  samskaras , most people followed smritis and were called smarthas. Those who followed Vedic rituals were called sroutis. 
Gratitude was considered the  important activity of a human - gratitude to nature, natural forces like sun, wind, rains, etc. This was the main focus of brahmanas to perform yagas to various gods though  a messenger - agni. This aspect of yagas was totally misinterpreted by the westerners as sacrifice. It is not just a sacrifice. Offerings are there  . But the main component is chanting and creating vibrations so that goodness prevails everywhere. The significance , meanings and effects of prayers were missed by most westerners. The main aspect of gratitude was ignored. All nature and natural forces were raised to a level of a daiva. 
This was followed by the next activity - aranyakas. They form  bridges building intellectualism leading to vedanta. Aranyakas discuss mind and jnana . They focused on the learning and knowledge component of the yagas and slowly moved away from rituals. Here is the starting point of spiritualism - nishkama karma. 
Then came a great development - the beginning of vedanta- the construction of upanishads. Brahman was a difficult concept not understood by most people, too abstract and very difficult to perceive.  So a good and detailed exposition was felt necessary. The result was a series of explanations by many gurus. We got many upanishads. All focused on Brahman and liberation. Most focused on the paths to liberation. Most upanishads talked about Atman and Brahman  and said that both are the same. 
Brihadaranyaka upanishad gives a long definition of Brahman through a long series of discussions with 1000 scholars. The Neti rule was introduced here. Sankaracharya built his edifice and structure on advaita based on this upanishad by Yajnavalkya.  
While we had one governing book dealing with dharma , that is the vedas , the philosophy produced many books over centuries and millennia because of the variety and diversity of topics and areas of discourses as well as several assumptions and methods of analysis and proofs. Hundreds of scholars worked and produced several disciplines. We saw the first six darsanas ,then added more and included arthasastra also. That was the starting point. Philosophy flourishes even now. Advaita has excited many. 
Our philosophy evolved over thousands of years and many hundreds of scholars worked on  not only the pursuit of knowledge, but most importantly on the creation of new knowledge . Several names come to our mind starting from yajnavalkya , Kapila, gowthama,  vyasa,  Patanjali, Kautilya, Aryabhatta, Brahma Gupta, Bhaskara,  varahamihira, Madhava, Ashtavakra, Sankaracharya, Ramanujacharya,  Madhvacharya, sureshwara, vidyaranya, Abhinavagupta, Appayya dikshita , Ramakrishna, vivekananda, Arobindo, Ramana maharshi and many more. 
Hope it is clear that vedanta generally means the essence of vedas. Unfortunately,  it does not fully reflect the essence of vedas. 
Vivekananda used the term "Vedanta" to refer to the culmination of the Vedic teachings, specifically the Upanishads, which he saw as the essence of spiritual knowledge. The Divinity of the Soul:A core principle of Vedanta, according to Vivekananda, is the recognition of the inherent divinity within each individual, the "Atman," which is ultimately one with the universal "Brahman".
Manifestation of Divinity:Vivekananda emphasized that the goal of Vedanta is not just to know the divinity within, but to manifest it through actions, thoughts, and emotions, leading to self-realization and liberation. 
Let us get back to knowledge.

Mr Gurumurthy has a query:
“Sir, you have outlined the focus of Brahmanas, Aranyacas and Upanishads. What was the focus of Samhitas, Sir?”
The actual core hymns of vedas are called samhitas. Samhitas are the hymns in vedas. The Rigveda Samhita contains about 10552 Mantras, classified into ten books called Mandalas. Each Mandala is divided into several sections called Anuvakas. Each Anuvaka consists of a number of hymns called Suktas and each Sukta is made up of a number of verses called riks.
 As I explained , these hymns  consist of
Basic principles of santana dharma.
Concepts of life- description of the various components of the universe, Cyclical system of the universe evolution , pralaya , destruction and regeneration. Samsara . The concept of Brahman.  
Ways of living dharmically and paths to moksha. Tasks segregation was done - the specialization. 
Methods to observe the dharmic way. 
Mantras and prayers to various devas who were the controllers of natural forces like rain, sun, wind, cyclone, air, river, floods, earth, soil, plants, animals etc. 
Symbolises our activities 
Importance of knowledge , thinking, consciousness and wisdom. 
Self understanding, Self learning, Self realization - Self here is Atman.  
Yagas , the procedures, 
To know the level of detailing let me quote verse1.159 from rig veda
HYMN CLIX. • Heaven and Earth,
 I PRAISE with sacrifices mighty Heaven and Earth at festivals, the wise, the Strengtheners of Law. Who, having Gods for progeny, conjoined with Gods, through wonder-working wisdom bring forth choicest boons, 2 With invocations, on the gracious Father’s mind, and on the Mother’s great inherent power I muse. Prolific Parents, they have made the world of life, and for their brood all round wide immortality. 3 These Sons of yours well skilled in work, of wondrous power, brought forth to life the two great Mothers first of all. To keep the truth of all that stands and all that moves, ye guard the station of your Son who knows no guile. 4 They with surpassing skill, most wise, have measured out the Twins United in their birth and in their home. They, the refulgent Sages, weave within the sky, yea, in the depths of sea, a web for ever new. 5 This is to-day the goodliest gift of Savitar : this thought we have when now the God is furthering us. On us with loving-kindness Heaven and Earth bestow riches and various wealth and treasure hundredfold. 
The above talks about qualities in a child. 
Resplendent with Clarity YL70
 Seer — Barhaspatya Bharadvaja 
1. Full of clarity, resplendent over the worlds, wide, vast, honey-yielding, beautifully formed, Heaven and Earth, by the law of the Lord of Heaven are up-pillared, prolific, undecaying. 2. Inexhaustible with an abundant stream, rich in milk, they yield clarity to the perfect worker whose vow is pure. Rulers of all this world, Heaven-Earth pour in us the seed that is auspicious to men. 3. Wise Heaven-Earth, he who apportions the offering to you for right action, that mortal reaches perfection. Through his creativity he is born  from the law. From you are poured diverse forms of a common nature. 4. Heaven and Earth, encompassed with clarity, resplendent with clarity, sprinkling clarity, who increase by clarity, wide, vast, placed foremost at the worthy in vocation — the sages adore them as bliss for furtherance. 5. Mix the honey-bliss for us, Heaven and Earth, you who pour honey, who yield honey, whose law is sweet. As the Godhead holding the sacrifice and the treasure for us, grant us a great inspiration and heroic vigor. ( see how clarity is a topic here. It's importance in understanding is stressed.)

One more hymn from Rig veda on Welcoming dawn - ushas - . See the  bubbling positive approaches here. 
Heaven-bom by truth,  the Dawn has shone out. Revealing her majesty she has come. She has concealed die unwelcome, hateful darkness. As the foremost of the flaming seers she has set the paths in motion. 2. Be awakened today for our great and happy journey, oh Dawn, into a great auspiciousness extend us. Goddess human in mortals, hold in us a wonderful splendor and glorious revelation. 3. The wonderfully clear immortal radiances of the Dawn have come for the vision. Generating the Divine laws, rilling the interior realms, they have spread afar. 4. When she is yoked from the beyond, she travels around the five races of men in an instant. Surveying the ways of knowledge of men, she is the daughter of Heaven, the queen of the world. 5. Full of power, the maiden of the Sun, possessing a wonderful beneficence, she is the ruler of the plenitude of splendors. Lauded by the seers, giving maturity, the beneficent Dawn shines, sung by the carrier-flames. 6. Wonderfully bright radiant horses appear conveying the flashing Dawn. She travels luminous by her chariot of the universal form, as she grants ecstasy to harmonious mortals. 7. The truth with the truth, great with the great, the Goddess with the Gods, holy with all the holy ones, she broke down the firm limitations and dispensed the radiant mornings, as her rays roared to greet her. 8. Now hold for us an ecstasy made of nourishing rays and heroic force, oh Dawn, the all enjoyment made of swift energy. May our mere humanity not stain this altar. Protect us with the powers of well-being forever, oh Gods.
Rig veda contains a lot of information that is not religious ,but useful in one's life. Tools like mathematics, geometry, astronomy, Grammar, logic and music are required to understand and perform our tasks. Materials for daily use like wooden implements, bricks, are discussed. 
A detailed level of specialization -the specialists for a yaga are
Reciters of Rig and Yajur Vedic hymns are called Hotri (for Rigveda) and Adhvaryu (for Yajurveda). 
Hotri:The priest responsible for reciting the hymns from the Rigveda, a collection of sacred verses and poems.
Adhvaryu:The priest who performs the rituals and chants the mantras from the Yajurveda, which focuses on sacrificial practices.
Udgatri:The priest who recites the hymns from the Samaveda, which are primarily melodic verses drawn from the Rigveda.
Brahma:The priest who is conversant with all three Vedas, but specializes in Atharva veda and coordinates between the other priests
Classification , grouping and detailing of functions for survival of the universe and their leaders - gods- like agni , Surya, varuna, ashwins, vayu,  marut, Indra- MD- , ushas, mother earth, etc. These are called devas. 
Running a village through sabhas and samithis - attendance, attention are discussed.

23/03/25, 3:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post  661
So we get the following conclusions  from our discussions on religion: 
Religion is not about God and prayers alone. It is a lot more than that. It teaches us how to live with goals and healthy habits and great values as well as  why to live - acquire jnana, help others. 
Religion is an integral part of learning and acquiring knowledge.
Religious literature contains a lot of information on the capacity to learn, problems in learning and methods of learning.
Many literature are philosophical in nature exposing us to humans, society, life ,living and values in a rigorous manner. These expositions are useful in our knowing our roots , our culture and our social history .
Sankaracharya had written extensively on knowledge and learning - acquiring jnana. Viveka Chudamani is an important classic work . Let me quote a few poems or slokas from it. 
11.The realisation of Truth is brought about by discrimination and not in the least by ten million of acts.

12. By adequate reasoning the conviction of the reality about the rope is gained, which puts an end to the great fear and misery caused by the snake worked up in the deluded mind.

13. The conviction of the Truth is seen to proceed from reasoning upon the salutary counsel of the wise, and not by bathing in the sacred waters, nor by gifts, nor by a hundred Pranayamas (control of the vital force).

19. First is viveka - enumerated discrimination between the Real and the unreal; next comes - vairagya- aversion to the enjoyment of fruits (of one's actions) here and hereafter; (next is) the group of six attributes, viz. calmness and the rest; and (last) is clearly the yearning for Liberation.

22. The resting of the mind steadfastly on its Goal (viz. Brahman) after having detached itself from manifold sense-objects by continually observing their defects, is called Shama or calmness.
23. Turning both kinds of sense-organs away from sense-objects and placing them in their respective centres, is called Dama or self-control. The best Uparati or self-withdrawal consists in the mind-function ceasing to be affected by external objects.
24. The bearing of all afflictions without caring to redress them, being free (at the same time) from anxiety or lament on their score, is called Titiksha or forbearance.
25. Acceptance by firm judgment as true of what the Scriptures and the Guru instruct, is called by sages Shraddha or faith, by means of which the Reality is perceived.
26. Not the mere indulgence of thought (in curiosity) but the constant concentration of the intellect (or the affirming faculty) on the ever-pure Brahman, is what is called Samadhana or self-settledness.
Need for self learning and understanding.
51. A father has got his sons and others to free him from his debts, but he has got none but himself to remove his bondage.
52. Trouble such as that caused by a load on the head can be removed by others, but none but one's own self can put a stop to the pain which is caused by hunger and the like.
53. The patient who takes (the proper) diet and medicine is alone seen to recover completely - not through work done by others.
54. The true nature of things is to be known personally, through the eye of clear illumination, and not through a sage: what the moon exactly is, is to be known with one's own eyes; can others make him know it ?
55. Who but one's own self can get rid of the bondage caused by the fetters of Ignorance, desire, action and the like, aye even in a hundred crore of cycles ?
56. Neither by Yoga, nor by Sankhya, nor by work, nor by learning, but by the realisation of one's identity with Brahman is Liberation possible, and by no other means.

24/03/25, 4:01 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 662

Kural, a Tamil classic, discusses knowledge in a detailed manner. 
Let me list a few concepts on knowledge given in those couplets. The translator calls the knowledge as wisdom. 

Wisdom is a weapon of defence, An inner fortress no foe can raze.
Wisdom checks the wandering mind And pulls it from ill to good.
. The weapon of wisdom saves one from evil. It is a citadel which cannot be destroyed by the foe.  

This is what Sankara calls viveka- discrimination , the important attribute needed for knowledge. 


Wisdom bridles the wandering mind, keeps it away from evil and bids it tread the right path.  ---- 

Wisdom seeks the truth of all things which are heard or uttered.  

This is a key to convert raw facts into knowledge. 

Wisdom unravels things subtle and seeks them in others.  

This is thinking, intelligence and analysis. 
To be one with the world is wisdom

Reality is knowledge .

Men of foresight who guard themselves against coming events know no distress.  

Here foresight - future prediction is considered as another Requisite to knowledge. 


In his play The Rock (1934) T. S. Eliot already asked the questions, ‘Where is the wisdom we have lost in knowledge?’ and ‘Where is the knowledge we have lost in information?’ Borrowing a famous metaphor from Claude Lévi-Strauss, it may be useful to think of information as raw, while knowledge has been cooked.

25/03/25, 4:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 663
We got enough information about knowledge. Let us see what knowledge is by consolidating our posts. 
As soon as we are born,  are bombarded with millions of signals every second about our surroundings, people, activities initially and later about neighbours , society and the world. These signals we receive are called data. 
A child picks up a very important tool for learning - observation using its senses - voices, pictures, smells , touch etc. 

It learns the next tool quickly. That is to convert the observed data into information. That is the first level of thinking and processing and understanding. Here, words like milk , sleep, etc are introduced to describe various things to a child . This is the first judgment by a child. The child learns to convert data into information slowly. It recognises mother, other relatives etc. It recognises milk. It learns to cry for milk when hungry.  Even here it may make erroneous judgments. Also it may know words, but the  actual many dimensional characteristics of words will come to it slowly. It may not understand a wall ,it will go and try to go through a wall. It experiments. Over a period of decades ,one gathers millions or billions of pieces of information. One  understands many terms and uses language as a big tool for learning. This continues throughout one's life. Language improves cognition. Still ambiguity remains. The first step in knowing facts is clarity. 
Now, information is still raw. 
But information plays a big role in our life. We build huge belief systems. We are told many facts like:
Fire is dangerous.  Don't go near it. 
Be careful about water. 
Don't touch a hot vessel. Your fingers will burn. 
Don't lift a heavy vessel. 
Don't cry. 
Wash your hands before eating.

All these instructions from parents - a major learning and information source - provides children with a lot of derived information from experience and the child builds a belief system. The belief system is what starts  a person  to become an intelligent one, a knowledgeable one. The belief system starts growing big. You add more information. But it is not a correct system. Fortunately quite a lot of our beliefs are good for normal routine living. 
Another big trait a child picks up is to extend the information and derive new facts. 
A child sees water in a bucket or on tap. When it sees water in a lake , it assumes it can use that water. 
But  It may be erroneous and ambiguous . Let us look at an example. A child was told a snake is dangerous , and instructed to run away while seeing a snake.  
It sees a rope and thinks it is a snake. Even adults make this mistake.
Next question : How does a child gather information?
1.by observation mostly. This goes on throughout one's life. 
2. By instructions from parents. 
3. By communicating with others . 
4 . The most prominent method is questioning. By age 4 , this is an important learning method. Starts with what and then moves on to why. That is the start of thinking and knowledge processing for a child. This process gets refined and changed with time. 

Unfortunately a  child is moulded by a teacher here. This has both good and bad impacts. 

So we have shown that information is not knowledge.  Why?
Information contains erroneous non true facts. 
Only when information is proved to be true , we get knowledge. Information is like a library of books. Can we call the books as knowledge? True, a library may contain some books containing raw data, some with processed data, but they are not understood by a person until a deep and understandable reading takes place and the understood processed information is stored in the brain. . 
Information is raw food. Knowledge is cooked food. Scientific Information is ready to eat food - most scientific information is verified and accepted.
AI today is at a state slightly above this state. AI can sift information from books or other sources , and put relevant facts together. It will do a good job with scientific data as that is almost a verified set of facts. It can do a little derivation to generate new data. 
So we come to the conclusion that 
Knowing a correct and true set of facts and understanding their effects and impacts is knowledge. 
Seeing milk and saying it is milk is not knowledge even though it may be true. Getting the full story of sources of milk, benefits , problems, etc will improve understanding. 
Let us see knowledge acquisition and processing in the next post.

26/03/25, 4:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 664
We are looking at an optimal education system for children . 
We saw how children start learning from childhood. They acquire data through observations and convert them into information. They use observation and language tools to convert data into information and create a large belief system.  But information is not knowledge,  because of many ambiguities and errors. So the question is how information is converted into knowledge.  It is a validation process. 

Like today's ML is doing, children also validate a fact through many hundreds of experiences or elders' advice. They learn from the environment and from peers also before going to school. That is why school education is better than online education. School helps in interactive and group based learning. 
Experiential learning is practised in mathematics and even learning alphabets in schools. 
So you can establish the validity of a fact through studies, analyses, experiments , past experiences and logical reasoning. A person picks up these skills in a school education . Most scientific facts are proven and we just accept them. These will help us to understand a fact. 
Knowledge requires a second requirement - characteristics of a concept and a third requirement - connectivity, derivative facts , impacts. 
Let me take an simple example - water. 
We say we drink water - that is a child's  first fact. Then we see water is used for cooking, washing , taking bath etc. We get new facts. 
Then we ask a question : I need water . Where do I get it from? 
We learn - from wells, canals and rivers or lakes. 
Where do they lakes or rivers get water? 
Now we get a new dimension - from rains. We learn about water falls, floods, etc.
Further enquiry takes us to our getting electricity through hydro projects.
Then we ask - where does waste water from my house goes?. 
That is the direction of creating connected knowledge, learning and understanding.  

The first requirement for learning is thinking. Thinking makes us what we are, what we will be. Thinking helps us at every stage in life - decisions and actions . Thinking leads us to knowledge. Thinking helps us to convert information to knowledge. 
Recently I was in a university.  A young faculty member met me and started talking to me about thinking. I was surprised and happy. We started asking what is thinking. She talked about reasoning. Then I realised that informal understanding of thinking is not enough . That is the weakness of our system. Thinking is the main objective of our education system. 


Today educators have become very interested in teaching ‘thinking skills’ of various kinds in contrast with teaching information and content. 
Most students simply do not pick up the thinking skills in question.
Socrates began this approach to learning over 2,000 years ago, but John Dewey, the American philosopher, psychologist and educator, is widely regarded as the ‘father’ of the modern critical thinking tradition. He called it ‘reflective thinking’ and defined it as: 
“Active, persistent, and careful consideration of a belief or supposed form of knowledge in the light of the grounds which support it and the further conclusions to which it tends.” 
What this means is - 
Start with curiosity and ask a series of questions to understand correctly and fully a fact. So questioning is integral to thinking. 
We spend our life thinking. It can be at different levels - materialistic level to aspirational and goal oriented levels to innovation levels to intellectual levels.
We think because we want something. 
Thinking is an internal self learning,  self understanding , Self analysis method- somethings explained in detail in upanishads. 

what matters are the reasons we have for believing something and the implications of our beliefs. It is no exaggeration to say that critical thinking attaches huge importance to reasoning, to giving reasons and to evaluating reasoning as well as possible. There is more to it than that, but skilful reasoning is a key element to most formal education. Many aspects like mathematical or analytical thinking, logical thinking, systems thinking, design thinking are picked up later in our higher education. But the basics of multidimensional and multidirectional thinking are not taught. There are many requirements for correct thinking and many hurdles. It is thinking that improves our cognition. 

The American schooling system was started since inception. George Washington, in his Farewell Address to the American people, said: "Promote, then, as an object of primary importance, institutions for the general diffusion of knowledge. In proportion as the structure of a government gives force to public opinion, it is essential that public opinion should be enlightened."  This is being dismantled now. Hope we don't follow the current examples of the US.

27/03/25, 4:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 665
Let us define knowledge now.
It is a collection of facts , not a random collection , but a connected collection. So you build a network of connected facts . 
Knowledge is a validated set of facts. Most scientific facts were tested and proven until rejected. Validation is an important step to convert facts into knowledge.
Knowledge is a set of understood facts for each person. Levels of understanding varies and improves with time. We retain a lot of acquired knowledge.  
Humans acquire knowledge throughout their life time. It is called learning. It is both a physical and mental process. There are several methods to acquire knowledge.  Education is one of them. 
One can derive new knowledge by modifying existing knowledge or discovering new facts. So our individual knowledge base is expanding with time .
There are several ways of testing facts to validate facts and understand them. The simplest is experiential , and then we have several methods to validate. Philosophers discuss and analyse several approaches. They use mostly logical reasoning. Scientists use both experimental and mathematical reasoning. 
Reasoning is basic to validate and understand our knowledge. 
The first step to reasoning is questioning every fact - what, why, how, where, etc. 
All knowledge one gathers is not usable. But quite a good amount is usable. 
What do we use our  knowledge for -  to learn good habits, to know what is good and what is bad, to be polite , to know what to eat and how to behave, to build friendships, to converse meaningfully with others, to question and find answers, to solve problems, to use mathematics, to decide  logically , to know the country and world, to apply your knowledge to develop or improve many gadgets and technologies, to innovate , to analyse and to get a job. The most important use is to improve our intelligence level and sharpen our mind. A person's effectiveness is determined by how he uses his or her knowledge, what is the purpose of its use - make money, help others, satisfy curiosity , build the nation. A life is fulfilled if one puts one's knowledge for the use of not only himself and his family but also for the betterment of the society. One gets immense satisfaction when one helps the society with his knowledge.  
We acquire knowledge from many sources . The initial step is by  observing people and their behaviour , society, offices, nature , social functions, etc. Then we learn from others. We listen to a large number of people. We argue with a lot of people. The most important source is by reading a large number of books and gathering information.  It is a big source. Education in schools and colleges is another important source to acquire Knowledge.

28/03/25, 4:31 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 666
Let me ask a philosophical question - why do we need knowledge? Why this quest or thirst for knowledge. ?
You see evidence for this from Socretes , Plato and our Vedic and upanishadic scholars. So it is a constant feature of human life. Knowledge and living are intertwined.  
This quest is there from the beginning in order  to live . Knowledge took us through many stages of development.  
The stages of life are- there are seven stages developed as Spiral Dynamics  by Don E. Beck and Cristopher Cowan, based on the pioneering work of developmental psychologist Clare W. Graves. They are 

1.The instinctive self - to survive first 
2. The Magical Self wants safety and security. It is tribalistic, magical, and mystical. Looks to gods and higher powers with rituals for protection.
3.The Impulsive Self
The Impulsive Self drives for power, glory, rage, and revenge. It is egocentric, exploitative, impulsive, and rebellious. The Impulsive Self believes it can take what it wants by aligning with power.

The basic theme of the Impulsive Self is “Be what you are and do what you want, regardless.”
4. The Rule/Role Self seeks the ultimate peace, locked in the battle of good and evil. It is absolutistic, obedient, purposeful, and authoritarian. By following the rules and exceeding its given role, it will know the fundamental truth. Most religions and religious values are rooted in this stage.

The theme of the Rule/Role Self is that “Life has meaning, direction, and purpose with predetermined outcomes.”
5. The Achiever Self desires autonomy, achievement, and self-interest. It is materialistic, strategic, ambitious, and individualistic.

The purpose of the game is to compete and win: material pleasure, acquisition, and the advancement of civilization. How? By learning how to excel at everything it does, setting and achieving goals, measuring success, and so on.

The Achiever Self builds businesses, science, medicine, and most of modern society. It creates hierarchies of domination (comparisons, “better than,” etc.). Orange became the dominant wave during the Industrial Age and continues through the technological and information age.

The primary theme of the Achiever Self is to “act in your self-interest by playing the game to win.”

6.The Sensitive Self lives by connection, community, egalitarianism, and consensus. It is relativistic, personalistic, pluralistic, and sensitive. Green appreciates diverse views and focuses on the needs of the many.

The underlying theme of the Sensitive Self is to “seek peace within the inner self and explore the caring dimensions of community.”

7. The integral self seeks to become whole and synthesize all the levels that came before it by awakening as many lines of intelligence as it can and integrating them into a cohesive whole. It is systemic, ecological, flexible, and conceptual. By learning and adapting, it can incorporate the levels that came before it.

The primary theme of the integral self is to “live fully and responsibly as what you are and are learning to become.”
Integral theory realised that unified theory pursued by scientists earlier is not the correct approach. So they call this theory the theory of everything. Ken werber has written a book on this , simple to read. 
8. The Holistic Self
Compassion and harmony guide this level.It seeks peace in an incomprehensible world by developing a more profound receptivity of multi-dimensional perspectives without privileging any of them.

The main theme of the Holistic Self is to “experience the wholeness of existence through mind and spirit.”
This stage brings together science and mystery , east and west philosophies.  

You see these levels and concepts were achieved through awareness, knowledge , self understanding and maturity. 
These stages can be identified with human traits as
Survival: individual survival (masculine)
Protection: tribal (feminine)
Power: power and dominance (masculine)
Rule/Role: follows moral precepts of the group (feminine)
Achievement: autonomy and achievement (masculine)
Sensitivity: egalitarianism and community (feminine)
Integration: integration and personal responsibility (masculine)
Holism: collective individualism (feminine)
According to this research, only approximately 1% of individuals are integrated.
80 percent of individuals have a center of gravity in either Rule/Roles, Achievement, or Sensitivity.
These percentages help explain interoffice, political, and marital conflicts.

All these seven traits can be seen in people. All are not at one uniform level. People moving to higher levels obtain greater intelligence and apply their knowledge effectively. They acquire knowledge throughout their life. They lead an intellectual life.
Any comments?

29/03/25, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 667

To understand the linking of knowledge and life further , let us ask 
Who is a human?
The Spiral Dynamics uses an evolutionary approach. It does not describe the stages of mental / consciousness development. Most psychologists do not talk about knowledge development but about levels of consciousness. The levels are
Perception and basic understanding of life . Mostly have local level knowledge. 
Literate level - command on language and speaking , reasonable understanding, reading , writing and mathematics is known. primary school level
Normal thinking and reasoning level 
Emotion and ego management level 
Knowledge application level - adaptable and having good learning capabilities
Emotion control level, mature state, move above materialism
Knowledge generation level - inventions , research , futuristic Thinking. 
Wisdom level. 
Spiritual level
Let me look at our yogic approach - a very detailed explanation of humans as many tattvas are described in upanishads, yoga sutra and mahabharata.  
First, 
The human body has three bodies where all these five koshas reside. They are Gross body (Sthula sharira) (स्थुल शरीर), Subtle body or Astral Body (Linga sharira) (लिंग शरीर) and Causal body (Karana sharira) (कारण शरीर). The gross body or Sthula sharira is the physical body.
According to yoga philosophy, we are not just our physical body or mind, we are holistic, multi-dimensional beings made up of many different, interactive levels.

In the yoga framework, these levels are referred to as the five sheaths of our being or the Five Koshas.
The first is Annamayakosha is our physical body, the most familiar aspect of our being. 
The second is Pranamayakosha -The Energy body.
According to yoga therapy traditions, this is the second layer of our being. Prana means energy, but not energy in the usual Western meaning of the word. Rather, prana is the life force, the vital energy that flows through and enlivens all our physical systems. The breath is the most physical expression of prana, and prana is closely related to the breath.
Third is  Manamayakosha -The Mental -Emotional body
Manas means mind, and the Manamayakosha is the layer of our being expressed as mind, emotions, and feelings. These are the mental faculties with which we absorb, process, and interpret input from our lives .
Mind is subdivided into Manas, ahamkara, chitta and buddhi. 
Fourth sheath is Vijnanamayakosha -The Wisdom body
The fourth kosha is considered part of the subtle body. Vijnana means knowing, and this sheath represents the higher mind, the faculty of wisdom, which lies underneath the processing, thinking, and reactive mind. This is the level of our being that has the higher wisdom to guide us through life and lead us to higher and higher levels of truth and integration. It represents the reflective aspects of our consciousness, which allow us to experience a deeper insight into ourselves and the world around us.
The fifth sheath is Anandamayakosha -The Bliss body.
This is the fifth and final sheath of our being. Ananda means bliss, not bliss in the sense of emotions, such as happiness or pleasure, but an expanded, unbounded experience of reality. The ancients viewed the experience of the Bliss body as an experience of the deepest level of our being, an unbounded, blissful state of peace, joy, and love.
There is another way of looking at humans. 
Humans have four realms. 
One realm or avatar is physical - basic wants or needs to be met. We need knowledge to survive, get wealth, and be healthy. We need to know how. This needs knowledge. 
Second realm is emotional. We live our life with a lot of emotions. Some like love, affection are good. Some like anger ,worry are to be avoided. Some are neutral.  We need knowledge for training our emotions and controlling them. 
Third is mental. This is where knowledge plays a dominant role to invent, to control, to be safe, to become an intellectual. This phase makes us realise - what is life? An integral view , a holistic view should be evolved based not on separate entities like house, property, family, job , society etc, but the interconnections and interrelationships amongst many existing and futuristic entities, their impacts. This understanding is vital and this changes our approaches completely. Our upanishads concentrated on the proper growth, and understanding and proper use of mind. Materialism is called a myth. Today psychologists call for empathy as the world is becoming highly self centred ,competitive,  money motivated and consumption based. 

The fourth realm of a human is spiritual. Here we go beyond our perceived reality - into cosmos, into unknowns, into the mysteries, into evolutions , Into the theory of everything.  
A good human should strive to reach upto the third level - to become an intelligent person . Even the third level has many sublevels. We need to climb them. 
It will be very great if we reach the fourth ,the spiritual level to become a holistic self. 
Our upanishads preach that we aim for the spiritual self. 
Human is an integral and living entity growing with intelligence - consciousness- through constant learning. You can even say
“Knowledge is human.”

Mr Gurumurthy has a question:
“Interesting. 

Sir, what does Research show about the path that human consciousness takes to evolve from the instinctive self level in awareness to the holistic level of one integrated whole Awareness? 

Do all human beings follow a preset hierarchical path or can they jump steps?”

My understanding - 
The first aspect is the evolution of consciousness in humans started slowly with perception and moved up the ladder. While it looks surprising that upanishads like Katha upanishad, kena upanishad , Brihadaranyaka upanishad etc  talked about mind and consciousness in a serious way in say 500 BCE,  the west didn't accept those concepts till 15th centuryCE. The obsession with mind , learning, consciousness continued in India till around 15 th century in a big way - understanding consciousness was considered the main purpose of life- , our interest reduced and the western intellectual pursuit increased. 
Most importantly , the objective of my writing these models of humans is to see how we become more conscious . That should be the objective of our education and learning system. That is to make people aspire for not material prosperity but intellectual prosperity. I had a chat with a young boy recently and told him to become an intellectual being and not an engineer or doctor or IFS officer. Our children's primary aim should be getting a strong intellectual foundation - not just during school days but even beyond. I was telling him that till the mid thirties in age ,i was focusing on the processes of sharpening my intellect. Doing a graduation, taking a job are needed, but one should spend half the time on intellectual pursuit.
The question of how one goes up the consciousness ladder is important. I was leading towards that. It has many steps and stages : The first one is calming the mind - meditate, do yoga, practice music, listen to good things, understand your culture . 
The second step is to start thinking . It is an essential step for cognition. Don't just listen, ask questions, look deeper, etc. This is a crucial step before acquiring knowledge.  The third step is the preparation step for learning. Learn methods for concentration and practice. Good examples are recite prayers , practice music, read for an hour a good book and spend ten minutes remembering and  thinking about what you read, write on some aspects, observe nature , sun, water sources, river flows, plants, animals for atleast half an hour , draw and paint for an hour etc. Don't scold your child if it spends a lot of time drawing pictures. This step is a boon for developing a child - creating a binding to life in a child and creating deep interest. I saw this evolving in my grandson.  The fourth step - the most difficult step - is understanding your levels of emotions ,their effects and controlling them. This may take constant thinking and take a long time. The fifth step is an early step of developing good habits . 
Upto this level is a normal behaviour and normally doable by people. Then comes the  significant step . The sixth step is learning , thinking, building capabilities and building intellectual strength. The next two steps are desirable but need a lot of effort. The seventh step  is ego- ahamkara- reduction to removal, a tough step. That will lead to the next step - eighth - empathy, and compassion - I call this humanism. Those who cultivate intellectualism can reach upto this level. The ninth step is reduce wants, don't expect results , perform nishkama karma.  One is now ready for the tenth step - spiritualism. 
The fourth point is do we follow all the steps? Yes. No skipping of any step. but we can do them in parallel,  not in a sequence. Start at an early age. Humans are multidimensional, and can do many things or activities in a long productive day of 10 hours. If you start being lazy, then that leads to intellectual disaster.  We are capable of  achieving a higher level of consciousness. This has been proved by the spiral dynamics.  Everyone should strive for it. The parental, societal and educational support in providing an intellectual strength to a child is lacking now. Support for formal education is there but not intellectual upgradation.The colonial materialistic outlook is strong. One should love one's children, but one shouldn't pity him or her, push them to work hard, think hard, read more . Pity is dangerous. Similarly pushing too hard and too much is not good. Most important - expectations - don't expect nobel prize from your child. Be a good assessor of your children's capabilities.  
I am sure many will disagree with the above statements and even curse me. Welcome comments. 
Thanks Gurumurthy

31/03/25, 4:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 669
Let me get back to   learning.
Let us use a story to know two levels of learning? 
The story goes like this:
There was a king in a place. His personal assistant was complaining regularly that the king was giving a high salary to his minister, and the minister didn't seem to be working hard,but he, the assistant  was working hard and got  a very low salary. That was unfair. The king wanted to explain the situation to the assistant through a concrete example. 
He saw a palanquin coming into the city one day. He called his assistant to find out who was coming. The assistant ran ,got an answer , came back and told the king that a swami was coming. The king asked his assistant where he was coming from. The assistant ran ,got the answer, returned and told the king. The king wanted to know where he would be staying. The assistant again ran , now a longer distance and returned with the answer. The king wanted to know the duration of stay. The assistant ran a much longer distance and came back with an answer. Similarly the king asked a few more questions. The assistant ran a long distance everytime and returned with answers . He was tired of running. 
After some time, the king asked his minister to get details of the person who was coming in a palanquin in the morning. The minister went, gathered all the information about the swami, who was he, where was he from, what was  his purpose and duration of visit, what would be his activities in the city , how many were with the swami etc. 
The minister told the complete details to the king. The king asked the assistant, “ Do you see why I pay a high salary to the minister ? “ . The assistant was a dumb person with no thinking. The minister knew what questions to ask based on what information the king wanted and collected complete information. That needed thinking and that is the process of higher cognitive learning- the minister built the knowledge base without the king's prompts. This differentiation should be understood by us . We should develop our processes accordingly by creating 10 to 20 such situations and asking children to generate knowledge bases. They think and learn. Don't give them ready to eat textbook information.  That won't make them to  think independently.  

Let me look next at
What is the primary purpose of learning?
 Acquisition of knowledge, understanding the knowledge , applying knowledge in one's life and solving problems and managing one's life situations may be  called learning. We learn constantly  from our expectations, actions,  advices, readings , experiences and mistakes. 

Learning is a simple word with a lot of implications. The question is do we learn?
Most of us read history - I didn't say study- . We pass the exams. But what did we understand from the history - moguls are the great rulers in India ,  a brainwashing done by our historians and school books. More importantly , what did we learn from Indian history for 2000 years? That is the sad part. We learnt nothing . We don't even get a picture of this country and how it meandered over thousands of years. We didn't talk about culture, people's lives, habits, scholarships, migrations . We talk about fights between kings. Are kings or rulers the only constituents of a society?
similarly we read newspaper stories or books a lot. That is a useful activity. But how much do we remember and understand from what we read? What did we learn from all the books we read?   Same situation applies for many subjects taught in schools. Let me give one example of not  learning relevant aspects and applying them . Children learn in their third standard or so that air is needed for combustion- oxygen from air combines with a fuel to create a fire. The same child sees the  cooking activity  at home in a traditional wood stove. Did a child  tell its mother that firewood is not getting oxygen and so the fire goes out. It may see the mother blowing air into the fire. But no improvements are thought of. That is where thinking comes. Learning is not just memorising separate facts . It is understanding and applications. It is to know the connectivity between various entities - physical to spiritual.

01/04/25, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 670
Let me continue with more features of learning. 
Learning is not a collection of facts ,but understanding and cognition of reality and applying knowledge to improve life . Learning should improve thinking and questioning capabilities .  This is a very important statement. 
Learning has a primary focus - the first step to learning  is a set of skills to improve cognition, not gaining knowledge . These are clarity of mind - obtained through yoga, concentration- meditation, good health- exercises , good habits , internal learning , and thinking skills. All these are normally innate but ignored or not used. These  can be learnt and acquired. After this important motivational  first step, we continue with many skills to improve cognition - discrimination - known as viveka- of right and wrong , good and bad, useful or useless, good for me or good for all ,  etc, analysis, reasoning, discussions, reading and critiquing, acquiring external knowledge,  and regular practices to assess one's knowledge capabilities.  This should be the focus for the entire schooling period. But parents play a crucial role here. Parents' behaviour affects the children. The base for all activities of learning and cognition is thinking. 
Learning has many subcomponents- learn, unlearn, relearn, learn throughout life etc. Methods of learning are changing now. 
Let us ask what is the knowledge stage of learning?
learning aids the process of knowledge acquisition, knowledge processing and assimilation . We apply the processed knowledge to decide, to analyse ,to solve problems occuring in our  life and in jobs and to invent something  new .  It is intended to know various aspects of life, living, habits etc. Its purpose is  to acquire relevant knowledge to survive and grow in the world in harmony with others - people, animals, plants, forests, water sources, nature, resources , etc . While knowledge acquisition is considered important and given attention by educators, philosophers and psychologists, and it itself consists of many learning steps, we have more stages in learning before it becomes intelligence. Remember knowledge is not intelligence.  
Acquiring knowledge is not enough. Application of knowledge is not simple. Our entire engineering activities depend on the application of knowledge to improve our societal functioning. That is again a major concern but without holistic understanding . This higher level analysing , applicative and integrated knowledge makes one relevant in today's AI world. 

Learning in the early days was mostly a strong internal process - observing the happenings in our surroundings and perceiving facts -that  we call information today. One transmitted to his family what he learnt in his lifetime.  At that time,  he became  a teacher and initiated external learning.  Still most people used internal processes to learn - say learnt how to make fire, how and why to cook, how to make and use tools etc. Farmers learnt from others' experiments and results. 
We see strong evidence of external learning from a guru - in our vedas. We learnt from rishis. But vedas emphasized internal learning also. But over a period of millenia, we moved to more external learning and less internal learning . We think that learning happens in schools and colleges. Are we becoming morons? Are we becoming slaves of external influences of powerful groups? We should improve our internal learning. 
Internal learning is still strong in many children. But it has to be cultivated. 
Children show interest in learning only if they know the reason Why they are learning a topic. 
Most children find what is taught is irrelevant , not relatable , not understandable and useless. So a teacher or parent should concentrate on  the why part of learning more seriously than what part. When a child refuses to drink milk,  the mother cajols it , tells reasons, stories to the child to make it drink milk or eat food. 18.  Most teaching ignores the why part. 
What is the function of the most important question - why? It helps children develop reasoning methods. More importantly, whether we like it or not, it builds judgmental capabilities. Judgmental capabilities are at a higher level to reasoning. They provide us solutions and compromises in life. Judgmental characteristics may be very rigid or rigid or empathetic or , relative or flexible. There are many shades. Most of us don't learn these formally. We don't understand how our Judgments will make us a moron or a scholar, a success or a failure, a leader or a follower etc. They help in cognition. 
19. How do we learn? We initially learn partly by observation and mostly from external sources - parents to books to TV. As we start growing and acquiring more information , we use our mind to analyse the information and we create a good picture of our knowledge. We start a good cognition process. At this point of time , learning becomes a push pull process. We get some knowledge from various external sources like teachers, family, society which is  pushed into us. But there is an important pull transformation that happens at this point in time. We start learning by internal thinking and analysis. This is self learning - swadhyaya. I saw my grandson using this strong internal pull process strongly and very effectively at the age of four itself. He was more knowledgeable than others including his teachers. That is an important transformation in one's life. Some may miss it. Some get it naturally. But it can be cultivated. 
More in the next post.
