Let me discuss more aspects on learning. 
20. How do we cultivate the pull process of learning and skepticism to the push process of teaching?
 The first and very critical requirement is ambience created by parents and school for children. Parents play a big role in building up their children. In today's uncertain , volatile , ambiguous and complex situation - known as VUCA world of the 21st century-, parent's roles have increased .Parental intelligent and adaptive guidance is needed by children. How many can be competent. Parents need education on this. Counselling by others is not a solution. 
One of the important things a parent should understand is the answer to the important question -  what should a child learn in today's VUCA world. 
This means we should know the changes that happened between the 20th and 21st century  and the requirements of the 21st century. I will discuss these aspects later. 
But do the parents do their job of creating a proper ambience correctly?
Parents think children don't know anything. And they believe that Children shouldn't know many things - taboo. They feel that  Children should obey their orders. These assumptions are incorrect and this is where everything goes wrong.
Parents should exercise their attention on their children and do things that are beneficial for their children. But they should treat children as knowledgeable individuals  , not as morons, explain situations and convince the children on habits and practices. They should motivate learning by first becoming good learners. 

 What do I mean by ambience ? It means a large number of factors. Peaceful and pleasant atmosphere in the home is the first step. Many children miss this basic step in India . Most households have a stressful , worrisome atmosphere. That destroys a child's interest and motivation.  
21. The second aspect of ambience is acquiring good habits - to be clean, to pray , to meditate and do yoga, to eat properly nutritious food, to be frugal , to be regular , to read , to work hard- if parents are lazy the children will follow them-,  to observe, to ask questions, to get interested to know about our neighbourhood and nature, to appreciate nature, and to follow certain best practices known as sadachara. It is a big list. Many houses follow these practices at different levels. Many good families discard these in the name of wokism. 
 22. The third aspect of ambience is very important - independence  for children to explore, to do their tasks, to decide on their daily activities,  to know the world , to be friendly with others, to create friendships, to go and buy things for themselves etc. 
23. The fourth aspect of ambience is injecting responsibility in children . Allow them to take care of themselves - from getting up early, to bathing ,to getting ready , to finishing  homework, to doing exercises, to reading and writing , to discussing many issues, to buying groceries and vegetables for the house etc. This brings in maturity in children. I have seen my daughter taking  responsibility to take care of her younger brother when she was five years of age. That was good. 
24. The fifth aspect is freedom. Children lack freedom. They are controlled by their parents. They are disciplined by schools. They are not allowed to ask questions, to talk and take part in discussions, are expected to display good behaviour . We convert them into morons. Get out of this discipline fix . Know when to exercise control, when to give freedom to children. Allow them enough freedom to feel happy and counted as part of the family.  Release them from the jail of discipline and blind love. 
25. Knowledge acquisition is a constant process to be made into a habit and used throughout one's life. One can improve the acquisition process or ignore it. This makes a person what he or she is, what he or she will become, what he or she will achieve in life . It will determine what he or she be doing in life -  will help the society, will be a burden to the society, will change the society. So this is crucial to be told to every child so that they decide what will be their future course, how to reach it and how they want to become an intellectual or a concerned person or a normal also ran person. It will make one decide the achievements he or she wants in life. Then one can decide on actions needed for what one wants to achieve. This is what we call goals. Unfortunately, most people today have materialistic goals - earn money, buy a house, travel to many countries - this is a common ambition for most. So the children were forced to prepare for IITs admissions. That is not the only route. 
26. We should look for intellectual goals - what aspects of knowledge i need to acquire, how do I acquire in depth knowledge , how do i build habits to analyse acquired knowledge, how do I sharpen my thinking and reasoning power, how do I use the acquired knowledge to help others and society and how do I do create new knowledge.  That should be our goal. If these aspects are articulated properly, children accept it and follow an intellectual thinking path away from the mechanical job and money path.

My last point in the previous post gave an important purpose in life - to become an intellectual . What does this mean?
When many people succeed in life with STEM education and many succeed with programming , the question arises - why don't I follow that path. A very important question that should not be ignored.  I didn't say don't go for STEM learning.  But i say do it intellectually to make a meaning and purpose out of your life .
Children should be allowed to  start contemplating from age five onwards or age 10 onwards - what should my life be ? What aspects will satisfy me?etc.  A lot of guidance is needed. 
First they should know the options . 
1.a mechanical life - a job, get money, acquire family and properties , struggle with daily needs , and life looking for stability and running away from changes and challenges, a life defying the behaviour of the changing world. 
This has a danger - you become a moron, get bored with life . 
But most opt for this mechanical life. 
They think it is safe - but it was true for your parents but not for your children- 
In the VUCA world of today, they may loose jobs and face crises. I have seen hundreds of such cases. So you need a better option - what is called an intellectual option .
Before I go into the intellectual option , let's ask what happens in one's life and job today?  
Routine clerical life is gone. No routine jobs now. 
What is  the reality of life  -  not just jobs alone. 
We face problems of health and finance  and crises , face unexpected situations, even disasters , face unknown situations - all these may happen in one's family life and professional life. We need to plan, manage and act through correct , good and futuristic decisions. That is the reality. So What  should one do ? You can’t run away. 
What you should do is to solve the problems to the best of your ability. That is where the qualities like calm mind, thinking , analysing, understanding and most importantly anticipating the problems before they occur- a future gazing - helps. Acquiring and practising these traits is what I call intellectualism.  These are life skills , not mathematics , science etc . Of Course you need them as secondary skills. Once you have the primary skills, you get the secondary skills easily. That is what happened for me. 
We need these traits. They are not easy to learn in a class. They need a lot of internal learning and lots of practice
If basic intellectual traits are well developed in a person, he or she can face life's problems confidently, take decisions correctly , help others, develop themselves and their society and build a satisfactory and successful life . So that should be the primary  focus for everyone, not engineering, IITs or computers. 
We need to embrace change rather than seek stability. "Instead of 'challenging myself within stability,' we should learn to 'create value within change.’
In order to understand this new trait called intellectualism, let me go back and look at Life in the early to mid part of the 20th century. It can give us many lessons. 
life was peaceful, because wants were minimal and there were no big aspirations. 
Many people lived their life  in two or three dimensions . What are these dimensions? One is materialistic dimension of earning money and managing the family within means . Second is the religious - dharmic -  dimension or traditional dimension. Believe in values followed by our forefathers , be pious , be regular , be calm , be helpful etc. Many people followed these two dimensions without any difficulty. They managed life well. The third dimension is the social dimension. Get involved with public activities, help in improving public infrastructure and facilities and help poor people. Educating others and advising people is also part of the third dimension.  There is also a fourth dimension.  A few people moved into a higher spiritual domain. 
How did many manage two or three dimensions? Their organized mind and time management were great. That worked for them. I had a great inspiration at my young age of five years. My uncle lived in the three dimensions. I watched his activities, his decisions, his organization, his calm analytical mind and his support ,help and advices to many people. He was my mentor. Silence was his way of teaching me , not anger or shouting.  I was always with him for more than 14 years , observed him, and followed his methods systematically. That helped me. 
Scholarship was recognized and respected greatly. It is true now also. 
The biggest advantage of that period was stability , no changes , no adaptations to technologies. 
People didn't have an opportunity for higher education in those days.   Most had only school education. So people believed that life is a great teacher and I should learn from life, neighbours , not depend upon schools or ChatGPT.  Remember cut and paste will not solve problems encountered in our life. Forget cut and paste solutions. Today we need to go back and follow that idea - life is the biggest teacher, not schools. 

To conclude , I am not suggesting we go back in time and follow our elders. But learn how they lived  and apply your intellectualism to solve the unexpected,  complex problems that will appear almost everyday in our life and just  not solving problems of today, but those occurring in the future- anticipate future.

04/04/25, 4:36 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 673
We feel that It is necessary for a child to be intellectually motivated. 
For becoming an intellectually strong person , what is required is thinking skills to be acquired as a habit first. Thinking needs  a questioning mindset. We need to encourage children to ask questions and even solve puzzles with them. The opposite is happening now. Please wake up . 
Let me ask - what is a mindset?
A child starts acquiring a lot of information from observations and from parents and others. Using this information and parents' advice, it forms opinions. parents say do this ,don’t do this, this is good, this is bad, don’t talk to him, etc. This process of forming opinions builds thousands of beliefs in every child. Beliefs may be correct or wrong , true or false , desirable or undesirable , simple or complex, generic or specific.  That is a major form of mind development. This set of beliefs guides the thinking and actions of a child and determines his or her capabilities. A child becomes a human with these beliefs. Genetics and tradition  also play a role in this formation of beliefs. This set of beliefs and opinions  is called a mindset.
A fixed mindset is the belief that your intelligence and abilities are unchangeable. Its antithesis, a growth mindset, is the belief that your intelligence and abilities can change over time as a result of new experiences. The belief in a growth mindset is a belief that individuals can purposefully change their circumstances through hard work, practice, or training.
So our job is to build an open and thinking and changing mindset. Contemplation and questioning should be taught and encouraged leading to self learning and understanding. 
This is a tough task. Who should do this?
Schools?
Unfortunately no as seen from the following two comments. 
Technology developments were not many in 20th century. Electricity happened in the beginning of the century. It brightened life. It helped households , offices and industries. Developmental focus was on manufacturing the needs of people like metals, machines, tools, textiles, coal, power generation, paper, household appliances ,  consumer goods and processed foods. The entire century focused on manufacturing. So jobs were mostly in the industry sector. Our education was tailored to meet these needs. 
In  the 1880s, when the movement toward compulsory schooling began in the US,  some railroad magnates opposed public education
for fear that they would lose the workers who did the hardest jobs,clearing roadbeds and laying track. However, the first U.S. Commi-
ssioner of Education, William Torrey Harris, reassured railway baron Ellis Huntington that the emerging American public education system was “scientifically designed not to overeducate” but to produce socially compliant workers.
In his book, Harris wrote these highly depressing but also very influential words:
“Ninety-nine [students] out of a hundred are automata, careful to walk in prescribed paths, careful to follow the prescribed custom. This is not an accident but the result of substantial education, which, scientifically defined, is the subsumption of the individual.” Macauley followed the same strategy in India- to produce non thinking, non analysing clerks. 

Thomas Edison also  noticed: “Somewhere between the ages of eleven and fifteen, the average child begins to suffer from an atrophy, the paralysis of curiosity and the suspension of the power to observe.
The trouble I should judge to lie with the schools.”
But today is the era of constant change , adaptation and innovation. We need to change our children's learning behaviours and mindsets , and shift them from rote mechanical learning to open , inquisitive , skeptical and deep thinking and contemplating learning leading to unknowns. It is my opinion that our education's focus on known topics  in our teaching should be reduced to 50% and remaining time spent on unexplained concepts, impacts , open questions, future trends and sharpening our mind. 
So it is the responsibility of parents to develop an open thinking mindset in children ,an essential requirement for an intellectual mind.

Let us enlarge the purpose of learning. We saw that we acquire knowledge through learning. Then we saw that knowledge is not enough, it has to be applied properly . Then we discussed that intellectualism is an important objective to learn. It makes our life great and meaningful. Now I want to look at other aspects of life. 
I said a human being is built with a lot of accumulated beliefs that are used in day to day activities, problem solving and deciding on many situations. Now let me look at the  characteristics acquired by people , some good , some bad, some desirable , some disastrous. A person is made up of beliefs , habits and many other characteristics.  
A human being builds a number of characteristics in his life. What are these - let me list a few. 
The first one we saw is the capacity to learn- acquire knowledge.
That needs some traits- observation, perception, questioning , reasoning , analysing , thinking , cognition . These are mental habits. 
Another essential characteristic, we acquire is:
Trust - it is the most difficult trait . Trust is a part of your mindset. Some don't trust anyone. Some trust everyone. But we can't live without trust. Trust builds relationships,  friendships and supports life. A woman marries a man on trust . Trust is not easily definable. Each one has a method of trusting others. One also has different levels of trust with respect to a person and the nature of the task.  You may trust some one to post an ordinary  letter  , but you may post an important letter yourself and don't trust that person. You may trust someone to give a loan of a few thousand rupees, but may not give a loan of a lakh of rupees. But one thing  is certain that we live as a society , meet many people in our life , have financial or property dealings, build partnerships etc- all these are based on trust. Zero trust in human life has restrictive meaning. Trust has two actors - people is one actor. your trust with other people - known, unknown, well known, relatives, etc - varies from person to person based on many factors - how long do you know him?, is he or she reliable ? Will he or she be able to do a job for you ? . 
The second aspect is the tasks that you entrust with a person. You may give a small low risk jobs to any person. But jobs with risks, you will be careful with your decision. You just don't go by trust alone but build checks and be cautious. 
Trust happens with experience. We build life long friendships based on trust. 
Trust is not a simple thing. Businesses run on trust. They succeed or get ruined because of good or misplaced trust. A mechanic in a factory is entrusted with a costly machine. 
A large company or bank trusts an IT services company like TCS with a operations critical for the survival of the company. 
We select a CEO of a company based on trust on a person - his or her competence . All selections for senior positions are based on a set of assessments and the decider trusts his wisdom. 

I should say that I lived my life and succeeded in everything I did , almost every major task , because of my trust in others and their trust in me. It is not just people to people , but with colleges, governments, industries, banks also. I handled purchases worth hundreds of crores - that was the trust established between me and the companies. 
To me, trust is the essence of myself. I value it even now. Society has millions of people to help you in dire need . Have faith in your society . I may keep away from getting closely involved with society,  but appreciate the actions of our society. Judgment is another trait we need to build a trust with people. Trust and judgment gives us maturity. 
Trust is not a simple process. It is a complicated mental judgmental activity , not arrived at one instance but decided based on several factors - observations of the behaviours of people, dependability of people , others experiences, references - most visit restaurants or choose hotels  based on remarks, many don't trust airindia based on comments of others  - , discussions and assessments etc. 
Trust is a complicated entity. It is a two way process - you trust someone and you expect some others to trust you. Trust has many levels - implicit, good, competence, helpful, low risk , no trust. Trust depends on and built through observations, perceptions, remembrances,  experiences and failures due to mistrust. Trust depends on many aspects in life.  Many times we trust strangers . I have gone through all these stages. 
As I said before I first built my trust in others and gained their trust and we all benefitted mutually. Mostly I established my capabilities , truthfulness, my strengths ,my judgment capacity and sincerity with others before I gained their trust. One sided trust doesn't work. Both persons should have trust and it should reflect in actions beneficial to both. I didn't go for a quid pro quo approach. Let me explain the roles trust played in my life next. Let me assure you that trust is significant for me. It made me who I am.

Let me explain my experiences in learning and societal relationships. 
First the ambience aspect particularly during the first 10 years. The ambience did play a major role in my acquiring habits and characteristics. 
I lived in a village for the first ten years of my life - and simple, spartan , frugal life. It was a period of pre independence and just after independence. The village was on the national highway in southern part of India about 40 km north of   Kanyakumari , close to or part of western ghats . It had a reasonable population. 
I lived with my grandmother and studied in the village school upto 5th standard.  My uncle living in Tirunelveli, the district headquarters,  used to visit us frequently ,almost every week and he will be in the village during the summer vacations. My parents and grandfather lived in a devakottai,  a city about 200 km from our village . So I was in the care of my grandmother mostly. But my elder uncle 
took care of my learnings. He was my mentor and played a big role in my upbringing.  
Life was simple , spartan and frugal. It was just around the last stage of the second world war and there was a big scarcity of commodities. So frugal living was necessary. 
People were simple. Life was simple. Simple  Food- rice , pulses, vegetables- was available. Most were happy. 
The situation was highly motivated due to the fight for independence and highly idealistic to build the nation. There was no corruption even in the sub registrar's office. Activities moved smoothly. 
As I said, life was moving around the three dimensions- jobs,society, religion. So I had to follow the three dimensions and learn from all three.
I first learnt that routineness in daily activities like getting up at 5 am , getting ready by 7am is very important and helpful. Then I learnt a lot of dos and don'ts.  That started my thinking process- why ? I realised - some dos and don'ts like - don't shout, be polite, help others, don't waste food, don't skip eating etc could be explained , but some others like pray in the morning and evening, could not be explained. But i understood the don'ts if not the logic , but the advantages and benefits. This kind of maturity was built up from my childhood itself. It was due to the ambience.


I realised that when I do  something in ways  that are not acceptable to elders , they - my grandmother or uncle-  told me directly. But most other things ,I had to learn by myself - that is self learning . This is a big revelation. My bringing up gave me certain freedom to learn by myself. 
How did I start learning by myself? By keen observations and understanding.  
So i started observing every activity in the home, outside, temple, tank etc and getting to know correct behaviours from others. So these experiences helped me to decide - what are good habits? 
I had a straight algorithm .
Some friend or a child does something and gets scolded . I decided - don't do such things. One common habit of senior people is that they didn't like children talking a lot. So I decided to reduce talking and also decided to listen a lot. It is being followed by me even now. It helped me. 
Regarding my observations , I created opportunities. I listened to a lot of discussions , fights and arguments happening around me in the village. It was a great opportunity to observe human beings and their pettiness , selfishness and behaviours. 
I could sit in many such conversations happening in the house and outside amoungst elders, because I kept quiet and didn't talk or intrude in their talks. 
But I learnt a lot a lot from my uncle by following him everywhere, looking at his meticulous habits and his hard work. I also learnt hardwork from my grandmother.

The most important aspect during childhood is the parallel recognition and understanding and development of appropriate habits. It is a high speed learning of many activities at the same time. During the first ten years of life  , one gets a reasonable level of understanding life and dos and don'ts for success in life .  We learn a lot during this period. Some learnings are 
1. regularity first. followed by several habits like 
 2. Cleanliness, respect to elders, piety , prayer, yoga , visit to temples, proper eating habits, exercises, play , discipline followed by 3. Concentration through yoga, questioning, reasoning,  analysing, other mental development , language , reading , writing , arithmetic , culture , games etc followed by 
4. freedom, independence , responsibility , understanding  human behaviours, followed by 5. Problem solving skills, Building trust , searching for truth , balancing aspects of material vs intellectual vs spiritual life , getting a holistic view of life , living and intellectualism etc leading to a mature state in life . Step 5 continues through our entire life. It keeps our mind sharp. It makes our consciousness increase and improve. 
Only a part of 3 above is handled by our school education. Others are given by elders , friends or by self learning and understanding.  This shows the importance of ambience and development of intellectualism that help a person to solve many small and big problems encountered in life. This gives us an important lesson-
Self help is the best help. Analyse every activity before performing it. 

The next step in learning was understanding discipline. I understood that discipline is not blind obedience to authority. Both my grandmother and my uncle never told me to obey what they say , they knew me well. But my parents wanted obedience. Fortunately I wasn't brought up based  on  my parents' way of thinking. 
I understood that discipline means order - rig veda emphasizes that  rthm - order- is essential for a person and society. Order implies organization. Every item has a place to store. Every activity has a correct sequence of steps. The world has both randomness and orderliness. For example , the water cycle - evaporation,  rainfall, rivers , sea cycle - runs our life. There are many things like floods, earthquakes , tsunamis , landslides ,etc that happen occasionally   and  they  are beyond our control . There is an order in a family, in  a street, village , in a city, in a country, in a school, in a temple, in offices,and in every place. Order is ensured through following a set of rules or guidelines, a transparent mechanism,  an organised structure , governance, planning , control and well defined procedures. 
Discipline is a method of building boundaries in running an organization or household, starting with dos and don'ts,  proper etiquette, proper behaviour , good understanding and we'll defined actions . 
Most organizations have a problem with control. Many wanted strong control and approval at the highest level. So every request, serious or trivial, goes up to the top level . This leads to delays , arbitrary decisions and unhappiness. So the current trend in organization is towards empowerment, not control.  I got empowerment at a young age of 7 years itself. 

I would say that  discipline is not obedience and strict control ,but independence and resilience within a well defined and well understood structure, activities, and decision management. Our life depends on that. Our jobs require this discipline, not slavery .  I learnt these aspects of discipline early in my life. 
Independence was a necessity. Let me discuss independence and  organization In the next post.

08/04/25, 4:50 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 677

We all learn a lot of aspects intuitively .  For instance, we learn about organization from childhood. But we don't understand that what we are seeing is organization. We get a hazy idea.
Organizing is an old habit. Hunters Joined together for more strength and safety. So forming groups was the first Organizing activity by humans. Later there groups became a society or village. The group should have order and synergy as well as work distribution for effective living. The organization grew towards functions in groups. 
Science also uses this concept to group materials, elements etc. When an organization becomes bigger, we build a structure . Family is an organization a child sees first. 
The other aspect of a good architecture is searching. A house wife searches for vessels or spices or rice or vegetables to cook.  So she organises storage. Both men and women organize their clothes and books. So grouping of items , a fixed storage place for each group came in next. A child helps in locating items in the house. This sharpens the memory. 
Search was a fundamental activity not only for humans but for computers also. So we divide the storage area to get the requisite data or file. 
We organize many functions like birthdays, marriages or we celebrate festivals in houses or temples. All these have many requirements and activities. 
organizing is a key activity in life.
Organization happens when people work together to accomplish some desired end state or goal. It can happen through intentionally designed activity, spontaneous improvisation, or some combination of the two, but it always depends upon coordinated effort.
Life pressures us to compete, whether that competition is over food or job or education etc. 
Every group or business or law organizes into functional units . So we see organization is built on a formal or informal structure. 
Normally children get some idea of an organization informally. 
But I was lucky to learn about activities , functions, preparations , by observing, helping and participating in many activities in the village organised by my uncle. So I got formal learning and understanding on many aspects of organization. 
I learnt about countries and the world , world wars and independence when I was seven years old. I saw the first public function of independence day in 1948. I saw several festivals . At the age of 10 , I saw the first election, a big organised activity. Most children have experienced many such events and seen organizations. 
But the biggest organization was conducting a marriage. It was planned for a year  .  Lots of activities were involved. I saw a number of marriages during this period . One needed  many months to prepare - major activity is purchases of dresses- readymades were unknown then ,  clothes were purchased and given to a tailor for stiching-, vessels, grocery - sugar was the most difficult commodity then , we needed  licences to buy several kgs of sugar, i went to the taluk office to get a permit for a large quantity of sugar for a marriage- . Then one works on many other aspects like cleaning the house,  putting up a pandal, getting musicians,  cooking snacks etc before a marriage. We need to make jewels . Then we needed  to arrange for milk, flowers, coconuts,   a hall, cooks etc. So it is a sequence of activities. The most difficult task for some is arranging finance based on estimates of expenses. Some borrowed money at a high interest rate, some sold lands . It was tough in those days. So if you are part of a marriage, you learnt about organization. Actually the first step is planning. As soon as a date for a marriage is fixed , we started preparing a list - list of items to buy , list of services required, list of people to be invited , lists of sources for purchase ,list of people to help , search for halls , supervision of activities ,etc. A note book was opened and used to contain most information regarding activities , materials, etc. While sitting with my father for my aunt's marriage , I wrote many things in a notebook.  It opened my eyes to the complexities of organizing a function . 
With marriage activities outsourced today, children now don't have an opportunity to detail activities in a marriage and understand the organization. It is simple for them. So teach them organization formally.
More on my experiences in the next.

09/04/25, 4:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 678

Let me look at another major activity- agricultural cultivation
There are a number of activities starting from enriching the field, preparing seeds, to plowing, planting, irrigation to harvesting and transport. It is a cooperative work .
From the age of seven onwards, i used to go to the field and observed the activities.  We cultivated paddy, pulses, banana and chillies. 
I regularly visited our field to  cut banana leaves for use in the house.
The major activity is harvesting. First a suitable date will be fixed. Then we should get many people to harvest . They will start early at 5am to harvest and after cutting the paddy , they will bring the harvested paddy to an open field for thrashing and winnowing. After paddy is segregated,  it will be measured  and transported to the house by a bullock cart. All these activities should be coordinated and completed by the evening. We have many huge pots to store the grains in the first floor. So the entire harvesting operations needed to be planned in advance. 
Now we need  to sell most of the paddy keeping what is required for our house consumption. We got market rates and sold the paddy within a short time. So it was a hectic time. As it was summer vacation for me ,I would  be present during the entire process lasting several days. It was a great learning - i learnt cultivation Processes, harvesting methods, measurements - in those days paddy was measured by volume not weight -,  market rates , our income etc. I understood how we lived. 
The major learning from organization happened when I observed my uncle in his activities in the village. I was always with him and learnt a lot from him. He was consulted by most people in the village for legal matters. He used to draft legal documents , sale deeds, promissory notes and petitions to the government on many village issues. I observed these activities with a lot of interest and curiosity. I learnt a lot about legal transactions . He will go to the taluk headquarters to the sub registrar's office to register loan documents or sale deeds. I accompanied him on several occasions. I also observed him when he used to negotiate with parties to help them. His just approach and fairness impressed me. 
He conducted the ten day program of Sri Rama Navami celebrations every year. I was with him , understood what he was doing and assisted him. I did this for more than 15 years. This is where I learnt many things. 
The first thing was planning. Supply chain was poor . We need to buy flowers , milk, coconuts, groceries etc. So we had to identify suppliers and give them an advance order. Similarly we arranged for cooks, musicians, speakers etc. We printed invitations and posted them. He built a team of people to help him. They redpected him. 
The most important aspect I learnt was the maintenance of accounts meticulously. I learnt the concept of a ledger from him , started posting in a ledger. His integrity was impeccable. I decided to follow him. I saw how he handled unforeseen circumstances - there were many - and how he solved them without worries. I participated in every activity and learnt a lot about organization from him. It was more than a formal training . The Rama Navami started by him is continuing even now. 
All these gave me independence and confidence to handle major problems. 
Another aspect I   learnt at the age of eight was house construction. Cement was not available in the late 1940s. So we used lime for plastering. lime was available as limestone. We used to put it in water , add sand and ground them using a bullock run grinder . We used that lime for plastering. After plastering, we used shell lime for whitewashing the walls. There will be repairs every year. I used to stay with the masons. Carpenters would make beams. We used mangalore tiles for a part of the roof. I learnt quite a lot about building materials. 
These were my summer camps. 
More in the next.

10/04/25, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 679
These direct involvements provided me with a deep knowledge , understanding and appreciation of the intricacies of organizing activities with limited innovations , limited finances, limited and unreliable supply chain and even some non cooperation issues. I learnt skills needed for real life problem solving. I realized that learning takes place everywhere. I was lucky to be allowed to participate In these activities in a small way. That was the mentoring by my uncle. Many children were treated differently , told to go and play and not talk to elders. But i stayed and listened to all the discussions. That was because I kept quiet and didn't participate in the discussions. So i was allowed to be there. 
I learnt that organization needed mental thinking , soft skills and it is necessary to build a good systematic, pragmatic process. We iterated and improved the process every year. 

 I was observing many tough negotiations happening in the village - the toughest one's were property distributions amongst sons. 
When my grandfather died , there were discussions for four to five days on arriving at a settlement of his property - a house and some agriculture land-  amongst the four brothers - my father and his three younger brothers. I sat with them all the time and saw the pain of the brothers . It wasn't easy. Since the properties were a few, their costs were estimated . They will discuss the details with my uncle in the evening everyday. I was present there also.  My father took the house and half the land and he paid for  the share of others in cash to them. 
All had financial problems . So I  understood the pain of my father and uncles, particularly the youngest one. He used to be carefree and jovial . He had just completed his schooling and needed to go forward in life at that time- a terrible situation. His future looked bleak. I felt sorry for him. He managed well in life , moved to Kolkata with a job in the British high commission. 
 In addition,  they had a major responsibility - getting their youngest sister married , not easy. That was a more difficult job with multiple opinions expressed by the brothers. Finally , after a lot of discussions, they left the job to my father. He took responsibility to conduct the marriage. Fortunately,  it was held in a year's time in the house in the village. I realised the pains of life. 
So my conclusion  is that life teaches many lessons through many people on many occasions and situations. Life lessons, if properly learnt, will make us good , adaptive , collaborative and socially sensitive humans. Social Sensitivity and empathy are essential qualities to be developed by us and I was fortunate to have learnt both qualities and to appreciate them in others. 
Well, let me return to my trust building work. One thing common with most children is that they want to become appreciated. So they learn to do things that please their elders. I followed this behaviour. I never questioned anyone, answered politely . I was trying to create a trust in others on me. I thought that I should make others trust me before I learn to trust them. 
So I put on the best behavior with most village elders , school teachers and helped them on several occasions. Due to my ambience in the house , there were mature expectations from me. Never once I was told to go and play. If I wanted to play, I went but if I wanted to stay , I stay. Even at that young age, I got the respect of many . Some even didn't like me for being meek. I was meek and timid , not adventurous physically . But mentally I was strong. 
One greatest learning I had was religious learning. I understood some Vedic hymns and many upanishadic statements. I listened to Taittriya upanishad a lot. So both my social and religious developments started at that time and continued throughout my life in different forms and manifestations. That was in addition to my intellectual and academic life.

There is a crisp comment from N Ramanathan, my cousin
“Old world charms make it an interesting read.”

There is another  comment and a query from AL Bharat, my nephew
“Beautiful messages Chithappa.
You almost make me imagine your childhood and how interesting it would have been.
So much learning happened along the way, largely because of your inquisitive nature and fortunately you were closely associated with your Uncle.
I too think the joint family system helps in a big way.”
“Is the the Uncle who was at Chinmaya nagar at Vadapazhani?”

My response 
Sorry. I should have clarified . It is my elder uncle, my mother's  elder brother. The other uncle you refer  to is my mother's younger uncle. He was studying and working and used to visit us in the village during summer vacation. 
I stayed with my grandmother, grandfather passed away. So we were alone. My uncle was working in Tirunelveli and he will visit the village on most weekends and stay here in the summer. He was actively involved in many activities in the village. I used to be with him, follow him everywhere , observe him and listened to him. I tried to please him and my grandmother. My younger uncle used to tell me stories. He introduced me to the Merchant of Venice at the age of 8 years. It was the first big story I heard from him. I still remember it. I was upset when Shylock insisted on his pound of flesh and was thrilled with Portia’s condition to Shylock - no blood to be spilled. I liked the finish line. I also heard Othello from him . He has told me many stories. I used to be free with him , though I kept my distance from my elders. 

1952 was the first turning point in my life. I call it a turning point and not a change. The reasons are :
My uncle who was a Hindi teacher in Tirunelveli made a momentous decision that changed my life's directions. The usual village boy's plan was to study upto eighth standard, attend typing and shorthand classes, and migrate to a big city for a job as a typist. Some people went to the high school in the nearby town of  Nanguneri and studied upto 10th. My family was different , two uncles got college degrees and MSc later. So my moving to Tirunelveli for continuous high school studies without disruption was a great boon. That opened up opportunities for higher education for me. Since my paternal grandfather died when I was in 4th or 5th standard,  my father moved to Madras without a solid permanent job. So I wasn't expecting support from him for my academic growth. So I feel this decision was a great turning point in my life, I almost consider it a liberation. I got clarity about my life's directions. I almost decided at that young age of 10 years on my goal - to become an engineer. So i consider this as a major turning point in my life - definitely it was the first turning point based on a conscious decision with great foresight by my uncle.

Dr TKM Babu has a comment:
“Great DKS
I have come to know of your young days.
I know you from 1959 from TCE.
Thanks for writing your experience at a young age.
Mahendra Babu.”

My response
Thanks. I am discussing stages of my learnings from childhood. 


My learnings went up several levels. My sources, areas and dimensions of learning increased. 
Let me look at the aspects in sequence.  
First I want to discuss the changes in my social interactions . 
I moved into a city from a village - so my lifestyle changed. The city was the district headquarters with many facilities. 
Socially , my village life was highly interlinked with others. I knew most people in my street and neighbourhood in my village and knew the happenings in the village and in most houses. Social and religious interactions were close. 
But that changed in the city - a big contrast. There were very little social interactions. I knew my classmates in the street and used to play with them. We played cricket at a nearby ground. A few teachers lived on my street . So I knew them.  I knew a few people living near our house - two bankers, one of them a kannadiga. Otherwise our interactions with neighbours were minimal. Most people from our  village came to the city regularly for some work and visited us. Many who  were living in big cities like Madras and Bombay etc came to our home while visiting the village every year. Most migrated persons from the village will visit the village at least once a year to see their parents. In those days , village life was active with many general festivals like deepavali, Sankranti,  navarathri etc , temple festivals, sriramanavami and sankara jayanthi celebrations  and many functions like marriages etc in many houses. The elderly lived in the village and their sons went to cities for jobs. Both sons and a few married daughters visited the village regularly. Some like my cousins D R Lakshman, D R Sundaram , D R Krishnan were visiting the village  to perform  puja to their house deities every year. Most stopped in Tirunelveli and visited my uncle . So I had better interactions with them.  
Next change was food. I was introduced to many vegetables like potato, cabbage etc. But our food was still the same simple food . The only change was the snacks like vada , bajji etc prepared occasionally by my aunt. The only luxury was that we got mountain bananas from sirumalai . That banana was small and tasty compared to the big payen variety cultivated in the village.  We lived frugally. Though more commodities were available,  there was a scarcity for sugar and most manufactured items. There were many grocery shops and hotels. I was eating in the hotels. This meant that i moved from the production based environment in my village to a market based environment and a convenience oriented society in a city. We didn't store many items in the city. We could buy any grocery item  as and when we wanted . 
My father used to visit us . While he was on a month-long visit ,  he stayed in another nearby  house .So I used to get him morning coffee from the hotel everyday. 
My visits to my  village were restricted to school vacations and festival times. I was more involved with both srirama Navami and sankara jayanthi celebrations in the village. I got a deep understanding of accounting, organization, operational management , supply chain handling with uncertainties, communications. My learnings and beliefs on integrity, correctness, transparency , commitment and dedication improved considerably. These qualities got etched in my mind. 
Since I was the only one in my age group from the village studying in a nearby city high school, I was treated differently by my erstwhile classmates and other friends. I liked that special  treatment a lot. But that also produced some jealousy and I realised it .

13/04/25, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 682
I felt social interactions are important . Today social deprivation in children due to TV and mobile phones is considered a major worry by psychologists. I learnt a lot from many adults during my school days by observing them and discussing with them. I learnt my geography of India- life in big cities like Bombay,  madras, Calcutta,  Delhi , Hyderabad from elders in the village living there. I learnt etiquette , opportunities , industrialisation , from them. Social interactions are  a great source for learning. We need to bring our children back into social circles. With technologies like video call they can talk to friends and relatives regularly. 
Social interactions help in many ways. First, it teaches us good behaviour, second it teaches us good communications and listening skills, and third it establishes trust with others when you find what others say is credible and trustworthy. My trust in many people including teachers  got built through this process of interactions. Interactions provide one means to assess the trust level of a person. If he bluffs, you won't trust him.
My next big change in learning is something I consider a big boon - voracious reading . I used to read a book every week - mostly Tamil fiction , biography . 
Most books depicted the social life in those days. They mostly reflected family life - a boy meeting a girl and marrying her story. All authors found great solutions to all problems. All's well that ends well. While you find a lot of tragic events in the novel, the end will be good .  But biographies impressed me a lot. That happened in my early years after listening to sankara vijayam of swami vidyaranya for several years. I found several biographies but what impressed me was U V Swaminatha iyer's  autobiography. It was simple , easy to read. Another biography was that of Yogacharya Sundaram . It introduced me to bangalore. There were books on yoga and also a lot of religious books.
 Similarly I read the weekly magazines also. This built a habit of reading everyday , assimilating the materials and this habit continues even now. Reading is not important , remembering the gist is more important. This wasn't easy. Out of the thousands of books I read , I can't recall the gist of most books. But some aspects of quite a few books  standout. 
My other source of learning about living , society and country, its famous people came from magazine stories - particularly Kalkis writings excited me. I understood pre independent India from Alaiosai. It was a classic masterpiece of informative and simple writing. I used to visit public libraries regularly to read books. I used to borrow a lot of books from the college libraries. This practice continued in Bangalore also. 
I got my first major lesson :
Every problem and every situation has many views. So binary solutions - true or false - leads to one view only. But many views are near true even though they are different. So there are many solutions ,many understandings possible for every view. The views look at a situation without constraints.
This contracts the rational scientific concepts taught in schools. Science provides constrained answers.
The second lesson 
Most views of people emanate from their range, their perception, their understanding , their experiences and their thinking. So you can't discard the processes.
The third lesson 
Keep an open receptive mind. Don't reject others' views  quickly. A useful tool is a clear mind and a constantly contemplating mind . Think, contemplate , judge the views , find the contradictions,  if any. 
My practice of meditation at an early age made me think and contemplate Whenever I was free and sitting alone undisturbed. This was another boon for me. Ideas flash occasionally. Mind is not blank. 
It is not necessary to have a problem to think about and contemplate , one can and should do it in vacuum also.

14/04/25, 4:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 683
Let me look at another aspect - technology awareness. 
I became aware of technology and its impacts . I found that electricity   extended our night time activities by about three hours. But most people used electricity for lighting only then . We saw electric fans being  used only by 1956 or so. Refrigerators were unknown . Even textile mills were run with steam turbines and long shafts with pulleys and ropes. Electrification was very slow. Most towns and villages didn't have electricity . 
Radio gave us news and music, particularly carnatic music by famous musicians . There were many cinema houses for entertainment. But I got disenchanted with movies after watching a few. The only  films  I remember now  are Sampoorna ramayan, Mayabazar, Insaniyat and jhanak jhanak  Payal bhaje. I was already familiar and used the two transport systems - buses and trains. 
I learnt many new languages like English, Sanskrit and Hindi. This was very useful. I got good aptitude and foundations for many languages. I hated no language. I even learnt a few Telugu and kannada words from our neighbours. My father knew quite a bit of Telugu having lived in madras for several years. I started reading a lot of classic  literature writings in these languages. I had good familiarity with Tirukural,  Raghuvamsam, meghadutam,  and many other books.

The people were changing. I was seeing many changes. Independence idealism was slowly dying. Greed was showing its head.  People’s aspirations increased.  Many wanted political power. The first general  elections were held then. I saw the campaigning .So I became aware of politics . True , congress won. In that election , no symbols were allotted to candidates, but colored boxes. Symbols were introduced in the next election. Congress didn't get a majority in Madras assembly . So Rajaji was brought in as chief minister to stabilise the state politics. His rule had many positives , and saw a great reorganization. The state of Andhra was carved out in 1953 and Rajaji fought to retain Madras as Tamilnadu capital despite demands by Andhra.(Andhra pradesh came in 1956 after the merger of Hyderabad .  State reorganization unified all states under a single category. ). Rajaji also had a tough time controlling the violence of DK and DMK against Hindi first. The anti Hindi agitation fizzled out until 1965. Similarly the two parties unleashed a violence against brahmins in south india - we were affected by it. He gave instructions to police to put the violence down. That helped a lot. He also made congress strong, reduced the influence of communists and almost destroyed the justice party. There was no opposition then. But kamaraj schemed to throw him out. Rajaji’s relations with Nehru were not good. So within a short period , he was removed in 1954 and Kamaraj became the chief minister.  
But from  1953 onwards, politics became the hotbed of hatred . DMK started anti Hindi, anti brahmin movements. They introduced the Aryan dravidian divide . They justified violence.  They started anti religious propaganda by decrying Tamil literature with agitations against kamba ramayanam and most other literature. But they introduced a different oratorical skill in Tamil. 
Communists were strong then and started agrarian agitations for agricultural reforms - land for the tiller. Many poor and old people lost their lands and livelihood support. 
So i got my good understanding of our political situation.

 I used to  visit the local library regularly and read more books. 
 
I found the local library very useful , and used them in many cities till I came to bangalore.

15/04/25, 5:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 684

Let me get back to my trust building activity. I had a few classmates as friends in the village. But I knew all the others in my street, talked to teenagers, adults and seniors. So I had a good relationship. Also topics were limited about school, people , about work, etc . Most times topics were on festivals, temple activities . Conversations were simple. 
But when I came to the city , the situation changed. 
Still I became friendly to my classmates , but I didn't know  students in other sections - there were four sections in each class- and other classes. I talked with my classmates everyday before the start of the class.  We even discussed life in big cities, and in other countries. We knew about England, Japan, Singapore, Penang, Ceylon,  USA, France and many other countries. When elections were going on, we discussed about speeches by local leaders. Caste was not discussed there. Similarly ,most were reluctant to talk about food due to strong likes and dislikes. 
We participated in many programs. One of my classmates was in Nawab Rajamanikkampillai's  drama troupe. So he acted in most school plays. We used to have cultural programs . I participated in debates and elocution contests . But i got the first or second prize for academic performance every year till 11th standard - all six years. So I collected a lot of books as prizes. I got an English dictionary and a wren and Martin grammar book and several other books. This also meant that many other students knew me. 
Since there were about five of my classmates in my street , I became friendly with them . While we used to play in the evening, there weren't many common topic. We talked about school, teachers and exams. Some were cinema addicts. So they will talk about movies they saw recently , about actors, scenes in the films etc . I didn't contribute to that conversation. Interest in cricket was picking up. There was a ground in the neighbouring street and another five classmates were living there. So we started playing there. We had a wooden piece for the bat and a tennis ball, but no wickets initially. Later we got some sticks for wickets. We used to play every weekend there. I tried to learn how to cycle there, fell down a few times and stopped that activity. I regretted the fact that I can't cycle. 
So that was my conscious effort to build friendships and trust- acceptance in a group is trust. Playing together is another trust. 
As regards seniors and elders, I talked to a few people in my street and my teachers- that is all restricted conversation. As I said before, I used to listen in on the conversations my uncle had with other teachers in the school - one was a regular visitor to our house everyday- and our visitors. I learnt a lot from that. 
Then I got another opportunity. About 12 senior students from high schools and the college used to come to our house to learn Hindi in the evening.  There were two batches. So I also joined these two batches and became busy learning Hindi and appearing for exams conducted by the Hindi prachar Sabharwal. My interactions with these senior and college students broadened my thinking , and opened up new topics. Hindi books revealed facts about life in the north. So my school life was hectic and I built a good trust network. I was in touch with one student in that group -R Rama lingam - who did IPS and joined karnataka police. I was in touch with him till his retirement.

16/04/25, 5:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 685
Let me collate my learnings during this period of schooling. 
I learnt religious literature in a good measure - upanishads, ramayana, mahabharata,  some aspects of vedas, Sankaracharya's writings, purely outside the school amidst a lot of ridicule. I admired the literature for its variety - thousands of books, -,  vastness of topics covered- prayer, yagas,  life styles, language, speech, grammar,  yoga and meditation, astronomy, geography, number theory, logic, construction, animal lifes, philosophy, explanations , siddhantas, agamas, tantras, etc-, depth of coverage  - going into unknowns , and unknowables, studying human systems in detail, health aspects, study of mind, astronomy, infinity , completeness , eternal etc- , stages of explanations, structures etc. Rational arguments don't stand today before big data and ML. Similarly rationalism is a limited view compared to the highly holistic and enlarged Views of our religious literature which is several thousand years to a few decades old. That establishes openness- we can criticize, freedom - not dogmatic- , and continuous interpretations ,  upgradations  and clarifications. I learnt about the ideas of Raja Ram Mohan Roy's Brahmosamaj,  Dayanand Saraswati's Arya Samaj, Swami sivananda , Ramana , Aurobindo Ghose, and many others - all these through reading books , magazines , critiques and discussions with many . 
I learnt a lot about our social life from the literature of hundreds of books and magazines  I read during this period. I also learnt more about history , geography . Most importantly I learnt about hundreds of people who got us freedom and who built India. Some names are - Gandhi, Rajaji, Nehru, Sardar Patel, Kumaran, subramania Bharati, VO Chidambaram Pillai, Vanchi, Tilak, Ranade, Gokhale, Bose, CV Raman, Rabindranath Tagore, UV Swaminatha iyer,  Sir M Visvesvaraya , JN Tata, Birla, Dalmia,  TVS, Sympsons Anantharamakrishnan, Easwaran, AL Mudaliar etc. I admired many of my teachers.
My learnings in the school were limited to Tamil, Sanskrit , English and mathematics.  My learnings on science was poor. Actually , I got a better meaning of science only after I joined the engineering degree program in 1959 in Madurai. I should specially remember Mr  R Ramaswamy who kindled our interest in atomic Physics , radio receivers in addition to basic knowledge. His knowledge level of modern topics was reasonable, not in depth. Similarly I got a huge foundation in mathematics built by Mr KK Menon , who learnt new topics like matrices and gave us a foundation in calculus. His energy and interest in learning were great.

17/04/25, 5:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 686

I am changing direction to look at another aspect of academics. It is a disease that afflicts many - academic freedom. Currently it is a  big issue  in the US. I have no opinion on what is going on in US . But i thought that a balancing is needed here. Many academics wanted to fight Modi in indian universities and institutions in the name of academic freedom. Again I am not taking sides in this aspect. I thought about it and N Ramanathan posed a query.
“Harvard may be a top Management Institute, but can it manage Trump?”
So i decided to look at this aspect. 
Let me define academic freedom as freedom to do academic work as per existing practices without political and administrative controls and influences but based on collective peer group decisions. That is a general enough definition. 
Why do we need it?
Academics want it to do independent unconstrained research work to invent new things and to take knowledge forward . That is how research works. So  It is a must. There are other types of research like sponsored research , output oriented research etc that have defined objectives so the researchers should follow them. So freedom is constrained here. 
While academics were free to do what they wanted, now due to funding conditions and sponsorship , their goals are defined and their progress checked by a third party. Free grants are reducing. So there is some kind of limits on research. But still academics have freedom to do the following :
1.To define research directions, research policies, and progress of research work 
2.To define a code of conduct for academics to follow
3.To frame rules to organize and run academic programs and activities - this is done by an academic senate.
4 To define and build a  curriculum , to teach courses and to evaluate as per defined set of rules.
5. To go by peer discussions and peer reviews
And 
6. To work for the furtherance of knowledge for the benefit of all. Knowledge for the sake of knowledge.  
Let me start with my first question :
Is freedom absolute ? This is particularly with reference to social sciences , speeches etc. An academic is bound by responsible behaviours , should not be biased , and should obey the code of conduct.  If an academic claims absolute freedom , it will destroy world order, and it can destroy the  peace for an individual. It is difficult to prove defamation in India because of intellectual freedom. That is where trouble begins. Freedom is not a licence to abuse some one or some system. But it is difficult to decide for some - it is judgmental and subjective- that something is a protest or abuse. Unfortunately freedom or absolute freedom hasn't been understood. 
That leads me to academic freedom. 
What is academic freedom ?
It is the freedom for a faculty or student 
To work on a topic or area of their choice by devising procedures for solving the problems and - importantly- defending their results to peers in the area. The divisions and specialisations have increased and it is difficult to look at thousands of publications. Peer review is constrained. Recently we are seeing some faculty withdrawing or retracting their publications. This may lead to AI reviewing research work. That is a danger academics are not concerned about. There are tools to detect plagiarism now. Normally,  even this freedom is questioned in some places - boundaries are fixed on the areas and relevance. 
To conduct their activities - research and teaching - according to the accepted norms. Here is where conflicts come. Each one interprets norms differently. Institutions review the work output not the methodology . How do you ensure integrity here. Most academics are honest. Here principally freedom meant that you use standard procedures and when you deviate , then you need to convince your peers that your methodology is correct and appropriate. But there is no mechanism unless the modified procedure goes for publications .  What freedom means is that you are mature. You decide your topics, your hypothesis and methods of proving . That can't be questioned by administration or other unconnected people ,but can be questioned by peers. 
Is there another aspect to academic freedom?
Yes , it is collective to decide on academic procedures, curriculum, evaluations, future directions etc. It is not individual freedom.
But the important question is:
Does it extend to political activities ?
 One can have his views on politics and that is democratic freedom. But can one insist on forcing it inside an institution on others through protests . Does he not infringe on the freedom to conduct academic activities by disturbing it.  In India protests are held outside ,not inside a campus. So that is an accepted norm. But can you invite a politician into IISc to discuss political aspects ? That is a question with no clear understanding.
What all these boil down is that there is nothing absolute here but a judgmental process . Judgmental processes are subjective. Opinions differ. 
That is what is happening with Harvard - their Hinduphobic views are hurting a large number of people  and are not scientific . What are the academics in it? While wokism and leftist philosophies are encouraged , opposite views are suppressed . Brainwashing is allowed. Is this academic freedom?
We had a similar situation in JNU- you needed to be a leftist. Rightist views are not allowed. That destroyed academics in science and engineering. 
To me the first requirement for academics who want to preserve academic freedom is an unbiased open and critical mind and thinking. Also in my view academic freedom doesn't exist in most Indian universities and institutions. Politics dominates everywhere. Earlier it was left centred and now right centred.
I know there are opposite views. Welcome comments. 
Greetings 
Dks

In our culture, we had public debates. Do you think this type of mechanism of challenging publicly with the objective of finding the truth together (samvaada) will bring in more academic freedom?

It is invariably circumscribed by the code of conduct ( written and unwritten), institutional norms, internal rules and regulations. Politics has its place and relevance but not everywhere. No businesses enterprise will tolerate political activity within its premise despite every employee having some political affiliation or preference. It shall be all the more so in educational institutions where the prime or sole focus is knowledge building besides personality development but not its political side. Students or professors can pursue their political choice and fight for it but outside the complex. I would anyday support totally curbing political activities inside an educational complex that can never be allowed a platform for any purpose other than their core focus.”
Thanks for your comments . I agree with you. But my concern was that the west was the guardian of academic independence. India didn't have any worthwhile independence in the universities and the situation is going bad in the US. There are no defenders of academic freedom. It doesn't sound good. So what will happen to the academic truth?

19/04/25, 4:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 688


Dr TKM Babu has a question:
“👍🙏
May I ask a question?
How did you get inspiration to study literature on Sanatana Dharma?( Vedas, upanishads, Puranas,apart from tow big epics R and M )
Thanks
Mahendra Babu”

My response
Several people asked me this question. It is not taught in school. 
I used to quote upanishads , Sankaracharya,  Swami Vivekananda , Bhartrhari in several talks in my public lectures at different places. I had this practice for more than 50 years. So many people asked me after my talk ,” where did you pick up so much interest and knowledge on such difficult subjects ?”
Let me first explain my interest - how it came about?
Three reasons
Ambience and freedom
Village atmosphere - spiritual and religious
Great Mentorship

Firstly it was the ambience in my house - I was hearing Ramayana from age 3 onwards - several times a year sometimes ,but definitely once a year. My uncle will recite a few slokas from Ramayana everyday. I heard it daily  and so I understood Ramayana well. Ramayana was popular then. 
Secondly, prayers are mandatory . I saw and heard it everywhere. So it stays in you. 
Thirdly, I lived in a village with a simple life with lots of religious activities . So I heard Vedic recitals and it was common to listen to Taittiriya upanishad regularly. That impressed me deeply. I was ,I don't know the reason , a very restless person , always thinking. Fortunately , not worrying , but thinking about people, their behaviours, belief systems , and about nature etc. My curiosity was not curtailed but encouraged. I was lucky to have a great scholar ,my uncle, as my mentor. I wasn't interested in reciting vedic hymns and slokas. I wanted the meaning. I wanted to know what they imply . My uncle was highly knowledgeable.  He answered my questions. He was well versed in Sanskrit and Hindi as well as religious and spiritual matters and a sthitaprajna. My learnings came from him to a large extent. He moulded  me . 
Fourthly , people in the pre-independence and post-independence period were idealistic, tolerant ,accepted different religious practices and synergy and order prevailed . They never mocked at vedic hymns , but appreciated them.  I mean people of every caste and community. Those qualities disappeared by the 1950s. That is a tragedy.
I had not much interest in rituals , but more interest in analysing what is said in vedas and upanishads and why they said it. This was a constant learning process. 
I heard a lot of stories from many people - panchatantra, hitopadesa,  mahabharata.  The greatest aspect of our early literature is the way many stories are intermingled with a main story. 
My knowledge of mahabharata came from the countless stories I heard during my school days and reading Rajaji’s Vyasar virundhu- very well written in simple language but with a lot of details. I liked several social practices -  Vidura , inter caste marriage, Ekalavya, karna, polyandry, evils of gambling , greed, etc.  I loved those stories. One thing that remained  in my mind from my younger days is the focus of Arjuna when Dronacharya asked his students what they saw in the tree? and got unsatisfactory answers. Arjuna's focused response remained with me. It wasn't a piece in memory. But I applied it in my education. It made me concentrate my mind and focus it on the task - learning in a classroom, reading a book . I applied it everywhere except in the workshop practice at TCE Madurai during my engineering education days.  Four  habits were started by me. First was a steady and calm mind due to meditation. Second  was a sharpened memory power to remember a lot of details . This constant practice paid rich dividends during exam time. Third was deep concentration for hours . Fourth was focus and determination . I didn't give up on tough math problems. I worked on them. Recall , I tried to solve most problems in Parkersmith on electrical engineering  and also in Pratt on hydraulics . That deepened my understanding.  
My second understanding from the question asked by Babu and others is their inner feeling that why are these vedantic teachings not taught in schools. I find that even religious institutions concentrate on rituals and not on vedanta. Vedanta is a big no no in our schools ,I don't know why. Universities don't work on vedantic philosophy. We threw  hinduism into the trash fully. Vedanta will help in our quests for spirituality in addition to materialism. Upanishadic learning is useful in doing research. The principles allow us to go deeper into a topic. 
So When will this-teaching of vedanta-  happen? 
Some institutions teach vedas ,but they concentrate on Chandas ,not the interpretation and meanings and relevance. 
Greetings
Dks

But you are modest in not articulating that you imbibed the learning’s into the core of your heart. You have internalised them. That is what makes you the person you are today - a wise man!  Uday”

My response
I like your term “ internalize” . It is a great term. While I agree with your comments, I am unable to describe the process. I was thinking about it and mentioned habits of contemplation and concentration as well as the concepts and practices of self learning, deep learning and keen observations. But I am not sure ,” how does one get the practice of internalisation”. I don't think there is one standard process. One need to learn at an  early  age the following which may lead to better internalization :(this doesn't include values and emotions. One can't get all these characteristics to a good level. Levels may vary with people.)
1.Meditation
2.Keen interest in everything happening around you
3.Open mind
4.Review your beliefs and adaptation 
5.Questioning  and curiosity 
6.Keen observation and listening, 
7.Thinking and contemplation 
8.Concentration, strong focused work culture.
9.Abstraction and comprehension
10.Memorizing and remembering practices
11.Futuristic thinking
12.Unsatisfied mind
13. Application of what you learnt to solve  the problems you encountered and deciding on future actions. 
I consider the last step of using your acquired intelligence to solve the problems coming before you very important. 
I believe one trait of internalization is memorizing facts, thinking and contemplating about them, understanding the facts holistically , analysing them and using them. That is a cognition process. If this is understood by teachers and parents and appropriate processes are built , our learning systems will improve. 
Getting your space, your freedom , your independence to think and your confidence boosts at a young age is essential to allow you to grow intellectually. That is the first hurdle. Most families don't accept the fact that their young child can think , can understand and they  need independence and freedom  . They don't expose their child to real world situations . They keep the child tied to them and tell them what to do at all times. They don't allow a child to experiment, to decide on its own and to make  mistakes and to correct the mistakes. That freedom doesn't exist. A child is considered ignorant - a moron incapable of doing anything - and needs to be supported always. That is the tragedy. You don't allow your children to grow. This is a serious problem. 
It takes a lot of time to build these qualities. I think the most successful people have acquired these qualities to easily move from task to task , discipline to discipline ,  and  company to company . I don't think I have these qualities. My level of these characteristics is very  limited.
