21/04/25, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 690

Sanjay  Chitnis has another take on academic freedom - he looks at another angle that was hurting me for several decades. 
“Hello Sir, It is interesting you brought this point of academic freedom immediately after your memories of learning Indian scriptures at a very young age. In our culture, we had public debates. Do you think this type of mechanism of challenging publicly with the objective of finding the truth together (samvaada) will bring in more academic freedom?”

This needs to be looked at from  many views.
Let me pose the concern of Sanjay. How do you prove your  research outputs are correct , consistent and new? How does one challenge the output of a research particularly in today's context of lakhs of publications and insufficient experimentation with many resorting to Matlab simulation?
Scientists evolved methods like observation, explanations, experimentation, analysis etc to prove their theory. Faraday and Volta  showed by demonstration. Newton  proved with experiments and mathematics. Bertrand Russell  did it with logic , mathematics and philosophy. Slowly methods evolved , but it wasn't enough.  So the Royal Society was started for discussions , recognition and approval. It was the first peer review and recognition mechanism. 
So Mechanisms exist. But still this question of proof is not satisfactorily answered by our current system.  Everything depends on the integrity of the individual.  
Let me see the current situation .
First ,we believe that publications provide a proof of correctness of a research work.
But with vast areas of research with faster changes happening, we don't have a vast and proven referee system. That is why retractions are happening. I am glad that there are people in many institutions who read, think and ask questions courageously. But these papers are only a  very small fraction. What about the other thousands being published? Who guarantees their authenticity? 
Another major approach for proof was through  the conferences. I have attended a few hundred conferences - from hyped ones of IEEE,  IFAC, IFIP,ACM ,VLSI
to institutional research conferences held at IISc, IITs,  and many foreign universities 
to professional conferences run by the institution of engineers, CSI,IETE, etc 
to specialised conferences organized by CBIP,COMAD,CISMOD,   Coal India, Department of Electronics 
to conferences organised by colleges.
 My experience is unhappiness - literally no discussions , most of the audience were disinterested , they wanted to present their paper and go sightseeing etc. So I didn't find conferences as fact finding missions. 
There is a third approach- PhD thesis evaluation and viva. At least at IISc,  this is a serious exercise with strong peer reviews. But that is not true with most universities today. I had many experiences. Let me narrate a few. 
In my PhD thesis ,i used multivariable Taylor series to prove the order of convergence of a nonlinear equation of thousands of variables. So I had an issue with notation. The not so  well known,  but well established approach of tensors was used by some to represent three or four dimensions particularly by Kron to analyse electrical machines . So I was familiar about tensors as I taught my course at iisc on electrical machines. Actually , this course revealed to me the fact that if you want to understand a concept, try to model it in detail and analyse the model. That was the method used by Leibnitz, the famous mathematician to analyse a fault in an electrical machine. I also realised that you get clarity through this process. I studied electrical machines for three years at my UG time, read several books, did several lab experiments ,but understood nothing. Only when information started using modeling and analysis , i learnt about transients and subtransients. Clarity evolves. 
 So I did my representations in 1968 using the  tensor notation. Dr C Devanathan ,professor in the  maths Department ,helped me . Unfortunately he passed away in 1970 just when I submitted my thesis. At my post viva, there were two external examiners , one who evaluated my thesis - Prof M V Hariharan,IIT, Bombay- and another who didn't- Dr R Sankar.  The second examiner, a mathematics/ numerical methods expert with a PhD from Oxford , questioned my notation. Prof Narayana Iyengar, Chair of the department,  got a book and a journal identified by me from the library and showed them to the examiner . He accepted it half heartedly. But what I show here is the fact that there was a serious attempt to check my thesis. There are scholars who took their job seriously and tried to reason out the methods used.  I was happy. That shows that  we have a rigorous process at iisc. 
I encountered similar situations in the case of reviews of my PhD students . So I realised that the system is working. When I was the dean of engineering,  I saw hundreds of reports of examiners and found a critical evaluation in most cases. So I am happy. But that is not true elsewhere.
In two cases of PhD evaluation , both  at IITs- Madras and Bombay,  the faculty supervisors openly expressed their displeasure that I was posing too many questions to the candidate. They thought post viva was a formality and I was fussy. 
But there were many cases where all corrections that I suggested were accepted by the supervisors and students  .
Many universities were after procedures , not the academic correctness.  I had many funny experiences with PhD thesis evaluations.

There is a fourth case of sponsored research. Normally , these are reviewed periodically. I had been involved in several reviews of DST but if I was too tough, I was removed from the review committee. 
Let me see the system proposed by vedas and upanishads and followed for thousands of years in the country in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks

“Cruelty has a human heart, 
  And Jealousy a human face; 
Terror the human form divine,
   And Secrecy the human dress.

The human form a fiery forge,
 The human face a furnace sealed,  
 The human heart its hungry gorge.”

We discussed academic responsibility as it exists now. Let me look at the concerns of vedas about discussions. 
Purva mimamsa describes methods of proofs of various theories. It focused on a multidimensional approach to prove a hypothesis. Nyaya was later built to provide a logical basis. The darshanas showed the possibility of multiple approaches to prove a concept through many analytical , logical, reasoning , experiential and evidential methods  
So the suggestion of Sanjay is timely and should be addressed. 
The idea of open discussions is stressed in rig veda itself. Rig veda says that open discussions are good methods for resolving disputes and bring in transparency , understanding and participation in governance and maintenance of order. It talks about sabhas and samitis.
What is a sabha ?
It is a committee of elders . Its main jobs are advising the rulers on various matters. It meets regularly. The veda talks about the proper methods for participation of members , their attention and interest in the topics . 
The samiti consists of all members of the village and it has the powers to elect a king and to remove a king. 
See the concept of participative democracy there. Further , we don't see caste references here - it is inclusive of all people . Sabha also refers to all elders ,not a community. 
Well , over a period of time , practices changed , I think,  leading to powerful kings and dynastic rule as we see in Ramayana and Mahabharata.   The concept of Raja dharma was forgotten. What Kautilya wrote was ignored.
While the sabha concept was retained in a diluted form, samiti disappeared. A tragedy. 
The second aspect was samvad - open challenges , arguments and discussions to establish one's theory. 
For millennia , Indian scholars were defending their scholarship and their methodology at many places . Many hundreds of  cities like Kashi,  Darbhanga, Kanchipuram , Tanjore, Sringeri,  and many other places nurtured scholars and encouraged samvad. 
The first detailed account of a big congregation of 1000 scholars questioning Yajnavalkya in King Janaka's court is to be found in Brihadaranyaka upanishad.  That tells us the purpose of a conference. Time is not an essence here but showing proof of scholarship. 
Dissent is also evident in Yajnavalkya. He rejected his guru's teaching of Yajur veda and developed a new stream of Shukla Yajurveda.  He has to return his learnings from his guru and relearn. So unlearning and relearning was established by him. 
Yajnavalkya also established rules for samvad - clarity, detailing , logic . He introduced the neti concept in samvad . He wanted a holistic definition of a concept and the importance of logic and languages. 
Samvad without fear was displayed in Thiruvilayadal , a Tamil classic, where a poet Nakkirar challenges Lord Shiva on the concept of the smells  in a lady's hair. Kings encouraged Samvad. 
Another example is about Sankaracharya.  He did Samvad in Benares and convinced a large number of scholars. He destroyed the concept of anatman in Buddhism and defeated most buddhists . He established thereby the concept of unity of Brahman and Atman,  known as Advaita .  
But his well known fight was with Mandanamishra which lasted for a week. The judge was Mandan Mishra's wife.  Sankara won  and Mishra became his disciple. 
Ramanuja also had many such philosophical discussions. This approach of samvad was well established and continued for several millenia. That led to the development of many philosophical ideas, thoughts and disciplines. It integrated many disciplines like mathematics, astronomy, logic , languages, human behaviours, ethics, and evolution of the universe. 
While we saw three major schools of Sankara , Ramanuja and Madhwa evolve , and we saw several other schools of advaita evolving in kashmir, Bengal,  karnataka,Andhra  kerala and Tamilnadu , we found some new avenues like bhakti marga and the development of  grammar,metallurgy, astronomy, logic, geometry , etc evolving over centuries producing hundreds of scholars and thousands of treatises. Gurukula and samvad were the major instruments to build new knowledge.  
One of the best examples of this is  Navya nyaya expounded by many scholars in Bengal and Bihar region with a different role from karnataka - both Udupi and Sringeri as well as kerala . They worked for clarity and correctness. Navya nyaya shows how a new discipline of logic evolved first  and gave birth to several logic schools. Sanskrit literature abounds with hundreds of theories and methods of proving them. We lost that culture of questioning. Today, We mistake questioners as a nuisance.  We kill curiosity. 
Thanks Sanjay for your comments. 
He has more comments

Dr TKM Babu expresses his concerns:
“Dr DKS
Some of the olden system of participative democracy and no caste system were core principles for governance. These were misunderstood by many partly by politicians and the like and through  the media they control.  
How can we reverse the trend.?
Thanks
Mahendra Babu”

My response 
I agree with you that we lost many such valued and idealistic solutions and practices over a period of centuries. 
Let me explain some reasons as I understood some that we can find answers. 
Many ideals are difficult to follow. The kings stopped looking at the Raja dharma as they found it difficult to implement. 
Kingdoms became large - from a cluster of villages to a large area. So the samiti concept didn't work. Kings became usurping the powers. 
Peace was disturbed and fights between neighbouring kings became common. This made the kings to define new rules and consequently they became powerful. None in his kingdom can oppose them. A well known example is the nanda dynasty. Chanakya was evicted from his kingdom. The king ignored the people. 
People were made to worship a king as a god. This happened even during the Ramayana time itself. Raghuvamsa started quite early. 
Once a king becomes powerful, he discards the samiti but retained a sabha in a different form. He decided to make his son his successor. 
Sabha was filled with people selected by the king. People had no role here.
But the most important aspect is gurukula system was restricted to a few. Most people weren't interested in learning from a guru. So it became restricted to brahmins for scholarship and kshatriyas for warfare. Though the kshatriya concept is found in mahabharata,  many later kings were non kshatriyas . Many examples are there in history. 
So the various factors built a hierarchy of the society which is called a caste now.

From the early days , people were jealous of brahmins taking over vedic rituals . That was the reason for buddhism and jainism to develop in the country. So the first step is to create an educational system open to all that teaches samskaras , and agamas in addition to vedic hymns. Is this possible? How does a government implement this when there is not much revenue and livelihood in it. Even brahmins have stopped learning these today. So it is a big ask.  


Babu’s query of turning back the clock is not difficult. 
We need an understanding for that . 
The most important awareness is that vedas are not caste oriented . They contain a vast set of values,  processes for acquisition of knowledge , maintenance of order,good  governance , and a number of practices to live a meaningful , harmonious , sharing life. Vedas and upanishads establish many good principles for a society. 
Are we prepared to accept the above premise?
If we aren't,  then there is no possibility of going forward to build  a unified egalitarian society. 
We mingle with different types of people , different cadres of people in our profession. We get adjusted to people of different castes working with us, people of different castes staying In our neighbourhood.  We accept that. So that is a big move forward. 
If we feel that Hindu scriptures are not casteist and brahminical,  we should start learning them and introduce the best in our school curriculum. What is wrong in saying
Matru  Devo bhava
Shraddhaya deyam
Ahimsa paramo dharmaha 
Satyam vada 
Lokas samastha sukhino bhavanthu
Vasudev kutumbakam
Yogakshemam vahamyaham
Poornamidam 
Etc.
 They explain some of the universal concepts and values.
There were lot more principles and concepts about human behaviours, human well being, logic, knowledge etc and they can be taught in schools
So the only solution is to educate all on these hundreds of concepts found in vedas and upanishads and darsanas. Making people change their mindset is tough. Only education can do it. 
Ethics is never taught in schools. Niti sastras are not taught. Even Tirukkural is learnt sporadically. Why don't the Tamilnadu government introduce Tirukkural as a full subject, not as a small part of Tamil,  for three years - third to fifth classes?. 
I don't have a fully implementable  answer for your question, Babu.

Vinod Vyasulu has another angle.
“Any comments on the current attack on Harvard University by the US President? Is it really about academic freedom or politics? Student protests about the atrocities in Gaza are being called anti semetic...a human rights  protest being made into a religious tolerance one in a highly explosive way.” 

My response
Actually that is my worry. It is destroying the foundations of university academics. It is definitely politics . 
It was well understood in India for millenia and in the west for many centuries that scholarship grows in an unfettered ambience and freedom. The Indian gurukula system emerged on the principle that the guru knows the best in jnana. Gurus gave their knowledge freely to all. There is a rigorous set of qualifications prescribed for a guru by Sankaracharya.   Academic freedom emerged in the west. England respected Oxford and Cambridge in the beginning.  Most senior government administrators and some politicians came from these two universities. Even today many universities in UK  train both administrators and politicians on several aspects and maintain their contacts with these people. So the relationship is strong. Also politicians are proud of the achievements by universities and nobel prizes  obtained by their faculty. Universities were respected. So academic freedom was permitted to produce better results. 
One of the important guiding principles ,a reason for academic freedom , is the fact that people appreciated the academics’  intellectual approaches to solve many problems facing a nation. Many academics advised governments on technology and economic matters. Academicians played a significant role in building public systems and solving serious problems like corona. 
The US almost followed that approach. Most politicians had gone to Harvard or Princeton or other universities.  They understood the importance of academics and research in the country's development.  So they adapted a hands-off approach. In addition, many faculty members join the government in senior positions for a short period. Faculty play an influencing role in the country's governance, economy and growth. 
Other countries followed. So there is a very strong academic culture and independence existing in most universities even today. 
But with the current administration,  universities are not considered important . That is dangerous. A great many centuries old tradition is under threat. 
But is the government the only entity responsible for this situation ? 
My answer is no. Academics moved away from fairness, truth and Justice. They became biased , left leaning and highly egocentric and scholarship was not a high priority focus in many universities across the world today. 
France is bringing legislation to reduce the biases. They are reworking the freedom of speech range. 
To me biased academics are more dangerous than interference by the government.  Many universities, particularly the technology based ones, were reasonably rational and objective, but not the liberal arts ones. 
Let me define what academic freedom is.
According to google
Academic freedom: is the freedom of teachers, students, and academic institutions to pursue knowledge wherever it may lead, without undue or unreasonable interference 
or restriction from law, institutional regulations, or public pressure.
French statute law declares about higher education that "teachers-researchers (university professors and assistant professors), researchers and teachers are fully independent and enjoy full freedom of speech in the course of their research and teaching activities, provided they respect, following university traditions and the dispositions of this code, principles of tolerance and objectivity".
The German Constitution (German: Grundgesetz) specifically grants academic freedom: "Art and science, research and teaching are free. Freedom of teaching does not absolve from loyalty to the constitution" 
I define academic freedom as
“Creation of an ambience conducive to learning, teaching, research activities performed by faculty and students without fear , biases but committed to truth and integrity .“ 
What is ambience ?
Peace in the campus
Intellectual and questioning atmosphere
Fairness ,truthfulness, academic commitments  and integrity 
Freedom from the  fear of slavery of dominant groups , from unfair dismissals, 
Freedom to perform academic duties, to discuss, to allow for many views 
No admin control of academic processes
Financial Support from government 
Today's concerns are 
1.actions by governments 
2.actions by university administration
3. Politicalization of university ambience 
4.private management's insensitive ,slave driven approach to academics
5. Selling education as a commodity. 
6. Avoiding laws and constitutional obligations.
Let me look at the Indian scene in the next post.

25/04/25, 4:04 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 694
Let me look at some aspects of academic freedom. 
First factor is fear. I see fear pervades in most institutions in India even now.  Till the end of the 19th century all higher education was controlled by Britishers.indians were slaves. So there was no academic freedom in Indian universities. There was no respect and no understanding of scholarship. The only exception I see is Ramanujan , the mathematics genius. The other aspect of scholarship I perceive is scholarship in sanskrit and some Indian languages. 
The situation took one step forward with great sparks of Indian scholarship in the early part of the 20th century. We know of C V Raman, K S Krishnan, S Radhakrishnan, TMP Mahadevan, Hiriyanna, AL Mudaliyar, and many more. 
What was the state at iisc during this period 1909 to 1945. It was the same as that of all Indian universities- Dominance by a few british professors and indian faculty members were not treated as equals. CV Raman was the first Indian to become director of IISc. The British professors didn't like it. They connived to get him dismissed as director , a black mark. The reason- Raman invited Max Born to visit iisc. So he was called a traitor to British government and removed as director. That was the state of academic freedom at iisc at that time. But there is a big difference between iisc and indian universities.  Academic research and scholarship. IISc focused on research and excellence in learning. That was consistent throughout the period of its existence and continues even now. 
When India got independence, the universities moved into top gear by respecting academic work and expertise . But fear persisted. So academic freedom was restricted to only a few. The fight between Prof GN Ramachandran and A Ramakrishnan was well known. Ramakrishnan had to leave the Madras university and start a separate institute . 
Academic freedom stayed up to the professors level in most public institutions like universities and government colleges till the sixties. But scholarship and research were getting recognition in both academic and government circles. But slavery persists in private institutions even now. Remember there are thousands of private universities and colleges now. Faculty are insulted , dismissed and spied upon by the managements.  So the concept of academic freedom is not appreciated in India except in a few places. 
The second major aspect for a working freedom is the processes for selection of students and faculty, recognition of good work and incentives and freedom to conduct research . A just set of processes should be defined for all and several consultative bodies With powers and responsibilities defined. IISc had a well defined set of bodies like governing council, court, senate , faculties , curriculum committee etc which have mostly academic members except council which has some administrators. Council is the Apex body. All matters are taken to the appropriate bodies for decisions. So transparency and participation  exist in governance. There are hundreds of subcommittees at both department and central levels. So a good consultative process is built. While the council, senate and faculties existed for a long time, most committees and structures came into existence from the sixties. A lot of flexibilities have been built in operating systems . 
Selections of faculties and students and promotions follow rigorous defined processes with the involvement of professors and external experts. 
I consider this as an ideal academic freedom.  A good structure, a well defined set of rules, a participative and transparent governance system and a strong academic ambience reduces the anxiety and fear in the stakeholders and allows the free growth of scholarship. That is a good prescription for both academic and administrative autonomy. Some IITs follow similar systems. Central universities have a reasonable degree of autonomy ,but not in the state governed universities.  
You don't find it in any other universities or institutions in India. 
Let me continue further in the next post.

“A book is written when there is something specific that has to be discovered. The writer doesn’t know what it is, nor where it is, but knows it has to be found. The hunt then begins. The writing begins.”
—Roberto Calasso, The Celestial Hunter

We saw the evolution of processes for academic freedom at iisc . I played a major role working in many committees and helping in the generation of policies . 
These efforts helped in transparency . There is no fear at iisc. Most have good freedom to pursue their academic work. 
Let me tell you how this freedom and more than that a great encouragement helped me in getting involved not only in most activities - not just day to day, but even creation of new innovations, - at iisc, but even at the national level. 
This academic freedom helped me
To conduct my academic activities like teaching ,research, lectures , participation in conferences, working groups , meetings - at inter institutional levels, at government level, at industry level, and at public professional institutional level. It helped me to grow in all directions and reach good levels. 
To participate in several national programs in power, energy, environment, computers and IT sectors in a significant way.
To participate in promoting Computer education in many institutions.
To develop and promote computer and IT culture in many institutions , government departments,  industries , banks etc. To assist in the transformation of many businesses to IT and digital era. 
To lecture in more than 400 institutions and hundreds of schools in rural areas. 
To conduct several major and large projects like Mahithisindhu, TCS sysgel,  DOS CoWAA, many corebanking and IT transformation projects in many banks, ERP selection and assessment for many organizations, modernization of electric power systems at IISc,  JNCASR, Vidhanasoudha, Jayadeva etc, advising on networking in more than 30 institutions and many banks, and many more . I learnt a lot about the distance between academia and practical implementation processes. 
To train  thousands of teachers , computer engineers, power engineers working in power boards and industries , IAS, IFS officers, and thousands of students. 
To play a big role in remodeling curriculum in several universities , institutions and colleges.
To help in inculcating research culture in many universities and colleges. 
To participate in several reforms in the higher education sector.

I could achieve a holistic life and  a fulfilling life, possible because of the  autonomy and ambience provided and actively nurtured at IISc IISc gave me purposes in life.I should remember some stalwarts at this time - Sir CV Raman ,who diversified science to include Physics,  Prof Dhawan, responsible for reforms and all round growth, Prof M S Thacker who brought in several engineering disciplines into the institute and made it a strong engineering institution, Prof  Ramseshan , who expanded the institute with ten new centres and visualised a supercomputer facility, and many Others.

We saw that academic autonomy needs administrative autonomy. It also needs another important component- financial autonomy. That is being attacked by the current US administration.  
Financial autonomy Is a must for academics to flourish. This is the biggest hurdle for all public universities in India and also in the universities everywhere. Universities never addressed this issue. Universities give a high quality and highly subsidised education to its students. Many climb up  the success ladder , but most Alumni don't fund their universities. That is a tragedy. Just like we have a law for industries to spend a part of their profit on charitable purposes through corporate social responsibility law, we need a law for past students to refund a part of the subsidy spent on them back to the university. 
We expect a highly subsidised education , but why don't we return the subsidies enjoyed by us back to the university? 
But the biggest problem , a problem more difficult to solve, to achieve autonomy is the mindset changes required amidst academics in our colleges and universities. We need commitment and integrity for academic principles and work. We should shed diffidence,  excuse mentality, and fear. We should establish independence , curiosity and critical thinking . 
It is the duty of every academic in our education system to get interested , get involved and work to improve autonomies and take academic independence to a higher level. 
That completes my discussions on academic freedom. 
Thanks Ramanathan,Vinod, Babu  and Sanjay for pushing these discussions.

“For if medicine is really to accomplish its great task, it must intervene in political and social life. It must point out the hindrances that impede the normal social functioning of vital processes, and effect their removal.”
—Rudolf Virchow, nineteenth-century German physician

Let me return to learning. 
Let me elaborate: what is learning now? 
A comprehensive  answer is:
Access , avoid errors, connect the dotted and undotted lines,  assimilate , adapt , extend , clarify.  analyse outputs, characteristics, impacts etc, adjust or improve, integrate,  apply, model  , design , decide and solve problems, innovate , discover. 
This means that learning is not a simple discipline. 

Learning is a multistage Process. You learn step by step, uncover new connectivities, integrate, get holistic views, discover new facts, analyse . Normally most people stay in the first stage - learn what is enough to survive - to get a job, to get along with others , to do tasks correctly. This stage doesn’t help one to deal with uncertain situations , complexities. It is more a frog in the well life. 
But a human can think ,can ask questions. So the second stage takes us through a number of tools to access,  absorb, analyse knowledge . This is the period where one learns a language, reasoning skills, mathematics, basics of universe , life and living and understands governance and social behaviours. This is done through a formal school or gurukula method where a teacher tells you the methods, encourages you to ask questions , and clarifies your doubts. 
Third stage takes us through more of the skills . 
But it is the fourth stage which is important. 
One learns through two methods. All of us follow one method - observe , read, listen, experiment ,analyse, and apply facts. This is the external method. One is guided by others - parents, relatives, neighbours, teachers , coworkers, etc - . Ninety nine percent of people follow this route. 
There is a second step here - get into a reading habit. This is the start of the second method. Calming the mind through meditation is the basic requirement. Reading, understanding , appreciation and application are  mostly the first level of the internal learning process. One is encouraged by others in these activities and one can get external help here.
Second level of internal learning is to cultivate four important habits. 
1.observation
2.questioning 
3.analysis and decision making 
4.thinking 
Each one has several levels - from simple outward looking level ,to finding errors and correct our data, to analysis, to establish connectivities, to open up new avenues, to find solutions , 
All of us start at this level from childhood and go through life using these techniques throughout our life. But the level of getting a mastery of these four habits varies from person to person. Unfortunately it is assumed that everyone can acquire these four traits . So our education system - both formal and informal ones- don't help you to develop these habits. One has to learn these through conscious efforts. The internal processes need to be developed by one according to one's capabilities. 
The next higher level is self realization. That is the area our upanishads concentrate on. It is not clear how one gets into this level. Very few get into this internal self learning level. It is an intense self Learning activity ,not well defined.
Greetings 
Dks
28/04/25, 4:52 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 697
“To Mercy, Pity, Peace, and Love,  
 All pray in their distress, 
And to these virtues of delight 
  Return their thankfulness.
 For Mercy, Pity, Peace, and Love,   Is God our Father dear; 
And Mercy, Pity, Peace, and Love,   Is man, his child and care.
William Blake 

Learning is a complex ,multidimensional ,lifelong series of activities. One acquires knowledge from everything - not every person but everything is a teacher-, 
sharpens them ,
adds more to the already collected knowledge- that is one grows knowledge like a tree-, 
Understands what is acquired ,
applies knowledge in many situations happening in one's life, society , and jobs 
And
Develops new knowledge - inventions, discoveries etc. 
So learning is as essential to a person as eating to survive. Eating and its scarcity are visible - hunger, obesity , poverty , malnutrition etc. But the impacts of learning are only partially visible to us through schooling , formal education, skills etc. The invisible part should be told explicitly for us to understand the importance of jnana. The question is
What converts  an educated person into an intellectual person? It obviously goes beyond formal education and degrees. The educated have to fill many gaps in their learning. 
Some people look at a few utilitarian gaps - lack of applications, no practical experience, poor problem solving skills, and even poor communications skills . There are discussions on these aspects by many learned persons like Narayana Murthy, his friend Mohandas Pai and now Mr Sridhar Vembu . They all think the main objective of education is skills generation. But with robotics and AI ,many experiences and many skills will become obsolete. We see coding activities are dying leaving millions out of jobs. 
So let us look at the two questions:
1  What should be the objectives of learning ? 2.what are the hidden gaps in our education system ? 
While these two can be analysed to some extent, finding remedial measures- i call them foundations of learning , or foundations of awakening and improving our consciousness-  and implementing them are not easy. They need strong commitments and qualities. 
I have discussed the objectives of learning already. Let me amplify that. 
First axiom 
Objectives of learning should align with objectives of a balanced life- material, spiritual, personal, family, society , and nature based components  .
So the question becomes - what are the objectives in one's life  whether one should live like a farmer, clerk, officer, teacher, scholar , researcher?
There are many requirements to lead a satisfactory life. I am looking at some requirements that prepare us to meet uncertain situations. 
One requirement is evident - 
Life is changing - we are seeing the changes in the past 50 years . 
Changes bring in new lifestyles and they bring in new requirements whether you are a farmer or scholar. Whatever techniques were applied before a change may not be useful after the change. This needs to be understood and accepted by educationists. 
Our education system failed in this important litmus test. It doesn't address changes, accept changes, develop ways of meeting changes and provide the necessary skills to students. It is doing it partially and there are   big gaps there.
Let me take a familiar example.
Computers and IT brought in a lot of changes In our life directly or indirectly. Let me look at the question : How prepared were we to adapt to these changes? That will give us a clue and many learnings that can be adapted for future changes. 
When computers appeared in the country in mid sixties, there were few academics even in IISc who were aware of it and fewer like me used computers. I could count a few - about 4 who used computers in iisc till 1970. I was the only one to teach computer courses almost at par with their developments. I taught operating systems in 1968  just when it was being developed by IBM. But the situation in iisc didn't change till 1975. The situation was better at IIT Kanpur and Bombay. IIT Madras started computer science  by 1970 only . So the best academic institutions were behind by more than a decade. Normal colleges started computer science by 1985 or later . They were behind by two to three decades. 
But the worst was the situation with software. IISc professors said in the late 1970s that software is not an engineering subject , this in open faculty / senate meetings. The situation was the worst in most institutions. They concentrated in programming - writing a 20 line code focusing on logic . Large scale programming - writing 50k lines- was questioned even in 2008. Only Dhamdhere built a compiler. I ,from the beginning, insisted on my students to build large programs in 1970 itself. I also developed two languages - cospro for simulation and sysgel for software automation. 
The story with PCs was similar - a decade or more lag for academics to know them. 
We completely missed the Internet bus. That was a tragedy. 
A good learning system should reduce technical content and focus on predictive and development systems. 
We need to address these requirements . That is a big ask in today's transformative life . 
Let me list the requirements. 
Understand changes happening by looking around - so a broad , generalised knowledge is a must , not a specialised skill alone.  We need a new course on change management. The 21st century has moved towards agile and continuous innovation . We should think seriously about how we move and change to meet the volatility of the world. 
Look at what are the characteristics required to adapt to a change. I call this as human engineering.  Prepare to change yourself. 
Predict changes - get social and society transformational knowledge. Learn look ahead techniques from past movements. This is where intellectual scholarship , thinking , and good analysis capabilities will be useful. 
Change yourself by relearning .
Accelerate the change . Develop new methods to adapt changes in many processes, prepare people to accept these changes , find alternatives, and teach the impacts.

29/04/25, 3:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 698
A more free-wheeling, anarchic organization is the secret of our greater capacities of creativity, imagination, thinking outside the box and all that, and the price we pay for it is our susceptibility to obsessions, mental illnesses, delusions, and smaller problems.
 We got risky brains.
Brockman.     Thinking

We saw that the main objective of learning is to aid and direct the purpose of life. 
In today's fast changing world, we need a futuristic Outlook. It will answer the question - what next? This implies 
Looking at the next five years - it takes three to five years for a change to start and settle down and also it takes three years to prepare for the incoming change - to relearn. 
Knowing what might happen in the next five years. Will my job be there ? Will my money be there ? 
Plan to be prepared for the change. It is a constant lifelong process. 
We all should become change managers. 
I recall the beautiful story.  
Three fishes were living in a small pond. One day ,they heard two people saying that the pond will be drained of water shortly. Land grabbing was common then also. 
One fish decided to take advance action and moved to another pond. Another fish waited and left as soon as it sensed the start of the draining operation. The third complacent fish stayed and died. 
A simple story,  but a powerful learning. 
This shows that we can't be complacent, but always alert and active. 
Don't be a frog in the well, look outside, see what is happening,  understand what is needed and act. This is what Prof Clayton Christensen called as disruptive innovation in businesses.
If you think I am fussy and life is fine, let me tell you - what happened to me and to many around me. 
When I was in the engineering college in 1962, i was studying vacuum tube electronics and built many circuits using these tubes. 
When I joined iisc in 1967, i started learning transistor electronics. Concepts were different and I had to forget most valve circuit theory. 
But in 1968, I found switching circuits and logic. I had to move from analog to digital electronics. Visualising a transistor as a switch was a major change . I realised transistors are magicians with many avatars . They act as rectifiers, high frequency amplifiers and oscillators and also switches. They were used in analog and digital circuits. Then I learnt they are used in computing. It is not learning as a bystander, but to design and build systems , which is deep learning. 
Within a year , i was looking at ICs and large computers. That was the transformation happening in the sixties. 
Similarly, i moved up the programming language ladder - machine language to assembly and autocoder to Fortran to Cobol, Pascal to C, and Java to Python - all self taught and learned. 
That capability should be built in children.
The best example for change management is the stock market brokers, advisors and investors - they have to be on their toes, their concept of fundamentals changes regularly, they have to be nimble, they should know the market every minute as the market is dynamic  , they should act quickly and can't wait , they should not count their chickens - their success or failure should not be counted every day ,but over a longer period. 
We need to learn several qualities from them - which a broker teaches his son from his childhood. 
hunger for correct knowledge at every instance - it is a constant hunger not quenched easily. Data dependent. 
      2.  A totally outward world wide look at many markets 
     3.  Detailed studies of markets, impacts due to political happening , impacts due to demands, impacts due to other country markets. 
    4. analysis without rules . Invent on your feet. 
    5.  flexibility to change the methods 
agility in actions . Try to do something new every week. Plan for every week, not just school timetable 
but financial planning and time planning.
networking , communications.
Quick decision making , take responsibility, don't delay , time is money.
Gita ‘s philosophy - nishkama karma - don't worry about results . Failures are part of the game.



Look at a lawyer. He gets a change of task at regular intervals. Each case needs learnings to understand , referencing past judgments and agileness to respond to arguments in the court. It is another effective change managing profession. 
The other example is a politician. He needs to study unknowns - people's feelings, emotions, needs etc. He should build a case for votes not just money for votes. He should rebut the arguments of others, play on the emotions of people ,play up injustices - Garibi  hatao of Indira Gandhi is a good example. They act like a behaviour expert, psychologist, communications expert, networking specialist, economist, advertiser , futurist etc, a multidimensional personality. All these are needed for survival. 
Let us put our children in uncertain situations by creating some uncertainties like no food one night . Let us follow the merchants, lawyers and politicians and train our children. Kids gloves are off now. No cuddling , pampering and destroying our children. Train them to face the future.

30/04/25, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 699

Let me continue  discussions on  my learning  process. 
As I repeatedly stated , I learnt from every person , every book , magazines and news sources. I also observed others activities and learnt from their approaches . When I say I learnt , it meant absorbing ideas, concepts  and methods and following them as far as possible. It also meant generating beliefs and habits. 
While in general ,the above statements are true , I had some influencers whom I respected and listened to and absorbed some of their qualities . I talked about my grandmother and my maternal uncle as my great influencers . Let me talk about my grandfather. He didn't live long, he died when I was ten or eleven and my interactions with him were not many and  happened on a few occasions. 
He was a scholar in many languages - Tamil, Malayalam and Sanskrit. He has read many sastras and ithihasas. He used to deliver lectures every day. Many people from different castes consulted him regularly. He led a stable and very frugal life. 
He lived far away from our place - about 200 plus miles from my village. I visited him a few times and he visited the village several times at least once a year till he passed away. Normally he stayed for a week or more. Twice, he stayed for 15 days - both times to perform the death ceremonies of his mother and Wife, that is my grandmother. 
We were scared of him. But he was kind to me , talked to me softly and kindly. He enquired about me . Normally , there will be several men sitting with him including my father and uncles. I observed his interactions with my uncle. That interested me a lot. I respected both. Both of them had a great respect for each other. While my grandfather was spiritually and religiously inclined, he left all matters relating to land , money to my uncle. He listened to my uncle. He also lost a lot of money which He loaned to two well known people - one was a rich man . They put the money in about 60 acres of land which went into litigation. The rich man became poor. My grandfather lost his money. After that he entrusted everything to my uncle. 
 My uncle used to tell me that my grandfather was his guru. Their discussions were serious. I listened to a lot of stories from him. My grandfather gave about 4to 5 talks in the village. I attended the talks and listened to his talks. 
Even though the time I spent with him was short, it gave me an appreciation of the values he discussed and practised - simple and frugal living, concern for others, serious interest in sastras, no gossips, effort needed to improve cognition. That is why I call him and influencer - a presentation day term. He would have given thousands of such talks and definitely many thousands would have listened to him and benefitted from his lectures. He introduced some freedom fighters in his talks and even permitted Subramania Bharati's  songs to be sung in his meetings. 
I had met later, in the late fifties , Mr Dikshitar who was an MLA from cheranmahadevi constituency during two train travels. He told me that he was encouraged by my grandfather to participate in the freedom struggle. He said he used to sing Bharathi’s  songs during  the lectures . Fortunately they were ignored by the authorities. 
At that period , we were under british rule . So such talks were not encouraged by the British.  

Lateron there were no influencers from whom I can learn .   There were only advisors , mostly relatives and teachers. 
Let me look at my next transformation point . I moved to St Xaviers college in Palayamkottai for my college education - initially for PUC. This introduced many changes in my life . I became independent. I managed the household - mostly buying groceries and managing money independently. I admitted my sister to school. We three of us lived in a house. My father visited us regularly and my grandmother also visited us occasionally. I visited regularly my uncle's house in Tirunelveli, a couple of miles away. Town bus service was available. All places are within walking distance. 
The second transformation was the fact that the medium of education shifted to English. My class had a strength of 100 - a big change from 30. It is a mixed class with about 50% Christians. So there were many adjustment problems , but I had no problems. I got into the system quickly. 
I participated actively in several topics like English, History, and Logic . I realised my potential in maths - I got 100% in all tests. I came overall  first in the class exams. I was liked by the rector and principal, Fr Soosai, the vice principal, and most teachers . I thoroughly enjoyed my studies. 
All these happened within a month of joining the college and changing three houses. 
This proved that my adaptation capabilities to new situations were good even at that time. That wasn't considered normal particularly when I saw many students struggling to understand the lectures in English . I was astonished that I could manage to listen the lectures, understand what the teacher spoke , ask questions and participate in the discussions - all these without difficulty . There were mostly short cryptic text books without explanations. But I managed easily.   
Let us look at more of this transformation in the next post.

01/05/25, 4:44 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 700
One major transformation that happened during this period of 1958 to 59 was my interest and understanding of British classic literature. My English teacher was remarkable. He used to tell many anecdotes about Boswell and Johnson , Goldsmith ,  Thomas Hardy, Thackeray,  etc. My reading of English books started with classics motivated by him .  I read several books during this one year and built an interest and taste in English literature. The college library had a number of books ,so I could borrow and read them . 
Also we had a relative , Mr Ramanathan, my aunt's brother, living in Palayamkottai. He was a very simple person without ambitions and expectations . He used to help a lot of people. He also helped us a lot during our stay . He resolved many problems we faced. His brothers in law studied in St Xavier's college . They had collected and read a lot of books and left them in his house. So I picked up books from his house and read them. Most were English classics. So I cultivated a different aptitude of reading and appreciating English books , a shift from reading hundreds of Tamil books during high school days. 
But those Tamil novels were very useful in my learning process. I learnt about our multidimensional social novel through them. Kalki taught me Tamil history and he also taught me about our independence struggle. I also learnt about life in villages, cities, big cities like Bombay,  Delhi, Burma  etc from his novels. Though i hadn't visited Bombay at that time, i knew about VT, church gate, marine drive, dadar, matunga, etc at that time through talks with visitors and stories from books.  I learnt a lot about famous people through reading biographies . It was Sundaram's biography that introduced me to yoga, healthy living , Ulsoor and bangalore. 
But the English books took me to English villages and London.  I got the best picture of London and its pie soup fog from Sherlock home stories. Dickens' great expectations was a great read and also Mark Twain's Tom Sawyer and Huckleberry Finn . 
But the major transformation was my mindset changes. I broadened my understanding of people, the world , and religion. But I moved away from religious rituals to spiritualism.  That meant that I developed an enquiring mind - skepticism , questioning, analysing , accepting correct beliefs, not criticising others beliefs and tolerances. I also developed a deep thinking habit. Surprisingly , I was not afraid of unknown disciplines. I was prepared to learn new topics. I never hated any subject. All these habits stayed with me till now. My learning of Sanskrit and Hindi came to a halt. 
This was the period when I made up my mind on  my future plans.  I didn't want to BSc but wanted to do engineering.  That goal got confirmed. I needed to find ways to achieve my goal - I had no idea how to go for engineering education.  My knowledge was nil . I started looking at colleges and their locations. 
I saw at close quarters my younger uncle struggling to get admissions into MSc. So I knew that getting a seat in engineering wasn't easy. I found out that there are two engineering colleges nearby - one at Karaikudi, another at Madurai, this being a new one. I decided on Madurai. 
Luckily the process of selection was streamlined with a centralised interview process from that year. That helped me to get admissions.

My response 
“He was a yoga teacher in the forties and fifties.  He lived in Ulsoor. His name as i remember is yogacharya sundaram.He wrote his biography in Tamil about his life and best practices to live. He described life in Ulsoor which was a Tamil dominated area then . It was part of Madras presidency then and not part of Mysore .I don't remember any details about his biography . Greetings 
Dks
Ther is a second query referring to my statement about  shifting from religious practices  to the spiritual aspects. Mr Srinivasan ,PNB enquired about it. 
Let me clarify and clear misunderstandings. 
Let us start with - What is religion? Let me give three  definitions. This is a generic definition ,may not apply fully to sanatana dharma. 
1.Oxford Dictionary defines religion as the belief in and/or worship of a superhuman controlling power, especially a personal God or gods.
2.religion, - human beings' relation to that which they regard as holy, sacred, absolute, spiritual, divine, or worthy of especial reverence
3."A religion is a unified system of beliefs and practices relative to sacred things, that is to. say, things set apart and forbidden .
The religion is divided into two broad  parts - operative part - beliefs and practices and scholarly part - philosophy, we call this vedanta or sastras or darsanas .
Each part may have subdivisions. Practices may include prayers, pujas,  festivals, pilgrimages, temple visits, samskaras etc. 
Normally practices include human values and ethics . These are not enforced. 
Practices include many defined rituals and code of conduct - sadachara-, involving  people, gurus, astrologers, sastric scholars , priests etc. 
While I was religious during the earlier years , i was attracted by upanishads and felt that they play an important role in understanding the purpose of a society, codes of conduct, values, scholarship etc. They make you think, analyse and understand many deep concepts. They provide a great opportunity to explore a whole new universe. 
Spiritualism according to me is to know and understand and follow the meaning of life, purposes and goals in life , appropriate actions , Acquisition of knowledge- jnana, finding the correctness and clarity in beliefs and actions. It implies that materialism is to be ignored , worry is to be avoided, emotions are to be controlled, thoughts and actions should be pure and benevolent,  and belief should be in values , order, truth, ahimsa, harmony .  Spiritualism implies respect to every aspect of nature, protection and conservation. 
Importantly spiritualism does not say ,starve . You don't give primacy to wealth. You earn to have a frugal life , not a flashy rich ,consumption oriented life.
Spiritualism doesn’t say , take sanyas,  but try to think good, speak good and do good most of the time. 
I don't say that i followed all these principles. I keep them with me , think of them when I act and do my best to correct myself when I deviate from these principles. 
Hope my concept of spiritualism is clear. 
Best way of differentiating between religious practices and spiritualism is to look at the philosophies of Sankaracharya and Ramanujacharya. 
Welcome discussions. I want to look at the queries of Mr Sambamurthy on this in the next post.

Timothy Snyder

Mr Sambamurthy had a few perceptive comments :
 A senior executive working for Arms manufacturer objected to this.

We have plenty of projects to build physical and digital infrastructure projects to connect people. We need Spiritual infrastructure projects.

: Dear DKS,
You made significant contribution to building Physical and Digital infrastructure.
You are well placed to help build Spiritual infrastructure project as well.
I do discern some such insights in your WhatsApp posts.

He recently posted another related  comment 
“Technological evolution is outpacing or has  already outpaced emotional and spiritual evolution. What are the negative consequences and how to mitigate them?
Appreciate your comments.
Thank you DKS.”

My response 
Well . Very thoughtful comments  with deep anguish that i see in these comments. Your concern for spiritualism is very necessary today. 
Let me discuss human life as envisaged by our dharma . 
Human life evolved from a survival mode of live to eat scenario to a technologically advanced complex and complicated life structure today. As you expressed , our dharmic concerns are very relevant today. 
Sanatana dharma envisages the following principles for a proper living. 
Life is a balancing act of many paths - karma ,the path of duty, bhakti, the path of religion, and jnana, the path of knowledge. Normally, a subsidiary path is added that of artha , wealth. 
But the most important aspect of overseeing actions of these four directions is dharma. Dharma means a very detailed set of  codes of conduct for proper living . It speaks about :
1.Healthy life practices - yoga , meditation, prayer, satvik food , emotions control, proper behaviours and conduct , etc. Both upanishads and yoga sutra talk about human systems and yoga in detail. The essential and central force is the mind - mind makes a human. There are discussions on developing a healthy and open mind. Our dharma says an unhealthy mind is dangerous.  They suggest ways of developing a healthy mind. That is the basis of a human. 
Our dharma is based on the axiom - you can modify yourself, you can change yourself. You can improve yourself and others.  
Self improvement , constant improvements, changes in living according to the changing world conditions , societal improvements - all these possible and it  should be the constant endeavor of everyone to adapt to the improvements.
Many rules have been defined to conduct oneself. 

2. Knowledge acquisition and learning - questioning, seriousness, concentration, contemplation, clarity in communications, thinking, enquiry, self learning, reasoning arguing and logic etc. Learning and gaining wisdom is considered the highest form of living. So there is a lot of emphasis on this aspect.There is a vast source of literature dealing with this. Our sastras emphasize the following important aspects of knowledge - 
-Knowledge is free 
-Respect knowledge 
- Knowledge is connected,  just like the human body is connected. 
-Clarity is important. Speak clearly. 
-Use multiple skills - perceptions, analysis, detailed observations, experiences, experiments, logical explanations, rebuttals of contradictions , completeness of definitions.
-Get knowledge from all sources . Analyse the facts and ascertain their validity , their characteristics, applications , their place in reality and their truthfulness etc. Nyaya and uttara mimamsa focus on proving assertions.  
-Give knowledge to others - share it. 
- Accept errors . Avoid ego . 
There are many more aspects dealing with different kinds of knowledge- para and apara-, absolute truth , unreality of worldly knowledge,  etc.
These scholars are the custodians of knowledge- sastras and interpreters of concepts. 
3.proper duties to family - samsara dharma . Artha and kama define the activities in this phase of life. While primary purpose is ensuring the continuity of human race, secondary purposes are 
Bringing up children  properly 
Taking care of samskaras 
Taking care of the welfare of all 
Earn your living . Live within your means ,in a frugal manner. 
No lavish living 
No greedy,  aspirational living 
Be a Roman in Rome. Follow the procedures specified for you at various stages in the smritis .

the samsara stage is considered  an essential stage. Multiple professions are suggested to individuals  . No one should be  idle and live a useless life. 
.

4. Duties to nature - respect nature, natural forces, avoid consumption. Nature belongs to god. Use it in a frugal and sacrificial mode. Protect nature. Show your gratitude to nature and natural forces. 
5. Duties to society - maintain order, help the society to lead a harmonious life. Share resources . Conduct properly and pleasantly. 
6. Duties of a king
7. How to address changes in lifestyle - four ashramas.  
8. Religious practices - there are three  kinds of activities - 
Bhakti cult - prayer , more individuals and community oriented 
Recital of vedas and conduct of yagas - sroutis - Shruti,  vedas based activities.
Temple rituals, samskaras , astrology etc. - smrithi based activities 
One should have a proper combination of these three paths in one's life. That is what I call multidimensional life. 
But what is the supervisor of a person to foresee that these paths are traversed correctly and properly ? That is dharma and let us call it spirituality. That is the operating system for humans that should govern them. But we have a corrupted operating system or none. Let us reconstruct and reboot this OS.
All actions of people are governed by dharma. But we ignored that.
As you rightly said we took the arthic  part seriously and ignored the dharmic part. That is the trouble. 
More in the next.

04/05/25, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 703

Spirituality means manythings to many people. There are different perceptions:
1.it is the power to talk to the spirits. 
2.it is the power to do mysterious acts like bringing a linga from nowhere or pouring a lot of stones into someone's house. 
3.it is the deep religious power of doing pujas. 
4. There are some who believe doing deep dyana or tapas is spirituality. 
But my perception of spirituality is different . I have two views or two levels of spirituality. 
First set of understandings:
Spirituality is not related to relation.
It is related to the mind.
Spirituality is building a good daivatva in oneself.
Spirituality has many dimensions. 
Spirituality coexists with our paths of jnana,  bhakti , karma and Artha.  It controls them.
Spirituality has many levels . One can keep moving up the ladder. Many stay at a lower level. A very few people  reach higher levels. 
Spirituality is gaining through acquiring many desirable habits and renouncing many undesirable habits. 
Self learning, self understanding and self realization is at a very high level.
Definition
The first type of  definition is the dharmic level: 
 I consider a person a spiritual person if he follows dharmic principles of good habits, pleasant behaviours, control of emotions,  open, flexible  and healthy mind, helping others, respect to every person and nature, gratitude, non violence, minimal desires and frugalism, aiming for truthful behaviour, following a set of laws to be a good member of the society etc. 
The basic tenet is selflessness - every action is pursued in the nishkama Karma principle . Gandhi said end alone is not important , means are more important. 
True ,it is not easy for everyone to follow all principles - but we have seen many who obeyed some core principles 
Selfless life
Minimal wants, frugal living
Not hurting any person or anything
Good and pleasant manners
Truthfulness
And we can say they are Spiritual.  
Some names come to my  mind - Ramana Maharshi, Kanchi Chandrasekara Saraswati swamiji , many saints in history  etc. They are jnanis, spiritualism at the highest level.
Gandhi had some qualities of spiritualism ,but he was thinking he was right and others were wrong and wanted everyone to follow his path. His tolerance was low. Single track mind is not a desirable feature of spiritualism. 
Abdul Kalam led a simple ,frugal, non greedy life , highly motivated and can be considered to be Spiritual. 
I consider highly motivated persons of great integrity as Spiritual. One name that comes to my mind is Prof. Dhawan . He wasn't religious, had communist thinking , but his clarity of thought, integrity, dedication,  and commitment are commendable. Prof AR Vasudeva murthy is another name that comes to my mind. He was  a good scholar in Sanskrit, a great researcher in chemistry , and led a simple life. I knew him in many ways - he taught me materials science , I worked with him in the hostel, and had many Spiritual interactions with him. Prof R Narasimha is another Spiritual person in my list. He had deep knowledge in sastras, a highly enquiring and open mind, a great commitment to research , a great scholar and a good friend and advisor. Discussions with him were great experiences.
I would rate most women with deep commitments to the well being of family and friends  without ill wills , desires and without gossiping tendencies as good candidates for spiritualism . 
I should confess to the fact that a large number of people that  I met with  in the industries, banks , colleges , a few politicians and governments had strong Spiritual inclinations. 
My first view of spirituality is that our paths - karmic, bhakti, jnana, arthic - are enveloped in a number of layers of dharmic principles like onion skins. 
You do your tasks but see that it is governed by Dharmic principles at every stage. Researchers have a major responsibility to see that what they produce will not harm people, animals, plants and nature. 
Most stayed at the start of spirituality. They had no greed, no violent actions, proper manners, satisfied with their life and humble - the first step. They had to go miles . Let us see the miles next.

05/05/25, 4:39 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 704

It is not difficult to develop the first level of societal structure which imbibes a Spiritual Purpose in our life. It balanced both material and human components well. This was the Indian tradition till the beginning of the 19th century. Our gurukula system implemented education on spiritual topics . 
But remember that spirituality has four major dimensions 
Human , ethical dimension 
Mental development 
Knowledge dimension 
Consciousness dimension.  

We Need to see how people climb this ladder, that is our objective. The first question - do we have enough source materials to guide us ? The answer is yes  . Let us look at them.

Fortunately , India had a very long , glorious and very strong tradition of spirituality spanning over 3000 years.  
When Vedas were developed , they were not purely religious documents. They were holistic sastras  dealing with many aspects - nature and natural forces , their impacts and uses in our life, our gratitude to nature,
 human life, purposes, meaning, habits,  practices, ideals, daivatva, 
 societal living, order, organization , harmony, sharing , etc
Knowledge - sources, types, methods of learning, truth, guru shishya tradition, 
eternal search, quest for new knowledge 
universe , evolution, temporariness of life 
Brahman 
Religious practices
Planets, their positions 
Mathematics , geometry, number systems etc 
Metallurgy , thermodynamics . 
So vedas were holistic. Vedas talked about several rishis - both male and female. Many gurus had established schools from that time onwards. 
 Based on vedas, upanishads developed and then came darsana sastras. They provided several views of spirituality. They are not pure religious documents. 
Then came hundreds of acharyas and gurus expounding the principles of spiritual concepts and interpretations. Aryabhatta, Varahamihira, BrahmaGupta concentrated on calculations and Astronomy. Many treatises were written In sanskrit and tamil. Sangam literature like Purananuru, thirukkural , etc also expound spiritual thoughts. 
Sankaracharya gave extensive analysis and interpretations on upanishads, Brahmasutra,  Bhagavad Gita.  He had written many treatises on spirituality - the eternal quest, jnana , qualities of gurus and shishyas in viveka Chudamani,  tattva bhodha,  atma bodha, upadesa sahasri. 
We know about Sankaracharya,  Ramanujacharya, Madhvacharya, followed by many disciples like sureshwara, and later Abhinava gupta . We had several scholars like Gargesa working on Navya nyaya, Bhaskara and Madhava on mathematics . Vidyaranya in 14 th century wrote panchadasi . His brother Sayana wrote interpretations for Rigveda. Many scholars like Allasani Beddanna in Krishnadevaraya's Court had also written on the essence of spiritual thinking.
Puranas like Shiva purana, bhagavatam,  Garuda purana , and many more 
But the greatest literary source for spirituality is mahabharata.  It contains a huge ocean of knowledge on spirituality. Vidhura, Krishna, yudhishtra, Bhishma discuss dharma , wisdom and various other topics. Bhagavad Gita gives a simple set of rules for spiritualism. 
Hitopadesha , Nitisatakam also discuss humanism and idealism. 
If we look at the19th and  20th centuries  , we don't see a vacuum, but we come across many stalwarts- like Ramakrishna, vivekananda, and hundreds of scholars in Ramakrishna mutt, Swami Sivananda ,  Aurobindo, Appayya dikshitar , Nilakanta dikshitar , and others . There are many nayanmars and poets like Kalidasa , Bhartrhari,  Bhana, Thirumoolar,  Kambar, Sekkizhar who have propagated these ideas and stories. 
Most exposition are available in a print form now . That is our luck. There are hundreds of writings based on these source materials. So we needn't worry about sources. 
The next question - how did spiritual living disappear ? Let me see it in the next post.

06/05/25, 3:57 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.   705
Before we discuss the reduction in the emphasis and practices of spirituality in India in the past century, let us see how we became spiritually strong.  Religion was considered an  essential requirement in one's life. The term we use is sanatana dharma . It is beyond religion.  our dharma allowed us a lot of freedom in performing worship and conducting rituals. Smritis gave a basic framework for religious activities. Upanishads discussed spiritual aspects. 
We used a term called Matha . It doesn't refer to religion but it meant a particular  sampradaya for a religious practice. These sampradayas were different in different parts of the country. Sankaracharya provided a framework to unify the sampradayas. 
 Temples, prayers, and chanting were the common practices . The Tamils have a saying,” don't live in a place where there is no temple”. The country had ,as seen in the last post,  lakhs of religious teachers and scholars. Unlike christianity , we are permitted to pray or worship or do puja without any priest. That is how it was  practiced  in most homes. Special and temple pujas and yagnas were conducted by priests. The entire country had built a strong religious infrastructure like temples, mutts, pilgrimages, festivals - family, society and temple-,  samskaras, etc. These kept religious practices vibrant and alive.

People build habits and beliefs  based on what is happening around them .  Their activities also depended on the local conditions . The family and societal environments and the  kings nurtured their habits and beliefs ,and  strengthened them. 
Kings supported both gurukulas and also religious activities. Most kings obeyed many religious and spiritual activities. We have enough evidences of vijayanagar empire, Maratha empire, cholas , pallavas and pandyas  playing a major role in promoting religion and scholarships.Even in our recent memory ,we saw Raja Sarfoji patronising spirituality , he set up the Saraswati mahal library and had hundreds of scholars working there. Similarly, the Mysore Wodeyar maharajas patronised such traditional scholarship . Jayachamarajendra wodeyar was a great scholar and supported the oriental library . He collected many scholars in Mysore. Kautilya's  arthashastra was discovered in Mysore by Shama sastry . The Travancore Maharajas also supported music, arts , and scholarships over centuries. 
The west never had this kind of support system. It had a strong religious hierarchy of churches with central authority. But it lacked a popular and decentralised spiritual infrastructure and scholarship that was existing in India. It was the extensive network of gurukula systems- close to 70000 according to one count-, prevailing across the thousands of towns and villages and the availability of major centres of scholarship that helped spirituality to live and thrive in India. Spiritual discussions and propagation of knowledge was a major profession of scholars in those days. 
Because of the king's support and the flourishing gurukula system, people followed many values of a spiritual life in addition to leading a normal material life, thereby keeping a bonding and balance between the two and using the values in their worldly actions. 

But how did that holistic system disappear? 
Unfortunately, the British destroyed that kind of life by destroying the root - the gurukulas and introducing a western totally materialistic education system. They banished Hindu religion and spiritual studies from our education System. That is followed even now. Both western scholars and Indian scholars collaborated to banish spiritual learning from schools and universities. Our historians also helped in this. They couldn't understand the difference between religion and spirituality. All these successfully created a set of  strong views that
“Religion is bad,
 religion propagates superstitions, 
religion is unscientific, 
religion is personal, 
Religion has no place in our education system.
 Banish religion and spirituality.”
 That was the death knell for spiritual living. So spirituality started dying in the 20th century. People  looked down on religion and spirituality. They stopped reading upanishads , stopped following a value system . People aped the west and became materialistic and abandoned religion. A new religion was established by Nehru and strengthened by Indira Gandhi in the constitution - secularism. It gave everyone a licence to attack our religious beliefs and symbols. 
Unfortunately while Gandhi talked about spiritual education, Nehru was opposed to it. He didn't like religion. So he effectively destroyed it by creating a public ridicule of dharma . We are seeing the cumulative  effects of Macauley and Nehru now.
Our education system is partial with a strong affinity to science and a distorted history . We also believed in communism strongly. 
Fortunately  these Nehruvian  concepts were not understood by most people. So our religious activities continued. But our scholarship activities on spirituality died. 
More in the next Post.

07/05/25, 3:53 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 706
“As to methods, there may be a million and then some, but principles are few. The man who grasps principles can successfully select his own methods. The man who tries methods, ignoring principles, is sure to have trouble.”
—RALPH WALDO EMERSON

We need to understand why religions came up in all major civilizations?
This is important to understand the role religion played in our life. True dogmas destroyed the purity of religions. It is not the mistake of religion , but the followers' dogmatism and fearful beliefs. 
Religion came with civilization. Philosophy came next. Philosophy concentrated on the unknowns and principles , not methods.  Science evolved later. It concentrated on the knowns and methods.  Curious minds played an important part in both. 
While science looked at matter and known phenomena - that what are seen and observable- and could not explain many natural cosmic phenomena - like what is not seen, observed or measured-  like rotation of planets around the sun , religions came to fill in that void with their hypothesis about mysterious aspects like space, nature, cosmos, planets , mind ,  consciousness etc. From religions , philosophy emerged. Religions have  two segments - daily religious and ethical practices and the spiritual scholarship in pursuit of knowledge of the unknown. 
Science in the west didn't look at space till the 15th century. The mathematics of the west with Roman numerals was primitive. But India was far ahead. It could measure the movements of planets and sun's positions , predict eclipses and perform detailed calculations in the BCE era itself. That was part of our understanding of the  unknowns  and all this knowledge was  considered part of spirituality. 
Our greatest theory ,which is getting attention today not only in India but in other parts also and by other practitioners also , is the concept of Brahman- an ununderstandable , permanent,  all pervasive, without qualities , highly intelligent, highly aware , phenomenon - satchitananda. That is fundamental to hinduism 
Our spirituality emphasized on jnana. It felt strongly that 
Purpose of life is to achieve liberation through a search for the eternal. 
It is possible through proper training of our mind and through good ethical practices to purify ourselves.
Scholarship is an essential requirement for living and doing actions. 
Jnana increases our awareness, cognition and our consciousness.
Learning is through concentration, contemplation,  self introspection and self realization. A free and open mind is needed for this. 
 Jnana was the second step of spirituality , the first step being the selfless,  ethical , orderly and frugal living .


Let me look at a higher definition of spirituality. 
Brahman, the creative intelligence flows through the universe which holds the key to living to your potential. You can feel it pulsating in every part of your being. Like the Sun, it constantly emits energy, and you can light up yourself and the world around you when you are plugged in and switched on. You are charged with spiritual energy which needs only to be released for you to create the kind of life you want to lead. 
‘Spiritual’ means ‘non-physical’. Our ideas, intelligence, imagination, sense of humour, kindness, creativity and so on – all the qualities that make us who we are – are non-physical. We seek happiness, love, friendship and peace, and all of these are non-physical too. 
Our spirituality creates our world, because our lives are simply a reflection of whatever we hold in our minds. ‘Spirituality’ also relates to the meaning of life in all its splendour. Have you ever wondered who you are, why you’re here and where it leads? The only thing we know for sure is that we were born and one day we’re going to die. But do our lives matter? How do we fit into the overall scheme of things? Many philosophers have offered their views down the ages, each shedding a little light on the subject. 
Practical spirituality is about self-knowledge, self-empowerment and searching for peace and contentment in a seemingly imperfect world.
We should  distinguish between two crucial terms: religion and spirituality. The word religion has many meanings; in particular it implies a concern with the sacred and supreme values of life. The term spirituality, on the other hand, refers to direct experience of the sacred. Spiritual practices are those that help us experience the sacred—that which is most central and essential to our lives—for ourselves. That is the highest conscious level of spirituality. 
More in the next.
