08/05/25, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  707 
Why do we need spirituality?
This is important. This is the point Mr Sambamurthy raised. Most will have doubts - we are doing fine , but why do we talk about spirituality? We have discarded religion.  Why do we need to bring in spirituality? 
Let us look at the country today . What is the state of living in our country  ?
What governs our life now?
The one thing everyone agrees is the growth of corruption. A person gets a good government job, gets a good salary ,but wants more money. He demands money for doing his job. Greed is catching up with many government servants and officers. Even rich officers became corrupt. 
Corruption led to insincerity, poor work culture.  poor quality etc. Even living by the common people is not peaceful. Anxiety and fear pervade everywhere.
Poor people suffered. This led to large-scale dissatisfaction and unhappiness. 
Society became consumption oriented. Aspirations and expectations went up. Every child should go to IIT,  every youngster should aim for a foreign job. Everyone should own and SUV and a big house and all amenities. 
Even rich people felt unhappy. The middle class was upset and stressed , and couldn't spend time with family. Recall Narayana murthy asking people to work for 70 hours per week. Work - Life balance was lost . 
People felt money is the only important thing in life, getting it by hook or crook is normal. So Ethical values disappeared. 
Value system is different from religious practices. 
The situation wasn't good. It has been happening over several decades and it is becoming worse.
What governs our life is greed, consumption, Pollution, stress , unhappiness and no ethical values. 
What should our life be? A simple materialistic , money making life? The answer is no. 
Life has greater purposes .  
Fortunately, the people in the villages  and many from traditional families had some value systems. Awareness of nature, conservation of resources are increasing. Most children are better informed on the environment  than academics and administrators. Most value friendships . Social interactions - whatsapp- have increased among common people. Community living is becoming better particularly in apartment complexes. 
Greed and money making made people move towards easy religious practices - and a larger number of people visit temples and astrologers today than before. Most temples are crowded. It looks like a picnic , not piety . 
 These made some people think about spirituality .

We come across a few questions in life like-
Most parents worry and say - my son / daughter is not interested in his studies . What should be done to motivate him,?
Some say - my son has no attention in his studies ?
Some say - my son is weak in mathematics.  What should be done?
Many say - I am unhappy with my job or my life or health . They even ask why God is troubling me? They even say - God is unfair. 
Many are uncertain about their future. 
Everyone wants to know the impact of AI on jobs, safety and survival. They are worried. 
Many even ask - why should I live ?- I knew a young mathematician in the seventies. He tried to commit suicide. It was detected in time and he was brought back to life. But the second time , he succeeded. He was less than 30 years of age then . Why did he die?
There are many more questions like these and they run to a psychiatrist for help. It is here spirituality helps us to understand life and tells us how to improve it. 
So the question is -  What should govern our
 life ?
For thousands of years , wise men and women from all traditions have sung the praises of the many benefits that flow into the lives of practitioners as they progress along the spiritual path. Gradually, the heart begins to open, fear and anger melt, greed and jealousy dwindle, happiness and joy grow, love flowers, peace replaces agitation, concern for others blossoms, wisdom matures, and both psychological and physical health improve. Virtually all aspects of our lives are touched and transformed in some way. 
Physical benefits are also dramatic. Spiritual practices can reduce stress, blood pressure, and cholesterol levels. They may help alleviate insomnia, muscle spasms, and diseases ranging from migraine to chronic pain. They may even reduce the effects of aging and lengthen the life span. One Harvard study showed that nursing home patients who were in their eighties when they first began meditation felt happier, functioned better, and lived longer than non meditators

Uncertainty leads to unhappiness.  AI will create uncertainties. So the need for spiritualism increases in the coming AI era. 
Let us see how to develop Spirituality in us in the next post.

09/05/25, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 708


What are the easy steps to spirituality?
Let us look at the first stage - physical and ethical stage .  
The first step is to define a set of values for yourself and a determination to follow them and act according to them.
These steps everyone can accept and follow. I list a few below.
Reduce desires and wants. Balance your aspirations to your capabilities and opportunities. Don't dream of moon in the beginning itself. See people of your kind , see how they live, learn from the satisfied people and also learn what not to do from the unhappy people. Become a realist. Greed minimization is an essential requirement. Remember there sayings in Isa Upanishad - “ everything in the universe belongs to God . Use them sparingly and frugally.” Gandhi rephrased this as “ Nature can meet our needs ,but not our greeds.”  Wants are there in our life . But choices are there - should I buy a 5000 rupees  shirt or 500 rupees shirt ? Should I keep 10 shirts or 100 shirts? How long should I keep a dress - one year , five years, ten years ? There are more important questions that come up - should I go for a local holiday or foreign holiday , a cheaper holiday or an expensive one?  So before an action to buy , avoid impulses, appearances , weigh the pros and cons , check on affordability , consider environmental factors and then decide. This will run contrary to our government's urge to increase consumption. Reduce , reuse, recycle should be our mantra, 
Be compassionate, empathetic and helpful. Remember Kennadys famous statement - ask not what the country can do for me , but ask what i can do for the country - society, neighbours, needy . This is the Rig vedic dictum - give and share. Recall Kanchi mahaswami's one handful of  rice scheme to feed the poor. A simple and affordable scheme.  Many drops become a flood.  This connects you to your society , brings in an attitude of participation and makes you happy. 
Create an environmental awareness and consciousness. This is important for our survival today. We have thrown sustainable living to the wind. Respect nature. Understand that our survival depends on the survival of nature. Nature sustains us. We moved away from reuse and recycle  mantra to use and throw philosophy. We are creating huge wastes due to this use and throw actions  and are also putting a burden on nature. Pollution of air, water, soil are our creations. 
Tolerance. We are short on this - community tolerance, habits tolerance, economic, caste and religious tolerances. We criticise others' appearances, status, performance and actions. We compare ourselves with others . We hate other castes , communities - Tamils call vadakkans now referring to north Indians and call many communities settled for thousands of years as immigrants , a despicable practice. The same Tamils proudly quote the poem “ all are our towns and all are our relatives “. They don't seem to follow what they preach. 
We also have another dictum here - “vasudhaiva kutumbakam”. Divisiveness and intolerance are not to be propagated as a section of  Tamils are doing for several decades.  Let us be human , treat all - humans , animals and plants and nature with dignity . Let us follow the sayings ,” Lokas samastha sukhino bhavanthu “. Tolerance implies “ not sticking to a set of rigid views ,but listening and analyzing other views also. Have an open, flexible mind”. Another factor leading to intolerance is our fixed set of beliefs. 

How do we implement a value system for people?
It needs a top down approach. 
It starts with governments. A big transformation in government priorities and actions are needed . We need a masive program for restructuring government goals. Forget the obsession with growth , GDP and consumption. Look at development and happiness.  Encourage them through awards , departmental rethink and rework,changes in  education  and awareness campaigns . Governments are anti nature. The lip service should go. Will these happen? If government doesn’t get into this, there is no way people will move in this direction. Remember the statement, “yatha raja thatha Praja- people follow rulers. “
Maybe,  RSS should put pressure on the government to spend 50% on ethical and human values development . It is a great investment. What do we get by becoming the fourth richest nation ? We need to have influencers to focus on ethics . Youtube is a good medium to push these ideas. 
If the government doesn't change its visible priorities , then we will get marginal success only. 
The next big thing is the emphasis on values, habits and beliefs in the ten years of schooling. We threw values with religion out and banished them from school education.  
We need to focus at least 70% of time on ethical , human and social education and rest on science and mathematics.  History should be people’s history , not kings and wars. This needs a major change in mindset, not an easy task . 
There are a few hundred schools implementing an ethical values based education system . That is not enough. 
One solution is to use AI and reach all people and children. Massive awareness activities and education helped in sensitising children to environment.  
Industries should implement a strong and implementable ethical policy. Focus should be on welfare and balancing of lifestyles , not on 70 hours work culture.

10/05/25, 5:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 709

The second stage in the development of spirituality is the improvement of mind. This stage concentrates on knowledge and proper application of knowledge in our life. 
It helps us to define the purpose of our life and living. 
What is a normal  purpose of life?
I will say ,” live for others” - service orientation . We have many examples for this saying ,” paropakarartham  idam sariram”. Some examples are - farmers, teachers, house wives, doctors, cooks,  all services people, house construction people, industry workers, counsellors, priests, merchants, soldiers, etc - almost covering a very large percentage of the adult population in the world. 
Let us look at different stages of life/ people according to another classification. 

Stage One people have little or no interest in spirituality. They appear to have few moral principles, live chaotic lives and are frequently found in prisons or on the street. Some, however, rise to positions of power, including some politicians, business leaders, etc.
Stage Two people look to authority and are dependent on an organisation for their governance. This could be the military, a business organisation, public institution or religious body. The majority of traditional religious believers fall into this category. They rely on its teachings and rituals for stability and to deliver them from uncertainty.
Sooner or later some Stage Two people (often the young) question the need for an organisation with rigid structures, rituals and superstitions and begin to move to Stage Three.
Stage Three - agnostics / public service-  These people dislike authority and feel no need to look to an organisation for direction. Some are agnostics or atheists; some are drawn to other philosophies. They are truth seekers and usually ahead of Stage Two in their spirituality without being religious in the usual sense of the word. They are often involved in causes working for peace and justice.
Stage Four  individuals believe in the underlying connectedness between things. They are comfortable with the mystery of life and seek to explore it more deeply. They are inspired by the great religions, but not bound to them. At first sight, Stage Two and Stage Four people appear opposites, yet they have much in common. They know the same passages of scripture but interpret them differently. Stage Three people are baffl ed by Stage Four. On the one hand, they aspire to their awareness and spirituality while being puzzled about their interest in those old myths and legends.
Stage four  may have subgroups.
We divided transformation to stage 4 into three sequences of spiritual upgrades - first is to improve the mental functions, second is to improve the knowledge capabilities and third is to increase consciousness levels. 


We saw most of us are doing a service. To perform this important task of service, we need knowledge and so we need to sharpen our mind.
What is mind? 
Mind was not accepted by the west in the beginning. Even now ,many think the mind is a part of the brain. It is not. Upanishads concentrate mostly on the mind, thinking, questioning, discovering ,  consciousness and ways of sharpening our mind. Understanding the mind and using it is the main task of our life. 

The word "mind" refers to the faculty of an individual that allows them to think, feel, perceive, understand, apply, decide and act. It encompasses all mental processes, including both conscious and unconscious activities. In essence, the mind is the seat of thought, emotion, and consciousness. 
Here's a more detailed look at the concept:
Key aspects of "mind":
Thought and Reasoning: The mind is responsible for thinking, reasoning, and processing information. 
Emotion and Feeling: It's also the source of emotions, feelings, and sensations. This leads to our well being. 
Consciousness: The mind allows us to be aware of ourselves and our surroundings. 
Will and Intent: The mind enables us to make decisions, have intentions, and pursue goals. 
Memory and Recollection: The mind stores and retrieves memories, allowing us to remember past experiences. 
Imagination and Creativity: The mind is the source of imagination, creativity, and the ability to form new ideas. 
Private and Personal: The mind is often considered a private and personal realm, where thoughts and feelings are unique to each individual. But according to Upanishads,  mind is connected to personal  consciousness , and to universal consciousness. 

Next, let us ask how one develops mental Spiritual behaviours in today's  context.
Spiritualism is the governance principle for the mind.
Self awareness: Understand yourself - strengths, weaknesses, desirables,  achievables, purposes and goals, directions, beliefs, behaviours, habits, health issues, etc. Realise that your mind controls your thoughts , emotions and actions. Mental reactions are more effective than chemical reactions due to medicines. 
Start building viveka - viveka plays an important role in discrimination 
Get rid of unhealthy mind- negative thoughts, destructive thoughts, hate, ,jealousy, one up- manship , comparisons, greed , 
Build a peaceful calm mind - this is tough. Be sincere, truthful, Don't get stressed about final outcomes , successes , define your comfort level , reduce consumption. be happy with what you have, hope for the best , 
Build a free, deep thinking  and open mind 
Develop curiosity- contemplate, create both in- out and out - in views, birds eye and worms views , analyse .
Build a positive and  Benevolent mindset - helping , learning, improving 
Build a good society around you. 
Improve consciousness
Aim towards self realizations.

My third  level aims for a higher level as envisaged in our vedanta and upanishads- building and increasing the  levels of consciousness - understanding brahman .

The current time has many challenges for people due to uncertainty , complexity, loss of jobs and fast technological changes. 
Society is changing everywhere now. But societies didn't know the proper directions. People are thinking of constant innovation, agility , adaptation to AI etc. Spiritual ways of improving mind and thinking and viveka and jnana will help in this turbulent time.

How do we build a spiritual society ?
Mr Sambamurthy asked about getting a spiritual infrastructure to be built in India. We saw what , why of spirituality. We need to see the how . 

Remember how yoga was brought into our thinking through several programs. 
It is not a mechanical streamlined process. 
It needs to evolve. It took a lot of effort. 
There were many private efforts to popularise Yoga . Mysore turned out thousands of yoga teachers. Vivekananda Kendra also produced thousands of yoga experts. It was swami sivananda who started the teaching and popularisation of yoga. Most religious units like sadhguru,  Sri Sri ,  and many others popularised yoga. There are thousands of such units of yoga across the country. These were functioning for more than 50 years. What are the results? 
Many foreigners came , got trained and started training centres in the west. But there was no major impact on India.  
When Modi decided to popularise Yoga, it reached a high level. People followed him and took to yoga. Yoga became known internationally. Its benefits are now known to many. It is now part of our school curriculum. 
So the authorities should show interest in spirituality in order to build a spiritual society. 
Government should spend 50% of its time on spiritual development instead of putting all its eggs in one basket of GDP growth and spending billions of rupees in business subsidies. Government should have a multipronged strategy , plan and implementation for the development of spirituality. There is an Ayush ministry. It should become a ministry of spiritual development.  The Ministry of education should focus on spirituality.
Government should set up hundreds of institutions specializing in spirituality , its applications in health and human resources . 
The primary  approaches of governments are of two types 
Revenue generation - taxes 
Law and order

Law presupposes people are criminals . So instead of punishment based system - some of it is needed to curb major crimes-, create an incentive system for values. We occasionally hear stories about the honesty of a poor person like a house maid or auto driver. Our response is to appreciate the act and then forget about it. Why are we not celebrating them ,rewarding the honest people, rewarding people who help during disasters. We think that the disaster relief team and fire fighting force should face hazardous situations and do their duty. Why ? We want to be safe and put them in danger . We don't appreciate the poura karmikas . They do a tough job daily. I call these as - honesty is punished. So create a value based system, create a monetary reward or salary based on the level of values and not skills. Will we be able to do this? 
Mindset change is the starting point. We think that money can buy anything. We don't thank people who repaired many things in our house on time. 
Also we need a major exercise on content development integrating upanishad concepts , Bhagavad Gita principles, Nitisatakam and hitopadesha stories. This should be in a simple language. We need to produce books focusing on values, ethics, life practices,  mind development , cognition , knowledge, etc. 

Next change is in industries going in for ethical focus. Ignore profit fixation. If you are doing fine, that should be enough. Don't look for increased profits. That is the seed for greed. So it should be stopped. Will they do it? Why do industries think they exist for shareholders , not for the people? 
As a country we think of two approaches - socialist and capitalist. Forget them. Money is needed upto a point. Go for ethics and consciousness awareness. Create a new model. 
That means a lot of rework for economists to build a new balanced model of values based economics away from today's monetary economics. 
Next ,we need a huge mindset changes in education.  I have written about it. 
Everyone should understand that 50% of your life is reserved for others .

We discussed our  minds . Mr Srinivasan aaked me what is the word for mind in sanskrit. We commonly use the term manas. But there are many other terms like chitta, ahamkara, buddhi etc.  I want to discuss one term in relation to mind - consciousness. 
What are the terms for consciousness in sanskrit?

In Sanskrit, there are many terms for  Consciousness  —
1.Chetna चेतना
2 Bodha बोध:
3 Sacheta सचेत:
4 Abhigyata अभिज्ञता
5 Jagriti जागृति:
6 Sachetan सचेतन:
7 Chaitanya चैतन्य:
All  mean awareness. But we are not aware of many hard conscious or shall I say super conscious states in our mind. The term is absolute consciousness, not relative , the word being chit in sanskrit.  If you look at an entity or action or an event or a situation , consciousness means we know about that. We have awareness. But what is awareness wrt an unknown all pervasive brahman ? You can't even imagine anything. So no understanding. It is a realization . So the term conscious doesn’t correctly reflect the Turia state - the highest level. We need a better English term than consciousness to translate the absolute, all pervading , unexplainable state. 
Sometimes we feel the unknown consciousness and its effects. You were struggling with a problem and the solution was  not there in the near horizon. Any amount of breaking your brain was not giving a solution. Tired, you go to sleep. In deep dreamless sleep you get a solution. This has happened to me several times. I am always puzzled about this occurrence.  So you have a super mind working inside ,but you don't know what it is, where it is and how it works. That relates to a higher level - chit level. We get solutions from somewhere - is it inside you or comes from outside? This needs a lot of clarity. 
I think consciousness implies low levels of activities and ignores higher level activities like intuition, spark. 
Any comments on this?
Let me look at the second query by Mr Sambamurthy:
“Technological evolution is outpacing or has  already outpaced emotional and spiritual evolution. What are the negative consequences and how to mitigate them?
Appreciate your comments.
Thank you DKS.”

My response
The world concentrated more than four centuries for technology developments and absorption. Millions of engineers worked on this. Billions of dollars were spent on this. All governments supported , encouraged , funded and adapted new technologies enthusiastically. There is a lot of organised effort, a lot of serious serious research, a lot of synergy and a lot of competition on technology based development.  
Technology diffusions were planned and executed systematically. We see the concerted efforts of millions of engineers, financiers, economists, developers , and politicians. 
People saw many benefits due to technologies like steam engine, electricity, electronics communications, computers, Internet,  IT, connectivity, mobile to AI.
When a technology like nuclear bomb was seen as a disastrous one, governments are controlling its use. We are going slow with caution in genetic engineering. 
There were a few isolated Luddites who tried to halt technological progress . But they didn't succeed. We see many attacking AI today. These are not simple Luddites , but serious researchers. But human ingenuity will find a feasible path for the AI dilemma. 
So all these factors helped technological evolutions faster. 21st century saw an exponential leap in technological developments with great successes. 
I should say we have mastered the art of materialism well and exploit them. 
The causality in this is the human values - empathy, concern,compassion, support, gratitude, humility ,collaboration, altruism etc. We have buried them. Surprisingly , we expect many will write about the problems . In the mid 20th century , many Irish writers wrote about the inhuman conditions in the textiles business. 
We don't see many economists, sociologists, writers and the so called intellectuals worried about the erosion of human values. The leftist minds are materialistic. I don't see a spiritual and sane human identified by the coterie calling themselves intellectuals particularly in India. We decry spiritual people, abuse them, and attribute motives to them. We think spirituality is a huge virus to be eliminated. The effect of this anti spiritual views and fast paced beneficial technology developments have resulted in today's situation of creating a highly mechanical and mechanized world.
I don't see a concerted effort to analyse all aspects - positive and negative - of this disease. I call this disease as humachinisation, a new word coined by me- humans behaving like machines. 
We ignored spirituality . Will this situation of machine orientation give us stability, peace and happiness? Those human qualities are going beyond the reach of many. 
Let us reverse with full force to give primacy to spirituality.
This completes my discussions on this topic . Thanks Mr Sambamurthy.

“To say that individuals transcend themselves means that individuals cannot be identified with their position in reality and in the society. 
Transcending oneself means openness to action, to project, to modification of one’s own conditions: it means doing plans for the future and hope in the future. It means that individuals are born free as regards their dimension in the world: they are not identifiable with the conditions they live in. “
Paulo Freire 
Let us return to my learning paths. I was discussing the year  1959 when I joined the college and moved to English medium education. Instead of saying i got adjusted to my college education, I want to say I got integrated into education. That was a great awakening. I had a large number of classmates and teachers who were Christians.  I understood their culture. I started reading a lot of English books during this period and it continues even now. My love for reading and absorbing information is quite high. I enjoy this task. I learnt the art of  abstraction of what I learnt into a few sentences or a page . This practice started at this time due to a strict tuesday afternoon composition activity supervised by the vice principal in the big college auditorium. We were given a story or an article and told to condense it to a paragraph. I loved this task and did it fast. 
I have already discussed my engineering college stay for five years at Madurai. It helped me to get a deeper exposure  in engineering.  I understood specialisation. But very importantly , I was a generalist at heart interested in many areas like literature, politics , economics , spirituality etc. Those interests  continued throughout my life . My mathematics got strengthened. Another revelation was that I had no fear of unknowns. By the time ,I reached the final year of UG engineering, I got interested in electronics, control systems and computers. 
I applied to IISc for ME in applied electronics and control systems and got admissions. But I didn't join that year. I took up a job in Madras. I got disillusioned with industry ambience and decided to apply for IISc again , selected for ME power systems - i applied to power systems this time based on advices from my seniors , including my boss S Balasubramanian . Later i realised it's advantages and disadvantages and rectified them. 
So I landed in Bangalore on July 28th 1965 and I was the first to get admitted on that day - incidentally for that year- my SR number is the first. I got advantages due to that. I finished my admissions procedure in the first floor of the main building by 10 am. I met Mr R Vaidyanathan, the assistant registrar and Mr SS Prabhu , the registrar. Immediately afterwards, I went to the hostel and got a room allotted in H block. I was lucky  because getting rooms in the hostel was not easy. I met Mr Kuppanna, superintendent,  who became a great friend of mine. I deposited my luggage in the room, and went to the EE department office to complete my admission formalities. I met Mr Seshachala, a great person , another friend and then met the head, Prof Ramachandra Rao. 
Classes started only on August 2, as first was a sunday. So I had three days to get acclimatized to the institute and city. I went to Malleswaram circle ,nearly 2 km distant from IISc and opened an account in canara bank there. 
The whole thing looked like a dream for me at that time. 
Let me give my first impressions of bangalore at that time and later my impressions of IISc.

“The charming landscape which I saw this morning, is indubitably made up of some twenty or thirty farms. Miller owns this field, Locke that, and Manning the woodland beyond. But none of them owns the landscape. There is a property in the horizon which no man has but he whose eye can integrate all the parts, that is, the poet. “
Whole is bigger than the sum of the parts. 
R Waldo Emerson.     Nature

My joining IISc was considered by me as a very wise move. I never realised then that it will transform me and keep me chained to it. That was a very lucky thing to happen for me. 
Bangalore looked very different for me in many ways. It was a totally new experience for me. 
I had by then visited several metro cities like Calcutta, Delhi, and lived in Madras. I lived for two weeks in Calcutta,  two weeks in Delhi and one year in Madras.  So I knew what was to be expected  from a big city . The first thing you expect is a great railway station . 
My disappointment with Bangalore started with the railway station. It was smaller than some small cities I had visited or stayed  like Madurai, Jamshedpur , Coimbatore etc. Some others like Kharagpur , Vijayawada were bigger than the  Bangalore station at that time. There were only about six platforms , no frontage building as it exists now.  Entrances were not noticeable,  small wicket gates. You went out of the station through a small gate in the fence - just like a village. That was my first shock about bangalore. The present front building came up only in the seventies. But Bangalore had all three gauges- broad gauge to Madras , meter gauge to Mysore , Hubli (Poona), and Guntakal and narrow gauge to near Nandi hills. So the focus was the meter gauge where many trains were going in different directions. The broad gauge was connected to Madras via Jolarpet. Two trains to Madras and one to Cochin were the only trains on that route. We can't go to Bombay , Calcutta or Delhi directly . We need to change at either Pune or Guntakal or Madras. Reaching Mangalore was only by a long, roundabout route via solar pet, Salem, shoranur  taking a day. All the improvements and direct  connections happened only in the post eighties period. Bangalore was not a priority for the southern railway. 
This meant a lot of inconveniences for me. I needed to take three trains to reach home , and two trains for other big cities. Ticket booking and reservations was a nightmare then. 
But once I came out of the station , the situation and my impressions changed completely. The weather was pleasant and cool. People were polite. People understood many languages. I asked an auto to take me to IISc and he asked , ‘is it Tata institute?’
I didn't get it ,but guessed that the locals called it Tata institute to show their gratitude to JN Tata . They pronounced Tata as Thatha- grandfather, appropriate as JN was the man who built a science capital here. 
The auto went through almost straight north. There was very little traffic . When we entered Malleswaram,  I saw broad roads, a huge Malleswaram circle , and two huge circles at IISc.  I saw lots of trees everywhere. The driver after taking a turn at IISc main circle entered into a road going inside . There was no gate, no IISc signboard then. I asked the driver and he affirmed that it is the Tata institute.  After some distance, I saw the tower of the main building. I got down and went to the main building for completing admissions. Most people - office staff and even class 4 employees were very polite and helpful. They understood Tamil, Telugu , Hindi , in addition to Kannada. Later I found we had a mixed population of employees from Tamilnadu,  coastal Andhra, kerala and karnataka. So getting along and knowing places were easy. As I didn't know Kannada then, I managed with Tamil and English and Hindi also outside in the city. 
Let me return to the description of  Bangalore city scenario later and concentrate on IISc in 1965 ,particularly my first few days. It was a great place. It looks big , almost formidable, but it was a very friendly place with a great ambience for learning. I used to tell my friends and acquaintances that IISc is designed for walking - a lot of open spaces, hundreds of trees , spaced out buildings and nice weather congenial for walks. I found out we get most things inside - there was a cooperative stores inside providing groceries , stationary items and toiletry. 
Let me discuss my first day in the next post.

“ we have a spiritual intelligence that is our potential guide to the real meaning of the human experience, why we are here, and what we are here to do. My first day was very smooth. As I said earlier , I got my admissions done by 9: 30 am, joined the hostel , met the HoD of the EE department- all done by about 11 o'clock. I also signed my scholarship application and handed it over to the office after I got my bank account. I visited the library and applied for library cards. 
The entire admissions process was an offline system . So how did all these separate units like the department office, hostel, library etc knew me as a new student? It was a simple trick. At the time of admissions ,all activities like checking admissions letter, my certificates, paying the fees , meeting the assistant registrar, deans etc,pertaining to admissions were done in the same place . So after paying the fees, I was given six copies of the receipt . Registrar signed them when I met him after completing the admissions procedure.  I should submit it in the hostel, department, library, health centre and gymkhana as proof of my studentship. I got no id card then , only my SR number   . So I finished these formalities. It was a clockwork completed by morning. I didn't go to the health centre and gymkhana by then. 
Let me look at the hostel situation then , not a good one. We had four old blocks - A B C D - with 20 rooms each and four new blocks - E F G H- . Most  rooms were double rooms and a few had three occupants.  I was given H4 . H block was the northern end at that time . It was a large open area after H block . 
I went to the A mess by 12: 30 pm for lunch. It was at the other end of the hostel block near the start of the F block.  We had only two messes then - A mess and NV mess. The IISc mess was very famous. It had mostly Malayali cooks and assistants . The supervisors were kannada  persons. Food was very good, very sumptuous and wholesome. It had both chapati and rice . It was hot. The mess had a seating arrangement of about 200 persons, I think. Again , it worked at a high speed. The organization in IISc impressed me. 
Later I realised there were flexibilities in the mess system. I can take guests for a meal . I can write off from the mess for a minimum of two days. The kitchen used firewood then. Later, I changed all these and modernized the kitchen . 
The mess gave us a good breakfast with variety. The one aspect that remained throughout is the Sunday breakfast - Masala Dosa, it never changed and the dosas were hot and good. Recall the effort required to serve hundreds of people fast within minutes . It is not easy , but it was happening in our mess. 
We got one  fruit every night. Saturday and Sunday nights were self service . Sunday lunch was special. We got ice cream. There was one ice cream factory in Whitefield and it will supply ice cream to us. 
In the evening I went to gymkhana . It was located in G block. It had a reading room with newspapers and magazines . It had a library named after Ranade. It had a lot of books in English, Hindi, kannada,  Tamil and Telugu - mostly fiction. So i started to read a fiction book every day . My reading intensity increased. 
I was thrilled with the many facilities in the campus and I had yet to see many other facilities . I was happy with IISc from day one. 
A few learnings on the first day.
1.people come from many parts of India. One hears many languages.
2.Most are multilingual. Most are helpful. 
3.organization is excellent and service oriented. I didn't see this level of support even in private institutions.  
4.System is built with flexibility and
5. The whole campus and ambience were great for learning . This without visiting the library and labs and without meeting any faculty member. 
So it attracted me and I was happy to be here at IISc.

“Spiritual intelligence is not just about “what” we learn or “how” we behave, it is about “why” we do what we do. Unless we are developing our level of self, we are missing the most precious resource of all—our engagement in living. Happiness, success, creativity, spontaneity, natural confidence, leadership—these are not things we can learn, not are they techniques or quick fixes that can be adopted. They are fundamental ways of engaging in a field of intelligence that is unlimited—spiritual intelligence.”
Bowell

Let me continue with my first few days experiences in IISc and also Bangalore. I visited the library . It was really an amazing experience seeing the large number of journals and back volumes and the great stack of books. The well lit ,study friendly ambience drew my attention. Library assistants were humble and very helpful persons. There was one person - it is a shame I forgot his name- who never used to sit , and went on arranging books and getting books any reader wanted. His energy and memory were amazing. He was really an unsung hero of the library in the sixties. 
I liked and used the textbook reservation and use facility that was in practice. All text books identified by instructors were kept separately with a reservation card for each book. One can reserve a book for one hour in advance , pick it up and read it during that hour. This helped the students a lot - books were available to all, not monopolised by a few . It was a great service. Library was open till 10pm. Many faculty from central college used to come in the evening and read books and journals. 
We had a big activity on Friday evenings. All recently arrived journals were displayed every Friday. Researchers and faculty used to wait and browse the journals for papers of their interest. That was the only way to know what was happening in other institutions in the world and keeping oneself up to date. 
Later when I was in the second year , I found out there is another effective networking approach to know what is happening . We used to write to researchers and request for their publications. Many helped us. While many used preprinted cards, I used airmail inland letters. I used this to keep in touch with hundreds of researchers in the west. 
So it became evident that the library was the main source of research in IISc. 
Let me give a description of IISc in the sixties. It was a natural working  place in a forest - like an Ashram mentioned in our early literature ,the only missing beings were the wild animals and water bodies. 
As you enter we see two long parallel roads ,one running to the other end . These roads and side roads were lined with a large number of trees. Most buildings on the main road had departments ,and  bungalows , built with a large offset and good garden space. These roads gave a great atmosphere for the institute- a silent, cool and calm atmosphere. We had open spaces everywhere. 
The main building was built after a huge vacant area from the main entrance - about 500 meters and behind the main building - or actually in front of it, because the back of main building faces the road - there is a huge maidan with some huge 100 year old trees patterned like the union jack flag of England . We can walk through the maidan through the straight path and some diagonal paths . 
Departments were located on both sides of the main building.  These are mostly  science departments. Engineering departments were beyond the science departments. My department was close to the end - boundary of the institute. Both electrical and mechanical engineering departments were located in a huge building known as Power engineering building - later I used to call the PE building Thacker's Taj mahal , so you know the size of this big building . That was the biggest building in the institute built by MS Thacker when he was the head of a short lived power engineering department.  He later became director of the institute.  The power engineering department was divided into four  separate departments - civil, mechanical , electrical and high voltage. Walking from the hostel to my department took about 6 to 8 minutes. 
At the other end of the main road after the residential bungalows is the ECE department. 
The institute had a large area of more than 560 acres donated by the maharaja of Mysore,  Nalvadi Krishnaraja wodeyar. Several public roads cut across the institute by creating four separate segments. Some parts of our land had been usurped illegally by people putting up houses and by Ramiah institute. Institute lost the case against Ramiah institute, I was told.

17/05/25, 4:16 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 716 
“Reality is a rich tapestry of interwoven levels, reaching from matter to body to mind to soul to spirit.”
 Ken Wilber
While buildings are important, I learnt one assessment metric - ambience, the heart of an institution. 
What is ambience? 
It is a combination of the type of people in a place, their motivation, behaviour , work culture, their focus, and the fearless, transparent, harmonious and helpful atmosphere they develop . It also includes governance models, relevance , a futuristic outlook , an outward look and understanding and adjustments to change. The infrastructure, labs etc are secondary.
Unfortunately , this aspect is not understood by most and ignored by all. 
 Most institutions in India are ranked by mostly external factors or outputs like admissions, pass percentage, publications, infrastructure like the number of computers. The assessors  can't measure the intrinsic and intangible but important aspects of ambience. 
What they see is what they assess. What they can't see, they ignore. So They ignore them. I discussed this aspect with my good friend Arvind from TCS who is passionate about quality and was involved in Tata quality assessments. 
Till now, when several hundreds of people asked me and ask me ,’what is special about IISc’, i respond with one cryptic answer  - ambience - excellent , freedom to work, independence etc. 
I have till this point in time observed four institutions - my schools and colleges. The best part of my feelings about these four institutions can be summarised as follows.
These institutions have traditional, limited and localised thinking , doing a routine job in a sincere manner .
The ambience is not negative - it varies from neutral to slightly positive. 
There is a lot of scope to improve their governance - increase the transparency, freedom, participation, futuristic Outlook, looking out to know how the world is changing its functions, and academic commitment.

That is the reason why these institutions didn't grow out of their local , traditional model to become known in the country. They don't have a change management practice . 
Most institutions in India have a poor ambience and live in the 20th century. I want to know 
When will they move to the 21st century models and practices ?
When will they break unwanted traditional practices ?
When will they build a dynamic change management structure?
When will they improve governance?

It is the ambience of IISc that made me love the institution from day one. It puts a newcomer at ease - that is a great achievement in the colonial minded , non helpful India.  
To be honest, if I look back at several hundred institutions , banks, industries and organizations, the sore thumb is 
the unhelpful and sometimes hostile and sometimes corrupt attitude found in all these organizations.
Can we go to a branch of a bank and transact business easily. You are always treated as a newcomer , not a long time customer. Service depends on your encounter with the person. I had a lot of disappointments. 
But there are many good and sincere people helping you out. Because of them , the organization runs. Mr RP Krishnamurthy, undersecretary to the chief minister ,was a classic and shining example . He was simple, sincere, hardworking and helpful. I can give many such examples. But generally , unhelpful attitude prevails. 
Let me illustrate two great services I encountered at IISc on the first day - admissions process, hostel room allotment. 
Every year the institute admits about one third of its students strength and this is conducted for just three days. So a well oiled process and operational system has been developed and tested over decades and it is still running.  The students go through about ten persons each one doing one task - a human parallel processing system . At the end he gets the admissions letter which will be signed by the assistant registrar.  Then the students meet the deans. That is it. There is a limited wait time and it goes on fast. This system continued for more than 50 years .
The hostel allocation is more complicated. Students have choices staying with a roommate from his state or with friends. Many students don't vacate the rooms after completing the course. Some might not have completed their project work by July. There were not enough rooms to accommodate the students. I had seen the hostel staff working hard to allot rooms. It wasn't easy.
But all worked hard and were excited about their job. Many felt they were doing God ‘s job. That is the commitment of our people. 
But later I realised our admin didn't live up to this level. They put impediments in our work. 
Greetings
Dks
18/05/25, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 717


I want to clarify my understanding of ambience to avoid confusion. I consider ambience is an important concept to be understood. 
It is not just  infrastructure. It is a human ecosystem. It is a dynamic , adaptable and accepted set of human behaviours - like transparency, freedom, independence, helpful attitude, understanding changes  and management, futuristic and outward outlooks , sincerity, seriousness etc. It is a human dependent phenomenon . There is no concept of a physical ambience ,only infrastructure.  But a pristine , calm and soothing nature  ambience helps further in enhancing the mental ambience. The pristine, calm and cool nature at IISc attracts everyone. Then  I found the academic ambience at IISc to be great .  This is a major lesson I learnt. It is not a one day revelation . It is not a miracle.
This understanding of mental ambience and its relationships to success governed my life from early days. This may look like a surprise or even unacceptable statement from me. So let me explain in detail , while deviating from my description of IISc life in the mid sixties. 

We always assume that success depends on hard work, involvement, concentration and collaboration. Definitely yes. I added ambience. But we miss other ingredients . I added some more to that list , the most important one being contemplation , thinking about what happened now, why , what are its impacts. I did this contemplation at the occurrence of every new or unforeseen event that happened to me everyday in my life and made me either happy or sad. I learnt a lot from this activity. I applied it to even the lectures I heard from many leaders or even classroom lectures. This detailed contemplation allowed a few habits to be built - it was self feedback and analysis , it made me abstract facts and remember them for a  long time , it motivated me to learn from everything , it made me look ahead and removed the fear of new changes.
 I have seen many people with such contemplation like Prof. Dhawan, Mr JRD Tata,  Mr Jayakumar Anagol, Mr N. Chandrasekaran, Prof CNR Rao, Prof NS Ramaswamy , and many more. They had clarity of mind, quick grasping power ,an  open and questioning  mind  and futuristic outlook. They observed and understood and acted,  all quickly. 
But to me the best example was Homi Bhabha. He was not just a visionary who could foresee the future of nuclear science for the next hundred years plus, and not only a detailed planner preparing a complete blueprint of the department of atomic energy with the great innovative step of setting up an independent,  non bureaucratic institution in Bombay away from Delhi and most importantly executing meticulously his plan. He understood the gaps in India and decided to set up a school in BARC to produce nuclear scientists, set up a very high quality research establishment ,TIFR and conducting intense yearly summer schools at IISc with great nuclear science , mathematics and computer science experts in the mid sixties, when some fields were just emerging as seeds. We should emulate his vision and work culture. I met him in December 1965  and he died in January 66.  Vikram Sarabhai whom I had met many times in TIFR west canteen emulated many characteristics of Bhabha to build the space activity. But he didn't concentrate and focus on building a holistic vision and plan for Space. It was left to Dhawan , who followed Bhabha’s concepts in a practical way. 
There are many- thousands of people-  who held high positions as heads of a country, directors of institutions who failed to understand their organization and outside world and failed miserably. 
I knew and met  hundreds of directors of IISc, IIMs, IITs, NITs, CSIR labs,DRDO labs and Vice chancellors.  Most don't know what their job is - they do day to day routine administration,  no vision, no direction, no innovative thinking, no futuristic thinking and planning. They think of their position, status, ego  and respect . They are sincere ,no doubt and hardworking.  But the institutions languish. Very few - a handful - are futuristic and plan their institution for the next ten years. Senior persons - scientists ,educationists, academics etc- don't support them. Even Dhawan faced a strong opposition. He could carry forward reforms with some difficulty but with support from middle level faculty.

“Human relationships with the world are plural in nature. Whether facing widely different challenges of the environment or the same 
challenge, men are not limited to a single reaction pattern. They organize 
themselves, choose the best response, test themselves, act, and change in 
the very act of responding. They do all this consciously, as one uses a tool to deal with a problem. 
Men relate to their world in a critical way. They apprehend the objective data of their reality (as well as the ties that link one datum to another) through reflection-not by reflex, as do animals. And in the act of critical 
perception, men discover their own temporality. Transcending a single 
dimension, they reach back to yesterday, recognize today, and come upon 
tomorrow.”
Paulo Freire 

We used to be told when we were young and at school going age that we should play with good children and not join evil ones, we shouldn't even talk to the evil ones . That shows that a good ambience is a networking of good people and a good vibe will flow . We get benefited with that association. That helps in developing goodness in us and we ,when we understand and appreciate these, become spiritual and achieve many things in life. 
This kind of learning is free and is not taught in schools. This is the reason that religions bring in congregations together in churches, mosques and temples. That helps in good vibes flowing . But be cautious that hatred and bad vibes shouldn't flow from there. Be careful to choose your networks. 
What is required to know and take advantage of a human society and its ambience? 
Awareness. We need keen powers of observation of humans, their habits, attitudes ,behaviours and their effects. The society creates a huge drama every day in many places and youtube and TV depict them giving us an opportunity to observe and learn. While I admired determined persons and understood that their efforts will deliver good results, I didn't fully accept  the dominant personalities , starting with Churchill to Nehru, to Indira Gandhi to many current leaders .  I felt that dominance implies a fixed mindset and dictatorship. It instills fear.
Combine it with contemplation.  You get better results 
The third factor is a forward looking capacity that can be built through imagination, heuristics and also the current happenings. Here inanimate activities are merged with human behaviours.  
I  observed the political, economic, environmental and technological changes happening in and around me first and in the world later. This meant that we should be prepared for changes and not resist changes. That meant that I should look for changes ,say, ten years ahead. It is not easy. We go wrong on many guesses. But I understood the importance of some changes . I saw the potentials of computers in 1963 and saw the arrival of operating systems before it was known. Similarly, I predicted relational databases, distributed transactions and many others.  
The fourth factor  I learnt was the concept of connectivity - technology alone is not important . Its connection to usage to help society is more important. So my focus on research was more application centric - like design of large power networks with digital load flow applications , optimization of power production with applications in the country, distributed transactions for banking etc. Connectivity is the basis to understand most happenings in our life - not individual entities or acts . Its the cumulative impact of a person's conversations with others or his acts that have after effects and we need to predict that . Connectivity is not taught in schools. Engineering discusses some form of Connectivity, but in a limited way. But medicine looks at Connectivity more seriously- blood flows, nervous systems ,skin ,digestive systems etc, but a lot of work is going on now on emotional Connectivity. 

These are some guiding principles I adopted in my life.

20/05/25, 5:26 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 719

Let me resume my first days in IISc in the year  1965. My experiences were ,from the beginning,  highly immersive ,educative  and enjoyable.
 I had by this time visited some institutions in India like Annamalai University, Engineering college, Guindy, IIT Madras , and a few colleges in the north ,east and south . Madras university , from where I graduated and attended my convocation, didn't have a proper campus. It functioned in a few buildings in the Marina beach .
Classes began on August 2, Monday for me.  I met my classmates. I saw and understood the diversity. There were seven of us each from a different state. That was a good experience and opportunity to learn and  know the different languages, different cultures, habits and different ways of learning. This taught me tolerance while broadening my understanding.
That was another requirement for a good life that I learnt and practised- adjustments, understanding and tolerance. I see around me many languages spoken  and people from different parts of the country - and good fusion. I liked it as I loved many languages and started picking up both kannada and a bit of Telugu. 
We had two streams in ME in the department. The other stream - control systems - had 12 students. We had a lot of common classes like Mathematics, Electronics ,Instrumentation etc . So we were together for a good amount of time. So the free gaps in time was used to talk and understand each other. I became friendly with all. 
Classes were again very different , intensive and very serious . Almost all faculty were experienced and knowledgeable except one . Electromagnetic field theory , a tough subject, was taught by a research student . It was cryptic and we didn't understand it . His expression was poor. A lot of individual effort was needed to learn the subject. He was trying to disprove Maxwells equations , but couldn't do it and left the institute after a few years. Also , most faculty had in depth knowledge in the subjects they were teaching  .  The most important difference in teaching I found was forcing students to perform some parts of learning . 
The first major adjustment needed was to start running from day one. Institute followed continuous assessments. Many faculty announced surprise quizzes and many gave large assignments and problem sheets. Most students were not exposed to this and had difficulty in picking up speed. They were spending time getting adjusted to the new place, hostel, food, climate etc. So  when they faced the first test , they found themselves not ready and failed in  the test. This made some lose confidence in their studies. But most did cope up by concentrating on their studies. They learnt quickly. The atmosphere and advice from seniors helped many to pick up the pace of using the library regularly and studying the topics. 
But I seem to be different. I was at ease at IISc from the beginning.  I found the lectures interesting and easy to understand. I was regular in my visits to the library in the evenings. But i felt I had more free time . This despite reading a lot of fiction . So I decided to audit some control systems courses taught by Prof MD Srinath. I also decided to attend the German language course run in the evening by Mr Klatt in the foreign languages section.  He was a very friendly person. He took us to his house once and we met Madam Lokasundari Raman there. I built a good rapport with many faculty like Prof Prabhu and Prof Devanathan  as well as the other faculty in foreign languages section. So life was smooth.

21/05/25, 4:50 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 720
“Will you live in a house full of wastes, junk and dirt? Everyone says, no. 
Then why do you accumulate junk in your mind? Use spirituality to clean up your mind. “
Dks 

So I got settled at IISc with my academic work. I didn't find any difficulty. Everything was smooth and happy. The mess bill I got at the end of the month was less than 60 rupees. So my total expenses were only about 100 rupees per month for room rent and food . Financially, I became a well off person. 
Life was very good . I could read a lot of books , classes were nice, made a lot of friendships, understood linguistic differences and cultural differences , and got a lot of exercise.  
During the first week of stay ,- I think it was 5th of August, I met the director. In those days , it was a standard practice for all new graduate and research students to meet the director. He spent about five to ten minutes with me, asked me - why I chose IISc,  and what I want to do and wished me the best time here. It was a surprise that the director invests his time on each student. I didn't have this experience in any other institution. I found him friendly and knowledgeable.  I felt he could be approached in case of need.
The next requirement was a health checkup. I met Mr. Nagaraja Rao, our health centre lab in charge , who conducted the test. I didn't remember meeting Dr TB Subba Rao, the doctor at that time. It was a simple physical checkup then for about 10 minutes. I liked Nagaraja Rao and found out his interests in several things like studying more courses, producing movies etc. The health centre was a small two room set up. It was located in the same place as now , but much smaller. I was told it was a dining place for orthodox persons before the mess integration in 1954.  There were two compounders - tablets were yet to come in - and a doctors room and a place for lab and dressing . Ramu was an efficient dressing expert. He did a good job. That was the simple and bare  health centre with no facilities. 
Institute closed admissions by August 16 and there was a director's tea on that evening for fresh students . Normally it used to rain on that day. Many schedules were continued by the institute for several decades. That was a wonderful tradition. Many schedules like opening date ,vacation times, exam dates are maintained even now. 
For close to a century , the institute remained a pristine , natural area with lots of greenery. Unfortunately that has changed now with too many buildings without proper spacing giving a crowded urban space . Still most trees were in place , and lots more were planted  during the tenures of Prof Dhawan  and Prof CNR Rao . 
I got settled by then and got busy with work. I found all tests and semester end exams easy and got the first rank in the first semester itself and continued it afterwards. I will discuss how I developed my views on teaching later. 
During the two years of ME , joining IISc after ME didn't occur to me. I saw many getting jobs in Tata electric and was thinking of joining Tata electric company. Even after ME ,I didn't think that I would be in IISc for long. It was destiny and my good fortune that kept me in IISc and allowed me to grow and use my potential to learn and do research and assist others. 
One directional change was directed by my project work that got me interested and involved in research and my flair for teaching.   
In the second semester we had to select a project guide. I went to Prof Ramachandra Rao for advice. He suggested that I should meet N Dharma Rao , who would give me a good topic. So I went to meet Dharma Rao. He had worked on nonlinear stability and suggested a stability problem . I worked throughout the summer vacation and completed the work. Then he suggested load flows. He didn't have any idea on the topic. It was a green field. That excited me. 
I was interested in computers and I had a good foundation in mathematics.  So this problem will lead me to use computers, program the load flow problem and solve it. It was exciting. No one was doing programming work at that time. So I started on a journey that got me ME and PhD and introduced me to computers, programming , computational methods , optimization and solving large problems. That opened a big door. Opportunities dragged me. I couldn't resist it despite advice from many. More on these later

“Why be elated by material profit?” Father replied. “The one who pursues a goal of evenmindedness is neither jubilant with gain nor depressed by loss. He knows that man arrives penniless in this world, and departs without a single rupee.”
Autobiography of a yogi
Paramahansa yogananda

Mr Nagaraj has a query:
“Good morning sir. Your Today's message in quotes (starting para) is easy to understand but difficult to practice. Hence unhappiness is inevitable. Is it not so?”

My response
It is a good query. 
I didn't talk about happiness here. It is a higher state. I talked about slavery here. 
I talked about some things that we can change , not about difficult things. 
I do not believe that  unhappiness is inevitable. I think it is a state of emotions. But we will discuss it later. I was asking a question - are we free of slavery? 
Everyone talks that we are slaves of data today and will become slaves of AI tomorrow.  I am not looking at that aspect. I am worried that our mind has a lot of cobwebs.  We need to remove them. That was the purpose of my quote. Our vedanta focuses on that aspect with the highest goal of moksha . 
Let me explain. Humans are built  up of 
Habits - good , bad and unwanted ; 
Beliefs in several  thousands ;
Principles - may be hundreds or more;
Practices - again thousands , these depend upon habits , likes, dislikes, and /or beliefs ;
Emotions 
Ego
Buddhi
We accumulate these and build ourselves over a period of years. 
We get these from parents, relatives, society, school, friends and observations etc in addition to some genetic factors.
Mr Srinivasan ,PNB was talking to me about beliefs. 
Have we become prisoners of our beliefs? 
For example ask ourselves - 
Why this fight between vadakalai and thenkalai? Why the fight between religions, castes , between temple practices , between advaita, dvaita etc? Why do you say one sampradaya is better than others. 
Please live and let others to live, each with their traditions. 
Are we humans with Buddhi or only a large bundle of beliefs?
Do we need all these rigid beliefs to be humans? 
Tamils have a pride for only Tamil language , not for other languages.  Many hate Sanskrit. 
There has been a continuous language fight going on in Bangalore for several decades.
Is India made up of people or Hindus ,Muslims and Christians etc or Tamils, Telugus,  kannadigas , north Indians, Bengalis,  punjabis etc? 
Why do we divide people , create disunity,  create fights , ?
All these, I felt ,  were due to our being rigid and slave to the beliefs we accumulated. We think our beliefs are better and should be followed by others. That creates troubles. 
If beliefs are good , then there is no problem. Some beliefs are good . But not some others. 
The next question is : Are beliefs sacrosanct? Are beliefs unalterable?
At least smart people adjust and learn to live with changes. We as humans are capable of living and adapting to changes. When rice was not available ,we moved to wheat chapati. But  Some don't.  If someone didn't know Kannada,  we still try to help them. Most security and maintenance staff we have don't know Kannada. Can we fight with them? We try to adjust to their behaviour. 
My simple question is 
Why do you judge people. Tell people learn Kannada. If it is done with a good intention, then it is OK,  but it is unfortunately done to show your pride. That is the start for fights. 
Before we start judging others, my request is
Let us judge ourselves, assess ourselves and see how to change us. That i called as the process of dirt removal from the mind. I think it is doable.  
So my point was that  many beliefs , habits, practices etc were not necessary and may be a burden. 
As rational human beings ,can we have an open mind ,a flexible mind , analyse our beliefs, ask ourselves whether some beliefs are right , Whether some beliefs hurt others or nature, some habits like excess consumption and wastage affect the society and nature, ; and review our beliefs and have the courage to discard some or atleast accept people with different beliefs .
That was my intention. 
If you see the world and people, Mr Srinivasn was telling me that there is a lot of intolerance in the societies. We talk of global living, but still live in this deep well of beliefs, habits and practices.
I don't say that “don't believe in many things. “ But ask a few questions periodically -
Do my beliefs hurt others?
Does my belief bring me benefits or am I a slave to a meaningless practice, should I keep it or discard it ? 
Can I live without a particular belief? 
So my suggestion was to analyse yourself and assert that your buddhi is the boss, not your beliefs, habits, emotions and ego. These are Maya and changeable. If you understand this principle, you understand the first step of vedanta.
We should live with a set of principles , purposes and goals, but not with a huge burden of beliefs, habits and practices and a rigid mindset without viveka and jnana. 

Just like you clean yourself by taking a bath regularly, and clear  your house of dirt and waste, please clean your mind, and clear unwanted beliefs and habits. That is my appeal. 
Greetings
Dks
23/05/25, 5:31 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 722

Let me continue with my discussions on - what are the wastes in mind. I wrote the quote in the context of spiritual aspects or dimension of life to emphasize the essentially of incorporating spiritualism in our life. Pure materialism collects wastes and spiritualism helps in removing the wastes. Science unfortunately hasn't given enough attention to this problem though some neural scientists and psychologists look at it. 
I consider the following as harmful wastes in mind
Wrong and erroneous understanding of facts - see a rope ,think it is a snake.
Wrong assumptions and analysis- this happens in both formal and non formal education. We learn some aspects of science , don't understand the principles correctly and apply them wrongly. We are taught a lot of biases in history and social sciences. 
Beliefs breeding passion, pride, hatred, intolerance, violence, affecting others. 
Ego clouding our decision ingenuity powers 
Emotions like anger affecting  our thinking processes ,clouding our mind  and thereby affecting our welfare and harmony. 

How do we remove these clouds and see a better reality?
Just as we spend time everyday cleaning ourselves and our environment , we should also spend time cleaning our mind. Religion provided some approaches. Satsang is a good approach. Meditation is another approach. Yoga can calm our mind. All these help us to understand our weaknesses and remove them . But the most important activity to be done atleast once in a month is a self review. This is a very detailed review . You should look at your major habits and find out the effects of your habits and beliefs  in your life and identify the positive and negative ones . Look at the connections of your habits and beliefs with society and how it affects you. Look at the impact on your job , your future and decide on actions. Then decide on  what are  to be retained, what are to be discarded,  what are to be modified .


Actually , the cleaning up of our mind is a spiritual activity. 
We moved from IQ to EQ. Neuroscientist Joseph Le Doux raises the issue that some kind of integration or synthesis of heart and head, emotions and thinking (or even brain science and philosophy) might be the way ahead: 
I conclude with the hypothesis, based on trends in brain evolution, that the struggle between thought and emotion may ultimately be resolved, not simply by the dominance of the neocortical cognitions over emotional systems, but by a more harmonious integration of reason and passion in the brain, a development that will allow future humans to better know their true feelings and to use them more effectively in daily life.           
We need to move to SQ now. 


Let us move to my story on IISc. Let me write about my first impressions of bangalore.  
I found Bangalore spread out as a city, not a cramped one like Kolkata or Chennai. 
But it is a city of contrasts . Let me look at Malleswaram first, the sub city near IISc.  
There were many broad roads with good footpaths and a large avenue of trees . The two major roads were called Sampige road and Margosa road. There were huge neem trees at that time. Most houses were bungalows with a large open space with trees. You see jackfruit trees everywhere.  Houses were spaced out. Traffic was minimal. I could walk freely in Sampige road during the evening. We can't dream of it now. 
Combine it with the pleasant weather. It was a paradise for walking. Later I found Basavanagudi to be similar. 
I said it is a city of contrasts. So if we go to Yeshwantpur ,we find the area crowded , streets narrow , houses small with almost no garden and a few trees. Both Malleswaram and Yeshwantpur are adjacent to IISc on opposite sides and present a great  contrast. This surprised me. Fortunately there were no slums in Bangalore then like Bombay and Madras.  I used to see people - families- living in the street in Linghi chetty street where i stayed for a year. 
Bangalore was a divided city . During the pre-independence period, the city part comprising Chamrajpet , Basavanagudi and Malleswaram were governed by a municipality under the control of the Mysore maharaja.  The cantonment was under the military . The civil areas in the east like Ulsoor were under the administration of Madras collector. IISc was attached to the British administration.  So the total city provided a divided look with a mixed population. The city had a lot of kannadigas living there , the civil areas had a lot of Tamils living there and many others like Muslims, Telugu, marattas lived in different areas or pockets. 
Bangalore had many - about 160 - lakes at that time. But unfortunately ,Bangalore had a culture of converting lakes into buildings. Initially bangalore got water from two big lakes - Dhammambudhi and kempambudhi . Both were converted - Dhammambudhi became the bus stand near the railway station later. In 1965, it was a maidan with theatre activities in a tent and exhibition on the ground. By 70s it was converted into a bus stand. Similarly kempambudhi has become a sports complex. we lost other lakes like Mathikere near the institute yielding to houses. This is the starting point of destruction of the drainage system in the city leading to flooding during heavy rains. 
While the narrow north south corridor from yeshwantpur to basavanagudi had a series of settlements, other settlements like Domlur, Ulsoor, Banaswadi , Cantonment were separate and segregated. 
Bangalore with a good climate and lots of gardens was known as a pensioners city. We had a few factories then - BEL and HMT close to IISc, HAL and ITI at other ends being the major ones and a few small ones were there.

“Good morning sir.
I understand that IQ, EQ & SQ are interconnected & hence can influence one another. Is it not so? Or is my understanding wrong?.”
My response 
I wrote about a detailed review of one's  actions, beliefs and habits etc with the purposes of understanding the  strengths and weaknesses , the good and bad, the desirability and undesirability of your beliefs , making corrections, changing  some beliefs or habits etc. It improves our life style and we can connect it to emotions. This is the first step to minds management- i believe we have many minds , they interact with each other , they cancel each other minds views and may create confusion. Mind management is an important task of spiritualism.
We go through three stages as a human -
1.Knowledge acquisition , analysis , actions and decisions . We do this in personal , professional and societal life everyday.  We call the different kinds of knowledge, their analysis , application and generation of new knowledge as intelligence. IQ is one , not correct or accurate , index or metric on intelligence.
2.Emotion control  - understand the impacts of emotions and control them . It is EQ
3. Spiritualism - it connects - it is a bridge - it becomes a decider in case of conflicts between minds . It is a bridge between physical material life, intellectual life, emotional life and mental life. It balances our life , brings in happiness. 
Yes ,Nagaraj . There is interdependence between knowledge and emotions. You may create a wrong and erroneous set of facts when you read or listen to a lecture when you are emotionally upset or disturbed or your mind is wandering. 
Again you may act wrongly when emotions are strong. 
That is why mind control - calming the mind, creating a pleasing ambience , prayer - helps in directing our intelligence to action correctly away from emotions.
I focused here on only one aspect - removing some unwanted beliefs and habits through a review and with an open , flexible and adaptable mind 
This detailed review is extremely useful to improve oneself and move up in life. 
I practiced this review regularly. I changed my habits and beliefs and even opinions. 
The first one was a strong habit of daily reading of books . This happened when I joined the high school. In addition to understanding society, people , country , it sharpened my memory. I used to read magazines and there were serials which appeared once a week. I still remember three serials - washingtonil tirumanam by Savi,  Ponniyin selven by Kalki and Thillana mohanambal . When we read a particular issue , we need to remember what happened before. This increased my memory power. I also learnt to remember what I read in a book. That helped in retaining my classroom lectures and even statements by teachers. I learnt about suez crisis by listening to its explanation by a teacher. 
My second habit happened during my College days during PUC time in 1959. It is a new habit built by me of reading English classics and novels. 
The third change that happened was during my college days , it was a change in a belief - shift from religious to spiritual approaches. That was the time I followed a detailed analysis and review of my beliefs. 
The fourth change or I should say a continuous change to be even considered as a first change is my broadening my scope of subjects / disciplines and interests. It started at school level and continued. I showed interest in economics, nature, building construction. I didn't hesitate to learn new subjects or topics unconnected with my curriculum. 
I will discuss more changes in later posts.

“No, not I, I will survive
