I spent so many nights feeling sorry for myself
How I cried
But now I hold my head up high
And you see me, somebody new
I'm not that lonely little person”
Sung by Gloria Gaynor
Let me continue and look at a few thoughts which changed my thinking directions of my life. 
I was confused about many things in life that were happening. This confusion made me think about them and not worry about my future. Sure I did worry about jobs many times .Let me illustrate a few . 
When I was studying engineering in Madurai  during 1959 to 64 , several thoughts ran through me and I did the reviews and looked for modifications in my thinking and actions. My belief systems changed. 
Let me talk about engineering education's biggest hurdle that is there even today - problem of specialization . Prior to my joining ,engineering was a four year program with only the last two years being for the branch level courses . A lot of general engineering subjects were taught . So specialisation as electrical or mechanical engineer was not considered to be very effective. 
In order to strengthen the knowledge in the specialised areas , the degree program was extended by one more year to six years with the students spending three later years on their area of specialization. This was a major reform then. 
While the curriculum focused on mostly engineering subjects and strengthened both mathematics and sciences, it ignored economics, literature, and humanities. 
I had a nagging doubt - is a narrow specialization a good idea ? 
What was my decision ? To look beyond. 
My decision to go for more serious learning outside the curriculum started at this time. I created interest in knowing about what is happening everywhere and what the future has in store for us . These two questions kept me going throughout my life , kept me active mentally and allowed me to prepare for new areas. Anticipation of change helped me. 
What is the starting point for this change?

I mentioned this before - Schumacher’s lecture in 1959 60. He was a visionary. Later he set up the intermediate technology group in UK. I was involved with discussions on IT - not the present information technology but intermediate technology.  
He talked about dangers for India with Nehruvian policies and suggested alternatives. He quoted extensively from Gandhian thoughts on economy and industrial development. He said that if we followed Nehruvian model, we will create problems and cities will grow huge and uncontrollable.  Surprise is we see what he predicted in 1959 60. He discussed the advantages of intermediate technology as job creation, lesser consumption, lesser use of transports, and lesser pollution. He said biscuits made in Manchester were transported to London and those made in London were transported to Manchester- more diesel use and pollution , not required. He said that was the problem of marginal utility. 
It opened my eyes. But the world was moving towards specialization, I was thinking of generalization. If you have a generalist thinking , you will get outside views, not just inside views. If you are a specialist ,you will get internal views only, outside will escape you leading to Christensen's disruption concept. That is why administrators today have control over engineers, doctors and even generals of army. 
I felt strongly that  generalist have a broader view and multidirectional views compared to specialists.  Generalists have open mind , not a fixed and rigid mind and they consider options and alternatives, but they need inputs from specialists. So a combination is good . But world thinks the other way. 
That was a big concern. Today's major environmental problems were the creation of engineering views . That is why I built an alternate view for Bedthi hydro projects. That was opposed by many engineers.
Greetings

Pythagoras taught spirituality through instruction in mathematics, and Plato instituted mathematics as part of the curriculum of his academy. Logical debates were part of Plato’s spiritual training, a practice inspired by Socrates.”

There was an arithmetic error in my last  post. It was pointed out by Mr Thiruvelan.

"...the degree program..by one more year..to six years..."
You meant five years!”
Thanks Tiruvelan for pointing out the error and correcting it quickly. 

Let me go back to 1965 and see bangalore of those traffic free , spacious bangalore cities - yes I still think that Bangalore was a cluster of small and big towns , not a well interconnected city. I am going to write about public transport . 
I saw and experienced the public bus system in Madurai for five years . It was the best, run by TVS , not nationalised , punctual and with a dynamic scheduling and traffic management. It handled varying traffic load with peak loads like chitra pournami, teppakulam festival and tiruparamkundram festivals when loads were high. That was an ideal design for city transport. Even now I think that TVS understood the importance of a public transport system and realised that the objective is to provide a service - not buses- to meet the dynamic demands of people efficiently , effectively and at affordable cost. They built a highly integrated man- machine- operational managing system . I don't think any city transport has understood this . They realised transport system is not about buses and routes , but about load and traffic management with minimal wait times. We can find excuses for not providing a good service interface most of our cities and look for fancy solutions without understanding the basic principles of urban needs. 
I found the traffic in Calcutta being managed by several modes - buses, minibus, trams and even rickshaws.  Travel took time , traffic jams were common , but transports were available and most inexpensive . The frequency of minibuses and trams were good. You don't have to wait for long. Travel  to Howrah and Ramakrishna mutt took more than an hour . But it was easy to reach Esplanade and other places from RB Avenue. Walking was a good option . Even in the eighties, I found the transport systems in Kolkata to be the same with the metro being an addition. 
Public bus transport in Delhi wasn't good. But Delhi had options in minibuses . But minibuses were crowded - people were packed inside like sardines , you couldn't breathe. Once my friend and I were in a mini bus. He was tall, felt suffocated ,and  so we got down after a few stops. The only common mode of transport was autos - scooters- an expensive option. Connaught place had some more options like motor cycle driven vehicles. Fortunately, the extensive metro network helps in handling some people movements. But poor transport led to people relying on themselves - buying scooters, motor cycles and now cars.
The bus transport system in Madras was reasonable . I used to go to Tiruvottiyur in the north every week days and had no problem in bus availability and travel times. I reached almost every part by bus in Madras.  It also had suburban train service and local services . So Traveling in Madras and later in Chennai even in the nineties was  easy . It wasn't an arduous task even during heavy monsoon rainy days. 
But Bangalore was a total disappointment. It had no options . Travel within the city was not easy- I am using a mild term .
Sometimes , people had  to wait for an hour for a bus  to arrive at the stop. Frequency was erratic. Most buses were terrible. Time management was unknown for the BTS - Bangalore transport service. By mid seventies, they stopped publishing the schedules. The system hasn't changed over a period of more than sixty years. There was no suburban train service . So urgent travel implied the use of autos. But the autos followed their own rules. When it rains, autos stop running. They don't oblige you. Their work culture and customer service were poor. 
Fortunately, there was one route which was good - route 11 going from Malleswaram 18th cross to Gandhi Bazar. North to south winding through the major parts of the city. The frequency was good, you got a bus within half an hour, but the wait time was normally about ten minutes. Some buses ran upto the institute gate. So we could travel to Malleswaram , Swastik,  Majestic , corporation and Basavanagudi   without long waits. But traveling  to NAL , which I had to do from 1966 onwards , to HAL again from 1966 onwards, both for computer work,   was tough. Visits to cinema houses in the night meant that we had to walk back. That state of poor transport system continues even now- one stable continuity.

“Because of our capacity to imagine, we were able to build Earth as it is today out of virtually nothing. 
In a sense, the notion that thoughts create reality is more than just a nice idea; it’s also a fact of evolution. It was because of language and thought that we could create a world within our minds, and ultimately, it is because of language and thought that we have evolved into the society we have today—for better and for worse.”
Brianna Wiest
DrBabu endorses my views on generalization and Bangalore transport. 
I made a comment that Bangalore is not an interconnected city. Let me explain that statement . 
If you take Chennai, it has a very long north south road connecting George town to Tambaram with mount road playing a prominent part. This allowed connectivity to many parts of Madras. Then the long east coast road is another connection. You can see a criss crossing network of roads connecting most parts. It is a well connected city. Bombay also has many long roads and is also a well connected city. Cities grew along highways. 
But Bangalore is not a connected city. It has patches of good roads. It has many roads leading out to Hosur, Hoskote, Pune, Mysore, Bellary , Kanakapura, Magadi  etc. But they were congested within the city and led to more heavy duty vehicle traffic. We can't go from north to south easily . Chickpet and cotton pet are narrow roads and don't aid large traffic flows. Our connectivity between areas is poor. That is the reason for traffic jams. 
In 1972, I conducted a short term course on  optimization and was discussing this course with Prof Dhawan, director. I told him about commonsense optimization and the public transport problem in bangalore. I said optimising the schedule is of not much use . Then   I told him that BTS has 450 buses , but the public sector companies like HAL,ITI, BEL and HMT had about 600 buses and they remain idle for some parts of the day. Some of the buses can be leased to BTS to run during peak hours. This will lead to total fleet optimization.  He liked the idea and wanted to pursue it . But the companies were not agreeable  and so it didn't succeed. 

Let me look at the next aspect - My interest in kannada
I had a small exposure to Kannada prior to coming to bangalore.  In my younger days , it was through two media - Purandara dasara kritis in carnatic music , and Udupi hotels in Tirunelveli . Surprisingly I didn't find a Udupi  hotel in Madurai. My Kannada knowledge took another small step in Madras. Two senior officers in the factory were kannadigas. I used to talk to them and picked up a smattering of Kannada. Also,  most hotels I frequented in Madras were run by Udupi  people. I used to hear some Kannada terms there. Still my exposure to kannada was minimal. I learnt more Telugu words. 
My eagerness to learn Kannada started in Bangalore from the beginning.  There was no opportunity to learn Kannada in the mess as most workers spoke Tamil. But I picked up a bit of Kannada in the department.  Office staff spoke in Kannada . Some faculty spoke to the office staff in Kannada . Most of my kannada speaking and understanding started with conversations with a few classmates, a few hostel mates and a few employees and staff. So by 1966, in more than a year, I picked up enough kannada vocabulary to manage conversations. I found learning and speaking kannada was easy ,as most words have a commonality with Sanskrit and Tamil . I continued with my kannada conversation in general and avoided speaking in Tamil to kannada people.   In 1968, I started learning Kannada alphabets. 
As I said earlier, I became friendly with Mr PA Bukebag from TIFR who used to visit IISc every summer to run the summer schools. I used to talk to him mostly in kannada and he wanted to see kannada movies . So i went with him. That also helped my kannada knowledge. He wanted to read Shivarama karanth's novels and we borrowed them from Ranade library. I got acquainted to kannada literature slowly. I also listened to G P Rajarathnam speaking simple kannada and liked his rhymes. So my understanding of kannada improved consistently. Constant practice helped me. Later my visits to rural schools and my stay at vidhana soudha improved my kannada.

28/05/25, 4:26 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 727

Vinod Vyasulu has commented on the divisiveness of Bangalore now Bengaluru.
“Bangalore was designed to be not connected. We had the pete, the Mysore part around City Market in the west. . Then to the east the British set up the cantonment...then called the City and Civil Station under their control. Cubbon Park, then much bigger than now, separated them  There was an east west zonation but the two had different cultures. I understand there was also at times a customs barrier between the two. Even now after so many years, there is a discernable difference.”

My response
Bangalore was never an integrated city. It had three separate sections - city comprising Basavanagudi to Malleswaram governed by Mysore government,  Cantonment under british military control and civil and military station under Madras collectorate. But that is not important . 
Bangalore had about 200 lakes , many big ones , these divided the Bangalore city. Next, Bangalore had many gardens , the biggest being Lalbagh and Cubbon park , they divided the city. 
The third was the composition of people . Most middle class people lived in traditional areas like Basavanagudi , Malleswaram,  Chamrajpet etc.  These people worked for the Mysore government.  Most people in the east area worked for the military and spoke Tamil. Merchants concentrated in the central part of the city like chickpet, Taragupet , Balepet, cotton pet etc. That was a congested area with narrow roads. 
Another important aspect ,reason for the divisiveness of Bangalore was the presence of a large number of agricultural areas in the city. I had seen the west of Rajajinagar was full of agricultural fields, similarly Domlur - reaching the HAL airport was difficult especially when it rained. Domlur was a low lying area and the road used to get flooded. So reaching HAL and NAL was not easy for me as autos won't go to that area. 
Similarly many outer parts in the south, east and west grew many vegetables and Bangalore was famous for chow chow- known as chayote squash -  and cucumber. The only problem was that these vegetables were grown in untreated water. 
Post independence, the city Planners didn’t want to integrate. They allowed the existing state to continue on the assumption that Bangalore won't grow. Even Sir MV had the same idea and he opposed the construction of the Tippegondanahalli reservoir on Arkavati saying that Bangalore's population won't increase. 
I recall a discussion in 1968 with the KEB executive engineer in charge of Bangalore city's electricity distribution. I was telling him that I had developed several regression models and wanted to test them for estimating the growth of Bangalore's demand. I wanted the city data. He dismissed me saying he didn't want any model. He said that he was sure  the majestic area won't grow and gave adhoc numbers for the growth of other areas. I was shocked. He was proved wrong. 
Also the city improvement board - later BDA - planned the huge Rajajinagar area and later Jayanagar area creating big islands . 
The city started growing with the arrival of public sector units like HAL, ITI, BEL and HMT and it had many electrical manufacturing units close to IISc like kirloskar, MEI, porcelain factory , Mysore lamps etc. 
With strong electronics , aerospace strengths , the city started growing fast from 1980 onwards. We in the institute felt the city's growth by finding difficulty in getting houses for rent by the mid 70s. Houses for rent became scarce by then. Rents went up beyond the reach of our faculty. Many faced eviction. By the 80s many areas like Vijayanagar , Indira nagar , palace orchards etc started developing to provide accommodation to the growing city. So the outer areas expanded , but inner areas went upwards and struggled to meet the traffic growth. Malleswaram now is crowded with many apartment blocks and has grown vertically. It is no longer that peaceful ,quiet area it was. 
Fortunately,  the planning for water supply to the city has been good. First , we got water from Arkavati river and then from Cauvery river. 
More in next

29/05/25, 4:11 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 728

Till the early seventies, many cities had many more industries than bangalore, with Calcutta and Bombay leading up front. But the scene changed from late seventies. Initially in the sixties and early seventies , Bangalore north had more industries.  R Venkatraman introduced the concept of industrial estates in guindy and it became popular. During Nijalingappa's period, Bangalore started with Rajajinagar industrial estate and then built a big estate or a city in Peenya. That was the largest. It took Bangalore up by a few notches in industrialisation. Then other estates came up one by one and the electronic city came up in 1978 by Keonics. These led to the growth of several hundreds to thousands of small, medium and large industries. Today industries are everywhere. There are thousands of startups, global centres and IT services units. Bangalore is way ahead of other cities now. I feel that IISc played a significant role in this transformation.  
The next adjustment for me  was  the food culture. Again there are many differences with Madras though many good veg restaurants in Madras were run by Udupi cooks. They in Madras prepared food  to suit the culture and taste of local people . 
But here you get the original Udupi food with changes suitable for bangalore. 
My opportunities to eat out were minimal as the mess food was sumptuous, and wholesome with Sunday specials. Still I experimented eating outside occasionally . My first experience was with coffee. In 1965, there was no eating place in the institute other than the mess and no coffee shops. I had a friend ,Mr J Purnanandam. He was quite a senior to me doing PhD work in the management department- it was not known as management department then ,but as humanities and social sciences department running an evening diploma program in management .
He wanted coffee . So during my vacation times, i used to go for coffee with him to Malleswaram 17th cross or 18th cross. There were two hotels . I found coffee served in small tumblers unlike in Madras. Even with the fact that the quantity is small , I saw a new practice - called  by two service, sharing of one coffee by two people . Later I saw many variations of this unique by two service becoming two by five or three by five etc. All were served without any grumbling and happily unlike the curse word ,” savu krakki”- dying fellow in literal translation-  which I used to hear in Madras.  
We didn't have many restaurants in Malleswaram. So alternates came up. The other prominent feature was many petty shops selling  bun butter and jam as well as Badam milk . Then I found many bakery shops called Iyengar bakery- a bangalore invention and very popular and useful for households and hungry people. These provided less expensive options. 
I have heard about the famous MTR and visited it once ,but they don't give Dosa in the evening, but the rava idly and coffee were fabulous. You had to wait first outside, then inside and later ushered into the dining area. You can see the open kitchen as you enter. It suffered losses when the government introduced price control and even closed its doors for a few years due to the unsustainable price restrictions imposed by the government.  Even now ,MTR is an experience . 
Bangalore had many special dishes like rava idly,  kali Dosa, set Dosa, Mysore Masala Dosa, Akki roti,  etc. Rice idly was  available only in the morning hours in those days. It was rava idly afterwards. Ragi is a staple food for many, but wasn't available in traditional Udupi hotels. They used jaggery and coconut in sambar. Rasam was known as tili saru.  
My classmate Thakkar started a coffee place in the gymkhana in 1966. Coffee culture started then. From then on , coffee shop  became a mandatory entity in the institute campus. Mid morning and mid afternoon coffee became a standard activity for many - students, staff and faculty. It affected our work culture.

30/05/25, 4:57 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 729
I observed a nice and consistent  tendency in Bangalore for the past 60 plus years.I don't know about other cities . But Bangalore settled people - I don't know whom to call bangaloreans, kannada chaluvalis may not approve- mostly compared Bangalore of their days and now ,saying that it was heaven then and hell now. It includes people who lived from the sixties or from the eighties or even from 2000 onwards. This was and is a constant refrain I heard and hear from many . Everyone of these settlers - whether they came in the 60s or 80s or 2000- established an affinity to the city. That is great despite all the difficulties of traffic . The city maintains its unique intellectual culture , very different from Delhi. Still it lags Chennai on other aspects like music, dance, public lectures etc. But while it supported literature, it was stronger in Mysore ,Belgaum,Dharwad and Mangalore. 


Let me return to look at my life at IISc.  
IISc offered a lot of opportunities to learn and to do research. Most faculty teaching courses had in-depth knowledge and practical exposure. 
The institute always assisted Indian industries , public sector companies, utilities and defence establishments. It had fantastic experimental facilities . To name a few 
The electron microscope - only one in the country- , in CISL, wind tunnels - again unique-, extensive hydraulics facilities, AC network analyser - the only one in the country-  ,a well equipped workshops rivaling big machine tool industries, ,an anechoic chamber in ECE etc. Institute excelled in experimental research and development work in science and engineering.  Let me give one example of  practical exposure . 
The EE department had an AC network analyser . It can simulate large power grids . It was used to design all power grids of India. Throughout a year, it was kept busy by the electricity boards in the country doing their planning studies. Each board booked it for a month of studies. Prof Narayana Iyengar did these studies.  So he is knowledgeable about Indian power systems . He was teaching power system analysis for us. 
The second great advantage is the global connect. Many faculty had a PhD from a good foreign university. This helped us to know what is happening in the western universities and what are the current research areas. Our curriculum was compared with that of MIT. We are not frogs in the well. Prof Joseph was conducting research in the growing ,new area of power electronics . Prof M D Srinath used to teach advanced topics in controls. So we could do research in the latest cutting edge areas. This is true of many departments. So it spurred our research by students and faculty and established a competitive work ethics - everyone gets into a serious study and research framework. No one sits idle on his past laurels.  
The third advantage and opportunity of global connect is the visit by many experts from abroad and their lectures and discussions. These were planned as lectures by Prof Kalman and may be unplanned also. There are a large number of unplanned lectures.Let me recount one such incident . Prof Sana from university of Chicago was in Bangalore on vacation  and called Prof HNR . Prof HNR invited him to give a talk. He came the next day and gave a talk. That is the informal level of connects. Sana later became a good friend of mine. He started visiting us regularly. Prof Kailath is another regular visitor. 
There were many general lectures . I listened to CV Raman , Sir MV, Tandan,  MD of Lever, JRD Tata, and many more. Most students attended these lectures then. The PE 303 hall was overflowing during many lectures. It enhanced our knowledge and gave us a peep into what is happening here and elsewhere in many sectors. 
The next was the opportunity of attending conferences. Many conferences were held in the institute . At that time ,we had only two halls - one was the library seminar hall ,another was PE 303 in our building . Faculty hall came later. Seminar complex came much later due to the efforts of Prof Ramaseshan and later Prof CNR Rao. I got into the conference presentations process from 1967 onwards and had till now attended hundreds of conferences. Look at my exposure and networking advantages - all due to IISc encouragement,training and culture.  All these and an egalitarian outlook are part of IISc ambience. 

That is what I mean by the opportunities for learning afforded by IISc.  Such opportunities were not available in most institutions in India at that time or even now. 
IISc is shy of showcasing its achievements and its great ambience. 
Greetings 
Dks
31/05/25, 4:44 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 730

Let me discuss the heart of IISc. What does the general ambience reveal to you and make  you learn ? I understood these after a few years of staying at IISc.  These are some core principles that were evolved over a period of time, accepted and practised. They were not told to you explicitly. You observe , learn , understand and adapt to them as far as possible. If not, you are left behind.

It teaches ,adapts and follows certain characteristics or qualities that a person should follow. Let me list them. 
Seriousness, interest  and commitment in your work . I found that even clerical staff and mess staff followed this. 
Freedom and independence. No fear of the unknown. 
Open and flexible and adaptable thinking and actions
Curiosity to know why and how . Participation in most academic activities of the department by even a junior faculty. This creates awareness , answers the question why , builds friendship and inculcates and improves decision making capabilities of individuals. Many decisions by the institute are taken after a lot of discussions at many levels. We don't mind reversing decisions. 
Strong affinity to fundamental principles ,modeling, design , analysis, experimentation, . The faculty understood the underlying core of some basics of engineering like fluid mechanics, solid mechanics, thermodynamics,modeling, analysis etc. Also we started looking at instrumentation as a core area with a separate centre for instrumentation.  Similarly, energy was looked at with a broader view . That was the reason for setting up an integrated power engineering department. Borders were ignored . Electronics was an important area in many departments. 
Focus on engineering constraints like reliability, repeatability, affordability, etc.
Forward, futuristic , outward looking and thinking.  This is a fundamental quality in the institute. First you look outside - what other institutions are doing, what your peers are working on, how the world is developing, what are the hurdles, what should one anticipate etc. Looking outside and working in another institution in India or abroad is mandatory for promotion. It promotes a holistic vision. Frog in the well approach was discredited.
Futuristic assessment and thinking allowed the institute to grow and be in the forefront of research. It allowed IISc to lead in many areas. Futuristic thinking is essential in today's fast changing volatile world. It prepares you to adapt to these changes, modify your life and maintain stability . Agility is also needed. Agility and futuristic thinking need a flexible open mind. 
Dynamic restructuring , decentralization, delegation and responsibility. This is a key distinguishing component of IISc and I played a big part in this. 
Quick movement to newer areas and setting up groups, centres of learning in the newer areas. This helped in cross fertilization of ideas. From ten departments in 1965, the institute grew into more than 40 units now. Informal grouping was useful. 
Decision making capabilities.- Even a junior most faculty can independently decide on what he should do, how he should teach, how he should conduct research, how he should grow etc. Similarly, when industries came up with their problems , faculty could assess them, analyse them , decide on the issues, decide on solutions and come up with total solutions. This helped in creating a thriving industrial consultancy. 
Realism ,applications , helpfulness. 
Experimentation was given importance. 
Importance for intrinsic qualities and not external show. 

Greetings 
Dks
01/06/25, 5:25 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 731
I looked at the academic side of IISc.  So 
Let me talk a bit about the cultural ambience at IISc. It was quite rich, embracing many languages and regional cultures . We had several associations representing the culture of linguistic groups like Kannada Sangha, malayalam group, sanskrit group, Telugu samiti, Tamil peravai , Hindi group etc.  They organised many functions and cultural programs  and so you get a better understanding of the cultures. Your appreciation leads to tolerance.  Many giant literature writers and poets  visited and lectured . Ranade library contained thousands of books in different languages in addition to a large collection of English novels. 
This mixing of people from different places reveals different lifestyles. One thing the south Indians got upset about was seeing a north indian eating with their left hand , southerners consider it terrible. You learnt to ignore it and most north Indians over some time period also avoided that habit. 
One very important thing I learnt at IISc is to look at the intrinsic worth of an aspect and not the  outer shows. I recall an anecdote in Ramakrishna Paramahansa's life. He had three disciples.  One day he told his three disciples to go into the next room one by one and drink milk kept in tumblers. 
The first disciple went and drank milk from a gold tumbler , the second from a silver tumbler and the third from a copper tumbler. The milk was the same in all tumblers.
He asked his disciples,” how satisfied are you?”. The third one said that he was unhappy that others drank from gold and silver and he got only a copper one. The second one who drank from silver was partially unhappy. This shows how much importance we give for appearance. 
This behaviour is common even now. We go to a restaurant because of its great appearance , not because of the food served there. 
I imbibed this intrinsic look in my activities . Later when I went to hundreds of institutions to lecture or inspect , I always told them about this and said that choices are in the hands of an individual. He should decide whether he wants a beautifully packaged item or a good quality item. Even educational institutions try to attract students by showing their infrastructure , not their teaching capabilities. 
You may ask me how I look for intrinsic worth of a project or institution? 
I had some discussions with my friend Aravind of TCS about this . 
I will look for several things
1.Definitely outputs like products developed, employment status , no of distinctions , internal qualities like transparency, participation , good governance etc.
2.Publications and research areas as well as new research areas
3.transparency, participation , delegation and discussions as part of governance
4.impressions of peers and others
5 periodic reviews by outsiders and corrections
6.mindset of people - fixed , open, forward looking etc.
7.institutional history. 
8. Analysis of efforts 
9. Clarity on future directions. - I tell you one incident  that there was a mandate that all institutions should have vision and mission statements. Well dutifully many built a statement - may be copied and modified. But it was there on their display- no review was done to find out whether they followed them. No understanding of them by management, faculty and students. I consider this as great intellectual failure.
10. Even though I put it last , I consider this as the biggest malaise - the emphasis on discipline and control and the consequent fear it generates from these  focuses. Fear produces morons and slaves, not inquisitive , always searching for truth , and intelligent humans. 
Alister Mclean wrote a novel “ fear is the key” in the 60s. The title made an impression in me . I recall the first departmental faculty I attended after joining as a faculty. I was the junior most ,but expressed my opinions even in the first meeting,  even though I was sitting at the end of a big table  ,not very strongly but mildly and didn't allow it to die. I remember the reaction of Prof Joseph at the end of the meeting. He said,” You are fearless”. I cherish that statement. Many disliked me because of my openness. Some seniors wanted obedience,  not openness. 
Greetings
Dks

[31/05, 10:33 am] V.Sridhar Ex PESCE: 👆🏼 wonderful write up sir. This is a very interesting write up. This will be a road map for any instruction to grow and excel.

[31/05, 11:45 am] V.Sridhar Ex PESCE: The greatest lessons from IISc are not inscribed in its manuals, but in its quiet corners—in the way people work, collaborate, and think. An institution grows not just through plans and policies, but through a collective mindset built on seriousness, freedom, curiosity, forward-thinking, and realism.

Any institute that aspires to grow must do more than build departments or expand infrastructure. It must build culture. And culture is built in the unnoticed moments—how a junior faculty participates in meetings, how a technician cares for a machine, how decisions are made, and how freedom is honored.

This roadmap, inspired by IISc, shows that the heart of a great institution beats in its values. If followed with sincerity and adapted with contextual sensitivity, these principles can guide any institution toward lasting greatness.

Dr Chanakya has some deep comments:
“Fear, more often designated as hesitation, has almost always made us miss the bus carrying opportunities leaving us unhappy and unfulfilled in our yearning to rise in the academic ladder.  It always leaves a deep sense of regret and frustration for not acting it out when one could very well carry things forward.  Prof DKS Please discuss some details as to how such accumulated misses build up the frustration levels that need very strong internal efforts to break away from.

I believe that intricately connected to the inability to be fearless is also our inner fear to be proved wrong.  In such cases it is "safer to withhold" than to speak up/out.  Would you please elaborate as to how well connected it is to our paucity of the required type of humility.  

Alistair MacLean's "Fear is the key" was a powerful eye opener for me too.  A key character in this story 'Jablonsky' went along with an interesting motto in life "what is possible shall be done, what is impossible will be done".

Prof. please reflect up on the nexus between being fearless, hesitation in the face of being wrong and the type of humility needed to accept other's opinion, if they are better than yours.  The path of science and technology is replete with this "trianglular trials and trails".
 My response 
There are a number of points in the comments all centering around courage and determination and humility as three pillars. 
You have hit the nail where it hurts. 
Most people don't know themselves. That is the starting point and the lesson taught first to most middle class children - don't do this, that , don't talk to him, don't get involved , don't touch the insect, go away from places where people quarrel etc- a lot of nos.  It builds timidity and kills curiosity.So they doubt their capabilities. They don't know what they are capable of doing. So they don't want to experiment and venture out. Like the three monkeys , they run away from evil or suffer silently . That is why mafias thrive.  Not even they don't venture out , but they don't think about what is happening, what will happen and why all these are happening. They close their eyes and stay where they are, not interested in taking a step forward. It is what the youngsters call today a comfort zone. Is it a good strategy? It worked in the 20th century but will not work during the coming days . These people will be slaves throughout their life and never become leaders. 
Let me give a few incidents witnessed by me. I have come across hundreds . 
In the sixties , the institute was an open area without compound walls . So people from outside used to leave the cattle to graze near our hostels . The security persons were afraid of driving them as they had to face the wrath of the cattle owners when they went to Yeshwantpur. 
I had seen many Bangalore students refusing to take up jobs outside the city. I had argued with them , but without success. One of them when offered a promotion and transferred to Hyderabad, he didn't want to go and refused transfer. He was timid and comfortable. 
Recall the regime of Mr Gundu Rao as our CM. Mafia was ruling many areas like Yeshwantpur and subramania Nagar. KVIISc was threatened. Agitations and disruptions were common.  When Ramakrishna Hegde became the CM , he ordered all these to be rounded up and city became calm. Mr Sethu Rao who lived in Subramania Nagar told me that they rounded up six persons and everything was fine. Today , all cities are ruled by Mafias. CMs ignore that. 
Bangalore had seen many big and violent agitations and the police stood by. Why? They are not doing their duties due to fear. 
I saw courage for the first time in my life on foreign policy matters . Mr Jaishankar stood firmly against Europe, US and China courageously . His clarity was great.  
Many faculty don't open their mouths in most institutions. They define their boundaries, close their eyes  and don't see evil in the college. I have witnessed hundreds of such instances across the country. I didn't see this in many western countries.
Surprisingly ,I had two senior colleagues in the two departments - EE and CSA- I was associated with for decades , who were afraid of facing audiences and speaking in conferences. One didn't attend any conference and another didn't even teach properly. Don't mistake me , both are great researchers producing many PhDs. They didn't participate in discussions in the meetings. 
So it is clear that  fear destroys humanism .
Let us not loose hope. There are millions of courageous people. Courage is encouraged by open social networks today. 
More in the next.
Greetings 
Dks
04/06/25, 4:39 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 734
“I have striven not to laugh at human actions, not to weep at them, not to hate them, but to understand them.”
—Baruch Spinoza,
This quote is repeated again as it is relevant for our discussions on human actions. 
We need to understand that fear like all behavioural factors is not absolute. It varies according to situations . One may be forceful in professional life, but timid in family life. One may be forceful in individual discussions ,but silent in groups. One gets angry at unfairness to him, but quiet when unfairness happens for others - self interest. 
While fear is more generic and may be due to past behaviours and adrenaline generation of the hunter era , our dependence on fear today under various circumstances is inappropriate. We need to build strong corrective mechanisms at a young age. 
Here is where the importance of viveka - discrimination - as told by Sankara comes in. We have many complex behaviours resulting in different reactions and actions for each event happening in our life. The responses can be impulsive or thoughtful. We need to understand the overall situation ,analyse, find fault with our reasoning and make corrections. That is Viveka. 
Chanakya is right that fear has led to frustrations. Many people lost opportunities to advance their career because of not explaining their work properly , and because of wrong notions. Fear can lead to misunderstanding and problems of communication. I have seen frustrations and failures in the institute . Many don't have clarity of purpose in their work, in their approaches and in their thinking. They all need behaviour training and mental improvements.
But hesitation is more general . It occurs when you have to act or decide on a specific issue . It may happen due to many reasons like 
1.not sure of the facts
2.not sure of what will happen if I decide- a lack of clarity and analysis. I have seen some people act based on the clarity they generate on a situation. Two people that  I had interacted with come to my mind for their clarity of thinking. One is CNR Rao and another is Chandra, chairman , Tata sons. CNR is a quick thinker and acts fast. Chandra is  a clear and measured thinker and takes correct decisions. 
3.non availability of data - you are yet to collect data and so you  delay actions. Some researchers use non availability of data as an excuse for not working on a problem. Some want to do a complete literature survey and data collection and only after that will proceed with actions. These lead to considerable delays. 
4.limited views - not aware of what is happening and what will happen.
5. As TV Gopal says , fear of failure may be another reason. But it means that  you have ideas about the approaches to solve a problem . Research may lead to dead ends . One should be prepared for that. Our existing system accepts only successes and does  not accept a concluding statement from a researcher that a particular approach or a method does not work due to some reasons . A mindset change is needed here.
5. Timidity , fear are the other reasons for hesitation.
6. Procrastination is a habit with some. I know one chair of a department who will push all papers inside his table drawer without taking any action. Students used to pick their requests from his drawer and ask him to sign it. Decision making is not known to him. He was such a slow and timid person that I lost patience with him. 
What are the aspects which have to be rectified?
Improve the behaviour. 
Better thinking practices.
Better self awareness. Know your weaknesses
Better mentoring . This is totally missing in our system. Some seek advice from others, some listen to what is going on and understand it, some listen to others advice, some reject advice given with good intentions. Three major hurdles exist . One is  shyness - you don't know people, you can't assess people ,you don't get data , you misinterpret. So you make a mistake and go deeper into your shell.  Shyness needs a cure. Second is ego - at the other end . Ego clouds your mind. You think that you know everything and don't need to listen to others. This is the reason for problems encountered by many. Third is a closed mind. We need an open mind. 

Humility is definitely an important trait to have. Humility minimizes one's ego. It puts a check in our wrong thinking. It makes us to learn from failures. 
One example of humility at the leadership level that I have seen was an incident with Prof Dhawan, our director. 
He was supporting a particular change in a procedure in the senate. After hearing the opposition to the proposal from a few members , he told the senate that he would withdraw the bill and come back with a new modified one. We discussed our admission ranking parameters for several years and changed them regularly. It is the open mind that tells us that ego is bad and humility is desirable. 
Humility doesn't mean fickle minded person ,but it means that you build an opinion based on your perceptions and facts ,but be prepared to accept better suggestions from others. That means “ be open to ideas from others, be flexible, keep looking at your opinions for improvements.  Know that nothing is real and fixed all the time. Follow the Shankara - jagat Mitya - principle.”

Greetings
Dks
05/06/25, 4:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 735

In the world , nothing has more variety than the dynamic variations in human behaviours and moods  - developing habits and emotions , using and changing. Your actions depend on your behaviours. Life depends on this - not your behaviours alone . Your action depends on others behaviours and moods and your interactions with others Observations and communications play a major role in the behaviour development of a person. We miss this aspect in our teaching and learning program . Some parents train their children to have good habits and behaviours.  
Agatha Christie was a well known crime writer. But while I love Hercule Poirot,  I admire and analyze Miss Marple . The stories pertaining to Miss Marple are based on Observations of behaviours of normal people . The behaviours provided clues to solve murders. These books tell us the importance of understanding behaviours. 
Our behaviours come from our training and beliefs. Those with a flexible and open mind can change their beliefs and retrain to adapt to different situations. But many have fixed minds and follow their beliefs religiously. 
Most of us are led to a blinked life with very narrow views. This is not desirable ,but like cattle we are herded into a pen of society determined, science determined and education determined beliefs , notions and conjectures and loose independent and open ended  views and a broad world view. 
We are made into  conditioned and blinkered people. This destroys our independent thinking leading to a question - since a human is distinguished as a thinking animal and we lose that power , are we humans or simulated robots?
There is an urgent need to build an alternative free thinking curiosity building education system  - not a few schools but lakhs of schools. That is required to meet AI challenges and new jobs - skilling the mind ,not physical skills. We need mental education, not mechanical moronic education.

“How do you listen? Do you listen with your projections, through your projection, through your ambitions, desires, fears, anxieties, through hearing only what you want to hear, only what will be satisfactory, what will gratify, what will give comfort, what will for the moment alleviate your suffering? If you listen through the screen of your desires, then you obviously listen to your own voice; you are listening to your own desires. And is there any other form of listening? Is it not important to find out how to listen not only to what is being said but to everything— to the noise in the streets, to the chatter of birds, to the noise of the tramcar, to the restless sea, to the voice of your husband, to your wife, to your friends, to the cry of a baby? “
Jiddu Krishnamurti

Dear Sir!

My response
Thanks. Prashant. 
I have earlier talked about leadership role , some great indian leaders and the lack of vision with most Indian leaders not only in the academic field but also in research, politics and industries. 
Most Indian leaders are administrators . That is the tragedy in this country. We give phenomenal emphasis for rules and administrators. They love it and it gives them control over others.  That is ruining us. 
First a leader should have courage. He should run a participative,  open and transparent governance system . 
A leader should have viveka - discriminatory power. 
If a leader is good, the unit under him runs well. 
If a leader is transformative, he changes an institution or government. I gave examples of Prof Prahlada Rao of MSRIT, Ramakrishna Hegde, ex CM of karnataka in this category. 
If a leader is great, he is visionary,  he builds a futuristic unit. Dr Bhabha, Dr Sarabhai, Prof Dhawan, Mr Chandrasekharan.
What the country needs is a lot of visionary leaders and we don't have them or more correctly we  don't search for them and choose them. Vice chancellors are chosen by caste and money . We find a goal setting leader in Modi ,the first PM to set goals. No academic leaders set long term research goals.

07/06/25, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 737
“Whatever the mind can conceive and believe, it can achieve.” it is the secret to all that has been and all that will ever be. Yes, critical thinking is the secret. The human mind is so powerful and it is factual that everything that’s happening in our life is attracted by our thoughts. Thoughts become things.”
Christ Lewis

Before I go for more comments , let me get back to IISc and its important demerit . 
While academically IISc ticked all quality boxes , it has its warts also. That is what Mr Chandrasekhar referred to in his comments.
Academics needed a supportive and understanding administration and a fast acting positive finance. 
We initially had no separate finance department.  Administration was very minimal. 
So things were smooth. All decisions were done at the department level. We didn't depend on a central admin . It was a great period. 
SS Prabhu as registrar, a clever devil ,wanted to grab power as much as possible. 
Though prof Dhawan was the director, and a dominant one, Mr Prabhu had worked with AG Pai as registrar. Pai was known to be a terror and didn't care for the then director ,Prof Bhagawantam . Prabhu couldn't ignore the director ,so he diplomatically maneuvered the rules and built a big structure with many officers. One single admin office became eight units and from one assistant registrar, the officer's numbers went up to seven  in his tenure. He built a huge administration structure. That started the problems for the institute. Today the admin occupies several buildings. It has grown huge. It created labour problems leading to the creation of unions and agitations. 
Control of administration depended only on the director initially . So they didn't serve the institute but treated themselves as masters. If a director understands the role of administration,  then the institute can function efficiently. If a director is weak , administration overrides him. 
We had only two directors who were powerful enough to control the administration.  They are Prof Dhawan and Prof CNR Rao. Even a weak director like  Prof Ramaseshan did well and created a chief of administration post and appointed a professor in that position. Many senior faculty didn't support him. But he was active , understood the ethos and planned for growth. 
Ramaseshan's short period saw great growth. A large number of centres came up. He set up executive committees to reform the library and computer centre. I was part of many reform activities of Prof Ramaseshan.  He planned for a super computer. Part of admin control went to the new chief. As a whole his governance was research and academic growth focused. Only a few buildings had come up in his time. CES , centre for ecological sciences and CAS ,centre for atmospheric sciences were started during his tenure. I became a member of CES. 
Some other directors understood the importance of academics and didn't bother about admin.
But some directors didn't understand the ethos of IISc and they left all governance to the administration.  It affected our research and academics . It led to stagnation.  It was a tragedy. 
I call some regimes golden periods and some dark periods. 
Our admin is known for its inefficiency - it misses papers . It even missed my life certificate once and stopped pension payments- . Administration delayed processing papers. Delays become epidemic. 
Our bills and schemes units were not fully organized. With ERP in place, I hope there is improvement in the functioning of the institute. 
Another problem I see and feel sad about is the fact that the institute, from a natural paradise, is becoming a slum - too many buildings, too crowded, less open space, poor drainage network . Buildings are not planned properly. There is no soul or synergy in our expansions. So  the institute is now an architectural chaos . 
But one reason the institute survived was the committee structure expanded by Prof Dhawan.  Most activities were decided by committees. Admin implemented the decisions. So faculty, especially the chair of the committees, had control over the implementation. I  was the chair of power committee, time table committee, central evaluation committee , grievance committee , campus amenities committee , CSA curriculum committee and CS programs development committee and member of several committees. This structure gave opportunities for faculty to develop policies, to decide on many matters of academics and governance, and to get to know the culture of IISc.  
But I have to say this as an observation of mine:
Faculty did their job even when directors didn't direct.. They did their research. They were active and participative . So the institute didn't suffer. It went on growing academically. Its status as number one in the national ranking for the past years proves this. 
Admin delayed decisions. But Some technical staff are identified by departments to chase the files. So the follow up was very good. The reason for this is the fact that while central admin is not responsive, the departmental admins who were controlled by the chair and faculty, were quite active and responded fast . They helped in the smooth running of the department.

08/06/25, 4:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 738

Listening is an art not easily come by, but in it there is beauty and great understanding. 
We listen with the various depths of our being, but our listening is always with a 
preconception or from a particular point of view. We do not listen simply; there is always 
the intervening screen of our own thoughts, conclusions, and prejudices...To listen there 
must be an inward quietness, a freedom from the strain of acquiring, a relaxed attention. 
This alert yet passive state is able to hear what is beyond the verbal conclusion. Words 
confuse; they are only the outward means of communication; but to commune beyond the 
noise of words, there must be in listening an alert passivity. Those who love may listen; 
but it is extremely rare to find a listener. Most of us are after results, achieving goals; we 
are forever overcoming and conquering, and so there is no listening. It is only in listening 
that one hears the song of the words.
Jiddu Krishnamurti

My response
I am glad that some of you find my posts meaningful. It kindles the memory for some, gives ideas to some, opens the eyes for some. 
Mathew implementation is not in my hands unfortunately. The younger persons should take it forward. My only hope for several decades was that some institution will aspire to rise to the  IISc level. I tried for decades seriously .But Most leaders felt diffident , found excuses and said that it can't happen and can't be done. Only recently I came across a HoD in Reva university who showed interest . SDM dharwad had some inclinations and interest but didn't take it further. 
I have not just given lectures in colleges and universities,  but also observed them and interacted with hundreds of universities, colleges and NITs and found huge walls of resistance. Let me quote an example. When I told a simple thing on the curriculum that the engineering syllabus is stagnant for 50 years despite new courses and disciplines being regularly added , the response from a professor shocked me. He said the syllabus has stood the test of time and so there is no need to change it. Even now  the data structures , operating systems and data bases courses contain  the same aspects that I taught in the seventies and eighties . 
While I have hundreds of encounters in hundreds of institutions , let me recount a few. 
I saw Prof Nityanandan taking TCE Madurai to a research culture. He started research activities there. Then I interacted with Prof Srinivasan a lot. Later, I had interactions with Abhaykumar and Raja of TCE and both showed a lot of interest. I gave a number of lectures . But while management support was easy to get, senior faculty lacked interest and motivation. It was a hard ask. 
I recall my friend KA Krishnamurthy , principal at MCE Hassan and SSIT Tumkur, listened to me a lot and was a good researcher , but again the story was the same. He had struggles in his colleges. I had a long interaction with PESCE Mandya with both Sridhar and Ravindra as principals. We did achieve a few things there with their cooperation. Teaching processes were improved. Research culture was initiated thanks to TEQIP.The student project works were transformed to a higher level with holistic interdisciplinary approaches. They showed a lot of enthusiasm to go for patents. 
Even a good institution like BVB Hubli with Prof Shetter as principal and BV Desai as registrar  had a problem. When I told the faculty that they should look for engineering in the large and computer students should write programs with 50000 lines of code, the response was total disbelief. So they shun me now. 
Well I can go on with many incidents of  my partly  successful efforts to improve our institutions. But the few gives an idea about the difficulties encountered by me. 
I found the reasons for my partial successes.
1.Most faculty had a totally limited inward look and understanding. 
2.They are happy with college and university and don't have an urge to look beyond and do better. 
3. They had commitment to what they taught and were satisfied with that. No other goals. 
4. Excuse was another reason not to do any new experiments , not to change course curriculum and not to do research until forced to do something. 
Dependence on higher authority is high . Every initiative should come from the top. That is a big problem with us. 
Lack of initiative. I used to say that Most Indians retire at the age of 30 years mentally if not physically. This is a disease. Startups changed this Indian Ness.
Most importantly,  fear of losing jobs, promotions , and perks was an important reason. This colonial mindset needs to go. Institutions don't have a nice, friendly, communicative , fearless working environment. 
What I said applies to industries also. The 21st century has given a wakeup call. Let us heed to it. 
Be agile, be prepared for changes, accept changes, and adapt fast for survival as a human thinking person.

“Dear Anna,
Namaskarams.
Your recent posts about your early experiences at IISC and the ambience of Bangalore are deeply engrossing.Your vivid description of  the various aspects of the Bangalore of yesteryears, such as the climate, transport, interconnectivity, language, diverse population, culture. food etc etc makes me feel as though I am  reading a novel by the famous tamil author 'Kalki', His skillful narration of historical events integrated local customs,traditions and landscapes ,making the story hugely enjoyable and intellectually stimulating and transported one to those early times in history. My response 
Thanks.
It is good to read your comments. I am glad about your memories of the old Bangalore.  
It makes me ponder over an important question - what is life about? There are hundreds of answers to this question. 
I will look at different aspects of this in subsequent posts. 
I want to give one relevant answer here. 
Life is about small pleasures and long memories. 
What you wrote talks about these small pleasures. 
Most people don't understand the important role of small pleasures in our life. Our survival and daily life is sweetened by many small and big pleasures. You see a rose blooming in the garden. You feel happy. You feel the rain falling and are happy. 
So hundreds of small pleasures make up the happiness in  our life. I remember our grandmother saying at the end of a day that everything went off smoothly. And you should see the great relief in her eyes when she is through with a function . She used to say ,” thank God everything went off well without a hitch. “
What is important is to remember these small pleasures regularly . That is nostalgia. Most of us revisit our school life, hostel life , and family life with happiness. There are some who don't understand this . They ignore these small pleasures. They worry all the time. 
Small pleasures are easy . A nice lunch gives us small pressure. Appreciation from elders gives us pleasure. We should remember them so that life becomes joyful. 
Even reading serials like washingtonil thirumanam and books gives us pleasure. 
I get a great pleasure reading PG Wodehouse. I get a lot of pleasure when I answer a tough question in a class or seminar, when past  students remember my teaching after several years , when you see a friend  and both  express joy , when I  talk to my  children and relatives , when I hear the voice of my grandsons etc- i get endless small pleasures of these kinds. I had hundreds of encounters with my past students in airports, hotels , and even tourist places like Niagara falls. They reminise their experiences with me and that gives me great joy. . All my encounters with my past students are memorable. 
I get not a small but a great pleasure when I counsel a young high schooler on the future paths and directions. 
Definitely we got bigger joys while getting admissions, and jobs, and getting a child, getting children through graduation etc. 
Life is full of joys and some pains. 
How many of us reflect on this?

10/06/25, 4:56 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 740
“Listening itself is a complete act; the very act of listening brings its own freedom. But are you really concerned with listening, or with altering the turmoil within? If you would listen, sir, in the sense of being aware of your conflicts and contradictions without forcing them into any particular pattern of thought, perhaps they might altogether cease. You see, we are constantly trying to be this or that, to achieve a particular state, to capture one kind of experience and avoid another, so the mind is everlastingly occupied with something; it is never still to listen to the noise of its own struggles and pains. Be simple...and don’t try to become something or to capture some experience.”
Jiddu Krishnamurti 

Dr Babu also confirms the lack of motivation and planning in our colleges. 
Let me sum up again for clarity. 
We have some excellent educational institutions, mostly funded by the central government.  But BITS Pilani, TISS, MIDS ,TAPMI , XLRI, VIT, SRM, and many colleges in Delhi, Mumbai, Pune were good ones with private management. Many state government colleges have poor quality consciousness.so it is not correct to say private colleges are bad and government colleges are good. There are a lot of bad apples. 
Why are the colleges bad? Managements don't spend money. Faculty find excuses for not looking forward. Syllabus is dated. 
The most important reason is fear. Most managements and colleges don't have any governance mechanism. So no faculty take responsibility in improving the institution . A small push will get wonders. We don't do that push. We stay where we are - stagnant. 
Another reason is the frog in the well thinking . Futuristic vision is lacking. National ranking is useful. The magazine's rankings are a big fraud. 
Most faculty retire mentally by the age of 35 years.
But students are not bad. More than 50% can learn 20% more than the curriculum. We need to create opportunities for extra learning. Most students learnt programming, databases from private tutorial institutions in the 70s and 80s. 
Around 20% of students in engineering colleges get distinctions and get jobs and grow up well. We miss this. So there are many silver lines in the sky. Let us use them. 
We need a strong and regular training on motivation, thinking, and concepts of learning to the faculty.
Many schools experiment alternate approaches. But they are too small. We need thousands of schools looking at alternate ways of teaching.
NEP is a good approach to move forward. But politics is destroying the good points. 
That Closes our discussion on fear and its impacts .


Let me return to our main topic IISc and Bangalore in the 1965 period. 
I liked the lectures by faculty and also by visiting experts. I liked our interstate and interlanguage discussions in the department with my classmates and in the hostel and mess. I visited the library to browse and read journals and books. I borrowed books from Ranade library of gymkhana. 
But towards the end of 1965, I saw some big changes in the Indian situation. War with Pakistan started and the news was not good. Indian army was ill prepared for the war. We had a weak government in the centre . It was an interim government headed by Lal Bahadur Shastri. The old guard in congress headed by Kamaraj, Nijalingappa etc tried to control the government . Economy was in bad shape. Our foreign exchange was in a precarious situation. 
I saw poverty and scarcity during the war time and independence period - 1940 to 1950. The situation in 1966 was similar. This continued till 1977 during the next regime of Indira Gandhi. 
The war resulted in scarcity of food grains. There was a scarcity of rice. So the mess gave us a limited quantity of rice - two cups. Tuesdays were declared as  no dinner days. You can't get dinner even in hotels and trains. These were government instructions to be obeyed by all. USSR intervened and negotiated peace between India and Pak  with an agreement known as the Tashkent agreement. Shastri died in Tashkent under unexplainable circumstances. The country quickly moved from a stable government to chaos. Fortunately we survived as wheat production went up. 
1966 saw changes in IISc  and in me also. 
Our mess was overcrowded and and provided one kind of meal only. So it was decided to set up another mess - called B mess. Most Telugus migrated to that mess hoping to convert it into an Andhra mess. About 150 people joined that mess. I continued with A mess. Our mess bill went up by about 20% due to this split. The only difference between the two messes was on vegetables - A mess used pumpkins and B mess potatoes. The breakfast menu was changed - now no  idly , but uthappam and masala Dosa continued as in A mess. 
Since the hostel was crowded and that in turn restricted our intake , the institute decided to construct two new blocks - M and N and an apartment block for married research students. Similarly the institute started the construction of the gymkhana building on the opposite side of the road after the Porcelain factory. As I said before we started a coffee place serving coffee, the first one in the campus. From 1966 till date , if something is a steady activity it is the coffee shop appearing in different appearances but being there always. 
More trees were planted in the institute.

“Evaluate your weaknesses and strengths in thinking. The following are questions you might ask yourself.
 What was on my mind today? Did negative thoughts disturb me today?
 Did I figure anything out today?
 When did I do my best thinking today?
 When did I do my worst?
 Have I done something beneficial to my goals today?
 If I had a chance to repeat the day, what would I do differently? Why?
 If every day was lived in this manner for a year, will something be accomplished at the end of the year?”
Christ Lewis

My life changed in 1966. From a student, I got transformed into a researcher. I was still a student studying in the  second year ME .  I was a keen observer of academic processes , activities and teaching methods and kept thinking about them and asking the question - why?I analysed the teaching processes and liked many things like
1.evaluation - continuous, instructor dependent , experimented like opening book exams , - instructor is supreme policy.
2.deep analytical knowledge of the faculty teaching the courses and depth of learning.
3.being kept busy throughout the semester with tests, assignments, term papers.
4. Term papers induce us to look deep into a topic, look for current developments , put together and write a long review. We learnt a lot with this approach. In the second semester - Jan to April 1966- i did a course with Prof KS Prabhu on instrumentation. We had to write a term paper by choosing a modern topic, selecting references , reading the papers, writing abstraction and putting them together. I took up an emerging topic - digital transducers. Even digital computers were emerging then. It was an audacious decision on my part. The topic was unknown . So I entered untested waters, put in more than 60 hours in reading more than 25 papers and writing their abstract . I classified and grouped the subtopics. Then I put these together  into a complete review. If we had computing surveys then, I could have published it. I submitted. When Prof Prabhu returned the term papers, he retained mine saying it will help his course in the coming years. He also praised my efforts and my review. I was literally on the moon that day. This episode taught me several things. If there is a will ,there is a way . It boosted my confidence. It excited me to traverse new areas. It removed my fear of unknowns. It built my writing skills. This opened my eyes to research. I followed this approach of term paper in all my courses. Till this time ,I wasn't thinking of research as an option. From then on it played a significant part in my job selection. 
5. Understanding of topics was good except the field theory course. 
6. I learnt time management as a young boy from my uncle while assisting him in conducting two major public functions. Later I created a time schedule for my studies. Here at IISc,  I saw its enforcement by instructors.  
Some drawbacks seen by me in the  teaching process were 
1.Most teachers went by text book. The why aspect was not explained. For example, the power systems analysis course didn't tell us the reason - what the purpose is in doing the short circuit studies. The course didn't talk about loadflows, not even a hint. It was a fortunate circumstance that i read papers about load flow and it became my work for research and publications from 1966 onwards.
2.Most teaching emphasized mathematical modeling and problem solving ignoring the physics of the problem. It was difficult for some students to understand and comprehend. But there were a lot of good books available in the library so that interested students could clarify their doubts and increase their knowledgebase. 
3.While most teachers were sincere , regular for their lectures and came prepared , there was a small minority who were good researchers but unfortunately were afraid of lecturing.  They skipped classes. 
By and large , the teaching processes were good , motivational and increased one's thinking and absorption capabilities. This is something I didn't see in most Indian institutions.

Dks

“After all, you only learn when you give your whole being to something. When you give your whole being to mathematics, you learn; but when you are in a state of contradiction, when you do not want to learn but are forced to learn, then it becomes merely a process of accumulation. To learn is like reading a novel with innumerable characters; it requires your full attention, not contradictory attention. If you want to learn about a leaf—a leaf of the spring or a leaf of the summer—you must really look at it, see the symmetry of it, the texture of it, the quality of the living leaf. There is beauty, there is vigor, there is vitality in a single leaf. So to learn about the leaf, the flower, the cloud, the sunset, or a human being, you must look with all intensity.”
Jiddu Krishnamurti 

I wasn't thinking of research as a career option till the end of 1965. The term paper I wrote in Jan- may 1966 gave me a lot of confidence and a thrill about research. I enjoyed that kind of activity. Further, I developed my methods for doing research- literature search in a directed manner, grasping a paper and not just copying it, finding the essence of the paper etc. Also i realised that i have to build a strong work culture - studying for hours with deep concentration and with rest pauses. This work culture is an essential part of research. Then I learnt to question - what and why . I developed all these qualities  due to the great research ambience and journals at IISc. 
I had already realised that  industry jobs were routine based on my experiences at Easun for one year. I didn't like routine jobs and found them boring. Also , industry jobs don't allow you to think and do things differently. You lose freedom and follow an established procedure. Actually ,Mr S Balasubramanian,  my chief engineer in Easun was a curious and innovative person . He was thinking about studying the effect of wind on transformer temperature rise. I got involved in the design and experimentation of a different type of meter. That was for a month . Otherwise my remaining 11 months in the factory were boring. To spend time, I did odd jobs like designing a new substation- not an easy one for a novice with no idea of design, participating in an exhibition etc. I did a new thing - built a detailed assets register of all equipment in the factory. That was a period when the concept of asset management was not known and I a fresher from college did it singly without any support.   But no understanding and appreciation came from others. So i rejected industry jobs though most seniors got jobs with Tata electric company.  I was moving towards a research career. 
A second booster happened during this period . We were supposed to identify a project area / topic and select a faculty to guide the project. We should complete the selection by April 1966 so that we can start work in the summer vacation. I was thinking about the project work. I met Prof HNR our head and he suggested that I meet Dr Dharma Rao as he has a good problem for the project. As per his advice, I met Dr Dharma Rao. He has worked on control theory applications to power systems. He didn't teach any course. He gave me a paper on control systems stability using the Lyapunov model , asked me  to read it and suggested that I should apply that method for power systems. At that time, I didn't have any idea about stability or power system stability. That course was scheduled for the second year of ME. So I started attending Dr MD  Srinath's course on control systems as an audit course outside my curriculum. 
While I was busy with my term paper research which I took seriously, I took this challenge seriously and started learning about control systems stability . Both were going on simultaneously along with my course work.The department had a very strong research base in control systems.So  I talked to Dr Deekshatulu also . I was getting knowledge on stability from a control systems point of view, not from power systems point of view. 
I worked hard  on the  project throughout the summer vacation - a period of three months from May to July end with one month of vacation spent at katpadi where my parents moved in at that time. I stopped going to the village now .  
I completed the project by summer's end . By this time , I got some ideas about computers . I knew about computers while doing my BE. I visited the engineering college in Guindy in 1964 and saw the IBM 1620 used for data processing mostly. Railways also was thinking of introducing computers and my cousin Subramanian told me about it in 1964. I had read about computers . But I got a better idea in 1966 after reading many books on computers at IISc library. I wanted to work on computers and look at a new area that none was working in India.  
Let me continue in the next post my greatest transformation into computer research.
