“Vātsyāyana’s commentary: When an object is comprehended through a knowledge source (pramāṇa), it becomes possible to engage in successful goal-directed activity. Thus, a knowledge source is useful (arthavat). Without a knowledge source, there would be no effective cognition of an  obj ect. Without such cognition, there would be no  successful action. (Commentary 1.1.1) “
Nyaya shastra 

As told in this sutra , I found my goal in life - to be a researcher. I also got an area for long term research - computer science. As the first step told in the sutra is to find a good and true knowledge source. I found the best in India at IISc.  This source is not just a great library with lakhs of books and journals ,but also a great intellectual ambience for learning and research. In  the next step  I moved towards using the source effectively. 
I should confess here. When I write about the happenings during this period , it looks like it was a well planned and executed set of actions. That is not true. This is a justification after the occurrence of these events. I am writing it as if everything fell in place nicely like in a story. I am writing a story. 
 I didn't plan and didn't know what to do and how to do it. I had no idea about my life. I was a confused person. It was pure serendipity that helped me. Fortuous circumstances came my way and I grabbed the opportunities. I didn't hesitate to talk to the faculty and students . I gathered a lot of information from these discussions. To give you an idea ,I spent a minimum of an hour everyday talking to classmates and hostelmates in the mess and many faculty members like Prof HNR, Prof  Devanathan, Prof AR Vasudevamurthy . I listened to many things happening in the institute and contemplated life in general. So I had a good idea about life , but no concrete plans about my future course. Maybe things were agitating in my subconscious mind and pushing me forward. That consciousness is what is known as Sat, one form of Brahman. I didn't and don't claim to know that consciousness . I believe that it took me on the right path. All that I write now  are my reflections and explanations of my life in 1966..
Let me explain the transformation in me. Two changes happened.
1.I became clear in my mind that I can do research and should get a research oriented  job. 
2.My future  area of activity - computers - was decided at this point. 
So after summer vacation, I met Dr Dharma Rao  and told him that I had completed the project assigned by him in the summer itself. So I wanted  to do another project. He gave me a paper , but he didn't have any idea about the topic or the approach in the paper. The IEEE paper by Ward and Hale opened  my eyes to two areas - computerised problem solving , numerical solution of power systems problems starting with load flow solutions. I got excited about the prospect of work and then started worrying about the feasibility of doing the project.
A run of the mill project will be easy to do with a grade guaranteed. You have no challenges. I had no interest in that. I wanted to take up something new. But this is getting into deep waters without knowing about the water qualities and my capabilities. I had confidence that I could do it. 
But the first hurdle was that  I needed to work on a computer. IISc didn't have a computer then. It acquired one only in 1972. 
I found out that NAL had a computer and they gave us access to their computer. It was a second generation machine - very small compared to today's computers- it operated at millisecond speed and had a 4k word memory. It didn't have a programming language. So I had to write programs with a primitive instruction set.  Actually the machine was an improved version of Alan Turing's design. Travel to NAL wasn't easy. I had to change two buses to go to the main campus of NAL and then  go by NAL bus to their Belur campus . So it took a lot of time - more than two hours one way. Lastly, computer time was charged and I had to pay for it. These are logistics and finance issues . I was yet to get into the actual efforts required for the project - modeling, numerical methods, programming , data.  
I had to manage the logistics issue and spend time on travel. But I couldn't afford the computer hire charges. 
So I talked to Prof HNR about the finance to hire computer time.

“Is not the knowledge of words and their meanings acquired without the study of Grammar? Cannot the knowledge of material substances be acquired without logical discussions? Cannot the rituals, practices and ceremonies (i.e., the practical part of religion or Karmakāṇḍa) be known without Mīmāṃsā philosophy (i.e., the Pūrva Mīmāṃsā of Jaimini)? Cannot the frailty and destructibility of material body (and earthly possessions) be realised without the Vedānta philosophy (i.e., the Uttara Mīmāṃsā of Vyāsa, which treats of the eternal verities of the universe)?”
Sukra niti

I didn't realise that my talk with Prof HNR will lead me to another new and developing area - schemes funded by external agencies. At that time it wasn't well known . The central board of irrigation and power , an autonomous  research funding agency of the central government , decided to fund only two academic institutions in the country - Roorkee university and IISc. It funded mostly state electricity boards and water resources departments for research.  The funds were given to three departments in IISc- civil and hydraulics, electrical and high voltage. 
My work came in handy for funding. Only two projects got funds then - one was Prof Parthasarathy , who was a faculty doing PhD at that time and mine, an ME student. I got 10000 rupees, a very big sum . It was Prof HNR who told me about this possibility. I accepted this great offer . The problem of funding was resolved. But IISc rules ,as I told you before, got stricter. We had to get prior approval before spending it. So Prof HNR ,who was unhappy with this new rule of approval before spending, got approval from the registrar for 5000 rupees expenditure so that  I can use computers without monetary restrictions. I got a rupee for each day's travel expenses as a student. It just met my BTS bus charges . 
Still I was extremely lucky.  I was the only ME student to get external funds for my project. No one got this opportunity. Later I got funds from CBIP for my computer and travel expenses during my  PhD period also. I had CBIP sponsorship till 1973 . My association with CBIP was a memorable one. 
I visited NAL and met Mr Janardhan and Mr Panchapakesan in the computer centre. They became friendly,  but my helping were two very nice and helpful people operating the computers, Mr Nagaraja and Mr Ananthakrishna . They gave a manual about the Ferranti Sirius computer , its organization, its registers, memory  etc. It also had an introduction to programming in the computer. It had instructions similar to machine language only . Input is through a paper tape. It had only five punches meaning we could type 32 characters. So we had to use a shift register . All variables started with v - like v1,v2 etc. All these four became my friends for more than a decade. Dr Sankar, the centre head and later my thesis oral examiner was in the UK doing PhD in numerical mathematics with Prof Fox. 
I read the manual, tried to understand programming  and wrote a simple 10 line  program , but it didn't work. I modified the program and after a few modifications it worked. 
Now I worked on the loadflow model based on the paper by Ward and Hale ,  wrote the equations and solved them using an electromechanical calculator to get a feel of the iterative method. 
Then I wrote a program for the load flow model ,  and ran it a couple of times to debug it and finally  got  the correct results. It took a few weeks. I got my first result and was thrilled. This happened in feb1967. I used the remaining two months for experimentation - solving large problems - a realistic 40 node power grid . I tried several new algorithms - it became a numerical algorithms development project. I moved from Gauss Seidel to Newton Raphson method. I suddenly find many researchers abroad working on the solution of load flows. 
All my experiments showed that computational errors, particularly round off errors, affected the solutions for large problems. The computer had a 16bits arithmetic and so round off  errors were high. Convergence was difficult. I tried several convergence criteria and several modifications to the model. 
Based on these,  I wrote a paper for the CBIP annual conference and also a journal paper by June 1967. By then,  Dharma Rao left for a job in Canada.

“Vātsyāyana inaugurates his commentary by noting a close tie between knowledge sources and successful action. He notes that in veridical cognition, four factors all come  together: the knower, the thing known, the means of knowing, and the knowledge itself. For knowledge to occur, these four must be properly related to each other. Centrally important here is the notion of “usefulness” (arthavat). The term arthavat has a semantic range that includes “effective,” “accurate,” “successful,” “significant,” and “fitting.” To say that a knowledge source is useful means that it serves our purposes because it functions properly in revealing  objects.”
Vatsyayana     Nyaya 

My project work gave me a lot of excitement. I had a lot of difficulties in programming- debugging-, looking at solving a large problem numerically - class room exercises dealt with small sized problems with three or four variables and here I was dealing with a problem of 100 to 200 variables, travel etc . I didn't worry about troubles and went about solving them . So to a large extent ,I was   convinced that research is my cup of tea .
I also understood that research is a quest to reach a goal - the  goal is not clear sometimes, paths  unknown- , and a researcher's job is to discover the path to reach the goal . The path you take may be wrong and you have to retrace the path. The path may have unexpected obstacles. You have to discover a new route to handle the obstacles.
Consider one obstacle that  I encountered: the major problem of errors - computational errors. Later computer designers tried to increase the word length to 32 bits, 64 bits and 128bits etc to reduce this error and help in solving large problems with 1000 variables. But at that time, I had to find a different solution. I tried double length arithmetic .
This area chosen by me for project work was a great learning for me. It was a three way interdisciplinary project - computer based algorithms for problem solving, power systems modeling and analysis, numerical methods for large problems with implications of convergence and errors. 
I knew it was a big challenge , but liked it. At no point in time, I felt like giving it up. The surprising aspect was my evaluation. None understood my work except Prof HNR. But they appreciated my work as a  new work. They didn't see its future potential. That puzzled me. For the next 10 years and afterwards ,computer science went through a fast pace of innovations and discoveries, but many didn't see its potential in India and in IISc.  Even in the mid seventies, the computer society conventions were filled with mostly business users and only a few academics. Still I got the highest mark for my project work and the medal for scoring highest marks in ME. The institute awarded me a medal,  but didn't give me a medal. It was virtual. 
This experience made me firm to get a research job. But there was a dilemma . Where to apply?
I didn't want to apply for a job in Tata Electric . 
Many rank students applied to major schools - universities in the US. I was tempted , but my finances were not sound enough to pay for travel , application fees etc. Even sending applications to a few schools was expensive . So I decided to look for a job in India. 
 So I applied for a post in a newly established research institute at Bhopal by Heavy Electricals and got an offer. 
Prof HNR didn't want to lose me and told me to join for research.  I said that I needed a job to support my family. He said ,” you join as a research student and I will make you a lecturer as soon as a vacancy comes.” He told me that he would  inform Mr Swayambhu that he won't release me to join Bhopal. So I joined the department as a research student  after my ME. 
First there was no gap. My ME scholarship ended on July 31st and the research scholarship started from August 1, 1967. I didn't change my hostel room. The admission process was the same but was easier for me. I had to handover receipts to various places, that's all. I was allotted a place in the big room opposite to the head's room. So HNR used to call me often. I made him my guide. 
I applied for a UGC senior fellowship which was giving a monthly fellowship plus research grants. I got it. So from September first, I became a UGC senior fellow.I had the CBIP grant also available.  So there was no problem for funds for computer work. 
I didn't choose any new topic but worked on loadflows.

16/06/25, 4:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 746
Let me pause here and reflect on the above discussion on project work for my ME degree . Is it very important? 
My answer is yes. It reveals some essential qualities for decision making . It clarifies the distinction between information, knowledge, decisions, actions and process. It is a simple problem.
Let me rephrase the question- 
Why and how did I decide on this project topic - a totally new and unknown area?
I want to construct the process followed by me in my mind.
I consider working on the project as action.
This action of implementing a project is initiated by a trigger - requirements for doing a project work. 
The action  was preceded by a decision - topic selection.
Let me define the steps taken by me. 
My data, knowledge ,guiding principles and application comes into play in my thinking and decisioning process.
Step 1. I needed to collect data - topics in areas .
How do I get this- by searching papers in journals.  This is information collection , not knowledge. 
That means I am looking at existing work . 
Step 2:  I apply my knowledge to filter the information and extract the relevant information. Here is my first principle of  knowledge to filter and extract - relevance, usefulness, feasible.
My knowledge is limited- that means my extraction may be not fully knowledgeable.  
Step 3: So I will go to the next step. 
So I talked to the faculty - Prof HNR,Dr Dharma Rao. 
They gave focused topics using their search, filter and knowledge.  
So I get a few better choices
Step 4: Now I use my knowledge and guiding principles to select. I tried to understand the implications by asking the questions
How is the problem - meaningful ,acceptable, technically sound? 
Is the problem recent, relevant?
How does one plan it? Is it doable ? Does it have enough scope ? 
Why this problem? Is it futuristic? Is it multidisciplinary? Is it challenging?
Step5 : I assess the overall situation. 
1.Problem is complex, new ,needs computer , needs lots of learning in new areas.  No one is there to discuss or guide,- that is analysis to identify potential obstacles 
2.my confidence level - can i do it or not. Can I take risks? 
Steps 2,4,5 are analysis and thinking steps - they constitute my knowledge level. Now the distinction between information and knowledge will be clear ,I hope. Knowledge is processed ,usable ,applicable information. Knowledge is a mental process and it leads to action. Let us not be confused about the differences between these three terms. 
I also introduced a fourth term called process. That refers to the steps in arriving at a decision. 
Let me move  to Step 6. I weigh the various factors using some guiding principles. These principles evolve through our learning , and listening to knowledge sources like gurus. You get maximum knowledge not by reading books but by listening to scholars and understanding the implications. 
I used a few principles to decide
1.is it exciting , challenging ? Are the requisite facilities and resources available?
2.can I do it in the time frame ? How good is my confidence level?
3. Will this lead to new knowledge for me and others?
4.Will this be a futuristic one? Does it take me forward?
5.  Is it a mediocre project or a good one or a great one ? Will it satisfy my curiosity? 
Step 7: Arrive at a decision. You may use your knowledge to arrive at a reasonably good decision. 
So we need to act based on a trigger. That involves decisions. So you collect information , process it using your mental facilities to produce a derived , filtered and relevant knowledge,  analyse it to arrive at decisions. The whole thing is a process. Hope it is clear.
Greetings 
Dks
17/06/25, 4:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 747
‘loneliness is ‘an enduring condition of emotional distress that arises when a person feels estranged from, misunderstood, or rejected by others and/or lacks appropriate social partners for desired activities, particularly activities that provide a sense of social integration and opportunities for emotional intimacy’.
Prof Lars Andersson 
We can now get a clearer understanding of what knowledge is.  
Knowledge is instantaneous processing of information in the mind in a general sense. A simple series of actions we do mentally to convert information to knowledge is
Search and select the information we need for the situation in hand
Filter the information
Get more information
Group and link  the information 
Find the truth of the information 
Reason the information to get the result you want. 
A simple example is 
You are hungry. That is the trigger for action. You go to a restaurant to eat. 
First you list what food items you like.
Next get the menu - items available in the restaurant 
Find the pros and cons of each item
Find the taste component of each item
Find the health aspects of each item
Find the reasonable of cost 
Now you have knowledge about the food items there . So select the items that you want to eat. That is the decision.  It took sometime for you to get the requisite knowledge. 
Remember knowledge is dynamic . 
Actual eating is action.
Knowledge goes by reasoning known facts and acquired facts. 
Wisdom goes beyond facts and rules. It is a much more complex , not a  fully understood processing mechanism. It is a mix of observations, high levels of cognition, discrimination, comparison with standards ,futuristic thinking and intuition. It doesn't come to all. All are not wise in every aspect. Some famous industrialists or people who worked in the industries or politicians  give their advice on every problem facing this country assuming they are wise. We have many such people , we see many in the TV debates. We glorify many. 
Let me see how we generate and use knowledge in general. What are our artifacts ? 
The earlier generations had limited information and used intuition more. 
Intuition was their tool. 
The current senior generation has a lot more global information and uses better reasoning to generate knowledge and arrive at decisions. It is a mix of logic, data, and general reasoning with some amount of intuition.
The Current middle age generation has more information and gets more through crowd sourcing and depends on feedbacks . Most don't believe in intuition. 
All these generations have strong beliefs and almost a near closed mind. 
But the youngest generation - 10 to 20 years old- are open minded, have less beliefs and depend mostly on data . They depend on devices, connectivity, multiple platforms, social networks, social groups, interactions and current data. Their adaptability is high. They are very aware. They learn faster due to a minimal baggage of beliefs.  They seem to know upanishadic ideas better. Intuition has a limited role here. 
The day before yesterday, I had a talk with Mr Srinivasan. He was asking about knowledge and wisdom. So I wanted to  explain both and that is the reason for these two posts - 746 and 747. 
Greetings 
Dks
18/06/25, 5:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 748
“Loneliness is not the state of being alone, then, though it is often mistaken as such. It is a conscious, cognitive feeling of estrangement or social separation from meaningful others; an emotional lack that concerns a person’s place in the world.
Loneliness is entirely subjective.”
Fay Alberti

Let me go back to my IISc days as a research student. As I wrote earlier, I joined in August 1967 and started work. Fortunately, there was no course work requirement. So I didn't have to do any  courses. I had to take a foreign languages course as a requirement.  I had done two years of  German with Dr Klatt, a friendly teacher, during my ME course. So I decided to take a Russian course. Dr Ganeshasundaram was teaching that course. I did Russian for two years. 
The institute  had recruited faculty for English.   We got a professor from Wales, Prof Lewis to teach English. I took this course and spent two years with him. It was a great experience. 

 There  was no need to spend time searching for a problem. My problem was decided.   Such a great relief and saving about a year almost. I saw many students spending a year in search of a problem to work on. But I had a problem identified by me based on my ME project- computer methods and optimization for power systems. So problem search, the first step in research was over. 

The second step was a literature survey . I had done quite a bit and knew most papers - not many were there at that time . My important task - it is for all researchers - is to know what is happening in the world in most institutions in the area of load flows and later optimization. This is crucial , because we may reinvent the wheel. I had many disappointments due to this, some major. For example ,I submitted a paper to IEEE and it was rejected  . But later a similar one by Y Wallach was published. This demotivated me from publishing.

I was discovering research methodology slowly in many steps . 
The first question I posed to myself was:
Should I go deeper or broader?
It is a major aspect with both researchers and reviewers. Some believe that one should be focused and go deeper. Some others believe that expanding the area is better. 
The concept of interdisciplinary work was just picking up at that time. 
The answer is:
Ifelt that Load flows as an area may not open up more research opportunities.  
Also Since the topic was new and evolving and facilities were not good, I decided to expand my area. 
I enlarged the scope of my ME project and focused more on numerical methods, programming, optimization,  and most importantly computerization . That meant that my focus was not so much on power systems but I decided to focus on  computerization. 
This opened up a new area and more opportunities for problems for research.

The next step ,question 2 , is
What is the current state of the overall areas?
I will discuss this in three areas - power, computers,  programming and numerical methods. 
Let me start with power systems. It was not viewed as a system. Even optimal control researchers simplified it. Linearization was the focus. Most power engineers simplified the grid, aggregated generation and loads by ignoring minor load nodes and used manual approaches. Only the AC network analyser did a decent and accurate job. Otherwise most work was on devices- generators,  transformers, etc, transmission, lines, voltages, circuitbreakers , etc. The only analysis done was on short circuits to determine current ratings for circuit 
breakers with a highly simplified grid . 
 The concept of a system was conceived by some researchers from 1934 onwards. Power systems is the only area which created a systems view of an interconnected system with hundreds of load points and many generators. This was the first modeling of a large system attempted in engineering and science disciplines . Only IISc had a full ME power systems course at a time when most institutions including IITs were focusing on electrical machines. Roorkee taught more on components,  protection, earthing etc but not on a systemic view. We had at IISc three important systems level courses in power systems - power systems analysis, stability and economics all three on modeling and solutions using numerical methods. So we got a better picture of a large system. Even in IISc,  many senior faculty in EE and high voltage departments were working on devices and concepts. 
More in next.

Dks
19/06/25, 4:08 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 749
“Vātsyāyana [8.17–9.5]: To follow the etymology of the word, perception (pratyakṣa) is the functioning of each sense faculty (akṣa) upon its own proper (praty) object. . . . 
To follow the etymology of the word, inference (anumāna) is the source (māna) that works after (anu-). Inference normally occurs after perception, giving knowledge about something that possesses an inferential mark by means of knowing the mark.
 To follow the etymology of the word, analogy (upamāna) is the source (māna) that shows proximity (upa-), nearness, as in “A buffalo is like a cow.” Proximity should be understood here as a connection with universals, shared properties.”

We saw the power systems scene in 1967. I felt that it wasn't great. It gave me a lot of research opportunities.
 Let me explain the computer scene next and the computerization scene afterwards. Since  computers form the backbone of my work and computer capabilities put in a lot of limitations in our research work, understanding of the computer scene particularly in India  is important. We need a realistic appraisal here. The main purpose of this understanding is to find ways and methods to solve the limitations like speed , capacity ,wordlength and computational errors and availability of computer time. 
Computers were in the second generation in 1966 and going towards the integrated circuits mode. They had limited memory of 4k to 32k . Magnetic tapes were appearing then. Disk was yet to come. Speeds were in milliseconds range moving towards microseconds . Floating point arithmetic was just being built in. Only by 1970, we got better and faster machines in other countries. 
The Indian scene wasn't bad, surprisingly. TIFR built an indigenous machine in 1963 called TIFRAC , which used vacuum tubes. It was a toy and usage was not easy. But it was a big achievement. I was familiar with this machine. We had discussions about getting this computer to the institute as TIFR didn't want it. It was a usable computer . Similarly, ISI Calcutta and Jadavpur university tried to build a computer . I am not sure about its usability. So there was a very small, but unsustainable nucleus of computer activity in India.  This at a time when Professors at IISc were quibbling about the need for computers and computer research. Only Prof HNR had some understanding. None else in the departments of EE and ECE understood the area of computers and its role. It was a big tragedy. This misunderstanding continued till 1971. So I had no chance of even hoping for a computer to be available in the institute.  
There were three operational computers in India in 1966 . Two were in Bangalore and one in TIFR. I was initially using the Ferranti Sirius computer at NAL and then moved to the Elliot 803 at HAL. When I completed ME ,I was using both these two computers. TIFR had a bigger , powerful and 64 bits computer. So I decided to use TIFR's CDC3600 computer in Bombay. Financial grants were no problem. I had to visit Bombay regularly . The 64 bits world length meant that I didn't worry about errors. 
 Initially I used a simple programming approach - not a language - in the Ferranti machine. Later the programming help was better in Elliot 803. But programming larger programs was difficult. So most people solved small problems. I tried large problems with hundreds of variables for the first time. It led to errors. Still I solved some problems using these computers. 
But I had to learn FORTRAN for the use of the CDC3600 computer in TIFR. So I got a manual and started learning FORTRAN commands and variable definitions , dimensions . But the difficult aspect was using control statements. I learnt within a few days and started writing programs. I can write large programs with dimension facilities. I had to key in programs using punched cards. I did that . Within a week,  I was solving problems in that machine. 
So ,the computer scene in India wasn't bad. All computer centres allowed access to other users. TIFR provided access to all researchers - from IISc, IITs, SINP , Calcutta , and even from universities. Access was good. We used to get several batch runs in the day. 
I preferred doing computer runs at night both at HAL in Bangalore and TIFR ,Bombay.  So I became a nocturnal worker. 
I was using the HAL computer extensively till 1973. I used to book time in the night and visited HAL for two to three days in the week. While going was difficult ,because  I went by two BTS buses and it took close to 1 hour thirty minutes, I used a HAL bus to return in the middle of the night. I used to  complete my lectures during the day and also complete my department duties and then leave in the evening to HAL. I was living in the hostel and I used to miss my night dinner on those days. 
But support was great in all the computer centres. I am grateful to all these organisations for providing me with opportunities for research.  Collaboration and cooperation were great.  IISc didn't support me at all. Prof HNR helped me till 1968 and he moved to Delhi afterwards. I had a mix  of good and difficult times afterwards.
More on computerization next.
Greetings
Dks
20/06/25, 4:11 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 750
“Knowledge that arises from the connection between a sense faculty and an object is perceptual. There are six kinds of connection between the two: 1. conjunction (connection between a sense faculty and a substance; for example, seeing a pot where the  visual organ contacts the pot there where it is);
2. inherence in what is conjoined (connection between a sense faculty and a property that inheres in a  perceived substance; for example, seeing the color of a pot, the color inhering in the pot that is in contact with the visual organ); 3. inherence in what inheres in what is conjoined (con nection between a sense faculty and a universal that is instantiated in a property inhering in a substance; for example, the smell of sandalwood in general that inheres within a particular trope or instance of san dalwood smell inhering in a piece of sandalwood); 4. inherence (connection that makes auditory perception possible); 5. inherence in what inheres (connection between the auditory faculty and a universal that inheres in a sound; for example, the loudness of loud music); 6. qualifier qualified relation (connection involved in perception of an absence).”
Nyaya sutra

Next, let me look at the next aspect-  computerization. What I mean by computerization is the use of computers to solve major analysis and design problems in engineering disciplines and in Physics . It was not simple or straight forward. It was an evolving process. We need supporting components like numerical methods, devices, simulation etc..   Researchers and developers learnt new ways and new applications of computers everyday. 
The state of  computerization at that time needs an explanation. It was in a primitive state even in the US and UK. Literally, there was no computerization happening to solve large problems. Computerization  was used by academics- mostly physicists and electrical engineers -  to solve simple and small problems using some numerical methods like Gauss Seidel method and numerical solution of differential equations. Pure mathematicians didn't approve computer based solutions. They didn't accept numerical methods as a mechanism to solve problems. Most talked about closed form analytical solutions. Even in the institute, I was loner against most senior faculty who were aghast at numerical methods. Even G Krishna changed only after going to the US and started solving economic dispatch problems in 1969. During 1967, he was against numerical solutions. Discussions are impossible. 
So computerization had opportunities and challenges. I introduced a new course on computational methods in 1970 and another on optimization ,which I taught with GK in 1970 or 71. 
As regards , business data processing , it picked up only after 1970 in India.  This needed two major developments - magnetic tapes and disks and COBOL language. It also needed a new learning process and  a different discipline.  HAL put in a lot of effort in giving identity codes to various inventory items. It was a big exercise.  The unique identifier concept was not understood then and even now. It needed persuasion.   HAL started stores inventory and accounting processing by 1970. 
Most business processing in India started in late seventies.  TCS became the first company to get into this business. Outsourcing also started at that time. Database took another five years - only by 1983 or so. We at IISc struggled with a slow and difficult network model. 
When one tried to tackle large problems, one encountered computational errors leading to wrong results. You can't invert a matrix of bigger size like 20 or more. That is why we moved away from direct solutions to iterative solutions. Fortunately ,the CDC machine helped me till 1973 . Even though IISc got the IBM 360/44 computer in 1971, I used the CDC machine even for a project work done by my student from Atomic energy in 1975. IBM 360 was a  32 bits wordlength machine and CDC3600 had 64bits . That mattered for accuracy and error reduction. We were solving a large eigen value problem and didn't get any of the  roots while using the IBM machine, but got all the roots when we solved it with the same program in CDC3600.  Surprising. 
I think the greatest aid for computerization occurred in 1969 and continued later . That is the development of programming as a separate discipline in computer science distinct from hardware designs and technologies. So two parallel paths started moving forward. Computer science gained a scientific look through formal languages and algorithms as well as it facilitated speedier development of applications. Until 1974 or so IISc didn't accept software as an engineering discipline. 
Next data was treated as a separate entity. Data structures became important. Database appeared later. IFIP played a significant role in these developments. 
But I understood their  impacts  very early  and started introducing these concepts - operating systems and compilers - in my course on computing techniques from 1969 onwards , changed it into a computing systems and devices course later and changed my research focus to operating systems from 1970 onwards- a lot of changes in my life. I should say 1968 to 70 were eventful years in my life . More on it later.
The 1970s saw great developments in computer systems designs and technologies and also on applications. But India got into this stride only by the late 1970s - almost a decade later and didn't grow due to the rigid ,isolationist , anti-US policies of our government of Indira Gandhi and Morarji Desai. We missed several buses in that isolationist environment . Importing computers was difficult. The seventies saw only about ten or twenty computers operating in India.  Even IIT Bombay didn't have a good computer in that period. 
So we have a good abstracted picture of the state of three areas relevant for my research.
Let us look at the next step.

21/06/25, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 751
“Among these, consider the visual organ: when a colored  object such as a pot is known, the connection has to be con junction, since the organ and the pot are by nature substances. In contrast, with something that is not a substance, such as a color, the connection is inherence in what is conjoined, since colors and the like rest in substances that are in sensory connection when they are perceived.
connection is conjunction in the case of the organs of smell, touch, and taste: conjunction of the organ with things that have smell or another sensible property such as touch or taste. With regard to odors and other properties them selves that inhere in the things that have them, the sensory connection is inherence in the conjoined. And for universals of odor, etc., it is inherence in what inheres in that which  is conjoined. Universals are nested in—they rest in—such individuals. And for sound, it is inherence.”
Nyaya 



Research needs certain qualities like
Concentration in work
Contemplation and serious thinking, constant questioning 
Clarity in thinking and understanding 
Focus
Abstraction capabilities 
Outward looking ,know what others are doing 
Keep uptodate
Futuristic thinking - look forward , look at unknowns .


A recap: The first five  steps we saw are
1.General literature search
2.Problem selection
3.Detailed literature critiques
4.Depth wise or wide or expanded or multidisciplinary approach
5.Create a picture of the current scene in the research area.
Now one is having a reasonable knowledge of the problem area . The next step is gap analysis. This is the starting point to actual research - discovery, solutions,  designs and experimentation.
What is gap analysis ? 
Gap analysis means to find the new and innovative possibilities existing in your research area based on three aspects:
the current scene in your area 
The current lacunae and requirements in your area
The current technological and solution developments as well as possible future improvements . 
The purposes followed by most researchers are
1. To find improvements possible in models, solution methods like simulation , numerical methods
2.To improve accuracy through reducing approximations.  To look for new parameters and study their impacts.  
3.To find new models, new applications, 
4.To overturn theories ,principles,  etc. 
Most choose the first approach in their research. 
Gap analysis is a very deep thinking approach. There is no straight jacketed approach for this activity. It depends on your capability to abstract what you know into a set of points that are important and relevant, mull over them to find holes. Most researchers read a paper and start thinking about what to do next after the work. They ask “ is there something that I can do beyond this paper?”
My gap analysis is discussed not in detail but in brief next.
I found the following broad gaps and opportunities in power systems
There are considerable approximations existing in our studies. Linearization was dominant even though power systems are nonlinear systems. The best example was the economic dispatch problem.So there is a  lot of scope for developing new models for many analysis and optimization and dynamics studies..
Most problems are numerically solved using a lot of simplifications. Computers can improve both models and more accurate solutions.
There are new areas and applications  coming up frequently. For example,  the existing control is both analog and manual, and while load frequency control for a large grid needs faster response, else the load variations will be high. Digital control improves the response and accuracy. Digital control was just developing at this time. So we can build control strategies and define an architecture.  We saw a phenomenal transformation in this area till 1990 improving the performance and reducing operating costs. Actually ,in 1968, two kinds of discussions were happening in US and UK on the control of power systems . The US was moving towards designing online control of power grids. The UK was hesitant and power researchers were quite strong that online direct control is dangerous and they should go for an offline operations guidance model. That is a relevant aspect for discussion today on technology absorption rate.

These gave me opportunities for research and publications and helped me to complete my PhD by 1970. I submitted my thesis in may 1970. Let us look at my travel from 1967 onwards in the next post.

“A book is written when there is something specific that has to be discovered. The writer doesn’t know what it is, nor where it is, but knows it has to be found. The hunt then begins. The writing begins.”
—Roberto Calasso, The Celestial Hunter

Having prepared myself for research and getting enough leads, I got into my research work and happily it moved smoothly at a good pace. 
But many changes were happening in the department.  Post Tashkent agreement, USSR agreed to set up three advanced  schools in India, one of them in IISc.  The IISc school was to focus on automation.  Here automation didn't mean just control systems alone. It meant the marriage of computers and controls together, a great concept at that time. The idea was great and futuristic. It was conceived as an interdisciplinary school comprising the three departments of EE, ECE and mechanical engineering. It was the first major interdisciplinary focus of Prof Dhawan. He was trying to break the silos concept of departments with rigid ,unbreakable  boundaries. A team led by Prof Dhawan and comprising Prof HNR, Deekshatulu and Nagaraja visited Moscow for discussions and finalization of the plan. It was October 1967. I was just two months into my research work. So I was requested to teach Prof HNRs course on stability. So I started teaching that course- that was my starting of teaching activity on a serious level . I taught about 10 lectures. It was a nice experience and it opened the door of teaching for me. I found that I like teaching. I really loved teaching and I liked the glass board in the class. Remember the students were just one year juniors to me. 
 At that point in time, I was thinking of looking for a job outside and not continuing in the institute,but IISc was very attractive and I didn't want to leave it. So I was in two minds. Even Deekshatulu told me to decide quickly and not to waste precious years in indecision. 
Then an important event occurred. GK resigned as he was going abroad. I was given his tasks in the department . I even corrected his December exam papers . I started teaching his electrical machines course from the Jan 1968 term. I also taught his electrical drawing course. I was still a research student. But there was hope that I would get a faculty job in the department soon. So I concentrated on my research and teaching. As I said earlier, I used to teach in the morning and go in the evening to HAL returning at midnight. It was tough. 
I accepted that workstyle . It was a big change for me. I had very regular habits till then of going to bed by 9: 30 or 10pm and waking up at 6am. That changed. Similarly I missed my dinner for three days in a week. But more importantly my  regular reading of books  got disturbed and reduced. 
I used to borrow many books from IISc library and gymkhana library. I used to read for at least half an hour at bed time, mostly fiction . Both got reduced. Anyway, it didn't worry me. I was confident that I could resume my reading habits later. I was reading the relevant text books to teach my class as well as journal papers .
This concept and practice  of browsing and reading the latest journals every week is a well developed and followed one . I haven't seen this great practice followed elsewhere in other institutions. We were behind by at least a year on the latest research publications. We got current contents and became aware of a paper of relevance to our request and sent requests to the authors. Many authors sent their papers to us. Some wrote my name wrongly. One name given to me by a foreign author - submarine - is still lingering in me. I understood how difficult is my name. From that time, I used to request people to call me DK. Even Prof Dhawan called me DK . Most called me DKS. Subramanian was forgotten.

“Maximize your time for critical thinking through the following:
 1.Read an interesting book or feature when you’re feeling idle. 
2.Write down your thoughts about different topics. 
3.Test your brain regularly through quizzes or puzzles. 
4.Practice freethinking or logic-based thinking as you read.”
Christ Lewis

Since there was a vacancy, I got the job as a lecturer in 1968 . My research student tenure was short. Prof MV Hariharan from IIT Bombay and Prof Ganapathy from Warangal were in my selection committee. There were three candidates. I did perform very well. Prof Dhawan knew me better from that time onwards. I met him several times afterwards. I went to Prof HNRs house the next morning after the interview, and was surprised to see both the experts there having breakfast with Prof HNR . I was asked to join. Then HNR told me about my selection. I became a good friend of Hariharan. I knew Ganapathy before. 

As I said, I started teaching several courses. While in 1968, I taught electrical machines and drawing, I moved on to teach computer techniques and non-conventional energy systems . I got deeper into photovoltaic and nuclear energy teaching. I shifted the focus of computer techniques course to digital away from analog. I spent only about 8to 10 lectures on analog computers and 35 hours on digital principles. I used a book written  Burroughs corporation staff  to teach that course in addition to the only book available at that time. 
I defined a few principles for teaching courses. They are
1.explain difficult topics
2.provide practical applications, current scene.
3.introduce something new
4.look at engineering in the large. Go for systemic views . Look at impacts.
5.provide a futuristic scene .
6. Make the students think by asking questions. 
7. Allow for deeper learning through a term paper and a large project.
Also I learnt that teaching a subject gives you an opportunity to learn the subject in depth. Even during my engineering college days, I tried to understand a subject by reading many books . I developed that habit also to know more than what is taught in the class. This was necessary particularly for understanding electrical machines theory and class room teaching was poor. This practice continued over the years. When I was working at Easun in Madras in 1964, I found a good and well written book on oscilloscopes. That helped me to understand oscilloscopes better. I intensified this practice while teaching a course. I strongly believed in the axiom that a teacher should know more than a student. 
I also decided to teach new courses every year, update the curriculum so that my knowledge improves and expands every year. I don't want to be stagnant. I follow this even now . 
I taught at least one new course every year till 1987. My width and depth of knowledge in computer science increased in this manner. I was almost contemporary in many areas of computer science, optimization and power systems. This was useful in my life as I tried to remain current in these areas.

“Sounds are properties that inhere in ether, ether being the  medium through which sound travels. Sounds that are heard have traveled to the portion of ether in one’s ears, and hence the “connection” in play is the inherence of sound in the ether in one’s ears. We perceive absences by noting that an object in perceptual range (e.g., the floor of a classroom) is qualified by the absence of some object in question (e.g., an elephant). A person knows there’s no elephant in the room because she has looked and would have seen one if it were present. In his opening comments above, Vātsyāyana anticipates this idea by arguing that knowledge of unreal things is based on cognition of real things, coupled with the background notion, “If something were there, I would have noticed it.” Below, Vātsyāyana and Uddyotakara consider the second condition for perceptual knowledge, that it be independent of language.”
Nyaya
  

  1968 was a very eventful year. Prof  Devanathan, Mathematics department,  came back from England after a sabbatical and we got into working together.  I started to look at higher order Newton's method and proved a couple of convergence and order conditions. I used the multi-variable Taylor series approach to represent the given set of algebraic equations. We were trying to find easy ways of representing a multivariate Taylor series. 
I had studied tensors in my machines course using a book by Kron on tensor applications to machines. I also taught tensors using that book. Prof Devanathan was familiar and helped me in using tensors in representing multi variables algebraic equations. I used to spend most mornings with him after my class. I attended Russian and English in the afternoons and went to HAL for my computer work. Somedays,  I used to walk with Devanathan in the evening to his house in Malleswaram . 
The next thing that happened was my interaction with Prof Dhawan on October second. I showed him the poor power connection that was existing at that time in IISc - without any security of circuit breakers . The 11kv supply to the institute came from a rural feeder. This was done by showing him the incoming transformer at the physics department with a fuse box and nothing else. Feeders going from it to departments.  When I told him that if we wanted to isolate the institute's power from KEB, we had to remove the fuse- no switches or circuitbreakers.  He was aghast . So I got involved with the IISc power system from that time till I retired, a long 36 years involvement.  That was a momentous day. We designed a reliable power supply for the institute. I updated our supply to 66kv to meet the increasing load. 

 Then another event happened. Prof AR Vasudevamurthy became the warden of the hostel. He wanted me to become an assistant warden. So I joined as an assistant warden, another burden of work. Handling  work loads was dependent on you. Prof ARV took a lot of interest in improving the systems in the hostel and mess. We had Mr Kuppanna as the hostel superintendent.  I took a keen interest and modernized the kitchen and improved the hostel. Dr Narain Mahishi was the senior assistant warden. We became good friends. I helped him in the various tasks he undertook in the institute like placement and Alumni association.  
I went for steam cooking in the kitchen and abolished cooking using firewood. That reduced the mess bill. I streamlined the mess grocery purchases. The B mess was taken over by north Indian students and now A mess was known as a pumpkin mess and B mess as potato mess. I will recall one incident that happened in the B mess. 
The mess representative introduced idly for breakfast. Immediately,  I got a representation signed by 173 inmates- there were 176 total in that mess- requesting the removal of the representative. I had to resolve it. I told the representative to remove idly from the menu and talked to the students to accept the compromise and continue him as their representative.  It was a major problem. There were many problems . 
I streamlined the rooms allotment  process by creating a correct database of vacant rooms and joined the allotment activity. We avoided a lot of confusion and dissatisfaction. 
In addition to making the kitchen modern and clean, I introduced more reforms. One was the staff structure. All mess and hostel employees were institute employees and not contract workers as in most places. But all employees belong to one class four cadre - head cook to cook to table attendants . I created a structure as head cook, cook, assistant cook, dining hall uncharged, hostel supervisor etc. 
I realised one characteristic of mine which appeared in all my activities and involvements in the institute, KSCST,other institutions, banks, governments etc . That is, I was impatient and wanted to improve systems. I followed this urge to look for problems and implement improvements in all my activities throughout my professional life. I continue this quality today with our FAER activities. We changed the structure this year.
More in the next.

25/06/25, 3:57 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 755
“Avoid being idle by maximizing your time spent for improving critical thinking. Challenge and train your thinking whenever you have a spare time and start building your foundation towards being a critical thinker.”
Christ  Lewis 

Let me discuss further my obsession with improving any activity that I get involved in . 
This urge started in my childhood. This became a habit with me throughout my life . I tried to find bottlenecks, problems, gaps and alternatives in every activity. I had looked at hundreds of activities and tried my best to improve them .Some are major and some are minor.
Let me list a few major ones. 
I changed my way of teaching every year with improvements . 
I started working on the IISc power grid from 1968 to 2004. I made lots of changes . First I moved from one 11kv substation to many. Second, I went for ring mains . I also had many 400v loops.  Third, I went for building level panels. Fourth I went for 66kv. I talked about our load growth reaching a limit and interruptions increasing every day to Prof Ramaseshan, director, in 1983. He didn't bother. When I raised it with Prof CNR Rao in 1985, he agreed and we designed a 66kv by 11kv ring mains. It is working even now. By 1990, I went for an integrated institute wide diesel generating station of 4MW connected to the ring mains. So our interruptions were almost zero.  This was a constant thinking to improve the quality ,availability and reliability of power supply in the institute. 
I used a redundant cluster approach to design a power supply system for JNCASR in 2004. I also redesigned vidhana soudha , Jayadeva , and Vikas soudha power systems. 
I restructured the electrical lab for BE students.
I propagated computer science education in most colleges in the eighties. 
I streamlined administrative procedures and policies in several activities like evaluation of staff at IISc,  extension lecture program,ASTRA administration, DST, government of Karnataka administration  etc. I spent minimum time on administration.  
I spent twenty years implementing corebanking in many banks. There were hurdles to be managed. Innovation was part of the game. 
I pushed curriculum development and modernization in most engineering colleges and universities. 
I assisted in getting autonomy to many engineering colleges in Karnataka under TEQIP. It involved many fights.
I rejuvenated KSCST as its secretary.  
And many more.

How did I get this habit ?

 As I said before, I was following my uncle closely when I was six years old . He was involved in several public activities and conducted two major functions every year- Sri Rama Navami and Sankara Jayanthi.  He constantly improved the processes. The first was streamlining supply chain for groceries, milk, flowers , musicians etc. It wasn't easy. He built a reliable system . I understood the importance of supply chain. I got involved in his activities totally and understood the need for improvements. 
Similarly he improved the accounting system. I learnt about ledgers at that young age. 
Then he wanted transparency in accounting public money. He printed receipts and expenses and distributed it to all. That was a great learning for me - transparency , meticulous working and integrity. 
We sent the prasad by post. He introduced innovations there every year. I observed it and understood the need for improvements. 
Getting addresses of the village people living in many cities was not easy. He created a dynamic and consistent database and I assisted him in maintaining this database. 
So I got this improvement urge from childhood and practiced it throughout my life in all my activities.
This had a multiplier effect and all these helped me in my research and in keeping me uptodate in engineering developments.
Greetings

26/06/25, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 756
“You need to reshape your character. Now you will need to choose one intellectual trait that you will strive to improve each month. It may be intellectual perseverance, courage, humility, empathy, or autonomy. Once you have done so, perform the following steps:
  1.Concentrate on how to improve your chosen trait. For example, you may have chosen intellectual humility. 
2.Be aware that denying your mistakes is in fact wrong. 
3.Be aware that ego and arrogance keep one away from educating himself. Own up to your arrogance so you can deal with it. Never think you possess all the knowledge in this world, or thinking that you are as knowledgeable as others.”
Christ Lewis 

I have discussed the importance of the constant urge to improve a process or technology or an activity in my life. Let me look at its importance in our economy and industries. 
Most public sector industries and organizations were just running to maintain the status quo. Some were fighting for survival. Recall how HEC Ranchi, HMT, ITI all failed . Only BEL is surviving. They had a niche business. Even many research organizations had gone stale. The reason is they were living in yesterday's world, they were inward looking. It is not just public sector units but many private sector units also failed . Let me name a few : Kirloskar except DG sets, premier automobiles, Hindustan motors, standard motors, many electrical high voltage cable companies, many computer manufacturing companies like ORG, DCM, Pertech, even Wipro and HCL ,Tatas NELCO and a few more companies,  TVS - ultra business machines,  many software companies like Mafatlal, Birlasoft, kirloskar , agarwals, - the list is a big one- . 
The situation is a mixed one in our education sector - most universities belong to the early 20th century. They don't look at current research areas. Most have failed. Same is the story with arts and science colleges. Fortunately quite a good number of engineering colleges keep pace with the latest developments. Most of them started teaching computer science by the nineties. Now Most of them run degree programs on AI. It is a happy state. But most universities and colleges are not seeing the potential of AI . 

Why did they fail? There are many reasons. 
One is sticking to old technologies. Two is single product focus.  Third and the most important reason is inward looking , not knowing or caring for what is happening outside. This is what made Prof Clayton Christensen coin the term disruptive technologies and innovation. This frog in the well attitude is dangerous.  
TCS, Infosys succeeded very well because they kept up to date with the latest developments in a fast growing IT area through continuous improvements and anticipation of future developments. TCS initiated research in AI about 20 years back and came up with a product called IGNIO. TCS started a cloud business ION quite early. 
Both TCS and Infosys as well as Wipro and HCL concentrated on multiple technologies, multiple sectors, and multiple geographies thereby hedging on obsolescence , loss of markets, and competition. That is how they are in the forefront. Hindustan Lever and ITC went big on diversification. Asian Paints succeeded with successful adaption of digital technologies. 
This urge for continuous improvements and innovation stands between life and death of industries, research institutions, educational institutions, and even defence. The current warfare uses drones and missiles , no foot soldiers.  You saw our country succeeding in operation sindoor  with drones and Brahmos as well as an effective and modern air defence system including S400. They are using the current technologies. Pakistan used old technologies.  We may move to build many iron domes next. Never rest on your laurels.  
So this urge for continuous improvements is considered a big principle of the 21st century.  
But I have been at it for a very long time from 1950 onwards. It was inculcated into me and became a habit.
More in the next.

27/06/25, 4:20 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 757
“It is not the taste of food that one should seek to understand; one should know the one who tastes the food.
 It is not action that one should seek to understand; one should know the one who acts.
 It is not joy and suffering that one should seek to understand; one should know the one who experiences joy and suffering.
 It is not the mind that one should seek to understand; one should know the one who thinks.”
 Kausitaki Upanishad Ch. 3: v. 8.

The need or the urge to improve various aspects in every activity that one is involved in will become an essential requirement for most employees now. Routine office work or factory work which occupied 70 to 80% of the workforce till now will be dead in the coming years. 
 The urge for improvements- another name for continuous innovation-  meant that we  have to develop many other habits from childhood. I developed these habits also. These are
1.thinking habits. I used to contemplate a lot and do it even now. Absorption of new knowledge happens due to contemplation and introspection internally. That strengthens one's mind. That is the important stage of learning, not reading or listening or cramming alone. Learning is not complete without this self analysis. It improves understanding and cognition.  It  is also needed to know the current state of any activity. The transformation in banking needed this habit. I am sure most people have this habit. 
I expect teachers to have this habit. They should introspect about their subject - what am I teaching? Is it correct,relevant, useful, latest or outdated ? Why is this topic? For whom it is intended,? - . They should introspect on what is the future outlook for the subject they are teaching. Then they should introspect on how they are teaching ? What is the problem with their teaching? Are they aware of the understanding or absorption by students? Are they using excuses to cover their shortcomings? What are the ways of improving their teaching?
Then teachers should be concerned about their curriculum. I find most teachers are not concerned about the curriculum. They should ask - does the curriculum give a completeness to the area ? Is it relevant, applicable, modern, ? Will it excite the students?Will it lead to innovation , research ? etc.
Thinking implies two qualities:
1.Accept true facts. Or find the truth of a fact. Don't have emotional or biased thinking. 
2.Discriminate facts to get clarity.
Let me give an example for emotional thinking. Recently kamalahasan said that kannada came from Tamil without any evidence. That led to fights. People are fighting with non truth or non verified fact. I don't think you should accept that statement because it lakhs evidence. 
Biased thinking - extensive biased thinking has been produced by westerners on the origin of Sanskrit again without evidence. They say that Sanskrit came from a hypothetical indo European language . This is without any proof . Similarly, they claim Arya is a race coming from Europe again without evidence or truth. 
Let us not become an automaton. Let us be thinking ,questioning, and reasoning humans.
Let me look at the next mental  aspect in the next post.

28/06/25, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 758
Sorry to break my discussions. 
Many will remember my uncle Dr Ramakrishnan . He read my posts with a lot of interest and wrote detailed  comments. He was my mother's younger brother.
He passed away in the morning of 27th June 2025 at the ripe age of 97 years.  He was bedridden for a week. It was sad news for me. It left a big void. 
As I was talking about the urge to improve, he had the urge. 
He studied in the village during the pre-independence time upto 8th standard and then went to high school in the neighbouring town - Nanguneri - by a bullock cart along with my father's younger brother , Narayanan.
Unlike most in the village, he wanted to study further in a college. I started my schooling at that time. Also his father passed away at that time. So it was his elder brother and my elder uncle , S . Narayana Sarma who took care of him. He took care of me also.
I used to see him during every vacation. I used to be with him always during those vacations. We used to talk a lot like friends. I still remember Shylock the jew story of the merchant of Venice which he told me at that time. Every time he visited the village, I pestered him to tell me  a story. He was a very jovial person. He was multitalented.  He used to sing well. He used to compose Bhajans to be sung during the Sri Rama Navami time. Both of us were mentored by my elder uncle and respected him a lot, assisting in the various activities conducted by him . 
He was a very hard working and very intelligent as well as a deeply religious person . After completing BSc he was keen on pursuing graduate studies. At that time, there was only one seat available in MSc chemistry at Presidency College Madras for our community. He struggled for nearly four years to get admission in MSc while working as a demonstrator. He got admissions in BHU, Kerala university and Madras university in the same year. He joined each by paying the fees and settled in Madras university. I saw him during the vacation. His hard work was unbelievable.  He used to study from early morning to night non stop except eating times. He was having a high degree of concentration. He used to sit in my paternal house in the village and studied there as there will be peace and quietness and no disturbance.  His work culture was admired by my two cousins ,DR Sundaram and Rajalakshmi,  in whose house he stayed during his MSc days. They remembered him and his MSc days even now. 
Later  he joined the biochemistry department at the medical college in Madurai as a faculty.His urge pushed him to get his hands on research and he pursued his PhD there. I stayed with him in his house during my first year BE at Shenoy Nagar and visited his lab in the medical college on many  occasions. He used to work till late in the night. He conducted a public exhibition with great success. 
 Later he moved to JIPMER, Pondicherry. He continued there till retirement.  He didn't move into the professor's quarters , staying in a small house. My aunt was also a simple, humble and  frugal person despite a brilliant enquiring mind . 
I took my young daughter at 4years of age to get some tests done at JIPMER , based on a useless suggestion by a retired pediatrics professor of JIPMER living in Jayanagar, Bangalore at that time. 
After retirement , he first joined Annamalai University for a few years and then had a long tenure at Sankar Netralaya. 
His research work was not theoretical. He focused on practical applications. 
He always encouraged his younger colleagues to work hard, have goals in life and pursue research. He guided them . He had an opportunity to go to the US to present a research paper. He didn't go,but sent his research collaborator to the US. He believed in nishkama Karma. 
He spent his Later years on mostly religious activities . He was a great scholar ,wrote a number of research papers , popular science articles and religious / spiritual articles. His clarity was remarkable. He became a sthitaprajna.  
Like my elder uncle, he was interested in public work, but he was shy and kept out of many activities. 
He led a simple and frugal life in a small house without a lot of furniture and gadgets. His memory will stay with me. So no goodbye  to him from me. 
I pray for him . Om Shanti.

29/06/25, 4:31 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 759

Let me continue the important learning of the urge to improve habit. We saw thinking as an essential adjunct to working on continuous improvements. 
We need continuous understanding and improvements of our body and mind also, not just in our profes­sion alone. Our body changes with time . Our habits attack our mind. Our mind gets corrupted and converted with beliefs and opinions . So we need regular reviews. We need to motivate ourselves first by thinking about our body. Self learning through deep contemplation and introspection is the most effective approach for a happy life. 
Let me look at the other habits needed to improve ourselves . 

2. Associated with thinking is the questioning habit. I keep asking why frequently . Actually implicitly everyone asks this question when asked to do something or follow some procedure. People are satisfied if they know the reason why they are doing something. People are not dumb. The questioning habit  is needed to find out problems in the existing activity- what is called problem discovery and what is ignored in our education today -  and possible solutions. This helps in innovation. This leads to gap analysis . 
Further this questioning and consequential discoveries as well as analysis helped me in decision making . I have selected hundreds of technologies and hundreds of products and vendors for a large number of banks, government - both karnataka and india- departments, colleges and universities, and even public sector and private sector industries and have been responsible for hundreds of decisions . I can say that most of my decisions were right . In hind sight, I can say that  I had made only one wrong decision - the selection of the cosoft database for bangalore university. My assumption was disproved as the company failed us.  There were a few cases of vendors not delivering and we had to move to a new vendor. For example ,I selected a new computer company with many promises - Pertech - for the research institute of central silk board and they couldn't do even a  proper service of a printer. The company wound up after a few years. That was an unstable period in the computer industry , many big ones like uptron, Keonics,  pertech ,Zenith etc failing. It was tough. Actually , I spent days in due diligence for the Karnataka government rate contract for two decades in this turbulent period. I could get a correct ERP for many organizations like Jindal Vijayanagar and Karle after a lot of questioning and ascertaining the truth about their statements. . Also I could avoid the possibility of poor selections in many organizations like the shipping ministry. I had bitter fights in many cases like in my CSA department, KSCST ,KGCC , Bangalore Dairy,  in the selection of computers because of ignorance and vested interests. 
3. Innovation thinking - to look for new solutions.The mahithisindhu program of 1000 schools computerization needed a lot of innovations. 
4. Futuristic outlook. This opened up possibilities of future solutions and longevity of a feasible solution. I looked at IOT for the IISc and JNC power systems before IOT technology matured. The past is important. It gives us experience and helps us in avoiding mistakes. But it is the future that secures you in life. Future knowledge helps you avoid shocks and sudden  surprises . That is essential in life. 
5.flexible and open mind. I can change my beliefs. So I moved forward in my learning and research. There was no fear of unknowns. 
Because of these qualities, I could help many engineering colleges and universities  in the modern curriculum structuring of their courses , and many organizations in their technology transformations in many sectors like power, computers, networking , software, ERP , education . Another contribution from me for a large number of institutions was the human talent selections. It was a tough job, with many mine fields and political interference. I managed it well and most were happy. I have handled many critical cases in IITs,  universities . I am today happy that I could contribute to  the education, industry and government sectors correctly and with satisfaction . Most decisions worked for the organizations ,but not for the vested interests. I assessed the pitfalls apriori.  
I acquired all these additional habits as a bonus . This helped in my research work also. 
Let me discuss in the next post why these habits are important and relevant now.

30/06/25, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 760

There are two reasons why these habits of innovation , thinking, questioning, futuristic look and a changing mind are crucial now. 
One concerns our education system. Today people are saying most higher education is a waste. Our current education system is firmly rooted in the past and totally ignores the future. 
Let me quote
Bill Winters, the Group Chief Executive of Standard Chartered, has openly expressed his belief that his prestigious MBA from the University of Pennsylvania's Wharton School was "a waste of time." In an interview with Bloomberg, Winters discussed his thoughts on the evolving landscape of business education and what skills truly matter in today's fast-changing business world. His candid remarks have sparked significant conversation around the value of traditional US business schools and whether their offerings remain relevant, particularly in an age dominated by Artificial Intelligence (AI).
Winters questioned the necessity of business degrees, particularly MBAs, in a world where critical thinking, communication, and adaptability are paramount. As the banking titan acknowledged, the traditional business curriculum may not provide the competitive edge it once did, especially as AI continues to transform the way industries function.

Zerodha co-founder Nikhil Kamath recently lit a spark with a provocative statement: “The days of four-year college courses are over. Lifelong learning is the new norm—for everyone.” But according to Stanford expert Robert E. Siegel, as reported by CNBC Make It, that’s merely step one in preparing for the storm AI is bringing to the workforce.
Drawing from the World Economic Forum’s Future of Jobs Report 2025, Kamath’s post quickly gained traction online, highlighting the rise of automation, the decline of traditional jobs, and the urgent need for reskilling. The numbers are stark: by 2030, 92 million jobs are expected to disappear—even as 170 million new ones are created. Yet Kamath warns: only the adaptable will benefit. You can’t rely on what you learned a decade ago, he cautions.
Siegel stresses the need to break out of intellectual echo chambers and connect with diverse peers, especially younger, tech-savvy professionals who often act as a compass for what's next.
 Teachers, you have this great opportunity of mixing with new  younger minds every year. Don't miss it. Listen to them . Understand their ideas . Use them. Apply them. Even school children are great associates. 
Empathy is a key to success today. If machines dominate logic, humans must dominate emotional intelligence. Balance your learning with focus on humanism. Humanism doesn’t change fast . It is random , transformable , has multiple - millions- of shades , suitable for LLMs of a different kind.
Let me add a few more requirements. 
Engineering is an inclusive eco system. We are teaching only some concepts of some aspects of engineering.  We don't look at the transformations of industries, business, health, education, digital economy, governance etc and the multiple impacts. Our education is very narrow ,not holistic. 
Broaden your views and learning. Limit or even kill specialization. Go borderless. Go generalisation with focus and depth . Increase your domains, not restrict them. 
Break rules. AI will follow rules ,so don't worry.
Discuss, don't teach, analyse, think . 
Look futuristic. Be application centric.
Humanise learning. That is very important. 
Embrace change. Embrace unknowns. 
Fear of change is a bigger threat than change itself. Professionals must not only tolerate transformation—they must lead it.
Remember that survival in the AI age demands more than credentials. It requires mindset shifts, systems thinking, and emotional nuance.
Build a flexible, adaptive, curriculum with hundreds of topics , not a fixed set of subjects. Keep adding the topics. Ask why a fixed standard curriculum? Why not an open system - a student chooses what he wants? That makes many if not all to think and analyse and choose topics. IISc had an open ended non examination based system in the beginning ,when it was not offering degrees but a proficiency certificate. 
Encourage self learning . Don't teach. Cambridge allowed students to choose lectures to attend, no borders. 

Please think and ponder on changing our education system from school onwards.
It is needed for the survival of teachers. Indians are resilient and find ways of survival, not the teachers.
The second reason is for our survival in jobs. Routine , stable jobs are gone or going.

A Little Learning
by Alexander Pope

 A little learning is a dangerous thing ;
Drink deep, or taste not the Pierian spring :
There shallow draughts intoxicate the brain,
And drinking largely sobers us again.
Fired at first sight with what the Muse imparts,
In fearless youth we tempt the heights of Arts ;
While from the bounded level of our mind
Short views we take, nor see the lengths behind,
But, more advanced, behold with strange surprise
New distant scenes of endless science rise !
So pleased at first the towering Alps we try,
Mount o’er the vales, and seem to tread the sky ;
The eternal snows appear already past,
And the first clouds and mountains seem the last ;
But those attained, we tremble to survey
The growing labours of the lengthened way ;
The increasing prospect tires our wandering eyes,
Hills peep o’er hills, and Alps on Alps arise “
