02/07/25, 5:30 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 762
Prof Vasudev Parvati has a query
“Sir,  we had some knowledge about your invaluable contributions to the IT infrastructure growth in Indian academia and industry, but the scale you just mentioned is just stupendous! I wonder how you have not got a top Padma award yet!
Also, you have mentioned innovative thinking. Please throw some light on how this can be nurtured in young minds through our education system. Pranams!”

My response 
You have posed the right question.  I had an encounter with a fresh faculty, recently joined in Reva university.  She asked me about “thinking.”  I was taken aback. It was the first time I got into discussions on what the purpose of thinking is. I told her a simple answer
“To know the unknowns. “
But in reality thinking means hundreds of things and has hundreds of reasons , many without reason. 
I was planning to write about this topic. 
But I look for  two prerequisites with our young faculty. Those are the necessary conditions for thinking. 
Flexible mindset- please unlearn. Don't have a rigid mindset. A rigid mindset clouds your understanding ,leads to misunderstanding . That kind of rigidity will reject changes , and will be happy to live in its comfort zone. Further , a rigid mindset is formed by listening to parents, neighbours,  society , teachers, friends and government . So many beliefs may be wrong. So you build a wrong ,untrue database of beliefs . A rigid mindset doesn't allow one to correct the mistakes. That means there is no opportunity for thinking. Once you have fixed set of beliefs, your thinking windows get shrunk into a comfort zone. Your purpose in life becomes to lead a mundane life. You don't want challenges. You lead a timid life- fearful of everything . You want to stay indoors. That is your safe area.  I have seen thousands of teaching people like this. They want an easy life. They shun public service. They lack idealism. They may even lack a helping tendency due to hesitation. I even say that most people retire mentally by the age of 30 . So  the fixed mindset is a big mental hurdle for proper thinking. Making people to move a fixed mindset to a flexible one accepting changes is tough. I had seen faculty teaching microprocessors 8085 even after it was dead for a long time and refusing to believe that fact. I had seen faculty teaching 1980 vintage operating systems while today's OS is totally different . To me this says most don't think . Why do I say this? Because most don't come out of their shell. They don't read books . This means that thinking is not autonomous. You need a purpose. You should push yourself hard to think. 
Motivation - most lack motivation. When I used to select faculty in many institutions - I had looked at thousands of people in this- , I found most didn't have motivation to teach , to learn, to read and to think. Many said that they didn't get industry jobs and so they joined teaching. Most quality improvements were push type not pull type. In IISc,  it is a pull type. A faculty in IISc goes after new research  areas, new projects etc. It is automatic. In other institutions, there was a push by AICTE to make faculty go for PhD. Pull didn't exist. When I talked to many REC faculty, in the pre NIT days, I found they were comfortable teaching a known subject and didn't bother about learning new areas. They defended their approach of sticking to the known subjects. They had no motivation to learn a new area like computers. They were against research. It was shocking for me. To some extent ,in the nineties, private colleges moved to new areas like computers and IT and forced their faculty to learn and teach computer courses.  

The above two are big and important hurdles. Surprisingly, most students don't have the inhibitions and fixed mindsets of their teachers. They love to learn new things, they love experimentation, they want to move towards current technologies and interdisciplinary work. They need guidance. That is missing. If we provide that , things will be bright . I tried this experiment through the FAER Scholar program. With mentorship ,it achieved very good progress. About 2500 students show interest in projects in current technologies. Some colleges support them. 
 I see a small and not insignificant  minority of faculty keen on improving their knowledge. So I focus my discussions towards them and budding young children , not the dead wood. I found, rightly or wrongly, that I can't correct the dead wood. I want to clarify here that age is not a reason for poor motivation. There are thousands of old people who continue on their golden path of reading, learning and thinking - like most of the people reading my posts enthusiastically. I appreciate them and that habit and curiosity.
Let us look at innovation next.

03/07/25, 4:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 763
Before we get into innovation, let us look at our hurdles for innovation.  
The first and biggest problem is our non imagination. At school level children are scolded for imagining. But imagination is crucial for innovation and an anti immigration mindset is not desirable. We have to listen to Einstein on  imagination.  
“Imagination is more important than knowledge. Knowledge is limited; imagination encircles the world.” Einstein. But our schools and colleges have the opposite views. 
How do we resolve this- by story writing sessions. 
So the first step in imagination  is to create simple stories. One can start with retelling ,embellishing or modifying well known stories. 
I liked the ant and grass hopper story - versions- and modernised it.
Ant and grasshopper
Ant was working through summer collecting food. The grasshopper was enjoying the weather lazing around.
The grasshopper told the ant to enjoy life like what it is doing.
Ant said no and continued its food gathering.
Winter came. Ants enjoyed life , ate well and stayed indoors.
The grasshopper was starving.
That is the old story.
Let us modernize or politicalize  the story. 
Politicians got into this. 
Communists cried that it is unfair. They said that the government should feed the grasshoppers. Environmentalists  worried about the extinction of grasshoppers. Activists cried hell.
A good communist felt that ants' food should be confiscated and distributed to all.
The second part 
After some time other communists started shouting that worker ants are exploited. They should get  rest and have a family. Capitalists said ants doing work need incentives.
So they built special cells for ants doing more work and appointed servants to them.
Communists egged on the ants to go on strike.
Since it is summer, no one is bothered.
There was not enough food in winter. Many ants died.
Communists and capitalists started a blame game.
The ant colony got destroyed.
Moral
Poor compartmentalized thinking. No holistic approach. No appreciation. No high thinking. Poor attitude.
Ask questions to write more content. 
What will be the reactions of people in your area to this story ?
Content creation is the focus here, not style or humour.  
You may ask students to look at thousands of such stories and modernized them with societal reactions. Students can create conversations between old world kings and ministers, between politicians etc . There are endless opportunities here. 
Ask them to write the reactions of Romans on the murder of Caesar.
Students can describe a day in the life of their neighbours, the interactions between neighbours,  etc. 
This is a big exercise of writing that will kick start innovative thinking. 
There is another hurdle - constrained straight jacketed thinking. This is again due to our education system. We teach the students that this is the only path, only approach, only solution .
I recall two incidents here. 
One - a child in 3rd or 4th standard was asked to give analogies for phrases like 
As white as
As brave as etc. 
Her mother and I felt that we won't go by text book - as white as snow, as brave as a warrior-, we gave her examples as - as white as milk and as brave as a lion - . Unfortunately the teacher didn't like that and gave her zero marks. 
I told my daughter about binary system when she was learning decimal system , she told her teacher and her teacher scolded her. 
We need to come out of this constrained knowledge and look for alternates. Practical systems work beyond mathematics and don't have a complete model. We need to apply our thinking . That aspect is neglected in our engineering education. More in the next
Greetings 
Dks
04/07/25, 5:15 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 764
“You don’t get to pick your family, but you can pick your teachers and you can pick your friends and you can pick the music you listen to and you can pick the books you read and you can pick the movies you see.
You are, in fact, a mashup of what you choose to let into your life. You are the sum of your influences.”
Austin  Kleon

How do we build the habit of free , positive unconstrained thinking in students ? That is the next question.
“You are the master of your thoughts, and therefore, the shaper of your life.” 
Allen.    As you thinketh 

It is not easy. But the digital world can help us. 
For a long time people were divided into rulers and ruled . The ruled are the majority with mental slavery. That is more dangerous than physical slavery. No system will help in removing this mental slavery. 
Learning is easy , but unlearning is difficult.  It needs constant practice. 
First we should understand our big enemy is not outside, but inside.
According to Pressman  ,the following is a list of the forces arrayed against us :
1.Resistance (i.e., fear, self-doubt, procrastination, addiction, distraction, timidity, ego and narcissism, self-loathing, perfectionism, etc.)
2.Rational thought
3.Friends and family

activities that most commonly elicit Resistance:
1.The pursuit of any calling in writing, painting, music, film, dance, or any creative art, however marginal or unconventional.
2.The launching of any entrepreneurial venture or enterprise, for profit or otherwise.
3.Any diet or health regimen.
4.Any program of spiritual advancement.
5.Any activity whose aim is the acquisition of chiseled abdominals.
6.Any course or program designed to overcome an unwholesome habit or addiction.
7.Education of every kind.
8.Any act of political, moral, or ethical courage, including the decision to change
for the better some unworthy pattern of thought or conduct in ourselves.
9.The undertaking of any enterprise or endeavor whose aim is to help others.
10.Any act that entails commitment of the heart—the decision to get married, to have a child, to weather a rocky patch in a relationship.
11.The taking of any principled stand in the face of adversity.


 Our understanding of ourselves is poor. We are diffident, thinking we can't do many things. Remember Obama's slogan “can do”. We don't have good confidence levels. We are doubting Thomas's.
Also we are highly emotional people with a lot of fear when doing a new thing. Some don't even travel to other cities.
Self learning is an important activity. Contemplate every activity you did. Review it.  Think how you did it. Criticize it. Find better ways of doing it. Be happy if it went well. Remember to do it better next time. That starts a good approach to thinking.  
Look around and find out how others do that same activity . Identify similarities and differences. That helps to improve yourself. 
Don't believe anything anyone including the teacher says. Question it. Try to prove it false . Accept it afterwards. 
The question is how does a teacher improve free thinking in his or her students. 
The answer is  very difficult.  We need mentors and counselors or psychologists who  can guide the students through regular weekly or daily interactions. It needs a mix of reading , listening and talking to mentors. Lectures won't help very much. Regular practice is the answer. It will be easier if it is done at a very young age. Unfortunately, parents, society and teachers do the opposite today. Education goes in the reverse direction. 
Regarding rational approach, the western system swears by it. But 90% or more of inventions and innovations come through intuitions and not through rational approaches. As I said before, rational approaches constrain students' thinking processes. Rational approaches are good for regular deterministic problem solving and not for problem discovery. 
More in the next post.
Greetings

“Aano bhadra krtavo yantu vishwatah" is a Sanskrit mantra from the Rigveda, meaning "Let noble thoughts come to us from all directions" or "May good and noble thoughts come to me from all directions from across the universe". It emphasizes an open and inclusive mindset, encouraging the acceptance of positive and beneficial ideas from various sources. The mantra is a call for wisdom and positive influence from all parts of the world”
I want to complete my discussions on free thoughts and open mind with the above rig vedic suktam. Please appreciate how our ancestors valued openness. This to me overrides all other precepts of learning. 
Sit , relax, calm your mind , listen to all thoughts , analyse , discriminate and find answers. That is self confidence. 
So the first step in this process is to get multiple views. Unfortunately, our education emphasizes a unified view . That is wrong. 
The best example of Education is to argue multiple aspects . 
What happens with engineering education is
It concentrates mostly on engineering principles. It ignores the following:
1.engineering practices
2.engineering applications - very much essential for survival of engineers. Applications are in many sectors- industries, business, trade and commerce, finance, health , agriculture , transport , utilities, security  governance and management, people and domestic sector, defence etc. Most applications are complex and involved . We ignore all these. We live in a well of engineering principles and some processes, not all. 
3.complexity of transformation and usage
4.customer and human issues
5. Impacts on the quality of life.  resources, nature, environment, economy,  society-higher  inequalities, rural urban divide. 
What we ignored is what is required for innovation.  That is our tragedy. 
We need a few courses in engineering 
Social engineering
Engineering practices
Engineering applications in business 
Digital economy
People oriented engineering 
Solutions engineering
AI applications. 
Focus should be on applications for innovations to emerge  in large numbers. Digital transformations have produced big innovations like ecommerce,  mcommerce, quick commerce with many innovations. 
Secondly, social innovations are more widespread than technical innovations. So social knowledge is a must. Current technologies focus on people. So we need to move in that direction. Industrial innovations are mostly AI based. 
Similarly ,solutions are seeing a sea of changes. We find statistical interpretation as an essential aspect of solutions - a shift from mathematics. Digital twins play a big role in understanding online activities. Covid parameters were selected by digital twins. It has a lot of applications in many sectors. Multiple solutions is another accepted fact now.

06/07/25, 4:38 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 766

Let me start with a quote .
Edison said, “Genius is 1% inspiration and 99% perspiration.” Why is it that some ideas just stay ideas, other ideas become interesting inventions, while yet other ideas not only become inventions but are considered so innovative that they change how people lead their lives? Edison had the ideas and the work ethic to try to repeatedly fail. In spite of those failures, he ended up with 1,093 patents and created the lightbulb and electricity, the phonograph, and film projectors and motion pictures. He had two focus points - practical applications and usability solving the last mile problem.
These two - applications and usability- are key parameters for innovation along with continuous improvements in processes, customer outreach and satisfaction. Performance factors today have shifted from efficiency ,latency, to agility,  effectiveness, functionalities, scaling for growth and interactive operations - not totally blind automation but responsive automation. 
In order to understand potentials of innovation, we need to have a realistic view of practical systems like industries, power plants, fintech, commerce etc. 
Initially engineering systems in the 18th century were closed systems - energy was produced in house- basically steam-, used in machines and machines drove the end-use devices like steel mills or spinning units . With the introduction of consumer products and with the need for resources and transport, the system opened up. 
In the beginning of the 20th century ,this changed with electricity becoming the main energy source. So engineering looked at multiple types of consumers. So it started looking outward. But engineering education was looking inward. With the adventure of computers , this changed further. Software development depended on users and their needs . The Internet and web changed user dependence  further and deeper. Do we understand that scenario? That is required for innovations to gallop faster. 
Let me look at a broad grouping of systems not necessarily an engineering system , may include banking , utilities etc. 
Every system has four subsystems - a back end - engineering end with technologies , design, architecture and integration - ,
 a front end - an operational end , customer connect , usage
a support end -, an infrastructure support system 
and most importantly a learning and innovations system. Normally we view only the front end and back end . The other two are implied or merged with the backend.
So , the current scenario is that social innovation edges technical innovations aside specially in India. That is unfortunate. We are catching the tail of a tiger all the time . We need to control the tiger by controlling its body. This is true in IT, renewable energy , materials, semiconductors , and many other sectors. 
Technical innovations today focus on AI and convergence of technologies . Don't ignore that. So we identify innovation into four  groups - backend, support end ,learning end  and front-end. The front end focuses on customers. Backend focuses on the overall system architecture, design, configuration , updating and integration with new technologies.  
Let me look at two examples in India - ecommerce and fintech/ mutual funds. 
Ecommerce uses more of social innovation and less of technical innovations. It has poor learning skills for better improvements. It still doesn't use AI or IOT or blockchain to a big extent. Focus is on the front end and not the backend. Backend architecture and integration is not organized for continuous innovation.  
The same is true with most applications in India. The front-end is getting attention . The backend is in firefighting mode. 
Another example is mutual funds management. While the US uses AI extensively for most backend operations, Indians don't use AI very much.
“In the U.S., over 35% of mutual funds are now powered by AI, a stark contrast to just 1% in India. “
Keith ,CEO of investor AI
“. In the U.S., AI is used for market sentiment analysis, stock selection, strategy reinforcement, and even writing research papers. In India, we’re still mostly using AI to enhance backend operations rather than drive front-end investment research, but the direction is promising.”
Keith 
Backend changes get better effectiveness, better long term benefits compared to front end customer activities. Both are important.  
So we have a lot of opportunities for innovation. We need to understand how a system works with multiple consumers and stakeholders. 
Greetings 
Dks

Industry is best at the intersection of science and art.
— Edwin Herbert Land
Edwin Land of Polaroid talked about the intersection of the humanities and science. I like that intersection. There's something magical about that place. There are a lot of people innovating, and that's not the main distinction of my career. The reason Apple resonates with people is that there's a deep current of humanity in our innovation. I think great artists and great engineers are similar in that they both have a desire to express themselves. In fact some of the best people working on the original Mac were poets and musicians on the side. In the seventies computers became a way for people to express their creativity. Great artists like Leonardo da Vinci and Michelangelo were also great art science. Michelangelo knew a lot about how to quarry stone, not just how to be a sculptor.
Steve Jobs

Let us ask what innovation is?
One view is
Innovation is thinking of, and then implementing, a better way of doing things. An innovation doesn't have to be something completely new and different. It can be any sort of incremental improvement. 
Another view is

Innovation is the process of creating something new or improving upon existing ideas, products, services, or processes to generate value or solve problems. It involves introducing novel concepts, methods, or devices that can lead to significant advancements or disruptions in various fields.
Invention is the creation of something entirely new, while innovation is the process of introducing that new thing or improving upon an existing one and making it useful and valuable.
A third view
Innovation isn’t just an idea or a tool. Instead, it’s a process that guides businesses through developing products or services that deliver value to customers in new and novel ways.

Innovation involves challenging the status quo, thinking outside the box, and taking calculated risks to drive progress and achieve breakthrough outcomes. Innovation is driven by a combination of factors, including curiosity, creativity, and the desire for improvement.

Fourth view 
To me,  innovation is the constant urge to improve anything you encounter including yourself , your activities, your job , society in general and to find better solutions. 
Better means better performance, fairness, satisfaction, improved revenue or profits, growing a business , knowing customers etc - hundreds of ways to improve our life, our surroundings , our society, our governance, our ways of living, our happiness, in short many aspects in many sectors - even in our cooking . Today I saw a news item about a Shimoga person making a dairy free millet ice cream. 
I saw several innovations in the food and restaurant sector in Bangalore like  by2 coffee, darshanis, mobile coffee tea supplies, house catering, etc. 
Innovation today means blending two to three areas -
- functional areas  like cooking, travel, stay , manufacturing, sales, supply etc,
- technology areas like mobiles, cloud, social networks, data , Internet and web, etc
-AI area affecting everything ,
-  Management and governance area,
-social area like media , entertainment, news, politics, 
And
-Consumer areas like consumer likes ,wants, satisfaction, behaviour etc. 
So innovation is a process of  mixing and blending changes in various areas and pasting it in a chosen area.  
Innovation needs triggers and thinking. Innovation is against the status quo. It implies change . An innovator  becomes a change generator.

“What a piece of worke is a man! How Noble in reason! How infinite in faculty! In forme and mouing
how expresse and admirable! In Action, how like an Angel in apprehension, how like a God!
The beauty of the world, the paragon of animals—and yet, to me, what is this quintessence of dust?
Man delights not me— nor woman neither, though by your smiling you seem to say so.’

Let me pose some basics for innovative thinking. It will be a good way of introducing innovative thinking.
Requirements
We have discussed these in detail. 
1.flexible open change seeking and accepting mindset. Looking out and ahead. 
2. Imaginative , unconstrained thinking
3.Motivation- urge to do something , 
4. Reading, contemplation , observations, questioning habits. 
5.urge to improve. Outward looking. Futuristic thinking.


Important principles for Understanding by us

We discussed these also. 
1.Today's systems are huge, connected , complex, growing and changing. 
2.Today's systems are multidisciplinary and use   several technologies, properly architected and integrated. 
3.Engineering is a unified, inclusive ecosystem consisting of several subsystems of different kinds and each with many components ,features , characteristics and functions. All are equally important and needed to run the system. Even the connections of subsystems and components are important. 

4.Innovation happens everywhere. 
Innovation doesn't happen in labs but in the streets. Innovation doesn't follow or respect principles, theories, procedures, assumptions  and rules. 
Ignore theory and rules and constraints in the beginning . Think with imagination ,not intelligence  to identify improvements. 
5.Observe innovation reality - know what is happening in many businesses, many organizations, industries. You will get plenty of ideas. 
6.Find out why technology absorption is slow. What factors affect technology adaption. 
7.Find out what people want. 
8.Remember innovation is not a plug and play activity. It is a sustained ,iterative process with possibilities of failures. 
All these need a mindset change ,an openness, a freshness in thinking. I go to the extent of saying - don't look at innovation with your learnt knowledge or expertise - like an electrical engineer or computer scientist- , but as a common man with a lot of common sense and good observation power. Learn from the people in the street. 

Characteristics 
1.Look at a big problem. Innovations are not easy in small ,isolated problems. It needs a modern big problem to find hundreds of possibilities. There are hundreds of example systems , businesses, utilities, organizations, health units in front of us.  A view of the whole is needed, not of parts. The whole includes business, technology , education and skills , environment and people. The whole is more sociological and less technical. 
2.Don't look for a universal solution - “one size fits all “theory is gone. 
While a universal solution may not exist, we can modify a solution existing in one industry or services to fit another industry or services. Many components like sensors, IoT, data analysis, control , accounting, finance, management , governance, some professional practices , supply chain have a lot of commonality across many sectors. 
3.We can develop a thinking process to innovate. Innovation can be adhoc or process driven .  Today most innovations occur with an iterative mechanism . 
4.Innovation depends on technologies, businesses, services, society and people and interactions between them. . 
Innovations lead to practical  improvements in design, production and development, operations, processes, business models, services, performance ,profits, growths, sustainability , future proofing etc. 
5.There are thousands of opportunities for innovation. It happens everywhere, including in your home. 
6.Cross cultural innovations are possible.
Innovations happen at the intersections of business, technology ,governance and people/ society. This is important to understand. Don't be a specialist in one area ,but a generalist in many areas. 

Steps for Innovation thinking
Lesson 1: choose a real life example for finding out the improvements . 
Thinking should be based on a concrete reality, not abstract entities. It should  begin  by looking at a practical  example - say a cooperative bank, hospital, a shop, a petrol station, hotel, restaurant,  small industries, service units etc . There are hundreds available in every small town.  Conduct a detailed survey of the unit chosen. Find the problems. Find the unit's goals and gaps . Find out their financial and business models. This opens a world for you. 
Lesson 2:
Discover the overall economic and social scene of the country. It is a good reality learning. It helps you to compare the problems of your unit with the problems in the country . It will build a realistic picture. 
Know why startups are succeeding. Most innovators and startup developers use similar mindset and principles. So understanding startup culture is necessary for innovative work. 
 discover - how that particular industry or unit works, how other units of similar nature work, how other industries work. 
Discover technologies available , their maturity, applicability, etc . Discover vendors. Analyse the technology. 
There are a number of innovations of digital technologies in many sectors. Know about them. 
Look at how banks went for automation with corebanking. Know the totality of that project. Know how ecommerce is evolving . Know how people are becoming digital literate and mobile friendly. 
See how Facebook built a social network for millions with periodic improvements. 
Look at Google searching the world and getting answers.
We see and feel  the impact of UPI and wallets every day in every market place. UPI is a remarkable innovation and it is destructive , removing or reducing the use of cards and cheques.  
Examples such as the above - mcommerce, UPI ,etc excite people and encourage innovative outlooks. 
I have seen hundreds of failures. Look for failures like ERP failing  in many cases, most accounting applications failing in the eighties with  only tally succeeding  , hundreds of computer applications for hospitals, examination systems, admissions, taxes management, government functions,  etc failing  in the eighties, dotcom failures in the late nineties,  branch automation had limited success , even financial inclusion was struggling in the beginning, many food supplying ecommerce units failed initially ,etc. Tatas conducted a dare to fail category in their innovation contest. Each failure led to a better system to be evolved. Study failures in order to avoid pitfalls. There is a large amount of culture and psychology involved in the adaptation of a new practice or new technique or system by any organization. See how our water and power utilities resist innovation even now. See why we are not solving the problems of traffic and garbage in big cities like Bengaluru.  
Understand that today is the day of AI applications and changes. 

Lesson 3:
Using your knowledge and the facts gathered , analyse the chosen unit. 
Create a big list of improvements that can be done, that are done , and that needs to be done. 
Lesson 4:
Start at the End- see what improvements are possible.starting at the end avoids worrying about implementation details. It is a great advantage to set goals. Starting at the end avoids constraints, engineering principles and rationalism. 
Here’s a trick that screenwriters use: work backwards. Begin at the finish. Write down a list of possible innovations.
Lesson 5:
This is the action period. But actions also need thinking. 
Now you are ready for a serious study of the improvements listed and use your principles of engineering to plan the sequence of actions for the improvements listed by you. If you have listed many improvements, prioritise them and shortlist some for further consideration. 

This concludes my response to Parvati's query. Appreciate comments.

Prof TV Gopal has comments.
“Sir, nice post to read. Industry has a relentless focus on the product. Academics need to build the people even if aspects of the product are showing Engineering. Compromises. I am inclined to think that when science reciprocated academics are best. Your thoughts please. - Dr. T V Gopal, Formerly, Anna University, Chennai, India”

My response
It is going to be a generic explanation. 
There are no generic principles applied universally. Universal theories don't exist though science is looking for them. There will be exceptions. So use viveka to know which principle applies in a particular situation, which doesn’t  ,which rules and axioms are applicable ,which do  not. 
Rules and principles are meant to be  applied carefully and selectively and not indiscriminately. Some even apply wrong rules in some situations. 
So your first statement on industry focusing on product was a 19th and early 20th century theory. By the second half of the 20th century ,it changed. Process became important , products were standard. By the beginning of the 21st century,  customer focus , customer satisfaction, and customer tailored products became the focus. People became important. Now we talk of innovation. 
My important conclusion was that : 
Ignore principles, theory and rules for innovation. My Indian examples for innovation are GD Naidu , the guys who built the idly batter grinder, the guys who invented darshanis , Bombay dabbawalas, etc. All these broke existing practices and came up with an innovative solution. I quoted two big western innovators - Edison and Steve Jobs. Let me clarify that there are thousands of innovators. 
In the early days, most innovations were accidental . Now it is possible to go through an iterative thinking process for innovation. It is reaching millions now. 
I agree with you that academics should build people. It is a very important statement. But do they do so is the question. Unfortunately it is not true. 
At school level , there exist some people focus. But at the level of higher education , people focus is ignored. Our focus is theory , concepts, and class room lectures. Interactions with students  are minimal. We work on the dictum 
Teachers teach , it is upto the students to learn, understand and apply. 
Only our interactions with a few students start at the project work level . 
I disagree with your last statement . 
Kindly recall my statement that rationalism, scientific thinking will help you in knowing the known concepts. It will be a hurdle for innovation.  
Basically, we are human beings who know when to apply what. You use rationalism or science in experimentation, analysis . But you use commonsense in design activities , integration and operations. Practical experiential knowledge doesn't come from science. 
Science based engineering education is good for pure research and detrimental for applied work, innovative thinking and operations. But post 1960 ,the science based engineering education became important and practices and engineering designs were given the go by by the US universities. Revival of engineering practices didn't succeed. We also followed that approach. IISc is a good example of that US theory. Fortunately, theory was useful in looking at large systems. Our focus was more on mathematical modeling, analysis and optimization.  It helped in better understanding and design of large systems. So my advice is that blending theory and practice  is necessary.
 Life is balancing contradictions. I keep saying that even education is a balance of skills, knowledge, theory ,practice ,applications, inclusion and innovation. We focus only on engineering theory . That is not correct. 
We seem to have opposite views. That is fine , good  academic freedom.

Prof Parvati has an additional query
“Thank you very much sir for a veritable roadmap for developing innovative thinking. I would like to push it a bit further and request you to quote, if possible, any example of a school/college/University in India where innovative thinking is  implemented in the curriculum, and it's highlights!
Pranams!”
My response 
A good and relevant query. 
Many schools and management institutions have a design thinking and innovation course. Stanford ,MIT and even Harvard have such courses. Many schools in India and some in Bangalore conduct a course on innovation. Focus is more on design thinking and critical thinking . Innovation is through projects. But a systemic approach was missing. Technology was missing. It was more theory and less output. 
AI generated curriculum is copied below. 
A curriculum for a course on innovation should cover topics ranging from foundational concepts like creativity and design thinking to more advanced areas like open innovation and managing R&D. It should also emphasize practical application through case studies, group assignments, and real-world projects. A good course will balance theoretical knowledge with hands-on experience to foster both an understanding of innovation principles and the ability to apply them. 
Here's a more detailed breakdown of potential curriculum components:
I. Foundational Concepts:
Introduction to Innovation:
Defining innovation, its types (incremental, radical, disruptive), and its importance in various contexts.
Creativity and Idea Generation:
Techniques for brainstorming, lateral thinking, and idea management systems.
Design Thinking:
A human-centered approach to problem-solving, including empathy, ideation, prototyping, and testing.
Innovation Strategy:
Understanding the importance of a clear innovation strategy and how to develop one.
Understanding the Innovation Process:
Exploring the different stages of the innovation process (idea generation, development, implementation, diffusion).
Barriers to Innovation:
Identifying and overcoming obstacles to innovation, both internal and external. 
II. Innovation Management:
R&D Management: Managing research and development activities, including portfolio management and team structures.
Open Innovation: Understanding the principles of open innovation, including collaboration, knowledge sharing, and external partnerships.
Intellectual Property Management: Protecting and leveraging intellectual property in innovation processes.
Innovation Ecosystems: Exploring the broader environment that supports innovation, including funding, infrastructure, and policy.
Measuring Innovation: Developing metrics to track and evaluate innovation performance. 
III. Application and Practice:
Case Studies:
Analyzing successful and failed innovation initiatives across different industries.
Guest Speakers:
Inviting industry experts to share their experiences and insights.
Group Assignments:
Working on collaborative projects that require students to apply learned concepts.
Final Project:
A larger, more in-depth project that allows students to demonstrate their understanding and skills.
Specific Areas of Focus (depending on the course level and target audience):
For Business Students:
Innovation in business models, product development, marketing, and entrepreneurship.
For Technology Students:
Innovation in technology development, product design, and systems integration.
For Social Science Students:
Innovation in social impact, policy, and community development.
For Education Students:
Innovation in curriculum development, teaching methodologies, and learning environments. 
V. Skills Development:
Critical Thinking: Analyzing complex situations and developing creative solutions.
Problem-Solving: Identifying problems, generating solutions, and implementing them.
Communication: Effectively communicating ideas and proposals.
Collaboration: Working effectively in teams and with diverse groups.
Adaptability: Adjusting to changing circumstances and new technologies. 
All the above reveal the following
Institutions are aware of innovation and teach it.
Focus is more management , less on social aspects
Technology based innovations - the largest innovations potential - is ignored mostly . And that led me to devise a curriculum.

11/07/25, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 771
Let me return to my life at IISc during 1968. 
I changed my status from that of a research student to that of  a faculty. 
The faculty position meant that I should continue my research for my PhD and should teach courses . The EE department had both a three year post BSc program for  BE and an ME program in two streams. I taught courses for BE and both streams of ME. We didn't have entrance exams for admissions to BE and ME at that time.  We received the applications and tabulated candidates ‘ details in big sheets. It was a laborious process. 
We shortlisted the best students in BSc for an interview for BE admission. We did many iterative direct admissions for ME, the first two or three steps shortlisted top ranking students for direct admissions. The final stage was a list of candidates for an interview. I participated in the tabulation as well as in the discussions for picking the high rank students. We decided to conduct an entrance test for BE admissions in 1969. That was the first time institute conducted entrance tests. Later we conducted our entrance exams for ME and research along with BE. We first conducted the exam in bangalore only and later extended to other cities. Initially, Chennai had the largest number of candidates and I conducted the exam in Chennai twice and in Mumbai once. 
As another duty , we have to guide ME students for project work. I got three students that year. One of them withdrew later. Both students did great work . It was a great start for me in academics in four aspects of education- teaching, research, students projects, students selection. 
The year 1969 saw many changes and involvements for me. 
The first one was in the electrical machines lab. We took charge of running the lab for second year BE EE and ECE students. I felt that a standard set of experiments is not a good way of learning about machines and their load behaviours . It is routine and ritualistic. I wanted to change it. I talked to Prof Joseph about it . He encouraged me to redo the lab experiment. So I restructured the experiments by grouping the experiments to provide deeper learning . So the students studied each type of machine in detail , conducted a few  standard experiments and designed new experiment.  It was my attempt at innovation. 
Also I got permission from Prof HNR to abolish the exam for the lab. It was a big change in mindset. He said that when he studied - He studied in the same department- , there were no formal exams. So he supported my idea. I decided to go by the lab work, designs and assign weight age for each experiment done by a student for sessional marks. We decided to conduct a viva for 15 minutes for each student. I formed a committee and included NV and VVS ,my colleagues, into the viva team. 
The viva was revealing. Students were upset by my changing the old pattern. Now they dreaded the viva. It was no more easy going. Viva is an unknown factor. You don't know what question you get to answer. So they were unhappy. One girl student even cried in the viva. But most did well. We could test their knowledge in a better way by posing to them situations of failures of machines. It was a learning experience for us. 
Greetings
Dks
12/07/25, 4:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 772
Let me continue with the happenings in the academic year  1969 70. 
Then the institute decided to go for a credit system. The institute set up a committee with Prof BS Ramakrishna as a chairman to develop the complete procedures for a new curriculum . By that time, Prof HNR had gone to Delhi as a member of UPSC . So Prof BNN Iyengar became the head and he consulted me on every matter. He trusted me a lot. He consulted me on even serious matters like promotion of faculties. So he nominated me as a member of the committee. So I got fully involved in that . I was a member of the main curriculum committee , in fact the juniormost member, all others being professors and also  its subcommittee which worked out the details of the new proposed curriculum. I had an opportunity to work with both Prof BS Ramakrishna and Prof R Narasimha and Prof AK Rao who was the dean. It was laborious work. The difficult part was convincing most heads and senior faculty to accept the proposals. But Prof Dhawan supported our recommendations. For the first time, we prepared a full scheme of instructions with details of all courses offered.  We had several days of discussions on grading . We decided on a 5 grade system - A,B,C,D,F. We decided on a GPA of 2.2 for pass and 3.2 for distinction. Then we discussed how an instructor decides on grades. In most cases, we can't apply any kind of normal distribution. We decided to set up a grades advisory committee. I was a member of this committee for about 5 years. We had a lot of hilarious incidents happening with faculty grading.
 After a few years, we moved to a 7 point grading system -S,A,B,C,D,E,F. Later E grade was abolished when CNR Rao was the director.  
This was a very valuable experience for me. It made me understand the system trying to cater to students with different capabilities by defining a varying workload and a number of electives. IISc was the first to introduce this flexible system in India. 
It was an innovative reform . It was also an innovative project deciding on many aspects to be done differently. It was pushed by a few senior faculty like Prof Dhawan, Prof Ramakrishna,  Prof Narasimha etc who got their PhDs abroad. Many heads were not comfortable about this. But most seniors supported it. So they went along with it. 
I understood the concepts. I came to know about the grading system followed in most American universities . The most important learnings I got were  :
1.Curriculum development is an extremely important and time consuming activity. 
2.It is integral to teaching . 
3.It is a serious design activity. I found that this aspect was ignored in all universities . It was treated as a copy and paste activity with no serious thought or design work. 
4. It threw up many questions on each component of the system. Since there was an open discussion, many views came out. So I could see many perspectives of a problem while analysing it and solving it. It taught me the need for integration - a seamless run through many processes - course structures, content development, sequencing, schedules, time table, evaluation and grading, advising students for course selections, registration and dropping a course, thresholds for course loads, planning for the full program etc. We built robust mechanisms like a grading committee,  timetable committee, departmental curriculum committee, registration organization, faculty advisors, etc. A lot of it was new and faculty training was required. It also meant  participation by most faculty in the overall process and individual components . Their support is necessary for the success of the project.
5.It is a dynamic activity - we need to have a relook  every year and make changes. It should be a part of academics. Most faculty don't get involved in this. Most don't even understand this. The autonomy given to a few colleges helped them to develop their curriculum. I played a big role in evolving the curriculum in several colleges and universities. I had big fights in many meetings. 
It helped me later in pushing better understanding and implementation of curriculum in many institutions. I did it with a lot of  commitment and passion.  It is also the first major reform project that I participated fully in.
Greetings
Dks
13/07/25, 4:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 773



Let me discuss a general aspect or trend or characteristics that people follow in arguments , discussions or taking decisions- I call this binary thinking. We feel someone is good or bad . A particular  college is good or bad. Hinduism is treated as totally bad because of casteism by a large number of people across the world almost universally and all other aspects of dharma are ignored  .  You divide people as wokists and non-wokists , optimists or pessimists, Luddites or utopians etc. Normally a blind belief is used by a person to arrive at a conclusion. 

  This binary theory uses a dominant aspect of a person or problem or organization or event or situation and ignores all other aspects. I see this single dominance  theory adopted by most. Recently I had a small and inconclusive discussion comparing Sankara and  Ramanuja. Again, it saw this binary theory based on one dominant fact dominated the conclusion-it was based on the inclusivity of  Ramanuja to accept other castes into brahminism. . All other aspects are ignored by us. It is carried by us even in professional decisions, friendships , political decisions etc. Only a very high degree of maturity and viveka will help us in analysing a problem in a holistic , multi dimensional manner to reach a conclusion which is more meaningful and useful. 
This single solution, dominant factor dependence based binary theory is implicitly taught in our education. It is dangerous. Only a small percentage of students come out of this syndrome. But most solution techniques and most assumptions in engineering are based on the binary theory. It is useful to simplify a problem so that it can be solved. A popular modeling concept , linearization is a good example. 

Let me return to my experiences at IISc. 
 I had talked about my hostel experiences. I continued with hostel wardenship till 1971 and quit from that position in 71. 
The hostel wardenship gave me a lot of learnings. I was very fortunate to have that experience at that young age . Even Prof Dhawan mentioned it during my interview for the selection of assistant professors held  around Jan 1973. He appreciated my overall involvement in the IISc activities in addition to academic excellence.  
Let me list a few learnings that I had due to my wardenship.
1.I realised that  understanding, interactions and management of people at various levels  are more important. I had many such  people oriented encounters. 
Prof AR Vasudevamurthy as warden introduced a system of inviting a few professors or senior faculty for lunch. He won't take them for lunch. But we ,myself and Dr Mahishi used to take them to the mess for lunch. These meetings had heated discussions. One senior professor complained about corruption in the office, another argued against his theory. It was interesting to observe these opinions. Prof AK Rao said idly came from Tamilnadu and Prof ARV protested that idly is there in Karnataka also . It is not a Tamilnadu product. I saw sensitivities here. These meetings gave me a tremendous opportunity to meet many seniors and discuss many hostel matters with them. They used to bring the views of their students. It was a good feedback mechanism. Also, it brought me closer to many professors . Prof AK Rao became a good friend. These meetings  were practised for a year and then discontinued.
2.It was Deepavali time. The messes organize a special feast every year for many functions and Deepavali is more special. Normally we had a special lunch with light fruit dinner on those days. One year 1969 or 1970, I don't remember, the B mess representatives wanted a special dinner instead of lunch on diwali day . The employees expressed their unhappiness. My other colleague  , Munjal, supported the dinner proposal and said employees should be happy as they get special food which otherwise they couldn't afford . I argued that celebration with family gives them  greater happiness and I was against the dinner request. It was turned down in the end. This shows the need to understand the psychology of an individual ,as PG Wodehouse's Jeeves says. 
More encounters in the next post.

14/07/25, 4:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 774
I was looking at interactions with people. Let me continue with my interactions with people during my hostel wardenship period.
3. Once , on an institute   council meeting day, the mess had to send food for lunch to the members after the meeting. I asked the assistant registrar for a vehicle to transport the food. But he said that  I should have requested in advance for a vehicle and refused my request . So we didn't send the food. The registrar got panicky and I told him the situation. He was a great diplomat and sent a vehicle immediately and food was sent to avoid embarrassment.  Hostel staff were tense at that time. 
4. I saw a few incidents attacking the ego  of students. Let me narrate one incident. A faculty from Engineering College, Guindy joined IISc for research in civil engineering.  He was a rank student in BE . He registered for courses in the first term. When the results came, he got poor scores. That destroyed him mentally. He became erratic. I talked to Dr TB Subba Rao and we decided to shift him to NIMHANS for treatment. His parents came the next day and abused us . All our explanations were not accepted by them. They took him back to Madras.  Similarly, another student was falling sick often . We both visited his room. It was dirty. So we shifted him to the ward and got the room cleaned. I encountered many such cases and many times,  I and Dr BS Subba Rao, who joined later, used to visit the hostel to check the health situations and conditions. 
5.I didn't agree with halal cutting of animals on the campus. I suggested that I don't want to encourage that practice in the campus, but they can buy such meat from the market. But the mess committee didn't agree with their request. 

6.There were many more incidents like students' unruly behaviours , students’ difficulties , adjustments with language, culture and food habits and poor value systems . I want to talk about what I mean by poor value systems.
7.Many students don't vacate the hostel room after completing their education . They stay unofficially in the hostel even after getting a job in Bangalore . The number was not small, it was in hundreds. 
8.Many students don't inform the hostel office about the guests they get and pay a nominal amount . 
9. But one thing I abhor is that some students left the hostel without paying  the last few months' mess dues. They misused the trust of the institute.  I didn't understand their behaviour.
So we introduced a no dues certificate mandatory for all those leaving the institute  to get an NoC  from mess, hostel, department,  gymkhana etc, to avoid such phenomena.
I had one episode which occurred in the mid eighties when I wasn't in the hostel.  It concerned a TCS associate, a past student of IISc. He was deputed to go abroad, but didn't have his degree certificate from IISc and that was required for visa.  Mr Krishnan, regional manager,TCS requested my help to get his degree certificate. I enquired in the students section for his certificate. I was told that his award hadn't gone to the senate for approval. Further enquiry revealed that he didn't pay his last three months' mess dues. I informed Krishnan about the situation.  Krishnan said he will pay the dues immediately.  I scolded him , and told him that values are more important and I won't be a party to help such mean minded people. 
This learning about different kinds of people was very useful for me in all my activities. I had interactions with thousands of students, faculty, experts, peers, vendors ,sales people, technical staff of many companies ,institutions and organizations.  In later years, I interacted with several people every day. I became more of a people manager. It had a lot of rough and difficult periods. 
Somehow , the concept of people management was not understood in general. We think that only managers need some HR skills. But I think that a holistic understanding of  people ,their behaviour and reactions  is necessary for all of us  to create an empathy, to understand the different  views of others , to know and react to the emotions and behaviours of people, to build good confidence in them, to elicit the required information, take proper  decisions and to negotiate successfully with them. I learnt all these and became a mature people manager successfully navigating difficult paths.

15/07/25, 4:46 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 775
Prof Vinod Vyasulu comments on binary thinking:
“DKS, I want to extend one aspect further. I agree with your points on binary thinking. But as one considers different aspects, there is often a need to balance the effects of one against another. In economics, except on ideal conditions, one cannot control both inflation and employment. In power projects as we found in Bedthi, environment effects vs just electricity. That was a complex case. 
A balance has to be sought. In that process, some may focus on aspect A, others on C. There may still be other aspects C, D etc. Different individuals will focus on different aspects, give different weighateges based on their values. This l ads to differences, often, sadly, bitter ones.
You mention that some say Hinduism is bad because of casteism. This is true. Consider Dr Ambedkar, born dalit, who gave caste discrimination high weight as a negative when compared with the concept of equality of all. On this basis of lived experience, he embraced Buddhism. It is the shruti, as you have shown that do not talk of caste as discrimination. But people live by the smriti, especially the Manusmriti and the Yajnavalkya smriti, which discuss caste hierarchies in detail. Until we find a way to rise above these discriminatory practices, there will be genuine reasons to move away from Hinduism as generally practiced. Q  see this inour society today. 
Caste discrimination is not Brahmins looking down on others it is others, from their position in the hierarchy, looking down on those below. It is a festering societal problem.”
My response 
Thanks for highlighting this aspect. 
I consider this binary thinking even by the socalled intellectuals , wokists and politicians as a non desirable, attention diverting mechanism. We all fall for that trick and we start following the binary thinking or dominant factor approach . We may be ignoring many important aspects. We ignored the environment for the economy for a very long time. We used coal and petroleum extensively and indiscriminately for a long time and have a climate crisis today. Traditionally both engineers and economists simplified problem solving and arriving at decisions. The reason is the dependence of most processes on formal mathematics and the consequent limitations of the models. But the mid 20th century looked at multivariate models successfully. So we need to adapt to multifactor thinking and I started thinking and finding the many factors from a situation that should be considered. 

Decision making is definitely a compromise between many factors. There are ways of modeling them with multiplicational weightages assigned to each factor. Penalty factors were used. All engineering problems were complex and we needed to look at many factors - costs, efficiency, reliability, reach,availability, maturity of technologies, ease of use. So the solutions were approximations to handle these factors by balancing them. I started thinking of multifactor thinking and modeling from my PhD work itself. My strong mathematics and use of iteration, computational methods and tensors helped me in my tasks. Computer solutions speeded up this process.  I followed it in many examples I was involved in.
Let me illustrate some of my experiences.  
1.Recall Bedthi hydro projects.   You know how we introduced the environment into the design by taking into account three factors - energy, environment and social displacement . The forest was costed in a better way. It took many decades to cost displacement realistically due to the myopic thinking and resistance  of the powerful. 
2. I did the same thing with rural energy studies by looking at energy, costs, efficiencies, maturity of usage and built a compromise model.  We used vector programming solutions. I developed an integrated energy model for rural areas. Many factors like conservation, focus on renewable, quality matching were built as constraints. That showed a method for taking into account many factors ,not just one dominant factor alone. 
3.We did a more detailed and less approximate model for energy planning for Uttara kannada district. It was the first detailed model which integrated many forms of energy. It focused on three aspects-biomass,  conservation and renewables. We used penalties for the use of nonrenewable energy .
More examples in the next post.

16/07/25, 5:11 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 776
Let me continue the discussion on multidimensional and multidisciplinary thinking. I consider this topic important and hence more explanations are given.
4. When I encountered frequent powercuts in the late seventies and early eighties, I wanted a reliable supply for IISc.  In 1969 , I moved to multiple 11kv substations at a higher cost ,but security , safety  and reliability were ensured. I built a good, safe, available and reliable power system for the institute. It was a totally new way of thinking and a new approach. Many complained that the cost was high. But I looked at the overall scenario and not just power supply costs. Due to power cuts many experiments were delayed and lots of costly chemicals were lost. In those days , importing chemicals was difficult. So I considered reliability as important along with overall cost reduction. Similarly ,I gave importance to safety.  
So in the early eighties, I decided to move to a 66kv power supply for IISc. I started this request with Prof Ramaseshan. He didn't support my proposal. When I told CNR Rao about it after he became the director, he supported it. I went ahead designing the new system including site selection, capacity , 11kv ring mains, supply to the new supercomputer building , transmission by overhead and underground lines, new jointing techniques etc. But our proposal  met with opposition from inside and outside  . Even the then KEB chairman met Prof CNR Rao and opposed the project. The KEB chairman was my friend and I didn't understand his motivation for opposing this project. 
But I convinced CNR with only one argument- do you want IISc to be one among the 1000s of substations suffering power shutdowns or one among the top 10 getting a reliable supply? He understood and gave me the go ahead signal. We did minimise the costs, but wanted a rugged and reliable system. So the complete design considered multiple factors. There was another decision to be made . The new supercomputer centre building was coming up and they proposed a substation in the basement . I opposed it due to the increased risks of fire. I suggested building  an outdoor substation just outside the building. It increased the cost by rupees six lakhs in the mid eighties.  It was high and I convinced both Prof MN Srinivasan and Prof V Rajaraman. 
I followed the same principles for designing power supplies and distribution for JNCASR, Jayadeva institute of Cardiology and Vidhana soudha. 
I had serious quality doubts about the purchase of a kilometer long 66kv cable bringing power from the KEB supply point to the IISc compound wall. We had a lot of discussions on this and we spent a week at the plant to study the manufacturing processes. Finally, we agreed on buying that lowest cost cable. So L1 is not always my first choice. Quality was my first concern ,but cost was also important. 
5.Recall my Bangalore water supply work. I got objections from KSCST for suggesting dual water distribution- separate high quality potable water and low quality non potable water-  due to costs of piping , but water wastage was ignored. Rain water harvesting suggested by me was not considered. The excom didn't consider conservation as important . It went by costs. 
6.Recall my modeling of a more complex problem - carrying capacity studies for dakshina kannada district. It considered several resources and optimised the location of projects in many areas. It used a lot of user inputs. I spent several months on it.
7. My involvement with computerization in a large number of organizations and banks was successful as I used applicability ,usability , reliability , maturity, legality and dependability  as the criteria for selection, not just costs. It was a well thought out mechanism developed by me to handle these as essential conditions. The two part tender was started by me and later it was made a law. 
If I hadn't done that kind of multifactor analysis and had gone by only the lowest cost for selection of vendors,  my mahithisindhu project wouldn't have succeeded with many powerful sharks looming nearby to grab the money. The project was a big success with multiple factors built into it. I succeeded in using faculty from 20 engineering colleges in it.

17/07/25, 5:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 777
Let me continue my discussions on the need for multifactor thinking . 
8.In the case of corebanking for the  Indian bank, I rejected iflex because the server used windows OS and it was vulnerable to security breaches. I had to defend it in the board meeting. In the case of banks , data security is vital. Here I looked at long term costs vs short term costs of servers and software. We designed an architecture in stages to improve reliability and minimise data losses. We had to discuss a lot before taking a decision on near data centre and redundant networks. I should say here that when RBI decided to open up ATMs and allow people to use any bank's ATM for cash withdrawal,  most transactions were rejected. We found that one big culprit was the inadequate network . Network capacity due to high rental costs were under low values. We can increase network bandwidth , no problem. But I found the bottleneck was the routers inadequacy and low capacity. So compromises with costs are necessary. Cost is not the ultimate criteria for selection.  Other factors like correct and complete customer service. 
9. I saw a bank rejecting TCS banks software because it needs compilation after each change. I consider it a good security check. We don't select a software package based on one factor. We have many factors to consider beyond functional completeness. We spent months of evaluation to arrive at a decision. We know the plus and minus of our selection. The dominant factors depend on reality and the situation. 
10.So I applied multidimensional and multidirectional thinking in my selections for these organisations.  We spent months deciding on a good ERP for many organizations, in particular for Jindal Steels , Karle infrastructure , Mysore sugar ,DG, shipping , etc. The decision depended on usability and adaptability and not costs.  We also considered the feasibility of integration.
11.My greatest involvement which used multidimensional thinking and approach was used for my inspections of colleges for approval and later for accreditation. Both used several factors like - infrastructure, equipment, computers, library, research, faculty competencies, teaching methods, curriculum etc. It was a complex task. 
12.Let me look at a question frequently posed to me by parents and students wanting to join an engineering degree program. What should be the selection criteria - selection based on a  good college or a better branch ? Most people were crazy about a good branch like computer science or ECE against a branch like civil engineering.  This question was posed to me by hundreds of people. I said that the best principle to follow is that the overall ambience of college is more  important than the branch . But I built   some caveats  in this principle. To me, it is a necessary condition that a student shouldn't get dissatisfied with the final selection. So I tell them that we will go through the  list of colleges and branches available  once we approach the selection time for a final decision . I had recommended some students to pick up mechanical engineering instead of electrical engineering as the students were in favour of mechanical engineering. Some are very clear on what they want . My son was clear that he wanted to join chemical engineering at KREC. So I yielded to his wish. Initially, I picked REC Trichy , but he was not happy. So I returned that allotted seat and waited for KREC counselling. 
13. Let me take you all back to our discussions on materialism vs spiritualism . 
A governing model now depends on GDP. Is that correct? What does it mean for poor people? Don't we bother about the environment? Is health and child mortality not important? Can we be materialistic without spiritualism? Does an agricultural produce belong to a farmer fully? Is there no role for nature - soil nutrients, rain water?Who owns nature?
All these lead us to the human development index over GDP.

Now let me return to my quote on Hinduism being assessed only on one factor ,that is caste , and Vinod's response. 
I agree with Vinod that today caste is a dominant economic and political factor for most  non brahmin communities. Brahmins lost control on most aspects of Hindu rituals for more than 50 years. The evidence is the surge of millions during many religious functions in many temples particularly in Tamilnadu . Hinduism today is governed and promoted by the OBCs . Most known astrologers are not brahmins. Brahmins have moved away from religion to commerce, from teaching to accountancy and banking to IT to AI. With the arrival of Mauchaley's education scheme, most brahmins moved away from vedas , scriptures and Sanskrit to English and jobs as clerks . So they moved far away from rigorous religious practices like daily vedic recitals to sandyavandanam . It has been Arbindo ghosh, mother, Swami chinmayananda, most maharashtrian  saints, purandara dasa, kanaka dasa, lingayat ,vokkaliga, kuruba and devanga saints, Narayana guru, Mata amritanandamayi, sadhguru and many others who have been propagating Hinduism for more than hundred years. They were and are brahminical as per manu smriti ,but not born as brahmins. 
One can do a better assessment of an aspect like energy or Hinduism by looking at the pros and cons. Hinduism is a lot more than caste. It is about dharma, culture, ethical practices and liberation. 
Hinduism assumes people have different habits, beliefs and behaviours. So it defined a four way path - karma, bhakti, jnana ,and yoga - to be followed by people. It allowed different types of rituals - puja at home, puja at temples, pilgrimages, festivals etc. It is more than a religion. It provides a purpose for life . If enjoins people to give, abhor violence, speak the truth, live harmoniously and follow order. It enriches our living through the asramas and our living culture. 
It took more than 1000 years to transform the religion through the puranas. We had  twisted the sastras to help the rulers and make the ruled ones as slaves. 
Discrimination happens even outside religion - in jobs . Why do we talk of class 4 employees in government service ? Is it not discrimination. The same kind of  discrimination happens in most organizations not only in India but in other countries as well. Caste syndrome exists everywhere. 
That concludes my discussions on binary thinking. 
Thanks Vinod.

19/07/25, 4:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 779
Let me return to my experiences in the institute in 1969-70. 
I was staying in F66  in the hostel from 1966 onwards till the end of 1970.  In  1970 ,two new hostels, one new mess designated as C mess , an apartment block for married students and a grand and huge gymkhana were built . Prof. Dhawan took a lot of interest in these constructions. The new hostels had single rooms . We fixed geysers in the bathrooms and discarded the inefficient, energy guzzling boilers - that happened due to a report submitted by me to the director comparing energy consumptions and savings. I showed substantial savings. So it was agreed to fit instant geysers in three bathrooms and storage geysers in others. This practice was followed afterwards in all hostels that came up. 
The C mess was a food pickup system from a central place like a buffet. It was a self service setup. It also had a cafeteria part.  No food was served at each table as was the practice in A and B messes. Unfortunately, the cafeteria,  a good idea, never came into operation . 
One other factor I observed in 1969 was a weak engineering services unit in the institute . The engineer we had till mid sixties ,Ramachar, retired and the new appointee was not upto his level of competence and so was relegated to maintenance . Even the maintenance was poor. So the construction work was supervised by a supervisor and Dhawan wasn't impressed by that. 
Similarly , we didn't have a good electrical unit. There was a supervisor and he died in 1969. Another electrical engineer was appointed ,but he was too mild and totally ineffective. We had a lot of power breakdowns in the hostel. Most electricians were not trained persons. I had to go at 11pm in the night and climb a pole and change the fuse. Electricians were scared . 
So Prof Dhawan decided to have a senior engineer from PWD on deputation as estate officer and project engineer. Mr K C Reddy from PWD joined the institute in 1970. I met him in the registrars room on his first day of joining. We became friends and he took my advice on many aspects. I knew him till his death. He planned his office well. He brought two engineers- Mr GovindaRao from PWD and Mr Thimme Gowda, an electrical engineer from KEB.  My friendship with Thimme Gowda became very close. He helped me a lot and I advised him on the  institute's power supply. He was committed to his job. The institute had professional help in its construction activities. By 1970, we got the new 11kv supply and ring main. We recruited some trained electricians and a young supervisor. Things looked up for the institute.  But regular maintenance was still poor. 
The estate office structure built at that time is continuing even now. Dhawan created an estate office for ISRO later and CNR Rao created an estate office for JNCASR.
