20/07/25, 4:24 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 780
Let me continue with my learnings from IISc.  I am still in the period 1969-70.  
Let us discuss the importance of reforms for a leader. The first thing one learns to become a leader is to learn and follow an effective and smooth method of deciding and implementing both major and minor reforms. I not only learnt this process - it is not an idea but a whole sequence of essential prerequisites and actions needed for successful implementation of a proposed change- ,but also implemented it in many situations. I simplified and changed rules in anything I look at. That was my first step in my urge to improve things habit . 

By this time in 1969, I saw four  big reforms by Prof Dhawan :
1.Faculty evaluation and promotions - introduction of a contract system and a complete structure for detailed and fair evaluation of individuals. Evaluation was based on several factors - research work, teaching, sponsored projects, equipment development , consultancy and extension work.  It is a big reform . It revolutionized our academics and research. It encouraged sponsored projects , consultancy, and training teachers in other institutions. 
2. Revamping our power supply - he entrusted the job to me. 
3. Curriculum reform with the introduction of a credit system ,called unit system , with new structures. 
4. Setting up the estate office with engineers deputed from PWD and KEB. 
All three improved and speeded up the working of the institute.
He did these after several rounds of  discussions and analysing the various problems and difficulties that may come up after implementation.
 He also started breaking a few rules. One rule was that each department had a fixed number of faculty positions. So the department can advertise for a post only when a vacancy arises. I had to wait till GKrishna resigned in 1967 and a vacancy came up in the EE department and consequently ,I got selected as a faculty. 
Prof. Dhawan decided that if an academically suitable person was available, the institute will select him even  if there is no vacancy in the department. He went through the council to approve this change. He said there are no faculty positions in the department. All faculty positions belong to the institute and a holistic look will be taken on selections. He created extra positions, increased the budget allocation for that purpose and implemented his idea. This changed rule had its drawbacks . Some departments grew fast and some stagnated. The aerospace department grew very fast and the electrical department, a growing department, stagnated. 
But one major change or shakeup happened due to this changed rule. He decided to select many senior academics from India, US, Europe . He identified people and formed   expert committees and they selected people  found suitable. He recruited a sizable number - 30 to 40 while faculty strength at that time was around 200 plus.- of persons at senior positions like associate professor or professor. It created a cultural shock to the senior faculty present in the institute and shook up the system. 
 Many senior faculty like Prof V Krishnan, Prof AKN Reddy, Prof SV Rangaswamy , Prof IG Sarma, Prof LS Srinath, Prof Narasimhan, Prof Raju, joined many departments during the period 1970 to 72. This metamorphosed the senate. We got views and practices of many Indian and foreign universities in the discussions in the senate. While that was useful, it was resented by the older IISc professors. But faculty at IISc started to look seriously at the happenings in other institutions across the world and modified the good ones for adaptation in IISc.  The institute became vibrant with discussions in the coffee shop.  
One rule  pertaining to inbreeding was discussed and debated. Many universities do not recruit their students as faculty immediately after graduation. There were some faculty who wanted that rule to be implemented at IISc.  But after looking at the problems we will have if the rule was implemented, it was rejected. We felt that we have a fair process that gives opportunities to all candidates from any institution and so it is unfair to reject our students and deny them that opportunity of fair competition. If the country was producing hundreds or thousands of PhDs like US, then that rule might have some meaning. Since the country was producing very few PhDs in engineering and most of them from IISc,  it was felt that the rule would lead to disaster if implemented.  That shows our openness to ideas. 
Greetings 
Dks
21/07/25, 4:52 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 781
“What is culture?
A society needs harmony and order in its survival and humanistic living. So it defines a set of mutually acceptable practices to be followed in public and that becomes culture. 
A person should know and  understand the daily , weekly and regular  interactions of individuals belonging to  different groups of  our society  in their lifestyle and living like eating, talking, working, habits, entertainment, rearing a family, helping others etc . He should  follow and act according to the established norms of the society  to become an accepted member of that society  . That is culture. “ my  simple view . Hinduism emphasizes harmony and order. So culture is embedded in it. 

Let me look at my first visit to Bombay. 
I needed to solve large problems for my optimization work for my PhD . So I decided to use the big CDC3600 computer at TIFR Bombay.  By this time  I became a friend of Mr PA Bukebag , an officer of the physics faculty. He visited IISc every summer to conduct the TIFR summer schools. It was an elaborate procedure. I used to have tea with him regularly . We used to go for kannada movies in the Majestic area. The majestic area had many cinema houses. Due to a fight between MGR fans and Rajkumar fans once, it was decided to screen only Kannada movies in the Majestic area and Tamil films in Ulsoor area. 
This kind of language fights occurred at regular intervals in Bangalore. In the seventies, eighties and part of the nineties, it was kannada vs tamil agitations and today it is kannada vs hindi fights. But I want to clarify that only a small fraction of kannadigas had this intense feeling. They made this agitation as a lucrative profession. Most kannadigas are very nice people , they definitely love their language , but they had adopted Lord Emsworth's concept of protest, a mild show of unhappiness with friends and no public dissent or resentment. Otherwise, they behaved like normal law following citizens minding their jobs. 

  I saw many kannada movies with him. I also picked up kannada alphabets and started reading kannada news items . 
My first source of  exposure to Bombay was through the several novels I read in my school days. Two of them gave me a good picture of bombay- kalki’s alaiosai and mayavi’s serial in Kadir. 
I learnt a lot about our villages , cities , Bombay, life of the poor and rich, independence struggle and common man's part in it , skepticism of Gandhi's non obedience movement etc. He gave a realistic picture of India of the early 20th century - 1930 to 1940s.  He painted a huge social and cultural canvas. A lot of people in Tamilnadu were addicted to his writings and even now many read his books. His historicals are also well researched and written.
 Mayavi wrote about Bombay culture. He described Matunga, kings circle, VT, flora fountain, Marine drive , and the people living in Bombay particularly two totally different communities - parsis and tamils. Tamils were conservative ,and stuck to their native culture. Parsis were very progressive. His novel talked about a relationship between a tamil girl and a Parsi leading to the birth of a child , abandonment of the girl and the willingness of a tamil boy to marry her. It was a very modern and progressive novel and it introduced me to a new urban sociological thinking and understanding about our different  societal groups and cultures. It gave a picture totally different from the usual conservative literature of that time. Surprisingly, it didn't post a revolutionary picture. It didn't preach. It described the happenings. The description was vivid and simple.  Inferencing is the reader's job. See the impact the novel had on me. I liked it , had a great interest in it and read it week after week as it appeared as a serial . 
My second source was the gossip and discussions that I listened to in my early school days from people visiting home for vacation. Many youngsters worked  in Bombay and came home for the summer holidays. They talked  about the small houses, suburban trains, concerns food - Thursday it was onion sambar- , and big Bazars etc. Actually ,a neighbour of ours died while traveling by a suburban train and his family relocated in the village . They used to talk about Bombay.  Another , a neighbour's son, was working as a scientist in Bombay.  He came with his children. His son, Ramakrishnan joined ITC for a job later  and became a good friend of mine . I got different perspectives of life and struggles in Bombay.  Many younger children wanted to go to Bombay for jobs. That was the charm of that city in our minds. 
So even though I hadn't visited Bombay till 1969, I had a good idea of that city and always hoped that I would visit it one day in my life. That happened in 1969. I didn't visit for a day, but spent 15 days in Bombay ,in south Bombay - Colaba.

“Hinduism encouraged social interactions through festivals, samskaras, pilgrimages. Even organizations like sabhas and samitis were described in the Rigveda.  So there is a strong cultural component in Hinduism. “

My stay in Bombay was a different and new experience. I wanted to stay close to TIFR . So I wrote to Bukebag. He organized a room for me in the atomic energy department hostel near the old yachts club close to Gateway of India. 
I chose a train route to Bombay via Miraj / pune instead of Guntakal. It was a leisurely travel, less crowded and so easy to get a reservation. I traveled by meter gauge train to Miraj, then took a broad gauge train to Pune and then changed to  another  broad gauge train to Bombay VT. I took the mail leaving in the night from Bangalore . I went to the hostel  from VT station . I went to TIFR the next day, met Bukebag . He introduced me to Mr GN Patak ,office manager in the computer centre. I got an account on the computer. 
I went to the west canteen for breakfast. My food style changed a lot. I didn't eat rice during the fifteen days stay. I ate breakfast, lunch in the west canteen and dinner in the east canteen. Both breakfast and lunch were western style - bread toast, cornflakes, cutlets, soup, boiled veg .  etc. I didn't eat eggs and so I didn't eat an omelet . Dinner was chapati and subji. 
I met several people in the canteen. I met Prof R Narasimhan during breakfast time . He wasn't a communicative person. But he was polite with me. I had mid day coffee also in the canteen. I used to walk for a short time after lunch with Bukebag.  I met Dr Sarabhai, N Seshagiri , and many others. I spent a whole day talking to Seshagiri. I met many people from Saha institute of Nuclear physics, atomic energy department who were using the computers , but none from IIT Bombay.  
 I used to come by 8am by TIFR bus and stay till 7pm. I left by TIFR bus. The bus started midway between Churchgate and VT stations. I had to walk to my hostel. That was my routine. 
In those days Dadabhai Nauroji road used to be almost deserted after 7pm . Walking in that street was risky. Too many fraudsters and touts. Marine drive was better. 
I went with Bukebag for dinner at Concerns . I got a full view of Matunga . It looked like a Tamil place. But Bombay had a lot of kannadigas . I found many in TIFR itself. 
I didn't go to any other place in Bombay.  I was busy at TIFR with my work from morning to evening.  
I hadn't programmed in Fortran so far. I bought a book at TIFR and picked up Fortran in a day. I had some familiarity before and so within two days ,I wrote my first loadflow program. Programming was so easy compared to the low level languages I used in NAL and HAL. The computer system used punched cards. So I learned how to use the key punch machine. I spent half a day punching my program and data . Data punching was a bit involved as it depended on the column strictly or the values would change.  
Access to computers is through a batch system and several batches were run day and night. So I used to run my program deck several times in a day and so debugging was fast. Within a few days I tried several load flow methods and tested them. 
Computer operations were well organized.  We got our deck and printouts after every batch run. There was no missing the  decks or results . Access was great. People were helpful. It was a dream period of computing on a high speed , long wordlength machine. It is a tragedy that CDC didn't succeed. Business was more in commercial computing and IBM excelled there and that destroyed both CDC and Burroughs.  
In England the computer companies merged to form ICL . It stayed for a longer period.

What is an intrinsic view of culture?
“It is a set of dynamic practices that makes one a societal human with harmony, empathy, tolerance, nonviolence, sharing as essential principles. Culture is non dogmatic, embracing multiple and even opposing views and practices. Culture is not based on ideal principles.Hinduism  embodies these practical and societal principles in its concepts of dharma. ”
Culture is an important and integral part of Hinduism. Hinduism is the only non dogmatic religion. It accepts every practice and many beliefs and views. It provides many paths to live . It allows multiple cultures by several groups to coexist. 


Let me discuss the ambience - both physical and human-  in TIFR and the computer centre. 
Physically, I found TIFR  a remarkable place- close to the sea, lots of open spaces , lots of walking areas, calm and serene, soothing, beautiful with lots of paintings chosen by Bhabha ,providing an aesthetics that makes you become serious and feel like sitting and contemplating about many things in the world. My reflections ,after seeing hundreds of institutions across the world ,are that I found Bhabha was very unique in blending several aspects to create a synergy producing ambience - an oasis in a mad, crowded , hurrying world of mostly  people who didn't have any time to relax , to think, to contemplate and who led a busy ,mechanical life. His decision to set up the atomic energy department away from the high handed Delhi bureaucracy , and to create a serene and serious atmosphere in a crowded city with a lot of thinking and meticulous planning in many areas is to be admired by all of us. 
After meeting with many greats in the country, I find him to be the tallest of all. 
Functionally also, the ambience was great. There are no lavish offices for even the senior professors . The entire place was clean , you didn't find garbage thrown anywhere.  Toilets were well maintained. 
But it is the human interactions that attracted my attention.  Most faculty were serious, aloof,and lived in their own world. But there were lively and intense professional interactions happening everyday. The urge to learn was evident. There was a class system. There was intense rivalry. The normal outlook was serious and critical . The system was to me a cruel but necessary one - it didn't tolerate tardiness, mediocrity and laziness. It wanted work of high quality . Most scientific people were on contract and were thrown out if found incompetent and didn't produce any good research  outputs . Later, I saw the full force of the system . I found that many scientific staff needed to find a job outside. Prof Narasimhan helped in finding jobs for many ,to name a few - SV Rangaswamy, Ramasubramanian,Mendiratta. 
Most employees were highly dedicated. They knew they had to work hard to help the scientists and help science to grow. Their dedication was something you won't find anywhere in India. I saw a similar intensity - both academic and administrative - later in the computer science department at the University of Manchester. 
The helping tendency was evident in the computer centre. It was an open system where a user wanted something and the office and operators helped them. Again ,I saw for the first time, a highly costly and high powered computer centre had welcoming arms to embrace everyone who wanted to use the computer. I wonder why we don't have this open arms attitude in any institution and with  most people in India? 
Definitely ,I have come across several people  with a public and open  helping attitude both in IISc and elsewhere. But that number is a miniscule in my acquaintances. I keep asking why we are so self centred, shy and not worried about others. Definitely ,most help their colleagues  with office work . But public spirit is lacking. I am not just talking about government staff , but faculty and teachers  in colleges and schools . Most are not open to share their knowledge and help others in any other place.  
Fortunately, IISc is  also different in this respect ,in helping others , but our operational systems are poor and not designed to help. But we helped most faculty in other institutions and teachers in schools. I will contrast this TIFR ambience with IISc ambience in the coming posts. 
Coming back to the outside computer users in TIFR, it was amazing to see the open support extended by the computer centre to them. All TIFR facilities - bus transport, canteens are open to them, no inspections are done. All are treated equally ,no discrimination. The computer charges were nominal. It is becoming difficult to see such types of systems today. It is still easier to enter IISc.  But I found the security barriers in many private engineering colleges in Bangalore highly irritating. They look at you as a criminal. The security in most of these institutions have not been trained to treat people as humans and had no clue as to whom to admit and whom not to. Such kinds of security is prevalent in most places in India.  We give powers to these people and that gets abused.
More in the next post.

24/07/25, 4:46 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 784

The users in TIFR were provided a separate room to work. There were users from different institutions and there were good discussions and help happening there . We were helping each other in debugging the programs. I helped many in rewriting the programs . Later I helped many in the institute in writing programs including Prof Joseph. 
This was the first time I saw a different institution. Mr Bukebag told me a lot about TIFR. I also saw the organization of the computer centre . It gave a new perspectives for me  to compare two institutions - IISc and TIFR.  I saw many similarities and differences. 
Before I discuss the differences between both institutions, let me pose a question:
What is an institution?
We can describe the infrastructure , faculty, outputs etc to define an institution. It was the first time in my life that I was thinking about this question after exposure to TIFR . I realised there is some inherent aspect unique to an institution. What is it? How do we find it? How do we follow it? 
This quest  led to another question 
Who is a human?
How do we define a human being ?
-As one with two hands, two feet, walks erect, talks etc. Or
-As a farmer, merchant, teacher, clerk etc.
- Or Good or bad etc. 
One answer was to discriminate between humans and animals .
some said a  rational person. Some said thinking capability. Some said they can learn etc. 
Religion came with an answer: humans have a  soul. A soul is a mix of many characteristics like good or bad or evil , many behaviours like shy, social etc , or many habits , capabilities.According to Hinduism,  even animals have souls. 
Even , you can't say humans are social , ants are also social. 
My take is to consider the overall aspect of human lives to define and the word I found is culture . Culture here includes all types of interactions in a society , not restricted to festivals and functions. Culture includes soul also here. It includes routine actions like farming, teaching etc. 
I don't know of any other word that defines a human holistically , not in a piecemeal / partitioned way or describing physical or functional aspects only. So I use the word culture here. I think that man is a cultural entity. Culture can be different for a person, a family , a family, a village, a city and a country. There may be hundreds of cultures we see in India , some with small differences ,some with large differences but mostly with a common core based on capabilities and interactions. 
It looks simple , but I am struggling to define this . I can use the concept used by Yajnavalkya in Brihadaranyaka upanishad to define Brahman using “not “- it is not this, not that etc. 
Soul is a good way of describing a human , but it reflects mostly on  internal actions and not on external interactions. The soul doesn't reflect our views on nature, resources, our adaptations to changes, our inventions etc. So I used the term culture to include all these aspects . I assumed that culture is by definition interactive and changing. But I also feel that culture is a vague term , not precise and is normally used for groups. So one can say that humans are a socially interactive entity? Is there a better way of defining humans? 
Your comments please.
I want to see a deeper intrinsic meaning in an institution. Let us discuss it in the next post.

A small partial break from my question - Who is a human?  and go back to culture.
Mr Sambamurthy comments :
“Your comments on culture are quite insightful, but brief. Societal harmony is key to Hinduism. But today 's politicians appear to use "Culture" to divide rather unify society. How do we course correct this. In my sense philosophers and intellectuals like you have a role to play. Look forward to your comments.
Regards”

My answer is simple and consistent. 
The malaise is :
All are confused about sanatana dharma. We don't have an authentic text . Many people add masala to vedic texts or misinterpret them . This includes a large number of people - foreigners as well as Indian religious persons. This needs correction by a group of unbiased and to some extent irreligious persons - some good Sanskrit scholars - to prepare a book on rig veda. RL Kashyap did a good job decades ago. 
There are many views of our dharma. 
Some look at as a caste system
Some look at it as a temple system
Some are very righteous thinking they are the authority to pronounce what is prescribed in sastra  and what is not.( Incidentally, sastras according to our religion are only the vedas including upanishads. There are many alternatives provided by the religion. There is no “one rule fits all “ doctrine. ) 
Some look at it as a purana - bhagavata based system with bhakti as the sole motive.
Some feel it is in danger and they have to fight to protect it.
Everyone thinks he is right and others wrong. So there is a world of confusion. Everyone reads some scripture and not the right ones and not fully, defines his or her interpretation and declares that is correct. Most people have converted a non dogmatic religion into a dogmatic one. Where are the dogmas defined in vedas for our practices and even prayers. All dharsanas go in different directions and that is accepted. 
It is time to call the bluff. 
So we need education at the school level . Teach the essentials of rig veda and some upanishads . Leave out practices - smritis and agamas. Children will start understanding sanatana dharma better. 
Secondly our universities should undertake interpretations and research on sanatana dharma in a non dogmatic manner. 
Those are the first steps. But religion is emotional, a calm approach is not easy. There are landmines around. 
Thanks Mr Sambamurthy.  

My understanding of humans has two broad segments - internal and external. While calling a human a cultural entity, I didn't portray a complete picture of a human. 
I want to look at three dimensions,  not just two. All three are not entities, but connectivities. 
The first is the internal dimension - who you are? What is your purpose and goals? Why should you live? How should you live? What should you do? How do you decide and act?
It is a big dimension. It is covered by so many disciplines like anatomy , physiology, psychology, yoga, nutrition, medicine, surgery, neurology, mental sciences, philosophy , behaviour sciences etc. 
The physical side of a human itself is complex . It is not a static phenomenon. We still are unraveling the processes of many thousands of reactions  happening in us every second. 
If the physical connectivity is complex and still has mysteries, the mental side is more complex. Mental components may act in opposite directions. Reactions are more complex. The mind is our connector to the outside along with our sensors. 
The second dimension is our external interactions. Again there are two subdivisions here- our interactions with society, and our interactions with nature. We have a good idea of culture as interactions with society. 
Let us look at interactions with nature. The interactions with nature are dominant in Rig veda. Rig veda thanks all natural forces in detail . It prays for all forces and all elements including animals. Its concept of ahimsa extends to animals and plants. It sees divinity in nature. Similarly chamakam in yajur veda details a complete picture of geology and geography and prays to them.
The third dimension is our religious and spiritual dimension. We will look at it in the next post.

Dks
26/07/25, 5:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post  786
We saw that humans are not alone. They are connected . First they formed a group called society and built culture to bind the connection. Qualities of a society are described in detail in the vedas.  The importance of harmony , sharing and order have been described in Rig veda and detailed in Atharva veda - sammannasya suktam.
“Like-heartedness, like-mindedness, non-hostility do I make for you; do ye show affection (hary) the one toward the other, as the inviolable [cow] toward her calf when born.”
“Having superiors (jyā́yasvant), intentful, be ye not divided, accomplishing together, moving on with joint labor (sádhura); come hither speaking what is agreeable one to another; I make you united (sadhrīcī́na), like-minded.”
“Your drinking (prapā́) [be] the same, in common your share of ​food; in the same harness (yóktra) do I join you together; worship ye Agni united, like spokes about a nave.”
“United, like-minded I make you, of one bunch, all of you, by [my] conciliation; [be] like the gods defending immortality (amṛ́ta); late and early be well-willing yours.”
We can consider the above as the foundational principles of culture. There are hundreds of superstructures , which give variety and variability to culture. A normal human life is built on this understanding of give and share, harmony and order principles. 

 Second, they depended on nature to live . Though Sanatana dharma realised the importance of nature, people did lip service to nature without understanding the implications of nature. But Sanatana dharma knew the forces of nature and their role in our life. 
Isa upanishad says 
“All belong to God. Use them sparingly in a sacrificial manner. “
We believe in ahimsa. 
So people connected and kept connecting with nature. 
There is a difference between the first connection - culture and the second - environment. 
The first is mostly local, and manageable with few uncertainties. Most objectives are achievable at simplistic levels. Mathematically, culture has variety and predictability  but it is finite and less complex . Not so nature. Nature is complex, infinite, and uncertain. Mysteries increase. Understanding is poor with many unknowns. 
Then the question arises :
I get food and shelter from nature , living from culture, then why religion is needed for me? 
I haven't given food to my minds. I haven't understood my minds. I live more mentally than physically . Mental connect involves curiosity,  eternal search, understanding and unraveling the mysteries. 
The first thing I want to know as a human is :what is this expanding mysterious brahmandam called universe, space and their evolution?  That connect is provided by  religion. Hinduism, particularly yajur veda, has a strong religious component .
So we have three connects - culture with society, environment with nature and religion with cosmos. Hinduism embeds all three. Spirituality goes beyond to search for Brahman and cosmic forces .

27/07/25, 4:36 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 787
“A soul is commonly understood as the immaterial essence or core of a living being—a principle that is thought to confer individuality, personality, consciousness, and, in many traditions, life itself.”
“the soul is seen as that which makes a being truly themselves—the seat of mind, will, emotions, moral character, and sometimes rational thought.”

Perplexity AI answer

Let us now look at the question
What is an institution ?
An institution is equal to humans in many respects. It has a culture. Many institutions are built on fear and subordination. 
First thing we need to understand is that dealing with people and interacting with people is a primary purpose of an organization. It is not trade, sales, banking, teaching, transportation, supply of goods and services etc.
People here means customers, suppliers, employees . Daily there are hundreds of interactions with people in an organization. This is true with academic institutions which deal with tens of thousands of students with a lot of energy, enthusiasm and expectations. A similar situation, but at a smaller scale,  exists with banks and retail outlets. 
So we need a different look at our organizations and institutions.
An educational should not worry only about equipments, computers, courses and examinations. It's job is mould the students and meet their expectations. 
The first expectation is transparency and openness. Students want to know why a decision is taken by the head, and what necessitated  that decision. Unfortunately , this aspect is missing in most colleges . Fear is pervading everywhere. Students were and are silenced . They can't ask questions. It is like a military camp. I even saw in some  colleges severe punishments given to students for a small noncompliance.  In one college students absenting from attendance to classes are fined heavily. The pleading of some parents that their son was sick didn't elicit any response. In all places the only interface for students and faculty is powerless ignorant and meek clerk. That is the interface. It is a mystery and puzzle  to all about the happenings.  
The second expectation is treatment. Don't treat students like cattle. Don't consider them as a nuisance.  Treat them as intelligent persons who enhance the level of the institution. Treat them as friends and associates. Most students don't bother about their college because of this negligence. Colleges don't utilise their distinction students with respect and they don't realise that they don't help their distinction students, but the distinction students bring credit to the institution. 
This treatment is a highly complex activity. There is inequality or class system prevalent in most places - poor vs rich, rural vs urban , etc. This is seen even at the level of students as well as faculty. In an NIT, one faculty member told me in an open meeting that professors promoted under career advancement scheme were considered inferior to selected professors even by the families. Failure is hurting deeply. 
Institutions treat all students as one with equal capability. They don't understand their students. So the third aspect is empathy . Empathy is absent in all our organizations. Power brings in arrogance.  Today's prime need is empathy. Treat people as humans first and not as dirt. 
The fourth and important aspect is fairness. People resent unfair treatment. I was never abused by our employees though I was the chair of the central evaluation committee for 14 plus years. They knew that I was fair, and didn't play the game of favourites. In many selections, the candidates told me that my questions were good and technical and openly appreciated me. As I said  before, I had thousands of interactions with suppliers . Most respected my decision. They appreciated my fairness. Even one company withdrew a case filed by them in high court. Next to an answer to the question why, it is fairness that satisfies the people. 
Next is straightforward behavior by faculty and management is another aspect. Some are devious. 
All these lead to the most basic component - creation of a good value system and maintaining them. That is the top most requirement. That will motivate all to do their best. 
So by and large, an institution has a soul and a culture . They are not easily visible but they play a role in making the institution a great one . 
People ask me why a college could reach the level of IISc? They say IISc is great because it gets the best students and they get mediocre students. They are not seeing beyond their noses. They don't see the worth of their best students. They don't create an open  culture in their institution. Their value systems are poor. They don't see it. Today these requirements are essential even for a trader or a bank. I hope that  government offices come somewhere near these desirable values. 
So we say an institution is a physical entity with its soul.

28/07/25, 4:44 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 788

Before I get back to discuss the soul of IISc as understood by me, let me discuss a comment by Prof. Karunakara:
“Apart from social interactions human can also elevate himself to realize Supreme(Brahma) with no form- (Nirguna Brahman) where all his cultural characters will shade out and can become one with universe. In this state he may not have cultural bindings and can guide other fellow beings to realize such unique state. Please correct me if anything wrong.”

My response
I agree with you. I tried to look at five dimensions of humans in their interactions with other entities :
Social  - culture
Nature - environment
Mental - knowledge and soul- jnana - from society, nature, universe , teachers. 
Religious - with universe
Spiritual - enlightenment state.
These five deal with human interactions. There are many dimensions for humans themselves like - body, internal and external sensors, input and output units, nutrients distribution, enzymes generators, pranic circulation, minds , behaviours, habits, emotions, multiple  control units. 
Spiritualists and vedantins try to understand Brahman through eternal search. They realised a number aspects of the universe like:
The universe is vast , expanding and changing. Nothing is constant. So reality is not to look at the universe through physical eyes but through intense mental , ununderstood processes to learn more about the unchanging ,unknown, all pervading, reality . That reality is called Brahman- nirguna Brahman.
There is a lot of energy in the universe . How did it come about? How this energy move around? What cosmic reactions are happening and why? What is gravitational energy? Physicists also look at these questions. There is a lot of mysteries here or unknowns here. 
How did life forms evolve ? Again, this question is looked at by Darwin and by Rig veda ,long before Darwin. Rig veda has multiple interpretations on evolution . It uses the creation part for initial seeding and then assumes that things are growing slowly . So there is an implication of initial creation followed by evolution and growth . We are today seeing the rapid mental - knowledge- growth in the past 200 plus years . It is not a pure selection only process . There is an intense learning and discrimination - viveka- process happening. 
Our knowledge of a wave is not clear. Ask a simple question - how solar energy comes to us . We have answers - photons. But we don't have full answers how photons get energy from the sun , how they transfer it into heat and electrical forms. Or heat comes in another form . We know electrons emit energy when going to a lower orbit in the form of heat. So are there more subatomic particles in waves? . How many particles do the sun rays carry? We do know that there is more space and very  less matter at subatomic level. We know that there are vibrations that are dominant at that subatomic level. So studying waves in many dimensions is useful. 
There was a strong feeling that we get some inputs to our consciousness when we are in deep sleep - turia state. 
But the greater problem is that waves carry many things. We know about radio signals moving in space over long distances. So consciousness is moving around the universe . Waves transmit information. We haven't decoded them yet . 
For the ease of understanding by common people , Hinduism introduced saguna Brahman- a god, a manifestation of nirguna Brahman, but with many gunas- features. People worship the gods. 

 Hinduism focuses on dharma and builds many additional constructs like rituals, philosophy, samskaras, living conditions, societal behaviours,  nature , forces, etc. Whatever is your approach, whatever is your beliefs, whoever is your god and whatever is your sampradaya or agamas, one thing is constant in hinduism - that is to follow all dos and don'ts prescribed in vedas like harmony, order, truth, peace, non violence, sharing etc. But we have  forgotten dharmic principles and have given great emphasis to rituals . We need to reverse this approach.

Dks
29/07/25, 5:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 789
I discussed the serious and concentrated research work and helping tendency at TIFR. Let me compare it with IISc.  I already talked about IISc ambience. 
Both are highly committed to research . IISc also is committed to the advancement of teaching and learning. 
Both work on cutting edge areas. But IISc works on several areas like many sciences and most engineering disciplines. IISc was a pioneer in engineering education and research. It started the PG programs before independence . IISc  also opened up many new areas of research. That became a landmark of IISc.  Instead of creating new areas of research in an existing department,  IISc decided to set up new units to provide more freedom for a  new area to flourish without influences from existing beliefs of departments.  This allowed free thinking . This destroyed stagnation of academic beliefs. Just like religious beliefs, there are stronger academic beliefs and they are difficult to change . These academic beliefs destroy futuristic thinking . I recall the fight to call electrical engineering as the electrical and electronics engineering department, the objections coming from the ECE department.  Similarly ,there were objections for CSA to use the name system science . Many departments changed names. These flexible approaches helped IISc to go forward without timelags. Many new units started in the institute. TIFR also did this experiment on a smaller scale. It spanned new institutions like NCBS. 
TIFR is focused on physics , computer science and mathematics . 
IISc admits students and awards degrees. TIFR focuses on doctoral work only. 
IISc focused on applicative  work very extensively . Some examples are health , water resources, wind tunnel research and testing, soils studies, power networks design, computerization, rural technologies, renewable energy, ASTRA Ole, biogas,  defence, and space related research , advices to the problems encountered by governments both state and centre . This is in addition to our regular basic research activities. But it became a major concern of the institute. We set up ASTRA,  CES , KSCST to look at alternatives.  
While both helped the academic and research communities in India, IISc is more open to visits and informal discussions , lectures and seminars etc. In the beginning ,the library was open to students and faculty of any university or college or research labs. In the sixties, I used to see many senior faculty from Bangalore university in our library in the evenings at regular intervals . When many engineering colleges came up , lots of students used our library for referencing. Spending a day in the library was common. Library staff were very helpful in locating books for users. NAL used to run a bus load of scientists to use our library every Friday. Later , access was restricted due to misuse of the library by some students.  
IISc  had an egalitarian atmosphere. No one interfered with others' work. Independence , freedom , and appreciation pervades in the institute. The freedom enjoyed by most is great , but it doesn't push research students to speed up their work. But there was no fear on the campus. Most were happy. This atmosphere helped the institute to grow.

30/07/25, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 790
After a week of fruitful stay at TIFR,  I returned to Bangalore via Miraj . 
I resumed my computing work at HAL. But the major change in my daily schedule was discussions with Prof Devanathan. I spent hours with him. My mathematics knowledge improved considerably with my association with him. 
Secondly, Prof Lewis joined the Foreign languages department to teach English . We had two lecturers teaching English already in the department.  I started associating with Prof Lewis to improve my English skills. I spent the next two years attending his lectures- not formal, but more discussions focusing on Indian practices, pronunciation etc. We gave seminars in the course. I was also attending a Russian language course with Dr Ganeshasundaram.
 It was unfortunate that the entire foreign languages department is non-existent now. That is a disadvantage of ad hoc experimentation. All directors are not like Prof Dhawan who spent considerable time in decision making and consulted many. This decision on foreign languages was almost an impulsive one. 
Another major event that happened was with Prof HNR. He became a UPSC member and resigned his job at IISc.  Prof Narayana Iyengar became the head . He depended on me on most of the things. 
I got involved with admissions of students for BE and ME. We introduced an entrance exam for BE admissions first in the EE department in 1970 and later it became a common exam for the three departments running the BE program. 
As I said earlier , the institute started to revamp the curriculum . I got involved in that fully. Mr R Vaidyanathan Asst Registrar handling academic matters became a good friend . We discussed many aspects of his section. He had a young  office supervisor by name Seshadri, a very smart person who ran the section very efficiently . Unfortunately ,he died suddenly, an untimely and unexpected and shocking death. So Vaidyanathan's role increased. We introduced for the first time a daily engagement diary for faculty in 1970. It was a simple one. It was improved over the years and became an important information and data companion for faculty. 
The telephone system was manual at that time ,not automatic. We had two incoming lines and connections to mostly hods,  officers, and offices, a very limited connection. If I wanted to make a call or receive a call, I had to go to the office. That was the state for most people.  As I mentioned before about untrained electricians, our telephone operator was not a trained person. He wasn't a disciplined person. One day , the registrar dismissed him for negligence. He brought his family and left them outside the registrar's chambers. So after the drama , he was reinstated. 
I also helped Vaidyanathan with admissions. We organised a huge program for a week showing MS Subbulakshmi's music program at the UN. We issued coupons to interested persons. It was screened in the library seminar hall. It was a grand activity and a very successful one. I would have heard MS several times each day. It was a memorable week. I also attended some carnatic music programs at chamrajpet with him. 
Mr Vaidyanathan left for Canada in 1970 . He was teaching a mathematics course for biochemists and I started teaching his mathematics for biochemistry students from August 1970 onwards . I continued teaching that course for three years. Prof Cama , the head of biochemistry also taught the course with some applications. 
More in the next.

31/07/25, 4:46 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 791
1969 was a year of travels for me. I visited Madras twice  to repair our electromechanical calculators.  I again visited Madras to see off Prof HNR to Delhi. I visited Ahmedabad , my first visit to Gujarat, for a CBIP conference in July 1969. That was the historic occasion of Neil Armstrong landing on the moon. Immediately afterwards, my IEEE paper was presented in September 1969. I visited Delhi for a CBIP conference in November 1969. I visited Bombay twice. I also visited IIT Bombay during my visit to TIFR.I visited IIT Kanpur in December 1969. This was my first visit to Kanpur. I went to Delhi and stayed with Prof HNR and went to kanpur the next day.  It was a cold time. I experienced very cold days for the first time. The food was good . I could relish the food. 
It was a great networking time for me. I met Prof Kelkar, IIT director. He was an old student of IISc- EE department.  I also met Mr FC Kohli there. I met Dr HW Dommel and Dr Dravid from Bonneville power administration who both conducted workshops before the conference.  We had a lot of lively discussions . Dr Sastry from the EE department of IIT Kanpur  asked Dr Dravid about his original place. Dr Dravid said it was Kanpur but Dr Sastry had doubts . I was discussing my work with Dr Dommel . He appreciated my work. We discussed the pros and cons of Nuclear energy. 
1969 was the first year that I attended several conferences. My exposure was very good. The CBIP conference gave me opportunities to network with chairmen and chief engineers of many electricity boards in the country. I became a good friend of Mr CVJ Verma ,secretary, CBIP. 

Let me look at my PhD program. After Prof HNR left for Delhi in 1969 , I had to decide on a new PhD supervisor. This was not easy. My real supervisor was Prof Devanathan . But I needed someone in the department.  I didn't want anyone from the power systems side as they were working on mostly older topics . I didn't have anyone in the computer area. So I needed a person who could support me. I chose Deekshatulu as my supervisor. He also left for the US on a sabbatical in 1970 . By then I submitted my thesis. I selected Prof Iyengar as my supervisor. He conducted my PhD viva. 
I had two learnings from these experiences. 
Change is happening most of the time and at unexpected times. 
Self learning and self directed work, though difficult, is essential in life. It helps one during eventualities. Since,  there were no power systems or computer specialists, I didn't lose my interest in my topic and shift to a new topic. I was firm and determined to struggle,  learn, understand  and apply my knowledge to solve the large problems. I struggled and succeeded in my research.  I don't want to be a part of a crowd or a bandwagon of researchers . Those two were common. I wanted to break new grounds, look at less understood and less researched areas and go at them. I have followed this research adventure throughout my life. That helped me to keep abreast of new frontiers. Even today ,I try to understand the research going on in AI. 
Another general aspect amongst researchers is to focus on one area and concentrate on it and go deeper in that area. I tried a mix and match approach ,a kind of applicative and interdisciplinary work. I moved into many areas- started with power systems, moved into computer operating systems and then to transactions processing and then to distributed databases, then to data mining and also moved into two new areas - large regional integrated energy systems, rural energy optimization, and environment. So I went through a large canvas and didn't narrow my research focus. 
Let us see why I chose this unusual ,uncommon path in the next post.

01/08/25, 6:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 792
Let me look at one question that used to bug researchers? 
There are two options 
1.To work in a reasonably known area with enough research going on in that area in the world with sufficient reference material or
2.Work in a new developing or upcoming area.
Most researchers chose option 1. I chose 2 with three areas interacting with each other and with mathematics.  The areas are
Power systems analysis and optimization
Computational methods and numerical analysis.  And
Computer based problem solving , programming and errors analysis. 
To know the situation at that time in the EE department,  let us see the broad picture of active researchers pursuing PhD.
About 3 faculty worked on power devices - relays and machines, but not on power systems. Dr.G Krishna who was senior to me by many years submitted his PhD thesis and got the degree ,but his work was on machines. Later he shifted to my area of power systems optimization and afterwards to pattern recognition.
About 5 faculty members worked on control systems . Deekshatulu got it going with his PhD on nonlinear systems. The control system  was just coming up as a developing area ,but there was a lot of work going on in that area for about a decade or more and about 40 people were working in IISc and IITs in India.  There were not many working in power systems as well as computers. Most worked on analytical approaches. No faculty was getting involved with numerical methods. Even in mathematics, there were very few working on numerical analysis at that time in India.  None worked on computational methods looking at errors and convergence for a large problem. 

What gave me a lot of confidence to plough a lonelier and unfamiliar path were the following facts based on my experiences in the past one year:
1. I learnt several low level programming languages in a few days and wrote large programs. 
2.I shifted to Fortran easily ,wrote large programs and solved large problems . It was not easy to get good data for large systems. 3.Data adjustments and corrections were big problems. I managed them. 
4.I developed several numerical techniques.
5.I got lots of results and published many papers. 
6.I could find fault with an IEEE paper. 
7.I wrote a short paper on matrices in IEEE transactions on control systems. 
8. One thing I quickly adopted was to teach what I learnt . I introduced in 1969 to 71, some new courses on computational methods, optimization techniques, computer applications to power systems , etc. That made me to go in depth and look for alternatives in these courses. This was very useful to keep me on my toes in learning the latest. The journal “ACM computing surveys “ was a useful tool for me to learn. 
8.I got a lot of exposure to computer systems in detail from Burroughs corporation books. 
9.I learnt about the just developing area of operating systems and included it in teaching my course. Data structures was taught by me for the first time  in India around the same  time Andrew Yg taught it . 
10. I upgraded my course to a much higher level and named it computing systems and devices.
11. I got a greater exposure to optimisation and taught the first course on optimization in 1970. I even devised a method for linear programming. OR was just then developing into a new discipline. I started working on simulation - both continuous systems and discrete systems . In 1970, I developed a new language called Cospro. 
All the above gave me the confidence to wade into new disciplines. My fear of unknowns went away and I enjoyed learning new concepts and principles. 
It opened my mind. I moved away from the general thinking and accepted new thinking. I didn't get scared of being a loner and a rebel. I got the courage to be different. The most important belief at that time was that numerical methods will not give better solutions than analytical solutions. Almost all others were working on analytical solutions . I was the only one to work on numerical methods guided by many IEEE papers. We had arguments. There were a large number of faculty at IISc who considered programming an inferior topic and software  not a discipline. They lived in their past world. It surprised me and pained me.
Despite all that being said,I believe that  it was providence that chose my area of research, not any rational thinking. It gave me courage .
More in the next.

02/08/25, 5:53 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 793
The main reason I wrote the last post was the struggle that most research students went through in the sixties and seventies and even the eighties for selecting an area and a topic. A few students spent years browsing the literature to choose a topic. 
Let me discuss the PhD research being done in the department by some of my contemporaries. 
I had been working on my topic , a predetermined one from my ME days,  from day minus one of my starting my research work. I registered with HNR.  I joined in August 1967 and I submitted a research proposal to UGC in August itself and became a UGC senior research fellow from September onwards till I became a faculty in April 1968. I finished most of my work by 1969 and submitted my thesis by April 2000. So due to my quick decision on my topic, progress of my research was quick and I submitted before three years time. 
 I had two colleagues of the same age , VVS Sarma and N Viswanadham. They were one year senior to me in ME. They  started their research one year before me. They picked their topics . They were guided by Dr. Deekshatulu. Their work was progressing satisfactorily.  Dr. Deekshatulu , fresh after getting his PhD ,was guiding several including these two. I was also close to him and normally met him almost everyday and discussed many things with him freely . It was a great lifelong friendship that goes on even now . So the research activities of ours were smooth without major turbulence.  We used to go for morning coffee together and also to the mess. I recall an  incident that both me and NV liked drumsticks and there was a competition between me and him on picking drumsticks from sambar in the A mess.
But we had a colleague in the department and that  colleague  also registered with Deekshatulu for his PhD. Everyday he used to talk about his PhD topic selection ordeal. His main problem was indecisiveness. Each day , he would talk about an area , saying that area is interesting. Then he would say after a few days that he didn't like that area and moved to another area. He openly expressed his dilemma to us when we went for coffee. This went on for a year and continued further until he was distracted by other things . He couldn't decide on his topic. The momentum was lost. He didn't do PhD at all. Later after some years , he left the institute and joined ITI. 
I also  saw two students  who joined as research students ,one in EE and another in ME , a few years later. At that time, not many joined for PhD. They couldn't select a topic even after two years. They were hard working, spent most of their time in the library reading papers , were regular in their habits and had a good and stable mind . But both couldn't choose a topic and left the institute without a degree after a few years. In some cases  this topic selection delayed their work by several years over the normally expected one year period. Hence the importance of this . 
One big problem in research is distraction .
Some get involved in games indoor or outdoor, some in fiction etc. Some start teaching or lab work. While that works in the US, it doesn't work here. A strong work culture of spending several hours working on a research problem is a must for doing decent research. 24/7 thinking is essential for success in research. Your supervisor will not think for you. Organization and planning are other requirements.  
Research aptitude is crucial. Some get a natural aptitude for research, but many need to consciously develop a good aptitude. That is something both supervisor and student should understand. Research motivation is another factor. Many are naturally inclined and some are passionate about research. But many need to be trained and schooled by their supervisors. Friends play a big role in this. Good research motivated friends will encourage a person during disappointments and discouragements which happen many times during one's research career.
Greetings
Dks
03/08/25, 4:09 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 794
Let me look at the physical expansion taking place in iisc in that period 1967 to 70 and its impact on the overall physical ambience . 
IISc had lots of open spaces and most buildings had a lot of in - between spaces . It looked like a small forest with many flowering  trees and birds.Most earlier buildings were made of dressed stone. The main building and adjoining department buildings - physics, IPC, biochemistry- provided a majestic ambience and the large open area in front and behind the main building gave a large appearance. It looked great. 
I used to see many buildings with lots of open spaces all around it in Malleswaram and I realised  that it is the standard planning of the old Mysore government . Later when I visited Mandya , I found that almost all office buildings, hospitals and educational institutions had an offset of more than 100 to 200 meters. You see the buildings constructed after a large open space with buildings and trees. Gardens with  flowering plants were to be found here and there and not in all such spaces. The importance was on trees - fruit trees and flowering trees and not on plants. The town planning and these spaced out constructions were planned and implemented by Sir M Visweswariah. 
But that scene was changing rapidly in Bangalore also.. Most big houses gave way for crowded apartments. This started in the late seventies and by the nineties ,Malleswaram had many apartments changing the face of a serene series of bungalows.  A similar situation started at the institute campus also. 
Our serene and open campus  was getting slowly eroded with new constructions being taken up in many places . This pace of construction increased with years and now has reached demonic proportions. It is converting that great institute into a slum. The greatest misfortune was that IISc didn't have a master plan earmarked areas for departments, hostels, residences, service units. Most decisions are localised and adhoc. Let me give two examples :
I wanted a separate place for the new 66kv / 11kv substation in 1986. The existing 11kv incoming supply from KEB to IISc was located north of the then aerospace department.  But that was not suitable for the new substation. I wanted a place in the south . The space opposite to CEDT was given to us.  CEDT protested saying they don't have expansion space. The reason is the absence of a masterplan that reserved space for expansion of departments. 
The second was the request by CES to the space near our new 66kv  substation. Fortunately, I heard about it and immediately told the director that I need the space for expansion of the substation. This happened in 1999 and IISc agreed to my proposal for an integrated and centralised DG unit connected to the grid . So we used that space for the DG centre in 2001. See the myopic views of people. 
I can talk more about the lapses. I was made chair of the campus development ,but I saw the breakdown of our suggestions even before the ink was dry.  Violation of rules was and is common in IISc.  
The non availability of a masterplan made adhoc decision making and that led to chaotic developments in the campus. The developments were such as to beat the famous Thuglakian approaches.
Greetings
Dks
04/08/25, 5:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 795

Let us look at the destruction of IISc's great rustic look. Some constructions started in 1968 onwards. We got a new estate officer. The power engineering building got two extensions at each end - one for EE and the other for Mechanical engineering.  Civil and hydraulics  also built a few extensions to the hydraulics lab . The HoD and office ,which were in the PE building, were  shifted to the civil engineering building. As I mentioned before, two new hostels and a new one for married students were ready by 1969. The new gymkhana building with grounds on the opposite side to the main campus and adjacent to the porcelain factory was also completed by then. 
A major development was the construction in the open space at the main entrance between the main building and the outside road going towards Mekri circle. Prof Choksi, who was the chairman of the council at that time , opposed any construction in that area. He was convinced that there would be  no construction in the large central space and two buildings were approved at both ends - one with three lecture halls , Mathematics department and Choksi hall and another with 16 classrooms. Both buildings were ready by 1970. This was the first attack on open spaces and buildings coming up in the so-called between spaces. Another example is organic chemistry building constructed in the space ,sandwiched between biochemistry and IPC. The great open space ambience between buildings was slowly getting reduced. Newer roads were not broad enough and didn't have good drainage . I felt one big vacuum in the institute was  in not having a lake inside the campus. There was no water body in the campus. 
While the institute had a lot of open spaces and a lot of greenery , the botanist father of Mrs Dhawan was consulted for planting more trees. He in 1967 identified many areas. Institute maintained its pristine and natural ambience till mid seventies. Even during the construction, Prof Dhawan was particular that no trees should be cut. So land was carefully identified for construction. But all that changed now.
There is no unified architecture. Walking in footpaths with bushy plants closeby,  encountering snakes on the path or corridors of the building,hearing the  squeaking bird voices,  cool summers,  calm atmosphere, lights on in many buildings, particularly library,  even at midnight ,quiet hostels - these were the scenes of those years at the  IISc campus. Scenes in the city were also similar except for snakes. Walking inside the campus or outside was a great pleasure. One could sit and allow the serene and calm atmosphere envelope him or her. All that is a dream now. No fans were needed then even in the director's office. Bangalore was a “ no fan “ city then. I bought a fan ,that too a table fan, only in 1974. 
 It is painful for me, but inevitable for many. That is the world - ever changing and surprising.
Change means - destroy the old , bring in the new. Sometimes what we destroy is beauty and memory. 
Greetings
Dks
05/08/25, 4:01 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 796

There is a comment from Prof karunakara:
“Sir,
Very surprised to know that no master plan for developments even though there are many scholars at Institute and  no thoughts for future expansion.
Thanks and regards.”

My response
When I talked about masterplan, it was only with respect to land use. Normally, we reserve some parts of land for housing, some for commercial, some open space , some businesses ,some industries etc in each city. This is what was known as town planning, but these plans were not followed. Sir MV didn't anticipate industries in Bangalore and so our roads were narrow compared to Mysore . The growth was not anticipated. 
When I said the master plan for IISc, I was talking about the land use plan for expansion, not about academic directions.  There is a reason for this. IISc didn't expand very much till the sixties. It started expanding at a higher rate from 1970 onwards adding several centres or units every year. Till 1990 many buildings came up in the campus and there were lots of discussions. Because of the uncertain situations, we didn't have space for expansions.  That led to lots of discussions.  This foresight for expansion was missing for land use. 
I should clarify that this type of construction led to haphazard growth of buildings . At no point in time,  IISc considered vertical growth. 
But it should be clarified that the growth of IISc was not hindered by land use. IISc grew academically at the highest rate in India. It was done by adding more buildings and stressing the land use.  That was the best feasible method economically and financially. Only by 2005, we got more funds to demolish older hostel blocks and construct newer hostels with more floors. Similarly we started shifting biology departments to the backside near D gate . Later even Aerospace was shifted out. So IISc growth went on unimpeded without hiccups.  We found alternate solutions. 
My comment was on the haphazard construction of additions during 1985 to 2000. At no point did I hint that IISc had no road map for academics. IISc constantly endeavoured to be on the forefront of the latest emerging areas. The faculty came up with new areas , got funding and put up buildings , bought equipment and faculty and ran programs. To give one instance, during the three years tenure of Prof Ramaseshan ten new centres were established and a new supercomputer education centre was set up. So we were moving forward in the academics and research front with no hindrances but with lots of support and encouragement. 
All I said was that we didn't have a land use plan which would have minimized some amount of adhocism.  That is all. Sorry that it gave a wrong view. Thanks, Karunakara for the opportunity to clarify that aspect and bring in a correct perspective to the tremendous growth of IISc. 
Greetings 
Dks
06/08/25, 5:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 797
There are three more comments. 
Mr Murali :
“Yes water body is a must for big campus”
My response 
IISc promised to let rain water go to Sankey tank in the beginning years of IISc.   So it couldn't build a lake on campus.  That is the reason for not having a water body in the campus. But our water consumption is very high. We should have treated used water. We didn't do that. 
Prof Vasudev Parvati 
“Sir, any comments on the Centre for Brain Research and other Medical related facilities coming up at IISc? Wouldn't it be better if such research were left to AIIMS and more focus be given at the Institute for advanced research in technical areas?”
My response 
Brain research is an important area which needs a multidisciplinary approach to solve many problems. There are many departments in IISc dealing with several cellular, biological, nano, molecular , modeling, computing  and AI areas and this has led to many good publications . So the centre for brain research is a good area and IISc is in a position to provide holistic solutions ,not a medical or psychological solution. We can move away from a disease oriented approach towards an understanding and discovery approach. So it is a welcome development. 
As regards medical education , it is a disastrous decision to start undergraduate courses in medicine.  It will divert IISc from research to operational aspects and dilute attention to many departments.  We may end up with financial problems. 

Prof. Karunakara
“Thanks. I was thinking how such a prestigious institution of our country grown with no clear plans. Thanks for details.  
But still a question is haunting to me is,  what we are lacking to  meet to the level of  Noble laureates. 
Regards.”
My response
The reasons why we didn't get nobel prizes are many like
Equipment not available. Raman gave the highest salary to his glass blower. He could do his experiments with simple devices . The situation is different now. 
Normally Nobel comes for indepth research. That is missing . Many got FRS . Some worked with Nobel winners. 
Nobel needs extensive work at a fundamental level. Many times it is done by groups. Fundamental research is still weak. Most work at IISc is applicative. 
Peer support with other Nobel winners is poor. 
Awareness of our work, continuity of work are poor. Normally Nobel is awarded only after some applications are developed by others . So we are missing this continuity. 
Nobel is relevant to only two disciplines in IISc- physics and chemistry.  Even the Nobel to AI researcher this year is given for physics. 
Work in India doesn't get known and applied in other countries. 
To conclude, a lot of research activities  going on at IISc are of high quality -definitely  at Nobel level as can be seen from publications in major journals of repute and many international honours and awards.

07/08/25, 4:45 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 798

I am glad about  your interest in IISc and the quality of research . Thank you. 
 Let me look at my next topic -  the freedom of work in general and at IISc in particular  and its erosion and the rise of the power of admin over academics, a very dangerous trend from which  the country is suffering even now. 
First I propose the necessary conditions for a good , non toxic , sustainable  and successful working atmosphere which  are:
1.openness and transparency- well defined goals, purposes, rules,and procedures and processes. Regular bulletins , discussions. Dynamic changes. Awareness of different aspects of the organization 
2.freedom from fear . Happy and non stressed atmosphere.  Well defined responsibility and ownership. 
3.good and calm ambience conducive for working by people. Good connectivity and access. Fairness and just decisions.
4.empathy and people centric 
5.primary focus or goals established clearly by management- this is crucial for academics to be in the forefront. Admin should work for this purpose and not for misinterpreted rules. Rules can be modified . Rules are not gods. Many organizations build rules and don't change them . Rules shouldn't  be rigid. 
6. Participatory work culture and decision making. Primacy of ethics.
7. Very low management control. 
Decentralization and empowerment of small teams. The key aspects are - planning, strategies, regular  feedbacks, advisory committees,  reviews by external experts , changes and implementations, etc. All aspects are crucial today. 
9. High degree of satisfaction for all - faculty, students , employees . 
10. Egalitarian, helping and supporting ethos.
11. This is the most important aspect for leadership - futuristic anticipation, thinking and vision . Remember leadership in academic institutions is diffused . It includes several people - management council, director or VC or principal , HoDs, deans, senate, advisory committees , external reviewers etc. 

I find many universities in the US and England follow many of  these conditions for governance ,but not in India. Private institutions have adhoc ,fearful dictatorships in most cases. Public institutions belonging to the state are politically controlled. I find autonomous and aided institutions slightly better. 
What needs to be done to meet most of the above 11 conditions ? Let me identify some basic requirements:
Define purpose , goals, vision, mission etc which are achievable,  relevant and meaningful. Have a futuristic thinking. 
Define the overall intent of the management. Develop a conducive intent. Mindset management is tough.
Prepare policies for governance,  ethics, operations, 
Define processes, feedbacks, reviews etc. Allow flexibility, changes .Allow reviews by external experts. Involvement of external experts in many activities like PhD evaluation, faculty selection ,lab development,  lectures and conferences has many advantages and provides fairness and transparency .
Capability and capacity building methods
Documentation to a good level. Open access to people. 
Positive attitude with all. 
Decentralise leadership , control, and empowerment.  

Greetings
Dks
08/08/25, 5:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 799
“As to methods, there may be a million and then some, but principles are few. The man who grasps principles can successfully select his own methods. The man who tries methods, ignoring principles, is sure to have trouble.”
RALPH WALDO EMERSON, ESSAYIST AND POET

Let me ask - what is the situation at IISc?
IISc operates on a few major unwritten principles . Let me list some.
1.in quest of excellence- is our goal
2.primacy to academics and research. Admin to support academics 
3. Forward and futuristic looking. 
4. Be in the forefront of areas and be prepared to move forward. 
5. Socially helpful research and development
6. Assist the  institutions in the country.
It scores high on all the above - a very high value - except for admin delays. IISc is  not only built on the eleven conditions mentioned in the previous post , but also followed them and changed them ,for over  a century  - a long history . Importantly, IISc never thought and thinks that rules are  permanent, rigid and interpreted in one way. It had several mechanisms to build rules and change them as well as give new interpretations. It keeps on building new mechanisms . It is dynamic and agile , giving serious thought and discussions on every change. It transformed at regular intervals. It never hesitated in experimentation One great feature of IISc is:
“Every person can initiate a change in its goals, purpose, processes and rules. Everyone thinks about changes and improvements.”  It is a 21st century people centric agile entity, a rarity in India, even in the 20th century.   
Admin was subordinate to academics. Academics controlled the rules and processes. All rules are framed by committees of academics and approved  by the higher level bodies like senate and council.
Let me talk about our students' admission process . It saw several changes in evaluation . We talked about inter university variability. We decided in the late sixties itself that university marks are not good indicators of merit. So we decided that the entrance exam is one reliable  route for uniform assessment and ranking. We were clear that interviews is a necessary method to assess the thinking capabilities of students. So we retained the interview system for admissions to research. Our learning mechanism was continuous and participative with all faculty involved in the discussions during the two joint faculty meetings held every year to decide on admissions processes and later to ratify the admissions. We used to have discussions on the learnings - problems faced and solutions found. It was a great experience . 

 We have dynamic thinking and agile structures. Prof Dhawan introduced participative decision making and operations through the concept of committees with academic members. These committees can change the rules, provide paths and directions. They controlled the  admin. It was a great system and many IITs followed this system.
We operated through several committees . Some are: 
Promotions and assessments committee
Buildings and works committee 
Senate committees - research, curriculum .
campus amenities committee - i was a member and chair of this committee for many years.
Time table committee - I was a coordinator for three years. 
Central evaluation committee with many subcommittees - I chaired this committee til my retirement. 
Grievance committee- i was it's member and later chair for two years
Extension lecture committee - I was a member and coordinator of this committee. 
Executive committees - this was introduced by Prof Ramaseshan for streamlining two major support centres- library and computer centre. I did a swot analysis for the computer centre and later was a leading member of the executive committee.  
Greetings 
Dks
09/08/25, 4:47 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 800

IISc's growth was continuing all these years.
It was the best in the sixties ,seventies and eighties . The era of Prof Dhawan,  was a golden era for IISc,  its growth and prominence and relevance to the country. He built an active, open ,participative, future proofed  and thinking campus , a great place to work. After him, Prof Ramaseshan became the director . He built the institute with scientific vision and took it to greater heights. But he was weak in admin. He took Prof Ramakrishna as his admin in charge. He was totally ineffective. His control of admin was poor and non-existent. So the admin grew and grabbed the power. These admin  changes came after the  eighties.  
Today what we  have  is an  auditors governance , L1 purchases and very strict and time consuming financial pre approvals .  This disease afflicted us at IISc in the mid seventies. This is killing research, innovation , and progress in India. We need to make the admin subordinate to a set of goals or vision or main purpose of an institution and not make them demigods. But the opposite is happening.  I am writing this with deep anguish. 
Let me look at the situation at IISc now. 
In addition to the natural,  pristine and beautiful campus, ours had a complete academic atmosphere at that time- 1965 to 70 -  with most admin powers  rested with  the departments . Admin offices were a few, dealing with admissions, scholarships, salary, bills and council section - that is it  . So the admin was subordinate to academics, an ideal situation. Freedom to work and get chemicals and equipments were excellent. There were no delays.  But this ideal situation didn't continue . Actually it started feeling turbulence frequently.
 During the tenure of Bhagawantam as director, AG Pai as registrar wielded the power ,director becoming powerless , and there were no additions to faculty, no new departments, no promotions , just a total stagnation . But AG Pai was efficient and didn't increase the admin influence away from the HoDs. That meant that HoDs power was still high and they controlled the department. But the institute's  growth suffered at a time when the institute was celebrating its golden jubilee. 

Prof Dhawan was doing his best to rebuild the institute through a series of reforms. 
But a tragedy - a hindrance to academics and a domination of admin over everything - started with Prabhu during the mid sixties and seventies. He tightened his grip and made the admin supreme. 
Greetings
