10/08/25, 4:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 801
What I am writing is my views of the dynamic changes that happened in IISc for about 60 years. It may not be a full or complete view but facts are correct - no fudging there. Let me look at the fast growth of admin at IISc.  But a word of caution. IISc has a lot of resilience. It absorbs changes  , adapts to a different state and excels in academics. Its principles are intact - no changes there .

Prabhu , the registrar in the sixties and the early seventies   wanted more control . He started his “grabbing the power" campaign in 1968 -69 with a conference of registrars of most centrally governed institutions including IITs.  It was the first major think tank on academic admin. But its aftereffects were not desirable. Power moved away from academics to admin. This was needed to some extent. But the balancing between tasks and delegation and decision making was not easy. 
He planned deviously to increase his power and admins stronghold on the working of the institute. He created a concept of dividing the administration into several units. From three sections, he converted admin to 11 units and kept on expanding admin. He built in centralised approvals for all expenditure. Purchases and stores were centralised. More admin officers were appointed and the admin became power hungry and top heavy . They had to create work and so started obstructing scientific work.

The effect was disastrous. The registrars, who succeeded Prabhu,  and their officers lived by rules. They never understood the following :
What should be their role? 
Why does the institute exist? 
What should be the main purpose of the institute and the directions of the institute?
They don't understand that institute directions are not top down ,but come from every direction and from outside. That is very difficult for admin , who are accustomed to a central command structure , to understand. But directors and professors  commanded the institute irrespective of admins role. But that is the essence of IISc.  
What is the importance of timely decisions and what are the effects of time delays on research ? 
How do you move from personalised admin to a general purpose and highly efficient and effective admin? 
How does digital technology help them in speeding up their work? 

The one effect of admin control is that  everything was delayed. Papers sent for approvals were misplaced. Time limits were not fixed for clearances. Admin resisted computerization for long. Only payroll was computerised but not properly. Departments found ways to tackle this delay and disappearance diseases. 
Every department had assigned a person to chase their  papers as a few papers sent for approval to the central office disappeared. Later we introduced an organized campus mail system.Similarly , every lab had assigned  a lab assistant to chase the progress of purchases.  I don't think any directors tried to reverse this trend. Prof CNR Rao helped many in a selective manner by overcoming  the decisions of the admin through discretionary powers . 
 Directors are getting entangled in rules and haven't come out of the rules. The only - but major and very important- thing is that our academics - curriculum, teaching , research ,labs development, degrees, new area development , faculty selection and promotion etc - are still with the faculty and handled by committees and the senate. The admin plays a subordinate role and listens to the deans. But not so with general admin - purchases, bills, finance, approvals etc. 
Greetings 
Dks
11/08/25, 4:34 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 802

True the admin increased its role slowly and steadily from the seventies in IISc.  That looks like a general trend in the country due to the 
Increased legal , fiscal and audit regulations of the government.  Those governmental oversight , audit interference and legal attacks are destroying the country. Egos and arrogance coupled with ignorance are a deadly cocktail of destruction . They don't allow the country to grow, to flourish and to be really free . I don't know when we will experience total freedom in India . Hundreds of regulatory, law,taxes  police and audit agencies attack us - both individuals and institutions. The governments think that all of us are thiefs.
Why am i writing about iisc? To show a dynamic picture of growth , impediments and their resolutions , improvements and reforms as seen by me. 
But some get the impression that IISc is a difficult place. Please remove that idea. IISc is still the best place in India or anywhere to live and work providing the maximum individual and academic freedom with a great satisfying life. There are a few hiccups that are minor aberrations. Our reforms progressed over years and decades. One aspect was that the power of HoDs was reduced . There is a stronger level of democracy in the departments. That credit goes to Prof Dhawan in creating a few new structures . Let me list them. 
He built a new concept called divisions. He combined several departments into one unit and called it a division. He created four divisions and  named them as physical sciences, chemical sciences, mechanical sciences and electrical sciences. He appointed senior professors to head each division. Their main task was organizing  faculty promotions.  He also created a committee of professors in each department entrusted with policy decisions and faculty promotions. 
He built a centralised senate curriculum committee to decide on structures of curriculum of departments. He set up a time table committee to devise the institute-wide timetable for all departments. Both committees had representations to all departments. He also made the departments set up departmental committees to operationalise the new credit system in 1970. Then we also had a grading advisory committee which functioned for about ten years.
So he ensured the isolation of academics from admin and avoided the necessity of ad hoc decision making . He inculcated and encouraged participatory systems. So academic freedom was ensured . But the admin created many hurdles in our day to day functioning. 
   Rajaji talked of licence permit Raj. But most government and semi government organizations suffered from another disease - audit torture.   I want to look at one issue of audit which created a major headache for me. 
This happened in the 1983 -84 period. Prof Ramaseshan was the director then. He was working on a plan for a supercomputer centre. He said he was dreaming about it. He evinced a lot of interest in improving the computing infrastructure in the institute and I was involved in his reforms from the beginning in various activities . We had several rounds of discussions with many committees and senior government officials from the US .
I decided to seek  additional power requirements from KEB as it takes time to get the additional power. That was objected to by CAG afterwards . We had to spend days convincing him about the need. He threatened us . Finally it got resolved.

My response
Thanks Vasudev. It is an important question occurring to many. Many feel that we can't reach IISc level and forget about it. Many told me this. There are many who feel IISc and IITs are doing great because they get large financial grants from the government.  There are some others who feel and say that IISc is doing well because it gets the best students. All these are myopic views . Most are like the blind men defining an elephant by feeling a part of it. I call the disease prevailing with most as the excuse disease - everything is fine with me. Others are preventing my institution from achieving the higher status of a good one,  forget the urge to becoming a great or excellent institution. This disease is visible in your query itself . 
Let me explain in two parts , the first part about IISc. I am glad you gave me an opportunity to clarify my comments. Have no doubt about the fact that IISc is doing well. 

All institutions are not static . They meet many challenges. There are regulatory changes , new compliances, market realities , import restrictions, uncertainties etc. Most indian institutions can't admit a large number of foreign students . So the diversity is limited. Diversity helps in broad and open thinking. Diversity is in two dimensions - wrt to students and second the exposure of faculty to many different institutional cultures. I will explain more on this later.
Success of an institution depends on meeting these  challenges , and not running away. That is the problem with your normal colleges and the big difference with IISc.  
So I did not write about the difficulties of IISc.  Also I didn't  say that these difficulties threatened our progress. i actually gave instances of our attacking these problems and solving them and marching on without diluting our firm academic and research commitments. We were and are very successful for maintaining our ethos for more than a century - not an easy task. We met challenges headon and found solutions and implemented them. We were constantly on the look out for improvements.  
The cocktail for success used by IISc contains the following factors and characteristics: 
Agility
Resilience
High visionary and multilevel leaderships 
Participation , commitment and ownership.- no excuses.
Openness
Free thinking and free working without fear or hindrance
Futuristic outlook 
No class system. 
So IISc is moving forward and never felt any issue as unsolvable. I was discussing the way we evolved over periods of uncertainty , changes , modernism and kept pace. I wasn't writing to criticise IISc.  
I discussed our ways of building new structures, and dynamic changes in rules. Participative opportunities through the large number of committees that governed IISc were provided to a large number of faculty members both senior and junior. That built a vast pool of thinkers and leaders and motivated most faculty. The system was open, fair and transparent. External experts' involvement is good. Many of these are missing in our colleges.
Let me look at your main question and concern in the next post.

13/08/25, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 804
Your concern :
Your query is oriented towards excuses. Even IISc faculty get external funding for research. But I will ask a different question.  
Why are many  other institutions not growing?
What are the reasons ?
Let me try to answer these based on my exposure - interactions with 1000s of faculty members in hundreds of engineering colleges through many programs like 
The SPP program of KSCST
The approval program of AICTE 
The accreditation by NBA
The audit and mentor program of TEQIP
Selection of faculty by many colleges
Several Short term courses
and 
My involvement with conducting courses and lectures. I will use these extensive experiences spanning four decades to answer the query. 
The primary reason for stagnation by engineering colleges is 
The abdication by faculty to own the discipline. This is the most unfortunate thing. Most faculty define their job as just teaching some course and  do their job not with their passion but as a duty and ignore participation in the growth of themselves and the institution.
 The faculty are the primary growth engine for an institution . They failed in most cases. Good and motivated faculty were subjugated by sycophants.  
Many faculty live in fear, feeling of unfairness and finding excuses for not doing anything IISc is doing. For instance, we did a lot for schools by different processes. Many are voluntary activities . For instance, about 10 of us initially started the extension lecture program for school students . We conducted about 200 lectures per year with 30 faculty participating in it. It was run for more than thirty years as a voluntary work without any remuneration and travelling by bus to most places. That was dedication to help education. I didn't see similar experiments by any college . Prof Vankudre tried some community radio programs  and Prof Shinde conducted training for jail inmates as a TEQIP activity. But most institutions didn't do much on this front. 
Wherever faculty in sizable numbers showed interest, motivation and the urge to grow , the institutions have done well. Determination by every  faculty member to grow academically as an individual and consequently for the institution is essential for success. Some faculty are genuine, have the determination and the institutions have moved from a satisfactory level to a good level. But none moved to a great level. That is a tragedy. 
But there is an opposite positive indication. The entire computer science program was handled by faculty getting into learning and teaching CS courses and later IT courses and now AI courses. It is commendable . The entire IT revolution happened because of these efforts. I should record my appreciation for this effort by faculty in engineering colleges. 
But faculty didn't get involved with admin or academics or future growth with vigor. 
More in the next post.

There are two comments. 
“Looking forward to the next post.
The mindset of the owners of many private colleges are very different except about 10. (SSN College of engineering, Mepco and so on.) My exposure is only to engineering colleges of Tamilnadu.
Infrastructure, Quality of staff and students ,placement numbers and accreditation place an important role  in many private institutions. Tuition fees are high in private engineering colleges.

I have yet to come across accreditation panel with one or two professors from private engineering colleges.”
The second one
Professor,
Truly said, and I would quote the following excerpt:

_" ... The faculty are the primary growth engine for an institution . They failed in most cases. Good and motivated faculty were subjugated by sycophants. Many faculty live in fear, feeling of unfairness and finding excuses for not doing anything ..."_

“This is a growing problem in many second-tier and third-tier private engineering colleges. Teachers have to deal with a heavy workload along with many compliance requirements. This leaves them with very little time or energy to try new ideas. As a result, teaching often stays limited to lecture notes that have been used for years, passed from one college to another, and from one batch to the next, without much change. Many batches of students simply use the previous year’s notes, and in the end, they learn very little beyond what is needed to pass exams.

The problem becomes worse with the way question papers are set. In many cases, if a student memorises answers from previous years’ question papers, they can score high marks without really understanding the subject. On the other hand, a sincere student who studies the course material but does not look at past questions may get fewer marks, not because they lack knowledge, but because the system rewards guessing the pattern instead of truly learning. Most universities repeat their previous year questions - I don't have concrete proof of this for more than 2 universities, but I have heard of the same.

From my own experience as a student in different schools and colleges, I believe that an institution grows only when each batch of students is taught differently from the last, whether in theory or practical classes. While theory may have less scope for creativity, practical courses (and the practical parts of theory courses) should be taught in a more flexible and adaptive way. This is where real learning happens, and where we can break the cycle of memorisation and repetition.

If we want real improvement, colleges must review their teaching and exam methods. Faculty should be encouraged and given time to update their content, experiment with practical work, and set question papers that truly test understanding. Small, consistent changes can make a big difference in how students learn, and in how institutions grow.”
My response 
These two comments tell us the need for  one important change - change the mindsets from negative, excuses oriented to a  positive mindset - we should be prepared to meet the challenges,and  find a consensus solution. We shouldn't use these problems as excuses for not doing our best. 
Remember that no system is perfect,  every system has defects. 
First do an ABC analysis and  understand the factors that influence the success of an institution in a big way. Students evaluation has low priority. Management support is essential ,but not the primary factor. Faculty can't absolve itself of its role in building an institution. 
I already said that success depends on how an institution meets the challenges. Faculty workload is there in successful institutions also. It is not unique to only unsuccessful private institutions. I have heard that argument many times. There is no excuse to a faculty in not upgrading his knowledge regularly. He or she can't stagnate. The primary role of a faculty member is to upgrade his or her knowledge by getting interested to know what is happening in his field in  other universities, how others teach courses, what are the new developments in his area , what new areas areas are developing , and reading books and papers , attending courses, improving the courses taught by him or her, doing research, discussing with other faculties and students and knowing the societal problems in his city or state etc. Is this happening? Why don't many  faculty members upgrade their knowledge ? Instead of talking about exams , please address your improvement. Be positive. Find out ways of changing management intentions. 
More in the next.

15/08/25, 5:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 806

How do we solve the first hurdle of faculty rejuvenation in engineering colleges?
Instead of talking about difficulties, let us look constructive and look for solutions. 
Instead of finding excuses , colleges should face this problem squarely and find a new solution. The 21st century saw the industries reinventing first with digital applications and infusions in the first two decades and now with AI infusions. Otherwise industries will die. It was a problem of survival. 
Similarly it is a problem of survival for colleges to rejuvenate and reinvent . Cosmetic solutions like internships are not enough. Wake up and restrategise education. Faculty energization is the first priority to improve colleges. Remember this is a tough task ,an almost impossible task ,some may say. 
Build several new and imaginative  structures to motivate the faculty, to encourage them, to create networking , to build a modern  dynamic curriculum and to conduct socially applicative research . Involve external experts. Bring them out of their built cocoons. Break these cocoons. I will first talk about motivation and then about the cocoons we humans  build. 
Motivate a core group of faculty,  prepare a plan of action, prepare a complete set of dos and don'ts for the faculty , build annual reviews and self assessments , and reinvent courses. 
Start with breaking some beliefs and building confidence.  
Ask - is IISc doing better than the colleges because of funds? 
The answer is no. 
See the commitment, passion, enthusiasm  and energy of the faculty at IISc. Why don't the faculty in colleges come half way to that level ?  Also remember that IISc faculty get equal or less salary than the faculty of a college. So come out of the Maya- unrealistic delusions to the reality of the faculty motivation. It is urgent for the survival of the faculty themselves. Business as usual is dead. 
Faculty needs to be 
Motivated to do their job with sincerity - ban text book reading in the class room as a lecture, ban copying from text books in their ppts, conduct evaluation properly , answer questions etc. Forget the current mode of lecturing by a faculty. 
Reinvent teaching a course with not lectures but with many more components like 
Reading , abstractions   critiquing, discussions,  , case studies, problem solving, applications, project work, question answer sessions, talks by students, expert talks , industry talks etc . Make It mandatory.
To assist the faculty   the colleges should prepare a format for each course containing the following - 
references.,
Goal of the course, expectations, complete evaluation scheme ,
a lecture schedule with complete details  and topics 
Problem sheets 
List of projects , experiments, term papers etc.
Google group or WhatsApp group for interactions
Discussion topics
Suggested books to read 
Futuristic trends .

Second , prepare a five year  plan of expectations  for each faculty - 
It should have the following components :
What are the courses that a  faculty member will teach for the next five years - at least two courses in a new area ?
What are the shortcomings and the consequent improvements in the course he or she  is teaching ?
What are the new courses he she wants to attend every year? 
What are the readings a faculty should be doing in the five years - some goals like three books per year?
What are the research topics he or she wants to work on ? Goals on publications. 
What is the new area he or she wants to take up and what are the preparations required? Goal is to take up  at least one emerging area in 5 years. 
Create opportunities to expose faculty to the current happenings . Conduct  online talks  by  experts in a new area atleast twice a week. Why do seminars , a common occurrence at IISc,  don't happen in the colleges ? Start discussion societies to discuss one technical problem each week. AI applications is a good starting point. AI impacts is another. AI directions is a third. 
Get involved with society. There are hundreds of problems in our  society - our cities, villages, schools, colleges, people, our utilities, resources, wastes, apartment management etc. Why not involve faculty to solve local, state, country level problems. Start with a social engineering program. Initiate innovative projects. 
Transformations engineering is another area.
Build startups. Faculty should be in the forefront, not leave it to the students. 

Who will do this rejuvenation ?
It should be a combined effort by a group of motivated young faculty from several colleges to form a Forum , device ways of motivation and talk ,guide and advice faculty members. College managements also deserve to be addressed by this forum. FAER can help in this. 
Greetings

16/08/25, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 807

The next reason is 
Excuses, low confidence and diffidence amongst faculty. Your query itself reveals excuses. Why do you blame management .Why not solve it. AICTE and TEQIP helped the institutions . But the faculty didn't take it forward. This belief in excuses should be attacked. Faculty lack pride in their jobs or have limited range and vision. Thinking negatively prohibited them from thinking positively. 
Faculty confidence needs to be built up. Faculty should break their borders and go beyond just teaching and get involved in their institution. 
Faculty didn't educate the management. Faculty were timid to discuss seriously with the management and oppose their views. I have seen many managements helping in the growth and providing funds once convinced. Why did most faculty shy away from this important and essential task. 
If managements are not well intentioned, it is the job of faculty to educate them through various ways. I have helped in giving shock treatment to managements. My involvement with the managements  of hundreds of colleges is very positive. There were a few money minded persons - it is natural they have to run the institution ,they can't put in more personal money. No faculty member generates revenue for the institution in my experience. More than 40% of our revenue at IISc is generated by our faculty through sponsored projects and consultancies. 

The third reason is 
Lack of leadership - driven by fear, most leaders have become followers. This is a big malady. Most principals spent time doing routine jobs and firefighting. They didn't plan for the future. They didn't know how to reach their goals. 
I sat in the room of the  principals of three or four colleges.  . Students were coming in one after another for some signatures mostly for trivial reasons. 
In one case, the principal was talking for 10 minutes with an employee who wanted a day's leave. Most principals love their power over the staff , faculty and students. Many don't delegate routine tasks to others. Most don't have strategic plans for the growth of their institution. Futuristic thinking is absent in most colleges. There are no structures for various functions in some colleges. Coteries consisting of a few people run some institutions. 
This lack of leadership is a big problem. 
I have seen a few dynamic forward thinking principals and a few courageous principals. I call MSRIT as having two stages - before Prof Prahlada Rao and after him. We saw good improvements there. 
Similarly Prof Holla changed RVCE into a good institution.  Prof AbhaiKumar  at TCE brought in many reforms . Prof KAK did improve SSIT. Prof MN Chennabasappa changed SIT into a good institution. Prof Ashok Shettar transformed BVB Hubli. Prof Viswanath set up standards in Gogte institute of technology. Prof NR Shetty built NMIT into a good institution. These are a few examples. But most institutions don't adapt and use their status of autonomy. Independent , free acting , involving faculty in decision making, openness , and forward thinking are traits to be built as cultures. Even many  NITs are in the state of an engineering college. They don't aspire to reach the level of an IIT. 
Where is the urge to improve, urge to transform , urge to move forward , urge to be in the front in a few areas? Until we have these cultures and urges , the colleges won't move forward and reach higher levels. Even the urge to get higher NIRF ranking is dying or reducing in many institutions. 
There are several institutions which broke away from the crowd and are doing well. Innovation and agility helped them. Some like UDCT, Galgotia, VIT, Nursee Monjee , Symbiosis, Amrita , etc are moving upwards. Jadavpur ,and  BE college were always serious about academics and research. That is how Roorkee moved up to become an IIT.

17/08/25, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 808

So far we discussed the question of how to upgrade a college to a higher level. 
Now let me see the reasons  why most colleges are at the low end of academics. There are some major problems here, not addressed by regulators. 
Let me look at some of them. 
Management awareness about education, their priorities  and their  intentions 
Academic culture -total  lack of it. Only those managements that had respect for academics prospered . 
Fear pervading amongst faculty.
Staff spying on faculty. 
Financial struggles.
No governance structures - run like a proprietary concern. This is again a serious problem. Managements operate on total adhocism and favoritism like a proprietary concern. I saw one chairman of a big college  asking his principal to shut up. I stood up immediately and said I am leaving and can't tolerate insults to faculty.  I have seen the bad treatment given by the managements to the retired IIT faculty employed by them. I saw the humiliation of many retired persons employed by private colleges. I know a person, who retired from ISRO and joined a college, was asked to canvass for the chairman who was contesting assembly elections. He quit the job.  Hence I decided that I won't join a college post retirement even though I got great offers from some big and well known managements.  I stayed away from employment by private colleges. 
Uniformity of treatment of faculty 
Faculty -  management discord or distrust. Both exist in their distinct domains .
Discriminatory treatment by regulatory agencies. 
It is strange that the regulatory agencies treat a private college- including its faculty - poorly, but when that management gets a university status , treatment changes. I can't understand this situation . Are agencies blind? 
I listed the nine problems above so that we understand what should be done to improve an institution as a first step. I haven't discussed the academic climb . Academic climb is focused on the motivation of the faculty, their urge to move forward and the development of a modern, relevant, balanced  and thinking curriculum . 
Many managements of engineering colleges are good and want to improve the education system. They need guidance. Principals should have vision, should be dedicated and practical and guide and use their faculty. They should educate the management without fear, delegate routine work to faculty, take collective decisions and build good teams with leadership skills. IISc excels because of commitment , leadership and collective , participative and open governance.  
There are positives which helped some institutions to grow up to higher levels. For many colleges, finance became easy after CS and IT became popular and admission requirements eased by AICTE and job opportunities increased . So it became necessary for colleges to have strong campus placements. So that resulted in some improvements in engineering education. Many managements learnt the art of academics . So the situation was better. Poorer institutions wound up. Financial markets played a role . 
That is the state today. We still have miles to go. 
Greetings

18/08/25, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 809

Prof Karunakara has another comment:
“Good morning sir,
We have read that Indians were good in many fields of science during the vedic period, any attempt has been made at Institute to look it those aspects and better that knowledge? 
Thanks and regards 
Good day”
My response
You raised an important point that touches my nerves. I am anguished that Hinduism and our ancient literature were not understood and don't find a place in our universities and schools.
There are two issues with this. 
The first issue is knowing and understanding Hinduism.  That is a big confused thinking on Hinduism even now. What we know comes from German and British writers ,who are not experts and who didn't understand or interpret our literature correctly . They are biased . Their understanding of Hinduism can be summed up as
Hinduism is a religion like Christianity or Islam.  It believes in hundreds of gods. It is a primitive religion , not scientific , full of superstitions.
Aryans migrated from Europe and subjugated locals.
Sanskrit is derived from an ancient European language. 
Hinduism is built on castes. 
Hinduism is full of rituals and sacrifices. 
It is dominated by Brahmins.

Most educated Indians believed the above aspects and shunned studying the literature and banned it from our schools and universities.  
But unfortunately the above statements proposed by Europeans is more fiction. Hinduism is different . It is more than religion . It integrates religious practices like bhakti , meditation with living practices like samsara, artha ,kama, karma etc and with dharma - value systems like dos and don'ts,  order , sharing, non violence, harmony, society, respect for nature and animals, governance , organization etc. It is not a unitary religion - it doesn't accept the theory “ one size fits all” . It accepts that each person has different capabilities and interests and provides different paths - jnana, bhakti, yoga , karma etc. It understood that living needs multiple tasks , and so segregated people on tasks ,not on birth. It went into a higher level of philosophical analyses by many in different disciplines like nyaya, samkya, viseshika, yoga , mimamsa and vedanta . 
Hinduism realised that life needs knowledge and evolved many sciences based on Observations and reasoning. That is what is known as vedanta. Many sciences evolved during the vedic period. Unfortunately,  there is no understanding and appreciation of the above aspects by most academics.


Our education system distanced itself from Indian achievements for a long time. At the time of independence, there  was a strong force of communism sweeping the UK and its effects were felt in India. Most administrators of the Nehru era and most academics were great lovers of communist philosophy.  Both the British rulers and academics decried Indian knowledge systems. They destroyed our heritage by saying every aspect of Sanskrit literature is myth and there is no truth in it. Indian elite believed in it and propagated it. So Indian educators steered clear of ancient Indian writings. 
None of the school books contained anything about our earlier history and living. Indian history was Mughal history. I read a bit of British and a lot of Mughal history in school. 
There was a strong wind blowing against casteism . Caste was equated with hindu ancient thoughts . So religion and Indian ancient science and philosophy were banned from our education system. 
IISc is no exception to this rule. While some may talk about it, most debunked our role in mathematics.  It took us decades and centuries to show the world that Arabic numerals had their origin in India. We continued to use the term Arabic numerals. 
We are not aware of metallurgy, surgery, geometry, astronomy, Mathematics. Geology, etc studied during the vedic era. We don't teach aryabhattiyam , Bhaskaras Lilavathy , or other trastises written by Brahmagupta, varahamihira, Madhava, etc. We are not aware of the 160 schools of nyaya - logic or navya nyaya - new logic. 
That is a big tragedy. We ignore our history. 
I was sad that even the IISc faculty didn't appreciate our scientific development. 
I conducted an international conference in 1986 on knowledge representation in sanskrit . Many didn't like it. I ran a workshop on vedic mathematics. Two of my colleagues abused me. One of them said that we shouldn't be associated with such pseudo scientific activities. But I got support from a few others. 
Greetings 
Dks
19/08/25, 5:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 810

Prof Vinod Vyasulu reminises on vedanta:
“DKS, I've been thinking over your many posts on the vedanta which I found very illuminating. But the vedanta is one--the major--dharma. We have also those associated with Mahavira and the Jains, the Buddha, Basava, Sikh and possibly others I do not know. These are all home grown. Have probably intermingled over the years. Would you consider a series on some of these?”

My response
I had a long exposure to Sankaracharya,  Yajnavalkya,  upanishads , vedanta and vedas for many years through reading a large number of books written by Indians and foreigners, listening to many talks and discussions and contemplating for days and months. I have been studying vedanta since childhood .  So I was able to write about vedanta related to Hinduism. I still feel that vedanta is interpreted differently. Even swami Vivekananda calls it practical vedanta in order to make common people accept it. 
We assume there are six darshanas . Unfortunately , that is a broad classification. But there is more than what we see. There are hundreds of variations. Nyaya has about 160 schools. Even vedanta is not restricted to uttara mimansa. It exists in brahma sutra, upanishads and obviously in vedas. Vedas also had many variations. 
It means that there is no one unified view about vedanta. It is natural as our vedanta did emphasize on Brahman- the eternal all pervading permanent reality ,but vedanta discussed on many aspects of evolution, human body, mind, living, health, yoga , meditation, dhyana, turiya,  ethics, reasoning, intelligence, education, gurus, learning , viveka, language and grammar, Mathematics, geology, nature and environment, geometry, logic, proving hypotheses, metallurgy, heat transfer, energy and forces, infinity, completeness, Neti- negation , not this -  rule, governance, organization etc to name a few.  Again ,most modern commentators restricted themselves to a few topics like  materialism, God, duality or nonduality, etc. So a good and comprehensive understanding is missing. 
Since vedanta provides pluralistic and multidirectional views, there is a confusion. Buddhism definitely has many concepts common to vedanta but with different interpretations. Even vedantins didn't agree to one interpretation. That is why tarka and Purva mimamsa evolved to provide the foundations for arguments. 
I view Hinduism as a multidimensional architecture of principles, philosophies, ideas, practices encompassing many areas affecting nature, humans,society,  space, living, organization  with a great emphasis to understand all aspects of reality in all their manifestations. (I see that  It is not easy to understand this statement .) The focus is on correct perception and understanding with good clarity, discrimination, permanence  and enormity of thoughts. The enormity is really amazing . Recall the facts that
1 Hindus can conceive of very large numbers upto googol and beyond, and of very small fractions. They even numbered them. Vedas are full of numbers. 
2 Hindus declared that space is infinite , but everything in it is finite in time also. 
3.Evolution theory builds uncertainty in it. 
4.Natural forces are immense . Nomenclatures of various levels of natural forces of sun, wind, rain, earth are mind-boggling. 
5.Hinduism talks of frugal consumption , needed for a sustainable society. 
6. It talks of many noble thoughts-  vasudaiva kutumbakam, Lokas  Samastha  sukhino bhavantu  , tamasoma jyotir gamaya , satyam vada, ekam sat, shraddhaya deyam and many others .
Secondly, my knowledge of other religious theories is very minimal. So I am not competent to discuss those. I consider it not correct to discuss them as my biases will show up. 
Maybe someone better equipped can do justice to other Indian philosophies. 
Greetings 
Dks

S R Venkataramanan comments on my posts on ancient Indian science research at IISc:
“you’re right on!  you may want to point to the book ‘Pride of India’ published by samskRta bhaarati, contributed by several people knowledgable in science and samskRtam.  you may know Prof. Ramasubramanian of IIT-B (son of shri Krishnamurty Sastrygal) and he has several lectures on the contributions of bhaarata to science and how the half-backed british and other europeans mis-interpreted them and subsequently our lazy population consumed them instead of going to the source and developed such inferiority. Dr. Raj Vedam (has several you tubes and Ted speeches) is also another who has been debunking these western interpretation of sanaatana dharma and contributions of vedic period to ‘modern’ science and technology. there’s a lot of material now in public domain to point out how europeans ‘stole’ bhaaratiia contributions and put their name on them. e.g., boudhaayana suutram became ‘pythogorous theorem’, pingala’s work became work by ‘Fibanacci’, etc. unfortunately, even after knowing these NCERT never cares to revise our school textbooks to update those euro centric materials, or still has resistance from ‘interested’ parties. there are still some people from bhaaratam who knowingly refuse to accept the contribution of bhaaratam to science and technology with a different agenda in mind. the more i learn about those works in vedic and post-vedic (pre mugal and european) periods of bhaaratam, i feel anguished and angry about how bad i have been taught in school. the only way i can quiesce is to talk about these and tell our own people from bhaaratam about some of our achievements and point them to some of the sources available now to read about them. oh, well....”

My response
Thanks.  
But there is a lot of diffidence amongst people about the contributions of ancient Indians to science. We should see why this is so ? 
There are many reasons . 
The first reason we need to look at is the question: how does one accept a new concept like gravity , atoms, germs or cells or computers etc? 
It is a multiple sequential and parallel paths. But the most important reason is the acceptance by academics. If a concept is accepted by academics it gets accepted by people and gets into the curriculum. That didn't happen with Indian contribution to science. 

The first step is a uniformly acceptable view presented to academics and accepted by them. This process is an intense one, going through many stages - publications of findings is the first step , followed by conference presentations and lectures to peer groups in different universities. This will be followed by discussions and researchers getting involved with the discovery. Once the publications reach a critical size, the concept gets accepted and becomes part of the curriculum. That is an involved process. We don't have a similar process and can't expect a similar process. But conferences and lecture series will help. 

Most physicists accept the findings of Newton, Pascal, Faraday, Einstein ,Max Born , Niels Bohr and propagate them in their lectures. That is how science achieved a dominant place in our society. There are scientists who did great research work . There are teachers who propagate these inventions to students. There are people who admire science without understanding. 
While Physics is a few centuries old, cell biology is recent and we see the same model in its growth and acceptance. Medicine despite hurdles of correct understanding grew by leaps despite struggles by Pasteur , Semmelweis, Lister, Robert Koch and acceptance of germ theories and a simple concept like washing hands 
Let us continue further in the next post. 
Greetings. 
Dks
21/08/25, 4:51 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 812

Let me discuss the process of acceptance based on my experiences. 
Most of us think that an academic's role is in teaching and research. Many follow that approach. But there is another important role for academics - to prove and accept a new idea or concept and to find faults with a concept and force its death. As I said before, not all academics get involved in this. Normally ,people in the related disciplines become curious and analyse and experiment to find many realities of the new concept. But many academics conjecture futuristic scenarios. It is done regularly. 
When do we do it ? It normally happens every day while going for coffee. The morning coffee is a ritual necessity and normally we go in groups of faculty. I used to go with my contemporary colleagues , my seniors for coffee. That is the time for a lot of discussions.  We used to discuss contemporary developments, relevance of new areas like remote sensing and possible future predictions . I also joined other groups from other departments. These discussions helped in evolving new activities and programs.  One discussion culminated in a new discipline called physical engineering. People gave their views freely , argued passionately in these discussions. Another series of discussions led to setting up ASTRA.  Even joint programs emanated from these discussions. Sometimes the discussions led to fierce  arguments. 
I was fortunate to grow with an emerging discipline - computers - from 1962 onwards. I saw new concepts coming in regularly like organization, hardware , logic , programming, numerical methods, data , software , networking, Internet,  etc. So I had lively discussions on computers with many other faculty in the institute. 

During the sixties and even part of the seventies , computer meant only hardware. I was working on numerical methods to solve large problems with thousand plus variables. They can't be solved analytically. But still mostly faculty felt that analytical approaches are the best and numerical methods were inferior. I had a tough time and it took years to convince others about the need for numerical problem solving. 
Similarly ,I had difficulty in convincing other faculty about the growth of programming into software. Most rejected software as a discipline. They said programming is a tool and not scientific. IISc didn't get a computer till 1972 and didn't set up a full fledged computer science program till the mid eighties. That is due to the myopic views of concerned departmental faculty. 
But my troubles increased whenever I talked about Indian contributions to sciences like metallurgy. Faculty from aerospace asserted  that Pushpaka Vimana is fiction. All activities described in vedic literature were dismissed as fiction. Even vedic mathematics was described as a gimmick. 
Most of these growths and acceptance by people are due to two major thrusts.
Enormous, coordinated,multiexperts defined, and  indepth work which can be proved theoretically and experimentally . 
The evolution of a coherent view with modern notations as accepted by scientists with solid proofs. These conditions were not met by the ancient  Indian scientific work.  While numbers were accepted , there was reluctance to accept geometry. Astronomy was discredited as a part of astrology. Unfortunately,  logic - nyaya - was not understood and hence was not  accepted by our science community. It was left to Noam Chomsky to appreciate and understand Paninian grammar structures. I made two suggestions - one to include problems in Lilavati in our school mathematics textbooks at appropriate levels and to include the neti concept to define various terms. We don't teach grammar of Indian languages. That is the state even now . Even concrete implementable suggestions are ignored.

Greetings
Dks
22/08/25, 3:52 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 813

Vinod Vyasulu has two comments.
VINODVYASULU: https://www2.math.uconn.edu/~glaz/Preparation_For_Sciences/Lilavati%27s%20Swarm%20with%20Solution.pdf
: DKS, would Ramanujan's Notebook fall into the tradition of Vedic mathematics? He provided numerous theorems that have all kinds of applications in different fields. Others have proved some, many remain to be proved. A criticism of Vedic mathematics has been that it's doesn't provide proofs as today's mathematics requires. Nevertheless they are true. And isn't Ramanujan like that? Hardy did the mainstream stuff.

My response
The first reference deals with use of problems in Lilavati in an American university -
University of Connecticut. My request to our education minister in 1986 didn't see the light of the day. What a shame?
Your second comment has two parts. Let me look at the first one - what is Vedic mathematics?
Initially it meant sulabasutras which provided easy ways of arithmetic calculations. That and geometry were the requirements for building huge yagashalas.  So both number systems and arithmetic were part of vedas in addition to geometry , astronomy, geology etc. 
Later Aryabhatta, Varahamihira,  Bhaskara, Brahmagupta , Madhava and others developed more aspects of mathematics - solution of non linear equations, algebra, Dynamics, etc. They primarily used for calculating the positions and movements of many planetary bodies like sun, moon, planets and some stars very accurately - this is the base for Astronomy. They further used complex calculations for astrology. The application here was astrology. There are two main streams of Astrology - vakya based on both mathematics and grammar and ganitha- mathematics , this was modified to thrikganitha , corrected mathematics to adjust to lunar year calculations. We may say astrology is pseudo science. But it gave birth to several mathematical aspects - time and measurements of time precisely, planetary movements , tools for measurements, precise calculations of days, months , years and calenders, prediction of eclipses and many occurrences with planets . 
So mathematics in India was developed over a period of more than a thousand years. So it is appropriate to call them Indian mathematics though its foundations were laid at the Vedic period. 
Mathematicians in our universities in the past centuries have worked on understanding many activities done in the first millenia of the common era . 
Ramanujan is a prominent person in our  era. But he didn't provide proof for most discoveries. Most looked like conjectures based on intuition. We are not familiar with the approaches and methods. But he had provided detailed notebooks on his methods. Even steps have been provided , but how the steps came out is not clear . Hardy solved some but many more are remaining to uncode. That became a research area for many mathematicians.
So the term vedic mathematics is not a clearly defined one. If we restrict to what was in vedas , then it refers to arithmetic, solution of equations and planetary motion. Works by Aryabhatta, Varahamihira, Brahmagupta, Bhaskara etc are extensions of that mathematics. But Ramanujan's work goes beyond that. It didn't focus on astrology or astronomy , but it dealt with pure mathematics, mathematics for its beauty and not applicability. Ramanujan's mathematics used the western notations and it was very advanced. So it is different from vedic mathematics.  
While a large number of researchers are excited about Ramanujan's work, there is no such interest in the work of earlier mathematicians. Godel was one of the few who provided a holistic view. Unfortunately the holistic views of our mathematicians was not understood due to notational differences.
Logic in India has a longer history of development - close to 1600 years. Most logic focused on truth based on meanings . Logical operations were developed by many nyaya scholars  which go beyond the western logic notations. Many methods were developed by many scholars. Navya nyaya was a transition. Logic focused on reasoning in addition to operations. Unfortunately these were not decoded fully by our scientists ,even now. 
Your second comment: 
I agree with your comment that most work in the early years lacked proof. They were cryptic. So it wasn't easy to understand for even Indians. I don't see evidence of training in mathematics in gurukulas or treatises in mathematics. Calculations exist in Aryabhattiyam and Lilavati . Whereas logic literature is available in plenty. Mathematics was embedded in applications and not separately. I may be wrong here. Maybe we lost most of our works. 
Greetings
Dks

My response.
Thanks Prof karunakara for your deep interest . I agree that unfortunately all our scriptures are in sanskrit and that meant we are ignorant about what those scriptures contain. If you ask 100 people about rig veda , you will get 200 answers each different from others. 
While there are translations in English , mostly by foreigners, Rig veda is cryptic and these translations are equally ununderstandable. Indians have only discussed the religious parts and ignored the scientific side. 
Actually rig veda is a big source of information. It has many principles of living. It discusses the universe , earth , nature and natural forces. It discusses life and societal organizations and practices. It contains prayers to gods . It also looks at existence, evolution etc. Many scientific concepts were spread across the hymns . It discusses reality, eternal time, uncertainty, large forces, universal order , universal connectivity , cyclic nature of life , numerical calculations, etc. Since terms like quantum were invented in the 20th century , their concepts are found in rig veda - particularly Dynamics, uncertainty, discreteness, connectivity, quantum entanglement, discreteness , infinity, eternal etc . Nasadiya suktam in rig veda talks about existence, uncertainty , evolution, etc. It implies that nothing is certain. Surprises and changes are happening regularly in our life. 
We need to involve faculty in bringing out the many concepts in vedas and upanishads . But unfortunately that is not happening. A few people here and there study our ancient science , but the scientific depth is missing. 
I feel that logic as found in our nyaya will be a very useful contribution. 
Similarly our ancient scriptures particularly vedanta and upanishads have detailed analysis of a human - body, minds, controls, flows and connectivities. We talk of chakras and nadis - what are they? How do we create energy , use it efficiently? There are great descriptions on jnana , minds which can help us in designing better AI systems. Varaha upanishad discusses in detail human systems. We have information. We need to mine them, decode them, reinterpret them and put them for use in areas like education, health, AI etc. 
While many foreign universities have a centre to study Hinduism, we don't have any such centre in Indian universities.  Why are we shying away from serious academic work on our ancient knowledge systems? In today's AI world , both logic and grammar can play a big role to give us a good command over cognitive AI. We can leap forward.
Hope we wake up and consider these as serious areas for research .
I recall a statement by the Tamil satirist  and political guru, Cho Ramaswamy who said in a lighter vain, “ if you can't do something good  , then criticise it, make jokes on it and attack it.” That is exactly what we are doing with the Indian knowledge repository. 
Thanks for all .

24/08/25, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 815
Having discussed comments from many friends and triggered by them , I now want to give my appraisal of vedic sciences .
First, I don't want to use the word science but systems . I try to distinguish ancient Indian systems from the western systems. I use the word system to include all aspects of development as science is restrictive in its use. 
It was Pythagoras who separated hard science from humanities  and languages. But vedic systems are holistic and integrated. Science was embedded in vedic systems. It looked at grammar, phonetics, logic, mathematics, construction etc in a unified and integrated manner. Vedas felt that  all aspects are strongly connected. Vedas took the complete path from conceptualization to application. Application was the outcome. 
The second difference refers to the methods of reasoning . 
How do we analyze or reason on a concept or statement ? 
There are three western methods :
Observations and experimentation 
Mathematical approaches
Logical approaches 
Indian systems use not one approach but a combination of observation , filtering, language, logic and mathematics plus intuition or self realization. Upanishads and vedanta give details. 
In addition, the Indian system uses enquiry - question and answers , discussions, multiple views etc to find truth about a statement. Brihadaranyaka upanishad is a great example of such analysis . Tarka is an accepted form of establishing a position. 
There is another difference. Western systems concentrate on matter. Indian systems understand connectivity, and waves. The western system still doesn't have a good picture of waves , energy, mind , body reactions, diffusion etc. Indian systems moved into the domain of unknowns easily while western system restricted itself to known domains, rarely straying into nearby unknowns. But pouring scorns on unknowns.
Let me look at some aspects of Indian systems :
True the vedas talk about metallurgy , construction , agriculture etc,. Let me divide systems  into hard and soft. Hard sciences are matter based, experimentation and observation oriented say upto molecular level and even atomic level propounded by physics and chemistry . These are well developed over the past 400 years. These are primarily hard process oriented. While vedas talk about heat transfer , metallurgy , astronomy - i consider astronomy as part hard part soft science- , the explanations , theory and processes are missing in vedas . Some aspects are found in darsanas like samkya and Visesika. By the mid  20th century , we embedded soft sciences into hard sciences, quantum theory is a good example. Vedas really talk about many concepts and principles of hard science, an example being detailed and extensive categorization and classification found in much literature with a lot of painstaking and detailed effort. 
Indian scientific and philosophical systems concentrated on mostly soft aspects and used hard experimentation as a support. So we built strong foundations in grammar, logic , Mathematics,  human systems, psychology, mind , thinking, education and learning . Our gurukula system allowed students to explore deeply into various concepts . So our learning was inquisitive and deep. There was a lot of clarity in understanding.This is inherent in our adaptations to new areas with speed and by large numbers. The best example is software - the domination established by us. Do you think that our training for thousands of years on soft systems has no role in these developments? We are well equipped to move fast into AI. During the late nineties and early twenties , I was asked a question by many - why Bangalore dominates the software scene in the world? My answer was synergy in thinking, learning, opportunities, infrastructure, critical mass of people, great enthusiasm, moving into unknown areas with ease.
Greetings
Dks
25/08/25, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 816

I thank the discussers for looking at topics of interest and importance . Let me return to my 1970 period in IISc.  
1970 was again another watershed year in my life. I submitted my thesis for PhD.  I learnt an important lesson . 
Success in life doesn't come from hard work, intelligence , productive work, sincerity , participation etc. I had all of them to a reasonable measure. I had the confidence of several important people in IISc like Prof Dhawan, Prof R Narasimha, Prof BS Ramakrishna , Prof Krishna Prasad of Mechanical engineering , Prof AK Rao, Prof AR Vasudevamurthy, Prof T Ramakrishnan, Prof Cama etc. I built a good support system with many in admin, and I became indispensable to Prof Narayana Iyengar,  head of the department.  My research has progressed well with Prof Devanathan's support. I submitted the thesis by april1970. I should consider myself to be safe and successful. But success needed a bigger and more important ingredient - psychology of humans as Wodehouse's Jeeves would say. Understanding people in positions, anticipating them and protecting oneself is the key to success. I saw a great powerplay and was affected by it for the next 20 years. I antagonised two people due to the power plays.  Let me talk about it later.
Another major development took place in 1970. Recall the visit by Prof Dhawan and group to the then USSR to discuss starting a joint school in collaboration with their institutions. IISc formalised the setting up of the school of automation in collaboration with Moscow Power Institute. Deekshatulu was identified as a coordinator. It was located in the EE department but with associate faculty - three members from the mechanical engineering department and a few from the  ECE department.  About half of the EE faculty were involved in the school. Most control faculty became members of the new school. I got attached to the school due to my work on computers. But I had a big role in the running of the EE department,  in the curriculum committee of the institute , the hostel and power committee. This was the first interdepartmental experiment at IISc.  The newly built extension  wing ,housing the controls group , was given to the newly established school. 
After Deekshatulu left for the USA in 1970 for sabbatical,  the school was managed by Prof Iyengar assisted by ISN Murty. Prof IG Sarma ,who joined the aerospace department from IIT Kanpur,  was associated with the school. Prof SV Rangaswamy also joined the school from ECE department.  But IGS created problems for the school. Prof Chidambara and Prof EV Krishnamurthy also joined in 1970 71 as well as Prof V Krishnan,and  Prof Joshi. Institute got a sizable number more than 20 at the senior levels of associate professors and professors . That meant that our stable ,business as usual , activities were disturbed. We became a top heavy institute. Till 1970, the institute was mostly a growing one with a majority of faculty at the lecturer level with most of them doing PhD. There was a lot of energy, a lot of enthusiasm ,and a lot of interest in the new areas. But with the new additions at higher levels, this enthusiastic situation  was affected. Further the new entrants tried to compare the functioning at IISc with the functioning in their old institution. They were suggesting changes which didn't go well with many seniors. It led to friction. Homogeneity was lost. But it was good. We started debates and discussions and accepted good suggestions. Also the new entrants liked our governance system and most of the new faculty became defenders of IISc quickly. Only a few carried a chip on their shoulder . We had many senior professors coming from IIT Kanpur and they wanted to impose IIT Kanpur rules here. Fortunately , it didn't work. They also realised the value of the institutes processes ,policies and practices were the best . They realised that many came here from IIT Kanpur asked they were not happy with the ambience there. They like our ambience and our stability. 
So new ideas started coming in and conflicts started building up both in the institute and the department.

26/08/25, 5:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 817
I saw from a front seat a power play at that young inexperienced - to power struggles- age and was affected by it for the next 25 years. Understanding the power play and finding ways to compensate for its effects helped me in my life. 
Prof Deekshatulu left for the US and the activities of the school , still in its formative stages ,were struggling. 
IGS wanted to take control of the school and was working towards that. He started with an act of establishing himself as the leader of control systems group in the EE department in the absence of Deekshatulu.  Let me give one instance. 
One of the major activities before the start of a new semester is to decide on the courses to be offered in the coming semester and the faculty to teach it. Normally this needs a certain amount of time due to the resolution of conflicts arising from faculty's interests ,time and research needs. I was assigned this tough task . So I went around and got the consent from most faculty and prepared a list of courses and faculty agreed to teach them. I was to present it in the faculty meeting. That is when I got the shock of my life. Before I made my presentation, IGS said that he has finalized the list of faculty who will teach the control courses. This shocked many senior faculty .  He made his presentation. Then Prof Iyengar told me to give my list. Since there were no differences , it was adopted in the meeting. Many seniors expressed shock after the meeting. 
IGS told Dhawan that progress in the development of the school was not satisfactory. So Prof Dhawan appointed IGS as the chair of the school and identified a committee to run the school. The school got delinked from the EE department from that day. The relationships got strained. 
IGS stopped attending the EE activities and concentrated his attention on the activities of the school. 
It was decided to send our faculty to Moscow. The first batch left in 1971 . I was identified for the second batch along with VVS, NV, and Hariharan.  We got a teacher from the USSR to train us in Russian language . It was intense learning every day with her. This continued for nearly a year. 
By then, the first batch returned and YV Venkatesh said that the training was useless. R krishnan explained his sufferings in Moscow to get vegetarian food. IGS was finding excuses to inform the director why the school is not moving forward and why progress is slow. IGS, working on his plans to ditch the collaboration , decided to cancel our proposed visit to the USSR. So the Russian training was a waste. The only thing that happened in 1971-72 was the starting of the PhD program in the school. 
During the 1970-71 period , Prof Lewis joined the Foreign languages department to teach English. I attended his classes also for two years. That was useful. 
I was busy with my other activities as assistant warden of the hostel, chair of the power committee, and a member of the curriculum committee in addition to teaching several new courses. It was an intense learning period.

27/08/25, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 818
Before Prof Devanathan was hospitalized , he told me that he recommended me to teach Fortran to students in a new training institution called Datamatics corporation founded  by a person Dr LS Kanodia , an early bird in computer training and the only one. 
So in mid june 1970 , when Mr Chandrasekhara met me, I wasn't surprised . He said he is the manager of Datamatics and wanted me to teach Fortran . The lectures were in the evening between 600pm to 730pm . Their office and class room were located in Kempe gowda road in the bank of India building . So I had to commute by bus to that place every day. These kept me busy as I had completed my PhD by then. I liked Chandrasekhara from the beginning , a nice , mild , friendly and well spoken person. We became close. He was my third friend after Mr Bukebag and Thimme Gowda , all three interested in my welfare and helped me throughout. 
My association with Chandrasekhara continued for about eight years till 1978. He died suddenly in 1988 due to a heart attack and I lost a great friend. The association with Datamatics allowed me to dig deep into programming languages. I taught Fortran to several hundred students and Autocoder to hundreds as well as COBOL to many . I did courses in many industries and R and D organizations. In addition ,I helped a large number of people in writing programs. 
Let me move to another activity of mine - timetable committee. Prof Krishnaprasad took charge as convenor of the time table committee and I assisted him. We restructured the time table and I simplified the process . I built a computer algorithm. Prof RNarasimha was impressed. So I was made the convenor of the time table committee in 1971. Prof Dhawan introduced me to the senate and faculties as I wasn't a member being a lecturer then. It was a great recognition for me being entrusted with the major task of preparing a time table for all courses run in the institute.  The lecture halls complex was ready and I allotted the rooms for lectures. As all members of the committee were either professors or assistant professors,  I had some run-ins with some faculty . But fortunately most cooperated and I could handle the activities successfully. Prof Dhawan introduced me to  Prof Kothari, the UGC chairman and I discussed my work of computerization with him. It was the first successful job I did as a leader.
 It was also a good experience in human relations. Midway in my term as convenor , Prof Dhawan left for Caltech on sabbatical.  I had problems even with the in charge director who diverted furniture from three classrooms to his department. When I said I had a shortage  in allotting rooms for courses, he told me that I could use the organic chemistry class room also. But the chairman of OC protested . I talked to the registrar to resolve it , but he laughed it off by saying that he loved fights between academics. So I had to do with the classrooms in the central lecture halls only. I managed the allocation.

Dks
28/08/25, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 819
I realised another characteristic existing in me - urge to explore. I talked about my urge to improve before. The year 1970 revealed my capability to explore . I explored not only scientific aspects but also on human behaviours and teaching.
  My teaching expanded from the motivated IISc students to interested groups of people of varying interests, ages and education that I encountered at Datamatics while teaching programming . I was teaching people older than me.  I had very senior researchers in my class as well as inexperienced youngsters, some with interest in learning , some without . But most did my daily assignments. I believed that programming is not just classroom lectures but mostly based on regular  practices.  So I gave exercises everyday and saw that everyone complied with it.  That experience made me understand ways of handling people who were not motivated or intelligent. This was revealed in a stronger sense and full force when I taught computer courses in many engineering colleges, in Proficience, and in public lectures at Rotary clubs. 
My urge to explore  diversified from research to teaching several new courses. I taught numerical analysis to the engineers in Kirloskar electric company . Prof G Krishna returned . So we floated a course on optimization for the first time in the institute. I also taught a new course on computational methods to our BE students. 
I changed my computer systems course . Instead of logic and arithmetic, I moved into details explained in an excellent book by Burroughs corporation which detailed most devices like card reader, buffers, etc. I reduced the time on hardware and spent about 5 hours on compilers and 5 hours on operating systems. I started on OS teaching and understanding in 1970 and this continued for the next 25 years. I started teaching OS later as a full course at IISc,  IIT Bombay and several colleges. This was my starting point. I used an IBM manual to teach OS concepts. 
I realised one important distinction. Learning does not come from outside - reading books, listening to lectures. It comes from inside. The outside provides data . Only your inside converts it into meaningful knowledge.  Unfortunately we don't appreciate this important fact-
Cognition is the process of taking facts, linking them, analysing them,  creating many views, applying them, looking for impacts and looking for future developments. This has to be done by our mind, not by our teachers, mentors or books . 
We don't build these capabilities of self learning in our students. That is why I considered term paper- writing on a topic based on latest research - is a good learning tool according to me. I also used another tool - writing large programs of thousands of line - at a time when computing power was meagre. I did my programming in low level languages and solved large problems by writing 1000s of lines of code. That gave me the confidence that my students can do it. 
Initially,  I asked the students to submit a topic for the term paper and a problem statement for problem solving. Later, based on my experiences,  I divided the total activity into three or four stages and created timelines so the students can be checked for being on track. This reduced default time and kept the students on track. That was very useful. 
I assigned about 50% to 60%  weightage for these two in the sessional marks, and that helped most students to go past the pass grade. Failures were almost nonexistent in my classes. Let me continue this further in the next post.
Greetings 
Dks
29/08/25, 4:50 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 820
Let me look at teaching activities. I worked on ,thinking about teaching all the time. 
Teaching is no stranger to me. 
What I was trying to state was the fact that I did a deep thinking and evolved a concept on teaching based on my experiences - 1.observing teachers and speakers - more than 100 teachers for 18 years. 
2.Observing and understanding the  many paths for learning - from the family, neighbours,  friends, society, news media, fiction books, movies, conversations , discussions etc.
3.Remembering the facts through contemplation,
4.Analysing the collected data to produce knowledge, questioning the facts, filtering the facts 
5.Experimenting with the facts ,testing their validity 
6.studying the facts from various angles to get a holistic knowledge 
7. Producing new facts
8. Family tradition of teaching. 
What was my realization on teaching going on in our colleges? This depicts the current trends in teaching in general.
It is a pure backward looking activity dependent on text books with no motivation for curiosity and innovation. 
It deals with existing knowledge without kindling students to look at new changes happening. .It is not modern or relevant. I never understood applied mechanics . If beams and bridges were brought into the picture , I would have understood cantilever better. Arch was ignored. Concepts should be linked to applications. That was missing. 
It is one directional - teacher to student. Cognition is incidental and reserved for the best teachers.  
It is not connected , and looks artificial. Engineering is today a strongly connected environment. So whole is more important than parts . Our curriculum is parts based . Integration is not attempted. Top down approach is missing. 
These led me to believe that a person has to use his mind extensively to learn and that defined my role as a teacher - not just a knowledge transferring person but a facilitator , a mentor, an inspirer , a provider of paths for learning as a guru or Acharya was doing in the past. This led me to assume some aspects.
My axioms are 
1.Teachers and students are co-travellers in search of new knowledge,  not just looking at the existing knowledge.  This was necessary for me to get into a rapidly changing field of computer science. 
2. Cognition is an activity to be executed by a student. So teachers should energize them and push them to self learn.
3.A teacher should be a learner first , should know more than his students. 
4. A teacher should know an overall aspect of his area . He should not focus on the topics of his course only . He should connect it with other topics in relevant topics and applications. He should get a feel of the overall environment of his area including futuristic changes , impacts on society. This understanding came to me faster because I was getting into a new area of computer science with a large untapped potential for developments and applications and ample opportunities for thinking . 
5.A teacher should explain and  clarify difficult topics , not ignore them, in order  to aid in the cognition of students. Understanding of concepts is an important purpose of teaching and learning.  
6. A teacher should have a futuristic outlook , not look backwards , a disease I find in our teaching community. Research is not a vehicle for promotion, but a quest for futuristic thinking.
More in the next.

30/08/25, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 821

Let me list some principles adopted by me in teaching.
Don't assume students are bad. Don't fail them as far  as possible. Do your evaluations seriously. Review them carefully . Provide opportunities for students to get through the exam. 
Understand your students' knowledge levels and capabilities. Help them to acquire the prerequisite knowledge. My students at IISc were attentive but didn't ask questions. My students at IIM asked me a lot of questions. They were thinking on their feet. My students at many  colleges were a mixed lot, many very keen and many disinterested totally. My industry participants were looking from the point of view of using what they learn . 
Prepare the complete course plan . The plan should have a focus - what do you want your students to be capable of at the end . Say , in an OS course, do you want students to design an OS at the end or use an OS? I always built my course with designers in mind. The plan should have a clarity - what contents are needed? What contents connect well to form a whole? How do we connect to the broader discipline? What is the future directions in the area? Then identify sources .Go through the books before the semester starts as far as possible but add more if you come across new aspects. That was necessary for computer courses as concepts were evolving.  Read more books, papers and manuals. Be thorough.  Plan for the next lecture in detail. The complete plans are necessary for the success of a course. Success is measured by the satisfaction of students.
Try to enhance their learning and cognition through self work and self thinking. Individual term paper and problem solving skills are important. 
Build innovation into the course
Make the contents modern . Change it every year. Give a futuristic perspective. 
Identify difficult concepts. Explain in detail. This is necessary . For example text books don't explain the differences between a program, a process and a thread. So it is a teacher's job to distinguish between these. Teaching second law of thermodynamics is tough 
Don't follow the text in the book verbatim. Add additional contents. I always do that and try to include it in evaluation. Go for in depth explanation. Provide social ,economic and technological impacts. Give practical examples. 
Spend time on detailed explanations of definitions. Don't use jargon, clichés to define a term or concept. Make definitions holistic . That is a big art. Brihadaranya upanishad gives a detailed way of defining what are not Brahman using the neti rule. 
Provide practical examples, anecdotes to improve attention. We should present a whole situation  first, and then discuss  the problems found in it  and later discuss the detailed solution methods. Don't jump into models and mathematics first. That is unfortunately the trend now. Disconnected parts won't easily lead one to visualise the whole. 
Ask questions and try to extract answers. It is another approach to improve cognition. I normally ask two or three questions in every lecture . This helps me in many ways. One , I understand the level of understanding by the students. Two it makes them think deeper . Three it creates some excitement . 
Allow questioning by students. 
Provide an abstract of each lecture in the end and recap it in the beginning of the next lecture. 
Speak clearly. Don't mumble. Use simple short sentences. Write on the board or ppt clearly , visible to all. Give time to assimilate. 
Certain aspects followed by professions and in practices are not understood easily or ignored by most. Talk about processes, performance requirements, metrics, resilience, benchmarks, stress tests, project evaluation, vendor evaluation,  technology evaluation etc.
