01/09/25, 4:38 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 823

Let me return to 1970 and IISc.  I attended several conferences - one at IIT Delhi,  another at Jaipur - a CBIP annual conference, a third at Delhi - a CBIP workshop and research session on load forecasting - and a fourth one at Bombay - these I remember now. It was the second time I visited IIT Delhi and the first time I met the power systems faculty. I had met the control systems faculty in 1969 at our control theory conference at Bangalore. I met a large number of researchers in the IIT conference but not any faculty from any other institutions. Most researchers were working on power systems Dynamics or protection but I was the only one present there working on computer applications to load flow and optimization. There was no computer science activity in IIT Delhi then. It was close to five years I was working on computer algorithms and I had several ME students who did project work in these areas. But no trace of any in other places except Kanpur- Dr Ramamurthy . 
What I realised then was that there wasn't much interest in power systems amidst researchers in the country. So I will be a lonely person in this area. 
So my decision to move to computer systems in 1968- 69 looked a good one. These facts validated my decision even though there weren't many faculty working in the computer area in India then. IIT Bombay had a computer group and I met Prof Isaac in 1970. But there was no research going on there. IIT Kanpur had a good group . The big place for computer science awareness and research was TIFR. I made  three trips every year to TIFR and used their computers and library and this continued till 1973 . 
The CBIP conference at Jaipur was a fruitful one. My work was appreciated. I was in the midst of many practising power engineers from state electricity boards and some from private companies like Tata electric,  Calcutta electric and Bombay suburban. I also became a reporter for a session. I became an important person in CBIP. Since Prof HNR left in 1969, none from the EE department attended the research conference except me. Prof Gopalakrishna attended the conferences regularly. The conference was good. Since I was the only one attending the conference, I attended two working group meetings - one on the future voltage of transmission - 400kv or 500kv. While most in the committee favoured 500kv , BHEL ,the manufacturer of HV equipments like transformers and switchgear was for 400kv, the only reason being their agreements with a foreign company for the manufacture of 400kv equipments. Again I saw politics at work here. I also attended the research committee on power which is the apex body recommending grants to all participating institutions. 
We stayed in the engineering college hostel . I didn't relish the food . It wasn't spicy and had no rice. It was a rich food. Somehow I managed the food. 
Since I was the convenor of the time table committee , I had meetings and so I had very little time available for the jaipur conference. So I got approval from the director to fly from Bombay to Jaipur and back and for a train journey between Bangalore and Bombay.  The Jaipur flight came from Delhi and on my return trip, the scheduled flight couldn't land at Jaipur and it went straight to Bombay.  Fortunately there was another shuttle flight. I took that. But I have to rebook from indore and Ahmedabad to reach Bombay.  I reached by 4pm. I bought a lot of handicrafts and gave them to Venkatachalam , my cousin's husband and took the train to bangalore via Pune. It was a great journey and a good learning experience.

02/09/25, 4:39 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 824
The CBIP research conference on load forecasting was another revelation for me. I took a lot of effort , went through different trend analysis and regression methods, tried different types of curves and prepared a list of errors. It was a comprehensive work and got the merit award of CBIP. But I was unhappy at the response by the then  director of power survey , the main organization of the Central electricity authority for load growth predictions in the country , who dismissed all mathematical approaches. I was wondering when will these people wake up to the importance of analysis, statistical tools and comparisons. I had a similar  experience at Bangalore also. I was looking at load growth for Bangalore city, and talked to Mr Narayana Rao,KEB,  who became a good friend, said that they assign growth rates to each area and calculate the load growth,- a highly ad hoc procedure. It failed miserably. They assumed that chickpet areas wouldn't grow due to the congested buildings and didn't anticipate vertical growth ,but electricity consumption grew faster in that area.  We lived in this adhocism for long ignorant of available techniques used by the west. 
Actually this work on load prediction got me involved for the next two decades on debates. 
The biggest effect of such fallacious load growth predictions was the power cuts seen in many states in the country for the next 20 plus years. Even in the late eighties , there was a big argument between Prof AKN and the board authorities and Mr SG Ramachandra, an eminent and highly respected electrical engineer , who was actively involved in several public activities. While the board and SGR projected a high load growth with 10% growth rate - not supported by data or analysis, AKN argued that we should move away from the supply side and seriously look at the demand side - go for conservation. He also skipped analysis . So I was roped in by the greater Mysore chamber of industry to come up with a working paper. I got data for the previous 20 years and used several models to study the growth predictions. I came up with my numbers. I should say that my predictions were correct . They matched with  the actual load - maximum demand for karnataka- values measured for the next ten years. 
Then SGR spilled the beans of practicality. If you increase demand, the government might come up with finances to meet half the demand . There will be construction delays . Hence the need to project a higher demand. 
The regular power cuts produced an alternate , more costly and more polluting solution. Installation of in house  diesel generators by thousands of establishments and all industries in Bengaluru became a necessity. Our DG capacity exceeded our maximum demand. 
I had to put up a 4MW DG power station at IISc to meet our needs for continuous power. I also recommended DG power for JNCASR and Jayadeva also. 
When we started the mahithisindhu program of 1000 high schools computerization in Karnataka in the late nineties,  we didn't plan for a DG set. But power disruptions of six to eight hours in rural areas were common and we had to install DG sets in all these schools. 
That is the tragedy of poor planning of electricity demands in India. 
A neglect of proper tool resulted in devious and costly solutions reminding us of the saying - pennywise pound foolish - that were our electricity boards where adhocism and total inward thinking were ruling .

03/09/25, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 825

I found that there are many advantages to attending conferences. 
One major advantage of attending conferences is an increase in my network. You come to know many people - students and faculty as well as industry and government senior engineers.  By 1970 , just three years into my  job, I knew several senior chairman and chief engineers of state electricity boards , several faculty at IITs,  several senior directors of CEA and several senior power engineers in Tatas and other companies. That was very helpful. 
One person whose association was very useful to me was Mr SS Murthy, director of CEA, and later became a member of CEA . I continued my association with him for more than 25 years. He got us several projects like the Bharat Coking Coal Ltd, Delhi electric supply undertaking etc. He was one person in CEA who had ideas of a systemic approach to power grids and operations in India.  He proposed a separate power systems section in CEA and became its first head.
While the west was setting up integrated control centres for power grids so as to provide a trouble free operation , we were not even thinking about it. 
The second advantage is your technical interactions, your delivery of the talk and your capacity to make the audience interested in your topic and understand some concepts that you are expounding. This increased my clarity first and also my confidence level. I wasn't afraid of talking to hundreds of unknown people and even non academics. 
I recall the statement of a chief engineer of Kerala state electricity board. He said that he dreaded the  mathematics in my talk and thought he would sleep of. He said that he understood the way I spoke and complimented me for my talk. This led me to my next activity. 
The other advantage is that you know the pulse of people. You may learn from them human behaviours, may not be technical. You know some of the moods of India.
You also know the kind of work, if any, going on in India. That is why I said no CS work is going, power systems work picked up post 1980 only, the major activity was in control theory at some IIT s and IISc as well as the Institute of radio physics, Calcutta.  Most research was going on in a few central institutions. Lots of work was going on on devices, transmission line sagging,  switching systems, insulation testing  and protection in power systems. Lot of work was going on in water resources and hydraulics. CBIP conferences were much better and practical  than the institution of engineers conferences - I had attended several CBIP and IE conferences. 
Greetings 
Dks
04/09/25, 4:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 826

Let me look at another problem. Since I completed my PhD , I was  looking at some new problems pertaining to an overall picture of power systems operations and control. It is a complex subject , nobody in India even understood this subject. 
There are broadly two major controls in a power grid - load frequency control to handle load variations and generation control which maintains supply voltages within permissible limits . 
The control at that time was  done with an analog controller at power stations and manual adjustments . But it wasn't good. While digital computers can fully automate these controls with fast responses, we didn't even think of that direction . Control activity in India is poor both for frequency and voltage. 
In order to get ready for digital control ,there was a discussion in 1969  about the policy to be adapted - total automatic control or control with human guidance. Americans moved to total automatic control ,but the British used the existing control equipments,  added a computer to calculate control decisions and had strategies to help operators with  these decisions . It superimposed a computer to an existing and integrated  control system for LFC.  The operator can modify  and then implement the modified  decisions. So I decided to work on an integrated total control strategy for India.  I realised that the British strategy was temporary and would die in a decade. But India is getting into this new area . So we should be prepared for this big transition. While Tatas installed a digital control system in Trombay , the state electricity boards waited till the nineties to implement the digital computer based load dispatch systems. 
That was the time interstate power flows were increasing and centralised super power plants were being set up. So each state cannot look at its control in isolation. It won't work. This is due to the basic rule that you can interconnect two systems only when their frequencies are the same. Load frequency control should be common. So the government was thinking of setting up regional electricity boards to take care of interstate exchanges of energy and power. So I included this structure and built a reliable network between the load dispatch computers of the participating states. That was at a time when there were no load dispatch centres and networking was unknown.  Wide area network concept will come up later after more than 20 years- mostly in the  late nineties. 
I prepared an exhaustive paper on digital integrated control of interconnected power systems and presented it in the CBIP research session in 1971. It was appreciated by most participants. It was awarded a gold medal. 
Based on my paper, CBIP decided to set up a new working group on power system control and I, a junior faculty, was made its convenor . I considered it a great honour and the job as a necessary one for India.  
Unfortunately, the committee members had no idea of this topic. It was ahead of their time. 
Later in the eighties ,the central government decided to go in for computer based load dispatch centres in all regional boards, and gave orders to Siemens and Brown Baveri. They selected engineers from CEA and CMC for training abroad. It was decided to train them on both power systems and computers .I got involved with PSTI to train these engineers on computer fundamentals, power system operations and  load dispatch operations. I bore the brunt of lectures. There were three separate programs of 15days each for a larger group of engineers. It was a good experience. I visited Brown Baveri and looked at their systems . 
Greetings 
Dks
05/09/25, 4:04 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 827

Let me now change the topic. My long exposure to load growth predictions and control revealed an interesting fact about our institutions . Most people - engineers ,administrators , managers, policy planners etc lack vision, imagination and more importantly curiosity. We live in our cocoons - a comfortable reality created by each one of us and we live in that world of our beliefs ,our activities - almost like a frog in a well scenario. While youngsters break the mould initially ,sooner or later they also fall into the cocoon state. They lose energy and initiative. I say that many either do firefighting or retire mentally. This situation - mediocrity-is pretty serious and needs to be addressed. 
We saw the concerns of Professors Parvati and Karunakara. I have heard this question “ why do we have many mediocre institutions? Why do we have no research breakthroughs?
The diagnosis by most  is poor. Most claim poor funding as the answer. I feel that increased funding will help ,but won't solve the problem. CV Raman got no funds , but he got the nobel prize by doing science everywhere including in the AG's office. I claim lack of motivation, interest in challenges , excitement , team work, synergy, futuristic vision  and deep work as more important reasons. 
When I was talking about this with my friend Gurumurthy, he asked me - what about startups? No doubt I see thousands of youngsters today bubbling with energy and are eager to work , directions and visions are missing. A low hanging fruit was identified and most startups don't succeed . So it was a short term enthusiasm leading to frustrations. If we have to make a sustainable success of the established startup culture , we need to work in a top down ,connected manner not as independent single application focused entities. 
Most youngsters have a lot of enthusiasm and energy but get frustrated very fast. Ambience - poor and toxic- is the main reason. The excitement disappears fast with years. I know a young engineer . He joined a defence research lab and did some good work. Lack of appreciation and credit, bureaucratic ambience, defence dominance pushed him to leave the job . He lost interest in research . This is not one case. 
The government introduced the QIP program  for motivating engineering college faculty to get involved in research and do a PhD. The results - many got benefited with PhD. But after returning to their parents institution, most went back to their old ways and led a routine teaching life with no research. 
Most of us are happy with a routine government job or a big company job and get settled into a routine life. But is that the purpose of living , is there a higher level of satisfaction that one should get , does one use his potential to live and improve living in general? , doubtful? We may anticipate  a turbulent lifestyle in the coming years with AI becoming more conscious than us humans , more alert, more informative and even knowledgeable.  
We will continue these discussions in the next post.

06/09/25, 4:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 828
In order to understand the situation, let me go back to the basics.
We need to understand that institutions are mediocre because people became mediocre. People play a big role in our institutions and organizations. We don't appreciate this factor. People should accept the brickbats for running mediocre organizations.
People at the helm created a toxic environment which further completely destroyed motivation and creativity. Let me look at people first. 
This further led me to again ask the question - Who is a human? - a worker, a farmer, a merchant a clerk , an engineer ,a teacher, a house wife  - this defined a normal person 
dealing with ordinary survival activities.  
That was the first stage of defining a human based on activities. 
Let us move to the second stage - a person goes through a standard ,predefined education and he or she is worried about a job for survival. 
This also deals with a normal person - may be an 18th century common person. 
Let us look at the third stage - a thinking person - someone who wants to improve his or her knowledge , his or her activities, his or her social interactions. This involves reading ,listening, basic levels of understanding , following rules, some common sense, some societal work etc. This is a real discrimination of a human from animals. One expresses one's opinions, concerns , interests and builds answers through choices. So decision making is important at this stage. But still the aspirational levels are low, the expectations are low. The human is becoming a normal, standard materialistic person. They don't use most of their mind or consciousness- more like a 19th century person. 
Most humans fit into the above four levels. 
All people in these four levels build their cocoons , a virtual cocoon of beliefs, mindsets, limited knowledge and understandings pertaining to their domain and live in them like a frog in the well. They don't look outside. They aren't future proofed. Worms are not the only things that build cocoons. We should understand that we also build a lifelong mental cocoon - it is not beneficial. It prevents us from knowing our potentials and realizing them to have a meaningful life. Up to this level, humans are a burden to the world and it is a negative living. We need to break free from this cocoon state. Even worms break their cocoon. Why not humans? 
Because of these states of living, our organizations, our government and our educational institutions suffer with mediocrity. We justify our existence  with excuses , we exult in externalities not on core aspects .


But progress , development , transformations come from the next level. I will call this transformation and innovation level . The people of this group are deep learners, know their self- capabilities, weaknesses,  strengths etc- , highly cognitive, deep thinkers, contemplators, futuristic and with a mission  - people with a purpose in life , wanting to achieve something, helping others and society, looking for sustainable living . Fortunately there are quite a good number of people at this level. We see many scientists, eminent engineers, industry and political leaders who fall in this category. But they lack holistic vision. They get trapped by firefighting and rat races. 
The sixth level is a very special level . I want to call this spiritual level. A person has many purposes in life, he or she goes beyond the materialistic domain , has a very broad and deep knowledge, has a foresight ,and  futuristic vision  and builds  critical questioning, analysing , thinking capabilities, increases  consciousness and understands  the reality , the problems and looks for better solutions. This is an achievement and leadership level. The number in this level is very small. A country with a better number of people in this category and their impacts will have greater strengths and successes. 
People move upwards from level 1 to a desired level. 
As a country, I see that most people in India fit into  the first four stages with a reasonable number in level 5. For a country to progress in all directions and in a sustainable and happy manner,  we need more people on level 6. We don't have enough at this high level. 
How do we do it?in the next post
Greetings
Dks
07/09/25, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 829
It- the question of taking people to the sixth level-  is a tough question. How do we get a few lakhs to a few millions of people to move to the spiritual high conscious level? 
Human evolution meant humans climbing a ladder of consciousness and going up . Even sixth level may have many sublevels and we can think of a seventh level or eighth level. Today , AI will reach the fifth level of consciousness. So it will beat us if we stay there. So there is an urgency to move up if you want to survive. 
First let us see the first  cause of mediocrity:  fear ,slavery, submissiveness , - toxic work culture .
Second cause - satisfaction - Macaulayen  education killed aspirations , quest and meeting challenges .
Third cause - lack of freedom , goals, vision ,long term approaches. 
While scientific approach ignores these three components of mediocrity , Hindu upanishads and darsanas emphasize them. By throwing the sanatana dharma out , we went back to mediocrity.
It means
We don't believe in ourselves.
We look for excuses and peripheral reasons . Most think money is the main reason for reaching top levels. Many think reading books will take us up. Many say attending courses will be enough. Sorry those won't work. 
We are not free
We are ignorant of our capacity 
We are afraid to come out of our cocoons. We won't break our cocoons. We hang on to our beliefs strongly. 

So the change involves awareness, understanding, maturity ,wisdom and  mindset changes , not an easy task. 

If you ask a secondary question -
Who or what  is  responsible for our not moving to the sixth stage of humanism ? 
The factors are
Lack of awareness.
Fixed mindset.
Satisfaction 
Poor ,non holistic, specialisation based education system ignoring the qualities required to go to the sixth level. 
Developmental mechanisms to help us to reach the sixth level are not clear or straight forward. We stopped contemplating and self learning. 
Our early gurukula system aimed at a holistic person. It helped in building leaders. Our vedas and bhagavad Gita emphasized on harmonious,  orderly , sharing living . They emphasized on mind development. 
Let me look at this in a different way.
What should be the food for a person?
Our nutritionists and we also look at food from our body and energy perspective. But as Tiruvalluvar said 
When there is no food for the mind , we get food for the body.
So food is essential for mind development. We need to take it everyday. 
What are the ingredients of mental food?
Pranayama, meditation, yoga, contemplation, prayer, observations, cognitive processes, thinking, analysing, reading, writing , helping the society, inventing,  deep work etc. 
This means we need a total revamp of our education . While it has components for physical and materialistic developments, we need equal or greater  weightage for mental development. It is a new paradigm ,new way of thinking , a new way of working , a highly open questioning mindset - don't accept anything, question, and find new truths, new realities. 
It does not happen by reading a book or attending a course.
It does not happen by talking about it .
It does not happen by talking about it. 
It is not part of any existing curriculum like human psychology. 
Then how do we go about getting higher consciousness?
Let us see in the next post.

08/09/25, 5:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 830
Going to the sixth level is a highly complex problem. You need to break many things and build a whole new reality - and new knowledge system, a new mindset. So I consider it an aspirational level - a level every human being should aim to get into in one’s lifetime . As our vedas say , it needs a fire inside every human - a fire to know what is not known, an eternal quest to unfold the reality and move away from maya ,the illusion. 
Let me pose the problem as a set of assertions. 
Assertion 1. Accept that we live in a world of maya - illusions. Many beliefs may be wrong. We need to break them.
Assertion 2. We use a one dimensional approach to problem solving . That is taught to us by science. But we decide in our life by looking at many factors.That doesn't fit into reality . It is Maya again. 
Assertion 3. We are happy to learn a small bit of  what is known - known facts about us and the world. Even there ,our cognition is poor. We accept a lot.  We live in a comfort zone - a nice cocoon . Break It. Look for unknowns . Then you move to the future, reducing surprises.
Assertion 4. We assume the world and activities are deterministic.  They are not. They are random. Change your approaches. 
Assertion 5. It is a life long regular practice. 
Assertion 6 . It involves involvement, dedication , belief,open mind and hard work.
Assertion 7. It is multidimensional.  There is no one panacea. 
Assertion 8. It involves knowing oneself , defining life goals. building self learning, getting knowledge from many sources, discrimination, building methods of finding truth from many statements and deep thinking plus an urge to improve , move forward , be future ready ,and lead teams. Our upanishads emphasize this aspect a lot. 
Assertion 9. Our energy level, enthusiasm , and motivation are low. We have a strong negative mindset. Be positive. 

The first prerequisite action needed to move up the ladder:
We should have rigorous training from childhood . The training is not theory , but rigorous ,daily practice.  The training should do the following:
1.Calm the mind 
2. Contemplate ,analyse and assimilate all what is learnt. 
3. Create a regular practice of getting information from various sources ,understanding the 360 degree views of that information,  converting them into knowledge , assessing them and applying them. 
4.Cultivate regular practices to review our ideas and beliefs and break the cocoon , make us look out and look at the future . Don't accept any fact before a detailed analysis. Accepting every fact received by us makes us morons. 
5.Create curiosity and critical thinking practices. Know that you are the search engine created by god - not google- and use those powers and keep on searching. 
6.Improve cognitive abilities. 
7. Become a thinker ,not an automated machine ,not a slave. 
8.Increase the self learning processes
9 .Increase the levels of consciousness. Curiosity, eternal quest, and meditation aid  in this. 
It means that we need a separate gurukula system teaching these in the mornings and practicing the principles and doing our regular education in the afternoon. It is also needed to absorb scientific principles and go forward in science particularly abstract science and deep space dynamics. We should move away from knowns to unknowns , break the existing rules, laws , simulate the universe, go beyond mathematics or create a new mathematics ,combine many solution methods, find ways to control and guide AI. 
A tall order. That is why I said it is mental food. Just as we need food to eat everyday, we need this mental food several times every day. We get the mental food and use it before every action, every thought, every speech, every listening and every learning.
As I said, the sixth level is aspirational. So at least we should have these practices done regularly as a minimum.
Get information by reading books, listening to talks, discussing with others, assimilate the information by converting it into knowledge. This is a lifelong practice. This is also a self learning practice. This will increase your confidence level. 
Question everything. Be a good search engine.
Define a purpose in life
Review your beliefs regularly. Try to change. 
Have an open, positive mind 
Think always . Don't shut your mind to the world. 
Accept challenges. Don't run away from them . That is the problem of living in a cocoon. 

In short 
Know thyself
Know thy job, people, students, organization .
Know thy world , society, environment 
Know the future . 
Keep learning always.

We saw the people related aspects so far. Unless an institution gets a reasonable number of  faculty  aspiring to go to the higher consciousness level or do its maximum to convert most to move up - that is difficult, needs a set of a critical mass of motivated faculty - , it can't even move from a satisfactory to good level, leave alone moving to a great level. 
As the work culture is concerned, some institutions have a reasonably good intention to improve the quality. If faculty cooperate, they can move up. 
Whatever is said in the previous posts applies to most organizations, not just educational institutions only . As I have written before, I count a handful of leaders in our country whom I know personally and who moved up and built their organization without political clout. Let me list a few names. 
Prof CV Raman - determination, focus, deep research, deep thinking, commitment, clarity,
Prof MS Thacker - vision, holistic thinking, determination, grand and ambitious implementation, setting up a new department of power engineering . 
Dr Homi Bhabha - visionary , predicted future trends, built a holistic system prepared expertise
Dr Sarabhai - visionary, assembled a team, not a good implementor 
Prof Dhawan - participatory governance, futuristic, visionary, reforms orientation, holistic thinking
Prof R Narasimha - great thinker, holistic thinker, analytical mind, clarity.
Dr A Ramachandran - dedication, commitment, built people , changed IIT Madras. 
And many more.
Let me return to the next and equally important reason for mediocrity - toxic work culture. 
Now let me start with what is an ideal work culture where a thousand flowers bloom - lots of innovations happen. 
Some qualities are 
1.peaceful ,work friendly campus 
2.motivated , sincere, serious , thinking faculty 
3. Keen interest in learning and research
4. Support to open minds, thinking and hard working persons, freedom to work without interference - it shouldn't be interpreted as freedom to politicalise the campus , harmonious living , 
5. Flexible ,transparent and adaptive rules facilitating the ambience- good governance. 
6. Fairness in actions 
7. Freedom from fear. 
8.Facilities to work
Who are responsible for maintaining a good academic ambience on campus?
Both management and faculty. 
Both have important roles , Both play complementary roles. The principal is a good link and should be a good leader. Toxicity and management dominance comes when the principal forgets academics and goes with the management instead of educating the management. 
The biggest problem is low confidence level leading to a negative outlook. I saw how Prof Holla, Prof Prahlada Rao transformed RVCE and MSRIT. I saw Prof Viswanath building up a good work culture at Gogte and Prof KA Krishnamurthy trying his best to improve institutions like MCE and SSIT. I saw Prof Nityanandan building research culture at TCE Madurai and Abhaykumar working on good governance. I saw REC Trichy destroyed by a total lack of synergy between principals and faculty for decades. I saw Prof MNC bringing in good academic values into SIT. I saw Prof Alladi Prabhakar struggling to improve MIT Manipal and building a research culture. All these and many others changed the mindset of the management. 
I saw potential for good growth in several colleges ,but due to the leadership vacuum and lack of synergy , those institutions didn't move up. 
While some institutions like VIT, SRM, Crescent, Galgotia, Nursee Monjee, Symbiosis,  Amrita, grew faster, we don't have any in Karnataka with a vision to pursue growth, research culture and excellence. PESIT tried but didn't move up. 
Principals are non-entities in several colleges, highly submissive persons.
That brings to a close our look at institutional mediocrity.

10/09/25, 4:45 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 832
Prof Vasudev Parvati has a query
-A Teacher's day special query sir, something I have always wondered:
Will apriori training (as an eligibility condition) in pedagogy and adolescent psychology help to make better teachers in higher (college and university) education?”
My response 
It is doubtful that training in human psychology will make a teacher a better one. 
What is required are a few important principles to become a better teacher. It is not enough to know the principles, but we have to follow them. 
The principles are
1.Understand what are  the functions , roles and responsibilities of a teacher ? This is left as a vague topic or mistakenly understood as repeating what is in a prescribed text  book . That is the tragedy. 
A teacher's job is to know a student ,  mold a student , to make him or her inculcate strong habits to read, contemplate, think , assimilate , apply and improve concepts. That is to make the students get good cognition capabilities. 
A student should follow a teacher. It is not a knowledge transferring activity. 
Is teaching only a classroom activity? Unfortunately ,it is made into a mechanical activity, not even an active aspect. Experimentation, discussions, reading , thinking ,practices are ignored. 
This tells us that cognition or understanding in a holistic way is more important and that is the first objective of a teacher - pass on an understanding of the world or reality as clearly as possible, as correctly as possible , as rigorous as possible,  as holistically as possible , as deep as possible and as futuristic as possible using several methods of learning . To me that is a good starting point for a teacher. I consider the above as a very important and impactful statement and I followed it as far as possible. I added one more aspect - as tough as possible , no kids glove please. Take students to their limits. So psychology is secondary here, cognition is primary. 
Ours is a country with more than 3000 years of history of thousands of gurus starting with  vedic rishis, Vyasa, Yajnavalkya, Panini,  Janaka, Aryabhatta, Bhaskara, Madhava to Shankara to Ramanuja to Madhwa and onto many  modern gurus. Most invented new sastras and interpreted old scriptures 
We remember Dr S Radhakrishnan as a teacher on September 5. Why ? Because he provided a new, complete, understandable , clear and scholarly picture of Indian philosophy. No one has done a similar thing so far. Another great person I cherish is C V Raman. He explained the difficult concepts of physics in simple terms . He explained color with hundreds of sarees. He explained light scattering with diamonds. He explained sound with many musical  instruments . He gave in depth concepts to a topic. The one difference between both is that one is a scholar , another is a researcher and teacher. 
When I visited a college under TEQIP, I was exposed to two major problems in the first visit itself. 
The first was a mindset leading to low confidence level and diffidence - the belief that we are a rural area , not like Bangalore and so we can't do anything. My job was to break this belief, not easy.
The Second was language or lack of it.
The faculty complained that students don't understand English. Students complained that faculty don't know English. So language is the big vehicle for understanding. 
Let us look at cognition next.
Welcome comments.

11/09/25, 5:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 833
Let us ask : What are the steps needed to improve cognition of students?
Go for the whole before discussing parts. Build a universe of your course and its relationships with other areas.
For example,  I read the poem  BRAHMA by Emerson . It is a deep poem . I realised that it is difficult to understand it for foreigners as they don't understand the total context and circumstances. People don't know gods, sacred seven mentioned in the poem. They don't understand Brahman.  To Emerson,  contradictory terms like slayer and slain, shadow and sunlight,  shame and fame,doubter and doubt etc are no different. But what about a reader , how will he understand the similarity in these contradictory terms - a subtle aspect ? 
So a reader should get an introduction to absolute reality and maya ,their differences and the concepts of nirguna and saguna Brahman.  Then only he or she will appreciate the essence of this poem. 
So cognition comes from a whole and not a part. Most teachers teach a part forgetting the whole. Concentrate on connections and relationships. 
The same poem uses examples to prove a point. Create examples, analogies ,anecdotes, practical happenings, to explain many concepts. I always talk about many practical occurrences for me. I even discuss current political aspects to stress a point. Sometimes ,jokes help. When I used to teach programming, I emphasized the importance of sequence and logic with a simple joke. 
A philosopher goes for a party and both his neighbours were women. He was shy and took advice from his friends - what to do? His friend suggested that it is simple. Ask two questions and the women will take over . The questions are - are you married? How many children you have? He asked the first woman - are you married? She said “no”. He asked the second question - . She was upset and angry and turned away. 
He changed the order and asked the woman on the other side - how many children you have? She replied - two. He asked her - are you married?. She got angry. 
This explains importance of logic and sequencing and stays with students. 
The next important step to improve cognition is to remember and not forget. Here our students excel. Memorizing is good. Taking notes is an important necessity for not forgetting the contents of a talk or lecture. I recall an incident here. I was invited to give a talk to the faculty of an engineering college in Bangalore. I was closely associated with this college. While I was lecturing , I observed that most faculty were just sitting . I looked at the secretary of the college, a great friend of mine. He got scribbling pads and pen for the faculty members. Still they didn't write any notes. What was the use of my talk? It reminded me of an old proverb - it was like irrigating a barren and  uncultivable  land . It is of no use. 
Also the teacher should know that he or she should pause while discussing a difficult topic . He or she should repeat complex topics again and again. 
Thinking, Contemplation , abstraction, learnings, - give references to what you are teaching in each lecture, give reading assignments , ask questions to stimulate thinking. Speak slowly so as to give time for assimilation . Ask the students to write a half para of their learnings . Ask them to suggest applications.

12/09/25, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 834
We are discussing the basics of improving teaching and teachers. We saw what the role of a teacher has. Then we started looking at the main objective of teaching - cognition. We identified four factors in the previous post. Let us look at more factors on improving cognition of students.

Computer science and management are more conceptualization oriented courses , not mathematical ones. Engineering used to be a conceptual course in the first half of the 20th century. But it started becoming more mathematical in the second half and we started ignoring the conceptual and practice part. Still civil engineering retains quite a good percentage of concepts. AI today has more conceptual part. Cognition for analytics and concepts are different. In mathematical or science oriented topics , we establish a firm foundation of rules, laws, representation by symbols and operations , procedures , processes and methodologies and use these to plough into the topics. Most of these are well defined, deterministic . So cognition is easier. That is not the case with teaching in areas like philosophy , religion , management , IT services ,AI areas. We normally have no universal rules or laws . There are multiple views , multiple approaches , multiple solutions, and it  is difficult to get clarity. Confusion prevails. So cognition here needs a different approach - detailed explanations, multiple views, their advantages, approaches , their relationships , their impacts etc. Cognition needs a discovery approach here , not an analytical approach. One size fits all works for science and mathematics ,but not to these conceptual topics and disciplines. Engineering has a lot of concepts. Our teaching methods and practices  ignore them ,and concentrate on analysis. That is a tragedy. Going with pure mathematical approaches have reduced an effective and holistic understanding of various concepts and their impacts on architecture, system integration, design, trouble shooting , diagnostics, sizing, performance, resilience and operations. So a good understanding is required on the balance between analytical and physical ideas and approaches. 
The main problem with science is it is one dimensional,  but the problems with engineering and management
 are :
They deal with multidimensional problems.
They deal with uncertainties.
They deal with multiple uses and users. 
They have multiple components. 
They deal with very large and complex problems. 
They go beyond borders- multidisciplinary.
It is a basic necessity that a teacher should spend enough time to think about the subject he or she will be teaching and create a list of topics to be taught, define their characteristics like analytical or descriptive, easy or difficult to grasp, etc, build a cognition plan accordingly and prepare teaching and reading materials.
I had a lot of difficulties in the early years of teaching . I was teaching computer science from the sixties onwards. It was evolving and changing with high speed. Text books were not available. It was a concepts oriented discipline , not mature. It wasn't easy for me to teach. I had to go on discovery. Discovery meant hours of thinking. Take the term - process. I needed to explain this to students. There is a cryptic definition - it is an instance of a program. I spent days to come up with a good set of explanations. This means that a teacher should think intensely and deeply and accept challenges,  not running away from them. It took me several years to understand entropy.  Even terms like isothermal and adiabatic processes were not explained properly and without applications . The problem here is that every teacher should ask three questions to explain a topic - why? What? and How?.

13/09/25, 4:15 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 835
“If you can dream—and not make dreams your master; 
If you can think—and not make thoughts your aim;
 If you can meet with Triumph and Disaster And treat those two impostors just the same; 
If you can bear to hear the truth you’ve spoken Twisted by knaves to make a trap for fools, Or watch the things you gave your life to, broken, And stoop and build ’em up with worn-out tools:”
If by Rudyard Kipling 

Connecting the dots, finding applications, impacts, future developments etc. Solve problems. Write applications, design,  build devices, look for pros and cons . 
Provide clarity by discrimination - viveka. I started this by discriminating mathematics symbols. We use” = “ symbol for  two separate aspects - equality - a condition and an assignment - calculation symbol. ALGOL differentiated these two symbols . Fortran used = for assignment and .EQ. for equality. There are several such cases like differences between programs, processes, and threads ; hub, switch and router etc. There is confusion between concurrency and parallel processes. 
The second aspect of clarity is to explain definitions. Most definitions are cryptic and need expansion. Socretes and Plato emphasized on definitions . Yajnavalkya gives a long definition of Brahman in Brihadaranyaka upanishad.  
“I learned very early the difference between knowing the name of something and knowing something.”
Richard P. Feynman

The next aspect is to discuss different stages of an activity. We have several terms like goals, roles, strategies, processes, procedures, architecture , models, designs, methodologies, etc and these need clarity. 
The next aspect is to build clarity in yourself first before passing on the concepts. This can be achieved through readings, contemplation, questioning , finding answers, discussing with others, posing in quora,  deep thinking etc. 
Seeing is believing . Cognition improves by projecting videos, not by talking. Perception is multisensual- seeing, hearing, reading, experimenting , analysing etc. Perception is the important step to Cognition. 
Avoid biases. We have created an approximated and unitary way of looking at problems ,but unfortunately our systems have multiple views, uncertainties, many options, and many solutions in reality. We avoid practices of engineering , industry practices etc. Due to practicalities, medicine assumes most humans as one category and treats them. With data and AI , multiple possibilities, multiple models and experiments may lead towards individualized treatments. We need to unlearn the old axioms and learn new axioms. 
Engineering should look for creating a  detailed model for our systems rather than an approximate unitary model. Jay Forrester wrote in 1960 and 1970 books on systemic models, world Dynamics giving an integrated view of engineering , economy, population  and development . That gives a holistic picture . 

Innovate.
   Let me conclude with Sankara's explanation  of qualities of a teacher: 
“Knowledge, should be explained again and again until it is firmly grasped”
“When the teacher finds from signs that know-
ledge has not been grasped (or has been wrongly grasped) by the disciple he should remove the causes of non-comprehension.”
“The teacher is one who is endowed with the 
power of furnishing arguments pro and con, of understanding questions  and remembering them, who possesses tranquillity, self-control, compassion and a desire to help others, who is versed  in the scriptures and unattached to enjoyments both seen and unseen.  who 
has renounced the means to all kinds of actions, is a knower of Brahman and established in It, is never a transgressor of the rules of conduct, and who is devoid of 
shortcomings such as ostentation, pride, deceit, cunning, jugglery, jealousy, falsehood, egotism and attachment. 
He has the sole aim of helping others and a desire to impart the knowledge .”
Upadesa sahasri- Sankaracharya

14/09/25, 4:20 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 836
We saw the role of a teacher first and then how the main task of making the students understand as a part of learning. 
Now let me move to the third aspect - how do you excite the students?
I have talked to thousands of teachers across the colleges and IIMs NITs and IITs in addition to IISc - a lot of my discussions were continuous over a period of time - a wide diverse group of seniors and young lecturers , some in private conversations and many in groups and belonging to many engineering disciplines and on a diverse range of topics - education, pedagogy, students, delivery, explanations, lab work, ambience, curriculum, ethics, roles and responsibilities , motivation, long term learning, habits , institution improvements - how to go from standard to good to great, improvements etc in addition to many topics in energy, environment and many areas of computer science. 
Similarly , I have talked to tens of thousands of students of different ages - high school students , undergraduates , post graduates and practising engineers. 
So I have a large sample size with me, the only drawback is that it is mostly restricted to the south with a few institutions in the west ,north and east . I lectured at  both normal colleges and elite institutions. I have taught and lectured outside India also. So my observations are based on my discussions, my observations and my learnings from these encounters.
While there are many bright people - I should assert that most faculty  start their career with a lot of enthusiasm and energy , it dissipates very fast in their perception of and influence by their college and university environment and soon they lose their confidence and hope . Fortunately ,quite a good but small percentage are determined and  feel that they should do something to improve themselves, their students and the education system. But they lack support , lose their directions and couldn't convert their enthusiasm into actions. 
So my question is 
How do such people build a better ,satisfying professional career in such a distressing atmosphere? 
The first requirement is to understand the environment one lives in.
Let me quote a saying quoted by Sir M Viseswaraya about Indians- not a complement but there is some truth in it and all our organizations have people of this type.
“Joseph Chailley says: “If India possessed a more fertile soil and were better endowed with mineral wealth, she would still languish in poverty if the natives continued to work with the same apathetic indifference as at present. Slackness is the worst curse of the country. At first sight, everybody seems to be taking an active part in some common toil; as a matter of fact, several persons are looking on at the labour of one. As has been cynically remarked, out of five people who seem to be working, one is doing nothing, one is resting, one is looking on and another is helping the previous three. Everyone endeavours to escape his full toll of toil.”
— Mokshagundam Visvesvaraya” 
Quoting a disappointing foreign criticism of Indian culture in his Address to Mysore Civil Engineers Association (14 Nov 1910). 
A country with a glorious and long tradition of great gurus like Yajnavalkya,to Sankara and more than 90000 gurulukas running till the end of 19th century gets this comment. So one thing is clear from this
We have great scholars. We continue to produce great scholars . But we have a large unmotivated ,negative minded population and the country does most activities inefficiently. We need to deal with this dichotomy. 
I have seen this situation in most government offices - there will be one or two very sincere , honest, and hardworking and helpful persons in almost every office. I know quite a few people . 
Mr RP Krishnamurthy was undersecretary to the chief minister and a very simple soul. He won't eat anything in the office. He was scrupulously honest running a high pressure office. I have seen several bank officers who were service oriented, committed to doing their job with intelligence. Let me give names of some persons with whom I worked - i am missing many names here-
Joshi, Suresh, Ramaswamy - indian bank
Kumar , Govinda bhat , Chandrasekar - corporation bank
Ravindranath, Atulkumar - syndicate bank
Kumar, Diwan, Sunil Mohan - PNB . 
Sometimes such people are trapped. I know the case of an honest subregistrar who was trapped and dismissed from service for being honest.
The second understanding is the strengths and weaknesses of our education system from the point of finding ways to identify challenges and look for solutions. Let us look at it in the next post.

 “ For those who have conquered the mind, it is their friend. For those who have failed to do so, the mind works like an enemy.”
Gita 6.6

Yesterday's post doesn't give a correct interpretation of my thinking. I concentrated mostly on teachers and advices are aimed at them. 
I have met thousands of faculty, thousands of people in governments, banks, industries, utilities, corporations, computer companies,  TCS, Integra microsystems , research labs , NGOs in addition to faculty members and lakhs of students. 
I had great interactions with most of them. My interactions are through lectures, discussions, lab visits, AICTE inspections and TEQIP visits and KSCST project visits as well as talks in several conferences . The interactions were useful. Most expressed themselves fearlessly to me. 
Most faculty were serious about doing something but couldn't do it. Our discussions here are aimed at  improving the mindset of our teaching faculty in colleges. Please remember that I cherish my association with all my coworkers, associates , friends and acquaintances. 


Let me return to the concerns of Prof Vasudev and Prof Karunakara on teaching .
As a first step in understanding our education system ,we need to get rid of many myths we generally believe in. 
The first myth is that our students are useless, not interested in learning . So there is no way we can improve education unless we get great students. This is not true. 
We should consider students as our assets and resources. Marks are no indication of their capabilities. I advised two students to shift from computer science to physics which was their interest and they hated programming. I strongly believe that we underestimate our students and underutilize them. We think they are burdens. 
I was talking to the new students last week. Their questions showed their understanding, their futuristic outlook and their keen interest in learning.  They asked me - what books to read? How do we know what will happen in the future? What are the developments expected? How do they prepare? Why did I choose computer science in the mid sixties when there were no facilities and it was evolving? etc. So students are smart . Respect their knowledge. 
The second greatest myth is belief in the exam system as an indicator of quality. If a student fails in an exam, we declare him useless. This is a mind block. We need to move away from exams, encourage thinking. Exams are great lifelong punishments meted out without proper justification. 
Sir M V quote

"To give real service, you must add something which cannot be bought or measured with money."
It is a great advice.

16/09/25, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 838

BG 6.8: The yogi who are satisfied by knowledge and discrimination, and have conquered their senses, remain undisturbed in all circumstances. They see everything—dirt, stones, and gold—as the same.
6.9: The yogis look upon all—well-wishers, friends, foes, the pious, and the sinners—with an impartial intellect. The yogi who is of equal intellect toward friend, companion, and foe, neutral among enemies and relatives, and unbiased between the righteous and sinful, is considered to be distinguished among humans.
Gita

Let us continue with our mindset and belief systems. These are common beliefs found across the state amongst a majority of teachers. 
The big myth ,the belief ingrained in most , is - “Teaching is a class room lecturing activity and it is one directional. No interruptions ,no questions. The objective is to cover the portions . If students don't learn, they are useless. “
We need to know that for a large number of students , learning is a multidimensional  activity, a serious activity mostly done outside a classroom involving hours of work  - listening, reading, seeing, solving problems, experimenting, seeing practices, generating doubts,  questioning, abstraction, thinking  and contemplation - all routes lead to understanding. This habit is not built into the students. 
Let me explain the above through an experience. 
When we started on the mahithisindhu program of 1000 high schools in Karnataka,  that program excited a large number of rural children. We designed the program with some features to help in learning and using computers - the focus was using the computer . So there were no separate class room , minimum talking by the instructors , most learning done on a computer - practice based. 
We prepared a time table with contents focused on applications. 
The instructors were taken from BE or MCA qualified young persons trained on the use of computers and not a trained teacher. 
We focused on group learning . 
We spent days building the contents of books for 8th, 9th and 10th standards after several rounds of discussions and revisions - fortunately the vendors were private companies and listened to me as their payments depended on my approval and so did all I needed to be done - prepared the new books. All students got these books in kannada . 
After such a lot of effort, we found students excited , read the book, attendance was 100% , and students hung around the school after the school was closed for the day. That motivated them in studies and the pass percentage in SSLC exams jumped by 5% every year. So a multiplier effect was also seen here. 

The next belief is - 
A text book is a Bible. What it says is truth and absolute truth. This is a rigid mindset. 
Unfortunately , this kills thinking. This declares non-believers and doubters as failures. But the biggest drawback is that  we  create frogs in the well students  - who live in that book's  world and don't know what is happening outside.

The next belief is - 
We look at the past ,teach the past , forget the future , and forget the Dynamics, changes and transformations happening in our society ,country and world. We have created a nice cocoon for us and our students. This kills creativity and innovation. 
I want to illustrate two incidents that happened to me in two NITs. 
I was talking to some faculty in one NIT. I asked ,” why is the syllabus and contents the same for 50 years?”. The response was , “ it is time tested and so good enough for us to teach.”
In the other NIT,  I was discussing the need to update the curriculum with current trends and future proof the students. Most senior professors didn't agree with me. Only a few younger faculty were interested in my opinion. 
There is a third incident - most colleges used to teach 8085 microprocessors for about  two decades , even after 8085 became obsolete and not used anywhere. Why? I got justifications that students should know evolution. But they don't teach complete evolution here , concentrating on one microprocessor. Some of my respected friends did this without thinking and defended this practice. 
Let me end this with Sir MV
“The Indian mind needs to be familarised with the principles of modern progress, a universal impulse for enquiry and enterprise awakened, and earnest thinking and effort promoted. A new type of Indian citizenship purposeful, progressive and self-respecting should be created, and self-reliant nationhood developed.
I walked my way to good health.
It is better to work out than rust out.
Even at the age of 102 he said this as quoted in Our Leaders. Children's Book Trust. 1989.”

Greetings
Dks
17/09/25, 5:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 839
Tolstoy
 “I divide men into two  lots.  They are free thinkers, or they are not free thinkers. …. Free thinkers are those who are willing to use their minds without prejudice and without fearing to understand things that clash with their own customs, privileges or beliefs.  This state of  mind is not common, but it is essential for right thinking; where it is  absent, discussion is apt to become  worse than useless.”
Let me conclude by looking at what should be done?
Faculty members need a continuous training , spent at least two weeks every year on teaching methods, digital methods and transformations,  AI, future technologies, human science, motivation etc. They need to learn continuously . No institution will gain even a satisfactory status without a dynamic faculty group. I am not saying all faculty should be research oriented , but all faculty should be motivated to improve their knowledge , their curiosity, their thinking skills, their working style. We need to build their confidence level - it is low now.
Faculty members should own responsibility to their students learning.  They should create interest in their students , make them think on impacts , future changes, alternate approaches. They should float discussion groups. First of all they should accept that they survive because of students, and the students come with a great new perspectives and can open the faculty's mind to a different world. So interact with them as equals. Remove superiority mindset. Understand them , encourage them and support them.
Faculty should spend an hour everyday reading a good sociotechnical book and analysing them . They should build a practice of writing critiques on books and papers and publish them as blogs. Blog writing is a good way to think and improve oneself. A faculty doesn't get a lot of time to open up and discuss their ideas in a classroom. Online  Discussion groups are one good option. Blogs will reach a larger audience.
Try to analyse your beliefs and change yourself slowly. The world is changing, technologies are changing , societies are changing , so look to the future ,not past. Self reviews as a weekly habit is a good one. Eagerness to improve is a good habit. Today I see a lot of excuse based mindset with many faculty . We should move from an excuse and defensive mindset to a positive ,can do, mindset. I observed on many occasions , there is a reluctance to listen to suggestions, ideas, criticisms, etc., leave alone accepting them and acting on them. We need to look at all acquaintances and students as friends ,not enemies. 
Let us build an inclusive mindset . Engineering is inclusive . So learn social sciences , behavioural sciences . A first level social interactions project opens our mind.

These need support from the management also. These can be done with diplomacy ,not opposition. Try to convince the management. Don't expect everything you want to do to be in a book in an orderly manner.  Innovate your life .

The importance of synergy and united approach.

A common purpose do I lay before you, and worship with your general oblation.”

“One and the same be your resolve, and be your minds of one accord.
United be the thoughts of all that all may happily agree.”

Let us move back  to the year 1971. A few changes came up in that year also. 
The major changes were
The installation and operations of the newly acquired IBM 360/44 computer. It was located in CISL and Dr Shamim was made in charge , a few programmers were appointed and some operators. It managed the teething problems like punch cards and key punch machines, not enough. Fortunately ,most students in several departments started using the system. Most students were not familiar with Fortran. So the programmers were supposed to help them , but that was not happening. Some got ready made programs . One important noticable aspect was the large number of students and faculty queuing up for submitting their card decks and get their printouts. 
By the next year, the work load increased at a very high growth rate ,and we had to introduce a separate manual  process for  scheduling CPU time for each user. Queues started building up . This led to some favoritism in booking and a lot of unhappiness. Most were not happy with the operations of the computer system. 
The second change was the operation of the school of automation with IGS. It had great impact on me. My relationships with IGS wasn't great. We just were courteous and our relationship was transactional. But we used to discuss a lot. He loved discussions on many topics of general interest . He loved to predict the growth of organizations, particularly the new department of electronics set up by Mrs Gandhi after the department of space. 
The first set of changes dealt with my involvement in organizing short term courses for industry persons. At Prof Dhawan's suggestion , myself and Prof Thathachar floated a course on optimization . It looked at optimization of bus transport for people of bangalore.  Bangalore had about 420 buses with BTS- the public transport service. It was inadequate for a growing city. Bangalore also had almost an equal number of buses with public sector companies like HAL, ITI, BEL,HMT and most research labs . Their utilization was poor . So my suggestion was to put to use the spare time of the buses of the companies on a win win situation. Prof Dhawan took this seriously and talked to the army general. But the egos of both sides didn't lead to a solution. So Bangalore suffered with a very poor public transport system and it is there even now. 
Like Delhi, Bangalore also had a large middle class population . So they moved to scooters and mopeds .

19/09/25, 4:25 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 841
Let me look at the methods I used to move into new areas. The computer scene was still primitive with poor facilities and little research work going on in India. 
I wanted to look at the performance of computer systems ,operating systems as well as general services. I wasn't keen on the generally accepted queuing theory models. They approximate a problem to a large extent. So I found an approach using Monte Carlo simulation. I used this for the next twenty plus years to study performance of many services including transactions in banks and distributed systems. 
I also built a simulator and a continuous system language called Cospro. Based on my work we floated a short term course on computer simulation. I started teaching a computer simulation course from the next year 1972 onwards. This course ran for the next 20 years like my optimization course and computer techniques course. I didn't teach this for many years. Later I taught this course again at IIT Bombay in 1978 79.
Another change that happened was my getting involved with the setting up of the power systems training institute. The ministry of power , government of India,  decided to set up a new training institute for power engineers throughout India and chose bangalore as the location. Mr Annavaradan , deputy director ,was posted to PSTI and came to bangalore and met me. I liked the idea as a teacher and helped in formulating a course plan. PSTI existed in paper - no building, no staff. 
Yet ,we decided to run a two week program and run in PE 303, in our department. I talked to Prof BNN Iyengar and convinced him to permit PSTI to run the program . He agreed. We didn't need approval from the registrar then. 
We framed the topics, identified experts - I took the major share of lectures - and started the new institute not with a building ,but with a course. Annavaradan  had to fight for months to get approval from the ministry for payments. One of the experts wrote nasty letters to him regarding honorarium. Anyway ,it started well. 
They found a house in palace orchards ,set up the institute and started running programs. Mr VR Narasimhan joined as the director . I was involved with this institute till 1985, more than 20 years. It was a rewarding experience teaching power engineers from various state electricity boards. Unfortunately ,Mr Annavaradan died of a sudden heart attack by 1980 or so. But the institute continued and I was involved with them . I became friends with many officers and directors. 
Greetings 
Dks
20/09/25, 4:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 842
I spent helping PSTI build into a good training institution. I had good relationships with all directors like Mr VR Narasimhan, Mr Anantharamiah, Mr MM Turabi and Mr NSR Rso and many deputy directors. 
After a few years of operating from a house in Palace orchards, they moved to subramaniapura after banasankari, quite a distance from my residence. It is at the other end of the city. Still traffic was manageable. PSTI sent a car to pick me and it took me from 30 to 45minutes to reach there, unlike nowadays. 
I met Mr SS Murthy several times. I also became involved with the southern regional electricity board because of SS Murthy.  He became member power systems in CEA. 
During this decade of 1970 to 80, I was involved with Datamatics also teaching programming and systems analysis and design.
 So it was a tight decade. I was riding multiple horses and the number was increasing . I was involved with both EE and automation at the academic level, got involved with electrical  distribution system designs for Dhanbad and Delhi, was active with the curriculum committee and power committee, and later got involved with ASTRA. I also had two outside assignments during this decade - first at the University of Manchester during 1974- 75, and IIT, Bombay during 1978 and 1979. I also visited the US for  two workshops in 1977. I also got married and had two great children born in 1975 and 1976 December. I bought a house site in the west of chord road layout in 1976. 
I moved to a rented house in Malleswaram after promotion.So a very hectic period, a very productive period for teaching most computer courses, conducting research in many areas and guiding students. I also got my promotion by the beginning of 1973. 
 From 1972, I started teaching mostly computer courses like data structures, systems programming and operating systems . 
This period,  I lost a  second friend Mr Annavaradan  , the first being Mr. Chandrasekhara of Datamatics,  with whom I  had an association and friendship for more than 10 years. 

Mr Chandrasekhara helped in furnishing my house. He got me cots, steel almirah, wooden   cupboards etc. The cots and almirah are still in use in my present house.

As usual I attended several conferences in 1972 and visited TIFR for computer work and attended workshops. I visited Shillong for the CBIP research conference in June 1972 along with both Prof BNN Iyengar and Prof HV Gopalakrishna . We stayed together in one room . Prof Gopalakrishna brought a heavy suitcase. He carried many things including an electric portable iron. 
I went by train to Calcutta from Bangalore via Madras - there were no direct trains from Bangalore to Delhi, Bombay ,Calcutta and also Madurai at that time. Bangalore was a neglected city till the end of this decade.Train connections are poor. All my travels were difficult , including reservations. 
 I went to Madras and stayed with my parents there in Ellaiamman colony and then went to Calcutta.  I stayed with my younger uncle Sankaran . Then I took a flight to Gauhati and a taxi to Shillong.
Unfortunately , I couldn't eat any food there. I had a severe headache and vomiting due to the bumpy road travel  to Shillong and also local travels to nearby hills. I ate only ice cream. A few people asked me,” you say you are unwell, but are eating icecream. Is it Ok? “ I replied  that I was allergic to mustard oil smell and sensed it even in the tea. It led to vomiting.  I visited a doctor and got a tablet. 
But professionally ,it was a good conference, my talks went on well. I chaired a session ,was a reporter for another session,  and attended some working group meetings. 
I visited a power plant and several places. Shillong was a good hill station.  It was well laid out, the hotel had big rooms and that is why three of us stayed in a room. The customs were British.  Missionaries were active and most people were christians and spoke English.  Fortunately the rains were mild then. 
While returning from Gauhati to Calcutta , there was a taxi strike at Calcutta and it was with difficulty that I managed to reach the city office of the airlines and took a rickshaw to my uncle's place in south Calcutta. I was told that my mother's uncle, my maternal grandfather's younger brother, had come to Calcutta and would be coming there to see me. He was a very old person and so I was overwhelmed by his affection for me.  I left for Bangalore the next day. 
Let me look at the happenings at IISc that affected me.

22/09/25, 4:57 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 844
The year we are looking at is still 1972. 
The first thing I want to talk about is the need to get a good number of quality faculty in an institution and Prof. Dhawan's  role in it for IISc. 
Prof Dhawan decided to increase the faculty size by a high percentage - almost doubling the number and went through  many approaches. It was at a time when the finances were tight and budgeting allocation for new faculty members is a Herculean task. His close relationship with UGC and its chairman helped to some extent. I admired his confidence and his efforts to take people along with him and his proposals.  It was a remarkable experience for me.
 I haven't seen any directors of not only IISc but also other institutions who realised that an institution’s strength is in its faculty and devised several innovative schemes till date. Most played safe with rules and excuses.  It is a strong statement. 
He introduced the five year review and promotions scheme in 1967  and built a number of mechanisms and structures to make it an open and fair process and to reward merit . The objective was not rewarding merit . The five year review made people think and helped them bring out their merits in research, teaching, projects , new areas and social commitment. Most realised they needed to provide a great review at the end of five years. It makes the faculty bring out their best in a positive way , no petty discussions and jealousies.  It is still in practice, an excellent motivating system. 
Then he went abroad to get talents at senior levels in 1969-70 from many universities based on global advertisements.  That was the start for IISc for global search - a very useful mechanism for getting good talent from anywhere. He brought many professors from many foreign universities and many from India. This effort added about 50 senior faculty and changed the thinking process of IISc.  We got multiple views of many institutions and universities as experienced by many newly joined faculty. This took us away from the “ frogs in a well “ mindset. It led to a lot of discussions about admissions, faculty selections, faculty expectations, research , new areas , social commitments and many aspects of academic work. Our networking with many reputed institutions increased by many times. 
I realised the importance of diversity of views on many aspects of culture, organization , governance , and approaches from childhood. I observed the functioning of my primary school, conduct of temple festivals, functioning of the high school, colleges, Easun group , IISc,  and TIFR at that time. I also played a leading role in building PSTI and training programs at Datamatics,  kirloskar , PSTI etc. It gave me a broad vision and outlook and helped me in improving systems.  What helped in developing a broad view in addition to keen observations, and analysis of the activities in several places is my voracious reading habits throughout and the learnings I got from them. I will discuss about reading in detail later

The third effort was the removal of a fixed quota of faculty allocations for each department in a phased manner that was a norm set by UGC and followed by IISc. So he started conducting selection meetings at the divisional level, a departure from the practice of selection for each department on the basis of vacancies. That benefited many of us . 

There was a selection committee set up for assistant professors in all departments. That was a major discussion point for most of us. Some were worried. In the end ,some,  who weren't promoted , were upset and unhappy. I applied along with several of  my colleagues. 
Prof Narasimhan from TIFR was the only  expert in computer science. It was a joint committee for four departments - electrical , communications, automation and high voltage. All professors of these departments were in the committee. My interview lasted for nearly an hour and Prof Dhawan supported me. I  was selected for the school of automation . I also got a higher start - maximum permissible three increments. Most of us from the department got promoted . 
So I moved from electrical engineering  to the school of  automation full time, a big shift . It was a difficult decision - to move from a friendly unit to a non friendly unit. But two reasons pushed me to join the school. First, it is a promotion. If I reject it, I may have to wait for a year to get my  promotion in the EE department. Second, the scope of SA aligned with my goal of computer science,as CS is a prominent area of SA. So I moved to the school.  But,  I agreed to my commitment to teach EE courses and was involved in EE activities for the next five years. It was a major shift ,not an easy shift and I had to do a tough balancing act between the EE department chaired by Prof Joseph and automation chaired by Prof IGS , who had  a non cordial relationship to EE. 
The movement was a good decision and I got more opportunities to diversify into two other major areas and proved my strength against adversity. 
Greetings

Today is the start of Sharad navarathri. 
There are four navarathris in a year. This is dedicated to three devis - Durga, Lakshmi and Saraswati. 
The belief is we come out of all our troubles, remove the evil in us , and lead a new pious happy and dharmic life with health, wealth and general prosperity. 
This year is special. Navarathri is celebrated for 10 days. 
Navarathri is special for Mysore ,celebrated with a lot of pomp and piety ,a social festival.
