23/09/25, 3:41 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 845

Let me take a break and look at two major concerns facing us, making many worry about their impact on India. 
Let me analyze both one by one. 
The first one is the uprising of youngsters in three neighbouring countries - Sri Lanka, Bangladesh and now Nepal. The question is 
Will it happen in India also?
My answer is 
It may not. The chances are low. Why?
There are many reasons. 
We are a diverse nation . Mobilizing the entire country happened only twice in the past two centuries - during the independence movement  and anti emergency revolt through ballot boxes. Both times people, not just youth but all were emotionally involved about injustice. People went against Indira Gandhi not because of the loss of democracy but because of random arrests and attacks on innocent people. People didn't tolerate injustice and disturbance to their normal life. Both times powerful leadership evolved and guided the people. Such a situation doesn't exist now. 
India is a highly resilient country. The people will adjust, innovate ,suffer and hope for better days. The faith and hope were great. Our people love challenges. We believe and  accept happenings as fate. Both opposites coexist . 
Our governments and governance ensure fairness. We have a strong judiciary to oversee our governments and ensure the proper and just handling of various public related  activities. There are many independent systems , many diverse groups ready to correct the wrongs. 
Our democracy is not weak . Our opposition is strong and agitates at every step. Youth is involved in the democratic processes. We have a stable political system. We have independent leaders in many states and corrective action is fast.
We have many systems in place , have a vibrant digital lifestyle ,and a large number of smart people with opposing views. We may be physically poor ,but mentally rich. We are a stable country ,an active community with strong religious connect and diverse beliefs . Tolerance level is reasonable. 
Still we can't deny the fact that there is unhappiness and we don't have an equal ,just and ideal country . But that situation is still a simmering one hasn't reached a critical stage. Governments do act on them to some extent. People's voice is strong and heard and not suppressed . 
The most important reason is the economic revival and food subsidies. Our governments are concerned with the welfare of the people. We have produced many welfare schemes. The rural employment guarantee program is a good example . The direct benefit transfer is a big boon. Distribution of food grains in a distributed and effective manner has reduced physical hunger. All parties play a big role in hunger alleviation.  Covid vaccination reached all - a massive operation. Concern for people is evident and perceived by common people. So the anger of youth is much less. Digitalization also helped people to move forward. 

So chances of such uprisings now  are poor. They need a lot of effort ,coordination and synergy. We went through a lot of such uprisings in the northeast, the naxal movement in many forest areas , the working of deep states , religious fights , but weathered them well . The situation is much better now even in Kashmir . So we can relax from fear but not from affirmative actions. Politicians are sitting on the edge for the next election ,not sure how people will vote. 
But it may happen if we have a brutal government.  As Krishna said in the Gita that he will appear when there is chaos and injustice in the world.  Let us hope that  a great leader will emerge when that happens. 
Greetings
Dks
24/09/25, 4:15 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 846

The second situation is the Trump effect - tariffs and a high charge for H1b visa and their impacts on India. Lots of people are worried. Many think IT will bear the brunt of this attack and so onshoring of work will come down. 
The question is 
Will this high charge impact India ? Will it be high or specific sectors only ? 
The answer is 
It will impact for a short time only. We will recover . The impact on tariff will be more pronounced and affect many sectors like textiles, pharma , etc. 
How is the impact of H1b charges  ? It will be marginal. 
Nehru realised the need to strengthen our capabilities. So he talked about self sufficiency , local manufacturing and import substitution. But he closed the country to new developments in technology , and also the private sector. His mistake was the licence and permit Raj. A liberal policy was needed. We are seeing it now. 
The number of semiconductor units being set up in India is a great boost to us. Defence, railways, tractors, earth moving equipment,  electric vehicles are increasing our capacity for production of high quality systems with the latest technologies.  That will take us forward. We are way ahead in digital and AI.
This is not the first time the US is arm twisting India. It was the worst during Indira Gandhi's tenure and even part of Vajpayee's time. 
I recall that we were not allowed to import supercomputers for IISc . The government set up CDAC to build supercomputers. NAL and DRDO built parallel computers with gigaflops. Param came out of CDAC. Later TCS also started supercomputer work.  So,  it was a blessing. 
Similarly ,the US prevented sale of GSLV technology to ISRO and even prevented the USSR from  transferring the technology to India. Again we went into it seriously and developed our own GSLV engines and rockets. PSLV could send a satellite to Mars. So again it was useful. 
Even in the late nineties ,ISRO and defence could not buy oracle database. We used Sybase for ISRO work. So obstructions are common. 
All defence and research organizations suffered with US export restrictions. Prof Deekshatulu wanted a transistor to be replaced in a device in his government research organization, NRSA. I had to go to some small vendors to get it. Our IISc chemistry departments struggled to import  chemicals from the US. 
Indians love challenges and jugaad. So I am sure that we will go full steam on atmanirbharta. Already defence production has gone up by several levels. There are a lot of activities in AI and quantum computing going on and even 15 year old school students are excited and working on AI agents .  There are trillions and trillions of code to be redone and we need new AI architects, designers,  integrators and engineers. 
We took the pole position in IT services for more than 25 years. No country is near us in IT projects and operations. Already most engineering colleges have started AI and data specializations and so soon we will have lakhs of AI known people available. So no worry. I don't see the  American universities reaching our level of talent development. 
It is the enthusiasm of our youth to explore new areas that will push us forward. 
So such actions are common for the US.
The major impact of H1b visa charges will be felt by the IT sector and it will be temporary. We will bounce back. 
 IT will move from onshoring to offshoring. Already for the past five plus years ,IT companies are setting up spaceports in many parts to strengthen communications . Also we moved from GDC to GCC. There are already thousands of GCCs operating in Bangalore alone. So more work will shift to India. 
So we should go full steam on modern technologies . Quantum and AI will open up thousands of new applications. 
That sums up the impact on H1b visas. 
Actually the fundamental question of brain drain remains - should we educate and create talent pools at considerable cost and they migrate to the US with no or marginal benefits to the country. At least  those who had gone to the Middle East had sent back considerable quanta of foreign exchange and they are mostly untrained or marginally trained persons.  We give a lot of emphasis to H1b and Indian diaspora in the US and nothing about our diaspora in Fiji, Guyana , Singapore, the Middle East and other countries. We need to reflect on this .
Greetings
Dks
25/09/25, 4:34 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 846
Let me return to my IISc saga .

Mrs Indira Gandhi set up several new science departments. Both the department of science and technology and the  department of space were  set up in 1971
Dr A.Ramachandran who was then the director of IIT Madras,  became the secretary of DST. Prof Dhawan organized a one day brainstorming session on future research areas to help DST to look forward . Dr Ramachandran participated in the morning session.  More than 150 IISc faculty attended . I also attended and talked about computer linguistics and AI in that meeting. None except Prof Dhawan showed interest. But what was striking was the fact that there was no futuristic area being proposed there or came up for discussions. There was a total lack of futuristic outlook and imagination. Instead of discussing futuristic science, discussions moved to what area we should work on for the next ten plus years. The meeting was a very useful one for most of us to know the pulse of research and development.  It was not a structured one. But many faculty members participated and gave their ideas. 
Prof AKN Reddy stressed the need at developing rural technology based research activities in the institute. I had taught a course on alternate energy sources in 1968. So renewable energy ,particularly biogas from cow dung was considered a focus area. I got interested in it. We looked at community biogas systems. 
Those discussions led to the setting up of ASTRA in 1974- 1975 at IISc. Prof Krishnaprasad became the convenor of ASTRA in the absence of Prof Reddy. I got involved with the activities of ASTRA and I produced the first annual report of ASTRA.  More on it later. 
Though I got a job in automation , there was no office space in automation. One wing of the first floor was under construction and would take a year or more to complete.  So I continued in my old office in the EE department with approval of the EE chair.  I was still teaching EE courses and a few computer courses under automation with students from EE,ECE registered for my courses. 
I was staying in the hostel in E block during 1972. I had three close friends in E block - Sivan and Raghavan  doing PhD in Metallurgy, and Palanichamy doing PhD in civil engineering. I used to walk with Sivan to Majestic every Sunday. Both Sivan and Palanichamy joined REC Trichy after their PhD. Raghavan joined IIT madras. I also used to walk with Dr  Mahishi on some days. My walking regularity and distance  increased in this period. Mr Kuppanna, hostel superintendent, helped me a lot. 
By the beginning of 1973, I had to quit the hostel after promotion as assistant professor. I started looking for a suitable house to stay in. Mr Harihar of IC Engines department suggested a house in Malleswaram 11th main and 13th cross and helped me in getting it for rent. It was next to the Cluny convent and the railway line. It was on the first floor of a house with four flats. I took the house and moved in. I stayed in this house till 1985 December, a long time. 
At that time, it was a quiet area with a number of big bungalows and open spaces and no traffic,an ideal place to live. It was about 2kms from my department. So I walked to the institute everyday. My eating habits changed. I ate lunch at the mess but both breakfast and dinner were irregular , hotels , canteen or home. By this time ,we had a canteen opened by Mr Babu Rao from the airlines hotel giving good quality food and coffee. So I frequented that cafe regularly for tiffin and coffee. 

By this time, I worked on a conceptual framework for real time power system control using digital computers and submitted a paper to CBIP. The session was held in Madras and my paper got a great response from most people. I had met Prof Sethuraman of IIT Madras in previous CBIP meetings . He was there in this CBIP research session also. The conference was organized on a grand scale. We were put up at the hostels in Guindy. The  Inauguration was in Poondi , a very difficult program with a huge crowd attending the inaugural. We didn't get a proper lunch. We had dinner one day in the Connemara hotel and  another day  in Vijaya Gardens- it is gone now. We also had a reception in the Raj bhavan in Guindy. I met Mr Easwaran , director ,Easun Engineering , in the Raj bhavan.  It was a great ,lovely meeting. My parents had moved to Katpadi by then. 
I attended the conference with sponsorship  from the EE department even though I had moved to automation.  Our paper got the gold medal. I attended the research session. It grilled Prof Gopalakrishna on misuse of funds. I was made convenor of a new working group on power system control. 
Mr SS Murthy introduced me to the TNEB chair and wanted me to meet the superintending engineer at Basin bridge thermal power plant and suggest use of my optimization methods. I went to the basin  bridge ,met the SE. He thought that I am too young and he knows better about his plant. So the meeting was not fruitful.

26/09/25, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 848

In April 1973, I got another good news that I was selected for the commonwealth scholarship. I didn't apply for it but Prof Dhawan recommended my name. Prof SN Rao had gone to England the previous year.
I chose to go to the University of Manchester. 
I had to send an application to the British council suggesting university names ,area of work and a writeup. 
I prepared the application and submitted it through the registrar. I chose operating systems as the area of work and wrote about my interest in scheduling. I chose Manchester as my first preference and Cambridge as a second one. I got confirmation after a month.
 
The time was limited and I had to leave by September 1973. I needed to get a passport and get necessary approvals. But more importantly, I was teaching two computer  courses and alternate arrangements were not possible. So I wrote to the British council seeking permission to join by jan1974. I got the permission . 
I informed the institute and asked for approval. The registrar wrote a critical remark on it while seeking the director's permission . The director approved my request. I got a call from our deputy registrar ,Mr Nanjunda Rao. So I went to his office. He showed me the file containing my request, their note and director's approval. The director has instructed the registrar to show me their remarks. 
I couldn't decipher why Prof Dhawan wanted me to see the office note. Was it a mild warning that I shouldn't take such a direct approach in future? Anyway ,I got the clearance to leave by jan1974. 
As I didn't have a passport, the next thing I did was to apply for a passport. It was simple. I got an endorsement from the institute to the regional passport office stating that it is an approved official visit. There was no passport office here. I applied to the Madras office. Without any difficulty and without long delays, I got my passport. That hurdle was through.  I didn't know about other hurdles at that time. Many came my way afterwards. 
The first was personal. My grandmother was upset. I went to Tirunelveli and met my uncle and grandmother . I had to promise to get married so that I can proceed to England. The old belief which Gandhi faced , seems to permit even at that time. They thought that I may get spoiled in England. So I agreed to marry. It was to be an arranged marriage. Many in the institute tried to get me married ,but I wasn't keen and so those efforts didn't succeed. 
My marriage was conducted in September 1973 in Thiruttani. It was all done in a hurry.  I just went a day before the marriage and returned two days after marriage. Since I had a house,it was easy to set up the house . 
I didn't have gas then. I applied for gas. I was told it will take a year to get a gas connection. So I talked to Mr Harihar and got an electric coil stove for cooking. 
Since many  friends kept asking me, I arranged with help from Annavaradan for a reception at the Rotary hall of friendship . It was a simple get together . I got a contractor to supply food with the help of Mr Chandrasekhara. My uncle ,Dr Ramakrishnan was luckily in my house at that time . So he also attended the reception. More than 200 people attended the reception. He left for Pondicherry in the night. The reception went off well.

27/09/25, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 849

I realised that the conditions of my life were changing. I learnt that business as usual is dead. I always felt that an assistant professor should achieve at a higher level and the professor should teach a still higher level of activities. I felt that I had to build a new profile as an assistant professor. I should widen my knowledge base, go deeper, get deeper in research and reach out to peers. But a lifestyle change was an immediate requirement.
I quickly adjusted to the changed circumstances -  my personal life- setting up the house , buying furniture , household items etc ;  my teaching at EE and a new course on simulation ;  new assignments and adjustments on working in two diverse and difficult environments that existed in EE and SA;  as well as my lectures at PSTI and Datamatics.
I should discuss my difficulties in the institute here. 
While EE had a harmonious group of faculty with no agenda and with traditional values mostly except Parthasarathy being a bit distant , SA had IGS working with a plan and an agenda. I was part of most activities in EE and was involved in most developments, that was not the situation in SA. IGS built a group of faculty who listened to him - three IITK persons, and three who joined from EE and ME - , and created his close circle. Others were not part of this circle and stayed as separate entities . I belonged to this external group. My relationship with IGS wasn't a great friendly one. It had its strains. I realised that aspect even before taking up my job at SA. He didn't like my association with EE , he didn't like my teaching courses in EE and he wanted total loyalty to him and the school. He saw my loyalty spread everywhere in the institute. He didn't like that.
I felt that life is not a simple black and white situation. It has its complications and uncertainties. I realised that your talent and work takes you up to a point . Beyond that level, relationships, people's opinions, people's beliefs and biases matter a lot for making life acceptable to us. Some  I was to be prepared for a new way of living in my world and not in the school's world. My world ,I decided, had to build connections outside the school to build a good academic strength in me and protect me. I built a strategy and  started working on it. I was already involved in many institute activities like hostel wardenship, curriculum committee membership ,time table committee convenorship ,power committee chair etc. I felt that I should improve my network and started working in that direction seriously. 
I had a lot of interactions with Prof Krishnaprasad during our time table committee days and it brought me into contact with faculty in CTS like Rajaraman and Madhav Gadgil as well as AKN . I had good working relationships  with professors R Narasimha, AK Rao ,AR Vasudevamurthy,  T Ramakrishnan ,CC Patel, HR Cama - biochemistry- and many others. Prof Dhawan liked me. I became close to Prof CS Vaidyanathan . We became coffee friends visiting the cafe regularly. I stopped going for coffee with colleagues in the school till 1979. That coffee friendship with CSV  continued for decades . I had great support from our administration and I had a good relationship with Dr BS Subba Rao.  I also was in harmony with the other professors in the school - Prof Deekshatulu, Prof SN Rao, Prof SV Rangaswamy ,Prof EV Krishnamurthy and later Prof MRC. Prof GK joined this group. Most of the  professors developed a cold relationship with IGS. 
Prof Sana from the University of Chicago was visiting the school and I was close to him. We discussed several social and technical  topics . He was one of the first whom I met in the guest house with my wife after my marriage. He commented on my marriage invite, liking its simple wordings . 
My network with many outside companies like  Kirloskar electric company, Tata electric company, the electricity boards, HAL, NAL, CPRI,SREB , defence research labs  etc. So I had a big network both inside the institute and outside.  So I wasn't lost . I was well known inside and outside. Lastly, I also got support from students. My PR at the Institute is great and so IGS ,whose PR is poor, needed me to get things done in the workshops , hostel and mess , admin, and in other  departments, even arranging talks and courses , coffee supplies etc . 
When IGS realised my network and the support I got from many including the director , his approach towards me changed from opposition to a neutral level. His knowledge and contacts at the institute was poor. Same is the case with the faculty in his inner circle. He used to query me on the happenings in the institute afterwards for decades.
I got involved in the recruitment of mechanics for the new workshop in SA to improve my acceptance with IGS.

28/09/25, 4:03 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 850
  I had some hurdles with my Manchester visit. First,  the government put up stringent conditions for foreign travel and we needed approvals  . I needed to apply to three ministries and get their clearances. It took months to get it. Secondly I needed RBI clearance for foreign exchange. I needed health checkups and inoculations, and a visa from the British consulate at Madras. It was a series of activities. 
I needed to clear the approval problem. Since I got the scholarship from the government,  I thought I didn't need the approvals. So I wrote a letter to the director. He asked the registrar to phone Delhi and check up about the need for approval. I sat with the registrar and he got confirmation that I didn't need approval. So that major hurdle was gone thanks to prompt action by the director and the registrar. Both took personal interest in me. 
I was eligible for sabbatical and applied for sabbatical leave and got it approved. I went to Madras and got my visa without any difficulty. I also got my vaccination done and got a  certificate. Next was ticket booking. I got a ticket to London - Bangalore to Bombay by Indian airlines and then to London by British airways. It was a one way ticket. 
Next I needed foreign exchange. The Indian rupee was worthless outside India. I had to go to the main SBI branch in MG road and I was told that I can get only 18 pounds in travellers cheques. If I wanted more, I should get a sanction from the RBI. So I just got 18 pounds with fingers crossed hoping there will be arrangements by the British council at the London end. 
Then I needed to find a place to store a large quantity of my books in my office in the EE department as I had to vacate it. I went to Prof Joseph ,asked him whether I can keep my books and papers in the two Godrej cupboards that I was using. He permitted and told me to shift the cupboards to the lab so that the office  space would be given to someone else. I did that . Later on,  he told me that there were objections from some senior faculty in EE for permitting me to retain the cupboards. So he told me that I should vacate the cupboards immediately after returning from England.  I promised to do so. 
The second problem was packing - what items  to take with me and what items to send with accompanied baggage. I took more food items and less dresses. I didn't have a top coat. So I decided to put on a sweater - a thick one - and a woollen jacket. 
The next problem was - what to do with the rented house? I didn't want to leave it and thought it better to retain it. So I looked for a short term tenant. I got Mr Lakshminarayan who was constructing a house nearby. 
I left alone in jan2024 by a morning flight to Bombay.  Security check was introduced by then and visitors can't go beyond the security point. Previously visitors can stand outside and bid farewell till passengers get into a plane. 
I got accommodation in the sun and sand five star hotel on the Juhu beach. Mr Bukebag met me in the evening . I did some last minute purchases including two Bedekars pickle bottles.
I went by taxi to the airport by 730am. At the check-in counter , a policeman was waiting for me. I was worried. Fortunately it was a mistaken identity.  Check-in and security  were fast . But customs were terrible. They checked your items, and cash with you. I was told that they took away the cash and so it would be desirable to  keep it a secret place and not tell them. So I cleared customs after they opened my checked in suitcase .  Then  I boarded the flight  . The flight had two halts and reached London by 4pm local time. 
Surprisingly I had no recollection of that travel - what I ate except a great experience of drinking refreshing orange juice several times. The juice was tangy and I liked it . I drank a lot of juice in Manchester during my stay. That was the point I picked up  the habit of drinking juices. 
I was received by a representative from the British council. He gave me 10 pounds, took me to the Indian YMCA and told me that another person would come on Monday morning to take me to the British council office to complete the formalities.

Mr Uday Singh comments :
“Your narration of your foreign travel brought me to reminisce my own first foreign trip in 1975 to the then USSR. The only difference being I was allowed only 8 USD, rest of the bureaucracy was identical. It seems we lived in an era which was radically different from today - so nice, quick, technology based. But, value system has gone under radical changes - for the worse.”
Thanks , Uday. 
People won't remember these situations and  troubles now . These kinds of situations helped in good practices like future proofing, planning, preparedness, good communications, etc and good values like frugality, honesty, politeness , language, living within one's means without borrowing, hard work culture, reading, thinking,  satisfaction ,  etc. 

I was comfortable staying for two days in the Indian YMCA. I was alone, no one talked to me. I got breakfast and dinner. The food was Kerala style. I spent time watching TV in the common room - mostly sports like soccer and show jumping. 
A lady from the British council came at 9 am on Monday morning. I checked out and paid my dues,the result being  no cash left .  It was a cold February day , the temperature was around 3°c. I didn't have a top coat. But they were satisfied with my sweater and jacket ,and advised me to buy a top coat. We went to the British council office. The officer I met was a srilankan.  I was given an advance and told that they had booked a hotel in Rusholme in Manchester. I took a train to Manchester with my luggage, and reached around 100pm. I went by taxi to the hotel and checked in. The locality was Pakistani and the hotel was not great ,but the room was clean and good. 
I left immediately to a nearby branch of a  bank - NatWest bank and opened an account. I took a bus to the University. The bus facility was convenient and frequent. So travel was easy , but costly. The running cost of a car was less expensive than the fare for a bus travel - opposite to the situation in India. The postal services were fast. But I needed to use a public phone booth for calling people, offices and offices. 
I went to the University and joined duty. I met Prof Derrick Morris with whom I was to work. He told me briefly about the operating system and my work. It was a new experience working with someone. I met Dr Capon ,a faculty in the department . I did all my correspondence with him. He showed me the facilities, the faculty tea area and the office items delivery centre.  He  took me to my office space on the mezzanine floor. Two of us shared the office room, the other person being a PhD student. There was no departmental office unlike in India. So I didn't know whom to reach in case of need. I had to go to Dr Capon only,but I didn't want to disturb him. I thought I would learn from others as the time passed.
Then Dr Capon introduced me to two Indian students - T Visvanathan from ISRO and RD Kumar from IIT Bombay.

30/09/25, 5:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 852
I am still on working day one in England.
Let me talk about the department. Both the University computer centre and computer science department were in a dark red building. The centre had a CDC 7600 computer.
The building was heated by  the computers , no separate heating system was installed. I liked that and found the temperature comfortable. 
The department had a long history of designing , building and operating its own computers - the last one was called Atlas. It was modern and futuristic  for its period . Atlas was a popular working machine with many advanced features . It was operated and maintained in three places by the department for several years. It was designed by Prof Kilburn FRS. I enjoyed talking to Prof Kilburn regularly during coffee time in the faculty room. 
 Then   in 1973,  the team designed an advanced mainframe known as MU5. I found it operational in 1974. A large number of students , both graduates and research students, were working on systems programming. 
Actually , I saw one big difference between Manchester and most of the western universities and TIFR. The big ones worked on hardware design in the sixties , but mostly shifted to many theoretical areas,   operating systems  , software methodology , automata , data structures as the US universities felt that these are the modern areas and it is easier to publish. This belief minimised engineering and technology focus and concentrated on mathematical work . It was definitely high class as I appreciated Denning’s work on the working set theory and his exposition on virtual memory . But Manchester university persisted on applications research, had a very strong hardware group and a  large systems programming and OS group. The students used ALGOL and Fortran was getting popular then. But I found ALGOL a great language. The Americans didn't accept it then but later built Pascal and much later C - both discarded Fortran's structures and used ALGOL's structures. 
The department undertook very little theory work. So it was a revelation and learning for me that such application work is possible and meaningful . I felt that I should personally undertake application research with a strong theoretical base and followed it throughout my life . Mathematics was important but secondary , application was primary. My PhD work shows this clearly. 

We didn't use the University computer but used MU5. It was the most advanced machine of that time . It used virtual memory, large pipelining, and the concept of a kernel of  an operating system was introduced there even before the  Unix OS  came up later. ICL, the only computer manufacturing company in England bought the rights of MU5 and designed and built its new range machines based on MU5 features.  It graduated from the 1900 series to the 2900 series of computers . Prof Morris designed , rebuilt and repaired the operating system. Two features of the OS impressed me :
1.It was a modular OS with a kernel called MUSS , which was locked in the core memory and a number of functional units known as supervisors . Virtual memory was used with paging . 
2.It was designed using both a high level language called SPG - systems program generator and a low level assembly language. 
Based on user feedback and system usage measurements - a monitor was built in the hardware to sample and record details of use, the OS was redesigned regularly to improve its efficiency . 
I collected a number of manuals to read and two new languages to read and practice.

01/10/25, 4:04 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 853
There was another task pending on the first day - to establish contacts with the local British council office.  
After completing the formalities in the department,   I left by bus to Baltimore road to the local British council office around 400 pm . I got a contact person with a phone number. I gave my bank account details. My payments would be sent to my account. Normally payments were done on a weekly basis, not on a monthly basis as in India. After that ,I returned to the hotel by 5: 30 pm . 
It was the first working day for me in a totally unknown place with unknown culture in a foreign country. It started in London and ended in a hotel in Manchester. I could achieve many things, I learnt a lot about living in Manchester and work requirements quickly. Everything happened by clockwork precision without any hiccups. It was a very satisfying working day for me. I quickly adjusted to a new type of life . I never thought I would adjust like this. My experience of living in Bombay during my TIFR visits had trained me to adjust to new ways of living . The fear of the unknown was reducing. 
I didn't know what to do for dinner. There was an “Indian “ restaurant run by a Bangladeshi near the hotel. So I picked up a Nan and curry and ate in the hotel. I bought some groceries - juice, chocolates etc for use. 
I slept well . 
I had breakfast the next day in the hotel . The place smelled awfully with the frying of bacon. I couldn't stand the smell. So I quickly had toast , boiled eggs, orange juice and coffee and left for the University. 
Let me talk about the University. I was disappointed that the University was a set of buildings, not a campus . The buildings were located in the main road and adjoining roads. We were close to the city centre- downtown as Americans call it. Fortunately , there was a precinct centre next to the department. I can buy some food items there. There was a branch of my bank and I transacted all my business throughout the year in that branch and never visited the branch where I opened the account, the city wide network was good. I was given a cheque book ,but no passbook. I used to get statements from the bank. I got a credit card after a few months. 
There was a refectory nearby where I took lunch in the beginning. The students union was close by. The coffee here was cheaper- less than half the price elsewhere . So I used to buy coffee and cakes here. 
The city centre was close by ,about a km away . Initially I went by bus ,but later I walked to the shopping area mostly. 
So I went , found a large number of department stores like Marks and Spenser, C and A etc and bought a top coat. I also bought an A to Z book - a map of the city , a very useful book.  
Now I had to hunt for a house to stay for a year, the hotel stay is restricted to a few days. I went to the housing office and collected about four places . I went round a few in the evening , didn't find anything suitable, and went back to the office for more options. This went on for a few days. Then I settled on a bedsitter in a locality called Withington, a good locality,on the bus route . It was on the same road leading to the University and city centre in one direction and the British council in the opposite direction . It passes through Rusholme. I walked to the University during summer.  It was pleasant to walk in summer. 
There was a library close by which I visited regularly and borrowed a lot of fiction . Close to the library ,there were many shopping places and I did my shopping there.  The landlord was Mr Khurshid, a Pakistani, a nice person. I paid the rent and moved into the bedsitter , with a common bath room . He promised to get me a one room flat with a kitchen and bath in two months. I continued to stay  in this place till I left Manchester in Feb 1975. 
The weather was gloomy, raining occasionally and cold - 2 to 4°C mostly.  Since it was  winter, it used to get dark by 2pm . It was dark in the morning when I left for the University at 9am.

02/10/25, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 854
I met both Kumar and TV on the second day . They were helpful and gave me a lot of hints. 
The department had many foreign students in the PG program but had only a few Indian students, two for PhD and one in MSc in addition to me and I found a  similar situation in the University also, not many Indian students. Most Indians whom I met during my stay were there on a commonwealth scholarship. This situation is different now. 
After moving into the house , I went to a notary, got an affidavit done regarding my status and accommodation so that my wife would get a visa easily. The affidavit cost me  a fortune - equal to a week's salary. 

I met Mr Nanjunda Rao ,our deputy registrar,  in the precinct centre one day by accident and found out that he was attending a short training program in the overseas communications services.He was staying close to my house. He took me to a prayer hall located in the adjoining street on Sunday . But that was the only time I visited the prayer hall. 
I got my two unaccompanied bags after a month . It came to London. I had to make arrangements to transport it to Manchester and I took it home. It was expensive. The janitor was in Bangalore during the second world war and was familiar with India. He remembered his days in India.  He commiserated with me about the high cost and said transportation would be much cheaper in India. He used to talk to me often. 

My wife got her UK visa ,booked her ticket and was expected to arrive here  one month after my arrival. I went to London, picked her and took the night coach -bus to Manchester. We reached by 4am ,and took a cab home. 

Later I met many Indian students like Mr Keshava Rao in the English department,  Dr Rangan in the mathematics department , both on short training assignments and several others.

I met two important people, both had the same name- Narayanan-  and both were alumni of IISc.  Both were working in the civil engineering department of UMIST. One Narayanan was from Delhi college of engineering and was working in the structures lab ,another Narayanan was a hydraulics faculty working on fluid mechanics and continuum equations. I became close to both of them. We visited them a few times. I was in touch with the hydraulics Narayanan even after leaving Manchester. 
I had another encounter with Prof Dogra , my first was at IIT Delhi in 1967 when he was the director. This time he was the education attache with the Indian embassy. He came to Manchester and we were invited to a meeting with him. He didn't show any interest in our discussions and wanted to go to Old Trafford to see a cricket test  match India was playing against England.  
 Another person we met in Manchester was Dr Padmanabhan working for his FRCS . They were living closeby. When I met him , his father-in- law was also with him. They  left for India after a  month and we got his old TV . 

We had a high degree of socialization, visited the houses of kumar and TV regularly. Later Kumar moved into a house near ours. We met several Indian scholars and visited their houses. 
I attended some  lectures by Frank Sumner and participated in the programming course conducted for the first year students . They were taught both Algol and Fortran.

03/10/25, 4:22 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 855
I found it not easy to make people understand vegetarianism in England. Even vegetable soup may have either meat pieces or lard  in it. Margarine had fish oil.  It was a big task to explain vegetarianism to many people. So I avoided eating outside. 
But there were many receptions held in Manchester, Liverpool, London etc .I had to participate and I found it difficult to eat anything there. Even pies were not always vegetarian. The habit of looking at the ingredients started at that time. Also I used to read about food in many magazines to understand the contents. I wanted to eat chips but definitely not fish . But the doubt that the chips may not be vegetarian lingered in me. So I never ate chips in England. 
I made it a habit to eat bread ,butter, cakes , and many fruits , and drank juice. Some local varieties of rice from south east Asia were available . So we used that rice for cooking. I made tomato soup. We had taken a pressure cooker from India. We liked yoghurt ,so I bought a lot of yoghurt cups . There were not many types of  vegetables available. Potatoes and tomatoes were available. They became our staple food. Nuts like almonds were available . Milk was delivered home every morning in bottles. A milk van came to the houses. Coffee was mostly instant coffee. I also bought and used  oval tin regularly , a childhood memory. I started eating boiled eggs regularly . 
My lunch was usually bread with pickles or jam. We tried to grind rice and black gram dhall in a grinder ,but we didn't get good dosas. So we didn't prepare dosas regularly. Chapati flour was available in an Indian shop run by one named Patel. I bought a huge bag. We used that to make chapatis regularly. 

That year saw  a number of social turmoil happening in the UK. Idi Amin threw away Indians from Nigeria and most landed in England.  England had a large number of Punjabi Indians living there at that time . The Indians from Africa were Gujaratis. While most Punjabis had low paying jobs, most Gujjus set up shops and made money. So there was a simmering friction between both groups. Indians and Pakistanis lived together without any problems.  Most Indian restaurants were taken over by Bangladeshis. 
There was a much serious unsettled problem in Northern Ireland.  The British government split Ireland into two parts and kept Northern Ireland with it while giving freedom to the main Ireland. This led to fights between catholics and protestants in northern Ireland.  Bombing was happening occasionally. 
There was a lot of poverty as pay was low for most categories of jobs. Coal mine workers went on strike. That led to a severe energy shortage. 
England actually started reducing dependence on coal use in houses for heating by the sixties.  We knew about cities like London from Sherlock Holmes . We have heard about pie soup thick  fog and poor visibility. By banning the use of coal, the city atmosphere improved during winter. Visibility was much better. Buildings were free of soot and cleaner. But coal was used mostly in power plants . Britain didn't have any hydro power and nuclear power was just coming up. So if coal was not there ,then no electricity. The coal miners strike led to energy shortage . People were advised to reduce electricity consumption. So they were unhappy.  As a consequence , the conservatives  lost the election and labour under Harold Wilson came to power. He seemed calm and practical. He said after winning the elections that he has work to do. Things improved afterwards . Watching the royal practices and parliamentary proceedings were fun. I saw the keen interest by people in sports and budget. Budget analysis which is common now in India was a big activity in England in 1974 itself. 
Manchester had two soccer teams and United was the addiction to a large crowd then. It lost that charm now. Now the city team  seems to be doing better. Betting was  common. 
The most impressive service then was the NHS - national health service . We became members, were attached to a doctor in the neighbourhood, medicines were cheaper and it was good. The government gave free nutrition supplements to poor people. 
Piped gas was used for heating and or cooking. 
Though many houses had moved to central heating using gas and boilers with hot water circulating in pipes ,some had a separate room heater . The old concept of burning wood in a fireplace was extinct then.
Greetings
Dks
04/10/25, 4:24 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 856
N Ramanathan looks at the difference today.
“This one captures many interesting things then prevailing. Global availability of goods and services one needs anywhere is a boon to current generation.”

My response
Thanks
That is one view and very correct one. Another view is a continental view - what we do is right. What we eat is correct. How we live is correct. All other approaches to life are wrong. We know everything. You don't know anything. You are primitive. We are advanced. 
That was a strong colonial perception which we believe even now. I saw the lifestyle at that time when western powers were very much at a high point . But I saw one major change that happened after the second world war - Russian thinking , approaches and life style. Russian power threatened Europe and led to very strong McCarthyism in the US. So two camps started developing then ,but Africa and Asia were still ignored. 
Let me continue my perceptions of England then in 1974. 
Many  students were agitated about apartheid in South Africa and were canvassing support to attack apartheid and the regime in SA. I sympathized with their objective, but couldn't do anything meaningful. 
I had a new addiction - watching serials , movies and news on two TV channels. India didn't get a TV broadcast till the beginning of the eighties.  Cable was unknown even in the UK. There were primarily two BBC channels , government owned and ITV ,a private channel. I used to watch TV in the students union . The habit at that time was that most people were smoking. Even my dress used to smell of smoke. Even on flights, a lot of people smoked. It was a big national addiction . It took decades to bring down that smoking habit in the UK and US.
I saw many Laurel Hardy movies and many social and detective series. I used to see the saint series starring Roger Moore on Saturday at 12: 30 am, a late night program. My introduction to jazz happened at this time.  I liked several contests, quizzes and science programs. Scientific explanations of common happenings were simple and easy to grasp. They were excellent. 
I didn't miss my reading habit. I used to visit the public library and borrow many books - fiction, detective novels, history, biography, technology in general, geography, markets and products etc. I used to read a lot of magazines and journals. One I remember now was called “which”. It contained information on many products that were available in the market and compared different brands - useful to buy. I also borrowed books from the University library . I used to read most computer journals there - IISc didn't have many computer journals including ACM journals at that time . I started this habit in TIFR during my visits to TIFR. I loved reading ACM computing surveys which I  continued for several decades. I got a lot of data and incidents from software journals that improved my knowledge on software methodology, programming models and designs and modularity concepts. That habit I continued throughout my life. 
All these readings broadened my knowledge, sharpened my understanding and made me technically strong. 
My daily walking got reduced during the winter. I decided to walk to the shopping areas , library , city centre and UMIST ,etc during the spring season . I walked a lot - mostly to the University- during summer. The one problem was regular rains happening in unexpected times and I was prepared for that with an umbrella. So I was hanging on to the habit of walking. 
Another habit I picked up from my friends -Kumar and TV - was pub visits. Draft beer was good. 
Actually it was Mr Desai, manager of WH Hardy and co, who introduced me and Nanjunda Rao to a very mild drink - Cinzano Bianca. Later drinking beer was a part of socialization. The beer was not a bottled one and it was known as bitter locally. The pub was a great socialising place for most people. It was a typical British institution.

05/10/25, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 857
In the summer, we visited several places in England. I visited Liverpool for a one day meeting, going in the morning and returning by night. Then we visited the lake district. We have all read the poems of Wordsworth.  I always admired his description of natural beauty. We saw many lakes and beautiful gardens in a vast expanse. It was beautiful. 
Jodrel bank observatory was close by . It was a big radio telescope. I went and saw this observatory. We saw the famous airshow at Farnborough. 
I gave a talk in a Rotary club about life in India.  One question that dominated was the caste system in India. The second one was on poverty in India. The third was a concern for protein deficiency amongst vegetarians. The meeting was simple, no big eating party unlike in India. The person who came to my house to take me to the meeting was a grocer. We discussed the disappearance of grocery businessEngland ,a topic discussed in the news channels due to the advent of large departmental stores and their popularity. Local grocers were loosing business. India has resilience - grocers still survive despite malls first followed by ecommerce now. 
We visited Blackpool to see the lights , a nice sight. We spent three days in London. I met Dr Virmani ,a faculty in the Polytechnic. TV lived in his house. He owned a house in London and it was unoccupied. He allowed us to stay in his flat in London. We traveled mostly by tube. It was convenient. We have read a lot about London through English literature. We lived in those mentioned places. We walked through HydePark and saw many soap box orators talking to empty audiences mostly. I never believed in the existence of this practice of speaking until I saw several people talking there. 
We saw the change of guards at Buckingham palace, visited and saw the 222B Bakers street Building, the supposed residence of Sherlock Holmes. We went to the tower of London exhibition and saw the Kohinoor diamond. We saw 10 Downing street and Westminster, the seat of the British parliament.  We went to Madame Toussads and saw the wax statues looking very real. We didn't have time to spend in the different museums. 
We couldn't visit Paris - tickets by train ,bus and air were unavailable.

06/10/25, 4:00 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 858
Let me add a new and major development that happened to me in Manchester.
I had met Dr Humphrey from the EE department of UMIST in Bangalore in 1973. He was working on load flow solutions. So I went to UMIST to meet him. Since promotional opportunities were poor in the UK, he applied and got a professor's position in Australia and would be moving there in a short period. He invited me for lunch at an Indian restaurant.  I agreed and we met and had lunch in a restaurant. I realized how British loved curry at that point. I heard the name vindaloo for the first time. It was a good learning activity for me to know the situation in UK academics.
I met Dr Sinha from IIT Delhi who was visiting UMIST then. UMIST was close to the University- about half kilometers away from my department. Dr Sinha was about to leave for India. I met Dr Barney , a senior faculty in the control systems centre , with him. After learning about my work , Dr Barney said that it would be most appropriate for their centre to have me and so requested me to get associated with them. I agreed and started spending some time in the control systems centre  at UMIST. I guided two MS students jointly with Dr Barney. My association was just a semester long with UMIST.  I learnt a new procedure in discussions with students. Students were required to maintain a notebook to record action points discussed during each meeting and students compliance on each point to be recorded by the student. It was a useful tool for project students. I feel that this is a practice that can be adapted everywhere and compliance can be easily established. One person I saw who followed the practice of taking notes during every meeting is Dr Nirmal Jain,TCS. He had several notebooks full of records of his discussions.  It was remarkable and I admired him. This quality  is the stepping stone and an essential requirement for proper understanding and decision making. 
I enjoyed my association with UMIST. It gave me a different view. While the computer science department of the University  focused on solving practical problems of building large systems, the centre at UMIST was dealing with more theoretical problems. 
I attended a special farewell dinner in July 1974 in London.  Fortunately, the vegetarian concept was known to the organizers and I got some good food. I spent a night in a bed and breakfast unit in London and returned to Manchester the next day. 
Another hobby that I cultivated while in the UK was photography. I did buy an instant clicking camera first and experimented with it, but wasn't happy with the photos taken from it. So I did more research, read books and understood that I needed an SLR camera with good lens and good focusing capacity even for near photos. Further research led me to buy an inexpensive but pretty good Russian SLR camera - zenith is the brand name. I found that printing films to hardcopy was quite an expensive activity. So I started putting photos in slides. The processing was very cheap. I started clicking everywhere and got hundreds of color photo slides. I bought a viewer and a projector. 
Let me discuss a major use of my camera later in India in the next post.

Later when I was back in India , I used the camera to produce a photo that became an important memory point for my friend, Mr Thimme Gowda. After returning to India , I found that Mr Gowda had moved to his house in Subramanyanagar , close to my house in 11th main, Malleswaram.  He requested me to take a photo of his family. I took a photo and printed it and gave it to him. Later that year, his son fell from the terrace and died. Thimme Gowda broke down. The photo taken by me was the only one capturing his son with others.  Later he asked me to take an enlargement of his son from that photo. I requested Marutisha of EE to process it from the negative. He made a good enlarged copy and Gowda was happy. I was happy to be of some small help at that stage of immense sorrow. 
I used that camera for several years taking photos of my children growing up. 
I bought some more electronics devices like radio receiver, cassette recorder etc. 
We were invited by a local family to spend Christmas night with them. It was interesting observing their dog giving a gift to their child. 
I wanted to extend my stay in the UK by an extra semester and wrote to Prof BS Ramakrishna and he said no to my request. So I decided to return after the completion of one year at Manchester.  
By mid December, we started planning to return to India.  I bought a huge box to transport my belongings to Bangalore by ship and Kumar transported it in his car. I bought a big suitcase to carry my dresses back home. Since my wife was pregnant, we bought several baby related items like a baby stroller, feeding bottles, warmer etc. 
I decided to send my wife to India at the earliest and so I booked a ticket for my wife by air India.  I took my wife to London by the night sleeper train - my first experience of traveling on a sleeper train in England. It was a private compartment. We got a wakeup call and morning tea service. I took her to Heathrow airport . My wife returned to India- Chennai - during early jan1975.  I returned to Manchester by night bus. My father picked her at Madras airport and she went to Chengalpet to her parents place.
 I returned in February middle. There was some difficulty in booking my ticket. The office said they would book via Delhi, but I said that I preferred Bombay as the transshipment point. I came via Bombay by air India and reached Bangalore in the morning by 730am. The customs was terrible. But the wait time was less and I could get into the morning flight to Bangalore though I had booked an afternoon flight.

On arrival at Bangalore, I took a taxi from the HAL airport and went straight to the guest room in the hostel. I had already written a letter to Mr Kuppanna, hostel superintendent, requesting guest room accommodation. My friend K Palanichamy was an Assistant warden. I met him on arrival at the hostel. 
I went by 9am to the department office and joined duty. I applied for leave and left for Madras by the afternoon Brindavan express.
I returned after two days and found that my house on 11th main was not available.  So I had to continue staying in the hostel guest room. Since it was mid semester, I didn't have any teaching assignment. 
The school of automation started a new ME program in 1973. The program was a combination of two disciplines - control systems and computers. I floated three new courses : data structures, systems programming and operating systems. I taught these courses for several years, more than a decade and more than two decades for the OS course. So I had to start teaching from August 1975 onwards and so I began preparing for these courses. I continued with lectures at PSTI and Datamatics.  
Immediately on arrival , I met Prof Dhawan one day on the road. He expressed his displeasure at the rejection of leave by Prof Ramakrishna and told me that I should have written to him. I was overwhelmed by his concern for me. 
Within a week of my arrival, many things were happening to me. Prof Joseph asked me to take my books and papers and release the two almyrahs. Fortunately the faculty rooms were ready in the school and I got a room. So I shifted all my materials to this new office and took days to organize the books and papers. 
I found two new faculty members in the school - Dr Ansari and Dr Dasarathy. Prof Sanathanan was visiting the department for a year. He was sitting in an office across my office. Prof MR Chidambara also got a position in the school. I also saw that the school had a deep divide with IGS on one side and all other professors on the opposite side. There was no harmony. Most assistant professors sided with IGS. I was neutral but friendly with Deekshatulu,  SVR and SNR. EVK was also very friendly with me and others. Still ,I had a lot of support in the EE department- from professors Joseph, Krishnan, KS Prabhu and many assistant professors. But I got the greatest support from Prof Dhawan.  
More involvement and more support was coming my way as the days progressed.
It was a very important period for Prof Dhawan. The department of space has built the first satellite ,named Aryabhatta and preparations were going on for launching it from USSR. A large number of scientists and engineers went to Baikanur to prepare the launch. Despite that busy period, Dhawan saw me and talked to me.  I had one pending task. Once I left the EE department in 1973, my connection with CBIP was cut off except for attending the research conference in July August 1973 at Madras . But I didn't write a complete report on the work done in the project due to the abrupt termination not by CBIP but by the EE department.  Many project reports from others in the  EE department were also pending . Even EE lost its interest in CBIP.  But CBIP wrote a strong letter to the director. Since I left for Manchester in 1974, I haven't written the report. So Prof Dhawan talked to me about the report. Actually I submitted all papers written with project funding from CBIP to the director. He was impressed but told me to prepare a report and close the case. He was annoyed by the CBIP letter. I promised to submit within a month.
So hectic work on writing the report started and kept me busy. Mr Ramaswamiah from EE office was assigned to type the report . The work was completed in two months and the report was submitted to CBIP through the director's office . I wrote to the director informing him about the completion of the report.

Mr HS Nagaraja observes:
Sir, I observe that God does not choose talented people, but gives talent to chosen people. I strongly feel you are one among the chosen by God. Greetings.  Have a pleasant day. With regards.Nagaraja HS

My response
I don't agree with your observation, Nagaraja. 
God does not discriminate between people, animals, plants and nature. God's consciousness is there with the entire universe. It is upto you to take it. 
Let me quote from Kantara brahmakalasha. The concept of the well being of everything is God's wish. 
Gottilla Shivane Bhakti Daariyoo
O Lord Shiva, I do not know the path of devotion.
Gottiruvudondhe Daivavaariyoo
I only know one thing – the grace of God.
Sarve Janahitam Praanam Sthaapitaa
For the well-being of all people, life energy is invoked.
Untagaasuthalee Parashiva ShaktiyaO Supreme Shiva, your energy is established here.
Deha Shuddhiranena Bhuyat
May this make my body pure.
Loka Anugrahave Daivaanugrahavoo
The blessings of the world are indeed blessings from the Divine.
Pranaswaroopaa Tejomayavoo
You are the embodiment of life and filled with divine light.
Punyasanchayave Parama Sukhavoo
The accumulation of virtue leads to supreme bliss.
Yatra Shraddhacha Bhaktim
Where there is faith and devotion,
Tatra Labhate Jayantham Daiva Chittam
There, the victorious divine presence is attained
The problem of diffidence, dependence and unevenness is due to the total misunderstanding of our capabilities and the urge to create forward movements and tackle challenges and not dependent on God. That  is a fact shown by many that we don't understand our mind and underutilize our intelligence. We don't know
 how much intelligence we have,
 how to use it and sharpen our minds,
 how to control our minds, 
how to live purposely,
how to introspect and review our decisions and actions,
 how not to be afraid of challenges but treat them as opportunities
 and how to handle unknowns and come out of our comfort zone. 
Let me start with self appraisal , a very important step for everyone. Its purposes are
Know oneself, good and bad, capabilities, diffidence, fear and emotions, 
Know one's minds, how they work, how to rework them, redirect them, control them, guide them - deep contemplation and review
Know how to improve your emotions, decisions, actions. 
Know how to increase your mind power and use it effectively. 
Then you see the benevolence of God. Kantara and Kantara chapter1 justify our beliefs and actions. It shows that Gods elevate us to a higher level of consciousness, higher purpose in life. 
I can write more on this but I want to stop here.

10/10/25, 5:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 862

The year 1975 became important for me . It led me into another major research area and opened my social consciousness and enlarged my vision. I was looking at engineering from a purely technical view and had interests in nature and social and cultural behaviours not  as a hobby ,but a serious learning activity . This was neither part of my formal learning in the school nor was taught to me by a learned person. It is a very broad canvas of societies , their behaviours, history, geography, nature , religious concepts, spirituality, human behaviours,  country's growth and development , technology impacts etc. It not only enhanced my knowledge but also provided me a great window to observe and learn . It improved my contemplation skills and intuitive thinking. I consider this approach important. I saw my grandson following this approach to sharpen his mind , schools didn't help him. This complimentary learning is more important than formal learning. Formal learning emphasizes a structure , logic to learn thereby restricting our thinking. But this informal learning improves our vision , maturity, intuition and decision making skills. 
It starts with the first practice - create a deep attention span. Some households put their children on many tasks - cutting vegetables, cooking, cleaning, fetching water , market visits and purchases, handling money, doing prayers and pujas , chanting , writing , listening to Ramayana and conversations . This is missing now. Children are rushed to school in the morning. They have no time to listen to people or nature. They are hurried and miss the attention skills. We need to get back on this fundamental mental activity. I am not against school education but compliment it with world education. School education gives us skills, logic, communications, mathematical knowledge and scientific approaches. 
I used to tell my students to spend an hour or two in the library reading books or journals and increase the time of reading and concentrating on what was read. 
The next step is contemplation.  Think on what you saw or listened to or read. Abstract the learnings. This is another great habit to develop.  After listening to a talk, or reading a chapter or observing an event, pause and think and try to list out the main points. This improves our interest, concentration and analysis skills.
The most important aid to learning is reading a lot of books and contemplating on them. That made me what I am today. 
Let me look at my journey through reading. It is a long and big journey and I can't tell the big story - only some bits. 
Let us look at the journey in the next post.

As I said i had great opportunities to listen initially and learn through books and lectures later. This book based learning continued throughout my life. Initially during the first ten years , I read more of religious literature, fiction dealing with freedom movement, village life, city life, social life, values, politics, history, geography,biography,leadership, mass movements, poverty, development, industrialization, philosophy etc - a very wide range of learning. It enriched me.
Initially I read Kalki's four-volume novel titled alaiosai - noise of waves. While its main theme was the struggle for independence and common people's role in freeing India from the slavery of the British, it depicted in detail village life- poverty, longing for food, no cash flow, people playing cards, post office, mail brought by a runner from the nearby town etc. Life despite scarcity was simple and satisfactory. Then it shifted to life in Bombay ,a great contrast it depicted. There were enough details in the descriptions. While its depiction of village life was similar to the village life I lived as a child, it opened my mind to freedom movement and struggles, attacks by solders on common people and created a big empathy in me for the millions of common freedom fighters. I understood the importance of economic muscle in salt satyagraha. I learnt a lot more about the freedom struggles from this novel than from the school text books. Gandhi, Nehru and Rajaji wrote books and articles on the independence movement and they provided a basis for my understanding of our country in the early 20th century. 
I read many books on the lives of many leaders and that created a deep impression in me. 
As I said earlier, I learnt a lot about railways, Bombay city, life there, etc from alaiosai. Most fiction I read centered around the lower middle class , their ordinary life , their surroundings , their societal interactions and life struggles. I also learnt about nature, plants ,animals etc from some books. 
Later I read books by many English authors and that gave me pictures of London , and life in general in England.  Many newspaper articles gave good descriptions of current life and politics. 
The novels gave me extensive education in many areas. They improved my visualization capability. I got a great in depth understanding of human relationships, interactions and behaviours by reading many fiction books. Contemporary politics, economics and development were very well described in newspapers and magazines so that we got a good front seat views of what is happening in the country. I realised that every situation can be viewed in different ways ,interpreted in different ways and mostly there is no unique solution to real life problems. You should mix and balance multiple solutions . 
So books are great companions to us.
