14/10/25, 3:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 865
Vinod Vyasulu
Any comments on this?https://theconversation.com/what-is-ai-slop-a-technologist-explains-this-new-and-largely-unwelcome-form-of-online-content-256554
My response
AI slop is low-quality media made with generative artificial intelligence. It is characterized by an inherent lack of effort and is currently being generated at an overwhelming volume.Coined in the 2020s, the term has a pejorative connotation similar to spam.
As early large language models (LLMs) and image diffusion models accelerated the creation of high-volume but low-quality text and images, discussion commenced among journalists and on social platforms for the appropriate term for the influx of material. Terms proposed included "AI garbage", "AI pollution", and "AI-generated dross".Early uses of the term "slop" as a descriptor for low-grade AI material apparently came in reaction to the release of AI image generators in 2022. Everyone is told they can become AI expeet in 10days. So they are picking easy fakes. 
AI slop is a deceiving audio or video expected to create a chaotic situation in a stable state or creating disturbances.
AI slop is a simpler one but it can creste serious problems.  It is ongoing from the days of photoshop. Actually we can divert the attention of people with photos like the child hugging a puppy. I have a feeling fake news played a role in many countries including may be Nepal . 
We need to setup a fake detector and alert system. With our mobile apps reaching billions we need to be proactive. We can't be complacent. Alertness is a constant reminder to all of us. 
But AI slop is manageable through education and alertness. What is tougher is the capability of AI to manipulate bank accounts leading to illegal money transfers. Similarly, AI can create nuclear explosions by errors. Well AI will improve the quality of products and buildings,but it can do mischief. This was predicted by Minsky in 1959 itself. Minsky is one of the four founders of AI.
We need to create a manual override function in many cases. AI has to be out of bounds for all process automation, banking transactions. We still need a strong digital presence in many sectors.
Life will become more stressful as AIs ill effects come to the fore. Already we are seeing the unemployment problem. 
Let us be clear that routine jobs will vanish, new jobs will be generated. We need to become transformative people, thinking and innovating most of the time. 
Individuals will be entrepreneurs in their life. That is most of us will be supported by AI agents - hundreds of them, not different from a mobile app driven life now but one has to look more sharply because agents are going to become complex. 
The deep question that I contemplate is how many people can lead a life without AI influence. Can we live without AI influence either directly or indirectly? The answer seems to be no. Indirect impacts affects all
We see digital capitalism in operation in India in full swing. Financial inclusion and UPI took banks to houses. So digital capitalism helped the poor a lot. 
The future will see another form of capitalism and it will take longer to settle down. It will see more Trumps everywhere. It will not be Man mohan Singh or Biden era but Modi and Trump era of actions. It will affect all deeper. AI capitalism will affect the world severely. Life is never dull. Are we going back to the hunter gatherer era?
Greetings
Dks
15/10/25, 5:20 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 866
Mr B Sambamurthy refers to the weak internet in remote areas and ekyc difficulties based on the following report. 
NASHIK/PUNE: A half-hour trek uphill, a line of women waiting in the midday heat for an OTP that rarely arrives. Their eyes fixed on a phone tied to a tree - praying for a single bar of signal.Tired of too many ads?
Digital welfare dream meets patchy network reality in the tribal heartland of Maharashtra's as hundreds of are trying to complete the state-mandated to keep receiving their monthly Ladki Bahin Yojana payout.
In Kharde Khurd village of Dhadgaon taluk, women walk nearly 30 minutes uphill to find a patch of signal.
More than 500 beneficiaries from the Bhamane group gram Panchayat and Kharde Khurd are forced to wait in the sun, hoping the phone - secured on a tree - will connect to a network, often from across the border in Gujarat or Madhya Pradesh. Success rate: below 5%.
He asks
"Can't this problem solved better?"
My response
Yes ,definitely. Indian geography has a good amount of internet shadow regions even for mobile towers. So this situation happens.
Todays times of India edit looks for a easyway out- typical Indian Jugaad mentality . That is shying away from a problem, not solving it. The problem is not a rocket science one. 
So there are many solutions. 
1. Mobile vsat carrier 
2. Simple rf receiver 
3. Signal amplifiers at both ends. 
IIT Bombay did some experiments. Google used experiments with balloons. 
There is a need to get form filling done offline before uploading. Streaming is faster. Also increase the timeout.
Banks should get involved in this and should not say it is not my problem. 
The problem is not restricted to ekyc. How will we implement distance learning for children , learning through youtube and learning centres ? How do people access government services ? Do we ignore them .
Cost is not the primary concern. We spent lakhs of crores for huge airports, 8lane highways and city roads and metros  but can't provide this critical infrastructure to the poor . 
The government should look at delivery issues before deciding on a program. It is imperative that RF towers are erected at critical shadow regions and connected to the main cell towers. We can't leave it to BSNL. Other operators should get into this. 
We should go for satellite phones facility. 
When ISRO could experiment with satellite based education system targetting remote villages in late 1970s , we have capabilities and should put it to use. When governments distributes lakhs of crores as freebies, they should take responsibility to build the infrastructure to take it to the last mile like Jandhan program.
Hope a solution comesout.
Greetings
Dks
16/10/25, 4:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 867
Mr Venkatesh ,KSCST has queries on AI.
"Dear Sir,Your response effectively addresses the complexities of AI slop and its implications for the society. Engaging in discussions about these topics is crucial as we navigate the future of technology in a discussion panel or a special talk.What do you think about the potential for regulation in AI-generated content? Does our Country has any regulation in this area.How do we regulate as we are afraid of some digital theft in banking sector, Sensex, digital weaponry and other important so called secured areas?Could there be a balance between innovation and safeguarding quality?Thank you for your continued Postings."
My response
We can't trust AI at this stage of development.  
My first assertion 
AI is a boon. It will immense benefits for medicine, health, governance, safety, education, agriculture, public utilities, etc. It can lead to much better governance, law and order, public safety, grievance redressal etc. Health and agriculture will jump several quanta for the poor.
But there are two caveats.
1. AI will need us to rewrite all our rules - is it possible. We need to reimagine, reeducate , rebuild our knowledge base ,our libraries, governments etc. We are seeing that the middle management is dead.
2.AI works today with billions of parameters, large amount of data, lots of computing units consuming gigawatts of power. Any savings in essential requirements will destroy our society, the poor and weak will be affected the most ; the corrupt and powerful will benefit most. 
Solution
Prevent AI from  overriding safety, security, fairness, justice, privacy, stability concerns of people .
So your question is timely.
Your question concerns regulations. 
My assertion 2
We can't run an AI-driven online system fully automated. AI will be used to provide a set of solutions and their probabilities. User decides which one solution is appropriate for the occasion. We need to build a context sensitive decision process. So automation should go through a verification process initially.
Just as we have a defined procedure for development software and production unit , we need to build distinct development versions separate from running versions for AI systems also for the next decade , definitely for critical applications. 
My assertion 3
Create a formal AI development model and dev ops procedures urgently.
My assertion 4
Strengthen the laws. 
Government regulations on generated AI are mandatory. All AI generated images should be labeled explicitly. We need to discriminate generated from original images. All data should identify the source. It should be an offense if not mentioned, as is being done for election publicity posters. It is necessary now. 
Most importantly, the governments role is to protect the weak against attacks. Our cyber laws are not strong, but initially , we need to bring in a strong public nuisance act against misuse of AI on personal images and data. Privacy is again pretty important. 
We need a strong criminal procedure to handle manipulation of data by AI. Since AI has an owner - declare ownership of every AI unit- , the owner should be made responsible for the ill effects. Such actions will not be welcomed. Most developers will hate it. But developers have no right to create a tool to attack others and destroy the privacy of the weak. But today, most developers arent aware of the impacts of their AI agent. Today, laws are mild and need corrections. There has to be an authority to brand a AI unit as substandard . We need standards similar to those existing for packaged food items -FSSAI.
All organizations handling data should build secure vaults to handle data. They should not be given permission for direct access to data . Access should be through a secure procedure. Even such stringent measures do not avoid frauds now. SQL injections are common. 
We need standard development and operating procedures for AI. We have good procedures for software, but weak ones for data now. We need to build maturity indices and models . That is not an easy process. Even today telecom has an involved set of protocols for operations and such rigour is missing in software . This is a good area for research . 
There is a lot of work for the government to reorient their governance strategies. 
The question is - are we hastening AI developments to reap some low hanging fruits? AI can give us a lot of advantages if used maturely. Otherwise ,we will reach the state posed by Max Tegmark in his book Life 3.0. Yuval Harari has also cautioned us in his book - Nexus A brief history of information networks. There are hundreds of books analysing the problems posed by AI. Most developers are unaware of them. They don't know they are creating demons. 
Miles to go for mature AI. 
Greetings
Dks
17/10/25, 6:09 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 868
Prof Karunakara raises an important aspect about god.
"Dear Sir,Are we giving all our responsibilities to God? Are we running away from our effort to correct ourselves and build a better society. Is it always God need to come and correct our society? I keep thinking on these. I look for your insightThanks and regards"
My response
Wonderful comments and concerns.
I will restrict myself to Hinduism. 
There is only one concept of god in Hinduism - Brahman - all pervading, all aware, permanent reality spreading bliss , consciousness and energy to all. He is nirguna , aroopi , and undefinable. Brahman does not support anything except indirectly. Brahadaranyaka upanishad uses Neti - not this, not this - to explain Brahman. Quantum entanglement - relationship between distant particles is possible because of this principle. Consciousness spreads throughout the universe through waves. Brahman is beyond our understanding. 
Our life's ultimate goal is the knowledge of this consciousness - moksha.
Upanishads established the primacy of this concept as the core  founding base of Sanatana dharma. It was found that Brahman was not understood by us and so we needed a perceivable god for worship. Vedic rituals are elaborate and can not be done everyday by everyone.  Later, it went through elaborate expansions to build a great structure for common people to understand god. 
All other gods are manifestations of Brahman in many forms - saguna . We talk of 33crore devas. The forms chosen give us a way to worship. Even here Shiva is worshiped in a symbolic form - Linga. You can invoke a god anywhere , perform prayers and allows him or her to go away to his place. 
According to Rig Veda - Nasadiya sarga - Brahman created the universe - an infinite space - with all entities and the life giving panchabhutas and allowed the entities to take care of themselves. 
So your point is well taken, Karunakara. God does not take responsibility for running our affairs. This huge multiverse was built to run autonomously and expand itself in many ways.
Let us look at a second view. Rig Veda talks of devas . It shows its gratitude to earth , nature, water, sun, wind , storms, mountains, animals, plants, trees etc. Its prayers request for a prosperous , healthy life, for order, harmony ,stability , for gifts to be bestowed on the performer. The main prayers are to show our gratitude to nature ,its bounties and its forces. 
So we see a change here. We are able to see many manifestations of gods and we pray to seek favors from them. 
The understanding of God changed with the next stage - ithihasic and puranic ages. 
New god forms evolved with many functions. We talk of Vishnu ,the protector, Brahma ,the creator and Shiva ,the constructive destructor. Devi worship also came up. Gods were seen as establishing order by removing demonic forces. 
Ramayana, Mahabharatha, Garuda Purana, Bhagavatam, Skanda Purana , Vishnu Purana, Shiva Purana and many more were developed. We got a rich tapestry of life, society, gods, prayers and worship, festivals ,avatars, life in other worlds etc. There are rich tapestries built around each god. We remember them every year through festivals and pilgrimages. 
Finally , each one chooses a god and prays to get solace and peace. That is important. This belief in a local god protecting us,our family , animals, crops is widely prevalent in every village in India . We live on faith in our god as a part of our family.
We tell our problems to our god . It is a process of contemplation and thinking . Many times a solution appears to us and we are happy. A child goes to its parent with its problems. Adults go to god, pray to him , talk to him like his parent and finds solace . We dont want god to help us ,but show a path . That is our intimate relationship with our personal god. 
Look at Panjurli , the god in Kantara. This worship appeared first in Chomana dudi by Shivarama karanth where a lowest caste member ,Choma believes in Panjurli. Kantara shows Panjurli protecting their land , resources, poor people from exploitation. It means " good survives over evil".

Many religious traditions believe that the existence of God is understood through faith and spiritual experiences, rather than empirical proof. Some philosophical stances, like fideism, hold that God's existence is beyond human reason and can only be known through faith. Aravind's response

Vinod vyasulu comments on Hinduism 
"DKS, you say that you will confirm your response to Hinduism. In this, Brahman is the base of sanatana dharma. Is sanatana dharma the only tradition/interpretation of our philosophical/ religious inheritance? There are others. To name just a few I have heard of, we have the Arya samaj tradition from northwest India. We have the Brahmo Samaj from the East. In the South, we have the Basavanna tradition of lingayats. There is Sikhism. All of these are indigenous. Do they all, with their differences, come under the religious label of 'Hinduism'? Or, as many claim, they are different although they have sprung from the same root?"

My response
Vinod , the question is political , not religious or spiritual. Unfortunately , minority means a special status in india and so everyone wants to jump into the minority bandwagon. 
Let me define Hinduism
1.it is not a unitary religion dependent on one god. It is not a religion with one rule book. 
2.  It is beyond religion. It combines many life styles, many kinds of activities, many purposes in life and many cultures. It is a holistic operating system for survival as humans. 
3.It provides multiple approaches or paths  to a person to follow. All paths lead to god. 
4. It is a dharma - harmonious, orderly, truthful , non violent, sharing ways of living. Live and let others to live is a broad guideline followed by Hindus. 
5. It allows hundreds of practices. While vedas are the source books, people developed and constructed many doctrines and paths. Sastras were reconstructed and recreated over thousands of years by thousands of scholars maintaining continuity. Sastras dealt with many topics like cosmic life, nature, astronomy, logic, transcendence, human systems, language, mathematics, thermodynamics, metallurgy etc. Particularly this was evident in the past four to five centuries. Many social changes came up and stayed within the religion. Veera shaivism was very ancient form. It preexisted lingayats. 
6. Hinduism is interpreted by many. Sankara provides several axioms and redefines the principles of Hinduism. Many felt that he accepted Buddhist principles.
7. Hinduism accepts all - believers in vedic practices, puranic practices, samskaras, temple worship, kula devata and grama devata worship , spirituality, asceticism, materialism, atheism, etc. So it is a broad umbrella of practices. 
8. It is an inclusive religion. It does not reject anyone. 
9. While Hinduism did not reject dalits ,but treated them badly, there are no dalits in jainism or early buddhism. Only Dr Ambedkar created a new Buddhist religion. Both cameout of power struggles of communities - an anti brahmin movement. While Buddhism favoured kshatriyas , Jainism moved towards Vaisyas. Emphasis was different in both. Buddhism did not practice non violence . Meat was allowed. Jainism followed Ahimsa very strictly. Buddhism followed scholastic pursuits like Hindu sastras. They participated in Tarka. Many brahmins moved to Buddhism. Both used similar worship practices like Hindus. 
10. Hinduism survived all onslaughts, maintained and preserved its vast collection of sastras ,rituals and samskaras - a remarkable feat. This was not any institutional work. It was the untiring work of thousands of gurus over three millenia. It is an individual religion, not institutional. 
11. Hinduism built resilience in people. People survived and survive due to this resilience property.
Take your answer.

20/10/25, 4:16 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 871
Let me complete my discussions on Hinduism
12. Hinduism is a highly diffused ,multipolar religion with no central authority like a pope. Only god may have an authority ,that too rejected by some Hindus. Rig veda says that a ruler is governed by a sabha and samithi . So a king is not an absolute authority, not a guru even. 
13. There is no mechanism in Hinduism to declare someone as Hindu and excommunicate someone out of the religion. That authority exists with churches , mullahs and deobandhis etc. They are powerful. 
14. The only power centre in Hinduism are the gurus , but there are thousands and they propound no unified single theory acceptable to all. Hinduism is an ocean of many theories and practices coexisting together with reasonable harmony. 
15. Hinduism was not easy to understand. So many scholars and acharyas reinterpreted and simplified the core principles so that the religion reaches not just scholars ,but also common people. Prominent acharyas known to the history are Sankara, Ramanuja and Madhva . Their disciples carried their messages forward. Later we had Ramakrishna Paramahamsa, swami Vivekananda ,  and many others appeared on the scene to build clarity to the abstract principles. That continued for a long time. We saw Ramana Maharshi in recent times, mother at Pondicherry , swami Sivananda at Rishikesh and his sishya swami chinmayananda , . Thousands of such preachers exist now. There were a large number of poets like Purandara , Kanakadasa , Thyagaraja, Sant Tukaram, Samart Ramadas ,  Kabirdas , Tulsidasa , Valmiki etc who carried popular easy to understand principles and practices across the country. Many gurus and teachers also propagated many concepts to people. Five basic components were made widely popular and acceptable across the country and were extensively practiced.They are music, astrology, yoga , Ayurveda , worship methods and bhakti. 
16. While there was no central authority, rulers were supposed to follow dharma - just rule for all. They get their advice from brahmin gurus. Initially ,Kshatriyas were not happy with this. Some revolted against this. That led to Buddhism. Later Vaisyas were not happy with the practices and some were unhappy with brahmin influence , some with animal sacrifices and they led to Jainism. 
17. Central authority was questioned in the vedic period itself. The first rebel is Yajnavalkya.  He developed Sukhla yajur veda. 
18. Hinduism had some warts. So reformers appeared periodically. Two famous ones are Basvanna and Narayana guru . They transformed society. Then we had Raja Rammohan Roy who developed brahmo samaj and Swami Dayananda saraswsthi ,Arya samaj.
19. While there is no common definition of a Hindu , there is an implicit understanding of what is not Hindu- non obeying of societal order. 
So in answer to the question by Vinod, I should say that
Hinduism considers all these as their children and allows them to coexist. They are not excommunicated by any authority.

21/10/25, 4:22 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 872
Let me return to IISc and the year 1975 . I had returned from England and was settling into IISc. I was staying in the hostel as my house was not free.
A Number of things were happening in that year. My daughter was born in march 75 at Chengalpet . I was present in the hospital when she was born. I saw her and then departed to Bangalore. 
I got a good opportunity to integrate sociology with engineering and create new views . I consider that to be very fruitful for me in my research. Let me explain my interest in sociological and cultural aspects of the world. This habit deserves a detailed look and analysis. 
I believed early in life that knowledge can come from any and every  source - a Rig Vedic dictum. 
In 1975, ASTRA was set up and AKN was the convener. He left for sabbatical to UNEP and Prof Krishnaprasad was looking after ASTRA . I had earlier worked with him for the time table committee.  So he asked me to become part of ASTRA and get involved with the renewable energy activities. That started my in-depth involvement in the area of energy. 
I moved from looking at energy from the electricity point of view and developed a holistic view of energy involving many forms like thermal, chemical etc. I taught a course on non conventional energy in 1968 - 1969 and was familiar with nuclear energy. I focused on bio energy from wastes - biomass and bio gas. 
One of the weaknesses of engineering thinking and education is the total lack of a holistic view. Engineering is viewed as a set of parts and the connections are missed. The connections are many fold - mechanical, electrical, at the first level and flows - water, air, heat etc at the second level. But at a higher level engineering is dependent on energy flows and uses. Later i looked at a still higher level of information flows. These flows were seen in isolation. Even power and energy units were different - like watts and units in electrical engineering, horsepower and hp hours in mechanical and calories and BTU in thermal. There were efforts to create a common base like tons of coal equivalent or oil equivalent. Bio energy was ignored.
Let me complete my comments on engineering. There is another connection - that is data control and command flows. 
That pushed me into a new  direction of thinking and work - integrated energy models and optimization. I started to look at efficiency at an integrated level - a second law approach. These aspects along with environmental concerns became my next research focus for the next 30 years along with computer science areas. I moved away from power systems completely. 
KSCST was just coming up and Mr Sethu Rao joined it. He was sitting in the IPC department. 
We conducted the first annual seminar of ASTRA and KSCST annual meeting. I presented my work on community biogas plant . Prof Dhawan attended the meeting. 
As I decided before, my teaching shifted to computer courses only. I taught a course in the EE department also. 
Greetings
Dks

22/10/25, 4:26 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 873

Aravind has another query 
"Hindu religious practices include devotion (bhakti), worship (puja), and meditation (dhyana). Diwali rituals include cleaning and decorating homes with lights and rangoli (colored powder or petal designs), lighting diyas (oil lamps), and performing Lakshmi Puja to honor the goddess of wealth and prosperity.Rituals are a manifestation of faith, and their evolution is a product of religious and cultural shifts over time. While some ancient rituals have been questioned and adapted, faith drives the continued performance and adaptation of these practices to ensure devotion and reinforce religious beliefs; The cultural context provides the framework for their modification and evolution. Performing rituals is a way to express gratitude to natural elements and deities, showing faith and building religious sentiments. Individual faith allows Hindus to choose how they practice rituals, such as going to temples or not, which highlights that the connection to the divine is not solely reliant on rituals.  Are rituals connected to religion or culture?"

My response 
Identification ,classification and separation of entities as religion, culture, sociology , cosmology, ethics and humanism, sadacharam - good conduct, spirituality, etc happened much later , definitely after the rig vedic times.
Rig Veda provided a complete picture of life - spiritual, religious, ritual, farming, knowledge generation and absorption, family related activities, conduct, ethics, nature, natural forces ,society related activities , governance etc. It provides a holistic picture of life as it should be lived. 
We segregated activities as vaidehic, loukikam- loka sampathith , daivic, Vyavaharic,  etc dealing with divine activities, household activities, societal activities etc later. These definitions came from the language side. 
The term religion was not applied to sanatana dharma till 19th century. Sanatana dharma core principles are enunciated in upanishads. 
What is dharma ? It defines many ways of life to be lived. Before we start living , the emphasis of dharma was understanding of our neighbourhood, our country, our ways of living , our conduct , etc . That is called sociology. Accept the fact that there are many ways to live . 
We need energy and food for survival. We get that from nature. That deals with the environment and profession.
We want to understand beyond what we already know. We want to know the cosmos and our relationships to the various elements of the cosmos.That is normally known as religion. But now part of it is science - astrophysics. 
We want to live in harmony and happy. That leads to humanism, good conduct, sadachar and values - ethics. Practices emerge to improve living. 
We live according to a large number of socially accepted practices and that may be called culture. Our social culture is submerged and combined with religious culture. We had extensive social and religious practices. For example, Deepavali is a social function, but it has religious connotations also. We offer prayers before the start of any public function. We light a lamp also. Those were part of our smriti. Smriti also provides laws for good governance. 
Culture is not a single entity. There are hundreds of cultures- work, food, farming, eating, worship, festival, pilgrimage, marriage etc. 
Culture-  the customary beliefs, social forms, and material traits of a racial, religious, or social group. also : the characteristic features of everyday existence (such as diversions or a way of life) shared by people in a place or time.There are seven elements of culture: social organization, customs and traditions, language, arts and literature, government, religion, and economic systems. These elements form the way of life for an entire society, including how people dress, the music they listen to, and how people greet each other.
So religion and culture are not distinct entities. They are highly connected. Don't see each part, but look at the connections. 
Life implies an eternal search for knowledge leading to better and happier living.
Hope this answers your query,Aravind.
Greetings
Dks
23/10/25, 5:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 874
Let me go back to 1975 and IISc. 
My personal task was to shift to a house so that I could bring my wife and daughter to Bangalore. I was staying in the hostel and Lakshminarayan was not vacating my 11th main rented house as his house construction was getting delayed. I didn't want to rent another house. I liked the 11th main house. 
The other reason why I didn't want to rent was the fact of unaffordable rentals.This was due to the fact that Bangalore started growing faster with a lot of small scale industries coming up in large numbers - thousands. Electronics was growing fast and most electronics activities happened in Bangalore. Peenya became the home of thousands of industries. It started growing before the electronics city at the other south eastern end came up in 1978.  A large number of research labs came up. They needed precision instruments, precision manufacturing and modern systems. This led to a number of people migrating to Bangalore and the demand for housing shot up leading to a very high rates in rentals. While both Rajajinagar and Jayanagar extensions were growing fast with thousands of houses at affordable prices, both were not easily reachable from IISc - our bus system was poor-. Also water availability in Jayanagar was poor till the arrival of Cauvery supply. I couldn't afford a house on the new rates of rent in malleswaram.
One day as I was talking to JVisvanathan, Prof Krishnan's research student, he said that Prof Krishnan's house in palace orchards was vacant as he was in the US on a sabbatical visit. My problem got solved. I took the key from him and moved into the house. Then I wrote to my wife to come back to Bangalore. We lived for three months in that house. I call that place an upper class slum as I was disturbed in the night by the blaring sound of automobile horns of returning house members in the neighborhood. The costs of most items were higher.
The economy was poor and costs of most items were shooting up. Some were not available. I had to stock pile baby food as it was in short supply. Open market prices of essentials like rice, sugar, pulses etc were high. We didn't have a ration card at that time. Coming from the UK with its affordable food prices, I needed time to get adjusted.   We lived there for about three months and then I got my 11th main house back. 
The economic and political situation in India was deteriorating faster. Indira Gandhi found governance tougher. So she imposed emergency and arrested most political leaders. Even congress leaders were afraid . Fear percolated everywhere.  Actually ,on the day emergency was declared, I visited my friend R Ramalingam , IPS . We both attended Hindi classes taught by my uncle during the fifties. I was in high school and he was in college. Our friendship continued till he retired from service around 2000. He was in a tense position having arrested many senior opposition leaders like Vajpayee, Advani, SN Mishra etc. I realized the seriousness of the situation. 
Greetings
Dks
24/10/25, 5:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 875
While the whole country was in uproar over the emergency governance without any law to protect common people. Some in IISc supported the government , most opposed it. There was fear everywhere. Police power was visible. But life continued as usual, but with difficulties.
Since I went to Manchester on sabbatical, my salary was paid by the institute. That meant I had a good amount saved. Mr Thimme Gowda introduced me to Mr Byrappaji MLA , a friend of Gowda. He told me that Byrappaji wanted to dispose of his site in the upcoming area of west of chord road extension. It was a corner site. Byrappaji was an honest politician and needed money. So he agreed to sell the site to me. He obtained permission from the CM to sell the site. 
Despite a difficult money situation, I decided to invest in the land. I was a little short of the required amount for the site - the agreed amount was 24000 rupees and I had only 22000. He agreed to collect the remaining balance after a year. Really a very nice person. As I narrated earlier, a tragedy struck and Mr Gowda lost his son. So there was a delay. At the beginning of the next year, the sale deed was registered. 
It was a good deed done and I was happy about the whole incident. 
As I said, this period was a redefining period for me in my research work. I moved into operating systems as my main research area. I focused on two aspects  - one was performance issues of operating systems and the other was optimal scheduling policies. 
I also looked at what the gaps in research at that time were. They are
1. Very little of holistic modeling. Most models were approximate. With a very high precise computing available , modeling can become more detailed . People still were solving small problems and prefered analytical solutions. Looking at bigger landscape in problems was not known. 
2. Computer usage was ignored by most engineers. For instance , communications systems researchers were studying only components and point to point connections. Mr Satchev, chief engineer ,ITI said that power engineers are using network approaches extensively and communications engineers should look at it and solve communications networks problems. Similarly , matrix methods in designing multi storeyed structures in civil engineering was just developing. There was no analysis of irrigation networks and urban water supply systems. I intiated research in these areas. 
3. Optimization techniques were not used to handle large problems. Performance analysis needed to go further. Most solution techniques were approximate and constrained. We needed to go forward in these areas. 
4. Computer science is evolving faster with applications in many domains. I should not restrict to engineering but look at other areas like social sciences ,economics etc.
5. Most research focused on known areas and focused inwards. They didn't look outward applying techniques from other disciplines. Holistic thinking cutting across borders was non existent. 
This knowledge was very useful in the selection of research areas and work. I constantly updated this list.
Greetings
Dks
25/10/25, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 876
The question is
How did I find the gaps?
Normally researchers looked at papers and identify gaps in that paper which can be attacked and improvements found. 
I didn't do that. I went beyond , looked at current contents and analyzed the list of titles. That revealed that most papers are improvements. We didn't see large changes. But occasionally one title talks of a new topic. The number of such new topics was very small. Then I looked at the abstracts to know what type of work was done. Based on these observations ,I realized the overall gaps that I discussed in the last post. That was a great guiding principle for me. 
Next I analyzed the types of research as incremental, small changes, new approaches, new interpretations etc. Based on these , I decided on my future research directions.-
1. Don't stick to an area and keep working on small improvements. Move to a new area. This is not an easy decision. It involves a lot of reading, contemplating, thinking , and developing new approaches. This leads me to another question - how do you know what new areas are emerging? This is a tough question. I will answer it a bit later.
2. One simple answer was to stay in an evolving area. My friends in EE department - about seven or eight of them - in the sixties saw that control systems was an evolving area. Nonlinear control , multi variable control and optimal control were good areas for research. Then they moved to stochastic control. These meant that you applied good mathematical approaches to solve control problems thereby providing opportunities for research. But I was the odd person at that time. I was working on power systems and there were a lot more topics that I could work on. I had opportunities for the next ten years in the power systems area. I found that most US and UK universities moved away from power systems research. But I saw that computers were seen in the horizon and realized that the opportunities for research will be much higher. But it is still in the infancy . I chose the tough option of moving into computer science area for my research.
 I tried to see what was evolving and move there. This principle helped me. I moved into online transaction  processing as the next area with a focus on distributed databases and concurrency control in addition to performance . Then I got deeper into databases and moved into data mining. 
3. Don't work on delta improvements. Focus on modeling, architecture, integration and applications. 
4. Be unorthodox. Don't look at journals for your research. Look beyond . Look for trends. Look at the market. Companies can provide a scope for research.
These helped me in developing a canvas for research and broadened my knowledge and thinking. It shifted me out of a narrow focus. 
Greetings
Dks
26/10/25, 4:28 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 877
Now let me address the question?
How do you know how research is done elsewhere and how new areas are emerging?
The answer is :
Don't focus on your work alone. Don't focus on your discipline alone.
Look outside- at your colleagues in the department, colleagues in other departments, colleagues in other institutions, in other disciplines, in other countries, at industries,at governments, at societies, at applications etc.
That is a serious hobby or activity one should develop and keep looking regularly at the outside world. This is an important learning process. 
Look outside means a little more - observe, identify what is happening, contemplate, understand and infer new ideas or applications. 
Most of us think that books are good sources of such information. Unfortunately that is not fully true. Most books do not provide futurist information. Look at all AI books. They write about existing techniques or impacts ,but don't say how AI will move forward in the next ten years. A few have written about futuristic scenario but pretty vague. Most writers write about the past. Discriminatory information, inferencing, conjecturing  etc are difficult to find in books.  We need to look for other sources - networking, conferences, discussions, email correspondence, attending lectures by experts , looking at youtube channels etc are more useful. 
This concept - look outside - is very useful to most people. A housewife picks better recipes ,better cooking practices from mother in law, from books like Samaiythupar ,from TV, from youtube channels etc. A manager finds better practices in another organization. 
I was lucky in that I got a lot of opportunities to look outside and learn a lot from many organizations. I kept my eyes open and talked to people and learnt what they were working on. 
I started by looking at how people and systems work in public institutions, shops, banks, libraries, schools, transport units etc during my school days. My involvement in many societal activities and my curiosity helped. 
As I had stated early, my regular habit of reading hundreds of books and contemplation broadened my world view. That was my second step in looking outside. 
More in the next.
Greetings
Dks
27/10/25, 5:38 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 878
When I joined IISc , I got a lot of opportunities to learn - first by absorbing a broad spectrum of the culture pervading at IISc. It was a mini India. You can live in your known world of Tamils or Telugus or Bengali or Kannada or Punjabi or you try to know the culture of others and understand India. 
The academic culture encourages all students to work seriously, to read a lot ,to think , to contemplate, to solve problems, to look at challenges and new areas and to work independently. It was a great place to generate large number of concerned and thinking engineers and scientists - deep thinkers, problem solvers who accepted challenges in life. This culture nurtured a number of emerging disciplines in addition to producing thousands of talented people. 
If one looks at research areas pursued at the institute, you will find basic research and you will find nationally useful research - water resources, wind tunnel experiments, power network design, waste water treatment, solar energy, acoustic design of theatres, antennas, space technologies etc. In addition , faculty got in solving hundreds of problems faced by the country in many sectors. This led the institute to be a leader in sponsored projects and consultancy studies. 
One great thing I saw in the institute was visits by many experts both from India and abroad. The invited talks held at regular intervals by experts from India and abroad provided a big window about research happening elsewhere. I learnt about multivariate control systems, Kalman filters, digital filters,  pattern recognition, computer linguistics, Graphics, image processing, space technologies, intermediate technologies - refined sugar vs Kandsari,and many more areas. These lectures opened my eyes to what was happening in the world. I got a very broad picture of the future trends in electrical engineering and computer science. 
The fourth aspect of my learning from the outside started with my computer use at NAL, and HAL in 1966. I saw many users and knew the problems they were solving. I started helping many in programming. I had written programs for many people in IISc and outside.
 Later this got extended to TIFR. I met many scientists there. My friendship with Bukebag brought me in contact with many senior scientists in their Physics and mathematics department. Of course, I interacted with several CS faculty there. It was a great revelation.My decades of association with PSTI and engineers from many states widened my vision about electricity generation and use in India. My association with CBIP helped me in knowing how people work like automatons without thinking. That led to many encounters with senior engineers who didn't understand what a power system was. I felt sad about this ignorance. We did our best to alleviate it through our training of engineers at PSTI. Though I didn't continue my work on power, I continued disseminating my knowledge of power systems and use of computers for more than two decades. 
This kind of opportunity increased with time ,led to my visits to hundreds of institutions and enlarged my vision of the past , the gaps and the future directions.
Let me look at my big  travel with computers next .

28/10/25, 5:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 879
I decided that I needed to know what was happening with computers from 1975 onwards. Computers had a high potential for applications. Initially till 1975, computers were mostly used for solving scientific problems in mostly R and D institutions. The IISc IBM computer was used by our students and researchers from NAL , and defense R and D labs. Similarly ,the TIFR computer was used by mostly scientific researchers. 
This situation started changing in the early part of 1970s. Karnataka government set up a computer centre in 1971. HAL moved away from aerodynamics calculations to store purchase inventory applications. Unfortunately that shift led to the death knell of the HAL computer centre by 1980. 
 IBM1401 was used for data processing and many reconditioned machines were imported during this period into India by IBM.That led to the slow use of computers for accounting and stores inventory.
 So the four decades 1975 to 2015 saw a highly innovative developments in computer science.I had a very close front seat view of computer developments all these years. I participated in these journeys. 
It wasn't just research and teaching but I got opportunities to participate in the computerization of many functions in the industries, governments, Institutions, R and D labs, utilities and later banks. I also introduced not only computer culture in a large number of institutions - many universities, RECs, engineering colleges , management institutions like IIM ,Bangalore, TAPME ,Manipal , and many industries like CMC, TCS, Jindal Steels, and many organizations like central silk board, Department of Space , Department of science and Technology, - ,but also advised them in building computer applications and culture. This culminated with mahithisindhu program and core banking in several banks. 
I introduced several new and just evolving courses in many institutions. Some of the courses are UNIX, distributed operating systems, artificial intelligence, databases, distributed transactions processing, expert systems, software engineering, systems analysis and design, federated databases, etc.
It was a very fruitful and wonderful journey. I will describe it in the next post.

29/10/25, 4:34 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 880
Looking outside is an important requirement not only for researchers but even for organizations, banks, finance institutions and for people employed in various professions. This is a necessity for all today in the changing and uncertain world. Unfortunately, this easy and important requirement is ignored by most people. IT industries like TCS ,Wipro ,Infosys work for other organizations and hence these industries are forced to look outside as a regular business, others go through routine activities and concentrating on internal firefighting. Most professionals and managers look inside and conduct their activities. They don't see what is happening outside. The technological changes kill their organization and their job. 
I am always puzzled why engineers think - you see this is true about our engineers in PWD and electricity and water boards- that what they learnt in their college days is enough and nothing more needed to be learnt? That is due to the looking inside approach. Most industries like ITI, HMT, Heavy engineering corporation etc died. But BEML survived with a transformative outlook. 
Fortunately, Consultants needed to look outside for survival. 
The next step in my work was to go beyond looking outside. Looking outside keeps you online with changes happening in the world. But i wanted to go beyond, and be a change agent and a transformation generator. That meant that I should be part of that change. The next 35 years saw me in that role of transforming education, transforming institutions and industries and initiating changes. 
I became lucky to form associations with many groups and committees in India to know what was happening from close quarters and participate in changes. 
I saw the first generation vacuum tube computer - TIFRAC - at TIFR. Then I saw an early second generation computer with a simple instruction set and a small delay line memory - a modified design of Alan Turing's first machine - called Ferranti Sirius at NAL. Then I used the next level second generation computer at HAL. 
Later I saw and used  a very advanced 64bit second / third generation scientific computer with punched card reader and core memory and FORTRAN compiler - CDC 3600 at TIFR  .Till then, there was no commercial computer development in India. 
The third and fourth generations of computers got developed in the first part of 1970s. This gave birth to 4 bit computers by Intel using VLSI. That year 1971 was the dawn of large and fast developments in computers and the starting of the famous conjecture by Gordon Moore - Moore's law which predicted the growth of computers . That law is true till now. The term microprocessor appeared on the scene.
Both DCM and ORG, a group set up by Vikram Sarabhai in 1961 developed digital calculators. Deepak cables also tried building a micro digital processor in Bangalore. But these didn't succeed. 
Then  I saw companies being set up, struggle and either surviving or dying. PSI was a great example at that time. Dr Ravindran , Vinay Deshponde and others set up this company at that time . They picked up quite a few of our students. It transformed and kept up with technology changes. HCL was set up in 1976.

Dks
30/10/25, 3:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 881
ECIL started building mini computers by this time. They established a big computer group. ECIL diversified into many areas like instrumentation, control and automation, and computers and employed many experts. This was the first serious effort in the country to produce computers - design , develop software and manufacture computer systems. It was a complete unit usable. Initially they built a 12 bits computer. It was used in earth quake tracking in Gouribidanur and it worked for a long time. Then they built 16 bit computers and later 32 bits computers. It was a reasonable business for them. I visited the computer division in 1976.ECIL lacked marketing . They could sell these computers at high prices because of import restrictions. But low cost microprocessors were coming into the market. ECIL didn't capitalize on that. Minicomputers became tougher to sell. So the computer division wound up by 1980 or so. 
By this period, the computer society of India was set up with Prof Narasimhan as the first president, followed later by FC Kohli. 
I have attended more than 50 conferences by 1975 - some conducted by IISc and IITs discussing research work going on in the country, some by professional institutions like Institution of engineers, IETE, IEEE, and IFAC and some by specialist groups like CBIP. The conferences play a great role in our research and our learning. We can test our work based on response received in the conference, we get to know what others are working on and we get a good pulse of the country. 
CSI conducted an annual convention, a well organized program with paper sessions, keynotes and exhibitions. 
It was my first experience of CSI convention with a good mix of academics, computer manufacturers and business users. The mix was very useful learning for me. I knew what was happening in the country. There was no other way of knowing these. And I had a great networking experience. Like CBIP, I could meet the CEOs of many companies . It was simply a great opportunity to know what the different kinds of uses of computers were, how the industries were evolving etc. So I started attending the annual CSI convention regularly for more than the next two decades. I also conducted several conferences under CSI.

31/10/25, 5:30 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 882
The first CSI annual convention I attended in 1976  gave me several revelations. As I said before, we got a good picture of hardware companies. There are quite a few like Hinditran, Computronics etc who supplied imported computers. Hinditron was very successful in supplying oscilloscopes of Textronics and PDP computers like DEC10. Many others supplied computer parts. Some new companies like IBM, ICL,ECIL, DCM ,HCL etc who were manufacturing computers were present in the convention. 
Very few academic institutions like IIT Kanpur, TIFR, NAL, IIM,Ahmedabad participated . IITK allowed several students to attend the convention. That was good practice. Later ,IIT Bombay also participated in CSI conventions.  TCS was coming up then . Many companies presented their efforts to develop programs for business applications with focus being on mostly input output related and data related. Panels discussed future directions on languages. 
Later on CSI introduced one day tutorials on some latest topics and I had conducted several tutorials. 
While in the beginning, in the late seventies, academics felt superior with focus on technical content, slowly business focus became dominant. Only the Bangalore chapter dominated with academic focus till the eighties. I wasn't unhappy with the presence of a large number of senior business leaders. I understood their struggles and their efforts to accept computers. Mr Balan of ACC was one of the first to use computers. He was Prof V Krishnan's brother in law. Mr Jayant of air India was another senior person who appreciated my courses. He made a special mention about my courses.
 It gave me an opportunity to study technology absorption in India. I also saw quite a few companies jumping into application software development.This led to a big change leading to the concept of professional software and the acceptance of outsourcing. TCS was the first to move into this business. This gave them international opportunities. 
This shows how we went through a step by step process in technology absorption. Two changes were observed by me. 
One, we moved from hardware oriented activities to software activities. The Academic world didn't consider software as an important activity. They thought programming was an individual activity for solving problems, not a discipline and not a serious pursuit. They felt work on applications is inferior to technology development. While I worked on technologies, I felt applications have a great potential and there are opportunities for technology based research. But the business world realized its needs and opportunities. 
Two, activities were shifting from foreign to Indian companies. Many companies developed in house applications. I remember Tata companies, ACC, HAL getting into computerization of their accounts. This in turn gave opportunities to consulting companies, creating a whole new concept of outsourcing. An outside company like TCS not only provides their advice - that was what TCE was doing at that time- , but also take responsibility of execution and operations. It is also an end to end responsibility. Trust was established between companies wanting services and companies providing services. It was an integration process, not just a programming activity. It was a great beginning of a new services business , not understood and appreciated at that time. Many even said that we should focus on hardcore computer design and manufacturing and not on applications. I was one of the few who felt that applications were a major futuristic activity. From that time, my relationships with TCS increased with time. Till 1975 , I had a good relationship with both Tata electric companies and Tata consulting engineers on power systems work. Now that shifted to TCS. It grew and continued till 2023 - a very long and fruitful association.

01/11/25, 4:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 883
Let us understand the Indian computer picture in the seventies. 
We can't import computers. There was a rigid import control.Only a few institutions like TIFR, IITs,IISc were allowed to import mainframe computers. Similarly a few companies like TCS imported a computer. A few cities like Calcutta and Pune set up regional computer centres. So computer was a limited resource in the country. It was a very bad situation . Though the department of electronics was set up by the Indira Gandhi government, computer development for business  was of low priority in the country. Activities related to computers were seen with suspicion - the compute will automate jobs and thousands of labour will loose jobs. So it was opposed. Railways and LIC were barred from importing computers. Even UGC was slowly waking up to the building up of computer knowledge in universities. Except JNU and BITS Pilani, no university had courses on computers - a big tragedy. We lived in that ignorant and myopic era till mid eighties. There was allround ignorance. 
Some businesses bought IBM1401. So companies with computers and programmers had advantage over others and got business easily. So business applications prospered. COBOL increased the speed of business application development in the eighties. Database systems ,ERP, internet took businesses to great heights. 
Prof RN realized that CSI is going the business way and there is a need for a serious conference on theoretical computer science. So he started another serious research focused annual conference on FSTTCS. It was run in Bangalore for many years. That was also very successful in building serious research activities in India.
Prof Karunakara raises another aspect here :
"Dear sir,Very nice to know the developments happened in early stages.India was able to grab new opportunities in software domain. What strategies were there at that time for hardware manufacturing? Why India failed(not successful)in that front? What was lacking in our vision?Thanks and regards"
My response
Hardware manufacturing started early in the seventies. As I said before ECIL produced costly mini computers. Later in the eighties, many companies came up - PSI ,HCL,Wipro,NELCO, Zenith,Eiko computers,PCL,  etc. They all struggled a lot. We got a few good designers like Ravindran and Vinay of PSI, Raman of HCL and Sridhar Mitta of Wipro . There were some others like Sun ray computers who stayed for a few years. Tatas went through decades of struggles in hardware manufacturing. They didn't succeed. Some companies died early. Some like HCL and Wipro survived till the early 2000 and then died or stopped hardware manufacture. 
Today, it is only foreign companies like HP, Dell,Acer,Lenovo who supply hardware in India. The high end Server market is dominated by IBM,HP and Sun- Oracle. It is a sad state .
What are the reasons for this sad state of hardware manufacturing.
1. The terrible and strict import control policy that persisted till 1990 didn't make hardware production easy. The cost went up. There was no technology transfer. There were delays in procurement of components. There were inverse tariffs - components taxed more than the finished parts. Keyboards, displays, printers, disk units were all imported. So costs were high. There was an anti IBM lobby in the country for a long time . Not only Congress but Janata party also opposed IBM. George Fernandez banished IBM. It was a disaster. Even Burroughs and ICL struggled in India. 
2. Technology was changing fast. Our companies couldn't afford disruptive modernization. Still ,they moved fast with both Intel and Motorola processors. They built client server architecture. They adapted to LAN. 
3. Cost was high, profit margins were low. Software services gave high profits. So Wipro and HCL moved into the profitable area .
4. Infrastructure required was almost nonexistent. There was no foundry. Getting the latest components was tough. 
5. Talent pool was low. We had just a handful of designers. Hardware design wasn't a priority area in most institutions. CAD labs didn't produce high end designers. They concentrated on low end circuits.
6. Computers were not just hardware. It has a strong software -OS, compilers ,utilities - component . That was ignored by many. Standard OSs like Unix, Linux and windows helped a lot . PSI struggled on software development. It designed its own OS. It had lots of bugs. There was no collaboration with software companies.CMC tried to help in building total systems. TCS had a hardware hands off situation. It wasn't allowed to import microprocessors in the early eighties. 
7. There was no holistic vision of integrating all. Only TCS in the beginning moved into applications. Wipro and HCL woke up very late. Wipro set up a separate company - Wipro systems - for software development. It didn't work well. 
7. There was no support from the government.
8. Networking became a separate activity with CISCO dominating the scene. Network architecting and installation became a separate activity.
Greetings
Dks

Mr Ramanathan shares his experience:
"In Seshasayee Paper & Boards, we tried to import process control equipment in early 80s. Argued that it is no computer simplicitor but in the form of Plant & Machinery integrated with pulp and paper production. Was to improve several process parameters and optimise cost. Spent months to push it at several stages on this rationale. Finally met respective Secretaries on the week before meeting who all appreciated the merit and offered support. In a couple of days at the meeting,  the proposal got rejected as the imported item can be used for office computing. Spending a year, we gave up on rejection !"
Mr Aravind has a comment and query:
"Sir- Did India miss the hardware manufacturing revolution in the 1980s? I recall Kohli talking about the need for this in late 1980s at the IISC., and spoke of protectionist and restrictive policies, bureaucratic hurdles and need for a vision on infrastructure."
My response 
Ramanathan - I have heard many such incidents. Prof Nag was removed as chair of electronics commission at mid night as he didn't get money on licenses. 
Aravind
Yes . Kohli was very upset. It is not Mr Kohli but many suffered by the most negative policy of Indira Gandhi and later Janata party. As I said earlier, we suffered from many sides 
1. A poor import restriction 
2. A high tariff used to harass companies by the License permit raj. You not only pay high tariffs, but pay bribes to the officers. 
3. The biggest was the total absence of hardware infrastructure - we had no foundries. Hegde and Golay set up 1965 to modernize soldering and production of printed circuit boards. Many industries came up but all died due to low business opportunities and profits. I saw bangalore growing fast in electronics in the sixties and seventies ,but the industry struggled by the eighties. It was a tragedy. ITI stayed too long with electro mechanical switches and didn't modernize. Even today, though I am happy with the foundries coming up , we still lag behind internet connectivity, speed, reliability , and we are not ahead of technology , but behind by more than 5 years.
4. The Serious problem is integration. As Ramanathan said we had thousands of industries who wanted to modernize and improve quality with automation and they didn't get permission . They all struggled. Our automobile industry is still struggling. 
5. Government ignored hardware industry, didn't build digital infrastructure. While Malaysia could produce memory chips, Thailand could produce most electronics devices, Japan could produce electronic entertainment, Taiwan produced processors and designs, China and South Korea moved mobile manufacture to very high volumes, what are we doing.Are we so inferior that we are good at tailoring and plumbing and nothing else. It is an unhappy situation. Even the present government is looking backwards, and looking at other countries for technologies. Our government, our R and D labs, our IITs, our industries all look backward and look at America. We are not futuristic. We don't even anticipate technology. 
6. Even when we know a technology evolving in other countries, we couldn't build it here in the initial stages. We need collaboration with foreign companies. When GIS was promoted by DST , I was involved in the program both at DST level and at KSCST level . We struggled to build a good GIS software despite funding. 
7. Our servile,and colonial attitude needs to change and our confidence level should go up. Can we learn from Sundar Pitchai, Satya Nadella,Shantanu ,Arvind Krishna and Arvind Srinivas?
8. Most important, we need a mindset change, a work culture transformation and a strong move towards collaboration.Atmanibharta is not just for manufacturing, but for our minds also, our thinking and innovation also. We have a poor work culture. We don't exert and go to the end. Most academics don't do research. Forget research, they don't have curiosity, don't read, don't think and don't work with passion and goals. They retire mentally at an early age.
Destroy secrecy, promote openness, transparency ,and participation. Contests like Atal innovation  help only at the first level to provide delta inputs.  They don't lead to breakthrough technologies or products.All funding are for survival and firefighting not to move up, not to build breakthrough systems. 
I feel very sad seeing these kinds of negativism in the country.

03/11/25, 5:09 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 885
Dr Sanjay Chitnis continues on this discussion.
"Dear Sir, Greetings for the Rajyotsava day!! Regarding compute infrastructure, we have been always lacking though the government pushed a lot through CDAC etc. Even today though we have a big market, we still do not have Indian companies in computers or the networking market from mobiles, laptops, desktops, to servers and data centers. In consumer electronics, LG India IPO has made their market cap more than the global LG Market cap, but our companies (except few exceptions) are still struggling. What do you think Sir is the reason for this? Why didn't CDAC succeed? Will we face the same problems when Quantum computers come? Thanks in advance!"
My response 
It is a good question and should be the focus of our ministers. 
The first ,biggest problem is to get 360°views on technology. Most have a very small fraction of views. 
Second problem is to know how technology can be developed, nurtured , manufactured, adapted ,used and modified. Most miss several steps in tech life cycle.
While Ashwini Vaishnav looks at one aspect - trying to correct our semiconductor vacuum after a long time. He is pushing many to get into chip manufacture and succeeded. Tatas got into it in a big way. He also diversified chip production to many states like Gujarat, Assam and Orissa. Chip design is with Bangalore and Hyderabad. It is a good decision. But he addresses one problem. 
Mr Piyush Goyal recently remarked on deep tech and has come up with a huge startup fund. I think that his idea is good but implementation was bad. I will discuss it later. 
Let me see the different technologies first. 
Technologies can be viewed as traditional , modern, complex, integrated , engineered, consumable- user oriented. The main complaint is that we focus on user-related issues in technology and ignore technology generation / inventions. That is true. 
Every Technology has a life cycle 
1.Idea generation
2.Development of principles and science
3.Technology development
4.Tech components production
5.Tech utilization
6.Tech diffusion , consulting , integration , etc
7.Tech agile operations and maintenance 
8.Tech modifications
9. Death and generation of new tech. 
We need to understand the life cycle . It is important to see that there are no gaps in all these phases and no actor is ignored or missing.
Who are the actors in that life cycle?
1.Tech generation -R and D labs ,academic institutions. They need long term plans ,goals and commitment, scholarship, futuristic vision. They shouldn't be satisfied with trivial publications. This is not there. 
2. Development - R and D labs, universities, engineering colleges, government policies, government support, grants. Who checks what the R and D labs are doing. Most work on yesterdays problems. 
3. Manufacturing - needs large facilities. Needs great designers. We need design centres to deal with complex technologies. We need architects also. 
4.  Consumer products development and uses- start ups today. Government support is needed. Facilities for supply chain , transport, use ,testing etc are to be built. 
5. Technology absorption - services , consultancy and integration companies. This is doing well.
6. Technology awareness - engineering colleges and polytechnics. They today unfortunately deal with existing technologies and not on current technologies and don't bother about emerging technologies. The entire degree program on AI is a poor showcase. They don't use real life big data.
7. Technology use - consumers, companies, industries, services units etc. 
Unfortunately most actors are not doing their jobs or doing it in part or in silos without integration. Our main weakness is here . The actors are unfortunately focused towards consumer technology usage. Diversity and depth are poor. That is another problem. As they say, we are spreading our resources very thin so depth is not easy to achieve. 
Remember the first essential condition for success 
All these tech segments should be developed ,all the above actors should be involved. 
First reason for our failure - the above holistic ecosystem and actors are absent . We are not solving this holistically but piecemeal. We have only two approaches-
1.Set up a new institution like CDAC with government funding to handle tech development only ,ignoring all other aspects.
2. Fund projects , startups. 
That leaves huge gaps. Industry wants low hanging fruits and concentrates on tech adaption and services, ignoring tech development. 
But there are a few successes in our country. Let us discuss them in the next post.

Dks

04/11/25, 5:36 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 886
We got great visionaries - Dr Homi Bhabha and S Dhawan - who built two organizations - atomic energy and space. Both had a holistic 360°approach in building the two institutions. They wanted freedom from interference from government, transparency, talent and leadership development, international exposure, participative systems, lower level empowerment ,promotional opportunities, good planning , reviews and systematic execution , great resilience. They achieved their requirements. Both created a great working ambiance. They were doing serious work in an integrated manner. They had futuristic plans, no stagnancy. Both organizations are vibrant and moving forward , handle tough problems like GSLV with cryogenic fuels and breeder reactors . Both did good integration of many technologies. 
To some extent, we can add Missiles development by Abdul Kalam to this list.Brahmos has shown its success. Similarly ,the multidimensional air shield displayed during the Sindoor operations is another example of a great design. 
That considerations of a complete life cycle displayed by ISRO/ Atomic energy departments  is missing in CDAC. It was a technology development centre,  but we don't know the users and their requirements. We don't have production units, user facilitation units, user support centres. I say that CDAC did a remarkable job . It built param super computer successfully. But the utility was the problem. 
CDAC is not an industry . It was a development centre and did its job. We didn't have production , application and distribution units to take param forward. I feel it was a success . But the model of propagation of technology was wrong. We need to address the model, not the R and D labs. 
Let us then try to understand the model next. 
What is a technology ? It can be a device, or machine or mechanism as understood by most. Technology is not standalone. It applies scientific principles to produce a useful entity.
Todays technologies are complex. The path from technology to use is not easy. It involves many steps like problem discovery,tech selections, architecture, integration,design,solution methods,building, testing and operations. We don't look at technology but at the connections. 
One thing we should understand and appreciate is the fact that application engineering ,solutions engineering and operations are not trivial. They play a big role in the countries' development and growth.
 Technology can be three fold 
1. Devices and systems for doing a task. They are oriented towards customers - people ,industries or organizations. 
2.Tools and techniques
3.solution provider .
It took a century to develop technologies to use electricity. So after electricity and communications came in, technology is no more an accidental activity. It is developed after a lot of work, some concepts like alternating current and many artifacts like generators, transformers etc before it becomes usable.

05/11/25, 4:47 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 887
Technology can be classified as 
1. It can be called a fundamental one. Examples are Electronics, atomic energy, computers , AI , quantum computing etc. It is a two stage development.  We develop the basic principles first. That is the science component. This may take a few years of research. Then comes the realization stage where we use the principles and build a visible entity . For example ,electronics started with thermionic emissions. Then we invented vacuum tubes. Then came transmission and receiving equipment. That is simpler and easier and done faster. But consider digital computer. While the principles went through decades , the realization of computer was a complex task starting in the late 1930s by Turing to 1950s with ENIAC and EDVAC and it took the next twenty years  to come up with working commercial computers by IBM, Burroughs and Rand. Biotechnology took longer time for science. AI took close to 60 years to get to a working level of principles, but realization was faster. We have been talking about quantum science for 70 plus years. It evolved slowly. Nobody looked at it beyond subatomic physics levels. Its first principle that world is not analog but digital led us to computers. Its next set of principles are leading us to realize quantum computers. Work went on for more than two decades to get some working realizations.
So one thing is clear - technology evolves over a period of time and not instantly. You are not taken by surprise.
This kind of fundamental technology is further divided into
1.Older technologies like electrical , transport etc.
2.Modern technologies like mobile phones , refrigerators etc
3.High technologies which are complex and combine many technologies . Examples are Computers ,IT.
4.Emerging technology refers to technologies coming up like AI ,nano tech, materials, renewable energy , quantum computers etc.
5.Consumer oriented technologies like consumer products and services like ecommerce, fintech etc. 
6.Engineering or manufacturing technologies deal with technologies used in manufacturing and utilities like control, instrumentation, automation, etc. 
7.Energy technologies are technologies used in energy production, distribution and use like solar, biogas, biomass, etc.
This is where the question of Sanjay comes. Where are we in India in this phase? 
While research happens in several places in many emerging areas like biotech, AI, to a reasonable level, research in nano technology , quantum technologies is at a low level. But our sad state is a very weak R and D on materials . It is the development of materials that is spurring most basic tech developments and we are missing it. The second problem is that we miss deep research in AI,quantum ,nano technologies. I call deep research as one which goes through the science ,tech and engineering stages and produce a tangible entity or product. We have disintegrated and disconnected research in our country. The third problem is poor science training. Interest in science is vanishing amongst students. The fourth and a crucial problem is the mindset and work culture of most researchers and academics. Both need major surgeries. 
Most faculty in engineering colleges,universities and even some NITs have a lot of diffidence ,a deficit of confidence, an excuse mindset and a non responsibility approach. They don't even own their courses, have minimal interest in research, don't know about product development, don't read, don't work hard and feel unhappy. The toxic work ambiance is an important reason. But they built the work ambiance partly. 
This aspect of fundamental basis for technology is weak in our country and no institution is looking at it, planning for a detailed list of deliverables , finding funds, building teams, putting them to work and conducting reviews. Government funding alone is not enough here. Peoples involvement, passion ,ownership and participation is needed. A good leader like Bhabha could do it. Why did the government not setup a department for AI or quantum learning from the experience of atomic energy and space departments?
Let us see what we can do in AI that will make our work fundamental  next.
