06/11/25, 4:33 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 888
Let us see how to improve our work in AI.
AI work can be classified as
1.Looking for better AI- more humanistic, general AI- this is fundamental work.
2.Improving existing models
3.Building better models
4.Rectifying biases, detecting fakes
5.Reducing compute time
6.Producing faster hardware
7.Applying AI - millions of apps can be built. India is here mostly. Lots of startups are working on AI apps.
8.Building AI engineering, AI software,AI architecture and AI operations - big IT is moving here.
9. Integration with existing systems , modifications, reengineering .
10.Data engineering,supervisory AI and AI automation.
11.Using AI- adoption and operations
Today I am happy that most institutions specialise in AI. That is great. I see even 15 year old students building AI agents.  But most interest and most activities centre around the existing AI technologies and tools and not inventing new technologies. Why do we focus on chatgpt or gemini or copilot ? Also we are focusing on applications . That is good. 
But can we look for breakthroughs in health, traffic management, security,medicine, garbage removal ,governance etc? Most AI workers don't use real life data. That is a big mistake.
One  good technologist in India says that we should not build LLMs. That is erroneous. Fortunately another good technologist ,Sridhar Vembu, says that we should build Indian LLMs. We need to build an LLM to follow Indian grammar rules and Indian materials and web sites to reduce biases. 
But I am surprised that we are not looking forward to new developments in AI. AI is still in the first stage and we need to build higher levels of explanations, analysis, discrimination, cognition, and intelligence.decision making using multiple methods, unbiased learning etc. 
2. Child technology - many foundational technologies give birth to many child technologies. Biotech gave birth to biomedical technologies. The internet gave birth to fin tech. Communications gave birth to optical fibres , mobile computing and satellite communications. Computers gave birth to data engineering, web engineering, transactions processing, data mining etc. 
2. At the fundamental stage you can't absorb technology. So Technology development takes us to the next stage - tools, artifacts, addons and incremental components and concepts. Look at computers. After computers were produced we got many addons coming up - input devices, printers, displays, tapes, disks, etc and many concepts like software engineering,compilers,operating systems, ether net, LAN, WAN, internet, web, cloud etc. 
Developmental institutions in addition to academic units act in this phase. 
3. We get a solution engineering phase. We transition from simple technology to complex engineering. This phase focuses on giving a great and full shape to a usable entity - a product or process. This phase needs great designers and architects. We lack both.
4. Then comes the application phase. It is here that we have a lot of jobs and most companies concentrate on developing applications or adopting and integrating technologies. The country has enough expertise here. It is not easy work. 
Governments are doing their best. Then where is the problem?
Let us see in next post.

Dks
07/11/25, 4:25 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 889
Let us see the positives for tech development in the country.
1. We have a strong and large set of engineers for the basic activities in high tech areas. Our thousands of years nurtured and practiced learning culture is not dead. It is still there simmering in our society, producing brilliant people.
2. We have a strong digital public infrastructure. This facilitates learning from anywhere ,any source and at any time. Class rooms are secondary now.
3. Our experience for the past 50 plus years - both at KSCST and FAER- shows one common feature of India - our students are keen to work on interdisciplinary and modern topics. They are prepared to learn from outside sources to conduct experiments and build devices. They are aware of the modern technology developments. They have curiosity ,and energy. They are not loaded with the 50 years of the many outdated beliefs carried by their teachers.  This shows we have a bright future if we nurture this curiosity and energy. Many institutions support the students . The number of students working on AI related projects is very high.I have enough evidence on this. 
4.Research awareness is increasing. Many institutions are entering QS ranking now. The NIRF is also pushing institutions. But NIRF needs research focus ,interdisciplinary focus, design focus. 
5. Startups culture is spreading across the country and reaching even tier2 cities. Many startups come up with technology use and absorption. Ecommerce is reaching every place and every item. This is a great positive. Without educational institutional support in most cases, our youngsters are venturing into startups. It is a very strong and thriving culture. Government funding is good.
6. Government liberalized our import and technology policies. Government focuses on improving both research and technology cultures in the country through many schemes and large amounts of funding and credit. Government plays a dominant role in pushing the country forward in tech development through a series of sustained activities. Atal innovation scheme is a good one. Many ministers have sound views on tech development. It is a great positive that government is aware and actively pursuing a set of futuristic policies to lead in technology development. 
Sanjay Chitnis quotes:
"Incidentally just 3 days back Mr. Ashwin Vaishnav had called for a meeting of all stakeholders related to deeptech in Bangalore. The government seems to be very serious about this. The government is also funding many venture capitalists now to in turn give patient capital to deeptech."
7. Some institutions take up big projects. IIT madras is building hyper loop experiments. Many colleges build satellites. A few quantum computers are built by a few institutions.
8. AI is the most impactful discipline today. We can ignore it at the risk of disaster. But most ordinary people are aware of AI. They use mobile phones easily.Most engineering students study and apply AI. Most IT industries have moved into AI and working on AI in the past 20 years. It shows that we are through the first step. It is a big step forward.
9. At last we are going with good speed to build the semiconductor infrastructure in India. It is a big move.
10. We have many great models to emulate . IITs are good examples a great educational and research institutions. NITs are not following them. CDOT and CDAC are determined initiatives to move towards deep tech of that day. Most importantly the departments of atomic energy and Space and also to some extent DRDO proved our capabilities and successes. Why not follow them?. 
Arun Tanksali comments on CDAC:
"There was one area that CDAC did work on which has been very important. This was the creation of ISCII which later on was the foundation for Indic languages in Unicode. Prior to ISCII (and, more practically,  Windows supporting Unicode with Indic languages) there were a plethora of incompatible formats which were in play. You needed to know which font/tool was used to display content back. ISCII, which matured in the mid 1990s, was quite transformational. I was part of a govt committee in the early 2000s to standardize Indic support in mobile phones and it took many years of pushing to get full support from the phone manufacturers in the feature phone era."
11. Government funding is remarkable. Are we,the recipients, using the funds properly?

08/11/25, 5:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 890
Then what are the negatives to tech development? 
Negatives mostly centre around two major entities - people and organizations. 
Let us look at people first.
1.  The biggest hurdle is our mindset. It is colonial, submissive, negative and with a careless attitude- chalta hai. We are loaded with lots of beliefs,and most of them are no longer valid. These traits make us a less mentally strong human. 
2.  We just move along, we have aspirations definitely.but we have low will power. We are normally risk averse, failure averse people. We are also timid and diffident people.
3. Some times, we hear that India is a traders country. We broke that myth with strong IT services and we have shown to the world that we are a strong digital nation. Unfortunately, we are happy being the followers , not the developers and depend on foreign imports. Now we find that attitude is putting us in danger and we are waking up to the realities. 
4. We lack killer instinct. 
"killer instinct" is a strong desire to win or succeed, often achieved through an aggressive, ruthless, and determined approach that prioritizes dominance and goal attainment over other considerations. It involves a powerful, tenacious will to succeed and can be seen in both sports and business as a determination to defeat opponents and crush obstacles to achieve a goal at any cost. 
That means we lack passion. We lose interest and don't put in sustained efforts to takeup a challenging job and complete it successfully despite failures. Most us don't own our job's responsibilities. 
I was interviewed by Mr Sapru from Indian Express in the late eighties. The question posed to me was what ails Indians ? My answer was we lack the killer instinct. That seems to be true even now. 
5. The first requirement for a strong tech development is curious thinking habit. This habit is effectively destroyed by our educational system. Managements think discipline is essential in an educational institution.
So managements control faculty - faculty shouldn't think . I visited a famous engineering college for AICTE inspection. I asked a question to the principal. He was replying . The chairman asked him to shut up . I had to take strong exceptions to that behaviour. Tamilnadu institutions overdo discipline to the level of slavery.
Faculty in turn tells the students to keep quiet. So thinking gets killed at a young age. We want mental slaves. Most organizational leaders love slaves - yes people. Even reading habits are coming down. This is a serious problem.
6. While our Indian youth is all set and ready to move into modern areas, others put brakes on that and consequently our tech movements. Who are they?
 There are two sets of people - influencers and powerful authorities. They are having 50 years old beliefs. They have not moved with time. Most senior faculty stick to old tech ideas and prevent others moving forward. It is the seniors who build and control the ambiance and don't allow changes and discourage others taking up challenges.
7. In addition to curious thinking, both teachers ,and taught as well as engineers need a cognition - to understand technology, engineering, design,multi dimensional problem solving of real life large problems and most importantly the cycle of transformations.  
Prashant Joshi gives an interpretation:
"An “ecosystem” like thinking is required is what I believe."
My response
"Holistic thinking on all dimensions are needed."
Joshi again
"Yes. I generally consult on such areas under the "Ecosystem" term and call most systems as "Systems of Systems". It is getting larger and interdependent. It will need great focus on getting things done. As you mention in your article above, a lot needs to be thought first; and I notice a lot of eager to get into action for an idea. So things need to move from idea->action to idea->thinking/debating/decision/evaluation->action."
Conceptual clarity is important. 
A wonderful explanation by MK Srikant on the need for out of the box alternative thinking :
"Interesting! Thanks for the detailed analysis, it made good reading. One of my observations ( possibly re- phrasing part of your text): right from the education system, we are tuned to adopt what is handed down to us, rather than think afresh to create totally new ideas. That leaves us forever playing catch-up with the leaders."
My response 
I agree. We need imaginative, constraint free out of the box thinking. It makes a person very successful in the tasks he or she takes up.Einstein gives importance to imagination. Newton says we sit on top of the learnings of many to look for ideas. Miss Marple -Agatha christie- looks at the behaviours of known people to solve murders.
“Immature poets imitate; mature poets steal; bad poets deface what they take, and good poets make it into something better, or at least something different. The good poet welds his theft into a whole of feeling which is unique, utterly different from that from which it was torn.”—T. S. Eliot
"All creative work builds on what came before. Nothing is completely original."
Austin Kleon

09/11/25, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 891
Let me continue with the negatives.
8. We have a big leadership crisis. That is a major lacuna in our system which for millennia respected and built a good and thriving leaderships. The traditional village Panchayat system developed good leaders.Today our system generates only status quoist leaders - just keep moving and performing fire fighting.
I have seen hundreds of leaders - university vice chancellors, directors of IITs and NITs, Principals of engineering colleges,CEOs of companies and CMD/EDs of banks, secretaries in the government- and interacted with them. Most, I think,are not competent to be leaders. I am very unhappy with the quality of leadership. Most leaders don't meet the requirements of a leader. 
What are some qualities you expect in leaders?
1.Look forward, be a futuristic thinker.Build visions for the next 50 years. See the Modi's vision of viksit Bharat. I have seen several visionary leaders - some names are Bhabha, Sarabhai, Dhawan, Abdul Kalam, GVK Rao- chief secretary of karnataka-.
2. Break the status quo ,static attitude. They understand the thriving ,living and dynamically changing organism that is their organization. Many leaders have led their organization to a higher level. I can't forget Mr H V Narayana Rao who rose to be chairman of KEB and CEA. He created a participative working style and changed CEA.I remember Mr Nambiar who built KELTRON. He constantly thought about improving and changing his company. Another person is Mr Sambamurthy, who introduced financial inclusion for the first time in the country. He also took IDRBT to great heights. Dr Chakravarthy , CMD Indian bank and later PNB and later deputy governor, RBI was a clear thinker and knew the future directions. He was fearless. 
3. Focus on building organizational synergy, strength and strategies. Again, we may find many leaders who were great human analysts, who understand their organization and customers.
4. Change agents - initiate changes to take the institution to a higher level. I recall both Prof Prahlada Rao and Prof Holla changing MSRIT and RVCE. Both institutions grew better over the years. Prof Nityanandan brought research culture in TCE ,Madurai, converting a pure teaching institution into a teaching and research institution.
5. Trend setters. Mr FC Kohli is a good example of creating a new area and building a large organization.
6. Builders who take the organization through new evolutions ,transformations, uncertainties and failures . I see both Mr Ramadorai and Mr Chandra of TCS as great examples of this category. They took TCS through many innovations and transformations.
7. Leadership builders- Tata trust is a good example here. Hindustan lever built a culture of leadership generation. Leadership generation is important in business and politics. ABVP has been generating leaders. Prof CNR Rao built science and scientists across the country.  IITs generate leaders who are fearless, who think independently and work for building  greater institutions. PV Indiresan,director,IIT,Madras, is a good example of a fearless leader. Most IIT , CSIR labs,DRDO directors lacked leadership qualities.
 Let me summarise my views based on my observations and discussions. 
1. Most followed existing practices. Family oriented businesses dominate India. Most obeyed their bosses. Most followed rules
2. Most didn't understand their institutional goals ,functions , the environment. This is a sad but real factor in India. I can even say that most were just time keepers.
3.Most leaders follow a safe route - follow rules, don't displease the authority, create a submissive work culture, stabilise the activities etc. They don't want challenges , don't even want changes leave alone transformations. They are static and totally inward looking, myopic people without goals, vision, and future proofing. 
4. Most leaders follow some established outcome based factors like production, growth, profit, number of students passing the exams or getting placements etc. They don't think. 
5. Most leaders don't have goals , or 360° views or futurist visions . This is a huge drawback.
6. Most are inward looking. They ignored the outside . They don't see changes happening outside. They don't plan or strategise or go forward. They think their organization is a static entity . Most engineering college seniors and principals are rooted in the 20th century thinking. They don't know what changes are happening in this 21st century. Nobody changes an organizational structure. Mr Kohli, Prof Dhawan, Mr GVK Rao made great and highly transformative changes in their organizations. They set the trends and were agile. We don't have agile leaders. 
7. Transformative leadership - people who create a new culture. Mr Nandan Nilekani is a good example. He built aadhaar ,a unique ID for all Indians. It transformed the country. Similarly, UPI is another great transformative system. Another example where I was involved is TEQIP,a world bank funded program. It transformed our engineering education. It brought in strong research and computer culture. Mr RN Mathur is the leader responsible for this.
8.Managements are myopic and don't have transformative mind. One big failure was Infosys - not understanding the AI vision of its then CEO, Vishal Sikka. They missed the bus. There were many known and good leaders in Infosys who torpedoed Sikka's vision. 
9.Actually, none of our companies had a bold plan for either AI or big data or quantum computing or nano technologies. Only the government came up with many bold plans like swatcha Bharat and smart cities and large plans for renewable energy transformations. Modi is a tall leader based on his initiatives like Nehru in those days.
10. Management education focused on business. But Prof NS Ramaswamy saw the importance of people, society, traditions and tried to build a transformative IIM at Bangalore. He assembled a lot of eminent members of faculty.Unfortunately, he couldn't carry his colleagues with him. He worked tirelessly on his ideas of bullock carts, bullocks throughout his life. He was a great friend of mine. 
But the biggest problem is that we are not building education systems which can integrate theory and practice and inculcate leadership culture in a large number of young people who then can achieve breakthroughs in science, technology ,politics and governance and face AI happily. That is the need of the hour.
Mr HS Nagaraja concludes
"What we need to take home from this post is " we should stop developing Slaves, but stop not Creating Leaders" Sir, Hope my comprehension is correct.Thanks, with regards. Nagaraja HS"
My correction
We should convert slaves to thinking humans and start creating visionary leaders.

Thanks,Srinivasan
Let me return to our discussion on negatives.
I said the negatives depend on two entities. We saw the negatives related to people . Let us look at the negatives with organizations. 
Most of our organizations -be it a governmental one or research labs or educational or bank or industrial or services one - have been mostly built during the British time by the British. So they followed British policies ,traditions and methods. Unfortunately , we still follow those British colonial policies and methods. We didn't change .We didn't understand the problems due to these outdated organizational principles and practices.
1. If we want to move forward , then we need to know about 21st century principles and not 19th century principles. The world has changed a lot. Let me list a few aspects that need to be addressed.
2. 21st century organizations are decentralised . That is no central control. Even IT is decentralised . Most computer and software purchases have been left to individuals. BYOD is acceptable. People adopted technologies like internet, whatsapp, social networks ,mobile apps faster than organizations. Responsibility rests with people. Management has little role in control.
3.21st century is innovation centric. We want 1000 ideas to bloom.Routine work is dead. Managements should focus on innovations. Managements should understand changes and the impacts of technologies like quantum and AI. Existing generic management principles need a total revamp.
4. An organization is a lively and dynamic one changing with time. Most of our people stick to old theories and principles. This frustrates younger people. It is a serious problem. One reason for a large number of startups coming up in the country is this. Startups don't follow the outdated rules, they have freedom to work, they don't need approvals. There is no hierarchy, no central approvals, no delays, no budget constraints. Startups are agile, and change rules every day. 
5.our organizations focus on rules and not activities. They ignore people. But 21st century organizations give primacy to people. We have to move there. 
6.21st century is a digital organization based on AI,data, mobile, online ,web based, and not paper and files based, multi layer approval based, not slow in decision making. Automation dominates now.
7. We have a toxic organization today, not a happy working place. Everyone has many pressures- work pressure, supply chain problems, electricity problems, customer problems, regulatory problems, delivery problems, manager"s abuses etc. Most people lead a stressful life.
Here is a comment by   Sonamkumar 
"Good morning, Professor.I have an observation:"Authorities and donors are more interested in evidence of efficiency, than in children's well-being and learning."This was reported in a news article too, and it's very true - we face this emergency of compliance at the 'junior' level to the very core, that most of our efforts need to be channelised to documentation.How can we get our of this and concentrate more on the teaching-learning practice?"
Let me look at my encounters with this toxicity. 
I had full freedom at IISc to teach and evaluate and select my research areas. Nobody compelled me to work. But when I want some purchases done or attend conferences or spend my project money, the administration, purchase and finance come in the way with a ton of hurdles, delaying my activities. I missed many conferences because of these delays. Our admin is notorious for losing requests. 
Initially we had an admin to take care of student admissions and scholarships and a small accounts unit. Departments were responsible for all activities. Institute appointed a deputy registrar in 1965, then a finance officer, then a purchase officer and so on. All these officers destroyed us. Centralised admin is a bane of India. 
Let me look at my worst encounter. I went to Trivandrum to conduct a PhD oral for a student at Kerala university. The supervisor left the university. No supervisor was appointed. I conducted the open viva. None from the department attended the viva. Then I was told that i had to visit the finance department of the university to submit my travel bill. I went there with the candidate's husband. The officer wanted me to be identified by someone from the university. That was tough ,since the supervisor had left the university and none from the university attended the viva. See my situation. I had many such encounters in many universities. Most had really archaic ,impractical rules which effectively destroyed work and made people lazy. "Not working" became a credit. Responsibility and ownership were ignored. 
This shows we ignored the primary function and moved to a secondary function or a non function. 
This toxicity kills innovation, thinking and motivation.

11/11/25, 4:04 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 893
Let me continue with organizational problems.
6. Most organizational practices in India are based on mistrust. So we build a hierarchical structure- worker, supervisor, manager, general manager,director,MD etc. But in todays environment and with younger population, this won't work. People should be treated with empathy, respect and freedom. All these three are missing. Fear is ruling in most colleges and universities. Many Indians working outside India don't want to comeback even though there are great opportunities. They hate our toxic workplace. Government created opportunities but organizations - private,public or government - have not gone through reforms. Some government organizations like Space, Atomic energy, IITs succeeded in providing motivation , freedom and respect to their people. But others including many CSIR and DRDO labs used a lot of centralized bureaucracy and didn't give freedom or fast approvals to progress in research. I was puzzled with that level of babudom in other places and then I encountered it at IISc. IISc fortunately had escape mechanisms to solve admin problems. We changed rules, created new structures with power with committees of faculty, bypassed admin sometimes etc. 
7. The existing structure doesn't give freedom to people to experiment and innovate. Budgets are not allocated in a flexible manner. So this kills innovation and creativity. This means most faculty look for easier and small problems that don't involve large experimentation, many equipment and large budgets. Purchase of equipment is a torture in most institutions. You go through hundreds of approvals and get finance. We could modernize many institutions through TEQIP funding. Breakthroughs won't come through such a fixed, rigid, top heavy organizational structure. Even our industries follow archaic practices and organizational structures suited for the 20th century. They think that innovation is someone else's responsibility, not theirs. So they grow in one direction. AI will give a deathblow to all Indian industries unless they change. Industries don't know what should be the role of many managers. Automation of office, finance, HR, supply chain have made managers redundant. Our management education is blissfully following old practices, unaware of this constant tsunami.
8. All organizations think that building and nurturing new and emerging areas in science and technology is the governments responsibility and not theirs. We need to kill this excuse mindset of organizations to prosper intellectually. 
9. The first change - remove fear, trust people, give them freedom to work. This means in the case of industries, government and banks - remove supervisors and automate supervision, remove managers.Create a strategy group. Focus on innovation. Form teams and give them freedom and budget to work.  Empower groups with small number of people. Don't just focus on profits but on customer satisfaction.
10. The second change is the need to build a transformative agenda to meet the uncertainties and changes. Here we need to work together with government , universities and industries. That synergy is now available in a small measure. Make that - create a unified planning group- a priority. 
11. Go for a holistic planning and implementation methodology. The synergy and holistic planning starts with establishing linkages between academic institutions and industries with government support. I think that this happened first in Bangalore - a collaboration between IISc, HAL, Defence and later NAL,ADE,GTRE . I have seen this collaboration functioning effectively. Then we had collaborations between IISc, CEA and electricity boards, CPRI and power industries . Much later the IT wave came up with collaborations on many sides with government support, with talent pool generation , government support and the flourishing of small scale industries without the toxic culture. We had similar collaborations in areas like atomic energy - Bhabha was in IISc initially -, space - Sarabhai was in IISc and Dhawan was the builder- and later defense. So I saw synergy solving problems. We need to take it a much higher scale and break the silos approach adopted now. 
What do I mean by holistic development?
It is not wholesale revamp with systemic changes . That will lead to resistance.
It is not development across the board - a general set of rules governing all stakeholders.
It is a connected sequence of developments removing gaps and problems for each area and group. We need to build
1.synergy and connectivity or alliance- government -institutions- industries
2.visionary leadership
3. Large projects coordinated by the various entities
4. Large participation and team work.
5. Lab to market links.

12/11/25, 6:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 894
Let me conclude the discussions. 
1. Do we need tech development? Even Prof Raghuram Rajan was skeptical. There are many who feel we are doing fine. But the power circuit tripping that happened several years ago and the recent Delhi airport GPS spoofing as well as many incidents highlighted by CERTin show that we will lose our security and independence if we don't get into serious tech development.
2. The government realised this before academics and industrialists and is planning to put in massive finances towards AI, quantum , and other emerging areas. But a concerted effort on data generation, protection, privacy and use is needed. 
3. I am sure organizations and industries will wake up to reduce toxic working atmosphere and create a clean,open, participative, thinking and fearless atmosphere. Our strong startups culture and our great support to the growth of GCCs promises this happening.  But what about private colleges, universities and research labs? Who will clean them? Who will change them? Government should open up and support building a large number of Coursera kind of models from school level onwards. Otherwise that gap will exist. Many of our educational institutions running as a business and for profit will die.
4. But the biggest problem is the faculty in our institutions. How do you change their attitude from excuses and escapism?How do we make them to go positive, to be curious, to think,to read, to innovate, to build large projects? How do we make them take responsibility to be up to date on technology? How do we motivate them? I have no answers. Maybe the millennials will change these negatives. I still see several blaming the environment and not putting their efforts. 
5. But the future looks good. The threat of AI will wake up our faculty and school teachers. It will hive a huge jolt to the 18th century vintage colonial universities.
Quantum will change our existing analog concepts and analog thinking and analog problem solving and all areas will move to quantum sciences like the mid 20th century shifted engineering to mathematical problem solving and late 20th century shifted engineering to computer solutions. Survival depends upon updating ones knowledge, relearning the new engineering and rethinking about jobs.
The alpha generation will destroy the other generations in the next 10 years,I guess. Even Z gen have to change their attitude. 
The impacts of AI will be severe. Most industries will dismantle their C suite and shed their managers. Everyone will be part of the industry and business. A new culture will replace the existing toxic culture.
We have a proactive government. We will move forward. The march will be led by Z gen and alpha gen.So the situation is not bad or dismal but seniors need to wake up to this new reality.

13/11/25, 4:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 895
Let me return to my IISc timeline of 1975 76 again.
A quick review of my stay at IISc:
The period 1965 to 1973
It is a period of initialization into research culture and also a perood of good research work in a modern area- computer based problem solving for power systems . It gave me a lot of exposures:
Work in several emerging areas like programming, nonlinear equations, nonlinear optimization, computational problems of convergence and errors, data collection,etc.
The period also gave me conference exposure and networking with most of the countries' leading power engineers. I also enlarged my knowledge scope by teaching many courses at IISc , programming at datamatics , power systems at PSTI. That broadbased me . That reduced my fear unknowns. That increased my appetite for learning. It defined me as a person who wants to learn, explore, help others and organizations, teach and look at new problems. These improved my confidence level and my competency to high levels. I want to call this period a period of gaining maturity and academic respectability.

The period of years from 1975 was definitely an active period for me - a period of broadening my areas and vision and I call this a consolidation period. The institute started expanding in the seventies. The institute got into a major ,long lasting reform - setting up new departments and centres. We got CTS , centre for theoretical studies,a unique experiment initiated by George Sudharshan. It was set up by Prof Dhawan in 1972.  It brought in physicists , Prof Sharatchandra, genetics expert and two biologists, Prof Madhav Gadgil and Sulochana Gadgil. Dhawan also brought in Prof GN Ramachandran to start the new molecular biophysics unit and Prof CNR Rao to set up the solid state chemistry department and later the materials research lab. It was also a period of many senior faculty joining IISc from abroad. 
So i started learning organizational structures and cultures and developed many friendships.
But my interest was kindled and I became excited by two developments initiated by Prof Dhawan .AKN started ASTRA and I got fully involved with it working with Prof Krishnaprasad. KS Jagadish was involved in developing sustainable building technologies like compressed soil blocks, composite roofing etc. ASTRA got land at a village called Ungra, about 100 kms away from Bangalore in Kunigal Taluk. 
Similarly ,KSCST was started and I got involved with it also and built a strong linkage to KSCST , getting involved with most of its activities . I got acquainted with Mr MA Sethu Rao, the first assistant secretary of KSCST. He was running the office from IPC with Panduranga assisting him. We produced the first annual report. We started a project on community biogas and I designed one for IISc employees. Our focus of use at that time was cooking. I realised electric lighting will be a better option.

Greetings.dks
14/11/25, 4:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 896
As I discussed earlier, this association with ASTRA brought me close to the rural problems and I started looking at energy in a holistic manner.
I normally don't see a technology and start working on it. From the beginning, say from 1962 onwards,I had two principles for exploration. I don't just jump into a discipline. My two principles are:
1. Interdisciplinary - can I link another discipline with my discipline. This principle made me do a BE project on using electronics for speed measurements - stroboscope. That got enlarged during my ME days. I wanted to solve power systems problems using computers. That opened up many areas and avenues. I could create a variety of views and build a large picture of power systems planning,control and operations and that gave a clarity to the discipline of power engineering.
2. The second principle was applicability. When I looked at power systems, the application area was clear - utilities and my initial focus was planning and my later focus was control. With computers, while I initially worked on system areas like operating systems, later on I moved into a common activity seen in many sectors - transactions. I started my work on transactions processing.Banks and commerce were two major sectors where transactions were seen as the important activity and the transactions processing can be automated.While Commerce worked on it from 1980 onwards but moved forward faster, Banks started on it from 1970 onwards but were slow to move forward. 
While these two principles governed my research activities till 1975, I realised an important factor to doing better research. This brought in focus two new principles.
1. Look outside. Don't just look into the developments taking place in your disciplines. Many unknowns are waiting outside. 
2. Don't look at parts ,but understand connections. Connections may occur within a discipline like - in the case of computer science ,parts are hardware, operating systems, languages and grammar, algorithms, data, etc and these are interconnected . Recall the famous statement - Gates takes away what ever Grove gives. This explains the impact of OS on system availability or performance.
I also sensed areas which are loosing research interests. While I was in the engineering college at Madurai, I saw the syllabus was heavy with machines - motors, generators ,transformers, IC engines etc. There wasn't much of an understanding of power systems. I saw the IISc program on power systems in 1964 and quickly understood that electrical discipline was going away from machines towards systems - both power and control- and electronics. Similarly ,I learnt about analog computers and taught analog computers in 1968 ,but realised that analog computer is dying and transformed the course towards digital computing. But I found colloges teaching analog comouters even in the early eighties. I moved out of 8085 and taught 8086 and its variations, but most colleges were teaching 8085 even during the nineties. This capability of identifying obsolate topics was useful to me to be current in research. But I didn't ignore the old practices like wood burning stove.
So when I moved into ASTRA, i got several opportunities of choosing new areas based on realities. I got a holistic look at energy and its connectivities. 
1.Energy means many forms - chemical,electrical, thermal, mechanical etc.
2. We think that energy means only coal, oil and electricity. But I realized that there are more types of energy. Bio energy - bio gas, biomass - are other major sources of energy with many routes of compression. 
3. Rural people used both firewood for cooking and animal - bullock- energy for agriculture and transportation prominently in those days.
4. Energy conservation data were not available for rural areas. 
5. Energy is connected with sources, generation, transport, conversion, consumptions by many sectors. So there are many connections which can be studied. 
We- myself and Prof Krishnaprasad- ran a course on energy resources, systems, transformations and uses for engineering college faculty in 1975. I started looking at many problems of integration and use of several energy forms and also on improving energy supply and use in villages and districts. This activity continued for the next 25 years. 
Greetings. Dks

 I wrote before that 1976 was a very heavy year for me.I had to manage my family with my daughter growing up and falling sick. She used to cry a lot when she got fever. I consulted Dr Seth, a well known pediatrician living in palace orchards. We got the vaccinations done at our IISc health centre. She used to be scared of many things. She was attached to me then. She will be sitting on my lap whenever I was free and home. Both my uncles visited during this period. 
My elder uncle wanted to go to Shringeri and I accompanied him by night bus. We were to reach by 5am, but got there late, by 10am. We took bath , had darshan of Abhinava Vidyatirtha mahaswamiji. He knew about my uncle, his involvement with the village and his leadership in reviving and conducting Shankara Jayanthi in the village for more than 25 years. We had lunch in the mutt , a simple lunch. Since accommodation was not available, we decided to return by night bus to Bangalore. We got down at Yeshwantpur bus stop and reached home by 630am. As soon as my wife opened the door, my daughter jumped into my hands even before I stepped into the house. My uncle commented that she is attached to me. Whenever I got visitors and was talking to them, she would be sitting in my lap. Those were great days. Later in 2012 onwards ,I spent a lot of time with my grandson Neel. Those were also memorable days. 
I got into so many new activities. I started research on urban water supply and distribution systems while in Manchester and continued that work in Bangalore. We ,myself and my student Shirasagar, developed many methods and presented them at a CSI conference in Pune.This interest in water supply led me to the study of Bangalore water supply systems. It also started at that time. We found it difficult to get computer time at our IBM machine. So we did the computer work at IIT Madras which got a new powerful IBM 370 computer.My friend Muthukrishnan was there. My another IISc friend S Raman was also there. I knew many faculty in the EE department. Prof Mahabala joined IIT madras by then. So I spent time discussing with him.We became great friends. More on this study in the next post.

16/11/25, 5:26 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 898
Let me discuss my first consultancy project - planning a new power distribution system for Bharat Coking Coal , Jharia.It wasn't an easy task. 
The problem definition was vague - to improve their distribution system. What does it mean?
I went by the general knowledge on distribution:
The designs are poor. There are power shut downs. Costs are high. Losses are high.
So I got into an unknown situation. My job is to define the problem, describe the constraints and find solution methods. This was a challenge. There are no text book models or solutions. There were no text books then. Most practical designs used adhocism with assumed ,not realistic data- my discussions in 1969-70 with Mr Narayana Rao about data for Bangalore distribution came to mind. He didn't rely on data, didn't collect data but assumed growth rates for each region. Most substations didn't collect data. Let me discuss my exposures to different kinds of problems and my approaches to find solutions in a later post. It is an interesting learning experience for me.
I thought about the vagueness and newness of the distribution problem. I didn't run away, but wanted a novel approach to solve this. So I took the the first step that was to define the desirable outcomes of my planning exercise.
Distribution planning implied finding answers to questions :
1.What should be the optimum voltage for the next 10years ?
2.what are optimal locations for substations and what should be their sizes?
3.What should be the routing for connecting all substations and line - conductor- sizes?
4. What should be the optimal power factor? 
Unfortunately, there were no mathematical models and solution procedures available to deal with distribution systems. Power systems design of a EHV grid is well defined. We used analog models first- our use of AC network analyzer in the EE department from 1959 to 1972- and by mid sixties we moved to digital solutions. Both load flow and optimization models and solutions were developed including by me. But that was not the case for an urban distribution system.
The characteristics of electrical distribution:
Load density is higher. 
Load density varies. Load grows in each area with increased activities in houses, commercial areas, industries,and other utilities like water,transport etc. We need to estimate the load growth for each area based on past data.
New urban areas were developing. 
The entire city load grows very fast.
Improper design - use of extension philosophy- will result in a high percentage of energy losses, poor regulation, overloading and frequent power shutdowns. We saw the impact of this at the power supply at IISc in 1966 from a rural 400kv feeder and we converted IISc to 11Kv supply to reduce shutdowns and increase reliability in 1970.
So I had to devise a solution architecture and I used several models including brute force methods and searching in a big parametric space. It was challenging and I completed the work within a year successfully with a full design. 
I completed this project within a year and we had three reviews one at Delhi ,another at Kolkata with Gen Grewal, chair,Coal India and SS Murthy and a third a presentation at a conference at Ranchi. Then I wrote the final report.
Greetings. Dks
17/11/25, 4:35 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 899
The visit to Manchester university exposed me to the latest developments on computers - both hardware systems and operating systems. My foundation in OS became strong. I also got involved with performance evaluation and programming methodology. Programming methodology became the new software engineering area. My exposure to languages, particularly to ALGOL, also helped me to absorb programming methodology. These helped to develop new courses and conduct research in the scheduling aspects of OS. 
The years 1975 and 76 opened my mind. My work level was high. I got involved with three totally new areas - water supply and distribution systems , electrical distribution systems and energy systems. I completely broke the barriers in mind. Till 1970, I thought that I was an electrical engineer. These new developments broke the borders between disciplines. My mindset changed. Then slowly I diversified to new areas. So by 1976, I became a generic problem solver with a set of holistic approaches and a strong multiple ,close to 360° views on problems occurring in many disciplines. I had developed broader interdisciplinary views even beyond engineering. I had been merging solutions methods with large engineering problems. It grew further later into an integration with social constraints and user perspectives. I got a better understanding of what is engineering? I realised that it is not concepts ,not theory , not techniques , not models , not technologies but beyond all these. It is a highly interconnected , multilevel ecosystem. It is connected to the world, to the producers , the suppliers ,the transporters and the consumers. It consumes resources, changes living style, pollutes the environment, divides a society into haves and have nots. It has impacts on society, lifestyle, economy and nature.It was a great opening of my mind for me. The changes were slowly sinking in my mind during these two years.
I was teaching new courses and conducting research in new areas, teaching at PSTI and datamatics and took up big project on Jharia distribution. That itself is mouthful for a person starting a family with a new child. But there was more work. 
My involvement with KSCST led me into two new projects - one was on community biogas plant and another on electricity planning. KSCST normally built a working group around a project with faculty and user representatives. So our first project on biogas didn't have any user department. That project was the first project taken up in 1975 when AKN was not in India and Krishnaprasad was taking charge. But the second project was initiated after AKN joined back to ASTRA and KSCST. He left electro chemistry and got involved with these two organizations. 
The work on the project on electricity planning was challenging. It needed data and data was not available. So it didn't progress well. 
I almost spent more than  half an hour every day with AKN, as Krishnaprasad left the institute and went to Eindhoven.I got involved with discussions in both ASTRA and KSCST.I even attended meetings with Prof Dhawan and the then finance minister ,Ghorpade and met Mr GVK Rao, the chief secretary. That was my initial introduction to the karnataka government. I met several senior officers and politicians in the state. This practice continued and intensified for about three decades and got me involved in many projects of the state. I started learning in detail about the state. Initially ,I had a number of friends in KEB and I tried to solve the transmission design with the help of Mr TNR Rao and Mr Basavalingiah,SE technical in the early seventies. 
But in 1976, I met many IAS officers in different departments. My involvement with both DTE and DSERT started at this time and continued for many decades.
But it was ASTRA that opened my eyes further. Let us discuss it next.

18/11/25, 4:00 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 900
I was looking at engineering and solving large problems occurring in the engineering sciences and I expanded my scope to cover many aspects of engineering . I was quite successful in that effort. 
But now- in 1975-76-  I got an opportunity to add another major dimension to my knowledge and problem solving skills- looking at social and economic problems faced by people and solutions found from engineering systems. It enlarged my vision - from mostly inside views with a small window of outside- to an out - in view. I forced myself to look outside for problems, not inside. I moved away from thinking of power, water, etc towards people, their needs, their consumptions, equity,economy, affordability, finances and finding optimal and feasible solutions. It was a remarkable and wonderful shift. It changed my thinking and mindset. It enlarged my visions,understanding and perceptions.
I already had a reasonable exposure to economics - mostly technology based economics and supply side issues. I got interested in economics from childhood even before I became aware of engineering. The reasons were supply constraints, resource mobilization, inequality, poverty, and merchants tight control on the economy. I saw a full play of these in most fiction books at that time - again it was alaiosai of Kalki that gave a full picture of food scarcity and poverty. Low purchasing power in villages worried me. I saw the need for  management of micro economics while helping my uncle in the planning , gathering resources - finance, people, materials like milk,sugar, etc-, transferring information through transparent manner, accounting with integrity and implementing the plan successfully in spite of uncertainties. I realised that economics was connecting many domains and supply was the key issue.
Later I studied in high school about our five year plans. I got exposure to industrial and projects based problems. I got a vivid picture of consumption economics due to wrong planning when I heard Mr Schumachar in 1960 in Madurai. That induced me to look at economics in a mathematical optimization mirror. In 1963, I was looking at food supply issues in a student conference again as a student in Madurai. I moderated a panel then.So my interest in economics was growing steadily.
Both ASTRA and KSCST gave me some  totally different social perspectives. 
Look at technology not just from the implementation point of view ,but from many other views like
The views of consumers
The views of the society, social constraints, resources, sustainability
The impacts of trust and behaviours of people
The impacts on nature and environment.
The views of capital and economy
The views of suitability ,meeting the needs, acceptability, ease of use and adaptability
The views of technology alternatives and selections
The view of skills requirement 
The views of impacts, culture .
These opened huge doors to look at India - urban and rural, middle class and poor etc. Based on these we needed to generate policies of technology development ,adaption, and use. We were not doing this. The engineering research community  restricts itself  to a narrow window of technologies with other aspects ignored. This led to only delta improvements and not breakthroughs. We need a change to open up opportunities for great and useful research only by integrating humanities, sociology , economics ,statistics, data and AI with engineering.
 This is an urgent necessity today with AI exploding in every sector and data dominating all problem solving situations.
Greetings. Dks
19/11/25, 4:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 901
Another opportunity I got in 1976 was the first hand experience of the growth of ASTRA and KSCST. It meant a number of diverse projects by faculty from several departments and by other institutions. Prof KS Jagadish looked at new building construction models and technologies and experimented them by building the ASTRA building in IISc and the buildings in Ungra village. Prof MV Narasimhan constructed an instrumented biogas plant. It found out that 5feet depth is enough for good digestion. So I worked on the optimization of the biogas plant. I also worked on community biogas. But my major work was on optimal integrated energy planning for a village. More on it later.
Prof Raghavan studied the design of bullock carts. Actually Prof NS Ramaswamy,IIM Bangalore came up with a new bullock cart fitted with car tyres. Prof Raghavan proved that the older design of large wheels is better for village roads. Prof SPGovindaraju came up with a new alternative design of a wind mill with a scooter tyre water pump and took up its construction in Ungra. Prof Ramaprasad was looking at water pumping and irrigation.
Dr Lokras started looking at cooking. Prof Krishnaprasad was interested in it, but left IISc. So Lokras guided by Prof R Kumar spent years on a very impactful project of designing and developing high energy wood burning stoves. I considered this project the most important and impactful one. It took more than 5 years to produce a stove with a high efficiency of about 30% . It was christened as ASTRA ole. Ole in kannada means stove. It was based on in situ construction of many chambers with mud.Let me discuss later why  I considered  this as the most impactful project? 
I was formulating my research plans on energy. The plan had many stages. It wasn’t a one shot affair. It grew step by step. My first step was to start energy connectivity at the usage end and my first focus was rural areas. I felt that I needed real data. CMIE in Mumbai was providing a lot of data. So I started focusing on getting data and looking for sources. So I started looking at the data on cooking. Very few people used gas for cooking. 90% of people used firewood as fuel . They used inefficient stoves with low efficiencies of 5 to 10%. So about 300 million tons of firewood was consumed every year. This was cited as a major reason for deforestation. Hence increasing the efficiency to 20% with a new stove will reduce the consumption by half.

20/11/25, 4:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 902
I was getting excited by the opportunities provided by looking at energy in an integrated form. Three threads emerged:
1.What is an optimal and sustainable plan for rural energy ? This led to a PhD work.
2.Energy studies need a lot of data - both on the supply side and consumption side. How do we get data? This was the main focus of work initially taken up by me and later supported for decades by my student TV Ramachandra. This was a long journey. Most data was captured through surveys and measurements. 
3. How do we develop optimal models for energy planning based on consumptions and quality matching? 
With these, my decades long research direction is ready. I generated many new problems as we progressed. 
During this period , I met several social scientists, economists and agriculturists. Discussions with them enlarged my outlook.
There were many changes in my thinking and understanding which started at this time -1975-76.
The first social concern was nature. Isopanishad had a deep impression. I saw destruction in action- careless destructions, mindless activities all justified. Deforestation due to cutting of trees for firewood is a good example of consumption. So the importance of environment which was there in me from childhood took some specific and strong shapes .
My first understanding in this directoion started in 1960 with Schumakar's lecture and discussions. He had the foresight and courage to provide a contrarian solution which was totally opposite to the prevailing Nehruvian philosophy of ignoring the environment or even destroying the environment. 
That led to my first focus - resource conservation. Even in the 1950s, I saw that the focus of countries has been that development means more consumption of resources and destruction of nature. 
I am a firm believer that that concept of more consumption is a destructive force heralding pralaya. Today it is more urgent.AI is unleashing more trouble for us. 
My research focused on resources minimization and frugal living. I practiced it and reduced my wants as far as possible. Even now I believe in this conservation theory strongly. I feel our entire thrust on more consumption will be destructive to all. Lokas samastha sukhino bhavanthu will be meaningless.

21/11/25, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post    903
A big clarification is needed on yesterday's post. I mentioned schumakar and his speech in 1960. But i failed to talk about the greatest influencers pushing me in that direction of resources conservation. One was my uncle, the person with the greatest religious, spiritual, ethical, frugal, financial and organizational influences on me through practical daily examples for 15 plus years. 
Another person who influenced me on a totally alternate path in my school days was Gandhi. He was the constant topic for discussion by many elders and teachers. I also read a lot of his writings. His vision and thinking about the holistic and inclusive development of India were amazing. His writings on development made a great impression on me. That was a theoretical foundation on frugal economics. But Schumakar gave a graphic picture of the ills of the western developments - an after effect of industrialization.
We think that technologies force development and spend lifetime on that aspect. That is not true. My thesis here is that while I agree that technologies have facilitated development, it is a small coterie of media and both political and economic influencers who direct the development of a country in a direction of wealth and consumption. Technologies are tools in their hands.
We are still in the 1976 period. Let me return to my department.The school of automation, my new department, was just trying to grow with a lot of false starts. The Russians were shipping old equipment. We got about 25 analog computers built using vacuum tubes in the year 1975. They were costly. Everyone was upset. The Russians were planning to send an old model digital computer. The digital computer suggested was an old model and very expensive. 
Hence, the institute, after several rounds of discussions, decided to stop the Russian collaboration. We were on our own. 
We had five professors in the school. But there was a big rift. There were two camps. One was IGS camp. Another - Deekshatulu camp with the support of three professors .Another one was neutral. So the school didn't have a good, harmonious environment. 
But it didn't affect the functioning of the faculty. We went about our work. There was enough freedom .Most of us weren't after power. So life was normal but not pleasant.I was lucky to spend time with EE for the consultancy on distribution designs and teaching a course on computer systems as well as working on projects with ASTRA and KSCST,even though I had my commitments of teaching two courses and guiding several ME students and research students. My plate was full and overflowing.So I wasn't seriously involved in the fights. IGS realised my outside support. He used to comment that I got the latest information happening in the institute. That was because , I started going for coffee with Prof CSV and I spend a lot of time with AKN. He knew that I had a lot of support not only from several senior faculty in other departments but also the fact that l had the support of Prof Dhawan. We weren't close. He didn't like me. But he needed me because of my influence with the registrar, most officers and staff in the central office. So our relationship was both distant and near when he wanted something to be done. It was ok to me. But I knew that I needed some strong strategies to counter him. Fortunately ,a big opportunity came in mid 1977.
Since we rejected the Russian supply of a computer,  IGS worked on a plan to get a computer. But we can't buy a digital computer. So he proposed a hybrid computer for aircraft simulation. Prof Dhawan, our director, supported his proposal and it was decided to set up a central facility for the proposed hybrid computer. We got an expensive hybrid computer. But our luck was that the digital part was a PDP11 computer. It was a very useful tool for the department. It was a work horse for more than 20 years and I was a major user. I was in charge of the centre for 15 years till it was wound up. We gave it to REC Trichy. More on it later.
Since IGS became the chair, Deekshatulu was not comfortable. He roped me to write a proposal for a digital control computer system. We worked hard on that. The institute already decided to support IGS's proposal for a hybrid simulator and couldn't support Deekshatulu's proposal financially. We visited ECIL,Hyderabad, Dept of Electronics Delhi and also DST,but we didn't get any funds. IGS mocked at that proposal. I saw this fight. But we were unsuccessful. Dhawan realized the tussle and gave a job in NRSA to Deekshatulu and he left IISc and moved to NRSA,Hyderabad.So IGS won. That is the politics going on in the department.
But my learnings from this failure were:
1. Don't ape another.
2.Don't compete.
3. Plan well. Adhicism doesn't work. We were clueless about the source of funds. Srikantan ,ECIL, advised us to start with a small project and not to go for a big one. Strategic thinking was missing in this project.
4.Don't support without knowing about long term support. When Deekshatulu left IISc, I felt a bit vulnerable.
More in the next.

22/11/25, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 904
I got involved with the purchase of a new computer for the institute. The director felt that the IBM 360/44 computer was outdated and we needed to buy a new one. He set up a group to look at a suitable computer for the institute. Unlike the last time when we had no money for buying a computer and joined with NAL and DRDO to buy the computer, we had enough funds this time. The financial situation was getting better.
We still didn't have many options. We looked at ICL's 2900 series - designed and built based on the MU5 of the Manchester university - and had discussions with them. I was not happy with their software support and even maintenance support . So we were not impressed. But what struck me as strange was their argument that IBM's concept of designing computers with byte-8 bits- as the basic unit was inefficient. They didn't see that the world was moving that way. It became a standard to use 8,16,32,64,128 bits as standard word lengths for computers .DEC had been moving in that direction when they designed PDP/11 as a 16 bit machine. ECIL also designed TDC16 and 32 computers with 16 bits and 32 bits. By 1976, they came out with 16 bits computers.ICL had no idea about the direction. I didn't find any advantage in buying their computer. 
We had a good relationship with IBM for about six years as they were maintaining the 360 computer ,but IBM,s 370 wasn't well suited for our use even though IIT Madras had purchased one a few years back. Also IBM computers were expensive. Amdahl, the chief designer, left IBM and started his company. Further, only IIM Ahmedabad realised the advantage of time sharing computers with user terminals and bought a HP mini computer. 
We knew that Prof R Narasimhan created a new  UN funded  national centre for software development. He purchased a computer for their use distinct from the TIFR facility. He had purchased a newly designed DEC10 computer. It was also one of the first machines in the world with a good network model based database systems. It supported time sharing and had a user friendly OS, not very good ,but better than both IBM and ICL.  Prof RN focused on design and graphics areas as well as languages like Pascal. Both Deekshatulu and I visited TIFR to attend a course on computer graphics.I got travel grants from Prof RN to attend a week long course. We traveled to Bombay via Guntakal. Since Deekshatulu's father in law was a senior officer of railways security force, we were received by RPF officers and later put in the Bombay train- it was a Madras to Bombay express train. That train was crowded but we managed. I stayed through the course. It was useful. I also got an exposure on Pascal. Since I was an admirer of Algol at university of Manchester, I liked Pascal and decided to make my ME students to work on Pascal. C was yet to make an appearance then. 
I met Mr Hemant Sonawala during this course. They took responsibility for supplying, installing and maintaining the computer. Hinditron company was well known in supplying quality oscilloscopes - Tektronics. I had met Mr Gandhi, another boss of Hinditron before in late sixties.
But I had doubts about DEC 10's scalability and its capability to handle large IISc students workload. At NCSDCT, the new centre at TIFR, the DEC10 was not used by a large number of users. It had only a few tens of users unlike iisc where several hundreds were the users then. 
Prof RN visited IISc and convinced our director about buying DEC 10. He also convinced IIT Kanpur to buy this machine. Our biggest advantage was the affordability. It was sold at a low cost compared to IBM- almost at half the IBM cost. So we bought a DEC 1090 computer, but that was in 1978 or so.
It wasn't a great decision. We got into problems from 1980 onwards. It couldn't handle our work load. More on this later. But one thing was that  I got involved in looking at our computer centre problems by the directors - bothProf Dhawan and later Prof Ramaseshan-  in decision making about the centre operations for the next 5 years.

23/11/25, 4:53 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 904
There was another activity that I got into was on the computerization of our students' applications process. 
After returning from a short stay in Manchester, Mr Nanjunda Rao took charge of our academic section after Mr R Vaidyanathan left IISc to go to Canada. After my return from Manchester, I became close to Mr Nanjunda Rao and met him several times. He came home to our Malleswaram place a few times. We were once discussing the admissions procedure. I was involved with students admissions from 1967 onwards. I worked with Mr Vaidyanathan and Seshadri ,in charge of students section.But  Seshadri died suddenly and Vaidyanathan left. I had introduced many reforms in admissions process, the most successful one the introduction of the entrance exam for BE admissions in 1968.
The other laborious process done manually was the tabulation of students' essential data on to a large table. So I told NanjundaRao that we can automate the tabulation and merit listing using computers. A small group started working on my suggestion. There were lots of heated discussions on the approaches and methodology. Since I had differences on the approach, I delinked myself from the group. The group made the filling of the admissions application form very complicated and I didn't like it. 
After two years of that practice, the group which was implementing the software moved to my concept of ease of filling in the applications. The concept that was recommended by me was to put boxes after each of the relevant items and it is followed even now universally. 
By this time , I had a diverse set of experiences in problem solving. Let me summarize my learnings:
1. In the case of a decision for a higher voltage ,the CBIP committee was influenced by BHEL to go for 400KV instead of a much better alternative of 500kv.
2. I designed IISc's first 11kv power network with many new features like a new KEB incoming station, 11KV ring main, several 11kv/400 volts substations and LT rings. It was not affected by ego constraints. The only constraint was cost constraint. It was a pure technical exercise done in 1968.
3. One of the complicated problem solving that I encountered was the introduction of a credit based, flexible unit system curriculum . I learnt a lot here. There were many decision points, strong opinions, discussions. But we could sail through the rough seas. I saw how senior faculty and the director handled the problems in a very mature way. It was a great understanding and learning for me at an early age in 1969-70. Similarly, I solved the time table management successfully.
4.I could build a proper scientific procedure for the distribution system project for Jharia coal fields. I could convince the leaders. I was alone responsible for decisions here. So it was easy.
5. In the case of deciding a computer for IISc, we chose DEC 10. Cost was the consideration here as well as Prof RNs push.
6. In the case computerization of our admissions, non expertise and non understanding were the problems. It was a joint meeting. I couldn't convince others. 
These learnings were building blocks of my problem solving expertise and helped me in dealing with hundreds of problems faced by several organizations,government departments and banks. Particularly, both the curriculum design and distribution planning problems led me towards many important activities- problem discovery, problem decomposition,solution discovery, alternatives, data discovery,requirements analysis, decision making methods and skills. I developed my own processes for solving problems. I looked at most problems not in the traditional way and didn't solve them using traditional methods. I wanted to discover some thing new, some thing different that would improve the solutions. That became my regular thinking activity through out my life.

24/11/25, 3:51 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 905
Another clarification is needed.
When I wrote about IGS vs BLDeekshatulu tussle and the purchase of hybrid computer ,I didn't expect it to give a totally erroneous picture of IISc.
There was a misinterpretation that IISc is like any other government office with lots of politics and power play. I want to dispel that interpretation through this clarification. 
Most Indians, particularly educated ones and more particularly academics, have egos. These egos lead to jealousy and contests. It is a normal occurrence in all organizations. Our scriptures say ," remove ahamkara" but we all love it and build a mountain of ego. That is why a child is adorable. 
What we need to address is the answer to the question -does such ego clashes lead to disruption in the functioning of the organization? That is important. 
Many organizations address this human natural ego instinct through strict disciplinary rules ,governance and processes and that leads to fear and curbs innovation. 
But I want to state that IISc ambiance and work culture is very different. We ignore egos. The effect of egos is marginal. Politics is transient and harmless at IISc .
There is a lot of openness in our working. You decide what you want to do and prepare your action plans accordingly. Your respect and recognition come from your work, not your status.
Secondly, there is transparency. You know what is happening in the department. Not many secrets.
Thirdly, there is a lot of freedom to work. That is the most remarkable feature of IISc,not found in other places. The only other place with freedom is TIFR.
Fourthly, most people in positions of importance have limited powers. There is a well defined process for individual assessments and promotions. This applies to all, both faculty and employees. There is no possibility of one person having powers to destroy another. 
Fifthly, fairness prevails mostly. Grievance mechanisms exist.The processes are fair. This minimizes your anguish and brings in happiness.
Finally, IISc looks for excellence in a person's outputs in research, teaching,consultancy,sponsored projects,social activities and not on anything else. The evaluation follows many levels and many people. So most work without fear or favour. 
Work goes on despite ego clashes. Human behaviours are secondary. 
IISc is a great place to work. I loved working there and am happy with my involvements and associations. 
Hope the clarifications are clear.
Let me get back to my timeline 1976.
Bangalore city was growing and expanding. The government didn't spend money on infrastructure like power, roads and telecom. 
This resulted in power-cuts - both demand cuts and energy cuts. So IISc started buying diesel generating sets. Many sets were purchased by individual faculty members. Still, we felt that many activities and lab work were getting affected. So I was requested to talk to KEB chief engineer to get exemption from power cut for IISc. I visited KEB office, met Mr BG Rudrappa,chief engineer and made the request. He said he would do his best. We got some relief. This situation continued for more than a decade. 
Since Prof Dhawan had to handle both IISc and department of Space ,he decided to appoint a joint director. Prof Ramaseshan, who was with NAL and an eminent materials science researcher, was appointed as a joint director. I had lots of interactions with him.
Another great personal news for me was the birth of my son in December 1976 in Mysore.
