25/11/25, 4:44 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 906
My experiences in 1976 were not completely described. Two more important aspects will be described here.
Indira Gandhi declared an emergency and the situation in the country was one of fear. Everyone was worried that he or she may be arrested. Sanjay Gandhi took the law in his hands.
One day in mid 76, Mr SS Murthy met us and talked about an incident. Indira Gandhi was going to an event and power went off. Everyone panicked. So as an immediate measure ,a mobile DG set was added to her entourage.But the government wanted a permanent solution. So I got another project- study the Delhi electrical distribution system. They felt that the system needed a complete revamp.
This was ten times more complicated than the Jharia problem. Delhi had an archaic electricity system. 
So first, I should know the characteristics of the existing system to be followed by discovery of the current problems and then identify the outputs to be determined sustainable for ten years. Then we conduct the study starting with input data, methodologies and getting the outputs. Followed by the output would be detailed designs.
The first task was to understand the system. It meant two pronged approaches. First was the discussions we had with senior engineers in Delhi. We visited Delhi and went to meet the chairman along with Mr Murthy. The chief engineer, Mr Sawhney, explained the situation. Delhi gets hydro power from BEAS project, it had thermal power stations at Indraprastha and Badarpur and it imported power from MP. Unfortunately these three were not synchronized and so there were scheduled supply switch offs everyday once or twice. This was one cause of power interruptions. The internal distribution voltage was 33kv and 11kv. The most important distribution line was a complete 33kv line along the inner ring road. 
The second approach was we spent the next three days going through Delhi inspecting the 33KV line, substations, switching points, IP thermal power plants, incoming sub stations and LT 400 volts distribution. We learnt a lot. 
1.The system is pretty old. Bombay had a much better and modern system. So substations needed to be modernized.
2.The practices were archaic . There were 16 taps from the 33 kv line running in the ring road to connect to a 33/11kv substation. This was the worst encounter I had. Protection was poor. There was a good chance of sparking and fire. Most substations lacked instrumentation and control. 
3.There was no standardization. Each substation was of a different size. 
4.There were four levels of voltages -220kv to be upgraded to 400kv used for transporting power to Delhi from outside, 33kv ,11kv and 400volts. 
5. The real chaos was the LT distribution in buildings. We visited a shop in Chandni chowk. The engineer took us inside . It was going deep inside with many shops ,godowns - warehouses- , residences etc for about a length of more than 500 metres. A single LT connection had a connected load equalling about 200kw. Normally this would be 10kw max in house connections. Here rules were flouted. Politics played a key role here. Tariffs couldn't be increased. We were shocked by what we saw.
6. We studied the line diagram. Again another shock. There were many overhead lines and underground cables mixed together. 
7. We used a better jointing system at IISc. I introduced Raychem process to join two cables or a cable to an overhead line and discarded the old taping method of jointing that I first learnt at the Meenakshi mills in Madurai in 1961. But here in Delhi ,they were using the old tape based jointing.
8. The entire system was not properly designed. Extension philosophy was used. Overloading is common. Voltage regulation was poor. Everyone bought voltage stabilizers for many devices like refrigerator, TV etc. This practice was common across the country for decades.
9. There were no design engineers in the Delhi electric supply undertaking. 
So you see the complexity. Multiple surgeries were needed. 
More in the next
Greetings. Dks
26/11/25, 4:45 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 907
Let me continue with Delhi study.
The study was challenging. I don't want to go through the various processes.
As usual the decision questions were:
1. Is 33kv the optimum voltage or the next higher 66kv voltage better?
2. What are the optimum sizes of conductors, transformers, substations?
3. Optimal location of substation
4. Network routing
5.best power factor
6. Improve reliability
7. Modernize designs
I will just illustrate a few points here. 
1.we fortunately got a senior engineer to work with us. He got me data as required.
2. I did the first round analysis and came up with a standard voltage of 66kv for sub transmission. 
3. I suggested methods to modernize the inner ring road.
4. I suggested installation of automatic change switches for operations without interruptions. 
5.I suggested to go for ring mains and duplicate networks for improving reliability. 
6.I divided the regions, calculated the predicted load after 10 years for each region and optimized the numbers and sizes of substations for each region. I used branch and bound method to build ring mains connecting the 66kv/11kv substations.
The whole work was over by late 1977.
Two more important things were started at that time. Both were related to ASTRA and KSCST.
KSCST made two critical selections . Both NV Krishna and Amarnath Bhat from IIMs joined as systems analysts. Both were committed, took a lot of interest in development issues. They started monitoring about 10 projects. I had involvements in two. Bangalore university decided to abolish project work for civil engineering program. I got panicky and felt we should do something. So we had months of discussions. Several IISc faculty like KS Jagadish, Ramaprasad, Lokras, SPG, were involved in the discussions. Towards the middle of 1977 ,a detailed methodology was evolved. Due to intense discussions and hard work ,a complete strategy , a mechanism for selection, evaluation procedures were evolved. Initially, it was decided to focus on rural technology problems. In order to help the students, a list of about 100 titles was prepared. It focused on agriculture operations, irrigation pumping, bio gas, solar, cooking stoves, rural artisans like potters ,blacksmith, toy makers, jaggery making, use of rural materials like mud blocks, etc. Focus was on manual energy and animal energy use. While this was felt useful, it became an impediment within a few years. The program started in 1978 and it became a standard bearer for KSCST. It has become very successful. Several engineering colleges participate in this program.
Continued in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
27/11/25, 4:28 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post    908
AKN wanted to direct project work to focus on rural technologies so as to improve work efficiencies and support operations . His focus was on mechanical aspects. I wanted to strengthen project work to go deeper into technologies. There was a difference in our thinking just like in bio-gas. But the program was designed with the prepared list suggesting only rural activities and simple constructions using animal and human power. So most colleges were not happy. While some were eager to participate ,many were reluctant because of the rural thrust. Some even derided the program including a few in the institute itself. Fortunately, they accepted solar heating systems and wind mills,but didn't accept biogas plants. 
 I had several heated arguments with many faculty in many engineering colleges. Mostly the arguments came from the mechanical engineering department faculty. I said we need to open our eyes, know what is happening in rural areas and help them to improve operations. I also told them that we wanted to focus on modeling, design and problem solving skills in students.Then I challenged them to model a wood stove or a mud pot refrigeration. They said," those are trivial problems.". I disagreed and said they are complex modeling activities.
The focus was on construction in most cases. So the students were excited. Many water lifting pumps, solar water heaters, cooking stoves,wind mills, hand operated devices, better potter's wheels etc were built. 
All projects were inspected by a team of faculty from IISc. The motives were to encourage proper designing and selection of materials ,sizing and experimentation.At the end, we conducted a seminar cum exhibition in a college. 
I wasn't present fully during the first year of the students project program as I was in IIT Bombay at that time, but visited Bangalore four times in a year. More on it later.
I started changing the selection process to include electronics in a big way - electronics devices were becoming prominent in many sectors including hospitals and power electronics was gaining traction. This led to more participation from many colleges and all departments. Slowly, we moved towards more computer projects. 
This became a transformative project. It improved the students' understanding of practical engineering, and rural problems. It allowed them to think innovatively and conduct projects as a holistic problem solving activity. Its success was also due to the involvement of several IISc faculty and KSCST staff ,the excitement of students, the involvement of faculty and the support of the principals and HoDs. This program is continuing even now. That is the proof of its success.
When we started our trust FAER in 2005, we decided to replicate this in a modified manner. We changed our focus towards technology generation, interdisciplinary focus and innovation.
I had several learnings from this program. 
1. Success of a project depends on 
Detailed planning
Clarity of objectives- why it is initiated
Participation and discussions
Involvement of many
Deep interest
Gaps in identification, focus and solutions.
Involvement in running the program.
Involvement of IISc in the evaluations and advice.
Financial support
Final evaluation 
2. The need for reviews , constant improvements and change of focus from rural to modern technology.
3. The need to convince faculty to think on our lines on the need to improve villages. It was an education process.
4. Strong networking with hundreds of faculty, principals and hods. I got an opportunity to meet hundreds of faculty. It enlarged my understanding. I got a realistic picture of various engineering colleges in Karnataka. I saw the respect they had for IISc. My vision became larger. I also had a lot of discussions with faculty and students.
Greetings. Dks
28/11/25, 4:00 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 909
Though I was keen on the focus and activities of ASTRA and had a synergistic thinking with Krishnaprasad, I found myself an odd man in the group. I was more a systems person and a power and computer person and many felt my areas have no relevance to ASTRA. All others were technology oriented persons. I wasn't a believer in rural technologies. I thought that you couldn't chain rural people to traditional technologies like bullock carts, manual water lifting and agriculture operations for long. I saw people accepting pocket radio receivers and plastics.I felt that what we needed was an adaptation mechanism- a new type of engineering. I was a firm believer in renewable energy and bio energy. My focus was on conservation, optimization, enrichment of the environment. I was thinking about the approaches to solve the problems connected to those aspects. The answers were modeling and data generation.
I have so far talked about how I acquired new knowledge and moved into new areas. I wanted to explain why I understood a new area and started working in that area. We talk about data today. I started not only looking on data but working on data from both sides- generation and organization- from 1976 itself.
I saw computer science developments moving into a new direction. Most computer research till 1975 was on hardware and programming / algorithms. They continued afterwards also. But a new thinking emerged. After E F Codd developed his relational data model in 1970 , researchers in US started looking at data in a new way.So data became important as a separate topic of research. We realized that data is independent of actions. Data lives outside actions. Till then, data was embedded into a program. Data was transient and died. You created data, used it and threw it away. But the database concept gave a new look to data. 
The first change in our problem view was: don't look at an application like payroll , but look at an organization in a holistic way- its components, characteristics , connections and actions.
Data base hypothesized that data is permanent,it is independent of actions and applications, it is an essential component of an organization,and it gets used in all programs developed for different applications. Also there were two types of data - data internal to an organization like employee details, salary details, etc and dynamic data that is created due to the daily interactions the organization has with the outside world like purchases of materials, payments, visitors details, sales details etc. That is a whole new reality. We removed one illusion -perception.
While I started to add a new area- databases- to my research activities , I also looked at realistic ways of data collection. Economists have always been concerned with country's data and statisticians focused on data collection and analysis. Prof Mahalanobis created a great collection system. I looked at the data with both central and state government, more at the state government data available at taluk level. CMIE became another source for me. 
We decided to collect primary data through a survey. We believed that reality of a village would be revealed by actual data. So we got about four people to collect data from six villages based on a well prepared questionaire by visiting each household , no sampling. This was our energy consumption survey conducted by ASTRA in 6 villages near Ungra where our extension centre was located. That made me work with actual data. So I worked in a two directional approach on data - one database in CS and another generation and analysis of data to understand the reality. These two areas of research continued for the next 25 years. I will explain my work on energy consumption in detail later.
Greetings. Dks
29/11/25, 3:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 910
Prof HN Chanakya ,an early faculty who joined ASTRA, has written detailed comments on rural technology. His comments are:
Yes.  Very well brought out.  Thanks for bringing out a discussion on this subject.These were interesting times and deserve some soul searching.Many schools of thought of that period merely built upon existing practices or devices without understanding the underlying phenomena.  Your allusion to the biogas plant is a good example...Biogas plants were evolved with an "artisanic" bent of mind where the end device relied on repetitive earlier practice rather than an "engineered process /design" approach.  Your papers on design optimization during 1980 triggered engineered designs across the world.  These early efforts at ASTRA-KSCST had triggered attempts to create "novel and targeted" technological jnnovations and continue today even after nearly 50 years.The first GI based solar water heaters, vertical axis windmills, water pumps built upon "intermediate or grassroot" technological innovations addressed real and felt rural needs.  They consciously avoided photocopied options.I remember another key strategy or approach was the use of /deploying a systems approach and carring capacity of the ecosystem.  This highlighted the strong interconnected-ness and is once again a first /trail blazer in the field of Rural Development /Technologies.These outcomes very clearly defined R&D agendas for many an institution during that period.Strangely, today's Rural Tech units across the country are neither aware nor understand the need for the participative processes in rural technology and related R&D.  Could you throw light on what is lacking in today's Rural innovation predominantly created by urban trained actants.  Has the rural urban divide disappeared or is it reappearing in a different form?
My response
Chanakya brought out several aspects of our early days -1974 to 1980. While we know that ASTRA associate  faculty did innovative work on biogas, windmills, soil compacted bricks, composite roofs solar water heaters and very importantly astra ole, our focus was unclear. I felt that the focus on renewable energy was good,but the belief that there exist a new animal called rural technologies is wrong. I had strong disagreements with KSCSTs initial focus on rural technologies. One grass cutter built by a college was not safe. While operating the grass cutter, the blade flew and cut the hand of a young person in Bhairumbe. 
As Chanakya says,we had extensive discussions on the philosophy of ASTRA for several years in Bangalore, Gandhigram,Hubli,Madras,Bombay ,Delhi and many other institutions as well as with both state and central government officials. But opinions were divisive. 
I think our understanding of rural problems was weak. That is why focus on rural technologies disappeared from both ASTRA and KSCST by 1985 itself. It is not a fancy subject. That is unfortunate.Is climate change an important subject for ASTRA?
Technologies are not different ,but engineering is different. We didn't focus on engineering. 
We had no problem architect in both organizations and so no problem discovery happened.  Only Suresh Raina was serious about social aspects. None other had social interest. That was a tragedy. We all looked at technologies and not usability aspects. We need to address social acceptability.
That leads me to your last point. 
Rural urban divide exists, but academics have no interest in it. Surprisingly, the rightist government of PM Mr Modi adopted urban solutions to rural areas. Swatch Bharat scaled up the toilets program in all villages. The subsidized LPG, though wrong, took cleaner cooking to rural households.The focus on broadband to all is a very useful step. There is an approach change here. 
I hope we take AI to address three issues of people - rural and urban -
1.Health with ecommerce and econsulting focus
2.Agriculture production , wastage reduction in storage,distribution, and post harvest marketing
3.Education and social services.
There is a totally different take from me on this topic - a sociology angle. I will discuss it in the next post.

30/11/25, 4:34 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 911
A correction. Vinod Vyasulu reminds me - it is Dhruv Raina not suresh Raina. Sorry for the error. It is Dhruv . I still remember him and his interview for selection at KSCST.
"Are we really homo sapiens - wise humans?
Nobody disputes that humans today have a lot more information and power than in the Stone Age, but it is far from certain that we understand ourselves and our role in the universe much better.
Why are we so good at accumulating more information and power, but far less successful at acquiring wisdom?"
Yuval Harari    Nexus
Look at any area you want to develop. How do you go about it?
There is the popular approach. Try to invent a problem based on your perception and knowledge. That is what ASTRA did? It looked at problems perceived by newspapers and some so called experts. They said - improve agricultural operations, provide better cooking facilities, provide stronger and inexpensive housing ,provide better transport than a bullock cart, better water lifting etc. That was what ASTRA did. The early researchers left ASTRA and the in house persons got bored and found climate change attractive.
That is a weak perception based, lazy approach. 
We need to start with perception. Most decisions are unfortunately taken based on ones beliefs ,ideas and understandings. Again, understandings of a person is based on ones knowledge, readings and mappings on the read information with the existing knowledge. Cognition is restrictive and additive, not transformative, not changeable in most cases.
Our knowledge of solutions for problems faced by people is based on a silos approach. We look at many parts but miss the whole . But what I see is that we miss many parts also.
The best approach is to look for problems faced by people and get the best technology solution, none of our ideas and ideals please.So go from society to problems to solutions to technology , not the other way.This is crucial but hard. It will make us great researchers and lead to fundamental changes. Wisdom will come.
We need to understand there is no stand alone technology. What we have is a strongly interconnected system. That a practical system has the following subsystems:
Physical system- technology and tasks together.
Energy unit
Control unit
Computer and data
Communications
Marketing and logistics
Culture
People
We normally ignore the last two. All physical systems are socio technical physical systems. We may refer to irrigation, energy, transport, agriculture operations, food materials, but ignore the users. 
Computer systems and software ignored users . Then came user friendly system. Internet and web changed every thing. Mobile and social networks focus on people.
Take agriculture. The country had several hundreds of practices and we just ignore them. In those days ,we looked at bullock carts and were against tractors. We assumed ownership. But hiring concept changed the scene today. We see lots of tractors and bullock carts are disappearing. Our ideal solution for agriculture was organic fertilizers and pesticides. That didn't succeed. Yield goes down. The farmer goes bankrupt. 
APMC is an important institution - one stop solution provider. We didn't address marketing. I computerized APMCs in the early eighties.
When I designed the 1000 school computerization program for Karnataka, people suggested that i should not go for the latest PCs. But I went for the latest PCs. Similarly, they was opposition to teach MS office. One group suggested to use open source, another to go for non existing Kannada office. I rejected that. Similarly a strong Kannada group wanted to translate technical terms. I refused and retained English names for easy understanding. Otherwise, storage unit becomes ugrana and computers, calculators become ganaka yantra.
We had issues with broadband in banks during 2000 period. We wanted to connect all branches of a bank to its data centre. It was tough. We found the deep valleys in Himalayas didn't get signals. So we ran cables to the top and connected a VSAT terminal there. So problem centric approach is needed. Choose a technology that is correct for each situation. Don't preach one solution. We may find thousands of solutions. 
To conclude: 
1.Go for inclusive systemic solutions, acceptable to people. Don't go for past inferior solutions.
2.Study a process deeply. Get a good understanding of a society or community. Find gaps in their life style. See how many are affected and get solutions. Solutions can be many ,not one, not universal. 
3.Don't push your ideas, your theories . Search for appropriate and sustainable answers. We went for climate change and ignored water pollution.
4.Research focus should change from extension of a known technology to finding problems - discover problems at ground level. Solve the garbage problem.
We need to focus on sociology ,not on technology alone. Find systemic solutions. Systems should not be studied from mathematical perspectives alone.
Thanks Chanakya.
Greetings. Dks
01/12/25, 3:56 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 912
Let me get back to the 76-77 period. We started the detailed consumption survey in 1976 and completed it by mid 1977. 
We prepared a long questionnaire. It included many aspects like 
Food,types,sources, consumptions
Work schedules for all in the house
Activities like water - source, consumption, time to bring it home, firewood - quantity, purchase or gathering, who does it, quantity used per day, cooking - what, time spent,fuel used, types of choola or stove, cattle and other animals, grazing, milk, agriculture operations in depth. Lighting, kerosene or electricity.
Monthly income, purchases.
By mid 1977, all completed questionnaires were available. It was my task to analyze them. Since the data contained was quite a lot, it was agreed to computerize the data and perform the necessary analysis. But the big problem was to convert the data from the paper to a machine readable form. That was not easy. I was thinking about it. But in the meantime something else happened that delayed the processing work.
My family life was busy with two kids. My daughter started speaking early, but was feeling afraid of many things and she was falling sick many times. My son didn't reveal his true nature at that time. He was also falling sick. 
I consulted my uncle Dr Ramakrishnan. He suggested that I may consult Dr Balakrishnan ,the retired professor of pediatrics from JIPMER who was living in Jayanagar. I went to see him with my daughter. He wasn't a helpful person. He increased my worries. He gave me a big list of tests. At that time, we didn't have a lab nearby. So I decided to get the tests done in JIPMER. 
We went by train to Madras and bus to Pondicherry. We stayed for three days, got the tests done. My son fell sick one day. The tests were normal. The doctor there said that the tests were unnecessary. We returned by bus to Madras. A passenger sitting next to me was smoking. I requested him to move back. But he refused. My son vomited in the seat on the bus. Anyway, he was fine once we reached Madras.But vomiting became common with him. He had my temperament - start vomiting when I had a severe headache or fever. We returned to Bangalore.
I continued with my teaching ,and  research and worked on the Delhi project. I had to make a few trips to Delhi for discussions and clarifications.
In September/ October 1977, I was called for interview for the post of professor at IIT Bombay. I went to Bombay by train and stayed with RD Kumar, whom I met in Manchester and was a good friend. I met Prof Kekre with Kumar. I met Prof Isaac, I knew him before.I attended the interview the next day. Prof RN was the expert. Dr Mehndiratta was another candidate. The interview went off well. I talked about my work on simulation languages. Prof RN asked me the language I used. I said FORTRAN. Pascal came later. He wasn't happy,but appreciated my efforts in building queue data structures in FORTRAN.
Kumar took me to meet Isaac in the evening. Isaac said that I was selected as a professor. I expressed my worries that Prof Dhawan may not like it. 
I discussed with AKN after my return. He was also not happy. Adter discussions, I agreed that I would return back if promoted as associate professor at IISc. I had submitted my papers for assessment for promotions. We had a truce.
So my next journey was about to begin.

02/12/25, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 913
I instantly decided to go to IIT as the offer was good ,it will improve my academic standing and I will have time to think about the future by living in a new environment and staying away from a known environment .
I wasn't afraid of moving to Bombay. IIT agreed to give me a faculty quarters. I had gone to Bombay several times and so it was a familiar place. Even as a child, I read Kalki's and Mayavi's description of Bombay, Matunga. I was fascinated by Bombay. I loved the Colaba and VT area. Further, I had a relative - Venkitachalam,a cousin by marriage , who was living in Tilaknagar and working in RBI. ABL, husband of my wife's elder sister, had moved to Bombay and his family was living in Cheddanagar.So we had people to depend on. Also, my job at IISc was safe. I wont resign but go on leave without salary. 
I needed to prepare for leaving Bangalore to go to IIT Bombay. Those two plus years were very productive. I wrote three reports -each about 250 pages. It gave me a lot of confidence in my writing skills. I started working on designing energy systems for rural areas and built an optimal model. It was published. Similar optimization work was done to design bio-gas plants and that was also published. Then I moved into database research activity also. Another area I got into was performance metrics and measurements. So I was happy. 
Another development was happening which would take me to another level. I used to meet Madhav Gadgil frequently and started discussing with him about energy and environment. Gadgils lived close to our house. It took a few years to move forward. An opportunity came in 1980.That gave another path for me for the next 20 years. More on this later. 
What is an interesting observation for me was that opportunities come with several steps,through many discussions with many colleagues and acquaintances as well as some surprises. We should know it and catch it fast to get a meaningful life. Networking is very important for everyone to move up qualitatively in life. That is a very big lesson I learnt at a very young age. I am a shy person. So networking wasn't easy. But I succeeded. 
I discussed with AKN. We felt that I should complete the processing of the data collected for energy surveys in 6 villages. I agreed to come in summer and spend a month in KSCST to take care of some activities of ASTRA.Then I applied to IISc for a year's leave of absence. I got my letter forwarded by IGS without any problem. Prof BS Ramakrishna, who was the divisional chair,approved my leave.
I completed the Delhi project and submitted the report. Prof KS Prabhu, who was the chair of EE, was upset about my leaving IISc. He was worried as to who will teach my computer systems course in the EE department. It was a core course for ME students.
I had started to teach a new course on real time systems. I would complete it by December 1977. So I planned to leave by December end. Again,I decided to retain my 11th main house, not leave it. I decided not to take everything in the house with us to Bombay. That meant that I was planning to come back. I had a very strong attachment to IISc and Bangalore. I didn't want to leave Bangalore permanently.I was looking for a change from the School of Automation's strained atmosphere for some time.
I decided to send by road transport necessary utensils,beds, cots,dining table and chairs to Bombay- the minimum items needed to continue our life in Bombay. I decided to leave the rest - almyrahs, cupboards etc in the middle room. I released the LPG connection also.I decided to sublet the house for a year. Mr Govinda Rao was a research student in the EE department. He agreed to take my house for a year. He agreed to pay a part amount of rent. I said ok. I said he could move by December end. 
So while I was planning to move to IIT,another surprise happened. I will describe it in the next post.

03/12/25, 4:52 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 914
After most aspects of our moving to IIT were decided, I fixed a date for travel in December. Then came the surprise.
A cable came from US inviting AKN to attend a workshop in January 1978  first week at Harvard. AKN wasn't interested. So I took a decision to go. So it meant that I had to do many things.
First, I needed to inform Director Prof AK De and get his approval to delay my joining by a month. I wrote a letter and got his approval. 
Second I had to get a US Visa. Fortunately my passport was obtained in 1971 and it was extended in 1976. So it was operational. Fortunately, it was not as crazy as now to get an appointment for a US visa. I had to go to Madras to get the visa. I went and got the visa easily when I showed the invite cable. So that was solved. 
The next thing is travel planning. We had a travel agent by name Gopal. He got a ticket by Air France via Paris. When I went to Manchester in 1974, I had to waste a day visiting the airlines office to get a ticket. There was no travel agent for international travel then. The ticket was from Bangalore to Bombay to Paris to JFK to Boston and back. I had to return to Bangalore and then leave for Bombay. The schedule was a tight one,almost impossible today. I had a short time interval at JFK of an hour. I needed to come out, get my baggage and take a bus to another terminal to check in for the Boston flight. It is not possible today.Even then, it was crazy. I don't know why I took that risk. Travel agents take you through a rosy path but with hidden problems. I was a novice and believed him. I got wiser afterwards, and decided to double check their information.
 I had dealt with several travel agents afterwards and found their information unreliable. Only two were giving me pragmatic information, others promised me the moon.
The next was a big worry. Where do I stay in Boston? I consulted Prof V Krishnan and Mr TKS Iyengar,our librarian. They suggested YMCA. I wrote to the organizer to arrange for my stay there and pick me from Logan airport. He responded positively. So that aspect was taken care of. 
The next worry was the cold. It was January, winter season in the US. So what should I have as a protection? Prof Krishnan said that my UK top coat won't be sufficient. He gave me an additional jacket and said that I should use both.
The next problem to be solved was the fact that I promised Mr GovindaRao that I would vacate the house by December. But the situation had changed now. My family had to stay till January middle. So we decided to vacate the house by December end and that my wife and children would move to Madras and stay there till my return.

There was something worrying me a lot. I had not informed Prof Dhawan personally my decision. I felt that I should tell him directly not through someone. I went to the director's office and gave a request seeking time to meet him. Mr L Raghavendra Rao, his secretary told me that the director is busy and he wont give any advice to me. I, usually a dumb guy, was smart at that time and requested him to inform the director and I would abide by his decision. 
Prof Dhawan agreed to meet me. Rao was surprised. The meeting was scheduled in the fourth week of December 1977. The council was meeting at 1030am  . I got an appointment at 930 am and Rao told me to take only five minutes and come out. I said ok. But the meeting lasted 40 minutes. I promised the director that I would be taking a firm decision by june1978 on my return to IISc and he promised me that the processing of my papers for promotion wont be stopped. He was philosophical and told me that he cant ask me to stay back and not go to IIT. So I came out with a lot of clarity. Rao was unhappy that I took a long time with the director. So I was all set to go to Boston and then to Bombay. 
Let me discuss my long journeys next.
I left with my family for Madras by December end. Then I returned to Bangalore . I left on second January 1978 for New York. I had only a small bag with my dresses and two top coats were held in my hand. The journey was smooth. It was a big jumbo 747 plane. Seats were comfortable. Flight was not full. I liked the Paris airport. Its design was not linear like most airports, but it had many satellites connected by tunnels. I don't remember what I ate on the two flights. But it was definitely vegetarian.I was allowed to sit near the exit on the Paris to NY flight so that I could finish immigration and customs faster. I did both in a jiffy.I had a small delay at customs as I had a sealed box of Indian sweets given by my wife's friend for her brother. I explained it to them and they allowed me to go.I had no checked in baggage, a big advantage.I took a bus to the other terminal and boarded the Boston flight.
This is the first major international workshop I attended without preparation.

05/12/25, 4:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post    915 
The flight to Boston was for an hour. About 60% of the people smoke. I experienced this problem of smoking in doors in Manchester and I felt it here. It gave me a headache, but not a severe one. It went off on getting out and breathing the fresh air. They didn't serve any food in the flight - only coffee and soft drinks. Another cultural difference with Europe that I observed was that they used a lot of ice with soft drinks. The glass was filled to half with ice. I didn't see this large use of ice in Europe. Also, coke was a common drink in the US then. Sure,I had coke in the UK also. But people preferred beer to coke there. The consumption of coke was less. 
I arrived in Boston by 6pm. At that time and later on for several years , I found that people who came to receive friends could walk up to the gate. A passenger didn't have to come out and search for them as it happens now. That was not the case with international flights. The US didn't have a security procedure then. India had a strong security check by then.
So immediately on exiting from the gate ,I was relieved to see a person from Meta systems waiting there. He put me up in a motel. It was close to Harvard and he suggested that I could walk to the workshop hall. The motel was good. I felt a lot of relief and happy on reaching the motel. I started thinking that everything went off well despite the stupid schedule prepared by the travel agent. Everything worked with clock work precision. No delays, no missed flights. I was lucky. I realized the importance of planning the schedule with a lot of care and cushion.I decided to be careful in future. It was a good lesson.
Next day, I got a reasonable breakfast of juice,toast and coffee. It was a total self service,the items were left in the lobby for us to pickup and eat.First time, I saw people drinking coffee with a straw. I found it burning the tongue and later, the US discarded that practice.
It was January and pretty cold. I managed it. The workshop was for two days. The focus was to prepare an action plan for rural energy programs in developing countries. There were about 20 people attending the workshop.I was the only non US person there with ground level knowledge and exposure.So I was happy. I felt that I could contribute. My worries vanished. I was in my element and contributed a lot to the meeting. 
I talked about the most important problem - improving the efficiency of cooking stoves in order to minimize the consumption of fire wood ,thereby reducing deforestation. I wrote a page for them to include in their final report. I also told them about the magnitude of agro wastes. 
When I used the word 'waste', a person interrupted me and said that India used its wastes. I clarified that ,' yes. We use a lot of waste like straw is used as cattle feed and husk is used for cooking. But we still leave out a lot. The total production was about 200 plus million tons of collectible waste and only a part is usable.' But the point that I wanted to emphasize was that the efficiency of use was low - less than10% and it was possible to increase it to a higher level. We could achieve definitely 20%. I also told them about the smokeless chula used in Mysore and promoted by Gandhi. They all agreed with me. I found most participants knowledgeable about rural problems. So my discussions were understood and appreciated.

We had a working lunch and my choices were limited. We could pick up coffee and cookies anytime. So I was comfortable. Prof Krishnan's additional jacket was useful. 
After the day's session, I went to Boston by metro to a departmental store to buy a large VIP suitcase for AKN and some toys for my children. It became a standard practice for me to bring toys from big cities like Delhi, Bombay etc. I bought them and came back. We crossed the Charles river. It was frozen.
We had dinner with the organizer. He made a special veg pasta for me. I didn't drink. 
I talked to two people specifically during dinner. One was Prof Rogers from MIT. He said Prof Vedula, my colleague from the Civil engineering department, IISc, was with him at that time. Another person gave me an invitation - a similar workshop was being held at Smithsonian institution in Washington, that too immediately after this workshop. She wanted me to attend that workshop. That put me in a dilemma. My return tickets were booked and I had to join IIT immediately after return.I said that if she could get me a ticket from Washington to Amsterdam, then I would participate in the second workshop. She agreed. 
I saw another face of technology. She said she would organize prepaid ticket advice -known as PTA-for me. I should go to the check in counter at Logan airport. There, I would get my tickets - Boston to Washington Baltimore airport and another from WB to Amsterdam. So my travel plan got changed.
I completed the second day's workshop successfully talking about bio gas, biomass, briquette making, solar and wind. I bought some apples for my dinner. I did more shopping - more toys- on the way from Harvard to the motel.
As told previously, I brought some eatables for a student at the Boston university, given by his sister, a friend of my wife. Suddenly I remembered that and called him. He asked me whether I could deliver it to him in his university. I said that was difficult. He came at 930pm and picked up the packet without any word of greetings or thanks. I felt pretty upset about his behavior.
I also got a surprise call in the night from Prof Vedula. He invited me to his house for lunch. He suggested that I should check out from the motel, take a cab to his flat and after lunch go to the airport. I liked that clarity and agreed to do so. 
I went to his flat the next day ,spent time with him,had rice ,dhall and veggies for lunch- the first time I had rice- and left for the airport. I went to the check in counter and told the person there about my PTA. She asked for my passport and issued three tickets - one to Washington Baltimore, another from there to Amsterdam by KLM and a third from Amsterdam to Manchester. The first flight was smooth.Surprisingly, the WB airport was not crowded and was shining. I took a cab to the Smithsonian,got a room and went to the meeting venue. It was all easy and fine, no worries.

07/12/25, 4:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  917
Our food requirements from bf to dinner were taken care of by the Smithsonian. We didn't go out for anything. The outside was cold and frozen. The meeting followed without a break starting at 8am in the morning and going up to 8pm with breaks for lunch and coffee. The workshop was for two days. I left on the second day evening to catch my flight to Amsterdam. I took an earlier flight, but they didn't have a veg meal. I got only an apple and bread along with juice and coffee. Can we think of international flights with no food now?
My entire US trip was filled with anxieties, surprises and twists and it came to an end after five days of stay- a short period. My trip was very fruitful. Everything was easy and people were helpful. They had time for socialization.Even, the immigration and customs officials were friendly and helpful. It was a great trip so far. It is different now. But my surprise was that I didn't retain these contacts. There were no future opportunities for contacting any of them.
I reached Amsterdam in the morning of the next day.I planned to go to Eindhoven, but decided to skip that trip as I didn't have time for that trip. I went around Amsterdam, had pan cakes,and went to a diamond factory. The owner was pleased and welcomed me, a totally different response to an Indian in Europe. I took a ferry. I found people living close to canals and in the lake. They had electricity and water connections. I saw a nice custom. Most houses had three or four floors. Vegetables and grocery were delivered to their door steps. The people living on the higher floors didn't come down to pick up their deliveries. They had a pulley and rope system and they sent an empty basket down and the vendor put the deliveries in the basket. Then the basket was pulled up- effort saving mechanism. 
I see several houses with three floors in Bengaluru,but didn't see such a pulley installed in them. The only guy who doesn't go up the stairs is the morning paper boy who throws them into the balconies. I had seen paper boys struggling to throw paper to third floor balcony in Calcutta in the sixties in my uncle's house. 
I took the night flight to Manchester. I had no visa and didn't inform anyone there. So I didn't know where to stay. The decision was a stupid one. I won't do it now,having learnt the difficulties in life and knowing the importance of planning.
I landed in Manchester,told the immigration that I didn't have a visa,but would be leaving the next day for India. I didn't have tickets either. He allowed me in. I didn't have small change to phone. The immigration guy was so good that he allowed me the use of his phone. I called Narayanan,UMIST. He was available, came and took me home.
I bought a ticket to Bombay from London, left by bus in the night to London. I took the airIndia flight to Bombay and reached Bangalore on 15th Jan.
I met AKN,gave his VIP suitcase, returned Krishnan's jacket, gave a letter in the SA office about my going on leave , and left for Madras by the afternoon Brindavan express. My journey wasn't over. 
I left the next day with my family - my wife and two small kids - by the slowest Madras Bombay mail in first class. We spent two nights in the train and reached Dadar at 5 am.Both my kids were fine in the train, but couldn't eat anything. I managed to get milk and hot water in stations.We went to Cheddanagar to my sister in laws place. I went to IIT and reported for duty in the computer centre.

I met Mr RD  Kumar first. He took me through all initialization activities. He took me to Prof Isaac. Isaac was happy that I joined there. I joined duty by signing the joining report in his presence. Then Kumar took me to the estate office to get my house keys. I got a flat in staff hostel annexe- flat no21.We went to the annexe ,but I found that there was no connection for power, needed for cooking and geysers. So I met the estate officer. He wasn't helpful. He wrote a letter to the electrical engineer,a stupid activity writing to a subordinate in the office. I met Mr Rakshit,the electrical engineer. He got the connection done. Then I came back andmet the faculty in the centre. I got an office in the first floor. The first floor is common to both computer centre on one side and mathematics on the opposite side. We were four professors and Kumar in one wing. Later I went to the main building and met the director. We had a good meeting. I wasn't impressed by the director, may be I was biased with comparisons with Prof Dhawan.
I brought my family from Cheddanagar the next day and we were helped to settle down by Kumar and his wife. My household items sent from Bangalore had arrived. They were in Prof Hariharan's house. I got them transported. So the cots were ready for use. We had an electric stove used for cooking. I was told that it would take more than 3 months to get gas. 
I had met Mr Desai,director, Bharat petroleum in Bangalore before. So I went and met him. He immediately arranged a gas connection. So within a few days, we got a gas connection with a new gas stove.We started getting milk daily and groceries from the nadar shop in the road outside. A boy would pick our orders in the morning and deliver in the afternoon, a very helpful service common to Bombay.The staff hostel was quite far,about half km from the gate. The children were comfortable in the new surroundings. They weathered the travel well.
I was happy with my new environment, had good interactions with my colleagues and office staff,started teaching the operating systems course. I knew Hariharan well and met most faculty members in the EE department. I attended the EE faculty meeting also. I met several other faculty members and got not only adjusted to the system, but also became a part of it. I got into several committees and was welcomed by all. I started a good daily routine - morning walks - a fairly long route inside and by the lake side ,lectures, library work, discussions, tea with colleagues etc. I walked to the department and library was close by.Initially, the climate was good in January and February , but by march end, it became hot. 
AKN came with the energy survey questionnaires in a box and left those papers with me.But I couldn't process them at IIT. My association with PSTI continued and my first visit to PSTI happened in March 1978. I stayed in a small hotel in seshadripuram for two days.
I had discussions with AKN and agreed to spend the month of May in Bangalore. I went to Calcutta with Isaac for the CSI convention in March along with a few students. 
I had a new experience of the examination procedure at IIT.It was very different from the procedure at IISc. In IISc, you conducted the exam independently and you choose the method of examination and conduct it where ever you want. 
But here, there is a defined procedure. I had to go at 7am on the day of the examination of my course, get my question paper typed, copied and sealed. I picked up the question paper before the start of the exam. The exams were conducted in the large convo hall. The convo hall was used for many activities - exams, convocation, weekly movies mostly Hindi ones, and conferences.

09/12/25, 5:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post    919
“In the end, it's not the years in your life that count. It's the life in your years.”
Abraham Lincoln
I wanted to get a ration card. I found it easy. I went to the municipal office in Vikhroli and was given a form. The form was in Marathi. The lady in the counter filled it up for me. I got the ration card in a weeks time - a straight forward process, no bribes. I liked it.
But I found that times have changed. I used the card to buy sugar and rice in 1973 immediately after marriage. The shop was close by in 13th cross and the secretary was an IISc employee named Mr Hanuman.I wasn't keen on buying and using kerosene even though I experimented with three four stoves. After returning from Manchester in 1975, I bought rice from the open market and stopped buying it from the fair price shop. I bought only sugar from the rations. 
Now a major change has happened. We had a big political transformation in 1977. The Janata party came to power. Mr HM Patel became the finance minister and he abolished control on sugar in march 1978. A simple act transformed the market. Sugar for the first time was available on the open market at near the ration prices. So I didn't find any use for the ration card.
I saw several changes in life in Bangalore and Bombay. 
People in Bangalore take life easy. But people are active ,busy and running in Bombay. So your normal requirements get done fast, the best examples were getting the gas connection and the ration card.Most services were attended to promptly.
Travel in Bombay was easy. You took the BEST bus to Vikhroli or Ghatkopar from Powai and took the local. Buses had good schedules with low wait times. Even though I had to take a bus and train to most places, it wasn't burdensome. Locals were the lifeline for millions of commuters. So you didn't feel the distance. Autos were available in most suburban stations for short distance rides. Travel in Bombay wasn't difficult or worrisome. People believed in organization. They line up in a bus stand or taxi stand to get into a bus or taxi.
Travel in Bangalore was and is a night mare. Buses, you don't get them even after waiting for a long time. Buses were packed most of the time and there was a scramble to get into a bus- not a pleasant or easy task. Auto drivers were notorious, unhelpful and uncooperative. There were no suburban trains. The situation is worse even today - even your own cars can't take you to many parts of the city in defined times. The excuse,"I got delayed due to traffic" is a common one heard everywhere in Bangalore.
There is a very large gap in the shopping activity. Bangalore wasn't known for big shops in all areas in the 60s and 70s. We needed to go to majestic or commercial street to get clothes and tailor services. You had to go to the city market area-SJP road- for electrical items. Only in the 80s ,shopping in many areas like Malleswaram improved. But that wasn't the case with Bombay. Abundance is the term I use to describe Bombay. Even Madras was better than Bangalore. Delhi beats all cities in shopping.
Bombay had a great electricity distribution system despite being handled by three agencies- Tatas for supply, BEST for distribution in the south and Bombay Suburban for distribution in the north. The coordination was good. There was futuristic planning and power shortages ,power cuts and power failures were rare. It is heaven compared to Bangalore and Delhi. 
Bombay was a land of surplus not just in materials and resources but in thinking also. Bangalore is a city of scarcity of thinking. Bangalore suffers from a very big disease - mental poverty. Houses in the 60s and 70s had 8 by 6 feet bed rooms, even some hotels. Physical problems like water scarcity, poor electric supply and worst public transport- existing for several decades-are evidence of mental poverty - no vision, poor planning,extension approaches dominate city rulers.
Still I was attached to Bangalore. I felt Bangalore to be my home. I love Bangalore. While I was a bombay man in IIT, I embrased not just Bangalore but the entire Karnataka in the years to come. My attachment proved useful later. 
Greetings. Dks
10/12/25, 4:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  920
I was to spend a month at ASTRA during May 1978. So we left by Bombay mail to Chennai and after a day's stay there came to Bangalore. I found this route more convenient for my children.We got an apartment in the students block ,Dr Narain Mahishi was living in another apartment then. I got food from C mess.
I got a working office in KSCST and also visited my office in the school of automation regularly. My focus was on the analysis of our survey on energy use in villages.
One other task was my commitment to Prof Dhawan to inform him my decision whether I will return or continue at IIT. I decided to return to Bangalore by April may 1979. I wrote a long letter to Prof Dhawan on my reasons to return to IISc and my final decision to return. The letter led to an interesting conversation with AKN. I had written in the first part of that letter that I like IIT Bombay very much, l like the department and had many colleagues ,I had great opportunities,and most people like my wok etc,about my life at IIT
 AKN asked me what is the need for that part. I said that I want to state that despite a great life, I want to return and I also indicated indirectly that I wanted to return because of ASTRA and not because of computer research. He got convinced.I sent the letter by mid May 78. 
One ME student from EE met me and wanted to do his project work with me. I suggested to him a project on building an agricultural data base system using relational model. We didn't have any relational database available in India at that time.Our DEC1090 had a network DBMS. So it was a pioneering development work. It was both an enquiry based system and a transactional system. I was the first in the country to start work on relational database and online transactions processing.
It was a very fortunate thing to have sent the letter. Prof Dhawan enquired with Prof BSR about my promotion. Prof BSR said he had kept my papers pending without processing. Dhawan got upset and told him to process my papers. So my letter speeded up the processing of my promotion process. 
We returned to Bombay on first June following the same route via Chennai. As soon as we were back, I got my original allotted quarters -BTR. It was close to the main gate and lake side and schools. We shifted within a week. It was quite comfortable with a huge hall, a well done study, two bed rooms and a kitchen and a lot of open space. We had very good neighbors. Our neighbor Ratna Singh was helpful. My wife had a problem talking to his very friendly and affectionate wife in Hindi.our other neighbors spoke Kannada,Marathi,Hindi and Tamil. My daughter found a girl of her age and played with her.My colleague,Prof Mehndiratta lived in BTR. So I used to go on his scooter to the department some days. I didn't open the dining table. The table was in its original packed state till I returned.So we got settled fast here.
I had to admit my daughter to the LKG. They wanted a birth certificate from the municipality. I requested my cousin, Shankar - unfortunately he is no more in this world now and I miss him a lot- to get the birth certificate. It took a Himalayan effort for him to get it. I wanted to know an alternate. The school asked me to sign a sworn affidavit. So I got an affidavit typed, went to Vikhroli court, got it signed by a judge and came back for lunch- all done in half a day.I couldn't dream of such speed in Bangalore. With that affidavit,my daughter got admissions in LKG.
My daughter found the school difficult as everyone spoke Hindi. They had two sections and the teacher in the other section knew Tamil. Kumar suggested that I should request a transfer. I tried my best including talking to the director. I didn't succeed. My daughter continued and picked up some Hindi words. She became comfortable with the school.

11/12/25, 4:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 921
I met many faculty members in different departments in IIT. The diversity of faculty was lot more than IISc. At that time,IISc had a large percentage of south Indian population,but IIT had many people from north,east and Maharashtra in addition to south.So you see different cultures. Most even Maharashtrians spoke in Bombay Hindi- a comfortable language.
I met Prof Pandalai,ex director of IIT madras in Prof Panicker's house.I met Prof Anil Date. We had common interests in rural technologies. We had many discussions. But most faculty, including the director, saw a red herring in rural technologies. They had a misgiving that this emphasis would take us backward. I also felt that this would lead to confused thinking. But I realized many things by then. My learnings are
1.There is no such thing as rural technologies.Our focus in ASTRA was on development of rural technologies. I felt we should focus on technology adaption and absorption more than technology modification according to our perceptions.We saw a state in which villagers were living. We wanted to build technologies to suit that state. 
2.Instead, we should see how we could change that state leading to a better living - ease of work- better cooking, better yields, better housing,better lighting etc. So ASTRA focus was correct but thinking was wrong.
3.The idea of modifying implements to suit the current state as propagated through the student projects of KSCST was wrong. We needed more serious involvement and much better understanding of the conditions for technology adaptations. Engineers and engineering missed this crucial aspect. That reminds me the book "two cultures" by CP Snow.
4.We can't stop the march of technologies, we can only delay them. Despite all clarity and understanding that existed with AKN, he realised this aspect later, moved away from ASTRA and rural technologies  and he took to MS office later and looked at management of electrical energy. I saw a total change. That started the decelerating phase of ASTRA and they changed their name. 
5.Most researchers focus on improving a technology or a solution , but forget about the multiple aspects of technology conversions, usage and adaptation. I talked about identifying problems in real life and finding solutions. ASTRA while hoping to address this aspect of source level understanding , imposed its perceptions of technology. If we concentrated on identifying source level gaps, it would have taken us farther. Every engineering discipline should address this. I feel engineering has moved away from the users.The area of control system evolved by addressing the problems of quality in manufacturing. Fortunately,IT is forced to build systems for use by people, services and industries. 
But I saw IIT embarking on a big experiment. It set up a centre of studies in resources engineering. This was bigger than ASTRA. It had academics from several disciplines and practicing experts from forests,agriculture,geology and soils. The emphasis was on a lot of field work in Chandrapur district forests. So it was really a study project with the aim of generating primary data and developing a deeper understanding of not only people but also the geology, soils and forests.I liked the aims of CSRE,but I was not clear of any role for me there except secondary roles of discussions ,selection of faculty . I wrote a paper and presented it at their first major conference.
I met Prof Mallikarjun,the chair of CSRE several times. He was an alumnus of IISc from the metallurgy department. But he didn't have any idea of the future course of CSRE.
In addition ,I met many faculty members in several departments. Most were helpful. I could jell with most very well. Prof Ramesh, father of Jairam Ramesh, was a neighbor.
So life moved smoothly and briskly at IIT.
Greetings. Dks
12/12/25, 4:15 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 922
Prof Karunakara has a query:
"Dear sir,It is feeling that we are making journey along with you. As each institute has its own culture, how you managed it? Was there no conflicts? Thanks and regards"
My response
You are right. Whenever we go to another organization, we should first learn the practices happening therw, understand them ,adjust to them and become part of that institution. The proof happens when you are accepted as one of them. 
When I came to IISc, I was in a totally different world ,very different from my exposures. 
My world view enlarged. I learnt many languages. I was trying to understand multi Indian cultures across various states.
I saw many differences at IISc and needed a large scale learnings and adjustments.
The course starts right earnest from day one. You get a shock of tests in the third week itself. You are busy reading books in the library,doing assignments and solving tough problems. Everything was hard - language,teaching methods,exam, evaluations, reading culture ,work culture etc. I had seen a few students breaking down and leaving the institute.
My visits to TIFR for computer work exposed me to a totally different - intellectually intensive and collaborative- work culture. I had opportunities to network with computer users from academic institutions from Bombay,Bangalore and Calcutta and from several industries and utilities.
Then when I moved to Manchester university, I was in a foreign country and the culture was different. Again, I had to learn,accept and adapt to that culture. This was tougher.I also spent six months at UMIST. I learnt a lot of new things - how do students do project work? How is a PhD thesis evaluated? How do they teach? etc.
Then I moved to IIT Bombay. I quickly learnt the culture at IIT. There was a different aspect I needed to learn. While there were differences in academics, they can be adopted without difficulty. I discussed conducting exams. I saw a difference in project work evaluation. I saw several differences in administration of the department, estate office and main office. Leave rules were different. But I needed to be accepted by others as one of their own and not an outsider. I networked with many faculty members- close to 100 plus- and got into the IIT culture.
One reason I found in IISc why some new faculty took more to adjust was that they compared an IISc practice with the practice in their earlier institution- I found this prominent with those who came from IIT Kanpur- and keep criticising IISc. They couldn't adjust to IISc. I avoided this comparison. I wont talk about IISc unless some one asks for it. I explained in the senate about our practices and it wasn't mistaken. 
This was only a beginning. I moved into the secretariat of karnataka government later. The adjustment wasn't easy then. Later I taught at IIM Bangalore, AIT Bangkok and many universities and institutions. So my life was full of adaptations proving Darwins theory of success. 
One simple learning is
Understand people, be friendly and helpful, don't criticize, don't have value judgements.
That is the explanation
Thanks Karunakara
Greetings. Dks
12/12/25, 5:51 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: https://dksubra.blogspot.com/2025/12/accelerators-for-green-economy.html
13/12/25, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 923
After returning from Bangalore,I got involved in academic activities fully. I did write more than 10 papers during my stay at IIT. I attended conferences at several universities. I visited Delhi university and gave a talk on multi dimensional non linear optimization amidst many mathematicians. It was received well. I visited Jadavpur university ,Calcutta, Benares Hindu University ,Andhra University,Pune university and gave invited talks in conferences. I saw how universities were working. I gave a keynote at two CSI conferences- one at Calcutta and another at Bangalore, presented two papers at the Institution of Engineers ,gave a talk at an IEEE seminar on digital control systems and a paper on information and databases for resources at CSRE conference. So it was a productive year.
These visits were great experiences for me. I saw many cities  like Benares, Vishakapatnam, observed different cultures and got a much better perspective of India. The more important aspect was a great networking with academics and understanding the opinions of a highly diverse group of academics. Jadavpur had a good computer culture and the university housed a regional computer centre and I visited that centre.My friendship with Prof Radhakrishna murthy and Prof Raju of Andhra university continued for many years. I was glad that the computer science department was doing well and was way ahead of many universities.
I had a good experience of getting involved in social issues. There were many groups discussing many topics in IIT. I participated in some. One was about BHEL, and another was on nuclear power.

14/12/25, 5:08 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 924
I had a feeling that most academics are comfortable in their chair and don't want to stick their neck out even when it may concern them. The BHEL problem that exploded in 1978 was to me an important one. Gandhi talked about village independence ,economy and swadeshi. So it instilled a belief in me that dependence on  imports will not solve the country's economic and developmental problems. This is a perennial problem with divided opinions. Our first PM,Nehru talked about self reliance and self sufficiency,but his soviet model failed.
Let me discuss the BHEL problem. It started as internal politics and personal egos. In consequence,it led to the import of power equipment from foreign companies for the first time in the country away from BHEL. It involved power struggles between DrA Gopalakrishnan,I think son in law of Dr KL Rao, and Balakrishnan,ED. Gopalakrishnan,a renowned nuclear scientist was invited to come and head the R and D unit. The R and D unit became a football tossed amongst places. Dr Gopalakrishnan,being unhappy, started a failure analysis unit inside the R and D at Hyderabad and their findings criticized BHEL products.I felt that If there were defects, they should be discussed and rectified internally instead of open accusations. It was unfortunate,and it opened the gates for imports. 
Till then, BHEL had a formidable reputation for producing a variety of power equipment - boilers, turbines,generators,transformers, switch gear etc.It had been doing a great job in building most power plants in the country. 
I had many connections with BHEL. I knew its first MD, Mr Swayambhu, and met him a few times.He was the first director of CPRI. He always thought of improving the production and introducing new technologies. He showed a keen interest in power electronics.He set up a separate research institute. I got my first job with that institute.
So what was my opinion on this import aspect which would destroy BHEL?
When I was doing the second year engineering in 1960, I had a discussion with the principal,Prof Natesan. I saw a lot of small motors and asked him why we were importing thousands of fractional hp electric motors and why couldn't we manufacture them in India? He said that it was a tragedy. We even imported watches then. HMT was yet to begin manufacturing. I felt strongly about planning a manufacturing infra in the country in order to reduce imports. So later, I appreciated Indian manufacturing when I joined the Easun group and saw their efforts in manufacturing transformers, switch gear and relays. This opinion got strengthened when I saw BEL,HAL,HMT in Bangalore and I was happy. 
I felt that it was a tragedy to destroy a great and highly productive and useful organization through petty quarrels. We killed the machine tool sector starting with the killing of the heavy engineering corporation,Ranchi. Personal egos should not destroy an organization. So I spoke strongly in that meeting that we should oppose the move by the government to import power equipment from abroad. Later on in the nineties ,I was involved with two failure problems, one dealing with BHEL switch gear failure resulting in large financial losses to KPC.
Today, I am glad that BHEL is still there and still supplying most of the power equipment required in India. There are only a few public sector companies surviving in India and BHEL is one.
I talked about government supporting imports against local products of BHEL. This confusion was started by Nehru. He talked of self sufficiency, but prevented the Indian private sector from manufacturing through his license permit mechanisms. That introduced corruption and crony capitalism. Till mid eighties, imports were allowed partially but local production was curtailed. But the urge to import continued to exist even. Narasimha Rao opened the doors for imports while removing the permit license policies. India's production started reducing leading to the death of many big companies like HMT, Kirloskar etc. Imports are exploding now. So don't blame Nehru alone. It is in our colonial mind set- west is the best.We import a lot from China now. Idealism has no meaning with our governments. When will we learn? PM Modi brought in the make in India concept.Does Atmanirbar have any meaning when all electronics goods are imported from china despite knowing their dangers.

We saw several prime ministers in the country bringing in social reforms. But the reforms in the economy are not easy to for big bang. Many variables are there, like technology,skills, motivation, needs, pushes,market and policies. But we miss the underlying factors- competencies to produce/manufacture, physical infrastructure to distribute, communications to collaborate, futuristic thinking to move forward , controls ,flexibility, convictions and beliefs. I assumed that macro economic theory will address these basic requirements for the development of a country. Unfortunately, we are dogmatic and not practical, we are ignorant and not very knowledgeable, we are diffused in our thinking and not focused, we are emotional and not clinical, we believe in and are attached to past cultures and we change slowly many years after the need for a change arose. It will surprise us that organizations follow a rigid, static rule based, centralized top heavy governance system. It was acceptable for the early 20th century, but is obsolete for the uncertain, volatile and complex 21st century. But most organizations don't bother and stay in the 20th century mindset. They corrupt the 21st century kids. So our self reliance and atmanirbar as well as swatcha Bharat are struggling. 
I attended the Economists conference inaugurated by Mr Morarji Desai,our PM at that time. He and Mr Jag Jivan Ram landed at IIT. He walked briskly and went and sat in the front without any big protocol.He was a very simple person. Jag Jivan Ram had to be carried to the dais and it took 10 minutes and the PM was waiting patiently. I liked Morarji's energy and simplicity. His speech was short and strong without platitudes. 
I want to touch upon the faith of most planners and economists today - the total belief in market economy, market corrections,profit motives. Despite the government coming up with mandatory CSR, most industry or commerce units don't believe in it. They treat it like a tax without any involvement.
One of my major achievements at IIT was producing a well balanced - theory, hardware and software- computer science curriculum for the MTech program. While, most young faculty were enthusiastic and motivated and involved in research activities, I was surprised at the situation. Most concentrated on their area and didn't show a holistic approach to computer science, each knowing about his area only. So a balanced ,holistic view of computer science and algorithmic thinking- I consider that an important attribute of a CS student- were missing in them. Most faculty didn't show interest in data and algorithms. They thought that both were peripheral areas in CS. Except Sarda, none was working in a modern area. Futuristic vision was absent. I was always on the lookout for the latest developments, got into new areas of research and had a good knowledge and teaching exposure in many broad areas of CS. So I found these lacunae not acceptable. But they cooperated with us and agreed to a new curriculum. Prof Kekre didn't have any role there.We pushed the new curriculum through the senate easily. I defended the curriculum and removed the perception prevalent amongst many professors that CS is a part of EE- electrical engineering. Everyone appreciated my talk and i was happy that we started June 1978 with the new curriculum. I started teaching data structures and operating systems.

16/12/25, 4:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 926
Let me continue with my IIT Bombay activities in 1978. I became a member of PGPC- a committee that governs the functioning of graduate programs in IIT. The committee looked at curriculum development and conduct of courses in the entire institute. The Departmental program committee managed the curriculum at the department level. IITs follow this structure. In IISc we had a senate curriculum committee and departmental committees managing the programs. In all such cases, the senate gives the approval in the end. At IISc ,we have faculties - one for science and another for engineering- looking after the programs.
It is a good point to discuss one essential component of higher education- academic governance. It is a well developed area,but ignored by most and misunderstood by many.
The first problem is we don't know the differences between three terms applied with respect to the working of the organizations and governments- administration, management and governance. Governance is normally a set of objectives and another set of principles, organizational structures and procedures. Initially the focus was on stability and order. Then we introduced fairness, openness, transparency into governance. Then we looked at non centralized or distributed participative governance providing satisfaction to all. Now the focus is adaptation to the future with minimum time lapse- instead of waiting for the future events, create them.
Every high quality institution was concerned about the quality, modernness and relevance of the curriculum - both the framework and contents- followed by it, about changing them every year and also about evaluations. It should have a mechanism for resolving disputes and grievances. It should be seen as fair. That is academic governance. Unfortunately, that is centralized in universities and most functions are ritualistic or absent. All universities concentrate on exams ,not on a modern curriculum, a good teaching process and a better knowledge transfer mechanism. Our science education degraded because of the poor governance at the universities.
But it is the focus of life in IITs and IISc. 
I want to talk about the power of academic governance at IITB by explaining an incident that happened in 1978. 
A faculty member in the Mechanical engineering department was dismissed citing negligence in duties. Ramanna was the chairman of the board of governors and refused to discuss the case. The faculty threatened to go on strike - while they will do routine teaching and research, they won't do any admin or other work including setting up and evaluating JEE papers. So IIT yielded. The enquiry committee exonerated the dismissed faculty and he was reinstated. That is the power of good principles of governance.
We had some visitors while living in Bombay. My uncle Sankaran visited us and spent a few days. We visited the Mahalakshmi temple with them. Mr Thimme Gowda came and spent a few days in Bombay. He stayed in the city. He had consultations with an ENT specialist. I went with him to the consultant. He suggested minor surgery. After discussions, Mr Gowda decided not to undergo surgery. We visited a wholesale textiles shop and bought some sarries at a low rate. I was taken aback with the volume and variety - thousands of items in a single place.
Dr Deekshatulu came for a PhD oral. 
Let me talk about another learning about project work evaluation next.
