17/12/25, 4:03 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 927
IIT had a good evaluation mechanism for the project work of its MTech students. A year is divided into segments and at each segment, one aspect of progress by students is reviewed by three faculty members. The expectation of the first review done at the end of one month was to see whether 
1.the student has defined the problem well , in detail, the motives and deliverables defined, literature work done, resources required and available, difficulties etc.
2.the project work is feasible .
3.the student gives an action plan - the next stages, ways or methods of going ahead with each phase, timelines . 
The students provide a write up before the presentation. Most faculty read the reports before coming for the presentation. I appreciated their commitment to quality and seriousness to the students' work. They didn't say that it is the faculty supervisor's responsibility. The committee functioned not as an evaluation team,but as a guidance team in addition to helping in the evaluation. This system is very different from the IISc system. In IISc, there is only one final evaluation. The total responsibility is with the supervisor,who also plays a big role in the award of the final grade. If you let one person direct a project, it will have one view- the view of the supervisor. But in the case of IIT, a student get multiple inputs based on three views. That is good. Most practical problems have many solutions and many approaches for solutions using many technologies. So the IIT model opens the minds of students to these multiple paths.
The second review was to see their preparation, their plan and design work. The third review looks at the progress of implementation and experiments to be carried out. Then the final evaluation happens to award grades.
I thought it was a good system. That is the reason we use this system to direct the shortlisted proposals towards a successful completion in the FAER scholar program.
I had a previous experience on project work at UMIST where students should have a notebook which lists discussions with the supervisor by the student. The student should note down actions to be done and compliances. That was another good idea. 
These approaches make evaluation rational and justifiable, like the concept of continuous evaluation of course work. 
It is important to tell students that project work is not a free for all activity. It is a structured process having many steps and good documentation and good plans for action. It is not just to rig up a circuit and make it work. It is a training to solve a problem end to end in a professional manner.
Greetings
Dks
18/12/25, 4:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 928
I was invited for a keynote for the CSI annual convention in 1979 at Bangalore. I came to Bangalore in Jan 1979 for the talk. Isaac wasn't happy with the topic chosen by me - it was given by the organizers. The topic was computer controlled power systems. He didn't come. 
Surprisingly , I was seeing a trend in CSI- lesser involvement by academics. Initially when the computer society was set up with Prof R Narasimhan as the first president, many academics and researchers were happy and participated in CSI activities. When Prof RN handed over the presidentship to Mr FC Kohli, many weren't happy. Unlike the Institution of Engineers, the computer society started looking at business activities more, the reason being the number of academics was less and more business people became members. Initial years saw participation by faculty and students from IITs.
Actually many from IITs stopped attending the CSI conventions.  That was because of the bigger focus on business , technologies , exhibitions that became the hall mark of CSI. But I attended the conventions till the mid nineties. Why?
The exhibition told us about the latest developments in the computer area. We became aware of what is happening in the CS area on the developments and manufacturing side. It was very useful for me to advise industries. Some international companies exhibited their products.
Secondly,I saw how businesses were adopting computers in their operations. It was painfully slow.
Thirdly, I had an opportunity to talk about the latest developments. I gave a talk introducing databases. I saw again how people and experts rejected data bases. They said the database operations are slower compared to file systems. Most had invested heavily in file systems and didn't want to change. So there was an all round attack on databases and this continued for the next five plus years. I ran several tutorials in CSI conventions and introduced many new areas.
Fourthly, I liked the opportunity to move with business leaders to know their views. That gave me a totally different perception, away from academic understanding. I spent the sixties and early seventies attending the CBIP conferences and met many chairmen and chief engineers of electricity boards. I knew their views and it was disappointing, but a few understood my talks. I found one leadership trait in those meetings.These leaders may not understand or partially understand what one is talking, but they form an opinion based on audience response. Their perception is derived and intuitive. I found the business heads better in CSI. Some were well educated and most were well trained. So discussions were better. That revealed an important fact to me that ownership and responsibility gave better leadership than the seniority based leadership of electricity boards. That is why railways in the public sector succeeded compared to electricity and water boards also in the public sector, which are still not doing well. 
I met NV from the department. He told me that I was promoted in the December council meeting. Surprisingly, I was neither happy or elated. I expected it. So it wasn't a surprise. My return was now confirmed.

After returning from Bangalore after CSI, I had a major worry.

He got a different idea. Many senior faculty  in IIT were unhappy with the totally inadequate and poor computer facility. They felt that it hampered their research work. I supported their thinking about getting a better computer. So the dean was excited. He thought that he could use me to achieve the objective of getting a new computer. He asked me to give a note. He spent a lot of time to build the centre and was a part and parcel of the centre. I had a lot of respect for him.So I won't be happy to replace him. I would also antagonise Prof Kekre,a senior. 
To show how careful I was to get accepted by the department faculty, let me explain my relationship with Kumar. It was a close one. He helped me throughout my stay at IIT Powai. Without him, I would be lost. When he was in Manchester, I used to discuss his research work regularly with him. He had registered for PhD at IIT with Prof Kekre. I knew his close friendship with Kekre. So I decided not to discuss his research activities, as I felt that I would become an intruder. That was my advance thinking. Actually, I wasn't shooting my mouth at IIT whenever I talked to another faculty member. I had seen the impacts of my statements several times. People - most faculty I met with- liked me and respected me. So I should behave with maturity. I became a thinking,learning, observant,responsible and mature person at a very young age under the training of my uncle.
So I was thinking about breaking the news of my return to IISc and was feeling that it wasn't a happy statement. I felt miserable. I delayed it for some more time.  
We found that our daughter had short sight. I took her for an exam by an ophthalmologist. He prescribed glasses for her.
I bought a tricycle. This tricycle,a good and sturdy one, gave me some small pin pricks while traveling. I bought it and brought it home by the local train to Vikhroli. I was fined at Vikhroli station for not paying luggage charges. 
In BTR quarters , the hall was big. I thought my daughter could learn to cycle there. But I got a surprise. My son took to cycling. He was short,still he monkey pedaled and learnt to cycle. He enjoyed cycling. I was happy and discovered his energy. I discovered his curiosity when we were staying in the quarters at IISc during May,1978. He tried to put his finger inside a plug point and got a shock. He started crying.He was courageous,may be foolhardy, but was also timid many times. He would mimic my daughter's responses and fear. At IIT, he was growing up,but was feeling lonely after my daughter started going to LKG.So the cycle gave him a job to do. Kumar saw him cycling and commented that I didn't put up the dining table as it would have obstructed my son from cycling.

21/12/25, 4:45 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 931
Vinod Vyasulu
"DKS, it may be helpful if you explain how academic ranks like asst prof, assoc prof in IIT/IISc/IIM differ from the UGC system."
My response
Till the second pay commission, salary scales varied amongst states and central salaries were higher.Both designations and salary levels were different for college faculty and university faculty. Some states had several cadres like assistant lecturer, associate lecturer, reader, associate professor etc.
Till the second pay commission, IITs,IISc and IIMs followed central government class1 officers grades with lecturer salary starting at class 1 first level. Till 1967, there was no DA. Government of india started DA with third pay commission and linked it with price index. That was a big boon for people. States followed it after a time delay and state DA didn't match central DA. For instance when I joined the government of karnataka, my salary was protected . But within six or seven months, central DA went up, and all IAS and IPS officers got central DA and I didn't. So my salary, which was matched with my IISc salary when I joined, got reduced to a lower amount and I was losing money every month. That shows the power of DA. It becomes over a period of time almost equal to the basic pay. 
Fourth pay commission decided to bring in UGC scales within its ambit and it had a separate committee to decide on faculty salary. So the basic salary levels of IISc,IITs,IIM as well as UGC pay scales were made the same. 
One important impact of the fourth pay commission was the good hike in pay scales. For the first time, most could live comfortably, buy appliances and save money. This wasn't easy with our salaries till the end of the third pay commission. It is my personal experience. I started breathing easy and didn't worry about shortage of cash to pay for some requisites. Actually ,I bought a godrej refrigerator in 1980 and a color TV in 1984. I bought a washing machine much later in the early nineties.I added a lot of furniture from 1980 onwards. My first car came in the nineties with a loan from IISc.
Unfortunately , I feel this committee made a big mistake in removing the lecturer's position and making assistant professor as the entry grade.PhD was the minimum requirement for selection. Most institutions couldn't get eligible candidates for the faculty position. So the eligibility clause was watered down. That affected the general quality of academics.
But as I said, the DA made the difference. The other major advantage was housing provided by IITs,IIMs and IISc. 
Normally, these decisions have long term impacts. But the pay commissions looked for ease of implementation.
So to conclude, there is a marginal difference in salary levels at most institutions. Faculty in NITs get the same salary and facilities like those in IITs. But quality of research?
Greetings. Dks
22/12/25, 4:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post  932
Let me return to 1979. On my return to IISc , I got immersed in activities and quickly filled my plate with many types and areas of work. It filled up faster than my expectations.I involved myself in several activities. 
From childhood, I have been a multidimensional person focusing on many areas and many activities. I walked on the paths of Mathematics, Sciences, four languages, literature,religious studies together during my school time. While I loved mathematics , I also loved other subjects and hated none and no favorites.
First, I found some changes in the institute and city. Prof Ramaseshan, as joint director, was making changes. There were many complaints about the library. The librarian went ahead and acquired many books for the library without consulting departments and it wasn't liked by most faculty.Having been in the institute for more than ten years, he didn't understand the institute ethos. He was a man who thought of himself highly. He felt this job inferior to his earlier job in Chicago. He antagonized a lot of faculty. He was a close friend of mine. To his credit, I should say he was forward looking and wanted to change the working style, but didn't get support from his staff. The library wasn't bad but the image was ruined .Small problems were magnified. That is an important lesson for those who wanted to lead a team or department. Don't rush into the system like a bull without knowing the depth of your unit. Remember the proverb " Fools rush in where angels fear to tread’. The idea is that it is silly to become involved in a situation that you do not understand or are not equipped to deal with, and that a wiser person would hold back.
So the first task is to understand your system, people ,working conditions. The second is to know the problems ,resources, procedures etc. The third is to take your colleagues with you. Gain their confidence. Don't belittle them publicly. Discuss your ideas with them and convince them. The fourth is to build a positive,happy working environment. 
So Prof Ramaseshan revamped the library by setting up an executive committee and removing the librarian from running the library.
 His next attention was on the inefficient running of our computer centre. He roped me in that worrysome problem. The staff didn't know how to manage a time sharing system. Also,DEC10 was not intended for about 700 users in timesharing mode. It wasn't performance tested for a large number of users. System slowed down considerably with many compute bound jobs- remember our jobs are highly compute bound. As Prof Vijayan said , " we had a large number of crystallographers. They had compute bound jobs. This stressed the system." I started seeing the stress on computing at IISc and the totally unresponsive, incoherent and ineffective management of the centre. The only one who was interested in the technical aspects was R Krishnamurthy, a student of mine in ME and all others had no clue of running the system. Communications amongst themselves and with users didn't exist. I was involved with computer centre management till 1984. I will discuss more on this later.
Second, Prof G Krishna became the chair of my department,School of Automation. I became a member of the active committee of professors. The department breathed a fresh life and openness. Everyone was happy. It was a big change and transition. So I got involved with most activities in the department.
More in the next.

Let me continue with my involvements after return from IIT.
Third, GK asked me to head the hybrid computer facility. Though hybrid computer usage was declining , the PDP/11 mini was extensively used by our students. I gave assignments of assemblers,loaders and operating systems and students programmed then in the PDP/11 machine. I took charge of the facility and continued till 1995, a long tenure.
Fourth, I was made chair of two committees - one to revamp the new ME computer science program and the second one relating to starting a BE program in Computer science. 
Fifth, another major change was the setting up of an evaluation process for employees. A process was evolved with seven evaluation committees and a central evaluation committee. I was made the chair of the central evaluation committee. It was a big job with responsibilities to set up the seven committees, schedule the interviews,get their assessments, analyze and declare the final results. Evaluations are done twice a year. I continued with this committee till retirement, again a long tenure.
Sixth,ASTRA activity was picking up. Now many were living in Ungra village. We started on a community bio gas plant project. Lots of purchases had to be done. Most buildings were under experiments. So I was appointed as a joint convener of ASTRA and took over the admin responsibility. We set up an office. I was traveling to Ungra not regularly ,but several times in a year. So I had a tight job in ASTRA signing hundreds of bills, getting approvals, coordinating the activities and checking the budget.
I was also sad about something else that I observed. AKN spent a lot of time reading and writing. But his human relations were not going well. Prof MVN who built the instrumented bio gas plant , Prof Ramaprasad, and many others who showed a lot of interest in ASTRA moved away. Only KS Jagadish was active in ASTRA. While ASTRA was increasing activities,it was becoming a loner. 
Seventh was the analysis of data collected through surveys. I started working on that. The first step was the conversion of the collected data in papers into a machine readable form. I found Mr Mallya who undertook the job. So I assigned that task to him. Once the data on punched cards were available, I would do the analysis. Programming was a simple task.

24/12/25, 4:11 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.   934
Let me continue with my new activities.
Eighth, a big path changer in my research areas happened. This started when I was in IIT itself. I mentioned about it- my interest in data and data base. I decided to work on database more than on OS.I liked relational model compared to the then prominent commercial models- hierarchical and network models. While both were available in the main frames, nothing was available in minis. So I wanted to build a relational database system - without any SQL at that time. I got a student to build this relational system. He actually met me when I joined back in march79 and I agreed to guide him. We decided to focus on an agricultural database. He completed his work by 1980. 
I started teaching the database course in the institute from 1979 onwards and continued it for two decades. I started with Date's book. But the book was bad. I had to rewrite his definitions of normal forms. After a few years,I got Ullmann's book in the Indian edition. That was a good book and I used it as a text book in my course afterwards. I tried to redo most definitions precise. I looked at many books, but the best was CJ Codd's classical paper on relational data model published in 1970. Indian companies started using relational databases only in 1985 or so. Even then, there were many hiccups in usage.
Nineth, I got involved with the students project program of KSCST. It was a major activity for me also.This was started in 78 and we invited project proposals from students from many engineering colleges in the state including private colleges and selected many and funded them. Initial projects were focused on rural area problems in agriculture, irrigation, cooking, renewable energy ,bio gas,rural transportation, support to artisans like potters, smiths , etc. I felt that this would be a transformative activity not only for KSCST, but also for many engineering colleges in the state.About fifteen plus colleges participated in this program in the beginning. Colleges outside Bangalore showed more interest than the city colleges. Most principals were excited and gave their support to this program.It energized about 100 faculty in the state. Before this KSCST program, colleges were doing routine non motivated,non focused teaching according to a dated curriculum of the universities. This program exposed them to the skill of solving a real problem faced by our people in villages using technologies. That was a great change. They found meaning in the project work.Normally researchers choose a problem focused on finding an application for a technology or modification of a technology. Here we said ," look at the problems faced by villagers and select a technical solution for that." That meant that they were solving real world problems. They could see the current methods and build technologies into them. Most students were happy. 
In a few years, I enlarged the scope to electronics and computers. I handled one project in 1978-79 but by 1982, I handled about 50% of the projects. It was a heavy workload.
Let me discuss my learnings from the SPP program. I knew about the actual working of many engineering colleges in Karnataka. I knew about the capabilities of faculty members, management, principals, Hods, knowledge levels etc. I got a very large exposure. We could expose students to engineering principles, design parameters, experimentation and excite them. The initial request for proposals made them to think and do some planning.
More in the next post.

25/12/25, 4:47 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 935
Let me move to a general problem faced by middle class people.Bangalore city was growing fast. But house construction didn't keep pace with requirements. Though Jayanagar and Rajajinagar were developing with lots of people buying plots and constructing houses, our neighboring areas like Malleswaram, Seshadripuram were not building new houses for rent at that time. Palace orchards was an expensive locality. Yeshwantpur, Sriramapuram were not suited for middle class families. So renting a house became a serious problem. Rents were going up.Malleswaram was yet to be bitten by the bug of multi-floor apartments. It was still in its conservative small town slumber. As the bus facility was poor, and scooters had ten years wait time and motor cycles were not well known ,IISc faculty preferred to live in Malleswaram and  walk to the institute. There were landlord gundaism with some being thrown out of the rented houses. Rents were shooting up. So this scarcity hit the basic requirement of peaceful living. Most faculty were upset about this and wanted the institute to build quarters for faculty. An organization called faculty association was set up to coordinate this issue of quarters on the campus. It was working for about two years by 1979. It wasn't getting any attention or support from the director. There was no progress in talks on building quarters inside the campus.
We had an apex body for the institute called court, which consists of several external scientists, industrialists and all professors of IISc. It meets once a year. Its president then was JRD Tata. He was against spending money on brick and mortar. 
The association decided to attend the lunch that preceded the court meeting, but decided to boycott the court meeting. 
I attended lunch. Most faculty left after their lunch.I was about to leave after lunch and in walked Mr Kohli. So I stayed on and was talking to Mr Kohli. I first met him in 1968 and met him afterwards in many seminars and CSI conventions. It was a peaceful talk between the two of us. He invited me to visit TCS and spend some time there. That was a small step,but it led to an association that continued till 2022. I cherish that new beginning. It provided a big window to the business world- how technologies are adopted, who adopted them, where are the opportunities, what type of areas exist, what is the level of opportunities, where should one focus on,etc. I saw how slowly TCS struggled to become the number one IT company in India. It was great learning,worth a case study in management courses.
JRD was shocked to see thin attendance at the court meeting, enquired the reason and on hearing about the acute housing shortage in the city,announced a grant of rs.50 lakhs for construction of quarters. So started the first major activity of building A to E quarters,which was taken up by Prof Ramaseshan and completed in 1984.

26/12/25, 5:06 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   936
I had another immediate task at home- my daughter's school admission. She had completed LKG at IIT and now I had yo admit her in UKG. I was hoping to get admission to Cluny convent,which was next door and hence easy for my daughter to go there. We tried through our neighbor and we didn't get a positive response. I didn't want to put her in a small garage running UKG program. I was looking for a school so that she could continue till 10th without changing schools.
I talked to Prof Joseph and he talked to the bishop. Unfortunately ,I got a recommendation for another school- Nirmala Rani school. So I admitted her there. Her teacher was a nice,soft person. She got adjusted to the school and liked the school.The school was in 18th cross, a few minutes walk from home, so no problem.
In the summer of 1979, I went to TCS to spend a week after the invite from Kohli. I stayed in TIFR hostels located in the campus. Me Kohli suggested that I meet all the leaders and know what is happening. He asked Dr PSB Murty to take care of me.I had met him before at IIT Delhi during a curriculum conference.The idea was to provide a window for business in the curriculum in addition to technologies and theory. But Dr Murty described the problems of payroll. He was on the offensive telling the academics the complexities of a payroll program. He thought his job was to convince the faculty about the complexity of business software. While the word software was well known, software engineering took a few more years to become a formal area of computer science. 
I met Dr Nirmal Jain first. We understood each other well.  He became a close friend. We are in touch even now. The first meeting was an ice breaking one and we started discussing many topics some days during lunch time.It gave me an idea on business operations and a view on software development. 
TCS was developing and running several application packages. So I focused my thoughts on improving algorithms, software development, and new technologies. I started on my familiarization program. That lasted for a week. I understood business, operations of TCS,and problems faced by TCS.
I met most groups in Bombay. I was impressed with the software group. I realized that they were working and resolving day to day problems. They worked in isolation and didn't network with the outside world. While Mr Kohli was a great networker with academics and business people- both technical and management persons- , most others weren't involved in networking at that time. So the organization had one drawback- it didn't know what was happening outside and what would be the future directions. 
Fortunately TCS had a strong relationships with Burroughs and IBM. But the models for business applications were evolving then. There were three models for business applications:
1.Total outsourced model - a precursor to cloud later. A computer intensive company would take a complete end to end job of writing the software, getting data and running the software supplying the results to the user. User doesn't buy or own hardware and doesn't hire programmers. This was the model TCS was concentrating at that time.
2.Partial outsourcing - develop software and sell it to a customer and help the customer to run it in his premises. There were a few projects in this direction in TCS.
3.inhouse model- some companies didn't like to outsource their operational processes to an outsider. So they built in house computer department , got programmers, built and run the programs in house with their own computer. Some didn't own a computer and used an outside computer on hire basis to run their in house programs. I knew several companies were going in this direction. Some examples were HAL,ACC,Tata electric companies etc. 
As I said ,most TCSers were proud of the company and their work ,but were inward looking without knowing what was happening outside except a few top brass. Attending CSI meetings would have partially solved this problem of ignorance. While Mr Kohli attended the CSI regularly, others didn't. 
The reason is the working style - a learning and firefighting mode. We will describe it in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
27/12/25, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  937
In order to understand the struggles and contributions of Mr Kohli, we should know the state of our computer economy at that time.
1.Computers were expensive and owned by a few organizations,mostly academic institutions. Imports were not easy and even mini computers were not available in the country.
2.Computers were used mostly for scientific work. Some organizations were using it for some business applications like payroll and inventory control.
3.Outsourcing was unknown. So convincing companies and banks to outsource their work was difficult. Many wanted in house computing.
4. Business model was different. It was B to B- a big change from the usual B to C model. Business requirements vary, individual organization based. No standardization existed. 
5. No business process was available.Customer involvement and domain expertise were essential.
6. Organizational structure is different. Empowerment at project level was needed. No central control. Talent search and talent allocation were major problems. 
7. Resources optimization was essential. All used a single burroughs computer. So allocation was crucial. Efficient usage was necessary.
Under the above conditions, Mr Kohli identified business opportunities very early and focused on applications. 
At that time, there was no business happening on program development in India. A few started writing Cobol codes.  Hardware sales were going on in small numbers. Government restrictions were high.
Though there were opportunities in hardware manufacturing, maintenance, system development areas, Kohli felt the future to be in software and services. He focused on project mode and not product development. He decided to concentrate on a new, untested area- software services. He established the services discipline. 
Mr Kohli's knowledge on the computer scene- present and future- came through many directions -
meeting with business heads in India and abroad;
meeting academics;
participating and lecturing in seminars and conferences;
Reading many computer journals regularly every day - a regular habit. 
His time management was very good. He was always looking for the future. He wanted to do many things on education ,agriculture and health.
In the initial period, he took projects end to end from application program development, data generation, running the programs, generating outputs and supplying to users- all with total confidence. Ramadorai was handling the business at the US end and I was told it was doing well. Both were pioneers.
So I visited every unit ,talked to the leaders and got a good understanding of TCS business in India. I met Mr Sahni and Dr Nirmal Jain several times. Nirmal was meticulous in details, had a lot of patience,listened to people.I didn't expect this kind of transparency and belief in me. I liked the software group headed by Haridas and the strong OR group. I submitted a report to Kohli. He read the report. I summarised my understanding and didn't put in writing my reviews and suggestions. I thought it to be inappropriate at that time.
I used to skip breakfast, had lunch at the nearby woodlands - sometimes with Dr Murty and sometimes with Dr Nirmal Jain. My student Lakshminarasimhan was working in Patni software with Narayanamurthy. They invited me for lunch one day. I had a simple dinner at TIFR canteen. I used to spend the evenings at TIFR library. I gave about six lectures on databases- my first lectures on databases. I was yet to start teaching database at IISc.
That was my story of my first TCS visit.
Greetings. Dks
28/12/25, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 938
What was my learning from my TCS visit?
It opened my views on the struggles of business. I learnt the following:
1. Business provides a large number of opportunities of applying computers to solve business sector. Academics ignored business programming. Only Mr Sarda taught Cobol programming at IIT Bombay then.
2. Though business has many dimensions, most businesses want to look at their accounting and financial operations. Banks had a lot of problems of scale. TCS looked at and applied computers to solve the inter bank transactions reconciliation activity. Similarly TCS also identified share market accounting as another major area. TCS identifications were correct.
3. Most jobs were outsourced jobs by banks and industries. User understanding and involvement were minimal. Trust was good between TCS and its business customers. 
4.TCS picked its employees from IITs and IISc and also picked several seniors at lateral entry level. It preferred ME/MTech pass out persons. It had some with CA experience and some from IIMs. Still most had no background in core computer science areas like data structures, systems programming, operating systems, programming design, problem solving etc. It was a big drawback.
5. Most employees were busy learning on their jobs and had a weak knowledge level particularly in CS area. They didn't have futuristic thinking. But most were committed to their jobs, spent hours on programming and were serious.
6.TCS top management had a strong customer focus and its actions were customer centric.Customer understanding and satisfaction were considered essentials 
7. No tools were used then. The employees called as associates- I consider the nomenclature very important- were learning programming and computing from experience. They didn't have formal knowledge of data-flow methods, design approaches to handle large programs. It was a bad period. Most trained on small problem solving were writing large programs- no modularity, no control, no design, no strong debugging procedures, no serious documentation of the software.Adhocism was evident. I put my students through a problem analysis and design phase before coding, but found people here starting with coding. I saw this adhoc approach followed by PSI to design their software. They spent all their time in debugging and changing the code with no documentation.I discussed with Dr Ravindran on this. I encountered this problem in most software industries in India even in the nineties. Dijkstra was not known to most programmers even though his book on the discipline of programming was released by Mr Kohli in 1975, I think, at the CSI convention. Both he and Prof RN released the book and I bought it.
8. Software engineering was just getting into production mode. I did performance studies in Manchester. I was reading several papers on software engineering. I read the maximum of papers in this area of software engineering. But no measurements were attempted at TCS.
The summer of 1979 got me involved in IISc admission activities. We were conducting entrance exams for admissions before the GATE was initiated. We conducted entrance exams for BE, ME, and MSc(eng.) admissions. I opted for going to Bombay to conduct the exams at IIT. I thought I could meet my friends.
I met Prof De, the director and was talking to him. I was telling him that I liked IIT a lot and would love to come back. I also visited TCS for a few days. 
The next academic year started with lots of activities. I met Prof Madhav Gadgil in the centre for theoretical studies. We discussed about energy and environment. He talked about the Bedthi river hydro power project and the agitation by local people in Sirsi in Uttara kannada district, about 400kms away from Bangalore. That excited me and got me thinking of alternatives. The forests department of Karnataka also was against the project. 
I studied the DPR prepared by the planning commission, a rebuttal by Paranjpay, and the objections by local people and forests department. Then I developed a new concept of total energy. While we get hydro power from the water drop and that is a plus point, we lose the energy from biomass that will be lost due to the submergence of 10000hectares of forest area. So I got the net energy obtained from the power plant and calculated the cost per unit of power and it was higher than thermal energy costs. Since the construction cost was about 60% of the total cost, I reduced the submergence area to one third, I got a much lower cost for power. This new idea was exciting.  I got different views on the hydro project.
Madhav said that the local people wanted to organize a conference on this and I agreed to participate and present a paper. Thus started my long journey in the area of ecology and environment. It deepened my studies on energy.
More in the next.

29/12/25, 4:03 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 939
These discussions with Madhav and others were interesting and provided two different angles,one environmental angle and another economic angle.We had discussions with Fr Saldanha, reputed botanist and taxonomy expert ,Dr Kailash Malhotra, who studied the locals in these areas particularly the nomadic gowlis, Dr Paranjpay, economist, and Mr Shyamsundar,Chief conservator of forests.  It revealed a few facts:
1.Hydro power planners don't care about destruction of forests and the life of people living and eking a livelihood there.  I found that the biggest project Sharavathi valley hydro power project was designed with a carryover reservoir storing not the rain water from the catchment area based on average annual rainfall  but the total submergence area was calculated based on the highest annual rainfall.This led to a destruction of larger forest area. Madhav had shown by then that people shifted from sharavathi project were settled in another forest area which was later submerged for the Kali project. They were shifted again, destroying their lifestyle and living. This social aspect was not considered by our planners and executing engineers.
2. The focus of hydro project planners was to show the economic viability- benefits to costs ratio should be above 1.5- that is the economy should get 50% more than what was spent. It was shocking to see that these numbers were manipulated to show a ratio of 1.5 in the DPR. Paranjpsy's recalculation showed the ratio to be less than 1.0.Economics of planning was manipulative.
3. The engineers maximized the submergence area to get maximum hydro energy and avoid spill overs. But they did only one design. No alternatives were considered. That was a tragedy. Most costs - about two thirds-were going into the dam and canals construction and only one third of the total cost went to the purchase and installation of the required equipment. There is an optimization existing there and not considered by the designers.
4. Engineers didn't understand the damage to the environment caused by the project. I learnt about biodiversity in these meetings.
I started defining the problem of power generation from a totally different angle. 
The first question is to look for optimal submergence area. So the area will be a variable and construction cost depends on it. It also affects the cost of displacement and afforestation.
The energy supply should consider both biomass energy available in the to be submerged area and hydro energy. So net energy available will be hydro energy to be generated- the lost biomass energy.
The cost of electricity generation should include not only the construction cost ,but also the cost of reforestation-compensatory forestry, cost of rehabilitation of the displaced people, and the cost of forest wealth lost. We should consider the social costs also. 
 I did the integrated, multidimensional model, and reworked the cost. We found the project to be not a viable one.
I also suggested a much better option of going for a run of the river hydro plant with minimal submergence - about one fourth of the proposal-, and the capacity of the generating plant to be increased from the proposed 200MW to 600 MW. That helps all. That was my paper for presentation at the conference. 
More in the next.
Greetings. Dks
30/12/25, 4:28 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 940
A conference on the Bedthi river hydro project was arranged in Sirsi in Uttara Kannada district,about 400 kms from Bangalore. Madhav arranged a bus for about 25 people and we went to Sirsi. We left in the morning and reached by night. I was put up in a hotel, but many stayed in the houses of a few farmers in neighboring villages. 
The conference was organized by the totagars cooperative society. It was open to all. Since most attendees didn't know English,but we spoke English, Prof LT Sharma, principal of the local college, took upon himself the task of translating the talks.
Madhav gave the first talk.Mine was the third talk. It was well received. I talked about reservoir size reduction, run of the river option and micro hydel projects. It was understood and appreciated. Prof Sharma explained my talk in Kannada to all and repeated my suggestions repeatedly.
I was told about some special breakfast given by the people. So I told the organizers. They shifted me to Bhyrumbe and I met another great person,Mr KM Hegde,a progressive farmer. I tasted their dosas ,very thin done on an inverted pot. It was great. I met Mr Hegde Kadve,MLA. He was a simple man living in a room in the hotel and didn't own a car. In addition to Kannada, they also had a language of their own -havyaka.
Mr. Shyamsundar,CCF,Karnataka said that Karnataka was losing forest lands rapidly. He said that people didn't support their views on deforestation. He also talked about large scale firewood supply from forests to big cities like Bangalore.
Another paper I remember was by a forester who estimated the cost of a tree taking into account its role in oxygen production, water retention and soil regeneration as 15 lakhs of rupees against a firewood value of a few thousand rupees. That is realistic costing. This means we can't bring nature into our economics. Nature will win hands down. Remember that we survive because of nature.
Some people told me that one Mr Ganapathy Hegde had set up a micro hydel generator in his house. They took me to his house. The villages were in the midst of forests and hillocks. The terrain was hilly.We found his house at a lower level and a spring was there with water flowing in from a height of 50feet above his house. He had put a pipe and connected a turbine and generator. He used the electricity for lighting and drinking water pump. 
He told me that in the 1960, the Sharavathy valley project was under construction. He met Mr Narayana Rao,chief engineer and got the idea from him. I told him that I know Mr Narayana Rao.I was impressed by his innovation without any technical knowledge.
We visited the gardens in the evening. These are areca gardens with many areca trees. They also cultivated pepper and cardamom. We were giving a demo of harvesting areca nuts from trees. The person climbed a tree, cut a whole bunch containing hundreds of nuts and with a rope he jumped into another tree- amazing skills. We saw the processing and also saw the production of paints organically from arecanut shells. It was a great experience. I was talking with Prof Sharma. We became close friends.
Then we were taken to the Sonda mutt. Prof Sharma introduced me to the Shankaracharya and told him about my suggestions. The swamiji blessed me. We had dinner there. We returned to Bhyrumbe. 
I met Mr Shivarama karanth the next morning. I sat with him and listened to him and learnt a lot from him about farming, education, ignorance and environment. He also told me about Yaksha gana. It was a great learning experience. I learnt a lot about the west coast of Karnataka. 
I decided to leave by bus that night. I got a packet of pepper and another of cardamom from the society and another set from my host. My stay was memorable. The enthusiasm and energy of the people were unbelievable.
I left for Bangalore by bus at 9pm. The bus broke down midway. I took another bus and came home in time for my lectures next day.
Prof LT Sharma quickly edited the proceedings of the conference with all publications and produced a book.
This conference was different from the hundreds of professional conferences that I had attended. It had a lot of public participation, involvement, interest and excitement. Considerable effort and planning had gone on to run the conference.It was conducted professionally, and the time management was good. My network of connections enlarged.

30/12/25, 6:24 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: The story behind Ekadasi, a sacred Hindu fasting day dedicated to Lord Vishnu, stems from ancient scriptures like the Padma Purana, primarily featuring a demon named Mura and the divine feminine energy, Ekadasi herself. When Vishnu rested after fighting Mura, the demon tried to kill him, but a powerful woman emerged from Vishnu's body (specifically from his Ekadasha Indriyas or eleven senses) and beheaded Mura. Pleased, Vishnu granted her a boon: whoever observed a fast on her day (the 11th lunar day) - no rice-would have their sins destroyed and attain liberation (moksha). 
Today is vaikunta ekadasi,more special day when the doors of Vaikunta, the abode of Vishnu will open. People in South visit many - at least nine- vishnu temples on this day hoping for moksha.

31/12/25, 4:22 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 941
When you talk to most people about the education of their children, the common worry is the building of mathematical skills in their children and mugging up to get good grades. They don't understand the importance of inculcating the qualities-contemplation, concentration, carefulness/ mindfulness,higher levels of consciousness. Another big weakness in school education is one dimensionality - specialization, expertise. This is dangerous. We don't understand the dangers of focusing on specialization at the cost of generalization. Unfortunately experts are overrated. Experts look at the past and work on a narrow topic or area. They can't look outside their domain. We need to break the barriers. 
When software was developing initially in the late seventies, I saw the tussle between programmers and use departments like Finance, sales, admin, stores etc. Programmers concentrated on coding and ignored the problems of users. Form filling was a complicated task unlike now. Even system analysts refused to understand usage aspects of a program. User friendliness came up only in the late eighties. Programmers had one dimensional approach - coding and optimized coding work.
Our problems are defined with many constraints:
1.We are good at looking at parts ignoring the connections and connectivity.
"You can't really know anything if you only look at isolated facts. If the facts don't hang together on a latticework of theory, you don't have them in a usable form. You have got to have models in your head. "
Charlie Mungar,Berkshire Hathway
2. We assume that solving a problem implies only mathematical approaches mostly. So we approximate and simplify a problem so that it can be modeled.
3. We simplify- use deterministic variables, known concepts, linearization etc.
4.we don't describe a problem. We look at a problem with ignorance, neglecting social ,environmental and economic aspects. The best example is the design of the Bedthi project by ignoring people, forests,real economic values etc.
 The best answer is to teach a general course on practical problem solving.
The experience of looking at a problem from multiple dimensions-energy,economics, the environment,society, technology, etc- gave me a lot of insights into what is missing in our life. It dwells on several practices we should do in our early life to be prepared to meet challenges. 
Greetings
Dks

01/01/26, 3:56 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 942
Let me continue with multidimensional problem solving.
Let me give some examples of challenges I faced in my life until 1980. 
We need preparations to meet challenges.I had many preparations from my early age from 7 years onwards. 
First get multidimensional affinity and thinking.
I became multidimensional at a young age - school studies, organization, finances and economy,moral values, religious studies, love for several languages like Sanskrit, Tamil,Hindi,English, political learnings and society. It was a very lucky happening for me. Let me start with one instance that helped me:
Conducting a ten day public sri ramanavami function- this function was organized by my uncle every year in the village. I was his assistant and student in this activity. This work taught me several aspects like identifying the activities and their requirements of men,materials,money; preparation of detailed planning; documentation and transparency; integrity and proper maintenance of accounts: time management; people management- get people to do the many tasks, satisfy the egos of some,fair dealings with all in food supply -. I was slowly initiated into this annual activity by my uncle. 
All these started when I was seven years of age and continued regularly till I was 20 years old. These were my life lessons:
1. Life should be organized like a function or an office. Order is key to a happy life, not just discipline. Be organized in whatever you do at home, office or society. Life is not adhoc, not impulsive,not dependent on others. Life is a complex multiple set of interconnected systems.
2.Independent thinking, acting and working are essential to free us from bondage and dependence on others. I started doing the shopping from age 8 onwards.That taught me responsibility.
3. Understand society, the behavior of people, 
4. Remember mind makes a person. This is a high impact statement to be understood every day by us. Confidence and positivity are the necessary elements in one's life, not for success alone but even for worry free living.
This gave me an important life lesson. 
Don't be afraid of unknowns, new items and accept challenges. I could face challenges as I had some basic tools to attack general challenges.
My purpose in life was to become a dynamic problem solver.
So I easily moved from power systems to computers to distribution systems to energy to environment to transformation without hesitation and solved several tough practical problems like Bedthi power plant, Delhi electrical distribution, IISc power design, computerization at IISc, and state government, IT for Jindal Steels, Central silk board, core banking, networking design for many organizations, COWAA for department of space, mahithisindhu program etc.
I used multidimensional thinking in these projects.
My second strength which helped me is my reading habit. I have loved reading, again from childhood, books and magazines. I loved social fiction, biography, history in the beginning years. In school days I used to read two fiction books every week. Later in my college days ,I moved to English books of both fiction and nonfiction.I loved detective stories and PG Wodehouse,Jerome K Jerome and more social novels by several English authors like Dickens, Thomas Hardy, Pearl S Buck, Hemingway and many others.I used to read at least one book per week. I also read some serious books on Yoga,Upanishads.I was familiar with Shankara's life and teaching. I have heard Ramayana about 50 times in those days and also read Mahabharata - all these during school days. 
That habit and training helped me in my life. First, I understood the multiple societies, cultures and lifestyles in India.I empathized with the problems of poverty and suffering. Most importantly, I learnt about people, their egos and behaviors from books and from my father and uncles.That was a great learning.I knew the political and economic problems faced by the country. I started building connections between all these- humanism, the economy, society,politics, education. My introduction to technology came later. These readings helped me to build a large canvas of views of several disciplines and more importantly their connections. I realized early that political strategies during the Nehru era delayed our development and economy. This also brought me out of my different wells in the village,city,state and exposed me to multiple new realities. So I started looking outside to gain awareness- outside meant looking at different kinds of people,culture state and society as well as different disciplines and domains. I don't want to call myself a computer scientist. That will be a partial view of me.
All these helped me in looking at all problems encountered by me through many dimensions. I didn't exclude any dimension. My outward looking approach also helped.I learnt that problem solving is a detective work,not a pure mathematical or logical or a computational activity.
Greetings. Dks
02/01/26, 4:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   943
The Sirsi conference was in mid 1980. After returning from Sirsi,I was getting involved with Madhav to prepare a proposal to set up a centre for ecological studies. Prof Ramaseshan became the director of IISc after Prof Dhawan's retirement. He wanted to improve the academic excellence of the institute and move into new areas.He wanted to set up many centres and planned a supercomputer centre also. He wanted to improve the operation of services like library and computer centre. He called me and talked to me about computers even when he was the joint director. He had good impressions about me. I used to meet him often. 
I want to recall one incident here. I always had a deep interest in education from school days. I have read Gandhi and Tagore on education, saw Rajaji's experiment and felt that educationists don't analyze the very complex problem systematically. We never had a common syllabus for the 90000 plus gurukulas - each followed its syllabus. When I read UV Swaminathaiyer's biography, I saw his search for gurus in many places and learning from many gurus. But educationists today believed that one size fits all is a correct approach.I was not satisfied then and to say frankly am not satisfied now about the state of education and engineering education in India. I was constantly thinking about solutions to a number of questions like 
What is the purpose of education?
Does our current system achieve those purposes?What is wrong? What are the solutions? How do we implement them?
Does our system provide effective understanding of what is taught?
What are the difficulties?
What are the remedies?
Can we improve the system?
Is it possible to build an education system that satisfies all?
Is uniformity of education a good idea?
How much engineering content is there in the engineering programs?
I was clear in my mind about teaching, quality and evaluation to a large extent. I used to read many books on education and continue to read them even now. I borrowed a book on education from our library and liked it. I discussed it with Prof Ramaseshan and gave the book to him to read. After about 15 days, I had to return the book and went to the director's office and got the book from him. His secretary gave me some advice- don't give a book to the director and expect it to be returned. They have thousands of books and it will be lost- a very sound advice. The incident was to show that there is a shift in relationship with directors. With Prof Dhawan, I wasn't in a discussing situation in the early days, but moved to a timid discussing situation later. My suggestions were politely worded and discussions were formal and strictly functional. But with Ramaseshan, it was more open. Later with Prof CNR, it was one sided.
Also my daughter completed her UKG by April 1980. I decided to admit her in the kendriya vidyalaya at IISc. An entrance test was conducted. She was shy and scared. She got admission.She joined in the school and went by a bus provided by IISc for children going to IISc school. The bus stopped in 16th cross,11th main - that is a short distance from my house at 14th cross. Madhav's daughter and son and SPG's sons traveled by that bus. So my daughter had companians.
