My response
Thanks. 
I am writing because of the encouragement from many of you. It surprises me that people read, filter out information not usable by them,and some give critical comments - and these for three years, really amazing. Mr. ABL who is past 90 years said yesterday that he reads most of my posts skipping technical ones. I thank every one.It gives me energy to go forward.
What I call multidimensional is a variation of the term polymath.
definition:polymath is a person with deep knowledge and expertise in multiple, diverse fields, demonstrating significant competence across various subjects like science, arts, and humanities, rather than just superficial interest. Driven by curiosity, they can connect different knowledge areas to innovate and solve problems, with historical examples including Leonardo da Vinci and Benjamin Franklin.
Polymaths offer a broader perspective, preventing the "tunnel vision" that can come from extreme specialization, fostering creativity, and helping to bridge gaps between different disciplines. That is the difference between these two terms. But today it is specialization. We need to reverse this trend. 
Polymath means a person who is an expert in many areas. Polymath people are rare. Hence I talk of multidimensional thinking.
Multidimensionality means something has multiple aspects, features, or layers, going beyond a single perspective to capture complexity, like how poverty isn't just lack of money but also poor health, education, or living standards, requiring integrated solutions. It applies to concepts (culture), people (complex personalities), and systems (data analysis), signifying a richer understanding emerges from considering various interconnected dimensions. 
Multidimensional thinking encourages us to dive into the layers, peel back the complexities, and embrace the richness of different viewpoints. A range of perspectives necessitates no simple answers that will work for everyone, in every context. One size fits all will not work here.
While polymath means discrete expertise in many areas unconnected, multidimensional thinking implies looking beyond one area and connecting to other relevant areas. It focuses not on areas but connections and flows. 
Let me look at another example. Ecommerce. It applies digital technology to commerce, but its success depends on
1.affordability, price, competitiveness
2.quality
3.ease of use ,user determinism, product clarity.
4. User behavior knowledge.
A designer of an ecommerce platform needs to integrate technology , commerce, price mechanisms,user understandable software,timely delivery,optimal operations. Here the key word is integrate.
Greetings. Dks
04/01/26, 3:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 945
Mr H S Nagaraja on Mrs Anantharaman's comments:
"Mrs. RA's excellently scripted comments  about the daily posts are by & large the views of every reader of these posts , I am sure.My special thanks to Mrs. RA and Happy new year 2026 to all.🙏🙏🙏🙏🙏"
Dr Chanakya makes very extensive comments on multidimensional problem solving.
"My outward looking approach also helped.  I learnt that problem solving is a detective work, not a pure mathematical or logical or a computational activity…………" DKSWhen we take involved /responsible roles in multiple domains, I have found that it helps one to simultaneously examine the problem at hand by wearing multiple coats/hats without need to provide immediate answers /solutions.  Learnings from these lived experiences allow us to simultaneously spread domain-based filters /examine the problem in multiple domains while also giving them various weightages for potential causation as well as identify path for potential solutions.  Here is where I found that the book reading habit gave us some strength, patience and perhaps a strategy; it allows us to pause the critical thinking process (to pause and examine tangential paths – seek out what is not said /stated explicitly and also question the veracity of the obvious path tendered) without losing out on our original search approach, strategy and rigour.  Various detective /whodunnit novels of our times – Agatha Christie, Arthur Conan Doyle, Erle Stanley Gardner, Sidney Sheldon, Le Carre’, Alistair MacLean, Fredrick Forsyth, etc. specialized in confounding the evidence to take us away from the real /hidden clues.  Add to these the ones quoted by DKS; Hemingway, Dickens, Hardy, PS Buck (to fill in more societal issues), we have almost always learnt that the case presented before us has multiple dimensions and needs to be analyzed with appropriate filters in multiple domains – quite often the answers exist in a different dimension.  Let me give an example from the environmental domain: The famous Bellandur Foams.The Bellandur Foams actually has a 20 year old history starting circa 2003-2004.  Various agents /agencies /institutions were blamed based upon using information gathered from the internet sources- for example, the heavy metals, phosphatic detergents, mysterious bacteria, etc.  In the subsequent years, as the problem grew stronger, even “Lake on Fire”, mysterious Industry discharges, commercial Laundries, Car washing units, etc. were blamed and the Courts even closed them.  The Foam didn’t stop even after six months.  The Foams continued and PCB quietly withdrew their orders.  Let us confound the problem a little bit more.  If one blames a particular or a group of substances, then heavy rains should dilute them and foams should cease.  Ironically, the foams emerged immediately after heavy rains negating the conventional logic of implicating high concentrations of a suspected substance.  Why should heavy rains that dilute the Lake’s water by over twenty times cause foam – counterintuitive!Detailed and sustained investigations revealed that there were over 55 domestic wastewater discharge inlets into the Lake.  All these released their share of detergents (washing machines, kitchens, shampoo, etc.), all of which gradually got loosely bound to the sludge settled in the Lake and got concentrated over a hundred times.  With every heavy rainfall -runoff event, this sludge was stirred up, detergents released and when it cascaded down over 50 feet over the spillways, foam production was inevitable.  Add to this our perception of what a good shampoo /detergent should do – recall the great ads for shampoo ‘displaying the girl with shampooed hair with a large foam corona!’  The detergents marketed had high foam stability and as a result the foam remained unbroken for over 100-150 minutes.  Stitch these from different domains, causative agents, environmental-ecological retention in water body sludge, re-dispersal during monsoon run-off, spillways dropping detergent laden water over 50-60 feet fall, foam stability as a quality of the detergent, dispersal of foam from Lake sludge, etc. you get an answer to the problem.  Any one domain could not have explained this issue.  By the way, such large foams are seen in Yamuna (Chat Festival nearly 20km long), Hyderabad, Chennai, Madurai, Ambur, Mysore, etc.  Yet only Bangalore foam becomes a problem of international importance.  To make a long story short, clearly a multi domain knowledge /experience /thinking /assessment could resolve this nearly 20 year old problem.  Water shortage problem of 2024 summer, treated wastewater recycling in Bangalore, etc. follow very similar paths.  Much of our problems solving efforts require multi-domain knowledge, forays and experience.  They don’t follow the older logical, linearized solutions generally purported as DKS has brought out.  Thank you DKS to raise this issue.  This also becomes important when we mentor our FAER mentees who are often stuck within their narrow ‘educational blinkers’.  Happy New Year"
Thanks to both. 
Greetings
Dks
05/01/26, 4:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 946
My response to Chanakya's comment. I feel that we need to address several issues here.
1.Make both teachers and students to understand the limitations of formal education. Formal education is not end all. It teases you and does not teach you. That means you get a small and not always true glimpse of reality.
Education is not a push system. It is not a one way system. It should be a pull system- students guided by teachers pick up what they want to know. They should have freedom to learn, question, critique ,analyse ,understand and go forward or deep.Education is interactive,not monologue.
PG Wodehouse writes ," education is not putting something into a child, but pulling something out from inside a child."
Mel Robins writes a simple principle - let them. Allow the children to decide. Don't assume you know everything in this fast changing, uncertain,complex world. Teachers are not the know-all people anymore. 
2.Both teachers and taught should  learn to be skeptical and question the so called facts. Just as religion started with a search for reality, education should be approached in a similar manner - to search for the many facets ,components and connections of reality and build a big structure of learnt concepts and seen/ experienced concepts.They should discuss the pros and cons of several beliefs produced by the formal education system. I feel that we know that religion is based on faith and beliefs. Don't you think that education, particularly social education, is also dogmatic based on biases,perceptions and beliefs. 
3. Education should focus on building confidence,inculcate reading,writing and thinking habits and provide opportunities for creativity. Dogmas have no place here.
4.Let me give an example of the point- how education restricts us to a well orchestrated system. 
I taught OS- operating systems- for 20 years. OS in reality changed a lot from the old IBM 360 OS to UNiX to MsDOS to windows to Android of many versions. But OS taught has remained the same. OS text books have remained the same. OS concepts have changed over time in reality but not in teaching. Dockers don't find a place. User interfaces to chatbots are ignored. I discussed this with many faculty for decades. I commented on this during reviews of OS books and in academic councils, but no understanding and hence no changes. So we are struck with 1973 mindset. This has to go. 
So we need to become open minded, flexible and accept that questioning and criticisms are fundamental rights. Multidimensional approaches to our disciplines is a high priority.
5. We don't teach generalized problem solving philosophies. That is the need today. Processes will be taken care of by AI.So move up in thinking.
6. Practical problems are fuzzy and partially defined. Consider a practical problem- selecting a future education program for your child after school. How do you decide? First you collect information on universities and courses. Then you decide on the money to be spent and the means of getting it. Then you decide whether you want the child to be near you or can go any place. Then you decide courses of interest. Now you sit as a family with your child, prepare a list of probable candidates- universities and courses, and start discussions on all the candidates with all members participating in discussions. You go through each candidate, identify its advantages and disadvantages, discuss in detail, and collect more data. Also look at the future, the growth of the areas,job opportunities, stability of the universities etc. People also globally looking at universities in many countries.Consult friends, look at market, and discuss. Then you look for compromises and take a decision based on emotions and reactions. Try to take all along. Intuition and heuristics play a big role. Multiple aspects will be looked at. Emotions, acceptability, costs,beliefs,aptitude,adjustments at the university,social advantages,cultural similarity, familiarity etc play a part in the decision making. Mathematics plays a marginal role. We need to address human traits in arriving at decisions. You may get many answers. So you go for another filtration. Also see that the decision is not based on a single parameter, but on many factors. It is a multi-stage process.Finding a job, life partner etc may be more challenging with too many uncertainties. That is what I call search, and discovery process.The study of problem solving by people should be taught to children.
Greetings
Dks
06/01/26, 4:50 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 947
As we enter the eighties, let me look ahead the eighties decade and its impacts on me and the country.
The eighties ,I consider as a waking period for the country. It woke up to new technologies.We, the normal Indians, became aware of technology and its advantages. We moved from an agrarian economy to a mixed agriculture and industrial economy. While the seventies focused on industrial goods, the eighties slowly moved into consumer goods. But quality is still a long way ahead. The high quality of HMT, which in the sixties established precision machine tools infrastructure and produced high quality lathes and great automatic watches, was not appreciated by the government.
 The eighties saw a major shift in the government focus from public sector to private sector. The Indira Gandhi government wasn't the original strong government. It was a weaker version. No bravados like Garibhi Hatao or Indira is India slogans. Even the planning commission lost its focus on large projects. It became an also ran institution. The vigor seen in Nehru's days and Indira Gandhi's early days was gone. We didn't see any major government projects . Even the power sector struggled.The telecom sector was totally ignored till the nineties.
Bangalore started changing from the sedate public sector focused industries and mini industries to a power house of electronics industries and educational institutions. This change was initiated in the seventies with the electronics city being set up. I think Bangalore had two flashes of great planning - the Peenya industrial complex in the sixties and the electronics city in the seventies. Both became the nucleus for the industrial growth of the city. But the eighties - particularly the latter half- started building Bangalore into what we now call as silicon valley of India. I saw the seeds of this and played a major role in this transformation of the city.More on this later.
Coming back to the country, electricity reached most urban households. Godrej refrigerators became available. I bought one in this period. TV entered India with limited public broadcasting by Doordarshan.By mid eighties, color TV was available. I bought an inexpensive Eiko TV by 1984.Telephones were still under ITI and the department of telephones. Getting a connection was difficult.
But the major technology making inroads into medium to large organizations was the computer. 8 bits VLSI Intel chips and Zilog etc were available in India. PSI set up in Bangalore in a small building, in the mid seventies, started with the production of controllers with zilog processors and by the eighties, it graduated to producing small computers with operating systems.But its focus was hardware and it neglected software principles.I bought one for KSCST from Zenith. It was called superbrain. While main frames were costing crores of rupees and were considered a white elephant, these small computers were costing less than 10 lakhs of rupees and the cost was coming down. It was a period of computer curiosity and experimentation.Fortunately, the government allowed the import of micro processors and a few companies like PSI in Bangalore,Zenith in Bombay ,DCM and HCL in Delhi started assembling small computers and selling them. Companies used them for calculations like an advanced programmable calculator and were typing letters.TCS moved fast in software services in many countries. It saw opportunities, accepted the challenges and built software systems for many companies across the world. It was a software building phase and COBOL became popular. Database started picking up only in the late eighties with both Oracle and DB2 becoming popular.
While India didn't embrace minis of PDP/11 , it started absorbing the micro computers.But there was no programming expertise available. COBOL was still strong, but couldn't be used in these small machines.So usage didn't pick up till mid eighties when Eiko came up with low cost 8bit processors with displays and keyboard integrated into a single unit. By 1987 we got a PC and email connectivity. ERNET was established giving us email facility.That led to a new life.
More in the next.
Greetings. Dks
07/01/26, 4:34 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 948
Let me list the changes that were introduced and built in the 1980 decade. It is a watershed period. Most of us think that the nineties is the watershed period in India. That is due to our combined single myopic -one dimensional- view of only the government control. 
Let me apply my multidimensional thinking here. I consider economy of a country as a mother discipline depending on many factors and engineering, science, mathematics are dependent disciplines. So let me ask - What are needed for a better economy?
The factors needed for better growth are:
Freedom to do what you want - to build and operate industries. Reduce government control
Resources- steel, cement, metallurgy,automobiles,electric devices,etc
Finances - capital and credit availability, purchasing power. Allow better financing by banks . Liberalize loans. 
Mindset change- communist to free market.
Leadership and entrepreneurship- awareness,innovation,collaboration, flexibility,empowerment,diversity etc.
Talent pool- education,skills,training
Infrastructure- electricity, gas, water,data centers,banks,etc
Logistics- roads,telecommunications
Government policies, plans, programs, incentives,promotions,interventions, regulations, subsidies etc
As I explained before , the free market concept started in the eighties itself. Let me list the changes brought in the 1980 decade
1. The biggest change was the mindset on the public sector focus. Policy planners realised that public sector alone can't help the economy of a country and so moved away from that mindset . They encouraged private sector in many areas.
2. Computerization picks up and moves across. A bigger number of industries showed interest in computers , bought small machines and played with them.
3.Engineering education grew by leaps. Many engineering colleges came up in the country. Karnataka took a lead in the sixties itself and was ready to bring in more number of students into engineering. Engineering education had moved into mathematical mode in the seventies. So the eighties saw a big growth not only in the number of engineering colleges in the country,but also in the number of new disciplines being added. The government facilitated this by setting up AICTE to direct our education. Also the computer availability helped. We were ready for IT revolution with enough talent.
4.Many software and system development companies came up in Bangalore. They helped many industries in the city and state. Similarly many training institutes came up in the city.I feel these small companies who did a great job were the early startups in Bangalore.Some big ones like NIIT also came up at that time.Many of these companies started teaching programming to school students as special lectures. So I saw the popular growth of computer activities.
5. I saw many leaders coming up during this decade. That was a critical factor for the growth of the economy.By late eighties, hundreds of companies came up bringing out leadership skills in many. Several from large IT companies also joined or started companies. Most of the leaders were self taught. I found it remarkable that during the eighties, hundreds of people learnt on their own various aspects of computers and applications. That gave me positive hope about the country. Similarly hundreds of engineering colleges started computer science programs by 1990 and most teachers were not trained on computers. They learnt on their own the computer courses which they had to teach. 
6. Computer manufacturing also picked up with CMC, HCL,Wipro, and many others getting into the act. 1985 to 2000 was the period when desktops were built in India and we were close to the latest technology available in the world. 
So I will say that our liberalization started in 1985 and not in 1991.
Let me discuss my developments in the 1980 decade.
Greetings. Dks
08/01/26, 4:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 949
I felt the mid 1980 to be a transition period for Indian economy. We moved towards industrial and services economy. With green revolution, agriculture stabilised in India. But it remained in the same state for a long time. There was no grains scarcity after the 1965 period.
I looked at the state of economy in the eighties with the following objectives in mind:
1.To set the stage for the readers to understand the state in which I was living.
2.To show that economy is a multidimensional activity but seen in a linear manner.
3. To show the linkages between education, technology, jobs, consumption,and leadership.
Let me next look at my state. By 1980,I had a lot of experience ,lot of learnings and lot of activities. My mature phase begins now. My attention moves to the societal factors.My India views became very broad . I had many kinds of friendships and encounters with bureaucrats and politicians. My multidimensionality increased. I became a societal problem solver ,not just an engineer. I applied my knowledge to solve hundreds of real problems faced by the country and state.
I was a loner - what do I mean by this? 
Not that I shun friends. I had a larger friend circle. I used to attend one meeting or a coffee sesdion,selection or purchase or formal discussion every day in addition to my lectures and talks and meetings with my research students every day. I met several sales and engineering persons every week. 
So I meant loner in a different sense. Loner meant two things for me.
One, even when I attended meetings or discussed with friends, my mind will think of some topics and will move through that. That means I am present in a meeting and contribute to the discussions, but also think on something. I take my mind away from the surroundings and immerse in some aspects. 
But the second is more important. All of us are loners for a good period of time while trying to get sleep. I spent hours everyday sitting or lying in the bed and contemplate about many things. This improved my cognition, my awareness and my knowledge level. No precision, no worry, no need for correctness, no conditions  will enter such contemplation. I don't get sleep easily. I will be awake for hours while lying in the bed before sleep occurs. This is my internal learning and understanding. This habit is very useful for my mental development.
While certain aspects of my work like academic performance, friendships and networking, moving into new areas and accepting challenges continued throughout my life, I added many new dimensions to my activities during the eighties. I put them in three baskets - societal concerns and social relevance for my work, environmental concerns, and overall energy situation. So I developed a new realistic research focus - realism through people surveys and data collection. The first major data collection was the rural energy survey- that was done in the previous decade and became a forerunner for my work. My second activity was looking at the water situation in Bangalore. We collected a lot of data.Pleasantness was not a virtue unfortunately with me.
I became mature. Fear of failure was reduced considerably. Confidence went up to a very high level. 
I became my master. I didn't look for approvals and appreciations from others. I started asking myself about my meaningful and useful role in society and the actions to be done by me to achieve that. Sirsi conference gave me the impetus. My work was defined and governed by my thinking and introspection. This period was a focused social period in my life. True I didn't discard my academics. The academic work continued with its vigor ,but I took on a lot of social work. My definition of social work is to educate government, institutions,organizations on the future course of action in addition to supporting public discourses on important factors affecting local population. So I donned several cloaks- one as a family man,another as advisor, an third as activist,a fourth as motivator/ educator etc.

09/01/26, 3:58 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   950
Let me look at my society oriented journey. Actually it started quite early in life, during my village life and school days. During those forties and fifties, India was just coming out of a darkness in economic aspects. There was a scarcity in every thing. Education facilities existed in a few metro cities and they were a few. Higher education was difficult and not affordable.
So we needed to live with society, the societal bonds were strong. To make a big story short, we lived most of our day life in public, all actions by us were in public view and spread across the community. There were very few secrets. We used to help others ,participate even in their decisions and definitely participated in most religious activities. We learnt scriptures from the elders in the village. 
My grandfather and father had good scholarship in Sanskrit and Tamil and delivered a series of lectures on Ramayana in many cities and villages and they played a psychology role to families in grief by quoting appropriate scriptures. Theirs was a public service. So I got that habit from them. The concept of service got strengthened and organized by my uncle as I have told several times. 
That is my preparation. When I joined IISc as a student and later as a faculty, I focused my public service concept to the institute. My focus was technical and managerial with empathy for people. It is a free service. I never expected any payments. My work as warden of the hostels, as chair of power committee to improve the power supply to the institute for over 35 years, as chair of evaluation committee for staff promotions for 14 years and many others prodded me for service to the institute. I did a minimal extension in the mid sixties and early seventies by providing free programming support to many outsiders. I understood and appreciated the help I got from NAL,HAL and TIFR computer staff propelled me further towards service to people.
I started public service with ASTRA by understanding the life and problems of villagers.
Now it enters into new areas like energy, environment and power plants. It moved with a big scale in computerization activities of the state government and many organizations. That continued till now. I moved from being a pure theorist to a practical applications solver in the field - in government departments,government units, universities, colleges, schools, hospitals,steel plant,organizations, banks,companies etc. My activities spanned many dimensions- selection of computers, software, networking, ERP, architecting, programmer selections, software installations, power supply systems,lectures, courses, curriculum formation, conference, etc. 
All my activities were conducted in several forms and with many institutions, NGOs , government departments, schools , NSS units, and professional institutions and not restricted to computer field alone .I might have participated in more than 600 activities during the three decades following 1980. That doesn't include my research conferences ,academic courses and consultancies and advices.
I will discuss these in the next series of posts.
From the personal side, my health had got some changes. I was diagnosed as a diabetic in 1980 and I had a heart problem in 1987. But I controlled them well till date. So no concern. 
This decade of eighties was a period of growth and education of my children. We put both into KV IISc and both went through schooling till 10th class in KV.
Quite a few things happened during this period. We will discus them in sequence.
Greetings. Dks
10/01/26, 4:37 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 951
Let me start with 1980 occurrences. My daughter was put in KVIISc after an entrance test. We were clear about the selection of school at that time. Several factors weighed in our selection. 
First was the curriculum. I saw the KV curriculum much better than state curriculum. The students had regular home work , they had broader and deeper curriculum, books were good and the science subjects at high school level was better than the state curriculum. The curriculum was broader with Hindi and social sciences. The students at high school level did a project work.I was happy that allows students to work independently, read books, search materials ,think and learn presentation- one stone hitting several mangoes. This also builds and boosts the confidence of a student. When I was in IIT, I observed the KV children. I found they were socially strong, they had good communication and discussion skills,and participated in many activities. Generally KV built better persons. Their egalitarianism was liked by me. Private schools built a non existent superiority complex in their students and that was absent in KV students. The children can study up to 12th class and no school changes are required.
Second, the question addressed was - should I admit her to a girls school? Cluny is next door, easy for her to go by walk. The disadvantage will be that my son should go to another school. That is not desirable. I felt that both should go to the same school.
Third was the reachability. IISc KV was reachable by bus.
Fourth, the proximity to IISc was a big advantage. They could talk to faculty in many departments. We organized special lectures for them.
So we decided on KV.
The next decision was admission of my son to LKG. We were lucky that MES college started a new school in 10th main, close to our residence in 11th main.We admitted our son there. Again, I didn't want to admit him in garage run play schools. He was happy there, found a good friend,Nikhil. We became close to Nikhil's parents- Kasturi and Uma. They were living in 11th main. This friendship is a long one,continued for several decades till now. Kasturi is no more. Unfortunately,Nikhil also died in an accident while doing his engineering studies.
Let me describe the detection of my diabetes. I had been close to our health centre from the beginning. I interacted with both TB SubbaRao and BS SubbaRao during my hostel wardenship. I saw the progress of the health centre under BS Subba Rao. I considered BSS to be a thinking and careful physician. He listened to the patients,and found treatment.He helped me at many stages in my life even after retirement from IISc.  I didn't have any major health problems till 1980 except the usual cough and cold appearing periodically.I was also a good friend of Mr Nagaraja Rao. I admired his passion for education- more courses, his interest in movie making and his friendly and helpful approach towards all.
Around 1980, Dr GR Nagabhushan joined the health centre. Dr BSS introduced general health checkups. So I opted for it and after the test, Nagaraja rao told me that I should meet Dr Nagabhushan. That was the time when patients were attached to a doctor. Till then, one can go to any doctor.I met Dr GRN and he told me about my diabetes. I was started on tablets and within a week ,my blood sugar came under control. I started drinking coffee without sugar. I avoided sweets, reduced the consumption of rice. I always had the habit of walking and I continued that. I started taking two to three spoons of soaked fenugreek seeds every day in the morning and I continue it even now. Regularity in food, tablets, exercises helped me a lot.
But the most important aspect to be considered by a diabetic is a positive frame of mind. I had seen my grandfather and father suffering from diabetes. So I knew that I would inherit it. I was prepared for it. So when the news was announced to me, I wasn't shocked.I wasn't worried. But I saw many people, when told of having diabetes, started worrying as if they had lost everything in their life and kept a glum and mourning face with no confidence to face life. The worry increases sugar level. Emotions affect blood sugar levels. So emotion control and creating a calm mind and positive mind are essential for a diabetic. Meditation helps a lot. I took this announcement as a routine one mentally, decided that I won't change my work schedules, travels and activities and I will regulate my living status with limited food, exercises and medication. I made it clear to myself that my quality of life will remain the same,diabetes or not.
I experimented with many natural remedies like eating cluster beans and bitter gourd. I also tried drinking the water from soaked okra and found it very effective. My breakfast routine changed then. I moved to oats and continue with oats even now. All these helped me to survive this long. Dr GRN assured me that I will live up to 70 years. I am surprised that I am still living now- a bonus addition to my life and am happy. It is due to my discipline and positive and active mind.
Greetings. Dks
11/01/26, 5:23 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 952
One of my major transitions or I can even say transformations that happened to me during the 1980 decade was my movement into environmental aspects of technology and energy. This involved a larger interaction with Madhav Gadgil. The first effect was the Bedthi project which changed my one dimensional thinking on hydro power and made me look at alternatives by looking at all forms of energy, not just electrical and cost optimization as well as environment. This holistic approach to problem solving led to me to address the challenges I faced in future through multidimensional approaches.
When I was planning to write about my interactions with Madhav, I heard the shocking news yesterday that he is no more. That was tragic. I know a lot of people working for the enhancement and enrichment of our nature, but the most effective person has been Madhav. He consistently worked for more than four decades to address several problems faced by the forests of the country. It was due to him that we got the ministry of environment set up by the government of India. He got involved with the western ghats program for several decades. He was a ground level working scientist, not a talker at higher levels. He was sincere, hardworking with a lot of in depth ground truth and a great reader and writer. 
I had four major activities in 1980 involving four persons. I was joint convener of ASTRA and so had a lot of interactions with AKN. With GK as chair of the school of automation, my responsibilities increased in the school with plans for ME computer science program and starting a new BE computer science program. I was also interacting with the director on the computer centre operations.In addition, my other activities like power committee , evaluation committee etc continued. 
A new development took place. IISc along with many professional societies in Bangalore formed a group called proficience. Its main task was for IISc to offer several evening courses at the same level of rigor as that of our regular courses for industry engineers. It was a full semester program, taught by IISc faculty and we followed IISc evaluation procedure.We started with about 10 plus courses in june 1980. I ran a course on computer organization and design. It was a new experience. It was a successful course. I continued teaching several courses for the next ten years.
I saw a lot of differences between Madhav and AKN. Both are deep thinkers, there ends the similarity. AKN talks well, but wasn't a ground level worker. He shifted away from rural focus by 1981. That was a tragedy. Madhav focused and concentrated on his core competency till the end. He was committed to his cause. He achieved a lot. He succeeded with stopping both silent valley project in Kerala and Bedthi project in karnataka. He made policy makers to look at endangered areas of western ghats and he worked tirelessly on that goal. 
Our discussions were focused on the Bedthi project. He also was preparing a proposal to the ministry of environment, government of India to set up a centre for ecological sciences at IISc. I was a cosignitory for that proposal. 
Meanwhile ,the state under Mr Gundu Rao as chief minister set up two departments - one for science and technology and another for ecology and environment. The chief secretary was looking for a scientist. Initially AKN raised the request , I didn't show any interest. Madhav raised that point with me in oct 1980. He said they want someone at additional secretary rank. He asked me. While I was initially reluctant, I said ok at that time. 
I met Mr Jayakumar Anagol, who was the secretary to the chief minister and a scholar in Philosophy. I discussed only my commitments with him. We agreed that
"I will continue teaching and research at IISc.I will continue visiting TCS and lecturing at PSTI. "
He agreed with my conditions and said that a government order would be issued immediately. I met Prof Ramaseshan and told him about the develooments. He welcomed the proposal and agreed for a year of leave for me. So I accepted the offer and informed GK.
Let us discuss that journey next.

12/01/26, 3:53 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 953
Let me describe my new journey with the government job.
The first question that occurred to me and others- why did I accept the job? Many, including Prof. Ramaseshan thought that I would change my direction and job towards government service.
I asked myself why am I going for this job?
One thing I was clear- my love for academics is very high. I love the freedom to learn, to widen my vision and to work very much.I pretty much did what I wanted to do and how I wanted to do with love, interest, enthusiasm and excitement. So I was clear that my main job is IISc, nowhere else. I won't leave it. This was true when I went to Manchester first and IIT Bombay later. That resolve stays with me.
But as seen in my discussions, I was concerned with the discovery of problems. I always looked at problems. The questions I pose to myself are
What are the steps in solving a problem?
We go through several stages
1.problem discovery- gaps,improvements, modernization, usability, new requirements etc.
2.problem description. Simplification. Formalism. Constraints
3.problem definition - define models , objectives,constraints,goals etc.
4. Possible solutions- solution discovery. It includes discoveries of technologies, business models, commerce, culture, transformation methods, metrics etc.
5.detailed solution methods and approaches
I said look outside for problems not just your discipline. My second view was to look at users and applications. My third view was to look into the future and identify changes. I always felt humanism is a dominant factor in problem discovery.
So I wanted to understand the problems of people. My talk with Anagol emphasized that aspect. He said that their emphasis was to Identify the problems faced by many departments and use science to find solutions.I liked that clarity. That was new for me and I got excited by another approach to problem discovery. It opens a whole state for me.Our education neglects problem discovery from the user angle and looks at it from the STEM angle. It is not realistic. While the focus of education is to know the reality, the methodologies of education lead to artificiality.
But this job gives me a different approach. It became my mission. I was happy with the clarity I got about my role.
I made another change to Anagol's approach. It was easy to look for problems from the government departments. But I decided to enlarge my scope- to look for problems from organizations, groups of people and NGOs. I didn't reduce their values. So I visited many parts of the state. I felt that it is important to understand the genesis of a problem along with its characteristics. That meant visits and more importantly discussions. I followed this throughout my life.
My second task was to propagate technology and concepts adoptions and absorption.
I joined duty by end October 1980. Mr Jayakumar Anagol took me to the chief secretary. I was assigned an office in Vidhanasoudha. I had two sections headed by two section officers and one under secretary. Mr R Krishnamurthy was the undersecretary, a very duty conscious, a straight forward and highly pious person. He was also humble. I admired him. 
We all hear of corruption in the government. I was taken aback by the honesty I found in the Vidhanasoudha. Most IAS officers were honest, though I heard about a few dishonest ones and they were kept out of the mainline. Most staff were honest. Several legislators were honest.Most were simple and honest people. They did their job sincerely. But ignorance and disinterest pervaded amongst the staff. I didn't see motivation in most staff members in the secretariat.
My job gave me a lot of opportunities to meet people involved in governmental activities, from departmental heads to officers as well as farmers, foresters, teachers, and common people.

13/01/26, 4:33 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 954
It was a tough time. I had my lectures in the morning at IISc and left for Vidhanasoudha at 10am. I would return by 430pm back to IISc and attended my activities. 
My routine office work was not heavy. But I had to create my work. I followed the routine-Visit and talk to people,identify problems, understand, find solutions and implement it- an end to end activity.
I had two organizations under the two departments- KSCST and pollution control board.
I had lot more work in the environment area than in S and T. We had a lot of concerns with environmental issues. 
The pollution control board was not effective. They found excuses for not acting on complaints. Several cases were pending in the department and courts.
One case related to Harihar polyfibres polluting Tunga Bhadra river. They were manufacturing  rayon fibres from trees. They extracted cellulose from wood pulp and left most of the wood as waate into the river. The potability of water and health of people using the water caused great concern to the people living down stream. The case was going on for several years with no solution found. Birla's were firmly in command.
The second one was the pollution of Kabini river at Nanjangud. Industries discharged their waste water into the river and the drinking water inlet pipe was also there. So the city was getting polluted water. No industry was willing to put up a treatment plant. If they set up a water treatment plant, they didn't operate it. Both the administrative and regulative authorities were standing by the side and not taking actions. We had a meeting with many local people of Nanjangud and the industrialists, but no acceptable solution was found. So I instructed the board chairman to take strict actions as per law.
The third was in Mysore. A factory was letting of waste water into the Cauvery river. It was a paper factory. Its waste like Harihar polyfibres contained a lot of lignin.It is pure energy. The factory could convert it into bio gas. They didn't do it,but released this black liquor into a canal of Cauvery. That affected the local population. They couldn't drink this untreated water or use it for cooking.
There were several such problems.
Let me look at the fourth case.Similarly, another case was pending for long. A plywood factory located close to the world famous chennakeshava temple was burning plywood waste. The exhaust gas affected the flag post in the temple. This was going on for many years with no solution. The board said that the air pollution act can not be used to take action. I found a section in the archeology and museum act with provisions to act against such offenses. So I took the matter to the chief secretary. He approved the closure of this factory. I issued directions to SP Hassan to close the factory. It was done. Some newspapers wrote against the action.
Let me look at a problem concerning forests.It was the setting up of jungle lodges in Kabini reservoir area. It was to be set up in the forest area by a private company.There was an objection to this proposal. Many felt it was on the path of the migration of elephants. I met Ullas Karanth for the first time. He got a letter signed by a few eminent people like R K Narayan and Shivarama Karanth addressed to the PM, Mrs Indira Gandhi. Her office sent it to the CM for proper consideration of the case. The government appointed a committee and it didn't support the proposal. So that project was dropped. Ullas became a good friend. I supported his project financially. That made him change his path to ecology and tigers.
I addressed several problems like solar cookers, water heaters, wind mills, bio gas plants, wood stoves, afforestation, water pollution, air pollution, salinity, sedimentation in reservoirs,etc.

14/01/26, 3:55 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 955
Mr Srinivasan, ex PNB 
"Good morning sir.  Pollution control is a serious issue.  Please Increase the discussion,  by devoting a  separate chapter."
My response
I feel unhappy that even today, we are unaware of pollution. We suffer with low air quality in Delhi every year, but didn't find remedial measures. The current approach to development ignores the environment.
Let me discuss the scene in 1980. As I said before, our PM Mrs Gandhi realized the fact that the problems faced by the country were related to the abuse of nature. She felt that development could not continue on the usual belief - business as usual. So she took some measures- set up two departments, one for environment and another for renewable energy.
Mr Srinivasan's query allows me to go back to my learnings on environment and my role not only in the government but afterwards also.
Let me start with an understanding of what is environment?
Environment means natural resources like soils, water,air, biological resources and natural forces given by sun, wind, storms, rains,ocean waves and turbulence etc .
Chamakam lists all natural components like rivers, streams,canals,lakes, rivers, wells,mountains,multiple geologies, etc. Rig Veda discusses the forces and includes their effects. It shows gratitude to all. It considers fire as an intermediary.
When we talk of the environment, we don't address the dynamics of resources - accumulation, regeneration, consumption and depletion. Our living and our consumption affects our environment. Resources are of two types- renewable and depleting. The resources we obtain from the sun are renewable. Certain resources are built up and stored on our plant. Examples are- minerals like coal,ores, soil, natural lakes,petroleum,biomass, forest, corals,ground water,etc. When we use them, their total stored quantity reduces with time. So the cardinal principles are
1.use mostly renewable resources.
2.increase efficiency of use and reduce dependence on stored resources.
3. Our consumption produces waste. Minimize the wastes. Reuse the waste. Retrieve usable items from the waste. Nothing is a waste in the world.
4. Increase the storage units. Afforestation is one example. Increasing biodiversity is another example.
5. Substitute one depletable element with a producible element where possible. Aluminium in place of copper is an example.
6. Protect the vulnerable entities. Project tiger is a good example.
Adherence to these principles will lead to sustainable living.
We need to understand how our development activities were affecting our environment in the year 1980 and even now.
1.Deforestation is a major concern in the world and India also. 
We are losing forest areas at a high rate due to the clearing of forests for agriculture, industries,human settlements, mining, hydro power plants, irrigation reservoirs etc and also due to cutting of trees for firewood and timber. Many industries like paper, match,rayon,etc use wood from trees as raw materials. There are many examples of these types of industries depleting the neighboring forest areas. Wimco depleted the Dandeli forests.
Forests were destroyed for mining several mineral ores , coal etc. We depleted forests for extracting mica ore, copper, iron, manganese,gold, alumina etc. Coal fields are located across the east and central India.
We also destroy forests to construct reservoirs for water and power supply.
So forest destruction is going on unabated in our country.
We use firewood for cooking and water heating in most houses, hostels, canteens and hotels. India has a large number of small industries and those using boilers use firewood to fuel their boilers to get steam. As an example, the Vidhana soudha canteen used half a ton of firewood as fuel for cooking. 
The problem of firewood is severe. Our source is mostly trees in villages and forests. While many villagers collect firewood from their neighborhood, the supply was depleting and so they depended on firewood from forests. Cities depended on firewood from forests.Bangalore,in the sixties, used to get its firewood requirements from the neighboring forests reaching till Kanakapura ,about 60 kms from here. But in 1980, the city was getting its firewood from the Uttara Kannada district, a distance of about 500 kms by eight train loads and about 400 truck loads every day. In many parts of the country, people on a daily basis go into forests and cut or collect wood. They transport it on their head or bullock cart or by bus or train. Selling firewood in cities is a livelihood activity for poor people, like selling vegetables. All these also led to a higher diesel use to transport firewood. So the problem of wood for cooking and water heating was becoming serious social and ecological issues.
So we can't go on cutting more trees and reducing the forest cover in the country. We need to find alternate solutions. That was one of my main tasks in the state government.
You may ask - why are we worried about forests? We consider forests as nuisense and always destroyed forests. But our vedas  knew the importance of forests, trees and water and hence venerate forests. 
Forests are not just places with trees and insects and may be wild animals. A forest is a veritable treasure trove,a store house of trees, plants, fungi, hummus and enriched soil and rain water. It is the living support for not only plants but also for insects, animals and birds. It is a regenerative system. It provides biodiversity. It provides us with our needs. Isa upanishad says it belongs to god and use it sparingly. Gandhi says
Nature can meet our needs but not greed.
Since the forest department was traditional and the problems of ecology needed a fresh thinking, the new department was set up. Similarly Karnataka government also set up a department of ecology and environment. We had a running feud with the existing forest department. Fortunately, I had three advantages.
1. The chief secretary was the environment secretary.
2. I established a great rapport with Mr Hoskote, the secretary of forests and the special secretary. Mr Hoskote was related to Prof R Narasimha,my senior colleague at IISc and a great friend. He understood the problems of the environment better than forest officers.
3. I had met the principal CCF of forests ,Mr Shyamsundar during the Sirsi conference. He understood our views. More importantly, he appreciated my support to address the major issues of forests caused by the power, agriculture and irrigation departments. So while some considered this new department as their enemy, he considered us an ally.
But the department had a second urgent problem that is pollution.
More on pollution in the next post.
Greetings. Dks

15/01/26, 4:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 956

While ecology and forests were not on the radar of common man,pollution was the most perceived problem by the common people. By 1980, its effect was felt by people.
Pollution means deterioration of the usable quality of resources like water, food, air,ambiance,soil,etc. Pollution is caused by our actions.
We need resources to survive and live. But our usage affects the resources both in quantity and quality. 
A brief sketch of the situation in India in 1980 is given below.
The most serious problem was water pollution. Most rivers in India were polluted. Over a period of time, even pond water in villages was getting polluted. A study conducted in Kolar showed that even ground water was getting contaminated and fluoride content was either low or high. So the first effect was a struggle for potable water, some felt it was a struggle for water for living. Our ASTRA survey of six villages in Tumkur district showed the struggle of villagers to get water from ponds over distances of a km or more. Hand pumps weren't there in most villages. Only after Ramakrishna Hegde became chief minister, he took the installation of hand pumps with a mission mode. This shows that we shouldn't look at pollution problem as an end result but as a supply and demand- process interaction and consider treatment and reuse as an integral part of a municipality or an industry or an apartment block.
Most rivers were polluted with waste water discharges from industries and sewerage water from municipalities. Remember that people are dependent on river water for survival. So when they drank the contaminated water, they got sick.It was estimated that more than 20000 deaths took place every year due to Cholera alone. For example, there is a river called Lakshmana Tirta and it flows through Hunsur city near Mysore. During monsoon time, the river gets a copious supply of fresh water. But during summer, the fresh water availability becomes very low. The flow in the river was mostly sewerage water. So dilution theory won't work in such conditions. So every year, people in Hunsur are affected by cholera due to the use of contaminated water. 
That is one example.
We read in school test books that cities get treated water supply. This is a myth. The treatment was simple filtration,not enough to remove fecal, chemical and heavy metal impurities. The state of tap water was bad in the 1980s. With new filtration technologies like RO, situation is better now. But the recent incident in Indore- mixing sewerage water with drinking water- doesn't look like an isolated one. Nanjangud incident which I narrated before shows poor planning. Most cities didn't treat their waste water. 
Remember waste water is a good source if treated properly. It is collected through sewerage pipes and so can be treated in a central place. Bangalore started anaerobic treatment of sewerage water in the sixties itself. Prof SC Pillai of IISc was the primary consultant for that project. That plant could produce bio gas as another output in addition to treated water. It was a pioneering work.But this treatment plant wasn't maintained properly. It was ignored.Myself and Krishnaprasad visited this place in 1975 while working on the KSCST project on community bio gas plants. This opened our eyes for a holistic look on the problem. The waste water released by industries or urban use contains energy. 
This is what we saw in Mysore where black liquor released into a canal by a paper unit polluting the water stream was a rich source of bio energy. Normally the contents are measured by the oxygen levels in contaminated water- called BOD and COD.High BOD means a higher concentration of bio waste, that is bio energy.
Same is true with the Harihar polyfibres story - letting untreated water with a lot of cellulose and lignin into the Tunga bhadra river. Again ,it means waste of usable bio energy.
While nature looks at water as a closed cycle- ocean to clouds to rain to rivers to ocean. But our planners don't see a water cycle - supply to use to contaminated water- I am avoiding calling them as sewerage or waste water- to treatment to reuse- a closed cycle. They made it an open ended system. So everybody thinks " waste treatment is not my problem." Then whose problem is pollution?
Greetings. Dks

🌴🌄🌴🌾🌾🛕🪁🪁
16/01/26, 3:56 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  957
We got a glimpse of water pollution seen by me in 1980. Despite both the centre and state governments coming up with laws for prevention of pollution, every entity decided to ignore it. So the situation worsened. This despite the advent of better and cost effective technologies for water treatment. Fortunately most apartment complexes have gone for in house treatment plants. They found the reuse of treated water less costly compared to the municipal supply. RO Water filters are the necessary part of most houses. While during the period 1965 to 80 I used to drink water from the tap directly. I loved the cauvery water- cool and refreshing. But no more. We started boiling water before drinking and later started using RO filters.
The next pollution was air pollution. We passed another act on air pollution. One important instance of air pollution that I handled in 1980 was the pollution from a plywood factory affecting the world famous Chennakeshava temple at Belur.
There was also soil pollution. I encountered a case in Bellary district. The agricultural lands adjacent to the outlet canal from the TB dam were becoming saline and not suitable for cultivation. I inspected the place along with Mr Gokulram,IAS,the DC of Bellary district, found that the cause was water seepage from the canal. So I suggested that the canal should be lined properly. The canal was a high rise one. That was a mistake. Civil engineers looked for a gradient for water to flow and ignored the flooding of fields and making them uncultivable. Thousands of acres of good fertile land was lost.
So I now come back to my concept of multidimensional problem solving for such social problems.
The  concern here is with the important concept of a class of human problems which can be called "notechnical solution problems,"Only recently we saw a change of approach with the swatch Bharat program- an awareness campaign. Now we see some improvements in our rivers. Many municipalities are serious about treatment.
We should realize the fact we will greatly increase human misery if we do not, during the immediate future, assume that the world available to the terrestrial human population is finite. We are attacking the commons. Village commons are orphans, even the government abuses them by giving it to industries. The proposed peripheral ring road in Bangalore will displace thousands of villagers and remove common lands. I recall the fact that the Maharaja of Mysore earmarked a lot of land in many areas as Amritmahal kaval lands to grow and develop animal species. But the present governments gave away those lands to industries and institutions.
The tragedy of the commons reappears in problems of pollution. Here it is not a question of taking something out of the commons, but of putting something in -- sewage, or chemical, radioactive, and heat wastes into water; noxious and dangerous fumes into the air.
The rational man finds that his share of the cost of the wastes he discharges into the commons is less than the cost of purifying his wastes before releasing them. Since this is true for everyone, we are locked into a system of "fouling our own nest," so long as we behave only as independent, rational, free enterprises forgeting social obligations.
During this period, I met hundreds of people - farmers, industrialists, small entrepreneurs, government officers, engineers, lawyers ,environmentalists, IAS officers,and politicians. It was a great revelation for me. It changed my view of India,its people and their mindset.
Some learnings are
Most people are sincere, work hard but afraid of their bosses.
Ambiance and culture in most places don't allow freedom and curb curiosity. 
There is a lot of ignorance amongst these people on- why am I doing something in my job? what is expected of me? There was very little learning for most. It was monotonous and purposeless living. That is why they become religious- a way to let out their emotions and get peace. That was a tragedy. That continues now also. Since the purpose of doing a job wasn't understood, most don't work hard.
I see every government office run by one good human being and that saves the government.
But one aspect that I saw not only in the government but also in most educational institutions, and most private industries and organizations is that mediocrity prevails everywhere. The ambiance is dictated by mediocrity. That curbs innovation and excellence. But more importantly, everyone from the top to the last is satisfied with mediocrity, accepts it as normal and lives with it. This irritates me.
That is why many Indians do much better when they work abroad. This toxic ambiance needs the attention of all of us. People don't think big and aren't allowed to think big.
Greetings. Dks
17/01/26, 4:00 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 958
So far, my interactions were mostly with faculty, students,power engineers and computer persons. But during my job with the government in 1980-81, I met a lot of people from different walks of life and at multiple levels from the highest to the lowest. I met ministers, MLAs, politicians, public figures, writers like Shivarama Karanth,foresters, agriculture officers, activists, vice chancellors, faculty, secretaries,IAS officers, secretariat and department officers, Deputy commissioners of districts, and had many rounds of discussions. I met several hundred people running the government.I visited many places in the state going across the state from Mangalore to Hubli,Dharwad to Belgaum, Bangalore to Bellary to Bijapur, Challakere to Gulbarga, Bidar, Sirsi to Yellapur, Davangere to Hassan to Mysore to Bandipur to Mercara. 
I realised that there is a strong linkage between policies, people implementing them, organizational methods, awareness methods and people at the receiving end. This connections concept was not understood by most people working at lower levels. Each person restricts his views to the task assigned to him or her and didn't bother about the impacts and hurdles. 
If I stayed at IISc, would I have these opportunities of looking at real world problems and the perceptions of concerned people. What I learnt was - every problem has many parts, connects many disciplines and all parts or entities are strongly interconnected/ intertwined, but the most important aspect to consider is the human angle and not the technical angle. Success depends on understanding the perceptions of people and adapting a solution to suit their views and convincing them and not the other way - push a technical solution to a problem and invariably you will create more problems - a good example was the Tungabhadra dam high rise canal designed for flow but destroyed a large area of cultivation.
If human behaviors and perceptions are more important for the success of a solution, then the important question is " today we have barriers and not borders between disciplines. How will we make the barriers into a thin and movable border with very strong connectivity to other components and disciplines? How do we reach people to make them understand the complexity of connections? "
The 21st century expects a better answer to these questions. Remember AI connects well through the millions of websites and connects data from many disciplines to come up with much better answers. It discards existing one dimensional models and can move into multidimensional approaches. It doesn't depend on mathematical models alone. But we live on a small isolated island. No wonder AI will overtake us if we don't start creating a new discipline of connections and create a structured knowledge focusing on connections and not on parts. AI will do the parts well, so let us move to connections. I consider this as a big learning for survival. The Current education system won't lead us to this path. Tinkering with our education won't help. We need mindset change and a whole new education mechanism.
In your opinion, Are these important problems or normal unsolvable ones?
Any comments on how we address the twin problems- acceptance of mediocrity and parts oriented totally isolated discipline focused learning?
Greetings. Dks

Mr Srinivasan,PNB
Good morning sir.  After going through your post dealing with air pollution,  I  thought next should be noise pollution. Honking the horn, in a congested road is a regular occurrence. Yesterday  night,  someone in the complex WhatsApp group , has mentioned about the noise made by the fan in some other house with an appeal to get it repaired You may like to touch upon the economic impact of noise pollution at a macro level. Namaste.
My response
I agree with you about noise pollution.In 1975, when my daughter was a few months old, we moved into a house in the upper class residential area, Palace orchards. The neighbors used to go to the clubs and return by 12 mid night. They used to put their car horn at full blast so that their servant sleeping inside would wake up and open the door. Many people would do this everyday waking up many and disturbing the peace of the neighborhood. We suffered it for a few months and moved back to Malleswaram afterwards.
But when I discussed this with Mr Anagol, secretary to the chief minister, he responded by saying that Indians love noise. So there is no noise pollution according to him. 
Our cities weren't planned with silence zones. We find noisy workshops in residential areas. 
We have a bigger problem - space pollution in cities. Bangalore's foot paths have vanished. Many areas had garbage thrown all over. You can't walk in Bangalore nowadays. 
There are many such pollution problems. Sadachar is the only mantra to live with minimal wants to reduce physical pollution. 
But nowadays, I am worried about mental pollution. Most people are brainwashed. For example, in Tamilnadu, even today most people hate brahmins,propagated by the Dravidian groups.Is that an acceptable 21st century mindset?
Similarly ,our education conditions children with dogmas. This pollution is more dangerous and we should allow freedom of mind to prevail. But how?
Greetings
