19/01/26, 4:16 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 960
Mr Srinivasan asked about the economical implications of pollution. He is thinking of noise only ,but I think we should address the impacts on all forms of pollution. 
Before I do that, let me give more history on noise from the book stillness is the key by Ryan Holiday:
"It was the late first century AD and Lucius Annaeus Seneca, Rome’s most influential power broker, its greatest living playwright, and its wisest philosopher, was struggling to work. The problem was the ear-shattering, soul-rattling noise that poured in from the street below. Rome had always been a loud city—think New York City construction loud—but the block where Seneca was staying was a deafening cacophony of disturbances. Athletes worked out in the gymnasium underneath his suite of rooms, dropping heavy weights. A masseuse pummeled the backs of old fat men. Swimmers splashed in the water. At the entrance of the building, a pickpocket was being arrested and making a scene. Passing carriages rumbled over the stone streets, while carpenters hammered away in their shops and vendors shouted their wares. Children laughed and played. Dogs barked." So noise pollution is an old problem.
Let me take one example well studied by SITRA - south Indian textiles research association, a thinking and active body.
It showed that noise in a spinning or weaving section having thousands of spindles was deafening and many workers became deaf. Most industries in India have a noise level higher than the standard prescribed decibel levels. 
The higher noise meant that the bearings are not greased, it increases friction and produces heat, that means more input energy required and that leads to increased wear and tear, maintenance and cost. The culprit was the loose cotton getting stuck inside the electric motors and all moving parts. That increases friction. The loose cotton in the atmosphere affects the workers also. They get bronchial diseases and go sick. That reduces productivity. That reduction in productivity and hence in profits have been estimated. 
But another aspect was studied - the relationship between pollution and maintenance. They found that more than 100 electric motors failed every three months in one normal polluted factory compared to less than 30 for three months for the same number of spindles in a well controlled factory. The second factory had air conditioned its entire spindles room. The atmosphere became clean and livable. Workers became healthy, no bronchial diseases, productivity went up, labor morale got boosted, motors worked well, input electricity consumption reduced- many benefits. Then why is it most textile industries follow this approach? Mediocrity. You don't see beyond your eye?
I told a paper factory that the black liquor they are discharging is a big energy source. But they were not interested. Their job is to manufacture papers, not energy. Same was true of a sugar factory burning tones of firewood.
Normally ,pollution control gives multiple benefits. Let me look at sewerage water treatment by means of a bio gas plant. You get treated water. You get energy through gas output. And you get an enriched manure- you get three times Nitrogen through anaerobic fermentation compared to open composting. You get a cleaner place.If you do a cost benefit analysis ,a bio gas plant is a boon for the country. 
When we put up our bio gas plant at IISc ,it was constructed next to the entrance.Many protested that the entrance will be smelly. But after sometime, they said the place was not smelly, but clean. I said that was our intention to set up the plant there- to show that the place is clean and had no flies or mosquitoes that you find in open composting.
So definitely there are great economic benefits in pollution control. In most instances, pollution control produces energy and usable water.
I think we need to see the many levels of impact of air pollution next.
Greetings. Dks
20/01/26, 4:33 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 961
Mr Debashish,TCS
"Air pollution is much more wide spread health hazard and dangerous compared to noise pollution. Air pollution permeates and grips large areas quickly whereas noise pollution is very much localized. However it doesn’t mean that we don’t need to take control of noise pollution, it’s very much required. Sir, as I mentioned earlier also your collective hands on experience can create a wisdom full book."
I was planning to write on air pollution next.
Air pollution has many types and sources, like dust pollution, pollen pollution, smoke pollution, accidental or hazardous pollution, radiation etc. While some are natural, most are human made.
We are familiar with several examples.
Dust comes from natural dust storms due to winds, construction dusts, cleaning dust etc. Those in the vicinity get affected by dust. North India gets dust storms from the desert winds.Dust particles enter our nose and go to the lungs and settle there. 
Pollen pollution comes from plants. Parthenium allergy was a well known problem in the seventies and eighties in Bangalore. It became a dominant species and started growing in most open spaces in Bangalore leading to allergy, sickness and productivity losses. KSCST took up this as one of the first problems to be addressed.
Bhopal gas tragedy , LPG blast etc are accidents and lead to loss of life and property. Forest fires are a major source of pollution.
The most dominant form of air pollution happens mainly due to the combustion- burning- of carbon fuel externally in a stove or boiler or internally in an IC engine running our generator sets or vehicles like trucks,buses, cars and motor cycles. We get carbon particles, carbon monoxide and carbon dioxide.These gases linger in the lower atmosphere for some time before moving up and diffusing with other gases in the atmosphere.
The first level impact is what Delhi experiences due to vehicular pollution. Delhi also complains about air pollution due to the burning of agricultural residues in the neighboring states of Haryana and Punjab.
We saw the effect of polluted air from a plywood factory on the world famous sculptures at Belur Chennakeshava temple. Mr Veerappa Moily, the finance and tourism minister at that time got terribly upset and annoyed at this pollution and called a meeting where the chief secretary was present along with me , the pollution control board chair and the superintending archeologist. Both officers were diffident and gave excuses. I told the minister that we can close down the ply wood factory using a rule in section 13cof the state museums act. The chief secretary said he would issue directions immediately. Accordingly I prepared a letter to the deputy commissioner directing him to close the factory. The letter prepared within 30 minutes after the meeting was signed by the chief secretary and conveyed to the DC Hassan. We closed the factory. The superintending archeologist was not happy. 
This was what London experienced as detailed by Sherlock Holmes - pie soup fog. That happened due to the burning of coal in buildings for heat.By banning coal fired boilers ,these cities became cleaner. 
India has a lot of indoor pollution particularly in north India and during winter months when coal is burnt to heat a room and cooking is done inside the same room.This affected children and elderly.
The first effect was felt by our lungs. Carbon particles stick inside. We are also affected by carbon monoxide poisoning. A large population gets affected by asthma. The quality of lifestyle becomes poor and productivity becomes low. Visibility on the roads will become low leading to vehicular accidents.
Greetings
Dks
21/01/26, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 962
Let me continue with air pollution. Industrial emissions include other chemicals also in addition to CO2. Coal burning, common in thermal power plants and many industries, leads to the emissions of sulfur. Sulfur combines water vapor and oxygen in the atmosphere to produce sulfuric acid and it produces acid rain. Some industries like cement produce dust also.So air pollution is a complex problem.
But the next level of the effect of carbon accumulation is more serious and that is what we talk about a lot now as climate crisis. Carbon di oxide is a stable compound found in our atmosphere. It permits sun light and heat into our atmosphere. This filtering and trapping of sun's energy is called the green house effect.That helps in maintaining life in our planet. If the content increases, then the amount of light and heat from the sun getting trapped in our atmosphere increases leading to a higher temperature.Higher carbon content will block sun light and heat. That will be ice age. Our higher consumption of energy from carbon fuels increases the carbon content in the atmosphere. At the moment, we are seeing the first phase where the carbon acts like a trap and more heat is retained in our atmosphere. This increases our temperature in our atmosphere. That affects a lot of happenings in our atmosphere. Climate gets affected. That is a problem affecting the livelihood of billions of people.
Monsoons become erratic. It became a global problem not a local problem like air pollution.When atmospheric temperature goes up, glaziers melt and sea level rises leading to submersion of a large land area. Mumbai and Chennai will be affected. So all are concerned. If the monsoon becomes erratic, our agriculture is at stake and our life is more at stake.
So the world is worried and many governments want to reduce the added carbon in our atmosphere. The world is keen on a carbon reducing economy.
Let us look at another pollution that affects our agriculture- soil pollution. I talked about the salinity problem in TB dam area, reducing cultivable area and livelihood. 
Another major problem is garbage disposal.We see a pollution multiplier effect in the thoughtless way municipalities dispose their garbage- landfill approach. This leads to leaching- leakage into the soil of harmful chemicals and bacteria. This further leads to ground water pollution. water becomes saline and non potable. The air in the area becomes polluted. People become sick frequently. Land is not usable for agriculture.
The soil is further polluted by fertilizers and pesticides residues coming from the fields.
So we destroy the treasures of nature and make life painful and unhealthy.
What are the solutions?
Greetings. Dks
22/01/26, 3:52 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 963
There are no easy solutions. There are many hurdles.
1.understanding the full picture of pollution. It is a multidimensional problem. Pollution depends on several parameters like lifestyle, consumption patterns, mindset- ignore it-, energy sector, population, industry approaches,non availability of easy solutions, land availability etc.
2. Willingness to start corrections. Belief in cleanliness is godliness is disappearing. Most don't want to address this ,don't consider this an urgent problem and don't want to change their lifestyle,their mode of spending and living. Today convenience is the key.
3. People think in one dimension and in binary. This shows why many parts of villages are not clean. 
4. The corrections actions are complex, need cooperation from several sections of people. We need not a solution, but a new culture drastically changing our beliefs,our activities, our efforts etc. Even temples need a change.
5. There is no clarity or holistic understanding of the problems of nature, their connections, their dynamics, time constants etc. We don't understand the irreversible effects of climate change.We are not able to predict properly the path taken by a cyclone in the bay of Bengal. Our mathematics is still not strong enough to model climate systems.
6. Political will is missing. 
7. While industries have their supporters and godfathers, nature is orphan. A few activists do part-time shouting. So nature is forgotten till it starts hurting.
8. Pollution control and enrichment of nature are the last items in our political, scientific, social and economic agendas.
All the above factors bring out the following aspects about the environment problem:
1.It is not a one dimensional technical problem. Technical solutions weren't accepted easily.
2.It is a culture change problem. Bring people back to clean and nature centric living. This is the key to solving environmental problems. Every sub problem meets with resistance from policy planners to executives to users. Our mind set is totally fixed to materialism. We discarded culture. We don't see culture in development, technology ,economics. We see culture is related to social life only- festivals,food,arts, practices etc.
That separation approach worked in a static society. But society as seen even in the 1980s was a changing society- consumption was increasing, demands were going up, resources were depleting,scarcity was not accepted, technology wanted more resources, money and wealth were important.
But in reality, culture can be defined as our common, not individual, belief system- commonly accepted beliefs on money, profits,development, the general economy, business,ethics etc. Let us call this as a professional system which is responsible for the problems to nature. We have two systems - social and professional. Culture change is tough not done by technology but by education and slow mindset changes.
The professional systems are focused on outcomes- profit, productivity, growth etc not on the internals or the processes. Processes are tuned for profit. We need a change here. Go for Gita's Nishkama karma - don't look at results. Do your duty.
So my problem is defined
Arrest attacks on nature.and 
Enrich nature.
The solution involves wisdom not just knowledge. You should know how to avoid or take care of the hurdles and go forward.
The Solution approach for enrichment of nature has two components. One to educate and convert people. Second is a series of actions. 
I operated on both fronts. Education aspect was addressed through introducing environment education in schools, popular seminars, world environment day seminars and exhibition etc. I spent the entire world environment day by participating in seminars in several organizations in Bangalore. In addition, we conducted world environment day as an exhibition and seminar in six cities - Mysore,Mercara,Mangalore,Sirsi,Hubli, and Shimoga. I visited three cities - Sirsi, Hubli and Shimoga with my family and participated in seminars. We also initiated steps to include environment subject in the school curriculum. Many NGOs started looking at environmental aspects of the state.
We also funded projects for studies in the universities and even coop societies like the Hulgol cooperative society.
Let me look at the second approach in the next post.

23/01/26, 4:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 964
While the actions to reduce pollution were restricted to regulatory and legal ones, the real solutions are still eluding us. Treatment technologies have improved and become cost effective. But we are a peculiar country. We have too many regulatory agencies like inspector of factories, municipal licensing, electricity inspector, safety and fire inspector and pollution control. I am not sure the regulators are effective. Also corruption plays a role. We haven't fully addressed the pollution problems even today. Dr Gita Gopinath gave an estimate of death due to pollution to a million plus. The problem is serious even now.
We initiated several actions on enrichment- that again on two fronts. One was to minimize consumption in several sectors, and the other to go for an alternate resource. Let me look at one example - cooking.
Cooking activity affects all. In 1981, the most prevalent form was cooking on a wood stove using firewood. Most canteens,messes, and hotels used firewood for cooking. What are the effects?
1. While part of the firewood was collected from local neighborhood by poor villagers, many others depend on firewood from forests. This has many secondary impacts.
2. Forests were getting depleted fast. As I said earlier, Bangalore city got its firewood from Kanakapura,a distance of 60kms, in the sixties-1960 , but by the eighties, Bangalore had to get its firewood from shimoga and Uttara Kannada forests, from a distance of 400 to 500kms. This meant that firewood needed to come from high biodiversity rich forests and transported over long distances by trucks and trains. You needed high quality diesel energy to do this job.
3. Most wood stoves were very inefficient. For every unit of energy used by food, you had to input ten units. If we increased the efficiency of stoves, the firewood consumption would come down by half. We would reduce deforestation considerably.
As I said before, we identified two approaches
Use alternate fuels- solar cookers, bio-gas for cooking
Supply high efficiency wood stoves.
We initiated actions on both fronts. We encouraged setting up bio-gas plants through financial subsidies and also conducting research to improve the design and operation of the bio digester process. The responses were very good.
We got KIMPCO to manufacture simple solar cookers. We popularized these cookers. But it didn't pickup. 
The second path-We looked at high efficiency wood stoves. CPRI had come up with a new stove with efficiencies in the range of 20% whereas the traditional stoves used by villagers had an efficiency of 10%. So use of this new stove would reduce firewood consumption by 50%
So I decided to popularize this stove. We agreed to pay the cost of 100 stoves to be manufactured and distributed. CPRI couldn't manufacture this stove. 
Fortunately I found a person interested in manufacturing this stove. Mr Sankarasubramanian, who was running a garage, met me to invite me to a seminar on the environment by rotary club. I focused my talk there on the problem of firewood scarcity, low efficiency cooking stoves and mentioned the CPRI stove. After my lecture, Mr Sankarasubramanian showed interest in taking up manufacturing the stove.I put him in touch with Mr Jayaraman of CPRI. They signed a contract. Since he had a workshop, he started manufacturing the stoves. He called it priagni. It became very popular and distributed across the country. This was a success story.
Mr Sankarasubramanian became a good friend for two decades. I started advising him on many matters and participated in his campaigns to sell the stove. 
Greetings. Dks
24/01/26, 4:28 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 965
A city-state comes to be for the sake of life, but it continues to be for the sake of living well. 
Aristotle, Politics, Book One.
During the eighties and nineties period, both the Indian and Karnataka governments focused on converting most wood stoves into ones with high efficiencies.  It was a massive activity. The first step was to get high efficiency, reliable, proven, and easy to use stoves developed. The governments funded projects.Many institutions worked on stoves to increase the efficiencies of stoves. Many models were developed and tested.
One of the best stoves was devised by Prof Kumar, Prof Lokras and their group at IISc.They found out the heat losses in stoves and designed a stove called ASTRA ole. It had an efficiency of over 30%. This happened around 1984-85. Karnataka government approved this stove and entrusted its popularization to KSCST. A large number of training programs for masons were conducted both at the state level and at district level. Many engineers were selected by KSCST and stationed in districts for conducting regular training programs, for supervising the construction work ,for trouble shooting and for providing feedbacks.It was a great arrangement. Prof KS Jagadish was enthusiastic about this program and guided it along with Prof Kumar and Lokras. 
This program of popularising high efficiency stoves worked because of the following:
1. Total understanding of human behaviors and acceptability. The usage doesn't change from the tradition. No new skills to be learnt. No new cooking techniques to be learnt.
Detailed thinking and planning of the entire process.
2. Facilitation process- stoves made available, skilled masons available to build, trouble shooting was done by an engineer
3. Good feedback mechanism
4. Financial subsidy.
Then I took another major activity. Planting trees everywhere in a scientific way in order to create a tree culture amongst farmers. 
We campaigned against eucalyptus trees. I visited Urulikanchan near Pune where Bharatiya agriculture foundation was experimenting by implementing new ideas.
I saw them experimenting with a fast growing babul tree and showed a good success. We took it up in karnataka. The program was handled by the development commissioner and all districts were involved in this program. 
We found that Bijapur could replicate the tree plantation successfully. We visited Bijapur and found out that the treatment of seeds with rizhobium helped in the faster growth. We decided to conduct a training program for all officers in charge of district rural development projects and the principal agricultural officers. I went with the development commissioner and conducted the training program. 
The program had mixed success. It was successful in north Karnataka and not successful in south Karnataka. 
We used the NSS program of the universities, funded NSS camps and focused the camps on environment- awareness of better efficient stoves, use of solar energy, tree planting etc. I visited many camps.

25/01/26, 5:13 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 966
I have covered most of my activities, but not all, on environment during my the period 1981. The effects of our actions were mixed- some were successes and some, mostly pollution focused ones, were failures. But I learnt a lot.
Let me look at how these transformed me - my understanding of the real, diverse and dynamic world.
First thing is, it allowed me to travel through a sequence of problems from beginning to end. I first understood the reality to a larger depth through readings, discussions and visits- all three played a significant role in understanding the reality- what is happening, why it is happening, how it is happening, who are involved in it,who are affected,what are the mindsets and motivations of people concerned, what are the difficulties, why the difficulties are not addressed, etc. I got a good picture of the reality of the main problem that I looked at. I saw a whole new world- away from the world of teachers, students and engineers. I created a mental map of the complex universe of the problem. This step is missed by most and even rejected by many. 
Let me give examples from the IT sector. A bank wants a solution to a problem, say it wants a core banking solution in the early 2000. How the bank goes about this problem .
The bank officers know the banks procedures, but had no tacit knowledge - not theoretical knowledge- of the market. 
It floats a tender. The tender has a lot of details about the functionalities to be performed by the software. There are a few vendors who respond. Discussions go on between both sides. The tender is awarded to one vendor.Then both face problems in execution. I had seen tussles, complaints, misunderstandings happening in several instances. Most banks are vendor driven. Some bank officers were smart, picked  up some depth of knowledge of the software and suggested modifications. There are two Kumars who were very good. But that didn't help. I saw Oracle dictating to the CMD of banks. Banks yielded to Oracle's demands.
This shows the need to build a total picture of the situation- user side like banks, technology side, vendor capabilities, banks role, vendor role, conflict resolution,alternate paths etc.
I had seen hundreds of user vendor misunderstandings leading to hot words and dissatisfactions. Users can't change vendors. It is even now vendors raj.
I had discussed the details of the Mahithisindu program of improving computer literacy in 1000 high schools. We built a complete map, identified gaps, identified vendor weaknesses in the beginning. So that helped us to build a detailed action plan. Our implementation had hiccups, but it was a success. We planned well detailing all steps, activities,and requirements and put it in paper upfront. I should say it was not easy, but there was no political interference there. But political pressure was high.Two companies cooperated well. One didn't.
I said that we should have tacit wisdom. What is that - knowledge about your problem, possible solutions, companies offering solutions, their competences, the maturity of their products, the dependability and stability of the company, fall back options, growth options, time expectations, quality of service, agility of changes, rework, redesign, security and confidentiality, ease of use etc. This is extensive. In the case of banks, they didn't have the time or expertise or capability of balanced and unbiased assessments. That is a reality. So the capability limits of both sides should be understood.

26/01/26, 4:45 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 967
In order to construct a complete universe of a situation correctly and effectively, we need to get a lot of information, analysis and tacit wisdom. It is a major thinking, and action based learning activity.To perform these we need some characteristics in ourselves.
The problem of constructing a universe of the environment in Karnataka needed the following characteristics:
Open mind, clarity of thought and actions, looking at the whole before parts- top down approach,look outside and beyond constraints and create a picture, gather tacit knowledge,assess without biases and past knowledge, braveness to discard beliefs and even theories- break the boundaries. We get knowledge in parts. We don't get holistic, unbiased, unconstrained and integrated knowledge - that is called wisdom. Our formal education misses it completely. It has become a total parental responsibility which most parents today don't understand and don't fulfill it. It needs to be built from childhood.
Fortunately, I had a fascination for people and world from childhood. All my readings of books increased my knowledge about people, their habits, behaviors, beliefs and interactions. I got a great picture in my high school days. That was extremely useful for me in addressing these problems in a holistic, people oriented, unbiased, sympathetic and balanced manner without value judgements.
Let me look at them in some detail.
1.open mind- see inside and definitely outside.Remove your assumptions and biases. Don't be too narrow focused. Have a universe of the environment in your view. Accept good statements even if they are wrong in principle. For example, Foresters and ecologists talk of multi-species cultivation of trees. But we propagated subabul, a single specie,because our motive was to get more firewood available in the neighborhood so that forests are saved. It wasn't to regenerate a degraded forest. Open mind looks around a lot. It gets knowledge from every where.
2. clarity of thought. 
In the first stage of understanding a problem, don't worry about processes, technologies, resources, culture of the organization, structures, implementation etc. These will be filled up later.
Clarity of a problem, which is not a technical one, is not easy. It is required to convert data into tacit knowledge. We need many habits to gain clarity of a problem:
Humility- I don't know anything.
Curiosity- to know more in depth and understand. As soon as a child is born, it wants to know what is happening around it within a few days. It starts exploring the room, the house, the outside, the climate, the people,their activities etc. Within a year, the child accumulate a lot of knowledge about its small universe. That is due to curiosity.By age three or four, it asks questions to understand, first from parents, and later from others. It listens. Listening is an adjunct characteristic to curiosity. In villages, a child of four listens to what people are talking and discussing in the houses and in the 'thinnais' of many houses and builds its understanding. Its mind develops well by then. 
Concentration
Contemplation: I built this practice of spending 15 to 30 minutes every day reviewing my work and thinking about what to do in the coming days. I used to mull on lectures. I used to read books, then contemplate on the material. I enlarged my knowledge through reading a lot of books and recapping on them.
Listening
Multidimensional thinking- intense thinking without constraints, without science and technology, without negative thoughts.
Looking at multiple sources- people, articles, visits, discussions,books etc. Gather tacit knowledge. Expertise doesn't help in the start of a problem. It helps only in building the parts.
Analysis
Pursuit
I have seen several people in my life with a phenomenal clarity of thought to construct a complete universe of a situation. If you head an organization, you need clarity. I saw it in Dr Bhabha, Dr Sarabhai, Prof Dhawan, Mr Schumachar,Prof R Narasimha, Prof AKN,Prof Madhav, Prof Ramaseshan,Mr FCK,Mr S Ramadorai , Mr Chandra, and many others. Gandhi had a remarkable clarity about the country in depth on various aspects like poverty, caste, education, industrialization etc. Nehru had a biased, one directional clarity - a clarity based on Soviet philosophy.
Clarity is a practice,a daily practice. It is an exercise. I recall one technique/experiment about getting clarity. Sometimes, you talk to someone and your sibling or parent observe it from a distance and when you go to them, they will ask for details of the conversation- what did the elder say and what was your response. This has three advantages: the child can recap, can abstract the talk, the parent can protect the kid from abuses- I got a lot of it in childhood, my father and grandmother protected me,but my uncle knew that I was mature enough to handle that-, the parent knows about the child better and understands him or her better. 
Similarly, parents, mostly mothers, ask their children on return from school the standard question: what happened in the school? What did you learn? etc. The understanding and bonding increases. The child learns to think and recap.
Another approach is to read newspapers and a lot of books and magazines every day. When I say read ,I don't mean - read and forget. I mean - read, recap, abstract, analyze, add to your picture of the world, review it. That is reading. That is what I did and do throughout my life. I read a variety of books, not for entertainment but for building a picture of the world as seen by us. Understanding the world is one of the main functions of our life. We build this understanding every day. If you build a clear and correct picture, your clarity is good.
Clarity improves through good practices of thinking,abstraction and conscious actions every day. Contemplation for at least 15 minutes every day on the days happenings and reviewing the good and bad will increase clarity.
We will continue other requisites in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
27/01/26, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 968
Let me look at the next requirement for problem solving.
3.Look at the whole before the parts. 
Build connections. Look at the problem architecture. 
We need to realize one important fact. 
No organization stands alone. 
All organizations, activities, successes, failures, growth or death or regeneration depend heavily on people, society and the economy. We ignore them.
If your universe is a bank under consideration, don't start looking at credit or deposits- they are parts.  We used bottom up approach in designing the core banking software and it led to poor design, difficulties of adapting to our conditions, problems of modification and operational inflexibility. Don't try to find out the answer to the question - what is a bank? It deals with the past. It is inward looking and not holistic. We should ask - what should be a bank?Mr MBN Rao, CMD of Indian bank and later Canara bank, said - 'all aspects of money is my business.'But we didn't go very far in achieving that objective.We are still far behind.
Financial gateways, a connection mechanism, was not understood by the banks in the 2000 period. When it was focused, we got a game changer - UPI and easy payment systems. Understanding rural users led to a better design of financial inclusion through imfast device.
Understand the objectives of a bank, its connections to outside- economy, humans and their behavior, businesses, commerce,technology etc. Build a connectivity diagram. Then look at internal and functional connections and flows. Build an architecture now. Clarity and open mind helps in building this picture.Even now we don't address the enterprise bus design.
Banks don't look at the way businesses have changed in the 21st century, don't understand the digital economy, green economy and AI economy and their impacts.
Banks have a large volume of data on transactions. With UPI, they get data on spending, but no analytics is getting done by the banks. They can help ecommerce companies with data analytics.
All these will happen if one sees the connections.
When ecommerce companies looked at functions only, they failed. When customer preferences on cost, better information on products, better services like tracking, return possibilities, speed etc- all external factors- were understood,and the system was re architected, the companies succeeded.
Designers understand a system and look at its implementation and may look across disciplines, but it was still inward looking. A good example is the design of a data centre- one looks at power, communications, air conditioning, business continuity,security etc. When Cray designed his supercomputer, the major problem he encountered was heat dissipation, not just computer design. Database designers looked at physical transactions and built a transactions processing system. They invented commit, acid properties and audit trail to take care of some physical issues.
This is our weakest level of intelligence. We start going into details. We see the tree not the forest. We see the activities in the house and not the behaviors of society or the mood of the village.
Most of us are trained to look at parts and not the whole. This behavior has made us prisoners of known things, makes us nervous about challenges. We don't want to venture out. We don't want to even know the outside.We are happy to be in our comfort thinking zone. That is mediocrity.
That is dangerous, making us slaves. Please come out of this parts syndrome. Look at the whole. Architect the connections. Don't worry about details. It will come later.
This suggestion for change is tough to follow when you are trained through your formal education to go for focusing on detailing the parts. When you join a job, you are assigned to look at a part and no one tells you the whole picture. I have several examples of this. 
Most students join IT companies as coders and when I talked to them after say 10 years, they didn't know what was going on in their company, what is the future direction of the country, how technology is moving and how his or her company works. The most important learning for a person is not programming, but understanding the business and culture of the company or organization.I felt sad. Curiosity was missing.
The fourth requirement is coming up next.
Greetings. Dks

29/01/26, 4:36 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.   970
Let me return to my discussion on the necessary qualities and mindsets required to solve a large problem correctly.
4. Another associated requirement to solve a problem- to discover problems particularly- is to have a futuristic view and futuristic vision and thinking. Science to a large extent lives in the known world. But our real world is changing. So we need to look at a changed world in the next ten years. So futuristic viewing is a mandatory requirement. Otherwise we will end up as a follower never a pioneer. 
Prof Clayton Christensen talked of disruptive innovation. One of the main reasons is a myopia of vision - not knowing what is happening in the world and what the future trends are and how they will change our organizations.
Consider the area- dev ops, a great concept applicable to operations in industries, schools, colleges,universities, banks, government departments, health and hospitals, agriculture and commerce units,-but the progress of research and development in this area is very slow. Very few are working in this area. It has a great transformative power. It will change our thinking and our processes,procedures and practices. But we ignore this. Dev ops is necessary for end to end looking and solving problems.
Let me give some examples. JN Tata was a pioneer on hydro power and steel and talent building. Sir MV saw the potential of irrigation, and its link to the environment. He made the farmers of Mandya district promise not to cultivate sugar cane and not to cut the trees in their fields.Bhabha had a futuristic vision of atomic energy, Sarabhai had a futuristic vision of satellites and launch vehicles. Swayambhu had a vision of power industry in India and BHELs role in it.FC Kohli was a pioneer moving into the unknown businesses of software and outsourcing. A true academic anticipates future trends in his area.
I don't want to list the pioneers abroad. There are a large number of inventors. GE is a good example of survival. Google pioneered search engines.
When I say futuristic vision, I am not even talking about why new inventions are not happening in India. I am talking about the survival of our companies. The failure of HCL,PSI and Wipro in the hardware field is due to this stagnation in futuristic thinking.
Despite knowing fully well that electronics killed several industries, and computers killed many, we still live in ignorance of the future. How many use block chain, IOT, cloud etc in their organizations. These are current tech not future tech.
One main reason is our attachment to the old. We don't discard old technologies. Our early auto industries suffered from this syndrome.
The protected economy made us shy away from risks and stay in the old areas and not venture into new unknown areas. So we remain followers. But we should be the first followers of new trends- avoid time delays. That is happening in AI today.
I have been looking at fin tech for more than two decades. But the progress of fin tech is not satisfactory. I looked at several books on the future of fin tech. Unfortunately they all talk of the past, not the future. Fin ops is evolving, but still in a primitive state. It is not practiced in India. Still I am yet to see a fin arch- financial architecture. When Samba Murty was the director of IDRBT, we discussed this aspect. I even made a draft.In every organization, we are in a fire fighting mode. Futuristic planning will lead to pioneers.
5. We need to understand that problem definition is not a simple mathematical exercise. It is a balanced tacit wisdom exercise. It is dependent on people and their intra and interpersonal relationships and behaviors. Dr Atul Gawande calls it ineptitude- you know how to solve a problem in theory but don't know how to go about it. All of us build tacit knowledge- not quantifiable, may not be connected, not applicable everywhere uniformly- from childhood and analysis based on it is wisdom. Miss Marple of Agatha Christie is a great example. Hercule Poirot is another example. Jeeves is a good problem solver.
6. Detective work- The main job in problem solving is discovery -
Of the universe 
Of the problems encountered
Of the gaps
Of the future trends
Of obstacles
Of the possible solutions and changes etc.
So every activity is a discovery.
Greetings. Dks
30/01/26, 4:03 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 971
That completes one major chapter of my learnings on the problems of the environment. Later I generalized that learning and applied it to define the requirements needed for problem solving in many sectors like IT, steel, space, hospitals,institutions, banks, education, government,construction etc.
I did a detailed description so that most of us understand problem solving holistically. I had this opportunity of working on people's problems directly and most academics don't get this opportunity. This broadened my vision, made me know the intricacies of human and societal problems and our role. It made me a wiser person.I have got a public role now.
I started with a good exposure to large practical problems immediately after BE with the design of a substation. I was totally unaware of how to go about it, but didn't back out. I read books, talked to people, saw a substation and built an idea in mind. That wasn't a mathematical problem.
Then I worked on power systems. It was a pure mathematical and computational problem. 
Next, I got into power distribution problems. It was a pathless path. I formulated a problem and modeled it with heuristics. It was a multi model complex problem. 
Next was my Bedthi power plant redesign, and my rural energy systems planning and design. This involved knowing not just the technical and economic aspects, but also the societal angle. It thrust me into multi dimensional problem solving methods. Still it was in my domain. 
But this problem of enriching the environment was not in my domain and involved people and government.I was happy I did something there. It enriched me definitely and created a new dimension of reality in me. 
During this period, I continued my association with TCS and PSTI. My interactions with Dr Nirmal Jain increased. One idea germinated in my mind. It was automation of COBOL programs through a generator. This will improve the accuracy and productivity. I put two students, Mr Garg and Mr Menon, on this task- to build a generator for commercial batch applications. They were working and I discussed it with Nirmal. He liked the idea. I designed a generator language called sysgel for TCS.
I had a kind of experience at TCS.At that point of time, FCK was well known in CSI and academic circles. Nirmal was cultivating friendships with many academics. That was necessary for campus recruitment. Campus recruitment started to pick up by late seventies though Tata Electric was doing it even in the sixties, but not regularly.Since TCS had a demand for more engineers , it started regular campus interviews and selections mostly from IITs and IISc and mainly ME and MTech students. Tata Burroughs also started campus selections.
There used to be competitions between both on day one slot. Initially ,the interviews were rigorous , but slowly their confidence went and they selected a large number of graduating students.Nirmal visited for TCS and Hebbalkar for Burroughs. So a big competition was going on for several years. I used to talk to Dr Mahishi and got day one slot for TCS. I saw the drama by Hebbalkar. He wouldn't sit, but kept walking and questioning the students. It was a proven technique to destroy the calm atmosphere.
I talked to FCK about increasing the catchment of TCS to BE/BTechs of IITs/IISc and later to other institutions. He agreed to slowly open up the recruitment.
But most other TCSers had no interactions with outside- academics or professional societies. TCS was almost a closed house.
More on TCS later.

And of course, as we grow older, our quota of difficult moments increases too. However, at the same time, experience has given us the means to overcome those defeats and find a path that will allow us to carry on. We need to include that on our mental video as well.
Paulo Coelho    Maktub
Let me continue with my interactions at TCS. Most associates were intelligent and hard working. They were committed to their projects and they wanted to learn different types of machines, OS etc and so eagerly moved from project to project. There was a great urge for learning. They lived in the present and didn't bother about the future. 
I discussed with many about going for databases. I found resistance from most. Still, I decided to give lectures on data bases. About ten people attended. Interest was there, but time wasn't there.
As I said they wanted hands on experience on whatever technologies were existing and applied in projects, but didn't want to look at the changes that were happening- mainframe to mini, pascal and C becoming prominent, PCs coming in, client server model becoming dominant. You see the eighties were ushering in new architectures, new areas like software engineering, data bases, performance measurements etc. I was working on online distributed transactions processing at that time. Most TCS Indian projects were batch processing COBOL projects. Most TCSers were inward driven. But there were some who were seeing the trends happening. Mr FCK talked about microprocessors lab. But Mrs Gandhi didn't permit import of computers.
Mr FCK wanted to set up a research centre for software engineering. He had some rounds of discussions with me. Then ,he brought Prof EC Subba Rao as director and set up the Tata RDD centre in TMTC, Pune. That has grown so big today. 
Prof EC Subba Rao asked me to visit his centre along with my family. His intention was to make me join TRDDC and he wanted to find opportunities for my wife. He knew my interests in rural technologies. So I went to Pune with my family, and stayed there for two days. 
I visited the centre and had a series of discussions with him. We had very extensive and interesting conversations on a wide range of topics. He arranged some visits for my wife. I visited a few villages where Tatas were working on rural development. But the most interesting was my visit to Mr Salunkye, who headed an organization called Pani panchayat. I thought it was a great concept. Pani panchayat distributes the water available in a village with equal distribution to all residents. Those who had land can buy extra water from those who had no land and wantes to sell their water rights. He appointed a water distributor and a panchayat at the village level to resolve disputes. He had implemented it in about 200 villages in Purandar tehsil. It was a drought prone area I felt that it should be experimented in karnataka also. 
I also visited the Bharatiya Agro industries foundation at Uruli kanchan . BAIF opened my eyes. They were doing a lot of work on agricultural machinery. They experimented with livelihood schemes for marginal farmers.I was impressed with their work.
I was reluctant to move to Pune. I was on leave from IISc then and was with the state government. I didn't want to burn my IISc boat and joinTata RDD centre. I could do better research at IISc, I could teach and get involved with social and environmental activities. Bangalore offered me a lot of scope. I didn't see any advantage in Pune. 
We took the Deccan queen and reached Bombay. I met Nirmal and told him that I won't be interested in joining TRDDC and would continue my association with TCS. He accepted my views.

01/02/26, 4:10 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 973
After TCS, let me look at my engagement with PSTI which was very active. I was lecturing there regularly. I knew Mr GV Anantharamiah, Mr NSR Rao, Mr Venkatakrishna, Mr Magudapathy, Mr Taranath and many others. The car driver, Mr Khan, and the van driver,Mr Yusuf, became very friendly to me.  In short,I knew everyone there and all liked me. I became part of PSTI. I am saying this because, I lost my friend, Mr TCA Annavaradan, and both Mr Narasimhan, director and GV Rao,deputy director were transferred and the set up was new. I wasn't sure of the response. But I didn't have to worry. The relationship became better and closer. The same continued for the next ten years.
Let me turn to my family.I encountered one small problem in May June 81. My son completed UKG from Kishore Kendra school and I wanted to admit him in KV IISc where my daughter was studying. I submitted my son's application along with the institute's certificate that I continue as an employee of IISc. Still some members of the committee raised an objection that I wasn't in the institute. I told them ,' I will return to IISc. My job is at IISc and not in the government.' So my son got admission in KV. His schooling was taken care of.He was going to school with his sister and another four children- Madhav's and SPG's children. So he had company and friends. Childhood was good. 
I found him very active and with a mind of his own. He didn't obey us some times.Let me explain one incident here. 
During my stay with the government,I visited several places, many were forest areas like Bandipur, Nagarhole and Mercara. I visited Sirsi, Bhairumbe, Yellapur and Hubli.I took my family everywhere as it would be an exposure of people to them and a great education. I feel that I spent a lot of time with my family during this period. My son was attached to our driver a lot. He wanted to be with him.
When I visited Mercara, I stayed in the guest house at Harangi dam. I was walking on the top of the reservoir with several officers, my children were walking with my wife. Suddenly my son broke free and started running in that place.It was a high plateau and I panicked, ran and caught him. It wasn't a pleasant experience. 
Actually, I took my family to many places, including Pune and Bombay. Later we visited Kemmana Gundi, Adi Chunchunagiri. They experienced travel by crowded buses. They had visited Mysore and Madras often. Later they visited Kolkatta, Delhi and Simla. So they had a great idea of India.
It was time to decide on the upbringing of my children. I had a fantastic childhood which built me into what I am and taught me the world and its dynamics. 
Let me recap on my upbringing. 
1.Home environment is essential to build a good, calm person. It helps a child to concentrate.
2.I knew that education happens at home. You learn many concepts like cleanliness, routine living, time management, respect to elders, listening carefully, diligence, contemplation, prayer, proper food habits, simplicity,etc.. I learnt many values and practiced them.
3.I knew that the next level of education happens in the society- relatives,neighbors, friends,elders, farmers, priests, vedic scholars, visitors, shop keepers and others with whom you come in contact. This is tacit learning, not instruction based. You should use your mind to learn. This makes you think and use your mind. I learnt to listen, be quiet and helpful. I listened to the talks in the thinnai and bathing steps in the village tank in the village.
4.I learnt a lot from rituals, festivals, temple functions, samskaras happening in the village. I learnt from news articles about the country.
5. Another important source is books. I read a lot of fiction.I didn't read for entertainment. The fiction books built a detailed view of life in different parts of the country.I learnt a lot about people, their lifestyles, beliefs, habits and behaviors.
So I decided to train my children on similar lines. I decided to give them independence, freedom,responsibility and decision making powers. I didn't decide on their education after admitting them in KV. They decided.

02/02/26, 4:48 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 974
Let us see some comments.
Dr TKM Babu
"Good morningYour view is   correct.After main frame computers mostly by IBM ,  mini computers by DEC ,Wang computers,  were popular in industry and educational institutions where main computers were costly for their applications.The development of powerful comparatively cheap microcomputers were so fast that these companies could not predict. They went out of business. WE can call this also destructive innovation.In your  view Dr DKS, could they have prevented this ?Thanks Mahendra Babu👍🙏
My brief response
India used mainframes from CDC, IBM, Burroughs and later DeC10. We didn't use Wang. We used PDP/11, HP at IIM Ahmadabad and ECIL minis manufactured in Hyderabad. 
While the initial pioneers,DCM and ORG didn't stay longer, we had a strong long term showing by a few companies like Wipro and HCL and a medium term operations by several companies like PSI, Eiko,CMC,Sunray,Zenith, PCS, NELCO and a short term by some companies like Pertech, Keonics, Uptron,ESPL etc. So it is clear that many came forward. Even TVS and Kirloskar tried manufacturing, but it didn't work.
There were many assemblers selling unbranded computers at a lower price. IISc supported some of them. One company I know well is Taurus. Most assemblers couldn't cope with change and couldn't import latest technology parts. Their enthusiasm was great and they worked very hard.
Electro systems associates started by Gopinadham and Rajanikanth concentrated on selling microprocessor kits to colleges and survives even now.
First, we should recognize the fact a number of companies jumped into the hardware market and persisted with hardware supplies.For about 20 years, both HCL and Wipro stood firm in our computer market. They produced servers and built PCs. They moved quickly into the latest developed processors very well. Both did a good job. I bought computers from all these companies listed above.
They survived till 2005, more than 20 years. I won't say they are failures. But there were many reasons for them to move out of hardware.
The eighties saw Intel going fast with new processors starting with 8085 to Pentium. Then came mother board and IO controllers, VME or multibus concepts. So adaptation was not easy. Our companies had no collaboration. They had a tough blind task of designing new systems.
Software services gave a very high profit. Hardware profit margins were low. Struggles were high. Government policies didn't encourage faster innovation. There were only two designers- Raman in HCL and Sridhar Mitta in Wipro. So sustainability is a problem. 
Then came the most important problem- scaling. Foreign companies were producing millions of PCs where as Indian companies struggled to make a lakh. Scale gave a great price advantage to foreign companies. Indian companies didn't export PCs to other countries and so couldn't reach the scale. 
Also we didn't manufacture printers,keyboards, displays, disk drives, mother boards,buses ports,interfaces, memory etc. So every item was imported. There was a customs duty on them. So it wasn't easy to minimize the costs.
After liberalisation in 1991, we made mistakes. We had two companies in Bangalore manufacturing highend mainframe level computers- digital corporation and PSI Bull. I visited both and did a technology audit for PSI Bull.There was no business. It was a wrong time. When the world was going towards the client server model, there were not many buyers for these machines. So both companies closed down .
On the other side, IT was growing fast. Most companies and organizations wanted networking and software. Opportunities were quite high. Scaleup was easy. Profits were high. TCS and Infosys were doing well. So CMC moved into software first. Tatas left computer manufacture and focused on IT.
So both HCL and Wipro moved to IT services. Our Hardware business died. In 2005-2006 period, I was struggling to buy PCs and small servers for banks.
So I won't call this disruptive innovation. All were clear about their growth paths. 
Actually both HCL and Wipro set up separate companies. Wipro infotech set up wipro systems to develop software and struggled for over a decade. 
HCL set up several companies like HCL Comnet, HCL technologies etc. 
So both moved to IT services. It was a conscious decision to move into IT by both companies, not a failure, but a shift they were working on for decades.
I wasn't happy that we missed the hardware bus. I will blame government policies for this. 
How do you explain the strange situation- a country which built two digital computers in 1963 at TIFR and later at ISI and Jadavpur university missed the computer manufacturing route?The reasons are:
1. We never built a hardware foundry. So we couldn't produce VLSI chips, a big drawback. Malaysia produced chips in 2000. The one company- semiconductors India -died a fire death. The government ignored this sector till now.
2.Thailand with no culture imported chips and other components by kilos and sold them on the foot path ,this I saw in 1987. They produced manuals in Thai language to assemble computers.We couldn't import them.
3. Imports were the big hurdle.
4. Most hardware companies didn't realize that a computer is an integration of hardware and software. They ignored utilities,OS,compilers etc.
5.Except HCL and Wipro, other companies had a poor services unit. HCL was also good at sales.
 I bought Zenith's superbrain and Eikos 8 bit computers in early eighties.I bought a lot from HCL,Wipro and PCS. Now it is HP, Acer and Dell.
Greetings. Dks

Let me clarify my credentials to respond to Babu's query yesterday. 
I interacted with most manufacturers from 1970 onwards starting with Computronix and Hinditron and later ORG,Zenith,HCL, and still later others . 
I started helping in the purchase of computers by many organizations, educational institutions, government departments, banks, from 1970 onwards till 2020. I met almost all senior officers and sales people of the computer companies- hardware, networking, databases and software, manufacturing, system integration and services.
I was responsible for the purchase of thousands of servers and several tens of thousands of PCs. So I knew the pulse of computer industries and basic manufacturers abroad like Intel, CISCO, HP,IBM,Sun, and many others intimately. My comments are based on my experiences and thousands of interactions.

Prof Vasudev Parvati
It is great to read about how you balanced your career between academia, government and industry which I'm sure provided you with a 360° perspective on educational priorities. Why is it that even today, in our IT domain industry academia partnership is still at a premium sir?
My response
Vasudev, you asked me a question that was a big concern for me for several decades. This topic is very close to my heart. But I didn't succeed in this mission of creating social understanding amongst our teachers and students. There are many reasons and let us see them. 
I saw a spark when I used faculty in 20 engineering colleges to conduct inspections in 1000 high schools in Karnataka and another interest when I organized the TBRP program in 10 colleges under our foundation,FAER. But both weren't enough.
Let me start with a few questions to teachers of science and engineering.
Should a person- engineer or Scientist - know about order in the universe ,not just order in matter?
Should a person - scientist or engineer- know the multidimensional nature of universe so that he can do meaningful activities relevant to reality?
Should a person learn theory and technical practices alone and not worry about applications and impacts?
How will a S and T education find solutions to the societal problems if it has no idea of society,nature and people? How will a person get knowledge of society?
Should a person know about the impacts of science and technology on a society, on people, their beliefs, their practices?
Should he not own the problems created by S and T on people and society and find solutions?
Should science and technology impose its restrictive materialist views on society and students?
Is education's purpose to create specialists only?
Should education be dogma oriented?
These questions should concern all people,not just teachers, but teachers have a deep responsibility. Even the recent budget looks at education as a partitioned set like STEM. Holistic approach is missing.
The answer to the above questions will tell us the importance of social understanding for all in today's complex and volatile world and more so for the problem solvers. It gives us an opportunity to solve the current problems of society and people correctly.
Let me continue in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
04/02/26, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post. 976

Let me establish my premise to socially sensitive- not politically- teaching and academics. I don't use terms like inclusive, but I strongly believe that all proposed engineering solutions by us should not be artificial or purely mathematical, but should be sympathetic to people, society, the environment and economy.
Towards that end, I believed in and followed four important - out based - principles that I should follow as a teacher.
1. Know your society, its problems and your responsibilities and efforts to address them as much as possible. This gives meaning to your teaching. This gives you fulfillment.
2. Know your people, their behavior, their mindsets, habits and culture. This makes you a good teacher. I call this tacit wisdom.
3. Know the reality of your current world- geopolitics, economy, living conditions, technologies, industries,business, opportunities, the good and evil of governance and society. This makes your teaching relevant and purposeful.
4. Make teaching inclusive, realistic and relevant. Don't make it only technical.
These four formed the basis for my activities. I believed in the Vedic statements.
'Shraddhaya deyam'- give and give with mindfulness and purpose.
'Paropakartam idam sareeram'- the body is for helping others.
What have I to give? Knowledge and solutions. I strived on that direction. I felt that Giving is my life purpose. I am doing it even now.
I have discussed certain qualities that teachers should build in themselves to understand the need for society education linkage and integration. Let me list some:
1. Open mind- look outside, get knowledge from every source,absorb them and reform yourself. Change your beliefs and habits. Fixed mindsets destroy us.
2.Holistic understanding. Look at connections and not the parts. Culture is an important connect to technology. The Economy impacts our life. We had very few millionaires in the 18th ,19th centuries. We see a large number of millionaires everywhere, even in villages. We see a lot of very rich people in villages. We also have a very large number of very poor people. So society is highly stratified and has a high degree of disparity. This leads to a great imbalance and many evil qualities like jealousy, hatred, violence etc. Since technology is responsible for some of these evils or even many of these, it is imperative that technology should address these issues. That means a holistic, interconnected understanding of people, society, the economy, commerce,business and technology necessary. So break the barriers, borders and fences.
3. A teacher's job is not information transfer, but to impart knowledge about reality and tacit wisdom. Focusing on technical or scientific topics alone is injurious to our education and children and our society. We build wrong materialistic values.
4. Be curious. Be innovative. Ask questions. Look beyond the known and look for unknowns. Holistic understanding will open up many pandora's boxes.
I tried my best,but didn't succeed in building an empathetic curriculum.
Let me trace a bit of history to understand this problem.
The social aspect of our life purpose was very high till the 18th century. Most people lived in similar circumstances - lower middle class and poor. There were very few rich people. So that created a strong bond and attachment. All village problems were settled by villagers. Religious activities were conducted by society. Festivals allow you to mix with others. Samskaras, like marriages, allowed one to feed others and others to help him. Most villagers respected death and showed their solidarity with the deceased family.Poor  children lived on vara oota- eating in one house every week.
So that ambiance built a homogeneous environment, a simpler reality ,a stronger attachment to religion,values and idealism and a greater purpose in life. It gave prominence to local problems, culture and religion.Society dominated ones thinking and actions.
Even in the 1940s when I was brought up, this consciousness of society, people, religion, values and nature was built in me very strongly. Those were still days filled with idealism and Gandhism.I lived in that kind of ethos and my reading a lot of social fiction imbibed in me a strong sense of empathy and responsibility to society. That gave me my first education.

05/02/26, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 977
WHAT are the major, inner problems of people in our day? When we look beneath the outward occasions for people’s disturbances, such as the threat of war, the draft, and economic uncertainty, what do we find are the underlying conflicts? To be sure, the symptoms of disturbance which people describe, in our age as in any other, are unhappiness, inability to decide about marriage or vocations, general despair and meaninglessness in their lives, and so on.
Rollo May
But the situation of our society changed.We were in a state of isolation and poverty till the mid eighties. During this period 1960 to 1985, we were ideologically moved to the left- socialism or soviet-ism.  But the liberalization of the economy ushered us into a new global era. Post nineties, we unabashedly became materialistic and specialization oriented. But the 21st century gave us another series of turns. It was considered the anxiety era. Anxiety is monotonically increasing. It is not an easy period.
With AI, while blue collar jobs of carpenters,masons,electricians etc will not be affected, engineering will get a jolt ,a cyclone.So social and human solving of problems is more important than technical solutions. 
Is there a need for all of us to be society and people minded? Yes.It is clear from our discussions.Before I ask how, let me paint an IT picture seen by me in both 20th and 21st centuries. 
While IT focused on industries ,banks and organizations in the mid twentieth century, PCs changed it in two ways. 
One -PCs reached a large number of small organizations and their business depended on PCs. Client server was dominant.
Two- all activities were online and automatic. Stakeholders and employees interacted with computers and databases directly. Both data flow and workflow were automated. This led to user friendly systems.
But the internet, social networks, mobile and cloud changed everything in the 21st century. IT became people centric.
Also the 21st century led to changes, complexity, uncertainty and volatility. So life is different. 
Organizations will change structurally, people will decide the success of all of us,AI will try to control us with much better knowledge - a frightening situation. So get ready to throw everything we know and do overboard and pick up new things.
Education needs a rethinking,reworking and ,reimagination. No tinkering will work. How do we do it?
Let me take one example - psychotherapy.
How does a therapist learn?. His is not a science with fixed rules. His area changes. 
A therapist learns from his clients not from books. people are his primary source. He doesn't build bookish knowledge, but tacit wisdom.
So we should accept that engineering is a practice not just an application of physics and mathematics. Move away from that firmly entrenched belief/ faith.
I got my social concern by getting involved in societal problems, government's problems and organizations problems. I built my reality and tacit wisdom through involvements. It is not luck,but looking for practical problems, however complex and different they may be. I looked beyond borders, beyond computer science, energy and environment. Mathematics is a tool for me.Solutions come from people.
So teach tacit wisdom to students by getting into practice.NEP is not helpful here.
Social understanding should be a part of our system. Every organization should have social systems department. 
Change curriculum - introduce two to three courses on social and human systems. Focus on social problem solving not just technical solutions. 
Introduce training for teachers. 
Build case studies.
Teach human-based decision making.
Send students to government offices for internships,not industries. The students should build a case study.
Have at least one social project in our curriculum. It should be problem discovery oriented.
But sensitise all on the relevance of social empathy as an integral part of engineering and solutions will come through exposure to people and their responses.
Greetings. Dks

My response
You raised many relevant aspects.As I said earlier, education is a continuous life long process. It comes from hundreds of sources, not schools alone. 
But the purposes of education are 1.to know reality, 2. to define life's purpose, 3. to define goals and strategies, 4. to understand the proper ways of learning, 5. to get good habits and values to make a person a good human and to gather tacit wisdom.
How do we get it? By training from childhood. It is a hard way. There are no easy solutions. 
We need to create a positive pleasant ambiance in home, schools and colleges. That is what we had at IISc, a fantastic study ambiance,an opportunity to question and find answers,an atmosphere to read ,listen,contemplate and understand. This existed in the 1950s and 60s. We had dedicated teachers. But we destroyed those institutions by politicalising everything, recruiting uninterested teachers, encouraging mediocrity and destroying curiosity.
But there is a positive side. India is a diverse country. We see thousands accepting challenges despite the bad vibe in schools. Many of us can isolate ourselves from the neighborhood and perform outstanding work.We are resilient people. A little push, a few committed, sincere, non commercial institutions will help students. RSS is very committed even though many don't subscribe to their ideology. ABVP is producing political leaders that I expected from communism. 
That is why we produce hundreds and thousands of CEOs and political and spiritual leaders. Many have a strong thinking, analyzing and arguing- tarka- mind. So we move forward despite being saddled with a not great and relevant curriculum. 
But I hope that we, with the help of AI, will flatten our learning system. People anywhere in India will get access to infinite data and information with analysis. Today's AI gives one response to your queries. Tomorrow's AI will give many responses with detailed analysis of what may be accepted and what may be rejected. A child of today will be able to learn hundred times that of a child of yesterday by sitting at home. Books will be passe. Schools will be minimalistic in learning. But family has a much bigger role in creating values and leadership qualities.
The most important concern for me is:
This is not a time to relax.
There will a pralaya- a huge flood- sweeping us and drowning us. AI will subordinate and even destroy countries. Most won't know what to do, how to live and survive. Destruction looms large.
But don't worry. Solutions will be found by Indians.
So get ready. Move away from petty thinking and petty caste politics.
We have a lot of work to do. We have to build huge mental bridges to survive this flood. That needs millions of new age thinkers. They need to come from genZ, not from other age groups. Elders can prepare genZ to brave the challenges and travel miles to be humans and not slaves.

Mr Bala Ajjampur
"Sir, with genZ being lured into metaverse and looking at everything as a game based learning, and, interactions sprouting without demographical boundaries,  the current education systems are becoming a burden for many students as they do not understand a simple conceot of learning the purpose of graphs without their applied use in the real world. We have one set of students who have excelled academically but lack digital literacy and skills to navigate a digital world that is exponentially getting intertwined with the real world. The other set is on top of this digital world intrusion, but  without any progress made on the academic side, they are not picked to be part of the workforce that would benefit from their skills. In the end, I have a feeling that the current education system will let genZ down, whichever set we are looking at. So elders have a tough time dealing with genZs whichever set they belong to. Are my fears justified?"
My response
My knowledge of gen Z in India gives me a lot of confidence. Most gen Z are serious and hard working. They do very well in 12th class exams. I have seen 15 year old building AI agents. Many high school students know both AI and quantum. They are also strong in math. Rural students are not using social networks and spend time on their studies. So I don't have any fears on this count. 
While most Indian students in engineering have reasonable knowledge of AI, they haven't got to an inventive mode. we are still followers. The government encourages AI research in a big way. But IITs are not very active. I am hopeful.
Let me continue with Karunakara's agony.
He is worried about our education and our schools. I am also concerned about education. 
But I am looking for alternate approaches not the Macaulay's model of clerks producing school education.
Education happens only to the extent of 10% in schools. Schools teach artificial aspects. Life and living teaches reality of how to live, how to decide, how to connect with people etc. So we get 90% education from listening to parents, neighbors and society, observing nature, people and their actions, getting involved with societal work, questioning whatever we encounter, and reading lots of books and seeing many useful learning videos. So let us strengthen these cognitive processes by creating a good and calm societal ambiance for it. 
I look back to my childhood. Life was calm, steady, unhurried and happy. Where did all these disappear now?
I feel that in the olden days, temples were built to create a great ambiance to calm our minds, focus them and contemplate. We had tanks, open spaces, river banks, parks which also gave us a great ambiance to listen and think and act. Even Sampige road in the sixties was a calm place. You can admire the trees, eat bun butter,jam and walk serenely.That was my activity then.But not today.
To me meditation is not vacuous sitting. It is deep contemplation. It is learning concentration. The vibrations of prayer also help us. Even temples are crowded now. People are in a hurry while visiting temples. We miss that serenity now.
When children are brought up in today's hurried atmosphere, what happens? They learn to worry and forget to be calm, to concentrate and contemplate,essential for education. We need to change this. How?
It is not easy, but necessary. We have to educate parents and teachers that a calm atmosphere induces in-depth learning. So build calm atmosphere in houses and schools. Make a room or a large area a silence zone first. See the impact. 
Then change your mindset. New ways of learning is the need now not just traditional schools. Most children have 20 to 30% extra learning capabilities. Use it and guide them to learn human behaviors and actions as well as AIs role in our activities.
Quantum mindset- randomness, digital, multiple solutions and uncertainties - is different and opposite to the school physics. I don't see opportunities for our school children to go quantum way. 
Lastly, remember there are a large number of intellectuals and dooms day prophets who say IT will die. Don't worry. GCCs are reworking strategies to operations of companies and most are in India.It will take a minimum of 5 years to embed AI in all existing software systems and that needs a million AI software architects. So, let us forget about the evils of AI and concentrate on mastering, applying and controlling AI.

Let me look at my fourth level interactions. My third level human relationships for the past 20 plus years were with students and academics, computer professionals and power professionals. Now there came a big change.
During my life ,I had met and interacted with thousands of students and several thousands of people in all walks of life. Next to books, these encounters made my knowledge very rich and broad, something no institution or university in the world teaches. I became wise to worldly knowledge and happenings. 
During my stay with the Karnataka government, I met different kinds of people. I interacted with ministers, IAS officers, politicians, government officers and staff. It opened my eyes to Karnataka- the state,its geography and its diversity, my concerns and people governing the state.
I won't discuss all my interactions, but with a few persons only.
The first one I met was Mr Jayakumar Anagol. I spent several hours every week with him. He was a philosophy graduate and had a lot of interest in education. He was the secretary to CM then. He used to read a lot of books. So we had a lot of intellectual conversation. I continued my discussions with him for decades even after returning to IISc.
We had a Friday weekly lunch meeting of secretaries and I met several IAS officers. I had intellectual interactions with many. A few names are Mr RA Naik, additional chief secretary, Mr MK Venkatesan, development commissioner, Mr AS Melkote, Secretary, forests, Mr Mohandas Moses, Home, Mr Vasudevan,Home, Mr BR Prabhakar, Industries and commerce, Mr Warrier, Finance, Mr Venkatesh,PWD and several others.
The next person was RP Krishnamurthy, our undersecretary, a pious and honest person. I was surprised to see such a simple and hard working person in a chaotic CMs office. He worked sincerely for long hours, and he wouldn't eat anything in the office. He didn't eat outside, a remarkable resolve. I liked his commitment to his job and his religious beliefs.I used his experience while filling the confidential reports of officers in our department. He normally deferred to my opinion.
The pollution control board was under me.So I met Mr Hanumantha Rao, chairman of the pollution control board and found him not active. He was looking for excuses. The secretary,Capt Raja Rao, was better. I attended several board meetings. I met them very frequently. I met other officers also. I felt that there was a recruitment problem in the board- all officers up to the  chairman came from the PWD. So they didn't know about pollution control. Air pollution was more difficult for them to understand. So the board was just managing. They couldn't handle most court cases.Most approvals were deemed approvals, not strict approvals.Many high polluting paper,sugar, chemical industries got deemed approval without installing control units. I started questioning the board's delays in approvals. I told them to remove the possibility of deemed approvals. All approvals should be after installing control units to treat the waste water. But he dodged that most of the time pleading for the industries. I found the secretary tougher and started instructing him mostly.
I had met Mr Veerappa Moily, the finance minister, several times. With his help, I took actions in the Belur temple. Moily was a sensitive person. He stopped many projects. He attended KSCST exe-com regularly. 
I met V Balasubramanian at Bhimarayanagudi , found him very efficient and he was intellectually inclined. 
I met several ministers and legislators during my period in the government. I found many politicians to be simple minded. Corruption was just growing up. But most senior IAS were not corrupt.
