Another important person I met was Prof LT Sharma, who was the principal of the Mahatma Gandhi college in Sirsi and conducted the Bedthi conference. He was appointed as a speech writer to the chief minister. He met me frequently. I gave ideas for the speeches and even contributed. I converted the focus towards the environment and renewable energy. Once, the forest minister asked me who wrote the CMs speeches. He wanted me to write his speech once.
Let me narrate three instances I remember.
The first instance relates to a forest department conference. I wrote about the need for reducing firewood consumption and use of high efficient stoves- we were promoting solar cookers, biogas stoves and priagni stoves then-. I attended the conference. After reading out the written speech, the chief minister talked in the opposite direction saying 'it is all nice. But poor people suffer and can't buy firewood. So the forest department should arrange supply of firewood.' I was shocked. Later, when I was talking to forest officers, they didn't understand the statement and opposed it. Education of renewable energy and energy conservation was a tough and developing area then. I talked to students about environment, resources conservation and renewables where ever I went. But when I talked to students at Karnatak university to the students about it , I found that they didn't understand anything. 
The second instance was the speech by the chief minister at the KSCST annual general meeting. Prof Sharma met me and I gave him a lot to write for that speech by the CM. After a day, I was asked to prepare the CMs speech. So I wrote the speech. The CM read the speech with some difficulty. I had written in detail about the activities of the newly created S and T department, about popularization of renewable energy devices, about Priagni, about rural database creation- incidentally this became later in the nineties a big project of DST government of India called NRDMS and KSCST and I played a big role in this project-,and our problem generation methods. Mr Anagol liked the speech. Even Mr Moily appreciated that speech. I felt that AKN was upset with that speech. After the meeting, he asked me ,' did you write the speech', I said yes. He wasn't happy. 

The third instance was ISTE convention. Prof Mahadevan, principal, KREC, Surathkal met me a few days before the convention and said that this convention would be a trend setter. He said that the CM would announce the setting up of a separate technological university for karnataka. 
There were two opinions trending in the state. Some wanted autonomous colleges. Many wanted a technological university controlling all engineering colleges in Karnataka state. I thought that concept goes against the starting of many universities that was happening in the country. The CMs speech prepared by Prof Sharma had a lot of inputs from me. I had spoken opposing a single university.
After the convention was over, Prof Mahadevan met me. He was crestfallen that the CM didn't announce a tech university. Then I revealed the secret to him. 
I made significant contributions in many speeches of the CM. He even spoke about the heritage of India in several places. I found Mr Gundu Rao,our CM, a very bold and courageous person. The opposition - Mr Deve Gowda- was not very active then. Mr Ramakrishna Hegde was in Delhi.

10/02/26, 4:41 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 982
I met a large number of senior officers in many government departments. 
I traveled with the CM to Delhi a couple of times and saw the respect shown to him by several senior congress dignitaries, some waiting till 2 am as our evening flights were usually delayed by several hours. Flight delays were common in those days with only Indian airlines operating in the country. During these journeys, I met Mr Karthikeyan,DIG of police. He saw to it that none offers drinks to the CM. I also met Mr GV Rao , DG of police. I spent a lot of time with him. He was Prof CNR Rao's brother in law. I remember one trivial conversation with him about choosing a good day for an activity. After asking me, he immediately said,' you are a scientist and you don't believe in good time or rahukala etc.'I met Bharathi Vishnuvardhan also once. I met Mr Nijalingappa, and also Mr Varadan IAS ,his secretary when he was the CM.General Kariappa was made the Field Marshall at that time and he was talking to Mr Nijalingappa in familiar tones.
The administration of Gundu Rao was effective and orderly most of the time, almost till the end of his term as CM. We didn't have any dispute with our neighboring states.Cauvery problem was non existent. He had a good relationship with MGR. MGR invited him for the Bharathiyar function at Ettayapuram in south Tamilnadu.Our CM took a large number of people with him.He was impressed about the function. He was asking ,'Why can't we do functions in such a grand manner.' He also replied that kannadigas didn't like grand functions.
 But unfortunately, he didn't realize the emotions of people on language. So the Gokak report created a big turbulence across the state. It wanted primacy to Kannada and wanted every student to study Kannada compulsorily. Unfortunately, many students in Karnataka got exemption from Kannada language and took up other languages like Sanskrit, Urdu,Telugu, Tamil, even French etc.Even though the government accepted the report and agreed to implement it in stages, the agitators had a different agenda.
That was a tough time.We couldn't go by government cars. The state car of law secretary was burnt by miscreants. Still I traveled in the state. My driver, Vijakumar, was very efficient, had painted Kannada license plate even before the agitation and so he managed the agitators.Most educational institutions were affected. The Kendriya vidyalaya  of IISc was located near Yeshwantpur and locals there threatened the school.My children were studying there. It was a worrying time for parents.Even Dr Rajkumar was heckled and had to be taken into Vidhana Soudha for safety.
I went with Mr Shivarama karanth to a college in Siddapur. He said that the Kannada medium was not a good thing at that point of time, since there were no books on science and mathematics in Kannada. The students appreciated his comments. 
Because of this agitation, congress lost the elections. Mr Ramakrishna Hegde became the chief minister in 1982. Within a week, he brought the state under control by arresting thousands of Rowdi sheeters.
I should talk about two senior persons - Mr RA Naik, chief secretary and Mr PR Nayak, development commissioner. Both were knowledgeable about the state and were innovative. RA Naik started a week long file clearance drive. Lots of files were pending in many departments and so he initiated this move. Vidhana Soudha gates were locked and all departments should report on the number of files cleared. It was an effective activity,cleaning up the files accumulated in the departments. My both departments were almost current with one or two pending files. So I decided on my usual practice of spending morning hours at IISc and reaching Vidhana Soudha by 11am. The gates were closed, but I managed to get in fortunately.
We were traveling from Gulbarga to Hyderabad after a meeting at Gulbarga. He identified many things on the way and predicted many things. His knowledge of that area was amazing.

"Character and intelligence. The poles your talent spins on, displaying your gifts. One without the other brings only half of success. It isn’t enough to be intelligent; you must also have the right character. The fool fails by behaving without regard to his condition, position, origin, or friendships."
Art of worldly wisdom.     Baltazar Gracian
Let me continue discussions on my interactions.
Another person who impressed me was Mr PR Nayak. He was the development commissioner of Karnataka,an important person in the state. 
Let me give a bit of history about this position of the development commissioner. It is a great innovation.
Mr.G V K Rao, a very well known administrator of Karnataka, a deep thinker and a reformer, wanted a government department without files, but with the main task of looking after the development of the state. He created this position in the late fifties. Instead of doing firefighting operations all the time, he felt that we should look at the growth of the state- "what activities are being done? How is the progress and what are the hurdles?What are their impacts and how to modify them? What needs to be done? How should the government go about the new activities?"
This brings me to another question- why is Bangalore growing fast in industries and science and technology? Many people- both Indians and foreigners ask me this question. I even gave a talk on this topic at Stanford university.
Mysore even in pre independence time had focused on development and education. Sir Seshadri Iyer as Diwan was responsible for laying strong foundations for todays status of Bangalore. He helped in setting up the Tata silk farm in 1896 in Bangalore. He was also responsible for the shivasamudra hydro power plant set up in 1903 supplying motive power to KGF and power for lighting to Bangalore- the first in Asia. His third act was the quick response and assistance to Tata to set up IISc in 1909. At that time, India, as a whole, had some ideas of surveying and civil engineering  but no idea about electrical engineering. These acts gave a great direction and primacy for Bangalore. From that time, the Diwan's focused on the development of the state. Sir MV constructed the KRS dam and set up the steel plant in 1937. Then came HAL and BEL and ITI which made Bangalore a tech city by 1950s.
 So GVK Rao's setting up the development commissioner post is not a surprise.I had met him in the early seventies for discussions on pricing the cost of electrical energy from Sharavathy Valley hydel project to Karnataka. I also met him when he was the chief secretary in 1975 -76 period, during the initial operational period of KSCST. 
By the 70s, Bangalore was moving from electronics to computers.
 The first reason for the industry culture is historical support. The second reason was the curious and cooperative nature of the local people.
But the most important aspect is the development of scientific culture of the people and city. We saw several electrical, chemical and electronics industries coming up at periodic intervals in Bangalore building an early entrepreneurial culture. This was complemented with development of many R and D labs in Bangalore- there were more than 60 by 1980.
I had discussions with PR Nayak several times on renewable energy, tree plantations and soil enrichment. We traveled to Bijapur to attend a two days workshop for the DRDO chiefs and district agriculture officers of all districts. He evinced keen interest in the project, asked many questions, did quick calculations on cost, revenue etc. and gave some suggestions. His participation was useful.
Greetings. Dks

"For years now I have sought enlightenment,” he said. “I feel I am very close now, but I want to know what my next step should be.”“How do you earn your living?” asked the master.“I’ve never yet had to earn my living, because my mother and father have always supported me, but that’s a mere detail.”“Your next step is to look at the sun for half a minute,” said the master.The disciple obeyed.When he had done as he was told, he was asked by the master to describe the countryside around him.“I can’t see a thing, because the sun blinded me,” said the disciple.“A man who seeks only the light and hands all his responsibilities to others will never find enlightenment. A man who keeps his eyes fixed only on the sun will end up blind,” said the master."
Maktub    Paulo Coelho 
I met many more officers and CEOs.  I got quite a lot of knowledge about the mindset of people - mild, duty conscious, knowledgeable, -, and the IAS- highly motivated, deep thinking, knowing a lot about the state, reading a lot, highly focused about the local problems in firefighting mode and working for personal growth and success-, and the culture of the state- mostly drought prone, agriculture based, mostly mild, active, maintaining their traditions , holding on to values etc. Actually, I don't want to typecast. But a sizable number of people follow that pattern. 
But the state is diverse. So the attitudes of people vary. Bangalore is at one end. North karnataka- Bijapur, Gulbarga- at the other end.
Somewhere I read -was it CS Lewis? -that when we read fiction, it enlarges our reality. We get new and expanded reality.That was true for me as a child. I gained a lot of knowledge about the different societies in India, different places ,cities and villages, different people and their behaviors, independence movement, middle class lives etc. It was a great journey starting with kalki- alaiosai,thyagabhoomi, ponniyin selvan etc- and going through hundreds of authors, I learnt a kaleidoscopic picture of India first and later about the west through Wodehouse, Arthur Canon Doyle, etc.
As I said before, the correct and current information that I got was from listening to discussions by people. I learnt about life,living, habits, values,motivation,positiveness,courage from family.I got a lot of knowledge about politics, about neighboring towns, about the behaviors of people,about temples and functions. Through observation ,I learnt about agriculture operations,money, banking,purchases, organized activities etc. So my world knowledge came through all these sources and little from the school.
But now I got a much deeper, much wider and correct exposure to Karnataka, its people and culture through my extensive visits, discussions and interactions with hundreds of people and readings. 
But there is a big difference in gathering knowledge from reading a book and from visits and discussions. Here I learn by observing, by focused questioning - what, why and how, difficulties- and getting clarifications,by analyzing the data and responses from several sources. The effects of such learning are
1. We know the context in detail. This means that we have a better, detailed picture of the topic or problem we are looking at. Book information is static, and may not be clear. Text books are cryptic. Manuals are not readable in many cases.
2. The learning is dynamic. We ask questions and get in-depth understanding. We can get more details,we can clarify our doubts. So we build a good picture. Analysis will be meaningful.
3. There are several sources and we can get reasonably accurate data and explanations.
Let me give one example. We started the tree cultivation program in a big way. We promoted one species - subabul-, as it was fast growing and provided leaf manure. Our discussions revealed that our tree planting worked in some places and not in others. Discussions on operations with many foresters didn't reveal any problem. But we found Bijapur district successfully implemented the scheme and lakhs of plants were growing fast without failures.
So we went and after hours of discussions and several field visits found out a simple fact that the farmers pre treated the seeds with Rhizome before planting and that was responsible for the success. So we conducted a two-day workshop for all districts in charge. We moved from foresters to agricultural officers. Our learning came from experiments ,expertise and discussions.
From 1980 onwards, Karnataka occupied a prominent place in my activities. I started my involvement with the problems of Karnataka with KEB in early seventies, and with ASTRA and KSCST in mid seventies  and now it moved to a higher level.
Greetings. Dks
13/02/26, 4:57 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 985
Mr Debashis has two comments. The first one:
"This part gives a clear picture of how computerisation progressed and involvement of  computer based infrastructure. It addresses the journey of computer systems that our respected Mr. Kohli once wrote down. I have an old writing pad of Mr. Kohli where he scribbled his thoughts on transformative path of computer infrastructure over twenty years or so. Thank you , Sir for your wisdom full texts. We need to organise these all text in a collective form which not only covers technology but it captures facts on society , life , environment , healthcare etc across the globe. "
My response
This comment looks at my mindset. While I did specialise many times over- first electrical engineering, then power, then computers and after that energy, I realised the main concept in Shikshavalli section of  taitreya upanishad early in my life. I was introduced to Shikshavalli recitation as a child even before I knew the word science. I knew mathematics. But I understood organization, sociology, ethics, human behavior much better than science. 
The Shikshavalli talks about connections between parts at three to four levels. It talks about connecting the connections or a central control. I didn't comprehend it at that age.But, when you see the connections of abstract concepts repeated with several examples, it forms an impression in your mind and that creates a major impact. So when I saw the first example - family connections, village connections. That brought a question - why do people have a bond with each other? This is easy to answer- safety, security, survival,mutual help, cooperation etc. The second question is deeper- how do they bond, how a society remains stable for a long time amidst warfare, destructions, natural calamities, migrations, petty minds, etc?
I was really curious and searching for answers. I looked at the second question throughout my life and found many nuances to answers.
Science concentrates on parts and doesn't look at connections. Flows are important.Ecology looked at water cycle, carbon cycle etc trying to knit different groups to produce a dynamic understanding. Roger Penrose has removed the mystery. There are no particles. Look at everything as fields. Fields are continuous. So light ,electricity, magnetism are fields. So it proves that quantum fields show the existence of through connections.
The area IT is a connective network. It needed architecture. But IT is related to business. FC Kohli understood it. Like me, he was also concerned about rural problems. He wanted to do something about agriculture. He asked me about it even in the year-2006 or 7. I found economy is linked with many aspects- manufacturing, food production, energy, ecology etc. To get a holistic picture, one has to look at the connections. Hence my posts.
My grandson,Neel, is a polyglot. He started with animals, moved to insects and to ecology and to history. His interests are deeply into many disciplines. He is passionate about animals that he turned into a vegan.
Debashis second comment:
"Outstanding!! I wonder how can still remember names of so many people you have interacted with."
My response
I was worried, when I started writing my biography at the request of my grandson,Vishak, whether I have enough to sustain writing about the several thousand incidents that happened to me professionally and socially and the changes happening in technology, society, politics and lifestyles.Initially, I was blank.I destroyed my diaries and all my papers. I didn't remember many things. But as I started writing, my memory opened up. This may be true for all of us. Focus, concentration, contemplation and constant thinking helped me in remembering the events and people.Many incidents reappeared in my memory and then came to the posts.
Greetings. Dks

Nagaraja ,ex Kirloskar
Good morning sir. We, who are living in this century are a witness to two great realities/challenges: one,Natural Stupidity  & the other is  Artificial Intelligence.I don't know whether my observation is correct and needs serious attention for the well-being of this world. Pl throw some light when you wish to change from your present line of thoughts . Thanks with regards. Greetings: HSN
My response
I want to contradict your assumption on natural stupidity. 
First, who are we,Indians?
This question can be answered by looking at the post independence period- that is also my period of observations and analysis.
There is no single typecast. I will classify not people but characteristics of the core group of people. We have two core groups- doers and policy makers or government people. Let me look at the core group - doers. The country is running because of them. This group has many sub groups:
Mechanical workers
Thinkers and teachers
Leaders- strategists and system designers and builders.
Our country depends on the leaders. 
Our leaders have the following characteristics to varying degrees. 
1.Studious, observant, deep learning
2.Questioning and curiosity,
3. Logical reasoning, mathematical precision and analysis
4.Flexibility, resilience and adaptability to change
5. Culture- social, religious,
6.Value system- family, truth, honesty, helping tendency,
The country runs because of these qualities present to a sufficient depth in a reasonable- critical size- number of people. These qualities allow the people in the group to work in different dimensions What is interesting is that this group size is not constant, but steadily increased over the years.
It is a steady improvement of our people that helps in our development, growth and stability. Let us then ask-how Indians improved over this period?
There has been multidimensional approaches by many agencies that allowed us to improve.
Government focused on providing education to most people at affordable rates. NGOs helped children to get nutritious food.
People realized that they should change their living practices and provided good education to their children. This change is a game changer for India. Today we produce the largest number of engineers every year in the world. 
People started another change- religious change. You can't go to a major temple today for peace. You find lakhs going to temple,lakhs following Samskara and sadachara and lakhs aspiring to do more, to help others and to come up in life. 
That transformation over several decades of people in many villages and cities was called sanskritization by Prof MN Srinivas. I call it brahminization. We accept scholarship, learning, meditation, yoga, prayer etc as important and desirable characteristics of our life.
So people are moving up the learning and leadership ladder. They will educate others and try to reduce the stupidity of going totally towards a materialistic way.
But this transformation reduced the pride of artisan's skills. Actually, jnana and artisan skills are like mathematics and physics. Both have to be balanced. They are not, and that is our problem. May be we will have robotic artisans.
Similarly, our resilience will help us go through the AI wave.
Greetings. Dks
15/02/26, 4:34 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 987
Let me return to my life paths. I had bought a site in West of chord road second stage, about two to three kilometers distant from my residence in Malleswaram.Mr Thimme Gowda, who was living close to me in Subramanya Nagar and working at the electrical inspectorate, told me to construct a house on my site. He had a mason who was good and reliable. He said that he would help me in the construction. I thought about it and said ok to take up the house construction without a contractor. At that time, I didn't know the problems that I would be facing in getting materials like cement, steel, bricks etc and problems with workers and theft. It was a foolhardy activity. But I agreed. 
There were two immediate requirements to start the work. The first was the preparation of a building plan and getting it approved by BDA. I defined our feasible requirements first- two bed room, a separate drawing room and dining room, kitchen etc.I decided on a single storey construction with staircase and a room upstairs. I decided on a big garage, a sump for water storage.After several trials, I made a draft plan. 
I was helped by PSTI in preparing the plan. Mr Magudapathy took care of the plan preparation.The draftsman there was very helpful. Then I prepared a detailed estimate,again a major exercise. I submitted these to BDA. Fortunately the Engineer in charge was related to Thimme Gowda and I got approval fast. 
The next step was more tedius- getting a loan for the construction. I had very little money. Fortunately,HDFC opened a branch in Bangalore at that time. I applied for a loan giving the documents. I had to go several times to the office. The manager was harassing me. I discussed it with Mr GV Anantharamiah,director,PSTI. I was lucky. He knew the chief of the HDFC branch.They played badminton together.So he talked to the chief and I got the loan sanctioned.Even private companies need recommendation to do routine things. The interest rate was very high, about 15%. I had to pay about rupees thousand as EMI, my salary being a little more than 2000 rupees per month.DSA was still low at that time. So life was tight financially.So I needed a tight fiscal control in construction. That was impossible as costs were escalating every day.
Now, I had to get the materials ready. I listed the activities and sequences. We employed the mason, whom Mr Gowda knew. We leveled the ground making it ready for house construction.
The state government had a bricks factory in Hoskote, near Bangalore. I talked to Mr Ashwatnarayana, chief engineer, PWD. He talked to the factory. I made the payment, arranged a truck to transport bricks to my site. We could get sand from Yeshwantpur and it was easy. Mr Ashwatnarayana became a friend for more than two decades and I had a lot of interactions with him when he was the MD of KPC.
Now another major problem - cement - arose.It was scarce and the market price was high. The government in those days gave us permits for buying cement at a controlled price, if one deposited dollars. My cousin Jayanthi's husband, Subramanian, was  an officer in the UCO bank. I kept about 1000 dollars that I got as reimbursement of my travel to Boston in 1978.So I opened an account in his branch and deposited the dollars. I applied for control cement and got a truck load of cement. It was not an easy decision. Mr Gowda preferred Sankar cement, but we got Birla cement from Ammapet.
Since many mini steel plants came up in Karnataka and even in Bangalore, steel was available in the market and I could buy them directly. I needed a structural design for the first floor roof. I approached Dr Venkatram Reddy, a faculty at IISc and a student of Prof KS Jagadish. He did the design.
Now we needed wooden structures for doors, windows, wardrobes etc. Again Mr Gowda came to my rescue. He knew Major Somasekhar, who was the GM of a wood working workshop. We discussed with Major and finalized the teak wood component. It was very expensive- almost 25% of the total cost. Still the quality was good ,manufactured in a factory. So I agreed. Major Somasekhar lived close to my site, just a few houses away. He started taking interest in the construction as he was a civil engineer. He again became a great friend and that continued for decades. He did all my furniture work afterwards.
So I kickstarted my house construction, now supported by many. Mr Anagol asked me whether I would use mud blocks instead of bricks. I said no. I had discussed it and found it difficult to implement. I didn't have the time to execute it. Instead  I decided to build the compound wall in mud.
My daily routine changed. I started the day by 8am , visited the site with Mr Gowda, then go yo IISc and later to Vidhana Soudha. In between ,I had to go to HDFC, banks, purchases, etc- a tough set of tasks.
The construction took more than a year.Let me continue to discuss it in the next post.
Greetings. Dks

16/02/26, 4:21 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 988
I visited the site almost every day. I would get ready by 8am, and went to Mr Gowda's house. Then I went to IISc. Initially I went by car, but later it was by auto. He went to his office. This routine was going on satisfactorily and the progress was steady. Quite a number of days, Major joined us and gave a lot of suggestions. I remember two suggestions. He said that alignment of the door frame area in a wall was poor. I saw it quickly. The second was a major change. He quickly redesigned the roof steel structure. That showed the difference between theory and experience. 
We decided on a white mosaic floor for the entire house except the kitchen and bath rooms.That appealed to me when I saw it in the Parijatha tiles factory in Dr Rajkumar road. That added to the cost of construction.
We ordered a granite single platform for the kitchen with a grinding stone and a washbasin carved in it.The walls in kitchen and bath rooms had glazed tiles. 
I found there were many hurdles and difficulties in house construction then in 1981-82. It is more so now. The major problems are:
1.Supply chain management in an unorganized sector. There was a lot of uncertainty leading to delays.
2.Manpower management and coordination in the last stage between the electrical contractor, the plumber, the painter, mosaic floor polishing,
I gave the electrical contract to my friend, Mahantesh from CPRI.
3.The third was cost control. This is a dangerous domain. My estimate was a little more than the loan amount. At the same time, I didn't want the quality to come down, long term durability maintained and aesthetics taken care of. So we went for teak, granite kitchen platform, high cost paints etc. I was fortunate to have maintained the cost nearer the estimate. I had no extra funds available. So it was not a simple task towards the end. It was chaos.
I had a lot of support from many. My new friend, Sankarasubramanian, also visited the site a couple of times, and gave me some steel panels for the railings on the first floor saying that I shouldn't buy them at that time.
I had difficulties in getting connections for water and power. Dr Satyanarayana, who worked with me on the water project and knew the engineers in BWSSB, helped in getting both water and sanitary connections. There was a considerable delay in getting the electricity connection. I talked Mr Rudrappa, Chief Engineer,KEB. He immediately ordered the concerned division in charge to give connection. The mechanic, who came to make the connection, was grumbling. He was unhappy. While most connections were DC- direct connections without meters as there was a shortage of energy meter-, I got a metered connection.
By the beginning of 1983, the house was completed. I organized a house warming ceremony in march 83. 
My cousin D R Sundaram had moved recently in 1982, attended the ceremony. My father attended the function and left by 12 noon. Most of my colleagues from the department came for lunch. GK admired the construction and appreciated the house.
So that episode was over and it taught me a new way of handling uncertainties and urban reality. 
Greetings. Dks
17/02/26, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 989
Let me return to my government job.During this period, the government decided to give a parcel of land in the new BEL road side to ISRO to set up a head quarters. I attended the meeting. I met Mr A Bharat, BDA commissioner for the first time in that meeting. He said that he knew about me. It seems his son attended a public lecture on computers given by me in Institute of World culture,BP Wadia road and was inspired by it to go for computer science specialization.
There was some land left out in that area- close to Sanjay Nagar. I talked to Prof Ramaseshan and Mr Veerappa Moily after a KSCST exe-com meeting about the land availability and the institute to ask for it. That land was given to IISc and today we have our faculty quarters there and it is called vigyan nagar. I was happy that I could do this for my Institute.
All these days ,I talked about understanding the world as a major purpose in our life. We call this understanding generated by each of us a reality. A reality world is built based on our learnings, our perceptions, beliefs and dogmas. This leads me to two questions:
What is one's world? I don't use the world as a normally accepted term. The world I mean here refers to one's reality. This world is the knowledge one acquires throughout his life in multiple areas, disciplines and dimensions. The first clarity is that this reality is not absolute, it is relative, dynamic and transient and may contain errors and wrong interpretations- rope perceived as a snake.
The second question is more complex. How does one comprehend the world of reality? Schopenhauer feels that we can conceive a world as ideas or as will.  You don't use the constructs of science, but that of philosophy. Because, we need tools to understand. Language, philosophy and science give us tools to help us to classify and group every idea into a box. For instance, science talks of matter and waves. Human science talks of body and mind and Hinduism goes into more groupings called panchakosa or thattvas. These constructs and mechanisms help us to store, retrieve, comprehend and apply what we learnt.
Even with the same person, reality changes depending on moods. A person is a communist one day and a rightist the next day; a person is a secular person one day, atheist another day and a religious person next day; a person is a Gandhian one day, becomes a fan of technology and so on. You see the transient nature of our individual reality.
Each one has some level - breadth and depth- and some understanding of a reality. If you sum up all these realities, you get a reasonably large universe. That is what GPT is doing now. It collects its reality from millions of sites to answer a question. Let me see how the reality manifests in decision making with an example.
We come to the end of 1982. I had spent nearly a year in the government. I was sitting in the vidhana soudha office on Oct 27th,1982 and started contemplating about the government job. 
My taking up the government job had many mixed reviews. Some felt that I had a good job. Even Ramaseshan felt that the job had potential to grow. Some others like Prof MS Naidu of HV department felt that I was not doing the correct thing. Some felt that I was wasting my time.
Keeping all these in mind, I got an urge to decide on this day - what would be the next step. I had completed a year in the government.My heart has always been in academics. I felt that I had reached a saturation level in the government job. I am impatient and wants things to happen, expect fast response from students ,don't want to wait in queues, or in the health centre and want to get things completed quickly and forget about it- BL Deekshatulu used to say - get rid of it. I am very unhappy if things hang on for a long time. Despite my best tries, I couldn't change this characteristic of mine even though I tell myself - why the hurry? Wait and have patience. What will I do after this activity? Sit at home and relax. Think of relaxation here. It doesn't work. I read or write even when I wait for something or travel by bus. Still, the impatience persists in me. I found that there seemed a lot of difference between my speed and the government's speed. IISc was my place of happiness. So I informed Mr Anagol that I would like to return back to IISc. So I returned on Oct 28,1982 back to IISc.
While I returned to IISc, I didn't delink myself from activities of the state government. I was associated with various activities of the government till my retirement from IISc in 2004.
Greetings. Dks

Anonymous quote
Prof Karunakara:
"Sir,We thank God for making you come back to academics. Otherwise, we could have missed an excellent academician who influenced thousands of peoples in their walk directly or indirectly and cherished in their life. Thanks and regards"
Thanks.
Let me give my reflections of this one year's activities with the state government. The reason is to address a doubt- was it a waste? Was it a crime for an academic to waste his years with such non academic activities?
First, I learnt a lot. I got adjusted to my job very fast from day one. I devised my own processes for both departments. I read a number of books on the environment and saw many real life experiments. The most important learning I had is:
People, including politicians, officers and employees, are good and helpful.
I remember two statements by Mr Anagol:
"Better should not stand in the way of good. "
I may not fully endorse this statement as I feel that it leads to mediocrity and not excellence.He was looking at mediocrity- government- and I excellence- academics - two differing poles. My belief is that if you aim for a higher goal, you may reach a middle to high level. If you aim for a high goal, you may end up at a low level,and feel satisfied and happy. But it gives us another dimension that you shouldn't aim for perfection. In the game for perfection, you don't do anything.
Secondly,people think that a higher goal is not achievable and keep quiet without doing anything. I have seen a large number of people with this attitude.
When I joined the Institute, the research culture was just building up in the engineering departments. So there were two groups- one keen on research, another of older people who felt that they were beyond the age for research.It is a belief. Later in the eighties,I saw several such non PhDs working seriously on research problems to get a PhD and many got PhD.
There was a faculty who said that we shouldn't do research in engineering as we don't have the necessary facilities, we are not Einsteins and we won't do great discoveries that will change our life. So he concentrated on teaching. Even in teaching he had ideas of some level of perfection and failed all students in a class leading to a fight with the head. He left IISc and joined IIT Bombay as a lecturer and continued as a lecturer. His concept may be ok for him personally, but it will not help in the education of students. 
But let me quote another person, who wasn't a great researcher. Prof BNN Iyengar used to say that research is not to come up with great and new discoveries like Newton. Research creates an in depth understanding of an area and awareness of current happenings in that area and this will help in making you a better teacher. Teaching and research are interlinked. I liked this concept.
That was the reason Prof Dhawan came up with the faculty contracts and promotions scheme. It spurred research work at IISc. Most old timers were sidelined.
The second comment by Mr Anagol relates to people. He was a great optimist. We used to discuss the capabilities and actions of different people and how they will handle their jobs. He was good at this. I recall one instance. There was a big delay in executing the cauvery water supply scheme for Bangalore city as the scheduled completion was nearing and most works were pending. He brought in Mr Gulam Mohammed as the chief engineer and he completed the project with good speed and efficiency.
At that time, there was a feeling that Congress would loose the coming elections, and Janata dal might win. He said whoever comes will have a self-interest for survival and so will do better. We needn't worry.As we now know that Ramakrishna Hegde came in as the next CM and the rule was very good with lots of new and fresh ideas and initiatives. But Anagol told him to appoint a person of his choice as secretary to CM. He withdrew from that job. He never underestimated people. I liked this character of him.
This stay in government changed my purpose. Earlier, It was to impart knowledge and wisdom to students and engineers. Now it got enlarged to help society by applying my knowledge to solve their problems- a big focus now on social problem solving and not just technology alone.
Greetings. Dks

As soon as I returned back to IISc, my plate of work started filling up fast. On the personal side, two major tasks were going on. My house construction work is continuing and might take a year to complete.
I decided that my children should learn to swim. So I took them at 7 am every day to the swimming pool at IISc. We normally walked to the pool and while returning, we sometimes got an auto to reach home. My children will be hungry. This training went on for more than a month. So we spent their summer mornings learning to swim. Then at 830 am, I went to Mr Thimme Gowda's house and then to the site.
At the department level, I saw a big need from me. I had already structured and started a ME CSE program and a BE CSE program in the department. We separated control systems discipline into a separate ME program- called system science and automation- to be run jointly with the EE department. Computer science activity was peaking in IITs in India.
I started teaching two courses in each term. I started new courses on data structures and operating systems in one term and databases in another term.My course registration was one of the highest, next to the Math courses. I got students from SA,EE and ECE departments. My research activity also increased. So my focus in the department was on computer science areas. I was also taking care of the hybrid computer facility. I decided to modernize it.
ASTRA activities shifted to a different mode. Most faculty from other departments reduced their activities in ASTRA. I moved out of ASTRA and AKN also moved away from ASTRA. Prof KS Jagadish was still busy with ASTRA and became its convener. The one major project of interest was the development of ASTRA ole by prof Kumar and Lokras. It was a big achievement. They could demonstrate the high efficiency stove for cooking. It was taken over by KSCST for propagation with a total commitment from the state government.
I became involved with Madhav and signed a proposal to the Ministry of Environment to set up the centre for ecological sciences. I also got involved with various activities of CES and started visiting Sirsi to set up the field station and to recruit staff.
Prof S Ramaseshan wasn't happy with the operation of the computer centre. So he set up a fact finding committee with three members- Prof Vijayan, Dr KS Prabhu from ISRO and myself. Dr Prabhu excused himself. So Vijayan and I sat together, went through the situation and prepared a report suggesting reorganizing the centre. So Prof Ramaseshan set up an executive committee with powers to run the centre. Prof ES Rajagopal was made the chair and I became a prominent member. Prof ESR consulted me on all decisions. We became close. Dr. A A Shamim, earlier head of the centre, was upset. I visited TIFR along with R Krishnamurthy- we had faith only in his capabilities and discounted others as per the instructions of the director. We had lengthy discussions with Mr Sadanandan on improvements on operations.I put two students to simplify computer accounting and that reduced connect time to zero and computer time to less than an hour for completing accounts for a month. We automated slot booking and did more changes in operations.

20/02/26, 4:24 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 992
KSCST was transforming. It initially focused on looking at the problems faced in the state and created working groups to solve the problems. This concept of a working group was a great idea. I was part of the karnataka electricity working group started in 1975 or 76. A working group consisted of investigators,government officers and supporting people like economists, statisticians etc. It permitted collaborative work, improved understanding between members, facilitated data collection and helped in interpretation. There was great synergy. This concept of working group got reduced over a period of time.
 Then came the focus on the project work of engineering college students and KSCST came up with an SPP- students project program. It is continuing even now.It has become the flagship program now.
In order to help in analysing the problems and supporting the working group, we introduced systems analysts. The initial two- Mr NV Krishna and Mr Amarnath Bhat - were good perceptive, interested and enthusiastic persons and they worked tirelessly. But that was also short lived. The later crop were rule based people. I still remember the statement of Mr Guha - "KSCST is losing its scientific strength. It is becoming bureaucratic, and one day Mr Panduranga, admin in charge, will be the most important person in KSCST." I selected many and interacted with them. Most became dissatisfied and the post vanished.
I see that KSCST and DST as well as CSIR all move towards bureaucratic rule based governance and ignore science and technology. The biggest reason is the loss of associations with younger minds. All such organizations should have a few core staff and others should be interns or students.
Even solar, wind and bio gas projects got reduced over a period of time.Fortunately,KSCST had a great luck when Prof Kumar and Lokras designed, built ,experimented and produced a working model of a stove. The propagation became a big job and it was funded by the government and executed by KSCST.
Let me talk about another major project that came up in 1982 and I was involved in it. That is studies on various services in Bangalore.
Let me look at the scene in Bangalore in early 1982. The city was expanding in all directions and population was growing at the highest rate. But supply systems and infrastructure were not moving at a similar pace. Our bus system was notorious for non availability and great inefficiency. Electricity supply was leading to regular power cuts.Most commodities came from outside. Even milk supply was erratic. The education system- schools designed for the sixties- couldn't meet the needs of the city. So KSCST decided to take up the supply issues of various items to the city. Prof AKN and Vinod became the conveners. This marked the departure of AKN from rural focused problems to urban problems. I felt this was a very important and urgent project. I didn't get the sectors of my expertise- energy, industries, but got water. So I started another of my socially relevant engineering studies. It was exciting for me. I spent the next three plus years on the Bangalore water supply systems project. I had project assistants helping in collecting data.This was a very satisfying project for me. I will explain it later in details.
Greetings. Dks

One more clarification on the quote by Dr Sanjay Chitnis:
The WATCH quote seems to have a basis from: "You are what your deep, driving desire is. As your desire is, so is your will. As your will is, so is your deed. As your deed is, so is your destiny." – Brihadaranyaka Upanishad IV. 4.5
Mr Sethu Rao was the conscience keeper of KSCST, a person who believed in doing research and development to help the downtrodden, poor and remote areas of the state. He also had a great love for the Kannada language. He wanted to popularise both Kannada and science to children.
So he started a society called Karnataka Rajya Vijnana Parishad. It ran a magazine called Balavijnana. My association with Sethu Rao was quite deep. He lived close by and I met him almost every day. He was a very stable, mature and sober person. I got a lot of enrichment about kannada literature through him. I met many famous writers. I participated in the annual KRVP meetings and delivered talks in Kannada.I also visited several places in the state to inaugurate KRVP shakas and deliver lectures.One project I funded from the state government was on Banamathi- study and education to remove the fear of the people. We got books printed and distributed them to all schools and libraries in the Gulbarga division.
Language creates passions and fights. But I accepted language as a medium of communications. Actually my love for Kannada started from 1965 onwards. I always loved learning a new language from childhood. I learnt Tamil and Sanskrit as a child and then picked up English and Hindi in school. During the school days,I had a neighbor who was a kannidiga and I picked a few words from him. A teacher was also a kannadiga. 
My father had some knowledge of Telugu. I picked up a few words of Telugu during my college days.
When I joined the institute in 1965, my class was a highly mixed one almost one per state. So we had vigorous discussions on languages during the break times. I started learning Kannada from my class mates- particularly Srinivasan- and hostel staff. Mess was populated with Malayali staff. Mr GP Rajarathnam gave a talk in kannada in 1965. That motivated me. 
My close associations with Prof AR Vasudeva murthy, Dr Mahishi  and others spurred my interest in kannada. I started learning Kannada alphabets in 1971. I knew the children of many Kannada poets like Puthina, kuvempu, DVG, Gorur,etc. I interacted with Shivarama karanth,Da ra Bendre, Ha Ma Nayak.

22/02/26, 4:39 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post  994
Let me look at my involvement with schools. It was a long time involvement and interest in helping schools.
That brings me to another activity we initiated in the Institute. Quite a good number of faculty in IISc were involved in improving school education. Some used to help in the development of text books. A group was training school teachers on science and mathematics topics. I and Prof MR Chidambara were involved in the high school teachers training program. Prof SV Subrahmanyam was very passionate about schools and teaching. So we decided to visit schools in various parts of Karnataka and give talks in Kannada in schools to students of 8th to 12th classes. It was called UGC extension lecture program. I was involved in this, visited schools in several places and gave talks on energy and computers. This activity continued till 2000. 
The directors, Prof Dhawan, Ramaseshan and later Prof CNR Rao supported this program. Its secretariat was located in the centre for continuing education.We produced a booklet every year containing a list of lectures and sent it to all schools and later colleges in the state.Actually after SVS, I became the convener of this program from early 1990 onwards. It was a very satisfying work for me,gave me a better picture of the state of schools in villages. I saw the excitement of children and their ignorance. 
Let me look at a few instances. I prepared a lot of slides to talk about computers. We bought a portable slide projector and carried it to schools. We had to travel to most places by bus. 
Let me give a few incidents. 
I visited Sirsi to give a talk on computers. I fell sick and was vomiting. Madhav was there and he was wondering whether I could manage to talk. We went to the college. My vomiting stopped when I started talking. My jokes were liked by Madhav. After the talk, I was fine.
I went to a high school in Nittur. The facilities were poor.  They had only small class rooms. We tried to talk to a nearby cinema theatre, but they refused to give the hall to us.The head master wanted me to talk to all classes. So I gave the talk four times. The children had no knowledge of even common English words like decimal or number. So I struggled to translate. But the pictures in the slides helped a lot.
Prof KI Vasu went as director CECRI, Karaikudi and called me to give a series of lectures to his officers. Similarly, Prof A Prabhakar went as Principal to MIT Manipal. He called me under this scheme to give a lecture to his students. Later I taught a few computer courses to their newly introduced computer science MTech students. But I had visited MIT several times before.
I visited several parts of Karnataka under this program.
More in the next post.

23/02/26, 4:41 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 995
My travels increased mostly to small towns in Karnataka. I had contacts with the NSS coordinators of universities and schools. So I was invited for lectures on environment in many NSN camps. I had a good friendship with Capt Umapathy, Mr Bhide, Mr Dilshad, Mr Narasimhiah, Mr Somasekhara and others. I visited several places in Karnataka and addressed NSS camps. I went to a camp at KD Gudi, a forest area without electricity in the BR hills, a great experience.I even visited camps in villages in Bangalore and Bijapur districts. All travels had to be performed by buses of KSRTC as the train connectivity was poor.I took my children to adichunchunagiri for a camp. 
I started advising Mr Sankarasubramanian in propagating his priagni stove across Karnataka. He organized a camp at Kemmannagundi and I took my children to the camp. They got good exposure to forest areas.
I got involved with Madhav in CES. I did a day's trekking in western ghats. I visited Sirsi, Kumta and nearby places. Some places didn't have toilet facilities.We recruited several persons for our research station and for CES at Bangalore.
My involvement with KSCST also increased. I should talk about my experience with Mr Jayadev. He was given charge of the SPP by KSCST. He literally harassed me ,with good intentions no doubt, with all matters relating to SPP. He was sincere. He took me to many colleges for evaluation. He started visiting my house at 7am when we were busy preparing to send the children to school. They ran away from him. Prof Dhawan also talked to me about him. He had a purposeless wandering life, a tragedy.
I started my project on Bangalore water supply in earnest. I had Dr Satyanarayana, a scientific officer, working with me. I put him on the data collection job. He went to all service stations of BWSSB and collected consumption data. 
During that period I also guided a BE projects by a batch of four students. The project was on energy survey. The students were energetic and hard working. I liked their enthusiasm. They met me and I wanted more data. They weren't disappointed, but went with more enthusiasm. I liked their approach. 
After they completed their BE, I decided to give them jobs. I took two of them - Ashok kumar Kattimani and Ramakrishna- and put them in the water project. They worked for about three years on that project. They meticulously collected data. They also pulled out a lot of information from the government archives.They worked hard.
Madhav felt that CES needed a person for working on energy. So I picked Mr Ramachandra, another student in that project and we appointed him in a permanent post in CES. He continued to work with me for two decades. He is still with CES.

Thanks to all who read it and commented on it.
Let me look at a question that was posed to me by many. Why did Bangalore develop faster and become a leader in electronics and IT?
To me the reasons were many.
1. Culture- compared to other cities in India, the culture here is different. The city consisted of mostly white collar employees. So a great degree of true middle class intellectualism prevailed here. It was a tolerant and unbiased intellectualism compared to another white collar dominant city, Delhi. Delhi intellectualism was political, leftist and elitist.Delhi ignored the middle class. Secondly, Bangalore didn't have a babu culture as seen in Delhi. A good number of people were great and free thinkers. There was a tolerance of castes and communities in those days. People from several communities participated and excelled in the intellectual pursuit. Chennai had a rigid community segregation and that wasn't seen here.
Bangalore welcomed migration.True there are hiccups, but people here live and let others live.
2. Another factor of culture- multiculturalism - multiple languages, multiple life styles, multiple food cultures. There was a lot of cross cultural interactions and cross cultural learning. It is this which made Bangalore a unique city in India. Most cities had mono cultures. Both Chennai and Kolkatta had local Tamil and Bengali cultures mostly. Bombay created a mono culture of its own even though most people were migrants. Delhi had a babu culture.
3. Collaborative spirit. People collaborated on projects. This gave a critical mass in many areas.
4. IISc was leading the country. It set up the first electrical technology department in 1909, the first communications engineering department in 1948 and the first aeronautics department in the fifties. So it produced a large tech expertise and research in these areas. 
5. With the setting up of BEL and ITI, Bangalore was way ahead of other cities in Electronics 
6. The city has a strong science and tech culture. Science and tech education, development, testing and research are strong.
Let me look at the decade 1984 to 1994. I consider this as a major inflection point in both computer science and computer usage. This sowed a seed for Bangalore becoming a silicon valley of India. The multi directional synergy was amazing. Actually start up culture didn't start in 2000 plus. It started with one demand in the electronics industry- the dominant industry in Bangalore in the sixties and not known elsewhere in India- soldering automation and circuit automation. This started in 1966 by Hegde and Golay Company and spanned hundreds of small units. That was the almost unglorified startup culture. So Bangalore attacked the computer's arrival in multiple directions. 
Greetings. Dks
25/02/26, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 997
We are seeing the developments in the computer area during the period 1984 to 94. This period saw a big leap in electronics- moving to VLSI technologies. That meant we were no longer talking about transistors, but large circuits and networks having hundreds of thousands of transistors. An entire computer processor came out in a chip. That reduced the size of a computer occupying a big room to a table- desktop- and reducing the power requirements from kilowatts to watts- directly pluggable systems. That opened the flood gate of computer sales and purchases. So many industries started manufacturing these machines- called PCs, personal computers.
The first part 1980 to 86, we had only 8 bit computers. While many companies like Zenith, Uptron etc sold the computer at a high cost, Eiko came up with an affordable low cost model. I introduced these computers for the APMC at Gadag, in Dharwad district. It came with a lot of fan fare and achieved a big purpose- create a correct valuation for computers. All companies fell in with a price range of about 40000 plus rupees for a desktop.
This period was a volatile period. Both Intel and Motorola competed with each other and brought out better and faster processors. Intel moved through 8086, 286,386,486 and Pentium. With mother board, SCSI, bus,ports etc, assembly was easy. MSDOS was the OS, not easy to use. This gave birth to a large number of manufacturers- both branded and assembled.
Unix came at this time. It was a big innovation- a giant step forward.Motorola 68020 was ported with Unix and I was one of the first to buy it and upgraded it with 68030 and later 68040. Client server model was easy to adapt. Computers reached more organizations, even small industries and banks. It was used for typing, storage and calculations.
Main frames were used by big companies. But servers became common and used by all.
Programming moved to C. C became a standard language. Cobol was used even during this period. But both FORTRAN and Pascal vanished from usage. PL/1 was a flop. 
Software engineering reached a mature state. Production of large software became common. Software did not deal with a single problem. It dealt with a large number of problems belonging to an organization.  It helped in automation- connects to other programs, provides connections to communication networks, links to storage, retrieves and loads data into data bases, connects clients and messengers etc. It became a complex generic engine.ERP is one good example of integration of many large applications. 
Data became independent truly. We have got good commercial database systems like Oracle and DB2. I had serious issues in pushing developers to adapt databases in their system design.Developers who resisted databases before, got converted and felt that applications can not be designed without databases.Again ERP brought out this necessity. Data moved out of programs and when we talk of data, it includes the entire organizational data, customer data, data on economy, supply chain data, shareholders data etc. Data became crucial for the success of an organization. That continues even now.
The Education scene also changed during this period.
Greetings. Dks
26/02/26, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 998
Let me look at the application software scene. While TCS was a dominant player in software services, it also branched into several areas like share and dividends processing, banking, accounting, etc. But it had more jobs abroad than in India. Seeing TCS activities, many other companies like Patni,Mafatlal, Computronics etc jumped into the field. Several companies built specialised software for in house use.But the effort required was high. We were using wordstar for typing.
Many companies like ACC, HMT, ITI and HAL started computerization of accounts, stores and purchase. Mr Balan of ACC was passionate about computerization. Most companies outsourced their payroll to reputed software companies. Application Software was custom built. Standardization was absent. Software practices were poor. I saw a senior person correcting codes and was aghast. Version management was not understood and hence not implemented. Even in TCS, documentation was not attempted. Software remained in the minds of the programmers. Software as an asset came much later. Software as a product was ignored by many and only a few built software products like Tally, word processors for Kannada, EX, etc came later. Software development remained a tinkering activity even in 2000.
Organizational rigidity and poor understanding of the market and service requirements, trust deficits continued to kill many services companies started by reputed companies. Even TVS and Kirloskar failed in this area. Agility was not understood by many. Novices like Infosys succeeded as they had no baggage.
During this period, most companies concentrated on private sector applications. Many private sector- big and medium ones- companies experimented with computers to solve their problems. Most used the outsourcing model.
The government also got into the software business. With Rajiv Gandhi becoming PM in 1984, computers got a favorable treatment from him. NIC which was set up earlier as part of planning played a big role. NIC recruited a lot of engineers ,trained them in programming and worked intensely in developing applications for the government sector. NIC set up units in all states to assist state departments in computerization.
While NIC did a great job in initiating computerization , it couldn't take it forward. The reasons were:
Formalism was not known to NIC. Most programmers worked in adhoc mode. So they were tinkering with applications. Users were not happy. But NIC was nimble, and helpful to the users and understood the reality better.
Power was diffused. NIC had no control with state governments and so had a limited role in implementation.
Centralised control and pressure of output didn't help NIC. NIC never thought of producing software as a product. 
CMC which was set up as a maintenance company also moved into software applications and hardware sales. They also focused on government jobs.
Banks were not using computers for bank work. They were using PCs for office work. RBI wanted banks to use computers for banking. So a committee under Dr Rangarajan recommended branch automation.So some banks moved into computerization only by early nineties with branch automation. I assisted Canara bank with branch automation. Fortunately, ECIL, Infosys and a few others developed a Cobol based software suitable for Indian banks and many banks propagated the software across a good number of branches. Banks built an in house IT department. The more important event was banks going full steam on Cobol training. Some banks trained a few hundred officers on programming. This helped banks later when banks moved to core banking.
The nineties saw computerization picking up a good pace. Most organizations , government departments,R and D labs and engineering colleges purchased computers- PCs and servers and tried to build some applications. The awareness was wide spread. 
Networking by the early nineties increased. Many organizations built a LAN. I designed LAN for Sri Jayadeva institute of cardiology, CPRI, Vidhana Soudha, KPC, and many organizations. I bought computers for many engineering colleges, KREC,and helped in the design of campus wide networking with fibre backbone. Syndicate bank and SBI went for a wide area network provided by BSNL and built a full fledged IT solution.
Details in the next post
Greetings. Dks
27/02/26, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 999
So by 2000, we had a networking system operational. Also by 1995, we had our email system ready. The department of electronics, government of India, funded a major project involving IISc, TIFR and all IITs to set up a global network for emails. ERNET, as it was called then became operational by 1995.Also by 2000, internet became operational. SBI was the first bank to operationalise a full fledged large, networked IT system, developed, designed ,built and operated by TCS- the first major working IT project in the country. It was a great achievement. 
For the first time, we proved to the world that we can do it. There was great skepticism in India and the world that we could not do a major multidisciplinary project involving many latest technologies interconnecting many software pieces and networking thousands of computers. I was even mocked at when I said that a wide area network is feasible in India. Even Dr. Seshagiri,DG,NIC didn't believe me. Mr S S Murthy who was the boss of power systems in CEA questioned me on this. I said,' we have technology, competent engineers, and a slow network. Interconnection is possible.' The state bank project by TCS brought in a positive outlook. Mr Bala of TCS managed the project efficiently.
Similarly, the syndicate bank also went in for core banking by 2000.Mr Samba murthy was the GM in charge of the project. Syndicate bank used the city bank core banking software iflex. Infosys also developed Finacle software used by many banks.
The period 95 to 2000 was a period of rapid development in application software and IT. India graduated in a big way to large software systems and also adopted quickly without time lapse on the latest developments in internet, web, HTML, Java, LAN,WAN, cyber security, data centre etc.IT systems became complicated. We moved into an era of large integrated and complex systems. The expectations by customers shot up. They wanted- good performance, affordable costs, maintenance, security, continuity, reliability,agility and changing functionalities, adaption of new concepts like net banking, mobile banking,etc.
When I taught software engineering in the early eighties, these user demands on software looked theoretical. But by the mid nineties, these became practical necessities and became implementable through an enterprise architecture.
 ERP was used by most companies. Ramco built an Indian ERP called Marshall. 
Indians changed completely. You saw a resurgence. We saw bright IIT,IIM students jumping into this challenge with ease.I found most engineers in many industries - both hardware, networking, security,software and operations- hardworking and eager to learn. There was a sea of change in India. We produced confident, quick to learn and adopt people who were willing to work hard and for long hours. Our engineers accepted challenges. This was a transformed India, no one talks about. I was in the front seat observing, watching and helping in this transformation. I was involved in about hundred plus major projects in several governmental and private organizations, hospitals, banks and educational institutions.
By 1995, India embarked on data centres. Tata VSNL ,Sify and Reliance started building and operating data centres in the country. The culture of hiring space in data centres was quickly accepted and adopted. Most organizations and banks kept their servers in data centres. 
So India was ready to take a leadership role in IT services.Y2K hastened it.
More in the next.
Greetings. Dks
