02/03/26, 4:42 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1002
MK Shrikant gives a thoughtful comment:
"Great memories, nostalgia, even! There was one school of thought that the developments were so rapid and different from conventional businesses that the bureaucracy was not quick enough to get involved, and that actually helped the IT revolution move faster and wider."
My response:
You guessed my next direction of writing,Srix. What you say is what I observed. But with a twist.Your theory applied in both directions. 
During the sixties, the labor in LIC and railways opposed computers and didn't allow its use in government or factories as they were told that automation will kill a large number of jobs.
During Indira Gandhi's time, it was the anti-computer attitude of government that delayed our progress by several decades, not benign or ignorant neglect. Recall that Prof Nag, a good friend of mine, was removed as the chairman of the Electronics commission because he gave licenses for manufacture easily. That happened in the Rajiv Gandhi era. So it is a three edged sword. 
The anti computer policy of the government eased by mid 1980s when Rajiv Gandhi took charge and when PCs manufactured in India,were available in large numbers at low prices in the market. So most officers felt the potential of the computer not fully but to the extent that it had its uses in computation. They didn't understand its potential fully. Even then TCS had problems of importing computers. TCS brought an IBM mainframe for some customer to India and located it in SEEPZ export zone and used it for a foreign customer. They couldn't dispose of it easily later. That was the rigidity.
Fortunately, Karnataka had a long history of electronics friendly approach. It became computer friendly in the seventies. We set up the first government computer centre in early 1970s. It ran several applications. Its major task was to rank applicants for engineering admissions and later to allot them to colleges. So everyone liked computers here.
But when bureaucrats became familiar with IT, they played another role of real estate in IT and corruption. They became know all, but still did many activities to take computerization to all corners of the government. We computerised the commercial taxes department in the late eighties. We streamlined computer purchases by introducing rate contract.The bureaucrats realised the power of computers in administration and its capability to control the people.
Let me give you one example to illustrate this. In 1987 or so, Mr Ramakrishna Hegde was the CM of karnataka. The PMs office installed a computer by HCL by then. Mr Hegde also wanted a computer in his office. I knew both Mr Balasubramanian, secretary to CM and Mr Gokulram, deputy secretary very well. I had to decide on the computer. So I asked Mr Gokulram on the purpose of the computer. The answer opened my eyes. He said, " many politicians pester the CM on transfers. So he decided to issue transfer orders using the computer and tell everyone that the computer is doing the transfers. He has no role in it." Actually, transfer orders were input into the computer and printed out. But it was helpful for the CM to run the government.
But by and large, the governments followed a helpful attitude. They realised the power of IT by mid or late 1990s. RBI pushed the banks to go for core banking. By 21st century, IT was bigger than the government. Government bowed to IT. Passports, taxes, aadhaar and UPI are good examples.
Thanks Srix.
Greetings. Dks
03/03/26, 4:14 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   1003

Let me continue with my discussions on the IT developments in India and by Indian companies from 1985 to 2000. 
We saw the hardware, software, networking and data centre absorption as they happened in India. 
For a large technology to take roots, we need a number of talented persons- programmers and engineers. Swami Vivekananda put the talent requirement seed to JN Tata which led to the setting up of IISc. But such a development didn't take place in India for computer science in the formal route. Except for TIFR,IISc, IITs and ISI, no university bothered about this discipline. The were blind to the fast paced developments happening in the CS area, a tragedy.
But it was compensated by the mushrooming of thousands of training institutes across India. Datamatics set up a training institute in Bombay and another in Bangalore in late sixties. I was involved with them and conducted about 30 plus training programs each lasting for a month. It helped in generating some programmers. HCL set up NIIT in the early eighties and it quickly spread across the country. It played a big role in training thousands of youngsters. Seeing the job and revenue potential, thousands of training institutes came up even in districts not just the big cities. Most engineering students picked up their programming ,CAD and database skills from these institutions. 
Many companies wanted programmers and approached me to select suitable persons. I had conducted hundreds of interviews and found, in many instances, NIIT trained students more knowledgeable than engineering graduates. I even selected some for foreign assignments.
But by 1986, there was a serious quality issue. Most training was useless. I wrote an article in the Times of India news paper titled- computer alchemists. But still, the training institutions did a reasonable talent development in the absence of talented persons coming from engineering colleges. Later in the early 90s, the government of India wanted to ensure the quality and set up an accreditation body called DOEACC with Brig Sundaram as its chair. I did inspections of hundreds of training centres in bangalore .
The biggest effort came with the setting up of AICTE in mid eighties with power to approve engineering colleges and approve starting of new courses. I was with two task forces - one for Kerala and another for Karnataka. We permitted hundreds of colleges to start CS programs in the beginning and IT later. I inspected most new colleges in Karnataka, Kerala and some in Tamilnadu. So when our IT activities were scaled up by the mid nineties, the talent resources were available due to the scale up of engineering colleges in the private sector.
It is these developments which defined my professional and social paths. These developments in IT in the world governed my research focus. And similar developments in India aligned my professional focus. So these helped me to generate four distinct paths for the next period of my life - about 40 years. I was interacting with people more than technology. I needed to be a behavior specialist and a mind reader. I didn't change my attitude, but most - 99.999% of people that I had interactions respected me and my opinions- even chief ministers. The four paths were:
1. Academics at IISc
2. Computer education in the state and engineering education
3. Professional development of IT in various organizations.
4. Social aspects- energy, environment and education
Lots of opportunities came my way and enriched me and I could help over hundred organizations, banks and educational institutions and also many karnataka government departments. These kept me going till I reached 80 years of age. I will discuss them later. 
Greetings. Dks
04/03/26, 4:11 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1004
I handled a large number of big projects, mostly IT and power infrastructure projects. Hundreds of organizations benefited from my work and advice. I was serious and committed and adjusted my knowledge to the requirements of the organizations.
I realised the following aspects regarding the problems that I handled:
1. Problems are not solved with technology focus and technical skills alone.
Today's problems are complex, use many subsystems and many technologies and are connected. 
The requirements are many- usability, reliability, fault tolerance, scaling for growth, resilience.
2. What is a practical problem? Some say it is a puzzle. I see it as a big set of puzzles, needing discoveries. There is no universal approach, but many steps are useful. 
Remember that a practical problem to be solved does not stop with finding a solution. We need to implement it and make the implemented solution work. It should do all tasks done before but with improvements- faster, less errors,better control, easier, less costly, more efficient and flexible to add more functions.
So a problem is viewed as a project. Consider core banking for a bank. We go through many steps:
Step 1. Establish feasibility- software is available. It meets our requirements. It will execute user transactions faster and without errors. The software is used by other banks. We can train our staff to work in the new environment. It meets our performance needs. Hardware is available. There is a clarity on the gains the bank gets after implementation.
Step 2 decide on whether to develop a software, buy an existing one from the market or buy and modify. Prepare requirements, scope of work, list of functions, technology components etc. Prepare a detailed request for proposals. It is a thinking and time consuming activity.
Step 3. Select a vendor. It is a tough process and an important one.Evaluation takes time. You have to do it meticulously.
Step 4.design and  Implement the project. Define milestones, reviews, progress charts, acceptance tests, inspections etc.
Step 5. Train everyone.
Step 6. Operationalise the new solution by adding more branches into the new system.
3.Problems are not simple, well defined, easy to solve by mathematical models alone. They are games played by several teams supporting or opposing the objectives with many motives. There are many hidden hurdles.
4. Problems are not stand alone, but depend on many sectors, most important being their dependence on people. I am not talking about social behavioural or motivational problems, but about engineering problems. Problems will get answers if solutions consider people's reactions, biases, beliefs, capabilities. Otherwise, solutions will fail and not succeed. The toughest problem to handle is the ego of people.
5. Analysis of a problem will lead to an acceptable, cost effective, feasible and futuristic solution. This involves discussions and decisions on various aspects. We prepare a detailed plan for its implementation. We also prepare a methodology for that purpose. The methodology includes procedures for reviews, progress, change approvals, vendor selections, purchases, time management and other related aspects.
6. Problems become projects for implementation. Multiple agencies may be involved in the implementation. So a proper coordination mechanism needs to be established.
So when I encountered or was involved in solving a major problem like core banking for a bank, a power supply and distribution system for IISc, a IT education for  children in1000 high schools, or automation of a steel plant or a huge government department like department of space or shipping etc, I evolved a set of principles to be followed by me. 
Let us look at them in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
04/03/26, 12:13 pm - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: https://medium.com/@dksubra/indian-computer-scene-1-focus-on-hardware-development-manufacture-and-use-prof-d-k-subramanian-92cab871d64e
05/03/26, 4:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1005
What is the difference between a problem and a project?
Normally we refer to a problem as something that needs to be done in an organization in order to remove obstacles or difficulties or to improve performance or productivity or to reduce cost etc. It does not normally involve routine activities like accounting, designs, budget preparation etc. Examples of a problem are restructuring an organization or a department, estimations for a new product, feasibility studies, cost benefit analysis, etc. Here we restrict a problem to a specific domain or a single well defined task. It is normally an internal activity.
But a project is a major one having a single definable purpose; it is a unique one-time activities; projects are temporary activities with a specific timeframe;they cut across organizational lines to draw from multiple skills;they involve unfamiliarity ;project has a deliverable part; and project means the execution of a set of steps within a specified time frame to achieve a goal. Projects involve money, people, teams, timelines, progress monitoring, development,testing, implementation,installation and operation.
The term project was used initially for construction of houses or industries and later to new technology introduction as it involves major changes in operations and skills.
In order to solve a problem and implement a project, we go through several steps:
1. Understand the various aspects of the problem and solutions. 
Define the current state and deficiencies.
Define gaps and improvements.
Understand the available technologies in the market.
2. Define and describe the problem
3. Prepare a detailed plan of action.
4. Evolve procedures and methodologies for decision making, reviews, approvals, vendor selection, technology evaluation
5. Select technologies, vendors and establish the scope of work and timelines.
6. Start implementation
7. Review, modify, change.
8. Acceptance tests and installation.
Since the projects are complex, we can't follow any rigorous process to attack the problem. I realised that a blank approach is not acceptable. So I thought about this and created a set of general principles that I will follow in making decisions and in building sub processes for review, acceptance etc. Let me explain some of them.
The first is the understanding that all problems are context sensitive. The Core banking approach for a bank was different from that for another bank. So we should study and build in our mind a context universe. My decisions depended on this and not just technological aspects only.
1.know the context of the problem , not just the problem. It means that I should assess the type, structure, culture of the concerned organization, the nature, interest, motivation of the people- management, senior officers, concerned officers, other staff and stakeholders. I should know the technical and financial limits and operating procedures of the organization. This helps me in deciding what should be done?; what solution will be appropriate and how to implement it?; what are the hurdles? Then I should know the market and customers. Then I should know about technology providers.
I encountered several problems while dealing with technology providers. Some are:
1.Some major components are handled by monopolies. For example,it was difficult to deal with oracle. They decide on prices unilaterally without any justification and make you buy a lot of additional items. Their sales promotion was unbelievable.
2. The supplier for the main application program is normally decided. Now the organization has to live the suppliers inefficiencies. The coordination was poor when a novice project manager was appointed and he or she didn't understand the client properly and would be creating tense situations. I have seen this in most cases. On one or two occasions, I had to suggest cancellation of the contract. We normally reach out to the higher ups, escalate the problem and I even suggested solutions. For instance, to improve coordination, I suggested more regular meetings to resolve issues. I even suggested that the project leader should start every day with a 10 minutes stand up meeting and end the day with a brief recap. It worked to speed up the implementation.
3. The project team may not have competent or experienced members. That led to serious mistakes and delays. Members were changed in some instances.
4. As usual documentation concept was not followed- a typical Indian belief and capability syndrome.
This meant that vendor understanding, vendor rating and vendor management are tough tasks and needed to be handled properly for the success of a project.

Greetings. Dks
06/03/26, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1006

We are looking at understanding the context as my first principle to understand and discover the problem. We saw the problems with vendors.
Let me now look at the problems with people in the organization. It is a big one and determines the success or failure of a problem. Project leaders need good assessment and judgmental capabilities in order to handle the multiple problems posed by people.
I encountered many problems in dealing with a problem. It is not technology. Let me give examples.
When I was advising a bank on core banking, I sensed a strong opposition from many in the IT department. That was easier to handle as the top management and the seniors in the IT department supported the move very strongly. The DGM had a great understanding of the mood of the staff and officers both at head office and branches. It helped us to devise mechanisms to diffuse the situations created by some.
Again the solutions were not technical. It involved creating champions, keeping the dissenters in the loop- remember the key dissenter was running the existing branch automation and maintaining the software-, recruiting more staff and transferring some to other parts of the bank.
Let us look at a second example.I was involved in developing a major project- computer assisted admin and accounts- for the department of space, government of India. The top management decided to do it in house. But the internal IT head said that it couldn't be done. So I had to work with a group of supporters and work with no cooperation from the internal IT. This was a major project involving the efforts of about 50 people and writing 400000 lines of code. It wasn't easy. But we managed. I built the confidence of the project members. We had regular meetings.The project administrator was very proactive. The top management talked to the teams regularly. The diffused nature of the computer talent was useful in diffusing the tension and building confidence in them.
In addition, the department couldn't get an Oracle database. We had to use sybase. We needed the engineers to be trained in sybase. NIIT did the training for us.
The third example was another bank. It was using a core banking solution which wasn't a core banking solution. I wasn't happy. But the internal support at the higher levels of bank officers for the small company operating the software was very high. Even the IT department was powerless. The company did unprofessional things. This created a monopolistic situation and many difficulties.It took me three years of effort. After a change of two CMDs, it was the third CMD who took the decision to go for a regular core banking solution. That wasn't easy.Even afterwards, we had lots of problems in executing the project. In house expertise was strong and it helped us.
Understanding the organizational culture- transparency, freedom, participation, enthusiasm, cooperation,- is an important requirement.I had to assess this carefully.
Let me give a few examples of organizational culture torpedoing changes in operations. 
I talked about load forecasting with computer models of trend analysis in 1968. The person heading the load forecasting unit of the central electricity authority dismissed my talk saying ,' we don't need these techniques.' The primitive way of load forecasting continued for decades. In 1984, there were discussions on Karnataka's load growth and requirements of additional generation. 
Mr SG Ramachandra projected a 100% growth in 5 years using supply side economics and taking into account energy and power deficits as 30%. His calculations inherently assumed the deficit to grow. On the other side, Prof AKN Reddy used a demand reduction approach and showed that there was no need for additional generation. 
GMCI asked me to predict the load growth. I used the computer trend analysis model and predicted the growth. After 5 years, my prediction was found to be correct. But everyone dismissed my studies.
I talked about load despatch using computers in a power system in 1972. Tatas set up a digital load dispatch centre by then. But all electricity boards rejected that. Only by 1982, the government pushed by Mr SS Murty went for computerised load despatch centres for regional electricity boards. I trained about 100 engineers of CEA and CMC for that project.But the situation is still primitive. Top managements are afraid of computers. Same is the story with the urban water distribution boards. I suggested computerised operations of bangalore water distribution system and computerised leakage detection system in 1985. Both were not implemented for decades.They are averse to computer control in these days of data analytics and AI.
There are many more examples of ERP failures in some industries. Even,IISc couldn't computerise its admin and accounting applications till around 2006 despite decades of effort.

07/03/26, 4:20 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1007
The second step is to study the problem under consideration or the organization or the business concerned in depth to discover the actual problems.
2. Know the current state of the organization.compare it with other similar organizations, study its governance structure. Identify weaknesses of the organization, gaps in current operations, and look for improvements. Study the people,commitments,expectations,in house capabilities, willingness to outsource etc. This is needed to decide on
what are the pain points- performance, cost, sales, competition?
What are the hurdles?
What changes are needed?
What remedial actions are needed?
What will be the impacts and advantages?
What implementation strategy should be?
What technology to be decided?
What is the scope?
How do we implement the plan?
Let me look at an example. In the mid nighties, I had a big role in implementing the Mahithisindhu program of computerization of thousand high schools in Karnataka.
What was my knowledge of the current state?
1. Datamatics corporation was training people from the seventies successfully and many trained persons started working on computers. 
2. In the eighties, many- thousands- training centres sprung up every nook and corner in big cities and by late eighties ,they percolated to small cities also.Private organizations like NIIT,Aptech started hundreds of training centres across India. There was a lot of awareness and enthusiasm amongst people and students. Most students attended these programs. Computer Society of India also ran training programs. Banks encouraged and funded their officers to have extensive COBOL training and they set up IT departments. It was useful later for branch automation.
3. Many individual trainers visited schools regularly and conducted regular training program. This spread across our cities quickly. I was personally involved in the training and advising activities of several schools and colleges. My friend, Mr Hegde,principal of the Gujarathi school in the Majestic area was very enthusiastic and organized my lectures for teachers of many schools. Mr Gopalakrishna of natiinal public school set up a computer centre and ran training and teaching activities.
4.But the problem with government schools was poor.I found that the supply of computers to schools under the class program of the government if India didn't work. Teachers were not trained, were scared to use a computer, didn't know what to do with a computer. So they just kept the computer locked in a steel cupboard. I had seen it in some schools. Even training by IISc computer staff was not effective or useful. So it was a failure.
5.Computer ignorance was prevailing amongst teachers.
6. Schools had poor infrastructure- power, space,communications were non existent in most rural schools.
7. Mr SM Krishna, the CM was keen. Mr Vijayabhaskar, the commissioner of school education, took it as a personal mission with enormous commitment. He spent a lot of time on the project. 
8. Director and senior officers of DSERT were not interested. They kept away.
9. So the burden fell on me and I knew the pitfalls, but being foolish,I accepted.
These understandings were useful in our decisions.Based on the above, we decided to go total outsourcing to reputed companies - a different approach. We wanted to design the program to the last detail- computer configuration, staff requirements, topics to be covered, evaluation methodology, payment approvals, etc. We also decided to get the infrastructure- computer room, power supply, telephones- ready before hand. These decisions helped us to make the program a success.

08/03/26, 4:39 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1008
Let me look at more requirements and steps to solve a problem.
3. Do a gap analysis and discover problems.
You know the current state and difficulties seen in the current state. The most important purpose of the problem solver is to define the final state of the organization and also define the deliverables and benefits expected when you reach the final state.
Sometimes, one may go through a few stages of transformation.
Let me discus a few examples here. 
i.Final stage for Mahithisindhu program:
All students get a desirable level of mastery about computers, use computers to solve simple problems, learn to use the Internet and web and email and even operate the computers.
More importantly, increase subject comprehension, increase pass percentage, and improve student enrollments.
ii. Final stage of core banking for a bank:
Create a centralise correct database of all customers. Provide data protection and data loss prevention.
Automate most branch operations.
Facilitate secure transactions at any branch for all customers at a faster pace.
Maintain accounts information correctly. Get all branch reports. 
Provide 24by7 service. Ensure business continuity. 
Introduce more functionalities, net banking etc., support CRM,MIS.
iii. KSSEB processing SSLC results
Correct and faster processing, faster access, better MIS.
No manipulation
Easier database maintenance.
Now let us look at the next step for another hurdle.
4. Know the technology scene- maturity levels, suppliers, implementation difficulties, available expertise in the organization and outside.
While technology understanding is clear and is part of my learning and expertise, technology in the market is a difficult discovery. This needs a lot of effort amidst a lot of non information or partial clouded information. It links technology to developers to managers to service providers to sales people- a big chain with many weak links.
Let me explain. 
I know core banking software is a feasible activity and hence banks decided to go for implementing core banking. 
I know from my technical expertise that core banking will universalise banking, will lead to banking from anywhere, will minimise errors and improve performance. I also know that core banking needs skills to operate the computer, understand the operations, manage the network, modify the code almost daily- to take into account changes in procedures and practices. I also know that bank staff need training and hand holding.
But I don't know
1.what software is available in the market? What are their functionalities? How are they implemented? How difficult is it to modify them to meet a banks requirements? 
2. What is the maturity level of the developers? Who is using the software?
3.How is the service support?
4.What is the trust level? Is the company stable, does it have enough developers and professional competency, has it executed any similar projects, what is the response of the client?
5. How about other vendors- for servers, operating systems,networking, PCs,UPS, database, etc? What is their track record, reliability, serviceability etc?
6. Most importantly, how are the components and subsystems interconnected? Does it allow flexibility to choose any vendor for servers,PCs, networks, media, security?
7. How secure is the system? We found in one case the software vendor using many servers- one with windows operating systems and rejected that company. This led to a lot of discussions in the board meeting of the bank convened to approve our decision on the selection.
8. How is the performance? What are the operational practices followed by the software vendor. I was unhappy with the unprofessional practice of tinkering with the code in the production server directly by one vendor. The bank couldn't stop that practice despite my objections.
And there are many more questions. It is a discovery and investigation process- very rigorous and detailed and difficult. It is known that the company representatives are mostly ignorant of internal technical details and will create false images. Some company reps were arrogant, some told lies and promised everything. It is my job to create a reality picture. That is necessary for smooth operations.
More in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
09/03/26, 4:29 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1009
Let me continue.
5.Crucial decisions. A problem involves taking a number of decisions at different points of time of execution.
Let me give some examples.
In the case of Mahithisindhu program, we took several decisions in the beginning.
The project will be a total turn key project. 
It will be outsourced to a competent agency.
Since we didn't want monopoly, we decided to divide the 1000 schools amongst eligible agencies. 
The selected agencies should take full responsibility for complete execution. No secondary outsourcing is allowed. 
The eligibility criteria should avoid small vendors who can't conduct the program in several districts. Experience clause should be strictly enforced.
Computer purchases will be inspected. The project will have a quarterly inspection.
Dedicated and qualified persons should be employed for each school. No relaxations.
Text books will be approved by me.
All payments are subject to my approval.
Later we agreed to install DG sets in all rural schools.

6. Create a process and operating principles and review them periodically.
7. Know your team- cohesion, talents, commitment, capabilities, support level, willingness to learn,
I normally meet the team frequently in most places. The team is responsible for decisions and success of the project.
8.  Establish fair and transparent processes for selections of technologies, vendors and implementors.Establish a good, transparent governance mechanism.
The outcome of these steps will be the production of a detailed plan for solving the problems and implementing the project with details on progress, time, cost, manpower, reviews, testing and reporting in addition to execution of various tasks.

Prof P Raja
"Sir, Given your extensive engagement with real-world engineering challenges, I would like to understand your perspective on the teaching and assessment of complex problem‑solving in engineering education. 
Let me look at his questions one by one as they deserve the attention of all. The questions were important.                                                                                                                                  1. At what stage or level do you believe complex problem solving should be introduced within the classroom?        
Please look at the life of a child. It may encounter scarcity, death, disaster and ask yourselves how the children handle the difficult situations? True, the parents will try to shield the children , but how long. They need to know their neighborhood, they need to know how it functions, they need to know why some people are negative, some are helpful, some are abusive, some are angry. They are puzzled. They need to know why there is inequality, why some live in a huge house, some in a hut. So they need to know that human behaviour, nature, economy, family relations are strongly interconnected. They need to know not only the individual parts like family, city, river, forests etc, but more importantly the connections. We need to teach them reality that people are different, their life style is different, their beliefs are different, their wealth is different and these create their attitudes and behaviors. 
The question is- knowing the interconnections is not complex or unnecessary, but a reality. 
Another factor is that our education formalises most life events. Formalization means a lot of assumptions and conditions. That approximates the real situation and we solve them using logic and mathematics. We may get a wrong solution. We need to prepare children for connections.
Physics talks of matter and concentrates on matter. But does it address fields and radiations and waves properly. Feynman says every thing is surrounded by fields. Do we know what is a wave. We say suns rays travel at a speed. Is it real? What are the constituents of suns rays? Why the spectrum? What is the implication- not definition- of frequency? It is still working on it as it refuses consciousness. 
If consciousness is not there, how does radio signals travel over many light years distance? 
So we need to first introduce the concept of multidimensionalism and connections at an early age.
Then we can introduce problem solving with data, experience etc and without math. That is easy. Ask questions:
How do you get food? How do you decide what to eat every day?
Why does paddy grow on some land and not on others? What resources are needed for the success of a crop?
Can a village produce enough food for the people?What are the resources needed?
Where do you get extra food materials from?
How do we live within a limited income?
Where do we get our water from?
Why does someone get angry? etc.
We can frame lots of questions for them to think.There won't be satisfactory answers. But create an understanding of interdependence between monsoon and food supply, human behavior and poor economy etc.
Next make them to learn discovery. Where do you get your dresses from, your grocery items from, your notebooks and books from?
I think the job of education is to build knowledge as close to reality as possible.
So we need to introduce multidimensional problem solving at different levels starting from high school. 
Then we start looking at solving real life problems.Forget about technical problems. Look at the social problems - garbage, traffic, quarrels, fuels,social disparities etc. 
Let me give an example. When I suggested that design should be taught and experimented in stages, a lot of people opposed. Now it is common practice.
Similarly, we didn't introduce social interconnections, human decision making, reading and reviewing, large problem solving in engineering. 
I always said that computer students wrote small programs, not a large program of 50000 lines of code. When I told the faculty in BVB, there was a gasp. I in the seventies made my students write assemblers and loaders. I made my students develop concurrent programs. 
So first remove your beliefs. If you believe that it is necessary, you will ways of doing it. It is urgent in todays AI and data driven world. We have to implement it.
More in the next.
I will look at the other questions in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
11/03/26, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1011
Let me talk about some experiments. We decided in 1975 that engineering students should design and build machines for agricultural operations as well as machines used by small industries. So KSCST started a students projects program. We had some great professors like Ramapriyan at NIE, Mysore, Narayana Rao at MCE,Hassan and Dhotrad at BVB, Hubli. They mentored the students and it led to learning and applying mechanical engineering principles for building useful devices. It showed that students are capable of taking up challenges.
My second example is our focus in 2004 to start a scholar program to build products with multidiciplinary inputs. That is going on even now and supported by our foundation, FAER. This also showed that students in small cities are in no way inferior to students in metros.I am seeing a lot of enthusiasm in AI now. We permit even second year students to participate in this program.
My third example is our technology barrier reduction program. We took eighth and ninth standard students from rural schools and gave them internship in a nearby engineering college for a period of a month. It was a great success.The students picked up a lot.
So I am sure the students from rural areas and poor families are eager to accept challenges and are confident to take up challenges. It is the faculty who have a problem- a crisis of confidence. They think their students are useless. I had heard this comment in most colleges by faculty. They even decry me. They said that I am familiar with IISc students and they are meritorious. They never realized the fact that I had seen thousands of students from these colleges doing good projects. Also they didn't understand the fact that I had conducted courses in more than forty colleges. So I make this statement from my deep experience. We need to accept the fact that even first students are capable of taking up challenges. I saw BVB students conducting social engineering projects in the first year itself. It was great and revealed many facts. Students have energy ,so provide them motivation.
Another reason why students should solve complex problems.
Let me continue on the question of dealing with complex problems and the appropriate time to do that posed by Prof Raja.
The first reason is
Is it necessary to solve complex problems?
Life has become complex. So solving complex problems is a necessary life skill for most of us. This leads me to the next question:
Do we cope with life correctly? Most don't. I observed ten problems we face in our daily life. they are:
Problems1.poor understanding of the functioning of the society, family or an organization
2.poor decision making skills.
3.poor or satisfactory leadership skills
4.poor use of human resources
5. Fairness and transparency.- ambiance and working conditions- empathy and concern.
6.poor improvement in the confidence level of employees.
7.work culture, practices - no freedom Firefighting
8. No encouragement for innovation.
9. Risk aversion.
10. Centralised control even in a household.
All of us know these ailments. I have written several posts to address these. I consider the first two as important for everyone. You agree that both are important life skills and both deal with complex problems. We also know that our school system doesn't address these two skills. So that is the need of the hour.
We should be prepared for this and push this- make engineering modern and complex and multidimensional.
Hope this convinces us. Comments welcome.

12/03/26, 4:12 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1012
Let me look at the second question of Prof Raja.
2.  In my view, many such problems are inherently multidisciplinary—how should we effectively address them both inside and outside the classroom learning environment?         
My response
I agree. I go further and say that engineering is not for engineering, by engineering; it doesn't stand alone; in the 18th century engineering looked at industries as its focus. But with the advent of electricity, engineering moved towards broader applications and got interconnected with industries, commerce, business and people. The bonding with people increased with computers and the Internet.
 Today's engineering is a highly connected system of technologies, industries, business and commerce. Todays engineering is no more just a set of components or parts like machines, motors, refrigerators, pumps etc. It is a complex system comprising hundreds to thousands of parts intricately interconnected. Todays engineering is an integrated, intertwined and architected environment of industries, utilities, businesses, logistics, infrastructure, commerce and services. So the problems it deals with are complex and multidimensional. You can't solve a problem by only looking at the technical aspects of modeling, analysis and synthesis, but by looking at business society, people, technology, governance interdependence.
Electronics engineers learnt this fast. They moved from transistors to ICs to VLSI. Today, electronics focuses on building systems with chips and software and user connections. We talk of raspberry pie now, we talk of buses and ports, we talk of C programs.students build systems for many applications with these subsystems almost effortlessly today. I have seen this happening for the past ten plus years.
What are the realities of today's engineering?
1.Engineering science is mature now. New technologies are developing faster.
2.A product or unit or plant is a complex system. It uses many technologies.
3.It is a highly interconnected system with users.
4.So communications is an integral part of today's engineering.
5. For better and continuous operations, we need status monitoring with IOT.
6. Most operations are controlled by IT. So IT components are integral to engineering. So present engineering systems are called cyber physical systems.Robotics is now part of manufacturing and services.We will see millions of robots everywhere. Drones will play a big role in our activities.
7. Engineering impacts services and people. So today's engineering is a multi disciplinary, multi media and multi dimensional one. So it is beyond disciplines and techniques.
8. Engineering a complex system of systems.
9. AI will be everywhere in a system. Systems will interact through AI.
10. So an engineer is a problem solver, a coordinator,an integrator, an architect, a manager and a planner- a holistic person, not just a technical expert.Technology experts are a small subset of the engineering profession. Tech knowledge is needed to be an engineer at the beginning level.
But our curriculum still looks at parts not the whole. Remember that the whole is much greater than the sum of the parts. So a lot is hidden inside. Unearth them. We should start with an engineering systemic understanding. That is what I wrote in the blog,' discovery of problems through systems architecture'. That should be the starting point for defining a curriculum.
We should start with three courses:
1. Multi dimensional System architectural views of engineering.
2. Multi dimensional problem solving
3.Life skills development techniques.
These three are foundations. 
Divide the curriculum into four parts - 1.principles, tools, designs, analysis , basic units etc
2. Multidimensional relationships
3. Engineering applications
4. Practical problems - discovery, understanding, reality, innovations,
The next step is a big one. 
Shift focus from engineering principles to engineering applications, look at large problems, understand the utilization of the latest technologies, look at the practical issues and discover problems. 
This means spending more time on the field and less in the class rooms. Field does not mean internships, but regular visits, surveys, discussions, analysis of several sectors- finance, water,power,transport,gas,and communications utilities, logistics, security, urban problems, hotels,hospitals, petrol stations, malls,PWD, police, etc.
It means one or two days per week for field visits, and one or two days for practical problem solving. 
Hope it gives information to sufficient depth on question 2.
Welcome comments.
Greetings.  Dks
13/03/26, 4:38 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1013
The third query from Prof Raja
3. Additionally, do you feel that our traditional assessment methods remain adequate in the current educational context, especially when accreditation bodies now increasingly emphasize competencies related to complex problem solving?                                                                  I would greatly appreciate your guidance on how academia should evolve to meet these revised expectations."
My response
I feel our current thinking on assessment itself is outdated. Our assessment system is the biggest hurdle to reforms.
Let us ask ourselves the question- how did gurukulas assess their students? It was done daily, progress watched every day, the student observed, his absorption seen and his questioning and thinking capabilities are carefully monitored. Once the guru finds that a student has assimilated whatever was taught and learnt, he declares him a snataka- graduate. He anoints him as such and sends him home.
Here AI is a big boon. It can help in assessing every person through discussions and problem solving approaches. It can assess the work done by a student. So exams are not needed. We need to use AI agent to be a help to a student and to assess him and correct him. It can lead him or her to reach mastery. The arithmetic and memory testing exams will die, a great day of deliverance for education. It will no longer be a punishment.
But teachers will feel their weapon of terrorism gone and protest. They have to become humans. It took centuries to remove corporal punishment. Bow is the time to remove this capital punishment- exams. That will do a world of good for students and the education system. Education should produce thinkers and not slaves, good humans not greedy people, happiness and not worries and fear.
Your query about academics adaptation is a timely one. Academics are living and following a path followed by millions before. They should wake up and realize that that path leads nowhere. That is a great ask to teachers-
Wake up to the reality. Forget the illusion you are living in.
Will they do it. As I said, the world will do it, make academics redundant. AI will start running an alternate system. People will move towards that. Gen Z will beat us completely and tske a march. I want to quote Altman here to warn academics:
Speaking at the Dogra Hall of IIT Delhi, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman warned students that blindly following traditional career advice, especially from parents, could be a mistake in a world being rapidly transformed by AI. 
 "I think listening to old people is the biggest mistake young people make. You will have to quickly develop your own intuitions and trust them. The traditional career advice is probably not going to work as well," Altman said.
Altman warned students against relying on older generations to predict the future of work.
That is a sensible advice. We carry very large baggages of wrong, useless beliefs. They don't. We lost intuition. They didn't. They are fresh with open minds and great energy and enthusiasm.
So we need to wake up to this reality. All teachers need to attend a one-year program to reinvent themselves to become more intuitive, more aware, more humanistic and more thinking. Most important- break all rules; frame new rules.
It is with heavy heart I write this. We should be leaders. Despite our archaic education system, we are producing leaders. That is due to our tradition supporting intuition and tacit knowledge.This is serious.
Greetings. Dks

15/03/26, 4:19 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1015
Let me return to IISc. Many things happened in 1983. We saw that we completed our house construction and did a house warming ceremony. My father came for the function. That was his last visit to Bangalore.
This is also the period when the government of India decided to modernize four regional electricity board's load despatch centres. I played a major role in teaching both computers and power systems for three batches- a hectic work. My effort of computer control of power systems started in 1971 was seeing the light of the day. But miles more to go.
I also spent a lot of time with TCS designing the sysgel activity.
At the department level, I started work on transactions processing and distributed systems.
I was selected for a summer course on renewable energy conducted at ICTP in Trieste. It was a one-month program. So I went to Trieste and completed the program. I stayed in an apartment on rent and went by bus to ICTP.I had bread for the breakfast, lunch was pasta and I cooked dinner - just rice. 
During this program, I visited Venice and some villages. 
After completing the program, I went to Zurich, Switzerland to visit and know about the load despatch computer control systems being built by Brown Baveri. Mr Srinivasa Rao from CEA was undergoing training there. I spent a day and then went to Neuchatel, gave a talk and left by train to Milan and then to Trieste. I picked up my luggage and went to the airport to go to Rome and then to Delhi.I returned to Bangalore from Delhi.
My first introduction to public work started with my becoming the chair of the computer society of India, Bangalore chapter in mid 1984. I took charge from HS Nagaraja. We organized a number of programs. We were entrusted with organizing and conducting an international conference on CAD/CAM/CAE for industrial progress. It was sponsored by IFIP and Gen A Balasubramanian was handling IFIP affairs in India. He made me a member of TC 7 on systems. Prof Rajaraman was the technical chair and I became the organising committee chair. It was a big start. We started with a call for papers.We decided to conduct the conference in the Taj hotel. At that time, that was a big one with a large hall capable of 700 people. I set up an office. Eiko gave us two computers. We got a tremendous response. More than 500 people registered for the conference. It was a great success. Both RNarasimhan and Gen A Balasubramanian attended the conference. There was one small problem. One of the speakers was from Canada. In the beginning of 85, the Airindia flight Kanishka from Canada was attacked and crashed killing all the people on the flight. So the speaker got scared and we had to find a replacement.
Prof Ramaseshan wanted to get outside experts to review the working of Computer science and computer centre. Prof Nag, Director IIT Bombay, Prof PVS Rao, TIFR came as experts. It was october 1984. We at CSI wanted to falicitate them and organized a program. My treasure Venugopalachar was a police officer in the police computer centre. He came to see me on that day and said that Indira Gandhi was killed by her body guard. I alerted both professors Nag and Rao and they left for home hurriedly. We canceled the function. We had another CS committee visiting us afterwards with both Prof PCPBhat from IIT Delhi and Prof Reddy from Hyderabad university. I was taking care of them. I was in the guest house to see that they were transported to the airport. The PRO goofed up and they went by other transport. I informed the director. He was upset about the lapse of our PRO.
