16/03/26, 5:09 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1016

We are in the year 1984-85.
I consider 1984 85 to be first turning point in the Indian computer scene. The price of a computer came from ten million- a crore- rupees to a few lakhs - hundred thousand- rupees, a hundred fold change. It came within the range of mid size organizations, colleges and government offices. Further, the price was coming down every day and computer power was increasing. Affordable, very inexpensive systems were available in the market. So this generated enormous interest amongst all people. This gave me a lot of opportunities to help hundreds of organizations with advice and selection of computers.
Initially computers were used mostly for office work and accounting, the fascination was the printer. Most offices had computers and copying machines- that was modernization. File systems came later when sizable storage units were available, initially it was floppy disks.
That led to an explosive demand for talented persons- from programmers to operators to maintenance persons to system designers. Training institutions couldn't handle this situation. So I focused on engineering colleges in order to propagate CS education. So my life started in two directions in addition to my academics, research and IISc involvement which was always my first love.
During this period, another big social activity began for me. It was an explosion.It was my strong association with engineering colleges and propagation of computer science to students and faculty. It was a long 35 years journey. I instilled the knowledge of computer science to thousands of students and faculty in normal engineering colleges, instilling algorithmic thinking. It was a fruitful journey.I visited several engineering colleges and gave lectures on topics of computer science.
It opened a series of big and time consuming activities, bringing me in contact with hundreds of faculty in engineering colleges. This took my involvement with engineering colleges to a higher level beyond my involvement with KSCST's SPP visits.
I understood that while IISc students were intelligent, a sizable number of engineering students in the normal colleges in small towns were very keen to learn and were very well behaved. I found many bright students in many colleges. There was a lot of excitement amongst students.
 It started with my intense involvement with lecturing at engineering colleges.Prof Viswanath, principal of GIT, Belgaum invited me to lecture his students on computers. I was also invited by SDMCE, Dharwad and I gave a series of lectures on operating systems. 
I was invited by Prof Prithviraj, of the IEEE chapter at MIT to conduct a two day workshop on UNIX operating systems. That was the first course on UNIX in India.  Later, I was invited by Prof A Prabhakar, principal,MIT,Manipal to teach courses on data structures and OS for his ME students. 
I visited KREC,Surathkal from Manipal and lectured to the ECE students.
I visited REC, Trichy and conducted a course on distributed operating systems.
I helped Prof KA Krishnamurthy, head of ECE, Malnad college of engineering, Hassan to start a new ME program on computer communications. I started teaching data structures and operating systems to the first two batches of students. This association continued for several years. Our friendship was close till his death.
I visited Andhra university on the invitation of Prof Radhakrishna murthy and delivered many lectures. I became a UGC visiting lecturer and spent two weeks at SV University, Tirupathi teaching a course on operating systems. 
During the international conference, I met both Prof R Srinivasan and Santhanakrishnan from TCE, Madurai. That was my first contact with TCE after 1964 - a period of 20 years has elapsed. They invited me to Madurai to give a series of lectures to their students. That started my long association with TCE. These were strong starting points for my involvement in promoting computer science education mostly in Karnataka but also in Tamilnadu and Andhra.
Later I taught at BDTCE, Davangere during Prof Bhandari's time , NMAMIT,Nitte during Sridhar Hegde principalship, and many other colleges and universities. It is a big list.
By December 1984, I moved to the Institute campus in E quarters. Children could go to school and play with security. From 1984, I stayed in the institute till retirement.
More in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
17/03/26, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   1017
My father had a serious heart problem in late 1984 and was admitted to the Isabella hospital in Chennai. I went there and he was enquiring about the quarters. He was treated with medicine and no surgery. The cardiologist who treated him belonged to the old school. Angioplasty was not recommended by many for diabetics and bypass was a difficult surgery and done at expensive Jaslok in Bombay. So after a few months of suffering. he passed away in April 1985. I was there in Chennai visiting him and he had intense pain and before we took him to the hospital, he died. I completed his rituals. My uncle from Tirunelveli came and stayed there to help us through the functions till the thirteenth day.Afterwards,I returned to Bangalore.My athimber, S Aiyaswamy also helped me during the functions.
After I completed the international conference in june 85 , I became busy with several conferences. I also became involved with the Institution of Engineers, karnataka centre as a committee member. I gave lectures at CSI,IE and IETE. In 1985 end we conducted the first science and engineering conference of CSI. Then we planned for a very novel experiment in 1986- the first and major international conference on Samskritam and AI based on Riggs paper. It was held in Sankar Mutt in Chamrajpet. It needed a lot of effort and thinking.More on it later.
There was another turning point in my life. Mr PJ Nayak was the additional secretary in the planning department. He was upset about the functioning of the government computer centre. It was losing credibility. It had two directors from navy, both were not able to run the centre properly. So Nayak came to KSCST one day and we discussed the problem. I told him the future would be computer dependent and we need to revamp. We decided to set up a technical advisory panel. It was a big move. It guided the state during the era of many chief ministers.It had lots of birth pangs in the first two years and then it moved forward helping the state to lead the country in computerization. I played a big and lead role in this till my retirement. I was party to many decisions. We streamlined procedures, allowed computerization, helped in the development of software packages, created purchase procedures, introduced the concept of rate contract, got LAN built in Vidhana soudha and MS buildings and many more things.  I guided many government organizations in developing computer infrastructure. As part of this TAP I assisted several government organizations like KSCST, Karnataka power corporation, Mysore Paper Mills Ltd, Karnataka state road transport corporation, Bangalore dairy, land army corporation, karnataka state secondary school examination board, DTE, Bangalore university, and many more organizations. 
We realised by 1986 that Karnataka government computer centre can not handle the large number of jobs generated by various departments. KGCC was doing only two activities, but I expected hundreds to come up. Already , we were discussing the computerization of the commercial taxes activities in a big way.So we felt that computerization should be left to the individual departments. This reduced the role of KGCC. This was at a time when companies and organizations were setting up centralised IT departments. We went the other way. We thought that individual motivation and efforts by officers will flourish the computerization activity.
We also realized that in house software development won't happen and we should out source software development to other agencies. Initially ,it was given to NIC, CMC, and other government agencies, but soon it was opened up for private companies to build and operate software for government organizations.
Greetings. Dks
18/03/26, 4:43 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   1018
Mr Rajan comments on the last post - our computer work for the state:
"Good morning sir. Tremendous recollections sir. Lot of learning’sFor people like us."
"Sometimes, it is some small sentence like “others were setting centralised computer departments. We went the other way”…. When i question myself whether one of my decision is right or wrong, these kind of statements from your article makes me feel comfortable with my decisions. 🙏🙏🙏"
My response
It is a very impactful statement. I am glad you got it. It was a strange situation - PCs were available in plenty at low cost reaching to lots of people. So it should lead to diffused computing activities at the lower level and not centralised. But on the other hand companies were growing bigger and they depended on large volumes of data. They are getting connected geographically to their offices and plants as well as suppliers. Software is becoming complex. Operations of mainframe and networks needed expertise of a high order. Software needed regular updates. So companies and banks set up IT departments. The budget was high. So decision making had to be done at a higher level. These departments couldn't retain talent. They could only handle planning and purchase of IT systems and not handle building them. So outsourcing started. Outsourced companies prefered talking to one person - IT and not individual functional units. That was a big mistake. Many projects failed because the user requirements were not understood. 
Let me recall a situation in 2003. A major government department had given a contract to a company to build its ERP sofware- a custom built one. The company built the software without consulting the users, not understanding users capabilities and not knowing the requirements. We had to reject the software and cancel the contract.
By 2005 or so with the arrival of social networks, mobile smart phones and cloud, the focus of IT shifted to users from a centralised system. The central IT department was powerless. I anticipated a similar situation with computerization in 1987. Development should be initiated and monitored with users involvement and approval. One major success was the computerization of commercial taxes and excise. It was done by the department with usage by department staff. It was a success.
This is what I said in problem solving steps- awareness of the context. There are very few universal rules in IT. Adapt to the situation. But CEOs love control and won't give away malicious pleasures of holding approval power. 
The local level empowerment has made startups a success over traditional monoliths.
Thanks Rajan for highlighting this.
Greetings. Dks
19/03/26, 4:41 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1019
Happy Ugadi to all.
"It was the best of times, it was the worst of times, it was the age of wisdom, it was the age of foolishness, it was the epoch of belief, it was the epoch of incredulity, it was the season of Light, it was the season of Darkness, it was the spring of hope, it was the winter of despair, we had everything before us, we had nothing before us, we were all going direct to Heaven, we were all going direct the other way..."
Charles Dickens       Tale of two cities
There is some confusion about the point discussed in the last post. I didn't say or more correctly, judge that centralization is wrong. I don't do value judgments. Our biases, our beliefs and our perceptions should not force us to a rigid conclusion.Value judgments should be avoided.
Before we take a decision, we should do a holistic assessment.We should know our people, our organization, culture and governance, our types of jobs, our customers, our stakeholders, market, technology, economy and society - a very holistic assessment of reality as it exists -, through experiences, reading, discussions, data analysis and introspection. That is a pre requisite to arrive at a decision.
Let me look at it from a different angle - how do I decide then?
We need to forget the following strong and dogmatic beliefs.
1. Organizations are governed by universal principles. Even science goes for axioms and universal theories. One size fits all is wrong. Sometimes, order,stability and uniformity lead to uniform rules across an organization. Everyone obeys them. That is what Ramayana tells us. Rama, the maryada purusha follows all rules. He is seen as an ideal person.But it doesn't always work. 
Look at Mahabharata and Krishna. Krishna doesn't follow rules and breaks rules. He even defines his own rules.That helps him to create a righteous country, and right ways of ruling. He ignored rules that are uniform, static, omnibus, applicable to all at all times. As a dean, I did that. I told my deputy registrar," you follow rules and I break the rules when necessary."
When I joined IISc , we had very few rules, departments were independent. But centralised power hunger encircled us with rules, made us slaves, delayed approvals, asked silly questions and it didn't improve the functioning of the institute or speeding up our research. Fortunately, academics was in our hands and we did what we wanted and survived in a global competition.
In today's environment of uncertainty, fast changes, complexity, we need a lot of flexibility, not rigidity and universality.
2. Organizations should be controlled centrally. This leads to delays, wrong decisions, biased decisions and poor transparency. Power corrupts.
Then what is the role of organization. The core act is arriving at decisions for the benefit - survival, happiness and prosperity- of an organization. So the control should not hinder any of these but aid them. So create appropriate control structures. Trust and empowerment are important. Strong strategies- not fixed but dynamic- and good feedback systems are more important, not approvals and permissions. People should be free to think and act with a goal in mind. Again, this is subject to organizational culture. You see the mistake of Apple board in removing Steve Jobs from the CEO position and its after effects.
3. Organizational structure should be rigid. This is not true anymore. Organizations may need flexibility, may need a change. Our government finds an alternate solution. It sets up special centres or special purpose vehicles. It came initially with public undertakings and later with SPVs. Even Tatas were bound by rules. So they set up Tata consulting engineers first and TCS later. Mr Kohli devised a new structure for managing the projects. Prof Dhawan, director, IISc,  created separate centres, units and labs to nurture innovation and develop new and emerging areas. He felt that existing departments can't focus on new areas of research.
4.There are also policies and procedures for taking decisions, for purchase, for promotions and for operations.  These are mostly static, copied from other organizations or governments. Do we ask why are these rules or procedures? Are they relevant? Why are they there?
We don't change them. Even today banks have a high hierarchical system- CMD,ED,GM,DGM etc-. Why ? Can we answer this? Are these positions relevant or necessary? We see some colleges having too many admin bosses controlling faculty.I saw in a college an attender was carrying information about faculty to the management and faculty were afraid of him. I have seen hundreds of such examples.It is counterproductive.
In one institution, the director spent 15 minutes questioning an employee requesting one day's casual leave. Do we know what is important and what is not?We do many things which may not be necessary. Think and eliminate unnecessary activities. Delegate.
5. What is the role of an organization? It is here that we have a poor understanding- flashy office, professional conduct, security, secrecy is one set of understanding. Profit, productivity, discipline is another set of understanding. The organization provides a working place.
Well, that is not the role. An organization is not an office or a hierarchy of people. It is a group of people and their role is to come up with ideas to build and grow their activities and make them produce something useful to some section of a society with a commitment to nature and humanity.
6. What are the main requirements of an organization? Discipline, work culture, control, fear, productivity, commitment are normally assumed aspects. But we need something different and more- enhancement of the minds of people, service to society, happiness, collaboration, active thinking, learning, enthusiasm etc.Organizations should build humanity and lead towards better societies, not just money making units. They should be places of peace and calm and not of tension and worries with daily firefighting activities. They should encourage thinking and creativity.
If we understand these, we will become innovative and active people. Organization is a means. With work from home, online collaboration, econferencing , online meeting rooms, the concept of organization needs to transform.
Remember, the USA lost its huge manufacturing activities by chasing profits. So their concepts of organizations are wrong. Look at organization as a soul of a company.
Greetings. Dks
20/03/26, 4:31 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post    1020
Mr Rajan
'Dr sir, it would be nice to hear your experience in the topic, “swimming against the tide” where u have taken decisions like the above (against general trend) and what issues have you encountered and how did you overcome them ? This will be super useful to startup’s because startup’s are supposed to disrupt the general trend. But the challenge we face is, swimming against the tide: hence this request 🙏'
My response
Understand that those who swim along the tide and even those who pluck a low hanging fruit are ordinary people. 
If anyone wants to get into a startups activity, he can't be a normal person. He is first and foremost a thinking and cerebral person, not a mechanical copy paste guy.
Secondly, he shouldn't be foolhardy. He should have viveka to discriminate between good and bad, easy and difficult, doable and not feasible, acceptable and not acceptable etc.
Third, he should be aware of the market, people, society and their needs. He should talk to people, consult,read and discuss regularly.Deep listening is a requisite quality.
Fourth, he shouldn't be afraid of challenges that will appear in his path. It can be technical, financial, work pressure, failures, time management,customer satisfaction , market changes, and even disasters. Try to understand the challenges. Don't be blind to the circumstances.
Fifth, he should anticipate the future course with some level of confidence. 
Sixth, he should plan in detail his paths and be flexible enough to change some aspects of your plan. Flexibility comes with reviews and feedbacks. 
Seventh, he should know his priorities. This is a weakness I see in some.
Eighth, total focus and concentration are essential. Think it is not a well run company. Innovate at every step.
Ninth, don't lose hope. Hope and positive thinking are necessary for success and for leadership. 
Tenth, he shouldn't be a diehard specialist and a lover of his area. Outward looking and general approaches are essential for success, more than expertise in an area. If you are a specialist, your universe is small and you get stuck with a product. You don't get out of it. Initially, ecommerce guys focused on technology and didn't understand market and failed miserably. Many single product companies are not doing well as compared to multi product ones. Diversification means safety. It is a good ring fence.
So start with a caution to meet the challenges.
Life implies that we get challenges. We should first accept that fact. This is true for all. We may decide to run away or face it. Those who run away from challenges become slaves and followers. In the present day world of AI, data, digital, uncertainties and complexities, everything- people, society, organization and governance- should become cerebral.That is where viveka comes in. The first two learnings are
1.Look for challenges. Don't shy away from them. 
2. Understand the full strength of the challenge. Don't fight against certain challenges like government regulations. This is known as hitting a wall. Avoid whirlpools while swimming. Keep away from sharks.I have seen people going to the court on every single problem faced by them thereby losing concentration on the problem.
Then came the next step- preparation. Swimming against tide needs alternate paths. Expect the path to have impediments. Look for alternates.
Expect challenges like customer acceptance or rejection, satisfaction, price and market fluctuations, technology changes, business disruptions, regulation changes, global turbulence, government policy changes etc. So create a list of problems you may encounter, list them, assess their impacts, find alternate paths. Prepare a detailed regular plan and contingency plans. 
To invoke a contingency plan, one needs an alert system. This is missing in many challenging businesses. Most have gut knowledge. That is not enough. Build an estimation system, a feedback mechanism, an alert system, a regular learning system that no school teaches. All these are missing in current business systems to a large extent. Looking outward us more important than looking inward and doing firefighting. That is what most are doing now. Build a ring fencing system for this purpose. You don't start swimming only with confidence but with wisdom, viveka, advance knowledge, prepared alternate contingency plans and constant assessments and reviews and corrections.
As I said before multi product thinking is missing in many startups today. Some diversify within business. Some look at technology and ignore business. Most ignore market trends. While swimming against tide is good, swimming against market is risky. We need to understand it.
Let me continue with the drawbacks of our people that doesn't help in being prepared.
Thanks Rajan.
Greetings. Dks
21/03/26, 4:28 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1021
Drawbacks hindering us from getting the thinking and mind needed for a stratup activity- let me call it as startup mindset.
Unfortunately, our lifestyle and our education have created us into beings with some disadvantages.
1. We look at the past mostly and comfortable with the past and not the future. But the future is where business is.
2. We are focusing on specialization without realising that your area is connected to people, their habits, behaviours, society, economy , global effects, geopolitics, governments etc.Reality is different from specialised knowledge. Our education creates silos and forgets integration. It misses out connections. Even where we learn connections like power network, the connection was internal, not external. Connections to business, people and economy are not taught.So learn connections and relationships.
3. We look at technology only. Project view is absent. That leads us through the path of realization.
4. We look at single product. Diversity has to be cultivated.
5. We focus on our product, don't know the world, the pitfalls.
6. We wait for disruptions. Don't anticipate it.
7. We have one view but problems have many views, many solutions. One size fits all doesn't apply now.
6. We look inward. But we need to look outward to know the ever changing reality.
7. World is changing. Nothing is static. Be aware of the pulse of the world.
The first step is to become a free and open thinker.Lots of constrained thinking happens due to three factors:
One, our beliefs.
Two, our education.
Three, our past experiences.
Question your belief system periodically. Change when needed. Listen for better understanding with an open mind.
Read daily,introspect, consolidate your learnings. Know the context. Introspection retains the essence of learning and connects the dots.
Break constraints. Allow a free flow of imagination. That generates ideas, products and alternate paths. Imagination is a powerful tool for startups but be skeptical. Do a serious feasibility study of ideas or products.
Greetings
Dks
22/03/26, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1022
Let me return to IISc and look at the major project that I took up in 1984 in IISc. I was the chair of the power committee. I found power interruptions were increasing. The 11kv incoming KEB power station supplying grid power to IISc, located near our gymkhana got connected to BWSSB jewel filters pumping station at 18th cross and to BHEL in addition to IISc. Initially in 1969, it was agreed that it would be exclusive to IISc. But by 1983, the power supply station was overloaded. So power supply to IISc was interrupted frequently.
One day, the then registrar, Mr Venkatramiah told me that I got the 11kv in 1969 and the institute was happy with the power position. He said that I should do something that the power position in the institute for the next 20 to 30 years would be good and trouble free. He pleaded with me. I felt it a reasonable request. I was moved by his request. By that time, Mr Thimme Gowda came back to the institute on deputation from KEB.
We started looking at options. I felt that increasing the capacity of the 11kv substation of KEB was an obvious and easy solution, but it would be only a short term solution. Recall that I did the design of Delhi's electric supply system in 1977. I proposed that Delhi should move to 66kv after a lot of studies and observations. So I thought 66kv supply to IISc would be a great long term solution.
Bangalore didn't have any 33kv lines. It had a small 110kv network. Our neighboring CPRI went for 110kv but had problems in getting reliable supply, but I wasn't aware of it at that time and came to know about it later.
The government was developing a new area called Sanjay Nagar, but popularly known as dollar colony. The sites were reserved for rich and dollar-paying people. So we found out that KEB was bringing in a big and new receiving station - 220kv/66kv. The station was about four kms distant from IISc. It was our luck. The major station was just then coming up and 66kv feeders were available. That information was obtained by Mr Thimme Gowda from KEB.
So we decided to put our claim. We met Mr Govindappa, ED, KEB and discussed with him. He agreed to reserve one feeder for us. 
Now my other struggle had started- to get our directors approval for this project. I talked to Prof Ramaseshan. He was not keen on it. He didn't see the need for it. He had other priorities. I was not happy. I waited. 
In 1984-85, Prof Ramaseshan retired and Prof CNR Rao became the director. I told him about the power situation. He mentioned it in the next chairman's meeting. 
Let us continue in the next post.
Greetings. Dks
23/03/26, 4:07 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1023
Somethings to mull - returning to the Indian concept of swadhyaya
"In a fast-changing world shaped by AI, volatility and information overload, the ability to understand and regulate your own mind is a key competitive edge.Leaders who cultivate inner stillness create space between events and reactions, leading to clearer thinking, better judgment and less reactive decision-making.Technology will surpass humans in speed and volume of knowledge, but the human edge lies in consciousness itself — awareness, compassion, ethical judgment and creativity born from silence rather than algorithms."
From what I told him, Prof Rao understood that the institute power supply was overloaded. The next time I met Prof Rao, I told him the advantages of going for 66KV supply from KEB. He was asking me for an express feeder from KEB. I told him that we would be amongst ten or twenty consumers if we moved to 66KV. But if we augmented the 11KV supply, we would be one amongst 10000 consumers or more. We get submerged in the consumer pool. We won't get a proper attention from KEB and the supply station near Gymkhana can't meet our increased requirements. At that time, KEB was not planning to expand that substation. So we would be suffering.Further, the 11KV augmentation would be a short term solution for may be another five years. Then we need to find another solution. 
My statement that we would be one amongst 10 consumers appealed to him. We didn't have a comfortable relationship, but he accepted my decisions many times and entrusted several tasks to me like both Prof Dhawan and Prof Ramaseshan, the predecessors to Prof Rao.He gave a green signal. All these discussions happened not in the director's office, but on the road. Decision of great relief was taken in the nice climate amidst a number of trees in the road near IPC department.
So I decided to go ahead with the planning activity. Conversion was not a simple activity. There were many issues to be resolved. The 66kv supply station was not fully ready. Mr Govindappa told us that the station would be operational in a years time. Since our project would take more than a year to be executed completely, I was hopeful that we would get 66kv power to IISc. While I took the most important decision that the future supply to IISc should be 66kv, that didn't end my decision making activity. I needed to look at several issues like-
1.Estimate the  load growth of the 11 existing 11kv/440 volts substations and loads of new buildings planned including the super computer?
2.What new substations were needed?
3.How should we augment the internal 11kv ring main?
4.How should we bring the 66kv supply from KEB station to the institute? It had to come through public roads and also one plus km inside the campus.
5.Where should I locate the new 66kv substation? 
6. Should we extend the 11kv ring to two parts of the campus outside the main area and across public roads? Our campus is a divided one. We have the main campus- a major area housing all academic and research activities and three other parts. Gymkhana was across the Tumkur road. Housing and the kendriya vidyalaya school were in the north part beyond the Mathikere road. In the south, we have the ICE area housing the high voltage labs, consultancy centre, KSCST, and SID.
We decided to get a detailed analysis and design to be handled by a competent agency. We knew the Esquire engineers in Madras and decided to entrust the work to them to prepare  both cost and load estimates , to develop the power networks, design the 66kv/11kv substation in detail and also design additional stations and workout a plan to get connecting line from the KEB station to our new proposed 66/11kv station.

24/03/26, 5:26 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1024
We spent months with the engineers from Esquire engineers to iron out many aspects. I addressed their queries and posed our requirements. They visited the IISc grid, got a personal knowledge of our system and prepared the drawings. We identified the location for the new 66kv station, chose the capacity of the transformer - 5 MVA to meet the load growth for the next 10 years, and decided on the number of 11kv panels and feeders. We identified the new substations. 
We decided to get power from the KEB station by an underground cable outside the institute connected to an overhead line inside the campus. The total distance was about 2.5 kms. We decided to go for two separate tenders- one for the internal systems and another for the external underground 66kv cable including erection. We drafted detailed specifications for transformers, circuit breakers, panels, the substation, earthing,11 kv cables, jointing methods etc.We put a condition that all items should be inspected and tested in our presence.I visited their office in Chennai to approve the drawings. So with all these our tender documents were ready.
I should reveal an important factor here. All decisions were not technical. Human involvement and human understandings and interests were vital for arriving at acceptable decisions. I had lots of problems with people. When we identified the location for the incoming 66kv station. It was in the open area opposite the then CEDT building. CEDT faculty were not happy. Later, CES wanted the space adjacent to our station. I had to tell them 'sorry.Not possible.'
Similarly location of the new 11kv substation for the new supercomputer centre was proposed in the basement of the building. So I had to call Prof Rajaraman for the meeting of the power committee. I told him that it won't be permitted due to fire hazards to locate it in the basement. I said we would locate it outside. He agreed immediately.
I spent several days on the cost estimates as I realized it to be a big point. The initial cost came close to 2 crores. We reduced it to 1.5 crores. Later, we had discussions with CPWD as they had to approve our tender document and cost estimation and convinced them. I had to go to Delhi to talk to them and convince them. I got the budget sanction from the director within a few days and started the process of preparing a detailed tender document. I believe in strict eligibility conditions for selecting a suitable vendor. We used a two part tender- technical and financial parts.
Since I put an essential condition that the tenderer should have experience in building and operating many 11kv substations, many local electrical contractors working in the institute and many contractors working on our buildings - a good number- were upset. They met Mr Muni venkataswamy, our estate officer and represented their problems. I vetoed their request and said that I couldn't dilute the condition- requirement of experience in building 11kv stations. So they were not issued the tender documents.
Prof MN Srinivasan became the chief of admin, but the approvals came from the director. Later MNS was involved in a few decisions. He supported me completely.
We scrutinised the vendors who applied. We had only three eligible vendors. We opened the financials and found Bombay suburban electric company, a reputed company running half Bombay's electrical distribution systems. 
Again I met Prof Rao on the way back to my house on the road and told him about Bombay suburban. He said ok, and gave a go ahead signal. Since he was leaving the next day, I took all the relevant papers, told our FC. He signed the document. Immediately, the registrar signed it. I took it to Prof Rao and got his written approval- all done within half an hour. The purchase order was issued the next day. It was fast work without delays- all done in a day.

25/03/26, 4:32 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  1025
The above narration of starting a project gives us many learnings:
1. There will be many hurdles, the biggest being human ego related issues. 
2. We need to establish a need for the project, get it accepted by the top management and concerned people in the organization. This was tough for me in core banking applications.
3.All relevant people won't be on the same page. We saw some examples, and will see more. Convince them.
4. Establish working rules, principles, practices and work culture. Make them transparent. Train involved persons. Involve users from the project stage onwards. It has been shown that transformation projects fail due to poor people policies and understanding. Remember every project has a culture, may be different from the organization. That is why we go for outsourcing. I couldn't implement the 66kv project internally. I couldn't use our technicians to even operate the system once completed. The work culture and ethics were poor. So I outsourced operations. It is continuing even now.
5.. Every step needs contemplation, discussions, reviews.
6.Every step involves many decisions. It was based on not only technicalities but also acceptance by the people. Most may not have data.So heuristics plays a big role. 
7. Project planning and execution are dependent on anticipations - performance of components, skills of workers, competence of vendors, subcontracting, payments and financial problems, approvals, market problems, cost escalations etc. So futuristic thinking is essential. Guess the trends and dynamics. It is micro level trend estimation that is important. I should know Bangalore's growth to decide on 66kv.
8. Project has lots of components and steps. Each needs different thinking. A step may involve technical aspects, HR problems,time management, human psychology, finance issues, regulations,taxes,and harmony. This is what Charlie Mungar called Lattice theory- multidimensional and multidisciplinary as well as multiple options based thinking and acting. He also looks at failure analysis as an important characteristics of problem thinking. He also emphasizes looking at alternate paths and solutions.
9. Implementation is outsourced to an agency. We can not sit and wait for completion. We need many check points - to review progress, to give approvals, to select and inspect components, to inspect testing, to inspect constructions, to resolve differences, to clear misunderstandings, to motivate the teams, to use escalation levels properly etc. As the involved party, we should be there everyday both physically and mentally.
10.  Project management is a conscious activity- not a routine one. It contains surprises, uncertainties, many unknowns,market situations etc. It wants hundreds of decisions. So constant, deep and conscious multidimensional thinking is a must. It is a highly cerebral activity. Each project has some uniqueness, some differences, different cultures, some new situations. We need to address this. I encountered these when I handled core banking projects for many banks.
11. People involvement is a critical factor in project management. Involve the top management in decisions and bind them. Keep them aware of the progress. Give realistic assessments.
12. Know your vendors. Agency may be reputed but the operating team may be at lesser level of competencies. Leadership may be inefficient. Time management may be poor. Work discipline may not exist. Egos may be high. Conflicts may occur if the leader is not strong. I have handled these kinds of problems in many projects. This was my biggest headache in several projects.
We will continue in the next post.
Greetings. Dks

26/03/26, 4:22 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1026
Let me continue with our new 66kv  power project.After we issued the work order for in campus work to Bombay suburban, we issued another tender for the 66kv underground cable to connect from KEB to the IISc boundary. This was decided as a separate tender. We got responses from Gloster, Siemens , Birla and a fourth cable company Premier. It gave us a surprise. We qualified all four. I had purchased cables from Birla and Siemens and Gloster was a well known manufacturer of HV cables. Premier cables located in Kochi was a surprise. While others quoted around a crore for our work, Premier quoted 50 lakhs rupees- half of others. So I was uncomfortable. We couldn't reject the company as the cost was attractive. We decided to investigate. A big team - Prof MNS, Prof Nagabhushana of our High voltage engineering department, our estate officer , Mr Thimme Gowda and I went to Kochi to inspect the plant. 
We met Mr Parthasarathy,the MD of the company. He was from railways and was the first director of karnataka computer centre.I knew him before. After seeing the operations, we decided to give the contract to them. I was having some misgivings and expressed it to MNS. I said the operations are done in blind and we didn't know the density of the cable in the entire length. But others accepted the quality and I was over ruled. I also ensured that they can draw a 1.1 km long cable. They needed a special cable holder for that. So we gave them the order.
In between I encountered another problem. Prof Chandrasekariah from our HV department met me and told me that we should go for 11kv supply from that substation instead of the 66kv supply. Here is a dilemma- cost vs future proofing. I told him that I wanted reliable and trouble free supply for the next 20 years. I wanted a long term plan and not expensive,but feasible. I left it at that. He didn't stop there. He talked Mr BG Rudrappa, the then chairman of KEB. I knew Rudrappa well, but didn't talk to him. Mr Rudrappa met Prof Rao and told him to go for 11kv and also told him that 11kv was more than enough to meet the institutes power demands.
So Prof Rao as usual saw me walking to the department in the morning and told me the discussions with the KEB chairman. I simply asked him - do you want IISc to be one amongst 10000 customers of KEB or one in ten. That convinced him. He said go ahead. So I crossed that hurdle also.
Taking that decision has stood the test of time. That station is still serving the Institute with robust power supply with fewer interruptions.
I didn't think my job over with work orders issued. I was onto details of orders of components. I also inspected all components at the manufacturing and assembly sites. That meant a lot of travels. I went to Bombay a number of times- transformers, panels, etc, Nasik for switch gear, Chennai for load break switches - that was krishnamachari Srikant's factory- , kochi for 66kv cable, Sanchi for 11kv cables etc. 
I had a problem with the transformer test at Cromptons- Mr Nagarkatti, our works manager at Easun was the works manager here and Ananthasubramanian,who joined Easun with me was in their designs group. I wasn't happy and we signed the test certificate with conditions - that was useful in the next year.

Post 1027
We completed the project within a one and half years and got it inaugurated by CNR. It was smooth and the system operations was outsourced to Bombay suburban. That was my firm decision.
Just a year after operations, the transformer failed. We didn't have a backup system. So I initiated the repair at Cromptons in Bombay. They wanted payments. I told the VP that is was within warrantee period, the fault was an interturn fault, a manufacturing defect and we had put a query in the test report. He agreed to repair it fast and free. 
Mr K C Reddy joined as an advisor to the estate office. He decided to send Mr Thimme Gowda back to KEB. The new registrar asked me about it. I said that I wasn't involved in that decision. But it was done and couldn't be changed. We were without a good electrical engineer. Fortunately BSES was managing the substation. But there was another big mishap that happened in this period after the transformer failure.
BSES engineer decided to do maintenance work on a pole near  A and B blocks of our staff housing colony near the ECE department. Line clear was not done properly and two workers were electrocuted. I visited the site. It brought bad publicity for us. We had to make BSES pay proper compensation to the families. We put the money in FD and gave the FDs to the wives of both persons. It was an avoidable mistake, a big operational blunder.
So BSES decided to leave our work and they left after a year and we got Mr Kumar to operate the system.This model is working well now also, not only in IISc but also in some other institutions.
This major project planning and implementation experience helped me in designing the substation at sri Jayadeva Institute of Cardiology in the late nineties when their new campus was coming up. I also did another set of projects for karnataka PWD, strengthening the power supply for Vidhana Soudha and Vikas Soudha in the early 2000. I also completely redesigned a cluster based power system for JN Centre for advanced scientific research in 2000.
Let me look at my other major foray into computer science education. Slowly, one by one, engineering colleges were starting computer science programs, but without qualified CS faculty. I decided to help several colleges. I started teaching in Nitte and BDTCE Davangere. Prof Bhandary was the principal of BDT and he requested me to give a course to his students on data structures. Similarly, I taught two courses in Nitte and became a good friend of Prof Sridhar Hegde, principal and Radhakrishnan , HoD.I used to stay in Karkala and give lectures. I maintained my contacts with Nitte for a long time.
While I was lecturing in Nitte in the afternoon after lunch and a visit to Gomateswara, climbing about 300 steps, I felt chest pain. So after returning to Bangalore, I got a treadmill test done by Dr Ramesh, cardiologist. It showed positive signs of blocks in coronary arteries. So I was suggested to get an angiogram done. It could be done only in Bombay then. 
So we went to Bombay leaving the children alone in Bangalore and met Dr Nair. He wanted the angio to be done the next day. We stayed in BSES guest house. The angio was done in the national hospital and it showed a block of 60% in the right ventricular artery, not a dangerous situation. Dr Nair gave me a prescription of medicines and aspirin. I have been continuing those till now.
I returned back, the expenditure was heavy and the institute didn't reimburse my expenses.
In 1986, I was invited to teach computer courses to IIM Bangalore PG diploma students. It was a new experience. I taught in IIM for four years. Prof Kaujalgi helped me on admin issues. I found travel difficult and so stopped teaching after four years. So my taking computer science across the state started well.

28/03/26, 3:57 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post    1028
Let me discuss one aspect of the project that strengthened my learning skills.The question that confronted me - do I know the complete reality of the project? My answer is no. I crossed the bridges when I came to them.
We learn to get a correct reality of a problem. We should know how to learn. Why we learn is to obtain a big picture of reality. What we learn is reality. Let us see how.
One great thing that I learnt as a child was about learning. Most people believe that you learn everything from books alone. That will give you bookish knowledge, not even partial understanding. You need to analyze what you read, find errors, ask questions like a child and write down what you learnt. Can we write down after reading a book on thermodynamics, what is thermodynamics? Can we become a teacher- as Feynman said- and explain thermodynamics to a child? Have we seen thermodynamics in operation? Do we know the context?
But many strongly believe that you get correct knowledge from books. My friend, Mr Jayakumar Anagol had strong beliefs or trust on books. Once I told him that   when cow dung is digested through a biogas plant, it gives three times more Nitrogen than open composted manure and he refused to believe me. I was giving him facts obtained from ASTRAs experiments. He said, 'show me a book with that information'. After a few days, I found it in a book and showed it to him. He accepted my statement.
He is better than many. He contemplated on what he read- he was reading regularly many books. So he understood better and retained a lot of information and he recalled them regularly. 
But most people don't retain what they read. Most students and faculty pick up terms from their reading - they know the term thermodynamics or electrons and can't describe them to a child. Their perceptions are poor.
I realised that reading a book is one small step in our learning process. It may create illusions. Reading itself is not properly done. I believe
Reading involves thinking;
Reading involves pauses;
Reading involves contemplation;
Reading involves preparation?
Reading involves questioning? Why am I reading this? How does this topic fit with the subject?what is its role, impact?where can I apply it? What are the advantages, disadvantages, impacts, errors in the description?
Reading implies explaining.
Reading leads to supplementary reading, discussions, listening to teachers, analysing, thinking, experimenting, looking for practical situations, looking for anecdotes etc. It is a serious business.
But understanding is multidimensional- book knowledge, crowd understandings, experiences, data collection, detective work, changing assumptions, models etc.
Understanding is a continuous process.
In this 66kv project, I didn't anticipate many things- opposition to the 66kv project; half the cost of 66kv cable, Cromptons reluctance in testing the transformer,some contractors' behaviors, several mindsets, great support from KEB and Institute, my heart problem etc.
We should expect unexpected and take the challenges head on. I have experienced many such incidents in many projects handled by me. We don't have full knowledge. We go on a discovery trip during the implementation. That was a big learning- flexible,open and alert mind, preparedness, cooperative spirit, team discussions etc.
Greetings. Dks
29/03/26, 4:17 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1029
TRUTH is the base that bears the earth; by Sūrya are the heavens sustained.
By Law the Ādityas stand secure, and Soma holds his place in heaven.
 By Soma are the Ādityas strong, by Soma mighty is the earth.
Rig Veda 10.85
To continue on yesterdays post on learning:
1.one of the purposes of learning is  to create a correct and complete- to the extent possible- of the overall reality of the problem or situation or concept you are looking at. Learning takes us from simple understanding to analysis to applications to innovations. It kindles our curiosity.
What is reality? It describes a problem or a concept or a theory completely and correctly. It is a localised reality , not universal.
Let me illustrate the building up of a reality based on our 66kv project:
Starting point: details about our existing power supply and distribution system in detail. This was got through technical knowledge and field study.
Discover the problem: frequent interruptions. It was due to over loading of KEB supply. 11kv quality was poor. This was got through experiences and events.
Solution - look at what is available.
Discovery - 66kv feeder available at dollar colony close to the institute. Start work on getting 66kv feeder to IISc. This was based on discussions.
Solution- build a new substation and connecting lines and cables. Design the supply system form KEB to the new substation. This was a pure technical activity.
What next- get approvals. Prepare plan get cost estimates using an outsourced agency. Get budget approval. Prepare tender document. Discover vendors, technologies, components etc. Build ring fencing.Select vendor. Get approval. Start work. All these are based on information, discussions, data, rules and procedures and activities.
We build reality step by step here. This can be enlarged running into hundreds of pages.
2. Learning needs an open and flexible mind.
3. Learning involves thinking, curiosity,contemplation, questioning, analytical mind, precision and assimilation of knowledge. Learning should lead to wisdom, not just a clerk or manager or worker of a small world like a typist or accountant.
4.Learning is a multi track activity, not a single track. Class room lectures or reading books alone will not give you a picture of completeness of the aspect you are learning. Books will not help us to apply the theory easily. You need to blend theory, availability, practicality, capability , human behaviour, resources, and real life happenings to get a solution, not just analytical intelligence alone. We mix reading, listening, observing, practical experiences, real life data, human behaviors, modeling, analysis, assessing, judging and arriving at/ deducing decisions.Learning is said to make an unknown into a known one.
5. Don't go for assumptions, simplifications and unified theories like one size fits all always. It provides an artificial reality- a maya. These concepts are useful in some situations. Examples are Newton's laws, Maxwell's equations etc. But they don't give a clarity of understanding sometimes. Maxwell's equations couldn't predict ferro resonance. 
Greetings. Dks
30/03/26, 3:54 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1030
HS Nagaraja comments:
Good morning sir. One of your statements in today's post "Most people don't retain what they read" , I agree as I am one among them.
My response
That statement was written in deep anguish, not a casual one. I expect teachers to know the subject and teach it to students. I expect teachers to have more knowledge than students. I saw in close proximity the devotion, sincerity and hard work with a phenomenal mental effort in my uncle, a teacher in my childhood. My family had many teachers and scholars and respected knowledge and instilled it to others freely without any expectations. I had seen my father consoling people throughout his life many people in distress by quoting the appropriate slokas and verses from Ramayana. I saw many going back happy. He didn't do it for money. It was a human duty for him.These impressed me and built an altruistic tendency to teach and transfer knowledge to whosoever wants it. I believed in the Rig vedic dictum, ' knowledge is free.'
I have seen many great teachers with indepth knowledge and understanding in my primary school and high school days. In my engineering college days, I can name only one- Prof Ramaswamy who had an intense desire to learn new things like radio and teach it to students even though it wasn't in the syllabus. I have seen several great teachers in IISc, Bangalore university, and engineering colleges. Some names from some engineering colleges are - Prof MNC, KREC,Prof KA Krishnamurthy, MCE,Hassan, Prof Ramapriyan, NIE,Mysore. That made me happy.
There are many great teachers I saw and listened to in IISc like Prof C Devanathan, Prof.AR Vasudeva murthy, Prof HNR, Prof VSV, Prof R Narasimha, Dr MD Srinath etc. What I admired in most faculty are their depth of knowledge, deep thinking, their scholarship, their way of organizing their lectures, their delivery, their experimentation and research capabilities. A lecture by these was worth listening to.I cherish my association with them.
But I had seen many in IISc itself who used to look and read from their notes in the class without explanations and even without feeling. I doubted whether they knew what they were teaching. One professor used to teach klystron in every course he taught whether it is digital or space communications. Another dictated notes in the class.
Let me look at one incident.Students complained to me about one faculty who was assigned to teach the data structures course. He was talking about supercomputers and didn't teach them data structures at all and ten lectures were over. I had to call him and tell him to follow the course outline and not what he wanted to talk about.
One of our common friends used to talk politics in the class - he was teaching automata and talked about GDP during the entire lecture, not an occasional aside and that too in a short term course to teachers. Once, his students protested and walked out of his exam.He used to confuse the students.That pained me.
 But the magnitude of cut and paste that was used by the teachers in most colleges destroyed me. I saw a total disinterest there in teaching.You come to a profession or job. Should you not be sincere to that job? I saw this disease everywhere and felt devastated. Prof V Krishnan once told me In the mid seventies,'Indians are good teachers by tradition. But that is no longer true.' How do we get that back is my worry. 
Gurukulas taught in depth and teaching was highly devoted with attention to each student. Each village had several gurus and several gurukulas, not just one school. UV Swaminathaiyer in his autobiography said that he learnt from several gurus in different places, several subjects like music, Sanskrit classics and Tamil classics. How do we bring that culture? We are happy that we destroyed that culture and we genuinely feel that is progress. Even VS Naipaul accepted that theory of progress.
Feynman blames text books for this state. He said that text books teach us just words, not knowledge. His examples, given from real school text books are
1.What makes a dog move? 
Answer    energy. 
Do you comprehend that as a child?
2.The second question was: why shoes become worn out with use? 
The answer - friction. 
Do you understand? A better answer would be - explain the process.
The earth is rough. The shoes scratch in the earth. The force pulls small pieces of leather away. So the shoes become worn out. 
Since we pick up only terms and jargon and not real understanding, we don't learn in our schools and we lose retention power. 
I will conclude with a shocking episode. 
I spent a week and interviewed about 160 assistant professors in an engineering college. One question that I asked each was- what general book did you read last year? The answer was none. Reading habit is dead for many. 
Another question was - why do you teach the subject that you are handling?
I got mostly stupid answers. Most couldn't explain why. Many said that the subject is important. Why don't we train them to think?
Those who read and retain knowledge move up the success ladder.
That is the reason I wrote the last post.
Thanks Nagaraja for making me open up.
Greetings. Dks
31/03/26, 4:18 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1031
What are the steps to get a first level of understanding of a concept?
You start with an Intuitive level, then move to a basic level, and then to an analytical level- questioning level and then to a deeper level. Don't look at it as a specialist.
1. Create a habit of reading. Create interest in the book you read.Create a conducive atmosphere. Clear your mind. Build a feeling of concentration- yoga and meditation help here. Then read or listen.Attention is crucial to understanding. Without attention, one doesn't learn anything. Mind should absorb the text and develop a mental picture. That is the preliminary step of learning.Gurukulas used to teach it. My teachers in my childhood used to say-,"don't look anywhere- in Tamil they used a phrase parakku for wandering mind- listen carefully " to anyone who was looking elsewhere. Attention leads to concentration and focus.
2. After reading a para or page, pause. Start thinking.
Ask-What I read is relevant, correct, useful or biased or trash?
Ask yourself what did I read- which area? What does it mean? Does it agree with the usual theories? What is its advantage,impact or applications.
Ask- what is it trying to convey? Can I explain or write an abstract of my learning. Abstraction is a key skill needed for a learner or researcher.
3. Ask is this correct. Visualise. Find analogies.Start building a big mental picture. 
4. You don't retain verbatim what you read, but the essence of the page or chapter say 2% retention. Go outside and experience it if possible.
Learning gives us knowledge.
Let us see the learning methods:
I.Knowledge gathering through practical experiences like visits and observations Know and describe facilities - post offices, taluk offices, banks, school , 
Know and describe supplies - water, energy, milk, food grains, vegetables etc
Know and describe activities like harvesting, cattle grazing , travel etc
Know and describe  personal tasks and activities. 
Identify problems and solutions at a later time. 
Describe your house
II. Observational learning See how elders work, discuss problems - normal like getting a registration in a sub registrar's office done , the processes involved, why village panchayat exists, what the shops are doing, how post offices work , why and how money transactions are done etcSee how functions are conductedSee how houses are constructed See what all can be sent by post, by parcel how. Why do you register a property in sub registrars office Observe negotiations on prices , property distributions.
 III. Situational learning - emergencies
Power supply fails,No milk supply , No water supply ,Heavy rains and flooding , Being stranded alone in the early night , Thefts, Legal aspects
 IV. Learning planning
 -Prepare plans for conducting Deepavali, birthday party, marriages, temple festivals V. Process learning
 Festivals - what are things done why Functions like marriages, ram navami, etc
School day
 VI. Learning from books
 Summarise what you learn. Ask questions, get answers ,Write critiques VII. Discussions with friends - cricket matches, cinemas, VIII thinking Decide where should one go for vacation, what to see, why Decide what to cultivate , when ,why, how.Decide what to buy , why , Decide on how to eat, what to eat, why to eat , what options are available, costs, affordability etc.
The more difficult question is what to learn.
Greetings. Dks

Let me get back to IISc and continue with my academics.
I spent more of my time on outside ,but I made substantial contributions to IISc. I introduced many new courses. 
One of my major contribution was changing the name of the school of automation into department of computer science and automation - CSA. I attended the institute level meeting and argued the reasons for the change. It was accepted. IISc decided yo wind up the BE program and instead start a 4 year integrated ME program. I also chaired a committee to prepare the curriculum for this new four year program.
Due to my involvement with CES, I visited Sirsi often. We got involved in planning the building and recruiting many staff for CES. I attended a one-week workshop in Delhi on environmental management. It was a change to hear many commoners giving their perception of the environment. One night three of us, one of them was a lady, wanted to go for a walk at 930pm. We went for a walk and came back, no incident. But next morning, we were warned that people were watching us. We were told there was a lot of violence in the streets at that time. We were lucky.I also visited Andhra university to discuss the proposal for environmental data management system. We got one project for CES.
 CES started on energy in a big way. We did a survey and data collection in some villages. Later TV Ramachandra did a serious data collection for his PhD. Madhav decided to popularise the newly developed high efficient wood stove named ASTRA ole. Mr Sastry was made responsible for that activity.
Father C J Saldanha moved to CES. He started a state of the environment report. I had solid contributions in that activity for the five years the report was produced.
Let me discuss my family activities.I moved into E7,a ground floor apartment with garage. We had a good garden space in the front. Dr Subba Rao stayed on the first floor-E8. I got them bicycles to go to school. I got them swimming pool passes so that they could go swimming regularly. They also saw movies in Gymkhana and I took them for a few movies. My children got settled well here, taking care of school activities, and playing with friends. They became independent. They got independent rooms.They used to go to the faculty club to borrow books. So I was very happy their reading habits became strong. I bought lots of books- Russian books, Amar Chitra katha, and story books. My son became meek by this time, bullied by his friends.
The children were taken by us to several places- Madras,Mysore, Bandipur, Sirsi, Kumta, Mercara,Hubli, Ungra village, Hassan, Belur, Shravana belegola, Kemmanagundi, Jog falls, Bombay, Pune,Kolkata, and later to Madurai,Delhi, Simla, and many other places. So they had a good exposure of the country. I took my son to Davanegere to BIET when I went for KSCST inspection for students projects. Prof Vrushabendrappa offered a seat in his college for my son when he would have completed 12th.
Our social life improved with many neighbors becoming friendly.All of us built a lot of friendships. So we had a peaceful and contented family life.It was really a great and enjoyable life for all of us.
My friendship with Major Somasekhara grew. He helped me a lot with my house construction.He repaired my furniture and made a big sofa set for my house.

02/04/26, 4:20 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  1033
The period 1986 87 was an exciting period taking me to new paths and territories. 
I started teaching real time systems which became a focus for my research for the next decade plus in Computer science. It included data bases and transactions processing. That was the first the course was offered in India. Mr Jayant, director, AirIndia mentioned about this course at a CSI conference.
I started looking at distributed systems, an evolving area with focus on distributed operating systems , distributed databases and transactions processing. Mr Haldar joined me as a PhD student and worked on distributed transactions processing. 
I started looking at concurrency control at a good depth and we showed that many algorithms in the literature did not maintain mutual exclusion, the main requirement for concurrent processes. We found the concurrency control algorithms in the OS book by Peterson and Silbershaft were wrong. That gave me a big lesson, ' don't accept what is in a book as truth. Analyze it.'
 When I was teaching operating systems from the mid seventies, I concentrated on concurrency. My OS teaching was focusing on algorithms and concurrency handling. I rejected many OS books like Donovan, Tannenbaum,Peterson etc and used the book by Per Brinch Hanson. That book was algorithmic. But it was cryptic. So I sharpened my explanatory skills. I explained every line in the algorithms.I loved the write up on The operating system by Dijkstra, but I couldn't teach it. I also loved the Multics OS book. I included my learnings from Manchester to make my course richer. I also followed a book which gave algorithms to build a complete OS. 
I had one constant thinking- how do I improve my understanding and how do I teach better. This made me look at literature- manuals,books, articles and papers in depth to understand many nuances of OS. I did this with my course on systems programming. I moved away from Donovan's IBM focused book to PDP/11 manuals and Ullmann's book on programming systems, one of his early books. I continued this urge with my course on databases.
I taught the OS course in the evening program of Proficience. I taught every year in Proficience. My course at IIM Bangalore gave me a different exposure to the response and different kinds of thinking of management students - students asked me questions. My lectures and teaching at several engineering colleges to average students exposed me to another human angle- a struggle to keep up with me and understand. Most students took keen interest in my lectures. My lectures at PSTI were again a new experience. I had varied experiences in teaching programming in datamatics. Similarly my lectures at Proficience exposed me to professionals ,totally different from IISc students. They were interested in practices and applications and not theory. They wanted me to clarify their doubts not even related to what topic I was teaching.
I also taught Unix at MIT Manipal and distributed operating systems at REC Trichy. Both were taught for the first time in India.
My travels for teaching increased to many engineering colleges in the state- MCE Hassan, GIT, Belegaum, Andhra university,TCE,Madurai, SV University, Tirupathi and others. I also gave lectures on computers and energy to school students. I continued to give lectures on environment at NSS camps. I assisted Mr Sankarasubramsnian in several ways including visiting villages and explaining to elders there.

03/04/26, 4:09 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1034
This period was also a very productive period. Let me look at a few projects that I was involved in. 
The first one was a joint project with Dr N S Somasekhara and KN Krishnaswamy of our management department in 1984 85. We were looking at solving some problems of electricity and transport in our city. I did a study to minimize the dead mileage for BTS buses. I came up with a simple idea of circle based allocation of buses to depots. I wrote a paper on this and submitted to IFIP conference.
The second one is not a single one, but a sequence of activities on energy. Till now, I was looking at energy at supply end and modeling of energy systems.Now, I shifted my focus of research to the use of energy in reality. I first chose to study the efficiency of energy use in Industries first. I asked TV  Ramachandra, who was working with me in CES, to collect data from 40 industries in Karnataka in order to study the efficiency of use. This was a major activity. He physically visited the industries, measured energy consumption and collected data available with the industry on energy consumption and production. It was a lot of laborious work and Ramachandra did it meticulously. We used energy used per rupee of production. This helped us to compare industries of similar kind like sugar, paper, spinning, oxygen etc. We compared it with international norms. The results created surprises for many people. They didn't believe it. Then we continued with small industries and only rice mills. We found small industries fail due to high energy consumption and high ratios of costs per rupee of production going to energy purchases.
The third one was the study of urban water distribution system for Bangalore, a KSCST project. Dr Satyanarayana collected data from the head office and service stations. That had a lot of gaps and didn't reveal the full picture. He left after a year. I took my BMSCE students Ashok Kumar Kattimani and Ramakrishna- I had four students in that batch who did their BE project with me and I recruited Ramachandra for CES and these two were also from that group. - to work on the project. They worked for about three years and did a marvelous job. 
First, they helped me to clean up the data on consumption. I decided to study consumptions by public fountains as the BWSSB figures were guesses. They experimented and measured water flow in a sampled set of public fountains. That gave us a good estimate. 
Then, we wanted to establish the number of lakes in the city. They visited each listed lake and came back with a shocking figures that out of 150 lakes, only 25 existed at that time and their water spread area had come down drastically. Encroachment was a problem going on for hundred years in Bangalore.
Then they did a number of case studies- 1.Bangalore dairy and its water consumption for cleaning bottles and the floor and machines. We got an estimate of 8 litres of water consumed per litre of milk output;
2. We wanted to estimate the water usage for car washing and the two member team measured water used for each car. They did this in several garages. Based on their study, we estimated the water consumed for car washing in the city.
3. The team did measurements of water use by several industries and that gave us dis aggregated data.
Unfortunately there was no data available on the quantity of water lost due to leakages in pipes. Our estimates showed that the losses may be around 30%.
We estimated the rain water quantity and it would meet about 30% of our consumption. 
Since a lot of water used was for cleaning purposes, we suggested water treatment and supplying to industries. That would reduce consumption by half.
We produced a big report, but none of the recommendations was considered for implementation.

04/04/26, 5:02 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1035 
Kindly recall my involvement with TAP,Karnataka government's path breaking decision on standardizing and advising on computer usage, architecture and selection of computers and software. This panel had difficulties in the first two years of operations - the director playing games and the secretaries non understanding as well as the members non aggressive attitude. The director came up with a proposal favoring a particular specs and a vendor. I opposed it strongly. Dr Srikantan suggested getting the programs converted by TCS and Rajaraman agreeing to the proposal. It was approved. Later the director, a Deputee from Indian Navy, faced a lot of operational issues.  One problem I remember- He rushed to my office one day and told me that a power cable in the computer centre was cut by rats. I went and saw that and told him to get it jointed quickly. He didn't get any support from his officers. So PJ Nayak took another decision. He sent him back to Navy and appointed a young IPS officer as director. That change helped in streamlining the organization. The TAP became involved in its functions. They set up a subcommittee and I became the chair of this subcommittee. The director was its convener and the additional secretary, planning, another member. So the single point decision making became operational and effective.
This involvement of mine became very deep with time. This gave me an excellent opportunity to know the problems and accelerators for the absorption of technologies by society. I got into the driver seat. There were several concerns staying with me for more than two decades.
1. Most electricity boards refused to accept computer based planning and control despite a lot of fast paced developments in the world. I found the culture and comfort zones are stronger than computer algorithms despite their advantages. I found the starting torque required to be very high. It should first simplify the absorption process and then we need facilitators for absorption.
2. Let me look at my involvement in popularizing bio gas plants and solar devices. Prof Vinod Vyasulu also questioned on this.
AKN tried very hard to push bio gas for cooking and put in enormous effort to push it amongst the villagers of Pura. It didn't succeed. We didn't have enough sustained facilitators. Our facilitators got tired and left that task. Once a technology is used by people, they need a support structure. It was missing here.
But ASTRA ole picked up because KSCST employed district level engineers for hand holding and trouble shooting. Also KSCST conducted training programs for more than a decade building local capability to build the stove. Thirdly, usage was similar to the old stoves. So no new learning was required. 
But the same was not true of solar cookers. Despite my effort in getting KIMCO to manufacture it and providing subsidies, solar cooking was not accepted by people. It was a deep cultural issue and also an availability issue.
3. I saw that computer usage was reasonable in research during the seventies and eighties, but computer applications failed in governments, public organizations, banks, industries etc. Commercial applications were complex, not standardised and led to errors. So even though many tried to use computers for several years, they failed. Outsourcing was a good route, but it had problems of trust, competence, sincerity and proper understanding. So computerization didn't work even for computer companies. Computer purchase was a hazardous job till PCs arrived in large numbers in the market at lower and affordable prices. We need multiple sustained actions in many directions to support the absorption of computers by organizations. This was an appropriate time.

05/04/26, 5:03 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1036
https://medium.com/@dksubra/the-implications-behind-learning-and-understanding-and-the-mechanisms-needed-prof-d-k-subramanian-fab36cf9d2ab
I realised the following facts about technology absorption.
1. AKN and all renewable energy people had a mistaken belief. If a technology is good technically, then you can force people to accept it. They thought it to be a push process. But unfortunately we forgot to understand that people are the decision makers, not the goodness of a technology only. It is a pull process.
2. Technology absorption is not a simple process in most cases. It is a complex multidimensional process. 
While technology absorption failed with renewable devices, I was confident that it will succeed definitely and will become the prime engine. Why did I feel that computerization will lead our development and economy and be a great success? There are many reasons:
1. It is not an idealistic push process, but a strong pull process supported by push.
2. It had benefits of several orders of magnitude over existing manual processes. It reduced errors, took decisions faster, dealt with large volumes of data, could do many new functions like alert, data acquisition,control, access, printing, etc. It increased productivity. It helped the top managements. 
3. I already showed its efficacy in several places including the gadag APMC.
4. Manpower was available in large numbers to build software and operate systems 
5. Manufacturers improved the processes.
6. It adapted innovation, alternate approaches easily. It allowed structural changes.
7. It gave power to people and users. Intermediaries were eliminated.
But you should know how to handle the hurdles.
So I came to the conclusion that 
1.the process is riddled with a lot of mistaken beliefs and understandings. Those lead to failures. Educating, advising, hand holding and correcting beliefs are essential to absorb computers in an organization. Enthusiasm is not enough.
2. The supplier side is also riddled with mistaken beliefs and grandiose potentials. I saw several vendors proclaiming that they have found universal solutions for an unsolved problem. That was for security issues. You see the ignorance.
3. There are cultural, organizational, procedural and behavioral issues with computerization. Where ever they are addressed carefully, the computerization activity was successful. 
1985 to 90 was the learning era. We learnt the problems due to computerization in different government departments.
After 1990, most computerization activities of the government were great successes. PCs were available at affordable prices and in large numbers. Many programmers were found in many departments. PCs were used for document preparation and accounting primarily. Servers used COBOL programs.Many departments and organizations like KSRTC, MPM, land army, had computer centres.
Some successes are:
Commercial taxes computerization
CET cell and engineering admission counseling
Mahithisindhu program
Karnataka secondary exam evaluation computerization
Bangalore one, later Sakala
Cauvery software for registration
