06/04/26, 4:40 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post   1037
https://medium.com/@dksubra/my-experiments-with-computerization-of-karnataka-government-departments-units-and-organizations-77e4690c598e

Let me discuss my changed approach to handle the computerization work in the Karnataka government.
My axiom was , ' don't go with prefixed ideas or past knowledge when looking at a new problem'.
You might have developed a process and found some ideas helpful in a case, but you may not succeed in another case by blindly following that process. 
Each case may have some nuances, some differences, some new behaviors of people, some new situations. So we need to adapt to those variations with an open mind. 
So the most important step is to build the context for each case. Then build a new process. Be prepared to change the process midway.
But don't change certain important principles. Principles should be evolved over a period of time and they should be applied uniformly. That gives credibility to your process. 
Some important principles are- communicate, be transparent, maintain fairness and integrity, discuss and convince, listen to them, know their problems and limitations, and uniform yard sticks.
Another axiom that I followed was, don't distrust all in an organization.I firmly believe that every organization and every government office has a few people who work sincerely- in fact the success of that office depends on them as they are honest, sincere and believe in their job. They may be god fearing and duty conscious. I have seen this many offices. Also, there are some thinking officers in each office who want to improve the procedures. I found most IAS, IPS and KAS officers to be smart, sincere and keen on improvements. I built my process based on them.
So I started on people-focused processes, not just only on solutions. I focused on a totally new area- interactions with people, spending 1000s of hours on this and making it a major task and negotiations with vendors. I needed different skillsets for these two tasks of interaction and negotiation. I started on that journey. 
The first step - the director KGCC should be on board. If he or she is not convinced, it is difficult to move forward. Most directors were either IAS officers like Vijayakumar and Ramana Reddy or  IPS officers like Sharat Saxena and MN Reddy. The only exception was Mr Shantappa. He couldn't handle the job.I had phenomenal rapport with all except one. All believed me and tried to understand my arguments. Similarly the additional secretaries from planning also were helpful.I had several IAS officers there. 
Second- the chairs of TAP were all good, helpful and cooperative. They wanted to promote IT.
Third- The difficult step was buyer education. It is the toughest job. Human ego, beliefs, commitments, permanence in that job etc mattered. Where there was synergy, interest and commitment, we had success. Commercial tax computerization was a great example of this. 
Fourth- The next people problem was vendors. This was not easy. Fortunately, I found many who were committed and true to their words. They realized that we were giving them opportunities and accepted it. But a few were bad.
Then I moved to purchase procedures. This was a tricky situation.
My proposition was that the TAP approves the configurations, numbers and software etc and the concerned departments buy the computers and other associated devices and packages. I didn't want a centralized purchases, as that would lead to delays and corruption. 
But leaving purchases to individual departments led to many problems. The departments didn't have expertise in computer purchases- who are good vendors, what is a correct price, etc. The usual three quotation process was myopic and led to considerable differences in the prices of same type of computers purchased by different departments. This led us to think and find solutions.
1.So I concentrated on standardizing computer specifications.
2. We centralized negotiations by our subcommittee to short list reliable manufacturers - we rejected assemblers- and to get their prices. We publicized these to all departments.
3. We moved into a rate contract mode. We started following a two part system. We were the first to start rate contract and two part tendering. This simplified purchases by all departments.
Let me look at my activities with KSCST. I started advising KSCST on computer purchases. DR Ramesh was handling the computer purchases and he took my advice and my approval seriously. I even helped DST to buy servers at a low cost. The cost in Delhi was three times the cost in Bangalore. So that reflects the effectiveness of our system. It was not followed by any other state or city. Lateron , many used the rate contract system.
Greetings. Dks

I am still in 1985 86 period.By this time , I decided to buy a car. I started learning to drive from Mani motors. After about two months of driving practice, I appeared for the test and got a driving license. Then I started on my car purchase. I looked at options- maruthi 800, dolphin and fiat. I was keen on dolphin by Sipany motors, a local car manufacturer. I even visited their factory. But maintenance was a big problem. Resale value was zero. So I thought of maruthi 800 but it was not available, booking closed. So I booked a maruthi van. But prof SN Rao, my colleague in the department, got a maruthi van and it turned turtle on the highway while driven by his son. Also there were problems with dust and straw collected by the fan. So I bought a Fiat car manufactured by Premier automobiles and named as Premier padmini. It was a good one and booked through Sundaram motors, a TVS company.I gave away my allotment of maruthi van to a friend of Mr Thimme Gowda.
After I bought the car, I drove it home, and unfortunately hit a car. So I felt I had to get more training in driving. We had a driver working in ASTRA by name Kodandapani. I got him to help me learn to drive. I spent the evenings for more than two months to get a good level of driving skill.We used to drive for about 40 to 70 kms to get endurance. I also learnt to drive in crowded areas.He was a great help for me.

I had discontinued my visits to TCS. I became busy with conferences. The biggest was the IFIP international conference on CAD CAM CAE organized by CSI.  It was followed by many conferences of both computer society and the Institution of engineers. 
I became a member of TC7 of IFIP. I submitted a paper to the TC7 conference in Tokyo and also had to attend the TC7 meeting. So I started planning my visit scheduled in the summer of 1986.
Gen Balasubramanian suggested a travel agent, one of the best I had seen till now. He told me that the charges for Tokyo and LA were the same. So he suggested that I should book a ticket up to LA, not just to Tokyo. He said that the best fare that was available was for Thai airways and so wanted me to book by Thai airways and not Singapore airlines.He agreed to all my requests.I said that I wanted to go to Bangkok first, then to Hongkong, then to Tokyo ,then to LA,US, stay for 10days and then to Singapore, to Chennai and Bangalore. Since Thai airways was flying from Kolkata, I had to go to Kolkata from Bangalore and then to take the flight to Bangkok.Bangalore was yet to get direct international connections. We had to struggle for another decade for international travel. We had to go via Mumbai or Delhi or Chennai or Kolkata, no other way possible.The ticket was booked. But I don't know where I would go, where would I stay and what would I do?
Now started my serious task of planning and logistics at each place. My cousin and family lived in Hongkong and so I had no problem there. I wrote to the CS department in AIT,Bangkok and arranged a talk there. Similarly I arranged a talk on rural energy in the rural development department. I got positive responses for my two talks in AIT and so two days were taken care of. My stay and travel to and fro to airport would be taken care of by AIT. 
I wrote to the organizer of the conference and he suggested a hotel for my stay. Now my arrangements at US and Singapore were pending.
I wrote to Prof AV Balakrishnan who was also a member of TC7 and he agreed to fix a lecture at UCLA. I wrote to UC Berkeley and one lecture on rural energy was arranged there. I wrote to Prof Tom Kailath and he said I could visit there for a discussions, but no lecture was possible at that time in the CS department. Then my student CP Ravikumar told me that he got admissions in USC, LA. He said that his supervisor was Raghavendra. He was also my student. So I wrote to CS Raghavendra and he arranged a lecture. He also informed me that another student of mine, Ram Chellappa was also there. So my lectures were fixed. But my stay wasn't fixed and I had no clue where I would be staying in LA for 10 days.

08/04/26, 4:05 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.   1039
I started applying for visas. Fortunately, I had to go to Chennai for all four visas- to Japan and the USA, Thailand and Singapore. My mother and sister were living in Chennai near the US consulate. So it was easy for me. 
I had some difficulty at the Japanese consulate. I didn't have an official letter in a letterhead, but a copy from a telex machine. They didn't believe in me at the entrance of the consulate. Arguments ensued. Finally the Indian clerk at the front office decided to take it to his boss. I showed them my tickets. He went inside, talked to someone and they issued me a tourist visa. 
The Singapore and Thai visas were easy. Then I went to the US consulate. I told them about my lectures at USC, UCB and UCLA. So I got the visa easily. 
Then I got a great relief and surprise and coincidence. Those who don't believe that there is a big conscious force operating on all, would be surprised. As I was about to leave the consulate , I saw a person walking in. It was still an easy time to get into the consulate, almost a walk in, not like now. 
That person introduced himself to me - his name is E Srivatsan. He said he was my student from the biochemistry department, took my math course and got 100 marks from me. He said he was in LA. See my relief. Then I told him that I would be going to LA. He invited me to his house. He said he would pick me from the LA airport and take me to his house. I took his phone number. My worry gone. I got a place to stay. He would take care of me. That was a big relief and I came back happy.
I was leaving in June 1986 and all the preparations were going on. But by march, I had almost everything stitched up- my travel by Thai airways, stay in different places including hotel accommodation in Tokyo, lectures etc. I didn't have enough foreign exchange. I got around 500 dollars from SBI. I had to go to SBI main office and spend a day to get the dollars. I took it as travellor's cheques.
Suddenly, I found Chakrapani one day in the department. He was working at the university of Red lands. It was close to LA. I invited him home for lunch. Dr Mahishi also joined. Chakrapani invited me to his house. He told me he would pick me from Srivatsan's house and dropped me back.
That was the year Gymkhana introduced T shirts. While email was yet to come in and laptops and mobiles were still far away, T shirts made entry by that time. My wife's cousin, Rajan, was going to LA then. He wanted the T shirts. They weren't ready. So I told him that I would bring them when I came to LA.
PPTs hadn't arrived by then. So I prepared my talks on transparencies.I started that from 1978 onwards and had prepared thousands of these sheets containing contents of my talks and lectures. I started using the overhead projector for my talks at conferences. Still OHP was years away from engineering colleges. We started using OHP quite early ,say mid 70s in IISc. Almost every department had a projector. But class rooms weren't fitted with projectors. The reason was the high cost of transparency sheets. Initially, we wrote on a rolling, thin sheet of plastic, erase the written material and reuse it. It wasn't effective. Only by late 80s , It became popular at IISc and IITs. 
We talked about technology absorption. But how many institutions and how many teachers used a slide projector in the 60s and 70s. We used it under our extension lecture program even in the village schools in the late seventies. I had several hundred slides which I used in schools. Even in the institute, only a few used these aids. We had to push its use in colleges during the TEQIP days. Teachers were reluctant to do the extra work. Even PPTs were used much later. I think even today PPT use is minimal in schools and colleges. Why? Even though we know the teaching aids to be effective, our teachers don't use them and stick to the traditional chalk and talk method.
So by May, I was ready to leave for this long trip.

09/04/26, 4:51 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post            1040
Since I had a meeting of the national executive of the Institution of Engineers at Hyderabad just before the start of my long overseas journey, I decided to go from Hyderabad to Kolkata to start my onward journey. 
I met Prof AK Rao in Hyderabad. He was the director of the engineering staff college established by the Institution of Engineers.
I didn't carry a big luggage. I had just two medium sized bags. I was carrying a lot of items for my cousin in Hongkong. I had a few dresses only. I stayed with my uncle in Kolkata. At that time, both national and international airports were in the same building in Kolkata and Chennai unlike Bombay and Delhi.I took the Thai flight to Bangkok. It was a short flight and a comfortable flight. I got Tofu and Thai curry, not Indian food. I liked the food.
On arrival at Bangkok, a driver from AIT picked me and dropped me in their guest house. Everything was smooth.
I had an opportunity to see the campus the next day. It was a nice and clean campus. I met Prof Sadananda and Prof Nanda and another professor from India. I knew Sadananda before when he was with JNU. Prof Nanda was from Roorkee.Prof Phien organized the my lecture. After my talk , I was taken to a restaurant for lunch. It was a shock for me. It was only non-vegetarian food and I could take rice and eat rice without anything. Nobody bothered about me. I ate rice and took beer. 
The next day, I gave a talk to another group on biomass technologies. Prof Desingu Setty had organized my talk. He gave me lunch in the guest house itself and we had veg fried rice and some juice. I left for airport in the evening to go to Hongkong. Along with me a friend of Sadananda from Malaysia also came to the airport. He wanted my opinion on manufacturing cDOT model exchanges. I told him that CDOT exchanges were not popular. He was pushed into entering into an agreement. I told him not to rush into it. He was happy and gave me 1000 ringit for that advice.
So One lap of journey was completed successfully.
Bangkok airport had crowded check in counters at that time. I checked in and got on the flight. I reached Hongkong at 11pm. My cousin's husband Subramanian, working in UCO bank, received me at the airport and took me to his house. 
Next day, I spent with them. We visited the city centre, bought a few things for me including a suitcase. We went for a short boat ride in the ocean. I left for Tokyo the next morning.
Greetings. Dks
10/04/26, 4:49 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post 1041
 https://medium.com/@dksubra/what-is-a-startup-mindset-2ba3501deaaf
I arrived at Narita airport from Hongkong, not a long flight. It was a great , modern and easy to move around airport, somewhat similar to Paris De Gaulle airport. It was much better designed than Heathrow or Schipol airports. 
One of my co passengers told me that a system exists here and it works.I walked through immigration and customs without any delays, changed dollars to yen, took a train to the city and then metro to the hotel - all in an hour. The room was small, no food items were available there. I got some tea from a vending unit outside the room. Food was the most difficult problem there. Only rice and bread were vegetarian. So I had to survive five days on these plus tea. I accepted that situation. So far everything is going smooth except food.
Next day, I walked to the venue and registered for the conference. I had several TC7 meetings scheduled in addition to my presentation. The transport systems were very good. You could go by metro to various parts of Tokyo. That was faster than taxi services. Traveling was very easy.
I met two Indians - Prof Keshava Rao from the US and Mr Arumugam doing PhD in France. One student working for his PhD in the Tokyo university invited all three of us for dinner in his house. We went to his house, had rice and sambar, a great change. The student's name was Swamy. He joined REC Trichy after completing PhD.
Mr Arumugam joined Madurai university after completing his PhD. I will discuss it later.
Tokyo weather was pleasant. It was raining at regular intervals.We saw lots of people walking and shopping even in midst of rains. They carried an umbrella and left the umbrella outside shops using a plastic cover to close the umbrella so that no water puddles form at the entrance. They were careful and considerate.
One day, I wanted to change dollars to yen. I walked into a branch of a bank. They were embarrassed and mumbled something to me. I didn't understand. They said that I should go to another bank to get dollars. I was unusually persistent and asked them, why? A senior officer came and told me that their system wasn't working and so they were directing me to another branch. I learnt a lot from this experience:
1. They are very polite and helpful unlike our branches.
2. Tech failed there also but they weren't making a big problem of it. They handled it nicely and quietly.
3. All were motivated to serve you and help you.In Indian bank, a clerk will tell you that network is down and so get out. Politeness is rarely known with us.
I loved the total experiences in Japan that I had in every place. The people were polite, disciplined and followed systems meticulously. Let me give another example.
You wanted directions to go to a place. If you ask a person passing by you, he or she will stop, try to understand your request and show you the directions. Some will even write them on a paper and give it to us. They know English, but can't speak fluently. But no one told me "gothilla" rudely. 
We went on a sightseeing tour. The hostess made jokes about their lifestyle. She said Japanese women wear four rings: engagement ring, wedding ring, boring and suffering.
Even today, I meet and see several Japanese here in our apartment complex. They are polite and nice. Their behavior hasn't changed over 60 years. We have changed a lot, become tense, uneasy, worried, and unhappy- a lot of changes I see in us. This is a very characteristic situation with us- say everything was better in the past and changed to the worse now. 
I read it in UV Swaminatha iyer's autobiography titled ' en Charithram' written in the 19th century. He says ,'his village was peaceful and people lived happily during his childhood and it changed a lot when he was 80 years old- that was the time he started writing his autobiography.'
I also feel that my village was a peaceful, happy, and harmonious place during my childhood and has changed into a crowded, tense and uncertain place today.
So everything went off well and smoothly without any problem.
Greetings. Dks
11/04/26, 4:24 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.  1042

Let me continue with my trip from Tokyo to LA.
So after the conference, I left for the airport, by metro,and then by train and lastly by bus. Every body followed the queue cheerfully. It was a new experience- no complaints like our desis.I took a longer flight from Tokyo to LA crossing the Pacific ocean and international dateline. Food was vegetarian, mostly vegan except milk powder given to us for coffee or tea. Even for that, they gave a whitener substitute. I got lots of Tofu, vegetables, fruits, fruit juices and tea.
We reached Seattle first. Then I took a local flight after immigration and customs. The immigration guy was given a computer and was struggling to key in my name. He was cursing the computer. He asked me "what are you doing?" I said , "I work on that wretched computer." He laughed at it. Those days, there were no caged counters for immigration people and they were friendly.
The next flight reached LA via San Jose at 4pm. As soon as I came out of the aircraft into the gate, I saw Srivatsan. I was relieved. I picked up my suitcase and he took me to his house located in the suburb- Burbank.
Both Srivatsan his wife were good and simple people. They followed the Indian traditions sincerely in LA. They were religious.They were great hosts. I had a lot of discussions with him every day. We definitely talked about the institute- faculty, his supervisor, his departmental people who were living in the neighborhood and others. He told me that Prof ES Rajagopal stayed with him. Then we spent a lot of time discussing his research and research in general. He felt that research in India needed a lot of changes. We argued about it. 
 I went to the post office to post the T shirts. It was a good experience of organization. I picked up a paper token and waited for my turn. Then I got a big and thick envelope, put the T shirt in it, sealed it, wrote the address and gave it to the counter person. I paid for envelope and postal charges and it was done. It was a simple task done pleasantly. It was a contrast to my experience in the Italian post office in Trieste. There there counter person was talking to other staff even while serving me, just like India. But both in England and the US, I saw professional approaches in all public offices like banks, post office etc.
I visited USC first on a Saturday. Since a football match was scheduled that day, it took me 40 minutes to travel a mile.
 I met CP Ravikumar first and he took me to meet Raghu. Raghu introduced me to another student of mine, Ram Chellapa, another faculty in the CS department. The EECS department was a big one with thousands of graduate students. I also met Dr Prasanna and Dr Sastry. My lecture was fixed on the next working day. Prasanna invited to his house for dinner. I completed my talk, spent time with others. Then I went out on the road to buy coffee. After buying coffee, I was returning back to the department. Suddenly two people started chasing me. I ran and got into the building before they caught up with me. I was lucky that it happened near the building and I could react fast and get into the building.

12/04/26, 4:59 am - d k Subramanian Whatsapp: Post.    1043
A query from Prof Mahendra Babu
It is  a suspense to know who chased  you and why?Thanks👍🙏
Mr Srinivasan ,PNB also raised this query. 
There is no mystery here. USC was located downtown LA. The intention of those chasers was to beat me and take away money from me. It was an attempted mugging incident.  That was the reason, I assume. I didn't check on this afterwards.
Let me discuss my tech exposure in USC.I got two big surprises.One was the modernized communications in the university. I had seen that most universities in the west had a good communications network- land lines and many newsletters. The library would house most reports of the university. So transparency was good. I saw it in University of Manchester. ICTP, Trieste, and later in AIT, Bangkok. But here in USC, I saw that most faculty and students communicated using email. The general emails came later. But they used Unix email feature.All faculty and students had a computer and they were connected to the central server. They used computers for calculations and preparing technical papers. This concept of campus connectivity impressed me. It took more than 15 years for this connected institution concept to percolate into a few Indian institutions connected by ERNET and campus networks. It was initially telephone lines and dialup , poor system. The main hurdle was our slow movement towards building a strong fibre network. We were yet to get dial up modem. That came up only by mid 1990s. We couldn't get dialup connections in most highschools by 2000 and our Mahithisindu program suffered a lot due to not being able to access internet.
Broadband came in small steps only by 2000. Fortunately, the telecom department and BSNL took the high bandwidth copper and optical network interconnecting most cities and towns seriously and implemented it by 2004.
It worries me a lot to think that:
'We ape western development models and technology after a long time gap without thinking and considering their models being to appropriate to India.'
Recall that the US went ahead prioritizing road and air transport, destroying the railways. It invested heavily in highways and freeways and airports. For them, infrastructure meant a huge network of highways and airports. 
We are initially aware of the high costs of highways- they need land and heavy maintenance. So we moved to railways. But, now, we are investing very heavily in building a massive highways network during the past decade and praise Mr Gadkari. But do we realize that highways don't solve the traffic snarls. Also highways island villages along the route. 
I am happy that while we invested heavily in highways and airports, we didn't ignore railways. We have improved travel speed and comfort through many Vande Bharat trains and gone for separate goods transport corridors.Our canal transport network needs more attention, particularly for transporting goods.
But we completely missed the bus and miss it even now- what is that? We never thought that telecom is a bigger and more important infrastructure than roads. Telecom moved beyond voice communication to information and data transfers.Information access is more beneficial than physical movement to cities.We missed the Telecom highway and Radio highway. We didn't modernize them for several decades depending on the old technology of ITI and a small digital exchange of CDOT. Fortunately, the opening of the country in 1991 brought in new players. Our electronics exchanges became modern and reliable and inexpensive. But our network was with BSNL. Even air tel, Tata couldn't do anything to improve the infrastructure. All private companies believed that infrastructure is the government business. Only reliance jio changed that attitude.
Communications was going through a lot of multidimensional changes like computers in the eighties and nineties. The US had high bandwidth networks, digital networks, VSATs and satellite communications , optical fibres, and mobile networks. But our efforts were minimal during this period. Only by 2000, we picked up speed to introduce digital networks, fibre networks, and mobile communications. We started understanding the importance of communications.
More in the next post.

Mr Bala Ajjampur has more comments
Bala Ajjampur: Sir, regardless of from where the growth in telecommunications came from, optical fibre network  has become a chaotic deployment in Bangalore.  While the purpose is served, the way the infrastructure was allowed to get deployed is big failure on the part of the government,  whether the power to impose regulations resided in state or central.
How can someone normalise these types of infrastructure developments that are totally at the mercy of private enterprises?
My response 
I am an utilization focused researcher, but don't ignore theory, modeling and solutions.
I am always a student trying to learn how technology developed and how it gets used or absorbed. I did it with electricity first, computers afterwards and also environment and energy uses. This trip gave me opportunities to enhance my knowledge of technology and development to a great extent, particularly the pros and cons. I found many similarities and many differences with India vs the US.
 I didn't get much opportunity to observe the tech scene in Tokyo. Yes, they were far ahead- most devices produced here, banks automated, communications great, most commerce used computers. Industries used computers effectively for control and data generation. Utilities were advanced in computer usage. But didn't see much of software work and PC based uses. Terminals were used everywhere. So it was a very good and efficient connected system which we can see, use and experience in Tokyo. I saw a holistic approach in development, manufacture and use, a good aspect we should learn. There were no silo usage patterns, no egos preventing tech absorption. Tech is accepted by all in Japan. Their approach is very different from ours- very planned, thought about, organized and holistic. Ours - unorganized , un planned, chaotic, uneven, egotistic.
Those, mentioned above are the primary reasons for Bangalore's development. While water was with the state government, they did a lot of studies and planned well. Arcavaty followed by Cauvery supplied water to the city. We are comfortable now.
The luck with electricity was - when sharavathy valley project came up in the sixties, there was a large surplus power available. So most- about 600 to 700 MW came to Bangalore through many 220kv lines in the sixties. So SRS was planned for about 30years. So both were planned reasonably well. But the distribution of both were chaotic. Same is true of roads, transport and telecom. 
Bala, you are right. Telecom falls between many stools. State has no role. TRAI , TRI and DoT regulate and delay the development. They live in the old times. Privitazation helped a lot. Our broadband improved considerably with private players. But mobile changed the scene by 2002.Bala , what you say as chaotic is the cable TV lines mostly and less of fibre optic lines. After a short period all fibre cables went underground. But they were cut by many agencies. I had seen a bank becoming non operational due to the loss of total data centre connectivity. This barbaric system of regular road cutting should be banned by the supreme court, I feel. We need underground tunnels to handle water, power,gas, sewerage etc connections, not the primitive system of cutting the roads that we have now. 
 Fibre was planned well. Bangalore had been far ahead compared to other cities - whether copper, optical, digital, satellite, or VSAT or data and control centers. We had data and internet centers of BSNL by 2000.I had visited them. Similarly many data centres came up in Bangalore - BSNL, Wipro, Tata, Reliance etc- came up by 2000.
Greetings. Dks
